Amsler grid

оАЭe Amsler grid evaluates the 20¬∞ of the visual оАЮeld centred on
оАЮxation (Fig. 1.8). It is principally useful in screening for and monitoring macular disease, but will also demonstrate central visual
оАЮeld defects originating elsewhere. Patients with a substantial risk
of macular neovascularization (MNV) should be provided with an
Amsler grid for regular use at home.

TIP An Amsler grid is a simple and easy method of monitoring
central visual оАЮeld and is commonly abnormal in patients with
macular disease.

Charts

оАЭere are seven charts, each consisting of a 10-cm outer square
(Figs 1.9 and 1.10).
оАБ Chart 1 consists of a white grid on a black background, the
outer grid enclosing 400 smaller 5-mm squares. When viewed
at about one-third of a metre, each small square subtends an
angle of 1¬∞.
оАБ Chart 2 is similar to chart 1 but has diagonal lines that aid оАЮxation for patients with a central scotoma.
оАБ Chart 3 is identical to chart 1 but has red squares. оАЭe redon-black design aims to stimulate long wavelength foveal
cones. It is used to detect subtle colour scotomas and desaturation in toxic maculopathy, optic neuropathy and chiasmal
lesions.
оАБ Chart 4 consists only of random dots and is used mainly to distinguish scotomas from metamorphopsia, as there is no form
to be distorted.
оАБ Chart 5 consists of horizontal lines and is designed to detect
metamorphopsia along speciоАЮc meridians. It is of particular
use in the evaluation of patients describing diоАЪculty reading.
оАБ Chart 6 is similar to chart 5 but has a white background and
the central lines are closer together, enabling more detailed
evaluation.
оАБ Chart 7 includes a оАЮne central grid, each square subtending an
angle of a half degree, and is more sensitive.

Fig. 1.9 Amsler grid chart.

Technique

оАЭe pupils should not be dilated, and in order to avoid a photostress eоАЫect, the eyes should not yet have been examined on the slit
lamp. A presbyopic refractive correction should be worn if appropriate. оАЭe chart should be well illuminated and held at a comfortable reading distance, optimally around 33 cm.
оАБ One eye is covered.
оАБ оАЭe patient is asked to look directly at the central dot with the
uncovered eye, to keep looking at this, and to report any distortion or waviness of the lines on the grid.
оАБ Reminding the patient to maintain оАЮxation on the central dot,
he or she is asked if there are blurred areas or blank spots anywhere on the grid. Patients with macular disease oоАЬen report that
the lines are wavy whereas those with optic neuropathy tend to
remark that some of the lines are missing or faint but not distorted.
оАБ оАЭe patient is asked if he or she can see all four corners and all
four sides of the square вАУ a missing corner or border should
raise the possibility of causes other than macular disease, such
as glaucomatous оАЮeld defects or retinitis pigmentosa.
оАБ оАЭe patient may be provided with a recording sheet and pen
and asked to draw any anomalies (Fig. 1.11).

Light brightness comparison test

оАЭis is a test of optic nerve function, which is usually normal in
early and moderate retinal disease. It is performed as follows:
оАБ A light from an indirect ophthalmoscope is shone оАЮrst into the
normal eye and then the eye with suspected disease.
оАБ оАЭe patient is asked whether the light is symmetrically bright
in both eyes.
оАБ In optic neuropathy the patient will report that the light is less
bright in the aоАЫected eye.
оАБ оАЭe patient is asked to assign a relevant value from 1 to 5 to
the brightness of the light in the diseased eye, in comparison
to the normal eye.

Photostress test

Fig. 1.11 Stylized Amsler recording sheet showing wavy lines
indicating metamorphopsia, and a dense scotoma.

оАБ Principles. Photostress testing is a gross test of dark adaptation in which the visual pigments are bleached by light.
оАЭis causes a temporary state of retinal insensitivity perceived
by the patient as a scotoma. оАЭe recovery of vision is dependent on the ability of the photoreceptors to re-synthesize visual
pigment. оАЭe test may be useful in detecting maculopathy when

ophthalmoscopy is equivocal, as in mild cystoid macular oedema
or central serous retinopathy. It may also diоАЫerentiate visual loss
caused by macular disease from that caused by an optic nerve lesion.
оАБ Techniques

{ оАЭe best-corrected distance VA is determined.

{ оАЭe patient оАЮxates on the light of a pen torch or an indirect
ophthalmoscope held about 3 cm away for about 10 seconds (Fig. 1.12A).

{ оАЭe photostress recovery time (PSRT) is the time taken to
read any three letters of the pre-test acuity line and is normally between 15 and 30 seconds (Fig. 1.12B).

{ оАЭe test is performed on the other, presumably normal, eye
and the results are compared.

{ оАЭe PSRT is prolonged relative to the normal eye in macular disease (sometimes 50 seconds or more) but not in an
optic neuropathy.

retinal dystrophies prior to the impairment of other visual parameters. Colour vision depends on three populations of retinal cones,
each with a speciоАЮc peak sensitivity: blue (tritan) at 414вАУ424 nm,
green (deuteran) at 522вАУ539 nm and red (protan) at 549вАУ570 nm.
Normal colour perception requires all these primary colours to
match those within the spectrum. Any given cone pigment may
be deоАЮcient (e.g. protanomaly вАУ red weakness) or entirely absent
(e.g. protanopia вАУ red blindness). Trichromats possess all three
types of cones (although not necessarily functioning perfectly),
while absence of one or two types of cones renders an individual
a dichromat or monochromat, respectively. Most individuals with
congenital colour defects are anomalous trichromats and use
abnormal proportions of the three primary colours to match those
in the light spectrum. оАЭose with redвАУgreen deоАЮciency caused
by abnormality of red-sensitive cones are protanomalous (2%
of men), those with abnormality of green-sensitive cones (6% of
men) are deuteranomalous and those with blueвАУgreen deоАЮciency
caused by abnormality of blue-sensitive cones are tritanomalous.
Acquired macular disease tends to produce blueвАУyellow defects,
and optic nerve lesions, redвАУgreen defects.

TIP Children who are found to have a congenital colour vision
deоАЮciency will оАЮnd it diоАЪcult or impossible to engage in certain
occupations and may beneоАЮt from early career counselling.

Colour vision tests

оАБ оАЮe Ishihara test is designed to screen for congenital protan
and deuteran defects. It is simple, widely available and frequently used to screen for a redвАУgreen colour vision deоАЮcit.

Colour vision testing

Introduction

Assessment of colour vision is useful in the evaluation of optic
nerve disease and in determining the presence of a congenitally
anomalous colour defect. Congenital colour vision deоАЮciency
(CVD) is a common X-linked recessive condition that aоАЫects
about 8% of Northern European men and 0.5% of women. оАЭe
prevalence in Black Africans is about 4% for men and 1.5% for
women. CVD is the decreased ability to see colour or diоАЫerences in
colour along a redвАУgreen axis. It can impair tasks such as choosing
clothing and seeing traоАЪc lights. Dyschromatopsia may develop in

8 Psychophysical Tests

A B

C

D

Fig. 1.13 Colour vision tests. (A) Ishihara; (B) City University; (C) HardyвАУRandвАУRittler; (D)
FarnsworthвАУMunsell 100-hue test.

оАЭe test can be used to assess optic nerve function. It consists
of a test plate followed by 16 plates, each with a matrix of dots
arranged to show a central shape or number that the subject is
asked to identify (Fig. 1.13A). A colour-deоАЮcient person will
be able to identify only some of the оАЮgures. Inability to identify
the test plate (provided VA is suоАЪcient) indicates non-organic
visual loss.
оАБ оАЮe City University test consists of 10 plates, each containing
a central colour and four peripheral colours (Fig. 1.13B) from
which the subject is asked to choose the closest match.
оАБ оАЮe HardyвАУRandвАУRittler test is similar to the Ishihara, but
can detect all three congenital colour defects (Fig. 1.13C).
оАБ оАЮe FarnsworthвАУMunsell 100-hue test is a sensitive but
longer test for both congenital and acquired colour defects.
Despite the name, it consists of 85 caps of diоАЫerent hues in
four racks (Fig. 1.13D). оАЭe subject is asked to rearrange randomized caps in order of colour progression, and the оАЮndings
are recorded on a circular chart. Each of the three forms of
dichromatism is characterized by failure in a speciоАЮc meridian
of the chart (Fig. 1.14).

Plus lens test

A temporary hypermetropic shiоАЬ may occur in some conditions
due to an elevation of the sensory retina вАУ the classic example is
central serous chorioretinopathy (CSR). A +1.00-dioptre lens will
demonstrate the phenomenon.

PERIMETRY

DeоАЯnitions

оАБ оАЮe visual оАЯeld can be represented as a three-dimensional
structure akin to a hill of increasing sensitivity (Fig. 1.15A).
оАЭe outer aspect extends approximately 50¬∞ superiorly, 60¬∞
nasally, 70¬∞ inferiorly and 90¬∞ temporally. VA is sharpest
at the very top of the hill (i.e. the fovea) and then declines
progressively towards the periphery, the nasal slope being
steeper than the temporal. оАЭe вАШbottomless pitвАЩ of the blind
spot is located temporally between 10¬∞ and 20¬∞, slightly below
the horizontal.
оАБ An isopter is a line connecting points of the same sensitivity,
and on a two-dimensional isopter plot encloses an area within
which a stimulus of a given strength is visible. When the оАЮeld
is represented as a hill, isopters resemble the contour lines on
a map (Fig. 1.15B).
оАБ A scotoma is an area of reduced (вАШrelativeвАЩ) or total (вАШabsoluteвАЩ)
loss of vision surrounded by a seeing area.
оАБ Luminance is the intensity or вАШbrightnessвАЩ of a light stimulus,
measured in apostilbs (asb). A higher intensity stimulus has a
higher asb value; this is related inversely to sensitivity.
оАБ A logarithmic rather than a linear scale is used for stimulus
intensity and sensitivity, so that for each log unit intensity
changes by a factor of 10. With a log scale, greater signiоАЮcance
is given to the lower end of the intensity range. оАЭe normal eye
has a very large sensitivity range, and assessment of the lower
end of the scale is of critical signiоАЮcance so that early damage

can be detected. With a linear scale, the lower end would be
reduced to a very small portion of a graphical chart axis. оАЭe
visual system itself operates on close to a logarithmic scale,
so using this method more closely matches the physiological
situation.
оАБ Decibels. Simple log units are not used in clinical perimetry,
but rather вАШdecibelsвАЩ (dB), where 10 dB = 1 log unit. Decibels
are not true units of luminance but a representation, and vary
between visual оАЮeld machines. Perimetry usually concentrates on the eyeвАЩs sensitivity rather than the stimulus intensity. оАЭerefore, the decibel reading goes up as retinal sensitivity
increases, which obviously corresponds to reducing intensity
of the perceived stimulus. оАЭis makes the assessment of visual
оАЮelds more intuitive, as a higher number corresponds with
higher retinal sensitivity. If the sensitivity of a test location is
20 dB (= 2 log units), a point with a sensitivity of 30 dB would
be the more sensitive. оАЭe blind spot has a sensitivity of 0 dB.
If, on a given machine, seeing a stimulus of 1000asb gives a
value of 10 dB, a stimulus of 100asb will give 20 dB.
оАБ DiоАЭerential light sensitivity represents the degree by which
the luminance of a target must exceed background luminance
in order to be perceived. оАЭe visual оАЮeld is therefore a threedimensional representation of diоАЫerential light sensitivity at
diоАЫerent points.
оАБ оАЮreshold at a given location in the visual field is the
brightness of a stimulus at which it can be detected by the
subject. It is defined as вАШthe luminance of a given fixedlocation stimulus at which it is seen on 50% of the occasions it is presentedвАЩ. In practice we usually talk about an
eyeвАЩs sensitivity at a given point in the field rather than the
stimulus intensity. The threshold sensitivity is highest at
the fovea and decreases progressively towards the periphery. After the age of 20 years the sensitivity decreases by
about 1 dB per 10 years.
оАБ Background luminance. оАЭe retinal sensitivity at any location
varies depending on background luminance. Rod photoreceptors are more sensitive in dim light than cones, and so owing to
their preponderance in the peripheral retina, at lower (scotopic)

light levels the peripheral retina becomes more sensitive in proportion to the central retina. оАЭe hill of vision оАЯattens, with a
central crater rather than a peak at the fovea due to the high
concentration of cones, which have low sensitivity in scotopic
conditions. Some diseases give markedly diоАЫerent оАЮeld results
at diоАЫerent background luminance levels, e.g. in retinitis pigmentosa the оАЮeld is usually much worse with low background
luminance. It should be noted that it takes about 5 minutes
to adapt from darkness to bright sunlight and 20вАУ30 minutes
from bright sunlight to darkness. оАЭe HFA (see below) uses a
photopic (preferentially cone) level of background luminance
at 31.5asb.
оАБ Static perimetry. A method of assessing оАЮelds, usually automated, in which the location of a stimulus remains оАЮxed, with
intensity increased until it is seen by the subject (threshold
is reached вАУ Fig. 1.16A) or decreased until it is no longer
detected.
оАБ Kinetic (dynamic) perimetry is now much less commonly
performed than static perimetry. A stimulus of constant intensity is moved from a non-seeing area to a seeing area (Fig.
1.16B) at a standardized speed until it is perceived, and the
point of perception is recorded on a chart. Points from diоАЫerent meridians are joined to plot an isopter for that stimulus
intensity. Stimuli of diоАЫerent intensities are used to produce a
contour map of the visual оАЮeld. Kinetic perimetry can be performed by means of a manual (Goldmann) or an automated
perimeter if the latter is equipped with an appropriate soоАЬware
program.
оАБ Manual perimetry involves presentation of a stimulus by the
perimetrist, with manual recording of the response. It was formerly the standard method of оАЮeld testing but has now largely
been superseded by automated methods. It is still used occasionally, particularly in cognitively limited patients unable
to interact adequately with an automated system, and for
dynamic testing of peripheral оАЮelds.
оАБ Standard automated perimetry (SAP) is the method used
routinely in most clinical situations. Automated perimeters
in common use include the Humphrey Field Analyser (HFA),

the Octopus, Medmont, Henson and Dicon (Figs 1.17 and
1.18). оАЭese predominantly utilize static testing, though
soоАЬware is available on some machines to perform dynamic
assessment.

TIP Visual оАЮeld results should always be used in conjunction
with the clinical оАЮndings.

Testing algorithms

Threshold

оАЭreshold perimetry is used for detailed assessment of the hill
of vision by plotting the threshold luminance value at various locations in the visual оАЮeld and comparing the results with
age-matched вАШnormalвАЩ values. A typical automated strategy is
to present a stimulus of higher than expected intensity. If seen,
the intensity is decreased in steps (e.g. 4 dB) until it is no longer
seen (вАШstaircasingвАЩ). оАЭe stimulus is then increased again (e.g. 2
dB steps) until seen once more (Fig. 1.19). If the stimulus is not
seen initially, its intensity is increased in steps until seen. Essentially, the threshold is crossed in one direction with large increments, then crossed again to вАШоАЮne-tuneвАЩ the result with smaller
increments. оАЭreshold testing is commonly used for monitoring
glaucoma.

Suprathreshold

Suprathreshold perimetry involves testing with stimuli of luminance above the expected normal threshold levels for an agematched population to assess whether these are detected. In other
words, testing to check that a subject can see stimuli that would be
seen by a normal person of the same age. It enables testing to be
carried out rapidly to indicate whether function is grossly normal
or not and is usually reserved for screening.

Fast algorithms

In recent years, strategies have been introduced with shorter testing times, providing eоАЪciency beneоАЮts with little or no detriment
to testing accuracy. оАЭe HFA oоАЫers the SITA (Swedish Interactive
оАЭresholding Algorithm), which uses a database of normal and
glaucomatous оАЮelds to estimate threshold values and takes responses
during the test into account to arrive at adjusted estimates throughout the test. Full-threshold values are obtained at the start of the test
for four points. SITA-Standard and SITA-Fast (Fig. 1.20) versions
are available. оАЭe Octopus Perimeter uses G-TOP (Glaucoma Tendency Oriented Perimetry), which estimates thresholds based on
information gathered from more detailed assessment of adjacent
points. TOP presents each stimulus once at each location, instead of
4вАУ6 times per location with a standard technique.

Testing patterns

оАБ Glaucoma

{ Most important defects in glaucoma occur centrally вАУ
within a 30¬∞ radius from the оАЮxation point вАУ so this is the
area most commonly tested.

{ 24-2 is a routinely used glaucoma-orientated pattern. вАШ24вАЩ
denotes the extent in degrees to which the оАЮeld is tested
on the temporal side (to 30¬∞ on the nasal side). оАЭe number aоАЬer the hyphen (2) describes the pattern of the points
tested. 30-2 is an alternative.

{ 10-2 is used to assess a central area of radius 10¬∞. Glaucomatous defects here may threaten central vision. оАЭe 10-2
pattern facilitates more detailed monitoring of the extent
of damage, especially in advanced glaucoma where there
is вАШsplitвАЩ оАЮxation.
оАБ Peripheral оАЯeld. Patterns that include central and peripheral
points (e.g. FF-120) are typically limited to the assessment of
neurological defects.

оАБ Binocular оАЯeld testing (e.g. Esterman strategy) is used to

Analysis

SAP provides the clinician with an array of clinically relevant information via monitor display or printout. оАЭe patientвАЩs name and age
are conоАЮrmed and a check made that any appropriate refractive
error compensation was used. General information should be
reviewed, such as the type of algorithm performed, the time taken
for the test and the order in which the eyes were tested.

Reliability indices

Reliability indices (see Fig. 1.20A) reоАЯect the extent to which the
patientвАЩs results are reliable. If signiоАЮcantly unreliable, further
evaluation of the visual оАЮeld printout is pointless. With SITA
strategies, false negatives or false positives over about 15% should
probably be regarded as signiоАЮcant and with full-threshold strategies, оАЮxation losses over 20% and false positives or negatives over
33%. In patients who consistently fail to achieve good reliability
it may be useful to switch to a suprathreshold strategy or kinetic
perimetry.
оАБ Fixation losses indicate steadiness of gaze during the test.
Methods of assessment include presentation of stimuli to the
blind spot to ensure no response is recorded, and the use of a
вАШgaze monitorвАЩ.
оАБ False positives are usually assessed by decoupling a stimulus
from its accompanying sound. If the sound alone is presented
and the patient still responds, a false positive is recorded. With
a high false-positive score the grey scale printout appears
abnormally pale (Fig. 1.21). In SITA testing, false positives are
estimated based on the response time.
оАБ False negatives are registered by presenting a stimulus much brighter than threshold at a location where the

threshold has already been determined. If the patient fails to
respond, a false negative is recorded. A high false-negative
score indicates inattention, tiredness or malingering, but is
occasionally an indication of disease severity rather than
unreliability. The grey scale printout in individuals with
high false-negative responses tends to have a clover leaf
shape (Fig. 1.22).

TIP If the reliability indices are poor, be careful when
interpreting the results of a visual оАЮeld test.

Sensitivity values

оАБ A numerical display (see Fig. 1.20B) gives the measured or estimated (depending on strategy) threshold in dB at each point. In
a full-threshold strategy, where the threshold is rechecked either
as routine or because of an unexpected (>5 dB) result, the second
result is shown in brackets next to the оАЮrst.
оАБ A grey scale represents the numerical display in graphical form
(see Fig. 1.20C) and is the simplest display modality to interpret: decreasing sensitivity is represented by darker tones вАУ
the physiological blind spot is a darker area in the temporal
оАЮeld typically just below the horizontal axis. Each change in
grey scale tone is equivalent to a 5 dB change in sensitivity at
that location.
оАБ Total deviation (see Fig. 1.20D) shows the diоАЫerence between
a test-derived threshold at a given point and the normal sensitivity at that point for the general population, correcting for
age. Negative values indicate lower than normal sensitivity,
positive values higher than normal.
оАБ Pattern deviation (see Fig. 1.20E) is derived from total deviation values adjusted for any generalized decrease in sensitivity
in the overall оАЮeld (e.g. lens opacity), and demonstrates localized defects.
оАБ Probability value plots of the total and pattern deviation (see
Fig. 1.20G and H) are a representation of the percentage (<5%
to <0.5%) of the normal population in whom the measured
defect at each point would be expected. Darker symbols represent a greater likelihood that a defect is signiоАЮcant.

Summary values

Summary values (вАШglobal indicesвАЩ on the HFA вАУ see Fig. 1.20F)
represent distilled statistical information, which considers agematched normal data. оАЭese values are principally used to monitor progression of glaucomatous damage rather than for initial
diagnosis.
оАБ Visual оАЯeld index (VFI) in the HFA is a measure of the
patientвАЩs overall visual оАЮeld function expressed as a percentage,
the normal age-adjusted value being 100%.
оАБ Mean deviation (MD) on the HFA (mean defect on the Octopus) gives an indication of the overall sensitivity of the оАЮeld. It
is derived from averaging the total deviation values.

CHAPTER

Examination Techniques 17
#### 1

оАБ Pattern standard deviation (PSD) is a measure of focal loss
or variability within the оАЮeld and considers any generalized
depression in the hill of vision. An increased PSD is therefore a more speciоАЮc indicator of glaucomatous damage than
MD.
оАБ Loss variance (LV) is a summary measure on the Octopus
Perimeter similar to PSD.
оАБ Probability values. Abnormal summary values are followed
by a probability value, representing the percentage likelihood
that an abnormal value of this level will occur in a normal
subject. оАЭe lower the P value, the more likely the result is
abnormal.
оАБ оАЭe glaucoma hemiоАЯeld test (GHT) compares corresponding
areas in the superior and inferior hemiоАЮelds and relates only
to glaucoma.

Computed analysis of serial оАЯelds

Computed analysis of serial visual оАЮelds for progression is in widespread use. A disadvantage is the requirement for several reliable
оАЮelds to be carried out before analysis is eоАЫective. оАЭe quality of
available soоАЬware has been improving steadily, with integrated
programs such as GPA (Guided Progression Analysis) on the HFA
and several trend analysis options on the Octopus.

High-sensitivity оАЯeld modalities

SAP tends to detect оАЮeld damage only aоАЬer substantial ganglion
cell loss is established. Attempts at detecting change at an earlier
stage include the adoption of stimuli intended to target speciоАЮc
ganglion cell types.
оАБ Short-wave automated perimetry (SWAP) uses a blue stimulus on a yellow background. Sensitivity to blue light (mediated
by blue cone photoreceptors) is adversely aоАЫected relatively
early in glaucoma. SWAP is more sensitive to early glaucomatous defects but has not been widely adopted because cataract
decreases sensitivity to blue light (the brunescent lens acts as a
yellow оАЮlter) and patients frequently dislike the lengthy test. It
is available on newer HFA models.
оАБ Frequency-doubling test (FDT). Large diameter axon (magnocellular) ganglion cells appear to be preferentially lost in
early glaucoma. оАЭe frequency-doubling illusion is produced
when a low spatial frequency sinusoidal grating undergoes
high temporal frequency counter phase оАЯicker (>15Hz). оАЭe
rapid alternation in which the light bars become dark and vice
versa produces the illusion of the grating having doubled its
frequency; magnocellular ganglion cells are believed to mediate the pathways used. Screening (Fig. 1.23) and extended testing (Humphrey Matrix) perimeter versions are available, the
latter being suitable for detailed assessment and monitoring of
glaucoma. Fig. 1.23 Screening frequency-doubling perimeter with display.

18 Perimetry

Sources of error

оАБ Inexperienced or unskilled technician. оАЭough less important with SAP than manual perimetry, correctly setting up the
test, explaining the procedure to and reassuring the patient
and monitoring performance are fundamental to obtaining an
accurate оАЮeld.
оАБ Incorrect patient details. оАЭe patientвАЩs date of birth must be
entered correctly to facilitate appropriate normative database
matching.
оАБ Poor patient performance.
оАБ Uncorrected refractive error can cause a signiоАЮcant decrease
in central sensitivity. If a patient with hypermetropia who usually wears contact lenses is tested wearing spectacles, this will
have the eоАЫect of magnifying and enlarging any scotomas as
compared with contact lenses. Most perimetry is performed
with a stimulus at approximately reading distance, so a near
correction should be used for individuals who are presbyopic.
оАБ Spectacle rim artefact. Spectacles can cause rim scotomas if
small aperture lenses are used or if incorrectly dispensed (Fig.
1.24A). Narrow-aperture trial frame lenses are unsuitable for
perimetry.
оАБ Miosis decreases sensitivity in the peripheral оАЮeld and
increases variability in the central оАЮeld in both normal and
glaucomatous eyes. Pupils less than 3mm in diameter should
therefore be dilated prior to perimetry; a consistent mydriatic
should be used for serial tests.
оАБ Media opacities (usually cataract) can have a profound eоАЫect,
exaggerated by miosis.
оАБ Ptosis, even if mild, can suppress the superior visual оАЮeld (Fig.
1.24B). Similar eоАЫects result from dermatochalasis, prominent
eyelashes and deeply set eyes.

оАБ Inadequate retinal adaptation may lead to error if perimetry
is performed soon aоАЬer ophthalmoscopy.

TIP Perimetry is a subjective examination and is not always
reliable.

Microperimetry

оАБ Microperimetry is a visual оАЮeld test that measures retinal sensitivity and оАЮxation behaviour in patients with macular disease and
focal glaucoma involving the central visual оАЮeld. It allows an exact
correlation between pathology involving the macula and the corresponding functional abnormality. Microperimetry is more sensitive than SAP in detecting subtle abnormality of visual function.
оАБ оАЭe MAIA perimeter is a table-top instrument (Fig. 1.25). оАЭe
test is usually undertaken aоАЬer 20 minutes of adaptation in
mesopic conditions. Fundus tracking occurs utilizing a linescanning laser ophthalmoscope (SLO), with super-luminescent
diode illumination using a central wavelength of 850nm.
оАБ Goldmann size 3 stimuli are projected onto the central 9¬∞ of
the fundus. A 4-2 staircase thresholding technique is used, with
each stimulus applied for 200 milliseconds against a white background. Using this instrument normal retinal sensitivity is 18dB.
оАБ Results are printed, together with reliability indices (оАЮxation
loss), probabilities and retinal sensitivity values, which are
colour-coded. Microperimetry is particularly useful in assessing the eоАЫect of existing and future therapeutic interventions
on the macula (Fig. 1.26). It can also be helpful in patients with
early glaucoma, particularly when there is subtle change close
to оАЮxation.

20 Slit Lamp Biomicroscopy of the Anterior Segment

Fig. 1.26 Abnormal microperimetry in a patient with geographic atrophy of the macula.

SLIT LAMP BIOMICROSCOPY OF THE
ANTERIOR SEGMENT

оАЭe purpose of slit lamp examination of the cornea and anterior
segment is to determine the position, depth and size of any abnormality (Fig. 1.27).

Direct illumination

Direct illumination with a diоАЫuse light is used to detect gross
abnormalities:
оАБ A narrow obliquely directed slit beam is used to visualize a
cross-section of the cornea.

Direct illumination

Scleral scatter

Retroillumination

Fig. 1.27 Technique of slit lamp biomicroscopy of the anterior
segment.

оАБ Further narrowing of the beam to a very thin optical section
moved across the cornea can determine the depth of a lesion.
оАБ оАЭe height of the coaxial beam can be adjusted to measure the horizontal and vertical size of a lesion or associated epithelial defect.
оАБ оАЭe use of a red-free оАЮlter makes red objects appear black, thereby
increasing contrast when observing vascular structures. A cobalt
blue оАЮlter is normally used in conjunction with оАЯuorescein.

Scleral scatter

Scleral scatter involves decentering the slit beam laterally so that
the light is incident on the limbus with the microscope focused
centrally. Light is transmitted within the cornea by total internal reоАЯection. A corneal stromal lesion will become illuminated
because of forward light scatter. оАЭis technique is especially useful
to detect subtle stromal haze, or cellular or lipid inоАЮltration.

Retroillumination

Retroillumination uses reоАЯected light from the iris or fundus aоАЬer
pupil dilatation to illuminate the cornea. оАЭis allows the detection
of оАЮne epithelial and endothelial changes, such as epithelial cysts,
keratic precipitates and small blood vessels.

Specular reоАЮection

Specular reоАЯection shows abnormalities of the endothelium such
as reduced cell density and guttata. Pseudoguttata probably represent reversible endothelial cell oedema and inоАЯammatory cells
beneath the endothelial layer.

CHAPTER

Examination Techniques 21
#### 1

Portable slit lamp

A hand-held slit lamp allows high-quality examination of the anterior segment in individuals who cannot be examined at a regular
slit lamp and is particularly useful when performing eye examinations out of a hospital setting (Fig. 1.28).

FUNDUS EXAMINATION

Direct ophthalmoscopy

оАБ A hand-held slit lamp direct examination of the structures
of the fundus using an ophthalmoscope can reveal disease of
the eye itself or may reveal an abnormality indicative of disease elsewhere in the body (for example: diabetes, systematic
hypertension, raised intracranial pressure). оАЭe major advantage of direct ophthalmoscopy is that it can be used at the bedside. оАЭe image obtained is signiоАЮcantly magniоАЮed (15 times
normal) (Fig. 1.29).
оАБ оАЭe direct ophthalmoscope can be used to examine the вАШredвАЩ
reоАЯex using retroillumination. By placing a +15D lens in position and looking at the fundus at a distance of 15вАУ20cm, lens
and vitreous opacities can be detected (Fig. 1.30).
оАБ оАЭe light beam can be used to (a) illuminate the cornea, allowing detection of a foreign body, (b) check the pupil for irregularity and (c) determine the light reоАЯexes. Some ophthalmoscopes
incorporate a cobalt blue оАЮlter, which allows corneal abrasions
and ulcers to be seen aоАЬer the insertion of оАЯuorescein.

TIP A direct ophthalmoscope can be used at the bedside
and provides a magniоАЮed view of the fundus, but there is no
stereopsis and the оАЮeld of view is small.

Technique

оАБ It is preferable to dilate the pupil if there is no contraindication. However, a good view can be obtained through an undilated pupil by darkening the examination room and by asking
the patient to look into the distance.
оАБ When examining the right eye, the clinician should stand at
the patientвАЩs right side, holding the ophthalmoscope in the
right hand. When examining the leоАЬ eye, the clinician should
stand at the leоАЬ side, holding the ophthalmoscope in the leоАЬ
hand and using the leоАЬ eye (Fig. 1.31).
оАБ оАЭe вАШOвАЩ on the illuminated lens dial of the ophthalmoscope
should be selected.
оАБ To overcome corneal reоАЯection, the small aperture can be used
and the light beam directed towards the edge of the pupil, rather
than through the centre. оАЭe linear polarizing оАЮlter may be helpful.
оАБ оАЭe examinerвАЩs free hand should rest on the patientвАЩs forehead.
оАБ оАЭe patient is slowly approached at a slight angle, from the
temporal side.
оАБ оАЭe light beam is directed at the pupil and the optic disc should
come into view at a distance of about 3.5cm from the eye. If the
disc is not in sharp focus, the ophthalmoscope lenses should
be rotated into the aperture using the index оАЮnger until the
disc becomes clearly visible.

Fig. 1.28 Portable slit lamp. (A) Technique; (B) Keeler PSL Classic instrument.

Fig. 1.29 MagniоАЮed image obtained from direct ophthalmoscope.

Fig. 1.30 Red reоАЭex showing anterior capsular thickening. Fig. 1.31 Technique of direct ophthalmoscopy (see text).

оАБ In hyperopic individuals use a plus lens (green) and in myopic
individuals a minus lens (red).
оАБ Examine the disc for clarity of outline, colour, elevation and
condition of vessels. Check for spontaneous venous pulsation.
Follow the vessels as far as possible into the retinal periphery.
оАБ To locate the macula, focus the light on the disc then move the
light about 2 disc diameters temporally. Alternatively, ask the
patient to look at the light.
оАБ оАЭe retinal periphery can be examined by asking the patient to
look up/down and to the sides.

Slit lamp biomicroscopy

A range of diagnostic contact and non-contact lenses are available for use with the slit lamp. Contact lenses should not be used
if a penetrating injury is suspected or in the presence of corneal
trauma, hyphaema or corneal infection.
оАБ Non-contact lenses

{ 60D. High-magniоАЮcation lens optimized for viewing the
posterior pole from a working distance of 13mm. When
estimating optic disc diameter use a correction factor of
√Ч0.88вАУ1.0 for the Volk lens and √Ч1.0 for the Nikon lens.

{ 90D. Wide-оАЮeld lens with lower magniоАЮcation and shorter
(7mm) working distance. Can be used with smaller pupils.
оАЭe correction factor is √Ч1.3 (Fig. 1.32).

{ 78D. Intermediate properties; ideal for general-purpose
examination. оАЭe correction factor is √Ч1.1.

{ Miscellaneous. Numerous other lenses are available, oоАЫering qualities such as a very wide оАЮeld of view and extremely
small pupil capability.

Technique

Indirect ophthalmoscopy utilizes a high-power convex lens which is
designed to obtain a wide оАЮeld of view of the fundus. оАЭe image is
vertically inverted and laterally reversed. оАЭe technique is as follows:
оАБ оАЭe slit beam is adjusted to a width of about one-quarter of its
full round diameter.
оАБ оАЭe illumination is set at an angle coaxial with the slit lamp
viewing system.
оАБ оАЭe magniоАЮcation and light intensity are adjusted to the lowest
settings.
оАБ оАЭe light beam should be centred to pass directly through the
patientвАЩs pupil.
оАБ оАЭe lens is held directly in front of the cornea just clearing the
lashes so that the light beam passes through its centre.
оАБ оАЭe fundus is examined by moving the joystick and vertical adjustment mechanism of the slit lamp whilst keeping the lens still.
оАБ MagniоАЮcation is increased to show greater detail.
оАБ To view the peripheral retina the patient should be instructed
to direct their gaze accordingly.

Using new technology for retinal
imaging in rural settings

In areas of the world with few specialist eye-care practitioners,
primary health-care workers can take retinal photographs with a

smartphone, using a clip-on device (Fig. 1.33). оАЭis can then be
sent electronically to a specialist centre for an opinion on the further management.
оАБ оАЭe Vula Mobile App, which has been fully evaluated in rural
settings in Africa, is a successful example of this technology.

Goldmann three-mirror examination

оАБ Goldmann three-mirror lens consists of four parts: the central lens and three mirrors set at diоАЫerent angles. Because the
curvature of the contact surface of the lens is steeper than that
of the cornea, a viscous coupling substance with the same
refractive index as the cornea is required to bridge the gap
between the cornea and the goniolens. It is important to be
familiar with each part of the lens, as follows (Fig. 1.34A):

{ оАЭe central part provides a 30¬∞ upright view of the posterior pole.

{ оАЭe equatorial mirror (largest and oblong-shaped) enables
visualization from 30¬∞ to the equator.

{ оАЭe peripheral mirror (intermediate in size and squareshaped) enables visualization between the equator and the
ora serrata.

{ The locking screw of the slit lamp is unlocked (Fig.
1.35A) to allow the illumination column to be tilted

{ оАЭe pupils are dilated.

{ It is therefore apparent that the smaller the mirror, the
more peripheral the view obtained.
оАБ Mirror positioning

{ оАЭe mirror should be positioned opposite the area of the
fundus to be examined. To examine the 12 oвАЩclock position
the mirror should be positioned at 6 oвАЩclock.

{ When viewing the vertical meridian, the image is upside
down but not laterally reversed, in contrast to indirect
ophthalmoscopy. Lesions located to the leоАЬ of 12 oвАЩclock
in the retina will therefore also appear in the mirror on the
leоАЬ-hand side (Fig. 1.34 B and C).

{ When viewing the horizontal meridian, the image is laterally reversed.

{ Anaesthetic drops are instilled.

{ Coupling оАЯuid (high viscosity methylcellulose or equivalent) is inserted into the cup of the contact lens; it should
be no more than half full.

{ оАЭe patient is asked to look up. оАЭe inferior rim of the lens
is inserted into the lower fornix (Fig. 1.36A) and quickly
pressed against the cornea so that the coupling оАЯuid is
retained (Fig. 1.36B).

{ оАЭe illumination column should always be tilted except
when viewing the 12 oвАЩclock position in the fundus (i.e.
with the mirror at 6 oвАЩclock).

{ When viewing horizontal meridians (i.e. 3 and 9 oвАЩclock
positions in the fundus) the column should remain central.

{ When viewing the vertical meridians (i.e. 6 and 12 oвАЩclock
positions) the column can be positioned leоАЬ or right of centre.

{ When viewing oblique meridians (i.e. 1.30 and 7.30 oвАЩclock)
the column is kept right of centre, and vice versa when viewing the 10.30 and 4.30 oвАЩclock positions (Fig. 1.37).

{ When viewing diоАЫerent positions of the peripheral retina,
the axis of the beam is rotated so that it is always at rightangles to the mirror.

{ To visualize the entire fundus the lens is rotated for 360¬∞ using
оАЮrst the equatorial mirror and then the peripheral mirrors.

{ To obtain a more peripheral view of the retina the lens
is tilted to the opposite side asking the patient to move
the eyes to the same side. For example, to obtain a more
peripheral view of 12 oвАЩclock (with mirrors at 6 oвАЩclock) tilt
the lens down and ask the patient to look up.

{ оАЭe vitreous cavity is examined with the central lens using
both a horizontal and a vertical slit beam.

{ оАЭe posterior pole is examined.

оАБ Scleral indentation at the slit lamp can be accomplished using
a three-mirror lens with a special attachment (Eisner funnel)
or a purpose-made ora serrata contact lens that combines a
mirror angled similarly to a gonioscopy lens with an incorporated attachment to facilitate scleral depression (e.g. Goldman
One Mirror Glass 904 lens).
оАБ Miscellaneous contact lenses are divided principally into
those conferring high magniоАЮcation for an optimal posterior
pole view, and those oоАЫering a wide оАЮeld of view, allowing
visualization extending to the ora serrata under optimal conditions. Small pupil capability is available, and a оАЯange is oоАЫered
on many lenses with the aim of improving stability of retention
and of lens position on the eye.

Head-mounted binocular indirect
ophthalmoscopy

оАЭe term binocular indirect ophthalmoscopy (BIO) is by convention used to refer to the head-mounted technique, though strictly
it also applies to slit lamp indirect ophthalmoscopy. BIO allows

26 Fundus Examination

retinal visualization through a greater degree of media opacity
than slit lamp biomicroscopy, and readily facilitates scleral indentation. Light is transmitted from the headset to the fundus through
a condensing lens held at the focal point of the eye, providing an
inverted and laterally reversed image that is observed through a
stereoscopic viewing system (Fig. 1.38A).
оАБ Lenses of various powers and diameters are available for BIO
(Fig. 1.38B). A lens of lower power confers increased magniоАЮcation, but a smaller оАЮeld of view. оАЭe magniоАЮcation can be
calculated by dividing the dioptric power of the examining
lens into 60. (For example, a 20 D lens would have a magniоАЮcation of 3.) Yellow оАЮlters may improve patient comfort.

{ 20 D (magniоАЮes √Ч3; оАЮeld about 45¬∞) is the most commonly
used for general examination of the fundus.

{ 28 D (magniоАЮcation with the head-mounted set of √Ч2.27;
оАЮeld of 53¬∞) has a shorter working distance and is useful
when examining patients with small pupils.

Fig. 1.37 The illumination column is tilted and positioned
right of centre to view the oblique meridian at 1.30 and 7.30
oвАЩclock.

examine small children. A broad fundus scan can be

{ Panretinal 2.2 combines magniоАЮcation similar to the 20 D

{ Ultra-high magniоАЮcation lenses for macula and optic disc

оАБ Technique

{ оАЭe patient should be supine on a bed or reclining chair
rather than sitting upright (Fig. 1.39A).

{ оАЭe pupils should be dilated. Reducing the ambient illumination is oоАЬen helpful as this improves contrast and allows
a lower incident light intensity to be used.

{ оАЭe eyepieces are set at the correct interpupillary distance
and the beam aligned so that it is located in the centre of
the viewing frame.

{ оАЭe patient is instructed to keep both eyes open at all
times. If necessary, the patientвАЩs eyelids are gently separated
with the оАЮngers.

{ оАЭe lens is taken into one hand with the оАЯat surface facing
the patient.

{ оАЭe peripheral fundus should be examined оАЮrst in order to
allow the patient to adapt to the light. оАЭe patient is asked
to move the eyes into optimal positions for examination,
e.g. looking away from the examiner to facilitate examination of the retinal periphery.

{ For the examination of small children (e.g. retinopathy
of prematurity вАУ see also Ch. 13) a speculum may be utilized to keep the eyelids apart, with an implement such as
a squint hook employed to direct the position of the eye.
оАБ Scleral indentation

{ оАЭe main function of scleral indentation (depression) is to
improve visualization of the retina anterior to the equator;
it also permits kinetic evaluation (Fig. 1.40).

{ Indentation should be attempted only aоАЬer the basic technique of BIO has been mastered. It requires practised

Fig. 1.38 (A) Principles of indirect ophthalmoscopy; (B) condensing lenses.

coordination between the relative position of the indenter
and the viewing apparatus, as well as care to prevent
patient discomfort. For example, to view the ora serrata at
12 oвАЩclock, the patient is asked to look down and the scleral
indenter (a cotton-tipped applicator is preferred by some
practitioners) is applied to the outside of the upper eyelid
at the margin of the tarsal plate.

{ With the indenter in place, the patient is asked to look up.
At the same time the indenter is advanced into the anterior orbit parallel with the globe, and the examinerвАЩs eyes
are aligned with the condensing lens and indenter (see Fig.
1.39B).

{ Gentle pressure is exerted so that a mound is created. AоАЬer
adequate viewing, the indenter is gently moved to an adjacent part of the fundus.

{ оАЭe indenter should be kept tangential to the globe at all
times, as perpendicular indentation will cause pain and
even risk perforation if the sclera is very thin.

{ For viewing the 3 and 9 oвАЩclock positions, indentation
directly on the sclera is sometimes necessary, facilitated by

Fundus drawing

When available, wide-оАЮeld photographic imaging can be an excellent aid in recording the features of a retinal detachment (Fig.
1.41), but documentation generally takes the form of a manually
drawn illustration that optimally is colour-coded (Fig. 1.42). If a
retinal detachment is found, the boundaries are drawn by starting
at the optic nerve and then extending to the periphery. Detached
retina is shaded in blue and оАЯat retina in red. оАЭe course of retinal vessels (usually veins) is indicated with blue. Retinal breaks are
drawn in red with blue outlines; the оАЯap of a retinal tear is also
drawn in blue. оАЭin retina may be represented by red hatching outlined in blue and lattice degeneration by blue hatching outlined

TONOMETRY

Goldmann tonometry
Principles

Goldmann applanation tonometry is based on the ImbertвАУFick
principle, which states that for an ideal, dry, thin-walled sphere,
the pressure inside the sphere (P) equals the force necessary to
оАЯatten its surface (F) divided by the area of оАЯattening (A) (i.e. P =
F/A). оАЭe intraocular pressure (IOP) is proportional to the pressure applied to the globe (in practice, the cornea) and the thickness of the wall of the globe (i.e. the thickness of the cornea, which
is variable). оАЭe human eye, however, is not an ideal sphere вАУ the
cornea is rigid and resists оАЯattening. Capillary attraction of the tear
meniscus, however, tends to pull the tonometer towards the cornea.

Fig. 1.42 Colour coding for retinal illustration.

Corneal rigidity and capillary attraction cancel each other out
when the оАЯattened area has a diameter of 3.06 mm, as in Goldmann
tonometry (Fig. 1.43A). оАЭe Goldmann tonometer is an accurate
variable-force tonometer consisting of a double prism (Fig. 1.43B).
оАЭe tonometer prism should be disinfected between patients and
replaced regularly in accordance with the manufacturerвАЩs instructions. Two per cent Na-hypochlorite (dilute bleach) oоАЫers eоАЫective
disinfection against adenovirus and herpes simplex virus. However,
disinfectants can cause the tonometer tip to swell and crack with
time, which can lead to disinfectant entering the tonometer tip
resulting in a corneal abrasion when applied to the cornea. Seventy
per cent isopropyl alcohol wipes do not oоАЫer protection against
viral infections. Disposable tonometer prisms and caps have been
introduced to address concerns of infection from reusable prisms.

Technique

оАБ Topical anaesthetic (commonly proxymetacaine 0.5%) and a
small amount of оАЯuorescein are instilled into the conjunctival sac.

A B

Fig. 1.43 Goldmann tonometry. (A) Physical principles; (B) tonometer.

оАБ оАЭe patient is positioned at the slit lamp with his or her forehead оАЮrmly against the headrest and instructed to look straight
ahead (oоАЬen at the examinerвАЩs opposite ear) and to breathe
normally.
оАБ With the cobalt blue оАЮlter in place and illumination of maximal intensity directed obliquely (approximately 60¬∞) at the
prism, the prism is centred in front of the apex of the cornea.
оАБ оАЭe dial is preset at 1 (i.e. 10 mmHg).
оАБ оАЭe prism is advanced until it just touches the apex of the cornea (Fig. 1.44A).
оАБ Viewing is switched to the ocular of the slit lamp.
оАБ A pattern of two green semi-circular mires will be seen, one
above and one below the horizontal midline, which represent
the оАЯuorescein-stained tear оАЮlm touching the upper and lower
outer halves of the prism. Mire thickness should be around
10% of the diameter of its total arc (Fig. 1.44B). Care should
be taken to horizontally and vertically centre the mires so that
two centralized semi-circles are observed.
оАБ оАЭe dial on the tonometer is rotated to vary the applied force.
оАЭe inner margins of the semi-circles align when a circular
area of diameter precisely 3.06 mm is оАЯattened.
оАБ оАЭe reading on the dial, multiplied by 10, gives the IOP in
mmHg.

TIP Inadvertent оАЮnger pressure applied to the globe when
undertaking applanation tonometry or squeezing of the eyelids
can cause an anomalously high IOP reading.

Sources of error

оАБ Inappropriate оАЬuorescein pattern. Excessive оАЯuorescein will
result in the mires being too thick, with consequent overestimation of IOP. InsuоАЪcient will make the semi-circles too
thin, with consequent underestimation (see Fig. 1.44B, leоАЬ and
centre).

A

CHAPTER

Examination Techniques 29
#### 1

оАБ Pressure on the globe from the examinerвАЩs оАЮngers, eyelid squeezing or restricted extraocular muscles (e.g. thyroid
myopathy) may give an anomalously high reading.
оАБ Central corneal thickness (CCT). Calculations of IOP by
Goldmann applanation tonometry (GAT) assume that CCT
is 520 ќЉm, with minimal normal variation. If the cornea is
thinner, an underestimation of IOP is likely to result, and if
thicker, an overestimation. Corneas tend to be thicker than
average in individuals with ocular hypertension, and thinner
in normal-tension glaucoma (NTG). Following refractive surgery procedures, the cornea is both thinner and structurally
altered, leading to an underestimation of the IOP. Some methods of IOP measurement (e.g. DCT вАУ see below) may reduce
the eоАЫect of structural confounding variables. Other corneal
mechanical factors may also be important but are less well
deоАЮned.
оАБ Corneal oedema may result in artiоАЮcial lowering of IOP,
hypothesized to be due to a boggy soоАЬening; the associated
increased CCT seems to be more than oоАЫset.
оАБ Astigmatism, if signiоАЮcant, may give distorted mires as well as
leading to mechanically induced errors. If over 3 dioptres, the
average reading of two can be taken with the prism rotated 90¬∞
for the second, or optimally the prism is rotated so that the red
line on the tonometer housing is aligned with the prescription
of the minus axis.
оАБ Incorrect calibration of the tonometer can result in a false
reading. Calibration should be checked before each clinical
session using the manufacturerвАЩs calibration arm (Fig. 1.44C).
оАБ Wide pulse pressure. It is normal for there to be a small oscillation of IOP in concert with the rhythm of ocular perfusion.
If this вАШpulse pressureвАЩ is substantial, either the midpoint or the
highest level observed may be taken.
оАБ Repeated readings over a short period will oоАЬen be associated with a slight fall in IOP due to a massaging eоАЫect on the
eye.

B C

Fig. 1.44 Applanation tonometry. (A) Contact between the tonometer prism and the cornea;
(B) оАЭuorescein-stained semi-circular mires вАУ the diagram at right showing the correct endpoint using mires of appropriate thickness; (C) tonometer calibration bar in position.

30 Tonometry

оАБ Other factors, including a tight collar and breath-holding,
both of which obstruct venous return, can raise IOP.

TIP The Goldmann tonometer loses accuracy with constant
use and should be checked for calibration error on a regular
basis.

Other forms of tonometry

оАБ Pneumotonometry (Fig. 1.45A) is based on the principle of
applanation, but the central part of the cornea is оАЯattened by a
jet of air rather than a prism. оАЭe time required to suоАЪciently
оАЯatten the cornea relates directly to the level of IOP. Contact is
not made with the eye and topical anaesthesia is not required,
so it is particularly useful for screening in the community. оАЭe
sudden jet of air can startle the patient. Accuracy is improved
if an average of at least three readings is taken.
оАБ Portable applanation tonometry (Perkins) uses a Goldmann
prism in conjunction with a portable light source (Fig. 1.45B).

It is hand-held and can therefore be used in bed-bound or
anaesthetized patients.
оАБ Dynamic contour tonometry (DCT) (e.g. PASCAL) uses a
solid-state sensor and a corneal contour-matching surface,
with the aim of measuring IOP relatively independently of
corneal mechanical factors such as rigidity. It is mounted on
a slit lamp in similar fashion to the Goldmann tonometer and
IOP is shown on a digital display. Studies comparing DCT and
GAT IOP readings with manometric intracameral IOP seem to
conоАЮrm DCT as providing a more physiological measurement.
оАБ Electronic indentation/applanation tonometry (e.g. TonoPen AVIA вАУ Fig. 1.45C) is a hand-held electronic contact
tonometer (a modiоАЮed version of the older MackayвАУMarg
tonometer). оАЭe probe tip contains a transducer that measures
applied force. Besides portability, its main advantage is the
facility to measure IOP reasonably accurately in eyes with distorted or oedematous corneas and through a soоАЬ contact lens.
оАБ Rebound tonometry (e.g. iCare вАУ Fig. 1.45D) involves a
1.8 mm plastic ball attached to a wire. Deceleration of the
probe upon contact with the cornea is proportional to IOP.

A B

C D

Fig. 1.45 Portable tonometers. (A) Keeler Pulsair pneumotonometer; (B) Perkins applanation
tonometer; (C) Medtronic Tono-Pen; (D) iCare IC100.

Anaesthesia is not required. оАЭe instrument can be used for
self-monitoring and for screening in the community.
оАБ Home tonometry (e.g. iCare HOME2) is available for patients
who require regular monitoring of their IOP, especially when
information is needed on diurnal IOP оАЯuctuation. It is fairly
easy to use and provides accurate measurements. It has a positioning system for correct alignment of the instrument and a
magnet within the applicator which prevents the probe from
falling out.
оАБ Indentation (impression) tonometry (e.g. Schiotz) is a
seldom-used portable device that measures the extent of corneal indentation by a plunger of known weight.
оАБ Implantable tonometers are under development and if a clinically workable device is realized should facilitate accurate lifelong 24-hour IOP measurement.

Ocular response analyser and corneal
hysteresis

оАЭe ocular response analyser (e.g. Reichert ORA) utilizes air-puоАЫ
technology to record two applanation measurements: one while
the cornea is moving inward and one when the cornea returns to
its normal position. оАЭe average of these two IOP measurements
provides a Goldmann-correlated IOP measurement. оАЭe diоАЫerence between the IOP measurements is called corneal hysteresis
(Fig. 1.46). Corneal hysteresis is not a measure of the stiоАЫness of
the cornea, but rather a measure of how corneal tissue absorbs and
dissipates energy during deformation and return. Corneal hysteresis appears to provide a measurement of IOP that is less aоАЫected
by factors such as previous laser refractive surgery. It also oоАЫers
information on which patients with glaucoma are more likely to
progress (patients with a low hysteresis value are at a greater risk of
glaucoma progression) and it may serve as a biomarker to aid glaucoma case detection. оАЭere is also a strong relationship between
corneal hysteresis and the magnitude of IOP reduction with topical prostaglandin therapy.

GONIOSCOPY

Introduction
Overview

оАБ Gonioscopy is a method of evaluating the anterior chamber
(AC) angle, and can be used therapeutically for procedures
such as laser trabeculoplasty and goniotomy.
оАБ Other means of angle assessment, such as anterior segment
optical coherence tomography (OCT) and high-frequency
ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM), oоАЫer advantages in some
aspects of angle analysis, but current clinical opinion suggests
they should supplement rather than replace gonioscopic evaluation (see Ch. 11).

TIP Gonioscopy is an indispensable technique that allows
the оАЮltration angle to be examined in patients with ocular
hypertension or glaucoma.

CHAPTER

Examination Techniques 31
#### 1

Applanation Signal Pressure (air pulse)

вАЬInвАЭ Signal Peak dslope1 вАЬOutвАЭ Signal Peak

Fig. 1.46 Corneal hysteresis as measured by the Ocular
Response Analyzer (Reichert). It is deоАЮned as the diоАЬerence
between the pressure at which the cornea bends inward
during air-jet applanation and the pressure at which it bends
out again.

Optical principles

оАЭe angle of the AC cannot be visualized directly through the
intact cornea because light from angle structures undergoes вАШtotal
internal reоАЯectionвАЩ at the anterior surface of the precorneal tear
оАЮlm (Fig. 1.47, top). When light travels from a medium of higher
to one of lower refractive index (such as cornea to air) it will
be reоАЯected at the interface between the two unless the angle of
incidence is less than a certain вАШcritical angleвАЩ dependent on their
refractive index diоАЫerence (46¬∞ for the tear оАЮlmвАУair interface). оАЭe
phenomenon is utilized in optical оАЮbre signal transmission, where
it ensures that light is retained within the core of a cable. Because
the refractive index of a goniolens is similar to that of the cornea,
it eliminates total internal reоАЯection by replacing the tear оАЮlmвАУair
interface with a tear оАЮlmвАУgoniolens interface (Fig. 1.47, bottom).
Light rays can then be viewed as they exit the contact lens, directly
or indirectly (see below).

Disinfection

Lenses must be cleaned between patients to remove any particulate
matter and then sterilized. A suggested regimen is to soak the lens

Indirect gonioscopy

Indirect goniolenses use a mirror to reоАЯect rays from the angle
such that they exit the goniolens at much less than the critical
angle. оАЭey provide a mirror image of the opposite angle and can
be used only in conjunction with a slit lamp.

Non-indentation gonioscopy

Fig. 1.48 Goldmann single-mirror goniolens.

substance of refractive index similar to the cornea is
required to bridge the gap between cornea and lens.

TIP Gonioscopy should be undertaken in a room where the
ambient illumination is low, as pupil size inоАЭuences the grade of
angle closure.

оАБ Technique

{ It is essential that the examination takes place in a room in
which the ambient illumination is low.

{ оАЭe size and intensity of the slit beam should be reduced to
the absolute minimum compatible with an adequate view.
Do not direct the beam through the pupil.

{ оАЭe patient is seated at the slit lamp and advised that the
lens will touch the eye but will not usually cause discomfort. оАЭe forehead must be kept against the headband and
both eyes should remain open.

{ A drop of local anaesthetic is instilled.

оАБ Goniolenses

{ оАЭe classic Goldmann lens consists of three mirrors, one
of which is speciоАЮcally for gonioscopy. Some goniolenses
have one (Fig. 1.48), two or four mirrors.

{ Lenses of similar basic structure but with modiоАЮcations
include the Magna View, Ritch trabeculoplasty and the
Khaw direct view.

{ Because the curvature of the contact surface of the lens
is steeper than that of the cornea, a viscous coupling

{ A drop or two of coupling оАЯuid (e.g. hypromellose 0.3%) is
placed on the contact surface of the lens.

{ оАЭe patient is asked to look upwards and the lens is inserted
rapidly so as to avoid loss of the coupling оАЯuid. оАЭe patient
then looks straight ahead.

{ Indirect gonioscopy gives an inverted view of the portion
of the angle opposite the mirror.

{ Once the initial examination has been performed and the
оАЮndings noted, increasing the level of illumination may
help in deоАЮning the angle structures.

{ When the view of the angle is obscured by a convex iris,
it is possible to see вАШover the hillвАЩ by asking the patient to
look in the direction of the mirror. Only slight movement
is permissible, otherwise the structures will be distorted
and a closed angle may appear open.

{ Excessive pressure with a non-indentation lens narrows
the angle appearance (in contrast to the eоАЫect of pressure
during indentation gonioscopy вАУ see below). Excessive

pressure also causes folds in the cornea that compromise
the clarity of the view.

{ In some eyes, suction on the cornea from the lens may
artiоАЮcially open the angle. Awareness of the need to avoid

TIP Indentation gonioscopy is a useful technique to
determine the degree of peripheral anterior synechiae in a
patient with appositional angle closure.

Indentation (dynamic, compression)
gonioscopy

оАБ Goniolenses include the Zeiss (Fig. 1.49), Posner and Sussman
(no handle), all of which are four-mirror gonioprisms.

Direct gonioscopy

{ оАЭe contact surface of the lenses has a curvature оАЯatter
than that of the cornea, negating the need for a coupling
substance.

{ оАЭe lenses do not stabilize the globe and are relatively
unsuitable for laser trabeculoplasty.

{ оАЭe lenses are useful for indentation gonioscopy.
оАБ Technique

{ оАЭe оАЮrst stages are as set out above for non-indentation
gonioscopy.

{ Indentation is performed by gently pressing the lens posteriorly against the cornea. оАЭis forces aqueous into the
angle, pushing the peripheral iris posteriorly.

{ If the angle is closed only by apposition between the iris
and cornea it will be forced open, allowing visualization of
the angle recess (Fig. 1.50).

{ If the angle is closed by adhesions between the peripheral
iris and cornea вАУ peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS) вАУ it
will remain closed.

{ Dynamic gonioscopy can be invaluable in helping to deоАЮne
the structures in angles that are diоАЪcult to assess, such as
in distinguishing an extensive or double highly pigmented
Schwalbe line from the pigmented meshwork.

Direct goniolenses work by constructing the viewing surface of the
lens in a domed or slanted conоАЮguration such that exiting light
rays strike the contact lens/air interface at a steeper than critical
angle so that they will pass through to the observer. оАЭis approach
is called вАШdirectвАЩ because light rays from the angle are viewed
directly, without reоАЯection inside the lens. оАЭey do not require
a slit lamp and are used with the patient in the supine position,
typically under general anaesthesia in the evaluation and surgical
treatment of infantile glaucoma.
оАБ Direct goniolenses include the Koeppe (Fig. 1.51A and B),
Medical Workshop, Barkan and SwanвАУJacob.
оАБ Technique

{ Gonioscopy is performed with the patient in the supine
position (note that this may deepen the angle) in conjunction with an operating or hand-held microscope or magnifying loupes.

{ оАЭe technique cannot be used with a desktop slit lamp so
clarity, illumination and variable magniоАЮcation are not
comparable with indirect lenses.

Surgical gonioscopy

оАБ Technique

{ оАЭe key to successful microinvasive glaucoma surgery is
adequate visualization of the оАЮltration angle.

{ оАЭis is best achieved by rotating the operating microscope
downwards (arrow) (Fig. 1.51C) and by tilting the patientвАЩs
head away from the surgeon by 30вАУ35¬∞ (arrow), until the
coaxial light is aligned along the iris plane. Surgical instruments can then approach the angle parallel to the iris plane.

{ A useful surgical landmark is the scleral spur, which separates canal-based surgery (via the trabecular meshwork,
anteriorly), from suprachoroidal-based surgery (via the
ciliary body band, posteriorly).

{ Pigment within the trabecular meshwork can serve as a
visual guide to the Schlemm canal. оАЭe canal can also be

located by applying gentle pressure with the edge of the
goniolens (Fig. 1.51D) on the perilimbal episcleral vessels
nasally, allowing reоАЯux of blood into the canal. Trypan blue
can be used to stain the trabecular meshwork when there is

IdentiоАЯcation of angle structures

Accurate identiоАЮcation of angle structures (Fig. 1.52AвАУC) is not
always straightforward, even for the experienced clinician.
оАБ Schwalbe line. оАЭis is the most anterior structure, appearing
whitish to variably pigmented. Anatomically it demarcates the
peripheral termination of Descemet membrane and the anterior limit of the trabeculum. It may be barely discernible in
younger patients. In contrast, there may be pigment deposits on or anterior to the Schwalbe line вАУ a Sampaolesi line вАУ
especially in heavily pigmented angles (e.g. pseudoexfoliation

syndrome). It may have a double-line conоАЮguration, when
the posterior component may be mistaken for the pigmented
meshwork (see Ch. 11).
оАБ оАЮe corneal wedge is useful in locating an inconspicuous
Schwalbe line. Using a narrow slit beam, two distinct linear
corneal reоАЯections can be identiоАЮed, one on the inner and one
on the outer corneal surface. оАЭe outer reоАЯection will arc round
across the corneoscleral interface вАУ due to the sclera being
opaque вАУ to meet the inner reоАЯection at the apex of the corneal
wedge that coincides with the Schwalbe line (Fig. 1.52D).
оАБ оАЮe trabeculum extends from the Schwalbe line to the scleral
spur, with an average width of 600 ќЉm. In younger people it
has a ground-glass translucent appearance. оАЭe anterior nonfunctional part lies adjacent to the Schwalbe line and has a
whitish colour. оАЭe posterior pigmented functional part lies
adjacent to the scleral spur and has a greyish-blue translucent
appearance in the young. Trabecular pigmentation is rare prior

36 Central Corneal Thickness

to puberty, but in older eyes involves the posterior trabeculum
to a variable extent, most marked inferiorly. Patchy trabecular
pigmentation in a suspiciously narrow angle raises the possibility of intermittent iris contact.
оАБ оАЮe Schlemm canal may be identiоАЮed in the angle, especially
if non-pigmented, as a slightly darker line deep to the posterior trabeculum. Blood can sometimes be seen in the canal,
either physiologically (sometimes due to excessive pressure on
the episcleral veins with a goniolens), or in the presence of low
intraocular or raised episcleral venous pressure.
оАБ оАЮe scleral spur is the most anterior projection of the sclera
and the site of attachment of the longitudinal muscle of the
ciliary body. Gonioscopically it is situated immediately posterior to the trabeculum and appears as a narrow whitish band
that yellows with age.
оАБ оАЮe ciliary body stands out just behind the scleral spur as a
pink, dull brown or slate grey band. Its width depends on the
position of iris insertion and it tends to be narrower in hypermetropic eyes and wider in myopic eyes. оАЭe angle recess represents the posterior dipping of the iris as it inserts into the
ciliary body. It may not be visible in some eyes due to a physiological anterior iris insertion, though оАЮxed pathological angle
narrowing due to PAS вАУ adhesions between the iris and angle
structures вАУ should be excluded.
оАБ Iris processes are small, usually tenuous extensions of the
anterior surface of the iris that insert at the level of the scleral
spur and cover the ciliary body to a varying extent (Fig. 1.53).
оАЭey are present in about one-third of normal eyes and are
most prominent during childhood and in brown eyes. оАЭe
processes should not be confused with PAS, which typically
extend more anteriorly and are more substantial.
оАБ Blood vessels. Radial vessels at the base of the angle recess are
oоАЬen seen in normal eyes. Pathological blood vessels run randomly in various directions. As a general principle, any blood
vessel that crosses the scleral spur onto the trabecular meshwork is abnormal. Larger circumferential vessels may also be
seen.

Pathological оАЯndings

CENTRAL CORNEAL THICKNESS

Central corneal thickness (CCT) can be measured using pachymetry or by Orbscan (see Fig. 11.5). оАЭe normal distribution is
540¬±30 ќЉm (mean ¬±1 standard deviation). CCT inоАЯuences GAT
readings. Eyes with a thin cornea have a true IOP that is greater
than the measured IOP. Conversely, eyes with a thick cornea have a
true IOP that is lower than the measured IOP. оАЭere is no validated
and useful correction algorithm for these two measurements.
Patients with NTG tend to have thin CCT measurements. It is an
important value when determining risk of conversion to glaucoma
in individuals with raised IOP (see Ch. 11). It is also an easy and
practical indicator of corneal endothelial function.

TIP CCT should be measured in individuals with ocular
hypertension, as it helps to determine the risk of conversion to
glaucoma.

37

38 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

Anatomy

оАЮe skin (Fig. 2.1A) consists of the epidermis, dermis and related
structures (adnexa).

Epidermis

оАЮe epidermis comprises four layers of keratin-producing cells
(keratinocytes). It also contains melanocytes, Langerhans cells
and Merkel cells. оАЮe layers of the epidermis around the eye are
described below. Cells migrate superоАЭcially, undergoing maturation and diоАЬerentiation through successive layers.
оАБ Keratin layer (stratum corneum or horny layer) consists of оАЫat
cells devoid of nuclei.
оАБ Granular cell layer (stratum granulosum) typically consists of one
or two layers of оАЫattened cells containing keratohyaline granules.

оАБ Prickle cell layer (stratum spinosum) is approximately
five cells deep. The cells are polygonal in cross-section
and have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. Their free borders are united by spiny-appearing desmosomes (cellular
junctions).
оАБ Basal cell layer (stratum basale) comprises a single row of
columnar-shaped proliferating cells containing melanin
derived from adjacent melanocytes.

Dermis

оАЮe dermis is much thicker than the epidermis. It is composed
of connective tissue and contains blood vessels, lymphatics and
nerve оАЭbres in addition to оАЭbroblasts, macrophages and mast cells.
Upward dermal extensions (papillae) interdigitate with downward
epidermal projections (rete ridges). In the eyelid the dermis lies
on the orbicularis muscle. Adnexa lie deep in the dermis or within
the tarsal plates.

A B

C D

Fig. 2.1 Eyelid skin. (A) Normal skin is composed of keratinized stratiоАЯed epithelium that covers
the surface; pilosebaceous elements are conspicuous in the dermis and a few blood vessels
and sweat glands are also seen; (B) dysplasia with loss of cell polarity; (C) dyskeratosis вАУ a
non-surface epithelial cell producing keratin; (D) parakeratosis вАУ retention of cell nuclei into
the surface keratin layer. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology,
Butterworth-Heinemann, 2001.)

оАБ Sebaceous glands are located in the caruncle and within eyebrow hairs. Tiny sebaceous glands are associated with the thin
(vellus) hairs covering periocular skin.
оАБ Meibomian glands are modiоАЭed sebaceous glands found in
the tarsal plates. оАЮey empty through a single row of 20вАУ30
oriоАЭces on each lid. A gland consists of a central duct with
multiple acini, the cells of which synthesize lipids (meibum)
that form the outer layer of the tear оАЭlm.
оАБ Glands of Zeis are modiоАЭed sebaceous glands associated with
lash follicles.
оАБ Glands of Moll are modified apocrine sweat glands opening either into a lash follicle or directly onto the anterior
lid margin between lashes. They are more numerous in the
lower lid.
оАБ Eccrine sweat glands are distributed throughout eyelid skin
and are not conоАЭned to the lid margin, in contrast to glands
of Moll.
оАБ Pilosebaceous units comprise hair follicles and their sebaceous glands (see Fig. 2.1A).

Terminology

Clinical

оАБ Macule. Localized area of colour change without inоАЭltration,
depression or elevation, less than 1 cm in diameter.
оАБ Papule. A solid elevation less than 1 cm in diameter.
оАБ Vesicle. Circumscribed lesion containing serous оАЫuid (less
than 0.5 cm across).
оАБ Bulla. A large (more than 0.5 cm) serous оАЫuid-оАЭlled lesion
(plural вАУ bullae).
оАБ Pustule. A pus-оАЭlled elevation less than 1 cm in diameter.
оАБ Crust. SolidiоАЭed serous or purulent exudate.
оАБ Nodule. A palpable solid area measuring more than 1 cm.
оАБ Cyst. A nodule consisting of an epithelial-lined cavity оАЭlled
with оАЫuid or semi-solid material.
оАБ Plaque. A solid elevation of the skin, greater than 1 cm in
diameter.
оАБ Scale. Readily detached fragments of shed keratin layer.
оАБ Papilloma. A benign neoplastic warty or tag-like projection of
the skin or mucous membrane.
оАБ Ulcer. A circumscribed area of epithelial loss. In skin an ulcer
extends through the epidermis into the dermis.

Histological

оАБ Tumour strictly refers only to a swelling, though is commonly
used to denote a neoplasm.
оАБ Neoplasia. Abnormal tissue growth, either benign (localized,
non-invasive and non-spreading) or malignant (progressive
growth with the potential for distant spread).
оАБ Atypia refers to an abnormal appearance of individual cells,
e.g. abnormal mitotic оАЭgures.
оАБ Dysplasia is an alteration of the size, morphology and organization of cellular components of a tissue. оАЮere is disturbance

CHAPTER

Eyelids 39
#### 2

of normally structured and recognized layers of tissue (e.g. loss
of cell polarity вАУ Fig. 2.1B).
оАБ Carcinoma in situ (intraepidermal carcinoma, Bowen disease) exhibits dysplastic changes throughout the thickness of
the epidermis.
оАБ Hyperkeratosis. An increase in thickness of the keratin layer
that appears clinically as scaling. Hyperkeratosis can be a feature of benign or malignant epithelial tumours.
оАБ Acanthosis. оАЮickening of the prickle cell layer.
оАБ Dyskeratosis is keratinization other than on the epithelial
surface (Fig. 2.1C).
оАБ Parakeratosis is the retention of nuclei into the keratin layer
(Fig. 2.1D).

General considerations

оАБ ClassiоАЯcation. Epidermal, adnexal or dermal.
оАБ Diagnosis. оАЮe clinical characteristics of benign lesions are
a tendency to a lack of induration and ulceration, uniform
colour, limited growth, regular outline and preservation of
normal lid margin structures. Biopsy may be required if the
appearance is suspicious.

{ Incisional biopsy involves removal of a portion of a lesion
for histopathology.

{ Excision biopsy is performed on small tumours and fulоАЭls
both diagnostic and treatment objectives.
оАБ Treatment options include:

{ Excision of the entire lesion and a small surrounding portion of normal tissue.

{ Marsupialization involves the removal of the top of a
cyst allowing drainage of its contents and subsequent
epithelialization.

{ Ablation with laser or cryotherapy.

NON-NEOPLASTIC LESIONS

Chalazion (meibomian cyst)
Pathogenesis

A chalazion is a sterile chronic granulomatous inоАЫammatory lesion
(lipogranuloma) of the meibomian, or sometimes Zeis, glands
caused by retained sebaceous secretions. Histopathology shows a
lipogranulomatous chronic inоАЫammatory picture with extracellular fat deposits surrounded by lipid-laden epithelioid cells, multinucleated giant cells and lymphocytes (Fig. 2.2A). Blepharitis is
commonly present; rosacea can be associated with multiple and
recurrent chalazia. Bortezomib, a proteasome inhibitor used in
the treatment of multiple myeloma, predisposes to the formation
of chalazia within 3 months of initiation of treatment. A recurrent
chalazion should be biopsied to exclude masquerading malignancy.

TIP If a chalazion recurs in the same lid position in an
older patient, consider undertaking a biopsy to exclude
malignancy.

40 Non-Neoplastic Lesions
