Treatment

оАБ оАЯe choice of treatment depends on (a) clinical activity, (b)
severity and (c) duration of the orbitopathy.
оАБ Anti-inоАЮammatory/immunosuppressive treatment is signiоАЭcantly less eоАЬective aоАЫer 18 months of disease duration.
Early referral to a specialized centre is fundamental for most
patients with moderateвАУsevere active orbitopathy. Counselling
is needed to explain the aims and expectations, beneоАЭts and
risks of diоАЬerent therapies.
оАБ ClassiоАЭcation. Treatment can be classiоАЭed into that of: (a)
mild disease (most patients), (b) moderateвАУsevere active disease and (c) treatment of post-inоАЮammatory complications.
оАБ Cessation of smoking should be the оАЭrst measure taken
in all cases. оАЯyroid dysfunction should also be managed
adequately.
оАБ If radioiodine treatment is administered in patients with preexisting TED, a short course of oral steroids should be given.

Mild and active disease

оАБ Lubricants for superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis, corneal
exposure and dryness.
оАБ Topical anti-inоАЮammatory agents (steroids, non-steroidal
anti-inоАЮammatory drugs (NSAIDs), ciclosporin) are advocated by some authorities.
оАБ Selenium (in selenium deоАЭcient areas): sodium selenite 200 ќЉg
(containing 91.2 ќЉg selenium) daily for 6 months.
оАБ Head elevation with three pillows during sleep to reduce periorbital oedema.
оАБ Eyelid taping during sleep may alleviate mild exposure
keratopathy.

ModerateвАУsevere active disease

оАБ вАШClinical activity scoreвАЩ. Using EUGOGO guidelines an activity score can be calculated to determine when immunosuppressives should be used. One point is assigned for each feature
that is present, using the activity classiоАЭcation (see above).
Treatment should be considered for a score of 3 or more out
of 7. During subsequent review, a point is allocated for an
increase in proptosis of 2 mm or more, a decrease in uniocular
excursion in any one direction of 8¬∞ or more, or a decrease in
Snellen acuity of one line (total 10).
оАБ Systemic steroids are the mainstay of treatment for moderateвАУ
severe disease. Oral prednisolone 60вАУ80 mg/day may be given
initially and tapered depending on response. Intravenous methylprednisolone is oоАЫen reserved for acute compressive optic
neuropathy (see below), but tolerability is better, and outcomes
are superior, when compared with oral treatment. Intravenous
steroids should not be given to patients with co-existing signiоАЭcant hepatic dysfunction, signiоАЭcant cardiovascular disease,
uncontrolled systemic hypertension or diabetes. A lowerintensity regimen of intravenous corticosteroids in the absence
of acute sight-threatening disease should be used in most cases
using the following regimen: 0.5 g once weekly for 6 weeks followed by 0.25 g once weekly for 6 weeks. A reduction in discomfort, chemosis and periorbital oedema usually occurs within 24
hours, with a maximal response within 2вАУ8 weeks. Ideally, oral
steroid therapy should be discontinued aоАЫer several months,
but long-term low-dose maintenance may be necessary.
оАБ Intravenous corticosteroids weekly and mycophenolate
sodium (0.72g/day for 24 weeks) have been shown to be of
greater beneоАЭt than intravenous corticosteroid monotherapy
and is the regimen of choice in specialized centres.
оАБ Orbital steroid (triamcinolone acetate) injections are occasionally used in selected cases to minimize systemic side
eоАЬects, but are typically considerably less eоАЬective than systemic treatment.
оАБ Low-dose fractionated radiotherapy is usually used as
second-line therapy. It may be used in addition to steroids or
when steroids are contraindicated or ineоАЬective, but because
of the delayed eоАЬect is not used as the sole treatment of acute
optic nerve compression. A positive response is usually evident within 6 weeks, with maximal improvement by 4 months;
around 40% will not respond. Adverse eоАЬects include cataract,

radiation retinopathy, optic neuropathy and an increased risk
of local cancer. оАЯe threshold for its use should be higher in
younger patients and diabetics, the latter because of a possibly
increased risk of retinopathy.
оАБ Combined therapy with irradiation, azathioprine and lowdose prednisolone may be more eоАЬective than steroids or
radiotherapy alone.

TIP Immunosuppression in the active inоАЮammatory phase of
thyroid eye disease is important to reduce acute inоАЮammation
and to prevent long-term complications.

оАБ Optic neuropathy, and less commonly intractable corneal
exposure, requires aggressive treatment. Pulsed intravenous
methylprednisolone is commonly used, regimens including 0.5вАУ1 g on three successive days with conversion to oral
treatment (e.g. 40 mg/day prednisolone) or 0.5вАУ1 g on alternate days, 3вАУ6 times, keeping the maximum dose below 8 g to
reduce the risk of liver compromise, followed by oral prednisolone. Appropriate monitoring should be instituted, including
liver function tests, as well as gastric protective treatment and
osteoporosis prophylaxis if necessary. Orbital wall decompression (see below) and/or orbital apex decompression may be
considered if steroids are ineоАЬective (20% receiving intravenous treatment) or contraindicated. Orbital radiotherapy may
also be administered, but is generally used only as an adjunct
to other modalities.
оАБ Second-line management for persistent, corticosteroidresistant disease or non-response to оАЭrst-line treatment
includes the use of monoclonal antibody treatment with
rituximab, tocilizumab or teprotumumab. Teprotumumab is
an inhibitor of insulin-like growth factor 1 receptor (IGF-1R)
and is eоАЬective in reducing proptosis and the Clinical Activity
Score in patients with acute TED. In addition, teprotumumab
appears to be eоАЬective in chronic non-inоАЮammatory TED,
resulting in a reduction of proptosis, diplopia and strabismus.
оАЯis medication has been associated with mild to severe hearing loss in some individuals.
оАБ Post-inоАЮammatory complications. Eyelid surgery should be
performed only aоАЫer any necessary orbital and then strabismus procedures have been undertaken, as orbital decompression may impact both ocular motility and eyelid position, and
extraocular muscle surgery may aоАЬect eyelid position.

TIP Surgical orbital decompression should be considered
when there is sight-threatening compressive optic neuropathy
in thyroid eye disease.

оАБ Proptosis. AоАЫer active inоАЮammation has remitted, the patient
can be leоАЫ with cosmetically and functionally signiоАЭcant proptosis, the treatment of which is essentially surgical. Surgical
decompression increases the volume of the orbit by removing
the bony walls and may be combined with removal of orbital
fat. Most surgery is undertaken via an external approach,
though the medial wall and the medial part of the оАЮoor can

CHAPTER

Orbit 123
#### 4

Fig. 4.15 Axial CT following bilateral lateral and medial wall
decompression. (Courtesy of A Pearson.)

be reached endoscopically. One-wall (deep lateral) decompression is eоАЬective (approximately 4вАУ5 mm reduction in
proptosis) and may reduce the risk of postoperative diplopia.
Two-wall (balanced medial and lateral вАУ Fig. 4.15) decompression provides a greater eоАЬect but with a signiоАЭcant risk
of inducing diplopia. оАЯree-wall decompression includes the
оАЮoor with a reduction in proptosis of 6вАУ10 mm but may lead to
hypoglobus and carries a higher risk of infraorbital nerve damage and diplopia. Very severe proptosis may require removal of
part of the orbital roof in addition (four-wall decompression).
оАБ Restrictive myopathy. Surgery is required in most cases experiencing persistent diplopia in the primary or reading positions
of gaze, provided the inоАЮammatory stage has subsided and the
angle of deviation has been stable for at least 6вАУ12 months. Until
these criteria are met, diplopia may be alleviated, if possible, with
prisms or sometimes botulinum toxin. оАЯe goal of operative
treatment is to achieve binocular single vision in the primary
and reading positions. Restrictive myopathy oоАЫen precludes
binocularity in all positions of gaze, though with time the оАЭeld
of binocular single vision may enlarge as a result of increasing
fusional vergence. Recession of the inferior and/or medial recti is
the most commonly indicated surgery (a rectus muscle is never
resected, only recessed in TED), generally utilizing adjustable
sutures (see Ch. 18). оАЯe suture is adjusted later the same day
or on the оАЭrst postoperative day to achieve optimal alignment.
оАБ Lid retraction. Mild lid retraction frequently improves spontaneously so does not require treatment. Control of hyperthyroidism may also be beneоАЭcial. Botulinum toxin injection to the
levator aponeurosis and M√Љller muscle may be used as a temporary measure in patients awaiting deоАЭnitive correction. M√Љllerotomy (disinsertion of M√Љller muscle) is eоАЬective for mild lid
retraction, but more severe cases may also require recession/
disinsertion of the levator aponeurosis and the suspensory ligament of the superior conjunctival fornix. Recession of the lower
lid retractors, with or without a hard palate graоАЫ, can be used
when retraction of the lower lid is 2 mm or more (see also Ch. 2).

124 Infections

INFECTIONS

ChandlerвАУHubert classiоАЮcation of
orbital complications of sinus infection

оАЯe classiоАЭcation helps to determine management and prognosis
(Fig. 4.16).
оАБ Type I: preseptal cellulitis
оАБ Type II: post-septal cellulitis
оАБ Type III: subperiosteal abscess
оАБ Type IV: orbital abscess
оАБ Type V: cavernous sinus thrombosis

Preseptal cellulitis

Introduction

Preseptal cellulitis is an infection of the subcutaneous tissues anterior to the orbital septum. It is considerably more common than
orbital cellulitis and though regarded as less serious, can still be
associated with severe complications such as abscess formation,

I

III

meningitis and cavernous sinus thrombosis. Rapid progression
to orbital cellulitis may occasionally occur. Organisms typically
responsible are Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes,
with causes including skin trauma such as laceration or insect
bites, spread from focal ocular or periocular infection such as an
acute hordeolum, dacryocystitis, conjunctivitis or sinusitis and
haematogenous spread from remote infection such as the upper
respiratory tract or middle ear.

Diagnosis

оАЯe condition manifests with a swollen, oоАЫen оАЭrm, tender red eyelid that may be very severe (Fig. 4.17A and B). However, in contrast to orbital cellulitis, proptosis and chemosis are absent and
VA, pupillary reactions and ocular motility are unimpaired. оАЯe
patient is oоАЫen pyrexial. Imaging with MRI or CT is not indicated
unless orbital cellulitis or a lid abscess is suspected, or if there is a
failure to respond to therapy.

Treatment

Treatment is with oral antibiotics such as co-amoxiclav 250вАУ500
mg/125 mg 2вАУ3 times daily or 875/125 mg twice daily, depending

II

IV

Fig. 4.16 ChandlerвАУHubert classiоАЯcation of orbital cellulitis secondary to sinus infection (see
text; Type V is not illustrated).

A

C

CHAPTER

Orbit 125
#### 4

B

D

Fig. 4.17 Orbital cellulitis. (A) Left preseptal cellulitis resulting from an infected eyelid abrasion;
(B) axial CT showing opaciоАЯcation anterior to the orbital septum; (C) right orbital cellulitis with
ophthalmoplegia; (D) axial CT showing both preseptal and orbital opaciоАЯcation.

on severity. Severe infection may require intravenous antibiotics. оАЯe
patientвАЩs tetanus status should be ascertained in cases following trauma.

TIP Visual acuity, pupillary responses, and ocular motility are
unimpaired, and proptosis is absent in preseptal cellulitis.

Bacterial orbital cellulitis

Introduction

Bacterial orbital cellulitis is a serious infection of the soоАЫ tissues
behind the orbital septum, which can be sight- and life-threatening.
It can occur at any age but is more common in children. Streptococcus pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes and
Haemophilus inоАЯuenzae are common causative organisms, with
infection originating typically from the paranasal (especially ethmoid) sinuses. Infection can also spread from preseptal cellulitis,
dacryocystitis, midfacial skin or dental infection and can follow

trauma, including any form of ocular surgery. Blood-borne spread
from infection elsewhere in the body may occur.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms consist of the rapid onset of pain exacerbated by
eye movement, swelling of the eye, malaise and frequently
visual impairment and double vision. оАЯere is commonly a
recent history of nasal, sinus or respiratory symptoms.
оАБ Signs

{ VA may be reduced and colour vision impaired, raising the
possibility of optic nerve compression. оАЯe presence of a
relative aоАЬerent pupillary defect in a previously normal eye
makes this almost certain.

{ Tender, оАЭrm, erythematous and warm eyelids, with periocular and conjunctival (chemosis) oedema, conjunctival
injection and sometimes subconjunctival haemorrhage.
оАЯe signs are usually unilateral, though oedema may
spread to the contralateral eyelids (Fig. 4.17C).

{ Pyrexia, oоАЫen marked.

126 Infections

{ Proptosis is common in established infection, but is oоАЫen
obscured by lid swelling. It may be non-axial (dystopia),
particularly if an abscess is present.

{ Painful ophthalmoplegia.

{ Choroidal folds and optic disc swelling may be present on
fundus examination.
оАБ DiоАЬerential diagnosis. Major diagnostic alternatives are listed
in Table 4.1
оАБ Complications

{ Ocular complications include optic neuropathy, exposure
keratopathy, raised IOP, endophthalmitis and occlusion of
the central retinal artery or vein.

{ Subperiosteal abscess, most frequently located along the
medial orbital wall.

{ Intracranial complications, which are uncommon (3вАУ4%)
but extremely serious, include meningitis, brain abscess
and cavernous sinus thrombosis.

TIP Because orbital cellulitis often occurs as a consequence
of a sinus infection, urgent radiological investigation of the
sinuses is mandatory.

RhinoоАЯorbital mucormycosis

Introduction

Mucormycosis is a rare aggressive and oоАЫen fatal infection caused
by fungi of the family Mucoraceae. It typically aоАЬects patients with
diabetic ketoacidosis or immunosuppression and is extremely rare
in the immunocompetent. Infection is acquired by the inhalation
of spores, which give rise to an upper respiratory infection. Spread
then occurs to the contiguous sinuses and subsequently to the
orbit and brain. Invasion of blood vessels by the hyphae results in
occlusive vasculitis with infarction of orbital tissues.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Gradual onset facial and periorbital swelling, diplopia and visual loss.

Investigation

Investigations may include:
оАБ Ascertainment of tetanus immunization status in cases of
trauma.
оАБ White cell count.
оАБ Blood cultures.
оАБ Culture of nasal discharge.
оАБ High-resolution CT of the orbit, sinuses and brain (Fig.
4.17B and D) is vital to conоАЭrm the diagnosis and exclude a
subperiosteal or intracranial abscess. MRI is also sometimes
performed.
оАБ Lumbar puncture if meningeal or cerebral signs develop.

Treatment

оАБ Hospital admission is mandatory, with urgent otolaryngological assessment and frequent ophthalmic review. Paediatric specialist advice should be sought in the management of
a child and a low threshold should be adopted for infectious
disease specialist consultation.
оАБ Delineation of the extent of erythema on the skin using a surgical marker may help in judging progress.
оАБ Antibiotics are given intravenously, with the speciоАЭc drug
depending on local sensitivities. CeоАЫazidime is a typical
choice, supplemented by oral metronidazole to cover anaerobes. Intravenous antibiotics should be continued until the
patient has been apyrexial for 4 days, followed by 1вАУ3 weeks
of oral treatment.
оАБ Monitoring of optic nerve function is performed at least
every 4 hours initially by testing VA, colour vision, light
brightness appreciation and pupillary reactions. Deterioration
should prompt the consideration of surgical intervention.
оАБ Surgery. Drainage of an orbital abscess should be considered
at an early stage. Drainage of infected sinuses should be considered if there is a lack of response to antibiotics, or if there
is very severe sinus disease. Biopsy of inоАЮammatory tissue
may be performed for an atypical clinical picture. Severe optic
nerve compression may warrant an emergency canthotomy/
cantholysis (see Ch. 22).

CHAPTER

Orbit 127
#### 4

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms typically consist of acute or subacute ocular and
periocular redness, swelling and pain (Fig. 4.19A and B). Systemic symptoms are common in children.
оАБ Signs

{ Pyrexia is present in up to 50% of children, but is rare in
adults.

{ Congestive proptosis.

{ Mild to severe ophthalmoplegia may occur.

{ Features of optic nerve dysfunction, particularly if the
inоАЮammation involves the posterior orbit. оАЯere may be
optic disc swelling.

Fig. 4.18 Necrosis of the eyelid in rhino-orbital mucormycosis.

оАБ Signs are similar to bacterial orbital cellulitis, but tend to be
less acute and with slower progression. Infarction superimposed on septic necrosis is responsible for the classic black
eschar that may develop on the palate, turbinates, nasal septum, skin and eyelids (Fig. 4.18).
оАБ Complications include retinal vascular occlusion, multiple
cranial nerve palsies and cerebrovascular occlusion.
оАБ DiоАЬerential diagnosis is listed in Table 4.1
оАБ Investigation is much the same as for bacterial orbital cellulitis.

Treatment

оАБ Correction of the underlying metabolic defect should be instituted if possible.
оАБ Intravenous antifungal treatment.
оАБ Daily packing and irrigation of the involved areas with antifungal agent.
оАБ Wide excision of devitalized and necrotic tissues. Exenteration
may be required in unresponsive cases in order to reduce the
risk of death.
оАБ Adjunctive hyperbaric oxygen may be helpful.

NON-INFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY
DISEASE

Idiopathic orbital inоАЭammatory disease

Idiopathic orbital inоАЮammatory disease (IOID), also called nonspeciоАЭc orbital inоАЮammation or orbital pseudotumour, is an uncommon disorder characterized by non-neoplastic, non-infective,
space-occupying orbital inоАЭltration with inоАЮammatory features.
оАЯe process may preferentially involve any or all of the orbital soоАЫ
tissues. Histopathological analysis reveals pleomorphic inоАЮammatory cellular inоАЭltration followed by reactive оАЭbrosis. Unilateral disease is typical in adults, although in children bilateral involvement
may occur. Intracranial extension is rare. Simultaneous orbital and
sinus involvement is also rare and may be a distinct entity.

{ Choroidal folds, if present, may be associated with reduced
vision but optic neuropathy must always be suspected.
оАБ Course. оАЯe natural history of the inоАЮammatory process is
very variable.

Treatment

оАБ Observation, for relatively mild disease, in anticipation of
spontaneous remission.
оАБ NSAIDs alone (e.g. ibuprofen) are oоАЫen eоАЬective and may be
tried in mild disease prior to steroid therapy. Co-prescription
of a proton pump inhibitor should be considered.
оАБ Systemic steroids should be administered only aоАЫer the
diagnosis has been conоАЭrmed, as they may mask other
pathology such as infection and granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Oral prednisolone is initially given at a dose of
1.0вАУ1.5 mg/kg daily, subsequently being tapered and discontinued over a number of weeks depending on clinical
response. Further treatment may be needed in the event of
recurrence.
оАБ Orbital depot steroid injection may be useful in some cases.
оАБ Radiotherapy may be considered if there has been no improvement aоАЫer 2 weeks of adequate steroid therapy. Even low-dose
treatment (e.g. 2Gy fractionation daily up to 20Gy total dose)
may produce remission, though much higher total doses may
be necessary (up to 36Gy total dose).

{ Spontaneous remission aоАЫer a few weeks without sequelae.

{ Intermittent episodes of activity, usually with eventual
remission.

{ Severe prolonged inоАЮammation eventually leading to progressive оАЭbrosis of orbital tissues, resulting in a вАШfrozen
orbitвАЩ characterized by ophthalmoplegia, which may be
associated with ptosis and visual impairment caused by
optic nerve involvement.
оАБ Investigation

{ CT shows ill-deоАЭned orbital opaciоАЭcation and loss of deоАЭnition of contents (Fig. 4.19C and D).

{ Biopsy is generally required in persistent cases to conоАЭrm
the diagnosis and particularly to rule out neoplasia and
systemic inоАЮammatory conditions.

{ A wide range of other investigations may be considered to aid
in the exclusion of alternative diagnoses, particularly infection, lymphoma and non-neoplastic inоАЭltrative disorders
such as sarcoidosis and granulomatosis with polyangiitis.

128 Non-infective InоАЯammatory Disease

оАБ Other options, usually as supplementary treatments or in
resistant cases, include cytotoxic drugs (e.g. methotrexate, azathioprine), calcineurin inhibitors (e.g. ciclosporin, tacrolimus)
and biological blockers.
оАБ Surgical resection of an inоАЮammatory focus may be contemplated in highly resistant cases.

Orbital myositis

Introduction

Orbital myositis is an idiopathic, non-speciоАЭc inоАЮammation of one
or more extraocular muscles and is considered a subtype of IOID.
Histology shows a chronic inоАЮammatory cellular inоАЭltrate associated with the muscle оАЭbres (Fig. 4.20A).

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Acute pain, exacerbated by eye movement and
diplopia. Onset is usually in early adulthood.

оАБ Signs are generally less obvious than IOID.

{ Lid oedema, ptosis and chemosis.

{ Pain and diplopia associated with eye movements
(Fig. 4.20B).

{ Vascular injection over the involved muscle (Fig. 4.20C).

{ In chronic cases оАЭbrosis of the aоАЬected muscle may occur
with permanent restrictive myopathy.
оАБ Course

{ Acute non-recurrent involvement that resolves spontaneously within 6 weeks.

{ Chronic disease characterized by either a single episode
persisting for longer than 2 months (oоАЫen for years) (Fig.
4.20D) or recurrent attacks.
оАБ Investigation consists primarily of MRI or CT, which show
enlargement of the aоАЬected muscles (Fig. 4.20E and F), with or
without involvement of the tendons of insertion. оАЯis is in contrast to TED-related muscle enlargement, in which the tendon
is always spared. Additional investigations may be required in
some cases.

130 Non-infective InоАЯammatory Disease

Treatment

Treatment is aimed at relieving discomfort and dysfunction, shortening the course and preventing recurrences. NSAIDs may be adequate in mild disease, but systemic steroids are generally required
and usually produce dramatic improvement, although recurrence
is seen in 50%. Radiotherapy is also eоАЬective, particularly in limiting recurrence.

Acute dacryoadenitis

Acute dacryoadenitis may be idiopathic or due to viral (e.g. mumps,
EpsteinвАУBarr, cytomegalovirus) or вАУ rarely вАУ bacterial infection;
the lacrimal gland is oоАЫen involved in IOID. Chronic conditions
such as sarcoidosis, Sj√ґgren syndrome, thyroid disease and some
chronic infections usually give a less acute onset and involvement
can be bilateral. Presentation in acute disease is with the rapid onset
of discomfort in the region of the gland. Lacrimal secretion may be
reduced or increased and discharge may be reported. Swelling of the
lateral aspect of the eyelid overlying the palpebral lobe leads to a
characteristic S-shaped ptosis and enlargement of the orbital lobe
may give a slight downward and inward dystopia (Fig. 4.21A) and
occasionally proptosis and other signs of orbital disease. оАЯere is
tenderness over the lacrimal gland and injection of the conjunctiva overlying the palpebral lobe may be seen on upper lid eversion
(Fig. 4.21B). Chemosis may be present. оАЯere may be local (e.g. preauricular) lymph node enlargement. CT shows enlargement of the
gland and involvement of adjacent tissues (Fig. 4.21C) without bony
erosion; the latter suggests a tumour. Biopsy is sometimes indicated,
particularly to exclude a tumour. Treatment varies according to the
cause, but in many cases is not required.

TolosaвАУHunt syndrome

TolosaвАУHunt syndrome is a rare idiopathic condition caused by nonspeciоАЭc granulomatous inоАЮammation of the cavernous sinus, superior
orbital оАЭssure and/or orbital apex. It is a diagnosis of exclusion and
should be investigated fully. Presentation is with ipsilateral periorbital
or hemicranial pain and diplopia due to ocular motor paresis, with
pupillary and eyelid involvement in many cases. Proptosis, if present,
is usually mild. Sensory loss along the distribution of the оАЭrst and second divisions of the trigeminal nerve is common. оАЯe patient may be
pyrexial. Diagnosis is with imaging, together with other investigations
to rule out identiоАЭable causes, including neoplasia. Treatment is with
systemic steroids and other immunosuppressants as necessary; the
clinical course is characterized by remissions and recurrences.

Granulomatosis with polyangiitis

Granulomatosis with polyangiitis, previously called Wegener granulomatosis (see Ch. 9), is an idiopathic multisystem granulomatous disorder that may involve the orbit, oоАЫen bilaterally, usually by
contiguous spread from the paranasal sinuses or nasopharynx. Primary orbital involvement is less common. оАЯis condition should
be considered in any patient with bilateral orbital inоАЮammation,
particularly if associated with sinus pathology. Antineutrophilic
cytoplasmic antibody (cANCA variant) is a useful serological

A

B

C

test. Other ocular features include scleritis, peripheral ulcerative
keratitis, intraocular inоАЮammation and retinal vascular occlusions. Treatment is with cyclophosphamide and steroids, which
are usually eоАЬective. In resistant cases ciclosporin, azathioprine,
antithymocyte globulin or plasmapheresis may be useful. Surgical decompression may be required for severe orbital involvement.

NON-NEOPLASTIC VASCULAR
ABNORMALITIES

Cavernous sinus thrombosis

оАЯis refers to clotting within the cavernous sinus, usually resulting from infection such as sinusitis, orbital or preseptal cellulitis
or otitis. оАЯere is a high mortality rate: 20% treated and up to
100% untreated die. Features are of rapid onset and may include
severe headache, malaise, nausea and vomiting, unilateral or oоАЫen
bilateral proptosis, chemosis, congestion of the facial, conjunctival
and retinal veins, reduced vision and signs resulting from compromised function of the third to sixth cranial nerves, which run
through the cavernous sinus. Diagnosis is with imaging, especially
MRI and MRI venography. Systemic investigation for infection is
also performed, including lumbar puncture. Treatment consists of
intravenous antibiotics and sometimes surgical drainage.

TIP Cavernous sinus thrombosis has a high mortality rate
if the diagnosis is delayed and if intensive treatment is not
instigated urgently.

CarotidвАУcavernous оАЮstula

Introduction

A carotidвАУcavernous оАЭstula involves the development of an arteriovenous оАЭstula between the carotid artery and the venous cavernous sinus (see Fig. 19.64) with a rise in venous pressure in the sinus
and structures draining to it. Ocular manifestations occur because
of venous and arterial stasis around the eye and orbit, increased
episcleral venous pressure and a decrease in arterial blood оАЮow to
the cranial nerves within the cavernous sinus. CarotidвАУcavernous
оАЭstulae are classiоАЭed into вАШdirectвАЩ and вАШindirectвАЩ forms.
оАБ Direct оАЭstulae are high-оАЮow shunts in which carotid artery blood
passes directly into the cavernous sinus through a defect in the
wall of the intracavernous portion of the internal carotid artery
as a result of trauma (75%), including surgery, spontaneous rupture of an intracavernous carotid aneurysm or an atherosclerotic artery, the latter frequently in a middle-aged hypertensive
woman; spontaneous оАЭstulae usually have lower оАЮow.
оАБ In an indirect оАЭstula (вАШdural shuntвАЩ), the intracavernous portion of the internal carotid artery remains intact. Arterial
blood оАЮows through the meningeal branches of the external
or internal carotid arteries indirectly into the cavernous sinus
and the clinical features are subtler than in a direct оАЭstula such
that the condition may be overlooked. Spontaneous rupture
of an atherosclerotic artery or of a congenital malformation
is the usual cause and may be precipitated by minor trauma

CHAPTER

Orbit 131
#### 4

or straining. Connective tissue and collagen vascular disorders
can be associated.

Diagnosis

{ Proptosis and bruit are mild if present.

оАБ Symptoms вАУ direct. Presentation may be days or weeks aоАЫer
head injury with a classic triad of pulsatile proptosis, conjunctival chemosis and a whooshing noise in the head.
оАБ Symptoms вАУ indirect. Gradual onset of redness of one or both
eyes is a typical presentation, caused by conjunctival vascular
engorgement.
оАБ Signs вАУ direct

{ Immediate visual loss may be due to ocular or optic nerve
damage at the time of head trauma.

{ Delayed visual loss may occur as a result of exposure
keratopathy, secondary glaucoma, central retinal vein
occlusion, anterior segment ischaemia or ischaemic optic
neuropathy.

{ Signs are usually ipsilateral to the оАЭstula but may be bilateral, or even contralateral, because of midline connections
between the two cavernous sinuses.

{ Marked epibulbar vascular dilatation (Fig. 4.22A).

{ Chemosis, commonly haemorrhagic, particularly in the
early stages (Fig. 4.22B).

{ Pulsatile proptosis associated with a bruit and a thrill, both
of which can be abolished by ipsilateral carotid compression in the neck.

{ Increased IOP due to elevated episcleral venous pressure and orbital congestion and sometimes angle-closure
glaucoma.

{ Anterior segment ischaemia, characterized by corneal epithelial oedema, aqueous cells and оАЮare and in severe cases
iris atrophy, cataract and rubeosis iridis.

{ Ptosis due to third nerve involvement.

{ Ophthalmoplegia (60вАУ70%) due to the ocular motor nerve
damage from initial trauma, an intracavernous aneurysm
or the оАЭstula itself. оАЯe sixth cranial nerve is most frequently aоАЬected because of its free-оАЮoating location within
the cavernous sinus. оАЯe third and fourth nerves, situated
in the lateral wall of the sinus, are less frequently involved.
Engorgement and swelling of extraocular muscles may
also contribute to defective ocular motility.

{ Fundus examination may show optic disc swelling, venous
dilatation and intraretinal haemorrhages from venous stasis and impaired retinal blood оАЮow. Vitreous haemorrhage
is rare.
оАБ Signs вАУ indirect

{ Milder epibulbar vascular dilatation than with a direct оАЭstula (Fig. 4.22C).

{ Exaggerated ocular pulsation, which is readily detected on
slit lamp applanation tonometry.

{ оАЯe presence of вАШcorkscrewвАЩ epibulbar vessels (Fig. 4.22D)
is a common subtle later sign. оАЯese are not pathognomonic and can be found in normal eyes.

{ Raised IOP, oоАЫen bilateral but higher on the side of the
оАЭstula.

of the superior ophthalmic vein (Fig. 4.23A) and diоАЬuse

these may be visible only with a direct оАЭstula. Orbital Doppler imaging may show abnormal оАЮow patterns, particularly
in the superior orbital vein. DeоАЭnitive diagnosis may involve
selective catheter digital subtraction angiography, especially
in mild dural оАЭstulae, though CT and MRI angiography can
be useful.

Treatment

Ocular complications may require speciоАЭc measures in addition
to treatment of the оАЭstula itself. Neurological subspecialist opinion

should be sought at an early stage, even if features are mild, as some
оАЭstula patterns (e.g. cortical venous drainage) carry a high risk of
stroke.
оАБ Direct. Most carotidвАУcavernous оАЭstulae are not lifethreatening; the organ at major risk is the eye. Surgery is indicated if spontaneous closure does not occur. A post-traumatic
оАЭstula is much less likely to close on its own than a spontaneous оАЭstula because of higher blood оАЮow. Treatment is likely to
consist of a transarterial approach to repair the artery (e.g. coil

  - Fig. 4.23C and D, other) or occlude the involved sinus (e.g.
coil, balloon, other). Craniotomy for arterial repair is occasionally needed.
оАБ Indirect. If required, treatment usually involves transvenous occlusion of the involved sinus. Spontaneous closure or
occluding thrombosis sometimes (up to 50%) occurs. Intermittent carotid compression under specialist supervision has
been reported to increase the likelihood that this will take
place.

CYSTIC LESIONS

Dacryops

A dacryops is a frequently bilateral cyst of the lacrimal gland that is
thought to develop from a dilated obstructed duct. A round cystic
lesion protrudes into the superior fornix from the palpebral lobe of
the gland (Fig. 4.24) and may present with inоАЮammation. оАЯe possibility of a malignant tumour should always be considered. Treatment involves excision or marsupialization, with histopathological
analysis.

Dermoid cyst

Introduction

An orbital dermoid cyst is a choristoma (a mass of histologically
normal tissue in an abnormal location) derived from displacement
of ectoderm to a subcutaneous location along embryonic lines of
closure. Dermoids are lined by keratinized stratiоАЭed squamous
epithelium (like skin), have a оАЭbrous wall and contain dermal

Fig. 4.24 Dacryops.

appendages such as sweat glands, sebaceous glands and hair follicles; epidermoid cysts do not contain adnexal structures. Dermoids may be вАШsuperоАЭcialвАЩ or вАШdeepвАЩ, located anterior or posterior to

Diagnosis

Dermoid cysts are one of the most frequently encountered orbital
tumours in children.
оАБ Symptoms

{ A superоАЭcial orbital dermoid cyst presents in infancy with
a painless nodule, most commonly located in the superotemporal and occasionally the superonasal part of the orbit.

{ A deep dermoid cyst presents in adolescence or adult life
with a gradually increasingly protruding eye, or acutely
with an inоАЮamed orbit due to rupture.
оАБ Signs

{ SuperоАЭcial: a оАЭrm round smooth non-tender mass 1вАУ2 cm
in diameter (Fig. 4.25A), mobile under the skin but usually
tethered to the adjacent periosteum. оАЯe posterior margins are easily palpable, denoting a lack of deeper origin
or extension.

{ Deep: proptosis, dystopia or a mass lesion with indistinct
posterior margins. (Fig. 4.25B).
оАБ Investigation

{ Imaging is required in all cases to rule out dumb-bell or
subperiosteal extension.

{ SuperоАЭcial: imaging shows a well-circumscribed heterogeneous cystic lesion (Fig. 4.26A).

{ Deep: imaging shows a well-circumscribed lesion (Fig.
4.26B). Some deep dermoids, associated with bony defects,
may extend into the inferotemporal fossa or intracranially.

keratin into the surrounding tissue typically results in severe
granulomatous inоАЮammation.
оАБ Deep dermoid. Excision in toto is advisable because deep
dermoids enlarge and may leak into adjacent tissues inducing inоАЮammation, oоАЫen followed by оАЭbrosis. If incompletely
excised, dermoids may recur with persistent low-grade
inоАЮammation.

Sinus mucocoele

A mucocoele (US spelling вАУ mucocele) develops when the drainage of normal paranasal sinus secretions is obstructed due to
infection, allergy, trauma, tumour or congenital narrowing. A
slowly expanding cystic accumulation of mucoid secretions and
epithelial debris develops and gradually erodes the bony walls of
the sinus, causing symptoms by encroachment upon surrounding
tissues. Orbital invasion occurs usually from a frontal or ethmoidal
mucocoele, but rarely from those arising in the maxillary sinus.
Presentation is in adult life with proptosis or dystopia (Fig. 4.28A),
diplopia or epiphora. Pain is uncommon unless secondary infection develops (mucopyocoele). CT shows a soоАЫ tissue mass with
thinning or erosion of the bony walls of the sinus (Fig. 4.28B).
Treatment involves complete excision.

Encephalocoele

An encephalocoele (US spelling вАУ encephalocele) is formed by
herniation of intracranial contents through a congenital defect
of the base of the skull and can be located at the front or back of
the head. A meningocoele contains only dura whilst a meningoencephalocoele also contains brain tissue. Presentation is usually during infancy. Anterior orbital encephalocoeles involve the
superomedial part of the orbit and displace the globe forwards
and laterally (Fig. 4.29A), whereas posterior orbital encephalocoeles (frequently associated with neuroоАЭbromatosis type 1)
displace the globe forwards and downwards (Fig. 4.29B). оАЯe displacement increases on straining or crying and may be reduced

TIP When removing an orbital dermoid cyst take care not to
rupture the wall of the cyst, as this can cause a granulomatous
inоАЮammation in the surrounding tissue.

Treatment

оАБ SuperоАЭcial dermoid. Treatment is by excision in toto (Fig.
4.27), taking care not to rupture the lesion, since leaking of

shows the bony defect responsible for the herniation (Fig. 4.29C).
оАЯe diоАЬerential diagnosis of anterior encephalocoele includes
other causes of medial canthal swelling such as dermoid cyst and

orbital lesions that present during early life such as capillary
haemangioma, juvenile xanthogranuloma, teratoma and
microphthalmos with cyst.

VASCULAR TUMOURS

Varices
Introduction

Primary orbital varices (combined venousвАУlymphatic malformations of the orbit вАУ see also next topic) consist of a plexus of thinwalled distensible low-оАЮow vein-like vessels that are commonly,
though not always, intrinsic to the normal circulation. оАЯey are
probably hamartomatous (hamartoma вАУ a disorganized overgrowth of mature tissues normally present in the involved area).
Associations include varices of the eyelids (Fig. 4.30A and see Fig.
2.63) and conjunctiva (Fig. 4.30B). оАЯey commonly present at any
time from early childhood to late middle age, but occasionally can

be acquired later secondary to a local high-оАЮow vascular lesion or
trauma.

Diagnosis

Most cases are unilateral and the most frequent site is upper nasal.
Intermittent non-pulsatile proptosis without a bruit is reported. If
there is free communication with the normal circulation, reversible
proptosis may be precipitated or accentuated by increasing venous
pressure through coughing, straining, the Valsalva manoeuvre
(Fig. 4.30C and D), assuming a head-down position or external
compression of the jugular veins. Imaging (e.g. MRI and magnetic resonance venography [MRV], CT, ultrasound, venography)
shows a lobulated mass with variable contrast enhancement and
may demonstrate phleboliths (Fig. 4.30E) and sometimes orbital
expansion (particularly in childhood) or an associated orbital wall
defect. Complications include acute orbital haemorrhage, thrombosis (pain, proptosis, decreased vision) and optic nerve compression. Patients with long-standing lesions may develop atrophy of
surrounding fat, giving enophthalmos with a deepened superior
sulcus (Fig. 4.30F).

Treatment

Small lesions generally do not require treatment. Surgical excision
is technically diоАЪcult and oоАЫen incomplete because the lesions
are friable and bleed easily. It can be complicated by severe orbital
haemorrhage and vascular optic nerve compromise. Specialized
techniques such as embolization and carbon dioxide laser surgery
may be helpful adjuncts. Indications include recurrent thrombosis,
pain, severe proptosis and optic nerve compression.

Lymphangioma

Introduction

Lymphangioma is a rare hamartomatous vascular tumour that
tends to enlarge and inоАЭltrate diоАЬusely with time. Some authorities
believe lymphangiomas to be a variant of venous orbital anomaly (varices) across a single spectrum and the term вАШcombined
venousвАУlymphatic malformations of the orbitвАЩ has been suggested.
Although usually isolated from the main circulation, bleeding into
the lumen may occur with subsequent formation of blood-оАЭlled
вАШchocolate cystsвАЩ that regress spontaneously with time. Presentation
is usually in early childhood. DiоАЬerential diagnosis is principally
from orbital venous anomalies and haemangiomas. Intracranial
vascular malformations can be present in association.

Diagnosis

оАБ Anterior lesions typically manifest as several soоАЫ bluish
masses in the upper nasal quadrant (Fig. 4.31).
оАБ Posterior lesions may cause slowly progressive proptosis,
or initially may lie dormant and later present with the sudden onset of painful proptosis (Fig. 4.32A and B) secondary
to spontaneous haemorrhage, which may be associated with
optic nerve compression. Involvement of the lids, conjunctiva
and oropharynx may be seen; intracranial lesions may also be
present.

Treatment

In many cases the visual prognosis is good without treatment.
Surgical excision is diоАЪcult because lesions are not encapsulated,
friable, bleed easily and commonly inоАЭltrate normal orbital tissues; repeated subtotal excision may be necessary. Persistent sightthreatening chocolate cysts can be drained or removed sub-totally
by controlled vapourization using a carbon dioxide laser.

Capillary haemangioma

Introduction

Capillary haemangioma is the most common tumour of the orbit
and periorbital area in childhood. Girls are aоАЬected more commonly than boys (3:1). It may present as a small isolated lesion of
minimal clinical signiоАЭcance, or as a large disоАЭguring mass that
can cause visual impairment and systemic complications. An
established tumour is composed of anastomosing small vascular
channels without true encapsulation. It is a hamartoma вАУ a disorganized overgrowth of mature tissues normally present in the
involved area вАУ and believed to be due principally to endothelial
cell proliferation. Large or multiple lesions may have associated
visceral involvement, which can lead to serious complications
such as thrombocytopenia (KasabachвАУMerritt syndrome, with
up to 50% mortality) and high-output cardiac failure and systemic investigation should be considered. оАЯe incidence of
infantile haemangioma in the general population is around 5%
and a small proportion of these, especially if a large facial haemangioma is present, will have PHACE (PHACES) syndrome,
which includes a range of possible systemic features including
eye involvement.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. оАЯe lesion is usually noticed by the parent, usually
in the оАЭrst few months of life. Approximately 30% are present
at birth.

{ Additional haemangiomas on the eyelids (see Ch. 2) or
elsewhere are common.
оАБ Investigation. Imaging is generally performed for other than
very small lesions, mainly to rule out more extensive orbital
disease. Ultrasound shows medium internal reоАЮectivity and on
MRI or CT the lesion appears as a soоАЫ tissue mass in the anterior orbit or as an extraconal mass with оАЭnger-like posterior

{ Preseptal (deeper) tumours appear dark blue or purple

{ A large tumour may enlarge and change in colour to a deep
blue during crying or straining, but both pulsation and a

{ Deep orbital tumours give rise to unilateral proptosis without skin discoloration.

оАЯe natural course is characterized by rapid growth (Fig. 4.34A
and B) 3вАУ6 months aоАЫer diagnosis, followed by a slower phase of
natural resolution in which 30% of lesions resolve by the age of 3
years and about 75% by the age of 7. Treatment is indicated principally for amblyopia secondary to induced astigmatism, anisometropia, occlusion or strabismus and less commonly for cosmesis,
optic nerve compression or exposure keratopathy.
оАБ Beta-blockers. Oral propranolol is now widely used. оАЯe prescription and monitoring should generally be carried out by
a paediatrician. Topical preparations including timolol are
equally eоАЬective, with excellent long-term results and no systemic side eоАЬects (Fig. 4.34C and D).
оАБ Steroids

Cavernous haemangioma

Introduction

Cavernous haemangioma occurs in middle-aged adults, with a
female preponderance of 70%. оАЯe growth may be accelerated by
pregnancy. It is the most common orbital tumour in adults and is
probably a vascular malformation rather than a neoplastic lesion.
Although it may develop anywhere in the orbit, it most frequently
occurs within the lateral part of the muscle cone just behind the
globe and behaves like a low-оАЮow arteriovenous malformation.
Histology shows endothelial-lined vascular channels of varying
size separated by оАЭbrous septa (Fig. 4.35A).

{ Injection of triamcinolone acetonide (1вАУ2 ml total of
40 mg/ml over several injection sites) or betamethasone
(4 mg/ml) into a cutaneous or preseptal tumour is usually
eоАЬective in early lesions. Regression usually begins within
2 weeks but, if necessary, second and third injections can
be given aоАЫer about 2 months. It is advisable not to inject
deeply into the orbit for fear of causing occlusion of the
central retinal artery due to retrograde introduction of the
suspension. Other complications include skin depigmentation and necrosis, fat atrophy and systemic eоАЬects such
as adrenal suppression.

{ Topical high-potency steroids (e.g. clobetasol propionate
cream) are sometimes appropriate but are slow to exert
their eоАЬect.

{ Systemic steroids administered daily over several weeks
may be used, particularly if there is a large orbital component or a rapid onset of action is required.
оАБ Laser may be used to close blood vessels in superоАЭcial skin
lesions less than 2 mm in thickness.
оАБ Interferon alfa-2a and vincristine may be used for some
steroid-resistant sight-threatening lesions.
оАБ Local resection with cutting cautery or carbon dioxide laser
may reduce the bulk of an anterior circumscribed tumour, but
is usually reserved for the late inactive stage unless a resistant
tumour is sight- or life-threatening.

LACRIMAL GLAND TUMOURS

Pleomorphic lacrimal gland adenoma
Introduction

Pleomorphic adenoma (benign mixed-cell tumour) is the most
common epithelial tumour of the lacrimal gland and is derived
from the ducts and secretory elements including myoepithelial
cells. On histopathology, the inner layer of cells forms glandular
tissue that may be associated with squamous diоАЬerentiation and
keratin production (Fig. 4.36A). оАЯe outer cells undergo metaplastic change leading to the formation of myxoid tissue. Young to
middle-aged adults are the predominantly aоАЬected group.

Diagnosis

without disruption of the capsule. Incomplete excision or preliminary incisional biopsy may result in seeding of the tumour
into adjacent tissues, with recurrence and occasionally malignant change.

Lacrimal gland carcinoma

Lacrimal gland carcinoma is a rare tumour that carries a high morbidity and mortality. In order of frequency the main histological
types are adenoid cystic (50%), pleomorphic adenocarcinoma,
mucoepidermoid and squamous cell. Histopathology shows nests
of basaloid cells with numerous mitoses (Fig. 4.38A). оАЯe peak
incidence is in middle-aged adults.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. A malignant mixed-cell tumour presents in three
main clinical settings:

{ AоАЫer incomplete or piecemeal excision of a benign pleomorphic adenoma, followed by one or more recurrences
over a period of several years with eventual malignant
transformation.

B

D

{ Palpebral lobe tumour is less common and tends to grow
anteriorly causing upper lid swelling without dystopia (Fig.
4.36C), which may be visible to inspection (Fig. 4.36D).
оАБ Investigation. CT shows a round or oval mass, with a smooth
outline and indentation but not destruction of the lacrimal
gland fossa (Fig. 4.36E). оАЯe lesion may indent the globe and
calciоАЭcation may be shown.

Treatment

Treatment involves surgical excision. If the diagnosis is strongly
suspected, it is wise to avoid prior biopsy to prevent tumour seeding into adjacent orbital tissue, although this may not always be
possible in the context of diagnostic uncertainty. Tumours of the
palpebral lobe are usually resected, along with a margin of normal
tissue, through an anterior (trans-septal) orbitotomy. оАЯose of the
orbital portion are excised through a lateral orbitotomy:
оАБ оАЯe temporalis muscle is incised (Fig. 4.37A).

with inоАЮammatory lesions.

{ Optic disc swelling and choroidal folds.
оАБ Investigation

{ CT shows a globular lesion with irregular serrated edges,
oоАЫen with contiguous erosion or invasion of bone (Fig.
4.38D). CalciоАЭcation is commonly seen within the tumour.

{ Biopsy is necessary to establish the histological diagnosis.
Subsequent management depends on the extent of tumour
invasion of adjacent structures as seen on imaging.

{ Neurological assessment is mandatory because adenoidcystic carcinoma exhibits perineural spread and may
extend into the cavernous sinus.

TIP Beware of an incomplete excision of a benign pleomorphic
adenoma as this can result in malignant transformation.

оАБ Signs

{ A mass in the lacrimal area causing swelling, ptosis and
dystopia (Fig.4.38B).

144 Neural Tumours

Treatment

Treatment involves excision of the tumour and adjacent tissues.
Extensive tumours may require orbital exenteration or midfacial
resection, but the prognosis for life is frequently poor. Radiotherapy combined with local resection may prolong life and reduce
pain. Adjuvant intra-arterial chemotherapy and/or brachytherapy
may be utilized in some cases.

NEURAL TUMOURS

Optic nerve glioma
Introduction

Optic nerve glioma is a slowly growing, pilocytic astrocytoma that
typically aоАЬects children (median age 6.5 years). Histopathology
shows spindle-shaped pilocytic (hair-like) astrocytes and glial оАЭlaments (Fig. 4.39A). оАЯe prognosis is unpredictable; some have an
indolent course with little growth, while others may extend intracranially and threaten life. Approximately 30% of patients have

A

C

B

D

associated neuroоАЭbromatosis type 1 (NF1 вАУ see Ch. 19) and in these
patients the prognosis is superior. Malignant glioma (glioblastoma)
is rare, has a very poor prognosis and usually occurs in adult males.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms

{ Slowly progressive visual loss followed later by proptosis,
although this sequence may occasionally be reversed.

{ Acute loss of vision due to haemorrhage into the tumour
can occur, but is uncommon.
оАБ Signs

{ Proptosis is oоАЫen non-axial, with temporal or inferior dystopia (Fig. 4.39B).

{ оАЯe optic nerve head, initially swollen, subsequently
becomes atrophic (Fig. 4.39C).

{ Opticociliary collaterals (see Fig. 4.8C) and other fundus
signs such as central retinal vein occlusion are occasionally seen.

{ Intracranial spread to the chiasm and hypothalamus may
develop.

Fig. 4.39 Optic nerve glioma. (A) Histopathology вАУ spindle-shaped pilocytic astrocytes and

CHAPTER

Orbit 145
#### 4

оАБ Investigation

{ MRI eоАЬectively demonstrates the tumour and may show
intracranial extension if present (Fig. 4.39D).

{ CT in patients with associated NF1 shows a fusiform
enlargement of the optic nerve with a clear-cut margin
produced by the intact dural sheath. In patients without
NF1 the nerve is more irregular and shows low-density
areas.

Treatment

As the tumour is intrinsic to the optic nerve, resection means that
all vision will be lost in the operated eye.
оАБ Observation may be considered in patients with a typical pilocytic astrocytoma on imaging in whom the tumour is conоАЭned
to the orbit, especially if there is good vision and no signiоАЭcant
cosmetic impairment. Serial MRI is important if this option is
chosen. Spontaneous regression has been reported, usually in
NF1, but is very rare.
оАБ Surgical excision with preservation of the globe is indicated in
those with large or growing tumours where complete resection
of the tumour can be achieved, particularly if vision is poor
and proptosis signiоАЭcant. A key goal is to prevent chiasmal
involvement and an intracranial approach may be necessary to
achieve adequate resection.
оАБ Radiotherapy may be combined with chemotherapy for tumours
with extension that precludes complete surgical excision.

Optic nerve sheath meningioma

Introduction

Optic nerve sheath meningioma is a benign tumour arising
from meningothelial cells of the arachnoid villi surrounding
the intraorbital, or less commonly the intracanalicular, portion
of the optic nerve. In some cases, the tumour merely encircles
the optic nerve whilst in others it invades the nerve, growing
along the fibrovascular pial septa. However, about two-thirds
of all meningiomas affecting the optic nerve arise from extension of primarily intracranial lesions. Primary optic nerve
sheath meningiomas are less common than optic nerve gliomas
and, as with other meningiomas, typically affect middle-aged
women. Histopathologically, meningothelial (irregular lobules of meningothelial cells separated by fibrovascular strands

- Fig. 4.40A) and psammomatous (psammoma bodies among
proliferating meningothelial cells вАУ Fig. 4.40B) types are distinguished. The prognosis for life is good in adults, although
the tumour may be more aggressive in children, in whom 25%
occur. They are more common in neurofibromatosis type 2
(NF2).

TIP In optic nerve sheath meningioma, the onset of visual loss
usually occurs before the onset of proptosis.

Treatment

Treatment may not be indicated in a middle-aged patient with a
slowly growing lesion, but excision is required for an aggressive
tumour, particularly if the eye is blind or there is a risk of intracranial extension. Attempts at optic nerve-sparing surgery commonly
fail but may be considered on a case basis. Fractionated stereotactic radiotherapy may be appropriate as a vision-sparing approach,
or as adjunctive treatment following surgery.

Plexiform neuroоАЮbroma

Plexiform neuroоАЭbroma is the most common peripheral neural
tumour of the orbit. It occurs almost exclusively in association
with NF1. Presentation is in early childhood with periorbital swelling. Classically, involvement of the eyelids causes mechanical ptosis with a characteristic S-shaped deformity (see Fig. 2.14B), but
diоАЬuse involvement of the orbit with disоАЭguring hypertrophy of
periocular tissues may occur. On palpation the involved tissues are
said to resemble a вАШbag of wormsвАЩ. Malignant change can occur and
should be suspected if there is rapid change; radiotherapy may promote this. Treatment is oоАЫen unsatisfactory and complete surgical
removal is extremely diоАЪcult. Orbital surgery should be avoided
when possible, because of the intricate relationship between the
tumour and important structures.

Isolated neuroоАЮbroma

Isolated (localized) neuroоАЭbroma is less common than plexiform
neuroоАЭbroma; about 10% of patients have NF1. Presentation is in
the third or fourth decades with insidious mildly painful proptosis,
usually not associated with visual impairment or ocular motility
dysfunction. Excision is commonly straightforward because the
tumour is well circumscribed and relatively avascular.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms typically consist of gradual visual impairment in
one eye. Transient obscurations of vision may occur.
оАБ Signs. оАЯe classic (HoytвАУSpencer) triad consists of progressive visual loss, optic atrophy and opticociliary shunt vessels, although the simultaneous occurrence of all three signs
in one individual is actually uncommon. оАЯe usual sequence
of involvement is the opposite of that seen in tumours that
develop outside the dural sheath:

{ Optic nerve dysfunction and chronic disc swelling followed by atrophy.

{ Opticociliary collaterals (30%), which regress as optic
atrophy supervenes.

{ Restrictive motility defects, particularly in upgaze (Fig. 4.40C).

{ Proptosis (Fig. 4.40D).
оАБ Investigation

{ CT shows thickening and calciоАЭcation of the optic nerve
(Fig. 4.40E).

{ MRI is the investigation of choice (Fig. 4.40F).

{ Ultrasonography (especially coronal) may be useful.

LYMPHOMA

Introduction

Lymphomas of the ocular adnexa constitute approximately 8% of
all extranodal lymphomas. оАЯe majority of orbital lymphomas are
non-Hodgkin and most of these (80%) are of B-cell origin (Fig.
4.41A). оАЯose aоАЬected are typically older individuals. оАЯe condition may be primary, involving one or both orbits only, or secondary if there are one or more identical foci elsewhere in the body. A
substantial proportion of apparently primary lesions will develop
disease elsewhere within a few years. оАЯe course is variable and relatively unpredictable. In some patients, histological features raise
suspicion of malignancy and yet the lesion resolves spontaneously
or with steroid treatment. Conversely, what appears to be reactive lymphoid hyperplasia may be followed by the development of
lymphoma. Small lesions and those involving only the conjunctiva
have the best prognosis. Conjunctival and intraocular lymphomas
are discussed in Ch. 20

B

D

оАЯe onset is characteristically insidious.
оАБ Symptoms. An absence of symptoms is common, but may
include discomfort, double vision, a bulging eye or a visible
mass.

{ Any part of the orbit may be aоАЬected (Fig. 4.41B). Anterior
lesions may be palpated and generally have a rubbery consistency (Fig. 4.41C).

{ Occasionally the lymphoma may be conоАЭned to the conjunctiva or lacrimal glands, sparing the orbit.

{ Local lymph nodes should be palpated, but systemic evaluation by an appropriate specialist is required.
оАБ Investigation

{ Orbital imaging, usually with MRI (Fig. 4.41D).

{ Biopsy is usually performed to establish the diagnosis.

{ Systemic investigation to establish the extent of disease.

Treatment

Radiotherapy is used for localized lesions and chemotherapy for disseminated disease and some subtypes. Immunotherapy (e.g. rituximab) is a newer modality that may assume a dominant role in the
future. Occasionally a well-deоАЭned orbital lesion may be resected.

RHABDOMYOSARCOMA

Introduction

Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) is the most common soоАЫ tissue sarcoma of childhood: 40% develop in the head and neck. It is the
most common primary orbital malignancy in children but is still
a rare condition. Approximately 90% occur in children under 16
years and the average age of onset is 7 years. оАЯe tumour is derived
from undiоАЬerentiated mesenchymal cells that have the potential to
diоАЬerentiate into striated muscle. Various genetic predispositions

have been identiоАЭed, including variants of the RB1 gene responsible for retinoblastoma. Emerging evidence supports the role of
epigenetic dysregulation in rhabdomyosarcoma. Four subtypes are
recognized:
оАБ Embryonal constitutes the majority (85%) of orbital lesions.
Cells may show light microscopic features of striated muscle
diоАЬerentiation (Fig. 4.42A). Embryonal usually carries a good
prognosis.
оАБ Alveolar makes up most of the balance of orbital RMS. Fewer
cells show skeletal muscle diоАЬerentiation than embryonal and
the prognosis is worse. Particular chromosomal translocations
are characteristic on cytogenetic analysis of biopsy material.
оАБ Botyroid (4%) and pleomorphic RMS are much less common
in the orbit.

TIP Rhabdomyosarcoma can mimic an orbital infection in a
child.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Rapidly progressive unilateral proptosis is usual
and may mimic an inоАЮammatory condition such as orbital
cellulitis.
оАБ Signs

{ оАЯe tumour is most commonly superonasal or superior
(Fig. 4.42B), but may arise anywhere in the orbit, including inferiorly (Fig. 4.42C). It can also arise in other tissues,
such as conjunctiva and uvea.

{ Swelling and redness of overlying skin develop but the skin
is not warm.

METASTATIC TUMOURS

Adult metastatic tumours
Introduction

Orbital metastases are an infrequent cause of proptosis and are much
less common than metastases to the choroid. If the orbit is the site of
initial manifestation of the tumour, the ophthalmologist may be the
оАЭrst specialist to see the patient. In approximate order of frequency,
the most common primary sites are breast (up to 70%), bronchus,
prostate, skin (melanoma), gastrointestinal tract and kidney.

Diagnosis

{ Diplopia is frequent, but pain is less common.
оАБ Investigation

{ MRI shows a poorly deоАЭned mass.

{ CT shows a poorly defined mass of homogeneous density, often with adjacent bony destruction
(Fig. 4.42D).

{ Incisional biopsy is performed to conоАЭrm the diagnosis
and establish the histopathological subtype and cytogenetic characteristics.

{ Systemic investigation for metastasis should be performed,
with lung and bone being the most common sites.

оАБ Signs. Associated with the range of tumours that can spread to
the orbit, presentation can take a variety of forms.

{ Primarily with cranial nerve involvement (II, III, IV, V, VI)
and only mild proptosis with orbital apex lesions.
оАБ Investigation

{ Dystopia and proptosis (Fig. 4.43A) are the most common
features.

{ InоАЭltration of orbital tissues characterized by ptosis, diplopia, brawny indurated periorbital skin and a оАЭrm orbit,
with resistance to manual retropulsion of the globe.

{ Enophthalmos with scirrhous tumours.

{ Chronic inоАЮammation.

Treatment

Commonly used guidelines for staging and a corresponding treatment protocol were produced by the Intergroup Rhabdomyosarcoma Study Group (IRSG). Treatment encompasses a combination
of radiotherapy, chemotherapy and sometimes surgical debulking. A single oncogenic fusion gene is associated with treatment
resistance and a 40вАУ45% decrease in overall survival. Entinostat
with chemotherapy may improve the prognosis in selected cases
of rhabdomyosarcoma, but has not yet been approved for clinical
use. оАЯe prognosis for patients with disease conоАЭned to the orbit
is good.

Treatment

Treatment is aimed at preserving vision and relieving pain, because
most patients die within a year (average 4 months). Radiotherapy

{ Imaging: CT (Fig. 4.43B) and MRI typically show a nonencapsulated mass.

{ Fine needle biopsy is useful for histological conоАЭrmation.
If this fails, open biopsy may be required.

{ A search for a primary must be carried out if the patient
was not previously known to have cancer.

A B

Fig. 4.43 Metastatic renal carcinoma. (A) Proptosis; (B) axial CT showing a non-encapsulated
retrobulbar mass.

150 Metastatic Tumours

A B

Fig. 4.44 Neuroblastoma in a child. (A) Abdominal CT scan showing a large retroperitoneal
mass (arrows) with calciоАЯcation, elevating the liver; (B) coronal MRI T2-weighted FLAIR

is the mainstay of local treatment, but systemic therapy may also
be of beneоАЭt. Surgical excision of the focus is occasionally carried
out. Orbital exenteration is usually only performed if other modalities fail to control intolerable symptoms.

Childhood metastatic tumours

Neuroblastoma

Neuroblastoma is one of the most common childhood malignancies. It arises from neural crest-derived tissue of the sympathetic
nervous system, most commonly in the abdomen (Fig. 4.44A).
Presentation is usually in early childhood. In almost half of all
cases the tumour is disseminated at diagnosis, when it carries a
very poor prognosis. Orbital metastases may be bilateral and typically present with the abrupt onset of proptosis accompanied by a
superior orbital mass and lid ecchymosis. MRI shows the orbital
tumour (Fig. 4.44B).

Myeloid sarcoma

Myeloid sarcoma (granulocytic sarcoma) is a solid tumour composed of malignant cells of myeloid origin. Because the tumour
may exhibit a characteristic green colour it was formerly referred
to as chloroma. Myeloid sarcoma may occur as a manifestation
of established myeloid leukaemia, or it may precede the disease.
Orbital involvement usually presents at about age 7 years with the
rapid onset of proptosis, sometimes bilateral and can be associated with ecchymosis and lid oedema. When orbital involvement
precedes systemic leukaemia, the diagnosis may be diоАЪcult.

Langerhans cell histiocytosis

Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a rare group of disorders due
to clonal proliferations of histiocytes. Presentation ranges from
localized disease, usually with bone destruction (eosinophilic
granuloma), through multifocal bone involvement to a fulminant
systemic disease. SoоАЫ tissues are less commonly involved, but
cutaneous and visceral lesions may occur. Orbital involvement

Fig. 4.45 Advanced maxillary carcinoma showing facial swelling and upward dystopia.

consists of unilateral or bilateral osteolytic lesions and soоАЫ tissue
involvement, typically in the superotemporal quadrant. Patients
with solitary lesions tend to have a more benign course and
respond well to treatment with local curettage and intralesional
steroid injection or radiotherapy. Systemic disease has a worse
prognosis.

Orbital invasion from adjacent
structures

Sinus tumours

Malignant tumours of the paranasal sinuses, although rare, may
invade the orbit and carry a poor prognosis unless diagnosed early.
It is therefore important to be aware of both their otorhinolaryngological and ophthalmic features.
оАБ Maxillary carcinoma is by far the most common sinus tumour
to invade the orbit (Fig. 4.45).

{ Otorhinolaryngological manifestations include facial pain
and swelling, epistaxis and nasal discharge.

{ Ophthalmic features include upward dystopia, diplopia
and epiphora.
оАБ Ethmoidal carcinoma may cause lateral dystopia.
оАБ Nasopharyngeal carcinoma may spread to the orbit through
the inferior orbital оАЭssure; proptosis is a late оАЭnding.

Bony invasion

оАБ Intracranial meningioma arising from the sphenoid ridge
may invade the orbit by direct spread and cause proptosis (see
Fig. 19.58). Occasionally tumours arising from the tuberculum sellae or olfactory groove may invade the orbit through
the superior orbital оАЭssure or optic canal.
оАБ Fibrous dysplasia is a disorder in which оАЭbrous tissue develops instead of normal bone, leading to weakening and a mass

eоАЬect usually in childhood or early adulthood. Within the
orbital region this may cause facial asymmetry, proptosis, dystopia (Fig. 4.46) and visual loss. Most orbital disease is due to
the monostotic form. Polyostotic disease is associated with
endocrine disorders and cutaneous pigmentation (McCuneвАУ
Albright syndrome вАУ 10% of cases).

Orbital invasion from eyelid,
conjunctival and intraocular tumours

оАБ Orbital invasion may occur from eyelid malignancies
such as basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma or
sebaceous gland carcinoma, from conjunctival tumours
(e.g. melanoma вАУ Fig. 4.47A) and from intraocular
tumours such as choroidal melanoma or retinoblastoma
(Fig. 4.47B).

SILENT SINUS SYNDROME

In this rare condition, which usually aоАЬects middle-aged people,
there is spontaneous asymptomatic collapse of the maxillary sinus
and orbital оАЮoor secondary to a negative pressure in the sinus. оАЯe
same problem may sometimes involve the frontal and ethmoidal
sinuses. оАЯe diagnosis is conоАЭrmed by undertaking a CT scan.

Signs

оАБ Facial asymmetry.
оАБ Enophthalmos (Fig. 4.48A and B).
оАБ Diplopia secondary to inferior oblique muscle entrapment
may rarely occur.
оАБ MRI shows maxillary sinus outlet obstruction, sinus opaciоАЭcation and sinus volume loss (Fig.4.48C).

Treatment

оАБ Early surgery produces better results than delayed surgery.
оАБ Surgical treatment involves restoring sinus function by
improving the drainage from the sinus. Reconstruction of the
orbital оАЮoor can be undertaken at the same time, but most
authorities recommend waiting a further 6вАУ8 months, as spontaneous remodeling of the orbital оАЮoor oоАЫen occurs.

THE ANOPHTHALMIC SOCKET

Surgical procedures

Removal of an eye or the contents of the orbit may be indicated
for intraocular or orbital malignancy or where the eye is blind and
painful or unsightly. A number of diоАЬerent surgical and rehabilitation techniques are available.

Enucleation

Enucleation (removal of the globe) is indicated in the following
circumstances:
оАБ Primary intraocular malignancies where other treatment
modalities are not appropriate. оАЯe tumour is leоАЫ intact within
the eye for histopathological examination.

оАБ AоАЫer severe trauma where the risk of sympathetic ophthalmitis may outweigh any prospect of visual recovery (see Ch. 22).
оАБ Blind painful or unsightly eyes can also be managed by enucleation, although evisceration is generally considered the procedure of choice.

Evisceration

Evisceration refers to removal of the entire contents of the globe, whilst
the sclera and extraocular muscles remain intact (Fig. 4.49). оАЯe cornea is removed to provide access to the ocular contents. Retention of
the sclera and lack of disruption of the extraocular muscles provides
better motility than is achieved aоАЫer enucleation. Evisceration provides disrupted and incomplete material for histology and should not
be undertaken in the presence of suspected intraocular malignancy.

Exenteration

Exenteration involves removal of the globe together with the soоАЫ
tissues of the orbit (Fig. 4.50). Indications include:
оАБ Orbital malignancy, either primary or where a tumour has
invaded the orbit from the eyelids, conjunctiva, globe or adnexa,
when other forms of treatment have a very poor chance of success. Anteriorly sited tumours may allow relative sparing of posterior orbital tissue and posterior tumours may allow sparing of
eyelid skin to line the socket. Following exenteration, prostheses
can be attached to the surrounding skin with adhesive, mounted
on glasses (Fig. 4.51), or secured with osseo-integrated magnets
mounted on the orbital rim bones. оАЯe socket may be lined with
skin or a split-skin graоАЫ, or leоАЫ to heal by secondary intention.
оАБ Non-malignant disease such as orbital mucormycosis is a rare
indication.

Rehabilitation

Cosmetic shell

A cosmetic shell is an ocular prosthesis that is used to cover a
phthisical or unsightly eye. оАЯe shell can restore volume and oоАЫen
provides a good cosmetic appearance, with reasonable motility as
a result of transmitted movements from the globe.

Orbital implants

оАБ Orbital volume reduction. Enucleation or evisceration leads

provide a satisfactory solution, due to stretching of the lower
lid under its weight and to poor motility.

оАБ An implant is usually inserted during the surgery at which the eye
is removed, though secondary placement can be performed later or
a previously inserted implant exchanged. Implant materials may be
solid (вАШnon-integratedвАЩ, e.g. silicone, acrylic) or porous (вАШintegratedвАЩ,

e.g. polyethylene, hydroxyapatite). Fibrovascular ingrowth into the
latter facilitates motility of an overlying prosthesis.
оАБ Post-enucleation socket syndrome (PESS) describes a combination of signs that occur as a consequence of a volume deоАЭcit of

the orbital tissues. PESS is particularly common if an implant is
not used. Some months aоАЫer enucleation, the upper lid loses its
support and drops down over the prosthesis. In time, the prosthesis tilts backwards and turns upwards, which puts forward
pressure on the lower eyelid shallowing the fornix and causing
drooping of the lower lid (Fig. 4.52A). оАЯe main features are:

in the majority of cases the condition is sporadic and associated with older parental age. As with Crouzon syndrome, it is
frequently the result of mutations in FGFR2.
оАБ PfeiоАЬer syndrome features midfacial hypoplasia and downslanting palpebral оАЭssures (Fig. 4.54E and F). Ocular features
are similar to Apert syndrome. Inheritance is AD with genetic
heterogeneity.

{ Enophthalmos.

{ Upper lid ptosis.

{ Superior sulcus hollowing.

{ Sagging lower lid.

{ Surgical options include: (a) the insertion of a secondary
implant if there is an absence of an implant; (b) the insertion of a silicone block into the inferior orbit if there is an
implant; or (c) the implantation of a dermis-fat graоАЫ.
оАБ A peg can also be inserted into porous implants to improve
later motility, though the peg must be covered in situ by socket
tissue and cannot attach directly to the overlying prosthesis.
оАЯe motility of unpegged implants is also usually good, particularly if donor sclera or a mesh wrap is used and the extraocular muscles secured to the surface.
оАБ Extrusion (Fig. 4.52B) is a signiоАЭcant concern with all
implants. Careful placement of an implant, ensuring it is sufоАЭciently deep and is well covered with vascularized tissue, is
more important than the choice of implant material.

Ocular prosthesis

AоАЫer enucleation or evisceration, a conformer (Fig. 4.53A) made
of silicone or acrylic material is placed to support the conjunctival
fornices and remains in place until the socket is оАЭtted with an artiоАЭcial eye (Fig. 4.53B). Initial socket impression moulds can usually be taken at around 6вАУ8 weeks postoperatively and a temporary
artiоАЭcial eye placed whilst waiting for manufacture of a prosthesis
shaped to оАЭt the individual socket and matched to the fellow eye
(Fig. 4.53C and D).

CRANIOSYNOSTOSES

оАЯe craniosynostoses are a group of rare congenital conditions in
which an abnormally shaped skull results from premature closure
of skull sutures.
оАБ Crouzon syndrome features a short anteroposterior skull diameter, with midfacial hypoplasia giving a prominent lower jaw
(Fig. 4.54A). Proptosis due to shallow orbits and hypertelorism
(wide orbital separation) are the most conspicuous ocular features (Fig. 4.54B). Vision-threatening complications include
exposure keratopathy and optic atrophy, mechanisms including
chronic papilloedema and cerebral hypoperfusion secondary to
sleep apnoea. Strabismus (вАШVвАЩ exotropia вАУ see Fig. 4.54B), ametropia and amblyopia can occur and other ocular associations
have been reported. Inheritance is usually autosomal dominant
(AD); allelic variants in the gene FGFR2 are responsible.
оАБ Apert syndrome is the most severe of the craniosynostoses. Oxycephaly (conical skull), midfacial hypoplasia with a
beaked nose and low-set ears (Fig. 4.54C), syndactyly (Fig.
4.54D) and developmental delay (30%) may be present. Shallow orbits, proptosis and hypertelorism are generally less
pronounced than in Crouzon syndrome, but the same visionthreatening complications occur. Inheritance can be AD, but

158 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

DeоАЯnitions

Dry eye occurs when there is inadequate tear volume or function,
resulting in an unstable tear оАЯlm and ocular surface disease. It is
an extremely common condition, particularly in postmenopausal
women and the elderly.
оАБ Dry eye disease is a multifactorial disease of the ocular surface
and tear оАЯlm accompanied by increased osmolarity of the tear
оАЯlm and inоАЮammation of the ocular surface.
оАБ Keratoconjunctivitis sicca (KCS) refers to any eye with some
degree of dryness.
оАБ Xerophthalmia describes a dry eye associated with vitamin A
deоАЯciency.
оАБ Xerosis refers to the extreme ocular dryness and keratinization that occurs in eyes with severe conjunctival cicatrization.
оАБ Sj√ґgren syndrome is an autoimmune inоАЮammatory disease of
which dry eyes is a feature.

Physiology
Tear оАЯlm constituents

оАЭe tear оАЯlm has three layers (Fig. 5.1):
оАБ Lipid layer secreted by the meibomian glands.
оАБ Aqueous layer secreted by the lacrimal glands.
оАБ Mucous layer secreted principally by conjunctival goblet cells.
оАЭe constituents are complex, with as many as a hundred distinct proteins identiоАЯed.

Spread of the tear оАЯlm

оАЭe tear оАЯlm is mechanically distributed over the ocular surface
through a neuronally controlled blinking mechanism. оАЭree factors are required for eоАЬective resurfacing of the tear оАЯlm:
оАБ Normal blink reоАЮex.
оАБ Contact between the external ocular surface and the eyelids.
оАБ Normal corneal epithelium.

{ Growth factors derived from the lacrimal gland, the production of which increases in response to injury.

{ Pro-inоАЮammatory interleukin cytokines that accumulate
during sleep when tear production is reduced.

Lipid layer

оАБ Composition

{ оАЭe outer lipid layer is composed of a polar phase containing phospholipids adjacent to the aqueous-mucin phase
and a non-polar phase containing waxes, cholesterol esters
and triglycerides.

{ оАЭe polar lipids are bound to lipocalins within the aqueous
layer. оАЭese are small secreted proteins that have the ability
to bind hydrophobic molecules and may also contribute to
tear viscosity.

{ Lid movement during blinking is important in releasing lipids from glands. оАЭe thickness of the layer can be
increased by forced blinking and conversely reduced by
infrequent blinking.
оАБ Functions

{ To prevent evaporation of the aqueous layer and maintain
tear оАЯlm thickness.

{ To act as a surfactant allowing spread of the tear оАЯlm.

{ DeоАЯciency results in evaporative dry eye.

Aqueous layer

оАБ Secretion

{ оАЭe main lacrimal glands produce about 95% of the aqueous component of tears and the accessory lacrimal glands
of Krause and Wolfring produce the remainder.

{ Secretion of tears has basic (resting) and much greater
reоАЮex components. оАЭe latter occurs in response to corneal
and conjunctival sensory stimulation, tear break-up and
ocular inоАЮammation and is mediated via the оАЯоАЫh cranial
nerve. It is reduced by topical anaesthesia and falls during
sleep. Secretion can increase 500% in response to injury.
оАБ Composition

{ Water, electrolytes, dissolved mucins and proteins.
