оАБ Functions

{ To provide atmospheric oxygen to the corneal epithelium.

{ Antibacterial activity due to proteins such as IgA, lysozyme and lactoferrin.

{ To wash away debris and noxious stimuli and facilitate the
transport of leukocytes aоАЫer injury.

{ To optically enhance the corneal surface by abolishing
minute irregularities.

оАЭe classiоАЯcation of dry eye usually applied is that of the 2007
International Dry Eye Workshop (DEWS), with a basic division
into aqueous-deоАЯcient and evaporative types. Most individuals
have considerable overlap between mechanisms and it is important to be aware during patient assessment of the likely presence of
multiple contributory factors.

Aqueous-deоАЯcient

ClassiоАЯcation

CHAPTER

Dry Eye 159
#### 5

CHAPTER

Mucous layer

оАБ Composition

{ Mucins are high-molecular-weight glycoproteins that may
be transmembrane or secretory in type.

{ Secretory mucins are further classiоАЯed as gel-forming or
soluble. оАЭey are produced mainly by conjunctival goblet
cells but also by the lacrimal glands.

{ оАЭe superоАЯcial epithelial cells of the cornea and conjunctiva produce transmembrane mucins that form their glycocalyx (extracellular coating).

{ Staining of diseased epithelium with rose Bengal indicates
that the transmembrane and gel mucous layers are absent
and the cell surface exposed. Damage to the epithelial cells
will prevent normal tear оАЯlm adherence.
оАБ Functions

{ To permit wetting by converting the corneal epithelium
from a hydrophobic to a hydrophilic surface.

оАБ Sj√ґgren syndrome dry eye (primary or secondary).
оАБ Non-Sj√ґgren syndrome dry eye.

{ Lacrimal deоАЯciency: primary (e.g. age-related dry eye,
congenital alacrima, familial dysautonomia) or secondary (e.g. inоАЮammatory and neoplastic lacrimal gland
inоАЯltration, acquired immunodeоАЯciency syndrome
(AIDS), graоАЫ-versus-host disease, lacrimal gland or nerve
ablation).

{ Lacrimal gland duct obstruction, e.g. trachoma, cicatricial pemphigoid, chemical injury, StevensвАУJohnson
syndrome.

{ ReоАЮex hyposecretion: sensory (e.g. contact lens wear,
diabetes, refractive surgery, neurotrophic keratitis) or
motor block (e.g. seventh cranial nerve damage, systemic
drugs).

Evaporative

{ Lubrication.

{ Deficiency of the mucous layer may be a feature of
both aqueous deficiency and evaporative states. Goblet cell loss occurs with cicatrizing conjunctivitis, vitamin A deficiency, chemical burns and toxicity from
medications.

оАБ Intrinsic

{ Meibomian gland deоАЯciency, e.g. posterior blepharitis,
rosacea.

{ Disorders of lid aperture, e.g. excessive scleral show, lid
retraction, proptosis, facial nerve palsy.

{ Low blink rate, e.g. Parkinson disease, prolonged computer monitor use, reading, watching television.

Regulation of tear оАЯlm components

оАБ Hormonal

{ Androgens are the prime hormones responsible for regulation of lipid production.

{ Drug action, e.g. antihistamines, beta-blockers, antispasmodics, diuretics.
оАБ Extrinsic

{ Vitamin A deоАЯciency.

{ Oestrogens and progesterone receptors in the conjunctiva
and the lacrimal glands are essential for the normal function of these tissues.
оАБ Neural via оАЯbres adjacent to the lacrimal glands and goblet
cells that stimulate aqueous and mucus secretion.

Mechanism of disease

оАЭe four core inter-related mechanisms thought to be responsible for the manifestations of dry eye are tear instability, tear
hyperosmolarity, inоАЯammation and ocular surface damage.
InоАЮammation in the conjunctiva and accessory glands as well as
the ocular surface is present in 80% of patients with KCS and
may be both a cause and consequence of dry eye, amplifying
and perpetuating disease. оАЭe presence of inоАЮammation is the
rationale for speciоАЯc anti-inоАЮammatory measures such as steroid
therapy. оАЭere is a strong association between dry eye syndrome
and reduced levels of systemic androsterone sulphate and epiandrosterone sulphate.

TIP Whether a patient has primary aqueous deоАЯciency
or evaporative dry eye, the end result is ocular surface
inоАЮammation and discomfort.

EоАЮect of environmental factors

As well as the basic classiоАЯcation, DEWS draws attention to the
eоАЬect of the environment on the type of dry eye with which a
patient presents. оАЭese can be both internal factors, such as age,
hormonal status and behaviour patterns, and external, such as the
exacerbation of evaporative factors in an atmosphere with low
relative humidity.

{ Topical drugs including the eоАЬect of preservatives.

{ Contact lens wear.

{ Ocular surface disease, such as allergic conjunctivitis.

160 Sj√ґgren Syndrome

SJ√ЦGREN SYNDROME

Sj√ґgren syndrome (SS) is an autoimmune disorder characterized by lymphocytic inflammation and destruction of lacrimal
and salivary glands (Fig. 5.2A) and other exocrine organs. The
classic clinical triad consists of dry eyes, dry mouth (Fig. 5.2B)
and parotid gland enlargement (Fig. 5.2C), but other features
are common and can affect all organ systems. The condition is
classified as primary when it exists in isolation and secondary
when associated with another disease, commonly rheumatoid
arthritis or systemic lupus erythematosus. Primary SS affects
females more frequently than males. Although in clinical practice the diagnosis may be made on less stringent grounds, the
American College of Rheumatology (ACR) criteria for diagnosis specify, in patients with a clinical picture suggestive of SS,
the following:
оАБ Positivity for anti-SSA or anti-SSB antibodies, or positive
rheumatoid factor together with signiоАЯcantly positive antinuclear antibody.
оАБ Ocular surface staining above a certain grade.
оАБ Focal lymphocytic sialadenitis to a speciоАЯed extent on salivary
gland biopsy (see Fig. 5.2A).
Widely used but older American-European Consensus Group
criteria are more extensive and include more clinical оАЯndings, but
give results consistent with the ACR criteria.
Treatment options for SS include a range of symptomatic
treatments for dry eye, as discussed below, dry mouth and other
manifestations, salivary stimulants (e.g. oral pilocarpine) and, in
some cases, immunosuppression and biological blockers such as
rituximab.

Clinical features
Symptoms

оАЭe most common ocular symptoms are feelings of dryness, grittiness and burning that characteristically worsen over the course of
the day. Stringy discharge, transient blurring of vision, redness and
crusting of the lids are also common. Lack of emotional or reоАЮex
tearing is unusual. оАЭe symptoms of KCS are frequently exacerbated
on exposure to conditions associated with increased tear evaporation (e.g. air-conditioning, wind and central heating) or prolonged
reading or video display unit use, when blink frequency is reduced.

TIP The symptoms associated with keratoconjunctivitis sicca
are exacerbated by exposure to wind, air-conditioning and
central heating.

Signs

оАБ Posterior (seborrhoeic) blepharitis with meibomian gland
dysfunction is oоАЫen present (Fig. 5.3A and B).
оАБ Conjunctiva

{ Redness.

{ Staining with rose Bengal (Fig. 5.4A) and lissamine green
(Fig. 5.4B).

оАБ Tear оАЮlm

{ In the normal eye, as the tear оАЯlm breaks down, the mucin
layer becomes contaminated with lipid but is washed away.

{ In the dry eye, the lipid-contaminated mucin accumulates
in the tear оАЯlm as particles and debris that move with each
blink (Fig. 5.5A).

оАБ Complications can be vision-threatening and include epithelial breakdown, melting (Fig. 5.7A), perforation (Fig. 5.7B)
and occasionally bacterial keratitis (Fig. 5.7C).

TIP A patient with symptomatic dry eye syndrome may
present with superоАЯcial punctate keratitis, which can be seen
clinically with оАЮuorescein staining.

INVESTIGATION

оАЭe aim of investigation is to conоАЯrm and quantify a clinical diagnosis of dry eye. Although the symptoms in a given patient do
not change, objective signs can оАЮuctuate. оАЭe correlation between
symptoms and tests is poor. However, the reliability of tests
improves as the severity of dry eye increases. оАЭe tests measure the
following parameters:
оАБ Stability of the tear film as related to its break-up time
(BUT).

{ оАЭe marginal tear meniscus (strip) is a crude measure
of the volume of aqueous in the tear оАЯlm. In the normal
eye the meniscus is 0.2вАУ0.4 mm in height, but in dry eye
becomes thin (less than 0.25 mm) or absent (Fig. 5.5B).
оАБ Cornea
оАБ Punctate epithelial erosions that stain well with оАЮuorescein
(Fig. 5.6A).
оАБ Filaments consist of strands of mucus and debris (such as shed
epithelial cells) and are typically attached at one end to the
corneal surface (Fig. 5.6B). оАЭe оАЯlaments stain well with rose
Bengal (Fig. 5.6C), but less so with оАЮuorescein.
оАБ Mucous plaques with similar constituents may occur in severe
dry eye. оАЭey consist of semi-transparent, white-to-grey, oоАЫen
slightly elevated lesions of varying size (Fig. 5.6D).

оАБ Tear production (Schirmer, оАЮuorescein clearance and tear
osmolarity).
оАБ Ocular surface disease (corneal stains and impression
cytology).
оАЭere is no clinical test that allows the diagnosis of evaporative
dry eye to be deоАЯnitively conоАЯrmed. It is therefore a presumptive
diagnosis based on the presence of associated clinical оАЯndings. It
is suggested the tests are performed in the following order because
the Schirmer strip paper can damage the ocular surface and cause
staining.

Tear оАЯlm break-up time

оАЭe tear оАЯlm BUT is abnormal in aqueous tear deоАЯciency and meibomian gland disorders. It is measured as follows:
оАБ Fluorescein 2% or an impregnated оАЮuorescein strip moistened
with non-preserved saline is instilled into the lower fornix.
оАБ оАЭe patient is asked to blink several times.
оАБ оАЭe tear оАЯlm is examined at the slit lamp with a broad beam
using the cobalt blue оАЯlter. AоАЫer an interval, black spots or
lines appear in the оАЮuorescein-stained оАЯlm (Fig. 5.8A), indicating the formation of dry areas.
оАБ оАЭe BUT is the interval between the last blink and the appearance of the оАЯrst randomly distributed dry spot. A BUT of less
than 10 seconds is suspicious.
оАЭe development of dry spots always in the same location may
indicate a local corneal surface abnormality (e.g. epithelial basement membrane disease) rather than an intrinsic instability of the
tear оАЯlm.

Schirmer test

оАЭe Schirmer test is a useful assessment of aqueous tear production. оАЭe test involves measuring the amount of wetting of a special (no. 41 Whatman) оАЯlter paper, 5 mm wide and 35 mm long.
оАЭe test can be performed with or without topical anaesthesia.
In theory, when performed with an anaesthetic (Schirmer 2)
basic secretion is measured and without anaesthetic (Schirmer
1) it measures maximum basic plus reоАЮex secretion. In practice,
however, topical anaesthesia cannot abolish all sensory and psychological stimuli for reоАЮex secretion. оАЭe test is performed as
follows:
оАБ Excess tears are delicately dried. If topical anaesthesia is
applied the excess should be removed from the inferior fornix
with оАЯlter paper.
оАБ оАЭe оАЯlter paper is folded 5 mm from one end and inserted at
the junction of the middle and outer third of the lower lid, taking care not to touch the cornea or lashes (Fig. 5.8B).
оАБ оАЭe patient is asked to keep the eyes gently closed.
оАБ AоАЫer 5 minutes the оАЯlter paper is removed and the amount of
wetting from the fold measured.
оАБ Less than 10 mm of wetting aоАЫer 5 minutes without anaesthesia
or less than 6 mm with anaesthesia is considered abnormal.
Results can be variable and a single Schirmer test should not
be used as the sole criterion for diagnosing dry eye, but repeatedly
abnormal tests are highly supportive.

#### 5

Ocular surface staining

CHAPTER

оАБ Fluorescein stains corneal and conjunctival epithelium (see
Fig. 5.6A) where there is suоАЪcient damage to allow the dye to
enter the tissues.
оАБ Rose Bengal is a dye that has an aоАЪnity for dead or devitalized
epithelial cells that have a lost or altered mucous layer (Fig.
5.8C). Corneal оАЯlaments and plaques (see Fig. 5.6B and D) are
also shown up more clearly by the dye and the use of a red-free
оАЯlter may help visualization. A 1% solution of rose Bengal or a
moistened impregnated strip can be used. оАЭe dye may cause
intense stinging that can last for up to a day, particularly in
patients with severe KCS. To minimize irritation a very small
drop should be used, immediately preceded by a drop of topical anaesthetic and the excess washed out with saline.
оАБ Lissamine green stains in a similar fashion to rose Bengal but
causes less irritation and may be preferred (see Fig. 5.4B).
оАБ оАЭe pattern of staining may aid diagnosis:

{ Interpalpebral staining of the cornea and conjunctiva (see
Fig. 5.4A and B) is common in aqueous tear deоАЯciency.

{ Superior conjunctival stain may indicate superior limbic
keratoconjunctivitis.

{ Inferior corneal and conjunctival stain is oоАЫen present in
patients with blepharitis or exposure (Fig. 5.8D).

TIP In dry eye syndrome lissamine green solution is
preferable to 1% rose Bengal solution as it stains in a similar
fashion, but causes less irritation.

Other investigations

оАЭe following tests are rarely performed in clinical practice.
оАБ Fluorescein clearance test and the tear function index may
be assessed by placing 5 ќЉl of оАЮuorescein on the ocular surface
and measuring the residual dye in a Schirmer strip placed on
the lower lateral lid margin at set intervals. Delayed clearance
is observed in all dry eye states.
оАБ Tear оАЮlm osmolarity measurement techniques are available
and are emerging as an accurate means of diagnosis (Fig.
5.8E and F). оАЭe threshold value that distinguishes between
a healthy eye and an eye with dry eye syndrome varies from
305 mOsm/l and 316 mOsm/l, depending on the degree of tear
оАЯlm instability. A widely accepted threshold is 308 mOsm/l
and a value of 316 mOsm/l appears to discriminate between
mild and moderate/severe dry eye. Tear osmolarity may not
correlate with ocular symptoms, but it does correlate with
eоАЬective treatment when evaluated in the long term.
оАБ Tear constituent measurement. Tear samples can be assayed
for the presence of markers known to be elevated (e.g. matrix
metalloproteinase-9) or decreased (e.g. lactoferrin) in dry eye.
оАБ Phenol red thread test uses a thread impregnated with a pHsensitive dye. оАЭe end of the thread is placed over the lower lid
and the length wetted (the dye changes from yellow to red in
tears) is measured aоАЫer 15 seconds. A value of 6 mm is abnormal. It is comparable to the Schirmer test but takes less time
to perform.

оАБ Tear meniscometry is a technique to quantify the height and
thus the volume of the lower lid meniscus.
оАБ Impression cytology can determine goblet cell numbers.

TIP Measurement of tear оАЯlm osmolarity is a useful tool when
used to conоАЯrm the diagnosis and response to treatment in dry
eye syndrome.

TREATMENT

Strategy

оАЭe underlying causative processes of dry eye are generally not
reversible, and management is therefore structured around the
control of symptoms and the prevention of surface damage.
DEWS have produced guidelines based on earlier International
Taskforce Guidelines for Dry Eye, in which suggested treatment
options depend on the level of severity of disease graded from
1 to 4. оАЭe DEWS guidelines can also be applied in a graded
approach, proceeding to the next level if the preceding measures
are inadequate.

Level 1

оАБ Education and environmental/dietary modiоАЮcations

{ Establishment of realistic expectations and emphasis on
the importance of compliance.

{ Lifestyle review, including the importance of blinking
whilst reading, watching television or using a computer
screen (which should be orientated below eye level to
minimize palpebral aperture size) and the management of
contact lens wear.

{ Environmental review, e.g. increasing humidity may be
possible for some environments.

{ Instillation aids for eye drops (manufacturer-supplied or
makeshiоАЫ, such as nut-crackers to hold plastic bottles)
should be advocated for patients with reduced dexterity
(e.g. rheumatoid arthritis).

{ Caution the patient that laser refractive surgery can exacerbate dry eye.
оАБ Systemic medication review to exclude contributory eоАЬects
and eliminate oоАЬending agents. Discontinuation of toxic/preserved topical medication if possible.
оАБ ArtiоАЮcial tear substitutes, including gels and ointments вАУ
see below. Some authorities advocate that use of preserved
drops should fall within level 1 and categorize non-preserved
drops as a level 2 measure. Mucolytic agents may be speciоАЯcally indicated for some patients.
оАБ Eyelid therapy. Basic measures such as warm compresses and
lid hygiene for blepharitis. Reparative lid surgery (e.g. entropion, ectropion, excessive lid laxity or scleral show) may be
considered as an early measure. Nocturnal lagophthalmos
can be addressed by taping the lids closed at bedtime, wearing
swimming goggles during sleep, or in extreme cases by lateral
tarsorrhaphy.

166 Treatment

Level 2

оАБ Non-preserved tear substitutes are categorized as level 2
treatment by some authorities.
оАБ Anti-inflammatory agents such as topical steroids,
oral omega fatty acids and other agents such as topical
ciclosporin.
оАБ Tetracyclines (for meibomianitis, rosacea).
оАБ Punctal plugs.
оАБ Secretagogues, e.g. pilocarpine, cevimeline, rebamipide.
оАБ Moisture chamber spectacles and spectacle side shields.

Level 3

оАБ Serum eye drops. Autologous or umbilical cord serum.
оАБ Contact lenses.
оАБ Permanent punctal occlusion.

Level 4

оАБ Systemic anti-inоАЯammatory agents.
оАБ Surgery

{ Eyelid surgery, such as tarsorrhaphy.

{ Salivary gland auto-transplantation.

{ Mucous membrane or amniotic membrane transplantation for corneal complications.

Tear substitutes

Tear substitutes have a relatively simple formulation that cannot
approximate the complex components and structure of the normal tear film. Their delivery is also periodic rather than continuous. Almost all are based on replacement of the aqueous phase
of the tear film. There are no mucus substitutes and paraffin is
only an approximation to the action of tear lipids. The optimal
frequency of instillation varies with agent and with severity.
оАБ Drops and gels. A large range of preparations is available. One
agent or category of preparation has not demonstrated superiority and particular agents are oоАЫen preferred by individual
patients with limited rationale.

{ Cellulose derivatives (e.g. hypromellose, methylcellulose)
are appropriate for mild cases.

{ Carbomer gels adhere to the ocular surface and so are
longer-lasting, but some patients are troubled by slight
blurring.

{ Other agents include polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), which
increases the persistence of the tear оАЯlm and is useful in
mucin deоАЯciency, sodium hyaluronate, povidone, glycerine, propylene glycol, polysorbate and others.

оАБ ArtiоАЮcial tear inserts emplaced once or twice daily oоАЬer
extended duration treatment and are preferred by some
patients.
оАБ Mucolytic agents. Acetylcysteine 5% drops may be useful in
patients with corneal оАЯlaments and mucous plaques, which
acetylcysteine dissolves; it may cause stinging on instillation.
Acetylcysteine is malodorous and has a limited shelf-life. Manual debridement of оАЯlaments may also be useful.
оАБ Preservatives can be a potent source of toxicity, especially aоАЫer
punctal occlusion. Numerous non-preserved drops are now
available, including some multi-dose products and in general
should be used in preference to preservative-containing preparations in any more than mild disease or with instillation more
than three or four times daily. If possible, preservative-free formulations should also be used for dry eye patients when other
topical medication is required, for example in the treatment of
glaucoma. Newer preservatives such as Polyquad and Purite
seem to exhibit lower ocular surface toxicity than older agents
such as benzalkonium chloride.
оАБ Haemoderivative treatment has been used to manage severe
ocular surface disease including graоАЫ-versus-host-related dry
eye disease, Sj√ґgren syndrome, post-LASIK dry eye persistent
epithelial defects and recurrent erosions (see below).

TIP Excessive use of preserved lubricating eye drops can
result in corneal toxicity.

Punctal occlusion

Punctal occlusion reduces drainage and thereby preserves natural
tears and prolongs the eоАЬect of artiоАЯcial tears. It is of greatest value
in patients with moderateвАУsevere KCS who have not responded to
frequent instillation of topical agents.
оАБ Temporary occlusion can be achieved by inserting collagen
plugs into the canaliculi. оАЭese dissolve over a number of
weeks. оАЭe main aim is to ensure that epiphora does not occur
following permanent occlusion.

{ In severe KCS both the inferior and superior canaliculi can
be plugged.
оАБ Reversible prolonged occlusion can be achieved with silicone
(Fig. 5.9) or long-acting (2вАУ6 months) collagen plugs.

{ Initially the inferior puncta are occluded and the patient is
reviewed aоАЫer 1 or 2 weeks.

{ If the patient is now asymptomatic and without epiphora,
the plugs can be removed and the inferior canaliculi permanently occluded (see below).

{ Diquafosol is a newer agent that works as a topical
secretagogue.
оАБ Ointments containing petrolatum (paraоАЪn) mineral oil can
be used at bedtime to supplement daytime drops or gel instillation; daytime use is precluded by marked blurring. Some practitioners do not prescribe these for long-term use.
оАБ Eyelid sprays are applied to the closed eye and typically contain a liposome-based agent that may stabilize the tear оАЯlm and
reduce evaporation.

{ Plugs that pass into the horizontal portion of the canaliculus cannot be visualized and although they can usually be
оАЮushed out with saline, if they cause epiphora this is not
always possible and surgical retrieval may be needed.
оАБ Permanent occlusion should be undertaken only in patients
with severe dry eye who have had a positive response to
temporary plugs without epiphora. It should be avoided in

{ Problems include extrusion, granuloma formation and

patients, especially if young, who may have reversible pathology. All four puncta should not be occluded at the same time.

{ Permanent occlusion is performed following punctal

{ Laser cautery seems to be less consistently eоАЬective than

TIP Punctal occlusion with a silicone plug can be a useful
therapeutic option in patients with moderateвАУsevere
keratoconjunctivitis sicca.

Anti-inоАЮammatory agents

оАБ Topical steroids, generally low-intensity preparations such
as оАЮuorometholone are eоАЬective supplementary treatment for
acute exacerbations. оАЭe risks of longer-term treatment must
be balanced against the potential beneоАЯts in each case.
оАБ Omega fatty acid supplements (e.g. omega-3 оАЯsh oil, оАЮax seed
oil) can have a dramatic eоАЬect on symptoms and may facilitate
the reduction of topical medication.
оАБ Oral tetracyclines for an extended course, oоАЫen 3 months at
a relatively low dose, may control associated blepharitis, especially meibomianitis and reduce tear levels of inоАЮammatory
mediators. Doxycycline may be preferred to minocycline on
the grounds of adverse eоАЬect proоАЯle.
оАБ Topical ciclosporin (usually 0.05%) reduces T-cell-mediated
inоАЮammation of lacrimal tissue, resulting in an increase in the
number of goblet cells and reversal of squamous metaplasia of
the conjunctiva.

Contact lenses

Although contact lens wear can exacerbate dry eye, particularly
due to inоАЮammatory, sensory and evaporative eоАЬects, the symptoms can be outweighed by the reservoir eоАЬect of оАЮuid trapped

behind the lens. In addition, contact lenses are eоАЬective at relieving symptoms resulting from secondary corneal changes. However, patients should be cautioned that there is an increased risk
of bacterial keratitis.
Several manufacturers have developed contact lenses speciоАЯcally designed to reduce dry eye discomfort during lens wear. оАЭe
materials used (silicone hydrogel) retain moisture for 12вАУ16 hours
and are single-use daily disposable. Occlusive gas-permeable
scleral contact lenses provide a reservoir of saline over the cornea
and are occasionally used in a patient with an extremely dry eye
and exposure. Silicone rubber lenses, which were used in the past,
are no longer available.

Optimization of environmental humidity

оАБ Reduction of room temperature to minimize evaporation of
tears.
оАБ Room humidiоАЮers may be tried but are frequently disappointing because much apparatus is incapable of signiоАЯcantly
increasing the relative humidity of an average-sized room. A
temporary local increase in humidity can be achieved with
moist chamber goggles or side shields to glasses but may be
cosmetically unacceptable.

Miscellaneous options

оАБ Botulinum toxin injection to the orbicularis muscle may help
control the blepharospasm that oоАЫen occurs in severe dry eye.
Injected at the medial canthus it can also reduce tear drainage,
presumably by limiting lid movement.
оАБ Oral cholinergic agonists such as pilocarpine (5 mg four times
daily) and cevimeline may reduce the symptoms of dry eye and
dry mouth in patients with Sj√ґgren syndrome. Adverse eоАЬects
including blurred vision and sweating may be less marked with
cevimeline.
оАБ Submandibular gland transplantation for extreme dry eye
requires extensive surgery and may produce excessive levels of
mucus in the tear оАЯlm.

168 Treatment

оАБ Serum/autologous blood eye drops. Autologous or umbilical
cord serum (20вАУ100%), the blood component remaining aоАЫer
clotting, has produced subjective and objective improvements
in studies in patients with dry eye and may aid the healing of
persistent epithelial defects. However, their production and
storage are associated with practical challenges. Finger prick
autologous blood is a low cost, readily accessible and practical
treatment that appears to be safe and eоАЬective.

оАБ Cenegermin 0.002%, a topical recombinant human nerve
growth factor (registered for use in neurotrophic keratopathy)
may have a role to play in severe Sj√ґgren disease. оАЭe safety
and eоАЪcacy are being investigated.
оАБ Photobiomodulation therapy applied to the eyelids. Low
level light, red light and intense pulsed light are relatively new
interventional options that may be helpful in the treatment of
dry eye secondary to severe meibomian gland dysfunction.

170 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

Anatomy

оАЯe conjunctiva is a transparent mucous membrane that lines the
inner surface of the eyelids and the anterior surface of the globe,
terminating at the corneoscleral limbus. It is richly vascular, supplied by the anterior ciliary and palpebral arteries. оАЯere is a dense
lymphatic network, with drainage to the preauricular and submandibular nodes corresponding to that of the eyelids. It has a key
protective role, mediating both passive and active immunity. Anatomically, it is divided into the following:
оАБ оАЯe palpebral conjunctiva starts at the mucocutaneous junction of the lid margins and is оАЮrmly attached to the posterior
tarsal plates. оАЯe tarsal blood vessels are vertically orientated.
оАБ оАЯe forniceal conjunctiva is loose and redundant.
оАБ оАЯe bulbar conjunctiva covers the anterior sclera and is continuous with the corneal epithelium at the limbus. Radial
ridges at the limbus form the palisades of Vogt, the likely reservoir of corneal stem cells. оАЯe stroma is loosely attached to the
underlying Tenon capsule, except at the limbus, where the two
layers fuse. оАЯe plica semilunaris (semilunar fold) is present
nasally, medial to which lies a оАЭeshy nodule (caruncle) consisting of modiоАЮed cutaneous tissue.

Histology

оАБ оАЯe epithelium is non-keratinizing and around оАЮve cell layers
deep (Fig. 6.1). Basal cuboidal cells evolve into оАЭattened polyhedral cells, subsequently being shed from the surface. Mucussecreting goblet cells are located within the epithelium, being
most dense inferonasally and in the fornices.
оАБ оАЯe stroma (substantia propria) consists of richly vascularized loose connective tissue. оАЯe accessory lacrimal glands
of Krause and Wolfring are located deep within the stroma.
Secretions from the accessory lacrimal glands are essential
components of the tear оАЮlm.

оАБ Conjunctiva-associated lymphoid tissue (CALT) is critical in the initiation and regulation of ocular surface immune
responses. It consists of lymphocytes within the epithelial layers, lymphatics and associated blood vessels, with a stromal
component of lymphocytes and plasma cells, including follicular aggregates.

Clinical features of conjunctival
inоАЯammation
Symptoms

Non-speciоАЮc symptoms include lacrimation, grittiness, stinging
and burning. Itching is the hallmark of allergic disease, although
it may also occur to a lesser extent in blepharitis and in patients
with dry eye syndrome. оАЯe visual acuity is not usually aоАЬected.
SigniоАЮcant pain, photophobia or a marked foreign body sensation
suggest corneal involvement.

Discharge

оАБ Watery discharge is composed of a serous exudate and tears
and occurs in acute viral or acute allergic conjunctivitis.
оАБ Mucoid discharge is typical of chronic allergic conjunctivitis
and dry eye.
оАБ Mucopurulent discharge typically occurs in chlamydial or
acute bacterial infection.
оАБ Moderately purulent discharge occurs in acute bacterial
conjunctivitis.
оАБ Severe purulent discharge is suggestive of gonococcal
infection.

Conjunctival reaction

оАБ Hyperaemia that is diоАЬuse, beefy-red and more intense away
from the limbus is usual in bacterial infection (Fig. 6.2A). оАЯis
вАШconjunctival injectionвАЩ should be distinguished from the ciliary injection of iridocyclitis (see Ch. 12).
оАБ Haemorrhages may occur in viral conjunctivitis, when they are
oоАЫen multiple, small and discrete (вАШpetechialвАЩ) and severe bacterial conjunctivitis, when they are larger and diоАЬuse (Fig. 6.2B).
оАБ Chemosis (conjunctival oedema) is seen as a translucent
swelling (Fig. 6.2C), which may protrude through the eyelids.
Acute chemosis usually indicates a hypersensitivity response
(e.g. pollen), but can also occur in severe infective conjunctivitis. Subacute or chronic chemosis has numerous causes:

{ Local, e.g. thyroid eye disease, chronic allergic conjunctivitis, ocular or eyelid surgery, trauma.

{ Increased systemic vascular permeability, e.g. allergic conditions, infections including meningitis, vasculitis.

{ Increased venous pressure, e.g. superior vena cava syndrome, right-sided heart failure.

{ Decreased plasma oncotic pressure, e.g. nephrotic
syndrome.
оАБ Membranes

{ Pseudomembranes (Fig. 6.2D) consist of coagulated exudate adherent to the inоАЭamed conjunctival epithelium. оАЯey
Fig. 6.1 Histology of the conjunctiva. can be peeled away leaving the underlying epithelium intact.

BACTERIAL CONJUNCTIVITIS

Acute bacterial conjunctivitis

Acute bacterial conjunctivitis is a common and usually selflimiting condition caused by direct contact with infected secretions. The most common isolates are Streptococcus pneumoniae,
Staphylococcus aureus, Haemophilus influenzae and Moraxella
catarrhalis. A minority of severe cases are caused by the sexually
transmitted organism Neisseria gonorrhoeae, which can readily
invade the intact corneal epithelium. Meningococcal (Neisseria
meningitidis) conjunctivitis is rare and usually affects children.

Diagnosis

Lymphadenopathy

оАЯe most common cause of lymphadenopathy associated with
conjunctivitis is viral infection. It may also occur in chlamydial
and severe bacterial conjunctivitis (especially gonococcal) and
Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome. оАЯe preauricular site is typically aоАЬected.

TIP Viral conjunctivitis tends to occur in epidemics and
commonly causes preauricular lymphadenopathy.

оАБ Symptoms

{ Acute onset of redness, grittiness, burning and discharge.

{ Involvement is usually bilateral although one eye may
become aоАЬected 1вАУ2 days before the other (Fig. 6.4A).

{ On waking, the eyelids are frequently stuck together and
may be diоАЪcult to open.

{ SuperоАЮcial corneal punctate epithelial erosions are

{ Peripheral corneal ulceration may occur in gonococcal
and meningococcal infection and may rapidly progress to
perforation.

{ Gonococcal and meningococcal conjunctivitis should be
treated with a quinolone, gentamicin, chloramphenicol
or bacitracin 1вАУ2 hourly as well as systemic therapy (see
below).
оАБ Systemic antibiotics are required in the following
circumstances:

{ Lymphadenopathy is usually absent except in severe gonococcal and meningococcal infection.
оАБ Investigations are not performed routinely but may be indicated in the following situations:

{ In severe cases, binocular conjunctival swabs and scrapings should be taken for urgent Gram staining, to exclude
gonococcal and meningococcal infection (Gram-negative
kidney-shaped intracellular diplococci).

{ Culture should include enriched media such as chocolate

{ Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) may be required for

infection.

{ Preseptal or orbital cellulitis (see Ch. 4).
оАБ Topical steroids may reduce scarring in membranous and
pseudomembranous conjunctivitis.
оАБ Irrigation to remove excessive discharge may be useful in
hyperpurulent cases.
оАБ Contact lens wear should be discontinued until at least
48 hours aоАЫer complete resolution of symptoms. Contact
lenses should not be worn whilst topical antibiotic treatment
continues.
оАБ Risk of transmission should be reduced by hand-washing
and the avoidance of towel sharing.
оАБ Review is unnecessary for most mild/moderate adult cases,
although patients should be cautioned to seek further advice
in the event of deterioration.
оАБ Statutory notiоАЮcation of public health authorities may be
required locally for some causes.

{ Gonococcal infection is usually treated with a thirdgeneration cephalosporin such as ceоАЫriaxone; quinolones
and some macrolides are alternatives. It is essential to
seek advice from a microbiologist and/or genitourinary
specialist.

{ H. inоАЯuenzae infection, particularly in children, is treated
with oral amoxicillin with clavulanic acid. оАЯere is a 25%
risk of developing otitis and other systemic problems.

{ Meningococcal conjunctivitis, particularly in children in
whom early systemic prophylaxis may be life-saving, as up
to 30% may develop systemic disease without treatment.
оАЯe advice of paediatric and infectious disease specialists
must be sought, but if in doubt treatment with intramuscular benzylpenicillin, ceоАЫriaxone or cefotaxime, or oral
ciproоАЭoxacin should not be delayed.

TIP A hyperacute purulent ocular discharge may be a sign
of gonococcal or meningococcal conjunctivitis. A specimen
should be taken for microscopy, culture and sensitivity (MCS) in
order to prescribe an appropriate systemic antibiotic.

Treatment

About 60% resolve within 5 days without treatment.
оАБ Topical antibiotics, usually four times daily for up to a week
but sometimes more intensively, are frequently administered
to speed recovery and prevent re-infection and transmission.
оАЯere is no evidence that any particular antibiotic is more
eоАЬective. Ointments and gels provide a higher concentration
for longer periods than drops, but should be avoided during
the day because blurred vision may follow. оАЯe following antibiotics are available:

{ Chloramphenicol, aminoglycosides (gentamicin, neomycin, tobramycin), quinolones (ciproоАЭoxacin, oоАЭoxacin,
levoоАЭoxacin, lomeоАЭoxacin, gatiоАЭoxacin, moxiоАЭoxacin,
besiоАЭoxacin), macrolides (erythromycin, azithromycin)
polymyxin B, fusidic acid and bacitracin.

Giant fornix syndrome

Giant fornix syndrome is an uncommon entity causing chronic
relapsing pseudomembranous purulent conjunctivitis. It is
believed to be due to retained debris in a voluminous upper fornix
acting as a focus for persistent bacterial colonization (usually S.
aureus) in an elderly patient with levator disinsertion. Large protein aggregations may be visualized in the upper fornix, though
double eversion with a retractor may be necessary to identify
these. Secondary corneal vascularization and lacrimal obstruction are common. It is frequently unilateral. Treatment involves
repeated sweeping of the fornix with a cotton-tipped applicator
and topical and systemic antibiotics; intensive topical steroid may
be helpful. Surgical reconstruction of the fornix may be necessary
in recalcitrant cases.

CHAPTER

Conjunctiva 175
#### 6

Adult chlamydial conjunctivitis
Pathogenesis

Chlamydia trachomatis (Fig. 6.5) is a species of Chlamydiae, a
phylum of bacteria that cannot replicate extracellularly and hence
depends on host cells. оАЯey exist in two principal forms: a robust
infective extracellular вАШelementary bodyвАЩ and a fragile intracellular replicating вАШreticular bodyвАЩ. Adult chlamydial (inclusion) conjunctivitis is an oculogenital infection usually caused by serovars
(serological variants) DвАУK of C. trachomatis and aоАЬects 5вАУ20% of
sexually active young adults in Western countries. Transmission
is by autoinoculation from genital secretions, although eye-to-eye
spread probably accounts for about 10%. оАЯe incubation period is
approximately a week.

Urogenital infection

оАБ In males, chlamydial infection is the most common cause of
non-gonococcal urethritis (NGU), also termed non-speciоАЮc
urethritis (NSU). It should be noted that the latter term is also
sometimes used to mean urethritis in which both gonococcal and chlamydial infection have been ruled out. Chlamydial
urethritis is frequently asymptomatic in men. C. trachomatis
may also cause epididymitis and can act as a trigger for Reiter
syndrome.
оАБ In females, chlamydial urethritis typically causes dysuria and
discharge. It may progress to pelvic inоАЭammatory disease
(PID), carrying a risk of infertility. Approximately 5вАУ10% of
women with PID develop perihepatitis (Fitz-HughвАУCurtis
syndrome).

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms consist of the subacute onset of unilateral or bilateral redness, watering and discharge. Untreated, the conjunctivitis becomes chronic and though self-limiting may persist for
several months. It is important to enquire about sexual exposure if chlamydial conjunctivitis is suspected.

conjunctivitis) agents venereum) agents

оАБ Signs

{ Erythromycin, amoxicillin and ciproоАЭoxacin are
alternatives.
оАБ Topical antibiotics such as erythromycin or tetracycline ointment are sometimes used to achieve rapid relief of ocular
symptoms, but are insuоАЪcient alone.
оАБ Reduction of transmission risk involves abstinence from
sexual contact until completion of treatment (1 week aоАЫer
azithromycin), together with other precautions as for any
infectious conjunctivitis.
оАБ Re-testing for persistent infection should take place 6вАУ12
weeks aоАЫer treatment.
оАБ It is important to be aware that symptoms commonly take
weeks to settle and that follicles and corneal inоАЮltrates can take

{ Watery or mucopurulent discharge.

{ Tender preauricular lymphadenopathy.

{ Large follicles are oоАЫen most prominent in the inferior
fornix (Fig. 6.6A) and may also involve the upper tarsal
conjunctiva (Fig. 6.6B).

{ SuperоАЮcial punctate keratitis is common.

{ Chronic cases have less prominent follicles and commonly
develop papillae.

{ Mild conjunctival scarring and superior corneal pannus
(Fig. 6.6D) are not uncommon.
оАБ Investigations. Tarsal conjunctival scrapings are obtained
using a spatula or the blunt side of a scalpel blade.

{ Nucleic acid ampliоАЮcation tests such as PCR are likely to be
the investigation of choice in time but validation for ocular
specimens is limited at present.

{ Giemsa staining for basophilic intracytoplasmic bodies is
performed by applying scrapings onto a glass slide.

{ Direct immunoоАЭuorescence detects free elementary bodies with approximately 90% sensitivity and speciоАЮcity.

{ Enzyme immunoassay for direct antigen detection is also
useful.

{ McCoy cell culture is highly speciоАЮc.

{ Swabs taken for bacterial culture and serology may be
helpful in selected cases.

Treatment

Empirical treatment may be given if the clinical picture is convincing pending investigation results.
оАБ Referral to a genitourinary specialist is mandatory in conоАЮrmed cases, particularly for the exclusion of other sexually transmitted infections, contact tracing and pregnancy
testing.
оАБ Systemic therapy involves one of the following:

{ Azithromycin 1 g repeated aоАЫer 1 week is generally the
treatment of choice, although a second or a third course is
required in up to 30% of cases. Some guidelines advocate
only a single 1 g dose.

{ Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10 days (tetracyclines
are relatively contraindicated in pregnancy/breastfeeding
and in children under 12 years of age).

Trachoma
Pathogenesis

Trachoma is the worldвАЩs leading cause of preventable irreversible
blindness. It is related to poverty, overcrowding and poor hygiene,
the morbidity being a consequence of the establishment of reinfection cycles within communities. Whereas an isolated episode
of trachomatous conjunctivitis may be relatively innocuous, recurrent infection elicits a chronic immune response consisting of a
cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity (Type IV) reaction to the
intermittent presence of chlamydial antigen and can lead to loss of
sight. Prior contact with the organism confers short-term partial
immunity but also leads to a heightened inоАЭammatory reaction
upon re-infection. Vaccination has an eоАЬect similar to primary

infection in sensitizing the individual and as a consequence is
not helpful. оАЯe family childcare group is the most important reinfection reservoir and young children are particularly vulnerable.
оАЯe оАЭy is an important vector, but there may be direct transmission
from eye or nasal discharge. Trachoma is associated principally
with infection by serovars A, B, Ba and C of Chlamydia trachomatis, but the serovars DвАУK conventionally associated with adult
inclusion conjunctivitis and other species of the Chlamydiaceae
family such as Chlamydophila psittaci and Chlamydophila pneumoniae have also been implicated.

Diagnosis

Features of trachoma are divided into an вАШactiveвАЩ inоАЭammatory
stage and a вАШcicatricialвАЩ chronic stage, with considerable overlap.
A World Health Organization (WHO) grading system is in use
(Table 6.1).
оАБ Active trachoma is most common in pre-school children and
is characterized by the following:

{ Mixed follicular/papillary conjunctivitis (Fig. 6.7A) associated with a mucopurulent discharge. In children under the
age of 2 years the papillary component may predominate.

оАБ Surgery is aimed at relieving entropion and trichiasis and
maintaining complete lid closure, principally with bilamellar
tarsal rotation.

TIP Systemic tetracyclines should not be used in pregnancy
and in children under 12 years of age because of the risk of
tooth staining.

Neonatal conjunctivitis

Neonatal conjunctivitis (ophthalmia neonatorum) is deоАЮned as conjunctival inоАЭammation developing within the оАЮrst month of life. It
is the most common infection of any kind in neonates, occurring in
up to 10%. It is identiоАЮed as a speciоАЮc entity distinct from conjunctivitis in older infants because of its potentially serious nature (both
ocular and systemic complications) and because it is oоАЫen the result
of infection transmitted from mother to infant during delivery.

Causes

оАБ Organisms acquired during vaginal delivery: C. trachomatis,
N. gonorrhoeae (now rare in wealthier countries, but
previously responsible for 25% of childhood blindness) and
herpes simplex virus (typically HSV-2). With all of these, conjunctivitis is not uncommonly associated with severe ocular
or systemic complications. C. trachomatis is the most -common cause in cases involving moderateвАУsevere conjunctival
inоАЭammation.
оАБ Staphylococci are usually responsible for mild conjunctivitis. Other bacterial causes include streptococci, H. inоАЯuenzae
and various Gram-negative organisms.
оАБ Topical preparations used as prophylaxis against infection
(see below) may themselves cause conjunctival irritation
(chemical conjunctivitis).
оАБ Congenital nasolacrimal obstruction. Despite poor neonatal tear production, a persistently mildly watery eye with
recurrent mild bacterial conjunctivitis may be secondary to a
blocked tear duct.

Diagnosis

{ Severe corneal opaciоАЮcation.

{ Dry eye caused by destruction of goblet cells and the ductules of the lacrimal gland.
оАБ Investigations are rarely used in the aоАЬected areas as in most
cases the diagnosis can be made based on the clinical features.
Various оАЮeld techniques (e.g. dipstick enzyme immunoassay)
are available and other investigations are similar to those for
adult inclusion conjunctivitis.

Management

оАЯe SAFE strategy for trachoma management supported by the
WHO and other agencies encompasses Surgery for trichiasis,
Antibiotics for active disease, Facial hygiene and Environmental
improvement.
оАБ Antibiotics should be administered to those aоАЬected and to all
family members. A single antibiotic course is not always eоАЬective in eliminating infection in an individual and communities
may need to receive annual treatment to suppress infection.

оАБ Timing of onset

{ Chemical irritation: оАЮrst few days.

{ Gonococcal: оАЮrst week.

{ Staphylococci and other bacteria: end of the оАЮrst week.

{ A single dose of azithromycin (20 mg/kg up to 1 g) is the
treatment of choice.

{ Erythromycin 500 mg twice daily for 14 days or doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10 days (tetracyclines are relatively contraindicated in pregnancy/breastfeeding and in
children under 12).

{ Chlamydia: 1вАУ3 weeks.
оАБ History

{ Herpes simplex virus (HSV): 1вАУ2 weeks.

{ Features of systemic illness in the child: pneumonitis, rhinitis and otitis in chlamydial infection, skin vesicles and
features of encephalitis in HSV. Disseminated gonococcal
infection is relatively rare.

{ Prior persistent watering without inоАЭammation may indicate a nasolacrimal duct that is not yet open.

{ Instillation of a prophylactic chemical preparation.

{ Parental symptoms of sexually transmitted infection (STI).

{ Recent conjunctivitis in close contacts.

{ Topical 1% tetracycline ointment is less eоАЬective than oral
treatment.
оАБ Facial cleanliness is a critical preventative measure.
оАБ Environmental improvement, such as access to adequate
water and sanitation, as well as control of оАЭies, is important.

оАБ Signs

{ A mildly sticky eye may occur in staphylococcal infection,
or with delayed nasolacrimal duct canalization (mucopurulent reоАЭux on pressure over the lacrimal sac).

{ Discharge is characteristically watery in chemical and HSV
infection, mucopurulent in chlamydial infection, purulent
(Fig. 6.8) in bacterial infection and hyperpurulent in gonococcal conjunctivitis.

{ Severe eyelid oedema occurs in gonococcal infection. It may
be diоАЪcult to distinguish severe conjunctivitis from preseptal or orbital infection. Signs of dacryocystitis should be
excluded.

{ Eyelid and periocular vesicles may occur in HSV infection
and can critically aid early diagnosis and treatment.

{ Corneal examination is mandatory and is particularly
important if gonococcal infection is suspected, as ulceration with rapid progression is common. Use of a pen torch,
insertion of an eyelid speculum and оАЭuorescein drops may
be helpful. оАЯe latter may facilitate identiоАЮcation of a dendritic or geographic epithelial lesion that may be present in
HSV infection (in contrast to the punctate epitheliopathy
seen in older children with primary herpetic conjunctivitis).

{ Pseudomembranes are not uncommon in chlamydial
conjunctivitis.

оАБ Prophylaxis is routinely performed but there is no standard

{ A single instillation of povidone-iodine 2.5% solution is

{ Erythromycin 0.5% or tetracycline 1% ointment.

{ Congenital glaucoma may masquerade as neonatal conjunctivitis and should always be considered, particularly in

{ оАЯe results of any parental prenatal testing for STI should

{ Conjunctival scrapings are taken for nucleic acid ampliоАЮcation (PCR), particularly for Chlamydia and HSV.

{ Separate conjunctival scrapings are applied to a glass slide

may be present on Gram stain in HSV infection.

{ Conjunctival swabs are taken with a calcium alginate
swab or a sterile cotton-tipped applicator, for standard

{ Silver nitrate 1% solution agglutinates gonococci and is

infection is present.
оАБ Chemical conjunctivitis does not require treatment apart
from artiоАЮcial tears.
оАБ Mild conjunctivitis. A mildly sticky eye is extremely common in neonates. Investigation is oоАЫen unnecessary and a
low-intensity regimen with a broad-spectrum topical antibiotic such as chloramphenicol, erythromycin or fusidic acid
ointment is adequate in most cases. Further investigation and
treatment can be instituted if the condition fails to settle.
оАБ ModerateвАУsevere cases should be investigated as above.
Microscopy with Gram staining alone is highly sensitive and
will oоАЫen provide a working diagnosis.

on an empirical basis aоАЫer samples have been collected.

may be present on Gram stain in HSV infection. { If bacteria are evident on Gram stain, a broad-spectrum

acin or gentamicin for Gram-negatives) should be used

оАБ Severe conjunctivitis, or when systemic illness is suspected,
requires hospital admission. Samples should be taken for a
range of investigations, including urgent microscopy and a
broad-spectrum topical antibiotic, such as erythromycin, commenced. оАЯe ocular risk is usually most acute from gonococcal
infection, so empirical topical treatment should cover this and
in most cases, consideration given to systemic treatment such
as parenteral ceоАЫriaxone.
оАБ Chlamydial infection is treated with oral erythromycin for
2 weeks. A longer or supplementary course may be needed.
Erythromycin or tetracycline ointment can be used in addition, but is probably unnecessary.

180 Viral Conjunctivitis

оАБ Herpes simplex infection should always be regarded as a
systemic condition and is treated with high-dose intravenous
aciclovir under paediatric specialist care. Early diagnosis and
treatment of encephalitis (PCR of cerebrospinal оАЭuid (CSF) is
positive in 95%) may be life-saving or prevent serious neurological disability. Topical aciclovir may be considered in addition.
оАБ Microbiological advice should be sought in severe cases, especially regarding local antibiotic sensitivities.
оАБ Paediatric specialist involvement is mandatory when systemic
disease may be present.
оАБ Genitourinary referral for the mother and her sexual contacts
is important when an STI is diagnosed. оАЯe neonate should be
screened for other STIs.
оАБ NotiоАЮcation of a case of neonatal conjunctivitis to the local public health authority is a statutory requirement in many countries.

VIRAL CONJUNCTIVITIS

Introduction

Viral conjunctivitis is a common external ocular infection, adenovirus (a non-enveloped double-stranded DNA virus) being
the most frequent (90%) causative agent. It may be sporadic, or
occur in epidemics in environments such as workplaces (including
hospitals), schools and swimming pools. оАЯe spread of this highly
contagious infection is facilitated by the ability of viral particles to
survive on dry surfaces for weeks and by the fact that viral shedding may occur for many days before clinical features are apparent. Transmission is generally by contact with respiratory or ocular
secretions, including via fomites such as contaminated towels.

TIP Viral conjunctivitis is highly contagious and great care
should be taken to prevent transmission of the disease.

Presentation

оАЯe spectrum of viral conjunctivitis varies from mild subclinical
disease to severe inоАЭammation with signiоАЮcant morbidity. оАЯere
will oоАЫen be a history of a close contact with acute conjunctivitis.
оАБ Non-speciоАЮc acute follicular conjunctivitis is the most common clinical form of viral conjunctivitis and is typically due to
adenoviral infection by a range of serological variants. Unilateral watering, redness, irritation and/or itching and mild photophobia occur, the contralateral eye generally being aоАЬected
1вАУ2 days later, oоАЫen less severely. оАЯe condition is usually
milder than the other clinical forms of adenoviral conjunctivitis. Patients may have accompanying (usually mild) systemic
symptoms, such as a sore throat or common cold.
оАБ Pharyngoconjunctival fever (PCF) is caused mainly by
adenovirus serovars 3, 4 and 7. It is spread by droplets within
families with upper respiratory tract infection. Keratitis develops in about 30% of cases but is seldom severe. Symptoms are
essentially as above, though sore throat is typically prominent.
оАБ Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC) is caused mainly by
adenovirus serovars 8, 19 and 37 and is the most severe ocular
adenoviral infection. Keratitis, which may be marked, develops
in about 80%. Photophobia may be correspondingly prominent.

оАБ Acute haemorrhagic conjunctivitis usually occurs in tropical
areas. It is typically caused by enterovirus and coxsackievirus,
though other microorganisms may present similarly. It has
a rapid onset and resolves within 1вАУ2 weeks. Conjunctival
haemorrhage is generally marked.
оАБ Chronic/relapsing adenoviral conjunctivitis giving
a chronic non-specific follicular/papillary clinical picture can persist over years, but is rare and eventually
self-limiting.
оАБ Herpes simplex virus (HSV) can cause a follicular conjunctivitis, particularly in primary infection. оАЯis is usually unilateral and there are oоАЫen associated skin vesicles.
оАБ Systemic viral infections such as those common in childhood, e.g. varicella, measles and mumps, can feature an associated follicular conjunctivitis. Varicella-zoster virus secondary
infection commonly causes conjunctivitis as part of ophthalmic shingles. Conjunctivitis secondary to HIV infection is
recognized.
оАБ Molluscum contagiosum is a skin infection caused by a
human speciоАЮc double-stranded DNA poxvirus that typically aоАЬects otherwise healthy children, with a peak incidence
between the ages of 2 and 4 years. Transmission is by contact,
with subsequent autoinoculation. A chronic follicular conjunctivitis can be associated and is due to skin lesion shedding
of viral particles. Chronic unilateral ocular irritation and mild
discharge is typical. оАЯe eyelash line should be examined carefully in patients with chronic conjunctivitis so as not to overlook a molluscum lesion.
оАБ SARS-CoV-2 virus, responsible for the COVID-19 pandemic, can lead to multi-organ involvement, including the
eye. Viral RNA can be isolated in the tears of up to 25%
of patients with laboratory-proven moderate or severe
COVID-19 infection. Conjunctivitis is the most commonly reported ocular manifestation and results in hyperaemia, chemosis and epiphora. оАЯe infectious potential and
risk of transmission from tears or ocular discharge is not
known. Other ocular consequences of the virus include retinal microangiopathy (common) and optic neuritis (rare).
COVID-19 vaccination generally does not increase the risk
of ocular adverse events, except in some patients with preexisting uveitis where reactivation of ocular inоАЭammation
may follow.

Signs

оАБ Eyelid oedema ranges from negligible to severe.
оАБ Lymphadenopathy is common: tender preauricular.
оАБ Conjunctival hyperaemia and follicles are typically prominent. Papillae may also be seen, particularly in the superior
tarsal conjunctiva.
оАБ Severe inоАЭammation may be associated with conjunctival
haemorrhages (Fig. 6.9A) (usually petechial in adenoviral
infection), chemosis, membranes (rare) and pseudomembranes
(Fig. 6.9B), sometimes with conjunctival scarring aоАЫer resolution (Fig. 6.9C).
оАБ Keratitis (adenoviral):

{ Epithelial microcysts (non-staining) are common at an
early stage.

оАБ Giemsa stain shows predominantly mononuclear cells in

оАБ Nucleic acid ampliоАЮcation techniques such as PCR are sensitive and speciоАЮc for viral DNA.
оАБ Viral culture with isolation is the reference standard but is

transport media. Sensitivity is variable but speciоАЮcity is around
100%.
оАБ A вАШpoint-of-careвАЩ immunochromatography test takes 10 minutes to detect adenoviral antigen in tears; sensitivity and speciоАЮcity are excellent.
оАБ Serology for IgM or rising IgG antibody titres to adenovirus
has limitations and is rarely used.
оАБ Investigation for other causes such as chlamydial infection
may be indicated in non-resolving cases.

Treatment

оАЯe treatment of herpetic ocular surface disease is addressed in
Chapter 7

Investigation

Investigation is generally unnecessary, but should be considered if
the diagnosis is in doubt or there is failure of resolution.

182 Allergic Conjunctivitis

A B

Fig. 6.10 (A) Molluscum eyelid lesion; (B) follicular conjunctivitis associated with a molluscum
lesion (arrow).

оАБ Spontaneous resolution of adenoviral infection usually
occurs within 2вАУ3 weeks, so speciоАЮc treatment is typically
unnecessary. No antiviral agent with clinically useful activity
against adenovirus has yet been produced.
оАБ Reduction of transmission risk by meticulous hand hygiene,
avoiding eye rubbing and towel sharing. оАЯere should be scrupulous disinfection of instruments and clinical surfaces aоАЫer
examination of an infected patient (e.g. sodium hypochlorite,
povidone-iodine).
оАБ Molluscum contagiosum. Although lesions are self-limiting
in immunocompetent patients, removal is oоАЫen necessary
to address secondary conjunctivitis or for cosmetic reasons.
Expression is facilitated by making a small nick in the skin at
the margin of the lesion with the tip of a needle.
оАБ Topical steroids such as prednisolone 0.5%four times daily may
be required for severe membranous or pseudomembranous
adenoviral conjunctivitis. Symptomatic keratitis may require
weak topical steroids but these should be used with caution as
they do not speed resolution but only suppress inоАЭammation
and lesions commonly recur aоАЫer premature discontinuation.
Steroids may enhance viral replication and extend the period
during which the patient remains infectious. Intraocular pressure should be monitored if treatment is prolonged.
оАБ Other measures

{ Discontinuation of contact lens wear until resolution of
symptoms.

{ ArtiоАЮcial tears four times daily may be useful for symptomatic relief. Preservative-free preparations may give
superior comfort and if supplied in single-dose units may
reduce transmission risk.

{ Cold (or warm) compresses for symptomatic relief.

{ Topical antihistamines and vasoconstrictors may improve
symptoms, particularly itching.

{ оАЯe place of non-steroidal anti-inоАЭammatory drops is not
well established, but may be eоАЬective in some circumstances such as steroid weaning. оАЯey are not thought to
promote viral replication.

{ Removal of symptomatic pseudomembranes or
membranes.

{ Topical antibiotics if secondary bacterial infection is
suspected.

{ Povidone-iodine is very eоАЬective against free (although
less so against intracellular) adenovirus and has been proposed as a means of decreasing infectivity.

ALLERGIC CONJUNCTIVITIS

Atopy is a genetically determined predisposition to hypersensitivity reactions upon exposure to speciоАЮc environmental antigens.
Clinical manifestations include the various forms of allergic conjunctivitis, as well as hay fever (seasonal allergic rhinitis), asthma
and eczema. Allergic conjunctivitis is a type I (immediate) hypersensitivity reaction, mediated by degranulation of mast cells in
response to the action of IgE. оАЯere is evidence of an element of
type IV hypersensitivity in at least some forms.

Acute allergic conjunctivitis

Acute allergic conjunctivitis is a common condition caused by an
acute conjunctival reaction to an environmental allergen, usually
pollen. It is typically seen in younger children aоАЫer playing outside in
spring or summer. Acute itching and watering are common, but the
hallmark is chemosis (Fig. 6.11), which is frequently dramatic and
worrying to the child and parents. Treatment is not usually required
and the conjunctival swelling settles within hours as the acute increase
in vascular permeability resolves. Cool compresses can be used and a
single drop of adrenaline 0.1% may reduce extreme chemosis.

Seasonal and perennial allergic
conjunctivitis

оАЯese common subacute conditions are distinguished from each
other by the timing of exacerbations, thought to relate principally
to diоАЬering stimulating allergens in each.

CHAPTER

Conjunctiva 183
#### 6

A B

Fig. 6.11 (A) Moderate and (B) severe chemosis in acute allergic conjunctivitis.

оАБ Seasonal allergic conjunctivitis (вАШhay fever eyesвАЩ), worse during the spring and summer, is the more common. оАЯe most
frequent allergens are tree and grass pollens, although the speciоАЮc allergen varies with geographic location.
оАБ Perennial allergic conjunctivitis causes symptoms throughout
the year, generally worse in the autumn when exposure to house
dust mites, animal dander and fungal allergens is greatest. It is
less common and tends to be milder than the seasonal form.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Transient acute or subacute attacks of redness,
watering and itching, associated with sneezing and nasal
discharge.
оАБ Signs. Normal vision. Conjunctival hyperaemia with a relatively mild papillary reaction, variable chemosis and lid
oedema.
оАБ Investigations are generally not performed although conjunctival scraping in more active cases may demonstrate the
presence of eosinophils. Skin testing for particular allergens is
rarely required.

Treatment

оАБ ArtiоАЮcial tears for mild symptoms.
оАБ Mast cell stabilizers (e.g. sodium cromoglicate, nedocromil
sodium, lodoxamide) must be used for a few days before exerting maximal eоАЬect, but are suitable (except lodoxamide) for
long-term use if required.
оАБ Antihistamines (e.g. emedastine, epinastine, levocabastine,
bepotastine) can be used for symptomatic exacerbations and
are as eоАЬective as mast cell stabilizers.
оАБ Dual action antihistamine and mast cell stabilizers (e.g.
azelastine, ketotifen, olopatadine) act rapidly and are oоАЫen
very eоАЬective for exacerbations.
оАБ Combined preparation of an antihistamine and a vasoconstrictor (e.g. antazoline with xylometazoline).
оАБ Non-steroidal anti-inоАЭammatory preparations (e.g. diclofenac) can provide symptomatic relief but are rarely used.

оАБ Topical steroids are eоАЬective but rarely necessary.
оАБ Oral antihistamines may be indicated for severe symptoms.
Some, such as diphenhydramine, cause signiоАЮcant drowsiness
and may be useful in aiding sleep. Others, such as loratadine,
have a far less marked sedative action.

Vernal keratoconjunctivitis
Pathogenesis

Vernal keratoconjunctivitis (VKC) is a recurrent bilateral disorder
in which both IgE- and cell-mediated immune mechanisms play
important roles. It primarily aоАЬects boys and onset is generally from
about the age of 5 years onwards. оАЯere is remission by the late teens
in 95% of cases, although many of the remainder develop atopic
keratoconjunctivitis. VKC is rare in temperate regions, but relatively
common in warm dry climates such as the Mediterranean, subSaharan Africa and the Middle East. In temperate regions over 90%
of patients have other atopic conditions such as asthma and eczema
and two-thirds have a family history of atopy. VKC oоАЫen occurs on
a seasonal basis, with a peak incidence over late spring and summer,
although there may be mild perennial symptoms.

ClassiоАЯcation

оАБ Palpebral VKC primarily involves the upper tarsal conjunctiva. It may be associated with signiоАЮcant corneal disease as a
result of the close apposition between the inоАЭamed conjunctiva and the corneal epithelium.
оАБ Limbal disease typically aоАЬects Black and Asian patients.
оАБ Mixed VKC has features of both palpebral and limbal disease.

Diagnosis

оАЯe diagnosis is clinical and investigations are usually not required.
Eosinophils may be abundant in conjunctival scrapings.
оАБ Symptoms consist of intense itching, which may be associated
with lacrimation, photophobia, a foreign body sensation, burning and thick mucoid discharge. Increased blinking is common.

184 Allergic Conjunctivitis

оАБ Palpebral disease

{ Early-mild disease is characterized by conjunctival hyperaemia and diоАЬuse velvety papillary hypertrophy on the
superior tarsal plate.

{ Macropapillae (<1 mm) have a оАЭat-topped polygonal
appearance reminiscent of cobblestones. Focal (Fig. 6.12A)
or diоАЬuse (Fig. 6.12B) whitish inоАЭammatory inоАЮltrates
may be seen in intense disease.

{ Progression to giant papillae (>1 mm) can occur, as adjacent smaller lesions amalgamate when dividing septa rupture (Fig. 6.12C).

{ Mucus deposition between giant papillae (Fig. 6.12D).

{ Decreased disease activity is characterized by milder conjunctival injection and decreased mucus production.
оАБ Limbal disease

{ Gelatinous limbal conjunctival papillae that may be associated with transient apically located white cellular collections (HornerвАУTrantas dots вАУ Fig. 6.12E and F).

following a long history of atopic dermatitis (eczema). Asthma is
also extremely common in these patients. About 5% have suоАЬered
from childhood VKC. оАЯere is little or no gender preponderance.
AKC tends to be chronic and unremitting, with a relatively low
expectation of eventual resolution and is associated with signiоАЮcant visual morbidity. Whereas VKC is more frequently seasonal
and generally worse in the spring, AKC tends to be perennial and
is oоАЫen worse in the winter. Patients are sensitive to a wide range
of airborne environmental allergens.

Diagnosis

оАЯe distinction between AKC and VKC can be made clinically. Eosinophils tend to be less common in conjunctival scrapings than with VKC.
оАБ Symptoms are similar to those of VKC, but are frequently
more severe and unremitting.
оАБ Eyelids

{ In tropical regions, limbal disease may be severe (Fig. 6.12
G and H).
оАБ Keratopathy is more frequent in palpebral disease and may
take the following forms:

{ Superior punctate epithelial erosions associated with layers
of mucus on the superior cornea (Fig. 6.13A).

{ Epithelial macroerosions caused by a combination of epithelial toxicity from inоАЭammatory mediators and a direct
mechanical eоАЬect from papillae.

{ Plaques and вАШshieldвАЩ ulcers (Fig. 6.13B and C) may develop
in palpebral or mixed disease when the exposed Bowman membrane becomes coated with mucus and calcium
phosphate, leading to inadequate wetting and delayed reepithelialization. оАЯis development is serious and warrants
urgent attention to prevent secondary bacterial infection.

{ Subepithelial scars that are typically grey and oval and may
aоАЬect vision.

{ Pseudogerontoxon can develop in recurrent limbal disease. It is characterized by a paralimbal band of superоАЮcial
scarring resembling arcus senilis (Fig. 6.13D), adjacent to
a previously inоАЭamed segment of the limbus.

{ Vascularization does not tend to be prominent, though
some peripheral superоАЮcial vessel ingrowth is common,
especially superiorly.

{ Keratoconus and other forms of corneal ectasia are more
common in VKC and are thought to be at least partly due
to persistent eye rubbing.

{ Limbal involvement similar to that of limbal VKC can be
seen, including HornerвАУTrantas dots.
оАБ Keratopathy

{ Skin changes (Fig. 6.14A) are more prominent than in
VKC and are typically eczematoid: erythema, dryness,
scaling and thickening, sometimes with disruption to epidermal integrity such as оАЮssuring and scratches (excoriation), the latter due to intense itching.

{ Associated chronic staphylococcal blepharitis and madarosis are common.

{ оАЯere may be keratinization of the lid margin.

{ Hertoghe sign: absence of the lateral portion of the
eyebrows.

{ DennieвАУMorgan folds: lid skin folds caused by persistent
rubbing.

{ Tightening of the facial skin may cause lower lid ectropion
and epiphora.

{ Ptosis is not uncommon.
оАБ Conjunctival involvement is preferentially inferior palpebral,
whereas in VKC it is worse superiorly.

{ Discharge is generally more watery than the stringy
mucoid discharge in VKC.

{ Hyperaemia; chemosis is not uncommon during active
inоАЭammation.

{ Papillae are initially smaller than in VKC although larger
lesions may develop later.

{ DiоАЬuse conjunctival inоАЮltration and scarring may give a
whitish, featureless appearance (Fig. 6.14B).

{ Cicatricial changes can lead to moderate symblepharon
formation, forniceal shortening (Fig. 6.14C) and keratinization of the caruncle (Fig. 6.14D).

{ Herpes simplex keratitis is more common than average,
though less so than in atopic keratoconjunctivitis. It can be
aggressive and is occasionally bilateral.
оАБ Eyelid disease is usually mild, in contrast to atopic
keratoconjunctivitis.

Atopic keratoconjunctivitis
Pathogenesis

Atopic keratoconjunctivitis (AKC) is a rare bilateral disease that
typically develops in adulthood (peak incidence 30вАУ50 years)

{ Punctate epithelial erosions over the inferior third of the
cornea are common and can be marked.

{ Peripheral vascularization and stromal scarring are more
common than in VKC (Fig. 6.14E).

{ Persistent epithelial defects (Fig. 6.14F), sometimes with
associated focal thinning, can occasionally progress to perforation with descemetocoele (US spelling вАУ descemetocele) formation.

{ Plaque formation may occur (see Fig. 6.13B and C).

{ Predisposition to secondary bacterial and fungal infection
and to aggressive herpes simplex keratitis.

{ Keratoconus is common (about 15%) and as with VKC,
may be secondary to chronic ocular rubbing.
оАБ Cataract

{ Presenile shield-like anterior or posterior subcapsular cataracts are common and may be exacerbated by long-term
steroid therapy.

{ Because of the high lid margin carriage of S. aureus, cataract surgery carries an increased risk of endophthalmitis.

{ Retinal detachment is more common than in the general
population and is a particular risk following cataract surgery.

General measures

оАБ Allergen avoidance, if possible. Allergen (e.g. patch) testing is
sometimes useful, but oоАЫen gives non-speciоАЮc results.
оАБ Cool compresses may be helpful.
оАБ Lid hygiene should be used for associated staphylococcal
blepharitis. Moisturizing cream such as E45 can be applied to
dry, оАЮssured skin.
оАБ Bandage contact lens wear to aid healing of persistent epithelial defects.

Local treatment

оАБ Mast cell stabilizers (e.g. sodium cromoglicate, nedocromil
sodium, lodoxamide) reduce the frequency of acute exacerbations and the need for steroids and so form the basis of many
regimens, but are seldom eоАЬective in isolation. Several days to
weeks of treatment are needed for a reasonable response and
long-term therapy may be needed (lodoxamide is not licensed
for long-term use).

Treatment of VKC and AKC

оАЯe management of VKC does not diоАЬer substantially from that of
AKC, although the latter is generally less responsive and requires
more intensive and prolonged treatment.

188 Allergic Conjunctivitis

оАБ Topical antihistamines (e.g. emedastine, epinastine, levocabastine, bepotastine) when used in isolation are about as
eоАЬective as mast cell stabilizers. оАЯey are suitable for acute
exacerbations but generally not for continuous long-term use
and courses of several preparations are licensed for use only in
courses of limited duration. A trial of several diоАЬerent agents
may be worthwhile.
оАБ Combined antihistamine and vasoconstrictor (e.g. antazoline with xylometazoline) may oоАЬer relief in some cases.
оАБ Combined action antihistamine/mast cell stabilizers (e.g.
azelastine, ketotifen, olopatadine) are helpful in many patients
and have a relatively rapid onset of action.
оАБ Non-steroidal anti-inоАЭammatory preparations (e.g.
ketorolac, diclofenac) may improve comfort by blocking nonhistamine mediators. Combining one of these with a mast cell
stabilizer is an eоАЬective regimen in some patients.
оАБ Topical steroids (e.g. оАЭuorometholone 0.1%, rimexolone 1%,
prednisolone 0.5%, loteprednol etabonate 0.2% or 0.5%) are
used for severe exacerbations of conjunctivitis and signiоАЮcant
keratopathy. Reducing conjunctival activity generally leads to
corneal improvement. оАЯey are usually prescribed in short but
intensive (e.g. 2-hourly initially) courses, aiming for prompt
tapering. Although the risk of elevation of intraocular pressure is low, monitoring is advisable if long-term treatment is
necessary. Stronger preparations such as prednisolone 1% can
be used, but carry a higher risk of steroid-induced glaucoma.
Shield ulcers are oоАЫen refractory to topical steroid treatment.
оАБ Steroid ointment (e.g. hydrocortisone 0.5%) may be used to
treat the eyelids in AKC, though as with eye drops, the duration of treatment should be minimized and the intraocular
pressure (IOP) monitored.
оАБ Antibiotics may be used in conjunction with steroids in severe
keratopathy to prevent or treat bacterial infection.
оАБ Acetylcysteine is a mucolytic agent that is useful in VKC for
dissolving mucus оАЮlaments and deposits and addressing early
plaque formation.
оАБ Immunomodulators

{ Ciclosporin (0.05вАУ2% between two and six times daily)
may be indicated if steroids are ineоАЬective, inadequate or
poorly tolerated, or as a steroid-sparing agent in patients
with severe disease. оАЯe eоАЬects typically take some weeks to
be exerted and relapses may occur if treatment is stopped
suddenly. Irritation and blurred vision are common.

{ Calcineurin inhibitors show increasing promise as an
alternative to steroids in the treatment of allergic eye disease. Tacrolimus 0.03% ointment can be eоАЬective in AKC
for severe eyelid disease. Instillation into the fornix has
been eоАЬective in modulating conjunctival inоАЭammation in
refractory cases. Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment is particularly
useful when applied topically in patients with shield ulcers
and corneal epitheliopathy and can be used without topical
steroids. Factors associated with a poor response to tacrolimus include the presence of a giant papillae and palpebral
abnormality.

{ Supratarsal steroid injection may be considered in
severe palpebral disease or for non-compliant patients.

оАЯe injection is given into the conjunctival surface of the
anaesthetized everted upper eyelid; 0.1 ml of betamethasone sodium phosphate 4 mg/ml, dexamethasone 4 mg/ml
or triamcinolone 40 mg/ml is given.

TIP Topical steroids should not be used long term in a patient
with allergic conjunctivitis without monitoring the IOP.

Systemic treatment

оАБ Oral antihistamines help itching, promote sleep and reduce
nocturnal eye rubbing. Because other inоАЭammatory mediators
are involved besides histamines, eоАЬectiveness is not assured.
Some antihistamines (e.g. loratadine) cause relatively little
drowsiness.
оАБ Antibiotics (e.g. doxycycline 50вАУ100 mg daily for 6 weeks,
azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days) may be given to
reduce blepharitis-aggravated inоАЭammation, usually in AKC.
оАБ Immunosuppressive agents (e.g. steroids, ciclosporin, tacrolimus, azathioprine) may be eоАЬective at relatively low doses
in AKC unresponsive to other measures. Short courses of
high-dose steroids may be necessary to achieve rapid control
in severe disease. Monoclonal antibodies against T cells have
shown some promise in refractory cases.
оАБ Other treatments that may be eоАЬective in some patients
include aspirin in VKC (avoided in children and adolescents
due to Reye syndrome risk), allergen desensitization and plasmapheresis in patients with high serum IgE levels.

Surgery

оАБ SuperоАЮcial keratectomy may be required to remove plaques or
debride shield ulcers and allow epithelialization. Medical treatment must be maintained until the cornea has re-epithelialized
in order to prevent recurrences. Excimer laser phototherapeutic keratectomy is an alternative.
оАБ Surface maintenance/restoration surgery such as amniotic
membrane overlay graоАЫing or lamellar keratoplasty, or eyelid
procedures such as botulinum toxin-induced ptosis or lateral
tarsorrhaphy, may be required for severe persistent epithelial
defects or ulceration. Gluing may be appropriate for focal
(вАШpunched-outвАЩ) corneal perforations.

Non-allergic eosinophilic conjunctivitis

Non-allergic eosinophilic conjunctivitis (NAEC) is a recently
proposed chronic non-atopic condition said to occur predominantly in middle-aged women in whom dry eye is also commonly present. It has been suggested that it is relatively common
but under-diagnosed. It is thought to be of similar pathogenesis
to non-allergic eosinophilic rhinitis. Conjunctival eosinophilia is
present without signiоАЮcant IgE levels in the serum or tear оАЮlm.
Symptoms are similar to those of allergic conjunctivitis вАУ itching,
redness, foreign body sensation and mild watery discharge. Treatment is with a 1вАУ2 week course of topical steroid for exacerbations
followed by maintenance with topical mast cell stabilizers, nonsteroidal anti-inоАЭammatory agents or antihistamines.
