Contact allergic blepharoconjunctivitis

Analogous to contact dermatitis, this refers to the acute or subacute T-cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity reaction seen most
commonly by ophthalmologists as a reaction to eye drop constituents and by optometrists as a reaction to contact lens solutions.
Mascara is a less common cause. оАЯere may be a conjunctival reaction, but signs predominantly involve the eyelid skin: erythema,
thickening, induration and sometimes оАЮssuring occur (Fig. 6.15).
Treatment is by discontinuation of the precipitant. A mild topical
steroid ointment can be helpful.

Giant (mechanically induced) papillary
conjunctivitis
Pathogenesis

Mechanically induced papillary conjunctivitis, the severe form of
which is known as giant papillary conjunctivitis (GPC), can occur
secondary to a variety of mechanical stimuli of the tarsal conjunctiva. It is most frequently encountered with contact lens (CL) wear,
when it is termed contact lens-associated papillary conjunctivitis
(CLPC). оАЯe risk is increased by the build-up of proteinaceous
deposits and cellular debris on the CL surface. Ocular prostheses
(Fig. 6.16), exposed sutures and scleral buckles, corneal surface
irregularity and оАЮltering blebs can all be responsible. A related
phenomenon is the so-called вАШmucus оАЮshing syndromeвАЩ, when, in a
variety of underlying anterior segment disorders, patients develop
or exacerbate a chronic papillary reaction due to repetitive manual
removal of mucus. Giant papillae can also be seen in other conditions, such as VKC and AKC.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms consist of a foreign body sensation, redness, itching, increased mucus production, blurring and loss of CL tolerance. Symptoms may be worse aоАЫer lens removal. Patients
should be questioned about CL cleaning and maintenance.

оАБ Signs

{ Changing the type of CL solution, particularly discontinuation of preservative-containing preparations.

{ Switching to monthly then daily disposable CL if the condition persists aоАЫer renewing non-disposable lenses.

{ Variable mucous discharge.

{ Substantial CL protein deposits may be present.

{ Excessive CL mobility due to upper lid capture.

{ Superior tarsal hyperaemia and papillae. By deоАЮnition,
вАШgiantвАЩ papillae are >1.0 mm in diameter, but the clinical
syndrome of mechanically induced papillary conjunctivitis commonly features only оАЮne/medium papillae, particularly in early or mild disease.

{ Focal apical ulceration and whitish scarring may develop
on larger papillae.

{ Keratopathy is rare because of the relatively subdued secretion of inоАЭammatory cytokines.

{ Ptosis may occur, mainly as a result of irritative spasm and
tissue laxity secondary to chronic inоАЭammation.

Treatment

Other causes of conjunctival papillae should be excluded, as well
as CL intolerance due to other causes, such as a reaction to lens
cleaning solutions and dry eyes.
оАБ Removal of the stimulus

{ CL wear should be discontinued for several weeks and the
current lenses replaced. For mildвАУmoderate disease, this
may be adequate for resolution, sometimes in conjunction
with reduced wearing time. In severe CLPC a longer interval without lens wear may be needed.

{ Removal of other underlying causes, such as exposed
sutures or a scleral buckle.

{ Assessment of the status and оАЮt of an ocular prosthesis.

{ Filtering bleb: partial excision, revision with nonpenetrating drainage surgery or glaucoma drainage device
implantation.
оАБ Ensure eоАЬective cleaning of CL or prosthesis

190 Conjunctivitis in Blistering Mucocutaneous Disease

{ Rigid lenses carry a reduced risk of CLPC (5%), probably
because they are easier to clean eоАЬectively.

{ Cessation of CL wear, substituting spectacles or refractive
surgery, may be necessary for severe or refractory disease.

{ Regular (at least weekly) use of CL protein removal tablets.

Progression of ocular disease has been divided into stages as
follows:
оАБ FosterвАЩs classiоАЮcation system

{ Stage II: forniceal shortening, particularly inferiorly.

{ Prosthesis: polishing, cleaning with detergent, coating.
оАБ Topical

{ Stage I: early stage associated with chronic conjunctivitis,
tear dysfunction and subepithelial оАЮbrosis.

{ Mast cell stabilizers should be non-preserved in patients
wearing soоАЫ contact lenses, or can be instilled when the
lenses are not in the eye, with a delay of perhaps half an
hour aоАЫer drop instillation prior to lens insertion. Most
can be continued long-term if necessary.

{ Antihistamines, non-steroidal anti-inоАЭammatory agents
and combined antihistamines/mast cell stabilizers may
each be of beneоАЮt.

{ Topical steroids can be used for the acute phase of resistant cases, particularly those where eоАЬective removal of the
stimulus is diоАЪcult, as in bleb-related disease.

{ Stage IV: surface keratinization and cicatricial
ankyloblepharon.
оАБ MondinoвАЩs classiоАЮcation system (based on inferior forniceal
depth loss)

{ Stage III: symblepharon formation.

{ Papillary conjunctivitis, diоАЬuse hyperaemia, oedema and
subtle оАЮbrosis (Fig. 6.17A).

{ Fine lines of subconjunctival оАЮbrosis and shortening of
the inferior fornices. Symblepharon (plural: symblephara)
formation refers to adhesion between the bulbar and palpebral conjunctiva (Fig. 6.17B and C).

{ Stage I: up to 25%.

{ Stage II: 25вАУ50%.

{ Stage III: 50вАУ75%.

{ Stage IV: greater than 75%.
оАБ Symptoms. Insidious or relapsingвАУremitting non-speciоАЮc
bilateral conjunctivitis; misdiagnosis (e.g. dry eye) is common.

CONJUNCTIVITIS IN BLISTERING
MUCOCUTANEOUS DISEASE

Mucous membrane pemphigoid
Introduction

Mucous membrane pemphigoid (MMP), also known as ocular
cicatricial pemphigoid (OCP), comprises a group of chronic autoimmune mucocutaneous blistering diseases. An unknown trigger
leads to a type II (cytotoxic) hypersensitivity response resulting in
antibodies binding at the basement membrane zone (BMZ), the
activation of complement and the recruitment of inоАЭammatory cells,
with localized separation of the epidermis from the dermis at the
BMZ and subsequent progression to scarring. Studies of HLA typing have found an increased susceptibility to the disease in patients
with HLA-DR4. оАЯe HLA-DQB1 allele in particular shows a strong
association with OCP and other forms of pemphigoid disease.
A wide range of epithelial tissues can be involved, including
the skin and various mucous membranes. Particular clinical forms
of MMP tend to involve speciоАЮc target tissues: bullous pemphigoid
(BP) shows a predilection for skin and ocular mucous membrane
pemphigoid (OMMP, also known as ocular cicatricial pemphigoid вАУ OCP) involves the conjunctiva in the majority of cases and
causes progressive scarring (cicatrization). оАЯe disease typically
presents in old age and aоАЬects females more commonly than males
by a 2:1 ratio. Other causes of cicatrizing conjunctivitis include
StevensвАУJohnson syndrome, trachoma, drug-induced, trauma and
severe or chronic conjunctivitis of many types. MMP should not
be confused with pemphigus, a distinct group of disorders.

Ocular features

оАБ Diagnosis is principally clinical, but biopsy of involved
mucous membrane oоАЫen shows supportive changes (linear
antibody and complement BMZ deposition).
оАБ MichelвАЩs transport medium should be used when taking a
specimen for direct immunoоАЭuorescence and immunoelectron microscopy.

Systemic features

оАБ Mucosal involvement is very common and is characterized by
subepidermal blisters, most frequently oral (Fig. 6.19A). Severe
manifestations include oesophageal and laryngeal strictures.
оАБ Skin lesions are less common (25%) and present as tense blisters and erosions of the head and neck, groin and extremities
(Fig. 6.19B).

Systemic treatment

Systemic treatment is the mainstay of management. Any detectable
inоАЭammatory activity should be suppressed.

{ Necrosis in severe cases.

{ Flattening of the plica and keratinization of the caruncle.

{ Dry eye due to destruction of goblet cells and accessory lacrimal glands and occlusion of the main lacrimal
ductules.

{ Monitoring should include the measurement of forniceal
depth and noting the position of adhesions.
оАБ Eyelids

{ Aberrant (trichiatic) lashes, chronic blepharitis and
keratinization of the lid margin.

{ Ankyloblepharon is an adhesion at the outer canthus
between the upper and lower lids (Fig. 6.17D).
оАБ Cornea

{ Epithelial defects (Fig. 6.18A) associated with drying and
exposure.

{ InоАЮltration and peripheral vascularization (Fig. 6.18B).

{ Keratinization and conjunctivalization of the corneal surface (Fig. 6.18C) due to epithelial stem cell failure.

{ End-stage disease is characterized by total symblepharon
and corneal opaciоАЮcation (Fig. 6.18D).

оАБ Dapsone (diaminodiphenylsulfone) is a useful оАЮrst-line treatment in patients with mildвАУmoderate disease. Approximately
70% of patients respond favourably. It is contraindicated in
glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deоАЮciency. Sulfasalazine
is sometimes better tolerated.
оАБ Antimetabolites (e.g. azathioprine, methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil) are alternatives for mildвАУmoderate disease if
dapsone is contraindicated, ineоАЬective or poorly tolerated and
are suitable for long-term therapy. Dapsone can be used in
conjunction if necessary. Cyclophosphamide may be reserved
for severe or refractory disease.
оАБ Steroids (prednisolone 1вАУ1.5 mg/kg) are eоАЬective for rapid
disease control, but adverse eоАЬects limit long-term use. IOP
should be monitored.
оАБ Other measures include intravenous immunoglobulin therapy and rituximab. Remission has been reported with a combination regimen.

Local treatment

оАБ Topical

{ Lid hygiene and low-dose oral tetracycline for blepharitis.
оАБ Subconjunctival mitomycin C and/or steroid injection may be
used as a temporizing aid or if systemic immunosuppression
is not possible.
оАБ Contact lenses may be used with caution to protect the cornea
from aberrant lashes and from dehydration.

Reconstructive surgery

Reconstructive surgery, preferably under systemic steroid cover,
should be considered when active disease is controlled. Seemingly trivial surgical intervention can lead to exacerbation of
disease.
оАБ Aberrant eyelashes (see Ch. 2).
оАБ Punctal occlusion to aid tear retention.

{ ArtiоАЮcial tears (preferably preservative-free) are an integral part of most regimens.

{ Topical steroids, ciclosporin or tacrolimus may be used as
an adjunct to systemic immunosuppressive treatment.

{ Retinoic acid may reduce keratinization.

{ Antibiotics when indicated.

оАБ Lateral tarsorrhaphy or botulinum toxin-induced ptosis may
be used to promote healing of corneal epithelial defects.
оАБ Entropion repair: conjunctival incision is avoided if possible.
оАБ Cataract surgery is commonly required.
оАБ Mucous membrane autograоАЫing or amniotic membrane
transplantation for conjunctival resurfacing and forniceal
restoration.
оАБ Limbal stem cell transfer may be attempted for corneal
re-epithelialization.

StevensвАУJohnson syndrome/toxic
epidermal necrolysis (Lyell syndrome)

Introduction

оАЯe terms вАШStevensвАУJohnson syndrome (SJS)вАЩ and вАШerythema multiforme majorвАЩ have historically been used synonymously. However,
it is now believed that erythema multiforme (without the вАШmajorвАЩ)
is a distinct disease, milder and recurrent, with somewhat dissimilar clinical features. Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN вАУ Lyell
syndrome) is a severe variant of SJS. SJS/TEN patients tend to be
young adults, though other groups may be aоАЬected. оАЯe condition
involves a cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity reaction, usually
related to drug exposure. A wide range of medications have been
incriminated, including antibiotics (especially sulfonamides and
trimethoprim), analgesics including paracetamol (acetaminophen),
nevirapine (widely prescribed as a part of combination therapy for
HIV infection), cold remedies and anticonvulsants. Infections due
to microorganisms such as Mycoplasma pneumoniae and HSV
and some cancers have also been implicated. Because symptoms
oоАЫen take weeks to develop, in many cases the precipitant cannot
be identiоАЮed. Mortality overall is around 5% in SJS (death is commonly due to infection), but is considerably higher in TEN.

Ocular features

In the acute stage there are oоАЫen practical obstacles to standard slit
lamp examination, as the patient may be bedridden and undergoing barrier nursing. A portable slit lamp may be helpful.
оАБ Symptoms. Acute ocular symptoms may include redness,
mildвАУsevere grittiness, photophobia, watering and blurring.
оАБ Acute signs

{ Haemorrhagic crusting of the lid margins (Fig. 6.20A) is
characteristic. Skin lesions may be conоАЭuent and it is oоАЫen
diоАЪcult for an examiner to open the eyes without causing
marked discomfort.

{ Papillary conjunctivitis, which can range from mild, transient and self-limiting to severe (Fig. 6.20B).

{ Conjunctival membranes and pseudomembranes (Fig.
6.20C), severe hyperaemia, haemorrhages, blisters and
patchy infarction.

{ Keratopathy: a spectrum of lesions from punctate erosions
to large epithelial defects, secondary bacterial keratitis and
occasionally perforation.

CHAPTER

Conjunctiva 193
#### 6

{ Iritis is not infrequent and panophthalmitis has been
reported.
оАБ Late signs

{ Conjunctival cicatrization (Fig. 6.20D) with forniceal
shortening and symblepharon formation.

{ Keratinization of the conjunctiva and lid margin (Fig.
6.20E), sometimes with abrasive plaque formation.

{ Eyelid complications include cicatricial entropion
and ectropion, trichiasis, metaplastic lashes and
ankyloblepharon.

{ Keratopathy including scarring, vascularization and keratinization (Fig. 6.20F) as a result of the primary inоАЭammation and/or infection, as well as cicatricial entropion and
aberrant lashes.

{ Watery eyes due to оАЮbrosis of the lacrimal puncta. Dry eyes
may also occur as a result of оАЮbrosis of lacrimal gland ductules and conjunctival metaplasia with loss of goblet cells.

Systemic features

Systemic treatment

оАБ Removal of the precipitant if possible, such as discontinuation of drugs and treatment of suspected infection.
оАБ General supportive measures such as maintenance of adequate hydration, electrolyte balance and nutrition (especially
protein replacement) are critical. Management in a specialist
burns unit should reduce the chance of infection when the
extent of skin involvement is substantial.
оАБ Systemic steroids remain controversial. оАЯere are reports
of increased mortality in older papers, but later research
has raised the possibility that early short-term high-dose
intravenous treatment may improve outcomes.
оАБ Other immunosuppressants including ciclosporin, azathioprine, cyclophosphamide and intravenous immunoglobulin

Skin biopsy may help to establish the diagnosis but is rarely
necessary.
оАБ Symptoms. Flu-like symptoms, which can be severe, may last
up to 14 days before the appearance of lesions. In many cases
the patient is very ill and hospitalization is required. Symptoms of systemic mucosal involvement include nasal pain and
discharge, pain on micturition, diarrhoea, cough, shortness of
breath and pain on eating and drinking.
оАБ Signs

{ Mucosal involvement is characterized by blistering and
haemorrhagic crusting of the lips (Fig. 6.21A). оАЯe blisters
may also involve the tongue, oropharynx, nasal mucosa
and occasionally the genitalia.

{ Small purpuric, vesicular, haemorrhagic or necrotic skin
lesions involving the extremities, face and trunk (Fig.
6.21B). оАЯese are usually transient but may be widespread.
Healing usually occurs within 1вАУ4 weeks, leaving a pigmented scar.

{ Widespread sloughing of the epidermis is uncommon.

{ вАШTargetвАЩ lesions showing the classic three zones are now
viewed as characteristic of erythema multiforme rather
than SJS/TEN.

may be considered in selected cases, but are controversial and
controlled trials are lacking.
оАБ Systemic antibiotics may be given as prophylaxis against skin
or other systemic infection, avoiding those known to be at
higher risk of precipitating SJS/TEN.

Ocular treatment

оАБ Acute disease. Daily review is advisable initially in most patients
to check the corneas and exclude symblepharon formation.

{ Topical lubricants are used as frequently as necessary, e.g.
hypromellose 0.3% preservative-free up to hourly, highviscosity ointment during sleep.

{ Prevention of corneal exposure, e.g. moisture chambers,
gel pads if mechanically ventilated.

{ Topical steroids may be used for iritis and for conjunctival
inоАЭammation, though a beneоАЮt for the latter has not been
demonstrated conclusively.

{ Topical cycloplegia (e.g. atropine 1% once or twice daily)
may improve comfort.

{ Lysis of developing symblephara with a sterile glass rod or
damp cotton bud.

{ A scleral ring, consisting of a large haptic lens, may help to
prevent symblepharon formation (Fig. 6.22).

{ Pseudomembrane/membrane peeling can be considered,
although the beneоАЮt is unproven.

{ Treatment of acute corneal problems such as bacterial
keratitis.

{ Conjunctival swabs should be considered for prophylactic
culture.

{ IOP monitoring may be prudent, using portable tonometry if necessary.
оАБ Chronic disease

{ Adequate lubrication, including punctal occlusion if
required.

{ Topical transretinoic acid 0.01% or 0.025% may reverse
keratinization.

Fig. 6.22 Scleral ring used to prevent symblepharon formation in StevensвАУJohnson syndrome. (Courtesy of S Tuft.)

{ Bandage contact lenses (typically gas permeable scleral
lenses) to maintain surface moisture, protect the cornea
from aberrant lashes and address irregular astigmatism.

{ Mucous membrane graоАЫing (e.g. buccal mucosa autograоАЫ)
for forniceal reconstruction.

{ Corneal rehabilitation may involve superоАЮcial keratectomy for keratinization, lamellar corneal graоАЫing for
superоАЮcial scarring (preferred to penetrating keratoplasty), amniotic membrane graоАЫing, limbal stem cell
transplantation and keratoprosthesis implantation in
end-stage disease.

Graft-versus-host disease
Introduction

Ocular graоАЫ-versus-host disease (GVHD) occurs in patients who
have undergone allogenic haematological stem cell transplantation,

{ Treatment of aberrant lashes (see Ch. 2).

196 Miscellaneous Disorders of the Conjunctiva

an operation that is undertaken on an estimated 25,000 people
annually worldwide. It can occur in patients who have acute or
chronic GVHD, although it is more frequently seen in the chronic
form. Clinically it manifests in a similar fashion to StevensвАУJohnson syndrome. Ocular manifestations develop in 40вАУ60% of
patients aоАЫer stem cell transplantation and can lead to signiоАЮcant
ocular surface consequences.
оАБ Ocular features

оАБ Symptoms include a foreign body sensation, burning, mild
photophobia, mucoid discharge and frequent blinking, and are
oоАЫen intermittent.
оАБ Conjunctiva

{ Dry eye syndrome (keratoconjunctivitis sicca).

{ Conjunctival hyperaemia with pseudomembrane and
membrane formation.

{ Chronic inоАЭammation leading to cicatricial changes in the
conjunctiva and occasionally entropion formation.

{ Hyperaemia of a radial band of the superior bulbar conjunctiva (Fig. 6.23A) that stains with rose Bengal and may
be best seen macroscopically.

{ Limbal papillary hypertrophy (Fig. 6.23B). Limbal palisades may be lost superiorly.

{ Papillary hypertrophy of the superior tarsal plate, oоАЫen
having a diоАЬuse velvety appearance (Fig. 6.23C).

{ Light downward pressure on the upper lid results in a fold
of redundant conjunctiva crossing the upper limbus.

{ Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis.

{ Corneal manifestations including punctate epithelial
keratopathy, marginal keratitis and оАЮlamentary keratitis.
Severe disease can lead to corneal erosion, thinning, ulceration and possible perforation.

{ Keratinization can be demonstrated on biopsy or impression cytology.
оАБ Cornea

{ Petechial haemorrhages may be present.

{ Superior punctate corneal epithelial erosions are common
and are oоАЫen separated from the limbus by a zone of normal epithelium.

{ Superior оАЮlamentary keratitis develops in about one-third
of cases.

{ Mild superior pannus resembling arcus senilis may be seen
in long-standing disease.

Treatment

оАБ ArtiоАЮcial tears can be helpful (see above).
оАБ Topical cyclosporine 0.5% is the mainstay of treatment.
оАБ Systemic treatment does not necessarily improve the ocular
symptoms and signs. Oral steroids are oоАЫen used.

MISCELLANEOUS DISORDERS OF THE
CONJUNCTIVA

Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis
Introduction

Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis (SLK) is a relatively uncommon
chronic disease of the superior limbus and the superior bulbar and
tarsal conjunctiva. It aоАЬects one or both eyes of middle-aged women,
approximately 50% of whom have abnormal thyroid function (usually hyperthyroidism). Approximately 3% of patients with thyroid
eye disease have SLK. It can be associated with graоАЫ-versus-host disease. оАЯe condition is probably under-diagnosed because symptoms
are typically more severe than signs. оАЯe course can be prolonged
over years although remission eventually occurs spontaneously.
оАЯere are similarities to mechanically induced papillary conjunctivitis and a comparable clinical picture has been described with CL
wear and following upper lid surgery or trauma. It is unlikely to be
caused by tight upper eyelids. оАЯe condition is believed to be the
result of blink-related trauma between the upper lid and the superior bulbar conjunctiva, precipitated in many cases by tear оАЮlm
insuоАЪciency and an excess of lax conjunctival tissue. With increased
conjunctival movement there is mechanical damage to the tarsal and
bulbar conjunctival surfaces, the resultant inоАЭammatory response
leading to increasing conjunctival oedema and redundancy, with
the creation of a self-perpetuating cycle. It may be analogous to conjunctivochalasis aоАЬecting the lower bulbar conjunctiva (see Ch. 3).

Diagnosis

Enquiry should be made about contact lens wear and previous eyelid surgery or trauma.

{ Keratoconjunctivitis sicca is present in only about 50%.
оАБ Investigation

{ оАЯyroid function testing should be performed if the patient
is not known to have thyroid disease.

{ Biopsy or impression cytology may reveal keratinization of
the superior bulbar conjunctiva.

TIP Check thyroid function in patients with superior limbic
keratoconjunctivitis.

Treatment

оАБ Topical

{ Lubricants to reduce friction between the tarsal and bulbar
conjunctiva should be used regularly and frequently.

{ Acetylcysteine 5% or 10% four times daily to break down
оАЮlaments and provide lubrication.

{ Mast cell stabilizers and steroids to address any inоАЭammatory component. Steroids may be best used in short intensive courses with rapid tapering and should be reserved for
severe cases.

{ Promising results have been reported with topical
rebamipide.

{ Ciclosporin 0.05% twice daily as primary or adjunctive
therapy, particularly in the presence of coexisting keratoconjunctivitis sicca.

{ Retinoic acid to retard keratinization.

{ Autologous serum 20% drops can be beneоАЮcial but may
require instillation up to 10 times a day.
оАБ SoоАЬ contact lenses, which intervene between the lid and the
superior conjunctiva, are eоАЬective in some cases. Interestingly,
a unilateral lens may provide bilateral relief.

оАБ Supratarsal steroid injection. 0.1 ml of triamcinolone 40 mg/
ml may break the inоАЭammatory cycle.
оАБ Temporary superior and/or inferior punctal occlusion.
оАБ Resection of the superior limbal conjunctiva, either in a zone
extending 2 mm from the superior limbus or of the area staining with rose Bengal, is oоАЫen eоАЬective in resistant disease. Lax
conjunctiva is removed, with regrowth tending to be оАЮrmly
anchored. оАЯere is no consensus as to whether underlying
Tenon capsule should be excised.
оАБ Conjunctival ablation by applying silver nitrate 0.5% (not
cautery sticks) or thermocautery to the aоАЬected area.
оАБ Treatment of associated thyroid dysfunction may improve
SLK.

Ligneous conjunctivitis
Introduction

Ligneous conjunctivitis is a very rare potentially sight- and even
life-threatening disorder characterized by recurrent, oоАЫen bilateral

оАЮbrin-rich pseudomembranous lesions of wood-like consistency
that develop mainly on the tarsal conjunctiva. It is generally a systemic condition and may involve the periodontal tissue, the upper
and lower respiratory tract, kidneys, middle ear and female genitalia. Death can occasionally occur from pulmonary involvement. In
susceptible patients abnormal patterns of damage repair are found,
particularly a failure of normal clearance of products of the acute
stages of the healing process. оАЯis is manifested predominantly in
mucosal tissue. A deоАЮciency in plasmin-mediated оАЮbrinolysis may
be a key common factor in many patients. Episodes may be triggered by relatively minor trauma, or by systemic events such as
fever and antiоАЮbrinolytic therapy.

Diagnosis

оАБ Histopathology shows amorphous subepithelial deposits of
eosinophilic material consisting predominantly of оАЮbrin (Fig.
6.24A).
оАБ Presentation is with non-speciоАЮc conjunctivitis, usually in
childhood (median age 5 years), although onset may be at any
age. A conjunctival lesion is commonly noted by parents.

оАБ Signs

{ Gradually enlarging redвАУwhite lobular conjunctival
masses (Figs 6.24B and C) which may be covered by a thick
yellowвАУwhite mucoid discharge.

{ Recurrence may be retarded by long-term ciclosporin and
steroid instillation.
оАБ Other modalities

{ Intravenous or topical plasminogen.

{ Amniotic membrane transplantation to the conjunctiva
following lesion removal.

{ Prophylactic heparin treatment may be of beneоАЮt prior to
ocular surgery in at-risk patients.

{ Corneal scarring, vascularization, infection or melting.

Treatment

Treatment tends to be unsatisfactory and spontaneous resolution
is rare. It is important to discontinue any antiоАЮbrinolytic drugs.
оАБ Surgical removal (Fig. 6.24D) with meticulous diathermy of
the base of the lesion. Preoperative topical plasminogen may
soоАЫen pseudomembranes and facilitate removal.
оАБ Topical

{ Following membrane removal, hourly heparin and steroids are commenced immediately and continued until
the wound has re-epithelialized, with subsequent tapering over several weeks until all signs of inоАЭammation have
disappeared.

Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome

Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome is a rare condition consisting of chronic low-grade fever, unilateral granulomatous conjunctivitis (Fig. 6.25) with surrounding follicles and ipsilateral
regional (preauricular) lymphadenopathy. It is synonymous
with cat-scratch disease (caused by Bartonella henselae вАУ see Ch.
12), although several other causes have been implicated, including tularaemia, insect hairs (ophthalmia nodosum), Treponema

pallidum, sporotrichosis, tuberculosis and acute C. trachomatis
infection.

Factitious conjunctivitis
Introduction

Self-injury (factitious keratoconjunctivitis) is most oоАЫen intentional, but can also occur inadvertently, as in mucus оАЮshing
syndrome and removal of contact lenses. Damage may be the
result of either mechanical trauma or of the instillation of irritant but readily accessible household substances, such as soap.
Occasionally over-instillation of prescribed ocular medication is
responsible.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Reported symptoms may seem disproportionate
to signs. оАЯe patient may have sought multiple medical opinions over an extended period, oоАЫen from a range of specialists
for diоАЬerent complaints.
оАБ Signs

{ Inferior conjunctival injection and staining with rose Bengal (Fig. 6.26), with quiet superior bulbar conjunctiva.

{ Linear corneal abrasions, persistent epithelial defects and
occasionally focal corneal perforation.

DEGENERATIONS

Pinguecula
Introduction

A pinguecula (plural pingueculae) is an innocuous but extremely
common asymptomatic elastotic degeneration of the conjunctival stroma. A yellowвАУwhite mound or aggregation of smaller
mounds is seen on the bulbar conjunctiva adjacent to the limbus
(Fig. 6.27A). It is more frequently located at the nasal than the
temporal limbus, but is frequently present at both. CalciоАЮcation
(Fig. 6.27B) is occasionally present. оАЯe cause is believed to be
actinic damage, similar to the aetiology of pterygium (see below),
which pinguecula resembles histologically. оАЯe distinction is
that the limbal barrier to extension has remained intact with
a pinguecula. Occasionally a pinguecula may become acutely
inоАЭamed (pingueculitis вАУ Fig. 6.27C), particularly if the lesion
is prominent or overlying calciоАЮcation leads to epithelial breakdown. оАЯere is a small subgroup of patients with an inоАЭamed
nasal pinguecula and impaired ocular surface lubrication, who
can progress to pterygium (Fig. 6.27D).

Treatment

Treatment is usually unnecessary because growth is absent or very
slow.
оАБ Irritation may be treated with topical lubrication.
оАБ Pingueculitis can be treated with lubrication if mild or with a
short course of topical steroid.
оАБ Excision may be indicated for cosmetic reasons or for signiоАЮcant irritation. In contrast to pterygium (see next), the recurrence rate is low and simple excision is usually adequate.
оАБ оАЯermal laser ablation can be eоАЬective. Gentian violet marking may be necessary to ensure adequate absorption in lighterskinned individuals.

{ Secondary infection with Candida spp.

{ Sterile ring inоАЮltrate and hypopyon.

{ Corneal scarring.

Management

оАБ Exclude other diagnoses.
оАБ Close observation may be required.
оАБ Confrontation oоАЫen leads to failure to return for review.
оАБ A psychiatric opinion may be appropriate.

Pterygium
Introduction

D

Fig. 6.27 (A) Pinguecula; (B) developing calciоАЮcation; (C) pingueculitis; (D) early progression
to pterygium.

Clinical features

A pterygium (plural pterygia) is a triangular fibrovascular subepithelial ingrowth of degenerative bulbar conjunctival tissue
over the limbus onto the cornea. It typically develops in patients
who have been living in sunny countries and, as with pinguecula, may represent a response to ultraviolet exposure and to
other factors such as chronic surface dryness. The condition
tends to run in families. A pterygium is histologically similar to
a pinguecula and shows elastotic degenerative changes in vascularized subepithelial stromal collagen (Fig. 6.28A). In contrast
to pingueculae, pterygia encroach onto the cornea, invading the
Bowman layer. Pseudopterygium appears similar clinically but
is caused by a band of conjunctiva adhering to an area of compromised cornea at its apex. It forms as a response to an acute
inflammatory episode such as a chemical burn, corneal ulcer
(especially if marginal), trauma and cicatrizing conjunctivitis.

оАБ Symptoms. Patients who present with a history of recent
enlargement are more likely to require early excision for subsequent aggressive growth. Aggressive growth or an atypical
appearance should prompt excision biopsy.

{ Most small lesions are asymptomatic.

{ Irritation and grittiness are caused by a dellen вАУ localized
drying вАУ eоАЬect at the advancing edge due to interference
with the precorneal tear оАЮlm (more likely if the head of the
pterygium is especially elevated).

{ Patients who wear contact lenses may develop symptoms
of irritation at an earlier stage due to edge liоАЫ.

{ Lesions may interfere with vision by obscuring the visual
axis or inducing astigmatism.

{ оАЯere may be intermittent inоАЭammation similar to
pingueculitis.

{ Cosmesis may be a signiоАЮcant problem.

{ Extensive lesions, particularly if recurrent, may be associated with subconjunctival оАЮbrosis extending to the fornices that may cause restricted ocular excursion.

Treatment

{ If pseudopterygium is suspected, there may be a history of
a causative episode.
оАБ Signs

{ A pterygium is made up of three parts: a вАШcapвАЩ (an avascular
halo-like zone at the advancing edge), a head and a body
(Fig. 6.28B and C).

{ Linear epithelial iron deposition (Stocker line) may be
seen in the corneal epithelium anterior to the head of the
pterygium (Fig. 6.28B).

{ Fuchs islets are small discrete whitish оАЭecks consisting
of clusters of pterygial epithelial cells oоАЫen present at the
advancing edge.

{ A pseudopterygium (Fig. 6.28D) is classically distinguished by the fact that the location is away from the
horizontal (though this may occasionally be seen with true
pterygia) and that there is a оАЮrm attachment to the cornea
only at its apex (head).

оАБ Medical treatment of symptomatic patients is as for pinguecula. оАЯe patient should be advised to wear sunglasses to reduce
ultraviolet exposure.
оАБ Surgery. Simple excision (вАШbare scleraвАЩ technique) is associated
with a high rate of recurrence (around 80%), oоАЫen with more
aggressive behaviour than the original lesion.

{ оАЯe pterygium is excised from the cornea and conjunctiva
leaving bare sclera (Fig. 6.29A).

{ Conjunctival autograоАЫing. оАЯe donor conjunctival patch is
usually harvested from the superior or upper-temporal paralimbal region (Fig. 6.29B). оАЯe graоАЫ is sutured into position
using 10-0 nylon sutures (Fig. 6.29C). оАЯe site heals rapidly.
Conjunctival graоАЫs can be secured with tissue glue (for example: TISSEEL оАЮbrin glue) rather than sutures, thus shortening
operating time and reducing postoperative irritation.

{ Adjunctive treatment with mitomycin C or beta-irradiation
can be used in place of patching techniques.

{ Peripheral lamellar keratoplasty may be required for deep
lesions.

of recurrence can be reduced by using either a conjunctival

Concretions

Concretions are extremely common and are usually associated
with ageing, although they can also form in patients with chronic
conjunctival inоАЭammation such as trachoma. оАЯey appear as multiple tiny cysts containing yellowishвАУwhite deposits of epithelial
debris including keratin, commonly located subepithelially in the
inferior tarsal and forniceal conjunctiva (Fig. 6.30A). оАЯey can
become calciоАЮed and, particularly if large, may erode through the
overlying epithelium (Fig. 6.30B) and cause marked irritation. If
symptomatic, treatment involves removal at the slit lamp with a
needle under topical anaesthesia.

Conjunctivochalasis

Conjunctivochalasis usually appears as a fold of redundant conjunctiva interposed between the globe and lower eyelid, protruding over the lid margin (Fig. 6.31). It is probably a normal ageing
change that may be exacerbated by inоАЭammation and mechanical
stress related to dry eye and lid margin disease. Symptoms include
watering of the eye due to obstruction of the inferior punctum
and interference with the marginal tear meniscus. Treatment consists of topical lubricants and treatment of any blepharitis. A short
course of topical steroids or another anti-inоАЭammatory agent may
be helpful. Conjunctival resection can be performed in severe
cases (see also Ch. 3).

Retention (primary epithelial inclusion)
cyst

Conjunctival retention cysts are thin-walled lesions on the bulbar conjunctiva containing clear (Fig. 6.32A) or occasionally
turbid оАЭuid or blood (Fig. 6.32B). оАЯey do not usually cause
discomfort, but may be a mild cosmetic blemish. Histology
shows a оАЭuid-оАЮlled internal cavity lined by a double epithelial
layer. Treatment, if required, is initially by simple puncture with
a needle under topical anaesthesia, but recurrence is common.
Bleeding should be encouraged within the ruptured cyst as it
may promote adhesion of the walls and reduce the chance of
recurrence. Cyst wall excision under topical anaesthesia can be
carried out for recurrent cyst formation. оАЯe diоАЬerential diagnosis includes secondary inclusion cysts following conjunctival
surgery and lymphangiectasia. оАЯe latter is characterized by
strings of cystic or sausage-shaped clear-walled channels, which
may become оАЮlled with blood (haemorrhagic lymphangiectasia

- Fig. 6.33).

SUBCONJUNCTIVAL HAEMORRHAGE

Subconjunctival haemorrhage is a very common phenomenon
that is oоАЫen idiopathic and apparently spontaneous, occurring
particularly in older people (Fig. 6.34A. It may result from surgery, conjunctivitis and trauma (Fig. 6.34B). оАЯe haemorrhage is
usually asymptomatic but a momentary sharp pain or a snapping
or popping sensation is sometimes felt. Coughing, sneezing and
vomiting are common precipitants. In younger people contact

lens wear is a common association and in older individuals systemic vascular disease (hypertension) is prevalent. A local ocular cause should be ruled out by slit lamp examination. Patients
are oоАЫen taking aspirin or similar products that have an eоАЬect

a substantially elevated haemorrhage leads to a large localized
corneal wetting deоАЮcit (dellen), which is oоАЫen uncomfortable.
A large bleed can track into the eyelids. Spontaneous resolution
over a week or two is typical, but two or three narrowly spaced
episodes are not uncommon.

205

206 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

Anatomy and physiology
General

оАЯe cornea is a complex structure which, as well as having a protective role, is responsible for about three-quarters of the optical
power of the eye. оАЯe normal hydration level is 78%. Nutrients are
supplied and metabolic products removed via the aqueous humour
posteriorly and the tears anteriorly. оАЯe cornea is the most densely
innervated tissue in the body and conditions such as abrasion and
bullous keratopathy are associated with marked pain, photophobia
and reоАЮex lacrimation. A subepithelial and a deeper stromal nerve
plexus are supplied by the оАЭrst division of the trigeminal nerve.

Dimensions

оАЯe average corneal diameter is 11.5 mm vertically and 12 mm
horizontally. It is 540 ќЉm thick centrally on average and thicker
towards the periphery. оАЯe normal hydration level is 78%. Central corneal thickness does not diоАЬer between males and females,
but varies between individuals and races. It is a key determinant
of the intraocular pressure (IOP) measured with conventional
techniques.

Structure

оАЯe cornea consists of the following layers (Fig. 7.1):
оАБ оАЯe epithelium is stratiоАЭed squamous and non-keratinized,
and is composed of:

{ A single layer of columnar basal cells attached by hemidesmosomes to an underlying basement membrane.

{ Two to three strata of вАШwingвАЩ cells.

{ Two layers of squamous surface cells.

{ оАЯe surface area of the outermost cells is increased by
microplicae and microvilli that facilitate the attachment
of the tear оАЭlm and mucin. AоАЫer a lifespan of a few days
superоАЭcial cells are shed into the tear оАЭlm.

Tear film

Surface cells

Wing cells

Basement
membrane

membrane

layer

Endothelium
Fig. 7.1 Anatomy of the cornea.

of the stroma. оАЯe membrane consists of an anterior banded
zone that is deposited in utero and a posterior non-banded
zone laid down throughout life by the endothelium, for which
it serves as a modiоАЭed basement membrane. It has regenerative
potential.
оАБ оАЯe endothelium consists of a monolayer of polygonal cells.
Endothelial cells maintain corneal deturgescence throughout life by pumping excess оАЮuid out of the stroma. оАЯe young
adult cell density is about 3000 cells/mm [2] . оАЯe number of
cells decreases at about 0.6% per year and neighbouring cells
enlarge to оАЭll the space. оАЯe cells cannot regenerate. At a density of about 500 cells/mm [2] corneal oedema develops and
transparency is impaired.
оАБ оАЯe existence of a sixth corneal layer between the stroma and
Descemet membrane (Dua layer) has been proposed, though
some authorities believe this to be a previously described continuation of the posterior stroma.

TIP The cornea is densely innervated and a corneal abrasion
is usually associated with intense pain, photophobia and reоАЮex
lacrimation.

{ Corneal stem cells are located at the corneoscleral limbus,
possibly in the palisades of Vogt. DeоАЭciency may result in
chronic epithelial defects and вАШconjunctivalizationвАЩ (epithelial instability, vascularization and the appearance of goblet
cells). оАЯey are thought to be critical in the maintenance of
a physiological barrier, preventing conjunctival tissue from
growing onto the cornea (e.g. pterygium). DeоАЭciency may
be addressed by stem cell auto- or allotransplantation.
оАБ Bowman layer is the acellular superоАЭcial layer of the stroma
and is formed from collagen оАЭbres.
оАБ оАЯe stroma makes up 90% of corneal thickness. It is arranged
in regularly orientated layers of collagen оАЭbrils whose spacing
is maintained by proteoglycan ground substance (chondroitin sulphate and keratan sulphate) with interspersed modiоАЭed
оАЭbroblasts (keratocytes). Maintenance of the regular arrangement and spacing of the collagen is critical to optical clarity.
оАЯe stroma can scar, but cannot regenerate following damage.
оАБ Descemet membrane is a discrete sheet composed of a оАЭne latticework of collagen оАЭbrils that are distinct from the collagen

Signs of corneal disease
SuperоАЯcial

оАБ Punctate epithelial erosions (PEE), tiny epithelial defects that
stain with оАЮuorescein (Fig. 7.2A) and rose Bengal (Fig. 7.2B),
are generally an early sign of epithelial compromise. Causes
include a variety of stimuli. оАЯe location of the lesions may
give an indication of aetiology:

{ Superior вАУ vernal disease, chlamydial conjunctivitis, superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis, оАЮoppy eyelid syndrome
and mechanically induced keratoconjunctivitis.

{ Interpalpebral вАУ dry eye (can also be inferior), reduced
corneal sensation and ultraviolet keratopathy.

{ Inferior вАУ chronic blepharitis, lagophthalmos, eye drop
toxicity, self-induced, aberrant eyelashes and entropion.

{ DiоАЬuse вАУ some cases of viral conjunctivitis and toxicity to
drops.

{ Central вАУ prolonged contact lens wear.
оАБ Punctate epithelial keratitis (PEK) appears as granular,
opalescent, swollen epithelial cells, with focal intraepithelial infiltrates. They are visible unstained but stain well
with rose Bengal and variably with fluorescein. Causes
include:

{ Infections: adenoviral, chlamydial, molluscum contagiosum, early herpes simplex and herpes zoster, microsporidial
and systemic viral infections (e.g. measles, varicella, rubella).

CHAPTER

Cornea 207
#### 7

Deep

оАБ InоАЮltrates are yellowвАУ or greyвАУwhite opacities located initially
within the anterior stroma (Fig. 7.3A), usually associated with
limbal or conjunctival hyperaemia. оАЯey are stromal foci of
acute inоАЮammation composed of inоАЮammatory cells, cellular
and extracellular debris including necrosis. оАЯe key distinction
is between sterile and infective lesions (Table 7.1); вАШPEDALвАЩ:
Pain, Epithelial defects, Discharge, Anterior chamber reaction,
Location is a useful mnemonic to distinguish these two types
of lesions. Suppurative keratitis is caused by active infection
with bacteria, fungi, protozoa and occasionally viruses. Noninfectious вАШsterile keratitisвАЩ is due to an immune hypersensitivity response to antigen as in marginal keratitis and with
contact lens wear.
оАБ Ulceration refers to tissue excavation associated with an epithelial defect (Fig. 7.3B), usually with inоАЭltration and necrosis.
оАБ вАШMeltingвАЩ describes tissue disintegration in response to enzymatic activity, oоАЫen with mild or no inоАЭltrate, e.g. peripheral
ulcerative keratitis.
оАБ Vascularization occurs in response to a wide variety of stimuli. Venous channels are easily seen, whereas arterial feeding
vessels are smaller and require higher magniоАЭcation. Nonperfused deep vessels appear as вАШghost vesselsвАЩ, best detected by
retroillumination.
оАБ Lipid deposition (Fig. 7.3C) may follow chronic inоАЮammation
with leakage from corneal new vessels.
оАБ Folds in Descemet membrane, also known as striate keratopathy (Fig. 7.3D), may result from corneal oedema. Causes
include inоАЮammation, trauma (including surgery) and ocular
hypotony.
оАБ Descemetocoele (US spelling вАУ descemetocele) is a bubblelike herniation of Descemet membrane into the cornea
(Fig. 7.3E), plugging a defect that would otherwise be
full-thickness.
оАБ Breaks in Descemet membrane (Fig. 7.3F) may be due to corneal enlargement (Haab striae in infantile glaucoma) or deformation such as keratoconus and birth trauma. Acute inоАЮux of
aqueous into the corneal stroma (acute hydrops) can follow.
оАБ оАЯe Seidel test demonstrates aqueous leakage. A drop of 1% or
2% оАЮuorescein is applied to the surface of the eye. Using a slit
lamp, the cobalt blue оАЭlter is used to detect a change in colour
from dark orange to bright yellowвАУgreen secondary to localized dilution at a site of leakage.

Documentation of clinical signs

Clinical signs should be illustrated with a colour-coded labelled
diagram. Measuring lesion dimensions is particularly useful to
facilitate monitoring (Fig. 7.4). Slit lamp photography is an increasingly used supplement or alternative, but must be of high quality.
оАБ Opacities such as scars and degenerations are drawn in black.
оАБ Epithelial oedema is represented by оАЭne blue circles, stromal
oedema as blue shading and folds in Descemet membrane as
wavy blue lines.

{ Miscellaneous: оАЯygeson superоАЭcial punctate keratitis and
eye drop toxicity.
оАБ Subepithelial infiltrates. Tiny subsurface foci of nonstaining inflammatory infiltrates (Fig. 7.2C). Causes
include severe or prolonged adenoviral keratoconjunctivitis, herpes zoster keratitis, adult inclusion conjunctivitis,
marginal keratitis, rosacea and Thygeson superficial punctate keratitis.
оАБ SuperоАЮcial punctate keratitis is a non-speciоАЭc term
describing any corneal epithelial disturbance of dot-like
morphology.
оАБ Filaments. Strands of mucus admixed with epithelium,
attached at one end to the corneal surface, that stain well with
rose Bengal (see Fig. 7.2B). оАЯe unattached end moves with
each blink. Grey subepithelial opacities may be seen at the
site of attachment. Dry eye is by far the most common cause.
Other causes include superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis,
neurotrophic keratopathy, long-term ocular patching and
essential blepharospasm.
оАБ Epithelial oedema. Subtle oedema may manifest with loss of
normal corneal lustre, but more commonly, abundant tiny epithelial vesicles are seen. Bullae form in moderateвАУsevere cases
(Fig. 7.2D). оАЯe cause is usually endothelial decompensation,
but it can follow acute elevation of IOP.
оАБ SuperоАЮcial neovascularization (Fig. 7.2E) is a feature of
chronic ocular surface irritation or hypoxia, as in contact lens
wear.
оАБ Pannus describes superоАЭcial neovascularization accompanied
by degenerative subepithelial change (Fig. 7.2F).

Specular microscopy

Specular microscopy is the study of corneal layers under very high
magniоАЭcation (100 times greater than slit lamp biomicroscopy).
It is mainly used to assess the endothelium, which can be analysed for cellular size, shape, density and distribution. оАЯe healthy

A

B

Fig. 7.5 Specular micrograph. (A) Normal corneal endothelium; (B) cornea guttata with marked loss of endothelial
mosaic and greater size of the remaining cells. (Courtesy
of J Sharp.)

endothelial cell is a regular hexagon (Fig. 7.5A) and the normal cell
density in a young adult is about 3000 cells/mm [2]

оАБ Physics. When a light beam of the specular photomicroscope
passes through the cornea it encounters a series of interfaces
between optically distinct regions. Some light is reоАЮected specularly (i.e. like a mirror) back towards the photomicroscope
and forms an image that can be photographed and analysed.
оАБ Indications

{ Evaluation of the functional reserve of the corneal endothelium prior to intraocular surgery is the most common indication. A clear cornea with normal thickness
on pachymetry is not necessarily associated with normal
endothelial morphology or cell density. Corneal oedema is
considerably more likely to occur with a cell density below
700 cells/mm [2] but unlikely above 1000 cells/mm [2]

{ Donor cornea evaluation.

{ To demonstrate pathology, particularly cornea guttata
(Fig. 7.5B), Descemet membrane irregularities and posterior polymorphous dystrophy.

Corneal topography

Corneal topography is used to image the cornea by projecting a
series of concentric rings of light on the anterior surface, constituting a Placido image. оАЯe reоАЮected light is analysed using
computer soоАЫware to produce a detailed surface map. A major
application is the detection and management of corneal ectasia,
principally keratoconus. Screening for corneal ectasia is especially important prior to refractive surgery. It is used occasionally for contact lens оАЭtting and to measure corneal thickness.
ScheimpоАЮug imaging is a new technology that may oоАЬer advantages in topographic imaging. Anterior segment optical coherence tomography (OCT) and ultrasound biomicroscopy can also
be used to image the cornea.

Principles of treatment
Control of infection and inоАЮammation

оАБ Antimicrobial agents should be started as soon as preliminary investigations have been performed. оАЯe choice of agent
is determined by the likely aetiology according to clinical оАЭndings. Broad-spectrum treatment is generally used initially,
with more selective agents introduced if necessary, when the
results of investigation are available.
оАБ Topical steroids should be used with caution as they may
promote replication of some microorganisms, notably herpes simplex virus and fungi, and retard reparative processes
such as re-epithelialization. Nevertheless, they are vital in a
range of conditions for the suppression of destructive visioncompromising inоАЮammation.
оАБ Systemic immunosuppressive agents are useful in some conditions, particularly autoimmune disease.

Promotion of epithelial healing

Re-epithelialization is of great importance in any corneal disease,
as thinning seldom progresses if the epithelium is intact.
оАБ Reduction of exposure to toxic medications and preservatives
wherever possible.
оАБ Lubrication with artiоАЭcial tears (unpreserved if possible) and
ointment. Taping the lids closed as a temporary measure is
oоАЫen used as a nocturnal adjunct.
оАБ Antibiotic ointment prophylaxis should be considered.
оАБ Bandage soоАЭ contact lenses should be carefully supervised
to exclude superinfection and duration kept to a minimum.
Indications include:

{ Promotion of healing by mechanically protecting regenerating corneal epithelium from the constant rubbing of the
eyelids.

{ To improve comfort, particularly in the presence of a large
corneal abrasion.

Cornea 211
#### 7

{ Botulinum toxin injection into the levator muscle to
induce a temporary (2вАУ3 months) ptosis.

{ Temporary or permanent lateral tarsorrhaphy or medial
canthoplasty and occasionally central tarsorrhaphy (Fig.
7.6B).
оАБ Conjunctival (Gundersen) оАЬap will protect and allow healing of a corneal epithelial defect. It is particularly suitable for
chronic unilateral disease in which the prognosis for restoration of useful vision is poor. Buccal mucous membrane is an
alternative.
оАБ Amniotic membrane patch graоАЭing (Fig. 7.6C) for persistent
unresponsive epithelial defects.
оАБ Tissue adhesive (cyanoacrylate glue) to seal small perforations. оАЯe glue can be applied to one side of a bespoke trimmed
patch of sterile plastic drape, which is pressed over the defect
aоАЫer the edges are dried with a cellulose sponge. оАЯe patch
remains in place to seal the defect and a bandage contact lens
is inserted for comfort and to aid retention of the patch (Fig.
7.6D).
оАБ Corneal cross-linking using ultraviolet light-A and riboоАЮavin
(photoactivated chromophore for infectious keratitis-corneal
collagen cross-linking: PACK-CXL) can be used to treat resistant corneal infections, particularly if there is evidence of corneal melting. оАЯis treatment is usually used in combination
with standard antimicrobial therapy, but has also been shown
to have a microbicidal eоАЬect when used as оАЭrst-line treatment
in patients with early bacterial keratitis.
оАБ Limbal stem cell transplantation may be used if there is stem
cell deоАЭciency as in chemical burns and cicatrizing conjunctivitis. оАЯe source of the donor tissue may be the fellow eye
(autograоАЫ) in unilateral disease or a living or cadaver donor
(allograоАЫ) when both eyes are aоАЬected. A newer technique
involves the inоАЯvitro replication of the patientвАЩs own stem
cells with subsequent re-implantation of the enhanced cell
population.
оАБ Smoking retards epithelialization and should be discontinued.

BACTERIAL KERATITIS

Pathogenesis
Pathogens

Bacterial keratitis usually develops only when ocular defences have
been compromised (see below). However, some bacteria, including Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Neisseria meningitidis, Corynebacterium diphtheriae and Haemophilus inоАЮuenzae are able to penetrate
a healthy corneal epithelium, usually in association with severe
conjunctivitis. It is important to remember that infections may be
polymicrobial, including bacterial and fungal co-infection. Common pathogens include:
оАБ Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a ubiquitous Gram-negative
bacillus (rod) commensal of the gastrointestinal tract. оАЯe
infection is typically aggressive and is responsible for over 60%
of contact lens-related keratitis.
оАБ Staphylococcus aureus is a common Gram-positive and
coagulase-positive commensal of the nares, skin and

{ To seal a small perforation (Fig. 7.6A).
оАБ Surgical eyelid closure is particularly useful in exposure and
neurotrophic keratopathy, as well as in persistent epithelial
defects. Lid closure may be used as a conservative method to
heal an infective ulcer in selected cases, such as an eye with no
visual potential in a patient with severe dementia.

212 Bacterial Keratitis

A B

C D

Fig. 7.6 Methods of promoting epithelial healing. (A) Bandage contact lens (arrow) in an eye

severe thinning. (Courtesy of S Tuft вАУ оАЯgs A and C; S Chen вАУ оАЯg. B.)

conjunctiva. Keratitis tends to present with a focal and fairly
well-deоАЭned white or yellowвАУwhite inоАЭltrate.
оАБ Streptococci. S. pyogenes is a common Gram-positive commensal of the throat and vagina. S. pneumoniae (pneumococcus) is a Gram-positive commensal of the upper respiratory
tract. Infections with streptococci are oоАЫen aggressive.

Risk factors

оАБ Contact lens wear, particularly if extended, is the most important risk factor. Corneal epithelial compromise secondary to
hypoxia and minor trauma is thought to be important, as is
bacterial adherence to the lens surface. Wearers of soоАЫ lenses
are at higher risk than those wearing rigid gas permeable
and other types. Infection is more likely if there is poor lens
hygiene, but it can also occur even with apparently meticulous
lens care and with daily disposable lenses.
оАБ Trauma, including refractive surgery (particularly LASIK

  - laser-assisted in situ keratomileusis), has been linked to

bacterial infection, including with atypical mycobacteria. In
developing countries agricultural injury is the major risk factor, when fungal infection should be considered.
оАБ Ocular surface disease such as herpetic keratitis, bullous
keratopathy, dry eye, chronic blepharitis, trichiasis and
entropion, exposure, severe allergic eye disease and corneal
anaesthesia.
оАБ Other factors include local or systemic immunosuppression,
diabetes and vitamin A deоАЭciency.

TIP Bacterial corneal ulceration should be excluded in a
patient who wears contact lenses and presents with a painful
red eye and blurred vision.

Clinical features

оАБ Presentation is with unilateral pain, photophobia, blurred
vision and mucopurulent or purulent discharge.

{ An epithelial defect with inоАЭltrate involving a larger area
and signiоАЭcant circumcorneal injection (Fig. 7.7A and B).

{ Stromal oedema, folds in Descemet membrane and anterior uveitis, commonly with a hypopyon (Fig. 7.7C) and
posterior synechiae in moderateвАУsevere keratitis. Plaquelike keratic precipitates can form on the endothelium contiguous with the aоАЬected stroma.

{ Chemosis and eyelid swelling in moderateвАУsevere cases.

{ Severe ulceration (Fig. 7.7D) may lead to descemetocoele
formation and perforation, particularly in Pseudomonas
infection.

{ Scleritis can develop, particularly with severe perilimbal
infection.

{ Endophthalmitis is rare in the absence of perforation.

{ Improvement is usually heralded by a reduction in eyelid
oedema and chemosis, shrinking of the epithelial defect,
decreasing inоАЭltrate density and a reduction in anterior
chamber signs.

{ Subsequent scarring may be severe, including vascularization. In addition to opaciоАЭcation, irregular astigmatism
may limit vision.
оАБ Reduced corneal sensation may suggest associated neurotrophic keratopathy, particularly where there is no other major
risk factor. Sensation may also be reduced in chronic surface
disease, herpetic keratitis and aоАЫer long-term contact lens
wear.
оАБ DiоАЫerential diagnosis includes keratitis due to other microorganisms (fungi, acanthamoeba, stromal herpes simplex keratitis and mycobacteria), marginal keratitis, sterile inоАЮammatory
corneal inоАЭltrates associated with contact lens wear, peripheral
ulcerative keratitis and toxic keratitis.

Investigations

оАБ Corneal scraping. оАЯis may not be required for a small inоАЭltrate, particularly one without an epithelial defect and away
from the visual axis.

214 Bacterial Keratitis

{ Scrape all ulcers that are: (a) more than 2mm in size, (b)
involve the middle to deep stroma, (c) within the visual
axis, (d) chronic in nature, (e) atypical in appearance.

{ A non-preserved topical anaesthetic is instilled (preservatives may lower bacterial viability for culture). One drop
of proxymetacaine 0.5% is usually suоАЪcient but tetracaine
may have a greater bacteriostatic eоАЬect.

{ Scrapings are taken either with a disposable scalpel blade
(e.g. No. 11 or Bard Parker), the bent tip of a larger diameter (e.g. 20- or 21-gauge) hypodermic needle, or a sterile
spatula (e.g. Kimura).

{ оАЯe easiest way to вАШplateвАЩ scrapings without breaking the
gel surface is with a spatula. If a fresh spatula is not available for each sample a single instrument should be оАЮamesterilized between scrapes (heat for 5 seconds, cool for
20вАУ30 seconds). Alternatively, a fresh scalpel blade or
needle can be used for each pass. Calcium alginate swabs
may also be satisfactory.

{ Loose mucus and necrotic tissue should be removed from
the surface of the ulcer prior to scraping.

{ оАЯe margins and base (except if very thin) of the lesion are
scraped (Fig. 7.8A).

{ A thin smear is placed on one or two glass slides for microscopy, including Gram stain (see below). A surface is provided
on one side of one end of the slide (conventionally вАШupвАЩ) for
pencil labelling. оАЯe sample is allowed to dry in air at room
temperature for several minutes then placed in a slide carrier.

{ Re-scraping is performed for each medium and samples
are plated onto culture media (Table 7.2), taking care not
to break the surface of the gel.

{ Routinely, blood, chocolate and Sabouraud media (Fig.
7.8B and C) are used initially and the samples are placed in
an incubator until transported to the laboratory. Refrigerated media should be gently warmed to room temperature
prior to sample application.

{ A blade or needle can be placed directly into bottled media
such as brainвАУheart infusion (BHI). оАЯere is evidence that
a single scrape, sent in BHI to the laboratory where it is
homogenized and plated, provides similar results to the
traditional multi-scrape method.

TIP Corneal scraping should be undertaken in most cases

Treatment
General considerations

оАБ Hospital admission should be considered for patients who are
not likely to comply or are unable to self-administer treatment.
It should also be considered for aggressive disease, particularly
if involving an only eye.
оАБ Discontinuation of contact lens wear is mandatory.
оАБ A clear plastic eye shield should be worn between eye drop
instillation if signiоАЭcant thinning (or perforation) is present.
оАБ Decision to treat

Local therapy

Topical therapy (Table 7.4) can achieve high tissue concentration

treatment.

Management of apparent treatment failure

It is important not to confuse ongoing failure of re-epithelialization
with continued infection. Drug toxicity, particularly following frequent instillation of fortiоАЭed aminoglycosides, may give increasing
discomfort, redness and dischargedespite the eradication of infection.
оАБ If no improvement is evident following 24вАУ48 hours of intensive treatment, the antibiotic regimen should be reviewed,
including contact with the microbiology laboratory to obtain
the latest report.
оАБ оАЯere is no need to change the initial therapy if this has
induced a favourable response, even if cultures show a resistant organism.
оАБ If there is still no improvement aоАЫer a further 48 hours, suspension of treatment should be considered for 24 hours then
re-scraping performed with inoculation on a broader range
of media (see Table 7.2) and additional staining techniques
requested (see Table 7.3). Consideration should be given to the
possibility of a non-bacterial causative microorganism.
оАБ If cultures remain negative, it may be necessary to perform a
corneal biopsy for histology and culture.
оАБ Consider collagen cross-linking treatment (PACK-CXL) if
there is a suspicion of corneal melting or bacterial resistance.
оАБ Excisional keratoplasty, penetrating or deep lamellar, may be
considered in cases resistant to medical therapy, or for incipient or actual perforation (see below).

TIP Topical drug and preservative toxicity may cause a failure
of corneal re-epithelialization, which can be confused with
persistent infection.

{ H. inоАЮuenzae infection should be treated with oral amoxicillin with clavulanic acid.

{ N. gonorrhoeae requires a third-generation cephalosporin
such as ceоАЫriaxone.
оАБ Severe corneal thinning with threatened or actual perforation
requires:

{ CiproоАЮoxacin for its antibacterial activity.

TIP Fluoroquinolone eyedrops have limited eоАЭectivity against
Gram-positive organisms (particularly Streptococcus).

Systemic antibiotics

Systemic antibiotics are not usually given, but may be appropriate
in the following circumstances:
оАБ Potential for systemic involvement, when microbiological/
infectious disease specialist advice should optimally be sought
but should not delay treatment:

{ N. meningitidis, in which early systemic prophylaxis may
be life-saving. Treatment is usually with intramuscular benzylpenicillin, ceоАЫriaxone or cefotaxime, or oral
ciproоАЮoxacin.

218 Fungal Keratitis

Perforation

A small perforation in which infection is controlled may be manageable with a bandage contact lens. Tissue glue is oоАЫen adequate
for a slightly larger dehiscence. A penetrating keratoplasty or
corneal patch graоАЫ may be necessary for larger perforations, or
in those where infection is extensive or inadequately controlled.
Occlusive surface repair techniques may be appropriate in some
circumstances, such as an eye with no useful visual potential.

Endophthalmitis

No clear protocol exists for the management of this rare complication, but a similar approach to postoperative endophthalmitis
should be considered, whilst continuing speciоАЭc management of
the corneal infection (see Ch. 10). Secondary sterile intraocular
inоАЮammation should not be mistaken for intraocular infection.

Visual rehabilitation

оАБ Keratoplasty (lamellar may be adequate) may be required for
residual dense corneal scarring.
оАБ Rigid contact lenses may be required for irregular astigmatism but are generally only introduced at least 3 months aоАЫer
re-epithelialization.
оАБ Cataract surgery may be required because secondary lens
opacities are common following severe inоАЮammation. Even
in the absence of severe corneal opaciоАЭcation, surgery may be
hampered by corneal haze, posterior synechiae and zonular
fragility.
оАБ Prognosis depends on the size, location, depth and aetiology
of the corneal ulcer.

FUNGAL KERATITIS

Introduction
Pathogenesis

Fungi are a group of microorganisms that have rigid walls and
a distinct nucleus with multiple chromosomes containing both
DNA and RNA. Fungal keratitis is rare in temperate countries but
is a major cause of visual loss in tropical and low-income countries. оАЯough oоАЫen evolving insidiously, fungal keratitis can elicit
a severe inоАЮammatory response вАУ corneal perforation is common
and the outlook for vision is frequently poor. Two main types of
fungi cause keratitis:
оАБ Yeasts (e.g. genus Candida), ovoid unicellular organisms that
reproduce by budding, are responsible for most cases of fungal
keratitis in temperate climates.
оАБ Filamentous fungi (e.g. genera Fusarium and Aspergillus),
multicellular organisms that produce tubular projections
known as hyphae. оАЯey are the most common pathogens in
tropical climates, but are not uncommon in cooler regions. оАЯe
keratitis frequently follows an aggressive course.

Predisposing factors

Common predisposing factors include chronic ocular surface disease, the long-term use of topical steroids (oоАЫen in conjunction

{ Other stains include periodic acidвАУSchiоАЬ, calcoоАЮuor white
(Fig. 7.10D) and methenamine silver.
оАБ Culture. Corneal scrapes should be plated on Sabouraud
dextrose agar, although most fungi will also grow on blood
agar or in enrichment media. It is important to obtain an
eоАЬective scrape from the ulcer base. Sensitivity testing for
antifungal agents can be performed in reference laboratories
but the relevance of these results to clinical eоАЬectiveness is
uncertain. If applicable, contact lenses and cases should be
sent for culture.

with prior corneal transplantation), contact lens wear, systemic
immunosuppression and diabetes. Filamentary keratitis may be
associated with trauma, oоАЫen relatively minor, involving plant
matter or gardening/agricultural tools.

Candida and оАЮlamentous keratitis
Clinical features

оАЯe diagnosis is frequently delayed unless there is a high
index of suspicion and oоАЫen bacterial infection will initially
have been presumed. Clinical signs are not a deоАЭnitive means
of distinguishing bacterial and fungal corneal infection. Signs
such as satellite inоАЭltrates (see below) can be caused by other
microorganisms.
оАБ Symptoms. Gradual onset of pain, grittiness, photophobia,
blurred vision and watery or mucopurulent discharge.
оАБ Candida keratitis

{ YellowвАУwhite densely suppurative inоАЭltrate is typical.
оАБ Filamentous keratitis

{ Grey or yellowвАУwhite stromal inоАЭltrate with indistinct
оАЮuоАЬy margins (Fig. 7.10A).

{ Progressive inоАЭltration, oоАЫen with satellite lesions (Fig.
7.10B and C) or a ring-shaped inоАЭltrate.

{ Feathery branch-like extensions may develop.

{ Rapid progression with necrosis and thinning can occur.

{ Penetration of an intact Descemet membrane may occur
and lead to endophthalmitis without evident perforation.
оАБ An epithelial defect is not invariable and is sometimes small
when present.
оАБ Other features include anterior uveitis, hypopyon, endothelial plaque, raised IOP, scleritis and sterile or infective
endophthalmitis.
оАБ DiоАЫerential diagnosis includes bacterial, herpetic and acanthamoebal keratitis. Bacterial infection may sometimes present in a subacute fashion, particularly when atypical organisms
are responsible. It is important to beware of co-infection,
including with an additional fungal species.

Investigations

Samples for laboratory investigation should be acquired before
commencing antifungal therapy.
оАБ Staining

{ Potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation with direct
microscopic evaluation is a rapid diagnostic tool that can
be highly sensitive.

{ Gram and Giemsa staining are both about 50% sensitive.
