оАБ Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) analysis of specimens is
rapid and highly sensitive (up to 90%) and may be the current
investigation of choice. Calcium-containing swabs can inhibit
polymerase activity and local collection protocols should be
ascertained prior to specimen collection.
оАБ Corneal biopsy is indicated in suspected fungal keratitis in the
absence of clinical improvement aоАЫer 3вАУ4 days and if no growth
develops from scrapings aоАЫer a week. A 2вАУ3 mm block should be
taken, similar to scleral block excision during trabeculectomy.
Filamentous fungi tend to proliferate just anterior to Descemet
membrane and a deep stromal specimen may be required. оАЯe
excised block is sent for culture and histopathological analysis.
оАБ Anterior chamber tap has been advocated in resistant cases
with endothelial exudate, because organisms may penetrate
the endothelium.
оАБ Confocal microscopy frequently permits identiоАЭcation of organisms inоАЯvivo, but is not widely available outside tertiary centres.

Treatment

Improvement may be slow in comparison to bacterial infection.
оАБ General measures are as for bacterial keratitis although hospital admission is usually required.
оАБ Removal of the epithelium over the lesion may enhance penetration of antifungal agents. It may also be helpful to regularly
remove mucus and necrotic tissue with a spatula.
оАБ Topical antifungals should initially be given hourly for 48
hours and then reduced as signs permit.

{ Candida infection is treated with amphotericin B 0.15% or
econazole 1% (alternatives include natamycin 5%, оАЮuconazole 2%, clotrimazole 1% and voriconazole 1 or 2%).

{ Filamentous infection is treated with natamycin 5% or
econazole 1% (alternatives are amphotericin B 0.15%,
miconazole 1% and voriconazole 1 or 2%).

{ Natamycin is the only topical drug approved by the FDA
for use in mycotic keratitis.

220 Herpes Simplex Keratitis

оАБ A broad-spectrum antibiotic might also be considered to
address or prevent bacterial co-infection.
оАБ Cycloplegia as for bacterial keratitis.
оАБ Subconjunctival оАЮuconazole may be used in severe cases.
оАБ Systemic antifungals may be given in severe cases, when
lesions are near the limbus and for suspected endophthalmitis. Options include voriconazole 400 mg twice daily for one
day then 200 mg twice daily, itraconazole 200 mg once daily,
reduced to 100 mg once daily, or оАЮuconazole 200 mg twice
daily.
оАБ Tetracycline (e.g. doxycycline 100 mg twice daily) may be
given for its anticollagenase eоАЬect when there is signiоАЭcant
thinning.
оАБ Perforation вАУ actual or impending вАУ is managed as for bacterial keratitis.
оАБ SuperоАЮcial keratectomy can be eоАЬective to debulk a lesion.
оАБ оАЯerapeutic keratoplasty (penetrating or deep anterior lamellar) is considered when medical therapy is ineоАЬective or following perforation.
оАБ Anterior chamber washout with intracameral antifungal
injection may be considered for unresponsive cases in which
there is a stable corneal inоАЭltrate but enlarging endothelial
exudation.

Microsporidial keratitis
Introduction

Microsporidia (phylum Microspora) are obligate intracellular
single-celled parasites previously thought to be protozoa but now
reclassiоАЭed as fungi. оАЯey rarely cause disease in the immunocompetent and until the advent of acquired immunodeоАЭciency
syndrome (AIDS) were rarely pathogenic for humans. оАЯe most
common general infection is enteritis and the most common ocular manifestation is keratoconjunctivitis.

Diagnosis

оАБ Signs

{ Bilateral chronic diоАЬuse PEK (Fig. 7.11A).

{ Unilateral slowly progressive deep stromal keratitis (Fig.
7.11B) may rarely aоАЬect immunocompetent patients.

{ Sclerokeratitis and endophthalmitis are rare.
оАБ Biopsy shows characteristic spores and intracellular parasites.
оАБ PCR of scrapings may have relatively low sensitivity.

Treatment

оАБ Medical therapy of epithelial disease is with topical fumagillin.
Highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) for associated
AIDS may also help resolution. Stromal disease is treated with
a combination of topical fumagillin and oral albendazole 400
mg once daily for 2 weeks, repeated 2 weeks later with a second
course. Patients should be closely monitored for hepatic toxicity.
Long-term fumagillin treatment may be required and it is diоАЪcult to eradicate the parasites in immunocompromised patients.
оАБ Keratoplasty may be indicated although recurrence of disease
can occur in the graоАЫ periphery. Cryotherapy to the residual
tissue may reduce this risk.

HERPES SIMPLEX KERATITIS

Introduction

Herpetic eye disease is the most common infectious cause of corneal blindness in developed countries. As many as 60% of corneal
ulcers in developing countries may be the result of herpes simplex
virus and 10 million people worldwide may have herpetic eye disease. Approximately 10% of those who have any history of herpes
simplex keratitis (HSK) will eventually have a оАЭnal visual acuity of
less than 6/60. Even in the patients who retain good vision, corneal opacities can result in aberrations, astigmatism and reduced

A B

Fig. 7.11 Microsporidial keratitis. (A) DiоАЭuse punctate epithelial keratitis; (B) deep stromal
inоАЯltrates. (Courtesy of S Tuft.)

quality of vision. In addition to visual loss, HSK also reduces the
quality of life of those aоАЬected, even when the eye is not inоАЮamed.

Herpes simplex virus (HSV)

HSV is enveloped with a cuboidal capsule and has a linear doublestranded DNA genome. оАЯe two subtypes are HSV-1 and HSV-2
and these reside in almost all neuronal ganglia. HSV-1 causes
infection above the waist (principally the face, lips and eyes),
whereas HSV-2 causes venereally acquired infection (genital herpes). Rarely HSV-2 may be transmitted to the eye through infected
secretions, either venereally or at birth (neonatal conjunctivitis).
HSV transmission is facilitated in conditions of crowding and poor
hygiene.

Primary infection

Primary infection, without previous viral exposure, usually
occurs in childhood and is spread by droplet transmission, or
less frequently by direct inoculation. Due to protection by maternal antibodies, it is uncommon during the оАЭrst 6 months of life.
Occasionally severe neonatal systemic disease may occur in which
early diagnosis and intravenous antiviral treatment are critical to
reduce mortality and disability. оАЯe presence of maternal antibodies means that dendritic corneal ulcers may be seen. Most primary infections with HSV are subclinical or cause only mild fever,
malaise and upper respiratory tract symptoms. Blepharitis and
follicular conjunctivitis may develop but are usually mild and selflimited. Treatment, if necessary, involves topical aciclovir ointment
for the eye and/or cream for skin lesions and occasionally oral antivirals. оАЯere is unfortunately no evidence that antiviral treatment
at this stage reduces the likelihood of recurrent disease.

Recurrent infection

Recurrent disease (reactivation in the presence of cellular and
humoral immunity) occurs as follows:
оАБ AоАЭer primary infection the virus is carried to the sensory
ganglion for that dermatome (e.g. trigeminal ganglion) where
latent infection is established. Latent virus is incorporated in
host DNA and cannot be eradicated with presently available
treatment.
оАБ Subclinical reactivation can periodically occur, during which
HSV is shed and patients are contagious.
оАБ Clinical reactivation. A variety of stress factors such as fever,
hormonal change, ultraviolet radiation, trauma, or trigeminal
injury may cause clinical reactivation, when the virus replicates and is transported in the sensory axons to the periphery.
оАБ оАЯe pattern of disease depends on the site of reactivation,
which may be remote from the site of primary disease. Hundreds of reactivations can occur during a lifetime.
оАБ оАЯe rate of ocular recurrence aоАЫer one episode is about 10%
at 1 year and 50% at 10 years. оАЯe higher the number of previous attacks the greater the risk of recurrence.
оАБ Risk factors for severe disease, which may be frequently
recurrent, include atopic eye disease, childhood, immunodeоАЭciency or suppression, malnutrition, measles and malaria.
Inappropriate use of topical steroids may enhance the development of geographic ulceration (see below).

CHAPTER

Cornea 221
#### 7

TIP Herpes simplex is the commonest infectious cause of
corneal disease in developed countries.

Epithelial keratitis
Clinical features

Epithelial (dendritic or geographic) keratitis is associated with
active virus replication.
оАБ Symptoms. MildвАУmoderate discomfort, redness, photophobia, watering and blurred vision.
оАБ Signs in approximately chronological order:

{ Reduced visual acuity.

{ Swollen opaque epithelial cells arranged in a coarse punctate or stellate (Fig. 7.12A) pattern.

{ Central desquamation results in a linear-branching (dendritic) ulcer (Fig. 7.12B), most frequently located centrally.
оАЯe branches of the ulcer have characteristic terminal buds
and its bed stains well with оАЮuorescein.

{ оАЯe virus-laden cells at the margin of the ulcer stain with
rose Bengal (Fig. 7.12C). оАЯis may help distinction of herpetic ulceration from alternative conditions, particularly
an atypical recurrent corneal abrasion.

{ Corneal sensation is reduced.

{ Inadvertent topical steroid treatment may promote progressive enlargement of the ulcer to a geographical or
вАШamoeboidвАЩ conоАЭguration (Fig. 7.12D).

{ Mild associated subepithelial haze is typical.

{ Anterior chamber activity may be present, but is usually
mild.

{ Follicular conjunctivitis may be associated; topical antivirals can also cause this.

{ Vesicular eyelid lesions may coincide with epithelial
ulceration.

{ Elevated IOP is not uncommon (tonometry should be performed on the unaоАЬected eye оАЭrst and a disposable prism
used).

{ Following healing, there may be persistent PEE and
irregular epithelium that settle spontaneously and
should not be mistaken for persistent active infection.
A whorled epithelial appearance commonly results
from assiduous, especially prolonged, topical antiviral
instillation.

{ Mild subepithelial haze (Fig. 7.12E) may persist for
weeks aоАЫer the epithelium heals. In some cases, mild
scarring may develop, which tends to become more evident aоАЫer each recurrence and may eventually threaten
vision.

{ Recurrence may occur aоАЫer a corneal graоАЫ, undertaken for
stromal scarring (Fig. 7.12F).
оАБ Investigation is usually unnecessary as the diagnosis can
be made clinically. Pre-treatment scrapings can be sent in
viral transport medium for culture. PCR and immunocytochemistry are also available. Giemsa staining shows
multinucleated giant cells. HSV serological titres rise only on
primary infection, but can be used to conоАЭrm previous viral

222 Herpes Simplex Keratitis

A B

C D

E F

Fig. 7.12 Epithelial herpes simplex keratitis. (A) Stellate lesions; (B) bed of a dendritic ulcer stained
with оАЮuorescein; (C) margins of a dendritic ulcer stained with rose Bengal; (D) geographic ulcer;
(E) residual subepithelial haze; (F) recurrent ulceration after a corneal graft. (Courtesy of S Tuft

            - оАЯg. C; T Carmichael вАУ оАЯg. F.)

exposure, usually in cases of stromal disease when the diagnosis is in doubt.
оАБ DiоАЫerential diagnosis of dendritic ulceration includes herpes
zoster keratitis, healing corneal abrasion (pseudodendrite),
acanthamoeba keratitis, epithelial rejection in a corneal graоАЫ,
tyrosinaemia type 2, the epithelial eоАЬects of soоАЫ contact lenses
and toxic keratopathy secondary to topical medication. It has
recently been reported that dendritiform keratopathy can be
caused by polyquadernium-1, a common preservative in contact lens solutions and tear replacement products.

Treatment

Treatment of HSV disease is predominantly with nucleoside
(purine or pyrimidine) analogues that disrupt viral DNA. оАЯe
majority of dendritic ulcers will eventually heal spontaneously
without treatment, though scarring and vascularization may be
more signiоАЭcant.
оАБ Topical. оАЯe most frequently used drugs are aciclovir 3% ointment or ganciclovir 0.15% gel, each administered оАЭve times
daily. TriоАЮuridine is an alternative, but requires instillation up
to nine times daily until the epithelium closes and then оАЭve
times daily. оАЯe drugs are relatively non-toxic, even when
given for up to 60 days. оАЯey have approximately equivalent
eоАЬect, acting preferentially on virus-laden epithelial cells and
penetrating eоАЬectively into the stroma; 99% of ulcers heal
within 2 weeks. Idoxuridine and vidarabine are older drugs
that are less eоАЬective and are toxic to the corneal epithelium.
оАБ Debridement may be used for resistant cases. оАЯe corneal surface is wiped with a sterile cellulose sponge or cotton-tipped
applicator (cotton bud). Epithelium should be removed 2 mm
beyond the edge of the ulcer, since involvement extends beyond
the visible dendrite. оАЯe removal of the virus-containing cells
protects adjacent healthy epithelium from infection and eliminates the antigenic stimulus to stromal inоАЮammation. A topical antiviral agent should be used in conjunction.
оАБ Signs of treatment toxicity include superоАЭcial punctate erosions, waves of whorled epithelium, follicular conjunctivitis
and, rarely, punctal occlusion. Absence of epithelial whorling
with a persistent epithelial lesion raises the possibility of poor
or non-compliance.
оАБ Oral antiviral therapy (e.g. aciclovir 200вАУ400 mg оАЭve times a
day for 5вАУ10 days, famciclovir or valaciclovir) is indicated in
most immunodeоАЭcient patients, in children and patients with
marked ocular surface disease. It is an eоАЬective alternative
to topical treatment when the latter is poorly tolerated, or in
resistant cases. оАЯe newer oral agents may be better tolerated
than aciclovir and require less frequent dosing, but optimal
regimens are not yet deоАЭned.
оАБ Interferon monotherapy does not seem to be more eоАЬective
than antivirals, but the combination of a nucleoside antiviral
with either interferon or debridement seems to speed healing.
оАБ Skin lesions (see Ch. 2) may be treated with aciclovir cream
оАЭve times daily, as for cold sores, and if extensive an oral antiviral may be given.
оАБ Cycloplegia, e.g. homatropine 1% once or twice daily can be
given to improve comfort if necessary.

CHAPTER

Cornea 223
#### 7

оАБ Topical antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended by some
practitioners.
оАБ Prostaglandin derivatives should be avoided if treatment is
required to control the IOP, as these drops promote herpes
virus activity.
оАБ Topical steroids are not used unless signiоАЭcant disciform keratitis is also present (see below).
оАБ Slow healing or frequent recurrence may indicate the presence of a resistant viral strain and an alternative topical agent
or debridement may be tried. In especially refractory cases,
a combination of two topical agents with oral valaciclovir or
famciclovir may be eоАЬective. A signiоАЭcant minority of resistant
cases are due to varicella-zoster virus.

TIP Topical steroids should not be used in epithelial herpes
keratitis as this can result in worsening of the ulceration and
may lead to corneal perforation.

Disciform keratitis

оАЯe aetiology of disciform keratitis (endotheliitis) is unclear. It may
be the result of active HSV infection of keratocytes or endothelium, or a hypersensitivity reaction to viral antigen in the cornea.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Blurred vision of gradual onset, which may be
associated with haloes around lights. Discomfort and redness
are common, but tend to be milder than in purely epithelial disease. A clear past history of epithelial ulceration is not always
present and the possibility of a mimicking infection such as
acanthamoeba or fungal keratitis should be borne in mind.
оАБ Signs

{ A central zone of stromal oedema, oоАЫen with overlying
epithelial oedema (Fig. 7.13A and B). Occasionally the
lesion is eccentric.

{ Large (granulomatous) keratic precipitates underlying the
oedema.

{ Folds in Descemet membrane in severe cases.

{ A surrounding (Wessely) immune ring of deep stromal
haze (Fig. 7.13C) signiоАЭes deposition of viral antigen and
host antibody complexes.

{ оАЯe IOP may be elevated.

{ Reduced corneal sensation. оАЯis may aid in distinguishing
other forms of infection.

{ Healed lesions oоАЫen have a faint ring of stromal or subepithelial opaciоАЭcation and thinning.

{ Consecutive episodes may be associated with gradually worsening subepithelial and/or stromal scarring and
superоАЭcial or deep vascularization (Fig. 7.13D).

{ Mid-stromal scarring from disciform keratitis is a cause of
interstitial keratitis.

Treatment

A broad approach to management is set out below, but regimens
should be tailored individually. Careful monitoring and adequate

treatment, dependent on severity of inоАЮammation, is critical to
minimize progression of scarring. Patients should be cautioned to
seek treatment at the оАЭrst suggestion of recurrence, though some
authorities feel that minimal inоАЮammation may not warrant treatment or can be addressed with cycloplegia alone.
оАБ Initial treatment is with topical steroids (prednisolone 1% or
dexamethasone 0.1%) with antiviral cover, both four times
daily. As improvement occurs, the frequency of administration of both is reduced in parallel over not less than 4 weeks.
It is prudent to keep steroid intensity and duration to the
minimum required for eоАЬective control of inоАЮammation. IOP
should be monitored. Cycloplegia can be used to improve
comfort if necessary.
оАБ Subsequently prednisolone 0.5% once daily is usually a safe
dose at which to stop topical antiviral cover. Some patients
require a weaker steroid such as оАЮuorometholone 0.1% or
loteprednol 0.2% on alternate days for many months. Periodic
attempts should be made to stop the steroid altogether.
оАБ With active epithelial ulceration it is reasonable to try to keep
the steroid intensity as low as possible for adequate eоАЬect, with

a more frequent antiviral regimen, e.g. initially topical antiviral
оАЭve times daily, with steroid two or three times daily, titrated
according to the signs of activity of both. Oral antiviral treatment may be helpful but its eоАЪcacy in this situation has not
been established.
оАБ Oral steroids are sometimes used in severe stromal inоАЮammation as an adjunct.
оАБ Topical ciclosporin 0.05% may be useful, particularly in the
presence of epithelial ulceration and to facilitate tapering of
topical steroids such as in steroid-related IOP elevation.
оАБ Fine needle diathermy and laser techniques have been reported
as successfully addressing established corneal neovascularization and improving vision.

Necrotizing stromal keratitis

оАЯis rare condition is thought to result from active viral replication within the stroma, though immune-mediated inоАЮammation is
likely to play a signiоАЭcant role. It may be diоАЪcult to distinguish from
severe disciform keratitis and there may be a spectrum of disease,

including overlap with neurotrophic keratopathy. As with disciform
keratitis, a similar clinical picture may be caused by other infections.
оАБ Signs

{ Stromal necrosis and melting, oоАЫen with profound interstitial opaciоАЭcation (Fig. 7.14A).

{ Anterior uveitis with keratic precipitates underlying the
area of active stromal inоАЭltration.

{ An epithelial defect may be present.

{ Progression to scarring, vascularization (Fig. 7.14B) and
lipid deposition is common (see Fig. 7.3C)
оАБ Treatment is broadly similar to that of aggressive disciform
keratitis, but oral antiviral supplementation, initially at the
upper end of the dose range, is commonly used. оАЯe restoration of epithelial integrity is critical.

Neurotrophic keratopathy

Neurotrophic keratopathy (see also separate topic) is caused by
failure of re-epithelialization resulting from corneal anaesthesia,
oоАЫen exacerbated by other factors such as drug toxicity.
оАБ Signs

{ A non-healing epithelial defect is an early sign, sometimes
aоАЫer prolonged topical treatment (see Fig. 7.31A).

{ оАЯe stroma beneath the defect is grey and opaque and may
become thin.

#### 7

elevation is common and is oоАЫen presumed to be due to trabeculitis. However, steroid-induced IOP elevation may also be relatively
common in herpetic iritis. оАЯe aetiology may be missed unless
there is a history of previous herpes simplex keratitis. Patchy iris
atrophy (Fig. 7.15A) may provide a clue and transillumination
(Fig. 7.15B) may demonstrate subtle lesions. Aqueous sampling
for PCR is diagnostic. Treatment is primarily with topical steroids,
but adjunctive oral aciclovir may be given.

Other considerations
Prophylaxis

оАБ Long-term oral aciclovir reduces the rate of recurrence of
epithelial and stromal keratitis by about 50% and is usually
tolerated well. Prophylaxis should be considered in patients
with frequent debilitating recurrences, particularly if bilateral or involving an only eye. оАЯe standard daily dose of aciclovir is 400 mg twice daily for 12 months, but if necessary,
a higher dose can be tried, based on practice in the management of systemic herpes simplex infection. Continual use for
many years has been documented for systemic indications,
but it can result in aciclovir-resistant HSV-1 viruses. оАЯe
prophylactic eоАЬect decreases or disappears when the drug is
stopped. Excretion is via the kidney, so renal function should
be checked periodically during long-term treatment.
оАБ Oral valaciclovir (500 mg once daily) or famciclovir are alternatives that are probably as eоАЬective as aciclovir, require less
frequent dosing and may be better tolerated.
оАБ Topical. Oral prophylaxis tends to be preferred to long-term
topical administration as epithelial toxicity may occur, leading
to mild blurring and persistent discomfort. Allergy and punctal stenosis are also potential problems.
оАБ Vaccination. оАЯerapeutic vaccination strategies are under
investigation.

{ Secondary bacterial or fungal infection may occur.
оАБ Treatment is that of persistent epithelial defects. Topical steroids to control any inоАЮammatory component should be kept
to a minimum.

Iridocyclitis

Herpetic iridocyclitis can occur without signs of active corneal
inоАЮammation and may be associated with direct viral activity. IOP

Complications

оАБ Secondary infection. Herpetic eye disease is a major predisposing factor for microbial keratitis.
оАБ Glaucoma secondary to inflammation or chronic steroid
use may progress undetected, particularly if there is a poor
view of the optic disc. Corneal thinning and distortion
may give rise to an inaccurate reading on applanation and
alternative forms of tonometry may be superior in these
cases.
оАБ Stromal vascularization and lipid keratopathy. Topical
aganirsen results in a signiоАЭcant reduction in corneal vessels
and an improvement in the quality of life, but no signiоАЭcant
improvement in visual acuity is likely to follow.
оАБ Cataract secondary to inоАЮammation or prolonged steroid use.
оАБ Iris atrophy secondary to kerato-uveitis (see Fig. 7.15).

Keratoplasty

A trial of a rigid contact lens is oоАЫen worthwhile prior to committing to surgery. Recurrence of herpetic eye disease and rejection
are common and threaten the survival of corneal graоАЫs.
оАБ Topical antivirals given during a rejection episode may
reduce epithelial viral reactivation but toxicity may delay
re-epithelialization.
оАБ Prophylactic oral aciclovir (400 mg twice daily) improves
graоАЫ survival and should be given to patients undergoing penetrating keratoplasty for herpetic eye disease. It should also be
considered in patients with severe atopic eye disease but no
history of ocular HSV involvement. оАЯe duration of treatment
and the optimum dose has not been established. Immunohistochemistry should be performed on the excised tissue to conоАЭrm the presence of herpes antigen.

HERPES ZOSTER OPHTHALMICUS

Introduction

Herpes zoster is common, and it has been estimated that one in
three people will develop the condition in their lifetime. Most
patients are not immunocompromised.

Pathogenesis

Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO) is the term used for shingles
involving the dermatome supplied by the ophthalmic division of the
оАЭоАЫh cranial (trigeminal) nerve. Ocular involvement can also occur
(though is rarely clinically signiоАЭcant) when the disease aоАЬects the
maxillary division alone. Varicella-zoster virus (VZV) causes both
chickenpox (varicella) and shingles (herpes zoster). VZV belongs to
the same subfamily of the herpes virus group as HSV вАУ the viruses
are morphologically identical, but antigenically distinct. AоАЫer an
episode of chickenpox, the virus travels in a retrograde manner
to the dorsal root and cranial nerve sensory ganglia, where it may
remain dormant for decades, with reactivation thought to occur
aоАЫer VZV-speciоАЭc cell-mediated immunity has faded. Re-exposure
to VZV via contact with chickenpox, or by vaccination, may reinforce immunity and protect against the development of shingles.

Mechanisms of ocular involvement

оАБ Direct viral invasion may lead to conjunctivitis and epithelial
keratitis.
оАБ Secondary inоАЬammation and occlusive vasculitis may cause
episcleritis, scleritis, keratitis, uveitis (including segmental iris
infarction), optic neuritis and cranial nerve palsies. InоАЮammation and destruction of the peripheral nerves or central ganglia, or altered signal processing in the central nervous system
(CNS) may be responsible for post-herpetic neuralgia. Cicatrizing complications may arise following severe eyelid, periocular skin and conjunctival involvement.

оАБ Reactivation causes necrosis and inоАЮammation in the aоАЬected
sensory ganglia, causing corneal anaesthesia that may result in
neurotrophic keratopathy.

Risk of ocular involvement

оАБ Hutchinson sign describes vesicles in the skin supplied by the
external nasal nerve, a branch of the nasociliary nerve supplying the tip, side and root of the nose and ophthalmic herpes
zoster (Fig. 7.16A). Contrary to previous teaching, HutchinsonвАЩs sign may not be predictive of ocular involvement, as
both false-positive and false-negative оАЭndings are common.
However, a rash in the distribution of the supratrochlear nerve
(lower part of the forehead) is predictive. оАЯere is no apparent
correlation between the severity of the rash and that of ocular
complications.
оАБ Age. HZO occurs most frequently in the sixth and seventh
decades. In the elderly, signs and symptoms tend to be more
severe than in the young and of longer duration.
оАБ AIDS patients tend to have severe disease, and shingles can be
an early indicator of human immunodeоАЭciency virus (HIV)
infection. A lower threshold for HIV testing should be adopted
in populations at particular risk. оАЯe development of shingles
in children or young adults classically has prompted a search
for immunodeоАЭciency or malignancy, though the requirement
for this has been questioned as an abnormality will be found in
only a small minority.

TIP All patients with herpes zoster involving the frontal nerve
(V1) should be fully evaluated for ocular involvement.

Acute shingles
General features

оАБ A prodromal phase precedes the appearance of the rash. It
lasts 3вАУ5 days and is characterized by tiredness, fever, malaise
and headache. Symptoms involving the aоАЬected dermatome
vary from a superоАЭcial itching, tingling or burning sensation
to a severe boring or lancing pain that is either constant or
intermittent. Older patients with early severe pain and a larger
area of involvement are at particular risk of post-herpetic
neuralgia.
оАБ Skin lesions

{ Painful erythematous areas with a maculopapular rash
develop (Fig. 7.16B) and may be confused with cellulitis or
contact dermatitis.

{ оАЯe rash respects the midline, which may aid in distinguishing shingles from HSV skin infection. Pain is also
markedly worse in shingles. Bilateral disease is very rare.

{ Within 24 hours, groups of vesicles (Fig. 7.16C) appear
and these become conоАЮuent over 2вАУ4 days.

{ Although the rash itself does not aоАЬect the lower eyelid in
HZO, boggy oedema of the upper and lower lids is common (see Fig. 7.16B) and oоАЫen spreads to the contralateral
side of the face (particularly if the patient sleeps on the side
opposite to the infection).

CHAPTER

Cornea 227
#### 7

{ оАЯe vesicles oоАЫen pass through a pustular phase before
they crust (Fig. 7.16D) and dry aоАЫer 2вАУ3 weeks.

{ Large, deep haemorrhagic lesions are more common in
immunodeоАЭcient patients (Fig. 7.16E).

{ оАЯe lesions heal to leave residual skin destruction and
depigmented scars (Fig. 7.16F).

{ Zoster sine herpete is shingles without a rash and may be
more common than previously realized.
оАБ Disseminated zoster involving multiple dermatomes and
organ systems may develop in immunodeоАЭciency or malignancy and with the advent of PCR testing, complications such
as meningoencephalitis have increasingly been identiоАЭed in
immunocompetent individuals.
оАБ Investigation. In the event that clinical diagnosis is uncertain,
typically in immunodeоАЭciency, vesicular оАЮuid can be sent for
PCR, or immunomicroscopy. A viraemia lasting a few days
occurs in acute shingles. PCR of plasma for VZV DNA may be
positive (40%), especially in immunosuppressed patients and
can be particularly useful if zoster sine herpete is suspected.
IgM antibodies to VZV are found in only a minority of early
stage and convalescent patients.

Treatment

оАБ Oral antiviral treatment, optimally given within 72 hours of
rash onset, reduces the severity and duration of the acute episode and the risk of post-herpetic neuralgia. оАЯe incidence of
late ophthalmic complications is also reduced by about 50%.
Patients presenting later than 72 hours but still at the vesicular
stage also derive beneоАЭt from treatment. Aciclovir (800 mg оАЭve
times daily for 7вАУ10 days) has been the mainstay of treatment,
but newer agents such as valaciclovir 1 g three times daily or
famciclovir 250вАУ500 mg three times daily have more convenient regimens, are better tolerated and are at least as eоАЬective as aciclovir. Brivudine is available in some countries. Fatal
interactions with 5-оАЮuoropyrimidines have been reported
and it should not be used in combination with even regional
5-оАЮuorouracil.
оАБ Intravenous aciclovir 5вАУ10 mg/kg three times daily is generally indicated only for severe disease, particularly encephalitis
and for moderateвАУsevere immunocompromise.
оАБ Systemic steroids (e.g. prednisolone 60 mg daily for 4 days,
then 40 mg for 4 days, then 20 mg for 4 days) remain somewhat controversial but are commonly used in moderateвАУsevere
disease, particularly for neurological complications. оАЯey
should be given only in conjunction with a systemic antiviral and should probably be avoided in immunodeоАЭciency. A
moderate reduction in acute pain and accelerated skin healing
is conferred, but steroids have no eоАЬect on the incidence or
severity of post-herpetic neuralgia.
оАБ Immunocompromised patients require the input of an infectious disease specialist. Antiviral treatment should be extended
and intravenous treatment may be optimal. Systemic steroids
should probably be avoided.
оАБ Symptomatic treatment of skin lesions is by drying, antisepsis
and cold compresses. оАЯe beneоАЭt of topical antibiotic-steroid
combinations is uncertain.

Prevention

Vaccination against herpes zoster reduces the incidence of HZO.
Two vaccines are available and both are approved for individuals
over the age of 50 years.
оАБ Zoster vaccine live is an attenuated live virus with an important limitation that eоАЪcacy is lost within 10 years.
оАБ Recombinant zoster vaccine requires two injections and has a
10% incidence of local or acute systemic reaction.

Eye disease
Acute eye disease

оАБ Acute epithelial keratitis develops in over 50% of patients
within 2 days of the onset of the rash and usually resolves

spontaneously within a few days. It is characterized by dendritic lesions that are smaller and оАЭner than herpes simplex
dendrites and have tapered ends without terminal bulbs (Fig.
7.17A). оАЯe lesions stain better with rose Bengal than with
оАЮuorescein (Fig. 7.17B). Treatment, if required, is with a topical antiviral.
оАБ Conjunctivitis (follicular and/or papillary) is common and
oоАЫen occurs in conjunction with lid margin vesicles. Treatment is not required in the absence of corneal disease, though
some practitioners give topical antibiotic and/or antiviral
prophylaxis.
оАБ Episcleritis occurs at the onset of the rash and usually resolves
spontaneously. A mild non-steroidal anti-inоАЮammatory may
be used if necessary.
оАБ Scleritis and sclerokeratitis are uncommon but may develop at
the end of the оАЭrst week. Treatment of indolent lesions is with
oral оАЮurbiprofen 100 mg three times daily. Oral steroids with
antiviral cover may be required for severe involvement.
оАБ Nummular keratitis usually develops at the site of epithelial lesions about 10 days aоАЫer the onset of the rash. It is

оАБ Stromal (interstitial) keratitis (Fig. 7.17D) develops in about

оАБ Disciform keratitis (immune-mediated endotheliitis) is less

оАБ Posterior uveitis (see Ch. 12). Progressive retinal necrosis is

Posterior segment examination should always be performed
in patients with HZO as retinal vasculitis may occasionally
occur.
оАБ IOP should be monitored as elevation is common, including
steroid-induced. Prostaglandin derivatives should be avoided
if treatment is necessary.
оАБ Neurological complications may require intravenous antivirals and systemic steroids.

be the only diagnostic clue. Reactivation of keratitis, episcleritis,

PostоАЯherpetic neuralgia

Post-herpetic neuralgia is deоАЭned as pain that persists for more
than one month aоАЫer the rash has healed. It develops in up to 75%
of patients over 70 years of age. Pain may be constant or intermittent, worse at night and aggravated by minor stimuli (allodynia),
touch and heat. It generally improves slowly over time, with only
2% of patients aоАЬected aоАЫer 5 years. Neuralgia can impair the quality of life and may lead to depression of suоАЪcient severity to present
a danger of suicide. Patients severely aоАЬected should be referred to
a specialist pain clinic. Treatment may involve the following:
оАБ Local

{ Cold compresses.

{ Topical capsaicin 0.075% or lidocaine 5% patches.
оАБ Systemic treatment may be used in a staged fashion:

{ Simple analgesics such as paracetamol.

{ Stronger analgesics such as codeine.

{ Tricyclic antidepressants, e.g. nortriptyline, amitriptyline,
initially 25 mg nightly adjusted up to 75 mg for several
weeks if necessary.

{ Carbamazepine 400 mg daily for lancinating pain.

{ Cranial nerve palsy aоАЬecting the third (most common),
fourth and sixth nerves usually recovers within 6 months.

{ Gabapentin (300вАУ600 mg up to three times daily),
sustained-release oxycodone (10вАУ30 mg twice daily), or
both.

{ Optic neuritis is rare.

{ CNS manifestations are rare but include encephalitis, cranial arteritis and GuillainвАУBarr√© syndrome.

INTERSTITIAL KERATITIS

Introduction

Interstitial keratitis (IK) is an inоАЮammation of the corneal stroma
without primary involvement of the epithelium or endothelium.
In most cases, the inоАЮammation is thought to be an immunemediated process triggered by an appropriate antigen. оАЯe term
is most commonly used to refer to the late appearance of feathery mid-stromal scarring with ghost vessels rather than the acute
presentation. оАЯe former is typically an incidental оАЭnding. Syphilitic IK is the archetype, but the relative frequency of causes varies
markedly by geographic region. оАЯere is a wide range of causes,
including herpes simplex, varicella zoster and other viral infections, tuberculosis, Lyme disease and other infections (parasitic
diseases are an important cause in areas where these are endemic),
sarcoidosis, Cogan syndrome and non-infectious inоАЮammatory
conditions.

Syphilitic IK

IK of syphilitic origin is usually the result of congenital infection,
though acquired syphilis can also be responsible. All patients with
acute IK, or with the incidental discovery of its chronic appearance, should be investigated to exclude congenital (and acquired)
syphilis, irrespective of the presence of systemic clinical signs.
оАЯere are numerous reported cases of congenital syphilis being
identiоАЭed for the оАЭrst time in later life on this basis.

Chronic eye disease

оАБ Neurotrophic keratopathy similar to that seen in HSV infection develops in up to 50%, but is usually relatively mild and
settles over several months. Prolonged severe disease occurs in
a minority (see also separate topic).
оАБ Scleritis may become chronic and lead to patchy scleral atrophy (Fig. 7.18D). Staphyloma formation is rare (Fig. 7.18E).
оАБ Mucous plaque keratitis develops in about 5%, most commonly between the third and sixth months. It is characterized by elevated mucous plaques staining with rose
Bengal (Fig. 7.18F). Treatment involves a combination of topical
steroid and acetylcysteine. Untreated, plaques resolve aоАЫer a
few months, leaving a faint diоАЬuse corneal haze.
оАБ Lipid degeneration may develop in eyes with persistent severe
nummular or disciform keratitis (see Fig. 7.18A).
оАБ Lipid-оАЮlled granulomas similar to those resulting from
chronic irritation may develop in the tarsal conjunctiva and
may progress to erosive calciоАЭed concretions.
оАБ Subconjunctival scarring may occur.
оАБ Eyelid scarring may result in ptosis, cicatricial entropion and
occasionally ectropion, trichiasis, lid notching and madarosis.

Relapsing eye disease

In the relapsing phase lesions may reappear years aоАЫer an acute
episode, which may have been forgotten and eyelid scarring may

232 Interstitial Keratitis

Congenital syphilis

Infection of the fetus can occur across the placenta, leading to stillbirth, subclinical infection, or a range of clinical features.
оАБ Early systemic features include failure to thrive, a maculopapular rash, mucosal ulcers, characteristic оАЭssures around the
lips (rhagades) and a range of organ involvement.
оАБ Late systemic signs include sensorineural deafness, saddleshaped nasal deformity (Fig. 7.19A), sabre tibiae (Fig. 7.19B),
bulldog jaw (mandibular prominence due to maxillary underdevelopment), Hutchinson teeth (notched, small, widely
spaced teeth вАУ Fig. 7.19C) and Clutton joints (painless eоАЬusions in large joints, especially the knees).
оАБ Ocular features include anterior uveitis, IK (see below), dislocated/subluxated lens, cataract, optic atrophy, salt and pepper pigmentary retinopathy (Fig. 7.20) and Argyll Robertson
pupils.

Presentation of syphilitic IK

оАБ Symptoms. оАЯe presentation of IK following congenital
syphilitic infection is usually between the ages of 5 and 25
years. оАЯe initial symptoms are those of acute anterior uveitis with severe blurring. Involvement is bilateral in 80%,
although usually not simultaneous. In acquired disease IK
is less common and usually unilateral, typically occurring
years aоАЫer the age at which the disease was contracted,
although it can occur as part of the syndrome of primary
infection.
оАБ Signs

{ Profoundly decreased visual acuity is typical in the active
stage.

{ Limbitis associated with deep stromal vascularization,
with cellular inоАЭltration and clouding that may obscure
the still-perfused vessels to give the characteristic pinkish
вАШsalmon patchвАЩ appearance (Fig. 7.21A).

{ Granulomatous anterior uveitis.

Cogan syndrome
Introduction

Cogan syndrome is a rare systemic autoimmune vasculitis characterized by intraocular inоАЮammation and vestibuloauditory dysfunction (particularly neurosensory) developing within months of
each other. оАЯe disease primarily occurs in young adults, with both

Diagnosis

Ocular and inner ear symptoms are oоАЫen separated by a substantial period; the acute phase may last from months to years. Susac
syndrome (retinocochleocerebral vasculopathy) should be considered in the diоАЬerential diagnosis.
оАБ Vestibuloauditory symptoms. Deafness, tinnitus and vertigo.
оАБ Ocular symptoms. Redness, pain, photophobia and blurred
vision.
оАБ Ocular signs. Corneal involvement commences with faint bilateral peripheral anterior stromal opacities. Deeper opacities and
corneal neovascularization (mid-stromal vascular loops) then
ensue (Fig. 7.22A), oоАЫen with central progression (Fig. 7.22B).
Uveitis, scleritis and retinal vasculitis may develop.
оАБ Investigations

Fig. 7.20 Salt and pepper retinopathy following congenital
syphilitic infection.

{ Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) may be elevated and the white cell count raised.

{ MRI may show inner ear and other abnormalities.

Treatment

оАБ Topical steroids for keratitis, with additional measures as

or retinal vasculitis.

PROTOZOAN KERATITIS

Acanthamoeba
Introduction

Acanthamoeba spp. are ubiquitous free-living protozoa commonly
found in soil, fresh or brackish water and the upper respiratory
tract. оАЯe cystic form (Fig. 7.23A) is highly resilient. Under appropriate environmental conditions, the cysts turn into trophozoites,
with tissue penetration and destruction. In developed countries
acanthamoeba keratitis is most frequently associated with contact
lens wear, especially if tap water is used for rinsing.

Diagnosis

оАЯe condition is oоАЫen diagnosed initially as herpes simplex keratitis. With more advanced signs the condition should be distinguished from fungal keratitis.
оАБ Symptoms. Blurred vision and discomfort. Pain is oоАЫen severe
and characteristically disproportionate to the clinical signs.
оАБ Signs

{ Gradual enlargement and coalescence of inоАЭltrates to form
a ring abscess (Fig. 7.23D and E) is typical.

{ Scleritis may develop and is generally reactive rather than
an extension of infection.

{ Slowly progressive stromal opaciоАЭcation and
vascularization.

{ Corneal melting may occur at any stage when there is stromal disease. оАЯe melt oоАЫen develops at the periphery of the
area of inоАЭltrate (Fig. 7.23F).
оАБ Investigations

{ Staining of corneal scrapings using periodic acidвАУSchiоАЬ
or calcoоАЮuor white (a оАЮuorescent dye with an aоАЪnity for
amoebic cysts and fungi). Gram and Giemsa stains may
also demonstrate cysts.

{ Culture. Non-nutrient agar seeded with dead E. coli, which
trophozoites consume.

{ Other investigations include immunohistochemistry, PCR
and inоАЯvivo confocal microscopy. Corneal biopsy may be
necessary for diagnosis.

{ In early disease the epithelial surface is irregular and greyish (Fig. 7.23B).

{ Epithelial pseudodendrites resembling herpetic lesions
may form.

{ Limbitis with diоАЬuse or focal anterior stromal inоАЭltrates.

{ Characteristic perineural inоАЭltrates (radial keratoneuritis) are seen during the оАЭrst few weeks and are virtually
pathognomonic (Fig. 7.23C).

TIP In a patient with acanthamoeba keratitis a good visual
outcome is achieved by making a speedy diagnosis, avoiding
a misdiagnosis of herpes keratitis and avoiding the use of
corticosteroids before commencing speciоАЯc treatment.

Prevention

Teach contact lens wearers how to clean their contact lenses.
Reduce their risk of contracting this disease by warning them that
they should not use tap water to clean their lenses.

Treatment

оАБ Early treatment. It is important to maintain a high index
of suspicion for Acanthamoeba in any patient with a limited
response to antibacterial therapy. оАЯe outcome is very much
better if treatment is started early.
оАБ Debridement of involved epithelium is believed to be helpful,
as it may facilitate eye drop penetration.

{ Simultaneous antibacterial treatment for co-infection may

the blood vessels) with a secondary lymphocytic inоАЭltration. оАЯe
lesions are culture-negative but S. aureus can frequently be isolated
from the lid margins.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Mild discomfort, redness and lacrimation. оАЯe
inоАЮammation may involve both eyes.
оАБ Signs

{ Inferior punctate epitheliopathy is an early manifestation.

{ Subepithelial marginal inоАЭltrates separated from the limbus by a clear zone, oоАЫen associated with an adjacent area
of conjunctival hyperaemia (Fig. 7.24A).

{ Characteristically, any epithelial defect will be considerably smaller than the area of inоАЭltrate.

{ Coalescence and circumferential spread (Fig. 7.24B and C).

{ Usually little or no anterior chamber reaction, even with
large inоАЭltrates.

{ Without treatment, resolution generally occurs in 1вАУ4
weeks, depending on severity. Occasionally there is residual superоАЭcial scarring and slight thinning with mild
pannus (Fig. 7.24D). Iris new vessels may develop in the

{ Chronic blepharitis is typical.

{ Oral miltefosine can be considered in patients where there has

inоАЮammation, which oоАЫen occurs on this treatment, responds
to steroid eyedrops. It should not be used in pregnancy.
оАБ Topical steroids should be avoided if possible although lowdose therapy delayed for at least 2 weeks aоАЫer starting antiamoebic treatment may be useful for persistent inоАЮammation.
Amoebicidal treatment should be continued in concert with
and for several weeks aоАЫer steroids.
оАБ Pain control is with an oral non-steroidal anti-inоАЮammatory
agent.
оАБ Corneal cross-linking treatment (PACK-CXL) can be considered
in severe cases, but two sessions of treatment are oоАЫen needed.

including perforation. Late scarring may also require penetrating keratoplasty.

TIP Acanthamoeba infection of the cornea results in pain that
is often severe and disproportionate to the clinical signs.

HELMINTHIC KERATITIS

Onchocerciasis

Onchocerciasis (вАШriver blindnessвАЩ) is discussed in Chapter 12. Keratitis is a common feature.

BACTERIAL HYPERSENSITIVITYMEDIATED CORNEAL DISEASE

Marginal keratitis
Introduction

Marginal keratitis is believed to be caused by a hypersensitivity
reaction against staphylococcal exotoxins and cell wall proteins

Treatment

A weak topical steroid such as оАЮuorometholone or prednisolone
0.5% is instilled four times daily for 1вАУ2 weeks, sometimes combined with a topical antibiotic. An oral tetracycline course (erythromycin in children, breastfeeding mothers and in pregnancy) may
be required for recurrent disease. Blepharitis is treated as necessary.

Phlyctenulosis
Introduction

Phlyctenulosis is usually a self-limiting disease but may rarely be
severe. Most cases in high-income countries are the result of a presumed delayed hypersensitivity reaction to staphylococcal antigen,
sometimes associated with rosacea. In low-income countries, the
majority are associated with tuberculosis or helminthic infestation,
but causation may be uncertain and a range of other agents has
been implicated.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Photophobia, lacrimation and blepharospasm,
oоАЫen in a child or young adult.
оАБ Signs

{ A small white limbal (Fig. 7.25A) or conjunctival nodule
(Fig. 7.25B) associated with intense local hyperaemia.

{ A limbal phlycten may extend onto the cornea (Fig. 7.25C
and D).

{ Spontaneous resolution usually occurs within 2вАУ3 weeks.
A healed lesion oоАЫen leaves a triangular limbal-based scar
associated with superоАЭcial vascularization and thinning

{ Very large necrotizing or multiple (miliary) lesions may

оАБ Investigation for tuberculosis is generally indicated only in

Treatment

A short course of topical steroid accelerates healing and is oоАЫen
given with a topical antibiotic. Recurrent troublesome disease may
require an oral tetracycline and it is important to treat associated
blepharitis.

ROSACEA

Introduction

Rosacea (acne rosacea) is a common chronic idiopathic dermatosis
involving the sun-exposed skin of the face and upper neck. Ocular
complications develop in 6вАУ18% of patients. Facial telangiectasia,
papule and pustule formation, rhinophyma and facial оАЮushing may

{ Focal or diоАЬuse corneal thinning in severe cases, usually in
the inferior cornea.

occur (Fig. 7.26A). In contrast to acne vulgaris, comedones (blackheads or whiteheads) are absent.
оАЯe aetiology is probably multifactorial and may involve vascular factors, together with an abnormal response to commensal
skin bacteria and Demodex follicular mites. Exacerbation by H.
pylori infection has been suspected.

cyst formation.

conjunctivitis, conjunctival granulomas and phlyctenulosis
may occur.

Introduction

Peripheral corneal ulceration/thinning, known as вАШperipheral
ulcerative keratitisвАЩ (PUK) when inоАЮammatory, refers to a presentation characterized by thinning and/or ulceration preferentially
aоАЬecting the peripheral rather than central cornea and spreading

around the margin. It should be noted that any cause of corneal

оАБ Marginal keratitis. оАЯis is discussed above.
оАБ Mooren ulcer.
оАБ Terrien marginal degeneration.
оАБ Dellen. Localized corneal disturbance associated with drying

Mooren ulcer
Introduction

Mooren ulcer is a rare autoimmune disease characterized by progressive circumferential peripheral stromal ulceration with later

central spread. It may represent a host reaction to calgranulin C,
a normal hidden antigen in corneal keratocytes. оАЯere are two
forms: the оАЭrst aоАЬects mainly older patients, oоАЫen in only one eye
and usually responds well to medical therapy. оАЯe second is more
aggressive and likely to need systemic immunosuppression, carries a poorer prognosis, may be bilateral and associated with severe
pain and tends to occur in younger patients, including widespread
reports in South Asian men. In at least some (usually milder) cases,
there is a precipitating corneal insult such as surgery or infection.
Associated systemic autoimmune disease and corneal infection
should always be ruled out.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Pain is prominent and may be severe. оАЯere is
photophobia and blurred vision.
оАБ Signs

{ Peripheral ulceration involving the superоАЭcial one-third
of the stroma, with variable epithelial loss. Several distinct
foci may be present and subsequently coalesce.

{ An undermined and inоАЭltrated leading edge is characteristic (Fig. 7.28A).

A B

Fig. 7.28 Mooren ulcer. (A) Local peripheral ulceration; (B) undermined and inоАЯltrated central
edge in advanced disease.

{ Limbitis may be present, but not scleritis, which aids in
distinguishing from systemic disease-associated PUK.

{ Progressive circumferential and central stromal thinning
(Fig. 7.28B).

{ Vascularization involving the bed of the ulcer up to its
leading edge but not beyond.

{ оАЯe healing stage is characterized by thinning, vascularization and scarring.

{ Iritis is not uncommon.
оАБ Complications include severe astigmatism, perforation following minor trauma (spontaneous perforation is rare), secondary bacterial infection, cataract and glaucoma.

TIP Peripheral ulcerative keratitis is caused by an infection
until proven otherwise. In the absence of an infection
investigate for systemic autoimmune disease.

Treatment

оАБ Topical steroids as frequently as hourly are combined with a
low-frequency prophylactic topical antibiotic. If an eоАЬective
response is seen, treatment is tapered over several months.
оАБ Topical ciclosporin (up to 2%) may be eоАЬective, but can take
weeks to exert a signiоАЭcant eоАЬect.
оАБ Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment is eоАЬective in controlling refractory cases.
оАБ Adjunctive topical therapy includes artiоАЭcial tears and collagenase inhibitors such as acetylcysteine 10вАУ20%.
оАБ Conjunctival resection, which may be combined with excision of necrotic tissue, is performed if there is no response to
topical steroids. оАЯe excised area should extend 4 mm back
from the limbus and 2 mm beyond the circumferential margins. Keratoepithelioplasty (suturing of a donor corneal lenticule onto the scleral bed) may be combined to produce a

physical barrier against conjunctival regrowth and further
melting. Steroids are continued postoperatively.
оАБ Systemic immunosuppression may be needed, including steroids for rapid eоАЬect, and should be instituted earlier for bilateral disease, or if involvement is advanced at оАЭrst examination.
Biological blockers show some promise, e.g. rituximab
оАБ Systemic collagenase inhibitors such as doxycycline may be
beneоАЭcial.
оАБ Lamellar keratectomy involving dissection of the residual
central island in advanced disease may remove the stimulus
for further inоАЮammation.
оАБ Perforations. Management is as discussed earlier in this chapter.
оАБ Visual rehabilitation. Keratoplasty (with immunosuppressive
cover) may be considered once inоАЮammation has settled.

Peripheral ulcerative keratitis
associated with systemic autoimmune
disease
Introduction

PUK may precede or follow the onset of systemic features. Severe
peripheral corneal inоАЭltration (Fig.7.29A), ulceration (Fig. 7.29B)
or thinning unexplained by evident ocular disease should prompt
investigation for a (potentially life-threatening) systemic collagen vascular disorder. оАЯe mechanism includes immune complex
deposition in peripheral cornea, episcleral and conjunctival capillary occlusion with secondary cytokine release and inоАЮammatory
cell recruitment, the upregulation of collagenases and reduced
activity of their inhibitors. Systemic associations include:
оАБ Rheumatoid arthritis (RA вАУ the most common). PUK is bilateral in 30% and tends to occur in advanced RA (Fig.7.29BвАУD).
оАБ Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener granulomatosis)
is the second most common systemic association of PUK (Fig.
7.29 E and F). In contrast to RA, ocular complications are the
initial presentation in 50%.
оАБ Other conditions include polyarteritis nodosa, relapsing polychondritis, systemic lupus erythematosus and Crohn disease
(see Fig. 7.29A).

TIP Peripheral ulcerative keratitis in the absence of other
ocular disease should prompt investigation for a systemic
autoimmune disease.

Clinical features

оАБ Crescentic ulceration with an epithelial defect, thinning and
stromal inоАЭltration at the limbus. Spread is circumferential
and occasionally central. In contrast to Mooren ulcer, extension into the sclera may occur.
оАБ Limbitis, episcleritis or scleritis are usually present. As with
a Mooren ulcer, there is no separation between the ulcerative
process and the limbus.
оАБ Advanced disease may result in a вАШcontact lensвАЩ cornea (see Fig.
7.29C) or perforation.

CHAPTER

Cornea 241
#### 7

оАБ Rheumatoid paracentral ulcerative keratitis (PCUK) is
thought to be a distinct entity, with a punched-out more centrally located lesion with little inоАЭltrate in a quiet eye (see Fig.
7.29D). Perforation can occur rapidly and there is usually a
good response to topical ciclosporin, with bandage contact
lens and tissue glue application, if necessary, rather than systemic treatment.

Treatment

Treatment is principally with systemic immunosuppression in collaboration with a rheumatologist.
оАБ Systemic steroids, sometimes via pulsed intravenous administration, are used to control acute disease, with immunosuppressive therapy and biological blockers for longer-term
management.
оАБ Topical lubricants (preservative-free).
оАБ Topical antibiotics as prophylaxis if an epithelial defect is
present.
оАБ Oral tetracycline (e.g. doxycycline 100 mg once or twice
daily) for its anticollagenase eоАЬect.
оАБ Topical steroids may worsen thinning so are generally
avoided; relapsing polychondritis may be an exception.
оАБ Surgical management is generally as for Mooren ulcer,
including conjunctival excision if medical treatment is
ineоАЬective.

Terrien marginal degeneration

Terrien disease is an uncommon idiopathic thinning of the peripheral cornea occurring in young adult to elderly patients. Although
usually categorized as a degeneration, some cases are associated with episodic episcleritis or scleritis. About 75% of aоАЬected
patients are male and the condition is usually bilateral but may be
asymmetrical.

Diagnosis

{ Pseudopterygia sometimes develop (Fig. 7.30C).

оАБ Symptoms. оАЯe condition is commonly asymptomatic, but
gradual visual deterioration can occur due to astigmatism. A
few patients experience episodic pain and inоАЮammation.
оАБ Signs

{ Fine yellowвАУwhite refractile stromal opacities, frequently
associated with mild superоАЭcial vascularization, usually
start superiorly, spread circumferentially and are separated from the limbus by a clear zone. оАЯere is no epithelial defect and on cursory examination the condition may
resemble arcus senilis.

{ Slowly progressive circumferential thinning results in a
peripheral gutter, the outer slope of which shelves gradually, while the central part rises sharply (Fig. 7.30A). A
band of lipid is commonly present at the central edge (Fig.
7.30B).

{ Perforation is rare but may be spontaneous or follow blunt
trauma.

Treatment

оАБ Safety spectacles (e.g. polycarbonate) if thinning is signiоАЭcant.

lamellar (Fig. 7.30D) or full-thickness transplantation вАУ gives
reasonable results and may arrest progression.

NEUROTROPHIC KERATOPATHY

Introduction

Neurotrophic keratopathy occurs when there is loss of trigeminal
innervation to the cornea resulting in partial or complete anaesthesia. In addition to loss of the protective sensory stimulus, reduced
innervation results in intracellular oedema, exfoliation, loss of
goblet cells and epithelial breakdown with persistent ulceration.
Causes include trigeminal ganglion surgical ablation for neuralgia,

stroke, tumour, peripheral neuropathy (e.g. diabetes) and ocular
disease such as herpes simplex and herpes zoster keratitis (when
sensation loss may be sectoral).

Diagnosis

A full cranial nerve examination is mandatory.
оАБ Signs

{ Corneal sensation is reduced.

{ Stage 1: interpalpebral epithelial irregularity and staining,
with mild opaciоАЭcation, oedema and tiny focal defects.

{ Stage 2: larger persistent epithelial defect with rolled and
thickened edges (Fig. 7.31A), subsequently assuming
a punched-out conоАЭguration with underlying stromal
oedema.

{ Stage 3: stromal melting, oоАЫen with minimal discomfort.
Secondary infection may occur.

{ Perforation is uncommon but may occur rapidly, especially with secondary infection (Fig. 7.31B).

244 Exposure Keratopathy

A B

Fig. 7.31 Neurotrophic keratopathy. (A) Large epithelial defect; (B) perforation with iris
prolapse. (Courtesy of S Tuft вАУ оАЯg. A.)

Treatment

оАБ Discontinuation, if possible, of potentially toxic medications.
оАБ Topical lubricants (non-preserved) for associated dry eye or
corneal exposure. Topical insulin-like growth factor-1, substance P and neurogenic growth factor have been evaluated
but are not commercially available.
оАБ Anticollagenase agents: topical (e.g. acetylcysteine, tetracycline ointment) or systemic (e.g. tetracyclines).
оАБ Cenegermin 0.002%, a topical recombinant human nerve
growth factor, appears to be beneоАЭcial in moderate (persistent epithelial defect) or severe (corneal ulcer) neurotrophic
ulcer in adults. Instilled six times daily for 2 months, this
medication increases the corneal nerve density and facilitates healing. DiоАЬuse lamellar keratitis has been reported as
a late side eоАЬect in some patients. оАЯe long-term eоАЬectiveness is not known.
оАБ Protection of the ocular surface

branches (Fig. 7.32F). Sensation returns 6вАУ8 months aоАЫer the
surgery.

EXPOSURE KERATOPATHY

Introduction

Exposure keratopathy is the result of incomplete lid closure (lagophthalmos), with drying of the cornea despite normal tear production. Lagophthalmos may only be present on blinking or gentle
lid closure, with full forced lid closure. Causes include neuroparalytic, especially facial nerve palsy, reduced muscle tone as
in parkinsonism, mechanical such as lid scarring, eczematous
skin tightening and post-blepharoplasty and proptosis.

Diagnosis

MISCELLANEOUS KERATOPATHIES

Infectious crystalline keratopathy

Infectious crystalline keratopathy is a rare indolent infection usually occurring in a patient on long-term topical steroid therapy with
an associated epithelial defect, most frequently following penetrating keratoplasty. Streptococcus viridans is most commonly isolated,
although numerous other bacteria and fungi have been implicated.
Slowly progressive, greyвАУwhite, branching stromal opacities are
seen, associated with minimal inоАЮammation and usually intact overlying epithelium (Fig. 7.34). A bioоАЭlm is present that allows survival
of the microorganism, reduces antibiotic bioavailability and makes
diagnostic detection diоАЪcult. Culture or biopsy is performed to determine the organism and topical antibiotics instilled for several weeks.
Adjunctive therapies to enhance the eоАЪcacy of treatment include disruption of the microorganism bioоАЭlm by laser, intrastromal antibiotic
and keratectomy. In cases that do not respond to treatment or where
corneal scarring is present, corneal transplantation is required.

Thygeson superоАЮcial punctate keratitis

оАЯygeson superоАЭcial punctate keratitis is an uncommon idiopathic, usually bilateral, condition characterized by exacerbations
and remissions. It is commonly of onset in young adulthood but
can aоАЬect patients of any age and may recur over decades.

Treatment

оАБ Topical

{ Lubricants may suоАЪce in mild cases.

{ Steroids. A low-potency preparation is used twice daily
initially, with gradual tapering to as little as once-weekly
instillation. High-intensity treatment may sometimes be
needed initially.

{ Ciclosporin 0.05% is generally used if the response to
steroids is inadequate, or as an alternative in longer-term
therapy. However, some authorities recommend ciclosporin for initial treatment. Tacrolimus may also be eоАЬective.

{ Antivirals have not been found to be consistently helpful.
оАБ Contact lenses (extended wear or daily disposable soоАЫ) may
be considered if steroids are ineоАЬective or contraindicated, as
an alternative to ciclosporin.
оАБ Phototherapeutic keratectomy brings short-term relief but
recurrence is likely.

Filamentary keratopathy

Introduction

Filamentary keratopathy is a common condition that can
cause considerable discomfort. It is thought that a loose area of

оАБ Symptoms consist of discomfort with foreign body sensation,
redness and sometimes photophobia.

{ Strands of degenerated epithelial cells and mucus that
move with blinking and are typically attached to the cornea at one end (Fig. 7.36A).

{ Filaments stain well with rose Bengal (Fig. 7.36B) and to a
lesser extent with оАЮuorescein.

{ A small epithelial defect may be present at the base of a
оАЭlament.

Aqueous deоАЯciency (keratoconjunctivitis sicca)

Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis
Bullous keratopathy

Prolonged or frequent eye closure

оАБ Mechanical removal of оАЭlaments gives short-term symptomatic relief.
оАБ Mucolytics such as 5% or 10% acetylcysteine drops.
оАБ Non-steroidal anti-inоАЮammatory drops, e.g. diclofenac.
оАБ Hypertonic saline (5% drops four times daily, ointment at bedtime) may encourage adhesion of loose epithelium.
оАБ Bandage contact lenses may protect the cornea from the shearing action of the lids.

{ Chronic оАЭlaments may form plaques.

Treatment

оАБ Any underlying cause should be treated.
оАБ Topical medication should be changed if a toxic eоАЬect is suspected and unpreserved preparations used where possible.

Recurrent corneal epithelial erosion

Introduction

Recurrent corneal epithelial erosion is caused by an abnormally weak attachment between the basal cells of the corneal
epithelium and their basement membrane. Minor trauma, such
as eyelidвАУcornea interaction during sleep, can be sufficient to
precipitate detachment. Erosions may be associated with previous trauma or rarely corneal surgery and with some corneal
dystrophies. Intervals between episodes can be very variable,
even in the same patient, but may occur in spates over a short
period.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Severe pain, photophobia, redness, blepharospasm
and watering typically waken the patient during the night or

Fig. 7.37 Recurrent corneal erosion syndrome showing epithelial defect stained with оАЮuorescein.

are present on awaking in the morning. оАЯere is usually (but
not invariably) a prior history of corneal abrasion, sometimes
years previously, which may have been minor compared with
the recurrent symptoms.
оАБ Signs

{ An epithelial defect (Fig. 7.37) may not be present by the
time the patient is examined, as healing can oоАЫen be very
rapid (hours), but the extent of loose epithelium may be
highlighted by areas of pooling of оАЮuorescein and rapid
tear оАЭlm breakup.

{ InоАЭltrate should not be present, though greyish sloughed
and rolled epithelium may sometimes be reminiscent of
this.

{ оАЯere may be no sign of abnormality once a defect has
healed, but signs of epithelial basement membrane disturbance, such as microcysts, punctate or linear/оАЭngerprint opacities, are oоАЫen present. оАЯese will typically be
bilateral in a stromal dystrophy and unilateral if injury is
the cause.

Treatment

оАБ Acute symptoms

{ Antibiotic ointment four times daily and cyclopentolate
1% twice daily.

{ Pressure patching should not be used as it may impair
healing and does not improve comfort.

{ In severe cases a bandage contact lens alleviates pain but
may not improve healing. Antibiotic drops rather than
ointment should be used.

{ Debridement of heaped/scrolled areas of epithelium with
a sterile cellulose sponge or cotton-tipped applicator (cotton bud) may improve comfort and allow healing from the
edges of the defect.

{ Topical diclofenac 0.1% reduces pain.

CHAPTER

#### 7

{ Topical anaesthetic dramatically relieves pain but should
not be dispensed for patient use.

{ Hypertonic sodium chloride 5% drops four times daily and
ointment at bedtime may improve epithelial adhesion.

A World Health Organization (WHO) grading system is set out
in Table 7.7
оАБ Symptoms are night blindness (nyctalopia), discomfort and
loss of vision.
оАБ Conjunctiva

{ Following resolution, some authorities advise using a prophylactic topical lubricant such as carbomer gel three or
four times daily for several months.
оАБ Recurrent symptoms

{ Topical lubricant gel or ointment, or hypertonic saline
ointment, instilled at bedtime used long term may be
suоАЪcient.

{ A combination of oral doxycycline and topical steroid may be helpful. Both have been shown to inhibit
metalloproteinase inhibitors important to disease

{ Xerosis is characterized by dryness of the conjunctiva in

{ Bitot spots are triangular patches of foamy keratinized epithelium (Fig. 7.38A) in the interpalpebral zone thought to
be caused by Corynebacterium xerosis.

{ Lustreless appearance due to secondary xerosis.

{ Long-term extended-wear bandage contact lens.

{ Simple debridement of the epithelium in involved areas,
which may be followed by smoothing of Bowman layer
with a diamond burr or excimer laser. Most patients

axis. It may not be necessary to remove the epithelium to
facilitate this.

{ Sterile corneal melting by liquefactive necrosis (keratomalacia), which may lead to perforation (Fig. 7.38B).
оАБ Retinopathy, characterized by yellowish peripheral dots, may
occur in advanced cases and is associated with decreased electroretinogram amplitude.

Treatment

Keratomalacia is an indicator of very severe vitamin A deоАЭciency
and should be treated as a medical emergency due to the risk of
death, particularly in infants.

Xerophthalmia
Introduction

Vitamin A is essential for the maintenance of the bodyвАЩs epithelial surfaces, for immune function and for the synthesis of retinal
photoreceptor proteins. Xerophthalmia refers to the spectrum of
ocular disease caused by inadequate vitamin A intake and is a late
manifestation of severe deоАЭciency. Lack of vitamin A in the diet
may be caused by malnutrition, malabsorption, chronic alcoholism or by highly selective dieting. оАЯe risk in infants is increased
if their mothers are malnourished and by coexisting diarrhoea or
measles.

A B

Fig. 7.38 Xerophthalmia. (A) Bitot spot; (B) keratomalacia with corneal melting.

250 Corneal Ectasia

оАБ Systemic treatment involves oral (oil-based 200000 IU) or
intramuscular (aqueous-based 100000 IU) vitamin A for keratomalacia. Multivitamin supplements and dietary sources of
vitamin A are also administered.
оАБ Local treatment consists of intense lubrication, topical retinoic
acid and management of perforation.

CORNEAL ECTASIA

Keratoconus
Introduction

Keratoconus (KC) is a progressive disorder in which central or
paracentral corneal stromal thinning occurs, accompanied by
apical protrusion and irregular astigmatism. оАЯe prevalence is
0.1% to 0.2% and men are slightly more likely to be aоАЬected than
women. Presentation is commonly during the teens or twenties, with features initially in only one eye. Approximately 50%
of normal fellow eyes will progress to KC within 16 years. Both
eyes are aоАЬected eventually, at least on topographical imaging, in

{ Retinoscopy shows an irregular вАШscissoringвАЩ reоАЮex.

almost all cases. It can be graded by the highest axis of corneal
power on keratometry as mild (<48 D), moderate (48вАУ54 D) or
severe (>54 D). Most patients do not have a family history, with
only about 10% of oоАЬspring developing KC; autosomal dominant
transmission with incomplete penetrance has been proposed.
Risk factors include allergy, asthma, eczema and persistent eye
rubbing from any cause. Systemic associations include Down,
EhlersвАУDanlos and Marfan syndromes and osteogenesis imperfecta. Ocular associations include vernal keratoconjunctivitis, blue sclera, aniridia, Leber congenital amaurosis, retinitis
pigmentosa.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Unilateral impairment of vision due to progressive
myopia and astigmatism. Occasionally, initial presentation is
with acute hydrops (see below).
оАБ Signs

{ Direct ophthalmoscopy from a distance of half a metre
shows a fairly well delineated вАШoil dropletвАЩ reоАЮex (Fig. 7.39A).

A B

C D

Fig. 7.39 Keratoconus. (A) вАШOil dropletвАЩ red reоАЮex; (B) Vogt striae in the deep stroma (arrow);
(C) Fleischer ring demonstrated by cobalt blue light as a blue circle (arrow); (D) typical cone.
(Courtesy of R Fogla вАУ оАЯg. C.)

{ Slit lamp biomicroscopy shows very оАЭne, vertical, deep
stromal stress lines (Vogt striae вАУ Fig. 7.39B), which disappear with pressure on the globe.

{ Epithelial iron deposits, best seen with a cobalt blue оАЭlter, may
surround the base of the cone (Fleischer ring вАУ Fig. 7.39C).

{ Progressive corneal protrusion in a cone conоАЭguration
(Fig. 7.39D), with thinning maximal at the apex.

{ Bulging of the lower lid in downgaze (Munson sign) (Fig.
7.40A).

{ Acute hydrops is caused by a rupture in the stretched Descemet membrane that allows a sudden inоАЮux of aqueous
into the cornea (Fig. 7.40B and C), with accompanying pain,
photophobia and decreased vision. Although the break usually heals within 6вАУ10 weeks and the oedema clears, a variable amount of stromal scarring (Fig. 7.40D) may develop.
оАЯis sometimes gives improved vision by оАЮattening the
cornea. Acute episodes are initially treated with cycloplegia,
hypertonic (5%) saline ointment and patching or a soоАЫ bandage contact lens. Accelerated resolution has been reported
with intracameral gas injection in the acute stage.
оАБ Keratometry readings are steep.

CHAPTER

Cornea 251
#### 7

оАБ Corneal topography (video keratography) and various novel
corneal proоАЭling techniques are highly sensitive for detection
and essential in monitoring. Characteristically, astigmatism
progresses from a symmetrical bow-tie pattern, through an
asymmetrical appearance to an inferotemporally displaced steepsided cone (Fig. 7.41). Sometimes a central (вАШnippleвАЩ) cone may
develop. Contact lens warpage can sometimes appear similar to
a cone on topography, but is generally more arcuate-shaped.

Treatment

оАБ Eye rubbing should be avoided.
оАБ Spectacles or soоАЫ contact lenses are generally suоАЪcient in
early cases. If thinning is marked, it may be prudent to consider wearing safety spectacles over contact lenses.
оАБ Rigid contact lenses, sometimes scleral, are required for
higher degrees of astigmatism to provide a regular refracting
surface.
оАБ Corneal collagen cross-linking (CXL), using riboоАЮavin drops
to photosensitize the eye followed by exposure to ultraviolet-A
light, can stabilize the cornea if progression has been shown.
CXL is successful in more than 90% of cases and in some cases

can even reverse the ectasia. оАЯe complication rate is low, but
because the epithelium is removed at the time of surgery there
is a small risk of infection or corneal scarring. A bandage
contact lens should be used for 3 days aоАЫer the procedure to
reduce postoperative pain. It usually takes 1вАУ2 weeks for the
eye to settle.
оАБ Intracorneal ring segment implantation (Fig. 7.42) using
laser or mechanical channel creation is relatively safe and typically provides at least a moderate visual improvement, facilitating contact lens tolerance in advanced cases. It can be used
at the same time as CXL.
оАБ Keratoplasty, either penetrating or deep anterior lamellar
(DALK), may be necessary in patients with severe disease. A
history of hydrops is a contraindication to DALK due to the
presence of a Descemet membrane discontinuity. Outcomes
may be compromised by residual astigmatism and by anisometropia, necessitating contact lens correction for optimal acuity.
оАБ LASIK is contraindicated. Patients should be screened for KC
prior to corneal refractive surgery.

Pellucid marginal degeneration

Pellucid marginal degeneration is a rare progressive peripheral
corneal thinning disorder, typically involving the inferior cornea
in both eyes. Presentation is usually in adulthood.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Slowly progressive blurring due to astigmatism.
оАБ Signs

{ Bilateral, slowly progressive, crescentic 1вАУ2 mm band of
inferior corneal thinning extending from 4 to 8 oвАЩclock,
1 mm from the limbus (Fig. 7.43A).

{ оАЯe epithelium is intact and the cornea above the thinned
area shows ectasia and appears оАЮattened.

{ In contrast to keratoconus, Fleischer rings and Vogt striae
do not occur and acute hydrops is rare.
оАБ Corneal topography shows a вАШbutterоАЮyвАЩ pattern, with severe
astigmatism and diоАЬuse steepening of the inferior cornea (Fig.
7.43B).

A B

Fig. 7.43 (A) Pellucid marginal degeneration; (B) topography showing severe astigmatism and
diоАЭuse steepening of the inferior cornea.

Treatment

Early cases are managed with spectacles and contact lenses. Surgical options, none of which is ideal, in patients intolerant to
contact lenses include large eccentric penetrating keratoplasty,
thermocauterization, crescentic lamellar keratoplasty, wedge
resection of diseased tissue, epikeratoplasty and intracorneal
ring segment implantation. Results of collagen cross-linking are
encouraging.

Keratoglobus

Keratoglobus is an extremely rare condition that can be present at
birth when diоАЬerential diagnosis is from congenital glaucoma and
megalocornea and associations may be present, or acquired, with
onset in adulthood. In contrast to keratoconus, the cornea develops globular rather than conical ectasia and is associated with generalized corneal thinning (Fig. 7.44). Acute hydrops is rare, but the
cornea is more prone to rupture on relatively mild trauma. Corneal

254 Corneal Dystrophy

A B

Fig. 7.44 (A) Keratoglobus; (B) Munson sign in keratoglobus.

topography shows generalized steepening. Surgery is diоАЪcult and
contact lens wear is oоАЫen unsatisfactory. Intrastromal ring segments and cross-linking may have utility. Special care should be
taken to protect the eyes from trauma.

CORNEAL DYSTROPHY

оАЯe corneal dystrophies are a group of progressive, usually bilateral, variable corneal opacifying disorders, many of which are
associated with decreased vision and discomfort. Based on biomicroscopical and histopathological features they are classiоАЭed
into epithelial, Bowman layer, stromal and Descemet membrane
and endothelial. One or more underlying genetic abnormalities
have been identiоАЭed for most.

TIP As a general rule, most types of corneal dystrophy are
bilateral, primarily aоАЭect one layer of the cornea and are slowly
progressive.

Epithelial dystrophies
Cogan (epithelial basement membrane)
dystrophy

Isolated familial cases of epithelial basement membrane (mapdot-fingerprint) corneal dystrophy have been reported. Since
most patients seen in clinical practice have no documented
family history of the condition, these corneal changes are now
considered degenerative or secondary to trauma. The disease
is often misdiagnosed, principally because of the variable
appearance.
оАБ Inheritance. оАЯe condition is usually sporadic and these cases
may be degenerations rather than true dystrophies, in contrast
to the rare familial (autosomal dominant вАУ AD) cases.
оАБ Histology shows thickening of the basement membrane with
deposition of оАЭbrillary protein between the basement membrane and the Bowman layer. Basal epithelial cell hemidesmosomes are deоАЭcient.

оАБ Onset is in the second decade. About 10% of patients develop
recurrent corneal erosions in the third decade and the remainder are asymptomatic throughout life. оАЯe occurrence of bilateral recurrent erosions with no history of trauma suggests
basement membrane dystrophy.
оАБ Signs. Lesions are oоАЫen best visualized by retroillumination or
scleral scatter. Over time pattern and distribution varies; they
may be absent or subtle in a fellow eye. Similar features can be
seen with recurrent erosions from any cause.

{ Dot-like and microcystic epithelial lesions (Fig. 7.45A).

{ Subepithelial map-like patterns surrounded by a faint haze
(Fig. 7.45B).

{ Whorled оАЭngerprint-like lines.

{ Bleb-like subepithelial pebbled glass pattern.
оАБ Treatment is similar to that of recurrent corneal erosions.

Meesmann epithelial dystrophy

Meesmann dystrophy is a rare non-progressive abnormality of
corneal epithelial metabolism, underlying which mutations in the
genes encoding corneal epithelial keratins have been reported.
оАБ Inheritance is AD.
оАБ Histology shows irregular thickening of the epithelial basement membrane and intraepithelial cysts.
оАБ Symptoms. Patients may be asymptomatic, or there may be
recurrent erosions and blurring (usually mild).
оАБ Signs

{ Myriad tiny intraepithelial cysts of uniform size but variable density are maximal centrally and extend towards but
do not reach the limbus (Fig. 7.45C).

{ оАЯe cornea may be slightly thinned and sensation
reduced.
оАБ Treatment other than lubrication is not normally required.

Others

Other epithelial and subepithelial dystrophies are: gelatinous
drop-like corneal dystrophy (Fig. 7.45D), Lisch epithelial corneal
dystrophy (Figs 7.45E and F), subepithelial mucinous corneal
dystrophy.
