256 Corneal Dystrophy

Bowman layer/anterior stromal
dystrophies
ReisвАУB√Љcklers corneal dystrophy

Stromal dystrophies
Lattice corneal dystrophy, TGFB1 type

оАЯis may be categorized as an anterior variant of granular stromal
dystrophy (GCD type 3 вАУ see below) and is also known as corneal
basement dystrophy type I (CBD1).
оАБ Inheritance is AD; the aоАЬected gene is TGFB1.
оАБ Histology. Replacement of the Bowman layer by connective
tissue bands.
оАБ Symptoms. Severe recurrent corneal erosions in childhood.
Visual impairment may occur.
оАБ Signs

{ GreyвАУwhite geographic subepithelial opacities, most dense
centrally (Fig. 7.46A), increasing in density with age to
form a reticular pattern. Histopathology, including electron microscopy, may be required for deоАЭnitive distinction
from оАЯielвАУBehnke dystrophy in some cases.

оАЯis is usually regarded as the classic form of lattice dystrophy.
Clinical variants (e.g. IIIA вАУ Fig. 7.47A and B) associated with
more than 25 heterozygous mutations in TGFB1 have been
described.
оАБ Inheritance. AD; gene TGFB1.
оАБ Histology. Amyloid, staining with Congo red and exhibiting green birefringence with a polarizing filter (Fig.
7.47A).
оАБ Symptoms. Recurrent erosions occur at the end of the оАЭrst
decade in the classic form, when typical stromal signs may not
yet be present. Blurring may occur later.
оАБ Signs

{ Refractile anterior stromal dots (Fig. 7.47C), coalescing
into a relatively оАЭne оАЭlamentous lattice that spreads gradually but spares the periphery (Fig. 7.47D).

{ A generalized stromal haze may progressively impair
vision.

{ Corneal sensation is reduced.
оАБ Treatment is directed at the recurrent erosions. Excimer keratectomy achieves satisfactory control in some patients.

ThielвАУBehnke corneal dystrophy

Also termed honeycomb-shaped corneal dystrophy and corneal
basement dystrophy type II (CBD2); features are generally less
severe than ReisвАУB√Љcklers.
оАБ Inheritance. AD; gene TGFB1 and at least one other.
оАБ Histology. Bowman layer вАШcurly оАЭbresвАЩ on electron microscopy.
оАБ Symptoms. Recurrent erosions in childhood.
оАБ Signs. Subepithelial opacities are less individually deоАЭned than
the granular dystrophy-type lesions (see below) seen in ReisвАУ
B√Љcklers dystrophy. оАЯey develop in a network of tiny rings
or honeycomb-like morphology, predominantly involving the
central cornea (Fig. 7.46B).
оАБ Treatment is not always necessary.

{ Corneal sensation is reduced.
оАБ Treatment by penetrating or deep lamellar keratoplasty is frequently required. Recurrence is not uncommon.

Lattice corneal dystrophy, gelsolin type

Also known as LCD2 and Meretoja syndrome, this is a systemic
condition rather than a true corneal dystrophy.
оАБ Inheritance. AD; gene GSN.
оАБ Histology shows amyloid deposits in the corneal stroma.
оАБ Ocular symptoms. Ocular irritation and late impairment of
vision; erosions are rare.
оАБ Ocular signs

{ Sparse stromal lattice lines spread centrally from the
periphery.

{ Corneal sensation is impaired.

оАБ Systemic features. Progressive cranial and peripheral neuropathy, mask-like facies and autonomic features. Homozygous
disease is rare but severe.
оАБ Treatment. Keratoplasty may rarely be required in later

Granular corneal dystrophy, type 1 (classic)

оАБ Inheritance. AD; gene TGFB1. Homozygous disease gives
more severe features.
оАБ Histology. Amorphous hyaline deposits staining bright red
with Masson trichrome (Fig. 7.48A).
оАБ Symptoms. Glare and photophobia, with blurring as progression occurs. Recurrent erosions are uncommon.
оАБ Signs

{ Discrete white central anterior stromal deposits resembling sugar granules, breadcrumbs or glass splinters separated by clear stroma (Fig. 7.48B).

{ Gradual increase in number and size of the deposits with
deeper and outward spread, sparing the limbus.

{ Gradual conоАЮuence and diоАЬuse haze lead to visual impairment (Fig. 7.48C).

{ Corneal sensation is impaired.
оАБ Treatment by penetrating or deep lamellar keratoplasty is usually required by the оАЭоАЫh decade. SuperоАЭcial recurrences may
require repeated excimer laser keratectomy.

Granular corneal dystrophy, type 2

Also known as Avellino and combined granular-lattice dystrophy.
оАБ Inheritance. AD; gene TGFB1.
оАБ Histology shows both hyaline and amyloid.
оАБ Symptoms. Recurrent erosions tend to be mild. Visual impairment is a later feature.
оАБ Signs are usually present by the end of the оАЭrst decade in heterozygotes. Fine superоАЭcial opacities progress to form stellate
or annular lesions (Fig. 7.48D), sometimes associated with
deeper linear opacities.
оАБ Treatment is usually not required. Corneal trauma accelerates
progression; refractive surgery is contraindicated.

Macular corneal dystrophy

оАБ Inheritance. Autosomal recessive (AR); gene CHST6; the condition is relatively common in Iceland.
оАБ Histology. Aggregations of glycosaminoglycans intra- and
extracellularly; stain with Alcian blue and colloidal iron (Fig.
7.49A).
оАБ Symptoms. Early (end of оАЭrst decade) visual deterioration;
recurrent erosions are very common.
оАБ Signs

{ Dense but poorly delineated greyish-white spots centrally
in the anterior stroma and peripherally in the posterior
stroma (Fig. 7.49B and C). оАЯere is no clear delineation
between opacities, which may be elevated.

{ Progression of the lesions occurs in conjunction with anterior stromal haze, initially involving the central cornea
(Fig. 7.49D).

{ оАЯere is eventual involvement of full-thickness stroma,
extending to the limbus with no clear zone.

{ оАЯinning is a fairly early feature, with late thickening from
oedema due to endothelial dysfunction.

{ Sensation is reduced.
оАБ Treatment. Penetrating keratoplasty. Recurrence is
common.

Schnyder (crystalline) corneal dystrophy

оАЯis is a disorder of corneal lipid metabolism, associated in some
patients with systemic dyslipidaemia. оАЯe use of crystalline in the
name is no longer recommended as corneal crystals are not a ubiquitous feature.
оАБ Inheritance. AD; gene UBIAD1.
оАБ Histology. Phospholipid and cholesterol deposits.
оАБ Symptoms. Visual impairment and glare.
оАБ Signs

{ Central haze is an early feature (Fig. 7.50A), progressing
to more widespread full-thickness involvement over time
(Fig. 7.50B).

{ Subepithelial crystalline opacities are present in only

Fran√Іois central cloudy dystrophy

It is not certain that this entity is a dystrophy; it may be clinically indistinguishable from the degeneration posterior crocodile shagreen.
оАБ Inheritance. AD has been reported, but not clearly established.
оАБ Symptoms. Almost always none.
оАБ Signs. Cloudy greyish polygonal or rounded posterior stromal
opacities, most prominent centrally (Fig. 7.51).
оАБ Treatment is not required.

Others

Congenital stromal corneal dystrophy, оАЮeck corneal dystrophy,
posterior amorphous corneal dystrophy and pre-Descemet corneal dystrophy.

Descemet membrane and endothelial
dystrophies
Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy

оАЯis disorder is characterized by bilateral accelerated endothelial
cell loss. It is more common in women and is associated with a
slightly increased prevalence of glaucoma.
оАБ Inheritance. Most are sporadic, with occasional AD inheritance. Mutation in COL8A2 has been identiоАЭed in an earlyonset variant and in the TCF4 gene in most other cases.
оАБ Symptoms. Gradually worsening blurring, particularly in the
morning, due to corneal oedema. Onset is usually in middle
age or later.
оАБ Signs

Fig. 7.51 Marked signs in Fran√Іois central cloudy dystrophy.
(Courtesy of W Lisch.)

{ Cornea guttata: the presence of irregular warts or вАШexcrescencesвАЩ on Descemet membrane secreted by abnormal
endothelial cells (Fig. 7.52A).

{ Subtle corneal endothelial radial lines which correlate with
the peripheral cell arrangement that is seen microscopically.

{ Specular reоАЮection shows tiny dark spots caused by disruption of the regular endothelial mosaic (Fig. 7.52B). Progression occurs to a вАШbeaten metalвАЩ appearance (Fig. 7.52C).

{ Endothelial decompensation gradually leads to central
stromal oedema and blurred vision, worse in the morning.

{ Epithelial oedema develops in more advanced cases, with
the formation of microcysts and bullae (bullous keratopathy

    - Fig. 7.52D) accompanied by discomfort. Rupture of bullae
is associated with marked acute pain secondary to the exposure of nerve оАЭbres. Subepithelial scarring and peripheral
vascularization may be seen in longstanding cases.
оАБ Treatment

{ Conservative options include topical sodium chloride
5% drops or ointment, reduction of IOP and use of a hair
dryer for corneal dehydration.

{ Ruptured bullae can be made more comfortable by the
use of bandage contact lenses, cycloplegia, antibiotic ointment and lubricants. Anterior stromal puncture may be
helpful.

{ Posterior lamellar keratoplasty (e.g. Descemet membranestripping automated endothelial keratoplasty вАУ DSAEK вАУ or
Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty вАУ DMEK)
and penetrating keratoplasty (see Ch. 7) have a high success rate.

{ Options in eyes with poor visual potential include conjunctival оАЮaps and amniotic membrane transplantation.

{ A promising new treatment, topical Rho-kinase inhibitor with prior transcorneal endothelial cryotherapy, seems
to stimulate endothelial cell proliferation and improve
function.
оАБ Cataract surgery may worsen the corneal status because of
endothelial cell loss and protective steps should be taken to
reduce this. Modern machines using torsional phacoemulsiоАЭcation use less ultrasound time than the older machines, which
used longitudinal phacoemulsiоАЭcation and are therefore less
likely to cause endothelial loss. A вАШtriple procedureвАЩ (combined
cataract surgery, lens implantation and keratoplasty) may be
considered in eyes with corneal oedema. Corneal oedema is
less likely to occur aоАЫer cataract surgery if the central corneal
thickness is less than 630вАУ640 ќЉm.

TIP Persistent corneal oedema may follow cataract surgery
in a patient with Fuchs endothelial dystrophy, particularly if
the preoperative central corneal thickness is more than
630вАУ640 ќЉm.

Posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy

оАЯere are three forms of posterior polymorphous dystrophy,
PPCD1вАУ3. Associations include iris abnormalities, glaucoma and
Alport syndrome. оАЯe pathological basis involves metaplasia of
endothelial cells.

Congenital hereditary endothelial dystrophy

Congenital hereditary endothelial dystrophy (CHED) is a rare dystrophy in which there is focal or diоАЬuse thickening of Descemet
membrane and endothelial degeneration. CHED2 is a more common and more severe form than CHED1 and is occasionally associated with late-onset deafness (Harboyan syndrome).
оАБ Inheritance

оАБ Symptoms. Photophobia and watering are common in
CHED1, but not in CHED2.
оАБ Signs

{ Corneal clouding and thickening (Fig. 7.54) are neonatal in CHED2 and develop during the оАЭrst year or two in
CHED1.

{ Visual impairment is variable and visual acuity may surpass that expected from the corneal appearance.

{ Nystagmus is more common in CHED2.
оАБ Treatment. Lamellar or penetrating keratoplasty.

{ CHED1 is AD with the gene locus on chromosome 20.
CHED1 may not be distinct from PPCD.

AgeоАЯrelated degeneration
Arcus senilis

Arcus senilis (gerontoxon, arcus lipoides) is the most common
peripheral corneal opacity. It frequently occurs without any

{ Stromal lipid deposition, initially in the superior and inferior perilimbal cornea, progressing circumferentially to

{ оАЯe band is usually wider in the vertical than horizontal

Vogt limbal girdle

Vogt limbal girdle is an innocuous condition that is present in
up to 60% of individuals over 40 years of age, more commonly
in women. It consists of whitish crescentic limbal bands composed of chalk-like оАЮecks centred at 9 and/or 3 oвАЩclock, more oоАЫen
nasally. оАЯere may be irregular central extension. Type I may be

a variant of band keratopathy, featuring a вАШSwiss cheeseвАЩ hole pattern and a clear area separating the lesion from the scleral margin
(Fig. 7.55B). Type II is more prevalent and is distinguished by the
absence of holes and typically also of a juxtalimbal clear zone (Fig.
7.55C). Histologically the changes in both are similar to pinguecula and pterygium.

Cornea farinata

Cornea farinata is a visually insigniоАЭcant condition characterized
by bilateral, minute, оАЮour-like deposits in the deep stroma, most
prominent centrally (Fig. 7.55D).

Crocodile shagreen

Crocodile shagreen is characterized by asymptomatic, greyishwhite, polygonal stromal opacities separated by relatively clear
spaces (Fig. 7.55E). оАЯe opacities most frequently involve the
anterior two-thirds of the stroma (anterior crocodile shagreen),
but on occasion may be found more posteriorly (posterior

West Indian punctate keratopathy

оАЯis is an unusual condition of unknown cause that is seen in
elderly asymptomatic individuals, generally of West Indian origin.
Males are more aоАЬected than females. It normally aоАЬects only one
eye, but is occasionally bilateral, with one to four lesions found in
the interpalpebral оАЭssure outside the visual axis. оАЯe lesions are
round, measuring 0.5 mm or less, and are located at the level of
Bowman membrane, extending just into the superоАЭcial stroma.
оАЯey usually have a central dot which is intensely white in colour,
surrounded by a paler halo which merges with the adjacent cornea.
оАЯe corneal epithelium appears normal and does not stain with
оАЮuorescein. Treatment is not required (Fig. 7.55F).

Lipid keratopathy

{ Gradual central spread to form a band-like chalky plaque
containing transparent small holes (Fig. 7.57B) and occasionally cleоАЫs.

{ Advanced lesions may become nodular and elevated with
considerable discomfort due to epithelial breakdown.
оАБ Treatment is indicated if vision is threatened or if the eye is
uncomfortable. It is important to recognize and treat any
underlying condition.

оАБ Primary lipid keratopathy is rare and occurs apparently spontaneously. It is characterized by white or yellowish, oоАЫen with a
crystalline element, stromal deposits consisting of cholesterol,
fats and phospholipids and is not associated with vascularization (Fig. 7.56A).
оАБ Secondary lipid keratopathy is much more common and
is associated with previous ocular injury or disease that has
resulted in corneal vascularization. оАЯe most common causes
are herpes simplex and herpes zoster keratitis (Fig. 7.56B).
оАБ Treatment is primarily aimed at medical control of the underlying inоАЮammatory disease. Other options include:

{ Photocoagulation or needle cautery (suture needle grasped
with cautery forceps) of feeder vessels.

{ Penetrating keratoplasty may be required in advanced but
quiescent disease, though vascularization, thinning and
reduced sensation may aоАЬect the outcome.

Band keratopathy

{ Chelation is simple and eоАЬective for relatively mild cases
and is performed using a microscope. оАЯe corneal epithelium overlying the opacity and a solid layer of calciоАЭcation are оАЭrst scraped oоАЬ with forceps and a scalpel
blade (e.g. No. 15). оАЯe cornea is then rubbed with a
cotton-tipped applicator dipped in a solution of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) 1.5вАУ3.0% until all
calcium has been removed. Adequate time (15вАУ20 minutes) must be allowed for chelation to occur and more
than one session may be necessary. Re-epithelialization
can take many days.

{ Other modalities: diamond burr, excimer laser keratectomy and lamellar keratoplasty.

Spheroidal degeneration

Spheroidal degeneration (Labrador keratopathy, climatic droplet keratopathy) typically occurs in men whose working lives are
spent outdoors. Ultraviolet exposure is likely to be an aetiological
factor. оАЯe condition is relatively innocuous but visual impairment
may occur. A secondary form can follow inоАЮammation or injury.
оАБ Histology. Irregular proteinaceous deposits in the anterior
stroma that replace the Bowman layer.
оАБ Signs

{ Amber-coloured granules in the superоАЭcial stroma of the
peripheral interpalpebral cornea (Fig. 7.58A).

{ Increasing opaciоАЭcation, coalescence and central spread.

Band keratopathy consists of the age-related deposition of calcium
salts in the Bowman layer, epithelial basement membrane and
anterior stroma.
оАБ Causes

{ Advanced lesions commonly protrude above the corneal
surface (Fig. 7.58B) and the surrounding stroma is oоАЫen
hazy. оАЯe conjunctiva can be involved.
оАБ Treatment. Protection against ultraviolet damage with sunglasses and superоАЭcial keratectomy or lamellar keratoplasty in
a minority.

{ Ocular. Chronic anterior uveitis (particularly in children
with juvenile idiopathic arthritis), glaucoma, phthisis
bulbi, silicone oil in the anterior chamber, chronic corneal
oedema and severe chronic keratitis.

Salzmann nodular degeneration

{ Age-related aоАЬecting otherwise healthy individuals.

{ Metabolic (metastatic calciоАЭcation) secondary to hypercalcaemia is uncommon and includes causes like hyperparathyroidism, vitamin D toxicity, milk-alkali syndrome,
sarcoidosis, end-stage renal disease, Paget disease and
multiple myeloma. Hyperuricaemia is a rare cause.

Salzmann nodular degeneration consists of nodules of hyaline
tissue, usually located anterior to the Bowman layer. It can occur
in any form of chronic corneal irritation or inоАЮammation such
as trachoma, dry eye, chronic blepharitis and chronic allergic
keratoconjunctivitis.
оАБ Signs

{ Hereditary causes include familial cases and ichthyosis.
оАБ Signs

{ SuperоАЭcial stromal opacities progressing to elevated whitish or blueвАУgrey nodular lesions that may be round or
elongated (Fig. 7.59).

{ Peripheral interpalpebral calciоАЭcation with clear cornea
separating the sharp peripheral margins of the band from
the limbus (Fig. 7.57A).

{ оАЯe base of a nodule may be associated with pannus and
epithelial iron deposition.
оАБ Treatment consists mainly of lubrication together with control
of the cause. Removal is via manual superоАЭcial keratectomy вАУ
the lesions can oоАЫen be вАШpeeledвАЩ away and the surface оАЮattened

Advancing wave-like epitheliopathy

Advancing wave-like epitheliopathy (AWE) is characterized by an
irregular advancing epithelial plaque encroaching gradually on the
cornea, typically originating at the superior limbus (Fig. 7.60) and
sometimes extending circumferentially from a pterygium. Topical оАЮuorescein generally demonstrates the lesion well. Irritation
and redness are common. оАЯe vision may be aоАЬected with central involvement. Reported risk factors include contact lens wear,
certain contact lens solutions, topical glaucoma medication, prior
ocular surgery and some skin conditions such as rosacea. Treatment of the cause may be curative, but otherwise is with 1% silver
nitrate solution to the adjacent limbus or cryotherapy (1вАУ2 seconds twice) to the limbus and abnormal tissue. Distinction from
neoplasia may occasionally warrant impression cytology or excision biopsy.

METABOLIC KERATOPATHY

Cystinosis

Cystinosis is a rare AR (gene: CTNS) lysosomal storage disorder
characterized by widespread tissue deposition of cystine crystals,
leading to paediatric renal failure and a range of other severe systemic problems. Non-nephropathic (ocular), nephropathic and
intermediate forms can occur. Keratopathy may develop in the
оАЭrst year, with progressive deposition of crystals in the cornea
(Fig. 7.61A) and conjunctiva associated with photophobia, epithelial erosions and visual impairment. Systemic treatment is with
cysteamine, which can be given in eye drop form to reverse corneal
crystal formation.

Fig. 7.60 Advancing wave-like epitheliopathy (arrowheads).
Inferior extent illuminated in the slit beam (arrow).

Mucopolysaccharidoses

оАЯe mucopolysaccharidoses (MPS) are a group of lysosomal storage disorders involving enzyme dysfunction along the pathways for
breakdown of glycosaminoglycans, long chain carbohydrates formerly
known as mucopolysaccharides. Altered metabolites accumulate
intracellularly in various tissues. Inheritance is mainly AR. Systemic
features vary with the type of MPS, but can include facial coarseness,
skeletal anomalies, heart disease and learning diоАЪculties. Keratopathy
comprises punctate corneal opaciоАЭcation and diоАЬuse stromal haze
(Fig. 7.61B) and occurs in all MPS except Hunter and SanоАЭlippo. Other
ocular features may include pigmentary retinopathy and optic atrophy.

Wilson disease

Wilson disease (hepatolenticular degeneration) is a rare condition involving the widespread abnormal deposition of copper in
tissues. It is caused by a deоАЭciency of caeruloplasmin, the major

Cornea 267

A B

C D

W Lisch вАУ оАЯg. D.)

copper-carrying blood protein. Presentation is with liver disease,
basal ganglia dysfunction or psychiatric disturbances. A KayserвАУ
Fleischer ring is present in 95% of patients with neurological signs
and consists of a brownish-yellow zone of оАЭne copper dusting in
peripheral Descemet membrane (Fig. 7.61C). оАЯis is best detected
on gonioscopy when subtle. оАЯe deposits are preferentially distributed in the vertical meridian and may disappear with penicillamine therapy. Anterior capsular вАШsunоАЮowerвАЩ cataract is seen in some
patients.

LecithinоАЯcholesterolоАЯacyltransferase
deоАЮciency

Lecithin-cholesterol-acyltransferase (LCAT) deоАЭciency is a disorder of lipoprotein metabolism that has complete (Norum disease,
with systemic manifestations including renal failure) and partial

(оАЭsh eye disease, causing only corneal opaciоАЭcation) forms that are
both AR (gene: LCAT). Keratopathy is characterized by numerous
minute greyish dots throughout the stroma, oоАЫen concentrated in
the periphery in an arcus-like conоАЭguration (Fig. 7.61D).

Immunoprotein deposition

DiоАЬuse or focal immunoprotein deposition is a relatively uncommon manifestation of several systemic diseases, including multiple
myeloma, Waldenstr√ґm macroglobulinaemia, monoclonal gammopathy of unknown cause, some lymphoproliferative disorders
and leukaemia. Corneal involvement may be the earliest manifestation. Bands of punctate оАЮake-like opacities are seen, mostly at
the level of the posterior stroma. Treatment is that of the underlying disease. Severe corneal involvement may require corneal
transplantation.

268 Contact Lenses

Tyrosinaemia type 2

Tyrosinaemia type 2 (oculocutaneous tyrosinaemia, RichnerвАУ
Hanhart syndrome) is a very rare AR disease (gene: TAT) in which
an enzyme deоАЭciency leads to elevated plasma tyrosine levels.
Ocular involvement may occasionally be the presenting feature.
Painful palmar and plantar hyperkeratotic lesions and variable
CNS involvement are seen. A bilateral pseudodendritic keratitis
with crystalline edges oоАЫen begins in childhood and causes photophobia, watering and redness.

Fabry disease

Fabry disease is an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder caused
by a deоАЭciency of the enzyme alpha-galactosidase A that leads to
abnormal tissue accumulation of a glycolipid. All males with the
gene develop the disease and some heterozygous females. Systemic
features include periodic burning pain in the extremities (acroparaesthesia) and GI tract, angiokeratomas (Fig. 7.62A), cardiomyopathy and renal disease. Ocular manifestations include white
to golden-brown corneal opacities in a vortex pattern (75%) that
may be the оАЭrst feature of the disease (Fig. 7.62B) facilitating early
intervention, wedge- or spoke-shaped posterior cataract (Fabry
cataract), conjunctival vascular tortuosity (corkscrew vessels) and
aneurysm formation (Fig. 7.62C) and retinal vascular tortuosity.

CONTACT LENSES

Therapeutic uses

оАЯe risks of оАЭtting a contact lens to an already compromised eye
are greater than with lens wear for cosmetic reasons. оАЯe beneоАЭtвАУ
risk balance should be considered in each case individually, with
education and regular review to ensure early diagnosis and treatment of complications. оАЯe choice of lens type is dictated by the
nature of the ocular pathology.

Optical

Optical indications are aimed at improving visual acuity when this
cannot be achieved by spectacles in the following situations:
оАБ Irregular astigmatism associated with keratoconus can be
corrected with rigid contact lenses aоАЫer spectacles have failed
and before corneal graоАЫing becomes necessary. Patients with
astigmatism following corneal graоАЫing may also beneоАЭt.
оАБ SuperоАЮcial corneal irregularities can be neutralized by
rigid contact lenses, which provide a smoother and optically
more regular surface. Visual acuity can only be substantially
improved if irregularities are not too severe.
оАБ Anisometropia in which binocular vision cannot be achieved
by spectacles due to aniseikonia, such as may occur following monocular cataract surgery with high refractive error
correction.

Promotion of epithelial healing

оАБ Persistent epithelial defects oоАЫen heal if the regenerating
corneal epithelium is protected from the constant rubbing of

A

B

C

Fig. 7.62 Fabry disease. (A) Angiokeratomas; (B) vortex
keratopathy (arrow); (C) conjunctival vessel tortuosity and
aneurysms.

the lids, allowing the development of hemidesmosomal attachments to the basement membrane.
оАБ Recurrent corneal erosions associated with basement membrane dystrophy may require long-term contact lens wear to

CHAPTER

Cornea 269
#### 7

reduce the recurrence rate. In post-traumatic cases, lens wear
can usually be discontinued aоАЫer a few weeks. Lens wear also
improves comfort.

Pain relief

оАБ Bullous keratopathy can be managed with soоАЫ bandage lenses
that relieve pain by protecting the exposed corneal nerve endings from the shearing forces of the lids during blinking. оАЯe
lens may also оАЮatten bullae into diоАЬuse оАЭne epithelial cysts.
оАБ Filamentary keratopathy resistant to topical treatment will
usually achieve some relief from soоАЫ contact lens wear.
оАБ Other indications include оАЯygeson superоАЭcial punctate keratitis and protection of the corneal epithelium from aberrant
lashes in trichiasis. оАЯey can also be used as a temporizing
measure in entropion prior to deоАЭnitive surgery.

Preservation of corneal integrity

оАБ A descemetocoele can be temporarily capped with a tightоАЭtting, large-diameter soоАЫ or scleral lens to prevent perforation and encourage healing.
оАБ Splinting and apposition of the edges of a small corneal
wound can be achieved by means of a contact lens. Slightly
larger perforations may be sealed with glue followed by
insertion of a bandage contact lens to both protect the glue
and prevent irritation of the lids from the glueвАЩs irregular
surface.

Miscellaneous indications

оАБ Ptosis props to support the upper lids in patients with ocular
myopathies.
оАБ Maintenance of the fornices to prevent symblepharon formation in cicatrizing conjunctivitis.
оАБ Drug delivery enhancement by a hydrogel lens imbued with
topical medication is an occasional indication.

Complications

Mechanical and hypoxic keratitis

оАБ Pathogenesis. InsuоАЪcient oxygen transmission through the
lens. A tightly оАЭtting contact lens that does not move with
blinking will impair tear circulation under the lens. оАЯis is
exacerbated by lid closure if the lens is worn during sleep.
Hypoxia leads to anaerobic metabolism and lactic acidosis
that inhibits the normal barrier and pump mechanisms of the
cornea.
оАБ SuperоАЮcial punctate keratitis is the most common complication. оАЯe pattern may give a clue as to the aetiology. For example, staining at 3 and 9 oвАЩclock is associated with incomplete
blinking and drying in rigid lens wearers.
оАБ оАЯe tight lens syndrome is characterized by indentation and
staining of the conjunctival epithelium in a ring around the
cornea.

оАБ Acute hypoxia is characterized by epithelial microcysts (Fig.
7.63A) and necrosis and endothelial blebs. Very painful macroerosions may develop several hours aоАЫer lenses are removed
following a period of overwear.
оАБ Chronic hypoxia may result in vascularization (Fig. 7.63B)
and lipid deposition. SuperоАЭcial peripheral neovascularization
of <1.5 mm is common in myopic contact lens wearers and can
be monitored.
оАБ Treatment depends on the cause but may involve:

{ Increasing oxygen permeability by reоАЭtting with a thinner
lens, a gas permeable rigid lens or a silicone hydrogel soоАЫ
lens.

{ Modifying lens оАЭt to increase movement.

{ Reducing lens wearing time.

Immune response (hypersensitivity) keratitis

A hypersensitivity response to bacterial antigen or the chemicals
used in lens care can lead to the development of sterile marginal
corneal inоАЭltrates. оАЯe mechanism is thought to be similar to that
of marginal keratitis.
оАБ Signs. Mildly red eye associated with inоАЭltrates, oоАЫen marginally located, with no or minimal epithelial defects.
оАБ Treatment involves cessation of lens wear until resolution
occurs. Topical antibiotics and steroids may be used in some
cases, but if the diagnosis is uncertain, treatment should be
that of bacterial keratitis.

Toxic keratitis

оАБ Pathogenesis. Acute chemical injury may be caused by inadvertently placing a contact lens on the eye without оАЭrst neutralizing toxic cleaning agents such as hydrogen peroxide. Chronic
toxicity can result from long-term exposure to disinfecting
preservatives such as thiomersal or benzalkonium chloride.
оАБ Signs

{ Acute pain, redness and chemosis on lens insertion, which
may take 48 hours to resolve completely.

{ Vascularization and scarring of the cornea (Fig. 7.63C)
and limbal conjunctiva in chronic cases.
оАБ Treatment may involve switching to daily disposable lenses or
using a non-preserved disinfectant such as hydrogen peroxide.

Suppurative keratitis

Contact lens wear is the greatest risk factor for the development of
bacterial keratitis (Fig. 7.63D). оАЯe risk is probably least for rigid
contact lenses. Bacteria in the tear оАЭlm are normally unable to bind
to the corneal epithelium, but following an abrasion and in association with hypoxia, they can attach and invade the epithelium.
Microorganisms may also be introduced onto the corneal surface
by poor lens hygiene or the use of tap water for rinsing.

Contact lens-associated giant papillary
conjunctivitis

See Chapter 6.

CONGENITAL ANOMALIES OF THE
CORNEA AND GLOBE

Microcornea

оАЯe normal neonatal corneal diameter is 10 mm and the adult diameter of 12 mm is usually reached by the age of 2 years. Microcornea is a
rare autosomal dominant (sometimes sporadic) unilateral or bilateral
condition in which the horizontal corneal diameter is 10 mm or less
over 2 years of age (Fig. 7.64A), or less than 9 mm in the newborn.
оАЯe cornea is steep on keratometry. оАЯere may be hypermetropia and
a shallow anterior chamber but other dimensions are normal. Ocular
associations include glaucoma (angle-closure and open angle), congenital cataract, leukoma (Fig. 7.64B), persistent fetal vasculature,
coloboma, optic nerve hypoplasia, aniridia and nanophthalmos. Systemic associations have been reported. Refractive error and amblyopia should be managed appropriately.

Microphthalmos

Microphthalmos (microphthalmia вАУ Fig. 7.65A) is a condition
in which the entire eye is small, with an axial length at least
two standard deviations below the mean for age. Simple or pure
microphthalmos (nanophthalmos вАУ see below) refers to an eye
that is structurally normal apart from a short length and complex microphthalmos to eyes with other features of dysgenesis,
for example a coloboma (Fig. 7.65B) or orbital cyst and a range
of other ocular abnormalities. It may be unilateral or bilateral;
when unilateral, abnormalities may be present in the fellow eye.
Vision is variably aоАЬected, in conjunction with severity. It is
typically sporadic but mutations in numerous genes have been
implicated. Around 50% of cases may be associated with systemic abnormalities, including of the CNS. Potential environmental causes include fetal alcohol syndrome and intrauterine
infections.

272 Congenital Anomalies of the Cornea and Globe

Nanophthalmos

In nanophthalmos the entire eye is small with an axial length of
less than 20 mm. Both eyes are usually aоАЬected. Ocular associations include glaucoma (especially angle-closure as the lens is
large relative to the size of the eye), hypermetropia, ametropia,
amblyopia and strabismus. In childhood, management of refractive error and amblyopia are critical. Surgery for glaucoma is
particularly hazardous and can result in aqueous misdirection
and choroidal eоАЬusion (вАШsmall eyeвАУbig troubleвАЩ). оАЯe best option
is lens extraction with IOL implantation. оАЯe surgery is technically challenging, but has the advantage of deepening the anterior
chamber and reducing the refractive error if a suitable highpower lens implant is inserted.

Anophthalmos

Anophthalmos (anophthalmia) refers to the complete absence
of any visible globe structure (Fig. 7.65C and D), though a
microphthalmic remnant or cyst may be present. It is associated with other abnormalities such as absence of extraocular muscles, a short conjunctival sac and microblepharon.
Causative factors are probably broadly similar to those of
microphthalmos.

Megalocornea

Megalocornea is a rare, bilateral, non-progressive condition that is usually X-linked recessive. Approximately 90% of
affected individuals are male. The adult horizontal corneal
diameter is 13 mm or more, with a very deep anterior chamber
(Fig. 7.66A). There is usually high myopia and astigmatism but

normal corrected visual acuity. Lens subluxation may occur due
to zonular stretching and pigment dispersion syndrome may
occur (see Ch. 11). Numerous systemic associations have been
reported.

Cornea plana

оАЯis is an extremely rare bilateral condition in which the cornea is
оАЮatter than normal (Fig. 7.66B) вАУ the radius of curvature is larger.
оАЯere is a corresponding reduction in refractive power resulting
in high hypermetropia. Two forms are described, cornea plana 1,
which is dominantly inherited (CNA1), being milder than cornea
plana 2, which is recessively inherited (CNA2). Associated ocular
abnormalities are common.

Sclerocornea

Sclerocornea is a very rare, usually bilateral, condition that may
be associated with cornea plana (see below). Sporadic cases are
common, but a milder form can be inherited as AD and a more
severe form as AR. Peripheral corneal opaciоАЭcation, with no visible border between the sclera and cornea, confers the appearance
of apparently reduced corneal diameter in mildвАУmoderate disease
(Fig. 7.67A). Occasionally the entire cornea is involved (Fig. 7.67B).

Keratectasia

Keratectasia is a very rare, usually unilateral, condition thought to
be the result of intrauterine keratitis and perforation. It is characterized by protuberance between the eyelids of a severely opaciоАЭed
and sometimes vascularized cornea (Fig. 7.68). It is oоАЫen associated with raised IOP.

Posterior keratoconus

Posterior keratoconus is a sporadic condition in which there
is unilateral non-progressive increase in curvature of the posterior corneal surface. The anterior surface is normal and

visual acuity relatively unimpaired because of the similar
refractive indices of the cornea and aqueous humour. Generalized (involvement of the entire posterior corneal surface) and
localized types are described (paracentral or central posterior
indentation вАУ Fig. 7.69).

KERATOPLASTY

Introduction

Corneal transplantation (graоАЮing) refers to the replacement of
diseased host corneal tissue by healthy donor cornea. A corneal
graоАЮ (keratoplasty) may be partial-thickness (anterior or posterior
lamellar) or full-thickness (penetrating).

General indications

оАБ Optical keratoplasty is performed to improve vision. Important indications include keratoconus, scarring, corneal dystrophies (Fig. 8.1A), pseudophakic bullous keratopathy and
corneal degenerations.
оАБ Tectonic graоАЮing may be carried out to restore or preserve
corneal integrity in eyes with severe structural changes such as
thinning with descemetocoele (US spelling вАУ descemetocele)
(Fig. 8.1B) and perforated corneal ulcer (Fig. 8.1C).

A

C

оАБ оАЯerapeutic corneal transplantation facilitates removal of
infected corneal tissue in eyes unresponsive to antimicrobial
therapy (Fig. 8.1D).
оАБ Cosmetic graоАЮing may be performed to improve the appearance of the eye, but is a rare indication.

Donor tissue

Donor tissue should be removed within 12вАУ24 hours of death.
оАЭere is an attempt to age-match donors and recipients. Corneas
from infants (3 years and under) are used only very occasionally,
even for paediatric transplants, as they are associated with surgical,
refractive and rejection problems. Most corneas are stored in coordinating вАШeye banksвАЩ prior to transplantation, where pre-release
evaluation includes medical history review and donor blood
screening to exclude contraindications and microscopic examination of the cornea including endothelial cell count determination.
Corneas are preserved in hypothermic storage (up to 7вАУ10 days)
or organ culture medium (4 weeks) until needed. Culture allows

B

D

Fig. 8.1 (A) Optical penetrating keratoplasty for macular corneal dystrophy; (B) tectonic lamellar patch graft for descemetocoele; (C) lamellar patch graft for a perforated corneal ulcer;
(D) penetrating keratoplasty for pseudomonas keratitis вАУ a dense cataract and posterior
synechiae are visible. (Courtesy of S Tuft вАУ оАЯg. B.)

Corneal and Refractive Surgery 277
#### 8

extended testing for infective contamination. Contraindications to
ocular tissue donation are set out below, though there is international variation and the list is not exhaustive:
оАБ Death from unknown cause.
оАБ Certain systemic infections such as human immunodeоАЬciency
virus (HIV), viral hepatitis, syphilis, congenital rubella, tuberculosis, septicaemia and active malaria.
оАБ Prior high-risk behaviour for HIV and hepatitis such as sex
with someone HIV-positive, men who have sex with men,
intravenous drug abuse and prostitution.
оАБ Within the last 12 months: sex with someone who has engaged
in high-risk behaviour, who has received blood clotting factor concentrates or has undergone tattooing, acupuncture, ear/
body piercing.
оАБ Infectious and possibly infectious diseases of the central nervous system, such as CreutzfeldtвАУJakob disease, systemic
sclerosing panencephalitis, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, encephalitis, Alzheimer disease and other
dementias, Parkinson disease, multiple sclerosis and motor
neurone disease.
оАБ Receipt of a transplanted organ.
оАБ Receipt of human pituitary-derived growth hormone.
оАБ Brain or spinal surgery before 1992.
оАБ Most haematological malignancies.
оАБ Ocular disease such as inоАЫammation and disease likely to compromise graоАЮ outcome, some malignant ocular tumours (e.g.
retinoblastoma) and corneal refractive surgery.

Recipient prognostic factors

оАЭe following host factors may adversely aоАЪect the prognosis of a
corneal graоАЮ and, if possible, should be optimized prior to surgery.
In general, the most favourable cases are keratoconus, localized
scars and dystrophies.
оАБ Severe stromal vascularization (Fig. 8.2), absence of corneal
sensation, extreme thinning at the proposed hostвАУgraоАЮ junction and active corneal inоАЫammation.

Fig. 8.2 Retroillumination of heavily vascularized cornea
resulting from recurrent herpes simplex keratitis. (Courtesy
of C Barry.)

оАБ Abnormalities of the eyelids, such as blepharitis, ectropion,
entropion and trichiasis. оАЭese conditions should be addressed
before surgery.
оАБ Recurrent or progressive forms of conjunctival inоАЫammation,
such as atopic conjunctivitis and ocular cicatricial pemphigoid.
оАБ Tear оАЬlm dysfunction.
оАБ Anterior synechiae.
оАБ Uncontrolled glaucoma.
оАБ Uveitis.

Penetrating keratoplasty

Component layer graоАЮing of the cornea is increasingly utilized,
but full-thickness keratoplasty remains commonly performed and
is the appropriate procedure for disease involving all layers of the
cornea (Fig. 8.3). Key surgical points include:
оАБ A common graоАЮ size is 7.5 mm. Small graоАЮs may lead to
high astigmatism and larger diameters are associated with an
increasing tendency to peripheral anterior synechiae formation and raised intraocular pressure (IOP).
оАБ оАЯe donor button is usually about 0.25 mm larger in diameter
than the host site.
оАБ Preparation of donor cornea should always precede excision
of host tissue, in case a problem with the former means that the
surgery cannot be completed.
оАБ Trephination. Either mechanically guided manual or automated (including laser) trephination is commonly used.
оАБ Sutures. оАЭe graоАЮ may be secured with either continuous (Fig.
8.4A) or interrupted (Fig. 8.4B) suture techniques, or a combination of both.

Postoperative management

оАБ Topical steroids (e.g. prednisolone acetate 1%, dexamethasone
phosphate 0.1%) are used to decrease the risk of immunological graоАЮ rejection. Initial administration is typically every 2
hours with gradual tapering depending on the likelihood of
rejection and clinical progress. Long-term instillation at low
intensity, such as once daily for a year or more, is usual.
оАБ Other immunosuppressants such as oral azathioprine and
topical and systemic ciclosporin are usually reserved for highrisk patients.
оАБ Cycloplegia (e.g. homatropine 2% twice daily) is typically used
for 1вАУ2 weeks.
оАБ Oral aciclovir may be used in the context of pre-existing
herpes simplex keratitis to minimize the risk of recurrence.
оАБ Monitoring of IOP. Applanation tonometry is relatively unreliable so measurement is commonly performed during the
early postoperative period with a non-applanation method.
оАБ Monitoring endothelial function. оАЭis can be done indirectly
and quickly by measuring the central corneal thickness with a
pachymeter.
оАБ Removal of sutures is performed when the graоАЮвАУhost junction has healed. оАЭis is oоАЮen aоАЮer 12вАУ18 months, although in
elderly patients it may take much longer. Removal of broken or
loose individual sutures should be performed as soon as identiоАЬed, as this reduces the risk of graоАЮ rejection.

TIP In a patient with a corneal graft, broken or loose sutures
should be removed as this reduces the risk of localized
vascularization and graft rejection.

Postoperative complications

оАБ Early complications include persistent epithelial defects, loose
or protruding sutures (Fig. 8.5A and B) (risk of infection вАУ
marked sterile reaction, papillary hypertrophy), wound leak
(sometimes with оАЫat anterior chamber or iris prolapse), uveitis, elevation of intraocular pressure, traumatic graоАЮ rupture
(Fig. 8.6A), cystoid macular oedema, microbial keratitis (Fig.
8.6B), endophthalmitis (Fig. 8.6C) and rejection (see below).
A rare complication is a оАЬxed dilated pupil (Urrets-Zavalia
syndrome).

оАБ Late complications include astigmatism, recurrence of underlying disease, late wound dehiscence, retro-corneal membrane
formation, glaucoma, rejection (see below) and failure without
rejection.

Corneal graft rejection

Immunological rejection of any layer of the cornea can occur.
Rejection of separate layers (endothelial, stromal and epithelial)
can occur in isolation, but typically a combination is present.
Simple graоАЮ failure can occur in the absence of rejection, although
rejection is a common contributory factor.

TIP The risk of corneal graft rejection is increased
signiоАЯcantly by the presence of host stromal vascularization.

280 Keratoplasty

A

B

C

Fig. 8.6 Postoperative complications. (A) Traumatic graft rupture and extrusion of intraocular lens implant; (B) microbial
keratitis; (C) endophthalmitis. (R Bates вАУ оАЯg. A; S Tuft вАУ оАЯg. C.)

оАБ Pathogenesis. оАЭe corneal graоАЮ is immunologically privileged, with an absence of blood vessels and lymphatics and the
presence of relatively few antigen-presenting cells. InоАЫammation and neovascularization contribute to loss of this privilege.

Important predisposing factors for rejection include eccentric
or larger graоАЮs (over 8 mm in diameter), infection (particularly herpetic), glaucoma and previous keratoplasty. If the host
becomes sensitized to histocompatibility antigens present
in the donor cornea, rejection may result. Human leukocyte
antigen (HLA) matching has a small beneоАЬcial eоАЪect on graоАЮ
survival. Gender incompatibility has recently emerged as an
important risk factor for graоАЮ rejection and failure. Whereas
the cornea of a female donor can be used in either male or
female recipients, the cornea of a male donor should not be
allocated to a female recipient.
оАБ Symptoms. Blurred vision, redness, photophobia and pain
are typical, but many cases are asymptomatic until rejection
is established. оАЭe timing of onset is very variable, occurring
from days to years aоАЮer keratoplasty.
оАБ Signs vary depending on the type of graоАЮ.

{ Ciliary injection associated with anterior uveitis is an early
manifestation (Fig. 8.7A).

{ Epithelial rejection may be accompanied by an elevated
line of abnormal epithelium (Fig. 8.7B) in a quiet or mildly
inоАЫamed eye, occurring at an average of 3 months.

{ Subepithelial rejection is characterized by subepithelial
inоАЬltrates, reminiscent of adenoviral infection (Krachmer spots вАУ Fig. 8.7C) on the donor cornea, with deeper
oedema and inоАЬltrative opaciоАЬcation.

{ Stromal rejection features deeper haze. It can be chronic
or hyperacute, the latter in association with endothelial
rejection.

{ Endothelial rejection is characterized by a linear pattern of
keratic precipitates (Khodadoust line вАУ Fig. 8.7D) associated with an area of inоАЫammation at the graоАЮ margin.

{ Stromal oedema is indicative of endothelial failure.
оАБ Management. Early intensive treatment greatly improves the
likelihood of reversing the rejection. оАЭe most aggressive regimen is generally required for endothelial rejection, followed in
order of severity by stromal, subepithelial and epithelial. Intraocular pressure monitoring is critical.

{ Preservative-free topical steroids hourly for 24 hours are
the mainstay of therapy. оАЭe frequency is reduced gradually over several weeks. Steroid ointment can be used at
bedtime as the regimen is tapered. High-risk patients can
be maintained on the highest tolerated topical dose (e.g.
prednisolone acetate 1% four times daily) for an extended
period.

{ Topical cycloplegia (e.g. homatropine 2% or atropine 1%
once or twice daily).

{ Topical ciclosporin 0.05% to 2% may be of beneоАЬt, but the
onset of action is delayed.

{ Systemic steroids. Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 1вАУ2
weeks with subsequent tapering. If given within 8 days of
onset, intravenous methylprednisolone 500 mg daily for
up to 3 days may be particularly eоАЪective, suppressing
rejection and reducing the risk of further episodes.

{ Subconjunctival steroid injection (e.g. 0.5 ml of 4 mg/ml
dexamethasone) is sometimes used.

{ Other systemic immunosuppressants such as ciclosporin,
tacrolimus or azathioprine can be considered.

оАБ DiоАЭerential diagnosis includes graоАЮ failure (no inоАЫammation), infective keratitis including fungal and herpetic, uveitis,
sterile suture reaction, raised IOP and epithelial ingrowth.

TIP In a patient with acute corneal graft rejection, early
intensive treatment greatly improves the likelihood of reversing

SuperоАЯcial lamellar keratoplasty

оАЭis involves partial-thickness excision of the corneal epithelium
and stroma so that the endothelium and part of the deep stroma
are leоАЮ behind as a bed for appropriately partial-thickness donor
cornea. оАЭe area graоАЮed depends on the extent of the disease process to be addressed.
оАБ Indications

{ OpaciоАЬcation of the superоАЬcial one-third of the corneal
stroma not caused by potentially recurrent disease.

Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty

Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) is a technique in
which corneal tissue is removed almost to the level of Descemet
membrane. A theoretical advantage is the decreased risk of rejection because the endothelium, a major target for rejection, is not
transplanted. оАЭe major technical diоАЩculty lies in judging the
depth of the corneal dissection as close as possible to Descemet
membrane without perforation and if this is not achieved the
visual outcome may be compromised.
оАБ Indications

{ Disease involving the anterior 95% of corneal thickness
with normal endothelium and the absence of breaks or

{ Marginal corneal thinning or inоАЬltration as in recurrent
pterygium, Terrien marginal degeneration and limbal dermoid or other tumours.

{ Localized thinning or descemetocoele formation (see Fig.
8.1B).

282 Keratoplasty

Fig. 8.8 Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty for chemical injury. A superior conjunctival limbal autograft has also been
performed (arrows indicate the edge of conjunctival graft).

outcomes and lower rejection rates are achieved, but intraoperative complication rates are higher than DSAEK.
оАБ Indications include endothelial disease such as Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy and a localised tectonic graоАЮ for
patching of a corneal perforation.
оАБ Advantages

{ Relatively little refractive change and a structurally more
intact globe.

{ Faster visual rehabilitation than penetrating keratoplasty.

{ Suturing is minimized.
оАБ Disadvantages

{ SigniоАЬcant learning curve.

{ Specialized equipment is required.

{ оАЭe visual outcome is oоАЮen suboptimal regardless of the
graоАЮ clarity. Reasons include: (a) diоАЪerences in graоАЮ thickness, (b) graоАЮ irregularities, (c) high order aberrations, (d)
donorвАУrecipient interface оАЬbrosis.

{ Endothelial rejection can still occur (Fig. 8.10).

scars in Descemet membrane (e.g. keratoconus without a
history of acute hydrops, superоАЬcial trauma вАУ Fig. 8.8).

TIP Endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) results in more rapid
visual improvement and less risk of rejection than penetrating
keratoplasty.

Limbal stem cell grafting

{ Chronic inоАЫammatory disease such as atopic keratoconjunctivitis that carries an increased risk of graоАЮ rejection.
оАБ Advantages

{ No risk of endothelial rejection, although epithelial/subepithelial/stromal rejection may occur.

{ Less astigmatism and a structurally stronger globe compared with penetrating keratoplasty.

{ Increased availability of graоАЮ material since endothelial
quality is irrelevant.
оАБ Disadvantages

{ DiоАЩcult and time-consuming with a high risk of
perforation.

{ Interface haze may limit the best оАЬnal visual acuity.

{ Postoperative management is similar to penetrating
keratoplasty except that lower intensity topical steroids
are needed and sutures can usually be removed aоАЮer 6
months.

Stem cells are undiоАЪerentiated cells that give rise to the diоАЪerentiated cells that form tissues and organs. Tissue-speciоАЬc stem cells
are important for maintaining homeostasis and for tissue repair
aоАЮer injury. Corneal epithelium is constantly self-renewing and
does so throughout life. Progeny of corneal stem cells divide and
diоАЪerentiate to form basal corneal epithelial cells (transient amplifying cells) that diоАЪerentiate into wing cells (post-mitotic cells)
and, оАЬnally, superоАЬcial squamous cells (terminally diоАЪerentiated
cells).
Various techniques have been described to replenish the
limbal stem cell population in patients with severe ocular surface disease and associated stem cell deficiency. These include
transplantation of a limbal area of limited size from a healthy
fellow eye, complete limbal transplantation of a donor annulus
and exоАЯvivo expansion by culture of either host or donor stem
cells with subsequent transplantation. In patients with unilateral ocular involvement in StevensвАУJohnson syndrome and
ocular cicatricial pemphigoid, donor tissue should not be taken
from the unaffected eye (autologous).
оАБ Signs of limbal stem cell deоАЬciency (LSCD) include:

Endothelial keratoplasty

Endothelial keratoplasty involves removal of diseased endothelium along with Descemet membrane (DM) through a corneoscleral or corneal incision. Folded donor tissue is introduced
through the same small (2.8вАУ5.0 mm) incision. Descemet stripping (automated) endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) uses an automated microkeratome to prepare donor tissue and is currently the
most commonly performed technique. A small amount of posterior stromal thickness is transplanted along with DM and endothelium (Fig. 8.9). In Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty
(DMEK) only the DM and endothelium are transplanted.
Because the graоАЮ is only 5вАУ10 ќЉm thick it is diоАЩcult to handle.
However, the graоАЮs are able to attach to the host tissue with less
unevenness, which leads to more rapid visual recovery. Visual

{ Conjunctivalization of cornea with goblet cells (conоАЬrmed
with confocal microscopy, impression cytology with acid
SchiоАЪ stain or monoclonal antibody against cytokeratin 19).

{ SuperоАЬcial and deep corneal vascularization.

{ Fibrovascular pannus.

{ Persistent epithelial defects.

{ Scarring.
оАБ Algorithm for surgical interventions for LSCD

{ All associated abnormalities (lids, conjunctiva, intraocular
pressure) should be treated оАЬrst.

{ In total bilateral stem cell deоАЬciency, use of allograоАЮs
from a living related donor or a cadaver donor is the only
option.
оАБ Indications

{ Congenital: for example, aniridia.

{ Traumatic: chemical and thermal burns.

{ Chronic inоАЫammatory disease: StevensвАУJohnson syndrome, ocular cicatricial pemphigoid.

{ Ocular surface malignancy.

{ Contact lens-related pathology.
оАБ Advantages

{ Regeneration of corneal surface epithelium.

{ Visual improvement and improved comfort.

{ When the visual axis is involved, sequential sector conjunctival epitheliectomy should be considered with a sector limbal transplant.

{ In total unilateral stem cell deоАЬciency, auto limbal transplantation is the procedure of choice.

{ Autologous graоАЮ: conjunctivalization, оАЬlamentary keratitis, scarring of donor eye.

{ Allogenic graоАЮ: infection (especially in immunecompromised patients), rejection.

KERATOPROSTHESES

Keratoprostheses (Fig. 8.11) are artiоАЬcial corneal implants used in
patients unsuitable for keratoplasty. оАЭe modern osteoodontokeratoprosthesis consists of the patientвАЩs own tooth root and alveolar bone
supporting a central optical cylinder and is usually covered with a buccal mucous membrane graоАЮ. Surgery is diоАЩcult and time-consuming
and is performed in two stages, 2вАУ4 months apart. In experienced
hands the retention rate is about 85% at 18 months. Approximately
80% of patients achieve visual acuity between counting оАЬngers and
6/12 and occasionally even better. A poor outcome is usually associated with pre-existing optic nerve or retinal dysfunction. High patient
motivation is a key factor to consider before proceeding to surgery.
оАБ Indications

{ Bilateral blindness from severe, but inactive anterior segment
disease with no realistic chance of success from conventional keratoplasty, e.g. StevensвАУJohnson syndrome, ocular
cicatricial pemphigoid, chemical burns and trachoma.

{ Vision of counting оАЬngers or less in the better eye.

{ Intact optic nerve and retinal function, without marked
glaucomatous optic neuropathy.
оАБ Complications

{ Glaucoma in up to 40%. Management is inevitably extremely
challenging and requires aggressive IOP-lowering medication and oоАЮen the insertion of a drainage shunt.

{ Membrane formation behind the prosthesis (25%); usually
managed with YAG-laser membranectomy.

{ Tilting or extrusion.

{ Retinal detachment and endophthalmitis (about 3% per
patient year).

or lens, the principal refracting components. Myopia, hypermetropia (hyperopia) and astigmatism can all be addressed,
though correction of presbyopia is yet to be achieved on a consistently satisfactory basis.
оАБ оАЯe patient population consists of people with stable refractive error who desire an improvement in visual acuity or function aоАЮer surgical correction.
оАБ A thorough preoperative assessment is mandatory. Not
everyone who requests this treatment is suitable. People who
require a high level of quality vision, e.g, certain professions
(pilot, air-traоАЩc controller, truck-driver) and speciоАЬc hobbies
(contact sports, marksman, amateur astronomer) must understand the limitations of the surgery before proceeding.
оАБ Informed consent is vitally important.
оАБ Contraindications include: people with unacceptably high
expectations, dry eye syndrome, non-stable refractive error,
some systemic diseases (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, immunocompromise) and those with ocular disease (chronic
blepharitis, previous herpes simplex, glaucoma, macular
degeneration).
оАБ оАЯe advantages and potential risks should be discussed and
the common adverse side eоАЪects such as under- and overcorrection, dry eye symptoms and eventual presbyopia should
be emphasized. Care should be taken to discuss which adverse
events are likely to be transient and which may be permanent.
оАБ Alternatives, including new and emerging technology for
treatment, should be discussed.
оАБ Patient satisfaction depends on patient expectations and the
surgical outcome. Most are satisоАЬed aоАЮer the surgery, but some
may be unhappy because of induced ocular side eоАЪects and
visual aberrations, even though the intended correction has
been achieved.

TIP People who require a high level of quality vision because
of their occupation or hobbies need to understand the
limitations of refractive surgery and make an informed decision
before proceeding to surgical correction.

Correction of myopia

оАБ Surface ablation procedures (see below) can correct lowвАУ
moderate degrees of myopia.
оАБ Laser in situ keratomileusis (LASIK вАУ see below) can correct
moderate to high myopia depending on initial corneal thickness, but for very high refractive errors one of the intraocular
procedures below is necessary.
оАБ Refractive lenticule extraction (SMILE вАУ see below) is a small
incisional laser technique for the correction of myopia and
myopic astigmatism.
оАБ Clear lens exchange gives very good visual results but carries a
small risk of the complications of cataract surgery (see Ch. 10),
particularly retinal detachment in individuals with high
myopia.
оАБ Iris clip (вАШlobster clawвАЩ) implant is attached to the iris (Fig.
8.12A). Complications include subluxation or dislocation due
to dislodgement of one or both attachments, an oval pupil,

REFRACTIVE PROCEDURES

Introduction

оАБ Refractive surgery encompasses a range of procedures aimed
at changing the refraction of the eye by altering the cornea

Fig. 8.12 Phakic intraocular implants for correction of myopia. (A) Anterior chamber iris claw

endothelial cell loss, cataract, pupillary-block glaucoma and
retinal detachment.
оАБ Phakic posterior chamber implant (implantable contact lens,
ICL) is inserted behind the iris and in front of the lens (Fig.
8.12B) and supported in the ciliary sulcus. оАЭe lens is composed of material derived from collagen (Collamer) with a
power of вИТ3 D to вИТ20.50 D. Visual results are usually good, but
complications include uveitis, pupillary block, endothelial cell
loss, cataract formation and retinal detachment.
оАБ Radial keratotomy (Fig. 8.13) is now predominantly of historical interest.

Correction of hypermetropia (hyperopia)

оАБ Surface ablation procedures can correct low degrees of
hypermetropia.
оАБ LASIK can correct up to 4 D.
оАБ Conductive keratoplasty (CK) involves the application of
radiofrequency energy to the corneal stroma in order to correct lowвАУmoderate hypermetropia and hypermetropic astigmatism. Burns are placed in one or two rings in the corneal
periphery using a micro-needle high-frequency probe. оАЭe
resultant thermally induced stromal shrinkage is accompanied
by an increase in central corneal curvature. Complications
are infrequent, but the disadvantages include early overcorrection, signiоАЬcant regression and induced astigmatism.
CK may also be helpful for presbyopia (see below). Laser thermal keratoplasty with a holmium laser is no longer used.
оАБ Other modalities include clear lens extraction and phakic lens
implants as described above for myopia. Intraocular surgical
procedures are the only options for high degrees of refractive
error.

Correction of astigmatism

оАБ Limbal relaxing incisions/arcuate keratotomy involves making paired arcuate incisions on opposite sides of the cornea

Fig. 8.13 Radial keratotomy. (Courtesy of C Barry.)

(Fig. 8.14A) in the axis of the correcting вАШplusвАЩ cylinder (the
steep meridian). оАЭe resultant оАЫattening of the steep meridian
coupled with a smaller steepening of the оАЫat meridian at 90¬∞ to
the incisions reduces astigmatism. оАЭe desired result can be controlled by varying the length and depth of the incisions and their
distance from the optical centre of the cornea. Arcuate keratotomy may be combined with compression sutures placed in the
perpendicular meridian, when treating large degrees of astigmatism such as can occur following penetrating keratoplasty.
оАБ PRK and LASEK can correct up to 3 D.
оАБ LASIK can correct up to 5 D.
оАБ Lens surgery involves using a вАШtoricвАЩ intraocular implant incorporating an astigmatic correction (Fig. 8.14B). Postoperative rotation of the
implant away from the desired axis occurs in a minority of cases.
оАБ Conductive keratoplasty (see вАШCorrection of hypermetropiaвАЩ
above).

Correction of presbyopia

оАБ Lens extraction, either to treat cataract or for purely refractive
purposes. Acronyms used include clear lens exchange (CLE),
refractive lens exchange (RLE) and presbyopic lens exchange
(PreLEx). Much research eоАЪort is being applied to the development of eоАЪective accommodating and multifocal prosthetic
lenses.

{ Implantation of a bifocal, trifocal diоАЪractive, multifocal
refractive, extended depth of focus or вАШaccommodatingвАЩ/
pseudo-accommodative intraocular lens implant can optically restore reading vision, but glasses may still have to be
used for some tasks. If appropriately screened, most recipients of multifocal IOLs are happy with the visual outcome.
However, dissatisfaction may occur as a consequence of
nocturnal glare, halo and reduced contrast sensitivity. In
patients with intractable visual symptoms IOL exchange
surgery should be undertaken. In some jurisdictions,
implantation of a multifocal IOL is a contraindication to
the holding of a private or commercial pilotвАЩs licence, or to
military service.

оАБ Corneal multifocality: presbyLASIK. Several diоАЪerent
approaches are under development utilizing a laser procedure
to alter the shape of the cornea such that a bifocal or transitional eоАЪect is induced. Laser manufacturers have developed
speciоАЬc treatment algorithms based on the near-addition target
(or planned according to the subjectвАЩs age) to create a multifocal corneal proоАЬle. However, the eоАЪective change in refraction
comes from the controlled change in the corneal asphericity
from the centre to the periphery. оАЭe beneоАЬt of presbyLASIK
over monofocal ablation is not yet clear.
оАБ Scleral expansion surgery. Results have been inconsistent and
unpredictable and this technique has not achieved sustained
popularity.
оАБ Intracorneal inlays provide substantial beneоАЬt in presbyopia,
though in the past the biocompatibility of some materials has
been relatively poor and complications such as corneal haze
and extrusion can necessitate removal.

{ Types: (a) refractive corneal inlays (Fig. 8.15A and B), (b)
corneal reshaping inlays and (c) small aperture inlays (Fig.
8.15C and D).

{ Inlay position. оАЭe inlay can be placed either in a stromal
pocket or under a оАЫap. Advantages of a stromal pocket
include stable centration, minimal damage to corneal
nerves and corneal striae. оАЭe advantage of placing the
inlay under a оАЫap is that the procedure can be combined
with excimer laser ablation to correct refractive error.

{ Complications involve: (a) lens decentration, (b) dry eyes, (c)
keratolysis, (d) vascularization, (e) opaciоАЬcation and (f) epithelial
ingrowth. оАЭese complications are less likely to occur with modern implants as they are thin, have a small diameter and have high
permeability with improved biocompatibility characteristics.

{ Late side eоАЪects include: (a) haloes, glare and disturbance
of night vision, (b) loss of contrast sensitivity, (c) diоАЩculty
reading in dim light, (d) tear instability and (e) variable
vision.

{ вАШMonovisionвАЩ consists of the targeting of IOL-induced
refractive outcomes so that one eye (usually the dominant) is optimized for clear uncorrected distance vision
and the other for near or intermediate vision, in order to
facilitate both good distance and near vision when the eyes
are used together. Suppression of the blurred image and
neuro-adaptation by the patient is required for this to be
successful.
оАБ Conductive keratoplasty (see вАШCorrection of hypermetropiaвАЩ
above). оАЭere is some evidence that CK can impart a degree of
multifocal functionality to the cornea.
оАБ Laser-induced monovision refers to the use of laser refractive surgery to optimize one eye for distance and the fellow for near or intermediate vision (see above under вАШLens
extractionвАЩ).

Laser refractive procedures

To settle any contact lens-induced corneal distortion prior to
deоАЬnitive keratometry, soоАЮ contact lenses should probably be discontinued for 2 weeks and hard/rigid gas permeable lenses for at
least 3 weeks (some surgeons suggest 1 week for each year of wear
to date).

Laser in situ keratomileusis

Laser (or laser-assisted) in situ keratomileusis (LASIK) is a very
common refractive procedure. Refraction should be stable for at
least one year before LASIK surgery is undertaken. оАЭe excimer
laser, which can ablate tissue to a precise depth with negligible disruption of surrounding areas, is used to reshape corneal stroma
exposed by the creation of a superоАЬcial оАЫap. оАЭe оАЫap remains
attached by a hinge to facilitate accurate and secure repositioning. Myopia is corrected by central ablative оАЫattening and hypermetropia by ablation of the mid-periphery so that the centre
becomes steeper. LASIK can generally be used to treat higher
refractive errors than surface ablation techniques (see below):

hypermetropia up to 3-4 D, astigmatism up to 5 D and myopia
up to 6вАУ8 D depending on initial corneal thickness. For refractive
errors outside these parameters an alternative technique should be
considered. To decrease the risk of subsequent ectasia, a residual
corneal base at least 250 ќЉm thickness must remain aоАЮer ablation.
оАЭe amount of tissue removed, and therefore the amount of refractive error correctable, is limited by the original corneal thickness.
It follows that high refractive errors can be addressed only by an
intraocular procedure. In addition to treatment of a wider range of
refractive errors, advantages over surface ablation include greater
postoperative comfort, faster visual rehabilitation, more rapid stabilization of refraction and milder stromal haze. оАЭe major disadvantage is the potential for serious оАЫap-related complications.
оАБ Preoperative evaluation includes:

{ Corneal pachymetry to determine residual stromal bed
thickness and to exclude mild keratoconus.

{ Corneal topography to exclude irregular astigmatism, keratoconus, and forme fruste keratoconus.

{ Complete ocular examination.

{ Tear оАЬlm osmolarity to exclude dry eye syndrome.

288 Refractive Procedures

{ Corneal OCT to produce an epithelial map of the cornea.

bed and undersurface of the оАЫap. Ethanol or mitomycin C
can be applied, but this can result in adverse eоАЪects.

{ Corneal and whole eye wavefront aberrometry.
оАБ Technique

{ A suction ring centred on the cornea is applied to the globe
which raises the intraocular pressure substantially.

{ оАЭe ring stabilizes the eye and provides the guide track for
a mechanical microkeratome, which is advanced across
the cornea to create a thin оАЫap. In recent years, the creation
of the оАЫap is undertaken using a femtosecond laser, which
allows a precise and uniform оАЫap thickness of 100вАУ120 ќЉm
to be made.

{ оАЭe оАЫap is reоАЫected and the bed reshaped by the laser, followed by оАЫap repositioning (Fig. 8.16A).

{ Wavefront-guided LASIK is a variation of LASIK, where
the excimer laser ablates a precise pattern based on measurements from a wavefront aberrometer.

{ вАШOptomisedвАЩ LASIK treatment refers to an ablation algorithm, patented by some lasers in standard mode to produce larger optical zones and better corneal asphericity, in
order to reduce unwanted visual aberrations and postoperative glare.

{ DiоАЪuse lamellar keratitis (вАШsands of the SaharaвАЩ вАУ Fig. 8.16E)
may develop 1вАУ7 days following LASIK. It is characterized
by granular deposits at the оАЫap interface. Treatment is with
intensive topical antibiotic and steroid.

{ Bacterial keratitis (Fig. 8.16F) is rare. оАЭe organism must
be cultured and the sensitivity determined so that an
appropriate topical antibiotic can be prescribed.

{ Epithelial hyperplasia can result in regression and is diagnosed using an epithelial OCT map.

{ Pressure-induced stromal keratitis (PISK) is a late-onset
visible оАЫuid cleоАЮ which occurs in the interface as a result
of elevated IOP, usually secondary to prolonged topical
steroid use. оАЭis may present as an under-correction or
regression.

{ Corneal ectasia (see Ch. 7). оАЭis is a devastating complication which is fortunately uncommon (0.2вАУ0.6%). Ectasia is
more likely to occur when there is: (a) abnormal preoperative topography (e.g. forme fruste keratoconus or pellucid
marginal degeneration), (b) low preoperative central corneal thickness, (c) low post-ablation residual stromal bed
thickness, (d) greater percentage corneal tissue altered, (e)
high myopia, (f) young age. Careful pre-procedure screening should be performed to detect any predisposition.

{ Persistent epithelial defects.

{ LASIK Plus is a procedure where cross-linking is undertaken aоАЮer the laser treatment. It might be considered
in patients at risk of developing postoperative ectasia or
refractive regression (refractive error greater than вИТ7D).
A riboоАЫavin solution is applied to the stromal bed while
the оАЫap is open, followed by irrigation of the stromal bed,
replacement of the оАЫap and application of ultraviolet light.
оАБ Intraoperative complications include вАШbuttonholingвАЩ (penetration) or amputation of the оАЫap, incomplete or irregular оАЫap
creation and, rarely, penetration into the anterior chamber.
оАЭis is signiоАЬcantly less likely to occur when the оАЫap is created
with a femtosecond laser.
оАБ Early postoperative complications

TIP LASIK may cause transient symptoms of dry eye and
glare secondary to subepithelial haze.

Surface ablation procedures

{ Flap decentration.

{ Wrinkling (Fig. 8.16B), distortion or dislocation of the оАЫap.

{ Interface debris.
оАБ Late postoperative complications

{ Refractive stability can take up to 3 months depending on
the amount of treatment performed. Under-correction
(primary treatment) and over-correction (retreatment)
may occur.

{ Dry eye and tear оАЬlm instability are the commonest side
eоАЪects, occurring in up to 70%, but usually improve spontaneously in time.

{ Transient light sensitivity syndrome. оАЭis usually occurs
between 2 and 8 weeks aоАЮer surgery and is more likely to
occur aоАЮer hyperopic LASIK (9%) than myopic LASIK
(5%) or SMILE (1%). Symptoms settle on topical steroids.

{ Subepithelial haze (Fig. 8.16C) leading to symptoms of
glare, haloes and вАШstar bursts,вАЩ which are especially noticed
at night. оАЭis usually settles within 3вАУ6 months.

{ Epithelial ingrowth under the оАЫap (1вАУ2%) (Fig. 8.16D).
Treatment is not always required, but when the vision is
aоАЪected, repeat surgery should be considered. AоАЮer liоАЮing
the оАЫap the epithelial cells are scraped from the stromal

Like LASIK, photorefractive keratectomy (PRK) employs excimer
laser ablation to reshape the cornea. PRK is able to correct myopia
up to 6 D, astigmatism up to around 3 D and lowвАУmoderate hypermetropia. оАЭe main disadvantages compared with LASIK are the
lower degrees of refractive error correctable and slower epithelial
healing with unpredictable postoperative discomfort. However, as
a оАЫap is not created there is a lower risk of serious complications
than with LASIK, including corneal ectasia and late оАЫap dislocation and it may be the procedure of choice for patients at higher
than average occupational or leisure-related risk of eye injury. It is
also suitable for patients rendered ineligible for LASIK due to low
corneal thickness. Other indications for surface ablation rather
than LASIK include borderline dry eye syndrome, epithelial basement membrane disease, prior corneal transplantation or radial
keratotomy and large pupil size.
оАБ Technique

{ оАЭe corneal epithelium is removed prior to ablation.
Methods used may include a sponge, an automated brush
(Amoils epithelial scrubber) and alcohol.

{ Ablation of the Bowman layer and anterior stroma (Fig.
8.17) is performed, generally taking 30вАУ60 seconds. In
modern systems, sophisticated tracking mechanisms
adjust laser targeting with eye movement and will pause
the procedure if the eye is signiоАЬcantly decentred.

A B

Fig. 8.17 Photorefractive keratectomy (PRK). (A) Laser ablation of Bowman layer and anterior
stroma after removal of the epithelium. This results in reproоАЯling of the anterior curvature of

{ оАЭe epithelium usually heals within 48вАУ72 hours. A bandage contact lens is generally used to minimize discomfort.
Subepithelial haze invariably develops within 2 weeks and
commonly persists for several weeks to months. Diminished оАЬnal visual acuity is rare but there may be decreased
contrast and nocturnal glare. Intraoperative application
of mitomycin C (mitomycin-LASEK or M-LASEK) may
reduce haze.
оАБ Complications include slowly healing epithelial defects, corneal haze with blurring and haloes, poor night vision and
regression of refractive correction. Uncommon problems
include decentred ablation, scarring, abnormal epithelial healing, irregular astigmatism, reduced corneal sensation, sterile
inоАЬltrates, infection and acute corneal necrosis.
оАБ Variations of PRK. A range of procedural variations with
correspondingly varying terminology have been described.
LASEK (laser epithelial keratomileusis or laser-assisted subepithelial keratectomy), Epi-LASIK (epipolis or epithelial
LASIK; epipolis is a Greek word meaning superоАЬcial), modiоАЬed PRK, advanced surface (laser) ablation (ASA or ASLA)
and Trans-PRK (trans-epithelial PRK) are variations of PRK
that utilize a variety of techniques to try to reduce discomfort
and post-laser haze and to speed visual recovery. ASA and
modiоАЬed PRK are sometimes used generally to refer to all surface ablative procedures. In LASEK the epithelium is detached
and peeled back aоАЮer pre-treatment with dilute alcohol. Laser

is then applied and the epithelium repositioned. Epi-LASIK
employs a mechanical device, an epikeratome, to elevate a
hinged sheet of epithelium with an oscillating blunt plastic
blade and alcohol application is not usually required. Some
recent reports suggest that healing occurs more rapidly if the
epithelium is simply detached entirely without replacement
(оАЫap-oоАЪ Epi-LASIK). In Trans-PRK, epithelial ablation is performed with the laser prior to the refractive ablation, reducing
operative time and possibly conferring other beneоАЬts.

Small incision lenticule extraction (SMILE)

оАБ Small incision lenticule extraction вАУ SMILE is a technique
that uses a femtosecond laser (Carl Zeiss VisuMax) to cut a
lens-shaped piece of corneal tissue (a lenticule) within the
intact cornea. оАЭis is then removed using a minimally invasive
4 mm incision. When the lenticle is removed via a LASIK-style
оАЫap the procedure is termed refractive lenticular extraction
(ReLex).
оАБ Results are similar to those obtained with LASIK.
оАБ Advantages

{ Surface disturbance is minimal in comparison to surface
ablation procedures.

{ Low risk of оАЫap-related complications.

{ Biomechanical advantage.

{ Faster recovery of dry eye symptoms.

{ Quicker corneal reinnervation.

ANATOMY

оАЯe scleral stroma is composed of collagen bundles of varying size
and shape that are not uniformly orientated as in the cornea and
so are not transparent. оАЯe inner layer of the sclera blends with the
uveal tract. Anteriorly the episclera consists of a connective tissue layer between the superоАЮcial scleral stroma and Tenon capsule.
оАЯere are three pre-equatorial vascular layers:
оАБ Conjunctival vessels are the most superоАЮcial. Arteries are tortuous and veins straight.
оАБ SuperоАЯcial episcleral plexus vessels are straight with a radial
conоАЮguration. In episcleritis, maximal congestion occurs at
this level (Fig. 9.1A). Topical phenylephrine 2.5% will also
constrict the conjunctival and 10% also the superоАЮcial episcleral vessels.
оАБ Deep vascular plexus lies in the superоАЮcial part of the sclera
and shows maximal congestion in scleritis (Fig. 9.1B). A purplish hue, best seen in daylight, is characteristic.

EPISCLERITIS

Episcleritis is a common, usually idiopathic and benign, recurrent and frequently bilateral condition. Females may be aоАЭected
more commonly than males, except possibly in children, in whom
episcleritis is rare. оАЯe average patient is middle-aged. It is typically self-limiting and tends to last from a few days up to 3 weeks,
but rarely longer. Associated disease, either ocular (e.g. dry eye,
rosacea, contact lens wear) or systemic (e.g. collagen vascular disorders such as rheumatoid arthritis, herpes zoster ophthalmicus,
gout, Crohn disease and others), has been identiоАЮed in tertiary
referral centres. Infectious causes are very rare but a wide range
has been reported. Investigation of recurrent cases is as for scleritis
(see later).

TIP Acute episcleritis is common, usually idiopathic and has a
good visual prognosis.

Simple episcleritis

Simple episcleritis accounts for 75% of cases. It tends to recur
(60%), decreasing in frequency with time. Features oоАЬen peak
within 24 hours, gradually fading over the next few days.
оАБ Symptoms. Discomfort ranges from absent (up to 50%) to
moderate. Grittiness is common and photophobia may occur.
оАБ Signs. More than half of cases are simultaneously bilateral.

{ Visual acuity is normal.

{ Redness may be sectoral (Fig. 9.2A) or diоАЭuse (Fig. 9.2B).
OоАЬen it has an interpalpebral distribution, in a triangular
conоАЮguration with the base at the limbus.

{ Chemosis, ocular hypertension, anterior uveitis and keratitis are all rare.
оАБ Treatment

{ If mild, no treatment is required. Cool compresses or
refrigerated artiоАЮcial tears may be helpful.

A

B

Fig. 9.1 (A) Episcleritis with maximal vascular congestion of
the superоАЮcial episcleral plexus; (B) scleritis with scleral thickening and maximal vascular congestion of the deep vascular
plexus.

{ A weak topical steroid four times daily for 1вАУ2 weeks is
usually suоАЫcient, though occasionally more intensive
instillation is needed initially or a more potent preparation
can be used with rapid tapering. A topical non-steroidal
anti-inоАЪammatory (NSAID) is an alternative, though may
be less eоАЭective.

{ An oral NSAID is occasionally required (e.g. ibuprofen
200 mg three times daily, or occasionally a more potent
agent such as indomethacin).
