Nodular episcleritis

Nodular episcleritis also tends to aоАЭect females more than males
but has a less acute onset and a more prolonged course than the
simple variant.
оАБ Symptoms. A red eye is typically оАЮrst noted on waking. Over
the next 2вАУ3 days the area of redness enlarges and becomes
more uncomfortable.
оАБ Signs. Attacks usually clear without treatment, but tend to last
longer than simple episcleritis.

{ A tender red vascular nodule, almost always within the
interpalpebral оАЮssure (Fig. 9.3A). Occasionally more than
one focus is present.

{ A slit lamp section shows an underlying оАЪat anterior
scleral surface, indicating the absence of scleritis (Fig.
9.3B).

{ Intraocular pressure (IOP) is very occasionally elevated.

{ An anterior chamber reaction may be present but is
uncommon (10%).

{ AоАЬer several episodes, inоАЪamed vessels may become permanently dilated.

{ It is important to exclude other causes of a nodule such
as phlyctenulosis (a phlycten is within rather than beneath
the conjunctiva) or a conjunctival granuloma.
оАБ Treatment is similar to that of simple episcleritis.

IMMUNE-MEDIATED SCLERITIS

Scleritis is an uncommon condition characterized by oedema and
cellular inоАЮltration of the entire thickness of the sclera. Immunemediated (non-infectious) scleritis is the most common type and
is frequently associated with an underlying systemic inоАЪammatory condition, of which it may be the оАЮrst manifestation. Scleritis
is much less common than episcleritis and comprises a spectrum
from trivial and self-limiting disease to a necrotizing process that
can involve adjacent tissues and threaten vision. A classiоАЮcation of
non-infectious scleritis is shown in Table 9.1. Recurrences tend to be
of the same type, though 10% progress to more aggressive disease.

{ In contrast to episcleritis, a slit lamp beam shows an elevated anterior scleral surface.

{ Multiple nodules may expand and coalesce if treatment is

{ Instillation of 10% phenylephrine drops will constrict the

the deep plexus overlying the nodule.

{ As the inоАЪammation in the nodule subsides, increased
translucency of the sclera becomes apparent.

{ оАЯe duration of the disease is similar to diоАЭuse scleritis.

{ More than 10% of patients with nodular scleritis
develop necrotizing disease, but if treatment is instituted early superficial necrosis does not occur and the
nodule heals from the centre leaving a small atrophic
scar.

DiоАЭuse disease is slightly more common in females and usually
presents in the оАЮоАЬh decade.
оАБ Symptoms. Ocular redness progressing a few days later to
pain that may radiate to the face and temple. оАЯe discomfort
typically wakes the patient in the early hours of the morning,
improves later in the day and responds poorly to common
analgesics. оАЯe vision may be blurred.
оАБ Signs

{ Vascular congestion and dilatation associated with
oedema. If treatment is started early the disease can be
completely inhibited.

{ оАЯe redness may be generalized (Fig. 9.4A) or localized to
one quadrant. If conоАЮned to the area under the upper eyelid the diagnosis may be missed.

{ Secondary features can include chemosis, eyelid swelling,
anterior uveitis and raised IOP.

{ As the oedema resolves, the aоАЭected area oоАЬen takes on
a slight grey/blue appearance because of increased scleral
translucency (Fig. 9.4B). оАЯis is due to rearrangement of
scleral оАЮbres rather than a decrease in scleral thickness.

Anterior necrotizing scleritis with
inоАЮammation

Necrotizing disease is the aggressive form of scleritis. оАЯe age
at onset is later than that of non-necrotizing scleritis, averaging
60 years. оАЯe condition is bilateral in 60% of patients and unless
appropriately treated, especially in its early stages, may result in
severe visual morbidity and even loss of the eye.

TIP Necrotizing scleritis is a serious and aggressive form
of scleritis that requires systemic immunotherapy to control
inоАЯammation and to preserve vision.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Gradual onset of pain that becomes severe and
persistent and radiates to the temple, brow or jaw. It frequently
interferes with sleep and responds poorly to analgesia.
оАБ Signs vary according to the following three types of necrotizing disease.

{ Vaso-occlusive is commonly associated with rheumatoid
arthritis. Isolated patches of scleral oedema with overlying non-perfused episclera and conjunctiva are seen (Fig.
9.6A). оАЯe patches coalesce and if unchecked rapidly proceed to scleral necrosis (Fig. 9.6B).

{ Granuloma formation may occur in conjunction with
conditions such as granulomatosis with polyangiitis or
polyarteritis nodosa. оАЯe disease typically starts with
injection adjacent to the limbus and then extends posteriorly. Within 24 hours, the sclera, episclera, conjunctiva and
adjacent cornea become irregularly raised and oedematous
(Fig. 9.7).

{ Surgically induced scleritis typically starts within 3 weeks
of a procedure, though much longer intervals have been
reported. It may be induced by any type of surgery including strabismus repair, trabeculectomy with excessive exposure to mitomycin C (Fig. 9.8A), excision of pterygium
(Fig. 9.8B) and scleral buckling. оАЯe necrotizing process
starts at the site of surgery and extends outwards, but tends
to remain localized to one sector.

{ Recurrences at the same location are common unless an
underlying cause is treated.
оАБ Prognosis. оАЯe average duration of disease is around 6 years,
with the frequency of recurrences decreasing aоАЬer the оАЮrst 18
months. оАЯe long-term visual prognosis is good.

Nodular

оАЯe incidence of nodular and diоАЭuse anterior scleritis is the same
but a disproportionately large number of those with nodular disease have had a previous attack of herpes zoster ophthalmicus. оАЯe
age of onset is similar to that of diоАЭuse scleritis.
оАБ Symptoms. оАЯe insidious onset of pain followed by increasing
redness, tenderness of the globe and the appearance of a scleral
nodule. оАЯe vision is oоАЬen reduced.
оАБ Signs

{ Scleral nodules may be single or multiple and most frequently develop in the interpalpebral region close to the
limbus (Fig. 9.5A and B). оАЯey have a deeper blueвАУred
colour than episcleral nodules and are immobile.

Investigation

оАБ Laboratory. Tests should be employed as adjuncts to clinical
assessment and evaluation by a general physician or rheumatologist should be considered. SpeciоАЮc tests may include erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP),
full blood count (e.g. anaemia related to inоАЪammatory connective tissue disease, eosinophilia for polyarteritis nodosa, atopy
or ChurgвАУStrauss syndrome), rheumatoid factor, antinuclear
antibodies (ANA), antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies
(ANCA) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (CCP) antibodies, serum uric acid, syphilis serology, Lyme serology, hepatitis
B surface antigen (polyarteritis nodosa) and antiphospholipid
antibodies. Investigation for tuberculosis, sarcoidosis or ankylosing spondylitis may be appropriate (see Ch. 12).
оАБ Radiological imaging. Chest, sinus, joint and other imaging may
be indicated in the investigation of a range of conditions such as
tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, ChurgвАУStrauss syndrome, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, ankylosing spondylitis and other conditions.
оАБ Angiography. Fluorescein angiography of the anterior segment helps to distinguish necrotizing disease by the presence
of non-perfusion and can be used for monitoring. Occlusion
is predominantly venular in inоАЪammatory disease and mainly
arteriolar in scleromalacia perforans (see below). Indocyanine
green is a more accurate indicator of disease activity.
оАБ Ultrasonography can help to detect associated posterior scleritis (see below).
оАБ Biopsy. оАЯis may be considered in resistant cases, especially if
infection is suspected.

Complications of anterior scleritis

оАБ Acute inоАЯltrative stromal keratitis may be localized or diоАЭuse
(Fig. 9.9).
оАБ Sclerosing keratitis, characterized by chronic thinning and
opaciоАЮcation in which the peripheral cornea adjacent to the
site of scleritis resembles sclera.

A

B

Fig. 9.8 Surgically induced necrotizing scleritis. (A) After trabeculectomy with mitomycin C; (B) many years after pterygium
excision.

оАБ Peripheral ulcerative keratitis is characterized by progressive
melting and ulceration and may constitute a severe risk to the
integrity of the eye. In granulomatous scleritis the destruction
extends directly from the sclera into the limbus and cornea.
оАЯis characteristic pattern is seen in granulomatosis with
polyangiitis, polyarteritis nodosa and relapsing polychondritis. Peripheral corneal ulceration can occur at any stage of a
necrotizing scleritis and, in rare cases, precede its onset. (See
also Ch. 7.)
оАБ Uveitis, if severe, may denote aggressive scleritis.
оАБ Glaucoma is the most common cause of eventual loss of
vision. оАЯe intraocular pressure can be very diоАЫcult to control
in the presence of active scleritis.
оАБ Hypotony (rarely phthisis) may be the result of ciliary body
detachment, inоАЪammatory damage or ischaemia.
оАБ Perforation of the sclera as a result of the inоАЪammatory process alone is extremely rare.

Fig. 9.9 Peripheral inоАЮltrative stromal keratitis.

TIP Glaucoma is the commonest cause of visual loss in
anterior scleritis, so control of the IOP is critically important.

Scleromalacia perforans

оАБ Scleromalacia perforans (5% of scleritis) is a specific type
of progressive scleral thinning without inflammation that
typically affects elderly women with longstanding rheumatoid arthritis, but has also been described in association
with other systemic disorders. Despite the nomenclature,
perforation of the globe is rare as integrity is maintained by
a thin layer of fibrous tissue. Differential diagnosis is from
the innocuous scleral hyaline plaque and senile scleromalacia (see below).
оАБ Symptoms. Mild non-speciоАЮc irritation. Pain is absent and
vision unaоАЭected and keratoconjunctivitis sicca may be
suspected.
оАБ Signs

{ Necrotic scleral plaques near the limbus without vascular
congestion (Fig. 9.10A).

{ Coalescence and enlargement of necrotic areas.

{ Slow progression of scleral thinning, with exposure of
underlying uvea (Fig. 9.10B and C).
оАБ Treatment may be eоАЭective in patients with early scleromalacia, but is less useful in advanced disease.

{ No consistent beneоАЮt from any speciоАЮc agent has been
demonstrated. Frequent lubricant instillation, local or
systemic anticollagenase agents, immunosuppressives
(including topical and oral, but not periocular injection of,
steroids and topical ciclosporin) and biological blockers
have been used. (See below.)

Posterior scleritis

Posterior scleritis is a potentially blinding condition in which
diagnosis is commonly delayed, with an adverse prognostic
eоАЭect. оАЯe inоАЪammatory changes in posterior and anterior scleral
disease are identical and can arise in both segments simultaneously or separately. оАЯe age at onset is oоАЬen less than 40 years.
Young patients are usually otherwise healthy, but about a third
over the age of 55 have associated systemic disease.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Pain does not correlate well with the severity
of inоАЪammation but tends to be more severe in those with
accompanying orbital myositis. Photophobia is not a dominant feature.
оАБ Signs. оАЯe disease is bilateral in 35%.

{ Choroidal folds (see Ch. 14) are usually conоАЮned to the
posterior pole (Fig. 9.11A).

{ Exudative retinal detachment occurs in around 25%. оАЯe
yellowish-brown subretinal exudative material can be mistaken for a choroidal tumour.

{ Uveal eоАЭusion with choroidal detachment may be present
(Fig. 9.11B).

{ Disc oedema with accompanying reduction of vision is common and is caused by spread of inоАЪammation into the orbital
tissue and optic nerve. Treatment must not be delayed in
these patients as permanent visual loss can rapidly ensue.

{ Myositis is common and gives rise to diplopia, pain on
eye movement, tenderness to touch and redness around a
muscle insertion.

{ Proptosis is usually mild and is frequently associated with
ptosis.

Fig. 9.12 B-scan ultrasonography in posterior scleritis showing scleral thickening and оАЯuid in the sub-Tenon space
(arrow), with the characteristic вАШTвАЩ sign (see text).

oedema, choroidal folds and retinal detachment. Fluid in the
Tenon space may give a characteristic вАШTвАЩ sign, the stem of the
T being formed by the optic nerve and the cross bar by the
оАЪuid-containing gap (Fig. 9.12).
оАБ MR and CT may show scleral thickening and proptosis.

DiоАЯerential diagnosis

оАБ Subretinal mass. Alternative lesions include miscellaneous
granulomatous conditions and choroidal neoplasia.
оАБ Choroidal folds, retinal striae and disc oedema may also occur
in orbital tumours, orbital inоАЪammatory disease, thyroid eye
disease, papilloedema and hypotony.
оАБ Exudative retinal detachment. VogtвАУKoyanagiвАУHarada
(VKH) syndrome and central serous retinopathy.
оАБ Orbital cellulitis may cause proptosis and periocular oedema
but is associated with marked pyrexia.

{ Occasional features include raised IOP, periorbital oedema
and chemosis. Associated anterior scleritis is a useful diagnostic aid but occurs only in a minority.
оАБ Ultrasonography may show increased scleral thickness,
scleral nodules, separation of Tenon capsule from sclera, disc

Episclera and Sclera 299
#### 9

Important systemic associations of
scleritis
Rheumatoid arthritis

оАБ оАЯe autoimmune disease rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is the
most common systemic association of scleritis and is characterized by a symmetrical deforming inоАЪammatory polyarthropathy, with a spectrum of possible extra-articular
manifestations.
оАБ Presentation is commonly in the third decade with joint swelling, usually of the hands (Fig. 9.13A and B). It is much more
common in females than males.
оАБ Rheumatoid factor autoantibodies are present in 80вАУ90%.
оАБ All forms of immune-mediated scleritis have been described
in RA and the clinical course is oоАЬen more aggressive than
when there is no systemic association. Other ocular manifestations of RA include keratoconjunctivitis sicca (secondary
Sj√ґgren syndrome), ulcerative keratitis (see Fig. 7.29BвАУD) and
acquired superior oblique tendon sheath syndrome (very rare).

TIP Rheumatoid arthritis is the most common systemic
association of scleritis and can manifest with any form of
immune-mediated scleral inоАЯammation.

Granulomatosis with polyangiitis

оАБ Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener granulomatosis)
is an idiopathic multisystem granulomatous disorder characterized by small vessel vasculitis typically aоАЭecting primarily
the paranasal sinuses (Fig. 9.13C), lower respiratory tract, the
kidneys and skin (Fig. 9.13D).
оАБ оАЯere is a male predominance. Presentation is in the оАЮоАЬh
decade on average, oоАЬen with pulmonary symptoms.
оАБ Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (cANCA) are found in
over 90% of patients with active disease.
оАБ Scleritis is oоАЬen rapidly progressive, necrotizing and granulomatous. Other ocular manifestations include peripheral
ulcerative keratitis (see Fig.7.29E), occlusive retinal vasculitis,
orbital inоАЪammatory disease, nasolacrimal obstruction, dacrocystitis and, rarely, tarsalвАУconjunctival disease.

Relapsing polychondritis

оАБ Relapsing polychondritis is a rare idiopathic condition characterized by small vessel vasculitis involving cartilage resulting in
recurrent, oоАЬen progressive, inоАЪammatory episodes involving
multiple organ systems such as the ears (Fig. 9.13E), nasal cartilage (Fig. 9.13F), respiratory system, heart and joints.
оАБ Presentation is frequently in middle age.
оАБ Scleritis is oоАЬen intractable and may be necrotizing or nonnecrotizing. Isolated anterior uveitis may also occur.

Polyarteritis nodosa

оАБ Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN) is an idiopathic aneurysmal vasculitis aоАЭecting medium-sized and small arteries, with a
wide range of manifestations across multiple organ systems.

Without treatment the 5-year survival is about 10%. оАЯe
causes of death are kidney failure, myocardial infarction or
stroke. Modern immuno-suppressive treatment improves the
prognosis considerably.
оАБ Presentation is in the third to sixth decades, oоАЬen with constitutional symptoms. оАЯe male:female ratio is about 3:1.
оАБ Ocular involvement may precede the systemic manifestations
by several years. About one-third of patients have hepatitis B
infection. Scleritis is oоАЬen aggressive and necrotizing. Peripheral ulcerative keratitis, orbital pseudotumour and occlusive
retinal periarteritis are other reported ocular features.

Systemic lupus erythematosus

оАБ Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic multisystem
autoimmune inоАЪammatory disease. оАЯere is oоАЬen a family history, but no single causal gene has been identiоАЮed.
оАБ Females are aоАЭected far more oоАЬen than males (ratio of 9:1).
оАБ Antinuclear antibody testing is the key serological test in this
condition. If the test is negative, SLE can be excluded (sensitivity: 99%; speciоАЮcity: 49%).
оАБ Up to 70% of patients with SLE have skin involvement, including the characteristic вАШbutterоАЪyвАЩ rash on the cheeks, which
is oоАЬen induced by exposure to sunlight (Fig. 9.13G). Red
scaly discoid patches can be seen elsewhere on the skin and
hair loss may occur (Fig. 9.13H). Joint and muscle pain has
been reported in up to 90%, but does not usually result in
joint destruction. оАЯe heart, lungs, kidneys and brain may be
aоАЭected.
оАБ Ocular involvement is found in up to one-third of patients.
оАЯe most common manifestation is dry eye syndrome and secondary Sj√ґgren syndrome in 25%. Retinopathy occurs in up
to 10% and is linked to the severity of the condition, indicating inadequate control of the systemic disease. Ischaemic optic
neuropathy and bilateral optic neuritis are uncommon features
of SLE. оАЯe incidence of episcleritis and scleritis is 1% and may
be the presenting feature of the disease, usually manifesting
with anterior diоАЭuse or anterior nodular scleritis.
оАБ Because of the long-term use of hydroxychloroquine treatment, maculopathy may follow in some patients (see Ch. 21).

Crohn disease

оАБ Crohn disease is a chronic, relapsing inоАЪammatory bowel disease of unknown cause.
оАБ Extraintestinal complications occur frequently during the
course of the disease. оАЯe commonest of these are peripheral
arthritis and erythema nodosum.
оАБ оАЯe eye is involved in 4% to 10% of patients and a wide spectrum of inоАЪammatory conditions may be found. Acute episcleritis, acute anterior uveitis and marginal keratitis (see Fig. 9.9)
are the commonest manifestation, although conjunctivitis,
optic neuritis, orbital inоАЪammatory disease and retinal vasculitis have been reported. Acute anterior scleritis is rare (see
Fig. 9.5B) and may be complicated by the development of scleromalacia perforans. Acute episcleritis and increased bowel
activity show a close correlation.

Treatment of immuneоАЯmediated scleritis

оАБ Topical steroids do not aоАЭect the natural history of the scleral
inоАЪammation, but may relieve symptoms and oedema in nonnecrotizing disease.
оАБ Systemic NSAIDs should be used on their own only in nonnecrotizing disease. It is oоАЬen necessary to try a number of different drugs before оАЮnding one that provides adequate relief of
symptoms. A cyclooxygenase (COX)-2 inhibitor may be preferred in elderly patients or if there is a history of peptic ulceration, but can result in cardiovascular side eоАЭects. Patients should
be warned of the risk of gastrointestinal tract haemorrhage.
оАБ Periocular steroid injections may be used in non-necrotizing
disease but their eоАЭects are usually transient and should not be
used in necrotizing scleritis.
оАБ Systemic steroids (e.g. prednisolone is 1вАУ1.5 mg/kg daily) are
used when NSAIDs are inappropriate or inadequate (necrotizing disease). Intravenous methylprednisolone (0.5вАУ1 g daily
for 3 days) may be used initially for severe cases.
оАБ Immunosuppressives and/or immunomodulatory agents
should be considered if control is (a) incomplete with steroids
alone, (b) as a steroid-sparing measure in long-term treatment,
or (c) to treat underlying systemic disease. Patients with scleritis secondary to the following conditions: granulomatosis with
polyangiitis, polyarteritis nodosa, RA (with necrotizing scleritis) and relapsing polychondritis, should be oоАЭered immediate
treatment with immunomodulatory agents (see Ch. 12). A wide
range of drugs are available, including cytostatics (e.g. cyclophosphamide, azathioprine, methotrexate), drugs acting on
immunophilins (e.g. ciclosporin, tacrolimus) and biologicals. In
necrotizing disease, rituximab is particularly eоАЭective.

TIP Rituximab is particularly eоАЭective in the treatment of
necrotizing scleritis.

PORPHYRIA

Scleritis is a rare complication of congenital erythropoietic porphyria and two of the adult forms of porphyria: porphyria

variegata and porphyria cutanea tarda. Overproduction and
excessive storage of porphyrins occur in the liver. оАЯese highly
photoactive chromophores are also deposited in the dermis and
sclera, which are subsequently stimulated by exposure to the sun.
оАЯe sclera becomes thin or excavated over a sharply demarcated
area localized to the sun-exposed interpalpebral region adjacent to
the cornea (Fig. 9.14). Acute diоАЭuse anterior scleritis and posterior
scleritis may occur. Patients should avoid sunlight and undergo
phlebotomy.

INFECTIOUS SCLERITIS

Infectious scleritis is rare, but may present diagnostic diоАЫculty as the
initial clinical features are similar to those of immune-mediated disease. In some cases, infection may follow surgical or accidental trauma,
endophthalmitis, or may occur as an extension of corneal infection.

Causes

оАБ Herpes zoster is the most common infective cause. Necrotizing scleritis is extremely resistant to treatment and may result
in a thinned or punched-out area (Fig. 9.15A and B).
оАБ Tuberculous scleritis is rare and diоАЫcult to diagnose. оАЯe sclera
may be infected by direct spread from a local conjunctival or
choroidal lesion, or more commonly by haematogenous spread.
Involvement may be nodular (Fig. 9.15C) or necrotizing.
оАБ Leprosy. Recurrent necrotizing scleritis can occur, even aоАЬer
apparent systemic cure. Nodular disease may be seen in lepromatous leprosy.
оАБ Syphilis. DiоАЭuse anterior scleritis may occur in secondary
syphilis and occasionally scleral nodules may be a feature of
tertiary syphilis.
оАБ Lyme disease. Scleritis (Fig. 9.15D) is common but typically
occurs long aоАЬer initial infection.
оАБ Other causes include fungi, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and
Nocardia.

Treatment

Once the infective agent has been identiоАЮed, speciоАЮc antimicrobial therapy should be initiated. Topical and systemic steroids may
also be used to reduce the inоАЪammatory reaction. If appropriate,

SCLERAL DISCOLORATION

Alkaptonuria

In this autosomal recessive condition, a defect in homogentisic
acid oxidase results in the accumulation of homogentisic acid
in collagenous tissues such as cartilage and tendon (ochronosis). Ocular manifestations include bluish-grey or black generalized pigmentation of the sclera and the tendons of horizontal
recti associated with discrete pigmented globules (Fig. 9.16).
Systemic features include dark urine and arthropathy.

Haemochromatosis

оАЯe systemic features of haemochromatosis are caused by
increased iron deposition in various tissues. Features may be more

Fig. 9.16 Alkaptonuria: pigmentation (ochronosis) of the
sclera and horizontal rectus tendons.

subtle than the classic triad of a bronze complexion, hepatomegaly and diabetes. Inheritance is autosomal recessive. Dry eye and
rusty-brown perilimbal conjunctival and scleral discoloration may
develop.

BLUE SCLERA

Blue scleral discoloration is caused by thinning or transparency
with resultant visualization of the underlying uvea (Fig. 9.17A).
Major associations are discussed below. Rare associations
include MarshallвАУSmith syndrome (accelerated prenatal skeletal maturation and growth), RussellвАУSilver syndrome (short
stature and other features) and HallermannвАУStreiffвАУFran√Іois
syndrome.

Osteogenesis imperfecta

Osteogenesis imperfecta is an inherited disease of connective tissue, usually caused by defects in the synthesis and structure of type

1 collagen. оАЯere are multiple types, at least two of which have ocular features.
оАБ Type I is autosomal dominant. Patients suоАЭer few fractures
with little or no deformity, hyperextensible joints, dental hypoplasia, deafness and easy bruising. Possible ocular features
include blue sclera, megalocornea and corneal arcus.
оАБ Type IIA is either sporadic or inherited in an autosomal
dominant manner. Systemic features include deafness, dental
anomalies, multiple fractures (Fig. 9.17B) and short limbs,
with death in infancy from respiratory infection. Ocular manifestations include blue sclera and shallow orbits.

EhlersвАУDanlos syndrome type VI

EhlersвАУDanlos syndrome VI (ocular sclerotic) is an inherited disorder of collagen formation. Patients have thin and hyper-elastic
skin (Fig. 9.17C) that bruises easily and heals slowly. оАЯe joints
are hypermobile (Fig. 9.17D), which may lead to recurrent dislocation and falls. Cardiovascular disease can be severe, including a

304 Miscellaneous Conditions

bleeding diathesis, dissecting aneurysms, spontaneous rupture of
large blood vessels and mitral valve prolapse. оАЯere are six major
types but type VI and, rarely, type IV, are associated with ocular
features. As well as blue sclera, other manifestations include scleral
fragility (globe rupture may be caused by mild trauma), epicanthic folds, microcornea, keratoconus, keratoglobus, ectopia lentis,
myopia and retinal detachment.

MISCELLANEOUS CONDITIONS

Congenital ocular melanocytosis

Congenital ocular melanocytosis is an uncommon condition
characterized by an increase in number, size and pigmentation
of melanocytes in the sclera and uvea. оАЯe periocular skin, orbit,
meninges and soоАЬ palate may also be involved.
оАБ Ocular melanocytosis, the least common, involves only the
eye. Multifocal slate-grey pigmentation is seen within the
sclera and episcleraвАУ the process does not involve the overlying conjunctival layers, unless there is incidental conjunctival
pigmentation. оАЯe overlying conjunctiva is mobile over the
episcleral pigmentation but the pigmentation itself is intrinsic
and cannot be moved over the globe. оАЯe peripheral cornea is
occasionally involved.
оАБ Dermal melanocytosis (one-third) involves only the skin.
оАБ Oculodermal melanocytosis (naevus of Ota) is the most common type and involves both skin and eye. Naevus of Ota is
bilateral in 5%. It occurs frequently in darker-complexioned
races but is rare in White people. оАЯere is deep bluish hyperpigmentation of the facial skin, most frequently in the distribution of the оАЮrst and second trigeminal divisions (Fig.
9.18AвАУC). It may be subtle in pale-skinned individuals, when
it is best detected under good lighting.
оАБ Ipsilateral associations

{ Iris hyperchromia is common (see Fig. 9.18A).

{ Iris mammillations are uncommon, tiny, regularly spaced
villiform lesions (Fig. 9.18D). оАЯey may also be found in
neuroоАЮbromatosis type 1, AxenfeldвАУRieger anomaly and
Peters anomaly.

{ Fundus hyperpigmentation (Fig. 9.18E).

{ Trabecular hyperpigmentation (Fig. 9.18F). оАЯis is associated with glaucoma in about 10% of cases.

{ Uveal melanoma develops in a small minority of patients
and long-term anterior and posterior segment review is
required.

TIP Patients with oculodermal melanosis are at risk of
developing glaucoma and occasionally uveal melanoma.

Idiopathic sclerochoroidal calciоАЭcation

Idiopathic sclerochoroidal calciоАЮcation is an innocuous, agerelated condition that usually involves both eyes of an aоАЭected
older adult.
оАБ Signs. Geographical yellowвАУwhite fundus lesions with illdeоАЮned margins (Fig. 9.19), oоАЬen multiple and located in the
superotemporal or inferotemporal mid-periphery associated
with the vascular arcades.
оАБ Ultrasonography shows highly reоАЪective choroidal plaquelike lesions with orbital shadowing.
оАБ Differential diagnosis is mainly from osseous metaplasia associated with a choroidal haemangioma and from
choroidal osteoma, which is usually a single вАУ though
often large вАУ lesion that usually (80вАУ90%) involves only
one eye.

Scleral hyaline plaque and senile
scleromalacia

Scleral hyaline plaques are oval dark-greyish, generally sharply
demarcated, areas located close to the insertion of the horizontal
rectus muscles (Fig. 9.20). Senile scleromalacia refers to a spontaneously occurring irregular, oval or kidney-shaped partialthickness scleral defect found at the same location, typically with
one or more scleral hyaline plaques at the opposite location in the
same eye or in the fellow eye. Separation of a scleral hyaline plaque
to leave an area of scleromalacia has been described. Both these
entities typically aоАЭect elderly patients, are innocuous and should
not be confused with scleromalacia perforans (see above), which
occurs in somewhat younger patients, may be located anywhere
in the anterior sclera, is not associated with hyaline plaques elsewhere and can progress to a full-thickness scleral defect with uveal
exposure.

308 Acquired Cataract

INTRODUCTION

оАЮe primary function of the lens is to focus light on the retina.
оАБ Presbyopia is the gradual loss of the ability of the lens to focus
on near objects. It is an inevitable ageing phenomenon which
occurs as a consequence of loss of accommodation secondary
to reduced lens elasticity. Spectacles will usually correct the
refractive error, but surgical treatment has become increasingly relevant in recent years.
оАБ Cataract (lens opaciоАЯcation) is the commonest cause of
reversible visual disability in the world. оАЮere is no medical treatment to prevent the development or progression of
cataract. However, phacoemulsiоАЭcation with intraocular lens
implantation is extremely successful in restoring vision and is
the commonest surgical procedure undertaken in the world.

Anatomy

оАБ Zonules hold the lens in position (Fig. 10.1A).
оАБ оАЮe capsule is an acellular membrane that encloses the lens
material.
оАБ Cuboid epithelial cells are located beneath the anterior capsule and extend to the lens equator; cells in the germinative
zone divide continuously and diоАЬerentiate to form lens оАЭbers
(Fig. 10.1B).
оАБ оАЮe lens substance is made up of a central nucleus surrounded
by corticular material.

EоАЯect on vision

Cataracts oоАЫen develop slowly and in the early stages the vision
alters subtly without the person being aware of the change. оАЮe eоАЬect
on vision depends on: (a) the type of cataract, (b) the extent of opaciоАЭcation and (c) the position of the opaciоАЭcation. Symptoms include:
оАБ Reduced visual acuity, which initially can be corrected with
spectacles.
оАБ Loss of contrast sensitivity.
оАБ Change in colour perception, particularly an inability to
accurately perceive blue colours.
оАБ Glare secondary to light scattering, causing diоАЪculty with
night driving.
оАБ Monocular diplopia, especially when looking at a point
source of light.
оАБ DiоАЭculty seeing in low-light situations, including poor night
vision.

ACQUIRED CATARACT

Age-related cataract
Subcapsular cataract

Anterior subcapsular cataract lies directly under the lens capsule
and is associated with оАЭbrous metaplasia of the lens epithelium. Posterior subcapsular opacity lies just in front of the posterior capsule
and has a granular or plaque-like appearance on oblique slit lamp

biomicroscopy (Fig. 10.2A), but typically appears black and vacuolated on retroillumination (Fig. 10.2B). оАЮe vacuoles are swollen
migratory lens epithelial cells (bladder or Wedl), similar to those
commonly seen postoperatively in posterior capsular opaciоАЭcation.
Due to its location at the nodal point of the eye, a posterior subcapsular opacity oоАЫen has a particularly profound eоАЬect on vision.
Patients are characteristically troubled by glare, for instance from the
headlights of oncoming cars, and symptoms are increased by miosis,
such as occurs during near visual activity and in bright sunlight.

Nuclear sclerotic cataract

Nuclear cataract is an exaggeration of normal ageing change (Fig.
10.2C). It is oоАЫen associated with myopia due to an increase in the
refractive index of the nucleus, resulting in some elderly patients
being able to read without spectacles again (вАШsecond sight of the
agedвАЩ). In contrast, in the healthy ageing eye (and in occasional
cases of cortical and subcapsular cataract) there is a mild hypermetropic shiоАЫ. Nuclear sclerotic cataract is characterized by a yellowish hue due to the deposition of urochrome pigment and is best
assessed with an oblique slit lamp beam. Retroillumination will
show a good red reоАЩex, but careful observation will reveal a subtle
distinction between the nucleus and cortex (Fig. 10.2D). When
advanced, the nucleus appears brown and, in rare cases, black.

Cortical cataract

Cortical cataract may involve the anterior, posterior or equatorial
cortex. оАЮe opacities start as cleоАЫs and vacuoles between lens оАЭbres
due to cortical hydration. Subsequent opaciоАЭcation results in typical cuneiform (wedge-shaped) or radial spoke-like opacities (Fig.
10.2E and F), oоАЫen found initially in the inferonasal quadrant. As
with posterior subcapsular opacity, glare is a common symptom.

Christmas tree cataract

Christmas tree cataract, which is uncommon, is characterized
by polychromatic needle-like formations in the deep cortex and
nucleus (Fig. 10.2G and H).

310 Acquired Cataract

Cataract maturity

оАБ Immature cataract is one in which the lens is partially opaque.
оАБ Mature cataract is one in which the lens is completely opaque
(Fig. 10.3A and B).
оАБ Hypermature cataract has a shrunken and wrinkled anterior
capsule (Fig. 10.3C) due to leakage of water out of the lens.
оАБ Morgagnian cataract is a hypermature cataract in which liquefaction of the cortex has allowed the nucleus to sink inferiorly
(Fig. 10.3D).

Cataract in systemic disease

Diabetes mellitus

Hyperglycaemia is reоАЩected in a high level of glucose in the aqueous humour, which diоАЬuses into the lens. Here glucose is metabolized into sorbitol, which accumulates within the lens, resulting in
secondary osmotic overhydration. In mild degree, this may aоАЬect
the refractive index of the lens with consequent оАЩuctuation of

refraction in line with the plasma glucose level, hyperglycaemia
resulting in myopia and vice versa. Cortical оАЩuid vacuoles develop
and later evolve into frank opacities. Classic diabetic cataract,
which is rare, consists of snowоАЩake cortical opacities (Fig. 10.4A)
occurring in the young. It may mature within a few days or resolve
spontaneously. Age-related cataract occurs sooner in people with
diabetes mellitus. Nuclear sclerosis is common and tends to progress rapidly. Around one in оАЭve people who undergo cataract surgery have diabetes. оАЮe presence of diabetic macular oedema prior
to cataract surgery is associated with worsening of the oedema,
resulting in postoperative visual loss. оАЮe retina needs to be monitored before and aоАЫer cataract surgery, even in patients with minimal oedema.

Myotonic dystrophy

About 90% of patients with myotonic dystrophy (see Ch. 19)
develop оАЭne iridescent cortical opacities in the third decade, sometimes resembling Christmas tree cataract. оАЮese evolve into visually disabling wedge-shaped cortical and subcapsular opacities,
oоАЫen star-like in conformation (Fig. 10.4B), by the оАЭоАЫh decade.

Later, the opacities may become indistinguishable from typical

Atopic dermatitis

About 10% of patients with severe atopic dermatitis develop cataracts in the second to fourth decades. оАЮese are oоАЫen bilateral and
may mature quickly. Shield-like dense anterior subcapsular plaque
that wrinkles the anterior capsule (Fig. 10.4C) is characteristic.
Posterior subcapsular opacities may also occur.

NeuroоАЯbromatosis type 2

NeuroоАЭbromatosis type 2 (see Ch. 19) is associated with early
cataract in more than 60% of patients. Opacities are posterior subcapsular (Fig. 10.4D) or capsular, cortical or mixed and tend to
develop in early adulthood.

Secondary cataract

A secondary (complicated) cataract develops as a result of other
primary ocular disease.

Chronic anterior uveitis

Chronic anterior uveitis is the most common cause of secondary
cataract, the incidence being related to the duration and intensity of
inоАЩammation. Topical and systemic steroids used in treatment are
also causative. оАЮe earliest оАЭnding is oоАЫen a polychromatic lustre at
the posterior pole of the lens. If inоАЩammation persists, posterior and
anterior opacities (Fig. 10.5A) develop. Cataract appears to progress
more rapidly in the presence of posterior synechiae (Fig. 10.5B).

Acute congestive angle closure

Acute congestive angle closure may cause small anterior grey-white
subcapsular or capsular opacities, glaukomоАЩecken (Fig. 10.5C), to
form within the pupillary area. оАЮese represent focal infarcts of the
lens epithelium and are pathognomonic of previous acute congestive angle closure.

High myopia

High (pathological) myopia can be associated with posterior subcapsular lens opacity and early-onset nuclear sclerosis, which ironically may increase the myopic refractive error.

312 Acquired Cataract

Hereditary fundus dystrophies

Hereditary fundus dystrophies (see Ch. 15) such as retinitis pigmentosa, Leber congenital amaurosis, gyrate atrophy and Stickler syndrome are usually associated with posterior subcapsular lens opacity.
Anterior subcapsular lens opacity is rare (Fig. 10.5D) because surgery
is usually undertaken at an early stage, when the opaciоАЭcation is limited to the posterior subcapsular region. Cataract surgery tends to
improve visual function even in the presence of severe retinal changes.

Secondary to medication

Systemic and topical steroids can lead to cataract formation. оАЮese
are usually posterior subcapsular in appearance and can be visually debilitating. Despite a good visual acuity, symptoms of glare
and reduced near vision are pronounced (see Ch. 21). Long-term
use of chlorpromazine leads to an anterior star-shaped lens opacity
(see Fig. 21.2B).

Traumatic cataract

Trauma is the most common cause of unilateral cataract in young
people.
оАБ Penetrating trauma (Fig. 10.6A and B).
оАБ Blunt trauma may cause a characteristic оАЩower-shaped opacity (Fig. 10.6C).
оАБ Electric shock is a rare cause of cataract, patterns including
diоАЬuse milky-white opaciоАЭcation and multiple snowоАЩake-like
opacities, sometimes in a stellate subcapsular distribution (Fig.
10.6D).
оАБ Infrared radiation, if intense as in glassblowers, may rarely
cause true exfoliation of the anterior lens capsule (Fig. 10.6E).
оАБ Ionizing radiation exposure such as for ocular tumour treatment may cause posterior subcapsular opacities (Fig. 10.6F).
оАЮese may not manifest for months or years.

314 Management of Acquired Cataract

MANAGEMENT OF ACQUIRED
CATARACT

Preoperative considerations
Indications for surgery

оАБ Visual improvement is by far the most common indication
for cataract surgery. An operation is indicated when the opacity develops to a degree suоАЪcient to cause diоАЪculty in performing daily activities. Clear lens exchange (replacement of
the healthy lens with an artiоАЭcial implant) is an option for the
management of refractive error.
оАБ Medical indications are those in which a cataract is adversely
aоАЬecting the health of the eye. Examples include phacolytic
or phacomorphic glaucoma. Cataract surgery to improve the
clarity of the ocular media may also be required in the context
of monitoring or treatment of fundus pathology.

Systemic preoperative assessment

For elective surgery, a general medical history is taken and any
problems managed accordingly. Table 10.1 sets out suggested further enquiry and action in relation to a range of systemic diseases.
Routine preoperative general medical examination, blood tests and
electrocardiogram (ECG) are not usually required for local anaesthesia. If general anaesthesia is planned, assessment is according
to local protocol, e.g. general examination, urea and electrolytes,
random blood glucose, full blood count and ECG. An anaesthetic
opinion may be considered for chronically unwell patients or those
with complex medical problems.
оАБ Current medication should be recorded. оАЮis will oоАЫen guide
general medical assessment. Medications relevant to eye surgery include:

{ Systemic alpha-blockers (e.g. tamsulosin) are commonly
associated with intraoperative оАЩoppy iris syndrome
(IFIS).

{ Management of anticoagulant therapy or an antiplatelet
agent should follow local protocol. Most surgeons do not
stop antiplatelet drugs for cataract surgery, though this
may be preferred for oculoplastic procedures and glaucoma surgery. Anticoagulation status, usually expressed as
the international normalized ratio (INR) level, should be
within the therapeutic range appropriate for the individual
indication (e.g. usually higher for heart valve thrombosis
prophylaxis than following deep vein thrombosis). A common approach is to check the INR within the 24 hours
prior to surgery in stable patients.
оАБ Allergy. True allergy rather than intolerance should be
conоАЭrmed.

{ Medication, including sulfonamides and antibiotics commonly used following cataract surgery.

{ Iodine or shellоАЭsh вАУ the latter may indicate an iodine allergy.
If allergy to iodine is present an alternative skin and conjunctival antiseptic such as chlorhexidine should be used.

{ Others: allergy to latex (latex-free gloves may be necessary), sticking plaster, local anaesthetics, insect bites

(cross-reaction with hyaluronidase that is oоАЫen used with
local anaesthesia).
оАБ Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) carriage. Relevant national and local protocols for the identiоАЭcation and management of patients at high risk for MRSA
carriage should be followed.
оАБ Transport (to hospital and to the operating theatre within
hospital): special arrangements may be needed for patients
with poor mobility or exceptionally high body mass.

TIP Systemic alpha-blockers (e.g. tamsulosin) are the main
cause of intraocular оАЭoppy iris syndrome when undertaking
phacoemulsiоАЮcation.

Ophthalmic preoperative assessment

A detailed and pertinent ophthalmic evaluation is required. оАЮe
following should be considered:
оАБ Visual acuity is usually tested using a Snellen chart.
оАБ Cover test. A heterotropia may indicate amblyopia, which carries a guarded visual prognosis, or the possibility of diplopia
if the vision is improved. A squint, usually a divergence, may
develop in an eye with poor vision due to cataract and lens
surgery alone may straighten the eye.
оАБ Pupillary responses. Because cataract never produces an
aоАЬerent pupillary defect, its presence implies substantial posterior pole pathology.
оАБ Ocular adnexa. Dacryocystitis, blepharitis, chronic conjunctivitis, lagophthalmos, ectropion, entropion and tear оАЭlm abnormalities may predispose to endophthalmitis. оАЮese conditions
should be dealt with before intraocular surgery.
оАБ Cornea. Eyes with decreased endothelial cell counts (e.g.
substantial cornea guttata) have increased vulnerability to
postoperative decompensation. Specular microscopy and
pachymetry may be helpful in assessing risk and precautions
should be taken to protect the endothelium (see below). A
prominent arcus senilis is oоАЫen associated with a surgical view
of decreased clarity, as are stromal opacities.
оАБ Anterior chamber. A shallow anterior chamber can render
cataract surgery diоАЪcult. Recognition of a poorly dilating
pupil allows intensive preoperative mydriatic drops, planned
mechanical dilatation prior to capsulorhexis and/or intracameral injection of mydriatic. A poor red reоАЩex compromises the
creation of a capsulorhexis, but can be largely overcome by
staining the capsule with trypan blue.
оАБ Lens. Nuclear cataracts tend to be hard and may require more
power for phacoemulsiоАЭcation, while cortical and subcapsular opacities tend to be soоАЫer. Black nuclear opacities are
extremely dense and extracapsular cataract extraction rather
than phacoemulsiоАЭcation may be the preferred option. Pseudoexfoliation indicates a likelihood of weak zonules (phakodonesis вАУ lens wobble вАУ may be present), a fragile capsule and
poor mydriasis.
оАБ Fundus examination. Pathology such as age-related macular
degeneration may aоАЬect the visual outcome. Ultrasonography
may be required, principally to exclude retinal detachment

оАБ Sclera. If a prominent explant/encircling band has been placed

316 Management of Acquired Cataract

IOL or a speciоАЭc adjunctive procedure. It is particularly important to obtain a postoperative refractive result from an eye previously operated upon so that any вАШrefractive surpriseвАЩ, even if
minor, can be considered.

TIP Careful preoperative examination of the posterior pole
is needed in a patient requiring cataract surgery to exclude
unrelated pathology that may aоАЯect the оАЮnal visual result.

General principles of informed consent

оАБ Consent can be defined as вАШthe process of providing information that enables the patient to make an informed decision to undergo a specific treatmentвАЩ. The process takes
time, patience and clarity of explanation. It is important to
establish whether the patient has the capacity to give consent and has the ability to understand, retain, weigh-up
information and make a decision without duress. A вАШcompetentвАЩ person has the right to refuse treatment, even if by
refusing the proposed treatment it could result in visual disability, blindness or death.
оАБ Consent for cataract surgery. It is essential that the patient
arrives at a fully informed decision before proceeding to cataract surgery. It is useful to provide written information and a
link to an online video of the surgery. As well as discussing
the beneоАЭts, risks should be conveyed at a level appropriate to
each patientвАЩs level of understanding, with an explanation of
the more common and severe potential problems.
оАБ Risk. Two legal principles are relevant in the United Kingdom:

eye drops, intraocular pressure (IOP) spike, iridocyclitis,
posterior capsular opaciоАЭcation.

{ ModerateвАУsevere but less common complications: posterior capsular rupture/vitreous loss (1% or less for
experienced surgeons, higher for trainees dependent on
experience), zonular dehiscence, dropped nucleus (about
0.2%), leaking wound, cystoid macular oedema (CMO),
corneal decompensation suоАЪcient to need corneal graоАЫ,
unexpected refractive outcome (may need contact lens
wear, lens implant exchange or corneal surgery), retinal
detachment (<1%), IOL dislocation, persistent ptosis and
diplopia.

{ Rare but invariably very serious complications: endophthalmitis (0.1%) and suprachoroidal haemorrhage (0.04%).

{ оАЮe risks of anaesthesia should be conveyed by the person administering it. Local anaesthesia carries a low risk
of problems. Some rare complications have the potential
to be very serious including loss of the eye and even death:
allergy to the anaesthetic agent, retrobulbar haemorrhage
(see Ch. 22), perforation of the globe and inadvertent infusion of anaesthetic agent into the cerebrospinal оАЩuid via
the optic nerve sheath, causing brainstem anaesthesia.

{ Risk should be disclosed if a reasonable body of medical
opinion would consider it important to disclose the risk.

{ If a reasonable person in the patientвАЩs position would
attach signiоАЭcance to the risk, they should be told about it.
оАБ Points for discussion with the patient may include:

{ Immediate sequential bilateral cataract surgery (ISBCS)
versus delayed sequential bilateral cataract surgery. оАЮe
option of ISBCS is subject to debate because of the potential risk of bilateral sight-threatening complications, especially endophthalmitis. However, large trials have shown
no diоАЬerence between the two alternatives in respect
of visual acuity or complications at 1 month and 1 year
postoperatively. Meticulous selection and preparation of
appropriate cases is essential.

{ оАЮe merits and disadvantages of the various refractive
options and in particular, the use of a multifocal or toric IOL.

{ Reassurance that most cataract operations are straightforward with good visual results.

{ Complications can be dealt with eоАЬectively and cause no
long-term diоАЪculties, but that some rare problems can be
very serious and may mean that a second operation will
be necessary.

{ A warning that in about 1 in 1000 cataract operations the
eye will be leоАЫ with little or no sight; in about 1 in 10,000
the patient will lose the eye.

{ Relatively mild and usually easily treatable but common
complications include periocular ecchymosis, allergy to

Biometry

Biometry facilitates calculation of the lens power likely to result in
the desired postoperative refractive outcome. In its basic form this
involves the measurement of two ocular parameters, keratometry
and axial (anteroposterior) length.
оАБ Keratometry involves determination of the curvature of the
anterior corneal surface (steepest and flattest meridians),
expressed in dioptres or in millimetres of radius of curvature. This is commonly carried out with the interferometry
apparatus used to determine axial length (see below), but if
this is unavailable or unsuitable, manual keratometry (e.g.
JavalвАУSchi√Єtz keratometer) or corneal topography can be
performed.
оАБ Optical coherence biometry (Fig. 10.7A) is a non-contact
method of axial length measurement that utilizes two coaxial
partially coherent low-energy laser beams to produce an interference pattern (partial coherence interferometry). Modern
biometry devices also perform keratometry, anterior chamber
depth and corneal white-to-white measurement and are able
to calculate IOL power using a range of formulae. Measurements have high reproducibility and generally require less skill
than ultrasonic biometry (see below).
оАБ A-scan ultrasonography is a slightly less accurate method of
determining the axial dimension and can be acquired either
by direct contact (Fig. 10.7B) or more accurately (but with
greater diоАЪculty) by using a water bath over the eye (immersion ultrasonography). оАЮe sound beam must be aligned with
the visual axis for maximal precision. Each reоАЩecting surface
is represented by a spike on an oscilloscope display monitor
(Fig. 10.7C).
оАБ IOL power calculation formulae. Numerous formulae have
been developed that utilize keratometry and axial length to
calculate the IOL power required to achieve a given refractive

A

B

outcome. Some formulae incorporate additional parameters
such as anterior chamber depth and lens thickness to try to
optimize accuracy. оАЮe SRK-T, Haigis, HoоАЬer Q and Holladay
1 and 2 are commonly used. SpeciоАЭc formulae may be superior
for very short or long eyes, but opinions vary and it is always
wise to plan individually for an unusual eye, consulting the latest research and recommendations. Short eyes in particular are
prone to unexpected mean spherical error following surgery.
Based on current literature, for shorter eyes (AL< 22mm) Hoffer Q, Haigis, Hill-RBF and Kane formulae are recommended,
whereas for long eyes (AL>26mm) BU II, Holladay, Haigis
(with optimized constant) and Kane formulae produce better
outcomes. Factors that confound axial length measurements
include:

situation. Most involve the calculation of the post-refractive
procedure вАШtrueвАЩ corneal power using a special process (refractive history method, contact lens method) and insertion of this
into a standard (e.g. HoоАЬer Q) or speciоАЭc (e.g. Masket) formula, but the Haigis-L regression formula uses statistical data
to facilitate calculation on post-refractive surgery eyes using
only standard inputs. It may be prudent to utilize more than
one method of IOL calculation. оАЮe patient needs to be warned
that an under- or over-correction may follow the surgery.
оАБ Contact lenses. If the patient wears soоАЫ contact lenses, these
should not be worn for up to a week prior to biometry to allow
corneal stabilization. оАЮose patients who have a long history
of wearing rigid gas permeable contact lenses require up to
6 weeks to achieve refractive stability before undertaking the
measurements.
оАБ Personalized A-constant. If a consistent postoperative refractive deviation is found in most of an individual surgeonвАЩs cases,
it is assumed that some aspects of personal surgical (or possibly biometric) technique consistently and similarly inоАЩuence
outcome. A personalized A-constant can be programmed into
biometry apparatus to take this into account.

{ Poor patient оАЭxation.

{ Irregularities in globe contour.

{ Silicone oil in the vitreous.
оАБ Previous refractive surgery. Any form of corneal refractive
surgery is likely to make a signiоАЭcant diоАЬerence to the IOL
power required and standard IOL calculations are unsuitable.
Several diоАЬerent methods have been described to address this

318 Management of Acquired Cataract

TIP To achieve accurate preoperative biometry, soft
contact lenses should not be worn for one week before the
measurements are taken. Refractive stability takes up to 6
weeks for those wearing rigid gas-permeable contact lenses.

Postoperative refraction

оАБ Margins of error. Postoperative unaided refraction is within
1.0D of predicted outcome in almost 90% of patients and
within 0.5D in two-thirds.
оАБ Emmetropia is typically the desired postoperative refraction,
though spectacles will be needed for near vision since a conventional IOL cannot accommodate. Many surgeons aim for
a small degree of myopia (about вИТ0.25 D) to oоАЬset possible
errors in biometry. Postoperative hypermetropia, which necessitates correction for clear vision at all distances, is less well
tolerated than myopia.
оАБ Contralateral eye. Postoperative refractive planning must consider the contralateral eye. If this has a signiоАЭcant refractive error
but is unlikely to require cataract surgery within a few years, the
postoperative target for the operated eye might be set for within
less than 2.0 D of its fellow, to avoid problems with image size
discrepancy and diоАЪculty with binocular fusion. In some cases,
such as when there is an early lens opacity in the fellow eye or
when ametropia is extreme, the patient can be oоАЬered lens surgery to the other eye to facilitate targeting both at emmetropia.
оАБ вАШMonovisionвАЩ is a concept in which the (usually) nondominant eye is leоАЫ with between 1 and 2 dioptres of myopia
to allow unaided vision for reading, whilst emmetropia is targeted in the dominant eye. оАЮis is attractive to some patients,
generally those who have previously been using contact lenses
or spectacles to achieve monovision.
оАБ Multifocal lens options use a variety of optical means to
achieve satisfactory near, distance and intermediate vision (see
Ch. 8). Most patients are satisоАЭed with the results, but a signiоАЭcant number are unhappy, complaining of phenomena such as
glare and loss of contrast sensitivity. Highly accurate refractive
outcomes, including limited astigmatism, are necessary for
optimal function and a greater likelihood of tolerance.
оАБ Younger patients. With a conventional monofocal IOL, patients
younger than about 55 years need to be aware that they will
experience a sudden loss of active focusing and that it will oоАЫen
take some time to adjust to the loss of unaided near vision.

TIP Patients younger than 55 years need to be warned
that active focusing will be lost after the implantation of a
conventional monofocal IOL.

Intraocular lenses

Positioning

An IOL (Fig. 10.8A) consists of an optic and haptics. оАЮe optic is
the central refracting element and the haptics the arms or loops
that sit in contact with peripheral ocular structures to centralize
the optic. Modern cataract surgery, with preservation of the lens

{ Collamer is composed of collagen, a poly-HEMA-based
copolymer and an ultraviolet-absorbing chromophore. It is
marketed principally on the basis of high biocompatibility
and a favourable track record.
оАБ Rigid IOLs are made entirely from polymethylmethacrylate
(PMMA). оАЮey cannot be injected or folded so require an
incision larger than the diameter of the optic, typically 5 or 6
mm, for insertion. For economic reasons, they continue to be
widely used in developing countries. PCO rates are higher with
PMMA lenses than silicone and acrylic. Some surgeons favour

capsule, aоАЬords positioning of the IOL in the ideal location вАУ вАШin
the bagвАЩ (Fig. 10.8B). Complicated surgery, with rupture of the posterior capsule, may necessitate alternative positioning in the posterior chamber with the haptics in the ciliary sulcus (a three-piece
IOL only, not one-piece including those with plate haptics, as these
may not be stable), or in the anterior chamber (AC) with the haptics supported in the angle вАУ AC positioning requires a speciоАЭc lens
type. In some circumstances a supplementary IOL may be placed
in the sulcus in addition to an IOL in the capsular bag, for instance
to address a residual refractive error following primary surgery
(secondary pseudopolyphakia) and thin-proоАЭle IOLs are available
for this purpose. It is preferable to avoid a secondary sulcus IOL
(primary pseudopolyphakia) in very short (e.g. nanophthalmic)
eyes due to the risk of angle closure. OоАЬ-the-shelf IOLs of power
up to 40 D are available and custom IOLs can be produced in even
higher powers.

Design

оАБ Flexible IOLs introduced into the eye via an injector and
subsequently unrolled inside the eye are now in general use.
Injector-based delivery utilizes a very small incision and also
allows avoidance of implant contact with the ocular surface, so
reducing the risk of bacterial contamination. Simple folding of
the IOL is an alternative, but requires a slightly larger incision.
Flexible materials are discussed below. оАЮere seems to be no
distinct superiority of one material over another.

{ Acrylic IOLs. Hydrophobic (water content <1%) acrylic
materials have a greater refractive index than hydrophilic
lenses and are consequently thinner, though this can result
in dysphotopsia (troublesome glare and reоАЩections). оАЮey
have been reported to produce a greater reaction in eyes
with uveitis, but outcomes do not seem to be materially
aоАЬected. Hydrophilic acrylic (hydrogel) in theory oоАЬers
superior biocompatibility, but the image of hydrogel IOLs
has been marred by the occurrence of calciоАЭcation requiring IOL removal in some types and inоАЩammation in others
(these problems have been resolved by lens manufacturers). Posterior capsular opaciоАЭcation (PCO) rates may be
higher with hydrogel IOLs than with other materials.

{ Silicone IOLs are available in both loop haptic (one- or
three-piece) and plate haptic (one-piece) conformations,
the latter consisting of a roughly rectangular leaf with the
optic sited centrally. Silicone IOLs exhibit greater biocompatibility than hydrophobic acrylic IOLs, but may be prone
to signiоАЭcant silicone deposition in silicone oil-оАЭlled eyes.

heparin-coated IOLs (see below) in eyes with previous uveitis,
particularly in children.
оАБ Sharp/square-edged optics (see Fig. 10.8A) are associated
with a signiоАЭcantly lower rate of PCO compared with roundedged optics and the former is now the predominant design.
However, square edges may be associated with a higher rate of
dysphotopsia (see below).
оАБ Blue light оАЯlters. Although all IOLs contain ultraviolet light
оАЭlters, a number also include оАЭlters for blue wavelengths, in
order to reduce the possibility of damage to the retina by this
higher-energy visible light (see Fig. 10.8D). оАЮe blue оАЭlters
impart a slight yellow tint to the IOL, though similar only to
that of the physiological young adult lens. Some evidence suggests slightly poorer visual function in scotopic conditions of
illumination.
оАБ Aspheric optics to counteract corneal spherical aberration
are widely available and have been shown to improve contrast,
particularly in mesopic conditions. A potential drawback is
that the aspheric element of the manufactured IOLs is set at a

single standardized level (this diоАЬers between manufacturers),
but the extent of spherical aberration varies between individuals; some will be over- and some under-compensated.
оАБ Heparin coating reduces the attraction and adhesion of
inоАЩammatory cells and this may have particular application in
eyes with uveitis. However, there is no clear evidence of the
clinical beneоАЭt of heparin surface modiоАЭcation in these eyes.
оАБ Toric IOLs (Fig. 10.8C) have an integral cylindrical refractive component to compensate for pre-existing corneal astigmatism. оАЮe main potential problem is rotation within the
capsular bag, which occurs in a small percentage and may be
corrected by early surgical repositioning.
оАБ Bifocal IOLs (Fig. 10.8D) (see also Ch. 8) utilizing refractive
or diоАЬractive mechanisms aim to provide clear vision over
a range of focal distances. So-called вАШaccommodativeвАЩ IOLs
attempt to оАЩex and thereby alter focal length but in practice
the amplitude of accommodation is slight.
оАБ Adjustable IOLs allow the alteration of refractive power following implantation. One version uses low-level ultraviolet

320 Management of Acquired Cataract

irradiation at the slit lamp about a week aоАЫer surgery to induce
polymerization of its constituent molecules in speciоАЭc patterns with precise spherical and cylindrical (astigmatism)
correction.

Anaesthesia

Most cataract surgery is performed under local anaesthesia (LA),
sometimes in conjunction with intravenous or oral sedation. General anaesthesia is required in some circumstances, such as children and many young adults, very anxious patients, some patients
with learning diоАЪculties, epilepsy, dementia and those with a head
tremor.
оАБ Sub-Tenon block involves insertion of a blunt-tipped cannula
through an incision in the conjunctiva and Tenon capsule
5 mm from the limbus inferonasally (Fig. 10.9A) and passing
it around the curve of the globe through the sub-Tenon space.
оАЮe anaesthetic is injected beyond the equator of the globe
(Fig. 10.9B). Although anaesthesia is good and complications
minimal, akinesia is variable. Chemosis and subconjunctival

haemorrhage are common but penetration of the globe is
extremely rare.
оАБ Peribulbar block is given through the skin (Fig. 10.9C and
D) or conjunctiva with a 1-inch (25-mm) needle. It generally
provides eоАЬective anaesthesia and akinesia. Penetration of the
globe is a rare but severe complication. For this reason, peribulbar should be avoided in long eyes (which also tend to have
a larger equatorial diameter).
оАБ Topical anaesthesia involves drops or gel (proxymetacaine
0.5%, tetracaine 1% drops, lidocaine 2% gel), which can be
augmented with intracameral preservative-free lidocaine 0.2вАУ
1%, oоАЫen infused during hydrodissection. Patients receiving
supplementary intracameral lidocaine have signiоАЭcantly less
pain than those receiving topical anaesthesia alone. Intracameral Mydrane, a preservative-free combination of tropicamide
0.02%, phenylephrine 0.31% and lidocaine 1%, is commonly
used for achieving mydriasis and anaesthesia during highvolume cataract surgery. Combined viscoelastic/lidocaine
preparations are also commercially available. Despite the
absence of akinesia, most patients can cooperate adequately.

A B

C

D

Fig. 10.9 Local anaesthesia for cataract surgery. (A) Conjunctival dissection for sub-Tenon

However, approximately 5% of patients experience pain
when the surgery is undertaken with topical or intracameral
anaesthesia.

Manual cataract surgery

When posterior chamber IOLs began to be widely used in the
1980s most surgeons adopted extracapsular cataract extraction
(ECCE), abandoning the older intracapsular technique (ICCE). In
ICCE, a cryoprobe is used to remove the lens complete with its
capsule (Fig. 10.10A). In ECCE, aоАЫer a large anterior capsulotomy
is created, an extensive limbal incision (8вАУ10 mm) is completed
and the lens nucleus is expressed following hydrodissection to free
its cortical attachments (Fig. 10.10B). Cortical matter is then aspirated, leaving behind a suоАЪciently intact capsular bag to support
an IOL. Suturing of the incision is required, sometimes inducing
considerable corneal astigmatism. Manual small-incision cataract
surgery (MSICS) is a variant of ECCE used to address the requirement for high-volume surgical treatment of patients with dense
cataracts in less aоАШuent geographical regions. It involves the creation of a small self-sealing sclero-corneal tunnel (Fig. 10.11A),
staining of the anterior capsule to facilitate capsulorhexis (Fig.
10.11B), manual one-piece expression of the nucleus (Fig. 10.11C),
manual aspiration of the cortex (Fig. 10.11D) and IOL implantation. Visual rehabilitation is comparable to phacoemulsiоАЭcation
but MSICS is faster and avoids the need for expensive technology.

PhacoemulsiоАЮcation

Introduction

PhacoemulsiоАЭcation (вАШphacoвАЩ) is the standard method of cataract
extraction in high-income countries and in regional centres in
most lower-income countries.

Phacodynamics

Choosing appropriate settings makes surgery safer and easier.

CHAPTER

Lens 321
#### 10

оАБ Level of irrigating bottle above the patientвАЩs eye is set to maintain AC stability with a reasonable IOP. Infusion оАЩow is proportional to the height of the bottle.
оАБ Aspiration оАЬow rate (AFR) refers to the volume of оАЩuid
removed from the eye in millilitres per minute. For a higher
AFR the irrigating bottle must be elevated to compensate for
increased оАЩuid loss. High AFR results in attraction of lens
material towards the phaco tip, with faster vacuum build-up
and swiоАЫer removal of lens matter but with less eоАЬective power.
A high AFR should usually be avoided by trainee surgeons.
оАБ Vacuum, measured in mmHg, is generated during occlusion
when the pump is attempting to aspirate оАЩuid. Vacuum level
determines how tightly material is held by the phaco tip when
occluded, providing the ability to manipulate lens fragments.
High vacuum can decrease the total power required to remove the
lens. As with AFR, a lower-vacuum setting slows down the speed
of intraocular events, reduces the intensity of surge (see below)
and makes inadvertent aspiration of iris or lens capsule less likely.
оАБ Post-occlusion surge. When occlusion of the phaco tip by lens
material is broken, pent-up energy results in a sudden temporary increase in outоАЩow вАУ вАШsurgeвАЩ. оАЮis may result in complications such as capsular rupture and as far as possible is
suppressed by modern phaco machines.

Pump type

оАЮe main implication of the type of pump employed by a particular phaco machine is the eоАЬect on vacuum behaviour.
оАБ Peristaltic (оАЬow) pumps pull оАЩuid and lens material into the
phaco tip by compressing tubing over variable speed rollers.
Vacuum is generated only when occlusion of the tip occurs,
following which the pump slows and stops when a set maximum is achieved.
оАБ Venturi (vacuum) pumps create a negative pressure in a vessel by passing compressed gas across its entrance. оАЮis has the
practical eоАЬect of synchronizing vacuum and AFR so there is
generally no independent means of adjusting AFR. Depression

of the foot pedal increases vacuum towards the pre-set maximum independent of occlusion and tip vacuum is therefore
always available.
оАБ Hybrid pumps are oоАЬered by some modern machines.

Handpiece

оАЮe phaco handpiece features a tip consisting of a hollow titanium
needle with an enclosing оАЩuid-cooling sleeve (Fig. 10.12) to protect the cornea from thermal and mechanical damage. Its emulsifying action is mediated by very high-frequency (ultrasonic)
vibration leading to jackhammer, cavitation and other eоАЬects.
Some machines oоАЬer variants such as a torsional phaco action or
water jet-mediated phacoemulsiоАЭcation. Phaco tips of diоАЬering
shapes and sizes are available, each having particular cutting and
holding characteristics.

Ophthalmic viscosurgical devices

Ophthalmic viscosurgical devices (OVDs or viscoelastics) are biopolymers that play a critical role in modern cataract surgery.

оАБ Cohesive OVDs are used to create and maintain intraocular
spaces, for example to maintain the AC during capsulorhexis
and inоАЩation of the capsular bag to facilitate introduction of an
IOL. Higher molecular weight variants maintain intraocular
space more eоАЬectively, but tend to promote iris prolapse in shallow ACs and confer a more sustained postoperative IOP rise.
оАБ Dispersive OVDs are more adherent to surfaces than cohesive
OVDs and are typically used to protect the endothelium. оАЮey
are more diоАЪcult to remove from the eye than cohesive viscoelastics, but are less likely to cause an IOP spike. оАЮe major
practical disadvantage of dispersive OVDs is their tendency to
retain air bubbles and lens fragments, compromising the surgical view.
оАБ Adaptive OVDs display mixed characteristics.
оАБ вАШSoоАЫ shellвАЩ technique involves the injection prior to the capsulotomy stage of an outer dispersive layer followed by an inner
cohesive nucleus. Some surgeons use this routinely, others
only for eyes at higher risk of corneal decompensation (e.g.
cornea guttata).

CHAPTER

Lens 323
#### 10

chamber and maintain plugging of the capsular defect, facilitating completion of lens removal.

Technique

оАБ Preparation

{ Topical anaesthetic is followed by povidone-iodine 5%
(Fig. 10.13A) or chlorhexidine instillation into the conjunctival sac and cleaning of the eyelids (Fig. 10.13B),
ensuring thorough eyelash application. оАЮe antiseptic
should be leоАЫ to work for a minimum of 3 minutes.

A

C

Fig. 10.12 Phaco handpiece. (A) With curved tip and sleeve;
(B) in position during surgery.

оАБ Pupillary manipulation. In an eye with a small pupil, a high
molecular weight cohesive viscoelastic (e.g. Healon GV) will push
the iris away from the lens and help to induce mydriasis. Adaptive
OVDsвАЩ cohesive eоАЬect can be used to dilate a pupil intraoperatively and their dispersive eоАЬect to maintain the dilatation. OVDs
can be used to break posterior synechiae with minimal trauma.
оАБ Cortical manipulation. OVDs can be useful to dissect cortex away from the lens capsule to minimize traction on fragile
zonular ligaments.
оАБ Capsulorhexis rescue. If a capsulorhexis shows signs of running out to the periphery, injecting a cohesive viscoelastic will
оАЩatten the anterior capsule, aiding the exertion of a centrally
directed vector and helping to expand the pupil.

TIP Trypan blue eоАЯectively stains the anterior capsule and
facilitates accurate capsulorhexis in a patient with a poor вАШred
reоАЭexвАЩ secondary to a dense cataract.

оАБ Capsular rupture. In a small posterior capsular tear, a dispersive viscoelastic will push the vitreous back into the posterior

{ Careful draping (Fig. 10.13C) is performed, excluding the
lashes and lid margins from the surgical оАЭeld and a speculum is inserted.
оАБ Incisions

{ Many surgeons locate the main corneal incision (Fig.
10.14A) on the steepest corneal axis, others prefer consistent siting. Temporal incisions can provide better access
and less induced astigmatism but may be associated with a

оАБ Continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis is performed with a

оАБ Hydrodissection is performed to separate the nucleus and

оАБ вАШDivide and conquerвАЩ is a widely used, safe technique for

are created (sculpting), the phaco tip and a second instrument engaged in opposite walls of the grooves and the nucleus
cracked into quadrants by applying force in opposite directions (Fig. 10.14D). Each of the quadrants is then emulsiоАЭed
and aspirated in turn (Fig. 10.14E).
оАБ вАШPhaco chopвАЩ has the advantage of slightly greater speed and
a lower total phaco energy requirement, but takes longer to
learn. In horizontal chopping, a blunt-tipped chopper is placed
horizontally underneath the capsule and rotated vertically as
the equator is reached. Vertical chopping is performed with
a pointed-tip chopper that does not need to pass beyond the
capsulorhexis. оАЮe nucleus is separated into several pieces for
emulsiоАЭcation.
оАБ вАШStop and chopвАЩ is a combination technique.
оАБ Removal of lens cortex. Cortical lens matter segments are
carefully engaged by means of vacuum, peeled away centrally from the lens capsule and aspirated. Automated coaxial,
bimanual automated (Fig. 10.14F) and manual aspiration (e.g.
Simcoe cannula) methods are available.
оАБ IOL insertion. оАЮe capsular bag is оАЭlled with cohesive viscoelastic. A loaded injector cartridge is introduced through the

{ A side port incision is made around 60¬∞ to the leоАЫ (in righthanded surgeons) of the main incision. Some surgeons
prefer two side ports approximately 180¬∞ apart.

{ Viscoelastic is injected into the AC.

Femtosecond lasers in cataract surgery

Femtosecond lasers, used in refractive surgery for several years,
have recently been adopted by some surgeons, replacing several
of the manual steps of phacoemulsiоАЭcation with an automated
process. оАЮe corneal incisions, the capsulorhexis and initial
fragmentation of the crystalline lens, as well as astigmatismrelieving incisions (Fig. 10.16), can be performed with laser.
Potential advantages include greater precision and integrity
of incisions, reduced phacoemulsiоАЭcation energy and possibly improved refractive outcomes due to more precise capsulorhexis placement. Disadvantages include substantially higher
cost, longer total operating time and diоАЪculties with technically
challenging cases (e.g. small pupils). оАЮere is a substantial learning curve.

Operative complications

Rupture of the posterior lens capsule

Capsular rupture may be accompanied by vitreous loss, posterior
migration of lens material and, rarely, expulsive haemorrhage.
Sequelae to vitreous loss, particularly if inappropriately managed,
include CMO, retinal detachment, endophthalmitis, updrawn
pupil, uveitis, vitreous touch, vitreous wick syndrome, glaucoma
and posterior dislocation of the IOL.
оАБ Signs

{ Sudden deepening or shallowing of the AC and momentary pupillary dilatation.

{ оАЮe nucleus falls away and cannot be approached by the
phaco tip.

{ Vitreous aspirated into the phaco tip oоАЫen manifests with
a marked slowing of aspiration.

{ оАЮe torn capsule or vitreous gel may be directly visible.

TIP Rupture of the posterior capsule manifests with a sudden
change in the anterior chamber depth and momentary pupillary
dilatation.

Posterior loss of lens fragments

Dislocation of fragments of lens material into the vitreous cavity
(Fig. 10.18A) aоАЫer zonular dehiscence or posterior capsule rupture
is rare but potentially serious as it may result in glaucoma, chronic
uveitis, retinal detachment or chronic CMO. Initially, any uveitis
or raised IOP must be treated. It may be reasonable to adopt a conservative approach for small fragments, but pars plana vitrectomy
will usually be required for larger pieces.
