Posterior dislocation of IOL

Dislocation of an IOL into the vitreous cavity (Fig. 10.18B) is rare.
Loss can occur via a posterior capsular dehiscence, or in an eye
with fragile zonular attachments (e.g. pseudoexfoliation) the entire
capsular bag may dislocate. Complications include vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment, uveitis and chronic CMO. Treatment
involves pars plana vitrectomy with IOL removal, repositioning or
exchange depending on the extent of capsular support.

Suprachoroidal haemorrhage

A suprachoroidal haemorrhage involves a bleed into the suprachoroidal space from a ruptured posterior ciliary artery. If suоАЪciently
severe it may result in extrusion of intraocular contents (expulsive
haemorrhage). It is a dreaded complication, but extremely rare
(0.04%) with phacoemulsiоАЭcation. Contributing factors include
advanced age, glaucoma, increased axial length, systemic cardiovascular disease, vitreous loss and conversion from phacoemulsiоАЭcation to ECCE. A high intraoperative index of suspicion is
critical and if there is any suggestion of a suprachoroidal haemorrhage the operation should be terminated and the incision sutured
immediately.
оАБ Signs

{ Progressive shallowing of the AC, increased IOP and prolapse of the iris.

{ Vitreous extrusion, loss or partial obscuration of the red
reоАЩex and the appearance of a dark mound behind the
pupil.

follows. Postoperatively, intensive topical and systemic steroids
should be used to reduce intraocular inоАЩammation, with standard postoperative antibiotic treatment and IOP management.
Non-steroidal anti-inоАЩammatory drugs (NSAIDs) should be
avoided for analgesia and any antiplatelet or anticoagulant discontinued short-term, provided this is safe.
оАБ Subsequent treatment, if spontaneous absorption fails to
occur, consists of drainage of a large haemorrhage. оАЮis can be
performed 7вАУ14 days later, by which time liquefaction of blood
clot has taken place. оАЮe visual prognosis for a large haemorrhage is highly variable. Prolonged chorioretinal apposition
(>14 days) reduces the prognosis. Pars plana vitrectomy may
be considered when the retina appears adherent or detached,
though even apposed вАШkissingвАЩ haemorrhages may resolve
spontaneously without apparent retinal problems. If appropriate, completion of cataract surgery may be considered aоАЫer a
further 1вАУ2 weeks.

Acute postoperative endophthalmitis

Pathogenesis

оАЮe contemporary reported incidence of acute endophthalmitis
following cataract surgery varies substantially between studies, but
is probably at least 0.1%. Acute intraocular infection is invariably
a severe event. Toxins produced by infecting bacteria and the host
inоАЩammatory responses cause rapid and irreversible photoreceptor damage and ongoing eоАЬects can continue long aоАЫer the ocular
contents have been rendered sterile.
оАБ Risk factors are diоАЪcult to establish but may include operative
complications such as posterior capsule rupture, prolonged
procedure time, combined procedure (e.g. with vitrectomy),
clear corneal sutureless incision, temporal incision, wound
leak on the оАЭrst day, delaying postoperative topical antibiotics
until the day aоАЫer surgery, topical anaesthesia, adnexal disease
and diabetes.

{ In severe cases, posterior segment contents may be
extruded into the AC and through the incision.
оАБ Immediate treatment involves closure of the incision with a
suture. Speed of closure is the key and residual viscoelastic can
be leоАЫ in the eye. Intraoperative posterior sclerostomy should
not be undertaken. оАЮe diagnosis should be conоАЭrmed at the
slit lamp as soon as possible. IOP-lowering medication such as
oral acetazolamide is given to treat the pressure spike that oоАЫen

TIP In a one-eyed patient, make sure that the adjacent socket
is free of infection before undertaking cataract surgery.

оАБ Pathogens. About 90% of isolates are Gram-positive and 10%
Gram-negative. Staphylococcus epidermidis is the most common and with early treatment carries a reasonable prognosis.
оАБ оАЮe source of infection usually cannot be identiоАЭed with certainty. It is thought that the оАЩora of the eyelids and conjunctiva are the most frequent source, including contamination
via incisions in the early postoperative stages. Other potential sources include contaminated solutions and instruments,
environmental air and the surgeon, and other operating room
personnel.

Prophylaxis

Because of the low rate of endophthalmitis it is very diоАЪcult to
establish the eоАЬectiveness of any preventative measure.
оАБ Instillation of 5% povidone-iodine into the conjunctival fornices and leaving this undisturbed for at least 3 minutes prior
to surgery.
оАБ Scrupulous preparation of the surgical site, with re-draping if
eyelash coverage is inadequate.
оАБ Treatment of pre-existing infections such as blepharitis, conjunctivitis, chronic dacryocystitis and infection in the contralateral eye or socket.
оАБ Antibiotic prophylaxis

{ Intracameral cefuroxime (1 mg in 0.1 ml) injected into the
AC at the end of surgery.

{ Alternatively, moxiоАЩoxacin (0.5 mg in 0.1 ml) injected into
the AC at the end of surgery could be used if cefuroxime
is not available.

{ Intracameral vancomycin has been reported to cause
haemorrhagic occlusive retinal vasculitis in some patients
and should be avoided as routine prophylaxis.

{ Postoperative subconjunctival injection can achieve bactericidal levels in the AC for at least 1вАУ2 hours.

{ Preoperative topical оАЩuoroquinolone antibiotics are frequently given in regimens from 1 hour to 3 days before

оАБ Early resuturing of leaking wounds rather than observation is
likely to be prudent.
оАБ Reviewing personal surgical practice to eliminate potentially
risk-prone elements, particularly if a signiоАЭcant rate of endophthalmitis is encountered.

TIP Preoperative instillation of 5% povidone-iodine to the
surface of the eye reduces the risk of endophthalmitis.

оАБ Symptoms. Pain, redness and visual loss.
оАБ Signs vary according to severity.

{ Eyelid swelling, chemosis, conjunctival injection and
discharge.

{ A relative aоАЬerent pupillary defect is common.

{ Corneal haze.

{ Fibrinous exudate and hypopyon (Fig. 10.19A).

{ Vitritis with an impaired view of the fundus.

{ Severe vitreous inоАЩammation and debris (Fig. 10.19B)
with loss of the red reоАЩex.

DiоАЮerential diagnosis

If there is any doubt about the diagnosis, treatment should be that
of infectious endophthalmitis, as early recognition leads to a better
outcome.
оАБ Retained lens material in the AC (Fig. 10.20) or vitreous may
precipitate severe uveitis, corneal oedema and raised IOP.
оАБ Vitreous haemorrhage, especially if blood in the vitreous is
depigmented.

IdentiоАЯcation of pathogens

measured with a caliper. 0.2вАУ0.4 ml is aspirated from the midvitreous cavity. If using a disposable vitrector, the tubing is
capped and both the vitrector and tubing sent for analysis.
оАБ Conjunctival swabs may be taken in addition, as signiоАЭcant
culture may be helpful in the absence of a positive result from

оАБ Microbiology. Specimens should be sent to the microbiology
laboratory immediately. Most laboratories prefer to receive a
sample in the acquiring apparatus and will divide the specimen
for microscopy and culture. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
can be helpful in identifying unusual organisms and if culture
results have been negative. However, its high sensitivity means
that contamination can lead to false-positive results.

Treatment

оАБ Intravitreal antibiotics are the key to management because
levels above the minimum inhibitory concentration of most
pathogens are achieved and are maintained for days. оАЮey
should be administered immediately aоАЫer culture specimens
have been obtained. Antibiotics commonly used in combination are ceоАЫazidime, which will kill most Gram-negative
organisms (including Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and vancomycin to address Gram-positive cocci (including MRSA).

{ оАЮe concentrations are ceоАЫazidime 2 mg in 0.1 ml and
vancomycin 2 mg in 0.1 ml. Amikacin 0.4 mg in 0.1 ml
is an alternative to ceоАЫazidime in patients with a deоАЭnite
penicillin allergy, but is more toxic to the retina. Table 10.2
provides details of preparation.

{ оАЮe antibiotics are injected slowly into the mid-vitreous
cavity using a 25-gauge needle.

Samples for culture should be obtained from aqueous and vitreous to conоАЭrm the diagnosis. Negative culture does not necessarily
rule out infection and treatment should be continued. An operating theatre with experienced staоАЬ is the best setting, but samples
can be taken in a minor procedure room to avoid delay.
оАБ B-scan ultrasound should be performed prior to vitreous sampling if there is no clinical view, to exclude retinal detachment.
оАБ Preparation

{ Povidone-iodine 5% is instilled.

{ Topical and subconjunctival, sub-Tenon or peribulbar

оАБ Topical antibiotics are of limited beneоАЭt and are oоАЫen used

Clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily may be helpful for culturenegative infections. Evidence suggests these may attack bacterial bioоАЭlm.
оАБ Oral steroids. оАЮe rationale for the use of steroids is to limit

12вАУ24 hours provided fungal infection has been excluded from
examination of smears. Contraindications must be excluded
and gastric protection (e.g. lansoprazole 30 mg once daily)

{ оАЮe eye is draped as for cataract surgery, with insertion of

оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy. оАЮe Endophthalmitis Vitrectomy
Study (EVS) showed a beneоАЭt for immediate pars plana vitrectomy in eyes with a visual acuity (VA) of perception of light
(not hand movements vision or better) at presentation, with a

Subsequent management

Subsequent management should proceed according to culture
results and clinical response.
оАБ Signs of improvement include contraction of оАЭbrinous exudate and reduction of AC cellular activity and hypopyon. In
this situation treatment is not modiоАЭed irrespective of culture
results. Ultrasonography may be useful in vitreous assessment.
оАБ If the clinical signs are worsening aоАЫer 48 hours antibiotic
sensitivities should be reviewed and therapy modiоАЭed accordingly. Pars plana vitrectomy should be considered if not previously performed. Intravitreal antibiotics can be repeated
aоАЫer 2 days. If amikacin has previously been used, repeated
administration should probably be avoided to reduce the risk
of retinal toxicity.
оАБ Outcome is related to the duration of the infection prior to
treatment and the virulence of organisms.

{ If VA at presentation is perception of light, 30% of eyes
achieve 6/12 following treatment. If VA is better than light
perception, this оАЭgure increases to 60%.

{ Infection with Bacillus cereus or streptococci oоАЫen has a
poor visual outcome despite aggressive and appropriate
therapy, with 70% and 55% respectively achieving a оАЭnal
VA of 6/60 or less. оАЮis poor visual outcome may be related
to early retinopathy from exotoxins.
оАБ Late problems

{ Persistent vitreous opaciоАЭcation. Aggressive and extended
topical, periocular and if necessary oral steroid treatment
will oоАЫen lead to resolution. Vitrectomy can be considered
if the opaciоАЭcation is persistent.

{ Maculopathy in the form of epiretinal membrane, cystoid
oedema and ischaemia.

{ Hypotony. Wound leak should be excluded and persistent
inоАЩammation addressed. Choroidal eоАЬusion should be
identiоАЭed and drained if necessary. Retinal detachment
and anterior vitreous membranes may require vitrectomy.

{ Other problems include chronic uveitis, secondary glaucoma, retinal detachment and phthisis.

Delayed-onset postoperative
endophthalmitis

Pathogenesis

Delayed-onset endophthalmitis following cataract surgery develops when an organism of low virulence, such as P. acnes, becomes
trapped within the capsular bag (saccular endophthalmitis).
Organisms can become sequestered within macrophages, protected
from eradication but with continued expression of bacterial antigen. Onset ranges from 4 weeks to years (mean 9 months) postoperatively and typically follows uneventful cataract surgery. It may
rarely be precipitated by laser capsulotomy release of the organism.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Painless mild progressive visual deterioration is
typical and оАЩoaters may be present.

332 Management of Acquired Cataract

оАБ Signs

{ Low-grade anterior uveitis, sometimes with medium-large
keratic precipitates (Fig. 10.21A). A degree of vitritis is
common.

{ оАЮe inоАЩammation initially responds well to topical steroids, but recurs when treatment is stopped and may eventually become steroid-resistant.

{ An enlarging capsular plaque composed of organisms
sequestrated in residual cortex within the peripheral capsular bag is common (Fig. 10.21B).
оАБ DiоАЪerential diagnosis is principally from other causes of anterior uveitis, particularly idiopathic, sterile post-surgical and
chronic/recurrent viral infection (see Ch. 12).
оАБ Initial management. Later-generation оАЩuoroquinolones, such
as moxiоАЩoxacin, penetrate the eye well and are concentrated
within macrophages. An empirical 10вАУ14-day course of moxiоАЩoxacin (alternatives include clarithromycin) may be worthwhile prior to more invasive options.
оАБ Investigation. Sampling of aqueous and vitreous should be
considered if oral antibiotics are ineоАЬective. Anaerobic culture should be requested if P. acnes infection is suspected and
isolates may take 10вАУ14 days to grow. оАЮe detection rate can
be greatly improved with the use of PCR, which should also
screen for the common causes of viral anterior uveitis.
оАБ Treatment if persistent

{ Intravitreal antibiotics alone are usually unsuccessful in
resolving the infection.

{ Removal of the capsular bag, residual cortex and IOL,
requiring pars plana vitrectomy. Secondary IOL implantation may be considered at a later date. Intravitreal antibiotics are combined: vancomycin (1вАУ2 mg in 0.1 ml) is
the antibiotic of choice and can also be irrigated into any
capsular remnant. P. acnes is also sensitive to methicillin,
cefazolin and clindamycin.

Posterior capsular opaciоАЮcation

Visually signiоАЭcant PCO, also known as вАШaоАЫer cataractвАЩ, is the
most common late complication of uncomplicated cataract surgery, occurring in 20вАУ25% of patients. It is caused by the proliferation of lens epithelial cells that have remained within the
capsular bag following cataract extraction. оАЮe incidence of PCO
is reduced when the capsulorhexis opening is in complete contact with the anterior surface of the IOL. PMMA (and probably
to a lesser extent hydrogel) IOLs are particularly prone to PCO.
Implant design is more important than material. A square optic
edge appears to inhibit PCO (though may have a higher rate of
dysphotopsia вАУ see below).

TIP Posterior capsular thickening is the commonest cause of
late visual deterioration after small incisional cataract surgery
and is easily treated with a Nd:YAG laser.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms include persistent slowly worsening blurring, glare
and sometimes monocular diplopia.
оАБ VA is variably reduced, though dysfunction may be more
marked on contrast sensitivity testing.
оАБ Signs typically include more than one pattern of opaciоАЭcation.

{ Vacuolated (pearl-type) PCO (Fig. 10.22A) consists of proliferating swollen lens epithelial cells, similar to the bladder (Wedl) cells seen in posterior subcapsular cataract (see
Fig. 10.2A). оАЮey are commonly termed вАШElschnig pearlsвАЩ,
particularly when grouped into clusters at the edge of a
capsulotomy (Fig. 10.22B), though strictly HirschbergвАУ
Elschnig pearls refers to globular or grape-like collections
of swollen cells seen following traumatic or surgical anterior capsular rupture.

A B

Fig. 10.21 Delayed-onset postoperative endophthalmitis. (A) Anterior uveitis with large keratic
precipitates; (B) white capsular plaque in chronic postoperative endophthalmitis (arrow).

C

Lens 333
#### 10

B

D

F

Fig. 10.22 Posterior capsular opaciоАЮcation. (A) Vacuolated or pearl-type on retroillumination;
(B) Elschnig pearl formation (arrow) on edge of capsulotomy; (C) slit-lamp appearance of
оАЮbrotic capsule; (D) Soemmering ring following congenital cataract surgery; (E) appearance
following laser capsulotomy; (F) laser pitting of an IOL.

334 Management of Acquired Cataract

{ Fibrosis-type PCO (Fig. 10.22C) is thought to be due to
оАЭbroblastic metaplasia of epithelial cells, which develop
contractile qualities.

{ A Soemmering ring (Fig. 10.22D) is a whitish annular or
doughnut-shaped proliferation of residual cells that classically
formed almost in the periphery of the capsular bag following
older methods of cataract surgery, but is uncommon now. It
may form at the edge of a capsulorhexis or capsulotomy.

Treatment

Treatment involves the creation of an opening in the posterior capsule
using a Nd:YAG laser (termed a posterior capsulotomy) (Fig. 10.22E).
оАБ Indications. оАЮe presence of visual symptoms is the main indication (reduced VA and glare). Less commonly, capsulotomy is
performed to improve an inadequate fundus view impairing
assessment and treatment of posterior segment pathology.
оАБ Technique. Safe and successful laser capsulotomy involves
accurate focusing and use of the minimum energy required.
Laser power is initially set at 1mJ/pulse and may be increased
if necessary. A series of punctures is applied in a cruciate or
circumferential pattern using single-pulse shots. оАЮe opening
should equate approximately to the size of the physiologically
dilated pupil under scotopic conditions вАУ this should average
around 4вАУ5 mm in the pseudophakic eye. A larger capsulotomy may be necessary if glare persists, or for retinal examination or treatment, but the capsulotomy should not extend
beyond the edge of the optic in case vitreous prolapses around
its edge. It may be prudent to adopt a higher threshold for
treatment, and minimizing its extent, in eyes at risk of retinal
detachment (e.g. high myopia), CMO (e.g. history of uveitis)
or lens displacement (e.g. pseudoexfoliation). Some research
suggests that the total energy applied should be less than 80mJ
in order to reduce the risk of a signiоАЭcant IOP spike.
оАБ Complications include pitting of the IOL (Fig. 10.22F) that
is usually visually inconsequential. оАЮe IOP may rise, particularly in patients with glaucoma, but is typically mild and
transient. A retinal tear or detachment may follow the treatment and myopic individuals should be warned to return if

they develop symptoms compatible with a posterior vitreous
detachment (PVD). CMO may occur, but is less common
when the capsulotomy is delayed for 6 months or more aоАЫer
cataract surgery. IOL subluxation or dislocation is rare.

Anterior capsular оАЮbrosis and
contraction

Since the advent of continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis, signiоАЭcant
contraction of the anterior capsular opening is sometimes seen
(capsulophimosis вАУ Fig. 10.23). It typically progresses over months
and if severe, Nd:YAG laser anterior capsulotomy may be required.
Risk factors include a small capsulorhexis, pseudoexfoliation
syndrome (where the capsulorhexis contracts postoperatively by up
to 25%), retinitis pigmentosa and plate-haptic silicone IOL.

Capsular bag distension syndrome

In this rare complication of phacoemulsiоАЭcation and IOL implantation оАЩuid accumulates between the IOL and the posterior capsule leading to distension of the posterior capsule, slight anterior
displacement of the IOL and a myopic shiоАЫ (Fig. 10.24). оАЮe
trapped оАЩuid becomes turbid in time, which results in a reduction of vision. Most cases have an axial length that is greater than
25mm and a small capsulorhexis, with adhesion between the
lens optic and the anterior capsular rim. Anterior segment OCT
can be helpful to conоАЭrm the diagnosis. A rapid improvement in
vision follows the application of Nd:YAG laser to the posterior
capsule.

Miscellaneous postoperative
complications

Cystoid macular oedema

Symptomatic CMO (see Ch. 14) is relatively uncommon following
uncomplicated phacoemulsiоАЭcation and in most cases is mild and
transient. It occurs more oоАЫen aоАЫer complicated surgery and has a

A B

Fig. 10.23 Anterior capsular contraction and оАЮbrosis. (A) Clinical appearance; (B) retroillumination.

CHAPTER

Lens 335
#### 10

peak incidence at 6вАУ10 weeks, although the interval to onset may
be much longer.
оАБ Risk factors include epiretinal membrane, a history of CMO
in the other eye, operative complications such as posterior
capsular rupture with vitreous loss, particularly with vitreous incarceration into the incision site (Fig. 10.25A), anterior
chamber IOL (Fig. 10.25B), secondary IOL implantation, diabetes (approximately ten times greater risk), uveitis and topical
prostaglandin treatment.
оАБ Symptoms. Blurring, especially for near tasks, and sometimes
distortion. Subtle CMO may not be readily visible clinically,
but is demonstrated well on OCT.

TIP In the event of vitreous loss and raised IOP, avoid
prostaglandin drops as this increases the risk of cystoid
macular oedema.

Fig. 10.24 Capsular bag distension syndrome. OCT showing
turbid оАЭuid between the posterior surface of the lens (arrow
heads) and the posterior capsule (arrow). (Courtesy of
J Wesierski.)

оАБ Treatment. One or a combination of the following modalities
may be used.

{ Anterior vitrectomy or YAG laser applied to a vitreous
wick if present.

{ Topical NSAIDs (e.g. ketorolac four times daily, bromfenac
twice daily, nepafenac three times daily) reduce the risk of
developing CMO in non-diabetic and diabetic patients. In
addition, these drops may be beneоАЭcial even in long-standing
cases. Treatment may be necessary for several months.

{ Steroids. Topically, by periocular or intravitreal (triamcinolone acetate 0.05вАУ0.1 ml of 40 mg/ml) injection.

{ Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors given systemically or
topically.

{ Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents.

{ Pars plana vitrectomy may be useful for CMO refractory
to medical therapy, even in eyes without apparent vitreous
disturbance.

Dysphotopsia

Up to 1 in 10 patients complain of annoying visual phenomena
following uncomplicated cataract surgery with monofocal IOL
implantation. Multifocal IOLs have particular issues that are discussed separately above. Improvement in and adaptation to the
symptoms typically occurs over several months and the number of
monofocal IOL patients actually requiring further surgery for the
symptoms is very small. Rounded-edged IOLs may be less prone to
negative photopsia and silicone IOLs may be less liable to result in
dysphotopsia than acrylic IOLs. Optic and capsulorhexis size may
also be important.
оАБ Symptoms. A dark shadow in the temporal periphery (negative dysphotopsia вАУ oоАЫen the most troublesome), scintillations,
haloes, peripheral or central оАЩaring or оАЩashes (positive dysphotopsia) and possibly monocular diplopia.
оАБ Treatment

{ Encouraging the patient that the symptoms usually
improve over time, both because of anatomical changes

A B

Fig. 10.25 Factors predisposing to cystoid macular oedema. (A) Vitreous incarceration in the
incision; (B) secondary anterior chamber IOL with pupil block after previous vitreous loss.

336 Management of Acquired Cataract

(e.g. capsulorhexis edge thickening) and because the brain
is able to ignore unwanted images.

{ Positive nocturnal symptoms can be helped with gentle
pupillary constriction (e.g. brimonidine), but dilatation
may help negative dysphotopsia.

{ Successful alleviation of symptoms has been reported with
a variety of techniques, including reverse optic capture
(vaulting the optic forward out of the capsular bag, leaving the haptics in place), ciliary sulcus IOL re-implantation
and piggyback IOL implantation in the sulcus.

{ IOL exchange (round-edged) may be considered.

{ Laser capsulotomy is best avoided as it markedly complicates IOL exchange. However, in some eyes, Nd:YAG laser
removal of a sector of capsulorhexis edge has resulted in
reduced symptoms.

A

B

C

Fig. 10.26 IOL malposition. (A) Decentered optic in the ciliary sulcus, with an acrylic haptic anterior to the iris; (B) decentred optic
with one haptic in the angle (arrows) and the other in the bag;
(C) red reоАЭex showing slight inferior decentration of an IOL.

TIP Inaccurate biometry is the commonest cause of a
refractive surprise. Make sure the biometry is in keeping with
the previous refraction.

Corneal decompensation

Corneal oedema (see Ch. 7) may occur postoperatively, but is
usually mild and transient. Eyes with pre-existing corneal endothelial pathology, particularly low cell counts, are at increased
risk. Causes of signiоАЭcant oedema include a dense nucleus,
which requires high phacoemulsiоАЭcation energy, complicated or
prolonged surgery, pseudoexfoliation, intraoperative endothelial trauma and elevated postoperative IOP. Use of a dispersive
viscoelastic may help to protect the corneal endothelium during
surgery in high-risk eyes.

Ptosis

Mild ptosis secondary to a variety of mechanisms (especially the use
of postoperative topical steroids), is not uncommon aоАЫer cataract surgery, but usually improves without intervention. Corrective lid surgery should not be undertaken for at least a year postoperatively.

Malposition of the IOL

Although uncommon, malposition (Fig. 10.26AвАУC) may be associated with both optical and structural problems. SigniоАЭcant malposition may require repositioning or replacement, occasionally
with an iris or scleral-оАЭxated lens.

Retinal detachment

Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD) is uncommon aоАЫer
uncomplicated phacoemulsiоАЭcation with IOL implantation. Preoperative risk factors include lattice degeneration, retinal breaks
and high myopia. оАЮe key intraoperative risk is vitreous loss. Pars
plana vitrectomy is usually the surgical modality employed for
pseudophakic RRD.

Refractive вАШsurpriseвАЩ

Small under- or over-corrections are not uncommon aоАЫer this surgery. оАЮe risk of this happening can be reduced by preoperatively
checking that the diоАЬerence in axial length between eyes is less than
0.7mm and that the diоАЬerence in average keratometry between
eyes is less than 0.9D. If there is a signiоАЭcant residual refractive
error as a consequence of a failure to achieve the intended postoperative refractive target, patient dissatisfaction may ensue.

оАБ Management

{ Identify the cause.

{ Obtain a subjective refraction.

{ Examine the eye to exclude conditions like a distended
capsular bag (myopic shiоАЫ) and CMO (hyperopic shiоАЫ).

{ Check the biometry and the strength of the implanted IOL.

{ Repeat the biometry, keratometry (undiagnosed keratoconus) and measure corneal thickness (undiagnosed previous laser refractive surgery).
оАБ Treatment

{ If no obvious cause can be identiоАЭed and the patient is
happy to use spectacles or wear a contact lens to correct
the refractive error, no further treatment is needed.

{ If the patient remains dissatisоАЭed and once the refraction is
stable, undertake further surgery.

{ IOL exchange is a good early option, but should be undertaken before capsular оАЭbrosis occurs.

{ Corneal refractive surgery could be considered, as this can
deal with a range of refractive errors.

{ вАШPiggy backвАЩ sulcus IOL implantation is a reasonable option
for high degrees of uncorrected refractive error, but is less
accurate than laser refractive surgery.

CHAPTER

Lens 337
#### 10

transferase (GALT). Unless galactose (milk and milk products) is withheld from the diet, severe systemic complications
culminate in early death. вАШOil dropletвАЩ lens opacity (see Fig.
10.27E) develops within the first few days or weeks of life
in a large percentage of patients. Exclusion of galactose may
reverse early lens changes.

Lowe syndrome

Lowe (oculo-cerebro-renal) syndrome is an X-linked recessive
(gene: OCRL1) inborn error of amino acid metabolism with neuromuscular, renal and other manifestations. Cataract is universal
and microphakia may also be present. Congenital glaucoma is
present in about half of patients. Female carriers may have visually
insigniоАЭcant cortical lens opacities.

Mannosidosis

Mannosidosis is an AR disorder with deоАЭciency of ќ±-mannosidase.
Infantile and juvenile-adult forms are seen, both of which feature progressive mental deterioration, musculoskeletal and other
abnormalities. Punctate lens opacities arranged in a spoke-like
pattern in the posterior lens cortex are frequent. Corneal clouding
can also occur but is less common.

Other metabolic disorders

Potential causes include hypo- and pseudohypoparathyroidism
and hypo- and hyperglycaemia.

Associated intrauterine infections

Rubella

Congenital rubella results from transplacental transmission of
virus from an infected mother and may lead to severe fetal malformations. Pearly nuclear or more diоАЬuse unilateral or bilateral
cataract occurs in around 15%. (See also Ch. 12.)

Toxoplasmosis

Ophthalmic features of congenital toxoplasmosis include cataract,
chorioretinitis, microphthalmos and optic atrophy. (See also Ch. 12.)

Cytomegalovirus infection

Systemic features of congenital cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection
include jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly, microcephaly and intracranial calciоАЭcation. Ocular features apart from cataract include
chorioretinitis, microphthalmos, keratitis and optic atrophy.

Varicella

Systemic features include learning disability, cortical cerebral atrophy, cutaneous scarring and limb deformities. Death in early infancy
is common. Ocular features may include cataract, microphthalmos,
chorioretinitis, optic disc hypoplasia and optic atrophy.

Others

Measles, syphilis, herpes simplex and human immunodeоАЭciency
virus (HIV). (See also Ch. 12.)

Postoperative dry eye syndrome

Cataract surgery is a risk factor for the induction or exacerbation
of dry eye symptoms in up to one-third of patients. Switching
to preservative-free postoperative medication and prescribing
preservative-free artiоАЭcial tears is key to the management. Additional treatment options are detailed in Chapter 5

CONGENITAL CATARACT

Aetiology

Congenital cataract occurs in about 3 in 10,000 live births. In
general, one-third are associated with systemic disease, onethird are inherited and one-third are of unknown cause. Twothirds are bilateral and a cause can be identified in about half
of these. Autosomal dominant (AD) inheritance is the most
common aetiological factor. Others include chromosomal
abnormalities, metabolic disorders and intrauterine infections. Isolated inherited congenital cataracts carry a better
visual prognosis than those with coexisting ocular and systemic
abnormality. Unilateral cataracts are usually sporadic, without a
family history or systemic disease and affected infants are usually otherwise healthy.

Associated metabolic disorders

Galactosaemia

Galactosaemia is an autosomal recessive (AR) condition
characterized by impairment of galactose utilization caused
by absence of the enzyme galactose-1-phosphate uridyl

338 Congenital Cataract

Other systemic associations
Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

оАБ Systemic features include learning diоАЪculties, stunted growth,
distinctive facial and peripheral features, thyroid dysfunction,
cardiorespiratory disease and reduced life span.
оАБ Ocular features. Cataract of varied morphology (75%). оАЮe
opacities are usually symmetrical and oоАЫen develop in late
childhood. Other features include iris BrushоАЭeld spots (see
Fig. 20.15B) and hypoplasia, chronic blepharitis, myopia, strabismus and keratoconus.

Edwards syndrome (trisomy 18)

оАБ Systemic features. Characteristic facial and peripheral features,
deafness, cardiac anomalies, learning disability and early death.
оАБ Ocular features apart from cataract include ptosis, microphthalmos, corneal opacity, uveal and disc coloboma and vitreoretinal dysplasia.

Miscellaneous

HallermannвАУStreiоАЬ syndrome features impaired growth and other
features, with cataract in 90%. NanceвАУHoran syndrome is an
X-linked condition comprising distinctive dental and facial anomalies together with congenital cataract and microcornea. Female
carriers may show Y suture opacities (see Fig. 10.27F).

Management
Ocular assessment

In the neonate, determining the visual signiоАЭcance of lens opacity
is based principally on the appearance of the red reоАЩex and the
quality of the fundus view. оАЮere are three possibilities:
оАБ A very dense cataract with no red reоАЩex.
оАБ A less dense but still visually signiоАЭcant cataract (e.g. central
or posterior opacities over 3 mm in diameter) will permit visualization of the retinal vasculature with the indirect but not
with the direct ophthalmoscope.
оАБ A visually insigniоАЯcant opacity will allow clear visualization
of the retinal vasculature with both the indirect and direct
ophthalmoscope.
оАБ Other indicators of severe visual impairment include
absence of central оАЭxation, nystagmus and strabismus.
оАБ Morphology

{ Blue dot opacities (Fig. 10.27A) are common and innocuous.

{ Nuclear opacities (Fig. 10.27B) are conоАЭned to the embryonic or fetal nucleus. оАЮe cataract may be dense or composed of оАЭne dust-like (pulverulent) opacities.

{ Coronary (supranuclear) cataract lies in the deep cortex,
surrounding the nucleus like a crown. It is usually sporadic
but occasionally hereditary (Fig. 10.27C).

{ Lamellar opacities aоАЬect a particular lamella of the lens
both anteriorly and posteriorly and may be associated with
radial extensions (вАШridersвАЩ вАУ Fig. 10.27D). Lamellar opacities may be AD or occur in isolation as well as in association with metabolic disorders and intrauterine infections.

{ Central вАШoil dropletвАЩ opacities (Fig. 10.27E) are characteristic of galactosaemia.

{ Sutural, in which the opacity follows the anterior or posterior Y suture (Fig. 10.27F). оАЮis may be seen in female
NanceвАУHoran carriers.

{ Anterior polar cataract may be оАЩat or project into the AC
(Fig. 10.27G). Occasional associations include persistent
pupillary membrane, aniridia, Peters anomaly and anterior
lenticonus.

{ Posterior polar cataract (Fig. 10.27H) may be associated with posterior lenticonus or fetal vascular remnants
including a Mittendorf dot. оАЮis form of opacity is oоАЫen
closely integrated with the lens capsule and/or a preexisting defect, with a very high risk of dehiscence during
surgery.
оАБ Associated ocular pathology may involve the anterior (e.g.
corneal clouding, microphthalmos, glaucoma, persistent fetal
vasculature) or posterior segments (e.g. chorioretinitis, Leber
amaurosis, rubella retinopathy, foveal or optic nerve hypoplasia). Its presence may give an additional indication of visual
prognosis. оАЮe diоАЬerential diagnosis of leukocoria may apply
(see Ch. 20).
оАБ Assessment of family members for subclinical familial cataract is prudent.
оАБ Ultrasonography should be performed if the fundus is not
visible and may reveal a deоАЭnitive cause such as persistent fetal
vasculature.
оАБ Special tests such as forced-choice preferential looking and
visual evoked potentials may provide useful supporting
information.

Systemic investigations

Investigation of familial cataract is unnecessary, but otherwise
the following should be considered. Assessment beyond a search
for infection and possibly urinary reducing substance is probably
unnecessary in unilateral cases.
оАБ Screening for intrauterine infections should usually be performed in all unilateral and bilateral cases.
оАБ Urine. Urinalysis for reducing substance aоАЫer drinking milk
(galactosaemia) and chromatography for amino acids (Lowe
syndrome).
оАБ Other investigations may include fasting blood glucose, serum
calcium and phosphorus, red blood cell GALT and galactokinase
levels. Children who have calcium and phosphorus anomalies
severe enough to cause cataract are likely to be unwell.
оАБ Referral to a paediatrician may be warranted for dysmorphic
features or suspicion of other systemic diseases. Chromosome
analysis may be useful in this context.

Treatment

оАЮe requirement for urgent surgery is balanced by the fact that the
earlier this takes place, particularly before 4 weeks of age, the higher
the chance of glaucoma developing during the juvenile years.
оАБ Bilateral dense cataracts require surgery between 4 and
10 weeks of age to prevent the development of stimulus

340 Ectopia Lentis

deprivation amblyopia. If severity is asymmetrical, the eye
with the more marked opacity should be addressed оАЭrst.
оАБ Bilateral partial cataracts may not require surgery until later,
or indeed at any stage. In case of doubt, it may be prudent to
defer surgery in favour of careful monitoring.
оАБ Unilateral dense cataract merits more urgent surgery. However, there is no consensus regarding timing, except that 6
weeks is the latest point at which elective surgery should be
performed. Many authorities would advocate surgery between
4 and 6 weeks, followed by aggressive anti-amblyopia therapy.
Despite this, the VA results are oоАЫen disappointing. If the cataract is detected aоАЫer 16 weeks of age, then the visual prognosis
is particularly poor.
оАБ Partial unilateral cataract can usually be observed or treated
non-surgically with pupillary dilatation and possibly part-time
contralateral occlusion.
оАБ Surgery involves anterior capsulorhexis, aspiration of lens
matter, capsulorhexis of the posterior capsule, limited anterior
vitrectomy and IOL implantation, if appropriate. It is important to correct associated refractive errors.

Postoperative complications

Surgery carries a higher incidence of complications than in adults.
оАБ Posterior capsular opaciоАЯcation is nearly universal if the
posterior capsule is retained and can have a substantial
amblyogenic eоАЬect. Posterior capsulorhexis with vitrectomy is
generally performed during the primary lens extraction.
оАБ Secondary membranes may form across the pupil, particularly if postoperative uveitis is not treated aggressively.
оАБ Proliferation of lens epithelium is universal, oоАЫen forming
a Soemmering ring (see Fig. 10.22D). It is usually visually
inconsequential.
оАБ Glaucoma

{ оАЮe younger the age at which the surgery is undertaken the
greater the risk of developing glaucoma.

{ Secondary open-angle glaucoma may develop in up to
20% of eyes by 5 years aоАЫer surgery.

TIP After cataract surgery in a child, measures need to be
taken to prevent amblyopia.

ECTOPIA LENTIS

Introduction

Ectopia lentis refers to a hereditary or acquired displacement of the
lens from its normal position. оАЮe lens may be completely dislocated, rendering the eye functionally aphakic (luxated), or partially
displaced, remaining partly within the pupillary area (subluxated).
оАЮe early stages of subluxation may manifest with a tremulous lens
(phacodonesis), demonstrated on the slit lamp by lens wobble on
rapid return of the eye to the primary position.

Acquired causes

оАБ Trauma.
оАБ Pseudoexfoliation.
оАБ InоАЩammation, e.g. chronic cyclitis, syphilis.
оАБ Hypermature cataract.
оАБ Large eye, e.g. high myopia, buphthalmos.

Without systemic associations

оАБ Familial ectopia lentis. оАЮis is an AD condition characterized
by bilateral symmetrical superotemporal displacement. It may
manifest congenitally or later in life.
оАБ Ectopia lentis et pupillae is a rare congenital bilateral disorder with AR inheritance characterized by displacement of
the pupil and the lens in opposite directions (Fig. 10.28A).
оАЮe pupils are small and dilate poorly. Microspherophakia
may be present.
оАБ Aniridia is occasionally associated with ectopia lentis (Fig.
10.28B).

With systemic associations

{ AD inheritance (gene: FBN1) with variable expressivity.

{ Angle closure may occur in the immediate postoperative period secondary to pupillary block, especially in
microphthalmic eyes.
оАБ Retinal detachment is an uncommon and usually late
complication.

Visual rehabilitation

оАЮe visual results of cataract surgery in infants are hampered by
amblyopia, which should be treated aggressively (see Ch. 18).
оАБ Spectacles are useful for older children with bilateral aphakia.
оАБ Contact lenses provide a superior optical solution for
unilateral or bilateral aphakia. After the age of about 2
years compliance may worsen as the child becomes more
independent.
оАБ IOL implantation is increasingly being performed in younger
children and appears to be eоАЬective and safe in selected cases.
Hypermetropia (correctable with spectacles) is initially targeted and as the child ages slow change towards emmetropia
and then myopia is usually found.

оАБ Marfan syndrome

{ Musculoskeletal features include a tall, thin stature with
disproportionately long limbs (arm span > height), long
оАЭngers (Fig. 10.29A) and toes (arachnodactyly), a narrow
high-arched (вАШgothicвАЩ) palate (Fig. 10.29B).

{ Kyphoscoliosis, sternal abnormalities, mild joint laxity, muscular underdevelopment and predisposition to
hernias.

{ Cardiovascular lesions include dilatation of the aortic root,
mitral valve prolapse and aortic aneurysm formation.

{ Bilateral ectopia lentis (80%); subluxation is most frequently superotemporal (Fig. 10.29C). оАЮe zonules are
frequently intact so that accommodation is retained,
although rarely the lens may dislocate into the AC or vitreous (Fig. 10.29D).

{ Other ocular features: angle anomaly may lead to glaucoma and lattice retinal degeneration to retinal detachment. оАЮere may be hypoplasia of the dilator pupillae,
microspherophakia and strabismus.

оАБ WeillвАУMarchesani syndrome is a rare systemic connective
tissue disease, conceptually the converse of Marfan syndrome.

{ Inheritance is AR or AD, the latter resulting from polymorphisms in FBN1, the same gene as Marfan syndrome.

{ Systemic features include short stature, short оАЭngers and
toes (brachydactyly) and learning diоАЪculties (Fig. 10.30A).

{ Ectopia lentis (50%). Subluxation is in an inferior direction
and occurs in late childhood or early adulthood. Microspherophakia (Fig. 10.30B) is common, so that pupillary
block with angle closure may ensue.
оАБ Homocystinuria is an AR disorder in which decreased enzymatic metabolism of the amino acid methionine results in systemic accumulation of methionine and homocysteine.

{ Systemic features include coarse blond hair, blue irises,
malar оАЩush, Marfanoid habitus, neurodevelopmental
delay, marked thrombotic predisposition and early atherosclerosis (Fig. 10.30C).

{ Treatment involves oral pyridoxine, folic acid and vitamin B12 to reduce plasma homocysteine and methionine
levels.

{ Ectopia lentis, typically inferonasal, is almost universal by
the age of 25 years in untreated cases (Fig. 10.30D). оАЮe
zonules, which normally contain high levels of cysteine
(deоАЭcient in homocystinuria), disintegrate so that accommodation is oоАЫen lost. Pupillary block may occur.

{ Other ocular features include iris atrophy, optic atrophy,
cataract, myopia and retinal detachment.
оАБ Other systemic conditions associated with ectopia lentis
include sulоАЭte oxidase deоАЭciency (ectopia lentis is universal)
and occasionally Stickler syndrome (retinal detachment is the
most common ocular manifestation вАУ see Ch. 16), EhlersвАУ
Danlos syndrome and hyperlysinaemia.

Management

оАЮe main complications of ectopia lentis are refractive error of any
type depending on lens position, optical distortion due to astigmatism and/or lens edge eоАЬect, glaucoma (see Ch. 11) and, rarely,
lens-induced uveitis.
оАБ Spectacle correction may correct astigmatism induced by
lens tilt or edge eоАЬect in eyes with mild subluxation. Aphakic

correction may also aоАЬord good visual results if a signiоАЭcant
portion of the visual axis is clear in the undilated state.
оАБ Surgical removal of the lens is indicated for intractable ametropia, meridional amblyopia, cataract, lens-induced glaucoma
or uveitis, or endothelial touch.

ABNORMALITIES OF LENS SHAPE

Anterior lenticonus

Anterior lenticonus consists of a bilateral axial projection of the anterior surface of the lens into the AC (Fig. 10.31A and B). оАЮe вАШoil dropletвАЩ
sign can be seen on retroillumination (Fig. 10.31C). Almost all patients
have Alport syndrome, a hereditary condition characterized by progressive sensorineural deafness and renal disease associated with
abnormal glomerular basement membrane (see Fig. 15.22). Retinal
оАЩecks and posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy may also occur.

Posterior lenticonus

Bulging of the posterior axial lens (Fig. 10.31D) is associated with
local thinning or absence of the capsule in posterior lenticonus.
Most cases are unilateral, sporadic and not associated with systemic disease. With age, bulging progressively increases and the
lens cortex may opacify. Posterior subcapsular opaciоАЭcation is
common (Fig. 10.31E). Progression of cataract is variable, but an
acutely opaciоАЭed lens is sometimes seen in early childhood.

A

C

CHAPTER

Lens 343
#### 10

Lentiglobus

Lentiglobus is a very rare, usually unilateral, generalized hemispherical deformity of the lens. It may be associated with posterior
polar opacity.

Microspherophakia and microphakia

оАЮe lens is small and spherical in microspherophakia (Fig. 10.31F),
which may be seen as an isolated familial (dominant) abnormality,
or in association with a number of systemic conditions including
Marfan and WeillвАУMarchesani syndromes, hyperlysinaemia and
congenital rubella. Ocular associations include Peters anomaly
and familial ectopia lentis et pupillae. Complications can include
lenticular myopia, subluxation and dislocation. Microphakia (Fig.
10.31G) is the term used for a lens with a smaller than normal
diameter. It may be found in isolation, but may also occur in
patients with Lowe syndrome.

Coloboma

оАЮis is characterized by congenital indentation of the lens periphery (Fig. 10.31H) and occurs as a result of localized zonular deоАЭciency. It is not a true coloboma, as there is no focal absence of a
tissue layer due to failure of closure of the optic оАЭssure. Occasionally a lens coloboma is associated with a coloboma of the iris or
fundus.

346 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

Aqueous production

Aqueous humour is produced from plasma by the ciliary epithelium of the ciliary body pars plicata, using a combination of
active and passive secretion. A high-protein оАЮltrate passes out of
fenestrated capillaries (ultraоАЮltration) into the stroma of the ciliary processes, from which active transport of solutes occurs across
the dual-layered ciliary epithelium. оАЭe osmotic gradient thereby
established facilitates the passive оАЬow of water into the posterior
chamber. Secretion is subject to the inоАЬuence of the sympathetic
nervous system, with opposing actions mediated by beta-2 receptors (increased secretion) and alpha-2 receptors (decreased secretion). Enzymatic action is also critical вАУ carbonic anhydrase is
among those playing a key role.

Aqueous outоАЬow
Anatomy

оАБ оАЯe trabecular meshwork (trabeculum) is a sieve-like structure (Fig. 11.1) at the angle of the anterior chamber (AC)
through which 90% of aqueous humour leaves the eye. It has
three components (Fig. 11.2).

{ оАЭe uveal meshwork is the innermost portion, consisting
of cord-like endothelial cell-covered strands arising from
the iris and ciliary body stroma. оАЭe intertrabecular spaces
are relatively large and oоАЫer little resistance to the passage
of aqueous.

{ оАЭe corneoscleral meshwork lies external to the uveal
meshwork to form the thickest portion of the trabeculum.
It is composed of layers of connective tissue strands with
overlying endothelial-like cells. оАЭe intertrabecular spaces
are smaller than those of the uveal meshwork, conferring
greater resistance to оАЬow.

Fig. 11.1 Scanning electron micrograph of the trabecular
meshwork.

E

C

D

B

A

G

F

Fig. 11.2 Anatomy of outоАЬow channels. оАЭAоАЮ Uveal meshwork;
оАЭBоАЮ corneoscleral meshwork; оАЭCоАЮ Schwalbe line; оАЭDоАЮ Schlemm
canal; оАЭEоАЮ connector channels; оАЭFоАЮ longitudinal muscle of the
ciliary body; оАЭGоАЮ scleral spur.

оАБ Trabecular outоАЬow (90%): aqueous оАЬows through the trabeculum into the Schlemm canal and then the episcleral veins.
оАЭis is a bulk оАЬow pressure-sensitive route so that increasing
intraocular pressure will increase outоАЬow.
оАБ Uveoscleral drainage (10%): aqueous passes across the face of
the ciliary body into the suprachoroidal space and is drained by
the venous circulation in the ciliary body, choroid and sclera.
оАБ Iris: some aqueous also drains via the iris.

Intraocular pressure

Intraocular pressure (IOP) is determined by the balance between
the rate of aqueous production and the rate of aqueous outоАЬow. оАЭe latter is related to factors that include the resistance
encountered in the trabeculum and the level of episcleral venous
pressure.

{ оАЭe juxtacanalicular (cribriform) meshwork is the outer
part of the trabeculum and links the corneoscleral meshwork with the endothelium of the inner wall of the Schlemm canal. It consists of cells embedded in a dense
extracellular matrix with narrow intercellular spaces and
oоАЫers the major proportion of normal resistance to aqueous outоАЬow.
оАБ оАЭe Schlemm canal is a circumferential channel within the
perilimbal sclera. оАЭe inner wall is lined by irregular spindleshaped endothelial cells containing infoldings (giant vacuoles)
that are thought to convey aqueous via the formation of transcellular pores. оАЭe outer wall is lined by smooth оАЬat cells and
contains the openings of collector channels, which leave the
canal at oblique angles and connect directly or indirectly with
episcleral veins. Septa commonly divide the lumen into 2вАУ4
channels.

Physiology

Aqueous оАЬows from the posterior chamber via the pupil into the
AC, from where it exits the eye via three routes (Fig. 11.3).

C

A
B

Fig. 11.3 Routes of aqueous outоАЬow. оАЭAоАЮ Trabecular; оАЭBоАЮ uveoscleral; оАЭCоАЮ iris.

Concept of normal intraocular pressure

оАЭe upper range of normal for adults is 21 mmHg on applanation
tonometry (see below). Some individuals develop glaucomatous
damage with IOP less than 21 mmHg, but others remain unscathed
with IOP well above this level. Whilst reduction of IOP is a key
modiоАЮable element in all types of glaucoma, additional incompletely understood factors are critical in determining whether a
particular individual or eye develops glaucomatous damage. оАЭese
include factors aоАЫecting the susceptibility of the optic nerve to
damage, such as the integrity of its blood supply and structural
vulnerability to mechanical stress at the optic nerve head.

Fluctuation

Normal IOP varies with time of day (diurnal variation), heartbeat,
blood pressure and respiration. оАЭe diurnal pattern varies, with
a tendency to be higher in the morning and lower in the aоАЪernoon and evening. оАЭis is at least partially due to a diurnal pattern in aqueous production, which is lower at night. Glaucomatous
eyes exhibit greater than normal оАЬuctuation, the extent of which
is directly proportional to the likelihood of progressive visual
оАЮeld damage and a single reading may therefore be misleading.
It is good practice to note the time of day in conjunction with a
recorded IOP.

Measurement of IOP

See Chapter 1

OCULAR HYPERTENSION

DeоАЯnition

In the general population the mean IOP is 16 mmHg. Two standard deviations on either side of this provides a вАШnormalвАЩ IOP
range for individuals over the age of 40 years of 11вАУ21 mmHg. оАЭe
distribution is Gaussian with the curve skewed to the right (Fig.
11.4). оАЭe upper range of normal in individuals over 70 years is 23
mmHg. It is estimated that 4вАУ7% of the population over the age of

{ Central corneal thickness (CCT). оАЭe risk is greater in eyes
with OHT and CCT <555 ќЉm and lower in eyes with high
CCT (>588 ќЉm). оАЭis may be due to under- and overestimation of IOP, although it is more likely that associated
structural factors, perhaps at the lamina cribrosa, might be
involved. CCT can be determined using optical imaging
(for example: Orbscan) or ultrasonography (for example:
Pachmate) (Fig. 11.5).

{ Cup/disc (C/D) ratio. оАЭe greater the C/D ratio the higher
the risk. оАЭis may be because an optic nerve head with a
large cup is structurally more vulnerable, or it may be that
early damage is already present.

40 have an IOP >21 mmHg with open оАЮltration angles and without
detectable glaucomatous damage. оАЭese individuals are referred to
as having вАШocular hypertensionвАЩ (OHT).
Approximately 1 in 10 will develop glaucoma over a 10year period and the majority will not develop glaucoma in their
lifetime.

Risk factors for developing glaucoma in OHT

оАЭe Ocular Hypertension Treatment Study (OHTS) was a multicentre longitudinal trial. In addition to looking at the eоАЫect of
treatment of individuals with ocular hypertension (IOP in one eye
between 24 mmHg and 32 mmHg), invaluable information was
gained about the eоАЫect of a range of putative risks for conversion
from OHT to glaucoma. оАЭe percentage of OHT patients likely to
develop glaucoma at 6 years taking key factors into account is set
out in Tables 11.1 and 11.2
оАБ Limitations include the following: the study goal was an IOP
reduction of 20%, which may not be suоАЩcient in some individuals, compliance with medication was not measured and there
is a possibility that early glaucomatous damage was already
present in some patients.
оАБ Conclusions based on factors that were signiоАЮcant on multiоА†
variate analysis

{ Intraocular pressure. оАЭe risk of developing glaucoma
increases with increasing IOP.

{ Age. Older age is associated with greater risk.

348 Ocular Hypertension

{ Pattern standard deviation (PSD). оАЭe higher the PSD
value the greater the risk. (оАЭis possibly signiоАЮes early
glaucomatous оАЮeld change.)
оАБ Conclusions based on factors that were signiоАЮcant on uniоА†
variate analysis only (these were over-ridden when the factors
discussed above were considered)

{ Diabetes.

{ Family history of glaucoma (which is curious, as patients
with glaucoma oоАЪen have a family history of the disease).
оАБ Conclusions of OHTS

{ African racial background (including Afro-Caribbean,
African-American) is associated with a higher glaucoma
risk.

{ Early treatment reduces the cumulative incidence of
glaucoma.

{ оАЭe eоАЫect is greatest in high-risk individuals.

{ Individualized assessment of risk is useful and helps to
guide management.

{ Early treatment of low-risk individuals is not needed.

{ Males are more likely to convert.

{ Heart disease is a signiоАЮcant risk factor.
оАБ Factors examined in the OHTS but found to be insigniоАЮcant

{ Myopia (although it is suspected that myopic discs are
more susceptible to glaucomatous damage at a lower IOP
than emmetropic discs).

TIP In ocular hypertension, validated prediction models using
an individualвАЩs ocular parameters allow the risk of developing
glaucoma to be determined.

Genetics of ocular hypertension

A single nucleotide polymorphism in TMC01 appears to be signiоАЮcantly associated with conversion to glaucoma in White people
with ocular hypertension (three-fold risk in individuals with two
risk alleles compared with those with no risk alleles). A recent
meta-analysis has identiоАЮed 112 genetic loci associated with IOP
and suggests a major role for angiopoietin-receptor tyrosine kinase
signalling, lipid metabolism, mitochondrial function and developmental processes as risk factors for elevated IOP.

Clinical evaluation

History and examination should be carried out as for glaucoma
(see below). Consideration should be given to whether any systemic medication is being taken that might be inоАЬuencing IOP,
either upwards (e.g. steroids) or downwards (e.g. beta-blockers).

Pre-perimetric glaucoma

оАЭis concept refers to glaucomatous damage, usually manifested
by a suspicious optic disc and/or the presence of retinal nerve оАЮbre
layer defects, in which no visual оАЮeld abnormality has developed.
оАЭe оАЮeld-testing modality for this purpose is usually taken as standard achromatic automated perimetry.

Management

Raised IOP is an important risk factor in the development of
glaucoma and can be modiоАЮed with treatment. However, it is
diоАЩcult to justify the treatment of all individuals with raised
IOP because of the high prevalence of ocular hypertension, the
low conversion rate to glaucoma and the cost and side eоАЫects of
treatment. In the OHTS, untreated patients with ocular hypertension had a 9.5% cumulative risk of developing primary openangle glaucoma (POAG) aоАЪer 5 years. Treatment (which aimed
to reduce IOP by 20% or more and to reach 24 mmHg or less)
reduced this to 4.4%.
оАБ Validated prediction models using the ocular parameters of
a given individual with ocular hypertension allow the 5-year
risk of developing glaucoma to be estimated and expressed as a
percentage. оАЭis helps in deciding on the frequency of testing
and whether to start treatment.
оАБ In general, only those at high risk should be treated, although
patient preference may be a decisive factor.
оАБ Age, and therefore life expectancy, is a key point to consider.
оАБ Most practitioners would treat every patient with an IOP of 30
mmHg or more (>40% 5-year risk of glaucoma). оАЭe decision
to treat in patients with varying risk proоАЮles is not straightforward and has to be made on an individual basis.
оАБ OHT increases the risk of retinal venous occlusion, an additional point to consider when deciding whether to start
treatment.
оАБ Treatment options are the same as for POAG, although a less
aggressive pressure-lowering approach is frequently taken.
оАБ Careful monitoring is a reasonable alternative in many circumstances: baseline visual оАЮelds and retinal nerve оАЮbre layer
(RNFL)/disc imaging should be performed.

OVERVIEW OF GLAUCOMA

DeоАЯnition

Glaucoma is the term that is used to describe a group of conditions that have in common a chronic progressive optic neuropathy that results in characteristic morphological changes at the
optic nerve head and in the retinal nerve fibre layer. Progressive
retinal ganglion cell death and visual field loss are associated
with these changes. Intraocular pressure is a key modifiable
factor.

Goal of treatment

оАЭe goal of glaucoma treatment is to slow the rate of progression
throughout a patientвАЩs lifetime in order to maintain visual function
and related quality of life, at a sustainable cost.

ClassiоАЯcation

Glaucoma may be congenital (developmental) or acquired. Openangle and angle-closure types are distinguished based on the
mechanism by which aqueous outоАЬow is impaired with respect to
the AC angle conоАЮguration. Distinction is also made between primary and secondary glaucoma. In the latter a recognizable ocular
or non-ocular disorder contributes to elevation of IOP.

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 349
#### 11

Epidemiology

Glaucoma aоАЫects 2вАУ3% of people over the age of 40 years, but up to
50% may be undiagnosed. It is the second leading cause of blindness in the world. POAG is the most common form in White, Hispanic/Latino and Black individuals (with the prevalence especially
high in the latter). On a worldwide basis, primary angle closure
(PAC) constitutes up to half of cases and has a particularly high
prevalence in people of Asian descent. With improved assessment
such as the routine performance of gonioscopy in a darkened
rather than a bright environment, PAC is more prevalent in White
people than previously realized.

Natural history of open-angle glaucoma

оАЭe Early Manifest Glaucoma Trial (EMGT) provides prospective
natural history data on progression of glaucoma in three common
glaucoma types: high tension glaucoma (HTG), normal-tension
glaucoma (NTG) and pseudoexfoliation glaucoma (PXEG), using
change in the visual оАЮeld and optic disc as end-points. Without
treatment to reduce the IOP the mean rate of change in each group
is as follows: HTG вИТ1.31 dB/year, NTG вИТ0.36 dB/year, PXEG
вИТ3.13 dB/year. Large diоАЫerences exist among individuals and different glaucoma types, but based on the mean rate of change in
those untreated, visual function in an average glaucoma patient
appears to deteriorate from normal to blindness over a period of
approximately 25 years. оАЭis is inоАЬuenced signiоАЮcantly by using
treatment to reduce IOP. In patients with glaucoma under routine
care, approximately 1 in 8 eyes exhibit a change of mean deviation
(MD) of more than 1dB/year and the same proportion also show
this in the central 10 [o] region. When seeking to prevent functional
disability it is important to also assess progressive central visual
оАЮeld loss in patients with glaucoma.

PRIMARY OPEN-ANGLE GLAUCOMA

Introduction
DeоАЯnition

Primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) is a chronic, progressive
optic neuropathy of adult onset. It is characterized by:
оАБ Retinal nerve оАЮbre layer thinning.
оАБ Glaucomatous optic nerve damage.
оАБ Characteristic visual оАЮeld loss as damage progresses.
оАБ An open anterior chamber angle.
оАБ Absence of signs of secondary glaucoma or a non-glaucomatous
cause for the optic neuropathy.
оАБ IOP is a key modiоАЮable risk factor.
POAG is the most prevalent type of glaucoma in people of
European and African ethnic origin. In those older than 70 years
of age the prevalence of POAG has been reported to be approximately 6% in White populations, 16% in Black populations and
around 3% in Asian populations. It aоАЫects both genders equally.

Risk factors

оАБ IOP. оАЭe higher the IOP, the greater the likelihood of glaucoma.
Asymmetry of IOP of 4 mmHg or more is also signiоАЮcant.
оАБ Age. POAG is more common in older individuals.

350 Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

оАБ Race. It is signiоАЮcantly (perhaps four times) more common,
develops at an earlier age and may be more diоАЩcult to control
in Black people than in White people.
оАБ Family history of POAG. First-degree relatives of patients
with POAG are at increased risk. An approximate risk to siblings is four times and to oоАЫspring twice the normal population risk, though surveyed оАЮgures vary.
оАБ Diabetes mellitus. Longitudinal studies show no increased
risk of glaucoma. Selection bias probably explains why clinicbased studies report a higher prevalence of glaucoma in people
with diabetes.
оАБ Myopia is associated with an increased incidence of POAG
and myopic eyes may be more susceptible to glaucomatous
damage.
оАБ AntiоА† VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor) therapy.
Patients undergoing anti-VEGF therapy for age-related
macular degeneration or diabetic macular oedema are
at risk of sustained IOP elevation. This is more likely to
occur after recurrent injections with bevacizumab than
with ranibizumab. The risk is significantly greater for
patients with glaucoma than for normal individuals. The
risk that glaucoma surgery will be needed increases after
six injections.
оАБ Systemic calciumоА† channel blockers. Meta-analysis of large
population-based studies shows a higher prevalence of glaucoma in people using this medication as monotherapy.
оАБ Vascular disease. A range of systemic conditions linked to
vascular compromise may be associated, though clear-cut
relationships have proved diоАЩcult to demonstrate consistently.
Systemic hypertension, cardiovascular disease, diabetes and
vasospastic conditions such as migraine have all been implicated. Poor ocular perfusion may be a risk factor for glaucoma
progression.
оАБ Translaminar pressure gradient. Studies suggest that a diоАЫerence in the levels of IOP and orbital CSF pressure may increase
the likelihood of the development and progression of glaucomatous damage, perhaps due to associated deformation of the
lamina cribrosa.
оАБ Optic disc area. Large discs are more vulnerable to damage.
оАБ Ocular perfusion pressure is the diоАЫerence between the arterial BP and the IOP and has been shown in population studies
to be linked to increased risk for the development and progression of glaucoma. A low ocular perfusion pressure increases
the risk.

Genetics

оАБ Loci. POAG has been associated with at least 127 loci in the
human genome (Nature Communications 2021). Most loci
have broadly consistent eоАЫects in all races.
оАБ Mutations in only the MYOC gene, coding for the protein
myocilin that is found in the trabecular meshwork and
the OPTN gene, which codes for optineurin, are broadly
accepted as causing glaucoma. A number of different mutations have been described in the MYOC gene, though the
normal function of myocilin and its role in glaucoma is as
yet undetermined.

оАБ Family history. If a single family member develops glaucoma
prior to age 35 years, the chances of a mutation in the myocilin
gene may be as high as 33%.Genetic investigation of a patient
and family may be considered if three or more оАЮrst-degree
relatives from two generations are aоАЫected, or for research
purposes.
оАБ A polygenetic risk score (PRS) has been developed that can
predict glaucoma susceptibility and the risk of developing
advanced disease. It is possible that in time the PRS will result
in a personalized approach for earlier treatment of high-risk
individuals, with less intensive monitoring and treatment
being possible for those at low risk.

Pathogenesis of glaucomatous optic
neuropathy

Retinal ganglion cell death in glaucoma occurs predominantly
through apoptosis (programmed cell death) rather than necrosis.
оАЭe preterminal event is calcium ion inоАЬux into the cell body and
an increase in intracellular nitric oxide. Glutamine metabolism
is intrinsically involved. AоАЪer initial injury, a cascade of events
results in astrocyte and glial cell proliferation and alterations in
the extracellular matrix of the lamina cribrosa, with subsequent
optic nerve head remodelling. оАЭe process of glaucomatous damage and the relationship with IOP and other potential inоАЬuences is
still poorly understood. One or both of the following mechanisms
may be involved:
оАБ Direct mechanical damage to retinal nerve оАЮbres at the
optic nerve head, perhaps as they pass through the lamina
cribrosa. Accumulating evidence of the inоАЬuence of mechanical deformability in the region of the lamina cribrosa supports this.
оАБ Ischaemic damage, possibly due to compression of blood
vessels supplying the optic nerve head. This may relate
to ocular perfusion pressure as a possible risk factor for
glaucoma.
оАБ Common pathways of damage. Both mechanisms might lead
to a reduction in axoplasmic оАЬow, interference with the delivery of nutrients or removal of metabolic products, deprivation
of neuronal growth factors, oxidative injury and the initiation
of immune-mediated damage.

Screening

Universal population screening for glaucoma has not been demonstrated to be cost-eоАЫective and current practice restricts caseоАЮnding to high-risk groups, such as: (a) older individuals, (b) those
over the age of 40 with a history of POAG in a close family member and (c) people of African racial background. In these groups,
screening tends to be performed sporadically via routes such as
commercial optometric eye examinations, which may lead to the
relative exclusion of underprivileged economic groups. Population screening with tonometry alone is unsatisfactory, since it will
label as normal a signiоАЮcant number of cases with other features of
POAG such as cupping and visual оАЮeld loss, and routine screening
eye examinations should include visual оАЮeld assessment as well as
tonometry and ophthalmoscopy.

Diagnosis
History

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 351
#### 11

Evaluation of the optic nerve head
Normal optic nerve head

оАБ Visual symptoms will usually be absent, unless damage is
advanced. Sometimes symptomatic central оАЮeld defects may
occur at an early stage, in the presence of a relatively normal
peripheral оАЮeld.
оАБ Previous ophthalmic history. SpeciоАЮc enquiry should be
made about:

{ Refractive status, as myopia carries an increased risk of
POAG and hypermetropia of primary angle-closure glaucoma (PACG).

{ Causes of secondary glaucoma such as ocular trauma or
inоАЬammation. Previous eye surgery, including refractive
surgery which may aоАЫect IOP readings.
оАБ Family history

{ POAG or related conditions such as OHT.

{ Other ocular disease in family members.
оАБ Past medical history

{ Asthma, heart failure or block, peripheral vascular disease,
which are contraindications to the use of beta-blockers.

{ Head injury, intracranial pathology including stroke, as
these conditions may cause optic atrophy or visual оАЮeld
defects.

{ Vasospasm: migraine and Raynaud phenomenon.

{ Diabetes, systemic hypertension and cardiovascular disease may increase the risk of POAG.

{ Oral contraceptive pill for several years may be associated
with an increased risk of glaucoma.
оАБ Current medication

{ Steroids, including skin cream and inhalants.

{ Oral beta-blockers may lower IOP.

{ Social history, including smoking and alcohol intake,
especially if toxic/nutritional optic neuropathy is
suspected.

{ Allergies, particularly to any drugs likely to be used in
glaucoma treatment, e.g. acetazolamide is contraindicated
if there is a history of sulfonamide allergy.

оАБ Neuroretinal rim. оАЭe neuroretinal rim (NRR) is the orangepink tissue between the outer edge of the cup and the optic
disc margin. оАЭe inferior rim is the broadest followed by the
superior, nasal and temporal (the вАШISNTвАЩ rule). оАЭis has high
sensitivity (81%) for glaucoma but is not speciоАЮc (32%), i.e.
eyes without glaucoma oоАЪen do not respect the rule.
оАБ Cup/disc (C/D) ratio. оАЭe C/D ratio indicates the diameter of the cup expressed as a fraction of the diameter of the
disc. оАЭe vertical rather than the horizontal ratio is generally taken (Fig. 11.6A). It is diоАЩcult to estimate the C/D
ratio accurately without imaging. As a basic principle, small
diameter optic discs have small cups (Fig. 11.6B) and large
diameter discs have large cups (Fig. 11.6C). Only 2% of the
population have a C/D ratio greater than 0.7. In any individual, asymmetry of 0.2 or more between the eyes should also
be regarded with suspicion, though it is critical to exclude a
corresponding diоАЫerence in overall disc diameter (Fig. 11.7A
and B).
оАБ Optic disc size. Optic disc size is important in deciding if a
C/D ratio is normal and is also a prognostic indicator. Large
discs are believed to be more likely to sustain damage, particularly in NTG. оАЭis may be the result of the larger diameter
conferring relative mechanical weakness and hence greater
vulnerability to IOP-induced displacement of the lamina
cribrosa, which has been found to be thinner in eyes with
NTG. Disc size varies on average between racial groups and
is largest in those of African descent. Imaging can objectively measure disc area, but vertical diameter is the parameter most frequently used clinically. Normal median vertical
diameter (for non-glaucomatous discs) is 1.5вАУ1.7 mm in a
white population.

{ A narrow slit beam is focused on the disc using a fundus
lens.

{ оАЭe height of the beam is adjusted until it matches the
distance between the superior and inferior limits of the
NRR (not the scleral rim surrounding the neural tissue)
and the diameter in millimetres is read from the slit lamp
graticule.

Examination

оАБ Visual acuity (VA) is likely to be normal except in advanced
glaucoma.
оАБ Pupils. Exclude a relative aоАЫerent pupillary defect (RAPD). If
initially absent but develops later, this constitutes an indicator
of substantial progression.
оАБ Colour vision assessment such as Ishihara chart testing
if there is any suggestion of an optic neuropathy other than
glaucoma.
оАБ Slit lamp examination. Exclude features of secondary glaucoma such as pigmentary and pseudoexfoliation.
оАБ Tonometry prior to pachymetry, noting the time of day.
оАБ Gonioscopy.
оАБ Optic disc examination for glaucomatous changes (see below)
should be performed with the pupils dilated, provided gonioscopy does not show critically narrow angles. Red-free light can
be used to detect RNFL defects.

{ A correction factor may be necessary, depending on the
lens used (Table 11.3). Refractive error aоАЫects measurement only minimally, although myopia above вИТ8 dioptres
may distort the result.
оАБ InterоА† eye disc asymmetry. A vertical cup/disc ratio asymmetry of 0.2 or more is found in about 5% of normal individuals
and in about 25% of those with glaucoma. However, asymmetry of the vertical cup/disc ratio alone is not useful in identifying patients with glaucoma. When comparing the two discs, a
diоАЫerence in the appearance of the neuroretinal rim is a good
sign of early glaucoma (Fig. 11.7 CвАУF).

TIP A large disc has a large cup and may be entirely healthy,
while any cupping in a small disc may be abnormal.

Changes in glaucoma

In some cases, it is not possible to be certain whether an individual optic disc is glaucomatous. оАЭe clinical оАЮndings and results of
investigation should be considered together to guide management.

Glaucomatous damage results in characteristic signs involving (a)
the optic nerve head, (b) the peripapillary area and (c) the retinal
nerve оАЮbre layer.
оАБ Optic nerve head. Pathological cupping is caused by an irreversible decrease in the number of nerve оАЮbres, glial cells and
blood vessels. A documented increase in cup size is always signiоАЮcant (Fig. 11.8). If an eye with a small optic disc and correspondingly small cup develops glaucoma, the cup will increase
in size, but even in the presence of substantial damage may still
be smaller than that of a large physiological cup.
оАБ Subtypes of glaucomatous damage. Four morphological glaucomatous disc appearances have been described and although
the majority of discs are unclassiоАЮable the descriptions encompass a useful overview of patterns of glaucomatous damage
and may provide clues to underlying pathological processes.

{ Focal ischaemic discs (Fig. 11.9A) are characterized by
localized superior and/or inferior notching and are associated with localized оАЮeld defects with early threat to оАЮxation.

{ Myopic disc with glaucoma (Fig. 11.9B) refers to a tilted
(obliquely inserted), shallow disc with a temporal crescent of parapapillary atrophy, together with features of
glaucomatous damage. Dense superior or inferior scotomas threatening оАЮxation are common. оАЭis morphology is
most common in younger male patients.

{ Sclerotic discs (Fig. 11.9C) are characterized by a shallow,
saucerized cup and a gently sloping NRR, variable peripapillary atrophy and peripheral visual оАЮeld loss. оАЭe peripapillary choroid is thinner than in other disc types. Patients
are older, of either gender and there is an association with
systemic vascular disease.

{ Concentrically enlarging discs (veriоАЮed by serial monitoring) are characterized by fairly uniform NRR thinning
(Fig. 11.9D) and are frequently associated with diоАЫuse
visual оАЮeld loss. IOP is usually signiоАЮcantly elevated at
presentation.
оАБ NonоА† speciоАЮc signs of glaucomatous damage. Other disc
signs of glaucomatous damage include:

{ Disc haemorrhages (Fig. 11.10A and B) oоАЪen extend from
the NRR onto the retina, most commonly inferotemporally. оАЭeir presence is a risk factor for the development
and progression of glaucoma. оАЭey are more common in
NTG but can also occur in healthy individuals as well as
patients with systemic vascular disease. When screening
for glaucoma, approximately three out of four people with
a disc haemorrhage will not have glaucoma.
