356 Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

{ Baring of circumlinear blood vessels is a sign of early thinning of the NRR. It is characterized by a space between
the neuroretinal rim and a superоАЮcial blood vessel (Fig.
11.10C).

{ Bayoneting is characterized by double angulation of a
blood vessel. With NRR loss, a vessel entering the disc
from the retina may angle sharply backwards into the disc
and then turn towards its original direction to run across
the lamina cribrosa (Fig. 11.10D).

{ Collaterals between two veins at the disc (Fig. 11.10E),
similar to those following central retinal vein occlusion
(CRVO), are relatively uncommon. оАЭey are probably
caused by chronic low-grade circulatory obstruction. Retinal vascular tortuosity may also occur.

{ Loss of nasal NRR (Fig. 11.10F) is a sign of moderately
advanced damage. A space may develop between the NRR
and the central retinal vasculature.

{ оАЭe laminar dot sign occurs in advancing glaucoma. Grey
dot-like fenestrations in the lamina cribrosa (see Fig.
11.10F) become exposed as the NRR recedes. оАЭe fenestrations sometimes appear linear and this itself may be a sign
of advanced damage, indicating distortion of the lamina.
оАЭe dots may be seen in normal eyes.

{ Sharpened edgeвАЩ or вАШsharpened rimвАЩ is a sign of advancing
damage. As NRR is lost adjacent to the edge of the disc,
the disc margin contour assumes a sharper angle backwards. Bayoneting of vessels is oоАЪen seen at a sharpened
edge (see Fig. 11.10 D). оАЭis should not be confused with
a вАШsharpened nasal polar edgeвАЩ, which refers to the sharp
angulation of the NRR at the nasal margin of a focal vertical polar notch.

TIP An optic disc haemorrhage is a risk factor for the
development and progression of glaucoma and can be missed
unless magniоАЫcation is used to examine the disc.

Fig. 11.11 Peripapillary changes. Alpha zone (white arrow) and
beta zone (black arrow).

оАБ Peripapillary changes. Peripapillary atrophy (PPA) surrounding the optic nerve head may be of signiоАЮcance in glaucoma
(Fig. 11.11) and may be a sign of early damage in patients with
ocular hypertension.

{ Alpha (outer) zone is characterized by superоАЮcial retinal
pigment epithelial changes. It tends to be larger and possibly more common in glaucomatous eyes.

{ Fibres arising from the macula follow a straight course to
the optic nerve head, forming a spindle-shaped area (papillomacular bundle).

{ Fibres arising from the nasal retina also follow a relatively
straight course to the optic nerve.

{ Fibres arising temporal to the macula follow an arcuate path around the papillomacular bundle to reach
the optic nerve head. They do not cross the horizontal raphe that extends from the foveola to the temporal
retinal periphery, demarcating the superior and inferior
halves of the retina.

{ оАЭe arcuate оАЮbres reaching the superotemporal and
inferotemporal aspect of the optic nerve head are most
vulnerable to glaucomatous damage. оАЭe оАЮbres in the
papillomacular bundle are the most resistant.
Within the optic nerve head, the retinal оАЮbres are arranged as
follows (Fig. 11.12B):
оАБ Fibres from the peripheral fundus lie deep within the retinal
nerve оАЮbre layer (i.e. nearer the pigment epithelium) but occupy
the most peripheral (superоАЮcial) portion of the optic nerve.
оАБ Fibres arising near to the optic nerve lie superоАЮcially within
the nerve оАЮbre layer (i.e. near the vitreous) but they occupy the
central (deep) portion of the optic nerve.
оАБ In glaucoma subtle RNFL defects precede the development of
detectable optic disc and visual оАЮeld changes. оАЭeir onset oоАЪen
follows disc haemorrhages. Two patterns occur: (a) localized
wedge-shaped defects and (b) diоАЫuse defects that are larger
and have indistinct borders. Defects are sometimes evident
following disc haemorrhages. Red-free (green) light increases
the contrast between normal retina and defects on slit lamp
biomicroscopy or fundus photography and typically makes
identiоАЮcation easier (Fig.11.13). Optical coherence tomography (OCT) and scanning laser polarimetry are highly eоАЫective
means of quantifying the RNFL (see Fig. 11.16 and 11.17). It
should be noted that RNFL defects are not speciоАЮc to glaucoma and can be seen in a range of neurological disease, as well
as in apparently normal individuals.

{ Beta (inner) zone is characterized by chorioretinal atrophy. It is distinct from the scleral rim, the white band of
exposed sclera central to the beta zone. оАЭe beta zone is
larger and more common in glaucoma and is a risk factor
for progression. оАЭe location of beta-zone PPA seems to
indicate the orientation of likely visual оАЮeld loss.
оАБ Retinal nerve оАЮbre layer. An understanding of the distribution of the 1.2 million ganglion cell axons as they pass across
the retina to enter the scleral canal to form the optic nerve
head (optic disc) is key to the interpretation of visual оАЮeld loss
in relation to optic nerve cupping in glaucoma. Within the
retina the arrangement is as follows (Fig. 11.12A):

Fig. 11.13 Retinal nerve оАЫbre layer defect. оАЭAоАЮ Red-free photograph (arrows show wedgeshaped defect) and a disc margin haemorrhage); оАЭBоАЮ corresponding superior arcuate defect

TIP Retinal nerve оАЫbre layer defects precede the

Imaging in glaucoma

Imaging has become an indispensable tool in the management
of patients with glaucoma. However, it is important to remember
that in order to determine a diagnosis or to assess change, imaging should always be used to supplement clinical examination and
visual оАЮeld results, rather than as the sole investigation.
оАБ Pachymetry, the measurement of corneal thickness, is an
essential part of the assessment of glaucoma patients (see
Fig. 11.5).
оАБ Stereo disc photography. Stereo photography has historically
been regarded as the reference standard in optic disc imaging and remains a valuable option. оАЭe images are taken by

repositioning slightly between shots, either manually or using
a stereo separator built into the camera.
оАБ Ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM). оАЭis is a type of ultrasound that creates a more detailed image than regular ultrasound and is used for imaging the anterior segment of the
eye. оАЭis results in resolutions up to 25 ќЉm axially and 50 ќЉm
laterally, but the depth of tissue penetration is less than this.
It is particularly useful to show the anatomy in patients with
primary angle-closure glaucoma (Fig. 11.14). In comparison
to anterior segment OCT, the main strength of UBM is its ability to demonstrate structures behind the iris, particularly the
ciliary body and lens.
оАБ Optical coherence tomography of the anterior segment.
Anterior segment optical coherence tomography has an
expanding range of clinical applications primarily in the evaluation of angle-closure glaucoma. It is useful when demonstrating the relationship of the peripheral iris to the оАЮltration angle
(Fig. 11.15).

Fig. 11.15 OCT of the peripheral iris. оАЭAоАЮ Thickened iris; оАЭBоАЮ abnormal iris insertion; оАЭCоАЮ peripheral iris roll on pupil dilatation.

оАБ Anterior chamber depth measurement. Objective measurement of the depth of the AC is often clinically useful
in glaucoma management. Indications include assessment
of PAC risk and monitoring of progression in conditions
where the AC is shallow, such as post-trabeculectomy
hypotony and ciliolenticular block. Older methods used a
slit lamp with or without a special attachment, but an accurate and repeatable measurement can be obtained using
OCT, ultrasonographic or optical interferometric methods
(e.g. Zeiss IOLMaster).
оАБ Optical coherence tomography of the posterior pole. OCT
is most useful when the patient is either a glaucoma suspect
or has earlyвАУmoderate disease (Fig. 11.16). It can be used
to detect damage and progression (Fig. 11.17). In advanced
glaucoma it is less helpful because of the вАШfloorвАЩ effect (i.e.
when the retinal nerve fibre layer reaches 45вАУ50 ќЉm it does
not decrease any further even though progressive damage
may be occurring). Macular thickness measurements may
be more helpful in these circumstances. Sensitivity and
specificity utilizing comparison with a normative database
is as high as 90%.

{ Peripapillary RNFL. оАЭis involves the acquisition of a
circular scan of the retina around the optic nerve head.
Because diоАЫerent OCT devices use diоАЫerent scanning
protocols, caution should be taken when comparing
RNFL thickness between machines. By using the Bruch
membrane opening rather than the optic disc margin,
centration of the image can be enhanced. оАЭe results are
compared to a colour-coded normative database. When
assessing structural changes over time, it is oоАЪen diоАЩcult to distinguish between glaucomatous change and
measurement variability or age-related structural loss.
Up to 4 ќЉm inter-test оАЬuctuation has been reported. A
new defect, widening of an established defect or deepening of a defect are important features of glaucomatous
change.

{ Optic nerve head. Radial cross-sectional scans permit an
objective and repeatable assessment of disc morphology,
with reasonable discriminatory value. оАЭis function is less
commonly used than RNFL.

{ Ganglion cell complex (GCC) analysis involves measurement of the thickness of the RNFL, the ganglion cell layer
and the inner plexiform layer at the macula in order to
detect early glaucomatous damage and avoids the confounding eоАЫect of optic disc anatomical variation. оАЭis is
as eоАЫective for diagnosing glaucoma and assessing progression as analysis of the RNFL. OCT signal quality is
good, particularly in the elderly and diseased eye. It should
be considered supplementary to RNFL assessment (Figs
11.18 and 11.19).

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 359
#### 11

{ Progression analysis soоАЪware has been introduced on several
machines and provides a computed assessment of the extent
of damage over time. Trend-based analysis using a number of
scans, measures the shape of change and is particularly useful
when conоАЮrming that progressive change has occurred.

TIP OCT imaging of the optic disc should not be used in
isolation but rather to supplement the оАЫndings of clinical
examination.

оАБ Confocal scanning laser ophthalmoscopy. оАЭis employs
a scanning laser ophthalmoscope (SLO) to build a threedimensional image of the optic nerve head and retina.

{ оАЭe Heidelberg Retinal Tomograph (HRT) is still in widespread clinical practice, but has been superseded by optical
coherence tomography. As with the OCT, it is used to distinguish normal from glaucomatous eyes by comparison
against a normative database (MoorоАЮelds regression analysis) and to monitor disease progression.

{ Keratometry values must be entered and signiоАЮcant (>1.0
dioptre) astigmatism corrected by means of a cylindrical lens. High-quality images can usually be acquired
without pupillary dilatation and through mildвАУmoderate
lens opacity. AоАЪer image capture, for greatest accuracy
the operator should manually mark the contour line that
deоАЮnes the edge of the neuroretinal rim.

{ Images, data and analysis can be examined on a computer
screen or print-out (Fig. 11.20).

{ Detailed stereometric data are presented, with abnormal
readings identiоАЮed.
оАБ Scanning laser polarimetry. оАЭe GDx (Glaucoma Diagnosis)
VCC (variable corneal compensation) RNFL analyser assesses
the nerve оАЮbre layer thickness by using its вАШbirefringentвАЩ
(resolving or splitting a light wave into two unequally reоАЬected
or transmitted waves) nature to change the polarization of
incident polarized diode laser light. оАЭe amount of alteration
is directly related to the thickness of the layer.

{ A display provides colour images of the optic nerve head,
together with RNFL maps in four quadrants. Deviation
maps show the location and magnitude of RNFL defects as
tiny colour-coded squares and parameters for each eye are
displayed in a table (Fig. 11.21).

{ A global value based on the entire thickness map is the optimal parameter for discriminating normal from glaucoma.

{ оАЭis technology has been superseded in recent years by
OCT.

TIP Imaging has become an indispensable tool in the
management of patients with glaucoma.

366 Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

Visual оАЫeld defects оАЭsee Ch. 1, perimetryоАЮ

Nerve damage in glaucoma is believed to be inоАЬicted at the optic
nerve head and the resultant visual оАЮeld defect corresponds to the
pattern of оАЮbres in the retinal area served. Standard automated
perimetry (SAP) is relatively insensitive in the early stages of the
disease.
оАБ Early changes include increased variability of responses in
areas that subsequently develop defects and slight asymmetry
between the two eyes. Special modalities such as frequency
doubling technology (FDT) and short wavelength automated

perimetry (SWAP) may demonstrate defects at an earlier
stage.
оАБ Small paracentral depressions (Fig. 11.22A) can form at a
relatively early stage, oоАЪen superonasally. оАЭey are more commonly seen in NTG.
оАБ Nasal step represents a diоАЫerence in sensitivity above and
below the horizontal midline in the nasal оАЮeld. оАЭe defect is
bounded by the horizontal midline, corresponding to the retinal nerve оАЮbre layer horizontal raphe. Inferior optic disc and
OCT changes with a corresponding superior nasal step are
shown in Fig. 11.22B

A B

C D

E F

Fig. 11.22 оАЭAвАУFоАЮ Grey-scale display showing progression of glaucomatous damage in diоАЪerent
patients (see text).

оАБ Temporal wedge is less common than a nasal step but has
similar implications.
оАБ Arcuate defects (Fig. 11.22C) develop as a result of coalescence of paracentral scotomas. оАЭey typically develop between
10¬∞ and 20¬∞ of оАЮxation as downward or upward extensions
from the blind spot around оАЮxation. With time, they tend to
elongate circumferentially along the distribution of arcuate
nerve оАЮbres (Fig. 11.22D and E).
оАБ A ring scotoma develops when superior and inferior arcuate
defects become continuous, usually in advanced glaucoma (Fig.
11.22F).
оАБ EndоА† stage changes are characterized by a small island of central vision, typically accompanied by a temporal island. оАЭe
10-2 perimetry pattern facilitates monitoring of the residual
central оАЮeld.
оАБ Minimal criteria for glaucomatous damage on SAP. One
of the following defects on Humphrey visual оАЮeld testing
(Hodapp, Parrish and AndersonвАЩs criteria)

{ Glaucoma hemiоАЮeld test outside normal limits on at least
two consecutive occasions. оАЭis provides information concerning diоАЫerences between superior and inferior halves
of the visual оАЮeld by evaluating threshold at mirror-image
points above and below the horizontal meridian. оАЭis program also compares the overall height of the hill of vision
with age-adjusted normal values.

{ A cluster of three or more non-edge points in a location
typical for glaucoma, all of which are depressed on PSD at
P <5% level and one which is depressed at P <1% level, on
two consecutive occasions (Fig. 11.23).

Fig. 11.23 Positive diagnostic criterion for glaucoma showing

(P50 component) and a positive deоАЬection near 95ms (N95
component). оАЭe amplitude and implicit time of each of these
components can be measured. оАЭe M95 component is reduced
in glaucoma.
оАБ Clinical uses. оАЭis technology detects subtle optic neuropathy
and may have a role to play in the follow-up of some patients
with glaucoma. It is not a substitute for clinical examination,
visual оАЮeld testing and OCT examination of the RNFL. Steady
state pattern ERG may demonstrate: (a) decline of ganglion
cell function, (b) treatment response, (c) recovery of ganglion
cell function (Fig. 11.28).

Management

оАЭe primary aim of treatment is to prevent functional impairment
of vision within the patientвАЩs lifetime and to maintain a good quality of life by slowing the rate of ganglion cell loss. Currently the
only proven method of achieving this is to reduce IOP. Both higher
mean IOP and substantial variation in IOP are predictive of progressive visual оАЮeld loss in patients with glaucoma, whether newly
diagnosed or advanced.

Patient instruction

An explanation should be oоАЫered concerning the nature of the disease and relevant literature provided. оАЭe timing of medication use
should be speciоАЮed, and the patient educated in the technique of
eye drop instillation. At follow-up visits the patientвАЩs proоАЮciency
at instilling drops should be checked. In order to maximize drug
contact time with the anterior segment and to minimize systemic
absorption, the patient should be instructed either to perform lacrimal sac occlusion by applying оАЮngertip pressure at the medial
canthus or to close the eyes for about 3 minutes aоАЪer instillation.
Common or severe potential adverse eоАЫects should be explained
at the commencement of treatment and their occurrence enquired
about at review visits.

{ Corrected PSD that occurs in less than 5% of normal individuals on two consecutive оАЮelds.
оАБ Summary measures should always be considered. On average,
an annual deterioration in mean total deviation of just over
1.0dB can be expected in untreated patients, but there is signiоАЮcant individual variation (see вАШNatural history of openangle glaucomaвАЩ above) (Fig. 11.24).
оАБ Staging. A discrete-levels staging system can be used to determine
the stage of damage. Early glaucomatous loss is a MD of < вИТ6 dB
(Fig. 11.25), moderate glaucomatous loss вИТ6 to вИТ12 dB (Fig. 11.26)
and severe glaucomatous loss > вИТ12 dB (Fig. 11.27). Any absolute
defect (0 dB) in the central 5 degrees is considered вАШsevereвАЩ.

Pattern ERG (pERG)

оАЭe electroretinogram (ERG) measures the electrical activity of
the retina in response to a light stimulus (see Ch.15). Pattern
ERG (pERG) uses a similar stimulus to that used when measuring visual evoked potential (pattern reversal) and targets macular
ganglion cell function. It is an objective measure of retinal function that can be recorded non-invasively under physiological
conditions.
оАБ Accurate refraction and a clear ocular media are important
for pERG measurements.
оАБ оАЯe pERG waveform consists of a small negative deоАЬection
near 35ms (N35 component), a positive deоАЬection near 50ms

Fig. 11.24 Progression of visual оАЫeld defect and deterioration of global indices over a period

Treatment goals

оАБ Target pressure. It is assumed that the pre-treatment level
of IOP has damaged the optic nerve and will continue to do
so. An IOP level is identified below which further damage
is considered unlikely: the target pressure. This is identified
considering the severity of existing damage (particularly a
greater vertical C/D ratio and a higher mean deviation on
visual fields), the level of IOP, CCT, the rapidity with which
damage occurred if known and the age and general health
of the patient. Greater age is associated with a higher likelihood of rapid progression, but a shorter life expectancy may
also influence management. Therapy should maintain the
IOP at or below the target level. If not achievable by conservative measures, a decision is made on whether to proceed
to surgery or to continue monitoring with an above-target
IOP.
оАБ A reasonable initial goal is to reduce the IOP to less than
18 mmHg. This is based on the results of the Advanced
Glaucoma Intervention Study (AGIS), which found that
significant visual field progression is unlikely to occur
in most patients in the medium term if IOP can be kept
below 18 mmHg at all times.
оАБ Progression risk, as found by the EMGT, is influenced by:
(a) magnitude of initial IOP reduction, (b) height of the
baseline IOP, (c) presence of pseudoexfoliation syndrome,
(d) presence of bilateral disease, (e) worse mean deviation,
(f) older age.
оАБ Response to progression. If damage progresses despite a target pressure having been reached consistently, the target IOP
is set to a lower level. As damage progresses, the loss of each
remaining ganglion cell has a greater proportional impact on
visual function and there is less reserve capacity. If further
visual loss occurs despite apparently good IOP control, surgery
should be considered.

Medical therapy

See later in this chapter.
оАБ Commencing medical therapy

{ Any drug chosen should be prescribed in the lowest concentration consistent with the desired therapeutic eоАЫect
and administered as infrequently as possible.

{ Ideally the drug with the fewest potential side eоАЫects
should be used.

{ Initial treatment is usually with one type of medication,
typically a prostaglandin analogue or beta-blocker.
оАБ Review

{ оАЭe interval to review aоАЪer starting medication is set
according to the individual patient, but is usually 4вАУ8
weeks.

{ Response to the drug is assessed against the target IOP.

{ If the response is satisfactory, subsequent assessment is
generally set for a further 3вАУ6 months.

{ If there has been little or no response the initial drug is
withdrawn and another substituted.

{ When two separate drugs are used the patient should be

{ Sometimes it may be worthwhile to allow a further month

{ Inadequate drop instillation technique should be considered as a cause of unsatisfactory IOP response.

{ When drops are administered in the morning, it is good
practice always to ask whether that dayвАЩs dose has been
used prior to attendance.
оАБ Perimetry. If IOP control is good and glaucomatous damage
mild or moderate with no substantial threat to central vision,
perimetry every 6вАУ12 months is generally suоАЩcient.
оАБ Gonioscopy should be performed annually in most patients
because the anterior chamber angle tends to narrow with age.
оАБ Optic disc examination should be performed at each visit,
as a disc haemorrhage may indicate ongoing damage. A new
haemorrhage should be recorded pictorially, optimally by
photography.

оАБ Serial imaging is increasingly viewed as standard care.
оАБ Causes of treatment failure

{ Inappropriate target pressure. If the IOP is maintained in
the upper part of the statistically normal range, progressive
оАЮeld loss is relatively common.

{ Poor adherence to the therapeutic regimen occurs in at
least 25% of patients.

{ Wide оАЬuctuations in IOP are not uncommon in patients
treated medically and are associated with a tendency to
progression.

{ Patients may deteriorate despite apparently good IOP control. Causes include occult adherence failure, undetected

readily detectable clinically such as impaired optic nerve
perfusion. оАЭe possibility of an alternative pathology, particularly a compressive lesion, should always be considered
in these circumstances.
оАБ Laser trabeculoplasty (see later in this chapter). Selective laser
trabeculoplasty (SLT) is as eоАЫective as medical monotherapy
in reducing the IOP. Based on the 6-year results of the LiGHT
trial (which shows that SLT results in reduced progression
rates, cost-saving and improved quality of life compared to
topical medication) SLT has recently been recommended as
оАЮrst-line treatment in patients with newly diagnosed ocular
hypertension and chronic open-angle glaucoma.
оАБ Surgery (see later in this chapter). Trabeculectomy is the
surgical procedure most commonly performed for POAG.
In recent years the threshold for glaucoma drainage device
implantation has been lowered by many surgeons. Nonpenetrating surgery (e.g. deep sclerectomy, viscocanalostomy) is utilized extensively in POAG by some specialists.
оАЭere is a role for micro-invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS)
in selected cases of glaucoma. PhacoemulsiоАЮcation alone is
frequently associated with a 15% fall in IOP, but is generally
only oоАЫered to patients in whom signiоАЮcant lens opacity is
present. It can be combined with a оАЮltration or a MIGS procedure. Progressive damage is more likely to be slowed aоАЪer
surgery, probably because the resultant IOP is oоАЪen lower
than the preoperative level, is less likely to оАЬuctuate and
because adherence is no longer a factor.
оАБ Prognosis. Most patients with POAG will not go blind in
their lifetime, but the incidence of blindness varies considerably depending on multiple factors such as the presence of
advanced damage at diagnosis and non-compliance with treatment. In a White population with POAG the lifetime chance
of blindness in both eyes has historically been 5вАУ10%. Given
the long-term nature of progression in glaucoma, the prognosis appears to have signiоАЮcantly improved with new treatment

strategies. оАЭe average period from diagnosis to death has
been estimated at around 16 years and only about a third will
still be alive 20 years aоАЪer diagnosis.

NORMAL-TENSION GLAUCOMA

Introduction

NTG, also referred to as low-tension or normal-pressure glaucoma,
is usually regarded as a variant of POAG. оАЭe distinction between
NTG and POAG is based on an epidemiologically derived range of
normal IOP. It is an arbitrary division that may not have signiоАЮcant
clinical value, though it is possible that a spectrum exists in which,
towards the NTG end, IOP-independent factors are of increasing
relative importance. Depending on ethnic background, 30вАУ65% of
patients with open-angle glaucoma may have an IOP that is within
the normal range on initial assessment.
NTG is characterized by:
оАБ IOP consistently equal to or less than 21 mmHg on diurnal
testing.
оАБ Signs of optic nerve damage in a characteristic glaucomatous
pattern.
оАБ Visual оАЮeld loss as damage progresses, consistent in pattern
with the nerve appearance.
оАБ An open anterior chamber angle.
оАБ No features of secondary glaucoma or a non-glaucomatous
cause for the neuropathy.

Pathogenesis

Any aetiological factors distinct from those in POAG have not
been conclusively determined, although various mechanisms
have been postulated including anomalies of local and systemic
vascular function, structural optic nerve anomalies and autoimmune disease. With the introduction of widespread CCT assessment, NTG in some patients can be explained by very low CCT.

372 Normal-Tension Glaucoma

Overall CCT in patients with NTG is lower than in POAG. A
small proportion of NTG patients have been found to have
marked nocturnal IOP spikes, sometimes only detected on testing in the supine position.

Risk factors

оАБ Age. Patients tend to be older than those with POAG, though
this may be due to delayed diagnosis.
оАБ Gender. Some studies have found a higher prevalence in
females.
оАБ Race. NTG occurs more frequently in Japanese people.
оАБ Family history. оАЭe prevalence of POAG is greater in families
of patients with NTG than in the normal population. Mutations in the OPTN gene coding for optineurin have been identiоАЮed in some patients with NTG, though also in patients with
POAG.
оАБ CCT is lower in patients with NTG than POAG.
оАБ Abnormal vasoregulation, particularly migraine and Raynaud phenomenon, has been found more commonly in NTG
than POAG by some investigators. However, others have found
abnormalities just as commonly in POAG. Systemic diseases
associated with vascular risk, such as diabetes, carotid insufоАЮciency, hypertension and hypercoagulability may also be
important.
оАБ Systemic hypotension including nocturnal blood pressure
dips of >20%, particularly in those on oral hypotensive medication (Fig. 11.29). оАЭe EMGT conоАЮrms that low blood pressure is a risk factor in patients with NTG. оАЭe duration and
magnitude of decrease in nocturnal blood pressure below the
daytime mean blood pressure predicts progression in NTG.
оАБ Obstructive sleep apnoea syndrome may be associated, perhaps via an eоАЫect on ocular perfusion.
оАБ Autoantibody levels have been found to be higher in some
groups of NTG patients by some investigators.
оАБ Low serum retinol levels are more likely to be present in NTG
patients.
оАБ Translaminar pressure gradient. оАЭis may on average be
larger than in POAG.
оАБ Ocular perfusion pressure may be relatively lower than in
POAG.
оАБ Myopia is associated with a greater likelihood of glaucoma and
of its progression.
оАБ оАЯyroid disease may be more common.

DiоАЮerential diagnosis

оАБ Angle closure should always be ruled out by meticulous darkroom gonioscopy.
оАБ Low CCT leading to underestimation of IOP. It is possible that
a thin posterior ocular wall may increase mechanical stress in
the region of the lamina cribrosa. Prior refractive surgery and
corneal ectasia also lead to falsely low IOP readings.
оАБ POAG presenting with apparently normal IOP because of
wide diurnal оАЬuctuation. Plotting a diurnal IOP curve over an
8-hour period (phasing) during oоАЩce hours may detect daytime elevation, but detection of nocturnal IOP spikes requires
substantial resource commitment.

{ Splinter haemorrhages on the disc margin are more frequent than in POAG and are associated with a greater likelihood of progression. оАЭese are oоАЪen missed if the disc is

оАБ Previous episodes of raised IOP may have occurred as a
result of ocular trauma, uveitis or local or systemic steroid
therapy.
оАБ Masking by systemic treatment such as an oral beta-blocker,
commenced aоАЪer glaucomatous damage has already been
sustained.
оАБ Spontaneously resolved pigmentary glaucoma. оАЭe typical
examination features of pigmentary glaucoma tend to become
less evident with increasing age. оАЭe IOP in some cases of
POAG may also spontaneously normalize over time.
оАБ Progressive retinal nerve оАЮbre defects not due to glaucoma
such as may occur in myopic degeneration and optic disc
drusen.
оАБ Congenital disc anomalies simulating glaucomatous cupping,
such as a disc pit or coloboma (see Fig. 19.24).
оАБ Neurological lesions causing optic nerve or chiasmal compression can produce visual оАЮeld defects that may be misinterpreted as glaucomatous and neuroimaging should be
performed if there is any suspicion, particularly in young
patients.
оАБ Previous anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AION),
particularly secondary to temporal arteritis may give rise
to a disc appearance and visual оАЮeld defect consistent with
glaucoma (see Fig. 19.14D). Non-arteritic AION oоАЪen occurs
in a вАШcrowdedвАЩ disc and the fellow eye should be examined
for this. Previous retinal vascular occlusion should also be
considered.
оАБ Previous acute optic nerve insult such as hypovolaemic or
septicaemic shock, or head injury.
оАБ Miscellaneous optic neuropathies including inоАЬammatory,
inоАЮltrative and drug-induced pathology will oоАЪen be clinically
obvious, but can occasionally masquerade as NTG.

Clinical features

History and examination are essentially the same as for POAG but
speciоАЮc points warrant attention.
оАБ History

{ Migraine and Raynaud phenomenon.

{ Episodes of shock.

{ Head or eye injury.

{ Headache and other neurological symptoms (intracranial
lesion).

{ Medication, e.g. systemic steroids, beta-blockers.
оАБ IOP is usually in the high teens, but may rarely be in the low
teens. In asymmetrical disease the more damaged disc typically corresponds to the eye with the higher IOP.
оАБ Optic nerve head

{ оАЭe optic nerve head tends to be larger on average in NTG
than in POAG (Fig. 11.30).

{ The pattern of cupping is similar, but acquired optic
disc pits and focal nerve fibre layer defects are more
common.

{ Peripapillary atrophic changes may be more prevalent.

not photographed or is examined without magniоАЮcation

{ Indications for neuroimaging in a patient with NTG:

оАБ Loss of VA out of proportion to cupping

оАБ Loss of colour vision on Ishihara testing

{ Pallor disproportionate to cupping should prompt a suspicion of an alternative diagnosis.
оАБ Visual оАЮeld defects are the same as in POAG although
there is some evidence that they tend to be closer to оАЮxation, deeper, steeper and more localized. In more than half of
patients, оАЮeld changes are non-progressive over a period of 5
years or more without treatment. However, perhaps because
of delayed diagnosis, patients tend to present with more
advanced damage than in POAG. A high level of suspicion
for a deоАЮcit pattern suggesting a lesion posterior to the optic
nerve is important.
оАБ Other investigations are as for POAG although in selected
patients the following can be considered:

оАБ Visual оАЮeld loss not consistent with retinal nerve оАЮbre
layer drop-out

оАБ Pallor of the neuroretinal rim of the disc

оАБ Rapid progression despite normal pressure

{ Carotid duplex imaging.

{ Ocular blood оАЬow assessment (e.g. laser оАЬowmetry) may
have useful clinical potential.

{ Assessment of systemic vascular risk factors.

{ Blood pressure measurement can be used to calculate ocular perfusion pressure. 24-hour ambulatory monitoring
will exclude nocturnal systemic hypotension in selected
patients.

{ Blood tests for other causes of non-glaucomatous optic
neuropathy such as vitamin B12, red cell folate, full blood
count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate/C-reactive protein, treponemal serology including Lyme disease, serum
angiotensin-converting enzyme level, plasma protein electrophoresis and autoantibody screen.

TIP In a patient with glaucoma undertake neuroimaging if
there is deterioration of colour vision or if the visual оАЫeld defect
is not consistent with retinal nerve оАЫbre layer loss.

Treatment

As a large proportion of untreated patients will not deteriorate
(approximately 50% at 5вАУ7 years), progression should be demonstrated before commencing treatment in most patients. Some
cases of NTG progress more rapidly than others and because further lowering of IOP is eоАЫective in reducing progression, treatment
should be considered in patients with advanced glaucomatous
damage, particularly if central vision is threatened and in those
with a long life expectancy. Regular assessment including perimetry should be performed at 6-monthly intervals initially.

374 Primary Angle-Closure Glaucoma

A B

Fig. 11.30 Bilateral advanced glaucomatous damage in normal-tension glaucoma. оАЭAоАЮ Right
eye; оАЭBоАЮ left eye.

оАБ NonоА† speciоАЮc advice. Patients should be encouraged to take
regular exercise. Yoga exercises that involve head-stands
should be avoided.
оАБ Medical treatment. Prostaglandins are usually prescribed as
initial treatment. Brimonidine may have a neuroprotective
eоАЫect in addition to an IOP-lowering eоАЫect. Topical betablockers should be used with caution, particularly at bedtime,
as they can be systemically absorbed and cause a signiоАЮcant
dip in nocturnal blood pressure. Betaxolol is the beta-blocker
of choice in these circumstances.
оАБ Selective laser trabeculoplasty is a reasonable initial option to
achieve IOP targets.
оАБ Surgery should be considered if progression occurs despite
IOP in the low teens. Antimetabolite enhancement of trabeculectomy is likely to be indicated in order to achieve a satisfactorily low pressure and to prevent IOP spikes.
оАБ Control of systemic vascular disease such as diabetes, hypertension and hyperlipidaemia may be important, in order to
optimize optic nerve perfusion.
оАБ Systemic calciumоА† channel blockers to address vasospasm
have been advocated by some authorities.
оАБ Antihypotensive measures. If signiоАЮcant nocturnal dips in BP
are detected, it may be necessary to reduce antihypertensive
medication, especially if taken at bedtime. Selected patients
might be encouraged to increase their salted food intake, in
consultation with the patientвАЩs cardiovascular physician.
оАБ EоАЭects of sleeping in a headоА† up position. оАЭe IOP is higher
in a оАЬat position than in a 30¬∞ head-up position. Although this
eоАЫect diоАЫers between individuals, patients with progressive
disease should be encouraged to sleep in a head-up position,
as the mean IOP is 20% lower in this position than in a оАЬat
position in at least a third of individuals.
оАБ Neuroprotective agents of proven beneоАЮt are not yet available.
Memantine is used to retard neuronal death in some central
nervous system (CNS) disorders, but has not been shown to
be beneоАЮcial in glaucoma. Ginkgo biloba (40 mg three times
daily) may confer some beneоАЮt in selected cases.

PRIMARY ANGLE-CLOSURE GLAUCOMA

Introduction
Overview

оАЭe term вАШangle closureвАЩ refers to occlusion of the trabecular
meshwork by the peripheral iris (iridotrabecular contact вАУ ITC),
obstructing aqueous outоАЬow. Angle closure can be primary, when
it occurs in an anatomically predisposed eye, or secondary to
another ocular or systemic factor. PACG may be responsible for up
to half of all cases of glaucoma globally and is particularly common
in Asia. It progresses rapidly and is more likely to result in visual
loss than POAG.

Gonioscopy

See Chapter 1

Grading of angle width

In practice, the angle is graded by many practitioners simply according to the number of structures visible (Fig. 11.31),
together with qualifying comments relating to the width of the iris
approach. оАЭe angle is usually narrowest superiorly. оАЭe main aims
are to evaluate the functional status of the angle, the degree of closure and the risk of future closure.
ShaоАЯer system. оАЭe ShaоАЫer system records the angle in degrees
between two imaginary lines tangential to the inner surface of the
trabeculum and the anterior surface of the iris about one-third of
the distance from its periphery. оАЭe system assigns a numerical
grade to each quadrant of the angle.
оАБ Grade 4 (35вАУ45¬∞) is the widest angle, characteristic of myopia
and pseudophakia. оАЭe ciliary body can be visualized without
tilting the lens.
оАБ Grade 3 (25вАУ35¬∞) is an open angle in which the scleral spur
is visible.
оАБ Grade 2 (20¬∞) is an angle in which the trabeculum but not the
scleral spur can be seen.

оАБ Grade 1 (10¬∞) is a very narrow angle in which only the
Schwalbe line and perhaps the top of the trabeculum can be

оАБ Slit angle is one in which there is no obvious iridocorneal contact but no angle structures can be identiоАЮed.
оАБ Grade 0 (0¬∞) is closed due to iridocorneal contact.
оАБ Indentation will distinguish appositional from synechial angle
closure.
Other systems
оАБ оАЭe Spaeth system is detailed but underused. It allows formal description of the position of iris insertion, the angular
approach and peripheral iris curvature.
оАБ оАЭe Scheie classiоАЮcation refers to the angle structures visible
and allocates a Roman numeral accordingly. In contrast to
common clinical use, in the original system a higher numeral
(e.g. IV) actually signiоАЮes a narrower angle.
оАБ оАЭe van Herick method (Table 11.4) uses the slit lamp alone to
estimate the AC angle width:

{ A thin but bright slit beam is set approximately perpendicularly to the corneal surface (oоАЫset from the optics by
about 60¬∞) to the patientвАЩs temporal side for each eye.

{ оАЭe beam is used to estimate the ratio of the corneal thickness to the most peripheral part of the AC.

{ It is useful as a screening tool, but overestimates angle
width in a proportion of patients, particularly those with
plateau iris conоАЮguration.

TIP Evaluation of the оАЫltration angle using gonioscopy is
key to the diagnosis and treatment of primary angle-closure
glaucoma.

ClassiоАЯcation

As knowledge about the epidemiology and mechanisms of angle
closure has increased, classiоАЮcation has moved away from a
symptom-based approach (acute, subacute and chronic) to reоАЬect
the stages in the natural history of the disease. оАЭe scheme below
has been suggested by a consensus group of the Association of
International Glaucoma Societies:
оАБ Primary angle closure suspect (PACS)

{ Axial anterior chamber depth is less than normal (Fig.
11.32A). Because of the convexity of the irisвАУlens

{ оАЭe risk of developing angle-closure disease in individuals
classiоАЮed as PAC suspects is low.
оАБ Primary angle closure (PAC)

оАЭe mechanisms involved in angle closure can be categorized according to the anatomical level (anterior to posterior) at which causative
forces act. In many patients more than one level is contributory.
оАБ Relative pupillary block

{ Failure of physiological aqueous оАЬow through the pupil
leads to a pressure diоАЫerential between the anterior and
posterior chambers, with resultant anterior bowing of the
iris (Fig. 11.34).

{ Usually anatomically relieved by peripheral iridotomy,
which equalizes anterior and posterior chamber pressure. Reduction of IOP will follow provided the angle has
opened adequately. оАЭis may not occur if there are substantial PAS or an additional mechanism of angle closure
is in eоАЫect. Trabecular meshwork damage can prevent
normalization of IOP even with an anatomically open
angle.

{ оАЭe lens vault quantiоАЮes the portion of the lens located
anterior to the anterior chamber angle. A common deоАЮnition is the distance between the anterior pole of the lens
and a horizontal line joining the scleral spur at diametrically opposite locations. A large lens vault is independently
associated with angle closure, though it is not clear whether
this is entirely via a pupillary block or non-pupillary block
mechanism, or both.

{ Gonioscopy shows three or more quadrants of ITC associated with raised IOP and/or PAS, best evaluated using
indentation gonioscopy (Fig. 11.33).

{ Normal optic disc and оАЮeld.

{ Some authorities further classify PAC into non-ischaemic
and ischaemic, the latter showing anterior segment evidence of prior substantial IOP elevation such as iris
changes or glaukomоАЬecken (see below).
оАБ Primary angleоА† closure glaucoma (PACG)

{ ITC in three or more quadrants, associated with glaucomatous optic neuropathy.

{ Optic nerve damage from an episode of severe IOP elevation, such as acute angle closure, may not appear as typical
glaucomatous cupping.

{ Usually rapid progression of lens intumescence (phacomorphic glaucoma) or anterior lens subluxation.

{ All cases of pupillary block have a phacomorphic element
that increases with age as the lens thickens.

{ SpeciоАЮc anatomical causative factors include plateau iris
secondary to anteriorly positioned/rotated ciliary processes (Fig. 11.35A) and a thicker or more anteriorly positioned iris. оАЭe concept of a вАШthick peripheral iris rollвАЩ has
been introduced by some authorities. A thick peripheral
iris may be relatively important in Asians (see Fig. 11.15C).

{ Plateau iris conоАЯguration is characterized by a оАЬat or only
slightly convex central iris plane, oоАЪen in association with
normal or only slightly shallow central anterior chamber
depth (Fig. 11.35B). оАЭe angle recess is typically very narrow, with a sharp backward iris angulation over anteriorly
positioned and/or oriented ciliary processes. A characteristic вАШdouble humpвАЩ sign is seen on indentation gonioscopy,
the central hump being due to the underlying central lens
supporting the iris and the peripheral hump resulting from
the underlying ciliary processes.

{ Plateau iris syndrome describes the persistence of gonioscopic angle closure despite a patent iridotomy in a patient
with morphological plateau iris. Factors such as a dark
environment or pharmacological pupillary dilatation may
be necessary to demonstrate the angle closure. It is divided
into a complete form in which occlusion of the functional
trabecular meshwork (TM) is present and the IOP is elevated and an incomplete form with occlusion to a lesser
extent and normal IOP.
оАБ LensоА† induced angle closure. Angle closure that is predominantly lens-induced or due to a retrolenticular cause is oоАЪen
categorized as secondary (see below).

{ оАЭis includes those cases in which a sudden change in lens
volume and/or position leads to an acute or subacute IOP
rise.

оАБ вАШCombined mechanismвАЩ has been proposed as a formal
label for the combination of angle-closure and open-angle
elements.
оАБ Reduced aqueous outоАЬow in angle closure has been postulated to be caused by the following mechanisms in varying

Risk factors

оАБ Age. оАЭe average age of relative pupillary block is about 62
years at presentation. Non-pupillary block forms of primary
angle closure tend to occur at a younger age.
оАБ Gender. Females are more commonly aоАЫected than males.
оАБ Race. Particularly prevalent in Far Eastern and Indian Asians,
where non-pupillary block is relatively more signiоАЮcant.
оАБ Family history. Genetic factors are important but poorly
deоАЮned, with an increased prevalence of angle closure in family members.
оАБ Refraction. Eyes with вАШpureвАЩ pupillary block are usually hypermetropic. Non-pupillary block mechanism can occasionally
occur in myopic eyes. Up to one in six patients with hypermetropia of one dioptre or more are primary angle closure suspects, so routine gonioscopy should be considered in all adults
who are hypermetropic.
оАБ Axial length. Short eyes tend to have a shallow AC secondary
to a relatively anterior lens position. Eyes with nanophthalmos
(axial length less than 20 mm) have a very short eye and are at
particular risk.

Diagnosis
Symptoms

оАБ Unilateral painful red eye. Most patients with acute congestive angle closure present with a unilateral painful red
eye. In about 5% the symptoms occur simultaneously in
both eyes.
оАБ Precipitating factors include watching television in a darkened room, pharmacological mydriasis or, rarely, miosis,
adoption of a semi-prone position (e.g. reading), acute emotional stress and occasionally systemic medication: parasympathetic antagonists or sympathetic agonists including
inhalers, motion sickness patches and cold/оАЬu remedies
(mydriatic eоАЫect), topiramate and other sulfa derivatives
(ciliary body eоАЫusion).
оАБ Presentation can be with intermittent symptoms of blurring
(вАШsmoke-оАЮlled roomвАЩ) and haloes (вАШrainbow around lightsвАЩ)
due to corneal epithelial oedema, or acutely with markedly
decreased vision, redness and ocular/periocular pain and
headache.
оАБ Systemic symptoms include abdominal pain, nausea and
vomiting.

Signs

оАБ Acute primary angle closure (APAC), previously called вАШacute
glaucomaвАЩ

{ VA is usually 6/60 to HM.

{ оАЭe IOP is usually very high (50вАУ80 mmHg).

{ Appositional obstruction by the iris.

{ Degeneration of the TM itself due to chronic or intermittent contact with the iris or damage sustained due to elevated IOP.

{ Permanent occlusion of the TM by PAS. оАЭe prognosis for
IOP control correlates well with the extent of PAS.

{ Conjunctival hyperaemia with violaceous circumcorneal
injection.

{ Corneal epithelial oedema.

{ оАЭe AC is shallow and aqueous оАЬare is usually present.

{ A non-reactive mid-dilated vertically oval pupil is classic
(Fig. 11.36).

Investigation

Fig. 11.36 Acute (congestive) primary angle closure showing
a mid-dilated vertically oval pupil.

{ оАЭe fellow eye typically shows an occludable angle. If this
is not present, secondary causes of angle closure should be
considered.
оАБ Resolved APAC

{ Early: low IOP (ciliary body shutdown and eоАЫect of intensive treatment), folds in Descemet membrane if IOP has
reduced rapidly (Fig. 11.37A), optic nerve head congestion, choroidal folds.

{ Late: iris atrophy with a spiral-like conоАЮguration, glaukomоАЬecken (Fig. 11.37B) (white foci of necrosis in the
superоАЮcial lens) and other forms of cataract and irregular
pupil due to iris sphincter/dilator damage and posterior
synechiae (Fig. 11.37C). оАЭe optic nerve may be normal
or exhibit varying signs of damage, including pallor and/or
cupping (Fig. 11.37D).

{ оАЭe greater the duration of an attack of APAC and the
extent of post-APAC PAS, the lower the likelihood of IOP
control with medical treatment alone.
оАБ Subacute angle closure is used to describe the clinical scenario
of intermittent episodes of spontaneously resolving mild/moderate APAC, usually in patients with pupillary block. оАЭe clinical course may be chronic, or may culminate in a more severe/
unresolving episode of APAC.
оАБ Chronic presentation

{ оАЭese patients are usually asymptomatic.

{ VA is normal unless damage is advanced.

{ оАЭe AC is usually shallower in relative pupillary block than
non-pupillary block.

{ IOP elevation may be only intermittent.

{ вАШCreepingвАЩ angle closure is characterized by a gradual
band-like anterior advance of the apparent insertion of the
iris. It starts in the deepest part of the angle superiorly and
spreads circumferentially.

{ Intermittent ITC may be associated with the formation of
discrete PAS, individual lesions having a pyramidal (вАШsawtoothвАЩ) appearance (see Fig. 11.33B).

оАБ Anterior segment OCT (AS-OCT вАУ see Fig. 11.15), ultrasound
biomicroscopy or ScheimpоАЬug photography may be useful to
supplement gonioscopic оАЮndings and for patient education.
оАБ Anterior chamber depth measurement is helpful in some
cases.
оАБ Biometry if lens extraction is considered.
оАБ Posterior segment ultrasonography in atypical cases to
exclude causes of secondary angle closure.
оАБ Provocative testing. оАЭis may aid decision-making in some
circumstances, particularly when plateau iris syndrome is
suspected.

{ Pharmacological mydriasis is a poor discriminator and
carries a small risk of precipitating APAC in susceptible
patients without a patent iridotomy.

{ Dark room/prone provocative test (DRPPT): the patient
sits in a dark room, face down for 1 hour without sleeping (sleep induces miosis). оАЭe IOP is checked before
and immediately aоАЪer the test, as IOP can normalize
very rapidly. An IOP rise of 8 mmHg or more is considered signiоАЮcant. Gonioscopy without indentation
should be used to conоАЮrm closure of the angle. If the
test is positive in a patient with a patent laser iridotomy,
the underlying anatomical cause is usually plateau iris,
which can be conоАЮrmed with ultrasound biomicroscopy
(see Fig. 11.14). A positive response is abolished aоАЪer
lens extraction.

DiоАЮerential diagnosis of acute IOP elevation

оАБ LensоА† induced angle closure due to a swollen or subluxated
lens.
оАБ Malignant glaucoma (aqueous misdirection), especially if the
patient has recently undergone intraocular surgery.
оАБ Other causes of secondary angle closure, with or without
pupillary block.
оАБ Neovascular glaucoma may occasionally cause the sudden
onset of pain and congestion.
оАБ Hypertensive uveitis, e.g. iridocyclitis with trabeculitis (particularly herpetic including cytomegalovirus), glaucomatocyclitic crisis (PosnerвАУSchlossman syndrome).
оАБ Scleritis with or without angle closure.
оАБ Pigment dispersion.
оАБ Pseudoexfoliation.
оАБ Orbital/retroоА† orbital lesions including orbital inоАЬammation,
retrobulbar haemorrhage and carotid-cavernous оАЮstula.

Treatment
APAC

оАБ Patients with acute and subacute angle closure should
receive urgent treatment.
оАБ Initial treatment

{ оАЭe patient should lie down in a supine position to encourage the lens to shiоАЪ posteriorly under the inоАЬuence of
gravity.

{ Optic nerve signs depend on the severity of damage.

380 Primary Angle-Closure Glaucoma

A B

C D

Fig. 11.37 Resolved acute primary angle closure. оАЭAоАЮ Residual corneal oedema and folds in

closure; оАЭDоАЮ optic atrophy вАУ combined pallor and cupping.

{ Acetazolamide 500 mg is given intravenously if IOP is >50
mmHg and orally (not slow-release) if IOP <50 mmHg.

{ Contraindications include sulfonamide allergy and angle
closure secondary to topiramate or other sulfonamide
derivatives.

{ A single dose of apraclonidine 0.5% or 1%, timolol 0.5%
and prednisolone 1% or dexamethasone 0.1% is instilled
into the aоАЫected eye, leaving 3вАУ5 minutes between each.

{ Pilocarpine 2% one drop to the aоАЫected eye, repeated aоАЪer
half an hour and one drop of 1% into the fellow eye. оАЭis
should not be repeated if the IOP remains >40 mmHg as
ischaemia may compromise its action, may exert a forward
vector and excessive dosing carries a systemic toxicity risk.

{ Mannitol 20% 1вАУ2 g/kg intravenously over 1-hour, oral
glycerol 50% 1 g/kg, or oral isosorbide 1вАУ1.5 g/kg, having
checked for contraindications.

{ Early laser iridotomy or iridoplasty aоАЪer clearing corneal
oedema with glycerol.

{ Paracentesis is eоАЫective, but carries a small risk of lens
damage.

{ Surgical options: peripheral iridectomy, lens extraction,
goniosynechialysis, trabeculectomy and cyclodiode laser
treatment.
оАБ Subsequent medical treatment

{ Analgesia and an antiemetic may be required.
оАБ Resistant cases

{ Central corneal indentation with a squint hook or indentation goniolens to force aqueous into the angle. Epithelial
oedema can be cleared оАЮrst with topical 50% glycerol to
improve visualization and to avoid abrasion.

{ Pilocarpine 2% four times daily to the aоАЫected eye and 1%
four times daily to the fellow eye.

{ Topical steroid (prednisolone 1% or dexamethasone 0.1%)
four times daily if the eye is acutely inоАЬamed.

{ Any or all of the following should be continued according to response: timolol 0.5% twice daily, apraclonidine 1%
three times daily and oral acetazolamide 250 mg four times
daily.

оАБ Bilateral laser iridotomy is performed once an attack has
been broken, signiоАЮed by a clear cornea and preferably normal
IOP. Topical steroids are continued for at least a week.
оАБ Gonioscopy needs to be repeated to ensure that the angle is
open.
оАБ Subsequent management is as for post-iridotomy chronic
PAC/PACG. A low threshold may be adopted for cataract
surgery, particularly if a signiоАЮcant phacomorphic element is
suspected. Trabeculectomy is occasionally necessary for persistent IOP elevation despite a successfully opened angle.

PACS

оАБ Laser iridotomy (Fig. 11.38). оАЭe ZAP (Zhongshan Angle
Closure Prevention) trial concludes that laser peripheral iridotomy (PI) has a small prophylactic eоАЫect over a 6-year
period and should therefore only be oоАЫered to those with the
highest risk of developing acute congestive angle closure or
chronic angle-closure glaucoma.

{ Symptoms suggestive of previous intermittent angle

{ Systemic medication with an atropine-like structure that

{ Where there is a need for frequent dilated eye examination

CLASSIFICATION OF SECONDARY
GLAUCOMA

OpenоАЯangle

Secondary open-angle glaucoma can be subdivided on the basis of
the site of aqueous outоАЬow obstruction.
оАБ PreоА†trabecular, in which aqueous outоАЬow is obstructed by a

{ Fibrovascular tissue (neovascular glaucoma).

{ Endothelial cellular membranous proliferation (iridocorneal endothelial syndrome).

{ Epithelial cellular membranous proliferation (epithelial
ingrowth).

вАШclogging upвАЩ of the meshwork (Fig. 11.40B) and secondary

{ Pigment particles (pigmentary glaucoma).

{ Red blood cells (red cell glaucoma).

{ Degenerate red cells (ghost cell glaucoma).

{ Macrophages and lens proteins (phacolytic glaucoma).

{ Proteins (probably an element in hypertensive uveitis).

{ Pseudoexfoliative material (pseudoexfoliation glaucoma).

{ Trabecular glaucoma may also be caused by alteration of
the trabecular оАЮbres themselves by oedema (e.g. trabeculitis in hypertensive uveitis) or scarring (e.g. post-traumatic
angle recession).
оАБ PostоА† trabecular in which the trabeculum itself is normal but
aqueous outоАЬow is impaired as a result of elevated episcleral
venous pressure.

{ Carotid-cavernous оАЮstula.

{ SturgeвАУWeber syndrome.

{ Obstruction of the superior vena cava.

{ Subluxated lens.

{ Phacomorphic glaucoma.

{ Capsular block syndrome with 360¬∞ irisвАУcapsule adhesion
in a pseudophakic eye.

{ Aphakic pupillary block.

{ Cilio-choroidal eоАЫusion.

{ Contraction of retrolenticular оАЮbrovascular tissue such

PSEUDOEXFOLIATION SYNDROME
AND GLAUCOMA

Introduction

Pseudoexfoliation syndrome (PXS) is an important ocular
manifestation of a systemic disorder and is the commonest
cause of secondary open-angle glaucoma. It is easily overlooked
in the early stages, as the signs are not always obvious. It is rare
before the age of 50 years, but the prevalence increases rapidly after the sixth decade of life, with a prevalence of 5% at
age 75вАУ85 years. It is more common in women than men and
although the condition is found worldwide, the prevalence is
highest in Scandinavia. Not all patients with PXS will develop
glaucoma and there is no reliable method of determining which
patients with the condition will subsequently develop optic disc
damage. The incidence of glaucoma (PXG) at diagnosis of PXS
is 15вАУ30% and the cumulative risk of eyes with PXS requiring
glaucoma treatment may be as high as 60% at 5 years. It should
be distinguished from true capsular exfoliation, which occurs
secondary to chronic infrared exposure (вАШglassblowerвАЩs cataractвАЩ

- see Fig. 10.6E).

TIP Pseudoexfoliation glaucoma progresses more rapidly
than primary open-angle glaucoma and is more likely to result
in signiоАЫcant visual loss.

Pathogenesis

Pseudoexfoliative material is a grey-white fibrillary substance
deriving from abnormal extracellular matrix metabolism in
ocular and other tissues. The material is deposited on various
ocular structures, including the lens capsule (Fig. 11.41), zonular fibres, iris, trabeculum and conjunctiva. Pseudoexfoliative
material has been found in skin and visceral organs, leading
to the concept that PXS is the ocular manifestation of a systemic disorder. PXS is associated with an increased prevalence
of high-tone hearing loss and cardiovascular disorders. The
pathogenesis is multifactorial, but in some populations almost
all patients with PXS have single nucleotide polymorphisms
(SNPs) in the LOXL1 gene on chromosome 15, which codes
for an enzyme that is involved in cross-linking of tropoelastin
and collagen and is therefore important for the formation and
maintenance of elastic fibres and extracellular matrix. However,
these SNPs are common in the general population and most
individuals with the SNPs do not develop PXS. Plasma and
aqueous humour homocysteine levels tend to be higher than
controls and inadequate dietary folate intake (folate reduces
homocysteine) may be a risk factor. Open-angle glaucoma
(sometimes termed вАШcapsular glaucomaвАЩ) is usually due to elevated IOP secondary to trabecular obstruction by pseudoexfoliative material and liberated iris pigment leading to secondary
degenerative outflow dysfunction. Curiously, despite the systemic nature of the condition and the fact that biopsy reveals
subclinical pseudoexfoliative material in the apparently unaffected eye, glaucoma remains confined to one eye in about twothirds of patients.

{ Malignant glaucoma (ciliolenticular block).

Clinical features

Diagnosis is usually incidental but can follow vision loss from
advanced glaucoma.
оАБ Cornea. Pseudoexfoliative material may be deposited on the
endothelium and scattered pigment deposits are common. A
vertical (Krukenberg) spindle may rarely form, similar to that
seen in pigment dispersion syndrome. Endothelial cell abnormality, such as low density, is more common than average.
оАБ Anterior chamber. Pseudoexfoliative material particles are
sometimes seen. Mild aqueous оАЬare from an impaired bloodвАУ
aqueous barrier is common.
оАБ Iris. Granular pseudoexfoliative material deposits, pupillary
ruоАЫ loss and patchy transillumination defects at the pupillary
margin (Fig. 11.42A).
оАБ Lens. оАЭe anterior lens capsule typically shows a central disc
and a radially indented peripheral layer of pseudoexfoliative material, separated by a clear zone maintained by pupillary abrasion (Fig. 11.42B). Peripheral capsular deposition is
oоАЪen visible only with pupillary dilatation (Fig. 11.42B and C).

Deposits may be оАЬaky, with scrolled edges. Cataract is more
common in an eye with pseudoexfoliative material, probably
because of reduced ascorbate levels in the aqueous. Phacodonesis (lens instability) due to zonular weakness may be present,
but spontaneous subluxation is rare.

{ Patchy trabecular and Schwalbe line hyperpigmentation is

{ A Sampaolesi line, which is an irregular band of pigment

Occasionally the IOP may rise acutely despite a wide angle,
mimicking acute angle closure.
оАБ Prognosis. оАЭis is worse than POAG as the IOP is usually
higher and may exhibit marked оАЬuctuation. Severe damage
may be present at diagnosis and can develop rapidly. In the
long term, these patients are at signiоАЮcant risk of visual loss
and blindness. It is therefore important to monitor patients
closely and it may be prudent for review in patients with PXS
to take place at intervals of no more than 6 months.
оАБ Systemic manifestations. оАЭese include: high-tone hearing
loss, ischaemic cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease and an
increased risk of pelvic organ prolapse in older women.

Treatment

оАБ Medical treatment is similar to that of POAG, but failure is
more common.
оАБ Laser trabeculoplasty is more eоАЫective than in POAG,
with mean IOP reduction around 30% following SLT. Care
should be taken not to apply excessive energy, as trabecular

{ оАЭere is an increased risk of angle closure probably because
of zonular laxity.
оАБ IOP. In most eyes the presence of glaucomatous damage is
associated with elevated IOP. оАЭe majority of patients have a
chronic open-angle glaucoma that is usually unilateral at оАЮrst.

pigmentation may confer higher absorption resulting in transient IOP spikes. A signiоАЮcant rise in IOP may occur approximately 2 years aоАЪer SLT.
оАБ PhacoemulsiоАЮcation alone may signiоАЮcantly lower IOP,
though it may give better control combined with trabeculectomy. оАЭere is a high risk of complications, due to poor
mydriasis, increased fragility of the zonules and lens capsule
and endothelial deоАЮciency. оАЭere is also an increased risk of
a postoperative IOP spike, postoperative corneal oedema,
inоАЬammation, capsular opaciоАЮcation, capsulorhexis contraction (capsular phimosis) and late IOL decentration or
dislocation.
оАБ Filtration surgery in PXG has a similar success rate to POAG.
оАБ Trabecular aspiration alone seems to confer at least a shortterm beneоАЮt and can be performed at the same time as other
intraocular procedures.

PIGMENT DISPERSION SYNDROME
AND PIGMENTARY GLAUCOMA

Introduction

Pigment dispersion syndrome (PDS) is characterized by the liberation of pigment granules from iris pigment epithelium and
their deposition throughout the anterior segment. Secondary
pigmentary glaucoma (PG) is common. PDS and PG are more
common in males, particularly young myopic White men. In
African people, where it is rare, the condition tends to aоАЫect
older hypermetropic women. About 15% of patients with PDS
will develop elevated IOP or glaucoma aоАЪer 15 years. AD inheritance with incomplete penetrance seems to be present in at least
some families and a range of genetic loci have been linked. Myopia is a risk factor for clinical manifestation, higher degrees of
myopia being associated with earlier and more severe glaucoma.
Secondary pigment dispersion can occur as a consequence of
trauma, intraocular tumour and rubbing of a malpositioned IOL
on the iris pigment epithelium.

Pathogenesis

In primary PDS/PG, pigment shedding is precipitated by
rubbing of the posterior pigment layer of the iris against the
zonules as a result of excessive posterior bowing of the midperipheral portion of the iris (Fig. 11.43A). It is believed that
an increase in anterior chamber pressure relative to the posterior chamber occurs due to reverse pupillary block, supported
by the observation that peripheral iridotomy flattens the iris
and decreases iridozonular contact (Fig. 11.43B). The pigment epithelium itself may be abnormally susceptible to shedding in affected individuals. Pigment shedding decreases from
middle age onwards due to physiological changes resulting in
decreased iridozonular contact. Acute IOP elevation can occur
due to direct trabecular obstruction by released melanin granules. Chronic elevation appears to be caused by pigmentary
obstruction of the intertrabecular spaces and damage to the
trabeculum secondary to denudation, collapse and sclerosis.

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 385
#### 11

Patients with pigmentary glaucoma have an increased incidence
of steroid responsiveness.

Diagnosis

оАБ Presentation. PDS and PG are typically detected at a routine
eye examination. оАЭe myopic individuals who tend to develop
the condition are likely to be under regular optometric review.
Occasionally symptoms from glaucomatous visual loss lead to
attendance, or from corneal oedema due to an acute IOP rise
following the release of pigment granules (particularly aоАЪer
physical exercise). Signs of PDS are usually bilateral but may
be subtle and go undetected.
оАБ Cornea. Pigment is deposited on the endothelium in a vertical
spindle shape (Krukenberg spindle; Fig. 11.44A) оАЭe spindle is
not always present, tends to become less obvious in longstanding cases and is not pathognomonic.
оАБ Anterior chamber (AC). оАЭe AC is deep and melanin granules may be seen in the aqueous.
оАБ Iris. Characteristic radial spoke-like transillumination defects
(Fig. 11.44B) are seen in lighter, but oоАЪen not in dark, irides.
To best demonstrate these, the room should be minimally illuminated and a short, narrow but intense slit beam directed
through the pupil. Visualization may be aided by asking the
patient to look up. Melanin granules may be present on the
surface of the iris, usually inferiorly, which become less obvious with age. оАЭere may be partial loss of the pupillary ruоАЫ
(Fig. 11.44C).
оАБ Gonioscopy. The angle is wide open and there is often a
characteristic mid-peripheral iris concavity where the iris
bows backwards. The trabecular meshwork is heavily pigmented in all four quadrants, with a dense homogeneous
circumferential posterior meshwork band (Fig. 11.44D).
Pigment may also be seen on or anterior to the Schwalbe
line. Pigmentation of the angle typically reduces with
increasing age.
оАБ Lens. Pigment granules may be deposited on the anterior surface. оАЭere may be a line (Scheie stripe) or a (Zentmayer) ring
of pigment on the peripheral/equatorial surface around the
zonular insertions.
оАБ IOP. оАЭis may be volatile, some patients exhibiting higher levels and wider оАЬuctuations of IOP than in POAG. Over time
the control of IOP may become easier as pigment liberation
decreases and occasionally the IOP may revert to normal.
NTG can be erroneously diagnosed if the IOP has normalized
spontaneously and the PDS signs resolved.
оАБ Posterior segment. Peripheral retinal pigmentation may be
seen and lattice degeneration is more common than in myopic
patients without PDS or PG. оАЭe incidence of retinal detachment may also be higher. Glaucomatous optic neuropathy is
dependent on disease stage and extent and may be markedly
asymmetrical. It is common to оАЮnd advanced disease in one
eye and relatively mild damage in the other.

Treatment

Individuals with PDS should be reviewed annually to exclude
the development of raised IOP and/or glaucomatous damage. If

can result in rapid deterioration of visual function.
оАБ Lifestyle measures. Excessive exercising can be associated
with increased iris pigment dispersion with acute symptoms
in some cases. If the history conоАЮrms this, appropriate advice
should be given to alter exercise behaviour.
оАБ Medical treatment is similar to that of POAG. Miotics are
theoretically of beneоАЮt because they decrease iridozonular
contact in addition to facilitating aqueous outоАЬow but are
poorly tolerated by younger patients. оАЭis medication has the
disadvantage of exacerbating myopia and also carries a risk of
precipitating retinal detachment in short-sighted eyes. Topical
thymoxamine, a selective alpha-adrenergic antagonist, induces
miosis without causing spasm of accommodation, but is also
poorly tolerated as it causes irritation.
оАБ Laser trabeculoplasty is oоАЪen eоАЫective. It is important not to
over-treat eyes with heavily pigmented angles. To reduce the
risk of an IOP spike, use the lowest laser setting and treat only
two quadrants.
оАБ Laser iridotomy has been proposed to retard pigment liberation by reversing iris concavity and eliminating iridozonular
contact. It can reduce the risk of intermittent IOP spikes, especially in patients under the age of 40 years, but does not reduce
the risk of developing glaucoma.
оАБ Filtration surgery is indicated more commonly than in
POAG. оАЭe use of adjunctive antimetabolites improves surgical outcome, particularly in younger patients in whom
there is a risk of failure. Postsurgical hypotony and suprachoroidal haemorrhage are more common in young patients
with myopia.

Acute bilateral iris pigment loss with
raised IOP

Bilateral acute depigmentation of the iris (BADI) and bilateral
acute iris transillumination (BAIT) have recently been proposed as
distinct idiopathic clinical syndromes involving pigment dispersal
from the iris into the anterior chamber. оАЭey are said to be more
common in young to middle-aged women, occurring spontaneously or following a оАЬu-like illness and possibly aоАЪer oral antibiotic
treatment, especially moxiоАЬoxacin. It is possible that the primary
process is inоАЬammation, but there is evidence that the mechanism
is phototoxicity following sensitization in predisposed individuals. Presentation is usually with acute bilateral ocular redness and
photophobia. Both may be associated with an IOP rise, but this
has been reported to be more severe and resistant in BAIT. Findings described in the two diоАЫer in that pigment is lost from the
iris stroma in BADI and the iris pigment epithelium in BAIT, with
marked iris transillumination defects and irregular mydriasis
in the latter. It is possible that BADI and BAIT represent diоАЫerent points on a common disease spectrum. DiоАЫerential diagnosis
is principally from viral anterior uveitis and pigment dispersion
syndrome.

Glaucoma 387
#### 11

NEOVASCULAR GLAUCOMA

Pathogenesis

Neovascular glaucoma (NVG) occurs as a result of aggressive iris
neovascularization (rubeosis iridis), which leads to progressive
angle closure and rapid glaucomatous atrophy (Fig. 11.45A and B).
оАЭe common aetiological factor is severe, diоАЫuse and chronic retinal ischaemia. It is postulated that hypoxic retinal tissue produces
angiogenic factors in an attempt to revascularize hypoxic areas.
оАЭe most important of these is VEGF.

Causes

оАБ Ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion accounts for 35вАУ50%
of cases. A VA of less than 6/60, a relative aоАЫerent pupillary
defect and extensive peripheral retinal capillary non-perfusion
on оАЬuorescein angiography are useful predictors of the risk of
subsequent NVG. Glaucoma typically occurs 3 months aоАЪer
the occlusive event (вАШ100-day glaucomaвАЩ), but intervals from 4
weeks to 2 years have been documented.
оАБ Diabetes mellitus accounts for a smaller proportion. Whereas
diabetes was the cause in 30% of cases in the past, this has
dropped to 10вАУ15% of cases in communities where yearly
screening of diabetic retinopathy occurs. оАЭe risk of glaucoma
is decreased by panretinal photocoagulation and by antiVEGF treatment. Pars plana vitrectomy in diabetics may precipitate NVG (7% overall in a large study), especially if angle
neovascularization is present preoperatively.
оАБ Arterial retinal vascular disease such as central retinal artery
occlusion and ocular ischaemic syndrome are less common
causes.
оАБ Miscellaneous causes include intraocular tumours, longstanding retinal detachment (RD) and chronic intraocular
inоАЬammation.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms vary from none to severe pain, decreased vision,
redness and photophobia.
оАБ Cornea. Elevated IOP, particularly when substantial and acute,
leads to corneal oedema.
оАБ IOP may be normal early in the disease process, but is frequently extremely high later on. оАЭe anterior segment will
oоАЪen be congested once progression to elevated IOP has
occurred. In advanced disease hypotony may supervene.
оАБ Anterior chamber. Flare, cells and posterior synechiae may
be present, depending on severity and stage. Presentation is
sometimes with anterior chamber haemorrhage.
оАБ Pupillary margin. Subtle vessels at the pupillary margin are
oоАЪen an early sign (Fig. 11.45C), but may be missed unless the
iris is examined carefully under high magniоАЮcation. Diagnosis
at this stage is likely to substantially improve the prognosis.
оАБ Iris surface. New vessels grow radially over the surface of the
iris towards the angle (Fig. 11.45D), sometimes joining dilated
blood vessels at the collarette. At this stage the IOP may still be
normal, but elevation can occur fairly acutely.

388 Neovascular Glaucoma

A B

C D

E F

Fig. 11.45 Neovascular glaucoma. оАЭAоАЮ Rubeosis and angle closure by PAS; оАЭBоАЮ progressive synechial angle closure; оАЭCоАЮ mild rubeosis iridis at pupil margin; оАЭDоАЮ severe rubeosis iridis; оАЭEоАЮ tube
