оАБ Gonioscopy. Angle neovascularization may commonly occur
without other signs, particularly aоАЪer CRVO and it is important to perform careful non-mydriatic gonioscopy in eyes at
risk. Early signs may be very subtle, even in the presence of
moderate IOP elevation. Neovascular tissue proliferates across
the face of the angle, forming an obstructing оАЮbrovascular
membrane that subsequently contracts to close the angle.
оАЭe angle closes circumferentially leading to very high IOP,
severe visual impairment, congestion of the globe and pain.
оАЭe visual prognosis is generally poor by this stage, but aggressive management can achieve comfort and retain useful sight
in some cases.
оАБ Cataract is common once ischaemia is established.
оАБ Posterior segment. Signs correspond to aetiology. Glaucomatous optic neuropathy may be present.
оАБ Investigations. Fluorescein angiography may be helpful in
conоАЮrming aetiology and delineating ischaemia. B-scan ultrasonography will help to exclude potential causes such as RD or
a tumour when the posterior segment view is impaired. Anterior segment OCT has been proposed as a useful tool for angle
assessment.

Treatment

It is critical to address the cause of the neovascularization as well as
the elevated IOP. Appropriate management of systemic disease is
also of key importance in order to reduce the risk to the fellow eye,
to reduce the risk of stroke and to prolong life expectancy. Factors
that predict a poor visual outcome include: young age, VA less than
6/60 and IOP >35 mmHg on presentation.
оАБ Review. Frequent review during high-risk periods is critical:
the оАЮrst few months following an ischaemic CRVO and the
оАЮrst few weeks following diabetic vitrectomy.
оАБ Medical treatment of elevated IOP is as for POAG but
miotics should be avoided. Topical atropine 1% twice daily
will resist synechiae formation, and increase the outоАЬow of
aqueous through the uveoscleral route. Topical steroids are
beneоАЮcial in the acute stage. Topical apraclonidine and oral
acetazolamide may be useful temporizing measures. Acetazolamide can be associated with renal dysfunction in diabetes, especially type 1 and should be used with caution in
these patients.
оАБ Panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) is usually eоАЫective in
inducing regression of neovascularization and, if performed
early, preventing progression to glaucoma. It will not reverse
an established оАЮbrovascular membrane. оАЭe timing of PRP in
CRVO is discussed in Chapter 13. If the retinal view is poor,
indirect ophthalmoscopic application may provide better
access; if necessary, performed in the operating room with iris
hooks to open a small pupil caused by posterior synechiae.
Trans-scleral cryotherapy may be used in eyes with opaque
media or as an adjunct for increasing peripheral retinal
coverage.
оАБ Intraocular VEGF inhibitors, e.g. bevacizumab (Avastin)
at a dose of 1.25 mg in 0.05 ml, is an eоАЫective adjunctive
measure whilst waiting for PRP to take eоАЫect, particularly if

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 389
#### 11

оАЮbrovascular angle closure has not yet supervened and usually leads to rapid pain relief. Intracameral (AC) injection is
an alternative to the intravitreal route. оАЭe duration of control
from a single injection is limited and most patients require
ongoing injections. In patients with ischaemic CRVO treated
in this manner, the onset of NVG can be delayed by up to 18
months. оАЭere is a small risk of central retinal artery occlusion
in patients with ocular ischaemic syndrome.
оАБ Retinal detachment repair should be performed once the IOP
is controlled, particularly if there is a tractional detachment
involving the macula.
оАБ Ciliary body ablation. Cyclodiode should be considered if
medical IOP control is not possible. оАЭese have conventionally been used only in eyes with poor visual potential, but can
be employed if there is reasonable vision in order to prevent
severe glaucomatous damage occurring whilst neovascularization is brought under control. Lowering a substantially raised
IOP generally improves comfort and clearing corneal oedema
may facilitate an adequate retinal view for PRP. Care should
be taken not to use excessive treatment, which can lead to
hypotony. Endoscopic cyclophotocoagulation is an alternative
to trans-scleral treatment.
оАБ Filtration surgery may be considered if VA is HM or better. Options include an artiоАЮcial оАЮltering shunt (glaucoma
drainage device) (Fig. 11.45E) and trabeculectomy with
mini-shunt implantation, adjunctive mitomycin C and
postoperative subconjunctival 5-оАЬuorouracil. Active neovascularization and inоАЬammation should be controlled preoperatively (including preoperative anti-VEGF treatment)
to improve the chances of surgical success. Even with treatment there is a relatively high risk of hypotony. Postoperative anti-inоАЬammatory treatment should be aggressive and
may include systemic steroids.
оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy with peroperative endolaser at an early
stage may improve the prognosis in eyes with vitreous haemorrhage, especially in CRVO. Preoperative intravitreal antiVEGF may be of beneоАЮt.
оАБ Retrobulbar alcohol injection is useful in relieving pain
but may cause permanent ptosis and does not relieve
congestion.
оАБ Enucleation or evisceration may be considered if all else fails.

TIP The underlying cause of neovascular glaucoma needs to
be determined and treated in order to reduce the risk of visual
loss in the fellow eye and to increase life expectancy.

Prognosis

Patient expectations need to be realistic and the best outcome is
oоАЪen a blind but comfortable eye. оАЭe prevalence of blindness in
the aоАЫected eye varies from 25% to 50% depending on the length
of follow-up (Fig. 11.45F). Life expectancy is reduced signiоАЮcantly
in these patients (approximately 50% of expected lifespan). A good
presenting VA in the aоАЫected eye is a useful predictor of longer life
expectancy.

390 InоАЯammatory Glaucoma

INFLAMMATORY GLAUCOMA

Introduction
Overview

Elevation of IOP secondary to intraocular inоАЬammation frequently
presents a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge. оАЭe elevation of
IOP may be transient and innocuous, or persistent and severely
damaging. оАЭe prevalence of secondary glaucoma increases with
chronicity and severity of disease. Secondary glaucoma is particularly common in Fuchs uveitis syndrome and chronic anterior uveitis associated with juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Posterior uveitis is
less likely to aоАЫect the aqueous outоАЬow pathway and consequently
less likely to lead to IOP elevation.

Diagnostic dilemmas

Assessment of glaucomatous damage may be hampered by a small
pupil or opacities in the media. Poor VA may also compromise
accurate perimetry.
оАБ IOP оАЬuctuation may be dramatic in uveitic glaucoma and
phasing may be helpful in patients with borderline IOP.
оАБ Ciliary body shutdown caused by acute exacerbation of
chronic anterior uveitis is frequently associated with lowering
of IOP that may mask the underlying tendency to glaucoma.
Even eyes with considerably elevated IOP (30вАУ35 mmHg) may
become hypotonous during acute exacerbations of uveitis.
Return of ciliary body function with subsidence of uveitis may
be associated with a rise in IOP in the presence of permanently
compromised outоАЬow facility.
оАБ Topical steroids, when used intermittently, can cause a оАЬuctuating IOP.
оАБ Iris vessels may give rise to diagnostic confusion with neovascular glaucoma.

AngleоАЯclosure glaucoma with pupillary
block
Pathogenesis

Secondary angle closure is caused by posterior synechiae extending for 360¬∞ (seclusio pupillae), which obstruct aqueous оАЬow from
the posterior to the anterior chamber. оАЭe resultant increased
pressure in the posterior chamber produces anterior bowing of the
peripheral iris (iris bomb√© вАУ Fig. 11.46A) resulting in shallowing of
the anterior chamber and apposition of the iris to the trabeculum
and peripheral cornea (Fig. 11.46B). Such an inоАЬamed iris easily
sticks to the trabeculum and the iridocorneal contact may become
permanent, with the development of PAS.

Diagnosis

оАБ Slit lamp biomicroscopy shows seclusio pupillae, iris bomb√©
and a shallow anterior chamber.
оАБ Gonioscopy shows angle closure from iridotrabecular contact.
Indentation may be used to assess the extent of appositional as
opposed to synechial angle closure.

AngleоАЯclosure glaucoma without
pupillary block

оАБ Pathogenesis. Chronic anterior uveitis causes the deposition
of inоАЬammatory cells and debris in the angle (Fig. 11.47A and
B). Subsequent organization and contraction pull the peripheral iris over the trabeculum, causing gradual and progressive
synechial angle closure (Fig. 11.47C) and eventual elevation of
IOP. оАЭe eye with a pre-existing narrow angle may be at higher
risk.
оАБ Diagnosis. оАЭe anterior chamber is deep but gonioscopy
shows extensive angle closure by PAS.

A B

Fig. 11.46 Secondary angle closure with pupillary block. оАЭAоАЮ Iris bomb√©; оАЭBоАЮ iridocorneal
contact.

OpenоАЯangle glaucoma
In acute anterior uveitis

In acute anterior uveitis the IOP is usually normal or subnormal
due to concomitant ciliary shutdown. Occasionally, however,
secondary open-angle glaucoma develops due to obstruction
of aqueous outflow, most commonly as acute inflammation is
subsiding and ciliary body function returning (Fig. 11.47D).
This effect, which is often transient and innocuous, may be
steroid-induced or caused by a combination of the following
mechanisms:
оАБ Trabecular obstruction by inоАЬammatory cells and debris,
which may be associated with increased aqueous viscosity due
to leakage of protein from inоАЬamed iris blood vessels.
оАБ Acute trabeculitis involving inоАЬammation and oedema of the
trabecular meshwork with secondary diminution of intertrabecular porosity may result in a reduction in outоАЬow facility.
It is thought that this is especially relevant in anterior uveitis

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 391
#### 11

associated with herpes zoster, herpes simplex, other viral anterior uveitides and toxoplasma retinitis.

In chronic anterior uveitis

In chronic anterior uveitis the main mechanism for reduced outоАЬow facility is thought to be trabecular scarring and/or sclerosis
secondary to chronic trabeculitis. оАЭe importance of this mechanism is, however, diоАЩcult to determine as most eyes also have
some degree of synechial angle closure. Because of the variable
appearance of the angle on gonioscopy, deоАЮnitive diagnosis of trabecular damage is diоАЩcult. In some eyes, a gelatinous exudate is
seen on the trabeculum.

Treatment
Medical

оАБ Medical control of IOP is more likely to be achieved if the
angle is completely open.

A B

C D

Fig. 11.47 Secondary angle closure without pupillary block. оАЭAоАЮ Deposition of inоАЬammatory
cells in the angle; оАЭBоАЮ gonioscopy showing inоАЬammatory debris; оАЭCоАЮ synechial angle closure

and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 вАУ оАЯg. A.)

392 InоАЯammatory Glaucoma

оАБ If there are a number of possible mechanisms causing the rise
in IOP and the patient is using a topical steroid, stop the topical steroid for 2 days and remeasure the IOP.
оАБ Long-acting depot steroid preparations should not be used in
known or suspected steroid-responders.
оАБ оАЭe eоАЫect of ocular hypotensive drugs is less predictable in
uveitis, e.g. some cases may be unexpectedly sensitive to topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (CAI).
оАБ A beta-blocker is usually the drug of оАЮrst choice.
оАБ Prostaglandin derivatives should be avoided if possible as they
may promote inоАЬammation.
оАБ оАЭe choice of additional agents oоАЪen depends on the IOP
level. If this is very high, oral acetazolamide may be required.
For moderate elevation (e.g. less than 35 mmHg on a betablocker) in the absence of signiоАЮcant glaucomatous damage, an alpha-adrenergic agonist or topical CAI might be
adequate.
оАБ Miotics are contraindicated as they increase vascular permeability and as miosis promotes the formation of posterior
synechiae.

Laser iridotomy

оАБ Laser iridotomy is performed to re-establish communication
between the posterior and anterior chambers in eyes with
pupillary-block angle-closure glaucoma.
оАБ An iridotomy is likely to become occluded in the presence of
active uveitis and intensive topical steroid should be used following the laser.
оАБ Correction of pupillary block alone may not control the IOP if
there is insuоАЩcient exposed functional angle, though a patent
iridotomy may retard progressive PAS formation.
оАБ Surgical iridectomy is the deоАЮnitive method of preventing further pupil block and may be required if laser fails to maintain
a viable iridotomy.

Surgery

оАБ Preoperative preparation

{ Control of chronic uveitis for a minimum of 3 months
before surgery is ideal.

{ Preoperative topical steroids should be used, not only as
prophylaxis against recurrent inоАЬammation but also to
reduce the conjunctival inоАЬammatory cell population.

{ In patients with particularly labile inоАЬammatory disease
oral prednisolone should be considered (0.5 mg/kg daily).
Immunosuppressive treatment has improved the prognosis in recent years.
оАБ Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C or the implantation of a
long-tube implant can be used in these circumstances.

{ Combined cataract and glaucoma surgery is relatively
contraindicated, but may sometimes be performed in
conjunction with goniosynechialysis; occasionally cataract surgery alone can be appropriate. In most cases cataract surgery should be deferred for at least 6 months aоАЪer
trabeculectomy.

{ Postoperative hypotony is a particular risk as a delicate
balance may exist between reduced aqueous production

and restricted outоАЬow. оАЭis is particularly likely to occur
if there has been a pronounced reduction in the IOP when
acetazolamide is introduced.

{ Following surgery, steroids are tapered more slowly than in
non-inоАЬammatory glaucoma.
оАБ Cyclodestructive procedures should be used with caution as
they may exacerbate inоАЬammation. Possible underlying ciliary
body insuоАЩciency also carries the risk of profound hypotony
that may progress to phthisis bulbi.

TIP Inappropriate use of topical steroids is a signiоАЫcant risk
factor for the development of raised IOP and glaucoma.

PosnerвАУSchlossman syndrome оАЭPSSоАЮ
Introduction

PSS (glaucomatocyclitic crisis) is a rare condition characterized
by recurrent attacks of unilateral acute raised IOP associated
with mild anterior uveitis. The mechanism is speculated to be
acute trabeculitis and there is evidence that infection, possibly
cytomegalovirus (CMV) or Helicobacter pylori, may play a role.
Prostaglandins are involved in the pathogenesis, particularly
PGE2, where the aqueous levels positively correlate with IOP.
The presence of HLA-Bw54 haplotype has been implicated in
some cases. PSS typically affects young to middle-aged adults.
Males are affected more frequently than females. Episodes are
unilateral, although 50% of patients have involvement of the
other eye at different times. The intervals between attacks vary,
but usually become longer with time. Patients should be followed even after the attacks have completely subsided, because
a significant proportion will develop chronic IOP elevation,
with the fellow eye also at risk.

Diagnosis

An acute IOP rise in PDS and demonstrable CMV or other viral
anterior uveitis can present in an almost identical manner. оАЭe
signs of the former may be atypical in older patients and those with
dark irides. Simple IOP volatility in POAG, especially the juvenile
variant, must also be distinguished.
оАБ Presentation is with mild discomfort, haloes around lights
and slight blurring of vision in one eye and sometimes
redness.
оАБ Slit lamp biomicroscopy typically shows a few anterior chamber cells and one to several оАЮne white central keratic precipitates. Injection is likely to be absent or minimal. Mild corneal
epithelial oedema is frequent.
оАБ Mydriasis is common. Posterior synechiae are not a
feature.
оАБ IOP is typically raised to over 40 mmHg, out of proportion to
iritis severity and untreated persists for hours to weeks. Elevation precedes the inоАЬammatory signs.
оАБ Gonioscopy shows an open angle without PAS.
оАБ Glaucomatous optic neuropathy is relatively uncommon in
most cases. Reversible cupping has been described.

Treatment

Topical steroids are used to control inоАЬammation, with aqueous
suppressants to lower the IOP. Topical steroids should only be used
while there is active inоАЬammation and should not be used prophylactically. Topical or oral non-steroidal anti-inоАЬammatory agents
may also be beneоАЮcial. оАЭe beneоАЮt of antiviral treatment is unclear.
Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C may occasionally be needed,
especially when there are signs of glaucomatous nerve damage or
progressive visual оАЮeld loss.

SteroidоАЯinduced glaucoma
Introduction

Around one in three individuals develop some degree of elevation of IOP in response to a course of potent topical steroid,
dividing the population into steroid вАШrespondersвАЩ and вАШnonrespondersвАЩ. Responders are more likely than non-responders
to develop POAG and a majority of patients with POAG are
responders. Steroid-induced glaucoma is a form of secondary
glaucoma, which is typically associated with the use of topical,
periocular, intraocular or steroid therapy, although it can occur
with any type of corticosteroid administration. оАЭe rise in IOP
is a consequence of increased resistance to the outоАЬow of aqueous secondary to altered function of the extracellular matrix or
endothelial cells of the trabecular meshwork. оАЭe IOP elevation is dependent on the steroid type, potency, dose, duration
and route of administration and usually occurs 2вАУ4 weeks aоАЪer
commencement of treatment. оАЭe rise in IOP aоАЪer intravitreal
triamcinolone typically lasts 2вАУ4 months and aоАЪer dexamethasone intravitreal implant (Ozurdex) up to 6 months. оАЭe clinical picture resembles that of chronic open-angle glaucoma with
a normal-appearing anterior chamber angle and an absence of
symptoms. Much less oоАЪen the condition may result in an acute
presentation with pain and a signiоАЮcant IOP rise.

Risk factors

оАБ Established glaucoma/ocular hypertension.
оАБ Family history of glaucoma.
оАБ High myopia.
оАБ Young age, especially childhood.
оАБ Connective tissue disease, especially rheumatoid arthritis.

Treatment

оАБ Discontinuation of the topical steroid is usually all that is
required to reduce the IOP back to normal. In the acute form
this occurs within days and in the chronic form within 1вАУ4
weeks.
оАБ A less potent steroid drop can be prescribed, for example
fluorometholone 0.1%, rimexolone 1% or loteprednol
etabonate.
оАБ In 3% of patients, elevated IOP may persist despite stopping all
steroids. оАЭe duration of steroid therapy inоАЬuences the reversibility of IOP elevation. оАЭis is usually seen in patients with a
family history of glaucoma.

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 393
#### 11

оАБ Persistent IOP elevation should be managed with medication
or glaucoma surgery. Steroid-induced raised IOP is unlikely to
occur in the presence of an established bleb.

TIP In any patient with raised IOP who is using topical
steroids, simply discontinuing the steroids will often result in a
rapid reduction of IOP (thus identifying the patient as a вАШsteroid
responderвАЩ).

LENS-RELATED GLAUCOMA

Phacolytic glaucoma
Introduction

Phacolytic glaucoma is a secondary open-angle glaucoma
occurring in association with a hypermature cataract. Trabecular obstruction is caused by high molecular-weight lens
proteins that leak through the intact capsule into the aqueous humour leading to trabecular obstruction. Macrophages
containing lens proteins may also contribute (Fig. 11.48A).
Phacolytic glaucoma should not be confused with phacogenic
(previously phacoanaphylactic) uveitis, an autoimmune granulomatous reaction to exposed lens proteins occurring with a
compromised lens capsule (see Ch. 12).

Diagnosis

оАБ Presentation is with pain. Vision is poor due to cataract.
оАБ Slit lamp biomicroscopy shows corneal oedema, a hypermature cataract and a deep anterior chamber. оАЭere may be large
оАЬoating white particles in the AC, consisting of lens protein
and protein-containing macrophages (Fig. 11.48B), which
may impart a milky appearance to the aqueous if very dense
(Fig. 11.48C) and can form a pseudohypopyon (Fig. 11.48C
and D).
оАБ Gonioscopy, if a reasonable view can be obtained, shows an
open angle with lens-derived material and inоАЬammatory cells
that are most substantial inferiorly.

Treatment

AоАЪer IOP is controlled medically, proteinaceous material is washed
out from the anterior chamber and the cataract is removed. Care
should be taken not to rupture the zonule, which is likely to be
more fragile than usual.

Phacomorphic glaucoma
Pathogenesis

Phacomorphic glaucoma is an acute secondary angle-closure glaucoma precipitated by an intumescent cataractous lens. Equatorial
age-related growth of the lens slackens the suspensory ligament
and allows the lens to move anteriorly. Associated anteroposterior
growth leads to increased iridolenticular contact and potentiates
pupillary block and iris bomb√©.

394 Lens-Related Glaucoma

A B

C D

Fig. 11.48 Phacolytic glaucoma. оАЭAоАЮ Lens protein-containing macrophages in the angle;
оАЭBоАЮ hypermature cataract, lens protein-containing macrophages оАЬoating in the aqueous; оАЭCоАЮ
dense milky aqueous with pseudohypopyon; оАЭDоАЮ residual particles in the anterior chamber
following incomplete irrigation after complicated surgery. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson,
from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 вАУ оАЯg. A.)

Diagnosis

оАБ Presentation is similar to acute PACG with a shallow AC and
mid-dilated pupil. A dense white cataract is evident (Fig. 11.49).
оАБ оАЯe fellow eye. Phacomorphic glaucoma is more likely in eyes
with a shorter axial length and shallower AC, but the fellow eye
may demonstrate a deep AC and an open angle.
оАБ Anterior segment OCT or US biomicroscopy may be useful.

Treatment

оАБ Medical treatment is initially similar to that of acute PACG.
оАБ Miotics are omitted as they tend to increase irisвАУlens apposition and shiоАЪ the lens anteriorly. Dilatation is sometimes helpful but should be implemented with caution.
оАБ Systemic hyperosmotic agents may be required more commonly than in PACG.
оАБ Laser iridotomy may be worthwhile but is oоАЪen not possible (due to corneal oedema or lensвАУcornea proximity) or

ineоАЫective. A similar procedure should be considered for the
fellow eye.
оАБ Laser iridoplasty may be a useful temporizing measure.
оАБ Cataract extraction constitutes the deоАЮnitive treatment, ideally once the IOP has been normalized and the eye is quiet.
оАЭe surgery can be diоАЩcult and carries a higher risk of
complications.

Pupillary block from disruption of lens
position
Causes

оАБ Blunt ocular trauma, even if relatively trivial, may result in
lens dislocation in eyes with a weak zonule as in pseudoexfoliation and homocystinuria.
оАБ Congenitally small lens (microspherophakia), e.g. WeillвАУ
Marchesani syndrome.

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 395
#### 11

Fig. 11.49 Intumescent cataract, shallow anterior chamber, di- Fig. 11.50 Traumatic dislocation into the anterior chamber of
lated pupil and corneal oedema in phacomorphic glaucoma. a normal-size lens.

Dislocation may be into the AC, the zonules may be
stretched or only part of the attachments may be disrupted so
that the intact part acts as a hinge and the lens may remain fully
or partially in the posterior chamber. Vitreous herniation may
be contributory.

Diagnosis

A lens fully or partially dislocated into the anterior chamber will
usually be evident (Fig. 11.50). Acute pupillary block will cause a
sudden severe elevation of IOP with associated visual impairment.
Imaging such as ultrasound biomicroscopy may be diagnostic.

Treatment

оАЭe IOP is initially reduced with osmotic agents, which reduce vitreous volume. Treatment should be urgent as prolonged lenticulocorneal contact, particularly in the presence of high IOP, may
cause permanent endothelial damage.
оАБ Initial treatment. The patient should adopt a supine posture with the pupil dilated, to attempt to reposition the lens
in the posterior chamber, following which a miotic can be
used with caution. Bilateral laser iridotomy may provide
extended control in some cases, but lens extraction may be
necessary.
оАБ DeоАЮnitive treatment consists of surgical lens extraction.
An anterior chamber IOL, iris- or sclera-оАЮxated IOL will be
necessary.

TRAUMATIC GLAUCOMA

Hyphaema
Introduction

In a patient with a small traumatic hyphaema, which is usually innocuous and transient, IOP elevation may still result
from trabecular obstruction by red blood cells. The size of a
hyphaema is a useful indicator of visual prognosis and risk

of complications. A hyphaema involving less than half the
anterior chamber is associated with a 20% incidence of complications and a final VA of better than 6/18 in 80% of eyes
(Fig. 11.51A). However, a hyphaema involving more than half
the anterior chamber is associated with an 80% incidence
of complications and a final VA of better than 6/18 in only
one-third (Fig. 11.51B). The visual prognosis is determined
mainly by the extent of injury to the retina sustained at the
time of the original injury. Severe and prolonged elevation
of IOP may cause corneal blood staining and damage to the
optic nerve. The optic nerve is endangered by IOP of greater
than 50 mmHg. Secondary haemorrhage, often more severe
than the primary bleed, may develop within 3вАУ5 days of the
initial injury and is associated with a reduced visual outcome.
Patients with sickle-cell haemoglobinopathy are at increased
risk of complications, especially IOP elevation due to trabecular meshwork obstruction by deformed red cells and vascular occlusion.

Treatment

{ A protective eye shield should be worn.

оАБ General

{ A coagulation abnormality, particularly a haemoglobinopathy, should be excluded.

{ Any current anticoagulant medication should be discontinued after liaison with a general physician to assess
the risk. NSAIDs should not be used for analgesia. Likewise, specialist advice should be sought regarding the
management of a patient with a haemoglobinopathy,
particularly before administering high-risk medication
(see below).

{ Hospital admission may be required for a large hyphaema.

{ Strict bed rest is probably unnecessary, but substantially limiting activity is prudent and the patient should
remain in a sitting or semi-upright posture, including
during sleep.

396 Traumatic Glaucoma

A B

Fig. 11.51 Hyphaema. оАЭAоАЮ Small with low risk of raised IOP; оАЭBоАЮ secondary haemorrhage with
raised IOP and increased risk of glaucoma and corneal blood staining.

оАБ Medical

{ Topical prostaglandins, beta-blockers and/or a topical or
systemic CAI is administered, depending on the IOP. CAI
should not be used in sickle haemoglobinopathies. Miotics should also be avoided as they may increase pupillary
block and disrupt the bloodвАУaqueous barrier.

{ Occasionally a hyperosmotic agent is needed, though as
with CAI and alpha-agonists a high threshold is adopted
in sickle patients.

{ Topical steroids should be used since they reduce the risk
of secondary haemorrhage and suppress inоАЬammation.

{ Atropine is oоАЪen recommended in patients with a large
hyphaema to achieve constant mydriasis and to reduce the
chance of secondary haemorrhage.

Angle recession glaucoma
Introduction

Angle recession involves rupture of the face of the ciliary body,
the portion between the iris root and the scleral spur, due to
blunt trauma. Although a large percentage of eyes with traumatic
hyphaema exhibit some degree of angle recession, glaucoma develops in less than 10% aоАЪer 10 years. оАЭe rise in IOP is secondary
to associated trabecular damage rather than from angle recession
itself. However, the risk of glaucoma is directly related to the extent
of angle recession and is unlikely to follow if less than three quadrants are recessed.

Diagnosis

оАБ Presentation is with unilateral chronic glaucoma.
оАБ Slit lamp examination may show signs of previous blunt
trauma. These may be mild, such as a small sphincter
rupture.
оАБ Gonioscopy may initially reveal irregular widening of the ciliary body band (Fig. 11.52). In longstanding cases, the recession may become obscured by оАЮbrosis and the angle may show
hyperpigmentation.

Treatment

оАБ FollowоА† up: Individuals with more than two quadrants of angle
recession should have their IOP checked on a yearly basis.
оАЭey should be reassured that their risk of developing secondary glaucoma is low.
оАБ Medical treatment is as for other types of secondary openangle glaucoma but is frequently unsatisfactory. Laser trabeculoplasty is of no beneоАЮt.
оАБ Trabeculectomy with adjunctive antimetabolite is generally
eоАЫective.
оАБ An artiоАЮcial оАЮltering shunt or cyclodiode should be considered if trabeculectomy fails.

{ AntiоАЮbrinolysis with systemic tranexamic acid or aminocaproic acid (ACA) may be considered under higher-risk
circumstances such as recurrent bleeding.
оАБ Laser photocoagulation of angle bleeding points via a gonioprism has been described, though gonioscopy should probably
be deferred for 5вАУ6 days post-injury.
оАБ Anterior chamber paracentesis or surgical evacuation of
blood is required in around 5%. Indications for surgery include
a total hyphaema, an IOP of >50 mmHg for 2 days or an IOP
>35 mmHg for 5 days. Surgical intervention reduces the risk
of permanent corneal staining and optic atrophy and prevents
the occult development of peripheral anterior synechiae and
chronic secondary glaucoma. Particular vigilance is required
in patients with sickle-cell anaemia (even moderate pressure
elevation can lead to optic atrophy), patients with prior glaucomatous optic neuropathy and in young children with a risk of
amblyopia. A glaucoma оАЮltration procedure may be necessary
in some cases.
оАБ On discharge the patient should be advised to avoid any activity with a risk of even minor eye trauma for several weeks.
Symptoms of a rebleed should prompt immediate review.

Fig. 11.52 Gonioscopy of angle recession showing irregular
widening of the ciliary body band.

GHOST CELL вАШGLAUCOMAвАЩ

Pathogenesis

Ghost cell вАШglaucomaвАЩ is due to trabecular obstruction by degenerate erythrocytes, which causes raised IOP and, rarely, glaucoma.
Approximately 2 weeks aоАЪer a vitreous haemorrhage, haemoglobin leaks from the erythrocytes, which evolve into ghost cells.
оАЭese then pass through a defect in the anterior hyaloid face and
pass into the anterior chamber. Because their pliability is lost, the
cells become entrapped within the pores of the trabecular meshwork and obstruct aqueous outоАЬow. оАЭe condition may occur in
the following settings:
оАБ Cataract surgery in the context of pre-existing vitreous
haemorrhage.
оАБ Vitreous haemorrhage in an already aphakic or pseudophaоА†
kic eye.
оАБ When cataract surgery is complicated by a vitreous
haemorrhage and hyphaema. The hyphaema clears but
the red cells in the vitreous persist and evolve into ghost
cells.

Clinical features

оАБ оАЭe cornea may be oedematous due to elevated IOP.
оАБ оАЭe anterior chamber exhibits ghost cells, which may be recognized as reddish-brown or khaki particles in the aqueous. оАЭey
should not be confused with leucocytes, since this may result
in unwarranted treatment for uveitis.

Treatment

оАБ Medical treatment with aqueous suppressants.
оАБ Irrigation of the anterior chamber with washing out of the
ghost cells if medical therapy fails.
оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy may occasionally be required for persistent vitreous haemorrhage.

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 397
#### 11

IRIDOCORNEAL ENDOTHELIAL
SYNDROME

Introduction

Iridocorneal endothelial (ICE) syndrome typically aоАЫects one eye
of a middle-aged woman. It consists of the following three clinical
presentations: Chandler syndrome, progressive (also termed essential) iris atrophy and iris naevus (CoganвАУReese) syndrome. оАЭe
pathological basis in all three is an abnormal corneal endothelial
cell layer with a predilection for proliferation and migration across
the anterior chamber angle and onto the surface of the iris, with
subsequent progression to glaucoma (50%) and corneal decompensation in a substantial proportion of involved eyes. Glaucoma
is due to trabecular obstruction by proliferating tissue followed by
angle closure secondary to contraction. Polymerase chain reaction
(PCR) testing shows the presence of herpes simplex virus DNA in
a substantial percentage of ICE syndrome corneal and trabecular
meshwork specimens, suggesting a possible viral aetiology.

Diagnosis

Clear diоАЫerentiation between the three presentations may be
diоАЩcult. Apparent transition from one to another has been
reported. DiоАЫerentiation depends primarily on iris appearance,
but there is oоАЪen substantial overlap. Gonioscopically visible
changes in some patients may be subtle despite elevated IOP, particularly in early disease. оАЭe specular microscopic appearance is
characteristic.
оАБ Chandler syndrome is the most common clinical presentation and is characterized by an abnormal corneal endothelial
appearance said to resemble hammered silver (Fig. 11.53A).
It frequently presents with blurred vision and haloes due to
corneal oedema. Iris atrophy is absent in about 60% and in
the remainder is variable in severity. Corectopia is mildвАУ
moderate when present. When glaucoma occurs, it is usually
less severe than in the other two presentations.
оАБ Progressive (essential) iris atrophy is characterized by iris
changes including corectopia (pupil malposition вАУ Fig. 11.53B),
iris atrophy in the early stage, pseudopolycoria (supernumerary false pupil) (Fig. 11.53C), and broad-based PAS that oоАЪen
extend anterior to the Schwalbe line (Fig. 11.53D). Severe iris
atrophy occurs in the late stage and is associated with ectropion uveae and severe glaucoma (Fig. 11.53E).
оАБ Iris naevus (CoganвАУReese) syndrome is characterized by
either a diоАЫuse naevus that covers the anterior iris, or by iris
nodules (Fig. 11.53F). Iris atrophy is absent in 50% of cases
and in the remainder, it is usually mildвАУmoderate, although
corectopia may be severe. оАЭe appearance may be mimicked
by a diоАЫuse iris melanoma.

Treatment

оАБ Medical treatment of glaucoma is usually ineоАЫective.
оАБ Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C can be tried, but the longterm results are poor.

GLAUCOMA ASSOCIATED WITH
INTRAOCULAR TUMOURS

Approximately 5% of eyes with intraocular tumours develop a secondary elevation of IOP. Potential mechanisms are set out below:
оАБ Trabecular block. Distinction between diоАЫerent mechanisms
may not be possible clinically.

{ Angle invasion by a solid iris melanoma (see Fig. 20.16F)

{ Trabecular inоАЮltration by neoplastic cells originating
from an iris melanoma (Fig. 11.54A). Rarely, tumour
seeding from a retinoblastoma may also invade the
trabeculum.

{ Melanomalytic glaucoma may occur in some eyes with
iris melanoma. It is due to trabecular blockage by macrophages that have ingested pigment and tumour cells, similar to phacolytic glaucoma.
оАБ Secondary angle closure

{ Neovascular glaucoma is the most common mechanism in
eyes with choroidal melanoma or retinoblastoma.

{ Anterior displacement of irisвАУlens diaphragm may occur
in an eye with a ciliary body melanoma or a large tumour
of the posterior segment (Fig. 11.54B).

Glaucoma 399
#### 11

GLAUCOMA SECONDARY TO
EPITHELIAL INGROWTH

Introduction

Epithelial ingrowth (downgrowth) is a rare but potentially blinding complication of anterior segment surgery or trauma, occurring when conjunctival or corneal epithelial cells migrate through
a wound and proliferate in the anterior segment (Fig. 11.55A).
Elevation of IOP is due to trabecular obstruction by one or more
of an epithelial membrane, secondary synechial angle closure and
desquamated epithelial and inоАЬammatory cells. оАЭe associated
glaucoma can be particularly intractable and the prognosis poor.

Diagnosis

оАБ Persistent postoperative anterior uveitis.
оАБ DiоАЫuse epithelialization characterized by a greyish translucent
membrane with a scalloped border that involves the posterior
corneal surface in the area of a surgical or traumatic wound.
оАБ Cystic and оАЮbrous proliferative patterns sometimes occur and
tend to have a better prognosis (Fig. 11.55B).
оАБ Pupillary distortion.

Treatment

оАЭe aim of treatment is the eradication of all invading epithelium
to avoid recurrence.
оАБ Block excision involves the simultaneous excision of adjacent
iris and pars plicata of the ciliary body, together with all layers
of the sclera and cornea in contact with the lesion. оАЭe resultant defect is covered with a tectonic corneoscleral graоАЪ. оАЭe

A B

Fig. 11.54 Glaucoma secondary to intraocular melanoma. оАЭAоАЮ Melanoma cells inоАЫltrating the
trabeculum; оАЭBоАЮ anterior displacement of the iris by a ciliary body melanoma.

400 Iridoschisis

Fig. 11.55 DiоАЪuse epithelial ingrowth. оАЭAоАЮ StratiоАЫed squamous epithelium lining the anterior iris

area of iris involvement may be delineated by applying argon
laser burns, which will cause whitening of the aоАЫected area.
оАБ Cryotherapy may be applied to devitalize the epithelium
remaining on the posterior surface of the cornea, in the angle
and on the ciliary body. Intraocular air is used to insulate other
tissues from the eоАЫects of the cryotherapy.
оАБ Intracameral 5оА† оАЬuorouracil has been tried with variable
results.
оАБ Intravitreal injection of methotrexate (400 mg/0.1 ml every
2 weeks for six cycles) has been reported to lead to successful
resolution of the ingrowth.
оАБ Glaucoma drainage devices are of value for medically uncontrolled glaucoma associated with extensive epithelial ingrowth
unsuitable for surgical excision.

IRIDOSCHISIS

Iridoschisis is a rare condition typically aоАЫecting both eyes of an
older patient. Primary angle closure and PACG is associated in
up to 90%. оАЭis appearance may rarely occur aоАЪer trauma. оАЭe
mechanism is incompletely understood, but it has been proposed
that intermittent substantial elevation of IOP results in iris atrophy, with severity ranging from stromal atrophy to оАЮbrillar disintegration of the anterior layer. Changes are more marked inferiorly
(Fig. 11.56A), but can occasionally involve the superior iris (Fig.
11.56B). оАЭe pupil is usually normal and the anterior chamber
is typically shallow. Gonioscopy commonly shows an occludable
angle, oоАЪen with PAS. Angle closure should be treated with a laser
iridotomy.

PRIMARY CONGENITAL GLAUCOMA
Introduction

Primary congenital glaucoma (PCG) is rare, with an incidence of
1:10000 live births, in many populations. Boys are more commonly
aоАЫected than girls in most surveys. оАЭe rate is 5вАУ10 times higher in
children from consanguineous relationships. Involvement is more
oоАЪen bilateral, but frequently asymmetrical. оАЭe condition can be
classiоАЮed as follows:
оАБ True congenital glaucoma (40%), in which IOP is elevated
during intrauterine life.
оАБ Infantile glaucoma (55%), which manifests prior to age 3.
оАБ Juvenile glaucoma, the least common, in which IOP rises
between 3 and 16 years of age.
Primary congenital glaucoma is, by deоАЮnition, not associated
with other major ocular abnormalities. It is thought to be caused
by impaired aqueous outоАЬow due to maldevelopment of the anterior chamber angle (trabeculodysgenesis). It is usually sporadic, but
approximately 10% are inherited in an autosomal recessive fashion
with variable penetrance. Angiopoietin receptor TEK mutations
play a role in the aetiology of some cases of primary congenital glaucoma and are transmitted as an autosomal dominant pattern with
variable expressivity. Other gene mutations have been implicated
in autosomal recessive congenital glaucoma, prominently CYP1B1coding for cytochrome P450 and LTBP2. Currently, the chance of
identifying a genetic cause is 40% when genetic testing is done. оАЭe
prognosis is dependent on severity and age at onset/diagnosis. Secondary infantile glaucoma can be caused by a range of conditions
including tumours such as retinoblastoma, persistent fetal vasculature (persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous) and uveitis.

TIP Primary congenital glaucoma is always treated surgically
as soon as the diagnosis has been conоАЫrmed.

Diagnosis

оАБ Presentation usually occurs when watering, photophobia or
blepharospasm is noticed by parents or a health-care professional, or when an abnormality is found, such as corneal haze,
large (Fig. 11.57A) or asymmetrical eye size (Fig. 11.57B).
оАБ Corneal haze is due to diоАЫuse oedema secondary to raised
IOP, or localized oedema due to breaks in Descemet membrane (Fig. 11.57C).
оАБ Buphthalmos is a large eye (see Fig. 11.57AвАУC) as a result
of stretching due to elevated IOP prior to the age of 3 years.
A corneal diameter of more than 12mm before the age of 1
should be treated with suspicion, especially if there is asymmetry. оАЭe thinned sclera oоАЪen appears blue due to increased
visualization of the underlying uvea. Complications include
myopia and lens subluxation.
оАБ Haab striae (Fig. 11.57D) are curvilinear healed breaks in
Descemet membrane.
оАБ Corneal scarring and vascularization.
оАБ Optic disc cupping in infants may regress once IOP is normalized. Most normal infants exhibit no apparent cup.
оАБ Evaluation under general anaesthesia is generally required.
Intravenous ketamine lowers IOP less than other agents.

CHAPTER

Glaucoma 401
#### 11

{ IOP measurement should be performed оАЮrst, optimally
with more than one method (e.g. Perkins, Tono-Pen, iCare).
It is preferable where possible for this to be measured in a
conscious or sedated child; 10вАУ12 mmHg is normal.

{ Anterior chamber examination with an operating microscope and/or portable slit lamp.

{ Optic disc examination; asymmetry or a cup/disc ratio of
>0.3 is suspicious.

{ Corneal diameter measurement; >12 mm prior to the age
of 1 year is highly suspicious.

{ Increased axial length measurement using ultrasound: a
measurement of more than 20.25mm at 1 month is usually abnormal, particularly if there is inter-eye asymmetry.
One of the most reliable signs of IOP control aоАЪer surgery
is stabilization of the axial length measurements.

{ Gonioscopy using a direct goniolens may be normal or
reveal trabeculodysgenesis (Fig. 11.58), vaguely characterized by an anteriorly located iris insertion and a hypoplasticappearing peripheral iris. оАЭe old concept of a discrete
(Barkan) membrane has not been deоАЮnitively conоАЮrmed.

{ Refraction.

Treatment

Surgery is always required and is successful in 80вАУ90%. Medication may be used as temporary or supplementary therapy. Caution
is required with selection of medication in young children, as most
are relatively contraindicated.
оАБ Goniotomy. Under direct gonioscopic visualization, an incision is made at the midpoint of the trabecular meshwork (Fig.
11.59A).
оАБ Trabeculotomy may be necessary if corneal clouding prevents an adequate view of the angle and is also an option when
repeated goniotomy has failed. A partial-thickness scleral
оАЬap is created (Fig. 11.59B) and a Harms trabeculotome (Fig.
11.59C) is inserted into the Schlemm canal and rotated into
the anterior chamber.
оАБ A modification of trabeculotomy is used in some centres.
An illuminated canaloplasty device is threaded into the
Schlemm canal followed by a 6-0 Prolene suture, which is
then pulled into the anterior chamber thus opening 360% of
the trabecular meshwork (Fig. 11.59D). The postoperative
IOP using this technique is approximately 5 mmHg lower
at 2 years than that obtained with the traditional approach.
оАБ Other procedures when angle surgery fails include trabeculectomy, tube shunt implantation and ciliary body ablative
procedures.
оАБ Monitoring of IOP, corneal diameter, axial length and other
parameters is required long term.
оАБ Amblyopia and refractive error should be managed
aggressively.

DiоАЪerential diagnosis

оАБ Cloudy cornea

{ Birth trauma.

{ Rubella keratitis (congenital rubella is also associated with
congenital glaucoma).

IRIDOCORNEAL DYSGENESIS

Posterior embryotoxon

Posterior embryotoxon refers to a prominent and anteriorly
displaced Schwalbe line, seen as a thin grey-white arcuate ridge
adjacent to the limbus on the inner surface of the cornea (Fig.
11.60A). It is an innocuous isolated finding in up to 15% of
the general population, but is one of the features of AxenfeldвАУ
Rieger anomaly. It is also seen in the multisystem genetic disorder Alagille syndrome, in which optic disc drusen are also
common.

AxenfeldвАУRieger syndrome
Introduction

AxenfeldвАУRieger syndrome is the umbrella term for a spectrum
of disorders featuring bilateral developmental ocular anomalies:

Axenfeld anomaly, Rieger anomaly and Rieger syndrome. It is
caused by defective neural crest cell-related processes during fetal
development. оАЭis can lead to incomplete development of the trabecular meshwork or Schlemm canal. An abnormal endothelial cell
membrane has been identiоАЮed on anterior segment structures in
some patients. оАЭe key implication of the syndrome is a 50% risk
of glaucoma. Associated variants in several diоАЫerent genes have
been found, including PITX2, PAX6, FOXC1 and RIEG2. DiоАЫerent
genetic abnormalities can give a similar clinical picture (genetic heterogeneity). Cases may be sporadic, but a family history is common,
when inheritance is autosomal dominant with variable expressivity
but very high penetrance. оАЭere is no gender predilection.

Clinical features

оАБ Axenfeld anomaly is characterized by posterior embryotoxon
(Fig. 11.60A) with attached strands of peripheral iris, the latter
best viewed with gonioscopy.

404 Iridocorneal Dysgenesis

оАБ Rieger anomaly oоАЪen manifests with an anterior segment
appearance similar to that of iridocorneal endothelial (ICE)
syndrome.

{ Posterior embryotoxon.

{ Iris stromal hypoplasia (Fig. 11.60B).

{ Ectropion uveae.

{ Corectopia and full-thickness iris defects (Fig. 11.60C and
D).
оАБ Gonioscopy in mild cases shows the Axenfeld anomaly. In
severe cases, broad leaves of iris adhere to the cornea anterior
to the Schwalbe line (Fig. 11.60E).
оАБ Glaucoma develops in about 50%, usually during childhood or early adulthood. Surgical management is often
necessary.
оАБ Rieger syndrome is characterized by the Rieger anomaly
together with extraocular malformations that, as with the ocular features, are caused by defective neural crest cell-related tissue development:

{ Dental anomalies (Fig. 11.60F), including hypodontia (few
teeth) and microdontia (small teeth).

{ Facial anomalies: maxillary hypoplasia, broad nasal bridge,
telecanthus and hypertelorism.

{ Other anomalies include redundant paraumbilical skin
and hypospadias. Hearing loss, hydrocephalus, cardiac and
renal anomalies and congenital hip dislocation are rare.

Aniridia
Genetics

Aniridia is a rare bilateral condition that may have life-threatening
associations. It occurs as a result of abnormal neuroectodermal
development secondary to a mutation in the PAX6 gene. PAX6 is
adjacent to gene WT1, mutation of which predisposes to Wilms
tumour.
оАБ Autosomal dominant aniridia accounts for about two-thirds
of cases and has no systemic implications. Penetrance is complete (all patients with the genotype will have the phenotype)
but expressivity (severity) is variable.
оАБ Sporadic, including WARG, previously known as Miller syndrome (Wilms tumour, Aniridia, mental Retardation, Genitourinary abnormalities), includes about a third of patients.
Children with sporadic aniridia have about a 30% chance of
developing Wilms tumour.
оАБ Gillespie syndrome accounts for only about 1% of cases.
Inheritance is AR but is not caused by PAX6 mutations. Cerebellar ataxia and learning disabilities are features.

Diagnosis

All patients with sporadic aniridia should have abdominal ultrasonography every 3 months until 5 years of age, every 6 months
until 10 years of age and annually until 16 years of age to detect the
development of Wilms tumour or until molecular genetic analysis
conоАЮrms the absence of a WT1 mutation.
оАБ Presentation is typically at birth with nystagmus and photophobia. оАЭe parents may have noticed the absence of irides or
apparently large pupils.
оАБ Aniridia is variable in severity, ranging from minimal, detectable only by retroillumination, to total absence (Fig. 11.62A
and B).
оАБ Lids oоАЪen show meibomian gland dysfunction.
оАБ Cornea

Peters anomaly
Introduction

Peters anomaly is a rare but oоАЪen severe condition that is bilateral
in more than half of cases. It is the result of defective neural crest
cell migration during fetal development. Manifestations range
from mild to severe. Most cases are sporadic, although autosomal
recessive inheritance has been described.

Clinical features

Peters type I aоАЫects the cornea alone, type II shows both corneal
and lens abnormalities.
оАБ Central corneal opacity of variable density (Fig. 11.61A and B).
оАБ Posterior corneal defect involving the posterior stroma, Descemet membrane and endothelium with or without iridocorneal or lenticulocorneal (Fig. 11.61C) adhesions.
оАБ Glaucoma occurs in about 50% due to associated angle anomaly. Onset is usually in infancy but occasionally in childhood
or later. оАЭe prognosis tends to be worse than in primary congenital glaucoma.
оАБ Systemic associations including craniofacial and central
nervous system anomalies have been reported. вАШPeters plusвАЩ
syndrome includes a particular constellation of systemic
abnormalities.

{ Total corneal central stromal scarring and vascularization
may occur in end-stage disease.
оАБ Fundus. Possible abnormalities include foveal and/or optic
nerve hypoplasia and choroidal coloboma.
оАБ Gonioscopy even in eyes with apparently total aniridia usually shows a hypoplastic or rudimentary frill of iris tissue (Fig.
11.62C).
оАБ Lens changes can include cataract and subluxation.
оАБ Glaucoma (75%) usually presents in late childhood or adolescence. It is caused by synechial angle closure secondary to the
contraction of rudimentary iris tissue. Treatment is diоАЩcult
and the prognosis guarded.

{ Tear оАЮlm instability, dry eye and epithelial defects are
common.

{ Limbal stem cell deоАЮciency may result in вАШconjunctivalizationвАЩ of the peripheral cornea.

Treatment

оАБ Glaucoma

{ Medical treatment is usually inadequate.

{ Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C or combined trabeculectomyвАУtrabeculotomy have been tried in the past but
usually fail to control the IOP.

{ Glaucoma drainage devices oоАЫer the best chance of longterm successful control of IOP.

{ Diode laser cycloablation may be necessary if other
modalities fail.
оАБ Painted contact lenses may be used to create an artiоАЮcial pupil
and improve both vision and cosmesis. Simple tinted lenses are
an alternative. Both may improve nystagmus.
оАБ Lubricants are frequently required for associated
keratopathy.
оАБ Cataract surgery is oоАЪen required. (Fig. 11.62D). A tinted
artiоАЮcial lens implant may be used to try to reduce photophobia. Trauma to the corneal limbus should be minimized in
order to preserve stem cell function.
оАБ Prosthetic iris implantation has been described in pseudophakic aniridic eyes, but may be complicated by, or worsen,
glaucoma.
оАБ Limbal stem cell transplantation with or without keratoplasty may be required.
оАБ Refractive errors, amblyopia and squint should be managed
aggressively.

GLAUCOMA IN PHACOMATOSES

SturgeвАУWeber syndrome
Introduction

SturgeвАУWeber syndrome (encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis)
is a congenital, sporadic phacomatosis (see Ch. 2). Glaucoma
ipsilateral to the facial haemangioma develops in about 30%
and in 60% of these IOP elevation occurs before the age of
2 years and may result in buphthalmos (Fig. 11.63A). In the
remainder, glaucoma may develop at any time from infancy
to adulthood. The pathogenesis is uncertain but putative
mechanisms include trabeculodysgenesis in infants and raised
episcleral venous pressure associated with arteriovenous communication in an episcleral haemangioma (Fig. 11.63B) in
older patients.

Treatment

оАБ Medical treatment alone may be adequate.
оАБ Goniotomy may be successful in eyes with angle anomalies.
оАБ Combined trabeculotomyвАУtrabeculectomy gives good
results in early-onset cases, but carries a relatively high risk
of choroidal eоАЫusion and suprachoroidal haemorrhage. Other
surgical options may be utilized.

NeuroоАЫbromatosis type 1

NeuroоАЮbromatosis is a disorder that primarily aоАЫects cell growth
of neural tissues. Inheritance is AD with irregular penetrance
and variable expressivity (see Ch. 19). Glaucoma is relatively rare
and, when present, usually unilateral and congenital. About 50%
of patients with glaucoma have an ipsilateral plexiform neuroоАЮbroma of the upper eyelid, or facial hemiatrophy (Fig. 11.64A).
Various mechanisms have been identiоАЮed, including congenital
angle anomaly, which may be associated with ectropion uveae (Fig.
11.64B and see Fig. 19.103).

MEDICAL TREATMENT OF GLAUCOMA

Introduction

It is important to attempt to maximize adherence by providing an
explanation of the disease and the rationale for treatment. A discussion of the medication being prescribed, including technique
and timing of administration and its potential adverse eоАЫects, is
also essential. оАЭe provision of written information may be helpful.
Most glaucoma medications are administered topically, but signiоАЮcant systemic absorption can still occur, with resultant systemic
adverse eоАЫects. Systemic absorption may be minimized by lacrimal
occlusion following instillation: simply closing the eyes for 3 minutes will reduce systemic absorption by about 50% and this can be
enhanced by applying digital pressure over the lacrimal sac вАУ these
measures also prolong eyeвАУdrug contact. EоАЫects on the periocular
skin may be reduced by blotting overоАЬow from the eyelids with a
clean dry tissue immediately aоАЪer instillation. Glaucoma medications should be avoided in pregnancy, if possible, with systemic
carbonic anhydrase inhibitors perhaps carrying the greatest risk
due to teratogenicity concerns.

Prostaglandin derivatives
Introduction

оАЭe major mode of prostaglandin (PG) action is the enhancement
of uveoscleral aqueous outоАЬow, although increased trabecular
outоАЬow facility and other mechanisms have been identiоАЮed. оАЭeir
IOP-lowering eоАЫect is typically greater than alternatives and a
reduction of 27вАУ35% from baseline can be expected. A prostaglandin derivative is typically preferred to a beta-blocker as оАЮrst-line
treatment for glaucoma because of the latterвАЩs potential for systemic side eоАЫects. Duration of action may extend for several days,
though administration once every day (at bedtime) is generally
recommended. Systemic side eоАЫects are few. оАЭe most commonly
troublesome ocular side eоАЫect is conjunctival hyperaemia. If one
prostaglandin fails to show adequate eоАЩcacy, inter-individual

receptor variation means that an alternative preparation may be
superior in a given patient. Paradoxically, the concomitant use of
more than one PG can cause a rise in IOP.

Agents

оАБ Latanoprost may cause fewer ocular adverse events than
other PG agents and so is oоАЪen used оАЮrst line. Approximately
5вАУ10% of patients show no response (IOP reduction of less
than 10%) to latanoprost, but may respond to one of the other
prostaglandins.
оАБ Travoprost is similar to latanoprost but fewer patients tend to
be non-responders. Polyquad is a novel proprietary preservative introduced by a major pharmaceutical manufacturer in its
travoprost formulation that may reduce ocular surface-related
adverse eоАЫects.
оАБ Bimatoprost 0.03% has a slightly greater IOP-lowering
eоАЫect than latanoprost, but is more likely to cause conjunctival hyperaemia. A new 0.01% preparation has a comparable
IOP-lowering eоАЫect to latanoprost with less hyperaemia.
Preservative-free bimatoprost is available. Sustained-release
intra-cameral bimatoprost is undergoing clinical evaluation and preliminary results indicate that IOP control can be
achieved in up to 70% of subjects for 6 months with a single
injection.
оАБ TaоАЬuprost is a preservative-free drop. Its IOP-lowering eоАЩcacy may be slightly less than that of other PG agents, but it is
well tolerated and seems to cause less disruption of the ocular
surface.
оАБ Omidenepag 0.002% solution is a new selective E-prostanoid
sub-type 2 agonist that is used once daily. It has a similar eоАЫect
on the IOP as other prostaglandin agents. It is eоАЫective in
patients who do not respond to latanoprost and does not cause
periorbitopathy. Hyperaemia occurs in about 5%.

Side eоАЮects

оАБ Ocular

{ Conjunctival hyperaemia is very common.
