Posterior uveitis

Fig. 12.7 Extensive iris atrophy following herpes zoster ophthalmicus вАУ predominantly sectoral pattern. (Courtesy of C
Barry.)

Posterior uveitis encompasses the clinical entities of retinitis, choroiditis and retinal vasculitis. Some lesions may originate primarily in the retina or choroid but oоАЬen there is involvement of both
(retinochoroiditis and chorioretinitis). SpeciоАЭc conditions will be
discussed later in the chapter.
оАБ Presentation varies according to the location of the inоАЮammatory focus and the presence of vitritis. For example, a patient
with a peripheral lesion may complain of оАЮoaters, whereas a
patient with a lesion involving the macula will predominantly
complain of impaired central vision.
оАБ Retinitis may be focal (solitary), multifocal, geographic or
diоАЫuse. Active lesions are characterized by whitish retinal
opacities with indistinct borders due to surrounding oedema
(Fig. 12.8A). As the lesion resolves, the borders become better
deоАЭned.
оАБ Choroiditis may also be focal, multifocal, geographic or
diоАЫuse. It does not usually induce vitritis in the absence of

436 Investigations

A

B

C

Fig. 12.8 Signs of posterior uveitis. оАЬAоАЭ Retinitis; оАЬBоАЭ choroiditis;
оАЬCоАЭ vasculitis.

concomitant retinal involvement. Active choroiditis is characterized by a round, yellow nodule (Fig. 12.8B).
оАБ Vasculitis may occur as a primary condition or as a secondary phenomenon adjacent to a focus of retinitis. Both arteries (periarteritis) and veins (periphlebitis) may be aоАЫected,
although venous involvement is more common. Active vasculitis is characterized by yellowish or grey-white, patchy,
perivascular cuоАЪng (Fig. 12.8C) sometimes associated with
haemorrhage. Quiescent vasculitis may leave perivascular
scarring, which should not be mistaken for active disease.

INVESTIGATIONS

оАБ Tailor the investigations. Investigations are oоАЬen negative,
with no clear underlying cause determined in many patients.
Rather than performing a battery of screening tests, investigation is tailored to each patient, directed by clinical features.
Sometimes a likely cause may be obvious, such as severe anterior uveitis following intraocular surgery when endophthalmitis will lead the diоАЫerential diagnosis list. In most cases, a
careful review for systemic symptoms is essential to detect any
clues to underlying disease, with referral to a specialist physician for further assessment where appropriate. Many associations of uveitis can present with a wide range of systemic
features.
оАБ Multimodal imaging of the posterior segment provides rapid,
objective and accurate assessment of the retina and choroid
and has become important in the management and follow-up
of patients with posterior uveitis. Widespread use of this technology is improving standards of care in clinical practice, as
well as deоАЭning outcome measures in uveitis research.
Investigation is generally not required in the following
circumstances:
оАБ A single episode of unilateral mild/moderate (no hypopyon)
non-granulomatous AAU with no ocular or systemic suggestion of underlying disease.
оАБ A systemic diagnosis compatible with the clinical features
(e.g. sarcoidosis) has already been conоАЭrmed.
оАБ Typical clinical features of a speciоАЭc entity for which investigation is not usually indicated (e.g. Fuchs uveitis syndrome).
Situations in which investigation of anterior uveitis is generally appropriate include:
оАБ Recurrent AAU.
оАБ Severe AAU.
оАБ Bilateral AAU.
оАБ Anterior uveitis that is persistent, chronic or resistant to
treatment.
оАБ Granulomatous inоАЮammatory signs (note that granulomatous
conditions may give non-granulomatous AAU).
оАБ Associated intermediate or posterior uveitis.
оАБ Ocular or systemic clinical features suggesting underlying
disease.
Repeating targeted previously negative investigations several
years later is sometimes helpful.
оАЮe following investigations should be considered:
оАБ HLA tissue typing (HLA-B27). оАЯe major histocompatibility complex (MHC) is a group of genes involved in white
cellвАУantigen interaction and other immune functions, including the encoding of cell surface glycoproteins. In humans the
MHC, found on chromosome 6, is called the human leukocyte
antigen (HLA) system. HLA typing is used to determine organ
transplantation compatibility and can also indicate predisposition to particular diseases. It has conventionally been performed by serological antigen identiоАЭcation, but increasingly
involves analysis of DNA. HLA-B27 is a common (e.g. 6вАУ8% of
the White population in the USA, 0.5% of patients of Japanese
ethnic origin) cell surface protein that presents peptides to T

cells. оАЯe phenotype has a very strong association with AAU,
ankylosing spondylitis and some other inоАЮammatory conditions such as reactive arthritis (Reiter syndrome), psoriatic
arthritis and arthritis in inоАЮammatory bowel disease. It is present in 50% of patients with AAU who are otherwise оАЭt and well
and 90% of patients with AAU who have an associated spondyloarthropathy, notably ankylosing spondylitis. Many HLA-B27
subtypes have been identiоАЭed and their signiоАЭcance is subject
to ongoing investigation. HLA types associated with ocular
inоАЮammatory disease are listed in Table 12.4. HLA-B27 testing
should be performed in any adult or child with recurrent or
chronic non-granulomatous anterior uveitis.
оАБ Syphilis serology

{ Treponemal antibody tests such as the ELISA (enzymelinked immunosorbent assay) are highly sensitive and speciоАЭc, but take around 3 months to become positive.

{ Non-speciоАЭc titratable cardiolipin antibody tests such as
the rapid plasma reagin (RPR) or venereal disease research
laboratory (VDRL) are more commonly positive in early
infection and are used to help monitor disease activity.
оАЯese tests become negative over time, typically in treated
disease. False-positive results can occur.

{ Both categories of test should be performed when screening for ocular syphilis.

CHAPTER

Uveitis 437
#### 12

оАБ Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP): acute-phase reactants that are probably of limited
value, but may be elevated in a range of systemic inоАЮammatory
disorders.
оАБ Complete blood count: leukocytosis may raise suspicion
of infection and, exceptionally, haematological malignancy.
Eosinophilia may occur in parasitic infection.
оАБ Lyme disease: serology may be considered, particularly in
endemic areas. Serology for other infectious diseases such as
brucellosis and leptospirosis can be requested if relevant risk
factors are present (e.g. endemic region).
оАБ Antinuclear antibody (ANA): limited use, except in children.
In those with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) its presence is
associated with a higher risk of CAU. Cases of possible subclinical JIA have been reported in ANA-positive children with
CAU.
оАБ Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA): limited
use in anterior uveitis unless associated with scleritis and/
or peripheral ulcerative keratitis, when cytoplasmic ANCA
(c-ANCA) testing should be considered as evidence of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener granulomatosis).
оАБ Interferon-gamma release assay (e.g. QuantiFERON-TB
Gold) blood test for tuberculosis.
оАБ HIV serology: indicated for selected patients, usually those
in whom an opportunistic infection has been diagnosed or is
suspected.
оАБ Sacroiliac joint X-ray may show evidence of sacroiliitis in ankylosing spondylitis and other seronegative
spondyloarthropathies.
оАБ Chest X-ray may show evidence of sarcoidosis or tuberculosis.
A high level of suspicion for both of these treatable conditions
should always be adopted, particularly if the inоАЮammation is
granulomatous.
оАБ Fundus autoоАЯuorescence (FAF) is a non-invasive technique
that detects lipofuscin and melanin in retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) cells. оАЯis provides information on RPE function and the integrity of the chorioretinal interface and is
extremely helpful when assessing inоАЮammatory conditions
of the choriocapillaris. Pronounced autoоАЮuorescence can be
detected in patients with multiple evanescent white dot syndrome (MEWDS) (see Fig. 12.60C), serpiginous choroidopathy (see Fig. 12.62D) and acute zonal occult outer retinopathy
(AZOOR) (see Fig. 12.68A).
оАБ Optical coherence tomography (OCT). оАЯis technique
obtains precise, reproducible, high-resolution images that correlate well with the histopathology. OCT can be used to detect
and monitor macular oedema, vitreomacular surface disease
and structural damage to the macula (see Fig. 14.49). Inner retinal inоАЭltrates that show hyperreоАЮective focal retinal thickening are seen in Beh√Іet disease and some viral infections. Outer
retina changes are seen in punctate inner choroidopathy (PIC),
MEWDS, acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE), serpiginous choroiditis, AZOOR and acute
macular neuroretinopathy (AMN) (see Fig. 12.67B). OCT can
be used to visualize choroidal granulomas (e.g. sarcoidosis and
tuberculosis) and primary choroidal inоАЮammatory diseases

{ Clinical features suggesting a diagnosis of syphilis should
prompt urgent referral to a physician specializing in infectious or sexually transmitted diseases.
оАБ Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE): a nonspeciоАЭc test that indicates the presence of a granulomatous
disease such as sarcoidosis, tuberculosis and leprosy. Elevation
occurs in up to 80% of patients with acute sarcoidosis but may
be normal during remissions. In normal children serum ACE
levels tend to be higher and diagnostically less useful. Vigorous
exercise can elevate ACE.
оАБ Lysozyme is a group of enzymes found in polymorphonuclear
neutrophils and numerous secretions including tears. It has a
strong antibacterial action, mediating breakdown of the bacterial cell wall. Serum lysozyme assay is generally slightly less
sensitive and speciоАЭc than serum ACE in the diagnosis of sarcoidosis, but performing both tests may increase sensitivity
and speciоАЭcity.

438 Treatment

like acute VogtвАУKoyanagiвАУHarada (VKH) syndrome (see Fig.
12.18E and F) and sympathetic ophthalmia.
оАБ Fluorescein angiography (FA; see Ch. 14). оАЯe оАЮuorescein molecule diоАЫuses easily out of minimally inоАЮamed vessels (retinal vasculitis; see Fig. 12.32B) and can also be used
to demonstrate occlusive vasculopathy (see Fig. 12.54B). In
inоАЮammatory eye disease FA is used to assess optic disc swelling and subretinal оАЮuid accumulation (see Fig. 12.32D). FA is
the investigation of choice when imaging inоАЮammatory retinal foci, showing early hypoоАЮuorescence and late hyperоАЮuorescence. Loss of the RPE shows up as a вАШwindow defectвАЩ and
pigment clumping gives a masking eоАЫect, as seen for example
in APMPPE (see Fig. 12.61B). Chorioretinal atrophy is shown
as a hyperоАЮuorescent area.
оАБ Indocyanine green angiography (ICGA; see Ch. 14). Indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) is rarely indicated in
anterior uveitis, but is the investigation of choice in patients
with predominantly choroidal inоАЮammation. Because the
ICG molecule is almost completely protein-bound, retinal
leakage only occurs when there is major inоАЮammation in
these vessels (e.g. in Beh√Іet disease). However, in the choroid
the protein-bound ICG leaks slowly, but unimpaired. AоАЬer
recirculation, more and more ICG is entrapped and gradual
impregnation of the choroid occurs. In the late phases active
absorption of ICG by the RPE results in diоАЫuse background
hyperоАЮuorescence. Alteration of choroidal impregnation is
the main parameter studied by ICGA in inоАЮammatory eye
disease. Two broad categories of inоАЮammation are seen: (a)
choriocapillaris inоАЮammation, where there is patchy hypoоАЮuorescent areas of diоАЫerent sizes, especially in the late images,
e.g., APMPPE (see Fig. 12.61E) and serpiginous chorioretinopathy (see Fig. 12.62F) and (b) stromal choroiditis, where
there are foci of hypoоАЮuorescence, followed by late diоАЫuse
hyperоАЮuorescence as a consequence of leakage from large
choroidal vessels. оАЯis can be a consequence of primary choroidal inоАЮammation (e.g. VKH, sympathetic ophthalmia) or
secondary choroidal inоАЮammation (e.g. sarcoidosis, tuberculosis and syphilis).
оАБ Ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM) is particularly indicated
in cases of hypotony and may demonstrate pathology such as
subtle choroidal eоАЫusion, cyclodialysis cleоАЬ and cyclitic membrane. B-scan ultrasonography can be helpful if the posterior
segment view is compromised by a very small pupil or opaque
media.
оАБ Aqueous tap: historically rarely performed in anterior uveitis,
but viral causes are now recognized as being more frequent
than previously realized. Herpesviruses (including cytomegalovirus) and rubella should be considered in clinically suspicious cases, especially unexplained hypertensive uveitis and
cases relatively unresponsive to topical steroids. An aqueous
humour sample can be sent for: (a) polymerase chain reaction
(PCR) analysis, to check for evidence of viral genetic material
and (b) for microscopy, culture and antibody assay. PCR may
also help to exclude Propionibacterium infection in a chronically inоАЮamed pseudophakic eye.
оАБ Iris biopsy is rarely performed.

оАБ Vitreous biopsy tends to be conоАЭned to the investigation of
obscure posterior segment inоАЮammation and suspected infectious endophthalmitis.
оАБ Chorioretinal biopsy. To determine the cause of a persistent
and severe posterior uveitis of unknown aetiology for which
all workup is negative, consider undertaking a retinochoroidal
biopsy.

TIP Multimodal imaging using OCT and indocyanine green
angiography is indispensable in the investigation of patients
with predominantly choroidal inоАЭammation.

оАБ Conjunctival biopsy: sampling of tissue such as a suspected
granuloma or inоАЭltrative lesion is occasionally indicated.
оАБ Referral to a specialist physician with resultant further
investigation. оАЯis is vital when systemic disease is suspected.
For instance, if respiratory symptoms are present, a chest physician may arrange additional testing such as a high-resolution
computed tomography (CT) chest scan, a whole-body gallium scan (sarcoidosis), a puriоАЭed protein derivative skin
test for tuberculosis (negative test is a diagnostic indicator in
sarcoidosis) and bronchoscopy with lavage/biopsy. Similarly,
neurological referral may lead to cranial magnetic resonance
imaging (MRI) and lumbar puncture, and gastroenterological
referral to endoscopy.

TREATMENT

For patients with a treatable cause of inоАЮammation such as an
infection, speciоАЭc treatment (see individual topics) is given either
instead of or in addition to the general anti-inоАЮammatory measures discussed below. Review frequency is set according to the
severity and chronicity of inоАЮammation. Patients with severe
inоАЮammation may need to be seen within a day or two of initiating treatment. оАЯose with mild recurrent idiopathic AAU may not
need to be seen for several weeks aоАЬer treatment is commenced.
оАБ Topical steroids

{ Prednisolone 1% or dexamethasone 0.1% is commonly
utilized as a оАЭrst choice. Other preparations, of varying
availability geographically, include diоАЮuprednate 0.05%
(may be administered at a lower frequency), loteprednol
etabonate 0.2% and 0.5% (moderate to marked potency
but a lower tendency to elevate IOP), betamethasone,
prednisolone 0.5%, оАЮuorometholone and rimexolone (the
latter three are of moderate to lower potency). оАЯe selection of topical steroid preparation can be modiоАЭed according to severity and other factors such as a known tendency
to IOP elevation. Steroid ointment (e.g. betamethasone)
may be instilled at bedtime to supplement the drops. Additional anti-inоАЮammatory treatment (see below) is necessary in some cases.

{ Treatment of AAU initially involves instillation at a frequency appropriate to the severity of inоАЮammation, typically starting with one drop hourly in moderateвАУsevere
cases. Once the inоАЮammation is controlled the instillation

frequency should be carefully tapered. A commonly
adopted regimen might consist of:
оАБ one drop hourly for 3 days, then
оАБ every 2 hours for 3 days, then
оАБ four times a day for 1 week, then
оАБ three times a day for 1 week, then
оАБ twice a day for 1 week, then
оАБ once a day for 1 week and stop. Treatment is oоАЬen discontinued by 5вАУ6 weeks.
Periodic review is undertaken as appropriate during the
treatment course, with further assessment a week or two
aоАЬer cessation, following which the patient can be discharged but cautioned to re-attend urgently should symptoms recur.

comfort. Constituent quantities vary according to manufacturer but 0.3 ml containing 0.12 mg adrenaline, 1 mg atropine
and 6 mg procaine is typical. It is usually administered by subconjunctival injection. An alternative to injection is insertion
of a cotton pledget soaked in Mydricaine into the superior and
inferior fornices for 5 minutes. Serious cardiovascular events
have been reported following injection (a transient sinus
tachycardia is common) and the patient should be monitored
aоАЬer injection. Mydricaine No. 1 is a paediatric version that
may also be eоАЫective in adults.
оАБ Tissue plasminogen activator (TPA). In severe оАЭbrinous
anterior uveitis 12.5вАУ25 ќЉg of TPA in 0.1 ml injected into
the AC (intracamerally) with a 30-gauge needle under topical anaesthesia will dissolve dense оАЭbrinous exudate and may
break down recently formed PS. Antiseptic precautions similar
to those for intravitreal injection should be taken.
оАБ Subconjunctival steroid can be administered in severe cases
or to patients in whom poor compliance is likely. For example,
betamethasone sodium phosphate solution (4 mg in 1 ml) can
be given alone or in a combined preparation with betamethasone acetate suspension for a sustained eоАЫect (e.g. Celestone,
6 mg in 1 ml).
оАБ Regional steroid injection. оАЯe use of an inferior approach
(вАШorbital оАЮoorвАЩ) or posterior sub-Tenon (Fig. 12.9) injection
of depot steroid preparations (e.g. triamcinolone acetonide,
methylprednisolone acetate) is common in the treatment of
posterior segment inоАЮammation, but is generally reserved
in anterior uveitis patients for the treatment of cases complicated by cystoid macular oedema (CMO) and for patients
non-compliant with topical administration. Periocular injections may also be administered at the time of surgery and may
rarely be used to supplement systemic therapy or when systemic steroids are contraindicated. оАЯe peak action is at about
4 weeks, with a maximum duration of action of around 3
months. Complications include subconjunctival haemorrhage,
globe penetration, refractory elevation of IOP (up to 25%),
cataract, ptosis, eyelid haemorrhage, eyelid ischaemic necrosis,
retrobulbar haemorrhage, subdermal fat atrophy, extraocular
muscle paresis, optic nerve injury, retinal and choroidal vascular occlusion and cutaneous hypopigmentation. Systemic
adverse eоАЫects are rare but can occur. Table 12.5 gives injection procedures; there is no clear evidence of the superiority
of one route over the other, but there may be a lower risk of
ocular perforation, of raised IOP and of ptosis with the orbital
оАЮoor approach. Utilizing a plastic intravenous cannula via a
superior sub-Tenon route has been described and is thought to
oоАЫer a lower risk of perforation.
оАБ Intraocular steroids. Intravitreal triamcinolone acetonide (4
mg in 0.1 ml, i.e. one-tenth of the orbital dose) is occasionally used in anterior uveitis for CMO unresponsive to other
forms of therapy and rarely may be considered at the time
of intraocular surgery in high-risk anterior uveitis patients.
Complications include elevation of IOP, cataract, endophthalmitis (sterile or infectious), haemorrhage, retinal detachment
and pseudohypopyon. Slow-release intravitreal implants may
occasionally be indicated.

{ Treatment of CAU is generally targeted at complete suppression of inоАЮammation, with no AC cellular activity or
оАЮare вАУ the latter is now thought to be an indicator of active
inоАЮammation in most or all cases. Even low-grade ongoing
activity is associated with a greater incidence of complications that outweighs the risk of complications from treatment. Exacerbations are initially treated in the same way as
AAU, though with more gradual tapering and typically a
maintenance regimen. Fuchs uveitis syndrome is an exception to this approach.

{ Common complications of topical steroids include transient elevation of IOP in susceptible individuals (вАШsteroid
respondersвАЩ). Long-term treatment may lead to permanent
IOP elevation with glaucomatous damage. Cataract can be
induced, but is less common than with systemic steroid
administration. оАЯe risk increases with dose and duration of therapy. Corneal complications are uncommon and
include secondary infection with bacteria and fungi, recrudescence of herpes simplex keratitis and corneal melting.
Systemic side eоАЫects are rare with topical steroids, but may
occur following prolonged administration, particularly in
children, in whom measures to reduce systemic absorption such as medial canthal pressure and blotting away of
overspill from the eyelids following instillation should be
discussed.
оАБ Cycloplegic agents. оАЯese are used in AAU and in exacerbations of CAU to prevent the formation of PS, to break down
recently formed synechiae and to promote comfort by relieving spasm of the pupillary and ciliary muscle. Commonly used
anticholinergic agents in order of increasing potency and duration of action include cyclopentolate (duration 12вАУ24 hours),
homatropine (3 days) and atropine (7вАУ10 days). In the acute
stage, phenylephrine 2.5% or 10% may be used to supplement
anticholinergics and break PS. In mild or CAU, a cycloplegic
can be instilled at bedtime to prevent diоАЪculties with accommodation during the day. In children, care should be taken to
avoid systemic toxicity, as a range of systemic adverse eоАЫects
have occurred including seizures. Prolonged uniocular cycloplegia may induce amblyopia in the susceptible age group.
оАБ Mydricaine No. 2. оАЯis is a preparation containing adrenaline
and atropine that is used to try to break fresh PS when drops
are ineоАЫective. It also contains local anaesthetic to improve

440 Treatment

оАБ Systemic steroids are very rarely required for anterior uveitis,
but may be needed where the response to topical treatment
is inadequate. оАЯey are sometimes given as a short course
prior to intraocular surgery as prophylaxis against worsening
inоАЮammation, having the advantage of rapid cessation of eоАЫect
in comparison with depot peri- or intraocular steroid injection, but have major potential adverse eоАЫects (Table 12.6).
оАБ Non-steroidal anti-inоАЯammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such
as naproxen and tolmetin may be eоАЫective in CAU and can
be used long-term under appropriate specialist physician
supervision.
оАБ Antimetabolites such as methotrexate and mycophenolate
mofetil are generally not required in the treatment of anterior
uveitis, though may be necessary in exceptional patients such
as juvenile idiopathic arthritis-associated CAU when other
measures fail to control inоАЮammation, or as a steroid-sparing

Table 12.5 Procedure for Inferior Transseptal and Posterior Sub-Tenon Regional Steroid Injection

Route Technique

Inferior transseptal A topical anaesthetic such as tetracaine (amethocaine) is instilled to prevent stinging by the
(вАШorbital оАЭoorвАЩ) injection antiseptic agents
The skin of the lower eyelid and maxillary area is cleaned with an antiseptic agent such as an
alcohol swab or povidone-iodine 5%
The vial containing the steroid is shaken
1 ml steroid (triamcinolone acetonide or methylprednisolone acetate 40 mg/ml) is drawn up
into a 2 ml syringe and the drawing-up needle replaced with a 25-gauge 5/8 inch (16 mm)
needle
The patient is asked to maintain gaze straight ahead
The needle is inserted through the skin (some practitioners inject via the conjunctiva) at
approximately the junction of the outer third and inner two-thirds of the lower orbital rim,
entering close to the bony margin whilst clearing the margin itself
The needle is slowly advanced tangentially to (or, anatomy permitting, away from) the globe in
similar fashion to a peribulbar local anaesthetic block up to the needle hub
The skin may be indented to ensure the needle tip is suоАЬciently posterior to deposit the
steroid away from the anterior subconjunctival area
The tip may be felt to engage the bony orbital оАЭoor and/or to pierce the orbital septum; as
with the superior injection technique, the needle can be moved from side to side to ensure
the sclera has not been engaged
The plunger is slightly withdrawn and, if no blood enters the syringe, the full 1 ml is slowly
injected, and the needle carefully withdrawn
Special care is required in a patient with a large eye (e.g. myopia) to avoid penetration of the
globe
Posterior sub-Tenon A topical anaesthetic such as tetracaine (amethocaine) is instilled
approach A small cotton pledget impregnated with tetracaine, lidocaine (lignocaine) 2% gel or an
alternative is placed into the superior fornix at the site of injection for 2 minutes
The vial containing the steroid is shaken
1 ml steroid (triamcinolone acetonide methylprednisolone acetate or 40 mg/ml) is drawn up into
a 2 ml syringe and the drawing-up needle replaced with a 25-gauge 5/8 inch (16 mm) needle
The patient is asked to look in the direction opposite to the superotemporal injection site
The bulbar conjunctiva is penetrated with the tip of the needle, bevel towards the globe,
slightly on the bulbar side of the fornix
The needle is slowly inserted posteriorly, following the contour of the globe, keeping it as
close to the globe as possible. In order not to penetrate the globe accidentally, wide side-toside motions are made as the needle is being inserted and the limbus watched; movement of
the limbus means that the sclera has been engaged
When the needle has been advanced to the hub the plunger is slightly withdrawn and, if no
blood enters the syringe, the full 1 ml is slowly injected
A method utilizing a plastic intravenous cannula introduced via the same route following
conjunctival incision and limited blunt dissection has been described

IMMUNOMODULATORY THERAPY FOR
NON-INFECTIOUS UVEITIS

Introduction

оАЯis therapy plays an important role in modifying the immune
response and should be supervised by a multidisciplinary team
that includes an ocular inоАЮammatory disease specialist. Shared
decisions should be made on when to start treatment and subsequently, on whether to modify or withdraw treatment. оАЯe
speciоАЭc medication to be used should be based on the type of
uveitis and the known eоАЪcacy and safety proоАЭles of the proposed agents. Before starting treatment, careful informed consent should be taken, detailing the advantages and potential side
eоАЫects of the medication and emphasizing the need for regular
follow-up. Active infections (especially tuberculosis) should be
excluded and haematological, renal and hepatic function should
be checked. оАЯe aim of treatment is to obtain steroid-free remission of the disease.

Indications

{ оАЯese agents take time to suppress inоАЮammation (2 weeks
to 3 months). Topical and systemic steroids may be needed
initially to control acute inоАЮammation.
оАБ An approach to severe uveitis when systemic immunosuppression is needed (based on the underlying immunology and
aligning with clinical experience) is as follows:

{ Birdshot retinochoroidopathy.

{ Serpiginous choroidopathy.

{ Multifocal choroiditis and panuveitis.

{ Juvenile inоАЮammatory arthritis.
оАБ As an alternative to steroids

{ оАЯerapeutic: inadequate response to topical and periocular
steroids or to an oral dose >0.5 mg/kg/day prednisolone;
intolerance of systemic steroids or as a steroid-sparing
measure.

{ Ocular: acute disease that is sight-threatening or chronic
and persistent, exudative retinal detachment or disease
involving the macula.

Side eоАЯects of medication

оАБ оАЯese need to be discussed with the patient and regular monitoring should be undertaken.
оАБ In the doses used, the commonly prescribed immunosuppressives (steroids, azathioprine, methotrexate, mycophenolate
mofetil, cyclosporin A) do not increase cancer mortality. оАЯe
use of azathioprine monotherapy or anti-tumour necrosis factor (TNF) monotherapy is associated with a small but statistically signiоАЭcant risk of lymphoma. оАЯe risk is higher with
combination treatment.
оАБ As a general rule most of these medications are well tolerated,
but side eоАЫects like headache, tiredness, gastrointestinal upset,
bone marrow suppression and increased risk of opportunistic
infections have been reported.
оАБ Individual agents have complications that are speciоАЭc to that
particular medication.

Classes of medication

оАБ Antimetabolites

{ Methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil and azathioprine
inhibit purine metabolism.
оАБ Inhibitors of calcineurin signalling in T cells

{ Cyclosporin A, tacrolimus, voclosporin and sirolimus.
оАБ Alkylating agents

  - Cyclophosphamide and chlorambucil.
оАБ Biopharmacologicals

{ оАЯis is a rapidly changing оАЭeld and new agents are constantly evolving that are eоАЫective in suppressing ocular
inоАЮammation.

{ InоАЮiximab, adalimumab, etanercept, golimumab and certolizumab are anti-TNF ќ± monoclonal antibodies that are
registered for use in non-infectious uveitis.

{ Tocilizumab is a recombinant monoclonal antibody that
binds to interleukin-6 receptors thereby inhibiting the
activity of pro-inоАЮammatory cytokines.

оАБ Immediate treatment

{ Systemic lupus erythematosus with retinal vasculitis.

{ Beh√Іet disease with retinal vasculitis.
оАБ Early treatment should be considered for:

{ Sympathetic ophthalmia.

{ VogtвАУKoyanagiвАУHarada syndrome.

{ Steroids and antimetabolites are expected to work for all
forms of severe uveitis.

442 Uveitis in Spondyloarthropathies

{ In T-cell critical uveitis (e.g. VKH, birdshot retinochoroidopathy) consider the use of calcineurin inhibitors.

{ In neutrophil and macrophage critical uveitis (e.g.
Beh√Іet disease, sarcoidosis) consider the use of anti-TNF
agents.

{ In B-cell critical inоАЮammation (e.g. scleritis, particularly
ANCA+) treat with rituximab.

UVEITIS IN SPONDYLOARTHROPATHIES

оАЯe spondyloarthropathies are a group of disorders featuring
HLA-B27 positivity and enthesitis as common factors. оАЯere is
oоАЬen a family history of one or more of the group, which comprises ankylosing spondylitis, undiоАЫerentiated spondyloarthropathy, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis (Reiter syndrome) and
spondyloarthropathy with inоАЮammatory bowel disease (ulcerative
colitis and Crohn disease). оАЯey are oоАЬen referred to as seronegative spondyloarthropathies, in that rheumatoid factor is not present and the pathophysiological basis diоАЫers. оАЯe American Uveitis
Society has recently endorsed the use of biological blockers such as
inоАЮiximab for second-line systemic immunosuppression in visionthreatening chronic uveitis.

TIP Monoclonal antibody treatment (adalimumab/inоАЭiximab)
has improved the prognosis in patients with vision-threatening
chronic uveitis.

Ankylosing spondylitis

Introduction

Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is characterized by inоАЮammation,
calciоАЭcation and оАЭnally ossiоАЭcation of ligaments and capsules of
joints with resultant bony ankylosis of the axial skeleton. It more
commonly aоАЫects males, of whom 90% are HLA-B27-positive.

Systemic features

оАБ Presentation is commonly in the third to fourth decades with the
insidious onset of pain and stiоАЫness in the lower back or buttocks.
оАБ Spondyloarthritis causes progressive limitation of spinal
movements and eventually the spine may become оАЭxed in оАЮexion (Fig. 12.10A). Spinal stenosis and fractures may occur.
оАБ Enthesitis is characterized clinically by inоАЮammation and pain
at ligamentous attachments to bone.
оАБ Cardiac complications are rare.
оАБ Radiology of the sacroiliac joints shows juxta-articular osteoporosis in the early stages, followed by sclerosis and bony
obliteration of the joint (Fig. 12.10B). CalciоАЭcation of spinal
ligaments gives rise to a вАШbamboo spineвАЩ. Radiological changes
oоАЬen predate clinical symptoms.

Ocular features

оАБ AAU is by far the most common ocular association and occurs
in about 25% of patients with AS. Approximately 25% of males
with AAU will have AS. Either eye is frequently aоАЫected at different times but bilateral simultaneous involvement is rare.
оАЯere is oоАЬen no correlation between the severity and activity of eye and joint involvement. Chronicity occurs in a few
patients. HLA-B27-positive AS patients tend to have worse
disease across a range of parameters, including earlier onset
and greater intensity of inоАЮammation with an increased frequency of complications.
оАБ Other ocular features include scleritis, episcleritis, keratitis
and mechanical ptosis.

Reactive arthritis

Introduction

Reactive arthritis (ReA, also known as Reitersyndrome) is characterized by a triad of non-speciоАЭc urethritis, conjunctivitis and arthritis.

A B

Fig. 12.10 Ankylosing spondylitis. оАЬAоАЭ Fixed оАЭexion deformity of the cervical spine; оАЬBоАЭ sclerosis
and bony obliteration of the sacroiliac joints (arrow). (Courtesy of P Beckwith вАУ оАЯg. A.)

Around 75% of patients are positive for HLA-B27. A range of infective agents can trigger the syndrome, which develops in 1вАУ3% of
men aоАЬer non-speciоАЭc urethritis and around 4% of individuals aоАЬer
enteric infections caused by a range of organisms including Shigella,
Salmonella and Campylobacter. Chlamydia trachomatis is the most
common cause of reactive arthritis following urethritis. Chlamydia
pneumoniae respiratory infection and others may also precede ReA.

TIP Patients with uveitis secondary to the various
spondyloarthropathies are often HLA-B27-positive.

Systemic features

оАБ Presentation is with the acute onset of malaise, with fever and
dysuria 1вАУ4 weeks aоАЬer a linked infection in a patient aged
between 20 and 40, with arthritis that may be preceded by conjunctivitis. A variety of other features may be present, though
not always the deоАЭning triad.
оАБ Peripheral oligoarthritis is acute, asymmetrical and migratory. Two to four joints tend to be involved, most commonly
the knees, ankles and toes.
оАБ Spondyloarthritis aоАЫects about 50% of patients, manifesting
with low back pain. оАЯis sometimes becomes chronic.
оАБ Enthesitis manifests with plantar fasciitis, Achilles tenosynovitis, bursitis and calcaneal periostitis. Reactive bone formation in the latter may result in a calcaneal spur.
оАБ Mucocutaneous lesions include painless mouth ulceration,
circinate balanitis and keratoderma blennorrhagica вАУ skin
lesions resembling psoriasis вАУ involving the palms and soles
(Fig. 12.11A).
оАБ Genitourinary involvement includes cervicitis, prostatitis
and epididymitis.
оАБ Aortitis occurs in 1вАУ2%.

Ocular features

оАЯe eye is involved in 50% of cases with a urogenital inciting infection and 75% of enteric ReA syndrome.

CHAPTER

Uveitis 443
#### 12

оАБ Conjunctivitis is very common and classically follows urethritis, but precedes arthritis. оАЯe inоАЮammation is usually
mild, bilateral and mucopurulent with a papillary and/or follicular reaction (Fig. 12.11B). Spontaneous resolution occurs
within 7вАУ10 days and treatment is not required. Some patients
develop peripheral corneal inоАЭltrates.
оАБ AAU occurs in 20%.
оАБ Episcleritis sometimes occurs.

Treatment

оАБ оАЮe main goal is to identify the causative organism and treat
with appropriate antibiotics if the organism is still present.
оАБ Non-speciоАЭc urethritis should be treated with oral
tetracycline.
оАБ Steroids and immunosuppressants may be needed for severe
symptoms that do not respond to simple treatment.

Psoriatic arthritis

Introduction

Up to 40% of patients with psoriasis develop arthritis. оАЯe arthritis
is more common in White populations than other racial groups
and aоАЫects both sexes equally. оАЯere is a оАЭrst-degree family history
in 40% or more and many genetic markers have been identiоАЭed.

Systemic features

оАБ Presentation of psoriatic arthritis is usually in middle age вАУ
later than skin features.
оАБ Skin. оАЯere are multiple types of psoriasis. Plaque-type, the
most common form, is characterized by well-demarcated
raised silvery inоАЮamed plaques (Fig. 12.12A) on the scalp,
trunk, arms and legs. Psoriatic erythroderma features widespread exfoliative skin changes with associated inоАЮammation
and pustular psoriasis inоАЮamed but non-infectious pustules
limited or generalized in distribution.
оАБ Nail changes (dystrophy) include pitting, transverse depression and onycholysis (Fig. 12.12B).

A B

Fig. 12.11 Reiter syndrome. оАЬAоАЭ Keratoderma blennorrhagica in reactive arthritis; оАЬBоАЭ acute conjunctivitis.

444 Fuchs Uveitis Syndrome

A B

Fig. 12.12 Psoriasis. оАЬAоАЭ Skin plaques; оАЬBоАЭ arthritis and severe nail dystrophy.

оАБ Arthritis is typically asymmetrical and involves the distal
interphalangeal joints (sausage digits). Some patients develop
enthesitis.

Ocular features

AAU occurs in approximately 7%. Conjunctivitis, marginal corneal inоАЭltrates and secondary Sj√ґgren syndrome may occur but are
uncommon.

FUCHS UVEITIS SYNDROME

Introduction

Fuchs uveitis syndrome (FUS), also known as Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis or cyclitis (FHC), is a chronic nongranulomatous condition diagnosed at an average of 40 years of
age. оАЯere is no gender or racial predilection. оАЯe cause is uncertain, but there is evidence that implicates the rubella virus. Signs
in toxoplasmosis can be similar and T. gondii has also been suspected as a cause. It is possible that most of the anterior chamber
(AC) activity is due to bloodвАУaqueous barrier breakdown rather
than inоАЮammation.

Clinical features

Detection is oоАЬen incidental. Findings are usually unilateral
(90вАУ95%).
оАБ Symptoms. Gradual blurring due to cataract is a common presentation, as are persistent оАЮoaters. Heterochromia (see next)
may be noted.
оАБ Heterochromia iridis (Table 12.7) is demonstrated most
eоАЫectively in daylight and in most patients the aоАЫected eye
is hypochromic (Fig. 12.13A). Its quality is determined by
the relative degrees of atrophy of the stroma and posterior
pigment epithelium. It may be absent or subtle, particularly
in brown eyes. In blue eyes, stromal atrophy allows the posterior pigmented layer to show through and become the
dominant pigmentation, so that the eye sometimes becomes
hyperchromic.

оАБ Posterior synechiae (PS) are absent, except occasionally following cataract surgery.
оАБ AC shows faint оАЮare and usually only mild cellular activity,
though exacerbations can sometimes be marked. оАЯe eye is
virtually always white, even during exacerbations.
оАБ Keratic precipitates are characteristically stellate and greyвАУ
white in colour and are located diоАЫusely over the entire corneal
endothelium (Fig. 12.13B).
оАБ Iris nodules (30%) on the pupillary border (Koeppe вАУ Fig.
12.13C). Tiny crystals (Russell bodies) may be present on the
iris surface.
оАБ Iris atrophy is diоАЫuse with loss of crypts. оАЯe iris appears
smooth, with a prominent sphincter pupillae and sometimes
blood vessels (see Fig. 12.13C). Pigment epithelial atrophy can
be demonstrated by retroillumination (Fig. 12.13D).
оАБ Iris vessels. Fine irregular iris surface vessels are commonly
present.
оАБ Vitritis. Opacities in the anterior gel may be dense.
оАБ Posterior subcapsular cataract is extremely common (Fig.
12.13E).
оАБ Glaucoma is typically a later manifestation but is occasionally
present at diagnosis. It develops in up to 60% of involved eyes.
Several mechanisms are suspected.
оАБ Gonioscopy may show оАЭne radial angle vessels or small irregular peripheral anterior synechiae (Fig. 12.13F). оАЯe vessels
are typically the source of the haemorrhage sometimes seen on
incision into the AC (Amsler sign).
оАБ Fundus: peripheral choroiditis foci/scarring have been
reported. оАЯere may be an increased incidence of retinal dialysis. Macular oedema does not occur, except following surgery.

Investigation

Diagnosis is clinical, though investigation may be necessary to
exclude alternative conditions.

Treatment

оАБ Long-term monitoring is indicated to detect glaucoma and
other complications.

Other chronic anterior uveitides

оАБ Topical steroids may be used short-term for moderate/severe
exacerbations, but are generally not thought to be helpful in
the management of chronic low-grade inоАЮammation.
оАБ Cataract surgery carries an increased risk of complications.
Poor mydriasis and the possibility of postoperative hyphaema,
increased inоАЮammation, worsening of glaucoma control and
zonular dehiscence should be considered. Preoperative topical
or systemic steroids are used by some practitioners.
оАБ Glaucoma can be diоАЪcult to control medically. Surgical
options include a glaucoma drainage device or trabeculectomy
with mitomycin-C enhancement.
оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy may be considered for visually problematic vitreous opaciоАЭcation.

UVEITIS IN JUVENILE IDIOPATHIC
ARTHRITIS (JIA)

Introduction

JIA is by far the most common systemic disease associated with
childhood anterior uveitis with a prevalence of about 1:1000. It is
deоАЭned as arthritis of unknown aetiology that begins before the
age of 16 years and persists for at least 6 weeks. Up to 50% of children aоАЫected have persistently active disease aоАЬer 10 years. It may
result from exposure to one or more unknown antigens in genetically predisposed individuals.

TIP Immunomodulatory treatment should be considered early
in the course of JIA, which is the commonest systemic disease
associated with childhood anterior uveitis.

Clinical features

оАБ Arthritis. JIA is classiоАЭed by the International League of Associations for Rheumatology (ILAR, 2004 revision), according to
the extent of joint involvement during the оАЭrst 6 months.

{ Oligoarticular is the most common form. Four or fewer
joints are involved, the knees most commonly, followed by
the ankles and wrists (Fig. 12.14A). Girls are aоАЫected оАЭve

times as oоАЬen as boys, with a peak age of onset around 2
years. Some patients subsequently develop polyarthritis.
About 75% of children are ANA-positive, a strong risk factor for uveitis, which is common, aоАЫecting about 20% of
children in this group.

{ Polyarticular (rheumatoid factor-negative) aоАЫects оАЭve or
more joints, typically both small and large joints symmetrically. оАЯe female:male ratio is about 3:1. оАЯe disease may commence at any age throughout childhood.
Systemic features such as fever and rash may occur but
are milder than in the systemic onset form (see below).
About 40% of children are ANA-positive. Uveitis occurs
in 5вАУ10% of cases.

{ Polyarticular (rheumatoid factor-positive) again aоАЫects
оАЭve or more joints and may resemble adult rheumatoid
arthritis. оАЯere is a very low risk of uveitis.

{ Systemic, also known as Still (StillвАЩs) disease. Systemic features such as fever, episodic erythematous maculopapular
rash, lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly may precede arthritis. оАЯe disease occurs with equal frequency in
boys and girls and may occur at any age throughout childhood. оАЯe majority are negative for ANA and uveitis is
rare.

{ Enthesitis-related (inоАЮammation of the connective tissue
where tendons/ligaments attach to bone), psoriatic and
undiоАЫerentiated are three other forms under the ILAR
classiоАЭcation. оАЯe оАЭrst two have a relatively high risk of
uveitis, but risk in the latter is usually low.
оАБ Anterior uveitis is a key cause of morbidity in JIA. It is particularly common in oligoarticular JIA and relatively frequent in
several other types. Progression to blindness has been high in
the past, but shows a declining trend in recent years associated
with improved screening and management. Arthritis usually
antedates the diagnosis of uveitis.

{ Presentation. оАЯe uveitis of JIA is particularly dangerous
because it is invariably asymptomatic and must generally
be detected by screening with slit lamp examination. Even
during acute exacerbations with +4 aqueous cells, it is rare
for patients to complain, although a few report an increase
in vitreous оАЮoaters. OоАЬen uveitis may not be suspected
until the parents recognize complications such as strabismus, or an abnormal appearance of the eyeball due to band
keratopathy or cataract.

{ Injection is usually absent even in the presence of severe
uveitis.

{ InоАЮammation is chronic and non-granulomatous. Both
eyes are aоАЫected in 70% and there is symmetrical severity of inоАЮammation. During acute exacerbations, the entire
endothelium shows вАШdustingвАЩ by many hundreds of cells,
but hypopyon is absent.

{ PS are common in longstanding undetected cases.

{ Band keratopathy and cataract (Fig. 12.14B) are common
in severe cases.

{ Other serious complications include glaucoma (common),
amblyopia, maculopathy (cystoid macular oedema, epiretinal membrane), cyclitic membrane and phthisis.

A B

Fig. 12.14 Juvenile idiopathic arthritis. оАЬAоАЭ InоАЭammation of the knee in a child; оАЬBоАЭ band keratopathy, posterior synechiae and mature cataract in chronic anterior uveitis.

{ Prognosis. In about 10% the uveitis is mild, with never
more than +1 aqueous cells and persists for less than 12
months. About 15% of patients have one attack lasting
less than 4 months, the severity of inоАЮammation varying
from +2 to +4 aqueous cells. In 50% of cases, the uveitis is moderate to severe and persists for more than 4
months and in 25%, the uveitis is very severe, lasts for
several years and responds poorly to conventional treatment. оАЯe presence of complications at initial examination appears to be an important risk factor for the
development of subsequent complications, regardless of
therapy. Good visual outcomes can be achieved by early
case recognition and rapid referral to a specialist for
immunomodulatory therapy.

Investigation

оАБ Systemic diagnosis and management should be performed
by a physician familiar with the management of JIA, typically a
paediatric rheumatologist.
оАБ Antinuclear antibody (ANA). Positivity denotes an increased
risk of uveitis.
оАБ HLA-B27 testing is useful in diоАЫerential diagnosis (see above)
and if present may indicate an increased risk of uveitis.
оАБ Rheumatoid factor is also useful in diоАЫerential diagnosis.
оАБ Screening. оАЯere has been a shiоАЬ in recommendation towards
long-term 3- to 4-monthly review intervals in all higher-risk
categories. Review should continue in most cases until the age
of 12 years.

{ Initial examination within 6 weeks of оАЭrst diagnosis of
JIA. Delayed early examination is an important cause of
morbidity.

{ Visual symptoms or a suspicion of ocular signs (synechiae,
cataract, band keratopathy) should lead to urgent ophthalmological referral and slit lamp examination within a
week.

{ Initial 2-monthly examinations for 6 months may be considered for all newly diagnosed oligoarticular, psoriatic,

{ Idiopathic juvenile chronic iridocyclitis: otherwise healthy
patients with juvenile CAU.

{ Other types of juvenile arthritis and uveitis including
juvenile reactive arthritis, juvenile inоАЮammatory bowel
disease-associated arthritis.

{ Juvenile sarcoidosis is rare. Pulmonary involvement is
less common than in adults, may be granulomatous and
involve the posterior segment.

{ Lyme disease usually presents with intermediate uveitis in
conjunction with signiоАЭcant anterior uveitis.

polyarticular and enthesitis-related patients, regardless of
ANA status, followed by 3- to 4-monthly intervals.

{ Polyarticular: every 3вАУ4 months, but some guidelines
reduce the interval to 6-monthly aоАЬer a number of years.
Higher-risk factors that might be considered in deciding
whether to alter the interval include the presence of ANA,
onset before 7 years of age and female gender.

{ Systemic onset and polyarticular RF-positive patients:
most authorities recommend at least an initial screening examination, with some guidelines suggesting annual
review.

{ Missed appointments must be eоАЫectively detected and
patients rebooked.

{ Information for parents should include an emphasis on the
importance of compliance with screening, as well as the
need to seek urgent advice should there be any cause for
concern such as visual symptoms, ocular redness or clouding, or abnormal pupils.

{ Self-monitoring. At eventual discharge from screening,
patients should be warned to self-monitor by checking the
monocular vision at least once a week because the risk of
uveitis has not entirely disappeared by this age. оАЯey should
also attend an optometrist annually for an eye examination. Selected patients such as those with learning diоАЪculties may require ongoing ophthalmological screening.
оАБ DiоАЬerential diagnosis: Particular considerations in children
include:

448 Uveitis in Bowel Disease

{ Intermediate uveitis: 20% of all cases of paediatric uveitis.

{ Neonatal-onset multisystem inоАЮammatory disease is a
rare, idiopathic, chronic relapsing disease that predominantly involves the skin, joints and the central nervous
system. About 50% of children develop recurrent anterior
uveitis. оАЯe absence of PS and no tendency to glaucoma
and cataract formation are characteristic.

{ Masquerade syndromes such as anterior segment involvement by retinoblastoma.

{ Familial juvenile systemic granulomatosis (Blau syndrome) is a rare autosomal dominant disorder characterized by childhood onset of granulomatous disease of skin,
eyes (panuveitis and multifocal choroiditis) and joints.

Treatment

оАЯe aim of treatment should be the suppression of all active inоАЮammation. оАЯese children should be referred as soon as possible to a
uveitis specialist.
оАБ Topical steroids are initially eоАЫective in 80% of cases.
оАБ Mydriatic agents can be used to prevent synechiae formation.
A relatively short-acting preparation such as cyclopentolate
should be prescribed and discontinued as early as possible,
particularly in monocular treatment of younger children susceptible to the development of amblyopia.
оАБ Periocular steroids.
оАБ Oral steroids.
оАБ NSAIDs.
оАБ Systemic immunosuppressive agents (e.g. methotrexate, infliximab and adalimumab) should be considered at an early
stage, as this has improved the overall visual prognosis in
recent years. оАЯese children are usually under the management
of a uveitis specialist, typically working in collaboration with a
paediatric rheumatologist. Etanercept should be avoided as it
can result in severe worsening of inоАЮammation. Tocilizumab,
an interleukin-6 blocking agent, has recently been shown to
be eоАЫective in the treatment of some children with refractory
uveitis secondary to JIA.

оАБ Secondary glaucoma. If topical medication does not control the IOP, surgery may be needed. оАЯe long-term results
of trabeculectomy with mitomycin C in children with JIA
are improved if they are using systemic anti-TNF medication. Baerveldt implantation also results in good long-term
IOP control, but is associated with a 5% risk of hypotony
maculopathy.
оАБ Cataract extraction is oоАЬen needed. Primary IOL implantation was a controversial issue in the past, but a low complication rate with good visual results has been recently reported
with this approach in children on immunomodulatory treatment. Posterior capsular thickening requiring treatment
occurs in approximately half.

UVEITIS IN BOWEL DISEASE
Ulcerative colitis
Introduction

Ulcerative colitis (UC) is an idiopathic chronic relapsing inоАЮammatory disease, involving the rectum and extending proximally to
involve part or all of the large intestine. оАЯe disease is characterized by contiguous surface ulceration of the bowel mucosa with the
development of crypt abscesses and pseudopolyps (Fig. 12.15A).
Longstanding disease carries an increased risk of carcinoma of
the colon. A genetic predisposition is thought to be important
as inоАЮammatory bowel disease is more common in patients with
other autoimmune diseases such as ankylosing spondylitis, psoriasis and multiple sclerosis.

Systemic features

оАБ Presentation is in the second to third decades with bloody
diarrhoea, lower abdominal cramps, urgency and tenesmus.
Constitutional symptoms include tiredness, weight loss, malaise and fever.
оАБ Cutaneous lesions include oral aphthous ulceration, erythema
nodosum and pyoderma gangrenosum (Fig. 12.15B).

A B

Fig. 12.15 Ulcerative colitis. оАЬAоАЭ Barium enema showing pseudopolyposis, lack of haustral
markings and straightening of the ascending colon; оАЬBоАЭ pyoderma gangrenosum.

оАБ Arthritis is typically asymmetrical and involves large joints
of the legs. Sacroiliitis and ankylosing spondylitis (AS) may
develop in HLA-B27-positive patients.
оАБ Hepatic disease may be in the form of autoimmune hepatitis,
sclerosing cholangitis and cholangiocarcinoma.
оАБ оАЮrombosis may aоАЫect both arteries and veins.

Ocular features

оАБ AAU occurs in about 5% and may coincide with exacerbations
of colitis. As expected, uveitis is more common in patients with
associated arthritis, AS and HLA-B27 positivity.
оАБ Other ocular features: conjunctivitis, episcleritis and scleritis
may all be more common than in the general population.

Crohn disease

Introduction

Crohn disease (CD) is an idiopathic chronic relapsing disease characterized by multifocal full-thickness granulomatous inоАЮammation
of the intestinal wall. It most frequently involves the terminal ileum
and colon but in contrast to UC any area of the gastrointestinal tract,
including the mouth, may be aоАЫected. оАЯere is strong evidence for
a genetic aetiological component such as mutations in the CARD15
(previously NOD2) gene. Infective agents almost certainly play a role.

Systemic features

оАБ Presentation is typically in the secondвАУthird decades with
abdominal pain and diarrhoea. Weight loss, fever, vomiting, oral aphthous ulceration and perirectal lesions such as
abscesses and оАЭstulae may occur.
оАБ Cutaneous lesions include erythema nodosum and pyoderma
gangrenosum (see Fig. 12.15B).
оАБ Anaemia is common.
оАБ Hepatic disease may occur.
оАБ Skeletal features include оАЭnger clubbing, acute peripheral
arthritis, sacroiliitis and AS (especially if HLA-B27-positive).

Ocular features

Approximately 12% of patients with CD experience some form of
ocular inоАЮammation. AAU occurs in 3%. Other manifestations
include episcleritis, scleritis, follicular conjunctivitis, myositis and
optic neuritis (see Ch. 9).

Whipple disease

Introduction

Whipple disease (intestinal lipodystrophy) is a rare chronic gastrointestinal inоАЮammatory condition caused by infection with the
bacterium Tropheryma whipplei. It occurs mainly in White middleaged men and when diagnosed (duodenal biopsy; DNA detection
in blood, ocular and other оАЮuids) can be cured with antibiotics.

Systemic features

InоАЮammatory bowel disease with malabsorption. Joint, cardiac
and central nervous system (CNS) involvement is common.

CHAPTER

Uveitis 449
#### 12

Ocular features

оАБ Uveitis. Keratitis, anterior uveitis, vitritis, retinitis with retinal haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots and potentially vascular
occlusion and multifocal choroiditis.
оАБ Neuro-ophthalmic manifestations can be varied, e.g. gaze
palsy, nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia, papilloedema and optic
atrophy. Oculomasticatory myorhythmia is characteristic.

UVEITIS IN RENAL DISEASE
Tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis
Introduction

Tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis (TINU) is an uncommon
disorder of immune origin characterized by a combination of
acute tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis. It typically occurs in
adolescent girls. Renal disease usually precedes uveitis.

Systemic features

Presentation is with constitutional symptoms, proteinuria, anaemia, hypertension and renal failure. оАЯe response to systemic steroid therapy is good and renal function usually returns to normal
within a few months without complication.

Ocular features

оАБ Bilateral non-granulomatous (occasionally granulomatous)
anterior uveitis that usually responds well to topical steroids.
Disc and macular oedema may occur. Many cases are relapsing and some require systemic steroids or immunosuppressive
therapy.
оАБ Intermediate, posterior or panuveitis may occur.

TIP In a patient with ocular and renal inоАЭammation, consider
the following in the diоАЯerential diagnosis: sarcoidosis,
tuberculosis, SLE, tubulointerstitial nephritis and IgA
nephropathy.

IgA nephropathy

IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) is a relatively common kidney
disease in which immunoglobulin A is deposited in the glomerular mesangium. Presentation is usually at age 16вАУ35 with recurrent
haematuria, oоАЬen associated with an upper respiratory tract infection, but may be asymptomatic. AAU and other ocular inоАЮammatory phenomena may occur but are uncommon.

INTERMEDIATE UVEITIS

Introduction

Intermediate uveitis (IU) is a chronic, relapsing disease of insidious
onset in which the vitreous is the primary site of inоАЮammation. It
incorporates the following entities: pars planitis, posterior cyclitis
and hyalitis. оАЯe diagnosis is made clinically. IU may be idiopathic
(at least half) or associated with a systemic disease. Pars planitis

450 Intermediate Uveitis

(PP) is the term used for a subset of IU in which there is snowbanking and/or snowball formation, but only if the inоАЮammation
is idiopathic вАУ that is, with no identiоАЭable underlying infection or
systemic disease вАУ otherwise the term intermediate uveitis is used.
IU accounts for up to 15% of all uveitis cases and about 20% of paediatric uveitis. A minority of patients have a benign course, with
spontaneous resolution within several years. In other patients the
disease is more severe and prolonged with episodic exacerbations.
IU associated with systemic disease has a variable course.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Presentation is with the insidious onset of blurred
vision, oоАЬen accompanied by vitreous оАЮoaters. оАЯere is usually
no pain or redness. оАЯough initial symptoms are oоАЬen unilateral, objective оАЭndings are typically present asymmetrically in
both eyes.
оАБ Visual acuity is variably aоАЫected depending on inоАЮammatory
activity and complications, particularly CMO. оАЯe disease may
last as long as 15 years and preservation of vision will depend
largely on control of macular disease. In follow-up of up to 4
years, 75% of patients maintain a visual acuity of 6/12 or better.
оАБ Anterior uveitis. In PP there may be a few cells and small scattered KP which occasionally have an inferior linear distribution. In other forms of IU, anterior uveitis and its associated
оАЭndings, such as PS, can be more prominent, especially in children and in sarcoidosis and Lyme disease.
оАБ Vitreous. Vitreous cells with anterior predominance are universal. Vitreous condensation and haze (Table 12.8) is found in
severe cases (Fig. 12.16A). Snowballs are whitish focal collections of inоАЮammatory cells and exudate, usually most numerous in the inferior vitreous (Fig. 12.16B).
оАБ Peripheral periphlebitis (Fig. 12.16C) is common, particularly in multiple sclerosis. Careful examination of a normal
fellow eye in apparently unilateral disease may reveal mild vascular sheathing.
оАБ Snowbanking (Fig. 12.16D) is characterized by a greyвАУwhite
оАЭbrovascular and/or exudative plaque that may occur in any or
all quadrants, but is most frequently found inferiorly.
оАБ Neovascularization may occur, particularly in the retinal
periphery (oоАЬen associated with snowbanks) and on the optic
nerve head. оАЯe latter usually resolves when activity is controlled. оАЯiscan sometimes lead to vitreous haemorrhage, retinal
detachment and cyclitic membrane formation. Focal peripheral
retinal vasoproliferative tumours (see Ch. 20) are uncommon.
Vitreous haemorrhage is more common in children.

Table 12.8 Grading of Vitreous Haze

Haze severity Grading

Good view of nerve оАЮbre layer 0
(NFL)

NFL

Disc and vessels hazy +2

Disc not visible +4

оАБ Optic disc swelling is common, especially in younger patients.
оАБ CMO occurs in up to half of patients and is the major cause of
impaired visual acuity.
оАБ Macular epiretinal membrane formation is common.
оАБ Cataract can be caused by steroid treatment or by the inоАЮammation itself.
оАБ Glaucoma may occur in eyes with prolonged inоАЮammation,
particularly if receiving long-term steroid therapy.
оАБ Retinal detachment is generally uncommon, but as it can
progress to hypotony and phthisis in advanced cases, prevention should be a major goal of management. оАЯe aetiology may
be tractional, rhegmatogenous and occasionally exudative.
Retinoschisis has also been described.

TIP An insidious onset of blurred vision accompanied by
vitreous оАЭoaters without pain or redness suggests a possible
diagnosis of intermediate uveitis.

Investigation

InоАЮammatory markers such as ESR and/or CRP should be checked,
together with a complete blood count, as they may raise suspicion
of a systemic inоАЮammatory process. OCT is key to excluding subtle
CMO and FA will help in assessing severity. Other investigations
are targeted at the exclusion of an underlying cause as below.
оАБ Multiple sclerosis. Enquiry should be made in all patients
about neurological symptoms, noting that IU may precede
other symptoms of demyelination. MS should be suspected in
patients aged 20вАУ50 and is twice as common in women. Granulomatous AAU may occur. Cranial MRI imaging should be
performed if any suspicion is raised.
оАБ Sarcoidosis. Sarcoid-associated IU is relatively uncommon
and as with MS may antedate the onset of systemic disease. оАЯe
presence of associated granulomatous anterior uveitis should
arouse suspicion. A serum ACE level and chest X-ray should
be performed in all adult patients.
оАБ Lyme disease-associated IU is oоАЬen associated with severe
anterior uveitis. Serology should be performed in individuals
from an endemic area.
оАБ Syphilis serology вАУ treponemal and cardiolipin antibody tests

  - should be performed.
оАБ Tuberculosis is an uncommon association that may give respiratory symptoms and be demonstrated on chest X-ray. Tuberculin
skin and/or blood (e.g. QuantiFERON) testing should be performed prior to steroid treatment in the presence of any suspicion.
оАБ Other conditions that may give vitritis mimicking IU include
FUS, intraocular lymphoma (older patients), Toxocara granuloma, Whipple disease, endogenous Candida endophthalmitis
(risk factors such as intravenous drug use) and toxoplasmosis.
оАЯese will commonly be suspected on the basis of the history
and speciоАЭc clinical оАЭndings.

Treatment

An identiоАЭed infection or other underlying disease should be
treated speciоАЭcally, supplemented by anti-inоАЮammatory measures.
Many authorities aim to abolish all active inоАЮammation regardless

A B

C D

Fig. 12.16 Intermediate uveitis. оАЬAоАЭ Vitreous inоАЭammatory activity showing vitreous condensation and haze visible in the slit beam (arrow); оАЬBоАЭ snowballs (arrow); (CоАЭ peripheral periphlebitis
and snowballs; оАЬDоАЭ inferior snowbanking and snowballs. (Courtesy of CL Schepens, ME Hartnett and T Hirose, from SchepensвАЩ Retinal Detachment and Allied Diseases, ButterworthHeinemann 2000 вАУ оАЯg. C.)

of whether vision has been aоАЫected. Factors such as the presence
of peripheral neovascularization may prompt earlier intervention.
оАБ Topical steroids do not reach the posterior segment in high
concentrations and therefore have a limited role. оАЯis medication is used mainly to treat any anterior segment inоАЮammation. It has been proposed that in mild IU a course of frequent
topical steroid of a few weeksвАЩ duration may exert some beneоАЭt
and identify individuals at high risk of IOP elevation without
committing to the extended action of depot injection.
оАБ Regional steroid injection. Orbital оАЮoor or posterior subTenon injection as described for anterior uveitis. Depending
on severity, injection is performed 4вАУ6 times at intervals of 2вАУ4
weeks, accompanied by IOP monitoring.
оАБ NSAID. If inоАЮammation persists aоАЬer regional injection, an
agent such as naproxen 500 mg twice daily can be commenced.
оАБ Cryotherapy (double freezeвАУthaw) to the pars plana and
retinal periphery under peribulbar anaesthesia can be highly
eоАЫective if inоАЮammation is steroid-resistant and may be

appropriate prior to systemic steroids. It can be associated
with transiently increased vitritis and other complications
including retinal detachment, cataract, vitreous and AC haemorrhage, epiretinal membrane formation and hypotony. A
repeat application may be needed aоАЬer several months. Some
authorities reserve cryotherapy for peripheral neovascularization with haemorrhage.
оАБ Peripheral retinal laser adjacent to snowbanking and/or to
ischaemic areas on FA is an alternative to cryotherapy and may
be as eоАЫective with a lower rate of complications.
оАБ Intraocular steroid. Intravitreal triamcinolone has shown
beneоАЭt, but the eоАЫect is of relatively short duration. Slowrelease implants have demonstrated promising results.
оАБ Systemic steroids. оАЯis modality is preferred over regional
steroid by some practitioners if symptomatic inоАЮammation
is bilateral. Others prefer to avoid systemic steroids in most
cases, proceeding directly to immunosuppressive chemotherapeutic agents. A large dose of 1вАУ2 mg/kg daily is commenced,

452 VogtвАУKoyanagiвАУHarada (VKH) Syndrome

tapered slowly over months according to response. It is essential to consider the contraindications and potential adverse
eоАЫects of steroids before prescribing.
оАБ Immunosuppressive agents. Mycophenolate, methotrexate,
tacrolimus, cyclosporine and others are alternatives in steroidresistant inоАЮammation or as steroid-sparing agents.
оАБ Other agents demonstrating eоАЪcacy in refractory disease
include interferon-beta (in MS-related IU) and the anti-TNF
inоАЮiximab.
оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy substantially reduces inоАЮammatory
intensity and recurrence, though the mechanism is imperfectly
understood. It is indicated in patients with tractional retinal
detachment, epiretinal membrane, refractory CMO, dense vitreous opacity, vitreous haemorrhage or substantial peripheral
neovascularization.
оАБ Cataract and glaucoma are managed medically and surgically
if necessary.

VOGTвАУKOYANAGIвАУHARADA (VKH)
SYNDROME

Introduction

VKH is an idiopathic multisystem autoimmune disease featuring inоАЮammation of melanocyte-containing tissues such as the
uvea, ear and meninges. VKH predominantly aоАЫects Hispanic,
Japanese and heavily pigmented individuals, usually in the 2nd
and 5th decade of life. It is associated with HLA-DR1 and HLADR4 across diоАЫerent racial groups. VKH is sometimes subdivided into (a) VogtвАУKoyanagi disease, characterized mainly by
skin changes and anterior uveitis and (b) Harada disease, in
which neurological features and exudative retinal detachments
predominate.

Clinical features

оАБ Prodromal phase lasting a few days: neurological (meningitis and rarely encephalopathy with cranial nerve paresis and

other focal lesions) and auditory manifestations (tinnitus,
vertigo and deafness). Cranial nerve palsies and optic neuritis
may occur.
оАБ Acute uveitic phase. Bilateral granulomatous anterior and
multifocal posterior uveitis with diоАЫuse choroidal inоАЭltration, DalenвАУFuchs nodules (see also sympathetic ophthalmia
below), vitritis, papillitis and exudative retinal detachments
(Fig. 12.17A and Fig. 12.18A). Ciliary eоАЫusion with irisвАУlens
diaphragm rotation can occur.
оАБ Convalescent phase follows several weeks later: localized alopecia, poliosis and vitiligo, depigmented fundus appearance
(вАШsunset glowвАЩ fundus вАУ Fig. 12.17B) and depigmented limbal lesions (Sugiura sign) in pigmented, especially Japanese,
patients.
оАБ Chronic recurrent phase is characterized by smouldering
anterior uveitis with exacerbations. Recurrent posterior uveitis
is much less common.
оАБ Diagnostic criteria for VKH are set out in Table 12.9. In complete VKH, criteria 1вАУ5 must be present, in incomplete VKH,
criteria 1вАУ3 and either 4 or 5 must be present, and in probable
VKH (isolated ocular disease), criteria 1вАУ3 must be present.
оАБ Ocular complications include macular neovascularization,
subretinal оАЭbrosis, preretinal and disc new vessels and vitreous
haemorrhage, cataract and glaucoma.
оАБ Prognosis is variable and is partly dependent on achieving
control in the early stages. Neurological and auditory manifestations tend to resolve but skin, lash and hair changes usually
persist.

Investigation

Systemic manifestations should be investigated and managed by an
appropriate specialist.
оАБ Lumbar puncture if diagnosis uncertain. CSF shows a transient lymphocytic pleocytosis and melanin-containing
macrophages.
оАБ FAF demonstrates hyperautoоАЮuorescence in areas of serous
detachment (Fig. 12.18B).

454 Sympathetic Ophthalmia

Table 12.9 ModiоАЯed Diagnostic Criteria for
VogtвАУKoyanagiвАУHarada Syndrome

1. Absence of a history of penetrating ocular trauma
2. Absence of other ocular disease entities
3. Bilateral uveitis
4. Neurological and auditory manifestations
5. Integumentary оАЮndings, not preceding onset of central
nervous system or ocular disease, such as alopecia,
poliosis and vitiligo

оАБ Ultrasonography shows diоАЫuse choroidal thickening and
excludes posterior scleritis. UBM can be used to demonstrate
a ciliary eоАЫusion.
оАБ FA of the acute phase shows multifocal hyperоАЮuorescent dots
at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE вАУ Fig.
12.18C) followed by subretinal pooling (Fig. 12.18D). оАЯe
chronic phase shows RPE window defects.
оАБ OCT allows quantiоАЭcation of subretinal оАЮuid. оАЯe subretinal
septae are typical (Fig. 12.18E and F).
оАБ ICGA during the acute phase of the disease shows regularly
distributed hypoоАЮuorescent spots, most of which remain
hypoоАЮuorescent during the late phase, when diоАЫuse hyperоАЮuorescence over the posterior pole is also shown. ICGA is useful
for monitoring.

Treatment

High-dose (1вАУ2 mg/kg daily) oral prednisolone, tapered over 3вАУ6
months. оАЯis may be preceded by intravenous methylprednisolone
pulse therapy (500вАУ1000 mg/day). Topical steroids and cycloplegics are used for anterior uveitis. Steroid-resistant patients may
require immunosuppressives. Biological blockers such as inоАЮiximab should be used early in the course of the disease if there is no
response to steroids.

SYMPATHETIC OPHTHALMIA

Introduction

Sympathetic ophthalmia (SO) is a bilateral granulomatous panuveitis occurring aоАЬer penetrating trauma where uveal prolapse may
have been a feature of the trauma. Less frequently the condition
occurs following intraocular surgery, usually multiple vitreoretinal
procedures. оАЯe condition has been associated with speciоАЭc HLA
haplotypes, which would suggest that in some patients there is a
genetic predisposition. Presentation in trauma-induced cases is
between 2 weeks and 3 months aоАЬer initial injury in 65%. оАЯe incidence is probably 0.2вАУ0.5% aоАЬer injury and 0.01% following intraocular surgery. A recent meta-analysis of sympathetic ophthalmia
following open globe injury found an incidence of 0.19%. Histopathology shows a diоАЫuse lymphocytic inоАЭltration of the choroid.
Scattered aggregates of epithelioid cells are seen, many of which
contain оАЭne granules of melanin (Fig. 12.19A). DalenвАУFuchs nodules, which also occur in VogtвАУKoyanagiвАУHarada syndrome (see
above), are granulomas located between Bruch membrane and the
RPE (Fig. 12.19B).

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. оАЯere is a history of causative trauma. оАЯe exciting eye is frequently red and irritable. оАЯe sympathizing eye
develops irritation, blurred vision, photophobia and loss of
accommodation.
оАБ Anterior uveitis develops in both eyes, which may be mild or
severe and is usually granulomatous (Fig. 12.20A). оАЯe severity of inоАЮammation may be markedly asymmetrical.
оАБ Fundus: multifocal choroidal inоАЭltrates develop in the mid
periphery (Fig. 12.20B), with sub-RPE inоАЭltrates corresponding to DalenвАУFuchs nodules. Exudative retinal detachment,
vasculitis and optic disc swelling may all manifest. As inоАЮammation settles, residual chorioretinal scarring may confer a
вАШsunset glowвАЩ appearance similar to VKH (see Fig. 12.17B).
Extensive chorioretinal scarring may follow (Fig. 12.20C).
оАБ Systemic manifestations similar to those in VKH can occur
but are uncommon.
оАБ Prognosis depends on the severity and location of disease
and the response to treatment. With aggressive therapy 75%
of sympathizing eyes retain a visual acuity of better than 6/60.
Long-term follow-up is mandatory because relapses occur in
50% of cases and may be delayed for several years.

Investigation

оАБ OCT is useful for quantifying and monitoring change.
оАБ B-scan ultrasonography may demonstrate choroidal
thickening.
оАБ FA shows multiple foci of leakage at the level of the RPE,
with subretinal pooling in the presence of exudative retinal
detachment.
оАБ ICGA shows hypoоАЮuorescent spots in active disease, which
resolve with treatment.
оАБ FAF is helpful when following these patients, as it provides a
non-invasive and objective method of monitoring disease progression and response to treatment (Fig. 12.20D).
оАБ Ultrasound may show choroidal thickening and exudative
retinal detachment in the acute stage.

Treatment

оАБ Enucleation of a severely injured eye within 10вАУ14 days of
the injury has historically been considered eоАЫective in preventing or reducing the severity of SO and should be considered for an injured eye with a hopeless visual prognosis.
Evisceration is an acceptable option provided all uveal tissue
is removed.
оАБ Steroids are the basis of initial treatment. High-dose oral prednisolone is given for several months and gradually tapered
according to response. Initiation with intravenous methylprednisolone may be used in some cases. Supplementary topical steroids and cycloplegics may be given to target anterior
uveitis and peri- and intraocular steroids, including slowrelease intravitreal implants, may facilitate reduced systemic
treatment.
оАБ Immunomodulatory treatment should be considered early in
the course of the disease (see above).

456 Sarcoidosis

LENS-INDUCED UVEITIS

Introduction

Lens-induced or phacogenic uveitis results from an immune
response to lens proteins following exposure due to incomplete
cataract extraction, trauma or rarely capsular degeneration in a
mature cataract (Fig. 12.21A). оАЯe most commonly encountered
modern scenario is that of retained lens fragments following
phacoemulsiоАЭcation, either in the posterior segment aоАЬer posterior capsular rupture or zonular dehiscence, or an overlooked
piece of nucleus or soоАЬ lens matter settling in the AC (see also Ch.
10). оАЯis must be diоАЫerentiated from bacterial endophthalmitis,
which is usually more severe.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Variable pain, photophobia, redness and blurring,
usually with a history of recent (complicated or uncomplicated) cataract surgery and uncommonly of injury.
оАБ Anterior uveitis may be mild, moderate or severe (Fig.
12.21B).
оАБ Corneal oedema is common adjacent to an AC lens fragment.
оАБ IOP is frequently elevated.
оАБ Lens fragments may be visible in the anterior or posterior
segment.
оАБ Vitritis of variable severity is usually present if lens fragments
lie within the vitreous cavity.
оАБ Lens injury may be evident in cases associated with trauma.
оАБ Complications include CMO, glaucoma, epiretinal membrane
and, rarely, more severe sequelae such as retinal detachment
and cyclitic membrane.

Investigation

OCT, B-scan and biomicroscopic ultrasonography may be
indicated.

Treatment

Treatment involves steroids, the route and intensity dependent on
clinical circumstances, with surgical removal of all lens material
from the AC or via pars plana vitrectomy as required. Small lens
fragments in the posterior segment can oоАЬen be managed conservatively and will absorb slowly over months. Cycloplegia and
IOP-lowering treatment are commonly indicated. Penetrating or
(uncommonly) blunt ocular injury should be managed concomitantly as appropriate, including removal of the damaged lens.

SARCOIDOSIS

Introduction

Sarcoidosis is a chronic disorder of unknown cause, manifesting
with non-caseating granulomatous inоАЮammatory foci. It can aоАЫect
any organ system, but the lungs and lymph nodes are the most
commonly involved. It aоАЫects Black people signiоАЭcantly more
oоАЬen than White people by a margin of 10:1 and is more common
in colder climates. It is one of the most common systemic associations of uveitis.

Systemic features

оАБ Presentation. Respiratory symptoms (cough, shortness of
breath on exertion) and constitutional symptoms (malaise,
arthralgia) each occur in about 50% of patients. L√ґfgren syndrome is an acute presentation carrying a good prognosis,
characterized by the triad of erythema nodosum, bilateral
hilar lymphadenopathy (Fig. 12.22A) on chest X-ray and polyarthralgia, usually seen in women. A minority of patients are
asymptomatic (incidentally abnormal chest X-ray). Diagnosis
may be made as the result of investigation of extrapulmonary
inоАЮammation such as uveitis.
оАБ Lung disease ranges from mild parenchymal inоАЭltration to
severe pulmonary оАЭbrosis.

A B
