оАБ Skin lesions are seen in about 25% of patients and can include erythema nodosum (tender erythematous plaques typically involving
the shins вАУ Fig. 12.22B), lupus pernio (indurated violaceous lesions
involving exposed parts of the body such as the nose, cheeks, оАЭngers
and ears вАУ Fig. 12.22C) and granulomatous papules or macules.

CHAPTER

Uveitis 457
#### 12

оАБ Neurological disease is rare, but meningitis and cranial nerve
palsies may occur. Pituitary involvement can lead to hormonal
abnormalities.
оАБ Cardiac involvement is relatively uncommon (5% clinically),
but is critically important as it may lead to arrhythmia and
sudden death.
оАБ Lymphadenopathy. Enlargement of superоАЭcial nodes is sometimes the initial clinical manifestation.

Ocular features

Ocular inоАЮammation occurs in 25вАУ70% of sarcoid patients
depending on ethnicity. Granulomatous anterior uveitis is the
most common manifestation. Blindness can occur if not adequately managed. AAU typically aоАЫects patients with acute-onset
sarcoidosis. CAU, typically granulomatous, tends to aоАЫect older
patients with chronic pulmonary disease. оАЯe International Workshop on Ocular Sarcoidosis (IWOS), reporting in 2009, identiоАЭed
seven key signs in the diagnosis of intraocular sarcoidosis:
оАБ вАШMutton fatвАЩ KPs (Fig. 12.23A) and/or small granulomatous KPs and/or iris nodules (Koeppe and/or Busacca вАУ Fig.
12.23B).
оАБ Trabecular meshwork nodules (Fig. 12.23C) and/or tentshaped PAS.
оАБ Vitreous opacities: snowballs (Fig. 12.23D) and/or вАШstrings of
pearlsвАЩ.
оАБ Multiple chorioretinal peripheral lesions (active and/or atrophic). Choroidal lesions are uncommon and vary in appearance: multiple small pale-yellow inоАЭltrates, sometimes with
a punched-out appearance (Fig. 12.24A and B) are the commonest and are oоАЬen most numerous inferiorly. Multiple large
conоАЮuent inоАЭltrates are less common (Fig. 12.24C). Multifocal
choroiditis carries a poor visual prognosis even aоАЬer resolution of activity, as a result of secondary macular neovascularization associated with macular or peripapillary chorioretinal
scarring. Retinal granulomas may also occur, seen as discrete
small yellowвАУwhite lesions (Fig. 12.24D).
оАБ Nodular and/or segmental periphlebitis (¬± вАШcandle wax
drippingsвАЩ) and/or retinal macroaneurysm in an inоАЯamed
eye. Periphlebitis appears as yellowish or grey-white perivenous sheathing. Perivenous exudates referred to as вАШcandle
wax drippingsвАЩ (вАШen taches de bougieвАЩ) are typical of severe sarcoid periphlebitis (Fig. 12.25A). Occlusive periphlebitis (Fig.
12.25B) is uncommon, but peripheral retinal neovascularization may develop secondary to retinal capillary dropout. In
Black patients it may be mistaken for proliferative sickle-cell
retinopathy.
оАБ Optic disc nodule(s)/granuloma(s) and/or solitary choroidal nodule. Solitary choroidal nodules are less common than
multiple lesions in sarcoidosis. Focal optic nerve granulomas
do not usually aоАЫect vision (see Fig. 19.11). Persistent disc
oedema is a frequent оАЭnding in patients with retinal or vitreous involvement (Fig. 12.25C and D) and papilloedema due
to CNS involvement may occur in the absence of other ocular
manifestations
оАБ Other ocular manifestations include conjunctival nodules
resembling those of follicular conjunctivitis, lacrimal gland

458 Sarcoidosis

A B

C D

Fig. 12.23 Ocular sarcoidosis. оАЬAоАЭ Very large granulomatous вАШmutton fatвАЩ keratic precipitates;
оАЬBоАЭ large iris nodules; оАЬCоАЭ nodular involvement of the trabecular meshwork; оАЬDоАЭ snowballs.

inоАЭltration (Fig. 12.26A), dry eye, eyelid nodules (Fig. 12.26B),
orbital and scleral lesions. Complications are those typically
seen in idiopathic uveitis, including cataract, glaucoma, posterior and peripheral anterior synechiae, band keratopathy,
vitreous haemorrhage, maculopathy (CMO, epiretinal membrane, macular neovascularization), retinal detachment and
phthisis.

Investigation

In addition to the acquisition of histopathological evidence, IWOS
judged the following оАЭve investigations to be of signiоАЭcant value in
the diagnosis of ocular sarcoidosis in patients having a compatible
uveitis:
оАБ Negative tuberculin skin test in a BCG-vaccinated patient
or in a patient having had a positive tuberculin skin test previously. A tuberculin skin test is negative in most sarcoid
patients. A strongly positive reaction to one tuberculin unit
makes a diagnosis of sarcoidosis highly unlikely.
оАБ Elevated serum ACE levels and/or elevated serum lysozyme
as described for investigation of AAU.

оАБ Chest X-ray showing bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL).
Chest radiography is abnormal in 90%.
оАБ Abnormal liver enzyme tests.
оАБ Chest CT scan in patients with a negative chest X-ray result.
High-resolution CT scanning is of considerably greater value
than standard resolution imaging.
Other investigations are discussed below:
оАБ Fibreoptic bronchoscopy with biopsy. Histopathological
conоАЭrmation of sarcoidosis is almost always required before
starting treatment. оАЯe lung is a common site from which to
establish this in the presence of clinical or investigational evidence of pulmonary disease. However, a more easily accessible
superоАЭcial lesion should be chosen if available.
оАБ оАЮoracic endosonography (endobronchial or oesophageal)
with needle aspiration has been shown in a large trial to be a
more sensitive technique than bronchoscopic biopsy.
оАБ Miscellaneous biopsy sites include superоАЭcial lymph nodes
or skin lesions, conjunctival nodules and lacrimal glands (up
to 75% of enlarged glands are positive). If the eye is involved,
vitreous biopsy is very useful (e.g. CD4/CD8 ratio).

Fig. 12.24 Choroidal and retinal involvement in sarcoidosis. оАЬAоАЭ Choroidal granuloma; оАЬBоАЭ

оАБ Other imaging modalities include MRI cardiac and CNS
imaging (MRI is less useful than CT for thoracic evaluation),
PET scanning and occasionally whole-body gallium scanning.
оАБ Calcium and vitamin D levels may be abnormal depending
on disease pattern and level of activity.
оАБ Hypercalciuria is common.
оАБ Pulmonary function testing.
оАБ Bronchoalveolar lavage оАЯuid (BALF) shows characteristic
changes. CD4/CD8 T cell ratios are a key indicator.
оАБ Induced sputum analysis correlates strongly with BALF and is
a non-invasive technique.
оАБ Four diagnostic levels were deоАЭned by the IWOS for ocular
sarcoidosis:

{ DeоАЭnite ocular sarcoidosis: biopsy-supported diagnosis in
the presence of a compatible uveitis.

{ Presumed ocular sarcoidosis: biopsy not done but chest
X-ray shows BHL with a compatible uveitis.

{ Probable ocular sarcoidosis: biopsy not done, no BHL on
chest X-ray but >3/7 of the intraocular signs above and
>2/5 positive laboratory tests.

{ Possible ocular sarcoidosis: lung biopsy negative but >4/7
signs and >2/5 positive laboratory tests.
It is critical that alternative causes of uveitis are adequately
excluded by appropriate assessment and investigation.

Treatment

Corticosteroids have conventionally been the major treatment
modality in ocular and systemic sarcoidosis, though immunosuppressives are being used more commonly, particularly as steroidsparing agents and in refractory disease. Treatment should be
initiated aggressively to prevent sight-threatening complications.
оАБ Treatment of anterior and intermediate uveitis is approached
in a stepwise fashion as for idiopathic inоАЮammation.
оАБ Posterior uveitis generally requires systemic steroids and
occasionally immunosuppressive agents such as methotrexate, azathioprine, ciclosporin and TNF inhibitors (e.g.
adalimumab).
оАБ Peripheral retinal neovascularization can be treated with scatter photocoagulation to ischaemic areas demonstrated by FA.
оАБ Cystoid macular oedema may respond to a topical NSAID.

оАБ Cataract and glaucoma may require treatment. InоАЮammation
should be suppressed prior to surgery, preferably for at least 3
months in the case of cataract surgery.

BEH√ЗET DISEASE

Introduction

Beh√Іet disease (BD) is an idiopathic, multisystem syndrome characterized by recurrent aphthous oral ulcers, genital ulceration
and uveitis. Vasculitis is a key pathogenetic component and may
involve small, medium and large veins and arteries. Mortality is
around 5% at 5вАУ10 years, typically due to cardiovascular or CNS
complications. оАЯe condition probably has an autoimmune basis
and may be precipitated by exposure to an infectious agent with
subsequent cross-reaction. It typically aоАЫects patients from Turkey, the Middle and Far East (the ancient вАШSilk RoadвАЩ route), with a
lower prevalence in Europe and North America. It is strongly associated with HLA-B51; the ethnic groups with a higher prevalence
of Beh√Іet disease also have a higher rate of HLA-B51 positivity.
оАЯe peak age of onset is the third decade. Reported gender prevalence varies with ethnicity.

TIP A transient hypopyon in a relatively white eye is typical of
Beh√Іet disease and JIA-associated uveitis.

Systemic features

оАЯe International Study Group for Beh√Іet Disease (ISGBD),
reporting in 1990, established criteria for diagnosis: (a) recurrent
oral ulceration (Fig. 12.27A) characterized by oral ulcers at least
three times in a 12-month period, plus at least two of: (b) genital
ulceration, (c) ocular inоАЮammation, (d) characteristic skin lesions
(erythema nodosum вАУ see Fig. 12.22B, pseudofolliculitis, acneiform nodules, papulopustular lesions) and (e) a pathergy reaction:
pustule 24вАУ48 hours aоАЬer a sterile needle prick (>95% speciоАЭc, but
oоАЬen negative in European and North American patients).

CHAPTER

Uveitis 461
#### 12

Presentation does not always conform to the criteria above.
Additional features include:
оАБ Vascular lesions. Aneurysms, including pulmonary and coronary and venous thrombosis/thrombophlebitis.
оАБ Arthritis occurs in 30%, though arthralgia is more common.
оАБ Dermatographia (Fig. 12.27B), similar to the pathergy reaction, indicates skin hypersensitivity and consists of the formation of erythematous lines following stroking or scratching.
оАБ Neurological manifestations (5%) such as meningoencephalitis of the brainstem, dural sinus thrombosis and cerebral
aneurysms.
оАБ Gastrointestinal inоАЯammation, especially ileocecal.
оАБ Hepatic and renal lesions are relatively uncommon.

Ocular features

Ocular inоАЮammation occurs in about 70%, tends to be more severe
in men and is the presenting manifestation in about 10%. Signs
are virtually always bilateral eventually. Relapsing/remitting acute
onset panuveitis with retinal vasculitis and oоАЬen spontaneous
resolution even without treatment is the classical pattern of eye
involvement. Retinal vascular disease (vasculitis and occlusion) is
the main cause of visual impairment.
оАБ AAU, oоАЬen bilateral, is typical. It is not granulomatous. A transient mobile hypopyon in a relatively white eye (Fig. 12.28A)
is characteristic.
оАБ Vitritis may be severe and is universal in eyes with active posterior segment disease.
оАБ Retinitis. Transient superоАЭcial white inоАЭltrates (Fig. 12.28B)
that heal without scarring may be seen during acute systemic
disease. оАЯere may be deeper more diоАЫuse retinitis similar in
appearance to viral inоАЮammation. Exudative detachments can
also occur. InоАЮammatory deposits analogous to KPs may be
seen on the inferior peripheral retina.
оАБ Retinal vasculitis вАУ arteritis as well as phlebitis, in contrast to pure
venous involvement in sarcoidosis вАУ can manifest with sheathing,
perivascular haemorrhages and occlusion (Fig. 12.28C). Vascular
leakage may give rise to diоАЫuse retinal oedema and CMO.

A B

Fig. 12.27 Beh√Іet disease. оАЬAоАЭ Major aphthous ulceration; оАЬBоАЭ dermatographia.

462 Beh√Іet Disease

A B

C D

Fig. 12.28 Ocular manifestations of Beh√Іet disease. оАЬAоАЭ Hypopyon in a white eye; оАЬBоАЭ retinal
inоАЮltrates; оАЬCоАЭ FA showing periphlebitis (arrow heads) and arterial attenuation (arrow); оАЬDоАЭ endstage disease.

оАБ Optic disc hyperaemia and oedema. Raised intracranial pressure can also cause optic disc swelling and optic atrophy in BD.
оАБ Disc and retinal neovascularization may be seen as a response
to inоАЮammation and ischaemia.
оАБ Uncommon manifestations include conjunctivitis, conjunctival ulcers, episcleritis, scleritis and ophthalmoplegia from
neurological involvement.
оАБ End-stage disease is characterized by optic atrophy, retinal
atrophy and gliosis and sheathing, attenuation and ghosting
of aоАЫected vessels (Fig. 12.28D). оАЯe vitreous tends to clear.
Other complications include PS, cataract, glaucoma and,
uncommonly, retinal detachment and phthisis. Severe visual
loss in males of up to two-thirds of patients at 10 years has
been reported, but is probably much lower with aggressive
management. оАЯe rate in women is about half that in men.

Investigation

оАБ HLA-B51 (see above).
оАБ Pathergy test (see above).

оАБ InоАЯammatory markers (e.g. ESR, CRP, complement levels,
white cell count) may be elevated.
оАБ оАЮrombophilia screening is appropriate in some patients to
exclude other causes of thrombosis.
оАБ FA delineates ischaemic areas and aids detection of posterior
segment inоАЮammation and monitoring of disease activity.
оАБ Laser оАЯare photometry of the AC correlates well with FA in
determining the level of inоАЮammatory activity.
оАБ SuperоАЭcial lesion biopsy, synovial оАЮuid aspiration and lumbar
puncture may be used to help rule out alternative diagnoses.
оАБ Systemic imaging may include brain MRI/magnetic resonance
angiography (MRA), CT/computed tomography angiography
(CTA) and conventional angiography to identify ischaemia.

Treatment

Immunosuppressants are the mainstay of treatment, but availability and expense may limit therapeutic options in many regions.
оАБ Topical steroids alone may be adequate if вАУ rarely вАУ there is no
trace of posterior segment involvement.

оАБ Systemic steroids and azathioprine (2.5 mg/kg daily) in
combination are recommended for the initial management
of posterior uveitis in European League Against Rheumatism
(EULAR) 2008 BD guidelines. Steroids should be tapered
slowly. Topical and/or regional steroids may also be used. оАЯere
may be a high rate of ocular hypertension with intravitreal steroid injection. Azathioprine may have a role in prophylaxis.
оАБ Cyclosporine (2вАУ5 mg/kg daily) or inоАЮiximab, in combination
with azathioprine and systemic steroids, is recommended by
EULAR for severe eye disease (>2 lines reduction in visual acuity
and/or retinal vasculitis or macular involvement). A recent study
recommended a single inоАЮiximab infusion as initial treatment of
posterior uveitis. Hypertension, nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity are concerns with cyclosporine, which should be avoided in
patients with CNS involvement unless it is determined that severe
eye disease warrants the risk. InоАЮiximab may lead to activation of
tuberculosis and screening-positive patients should receive prophylactic treatment (e.g. isoniazid). Intravitreal administration is
a novel alternative route of administration for inоАЮiximab.
оАБ InоАЯiximab or adalimumab should be considered early
for vision-threatening BD (American Uveitis Society
recommendation).
оАБ Interferon-alfa (6 million IU per day subcutaneously initially, gradually tapered) with or without steroids is a EULARrecommended alternative to the ciclosporin/inоАЮiximab/
azathioprine/steroid regimen above for severe disease. It
should not be used in combination with azathioprine (risk of
myelosuppression).
оАБ Anticoagulants are not recommended.

PARASITIC UVEITIS
Toxoplasmosis
Introduction

Toxoplasmosis is caused by Toxoplasma gondii, an obligate intracellular protozoan. It is estimated to infest at least 10% of adults
in northern temperate countries and more than half of adults in
Mediterranean and tropical countries. оАЯe cat is the deоАЭnitive
host, with intermediate hosts including mice, livestock, birds and
humans. Oocysts are excreted in cat faeces and then ingested by
intermediate hosts (Fig. 12.29), including via contaminated water
supplies. Cat litter disposal with subsequent transfer to food is
a well-known potential mode of infection in humans (though
indoor cats have a low rate of toxoplasmosis infestation). оАЯe
bradyzoite is an inactive stage lying dormant within cysts in tissues such as the eye, brain and skeletal muscle and consumption of undercooked meat (or eggs) from an intermediate host
can lead to infestation. Bradyzoite cysts can rupture to release
tachyzoites, the proliferating active form, stimulating an inоАЮammatory reaction. Conceptually it may be helpful to think of an
acute phase and a long-term chronic phase of infection. During
the latter, new retinochoroidal scars may form asymptomatically
over the course of years or decades. A critical mode of human
infection is transplacental haematogenous spread to the fetus in
a pregnant woman with active (not inactive latent) toxoplasmosis вАУ this is usually primary infection in an immunocompetent
host but occasionally reactivation of latent infection, the latter predominantly in the immunocompromised. Occasionally

water or soil

464 Parasitic Uveitis

infection may be transmitted via organ transplantation or blood
transfusion.

TIP Toxoplasmosis is the most common identiоАЮable cause for
focal retinitis, which typically causes inоАЭammation adjacent to a
chorioretinal scar.

Systemic features

оАБ Congenital toxoplasmosis. оАЯe mother is oоАЬen symptomfree or has only mild constitutional manifestations. оАЯe severity of fetal involvement is related to the duration of gestation
at the time of maternal infection, tending to be more severe
in early pregnancy, when fetal death may result (10% of all
congenital toxoplasmosis). Neurological and visceral involvement may be very severe, but many cases are subclinical,
especially in later pregnancy. Retinochoroiditis may occur in
over 75%, leaving scars that are commonly a later incidental
оАЭnding (Fig. 12.30).
оАБ Postnatal childhood acquisition probably accounts for over
50% of cases of childhood toxoplasmosis. As with adults, in

immunocompetent individuals this is usually subclinical.
Ocular lesions are probably common but may not develop for
years aоАЬer the initial infection.
оАБ Acquired toxoplasmosis in immunocompetent adults is subclinical in 80вАУ90%. Cervical lymphadenopathy, fever, malaise
and pharyngitis are common features in symptomatic patients,
but more serious systemic manifestations are rare. Early retinitis may occur in up to 20%.
оАБ Toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients may be
acquired or result from reactivation of pre-existing disease. As
well as the constitutional symptoms occurring in the immunocompetent, meningoencephalitis, pneumonitis, retinochoroiditis and a range of other features can occur.

Ocular features

Toxoplasmosis is responsible for 20вАУ60% of all cases of posterior
uveitis depending on the country surveyed (85% in Brazil). Reactivation at previously inactive cyst-containing scars is the rule in
the immunocompetent, although many represent new infection.
More than half of quiescent retinal lesions will have been acquired
from postnatal infection. Recurrent episodes of inоАЮammation are

A B

C D

Fig. 12.30 Retinochoroidal scars in congenital toxoplasmosis. оАЬAоАЭ Macular lesion; оАЬBоАЭ peripapillary scarring; оАЬCоАЭ large upper temporal scar; оАЬDоАЭ inferonasal peripheral scar.

common, especially in the year aоАЬer the initial episode and occur
when the cysts rupture and release hundreds of tachyzoites into
normal retinal cells. First presentation with symptomatic ocular infection occurs at an average age of 29 years, perhaps due to
decreasing speciоАЭc immunity. Ocular involvement from congenital infection may only be detected later in life with the incidental
discovery of typical retinochoroidal scars, though occasionally
macular or optic nerve damage may impair vision in childhood.
оАБ Symptoms. Unilateral acute or subacute onset of оАЮoaters, blurring and photophobia.
оАБ вАШSpill-overвАЩ anterior uveitis is common. It may be granulomatous or resemble FUS. Elevated IOP may follow.
оАБ A single inоАЯammatory focus of оАЮuоАЫy white retinitis or retinochoroiditis associated with a pigmented scar (вАШsatellite lesionвАЩ)
is typical (Fig. 12.31A and B). Lesions tend to involve the posterior pole.
оАБ De novo foci not associated with an old scar and multiple lesions
(Fig. 12.31C) are relatively uncommon in the immunocompetent but occur more frequently in the immunocompromised.
оАБ Vitritis may be severe and impair fundus visualization. вАШHeadlight in the fogвАЩ is the classic description of a white retinal
inоАЮammatory nidus viewed through vitritis (Fig. 12.31D).
оАБ Vasculitis may be arterial but is more commonly venous.
оАБ Optic disc oedema is common.
оАБ Extensive and fulminant retinal involvement is generally
conоАЭned to the immunocompromised, in whom it may be
bilateral and diоАЪcult to distinguish from viral retinitis.
оАБ Retinochoroiditis may be absent in the acute phase of acquired
disease, with activity consisting of anterior uveitis, vitritis and
retinal vasculitis. Typical retinal scars may form later.
оАБ Neuroretinitis similar to that seen in cat-scratch disease is
rare and may be a marker of acutely acquired rather than reactivated infection.
оАБ Punctate outer retinal toxoplasmosis is an atypical manifestation featuring clusters of small (25вАУ75 ќЉm diameter) greyвАУ
white lesions and tends to occur in younger patients.
оАБ Visual loss. Causes of permanently reduced vision (around
25% of eyes) include macular inоАЮammatory lesions and
oedema, optic nerve involvement, vascular occlusion (Fig.
12.32A and B), serous, rhegmatogenous and tractional retinal
detachment (Fig. 12.32C and D) and late secondary macular
neovascularization (Fig. 12.32E and F).
оАБ Healing in immunocompetent hosts usually occurs spontaneously within 6вАУ8 weeks, although vitreous opacities take longer to clear. оАЯe inоАЮammatory focus is replaced by a sharply
demarcated atrophic scar that develops a pigmented border
(Fig. 12.33).
оАБ Recurrence. оАЯe average number of recurrent attacks per
patient is 2.7. оАЯe frequency of recurrences does not diоАЫer in
geographic areas and is about 12вАУ15% in the оАЭrst 2 years. Within
5 years more than half may experience a further episode.

Investigation

Diagnosis is usually based on clinical examination оАЭndings.
оАБ Serology. Toxoplasma IgG antibodies are detectable in the
serum within 1вАУ2 weeks of initial infection and indicate

CHAPTER

Uveitis 465
#### 12

exposure to the organism at some point in the past, providing
circumstantial evidence to support clinical suspicion. However, community seroprevalence is high вАУ at least a third of
individuals in most communities. Positivity to IgM antibodies
usually means that infection has been acquired within the last
year and helps to distinguish between acute (newly acquired)
and chronic infection. It is a critical test if newly acquired
infection is suspected during pregnancy. Occasionally IgM is
positive due to persistence or reactivation following earlier
infection.
оАБ PCR testing of intraocular оАЮuid is variably sensitive (16вАУ67%)
but highly speciоАЭc and can be diagnostic in clinically uncertain
cases. Aqueous and vitreous probably give similar yields.
оАБ Ocular оАЯuid antibody assessment. Calculating the ratio
(GoldmannвАУWitmer coeоАЪcient) of speciоАЭc IgG in aqueous
humour to that in serum seems to be a reasonably sensitive
(48вАУ90%) investigation.
оАБ Imaging. OCT will demonstrate macular oedema. B-scan
ultrasonic imaging can be used to exclude retinal detachment
in the presence of severe vitritis and FAF may facilitate monitoring of inоАЮammatory activity.

Treatment

оАБ Active ocular toxoplasmosis is treated with antibiotics, but
none of the current approaches has been shown to be better
than another in respect of the оАЭnal visual outcome, lesion size
or recurrence rate.
оАБ Eradication of the parasite is diоАЪcult to determine, but parasite activity and multiplication may be reduced leading to a
decrease in size of the eventual retinochoroidal scar.
оАБ Treatment is not needed in every case. оАЯe agents that are
used have the potential for signiоАЭcant morbidity and spontaneous resolution oоАЬen occurs, even without treatment.
оАБ Clear indications include:

{ A sight-threatening lesion involving the macula, papillomacular bundle, optic nerve head or a major blood vessel.

{ Severe vitritis.

{ Active ocular toxoplasmosis in the immunocompromised.
оАБ Treatment duration: typically, 4вАУ6 weeks with complete healing of active lesions over 4вАУ6 months.
оАБ Treatment of congenital toxoplasmosis in neonates with
antimicrobials for 1 year may reduce the frequency of subsequent development of retinochoroidal scars.
оАБ Prednisolone (1 mg/kg) is given initially and tapered according to clinical response, but should always be used in conjunction with a speciоАЭc anti-Toxoplasma agent, most frequently
pyrimethamine combined with sulfadiazine (вАШclassicвАЩ or вАШtripleвАЩ
therapy, sometimes supplemented with clindamycin). Some
authorities start steroids only aоАЬer 24вАУ48 hours of antimicrobial therapy. Systemic steroids should be used with extreme
caution in immunocompromised patients and children should
undergo varicella zoster vaccination if they have not had
chickenpox.
оАБ Pyrimethamine is a folic acid antagonist that is believed
to be highly eоАЫective. It is administered as a loading dose
of 75вАУ100 mg for 1вАУ2 days followed by 25вАУ50 mg daily

466 Parasitic Uveitis

A B

C D

Fig. 12.31 Active Toxoplasma retinitis. оАЬAоАЭ and оАЬBоАЭ Typical вАШsatelliteвАЩ lesion adjacent to an old

for 4 weeks in combination with oral folinic (not folic) acid
5 mg three times a week to retard thrombocytopenia, leukopenia and folate deоАЭciency. Weekly blood counts should
be performed. In acquired immunodeоАЭciency syndrome
(AIDS) pyrimethamine is avoided or used at a lower dosage
because of possible pre-existing bone marrow suppression
and the antagonistic eоАЫect of zidovudine when the drugs are
combined.
оАБ Sulfadiazine 1 g four times daily for 3вАУ4 weeks is usually given
in combination with pyrimethamine. Side eоАЫects of sulfonamides include renal stones, allergic reactions and StevensвАУ
Johnson syndrome.
оАБ Intravitreal therapy with clindamycin (1 mg) and dexamethasone (400 ќЉg) may be as eоАЫective as triple therapy in reactivated infection. Two to three injections (at 2-weekly intervals)
may be required. It may be preferred in recurrent infection in
pregnancy, but would not generally be used in isolation in the
immunocompromised, and in newly acquired (IgM-positive)
infection systemic therapy has superior eоАЪcacy. Depot steroid

intra- and periocular preparations such as triamcinolone should
be avoided as uncontrolled progression has been reported.
оАБ Azithromycin 250вАУ500 mg daily shows evidence of reducing
the rate of recurrence of retinochoroiditis and its use in combination with pyrimethamine, folinic acid and prednisolone
is a promising new regimen. Clarithromycin may be a good
alternative to azithromycin.
оАБ Co-trimoxazole (trimethoprim 160 mg/sulfamethoxazole 800
mg) twice daily in combination with prednisolone is a low-cost
and better-tolerated option, but might not be quite as eоАЫective
as classic therapy.
оАБ Clindamycin 300 mg four times daily may be added to triple
therapy (see above) or used instead of pyrimethamine. Pseudomembranous colitis is a potential adverse eоАЫect.
оАБ Atovaquone theoretically attacks encysted bradyzoites, but
does not seem to prevent recurrence inоАЯvivo; dosing is 750 mg
two to four times daily.
оАБ Topical steroid and mydriatic may be given for anterior
uveitis.

оАБ Antimicrobial maintenance therapy is used in immunocompromised patients.
оАБ Pregnancy. Treatment of recurrent ocular toxoplasmosis during pregnancy should be chosen carefully and only started if
absolutely necessary, as several of the drugs discussed above
have the potential to harm the fetus. Intravitreal therapy (see
above) for reactivated disease, or systemic treatment with
azithromycin, clindamycin and possibly prednisolone may be
appropriate. SpeciоАЭc treatment to prevent transmission to the
fetus is not generally given except in newly acquired infection,
when urgent specialist management is appropriate. Spiramycin
alone or in combination is commonly chosen.
оАБ Vitrectomy may be performed in selected cases.
оАБ Prevention of recurrence. Co-trimoxazole (160mg trimethoprim combined with 800mg sulfamethoxazole) taken three
times daily for 12 months has been shown to reduce the recurrence rate from 22% to 3% for up to 3 years. AоАЬer that period
the likelihood of recurrence is as high as in patients who have
never received prophylaxis.

Toxocariasis

Introduction

Toxocariasis is caused by infestation with a common intestinal
ascarid (roundworm) of dogs, Toxocara canis. Puppies are more
commonly infected than adult dogs and are also more likely to
spread the organism. оАЯe feline variant вАУ Toxocara cati вАУ may also
be causative. Human infestation is by ingestion of soil or food
contaminated with ova shed in canine faeces. Young children are
at particular risk from the soil of parks and playgrounds. Once
ingested, ova develop into larvae, which penetrate the intestinal
wall and travel to various organs such as the liver, lungs, skin, brain
and eyes, with resultant inоАЮammation.

Clinical features

оАБ Asymptomatic infestation is common.
оАБ Visceral toxocariasis, also known as visceral larva migrans, is systemic infection of variable severity that usually occurs in a child
aged 2вАУ7 years. Fever, abdominal pain, pneumonitis, lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly and myocarditis are some of the possible
features. Spontaneous recovery is usual. Death is very rare and
usually occurs in individuals hypersensitive to parasitic antigens.
оАБ Covert toxocariasis is associated with mild systemic symptoms.
оАБ Ocular toxocariasis (ocular larva migrans) generally occurs
independently of visceral larva migrans and is associated with
a lower parasitic load. It is typically unilateral and in around
two-thirds causes some degree of permanent visual impairment. In contrast to visceral toxocariasis, it tends to occur in
older children and adults.

{ Chronic endophthalmitis (Fig. 12.34A) typically presents
with leukocoria, strabismus, оАЮoaters or unilateral visual
loss. Features may include anterior uveitis, vitritis, chorioretinitis, papillitis and a fundus granuloma (see below).
A dense greyish-white exudate, similar to the snowbanking seen in PP, may involve the peripheral retina and pars
plana. Complications leading to a poor visual outcome
include tractional retinal or ciliary body detachment with
hypotony leading to phthisis bulbi (Fig. 12.34B).

{ Posterior pole or peripheral granuloma without inоАЮammation (Fig. 12.35AвАУC) classically occurs in an older child or
adult with unilateral impaired vision or as an incidental
оАЭnding. A one to two disc diameter-sized round yellowwhite granuloma is present in the posterior fundus or
periphery. Vitreoretinal traction may lead to complications due to macular distortion and/or retinal detachment.

{ Chorioretinal scar (Fig. 12.35D).

{ DiоАЫuse unilateral subacute neuroretinitis (DUSN). See
below.

Fig. 12.34 Chronic Toxocara endophthalmitis. оАЬAоАЭ Leukocoria; оАЬBоАЭ a pathological specimen
showing an inоАЭammatory mass and total retinal detachment. (J Harry and G Misson, from
Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 вАУ оАЯg. B.)

A B

C D

Fig. 12.35 Toxocara granuloma. оАЬAоАЭ Granuloma with a vitreous band; оАЬBоАЭ peripheral granuloma
with a vitreous band extending to the disc; оАЬCоАЭ mid-peripheral granuloma with underlying
chorioretinal scarring; оАЬDоАЭ chorioretinal scar at the macula with granuloma.

470 Parasitic Uveitis

Investigation

It is particularly important to distinguish a Toxocara granuloma
from retinoblastoma. Other helminthic organisms can give similar
clinical manifestations.
оАБ Full blood count. Eosinophilia may be present, particularly in
visceral larva migrans and can become chronic.
оАБ Hypergammaglobulinaemia especially IgE.
оАБ Serology. Antibodies to Toxocara canis are detectable in only
about 50% of ocular cases. Positivity is common in the general
population (14% overall in the USA).
оАБ Ultrasonography may be useful if the vitreous is hazy.
оАБ Aqueous or vitreous sampling for eosinophilia, antibody
detection and PCR.
оАБ Biopsy of a granuloma of the skin or elsewhere for larvae is
sometimes possible.

Ocular treatment

оАБ Prevention by good hygiene practices and deworming of pets.
оАБ Steroids. Topical, regional and systemic may be considered.
оАБ Anthelmintic agents such as mebendazole and thiabendazole
can be considered in ocular toxocariasis, remembering that
worm death may promote inоАЮammation.
оАБ Vitrectomy for sight-threatening tractional sequelae.

Onchocerciasis

Introduction

Onchocerciasis, which aоАЫects the eyes and skin, is the second most
common cause of infectious blindness in the world. It is endemic

Simulium
fly

Microfilariae

Fig. 12.36 Life cycle of Onchocerca volvulus.

in areas of Africa and other regions and disease is particularly
severe in savanna regions, where in some areas more than 50% of
older adults are blind from the condition. Chronic onchocerciasis shortens lifespan by reducing resistance to other diseases. оАЯe
parasitic helminth Onchocerca volvulus is causative. оАЯe vector is
the Simulium blackоАЮy, which breeds in fast-оАЮowing water, hence
the colloquial term вАШriver blindnessвАЩ. Larvae are transmitted when
the оАЮy bites to obtain blood. оАЯey migrate to subcutaneous sites to
form onchocercomas, where microоАЭlariae are produced by adult
worms (Fig. 12.36). Degenerating microоАЭlariae excite an intense
inоАЮammatory reaction accounting for most of the clinical manifestations of the disease. оАЯe rickettsia Wolbachia lives symbiotically
in adult worms and microоАЭlaria and is important for microоАЭlarial production. Diagnosis is by skin-snip biopsy, serology, tear or
urine antigen detection (oncho-dipstick) and PCR of lesion оАЮuid.

Clinical features

оАБ Systemic features are principally dermatological and include
pruritus, a maculopapular rash (onchodermatitis вАУ Fig. 12.37A)
involving the buttocks and extremities and areas of hypo- and
hyperpigmentation on the shins (вАШleopard skinвАЩ вАУ Fig. 12.37B).
Scratching of itchy areas leads to licheniоАЭcation. Onchocercomata are non-tender subcutaneous nodules (Fig. 12.37C) that
enclose 2вАУ3 adult worms. Severe lymphadenopathy can occur,
with secondary lymphoedema. Eosinophilia is typical.
оАБ Live microоАЭlariae may be seen in the cornea, vitreous and suspended in the AC aоАЬer the patient has postured face-down for
a few minutes followed by immediate slit lamp examination.
оАБ Anterior uveitis is an early feature. Pear-shaped pupillary dilatation may be seen and is due to PS.

Fig. 12.38 Ocular onchocerciasis. оАЬAоАЭ Corneal scarring;
оАЬBоАЭ intraretinal pigment clumping and RPE atrophy; оАЬCоАЭ

472 Viral Uveitis

оАБ Keratitis. Punctate keratitis (snowоАЮake opacities) aоАЫects a
third of patients and consists of inоАЭltrates surrounding dead
microоАЭlariae. Initial lesions are most commonly located at 3
and 9 oвАЩclock in the anterior third of the stroma. Slowly progressing sclerosing keratitis may eventually involve the entire
cornea (Fig. 12.38A).
оАБ Chorioretinitis is usually bilateral and predominantly involves
the temporal fundus, sparing the macula until late. Widespread choroidal sclerosis and atrophy can ensue (Fig. 12.38B
and C). A perpetuating autoimmune response is hypothesized
for progressive longer-term chorioretinopathy that may persist
aоАЬer control of infection.
оАБ Optic neuritis may be acute.

Treatment

оАБ Ivermectin (supplied in many countries by Merck at no charge)
kills microоАЭlariae (but not adult worms) and is given at least annually for many years. It has been eоАЫective in substantially reducing
transmission rates and morbidity and no recent new cases have
been reported in many communities. Ivermectin occasionally
precipitates inоАЮammation, so prophylactic prednisolone may be
considered in patients with visible AC microоАЭlariae. Ivermectin
may cause toxic encephalopathy in patients with Loa loa infection.
оАБ Moxidectin is a newer drug that may be superior to
ivermectin.
оАБ Doxycycline 100вАУ200 mg per day for 6 weeks targets Wolbachia, indirectly preventing microоАЭlarial embryogenesis and
substantially reducing numbers for an extended period. It also
has some eоАЫect on adult worms and may be a useful adjunct to
ivermectin or moxidectin.
оАБ Suramin is eоАЫective against adult worms. It is given
intravenously.
оАБ Steroids. Anterior uveitis is responsive.

TIP Ivermectin given annually is eоАЯective in reducing
transmission rates and ocular morbidity in onchocerciases.

Cysticercosis

Introduction

Cysticercosis refers to infection by Cysticercus cellulosae, the larval
form of the pork tapeworm Taenia solium. Ingesting cysts of T.
solium in undercooked pork leads to intestinal tapeworm development (taeniasis). оАЯe infested human then sheds eggs that lead
to larval infection (cysticercosis) when ingested by the same or
another individual. InоАЮammation develops in response to antigens
released by dead organisms.

Clinical features

оАБ Systemic disease may involve the lungs, muscle and CNS
(neurocysticercosis). MRI and CT imaging are eоАЫective at
demonstrating cysts and plain X-rays may show calciоАЭed cysts.
Serology and stool analysis are useful for diagnosis.
оАБ Ocular features include cysts of the conjunctiva and rarely
the AC may show a free-оАЮoating cyst (Fig. 12.39A). Larvae

can enter the subretinal space (Fig. 12.39B) and may cause
exudative retinal detachment (Fig. 12.39C). оАЯey can also
pass into the vitreous where released toxins can incite an
intense vision-threatening inоАЮammatory reaction. A cyst
may occasionally involve the orbit, eyelids or rectus muscle
(Fig. 12.39D).

Treatment

Systemic steroids to control inоАЮammation are combined with
surgical removal of the larvae from the AC, vitreous and subretinal space. Anthelmintic agents such as albendazole may
be appropriate in systemic disease, but should be used with
caution under specialist guidance and oоАЬen with steroid
co-administration.

DiоАЫuse unilateral subacute
neuroretinitis оАЬDUSNоАЭ

Introduction

DUSN is a clinical syndrome due to the presence of a single motile
subretinal nematode such as Toxocara canis, Baylisascaris procyonis and Ancylostoma caninum. Misdiagnosis (e.g. multifocal
choroiditis) is common as the worm is oоАЬen small and may be
overlooked.

Clinical features

оАБ Presentation is with insidious monocular visual decrease. оАЯe
disease is diagnosed clinically as special investigations are not
helpful. Electroretinography (ERG) is subnormal, even in early
disease.
оАБ Acute disease. Crops of greyвАУwhite outer retinal lesions (Fig.
12.40),vitritis, papillitis and retinal vasculitis.
оАБ End-stage disease. Optic atrophy, retinal vascular attenuation
and diоАЫuse RPE degeneration.

Treatment

Photocoagulation (200 ќЉm, 0.2вАУ0.5 s, 150вАУ300 mW) is the treatment of choice when a worm can be visualized (<50%). If necessary, a slit beam or very light laser burns are оАЭrst used to shepherd
(sometimes lure) the photosensitive nematode away from the
fovea. Systemic albendazole (400 mg for 30 days) or vitrectomy
may be appropriate in some cases. Steroid cover may be prudent
with all treatment modalities.

VIRAL UVEITIS

Uveitis in human immunodeоАЪciency
virus infection
Introduction

Human immunodeоАЭciency virus infection/acquired immunodeоАЭciency syndrome (HIV/AIDS) is transmitted by unprotected sexual intercourse, via contaminated blood or needles and vertically
from mother to child transplacentally, during birth or breastfeeding. HIV depletes CD4 [+] T cells, which are vital to the initiation of

CHAPTER

Uveitis 473
#### 12

A B

C D

Fig. 12.39 Ocular cysticercosis. оАЬAоАЭ Anterior chamber cyst; оАЬBоАЭ subretinal cyst showing tract
of the larva; оАЬCоАЭ subretinal cyst with overlying exudative retinal detachment; оАЬDоАЭ axial CT
scan showing a cyst in the right medial rectus muscle. (Courtesy of Dr RSR Naik вАУ оАЯg. A;
A Pearson вАУ оАЯg. C.)

the immune response to pathogens. Although there is currently no
cure or vaccine, the progression of disease can be slowed radically
by combination drug therapy: antiretroviral therapy (ART). ART
is now recommended for all HIV-positive individuals irrespective
of CD4 [+] T cell count. Prophylaxis against opportunistic infections
may also be instituted.

Systemic features

Fig. 12.40 DiоАЯuse unilateral subacute neuroretinitis showing subretinal nematode (arrow) and multifocal greyвАУwhite
spots. (Courtesy of SR Rathinam.)

оАБ Stages of infection: (a) a оАЮu-like illness may occur 2вАУ4 weeks
aоАЬer infection, sometimes including a rash; (b) clinical latency
is a predominantly asymptomatic period of several (average
8) years, sometimes with minor clinical features such as persistent generalized lymphadenopathy; (c) AIDS develops in
about half of HIV-positive individuals within 10 years and is
deоАЭned as HIV infection with either a CD4 [+] T count <200
cells/ќЉl or the development of one or more AIDS-deоАЭning
conditions.
оАБ AIDS-deоАЭning clinical conditions include particular
opportunistic infections such as respiratory or oesophageal

474 Viral Uveitis

candidiasis, Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (Fig. 12.41A),
cryptosporidiosis and cytomegalovirus retinitis, speciоАЭc
tumours including Kaposi sarcoma (Fig. 12.41B) and certain lymphomas and other manifestations such as HIV
wasting syndrome (Fig. 12.41C) and progressive multifocal
leukoencephalopathy.

Ocular features

оАБ Eyelid. Blepharitis, Kaposi sarcoma, multiple molluscum
lesions and herpes zoster ophthalmicus.
оАБ Orbit. Cellulitis (e.g. aspergillosis, contiguous sinus infection),
B-cell lymphoma.
оАБ Conjunctiva. Kaposi sarcoma, squamous cell carcinoma and
microvasculopathy (up to 80%).
оАБ Cornea. Keratoconjunctivitis sicca and an increased incidence
of keratitis, e.g. herpes simplex and zoster and fungal.
оАБ Anterior uveitis associated with ocular infections, or (commonly) with drug toxicity, e.g. rifabutin, cidofovir.
оАБ HIV-related retinal microangiopathy. Retinal microangiopathy is the most frequent retinopathy in patients with AIDS,
developing in up to 70% of patients. It is associated with a

declining CD4 [+] T cell count and higher plasma HIV-RNA
levels and is a marker for increased cytomegalovirus (CMV)
retinitis risk. Postulated causes include immune complex
deposition, HIV infection of the retinal vascular endothelium and abnormalities of оАЮow. It manifests with cotton-wool
spots and/or retinal haemorrhages (Fig. 12.41D) and sometimes capillary abnormalities such as microaneurysms. In
contrast to CMV retinitis, lesions are usually asymptomatic
and almost invariably disappear spontaneously aоАЬer several
weeks.
оАБ Other viral retinitis: cytomegalovirus retinitis (most common), progressive retinal necrosis, acute retinal necrosis.
оАБ Protozoal: Toxoplasma retinochoroiditis, oоАЬen atypical.
оАБ Fungal: Pneumocystis choroiditis, Histoplasma chorioretinitis,
cryptococcal choroiditis, candidiasis.
оАБ Bacterial: syphilis, tuberculosis.
оАБ Neoplastic: B-cell intraocular lymphoma.
оАБ Neuro-ophthalmological. Usually secondary to meningitis or
encephalopathy due to an opportunistic infection (e.g. toxoplasmosis, cryptococcosis, neurosyphilis) or neoplastic process (e.g. CNS lymphoma).

A B

C D

Fig. 12.41 Complications of AIDS. оАЬAоАЭ X-ray showing Pneumocystis pneumonia; оАЬBоАЭ Kaposi sarcoma; оАЬCоАЭ HIV wasting syndrome; оАЬDоАЭ HIV microangiopathy.

TIP Retinal microangiopathy is associated with a declining
CD4 T-cell count and is a marker for increased risk of
developing cytomegalovirus retinitis.

Cytomegalovirus retinitis

Introduction

Infection with cytomegalovirus (CMV), a herpesvirus, is very
common in the general population, causing no or minimal constitutional symptoms in most healthy individuals. CMV retinitis is seen in patients immunocompromised from a variety of
causes. It is a common opportunistic ocular infection in patients
with AIDS, in whom it may represent reactivation of latent
infection. Without treatment, severe visual loss is essentially
inevitable. Since the advent of ART the incidence and severity
have declined, though the prevalence remains high, partly due
to increasing survival rates in AIDS patients in whom systemic
steroid therapy may be a risk factor. оАЯere is a very strong association with a low CD4 [+] count.

Systemic features

Severe involvement of a range of organs including the lungs, CNS
and skin can occur in the immunocompromised.

Ocular features

оАБ Presentation is with reduced vision from macular involvement
or with оАЮoaters from vitritis. One eye is usually aоАЫected initially, progressing to both eyes in 50% of patients if untreated.
Indolent retinitis (see below) frequently starts in the periphery
without symptoms and progresses over weeks.
оАБ Anterior uveitis can occur but is usually mild with little or no
injection. It is considered separately later in this chapter.
оАБ Cataract is a common later-stage оАЭnding.
оАБ Vitritis is typically mild, except in immune recovery (see
below).
оАБ Retinitis. оАЯe characteristic appearance is of one or two areas
of dense white retinal inоАЭltration associated prominently with
оАЮame-shaped retinal haemorrhages (вАШpizza pieвАЩ or вАШMargherita
pizzaвАЩ), beginning peripherally (centrally in 10%) and extending along the course of the vascular arcades (Fig. 12.42A).
Peripheral areas tend to appear granular, with fewer rounder
haemorrhages and little vasculitis. Indolent (more peripheral
and less aggressive вАУ Fig. 12.42B) and fulminant clinical patterns have been distinguished (Fig. 12.42C). оАЯe visual acuity
can be signiоАЭcantly reduced if the macula is involved.
оАБ Optic neuritis may result from direct spread or from primary
involvement.
оАБ Retinal necrosis is evident in areas where active inоАЮammation
has settled, leaving irregular pigmentation, atrophy and holes,
frequently leading to retinal detachment (Fig. 12.42D), a major
cause of visual morbidity (up to 50%).
оАБ Frosted branch angiitis (FBA) describes marked vascular
sheathing that occurs in about 6% (see Fig. 12.42D). оАЯis
appearance is seen in other conditions (see Fig. 12.71) and the

CHAPTER

Uveitis 475
#### 12

term is also used for a distinct idiopathic disorder (primary
FBA).
оАБ Immune recovery uveitis (IRU). оАЯis is a cause of limited
visual outcome in CMV retinitis, thought to be due to a rejuvenated immune response against residual viral antigen following immune reconstitution with ART. Manifestations can
be severe, progressing to phthisis in some cases.

Treatment

Close liaison with an infectious disease physician is critical. ART is
the mainstay of management, restoring the patientвАЩs innate ability
to suppress CMV activity. Discontinuation of antiviral treatment is
considered when the CD4 [+] count reaches >100вАУ150 cells/ќЉl.
оАБ Valganciclovir is a pro-drug of ganciclovir that is taken orally
and is as eоАЫective for both induction (900 mg twice daily for
up to 3 weeks) and maintenance (900 mg daily). Neutropenia
is a common side eоАЫect due to bone marrow suppression, but
can be eоАЫectively treated with оАЭlgrastim (granulocyte colonystimulating factor).
оАБ Intravitreal injection of ganciclovir (2mg/injection), foscarnet (1.2mg), or cidofovir (20 ќЉg) may occasionally be
indicated for lesions close to the macula or optic nerve. оАЯe
marketing authorization for ganciclovir implant has been
withdrawn.
оАБ Vitrectomy with endolaser demarcation and silicone oil tamponade is successful in around 75% of CMV-related retinal
detachments.
оАБ Steroids may be required for IRU, though intravitreal and systemic administration should be used with caution.
оАБ Prognosis. оАЯis is poor, despite treatment, if the macula is
involved (Fig. 12.43).
оАБ Screening of patients with low CD4 counts: 3-monthly if
< 50/ќЉl, 6-monthly 50вАУ100/ќЉl, yearly if >100/ќЉl.

Progressive retinal necrosis

Introduction

Progressive retinal necrosis (PRN, also known as progressive or
posterior outer retinal necrosis, PORN) is a rare but devastating
form of necrotizing retinitis which is usually caused by varicella
zoster virus (VZV) and possibly other herpesviruses. It occurs
predominantly in patients with AIDS, but may be associated with
other immunocompromised states, particularly drug-induced.
оАЯe prognosis is extremely poor, with no perception of light the
outcome in more than half of aоАЫected eyes.

Ocular features

оАБ Presentation is with rapidly progressive unilateral or bilateral
visual loss.
оАБ Anterior uveitis and vitritis are minimal, in contrast to CMV
retinitis and acute retinal necrosis (ARN) (see below).
оАБ Retinitis. оАЯree stages are recognized:

{ Early. Multifocal homogeneous yellowвАУwhite deep retinal
inоАЭltrates (Fig. 12.44A). оАЯe macula may be involved at an
early stage, oоАЬen giving a cherry-red spot.

476 Viral Uveitis

A B

C D

Fig. 12.42 Cytomegalovirus retinitis. оАЬAоАЭ Early retinitis; оАЬBоАЭ indolent retinitis with typical granular
appearance (arrow) and retinal vasculitis; оАЬCоАЭ advanced disease showing extensive macular
exudation; оАЬDоАЭ large posterior retinal tear with shallow localized detachment вАУ there is vascular sheathing reminiscent of frosted branch angiitis. (Courtesy of C Barry вАУ оАЯg. D.)

Fig. 12.43 Regressing CMV retinitis after treatment.

{ Established/middle. оАЯe signs typically spread rapidly
around the retina, with very extensive full-thickness
necrosis (Fig. 12.44B). Signs of vasculitis are absent or mild
and signiоАЭcant haemorrhage is uncommon. As inоАЮammation clears, perivenular translucency is seen (Fig. 12.44C).

{ Late. Retinal atrophy may follow as the arteriolar blood
supply decreases (Fig. 12.44D). Rhegmatogenous retinal
detachment (RRD) is common, as is optic atrophy.

Investigation

Vitreous and/or aqueous PCR assay for viral DNA; antibody assay
is less eоАЫective.

Treatment

Immune rescue with ART together with aggressive antiviral therapy, e.g. intravitreal and intravenous ganciclovir and foscarnet. Vitreoretinal surgery for retinal detachment oоАЬen yields poor results.

Fig. 12.44 Progressive retinal necrosis. оАЬAоАЭ On presentation, showing multi-focal homogenous

TIP Anterior uveitis and vitritis are minimal in a patient with
progressive retinal necrosis, which is in contrast to CMV retinitis
and acute retinal necrosis where signiоАЮcant inоАЭammation
occurs.

Acute retinal necrosis

Introduction

Acute retinal necrosis (ARN) is a rare but devastating necrotizing retinitis. It typically aоАЫects otherwise healthy individuals and tends to be
caused by herpes simplex virus (HSV) in younger and VZV in older
patients (other herpesviruses are also suspected). оАЯe prognosis is
relatively poor, with more than half of patients eventually achieving
only 6/60 as a result of retinal and optic nerve ischaemia or RRD.
оАБ Systemic features. ARN has been reported following and
occurring simultaneously with HSV encephalitis and herpetic
skin infection.

оАБ Ocular features. Presentation is initially unilateral with
blurred vision and оАЮoaters. Pain is usually a feature. оАЯe American Uveitis Society criteria for diagnosis are as follows:

{ Prominent anterior uveitis and vitritis (panuveitis).
Episcleritis and scleritis may occur.

{ One or more discrete foci of peripheral retinal necrosis.
Deep yellow-white inоАЭltrates with well-deоАЭned borders
are seen (Fig. 12.45A). Retinal haemorrhages can occur,
but are generally less prominent than in CMV retinitis.
оАЯe acute lesions resolve aоАЬer 6вАУ12 weeks, leaving behind
necrotic retina with hyperpigmented borders (Fig. 12.45B).
Secondary RRD is a major cause of visual morbidity.

{ Circumferential spread of retinal involvement. Posterior
pole involvement is late. Optic neuritis is sometimes a
feature.

{ Occlusive retinal vasculitis including arteritis. Preretinal
neovascularization can develop and may lead to vitreous
haemorrhage.

478 Viral Uveitis

A B

Fig. 12.45 Acute retinal necrosis. оАЬAоАЭ Advanced disease reaching the posterior pole; оАЬBоАЭ fullthickness retinal necrosis. (Courtesy of C Barry вАУ оАЯg. B.)

{ Rapid progression of disease in the absence of treatment.

Investigation

Vitreous and/or aqueous PCR assay for viral DNA. Antibody assay
is less eоАЫective.

Treatment

оАБ Aciclovir: intravenously (15 mg/kg every 8 hours) for 10вАУ14
days and then orally 800 mg оАЭve times daily for 6вАУ12 weeks.
оАЯis may hasten resolution of the acute retinal lesions and dramatically reduces the risk of second eye involvement. Longterm therapy is occasionally required.
оАБ Oral valaciclovir or famciclovir may be substituted for oral aciclovir, with similar outcomes but better tolerability. Valaciclovir
2 g, orally, three times daily can be used as an induction dose,
followed by a maintenance dose of 1g, orally, three times daily.
оАБ Intravitreal ganciclovir or foscarnet may improve the
prognosis.
оАБ Systemic steroids may be started 24 hours aоАЬer initiation of
antiviral therapy, especially in severe cases.
оАБ Laser retinopexy around necrotic areas has been used in
the past, but has no eоАЫect on the retinal detachment rate and
should be avoided.
оАБ Vitrectomy for RRD, commonly with silicone oil tamponade.

Herpes simplex anterior uveitis

Introduction

Anterior uveitis may occur with or without active corneal disease (see Ch. 7). Patchy and occasionally sectoral iris atrophy that
includes the pigment epithelium (Fig. 12.46A) is common, as is
elevated IOP. оАЯe pupil may be larger than its fellow. As in Fuchs
uveitis syndrome (FUS), KPs may be оАЭne, stellate and diоАЫusely
distributed across the cornea, but can also be large and grouped.
оАЯere is oоАЬen a past history of herpes simplex keratitis, cold sores
and sometimes genital herpes. Recurrent episodes of herpetic iritis

involve the same eye in almost all patients. Distinguishing herpetic
from cytomegalovirus iridocyclitis (see below) can be diоАЪcult.

Treatment

Topical steroids (e.g. prednisolone acetate 1% four times daily) and
a topical cycloplegic, in combination with an oral antiviral, e.g. aciclovir 400 mg оАЭve times a day. оАЯere is evidence that famciclovir or
valaciclovir is better tolerated than aciclovir. Steroids may be delayed
and used with caution if active epithelial disease is present, when a
topical antiviral may be added. Raised IOP is treated as necessary.

Varicella zoster virus оАЬVZVоАЭ anterior
uveitis

Introduction

Anterior uveitis of variable severity occurs in around 50% of
patients with herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO) and generally
starts 1вАУ3 weeks aоАЬer the acute skin rash (occasionally in patients
with HZO without dermatitis вАУ zoster sine herpete). Zosterassociated iridocyclitis may be recurrent, when diagnosis is usually
straightforward due to a past history of ipsilateral HZO. As with
herpes simplex, signs may be of granulomatous inоАЮammation.
Sectoral iris atrophy is oоАЬen present (Fig. 12.46B), corneal sensation may be reduced and IOP raised. PCR analysis of aqueous is
indicated exceptionally. Anterior segment inоАЮammation can occur
in primary VZV infection (chickenpox), particularly in the immunocompromised; neuroretinitis is rare. Uveitis has been reported
following VZV vaccination.

Treatment

Topical steroids and mydriatics, in addition to standard systemic
antiviral treatment of shingles. Systemic steroids are required
rarely (e.g. optic neuritis). All patients with HZO must be monitored by an ophthalmologist dependent on severity, e.g. up to
weekly for at least 6 weeks to detect occult ocular inоАЮammation
and subsequently possibly long-term to detect late complications.

CHAPTER

Uveitis 479
#### 12

A B

Fig. 12.46 оАЬAоАЭ Iris atrophy (arrow) with corectopia in herpes simplex anterior uveitis; оАЬBоАЭ sectoral iris atrophy in herpes zoster anterior uveitis.

Persistence or recurrence of anterior uveitis may respond to a
week-long course of aciclovir 800 mg оАЭve times a day and longterm systemic antiviral prophylactic treatment may be considered
for repeated recurrence. VZV vaccination oоАЫers protection against
shingles. See also Chapter 7

Cytomegalovirus anterior uveitis

Introduction

Cytomegalovirus (CMV) iridocyclitis in the immunocompetent is
more common than previously realized, albeit still less prevalent
than HSV- and VZV-related inоАЮammation. It may be recurrent
or chronic and unilateral or bilateral. Elevated IOP is very common. CMV has been reported as a cause of PosnerвАУSchlossman
syndrome (see Ch. 11). Little or no ciliary injection, limited оАЮare,
few cells, corneal endotheliitis, KP of a range of morphology and
sectoral iris atrophy have been reported. PS are rare. A key diagnostic indicator, in some cases only, may be a failure to respond to
aciclovir and/or steroids. PCR and antibody assay of an aqueous
sample should be considered if there is clinical suspicion.

Treatment

Oral valganciclovir in proven infection, which sometimes requires
long-term continuation. IOP elevation may be persistent.

Rubella

Rubella (German measles) is a common childhood infection and
usually follows a benign and short-lived course. However, transplacental transmission of virus to the fetus from an infected mother
can lead to congenital abnormalities of multiple organ systems,
with severity generally worse the earlier in gestation infection
occurs. Latent rubella virus may cause CAU relatively unresponsive to steroids and has been implicated in the causation of FUS.
Reported ocular features of congenital rubella include cataract,
anterior uveitis, вАШsalt and pepperвАЩ pigmentary retinopathy (Fig.
12.47A and B), glaucoma and microphthalmos.

Measles and subacute sclerosing
panencephalitis

Congenital infection with the measles virus can cause spontaneous
abortion or congenital systemic and ocular anomalies including
cataract and retinopathy. Infection acquired in childhood typically
features conjunctivitis and epithelial keratitis. Occasionally retinitis with macular and disc oedema can occur. Subacute sclerosing
panencephalitis (SSPE) is a rare progressive degenerative disease
of the CNS. оАЯe condition usually aоАЫects children and teenagers.
оАЯe onset of symptoms is 6вАУ15 years aоАЬer infection with measles.
оАЯe disease classically manifests initially with personality change
and behavioral problems, followed some months later by involuntary choreoathetoid movements and myoclonus. Further neurological deterioration slowly occurs and eventually death follows.
Papilloedema, nystagmus and optic neuritis can occur during the
course of the disease. Posterior uveitis is common and may be the
presenting feature. Ischaemic retinal vein occlusion secondary to
vasculitis has been reported but is rare (Fig. 12.48A). оАЯe classical macular lesion appears white or greyвАУwhite, resolving to leave
areas of scarring and irregular RPE atrophy (Fig. 12.48B).

Mumps

Iridocyclitis and interstitial keratitis are rare complications of
mumps.

Vaccinia

Smallpox vaccination using the vaccinia virus has been resumed for
some groups as a result of the perceived risk of bioterrorism. оАЯough
rare, a range of anterior segment manifestations has been described,
both from autoinoculation in vaccines and in close contacts.

Zika

Zika virus is transmitted by an infected Aedes mosquito. оАЯe virus
can be passed from a pregnant woman to her fetus, resulting in

480 Fungal Uveitis

A B

Fig. 12.47 Rubella retinopathy. оАЬAоАЭ вАШSalt and pepperвАЩ appearance; оАЬBоАЭ predominantly manifesting in the macula.

A B

Fig. 12.48 Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis. оАЬAоАЭ Retinal vein occlusion secondary to vasculitis in a 16-year-old girl; оАЬBоАЭ 11 months later showing subtle optic atrophy, resorbing haemorrhages, macular scarring and irregular RPE atrophy. (Courtesy of ADN Murray.)

signiоАЭcant birth defects, including microcephaly. In congenital
Zika, ocular eоАЫects include chorioretinal and macular atrophy,
focal pigmentary changes in the macular region, optic nerve
abnormality and glaucoma. In adults with an acute infection, conjunctivitis is the most common ocular manifestation, followed by
uveitis. оАЯe virus may persist in the lacrimal gland and in other
tissues of the eye, particularly in the retinal pigment epithelium.

Ebola

оАЯe Ebola virus, which is responsible for epidemics in West Africa,
has the potential to cause clinical disease in sporadic outbreaks. In
survivors, вАШpost-Ebola virus disease syndromeвАЩ (PEVDS) may result
in ocular disease, arthritis, hearing loss, abdominal pain and neuropsychiatric disorders. Uveitis occurs in up to one-third of survivors
and may result in acute or chronic visual loss. оАЯis can manifest as
anterior, posterior or panuveitis, with most cases occurring within

2 months of discharge. Up to 15% subsequently develop a cataract.
Optic nerve swelling has been reported to occur in 10% of survivors.
оАЯe virus has been detected in the aqueous in the acute situation
and can infect and persist in retinal pigment epithelial cells. Treatment with topical and oral corticosteroids is eоАЫective but needs to
be started at the onset of symptoms to prevent long-term visual loss.

FUNGAL UVEITIS

Presumed ocular histoplasmosis
syndrome оАЬPOHSоАЭ
Introduction

Histoplasma capsulatum infection occurs following inhalation of
the yeast form of this dimorphic fungus and can lead to the systemic
mycosis histoplasmosis вАУ pulmonary involvement is the most common feature. It is common in AIDS. POHS is relatively common

CHAPTER

Uveitis 481
#### 12

A B

C D

Fig. 12.49 Presumed ocular histoplasmosis syndrome. оАЬAоАЭ Peripheral вАШhistoвАЩ spots; оАЬBоАЭ circumferential peripapillary atrophy and вАШhistoвАЩ spots; оАЬCоАЭ linear streaks; оАЬDоАЭ macular neovascularization, showing a focal area of oedema and a few haemorrhages with a small вАШhistoвАЩ spot
temporally (arrow).

in areas of endemic histoplasmosis (e.g. the Mississippi river valley
in the USA). Causality has never been deоАЭnitively conоАЭrmed, but
over 90% react positively to histoplasmin testing. It is believed that
eye disease represents an immune-mediated response to microbial
antigen, rather than immediate damage due to active infection.

Ocular features

Sixty per cent have bilateral signs.
оАБ Presentation. POHS is usually asymptomatic unless macular
neovascularization supervenes. Signs may be discovered at a
routine eye examination.
оАБ Classic triad: (a) multiple white atrophic chorioretinal вАШhistoвАЩ
spots about 200 ќЉm in diameter (Fig. 12.49A); (b) peripapillary atrophy (Fig. 12.49B); (c) vitritis is absent. Linear midperipheral scars (Fig. 12.49C) also occur (5%).
оАБ Macular neovascularization (MNV) is a late manifestation occurring in less than 5% of aоАЫected eyes. It is usually
associated with a pre-existing macular histo spot. Associated
subretinal оАЮuid and haemorrhage lead to a fall in vision (Fig.
12.49D).

оАБ Acute chorioretinitis is almost always asymptomatic and
rarely identiоАЭed, but discrete ovalвАУround whitish lesions <400
ќЉm in diameter that may develop into classic punched-out
histo spots have been described.

Investigations

Skin testing is no longer undertaken.
оАБ HLA testing. POHS is associated with HLA-B7 and DRw2.
оАБ Serological testing is helpful if positive, but is usually negative
in the absence of systemic mycosis.
оАБ FA and OCT when MNV is suspected.

Treatment

Spontaneous regression of MNV may occasionally occur, but without treatment 60% of eyes with MNV have a оАЭnal visual acuity of
less than 6/60.
оАБ Intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF)
injection for MNV.
оАБ Amsler grid testing of the fellow eye at least weekly, particularly if a macular histo spot is present (25% risk of MNV).

482

Fungal Uveitis

A B

C D

Fig. 12.50 Candida endophthalmitis. оАЬAоАЭ Severe vitritis; оАЬBоАЭ вАШcotton ballвАЩ colonies; оАЬCоАЭ focal
chorioretinitis; оАЬDоАЭ retinal necrosis.

Endogenous Candida endophthalmitis

Introduction

Candida (usually the commensal C. albicans) can be introduced
into the eye from the external environment by trauma or surgery
or can spread from fungal keratitis, but endogenous infection is
an important alternative route. Risk factors for metastatic spread
include intravenous drug abuse, a septic focus associated with an
indwelling catheter, chronic lung disease such as cystic оАЭbrosis, general debilitation and diabetes. It is relatively uncommon in AIDS.

TIP Candida endophthalmitis is often acquired from an
endogenous source like an indwelling catheter or secondary to
intravenous drug abuse.

Clinical features

оАБ Presentation. Systemic candidiasis may already have been
diagnosed. Up to a third of patients with untreated candid

aemia will develop ocular involvement. Peripheral fundus
lesions may cause little or no visual disturbance while central
lesions or severe vitritis will manifest earlier. Progression is
typically much slower than bacterial endophthalmitis. Bilateral involvement is common.
оАБ Anterior uveitis is uncommon or mild in early disease but
may become prominent later.
оАБ Vitritis. May be marked (Fig. 12.50A), with оАЮuоАЫy вАШcotton ballвАЩ
(Fig. 12.50B) or вАШstring of pearlsвАЩ colonies, sometimes progressing to abscess formation.
оАБ Chorioretinitis: one or more small creamy white lesions with
overlying vitritis (Fig. 12.50C). Retinal necrosis (Fig. 12.50D)
may lead to retinal detachment, with severe proliferative
vitreoretinopathy.

Investigation

оАБ Vitreous biopsy (preferably using a vitreous cutter rather than
a needle) to identify the organism (PCR and culture) and identify sensitivities.
оАБ Systemic investigation, e.g. blood and urine cultures.

CHAPTER

Uveitis 483
#### 12

A B

C D

Fig. 12.51 Fungal infection with secondary uveitis. оАЬAоАЭ Multifocal choroidal pneumocystosis
lesions; оАЬBоАЭ large coalescent pneumocystosis lesion; оАЬCоАЭ multifocal cryptococcal choroiditis;
оАЬDоАЭ macular disease in Aspergillus infection. (Courtesy of S Mitchell вАУ оАЯg. A; A Curi вАУ оАЯgs C
and D.)

Treatment

оАБ Antifungal treatment. оАЯe agent should be chosen with local
microbiological specialist guidance. Infectious Diseases Society
of America guidelines suggest intravenous amphotericin-B in
combination with oral оАЮucytosine, but resistance is a concern.
Voriconazole orally or intravenously has a broad spectrum of
antifungal action with low reported resistance and high ocular
penetration. Adjunctive intravitreal treatment may be given
(100 ќЉg in 0.1 ml), with serial injections probably needed.
оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy should be considered at an early stage,
especially for severe or unresponsive disease. As well as providing a substantial culture specimen, it reduces fungal and
antigen load, facilitates therapeutic agent penetration and
clears the ocular media.

Pneumocystis choroiditis

оАЯe fungus Pneumocystis jirovecii, a pulmonary commensal,
is a major cause of mortality in uncontrolled AIDS. Systemic

antimicrobial prophylaxis has replaced pulmonary-only preventative treatment with inhaled pentamidine and along with immune
reconstitution has dramatically reduced the incidence of Pneumocystis choroiditis. Multiple slowly progressing deep round yellowвАУorange lesions (Fig. 12.51A and B), commonly bilateral, are
characteristic. оАЯere is minimal vitritis and visual loss is oоАЬen
negligible.

Cryptococcus choroiditis

Cryptococcus neoformans, a dimorphic yeast, enters the body
through inhalation and can spread to the eye in the bloodstream
or from the CNS via the optic nerve. As with pneumocystosis,
cryptococcosis was formerly responsible for much morbidity, but
clinically signiоАЭcant infection is now much less common since the
advent of more eоАЫective therapy for AIDS. Ocular involvement
may occur directly (e.g. multifocal choroiditis вАУ Fig. 12.51C) with
vasculitis and exudate or more commonly indirectly with papilloedema and ocular motility dysfunction.

484 Bacterial Uveitis

Aspergillus endophthalmitis

Aspergillus species are common environmental fungi, but cause
disease in humans less commonly than Candida. Spores undergo
airborne spread and risk factors for infection include intravenous
drug abuse, chronic lung disease, organ transplantation and blood
disorders; neutropenia may be of particular importance. Iridocyclitis and vitritis are common. Yellowish retinal and subretinal
inоАЭltrates tend towards macular involvement (Fig. 12.51D) at an
earlier stage than Candida infection. оАЯe disease progresses more
rapidly and the visual outcome is oоАЬen worse. Occlusive retinal
vasculitis is common. Systemic assessment is critical; endocarditis
presents a particular risk. Investigation and treatment are similar
to that of Candida endophthalmitis.

Coccidioidomycosis

Coccidioides immitis acquired by inhalation usually causes a mild
pulmonary infection, but wider systemic involvement can occur,
and reinfection can lead to chronic lung disease. Ocular features
include severe granulomatous anterior uveitis and multifocal choroiditis. Investigation and treatment are similar to that of Candida
endophthalmitis.

BACTERIAL UVEITIS

Tuberculosis
Introduction

Tuberculosis (TB) is a chronic granulomatous infection usually
caused in humans by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. TB is primarily a pulmonary disease but may spread by the bloodstream
to other sites. Ocular involvement commonly occurs without
clinically overt systemic disease. Immune deоАЭciency is a risk
factor, when atypical mycobacteria such as M. avium may cause
disease.

Ocular features

оАБ Anterior uveitis is common and is usually granulomatous. Iris
nodules may be present and broad PS may form.
оАБ Vitritis is very common and may be secondary to anterior,
intermediate or posterior primary foci. Macular complications include cystoid oedema and epiretinal membrane
formation.
оАБ Choroidal granuloma (tubercle): focal elevated dome-shaped
lesions (Fig. 12.52A) that may be unilateral or bilateral and
solitary or multiple (Fig. 12.52B). Extensive inоАЭltration may
occur in individuals with AIDS. A large abscess-like tubercle is
termed a tuberculoma.
оАБ Choroiditis independent of tubercles, typically multifocal and in
a centrifugally spreading serpiginous pattern (serpiginoid), has
increasingly been recognized (Fig. 12.53A and B). Choroiditis
that tracks retinal vessels may have reasonable speciоАЭcity for TB.
оАБ Retinal vasculitis is preferentially venous. Retinal haemorrhages are common. Vascular occlusion with extensive

ischaemia (Fig. 12.54A and B) and preretinal or disc neovascularization can occur. It is hypothesized that at least some
cases of Eales disease (see Ch. 13) represent a hypersensitivity
reaction to TB.
оАБ Other manifestations include reddish-brown eyelid nodules
(lupus vulgaris), conjunctivitis, phlyctenulosis, interstitial
keratitis, scleritis, exudative retinal detachment and optic neuropathy including neuroretinitis.

Investigation

оАЯe diagnosis is oоАЬen made clinically, considering evidence of previous TB exposure and other negative investigations. A systemic
assessment by an appropriate specialist should be arranged.
оАБ A tuberculin skin test may show a positive result within 48
hours.
оАБ T-SPOT.TB is a blood test where the number of antimycobacterial eоАЫector T cells that produce interferon-gamma are
counted. Because the test does not rely on the production of an
antibody response, it can detect latent tuberculosis. оАЯe prevalence of tuberculosis in a given community inоАЮuences the predictive value of the test: a sensitivity of 81% and a speciоАЭcity of
86% has been reported.
оАБ Other investigations include sputum testing with PCR and
the interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) blood test. оАЯis is
approximately as sensitive as skin testing (80% in active disease) but has the advantage of being independent of previous
BCG vaccination. HIV status must be determined. Chest X-ray,
CT, positron emission tomography (PET)/CT are among other
tests that may be considered.
оАБ Ocular. Aqueous or vitreous sampling rarely yields
demonstrable (smear вАУ acid-fast bacilli on ZiehlвАУNeelsen
staining вАУ or culture вАУ LowensteinвАУJensen medium) mycobacteria. PCR is highly specific but of variable sensitivity.
OCT is useful for macular evaluation. Fluorescein angiography may be helpful in establishing whether choroiditis is
active, as well as confirming preretinal neovascularization
and demonstrating ischaemia. FAF allows activity staging,
lesions becoming progressively hypoautofluorescent with
healing.

Treatment

оАБ Prolonged multi-drug therapy (oоАЬen four initially) should be
prescribed and monitored by a specialist with experience in
systemic TB management. If ethambutol is used, monitoring
for optic neuropathy should take place. Rifabutin can cause
anterior uveitis. Non-adherence to treatment is common.
Treatment with TB medication in patients with uveitis associated with latent TB halves the risk of uveitis recurrence and
delays the onset of оАЭrst recurrence.
оАБ Topical and systemic steroids may be used concomitantly
to reduce inоАЮammation-induced damage, particularly in the
early weeks of treatment, when they may retard paradoxical
worsening of the fundus appearance.
оАБ Laser may be applied to ischaemic retina to treat preretinal
neovascularization.

486 Bacterial Uveitis

Acquired syphilis

Introduction

Syphilis is caused by the spirochaete bacterium Treponema pallidum. In adults the disease is usually sexually acquired when
organisms enter through a skin or mucous membrane abrasion.
Transmission by kissing, blood transfusion or percutaneous injury
is rare. Transplacental infection of the fetus can also occur, when
the mother has become infected during or shortly before pregnancy (congenital syphilis вАУ see Ch. 7).

Systemic features

оАЯe natural history of untreated syphilis is variable.
оАБ Primary syphilis is characterized by a painless ulcer (chancre), commonly on the genitalia or anus.
оАБ Secondary syphilis consists of a maculopapular rash (Fig.
12.55) and other systemic features.
оАБ Latent syphilis.
оАБ Tertiary syphilis occurs in about 40% of untreated cases and is
characterized by cardiovascular manifestations such as aortitis,
neurosyphilis and gummatous inоАЭltration of bone and viscera.

Ocular features

оАБ Anterior uveitis occurs in about 4% of patients with secondary syphilis. It may be granulomatous or non-granulomatous
and is bilateral in 50%. Roseolae (Fig. 12.56A) are dilated iris
capillaries that may develop into yellowish nodules. IOP may
be elevated.

оАБ Chorioretinitis can be localized (Fig. 12.56B) but is oоАЬen multifocal, bilateral and associated with vitritis and exudative retinal detachment can ensue.
оАБ Acute syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinopathy
(ASPPC) is characterized by large pale-yellowish subretinal
lesions in the posterior pole (Fig. 12.56C). It is thought to be
a secondary infection of the choriocapillaris and occurs more
commonly in the immunocompromised.
оАБ Retinitis has a вАШground glassвАЩ appearance. Associated vasculitis may be occlusive and involve both arteries and veins. оАЯis
heals leaving diоАЫuse chorioretinal scars (Fig. 12.56D).
оАБ Optic neuritis and neuroretinitis.
оАБ Other features include conjunctivitis, episcleritis and scleritis,
IU, glaucoma, cataract and miscellaneous neuro-ophthalmic
features related to CNS involvement including Argyll Robertson pupils (see Ch. 19).

Investigation

оАБ Serology is the mainstay and is discussed under Investigations
earlier in this chapter.
оАБ Systemic assessment by an appropriate specialist, including
lumbar puncture to rule out neurosyphilis. HIV status should
be established.
оАБ Aqueous and/or vitreous sampling for PCR is sometimes
indicated, such as when HIV positivity makes serology less
reliable.

Treatment

оАЯis should be under the supervision of a specialist in infectious
diseases, but usually consists of an extended course of parenteral
penicillin. Alternatives may be required, for instance in penicillin allergy, but are less eоАЫective and parallel conоАЭrmatory allergen testing and desensitization may be considered. Topical and
systemic steroids may be given in conjunction with antibiotics to
ameliorate inоАЮammatory damage. оАЯe JarischвАУHerxheimer reaction is a systemic response to treponemal antigens released on
commencement of therapy and may include progression of ocular
signs.

TIP Commencement of treatment for syphilis can cause an
exacerbation of ocular symptoms and signs.

Lyme disease

Introduction

Lyme disease (borreliosis), like syphilis, is caused by a spirochaete. оАЯe disease is endemic in regions of North America,
Europe and Asia, but can be diоАЪcult to diagnose. оАЯe responsible
organism, Borrelia burgdorferi, is transmitted through tick bites,
with deer being an important vector (Fig. 12.57A). An adult tick
(Fig. 12.57B) can be distinguished from a head louse by the tick
having eight legs вАУ it is an arachnid вАУ and the louse six (though
Fig. 12.55 Maculopapular rash in secondary disease in larval ticks have only six). Several days aоАЬer a bite an annular skin
acquired syphilis. lesion, erythema chronicum migrans (Fig. 12.57C), forms at the

CHAPTER

Uveitis 487
#### 12

A B

C D

Fig. 12.56 Ocular syphilis. оАЬAоАЭ Roseolae; оАЬBоАЭ focal inоАЮltration with macular star; оАЬCоАЭ acute
posterior placoid chorioretinitis; оАЬDоАЭ old multifocal chorioretinitis.

site in 60вАУ80%, oоАЬen accompanied by constitutional symptoms
(stage 1). Neurological (e.g. cranial nerve palsies, meningitis),
cardiac (4вАУ8% e.g. arrhythmia) and other manifestations may
follow within a few weeks. Late (stage 3) complications include
chronic arthritis of large joints, polyneuropathy and encephalopathy. Some patients develop chronic symptoms that may not
respond to antibiotics.

Ocular features

оАЯese are varied and tend to occur in established stage 2 and stage
3 (late) disease.
оАБ Uveitis is relatively uncommon but can be anterior (granulomatous or non-granulomatous), intermediate (the most common), or posterior including multifocal choroiditis, vasculitis
and neuroretinitis.
оАБ Other manifestations include early (stage 1) transient conjunctivitis, bilateral stromal keratitis, episcleritis (Fig. 12.57D),
scleritis, orbital myositis, optic neuritis, papilloedema and
ocular motor and facial nerve palsy (up to 25% of facial nerve
palsy in endemic areas).

Investigations

Serology should be performed at least a month aоАЬer infection, but
false positives can occur. PCR of CSF or synovial оАЮuid is performed
in appropriate cases. Diagnosis relies on a combination of clinical
features and positive serology. Co-infection with other tick-borne
diseases is possible.

TIP Lyme disease can be diоАЬcult to diagnose in the early
stages and many of the later symptoms are non-speciоАЮc
in nature. The diagnosis depends on a history of exposure
to ticks and a combination of clinical features and positive
serology.

Treatment

Treatment of early acute disease is highly eоАЫective and involves
oral doxycycline (not children or in pregnancy), amoxicillin or erythromycin. Untreated, many patients with early or
