asymptomatic disease will experience no further problems.
Patients with established disease, including ocular, may require
extended intravenous penicillin or ceоАЬriaxone. Keratitis and
uveitis may need steroid treatment. Systemic steroids should
not be given without concomitant antibiotics. Appropriate personal protection (clothing, insect repellent) should be adopted
in endemic areas to reduce the risk of tick bite. Lyme disease
vaccine is available, but does not oоАЫer complete or prolonged
protection.

Brucellosis

Brucellosis is caused by the Gram-negative bacteria Brucella melitensis and B. abortus. It is usually transmitted from animals to man
through milk products or uncooked meat. A variety of constitutional and ocular (20%) features are reported, the latter including
chronic anterior and posterior uveitis, papilloedema and retinal
haemorrhages. Investigation should include serology and blood
culture. Treatment is with a combination of two antibiotics, e.g.
streptomycin and doxycycline, with adjunctive steroids if required.
Patients with known brucellosis should undergo ophthalmic
examination.

Endogenous bacterial endophthalmitis

Introduction

оАЯis is an intraocular infection that originates from sources within
the body. оАЯe incidence has increased in recent years. A wide
range of organisms can be responsible вАУ Gram-positives predominate in North America and Europe and Gram-negatives in East
Asia. Risk factors include debilitating disease of many types as
well as other factors such as intravenous drug abuse (where Bacillus species predominate). Spread can occur from any potential
focus such as an indwelling catheter, tooth abscess, endocarditis
(especially Streptococcus), urinary tract infection (especially E.
coli) or septic joint. Patients are usually systemically unwell and
mortality is relatively high вАУ 5вАУ10%. оАЯe two systemic diseases
that confer an increase in mortality are pneumonia and renal/UTI
infection, mainly because these infections are caused by virulent
organisms. оАЯe visual prognosis is poorer than with postoperative
endophthalmitis.

Ocular features

Diagnosis is frequently delayed. оАЯe main distinction is from
endogenous fungal endophthalmitis.

оАБ Symptoms: blurred vision, pain and redness.
оАБ Signs are broadly similar to those of postoperative endophthalmitis (see Ch. 10), though retinal inоАЭltrates may be an
early feature, reоАЮecting the route of infection.

Investigation

оАЯe search for a septic focus (blood and urine cultures, septic
arthritis, endocarditis, lumbar puncture etc.) should be in collaboration with an appropriate physician. Aqueous and vitreous
samples should be taken for microscopy and culture.

Treatment

оАБ Systemic infection is treated with intravenous antibiotics according to clinical suspicion and local microbiological advice. Broad spectrum empirical treatment may be
necessary.
оАБ Endophthalmitis is treated with intravitreal and an oral
оАЮuoroquinolone. оАЯe place of systemic steroids is undeоАЭned. Pars plana vitrectomy may improve prognosis in some
patients.

TIP Systemic antibiotics are key to the treatment of
endogenous endophthalmitis, in contrast to postoperative
endophthalmitis. The search for a septic focus should be made
in collaboration with an appropriate physician.

CatоАЮscratch disease

Introduction

Cat-scratch disease (bartonellosis) is caused by Bartonella henselae, a Gram-negative rod. Infection is usually transmitted by the
scratch (or bite) of an apparently healthy cat, though feline contact is not always described. One or more red papules at the site of
inoculation are followed by fever and regional lymphadenopathy

CHAPTER

Uveitis 489
#### 12

(Fig. 12.58A), though general symptoms are frequently absent.
Severe systemic disease occasionally occurs. оАЯe visual prognosis
is usually reasonable.

Ocular features

оАЯe eyes are aоАЫected in 5вАУ10%. Neuroretinitis (see Ch. 19) is the
most common manifestation and consists of disc oedema with
macular exudate in a star conformation (Fig. 12.58B); focal retinochoroiditis, vasculitis and, rarely, an optic nerve head angiomatous
lesion have been reported. Conjunctivitis with a 2вАУ4 mm conjunctival granuloma and associated pre-auricular lymphadenopathy
(Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome) may occasionally be seen. It
is unusual for both eyes to be involved.

Investigation

Includes serology for B. henselae.

Treatment

Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 4вАУ6 weeks should be used in
immunocompetent adults. Oral erythromycin can be used in children. Other oral antibiotics, e.g. co-trimoxazole, azithromycin,
rifampicin or ciproоАЮoxacin are eоАЫective. Steroids have been used
in some cases. Treatment is not usually given for mild systemic
symptoms alone.

Leprosy

Introduction

Leprosy (Hansen disease) is a chronic granulomatous infection caused by Mycobacterium leprae and M. lepromatosis. The
mode of spread remains uncertain, though nasal secretions
have been implicated. Infection is thought to lead to a chronic
immune response that leads to the peripheral neuropathy that
is the primary pathogenetic mechanism. Genetic factors are

A B

Fig. 12.58 Cat-scratch disease. (AоАЭ Ulcerated papule on the cheek caused by a cat scratch 2
weeks previously, with enlargement of submandibular lymph nodes; (BоАЭ neuroretinitis, manifesting with a partial macular star. (Courtesy of BJ Zitelli and HW Davis, from Atlas of Pediatric
Physical Diagnosis, Mosby 2002 вАУ оАЯg. A.)

490 Bacterial Uveitis

probably important вАУ only 5% of the general population is
thought to be vulnerable to leprosy on substantial contact with
the organism.

Systemic features

оАБ Tuberculoid (вАШpaucibacillaryвАЩ in WHO classiоАЭcation).
One or more hypopigmented macules and anaesthetic skin
patches.
оАБ Borderline (multibacillary). оАЯe most common form. Similar to tuberculoid, but more numerous and extensive lesions.
оАБ Lepromatous (multibacillary). Widespread cutaneous thickening with leonine facial appearance (Fig. 12.59A), peripheral
plaques and nodules, upper respiratory tract involvement and
peripheral nerve lesions facilitating trauma that may result in
shortening and loss of digits.

Ocular features

Ocular signs are principally due to direct bacterial invasion.
оАБ Keratitis: thickened, beaded corneal nerves, punctate subepithelial lesions, pannus and vascularization (Fig. 12.59B).

оАБ Anterior uveitis: chronic and low-grade and is classically
described as вАШplasmoidвАЩ (prominent оАЭbrin).
оАБ Miosis and iris atrophy (Fig. 12.59C) result from impaired
dilator pupillae innervation.
оАБ Iris pearls (pathognomonic): usually under 0.5 mm in diameter (Fig. 12.59D).
оАБ Other features: episcleritis and scleritis, retinal pearls, uveal
eоАЫusion, cataract, glaucoma, decreased corneal sensation,
facial nerve palsy, eyelid deformities and phthisis bulbi.

Investigation

Skin and occasionally ocular specimens show acid-fast bacilli. оАЯe lepromin test distinguishes between tuberculoid and lepromatous leprosy.

Treatment

оАБ Systemic. Combination regimens of extended duration with
antibiotics such as dapsone, rifampicin and clofazimine. BCG
vaccination oоАЫers some protection.
оАБ Ocular. Anterior uveitis is treated with steroids. SpeciоАЭc complications are addressed as indicated.

C D

MISCELLANEOUS IDIOPATHIC
CHORIORETINOPATHIES

Introduction

оАЯe term вАШwhite dot syndromesвАЩ has been used in the past to
describe several diоАЫerent idiopathic inоАЮammatory conditions of
the posterior pole. оАЯese conditions tend to occur between the
second to sixth decades of life, are more likely to aоАЫect women
than men, usually have a self-limiting course and the visual
prognosis depends on the condition. However, the term is considered by many uveitis specialists to be outdated because the
white dots are a variable оАЭnding and the diseases are not driven
by the same pathophysiological processes. Patients with other
entities may present with similar clinical manifestations and it
is critical to exclude alternative diagnoses, including infection
and neoplasia. Recent advances in the оАЭeld of ocular imaging
have allowed better characterization of these conditions. Indocyanine green angiography enables two main disease categories
to be recognized: (a) choriocapillaritis and (b) stromal choroiditis (Table 12.10).

Multiple evanescent white dot
syndrome оАЬMEWDSоАЭ

Introduction

MEWDS is an uncommon idiopathic disease of acute onset, typically occurring in young adult females, with 25вАУ50% describing

Uveitis 491
#### 12

a preceding viral-like illness. InоАЮuenza vaccination has been
reported to occasionally trigger the disease. оАЯe exact pathogenesis is not known, although immune-induced inоАЮammation
of the choriocapillaris has been suspected. оАЯe condition can be
diоАЪcult to diagnose as the characteristic fundal white dots disappear fairly soon aоАЬer the symptoms start (Fig. 12.60A). Multimodal imaging is key to making the diagnosis, as changes that
are not clinically apparent are obvious on FAF, OCT and ICG
angiography.

Clinical features

оАБ Presentation. Common: painless monocular blurring (6/9вАУ
6/60), subjective scotomas and photopsia. Less common: оАЮoaters, dyschromatopsia.
оАБ Subtle posterior vitritis in 50%.
оАБ Posterior pole lesions. Numerous small (100вАУ300 ќЉm), illdeоАЭned, deep greyвАУwhite patches sparing the fovea, which
has a characteristic orange granular appearance and a dulled
reоАЮex.
оАБ Optic disc oedema is occasionally present.
оАБ Recovery occurs over weeks, oоАЬen leaving subtle residual
signs. Recurrence occasionally (10%) occurs. If there is recurrence, idiopathic multifocal choroiditis (see below) should be
suspected, as these two diseases can overlap.

Investigation

оАБ Visual оАЭelds. оАЯe blind spot is commonly enlarged, with a
temporal оАЭeld defect.
оАБ FA shows subtle early hyperоАЮuorescence in the areas corresponding to white dots, with late staining (Fig. 12.60B). Vessel
wall leakage and disc staining may be seen.
оАБ FAF reveals hyperautoоАЮuorescent spots during active inоАЮammation which correspond to the macular lesions (Fig. 12.60C).
FAF has been used to demonstrate subclinical lesions in
patients with only foveal granularity.
оАБ ICGA shows hypoоАЮuorescent spots in the late angiographic
phase (Fig. 12.60D) that are oоАЬen more numerous than visible
clinically or on FA. Some authorities suggest that this could
be a sign of оАЮow void at the level of the choriocapillaris, while
others interpret this as outer retinal involvement leading to
reduced late uptake of ICG by the RPE.
оАБ OCT shows inner-segment/outer-segment junction (ellipsoid zone) disruption (Fig. 12.60E). It is sometimes associated
with reоАЮective focal lesions that cross the external limiting
membrane.

Treatment

оАЯis is generally not required as the symptoms and signs start
to improve spontaneously in most cases by 8вАУ10 weeks (Fig.
12.60F).

Acute idiopathic blind spot
enlargement syndrome оАЬAIBSEоАЭ

AIBSE is a rare condition reported in young to middle-aged women
and is likely to be a variant of MEWDS. Features include photopsia
and decreased vision, with blind spot enlargement and mild disc swelling. Recovery to normal or near-normal without treatment is usual.

Acute posterior multifocal placoid
pigment epitheliopathy оАЬAPMPPEоАЭ

Introduction

APMPPE is an uncommon bilateral idiopathic inоАЮammatory
disorder. It aоАЫects young to middle-aged adults of both genders
equally. оАЯere is a viral prodrome in one-third and it is speculated
to occur as a result of cell-mediated immunity to viral antigen. оАЯe
exact pathogenesis is not known, but it has been suggested that
inоАЮammation at the level of the choriocapillaris results in hypoperfusion and ischaemia of the RPE and photoreceptors. Associated cerebral vasculitis can occur but is uncommon and can cause
stroke. Erythema nodosum and other systemic manifestations of
vasculitis have been reported. оАЯe clinical picture of APMPPE can
be mimicked by other entities, such as sarcoidosis and tuberculosis.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Subacute moderate visual impairment with central/paracentral scotomas and photopsia. If the macula is
involved the visual acuity can deteriorate (ranging from 6/12
to 6/60). оАЯe fellow eye is aоАЫected within a few days or weeks.
Headache and other neurological symptoms are common and
can commence many months aоАЬer ocular disease onset.
оАБ Anterior uveitis and vitritis are usually very mild.
оАБ Fundus. Multiple large deep yellowвАУwhite placoid lesions, initially
at the posterior pole (Fig. 12.61A). Within weeks the majority
fade, with residual RPE disturbance of varying severity. Subretinal
macular оАЮuid may be seen. Vasculitis and papillitis are rare.
оАБ Prognosis. оАЯe visual symptoms and retinal appearance typically improve within weeks and the long-term prognosis for
most patients is good. However, there appears to be a subgroup
of patients whose symptoms may recur and who have a longer
period of disease activity. In 25% visual recovery is limited to
6/15 or worse as a consequence of RPE and photoreceptor damage to the fovea. Factors that predict a poor prognosis include:
(a) age older than 60 years, (b) unilaterality, (c) an interval
before involvement of the second eye of at least 6 months, (d)
disease recurrence, (e) leakage from choroidal veins.

Investigation

оАБ Alternative diagnoses should be excluded.
оАБ HLA-B7 and HLA-DR2 are associated in a substantial proportion of patients.
оАБ FA of active lesions shows early dense hypoоАЮuorescence
(blockage) (Fig. 12.61B) and late hyperоАЮuorescent staining
(Fig. 12.61C). Late hyperоАЮuorescence may result from vascular
leakage, which resolves in healed lesions.

CHAPTER

Uveitis 493
#### 12

оАБ FAF shows scattered hypoautoоАЮuorescence surrounded by
hyperautoоАЮuorescence corresponding to placoid lesions (Fig.
12.61D).
оАБ ICGA: active placoid lesions manifest as early and late hypoоАЮuorescence secondary to poor choriocapillaris perfusion
(Fig. 12.61E).
оАБ OCT of the macula shows focal disruption of the outer retina
(Fig. 12.61F).
оАБ OCT angiography (OCTA) may show abnormal оАЮow in the
choriocapillaris.
оАБ CNS imaging and lumbar puncture should be performed
in patients with neurological symptoms to rule out retinal
vasculitis.

Treatment

Treatment is not usually required, but steroids should be considered for patients with macular involvement. Steroids and possibly
ciclosporin may be given for cerebral vasculitis. Patients should be
instructed to seek medical advice urgently if neurological symptoms occur.

Serpiginous choroidopathy

Introduction

Serpiginous choroidopathy (choroiditis) is usually bilateral, though
asymmetrical. InоАЮammation occurs in the outer retina and choriocapillaris. It typically occurs in middle age, aоАЫects men more frequently than women and is associated with HLA-B7. оАЯe disease is
generally recurrent over years, with a relatively poor prognosis and
almost 25% of aоАЫected eyes have a оАЭnal visual acuity of less than
6/60. Tuberculosis (see Fig. 12.53) and syphilis (see Fig. 12.56D)
can occasionally result in a similar clinical picture (вАШserpiginoidвАЩ),
which can pose a diagnostic challenge, as the therapeutic approach
to these diseases is signiоАЭcantly diоАЫerent.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Initially unilateral blurring of central vision, scotoma or metamorphopsia.
оАБ Anterior uveitis and vitritis are common but usually mild.
оАБ Fundus. Active lesions (Fig. 12.62A) are greyвАУwhite and
may remain active for several months before becoming
scalloped and atrophic. оАЯe disease typically starts around
the optic disc and extends gradually (Fig. 12.62B), though
a variant starting at the central macula (5%) is recognized.
Recurrence is usually contiguous with or adjacent to existing
areas, eventually resulting in extensive chorioretinal atrophy
(Fig. 12.62C).
оАБ Complications. MNV (15вАУ35%), subretinal оАЭbrosis, preretinal neovascularization.

Investigation

оАБ Tuberculosis should be excluded, especially in endemic areas.
Test for syphilis.
оАБ FAF shows the extent of the inоАЮammation. Active lesions
show a hyperautoоАЮuorescent halo on the edges of areas of

hypoautoоАЮuorescence (Fig. 12.62D). Inactive lesions are dark
and uniformly hypoautoоАЮuorescent. It is a useful technique to
monitor disease progression.
оАБ FA of active lesions shows early hypoоАЮuorescence and late

оАБ ICGA of active lesions reveals marked hypoоАЮuorescence
throughout all phases of the angiogram (Fig. 12.62F)

Treatment

Oral or intravenous steroids may control activity. A variety of
immunosuppressives and inоАЮiximab may be eоАЫective, alone or in
combination.

Relentless placoid chorioretinitis оАЬRPCоАЭ

оАЯis refers to a rare entity showing features of both APMPPE and
serpiginous choroiditis and is also referred to as ampiginous choroiditis. In contrast to APMPPE which is limited to the posterior
pole, RPC is characterized by the presence of hundreds of lesions,
located anteriorly and posterior to the equator. оАЯe clinical course
is oоАЬen prolonged and relapsing.

Persistent placoid maculopathy оАЬPPMоАЭ

оАЯis rare condition features lesions similar to those of the macular
variant of serpiginous choroidopathy, but which generally behave
in a more benign fashion unless complicated by MNV (common).

Idiopathic multifocal choroiditis оАЬMFCоАЭ

Introduction

MFC is an uncommon, usually bilateral but asymmetrical, chronic/
recurrent disease that typically aоАЫects young and middle-aged adult
women. Many people present with оАЮu-like symptoms before the
onset of ocular disease. оАЯe severity and prognosis are very variable.

оАЯe aetiology is unknown and the condition is diagnosed clinically aоАЬer systemic and infectious disease (e.g. tuberculosis, West
Nile virus choroiditis, Candida) has been excluded.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Blurring, оАЮoaters and photopsia.
оАБ Anterior uveitis (50%).
оАБ Vitritis.
оАБ Fundus. Multiple discrete, ovoid, yellowish-grey lesions
50вАУ350 ќЉm in diameter at the posterior pole and/or periphery, sometimes with linear clusters and/or streaks. Inactive
lesions have sharply deоАЭned margins and pigmented borders
resembling POHS (Fig. 12.63A). Peripapillary atrophy may be
seen. оАЯe course is prolonged with the development of new
lesions and recurrent inоАЮammatory episodes. MNV occurs
in 25вАУ35%, CMO and subretinal оАЭbrosis resembling SFU (see
below) can develop.
оАБ Optic disc oedema and blind spot enlargement may be
present.

Investigation

оАБ Visual оАЭelds may show large defects not corresponding to the
examination оАЭndings.
оАБ FA. Early hypoоАЮuorescence and late hyperоАЮuorescence. Old
inactive lesions show window defects. If a neovascular membrane develops in the macular region, hyperоАЮuorescence is
seen due to leakage (Fig. 12.63B).
оАБ ICGA shows hypoоАЮuorescent acute lesions which may not
be clinically apparent. Old lesions remain hypoоАЮuorescent
throughout.
оАБ OCT shows elevation of the RPE with sub-RPE material of
intermediate reоАЮectivity and surrounding disruption of the
outer retina.
оАБ OCTA shows well-demarcated areas of reduced оАЮow at the
level of the choriocapillaris.
оАБ ERG remains normal until there is advanced retinal atrophy.

A B

Fig. 12.63 Idiopathic multifocal choroiditis. оАЬAоАЭ Inactive lesions; оАЬBоАЭ late venous phase оАЭuorescein angiogram showing hyperоАЭuorescence at the fovea due to leakage from neovascularization in the macula.

Treatment

Systemic and local steroids. Steroid-resistant patients require
immunosuppressive therapy (cyclosporine or tacrolimus, together
with mycophenolate mofetil). MNV is treated with steroids and
anti-VEGF agents.

Punctate inner choroidopathy оАЬPICоАЭ

Introduction

PIC typically aоАЫects young myopic women. Both eyes are involved
in 80%, but the involvement is usually asymmetrical. It is likely
to be a form of idiopathic multifocal choroiditis, but involvement
predominantly involves the macula.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Blurring, scotoma, оАЮoaters and photopsia.
оАБ Anterior uveitis and vitritis are usually absent or very mild.
оАБ Fundus. Several small yellowвАУwhite macular spots with fuzzy
borders at the level of the inner choroid and retina (Fig. 12.64A),
sometimes with an overlying serous sensory retinal detachment. оАЯese evolve into sharply demarcated atrophic scars with
little pigmentation, similar to the histo spots of POHS.
оАБ Prognosis. Central vision may be compromised by a lesion
at the fovea or, more commonly, by MNV (up to 40% вАУ Fig.
12.64B and C). Visual acuity improves to 6/7.5 in 50% of cases.

Investigation

оАБ Visual оАЭeld testing shows enlargement of the blind spot in
about 40% of cases and central and paracentral scotomas.
оАБ FAF shows hypoautoоАЮuorescent spots and areas of hyperautoоАЮuorescence (Fig. 12.64D)
оАБ FA shows early hyperоАЮuorescence and late staining of lesions
and demonstrates MNV (Fig. 12.64E).
оАБ OCTA/OCT shows submacular MNV (Fig. 12.64F) and acute
hyper-reоАЮective material with disruption of the outer retina in
the acute stage.
оАБ ICG shows multiple mid-phase hypoоАЮuorescent lesions in the
posterior pole. Choroidal vessels may show localized points of
hyperоАЮuorescence situated close to the vessel walls, indicating
vasculitis of smaller choroidal vessels and choriocapillaris.

Treatment

оАБ No treatment is advised for most patients unless there is evidence of MNV.
оАБ Anti-VEGF medication should be used for choroidal neovascular formation in the macular region.
оАБ Steroids and systemic immunosuppressive therapy can be
helpful in the management of recurrent CMV.

Progressive subretinal оАЪbrosis and
uveitis syndrome оАЬSFUоАЭ

SFU, also known as diоАЫuse subretinal оАЭbrosis, is an extremely
rare chronic condition. It typically aоАЫects myopic young women,
causing gradual blurring of vision in one then both eyes. Anterior

CHAPTER

Uveitis 497
#### 12

uveitis and vitritis accompanies subretinal mounds at the posterior
pole and midperiphery (Fig. 12.65A) progressing to widespread
subretinal оАЭbrosis (Fig. 12.65B). Steroids may be eоАЫective early in
the disease, but the prognosis is poor. Some experts view SFU as
part of a spectrum with MFC/MCP and PIC.

Birdshot retinochoroidopathy

Introduction

Birdshot retinochoroidopathy (also known as birdshot uveitis) is an uncommon idiopathic inоАЮammatory disease usually
aоАЫecting middle-aged women. It is chronic, bilateral and manifests with severe retinitis and stromal choroiditis that are independent inоАЮammatory events. Nearly all patients are HLA-A29
positive.

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Insidious impairment of central vision associated
with оАЮoaters, nyctalopia, dyschromatopsia and photopsia.
оАБ Vitritis is present in either or both eyes.
оАБ Fundus. Multiple ill-deоАЭned ovoid cream-coloured choroidal patches, less than one disc diameter in size, in the posterior pole and midperiphery (Fig. 12.66A). оАЯe lesions oоАЬen
appear to radiate outward from the disc but usually spare
the macula itself and are more concentrated in the inferotemporal area. Inactive lesions consist of well-delineated
atrophic spots (Fig. 12.66B). CMO, epiretinal membrane and
MNV may develop.
оАБ Visual оАЭeld testing (not visual acuity) is the appropriate functional test for monitoring disease evolution and response to
treatment.
оАБ Prognosis. About one-third of patients have an eventual best
visual acuity of less than 6/60. However, results are starting to
improve with aggressive and sustained immunomodulatory
treatment. Choroiditis responds well to this treatment, but the
retina less so.

Investigation

оАБ HLA-A29: over 95% of patients are positive (versus 7% of the
general population).
оАБ OCT will conоАЭrm macular oedema.
оАБ AutoоАЯuorescence will generally show more numerous hypoautoоАЮuorescent lesions than are seen on examination.
оАБ FA shows extensive vascular leakage (Fig. 12.66C), with disc
and vessel staining, and can show diоАЫuse macular oedema.
Strikingly, the fovea is oоАЬen spared and true cystoid macular
oedema is less common (15%).
оАБ ICGA: lesions are more numerous. оАЯese are hypoоАЮuorescent
(Fig. 12.66D), persist into the late phases and do not correspond to the retinal lesions.
оАБ OCTA shows areas of оАЮow void in the choroidal stroma, with
initial sparing of the choriocapillaris. With progression of the
disease the choriocapillaris can be aоАЫected.
оАБ ERG is normal in early disease but with time shows rod and
cone abnormalities.

500 Other InоАЯammatory Conditions of the Posterior Pole

TIP HLA-A29 histocompatibility antigen is present in almost
all patients with birdshot retinochoroidopathy when PCR
methodology is used.

Treatment

оАБ оАЯe оАЭrst step is to use sub-Tenon triamcinolone acetonide (40
mg), particularly in patients with monocular disease.
оАБ Up to 90% of patients need systemic treatment. Early referral
to a uveitis specialist should be considered.
оАБ Systemic steroids are used initially, but immunomodulatory
treatment is oоАЬen needed (cyclosporine or mycophenolate followed by inоАЮiximab).

OTHER INFLAMMATORY CONDITIONS
OF THE POSTERIOR POLE

Acute macular neuroretinopathy оАЬAMNоАЭ

AMN is a rare self-limited condition that typically aоАЫects healthy
young adult women. оАЯe disease may aоАЫect one or both eyes and
may be preceded by a оАЮu-like illness. оАЯe incidence has increased
since the start of the COVID-19 pandemic. Symptoms consist of
decreased vision and paracentral scotomas. RedвАУbrown wedgeshaped lesions are observed in a оАЮower petal arrangement around
the centre of the macula corresponding to the scotomas. FA is normal or shows faint hypoоАЮuorescence. Acute AMN is most easily
identiоАЭed by wedge-shaped parafoveal lesions on infra-red imaging (Fig. 12.67A). OCT shows a characteristic hyperreоАЮectivity
that starts in the outer plexiform layer (Fig. 12.67B). Over time
the outer retinal layers become thinner and disruption occurs to
the outer segments and RPE. Symptoms and signs fade slowly over
months, but the scotoma oоАЬen remains.

Acute zonal occult outer retinopathy
оАЬAZOORоАЭ

Introduction

оАЯe acute zonal outer retinopathies (AZOR) are a group of rare
conditions characterized by acute visual symptoms in one or both
eyes of young or middle-aged women, some of whom have an
antecedent viral-like illness. Acute zonal occult outer retinopathy
(AZOOR) is the most common of the AZOR syndromes and is
characterized by minimal fundoscopic signs early in the disease
course. оАЯe other postulated members of the group have more evident оАЭndings. оАЯe pathogenesis is unknown.

Clinical features

оАБ Photopsia and mild vitritis are frequent and subtle vasculitis
is sometimes seen.
оАБ Visual оАЭeld. оАЯere is an acute onset of one or more zones of visual
оАЭeld loss. Field loss may progress and recovery is infrequent.
оАБ Later оАЭndings include RPE clumping and vascular attenuation in the involved area and peripapillary region, although
the fundus may remain normal.

A

B

Fig. 12.67 Acute macular neuroretinopathy. оАЬAоАЭ Infra-red image showing a parafoveal lesion (arrow); оАЬBоАЭ OCT showing
hyperreоАЭectivity in the outer retinal layers (arrow). (Courtesy
of P Charbel Issa.)

Investigations

оАБ FAF may demonstrate abnormalities (Fig. 12.68A) and helps
to deоАЭne the areas of retinal involvement. It is helpful to monitor for signs of progression.
оАБ OCT and FAF show similar signs to MEWDS, but the choriocapillaris is preserved and demonstrates no evidence of
hypofluorescence on ICGA. OCT shows loss of the outer
retina in one or more zones (Fig. 12.68B). Chronically,
atrophy of the RPE and thinning of the outer retinal layer
is found, followed in time by thinning of the inner retinal
layer.
оАБ ERG is a less important investigation these days, but characteristically shows a-wave and b-wave amplitude reduction and
delayed 30 Hz оАЮicker (cones tend to be aоАЫected more than
rods). Electro-oculography (EOG) shows absence or severe
reduction of the light rise.

Prognosis

оАБ Stabilization occurs within 6 months in up to 90% of patients.
Final visual acuity is 6/12 in at least one eye in most cases.
Recurrence is sometimes seen.

A

B

Fig. 12.68 AZOOR. оАЬAоАЭ FAF showing speckled hypoautoоАЭuorescence nasal to the disc, indicating RPE/choroidal atrophy.
Note the abrupt transition between abnormal and normal
retina (arrow heads showing hyperautoоАЭuorescence); оАЬBоАЭ
OCT image of оАЬAоАЭ showing abnormality of the ellipsoid and
interdigitation zones (arrows). (Courtesy of P Charbel Issa.)

Treatment

оАБ оАЮere is no proven treatment, although there are reports of
favourable outcomes from systemic steroids.

Acute idiopathic maculopathy оАЬAIMоАЭ

AIM is a rare, self-limited condition that is most frequently unilateral and may be preceded by a оАЮu-like illness. Patients are young
adults who describe sudden marked reduction in central vision.
A viral prodrome is common and associated systemic infections
have been noted. An irregularly yellow or grey exudative retinal
detachment is seen at the macula (Fig. 12.69A and B) associated
with small haemorrhages and papillitis. Within a few weeks the
exudative changes resolve (Fig. 12.69C). An irregular bullвАЩs eye
appearance may develop following resolution and can be associated with persistent visual loss. OCT shows hyper-reоАЮectivity at
the photoreceptor outer-segment layer and RPE thickening. FAF
shows stippled hyperautoоАЮuorescence that becomes hypoоАЮuorescent. FA shows early irregular mild hyperоАЮuorescence at the

CHAPTER

Uveitis 501
#### 12

detachment, with subsequent intense staining (Fig. 12.69D). No
treatment is needed.

Acute multifocal retinitis

Acute multifocal retinitis is a very rare self-limited condition that
may be preceded by a оАЮu-like illness, which is possibly an atypical presentation of cat-scratch disease. It causes sudden onset mild
visual loss in young adults. Multiple areas of retinitis are seen
posterior to the equator (Fig. 12.70) along with mild vitritis, disc
oedema and sometimes a macular star. Recovery occurs over 2вАУ4
months. Treatment as for cat-scratch disease may be considered.

Solitary idiopathic choroiditis оАЬSICоАЭ

SIC is a rare entity that presents with mild visual loss or is asymptomatic. A discrete post-equatorial dull-yellow choroidal elevation
with ill-deоАЭned margins is seen; vitritis is present during active
disease. Contiguous subretinal оАЮuid and a macular star may be
present. With healing the lesion develops a better-deоАЭned margin
with resolution of subretinal оАЮuid and exudation. Treatment of a
vision-threatening lesion is with systemic steroids.

Frosted branch angiitis оАЬFBAоАЭ

FBA describes a characteristic fundus picture, usually bilateral and
may represent a speciоАЭc entity (primary) or a common pathway
in response to multiple stimuli. Secondary FBA may be associated with infectious retinitis, notably cytomegalovirus retinitis
and other conditions such as lymphoma and leukaemia. Primary
(idiopathic) FBA is rare and typically aоАЫects children and young
adults, in whom presentation is with bilateral visual loss (6/30 to
PL), оАЮoaters and/or photopsia. оАЯere may be a viral prodrome.
Florid sheathing of retinal arterioles and venules is seen (Fig.
12.71). Anterior uveitis, vitritis and retinal oedema are common.
Treatment is with systemic steroids, though some authorities
believe the prognosis, which is usually good in the primary form,
is unaоАЫected.

Idiopathic retinal vasculitis, aneurysms
and neuroretinitis syndrome оАЬIRVANоАЭ

Idiopathic retinal vasculitis, aneurysms and neuroretinitis syndrome is a rare entity that typically aоАЫects one or both eyes of
healthy young women. Fundus signs consist of arteritis and multiple aneurysmal dilatations of arteriolar branches and on the optic
nerve head, together with anterior uveitis and vitritis.
Disc oedema and a macular star (neuroretinitis) are common.
Marked macular and circumpapillary exudative retinopathy can
develop (Fig. 12.72A). оАЯe vascular changes are demonstrated
very well on FA (Fig. 12.72B). Extensive peripheral capillary nonperfusion may lead to preretinal neovascularization and early
panretinal photocoagulation should be considered. Intravitreal
anti-VEGF treatment and steroids have been associated with good

results in some patients and may be given in combination.

506 Diabetic Retinopathy

RETINAL CIRCULATION

Arterial system

оАБ оАЯe central retinal artery, an end artery, enters the optic
nerve approximately 1 cm behind the globe. It is composed of
three anatomical layers:

{ оАЯe intima, the innermost, is composed of a single layer of
endothelium resting on a collagenous zone.

{ оАЯe internal elastic lamina separates the intima from the
media.

Venous system

Retinal venules and veins drain blood from the capillaries.
оАБ Small venules are larger than capillaries but have a similar
structure.
оАБ Larger venules contain smooth muscle and merge to form veins.
оАБ Veins contain a small amount of smooth muscle and elastic tissue in their walls and are relatively distensible. оАЯeir diameter
gradually enlarges as they pass posteriorly towards the central
retinal vein.

DIABETIC RETINOPATHY

Introduction

Diabetes is a major concern for healthcare systems throughout the
world. оАЯe prevalence of diabetes is relentlessly increasing, particularly in working-age adults. Diabetic retinopathy is the commonest cause of new blindness in most industrialised countries,
mainly because of a delay in obtaining medical treatment. However, with eоАЮective screening and by embracing and implementing
the conclusions of the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study
(ETDRS) and the anti-VEGF clinical trials, the risk of severe visual
loss can be reduced to less than 5%.

Ophthalmic complications of diabetes

{ оАЯe media consists mainly of smooth muscle.

{ оАЯe adventitia is the outermost and is composed of loose
connective tissue.
оАБ Retinal arterioles arise from the central retinal artery. оАЯeir
walls contain smooth muscle, but in contrast to arteries the
internal elastic lamina is discontinuous.

Capillaries

Retinal capillaries supply the inner two-thirds of the retina, with
the outer third being supplied by the choriocapillaris. оАЯe inner
capillary network (plexus) is located in the ganglion cell layer, with
an outer plexus in the inner nuclear layer. Capillary-free zones are
present around arterioles (Fig. 13.1A) and at the fovea (foveal avascular zone вАУ FAZ). Retinal capillaries are devoid of smooth muscle
and elastic tissue. оАЯeir walls consist of the following (Fig. 13.1B):
оАБ Endothelial cells form a single layer on the basement membrane and are linked by tight junctions that form the inner
bloodвАУretinal barrier.
оАБ оАЯe basement membrane lies beneath the endothelial cells
with an outer basal lamina enclosing pericytes.
оАБ Pericytes lie external to endothelial cells and have multiple
pseudopodial processes that envelop the capillaries. Pericytes
have contractile properties and are thought to participate in
autoregulation of the microvascular circulation.

{ Recurrent styes.

оАБ Common

{ Maculopathy: diabetic macular oedema and macular
ischaemia.

{ Retinopathy: sequelae arising from retinal ischaemia (neovascularization of the optic disc and retina) and vitreous
haemorrhage.

{ Unstable refraction.
оАБ Uncommon

A B

Fig. 13.1 Normal retinal capillary bed. оАЭAоАЮ Periarteriolar capillary-free zone вАУ оАЮat preparation
of Indian ink-injected retina; оАЭBоАЮ endothelial cells with elongated nuclei and pericytes with
rounded nuclei вАУ trypsin digest preparation. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical
Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001.)

{ Xanthelasma.

{ Accelerated age-related cataract.

{ Tractional retinal detachment.

{ Neovascularization of the iris and angle.

{ Neovascular glaucoma (NVG).

{ Ocular motor nerve palsies.

{ Reduced corneal sensitivity.
оАБ Rare. Papillopathy, pupillary lightвАУnear dissociation, Wolfram
syndrome (progressive optic atrophy and multiple neurological and systemic abnormalities), acute-onset cataract, rhinoorbital mucormycosis.

Prevalence

оАЯe reported prevalence of diabetic retinopathy (DR) in people
with diabetes varies substantially among studies and even among
contemporary populations in the same country, but is probably
around 40%. People with diabetes are oоАЭen unaware that they
might have retinopathy. оАЯe prevalence of DR in type 2 diabetes is
67% 10 years aоАЭer the diagnosis has been made and 10% will have
proliferative disease. DR is more common in type 1 diabetes than
in type 2 diabetes.

Risk factors

оАБ Duration of diabetes is the most important predictor of diabetic retinopathy. DR rarely develops within 5 years of the
onset of diabetes or before puberty. People with type 1 diabetes
are at particular long-term risk. Duration is a stronger predictor for proliferative disease than for maculopathy.
оАБ Poor control of diabetes. оАЯe Diabetes Control and Complication Trial (DCCT) shows that tight blood glucose control,
particularly when instituted early, can prevent or delay the
development or progression of DR (subsequently conоАЬrmed
by the United Kingdom Prospective Diabetes Study). However,
a sudden improvement in control may be associated with progression of retinopathy. оАЯe higher the glycated haemoglobin
(HbA1c) the greater the risk of complications of diabetes such
as PDR. In people with advanced retinopathy even the most
rigorous control of blood glucose may not prevent progression.
оАБ Hypertension is very common in those with type 2 diabetes
and should be rigorously controlled (<140/80 mmHg). Tight
control of blood pressure appears to be particularly beneоАЬcial
in type 2 diabetics with maculopathy.
оАБ Nephropathy, if severe, is associated with worsening of DR.
Conversely, treatment of renal disease (e.g. renal transplantation) may be associated with improvement of retinopathy and
a better response to photocoagulation.
оАБ Drugs. Pioglitazone is associated with a signiоАЬcant risk of
worsening of diabetic macular oedema.
оАБ Pregnancy is sometimes associated with rapid progression
of DR. Predicating factors include (a) greater pre-pregnancy
severity of retinopathy, (b) poor pre-pregnancy control of diabetes, (c) control exerted too rapidly during the early stages
of pregnancy and (d) pre-eclampsia. оАЯe risk of progression
is related to the severity of DR in the оАЬrst trimester. Approximately 5% with mild DR and a third of those with moderate
DR will progress to PDR during the pregnancy. If substantial

CHAPTER

Retinal Vascular Disease 507
#### 13

DR is present, frequency of review should reоАЫect individual
risk and can be up to 2-weekly. Diabetic macular oedema usually resolves spontaneously aоАЭer pregnancy and might not
need to be treated if it develops in late pregnancy.
оАБ Other risk factors include hyperlipidaemia, cataract surgery
and anaemia.

TIP The risk of severe visual loss can be signiоАЯcantly
reduced by regular retinal screening and by embracing and
implementing the conclusions of the various clinical trials in
diabetes.

Pathogenesis

DR is predominantly a microangiopathy in which small blood vessels are particularly vulnerable to damage from high glucose levels.
Direct hyperglycaemic eоАЮects on retinal cells are also likely to play
a role.
Many angiogenic stimulators and inhibitors have been identiоАЬed. Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) appears to be of
particular importance in the former category.

ClassiоАЬcation

оАЯe classiоАЬcation used in the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS вАУ the modiоАЬed Airlie House classiоАЬcation) is
widely used internationally. An abbreviated version is set out in
Table 13.1, in conjunction with management guidelines. оАЯe following descriptive categories are also in widespread use in clinical
practice:
оАБ Background diabetic retinopathy (BDR) is characterized by
microaneurysms, dot and blot haemorrhages and exudates.
оАЯese are generally the earliest signs of DR and persist as more
advanced lesions appear.
оАБ Diabetic maculopathy strictly refers to the presence of any
retinopathy at the macula, but is commonly reserved for signiоАЬcant changes, particularly vision-threatening oedema and
ischaemia.
оАБ Preproliferative diabetic retinopathy (PPDR) manifests with cotton-wool spots, venous beading, intraretinal
microvascular anomalies (IRMA) and moderate to severe
retinal haemorrhages. PPDR indicates progressive retinal
ischaemia, with a heightened risk of progression to retinal
neovascularization.
оАБ Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) is characterized
by neovascularization on or within one disc diameter of
the disc (NVD) and/or new vessels elsewhere (NVE) in the
fundus.
оАБ High-risk PDR is deоАЬned as any one of the following: (a)
presence of vitreous haemorrhage or preretinal haemorrhage,
(b) presence of any active neovascularization, (b) location of
neovascularization on or within one disc diameter of the optic
disc, (d) NVD >1/3-disc area or NVE >1/2-disc area.
оАБ Advanced diabetic eye disease is characterized by tractional
retinal detachment, signiоАЬcant persistent vitreous haemorrhage and neovascular glaucoma.

Signs

Microaneurysms

Microaneurysms are localized outpouchings, mainly saccular, of
the capillary wall that may form either by focal dilatation of the
capillary wall where pericytes are absent, or by fusion of two arms
of a capillary loop (Fig. 13.2A). Most develop in the inner capillary plexus (ganglion cell layer), frequently adjacent to areas of
capillary non-perfusion (Fig. 13.2B). Loss of pericytes (Fig. 13.2C)
may also lead to endothelial cell proliferation with the formation of
вАШcellularвАЩ microaneurysms (Fig. 13.2D). Microaneurysms may leak
plasma constituents into the retina as a result of breakdown in the
bloodвАУretinal barrier, or may thrombose. оАЯey tend to be the earliest sign of DR.
оАБ Signs. Tiny red dots, oоАЭen initially temporal to the fovea
(Fig. 13.3A). May be indistinguishable clinically from dot
haemorrhages.

оАБ Fluorescein angiography (FA) allows diоАЮerentiation between
dot haemorrhages and non-thrombosed microaneurysms.
Early frames show tiny hyperоАЫuorescent dots (Fig. 13.3B), typically more numerous than visible clinically. Late frames show
diоАЮuse hyperоАЫuorescence due to leakage.
оАБ OCT shows microaneurysms in the middle retinal layers. Normal vessels look similar to microaneurysms, but are usually
seen in the inner retinal layers.

Retinal haemorrhages

оАБ Retinal nerve оАЮbre layer haemorrhages arise from the larger
superоАЬcial precapillary arterioles (Fig. 13.4A) and assume
their characteristic shape (Fig. 13.4B) because of the architecture of the retinal nerve оАЬbre layer.
оАБ Intraretinal haemorrhages arise from the venous end of capillaries and are located in the compact middle layers of the
retina (see Fig. 13.4A) with a resultant red вАШdot/blotвАЩ conоАЬguration (Fig. 13.4C).

оАБ Deeper dark round haemorrhages (Fig. 13.4D) represent
haemorrhagic retinal infarcts and are located within the
middle retinal layers (see Fig. 13.4A). оАЯe extent of involvement is a signiоАЬcant marker of the likelihood of progression
to PDR.

Exudates

Exudates are caused by chronic localised retinal oedema. оАЯey
develop at the junction of normal and oedematous retina. оАЯey
are composed of lipoprotein and lipid-оАЬlled macrophages located
mainly within the outer plexiform layer (Fig. 13.5A). Hyperlipidaemia may increase the likelihood of exudate formation.
оАБ Signs

{ Waxy yellow lesions (Fig. 13.5B) with relatively distinct
margins arranged in clumps and/or rings at the posterior
pole, oоАЭen surrounding leaking microaneurysms.

{ With time, the number and size tend to increase (Fig.
13.5C) and the fovea may be involved.

{ When leakage ceases, exudates absorb spontaneously over
a period of months, either into healthy surrounding capillaries or by phagocytosis.

{ Chronic leakage leads to enlargement and the deposition
of crystalline cholesterol (Fig. 13.5D).
оАБ FA will commonly show hypoоАЫuorescence only with large
dense exudates. Although background choroidal оАЫuorescence
is masked, retinal capillary оАЫuorescence is generally preserved
overlying the lesions.

Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)

Diabetic maculopathy (macular oedema, exudates or ischaemia) is the most common cause of moderate visual impairment in diabetic patients, particularly type 2. Diffuse retinal

Fig. 13.5 Exudates. оАЭAоАЮ Histology showing irregular eosinophilic deposits mainly in the outer

oedema is caused by extensive capillary leakage and localized
oedema by focal leakage from microaneurysms and dilated
capillary segments. The fluid is initially located between the
outer plexiform and inner nuclear layers. Later it may also
involve the inner plexiform and nerve fibre layers, until eventually the entire thickness of the retina becomes oedematous.
This is readily detectable using OCT (Fig. 13.6A) and FA (Fig.
13.6B).
оАБ Clinically signiоАЮcant macular oedema (CSMO) is deоАЬned in
the ETDRS as:

{ Retinal thickening within 500 ќЉm (or 1/3-disc diameter) of
the centre of the macula (Fig. 13.7, upper leоАЭ).

{ Hard exudates within 500 ќЉm of the centre of the
macula, with adjacent retinal thickening, which may
lie more than 500 ќЉm from the foveal centre (Fig. 13.7,
upper right).

{ Retinal thickening 1-disc area or larger, any part of which
is within one disc diameter of the centre of the macula
(Fig. 13.7, lower centre).

оАБ Focal maculopathy: well-circumscribed retinal thickening
associated with complete or incomplete rings of exudates (Fig.
13.8A). FA shows late, focal hyperоАЫuorescence due to leakage,
usually with good macular perfusion (Fig. 13.8B).
оАБ DiоАЭuse maculopathy: diоАЮuse retinal thickening, which may be
associated with cystoid changes. оАЯere are typically also scattered microaneurysms and small haemorrhages. Landmarks
may be obscured by oedema, which may render localization
of the fovea impossible (Fig. 13.9A). FA shows mid- and latephase diоАЮuse hyperоАЫuorescence (Fig. 13.9B).

Ischaemic maculopathy

оАБ Signs. оАЯe retina oоАЭen shows dot and blot haemorrhages and
cotton wool spots, but the macula may look relatively normal
despite reduced visual acuity (Fig. 13.10A).
оАБ FA shows capillary non-perfusion at the fovea (an enlarged
FAZ) and frequently other areas of capillary non-perfusion at
the posterior pole and periphery (Fig. 13.10B and see OCTA
appearance in Fig. 14.26D).

оАЭBоАЮ FA showing leaking microaneurysms and central diоАЬuse hyperоАЮuorescence with a оАЮower
petal conоАЯguration. (Courtesy of A Ambresin вАУ оАЯg. A.)

80% of the cotton-wool spots have hyporeоАЫective cavities
appearing as cysts.

Venous changes

Fovea

Fig. 13.7 Clinically signiоАЯcant macular oedema (see text).

Cotton-wool spots

Cotton-wool spots are composed of accumulations of neuronal
debris within the nerve оАЬbre layer. оАЯey result from ischaemic
disruption of nerve axons, the swollen ends of which are known
as cystoid bodies, seen on light microscopy as globular structures
in the nerve оАЬbre layer (Fig. 13.11A). As cotton-wool spots heal,
debris is removed by autolysis and phagocytosis.
оАБ Signs. Small оАЫuоАЮy whitish superоАЬcial lesions that obscure
underlying blood vessels (Fig. 13.11B and C). оАЯey are clinically evident only in the post-equatorial retina, where the
nerve оАЬbre layer is of suоАЪcient thickness to render them
visible.
оАБ FA shows focal hypoоАЫuorescence due to local ischaemia and
blockage of background choroidal оАЫuorescence.
оАБ OCT shows cotton-wool spots in the retinal nerve оАЬbre layer,
which show a hyperreоАЫective pattern in the acute stage. About

Venous anomalies seen in retinal ischaemia consist of generalized
dilatation and tortuosity, looping (Fig. 13.12A), beading (focal
narrowing and dilatation) (Fig. 13.12B) and sausage-like segmentation (Fig. 13.12C). оАЯe extent of the retinal area exhibiting
venous changes correlates well with the likelihood of developing
proliferative disease.

Intraretinal microvascular abnormalities

Intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMA) are arteriolarвАУ
venular shunts that run from retinal arterioles to venules, thus
bypassing the capillary bed and are therefore oоАЭen seen adjacent
to areas of marked capillary hypoperfusion (Fig. 13.13A).
оАБ Signs. Fine, irregular, red intraretinal lines that run from arterioles to venules, without crossing major blood vessels (Fig.
13.13B).
оАБ FA shows focal hypoоАЫuorescence associated with adjacent
areas of capillary closure (вАШdropoutвАЩ) but without leakage
(unlike neovascularization, where profuse leakage of оАЫuorescein occurs).
оАБ OCT. Unlike new vessels, IRMA do not breach the ILM.

Arterial changes

Subtle retinal arteriolar dilatation may be an early marker of ischaemic dysfunction. When signiоАЬcant ischaemia is present signs
include peripheral narrowing, вАШsilver wiringвАЩ and obliteration, similar to the late appearance following a branch retinal artery occlusion.

Proliferative retinopathy

Although preretinal new vessels may arise anywhere in the retina,
they are most commonly seen at the posterior pole. Fibrous tissue,
initially оАЬne, gradually develops as vessels increase in size.

оАБ New vessels at the disc (NVD) describes neovascularization on
or within one disc diameter of the optic nerve head (Fig. 13.14).
оАБ NVE describes neovascularization further away from the disc
(Fig. 13.15).
оАБ New vessels on the iris (NVI вАУ Fig. 13.16), also known as
rubeosis iridis, carry a high likelihood of progression to neovascular glaucoma (see Ch. 11).
оАБ FA (see Fig. 13.14C) highlights neovascularization during the
early phases of the angiogram and shows irregular expanding
hyperоАЫuorescence during the later stages due to intense leakage of dye from neovascular tissue. FA can be used to conоАЬrm
the presence of new vessels (NV) if the clinical diagnosis is in
doubt and also delineates areas of ischaemic retina that might
be selectively targeted for laser treatment.
оАБ OCT of the retina is good at detecting new vessels (100%), but not
regression (45%) or reactivation (10%). However, OCT angiography is useful for the objective monitoring of disease aоАЭer treatment.

Treatment

General

оАБ Patient education is critical, including the need to comply
with review and treatment schedules in order to optimize
visual outcomes. In type 2 diabetes this may involve counselling on weight reduction programs and emphasizing the
beneоАЬts of regular exercise.
оАБ Diabetic control should be optimized.
оАБ Other risk factors, particularly systemic hypertension and
hyperlipidaemia, should be controlled in conjunction with the
patientвАЩs diabetologist.
оАБ FenoоАЮbrate 200 mg daily has been shown to reduce the progression of DR in type 2 diabetics and prescription should be
considered. оАЯe decision is independent of whether the patient
already takes a statin.
оАБ Smoking has not been deоАЬnitively shown to aоАЮect retinopathy, but patients should be advised to stop smoking in order to
reduce the risk of macrovascular complications.

оАБ Other modiоАЮable factors such as anaemia and renal failure
should be addressed as necessary.

TIP The key to the prevention of diabetic retinopathy is
patient education and long-term control of blood sugar.

Ophthalmic treatment of diabetic macular
oedema

Until recently laser photocoagulation was the mainstay of treatment for DMO, reducing the risk of moderate visual loss by 50%
overall compared with observation. оАЯe availability of intravitreal
anti-VEGF agents and strong evidence to support their eоАЪcacy has
dramatically altered the approach to management in recent years.
Many of the current guidelines for care have arisen from the conclusions of large prospective randomised controlled trials undertaken
by the Diabetic Retinopathy Clinical Research Network (DRCR.
net). It should be remembered that these studies provide average
results in groups of patients and that individual responses may vary
considerably, with some patients deriving little or no beneоАЬt from
these interventions. Options should always be discussed fully with
the patient. In particular, patients with good vision (6/7.5 or better) who otherwise meet criteria for treatment might prefer to be
monitored once the risks and beneоАЮts of various interventions
are considered. оАЯe need for meticulous follow-up and the cost of
the proposed treatment may also be an issue for some patients and
needs to be considered when discussing therapy.
оАБ Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents

{ DRCR.net Protocol I concludes that intravitreal ranibizumab more effectively improves visual acuity than
focal/grid laser treatment for centre-involved DMO.
Laser treatment should be deferred for 6 months after
commencement of anti-VEGF therapy. Good outcomes
can be expected for at least 5 years, despite a sequential
reduction in the number of anti-VEGF injections.

{ DRCR.net Protocol T concludes that there are no diоАЮerences
in visual acuity at 5 years irrespective of whether aоАЫibercept, bevacizumab or ranibizumab is used in patients with

6/15. However, aоАЫibercept is more likely to improve visual

оАБ Intravitreal triamcinolone and sustained-release steroid
implants. In pseudophakic eyes intravitreal triamcinolone
steroid injection followed by prompt laser is comparable to
ranibizumab with regard to visual improvement and reduced
retinal thickening. оАЯis is a good therapeutic option in pregnancy or where there is a contraindication to anti-VEGF treatment (for example recent myocardial infarct). However, there
is a signiоАЬcant risk of inducing an elevation of intraocular
pressure (IOP) and this must be monitored regularly. In a phakic eye there is an increased risk of cataract. Sustained-release
intravitreal steroid implants (e.g. Ozurdex and Iluvien) have
demonstrated promising results.
оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy (PPV) may be indicated when macular
oedema is associated with tangential traction from a thickened
and taut posterior hyaloid (see Ch. 14 вАУ vitreomacular traction
syndrome). It has also been suggested that some eyes without a
taut posterior hyaloid may also beneоАЬt from vitrectomy. Clinically, a taut thickened posterior hyaloid is characterized by an
increased glistening of the premacular vitreous face. FA typically
shows diоАЮuse leakage and prominent CMO, but OCT is usually
the deоАЬnitive assessment. оАЯere are several other indications for
PPV in the management of diabetic eye disease (see later).
оАБ SpeciоАЮc recommendations

{ CSMO not involving the centre of the macula (particularly where exudates threaten the fovea) may be treated
with laser treatment in the form of continuous-wave focal
or modiоАЬed grid laser photocoagulation. If available, subthreshold macular laser treatment, intravitreal anti-VEGF
therapy or an intravitreal steroid implant may be reasonable alternatives for this indication.

{ CSMO involving the centre of the macula, but with a visual
acuity of better than 6/15 can be treated with aоАЫibercept,
bevacizumab, faricimab or ranibizumab. However, bevacizumab reduces OCT swelling less eоАЮectively and is more
likely to result in persistent DMO than other agents. Alternatively, subthreshold laser can be considered. If laser is to
be used, it is prudent to treat no closer than 500 ќЉm from
the perceived centre of the macula.

{ CSMO involving the centre of the macula, but with a
visual acuity of 6/15 or worse and signiоАЬcant foveolar
thickening should be considered for aоАЫibercept or faricimab treatment with initial induction using monthly
injections for 3вАУ6 months. An вАШas-neededвАЩ approach can
subsequently be adopted. Focal/grid laser should be
deferred for 6 months. An intravitreal dexamethasone
implant (Ozurdex) is an alternative treatment that may
be considered in patients with absolute or relative contraindications to anti-VEGF injections. In pseudophakic
patients who have a transient but signiоАЬcant reduction in
macular oedema switching to a оАЫuocinolone acetonide
implant (Iluvien) is a reasonable alternative, as it is longer acting and therefore will reduce the need for repeated
dexamethasone injections.

{ Faricimab is the latest monoclonal antibody that is available for the treatment of diabetic maculopathy. It works
by neutralising angiopoietin-2 and VEGF-A, leading to
reduced vascular permeability and inоАЫammation, inhibition of angiogenesis and improved vascular stability. It is
as eоАЮective in improving vision as aоАЫibercept and ranibizumab with a similar side-eоАЮect proоАЬle (YOSEMITE and
RHINE studies). AоАЭer four initial monthly injections
of 6mg faricimab a treat-and-extend approach can be
adopted, leading up to a 16-week dosing regimen (depending on visual acuity and OCT оАЬndings).
оАБ Laser photocoagulation

{ Although anti-VEGF therapy has replaced laser treatment
for most cases, macular laser is a well-proven therapeutic
option. It is signiоАЬcantly less eоАЮective than other options,
but it has its place and could be considered in cases with
oоАЮ-centre retinal thickening when exudates threaten the
fovea.

{ Focal laser. Frequency doubled YAG, diode or argon burns
are applied to leaking microaneurysms 500вАУ3000 ќЉm from
the foveola using a spot size 50вАУ100 ќЉm, duration 0.02вАУ0.1
seconds with suоАЪcient power to obtain a mild greyish
reaction beneath the microaneurysm.

{ ModiоАЬed grid laser (Fig. 13.18). A вАШmodiоАЬedвАЩ grid includes
focal treatment to foci of leakage, usually microaneurysms.
оАЯe use of grid laser is no longer recommended.
оАБ Subthreshold macular laser therapy. оАЯis modality uses a
train of very short laser pulses (typically 100вАУ300 microseconds) вАШonвАЩ, combined with a relatively longer interval вАШoоАЮвАЩ (typically 1700вАУ1900 microseconds), allowing energy dissipation.
оАЯis minimises collateral damage to the retina and choroid
whilst stimulating the retinal pigment epithelium. Subfoveal
therapy is possible. оАЯere are positive reports of favourable
results using this technology (Fig. 13.19), but there are no

Laser treatment for proliferative retinopathy

оАБ Scatter laser treatment (panretinal photocoagulation вАУ Fig.
13.20A) continues to be the mainstay of PDR treatment in most
healthcare systems. оАЯe Diabetic Retinopathy Study (DRS) establishes the characteristics of high-risk proliferative disease and
demonstrates the beneоАЬt of PRP. For instance, severe NVD without haemorrhage carries a 26% risk of visual loss at 2 years that
is reduced to 9% with PRP. However, recent research (Protocol S
from DRCR.net) shows that a course of injections of intravitreal
ranibizumab is as eоАЮective as photocoagulation in patients at high
risk of PDR at 5 years (see below). In addition, the CLARITY
study provides 1-year data which show that intravitreal aоАЫibercept injections are as eоАЮective as PRP in the treatment of PDR.
оАБ Informed consent

{ Patients should be advised that PRP may cause visual оАЬeld
defects of suоАЪcient severity to legally preclude driving a

the standard legally required in most countries.

oedema; a risk that can be reduced by fractionating the

{ Spot size. A retinal burn diameter of 400 ќЉm is usually

Mainster and Area Centralis, the retinal diameter equates
closely to the interface selection, so 400 ќЉm may be selected.

the PRP or at the same laser treatment session.

Targeted retinal photocoagulation (TRP)

Wide-оАЬeld FA allows accurate delineation of peripheral capillary
non-perfusion (Fig. 13.21). Selective treatment of these areas with
scatter laser has been reported as eоАЮectively leading to regression
of NV whilst minimizing potential complications.

Advanced diabetic eye disease

Advanced diabetic eye disease is a serious vision-threatening complication of DR that occurs in patients in whom treatment has
been inadequate or unsuccessful. Occasionally, advanced disease
is evident at presentation.

Clinical features

оАБ Haemorrhage may be preretinal (retrohyaloid), intragel or
both (Fig. 13.22A). Intragel haemorrhages usually take longer
to clear than preretinal because the former is usually more substantial. In some eyes, altered blood becomes compacted on
the posterior vitreous face to form an вАШochre membraneвАЩ. Ultrasonography is used in eyes with dense vitreous haemorrhage to
detect the possibility of associated retinal detachment.
оАБ Tractional retinal detachment (Fig. 13.22B and C) is caused by
progressive contraction of оАЬbrovascular membranes over areas of
vitreoretinal attachment. Posterior vitreous detachment in eyes
with PDR is oоАЭen incomplete due to the strong adhesions between
cortical vitreous and areas of оАЬbrovascular proliferation. Haemorrhage oоАЭen occurs at these sites due to stress exerted on NV.
оАБ Rubeosis iridis (iris neovascularization вАУ NVI) may occur in
eyes with PDR and if severe may lead to neovascular glaucoma
(see Ch. 11). NVI is particularly common in eyes with severe

Fig. 13.21 Wide-оАЯeld FA showing widespread areas of capillary
non-perfusion вАУ the arrow indicates a well-deоАЯned example.
(Courtesy of A Ambresin.)

{ Power should be suоАЪcient to produce only a light intensity
burn.

{ Spacing. Burns should be separated by 1вАУ1.5 burn widths.

{ Extent of treated area. оАЯe initial treatment session should
consist of 1500 burns in most cases, though more may be
applied if there is a risk of imminent sight loss from vitreous haemorrhage. оАЯe more extensive the treatment at a
single session, the greater the likelihood of complications.
Reported оАЬgures vary, but 2500вАУ3500 burns are likely to
be required for regression of mild PDR, 4000 for moderate
PDR and 7000 for severe PDR. оАЯe number of burns oоАЮers
only approximate guidance, as the eоАЮective extent of treatment is dependent on numerous variables.

{ Pattern of treatment. Treatment is generally restricted to the
area outside the temporal macular vascular arcades. It is good
practice to delineate a вАШbarrierвАЩ of laser burns temporal to the
macula early in the procedure to help to reduce the risk of
accidental macular damage. Many practitioners leave two disc
diameters untreated at the nasal side of the disc, to preserve
paracentral оАЬeld. In very severe PDR it is advisable to treat
the inferior fundus оАЬrst, since any vitreous haemorrhage will
gravitate inferiorly and obscure this area, precluding further
treatment. Areas of vitreoretinal traction should be avoided.
оАБ Review is dependent on PDR severity and the requirement for
successive treatment applications. Initial treatment should be
fractionated over 2вАУ3 sessions. Once an adequate number of
burns have been applied review can be set for 4вАУ6 weeks.

TIP In a patient with proliferative diabetic retinopathy and
macular oedema, оАЯrst treat the oedema then undertake
panretinal photocoagulation.

оАБ Indicators of regression include blunting of vessel tips, shrinking and disappearance of NV, oоАЭen leaving вАШghostвАЩ vessels or
оАЬbrosis (Fig. 13.20C and D), regression of IRMA, decreased
venous changes, absorption of retinal haemorrhages, disc pallor. Contraction of regressing vessels or associated induction of
vitreous separation can precipitate vitreous haemorrhage. Significant оАЬbrous proliferation can lead to tractional retinal detachment (see below). Patients should remain under observation, as
recurrence can occur with a requirement for additional PRP.

VEGF inhibition for proliferative retinopathy

PRCR.net Protocol S concludes that intravitreal ranibizumab treatment
is as eоАЮective as PRP in patients at high risk of PDR for up to 5 years.
оАЯe macular oedema rate is reduced and the visual оАЬeld is slightly better maintained with anti-VEGF treatment. Irrespective of the therapeutic option, approximately half will experience a vitreous haemorrhage

Indications for pars plana vitrectomy

Vitrectomy in DR is typically combined with extensive endolaser
PRP. Visual results depend on the speciоАЬc indication for surgery
and the severity of pre-existing disease.
оАБ Severe persistent vitreous haemorrhage that precludes adequate PRP is the most common indication (Fig. 13.22D). In
the absence of rubeosis iridis, vitrectomy has traditionally
been considered within 3 months of the initial vitreous haemorrhage in type 1 diabetics and in most cases of bilateral haemorrhage. However, the outcome may be better with earlier
surgery and the availability of intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy
may further modify the approach.
оАБ DRCR.net protocol AB concludes that eyes with vitreous
haemorrhage that undergo initial vitrectomy with PRP recover
vision faster than those treated initially with aоАЫibercept. However, the vision is similar in the two groups 2 years later, which

suggests that either option is a reasonable оАЬrst-line treatment
of vitreous haemorrhage from PDR.
оАБ Progressive tractional RD threatening or involving the macula must be treated without delay. However, extramacular tractional detachments may be observed, since they oоАЭen remain
stationary for prolonged periods.
оАБ Combined tractional and rhegmatogenous RD should be
treated urgently.
оАБ Premacular retrohyaloid haemorrhage (Fig. 13.23A), if
dense and persistent should be considered for early vitrectomy because, if untreated, the internal limiting membrane or
posterior hyaloid face may serve as a scaоАЮold for subsequent
оАЬbrovascular proliferation and consequent tractional macular
detachment or macular epiretinal membrane formation. Dispersion with Nd:YAG laser (hyaloidotomy) is oоАЭen successful
(Fig. 13.23B and C).
оАБ Anterior segment neovascularization and media opacities,
which prevent visualization of the posterior pole, should be
considered for vitrectomy and intraoperative laser therapy.

Diabetic papillopathy

Diabetic papillopathy (diabetic papillitis) has been speculated to
be an uncommon variant of anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy,
though is more commonly bilateral and tends to exhibit more diffuse disc swelling.
оАБ Pathogenesis. оАЯe underlying pathogenesis is not known. оАЯe
condition is more likely to occur in people with small optic
discs and aоАЭer a rapid decrease in the blood glucose level.
оАБ Clinical features. It occurs mainly in younger people with diabetes and manifests with mild painless visual impairment that
is unilateral in more than half of cases. Hyperaemic disc swelling is characteristic and disc telangiectasia is oоАЭen present
(Fig. 13.24A and B). Crowding of the fellow disc may be found.
оАБ Resolution occurs over several months (Fig. 13.24C), oоАЭen
leaving mild disc pallor. Final visual acuity (VA) is 6/12 or better in 80%, subject to the eоАЮect of co-existing DR.
оАБ DiоАЭerentialdiagnosis. оАЯe telangiectasia should not be mistaken
for neovascularization. Distinction from retinal vein occlusion
(RVO)-type papillophlebitis (see below) rests on the presence of
more extensive retinal haemorrhages and venous congestion in
the latter, but may not be possible. Raised intracranial pressure
should be excluded if there is bilateral disc swelling.
оАБ Treatment. оАЯere is no validated treatment. Intravitreal antiVEGF agents and steroids via various routes have been tried,
with no signiоАЬcant beneоАЬt.

NON-DIABETIC RETINOPATHY

Up to 10% of individuals over the age of 40 without diabetes mellitus exhibit вАУ usually very mild вАУ retinopathic features such as
microaneurysms, dot and blot haemorrhages and cotton-wool
spots that would be consistent with a diagnosis of DR. Assuming
that an alternative ocular cause such as RVO or idiopathic macular
telangiectasia has been excluded, this вАШnon-diabeticвАЩ retinopathy
tends to be associated with increased cerebrovascular and cardiovascular risk and may be particularly prevalent in patients with
known or incipient hypertension. оАЯere is evidence suggesting that
it may be a marker of preclinical diabetes in some patients; higher
venular calibre may also denote this. Appropriate management is
undeоАЬned, though evaluation and optimal management of systemic vascular risk factors may be prudent. оАЯe signs commonly
disappear spontaneously and this is more likely in those with lower
levels of cardiovascular risk.

RETINAL VENOUS OCCLUSIVE DISEASE

Introduction

Retinal vein thrombosis is strongly associated with age-related
local and systemic factors. It is the second most common retinal
vascular disease aоАЭer DR. оАЯe condition aоАЮects 5 in 1000 people in
the industrialised world. In branch retinal vein occlusion (BRVO)
arteriosclerotic thickening of a branch retinal arteriole is associated with compression of a venule at an arteriovenous crossing
point, exacerbated by sharing an adventitial sheath. оАЯis leads to

524 Retinal Venous Occlusive Disease

A

B

C

Fig. 13.24 Diabetic papillopathy. оАЭAоАЮ Hyperaemic disc swelling and telangiectasia (arrow) with no visual symptoms; оАЭBоАЮ

secondary changes that include endothelial cell loss, turbulent оАЫow
and thrombus formation. Similarly, the central retinal vein and
artery possess a common sheath at crossing points posterior to the
lamina cribrosa so that atherosclerotic changes of the artery may
precipitate central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO). Haematological
pro-thrombotic factors are thought to be important in a minority, amplifying an atherosclerotic anatomical predisposition. Once
venous occlusion has occurred, elevation of venous and capillary
pressure with stagnation of blood оАЫow ensues, resulting in retinal
hypoxia, which in turn results in damage to the capillary endothelial cells, extravasation of blood constituents and liberation of
mediators such as VEGF.

Risk factors

оАБ Age is the most important factor. Over 50% of cases occur in
patients older than 65.
оАБ Hypertension is present in two-thirds or more of RVO
patients over the age of 50 years and in 25% of younger
patients. It is a particularly important risk factor in patients
with BRVO.
оАБ Hyperlipidaemia is present in one-third or more of patients,
irrespective of age.
оАБ Diabetes mellitus is present in up to 15% of patients over 50
years of age. It is more prevalent in Asian and African patients,
but is uncommon in younger patients.
оАБ Glaucoma and ocular hypertension are associated with a
higher risk of CRVO.
оАБ Oral contraceptive pill. In younger females the contraceptive
pill is the most common underlying association and probably
should not be taken following RVO.
оАБ Smoking. Current smoking may be associated with an
increased incidence of RVO.
оАБ Uncommon. Dehydration, myeloproliferative disorders
(e.g. myeloma, polycythaemia), thrombophilia (e.g. hyperhomocysteinaemia, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome,
factor V Leiden mutation), inоАЫammatory disease associated
with occlusive periphlebitis (e.g. Beh√Іet syndrome, sarcoidosis, Wegener granulomatosis), orbital disease and chronic
renal failure.

Systemic assessment

оАЯe detection and management of associated systemic disease is
aimed principally at reducing the risk of future vascular occlusive
events, both ocular and systemic.

All patients

оАБ Blood pressure (BP).
оАБ Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) or plasma viscosity
(PV).
оАБ Full blood count (FBC).
оАБ Random blood glucose. Further assessment for diabetes if
indicated.
оАБ Random total and high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol. Additional lipid testing may be considered.

CHAPTER

Retinal Vascular Disease 525
#### 13

оАБ Plasma protein electrophoresis. To detect dysproteinaemias
such as multiple myeloma.
оАБ Other tests. Some authorities advocate routine investigation
for systemic end-organ damage related to the cardiovascular
risk factors commonly found in patients with RVO. оАЯis is
intended to help the prevention of further non-ocular damage,
as well as facilitating systemic management to reduce the risk
of recurrent ocular venous occlusion.

{ Urea, electrolytes and creatinine to detect renal disease
associated with hypertension. Chronic renal failure is also
a rare cause of RVO.

{ оАЯyroid function testing. оАЯere is a higher prevalence of
thyroid disease in RVO patients.

{ Electrocardiography (ECG). LeоАЭ ventricular hypertrophy
is associated with hypertension.

Selected patients according to clinical
indication

оАЯese tests might be considered in (a) patients under the age of
50; (b) in bilateral RVO; (c) patients with previous thromboses or
a family history of thrombosis; and (d) some patients in whom
investigation for the common associations is negative. Evidence of
a causative link for many of these is limited.
оАБ Chest X-ray. Sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, leоАЭ ventricular hypertrophy in hypertension.
оАБ C-reactive protein (CRP). Sensitive indicator of inоАЫammation.
оАБ Plasma homocysteine level. To exclude hyperhomocysteinaemia, for which there is reasonable evidence of an increased
RVO risk.
оАБ вАШоАЯrombophilia screenвАЩ. By convention this refers to heritable
thrombophilia. Tests might typically include thrombin time,
prothrombin time and activated partial thromboplastin time,
antithrombin functional assay, protein C, protein S, activated
protein C resistance, factor V Leiden mutation, prothrombin
G20210A mutation, lupus anticoagulant and in particular,
anticardiolipin antibody (IgG and IgM).
оАБ Autoantibodies. Rheumatoid factor, antinuclear antibody
(ANA), anti-DNA antibody, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA).
оАБ Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE). Sarcoidosis.
оАБ Treponemal serology. See Chapter 12
оАБ Carotid duplex imaging to exclude ocular ischaemic
syndrome.

Branch retinal vein occlusion

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms and VA depend on the anatomical location of the
occlusion. If the macula is involved, a sudden painless onset of
blurred vision and metamorphopsia is experienced. Peripheral
occlusion may be asymptomatic.
оАБ Iris neovascularization (NVI) and neovascular glaucoma
(NVG) are much less common in BRVO than in CRVO (2вАУ3%
at 3 years).

оАБ Fundus

{ Dilatation and tortuosity of the aоАЮected venous segment,
with оАЫame-shaped and dot/blot haemorrhages, principally
in the retinal area drained by the thrombosed vein though
an occasional haemorrhage may be identiоАЬed elsewhere.
Cotton-wool spots and retinal oedema may be present, but
this is not inevitable.

{ оАЯe superotemporal quadrant is most commonly aоАЮected.

{ оАЯe site of occlusion may be identiоАЬable as an arteriovenous crossing point (Fig. 13.25).

{ оАЯe acute features usually resolve within 6вАУ12 months
leaving venous sheathing and sclerosis and variable persistent/recurrent haemorrhage (Fig. 13.26). оАЯe severity of
residual signs is highly variable.

{ Collateral vessels may form near areas of limited capillary
perfusion aоАЭer weeks to months. оАЯey usually connect a
poorly functioning to a normally functioning segment of the
venous circulation and typically appear as tortuous or looping channels. оАЯey may cross the horizontal raphe between
the inferior and superior vascular arcades (Fig. 13.27A). оАЯe
appearance of collaterals is associated with a better prognosis.

{ Chronic macular oedema is the most common cause of
persistent poor VA aоАЭer BRVO (Fig. 13.27B).

{ Retinal neovascularization occurs in about 8% of eyes by
3 years. оАЯe risk is much higher in eyes with more than
about оАЬve disc areas of non-perfusion on FA (over onethird of eyes) (Fig. 13.27C and D). NVE are more common
than NVD. NVE usually develop at the border of ischaemic retina drained by an occluded vein. оАЯey typically
appear within 6вАУ12 months, but may develop at any time.

{ Recurrent vitreous and preretinal haemorrhage and occasionally tractional retinal detachment can occur secondary
to neovascularization.
оАБ FA demonstrates peripheral and macular ischaemia (capillary nonperfusion, staining of vessel walls, vessel вАШpruningвАЩ вАУ small branches
failing to оАЬll), haemorrhage and oedema with collateral vessels
commonly forming in established cases (Fig. 13.27D). Venous оАЬlling is delayed. In late or subtle cases, FA may be diagnostic.

TIP The commonest underlying cause of a branch retinal
vein occlusion is systemic hypertension, which needs to be
eоАЬectively treated to reduce the risk of late cardiovascular and
cerebrovascular complications.

Management

оАБ Systemic arterial hypertension and hyperlipidaemia, if present, should be treated. Further investigations are required in
order to determine the underlying cause in patients younger
than 50 years, in those with bilateral BRVO at presentation or
in those with a history of recurrent BRVO.
оАБ Observation without intervention is usually indicated if VA is
6/9 or better, or slightly worse but improving.
оАБ NVE or NVD are generally regarded as an indication for
sector photocoagulation, though some authorities withhold
treatment unless vitreous haemorrhage occurs because early

Burns of mildвАУmoderate intensity, 400вАУ500 ќЉm actual diameter, 0.05 s duration and spaced one burn width apart are

оАБ NVI is an indication for urgent sector PRP. Secondary glaucoma is likely to be less aggressive than in CRVO. See Chapter
11 for the detailed management of NVG.
оАБ Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents (Fig. 13.28C and D). оАЯese
have been widely adopted for the treatment of macular

maintained with вАШwhen requiredвАЩ treatment. Commence treatment as soon as the diagnosis is made. Combining intravitreal
injections with laser may allow a reduction in the frequency of

оАБ Intravitreal dexamethasone implant. оАЯis confers visual
improvement in BRVO substantially superior to observation alone. оАЯe treatment can be repeated aоАЭer 4вАУ6 months.
Adverse eоАЮects include an increased glaucoma and cataract
risk. As with anti-VEGF therapy, it may be used alone or in
conjunction with laser.
оАБ Laser to the macula. With the advent of intravitreal treatment,
macular laser has become less popular and should be regarded
as an adjunctive or combination modality. If VA remains
6/12 or worse aоАЭer 3вАУ6 months because of macular oedema
that is associated with good central macular perfusion on
FA, laser may be considered: 20вАУ100 mild burns of diameter
50вАУ100 ќЉm and duration 0.01вАУ0.05 s, concentrating on areas
of leakage on FA. Pulses at the shorter end of the duration
range seem to inоАЫict less damage to the retina whilst exerting

an approximately comparable therapeutic eоАЮect. Treatment
should not encroach within 0.5 disc diameters of the foveal
centre. Retinal haemorrhages and blood vessels вАУ especially
collaterals вАУ should not be treated.
оАБ Subthreshold (micropulse) laser appears to be as eоАЮective as
conventional photocoagulation for macular oedema and inоАЫicts
considerably less retinal damage. оАЯe onset of action is slower.
оАБ Intravitreal triamcinolone in a preservative-free preparation
is as eоАЮective as laser in eyes with macular oedema but has a
less sustained eоАЮect and a relatively high rate of cataract formation and IOP elevation.
оАБ Review in cases that do not require early intervention
should usually take place aоАЭer 3 months and then at 3- to
6-monthly intervals for up to 2 years, principally to detect
neovascularization.

TIP OCT allows rapid quantitative analysis of the macula
