Prognosis

оАБ BRVO generally has a good visual prognosis, with 50вАУ60% of
patients maintaining a оАЬnal VA of better than 6/15, even without treatment.
оАБ Poor prognostic factors are chronic macular oedema, macular
BRVO and new vessel formation leading to vitreous haemorrhage.
оАБ About a quarter are leоАЭ with a VA of 6/60 or worse.
оАБ Developing BRVO in one eye slightly increases the risk of a
similar event occurring in the fellow eye.

Hemiretinal vein occlusion

Hemiretinal vein occlusion is regarded as a variant of CRVO by
some authorities and may be ischaemic or non-ischaemic. It is less
common than either BRVO or CRVO and involves occlusion of
the superior or inferior branch of the central retinal vein (CRV). A
hemispheric occlusion blocks a major branch of the CRV at or near
the optic disc. оАЯe less common hemicentral occlusion involves

one trunk of a dual-trunked CRV that has persisted in the anterior
part of the optic nerve head as a congenital variant.
Prognosis depends on the severity of retinal ischaemia, but
management has been less well studied than BRVO and CRVO.
Extensive retinal ischaemia implies a risk of neovascular glaucoma
and should be managed in the same way as ischaemic CRVO (see
below), otherwise management, particularly of macular oedema,
may be as for BRVO.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. A sudden onset altitudinal visual оАЬeld defect (Fig.
13.29A).
оАБ VA reduction varies depending on the site of the occlusion.
оАБ NVI is more common than in BRVO but less than in CRVO.
оАБ Fundus shows the features of BRVO, involving the superior
(Fig. 13.29B) or inferior retinal hemisphere (Fig. 13.30A).
NVD may be more common than in either CRVO or BRVO.
оАБ FA shows masking by haemorrhages, hyperоАЫuorescence due
to leakage and variable capillary non-perfusion (Fig. 13.30B).

Impending central retinal vein
occlusion

Impending (partial) CRVO on average occurs in younger
patients than those developing more severe occlusion. оАЯe
prognosis is usually good, but a proportion will deteriorate to
ischaemic CRVO. оАЯe distinction between вАШimpendingвАЩ and
mild non-ischaemic CRVO (see below) is not clear and may be
artiоАЬcial. Symptoms may be absent or consist of only minor or
transient blurring that is characteristically worse on waking. On
examination, there is mild retinal venous dilatation and tortuosity (Fig. 13.31A), with relatively few small, scattered dot and
blot haemorrhages (Fig. 13.31B). оАЯere may be mild macular
oedema. Fundus autoоАЫuorescence (FAF) may reveal a fern-like
perivenular appearance (see below) and FA generally demonstrates an impaired retinal circulation. Treatment is empirical,

with lack of an established evidence base. Correcting any predisposing systemic conditions, avoiding dehydration and lowering IOP to improve perfusion have been postulated. Antiplatelet
agents, other anticoagulant measures and haemodilution are of
no proven beneоАЬt, but may be considered in some cases.

NonоАЯischaemic central retinal vein
occlusion

Diagnosis

Non-ischaemic CRVO, sometimes called вАШvenous stasis retinopathyвАЩ (also sometimes used to describe ocular ischaemic syndrome

- see later) is more common than the ischaemic form. Around a
third will progress to ischaemic CRVO, oоАЭen within months.
оАБ Symptoms. A sudden painless monocular fall in vision.

530 Retinal Venous Occlusive Disease

A B

Fig. 13.31 Impending central retinal vein occlusion. оАЭAоАЮ Retinal venous dilatation and tortuosity;
оАЭBоАЮ scattered dot and blot haemorrhages.

оАБ VA is impaired to a variable degree dependent on severity. Eyes
with initially good VA tend to have a good prognosis and vice
versa. Initial VA in the middle range (6/30вАУ6/60) is an unreliable predictor of outcome. VA worse than 6/60 commonly
indicates that substantial ischaemia is present. In cases that do
not become ischaemic, vision returns to normal or near normal in about 50%.
оАБ Relative aоАЭerent pupillary defect (RAPD). Absent or mild.
оАБ Fundus. Signs are present in all quadrants.

{ Tortuosity and dilatation of all branches of the central retinal vein, with dot, blot and оАЫame haemorrhages to a mildвАУ
moderate extent (Fig. 13.32A and B). Cotton-wool spots,
optic disc and macular oedema are common but generally
mild.

{ Patchy (or perivenular) ischaemic retinal whitening
(PIRW) in a perivenular pattern at the posterior pole
is an early sign occurring in younger patients with nonischaemic CRVO.

{ Most acute signs resolve over 6вАУ12 months. Later оАЬndings are variable, depending on severity but may include
persistent scattered retinal haemorrhages (Fig. 13.32C);
venous tortuosity, sheathing and sclerosis; epiretinal gliosis; macular pigmentary and atrophic changes; peripheral
and optic disc collateral vessels.

{ оАЯe main cause of poor vision is chronic macular oedema
and secondary atrophy.

{ Disc collaterals are common following CRVO, appearing as a small vascular loop on the optic nerve head.
They are also known as optociliary shunts or retinochoroidal collaterals and are thought to represent a compensatory circulation in response to impaired nerve
perfusion. Their development is believed to be associated with a markedly decreased risk of neovascularization. They can form in a range of conditions besides
CRVO, including chronic glaucoma and chronic optic
nerve compression.

оАБ FAF in acute RVO may show a characteristic fern-like perivenular hypoautoоАЫuorescence due to masking of background
signal by oedema. It corresponds to PIRW (see above) but is
more commonly identiоАЬable.
оАБ FA shows delayed arteriovenous transit time, masking by
haemorrhage, usually good retinal capillary perfusion and
some late leakage (Fig. 13.32D).
оАБ OCT is useful in the assessment of CMO, which is oоАЭen mild
in a non-ischaemic lesion.

TIP A VA of <6/60 and a RAPD normally indicates signiоАЯcant
retinal ischaemia and a poor visual prognosis in a patient with
central retinal vein occlusion.

Ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion

Ischaemic CRVO is characterized by substantially decreased retinal perfusion with capillary closure and retinal hypoxia. Macular
ischaemia and NVG are the major causes of visual morbidity.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Sudden and severe monocular painless visual
impairment. Occasionally presentation may be with pain, redness and photophobia due to neovascular glaucoma, a prior
reduction in vision having passed unnoticed or been ignored.
оАБ VA is usually вАШcounting оАЬngersвАЩ or worse. оАЯe visual prognosis
is generally extremely poor due to macular ischaemia.
оАБ RAPD is present.
оАБ NVI. Rubeosis iridis (Fig. 13.33A) develops in about 50% of
eyes, usually between 2 and 4 months (вАШhundred-day glaucomaвАЩ) and there is a high risk of neovascular glaucoma. оАЯe
pupillary margin should be examined at each review prior to
pharmacological mydriasis.
оАБ Gonioscopy. Angle neovascularization (Fig. 13.33B) may
occur in the absence of neovascularization at the pupillary

оАЯe presence of more than 10 disc areas of retinal capillary

of neovascularization.
оАБ OCT enables quantiоАЬcation of CMO.
оАБ Electroretinogram (ERG) is depressed and the extent of this
has sometimes been used to assess neovascular risk.

Treatment of the complications of CRVO

оАЯe management of non-ischaemic CRVO is generally much less
aggressive than ischaemic CRVO, so it is important to distinguish
the two as far as possible.

implantation and although this declines to baseline by 6
months, the treatment can then be repeated. As with triamcinolone, there is a slightly higher rate of IOP elevation
and cataract. Administration within 90 days of CMO onset
is likely to be associated with an improved outcome. Treatment can be repeated aоАЭer 4вАУ6 months. оАЯe COMRADE
study concludes that eyes treated with ranibizumab have
signiоАЬcantly better VA than those receiving intravitreal
dexamethasone (0.7 mg) at 6 months.

{ Intravitreal triamcinolone (Fig. 13.35C). оАЯe SCORE study
shows an improvement of three or more lines of vision at 1
year in over 25% of patients (versus 7% of controls) treated
with an average of two injections of 1 mg triamcinolone,
using a preservative-free preparation developed for intraocular use. оАЯere is a slightly higher rate of IOP elevation
and cataract than with observation. Risk factors for a rise
of IOP of >10 mmHg include: higher dose (4 mg vs 1 mg),
younger age and higher baseline IOP.

{ Laser photocoagulation. Although macular oedema is anatomically improved, laser is not beneоАЬcial for visual outcome, except in some younger patients.

{ Patients may still develop neovascular glaucoma, but the
risk is reduced. оАЯe onset of NVG may be delayed by up
to 18 months, so these patients need to be followed at
3-monthly intervals for at least 2вАУ3 years.

{ оАЯe management of NVG is discussed in Chapter 11.

{ Vitreous haemorrhage may respond to intravitreal antiVEGF, but deоАЬnitive treatment is with vitrectomy and
endolaser.
оАБ Review

{ Ischaemic CRVO. Where possible, patients with ischaemic
CRVO should be seen monthly for 6 months. Subsequent
monitoring should usually be for 2вАУ3 years to detect signiоАЬcant ischaemia, macular oedema and NVI. Once a disc collateral has developed, the risk of neovascularization reduces.

{ Non-ischaemic CRVO. In an obviously non-ischaemic
occlusion, initial follow-up should take place aоАЭer 3
months. Structured arrangements for review of test results
should be in place. оАЯe patient should be instructed to
make contact if the vision deteriorates as this may indicate the development of signiоАЬcant ischaemia. Pain or
redness, which may indicate neovascular glaucoma and
occasionally inоАЫammation without rubeosis, should also
be reported. Subsequent review is dependent on the clinical picture and response to treatment, with discharge from
follow-up usually taking place at 18вАУ24 months.

Prognosis

оАЯe main cause for poor vision aоАЭer a CRVO is chronic macular
oedema. оАЯe prognosis for ischaemic CRVO is extremely poor due
to macular ischaemia and neovascular glaucoma.
оАБ оАЯe prognosis is usually determined by the initial visual
acuity:

{ 6/18 or better: the VA is likely to remain so.

{ 6/24вАУ6/60: the clinical course is variable and the оАЬnal VA
is diоАЪcult to predict.

{ Worse than 6/60: improvement is unlikely.

{ Investigational treatments include chorioretinal anastomosis, vitrectomy with radial optic neurotomy and local
recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (rtPA) CRV
infusion.
оАБ Treatment of neovascularization

{ оАЯere is no beneоАЬt in performing PRP before the development of iris and angle neovascularization.

{ Frequent follow-up is key and PRP should be performed
without delay in eyes that develop NVI or angle neovascularization. оАЯis initially involves placing 1500вАУ2000 burns of
0.05вАУ0.1 s duration, spaced one burn width apart, with sufоАЬcient energy to produce a moderate reaction, avoiding areas
of haemorrhage (Fig. 13.35D). Treatment may be fractionated
and further photocoagulation is commonly required.

{ Adjunctive intravitreal anti-VEGF injections are commonly administered every 6 weeks until the eye stabilizes,

Systemic management in retinal vein occlusion

оАБ Control of systemic risk factors. Although there is no conclusive evidence that vascular mortality is higher in RVO
patients, cardiovascular risk factors are commonly identified after a retinal vein occlusion and should be dealt with.
As well as conferring systemic benefit this may also reduce
the risk of recurrent RVO. Smoking should be actively
discouraged.
оАБ Antiplatelet therapy. оАЯe role of aspirin or alternative antiplatelet agents in reducing the risk of further retinal venous
occlusion is unclear. оАЯis medication is not usually prescribed
unless there is a clear systemic indication.
оАБ Hormone replacement therapy (HRT). оАЯe risk of HRT
remains undeоАЬned. Most authorities would avoid continuing
or commencing oestrogen-containing HRT following RVO.
Appropriate expert advice should be obtained. оАЯe presence of

other thrombophilia risk factors is associated with a substantially increased risk of systemic venous thrombosis in conjunction with HRT.
оАБ Oral contraceptive pill. оАЯis should probably be discontinued
following RVO.
оАБ Others. A range of systemic treatment modalities (e.g. isovolaemic haemodilution, plasmapheresis, recombinant tissue
plasminogen infusion) has been employed to try to improve
visual outcomes in RVO, but clear evidence for beneоАЬt is lacking. Dehydration should be avoided.

Papillophlebitis

Papillophlebitis is an uncommon, poorly deоАЬned condition that
typically aоАЮects individuals under the age of 50 years who may have
a higher prevalence of hypertension and diabetes. Some or all cases
may simply be a variant of CRVO occurring in younger people,
though optic disc swelling or inоАЫammation of the central retinal
vein have been speculated as the initiating event in at least some
instances. оАЯere is likely to be some diagnostic overlap with diabetic

papillopathy. Papillophlebitis typically causes only mild to moderate
reduction of vision with no RAPD and has a generally good prognosis. Disc oedema is the dominant оАЬnding and retinal haemorrhages
and other signs such as cotton-wool spots are predominantly peripapillary and conоАЬned to the posterior pole (Fig. 13.36). CMO may
be present and posterior or anterior segment neovascularization have
been reported occasionally in concert with capillary non-perfusion
on FA. It may be bilateral, when raised intracranial pressure must be
ruled out. Investigation should probably be as for CRVO in a young
individual. Distinction from diabetic papillopathy may be diоАЪcult.
Treatment with intravitreal anti-VEGF or steroid has been reported.

RETINAL ARTERIAL OCCLUSIVE
DISEASE

Aetiology

оАЯe outer retina is supplied by the ciliary arteries via the choriocapillaris and the inner retina by the central retinal artery (CRA).

Fig. 13.36 Papillophlebitis.

оАЯe ophthalmic artery gives rise to both the CRA вАУ its оАЬrst branch

- and the ciliary arteries (see Fig. 19.5A). оАЯe latter also supply the
anterior segment via the rectus muscles.
оАБ Atherosclerosis-related embolism and thrombosis are
thought to be responsible for the majority of cases of retinal
artery occlusion, but the proportion of cases due to each of
these is unknown. оАЯe origin of emboli is most commonly
an atheromatous carotid plaque and since the ophthalmic artery is the оАЬrst branch of the internal carotid artery,
embolic material has an easy route to the eye. Emboli can
be refractile yellowвАУwhite cholesterol (Hollenhorst) plaques
(Fig. 13.37A), greyish elongated оАЬbrin-platelet aggregates
(Fig. 13.37B and C), non-scintillating white calciоАЬc particles
(Fig. 13.37D) and, rarely, vegetations from bacterial endocarditis, cardiac myxomatous material and fat. Other cardiac
causes include arrhythmia and mitral valve prolapse.
оАБ InоАЬammation in or around the vessel wall (e.g. giant cell
arteritis вАУ GCA, systemic lupus erythematosus, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, polyarteritis nodosa), vasospasm (e.g.
migraine) and systemic hypotension contribute in a minority.
оАБ оАЯrombophilic disorders that may be associated with retinal
artery occlusion (one-third of young patients) include hyperhomocysteinaemia, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome,
inherited defects of various natural anticoagulants and sickle
cell haemoglobinopathy.
оАБ Susac syndrome, a rare immune-mediated vasculo-occlusive
disease of young people, consists of the triad of branch retinal
artery occlusion, encephalopathy and sensorineural hearing loss.
Women predominate and both eyes are usually aоАЮected. Retinal
microaneurysms are found in 70% of aоАЮected individuals. Arteriolar wall hyperоАЫuorescence remote from the BRAO on FA is
typical of the condition. Treatment of Susac syndrome involves
immunosuppressive and immunomodulatory medication.

Systemic assessment

Following diagnosis and initial evaluation by an ophthalmologist
many elements of the assessment may be carried out by a specialist

CHAPTER

Retinal Vascular Disease 535
#### 13

stroke team. Urgent specialist vascular evaluation is essential
following a retinal arterial event, including amaurosis fugax.
оАЯe risk of a stroke is relatively high in the оАЬrst few days aоАЭer a
transient ischaemic attack (TIA) and occurs in 25% at 3 years. оАЯe
detection of atrial оАЬbrillation is of particular importance as admission for anticoagulation may be indicated.

TIP Urgent specialist cardiovascular evaluation is essential in
a patient with a retinal artery occlusion in order to reduce the
risk of a stroke or a similar event occurring in the fellow eye.

All patients

Many or most patients will already be aware of vascular risk factors
and/or disease.
оАБ Smoking should be enquired about.
оАБ Symptoms of GCA (1вАУ2% of central retinal artery occlusion вАУ
CRAO) such as headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness,
limb girdle pain, weight loss and existing polymyalgia rheumatica (see Ch. 19). GCA is extremely unlikely under 55вАУ60
years. It constitutes an ophthalmic emergency.
оАБ Pulse should be palpated to detect arrhythmia, particularly
atrial оАЬbrillation.
оАБ Blood pressure, checking for systemic hypertension.
оАБ Cardiac auscultation for a murmur.
оАБ Carotid auscultation is of limited value as the absence of a
bruit does not exclude signiоАЬcant stenosis.
оАБ ECG to detect arrhythmia and other cardiac disease.
оАБ ESR or PV and CRP to identify possible GCA.
оАБ Other blood tests include FBC (platelets may be raised in
GCA), glucose, lipids and urea and electrolytes, the latter to
exclude derangement including dehydration.
оАБ Carotid duplex scanning is a non-invasive screening
test involving a combination of high-resolution real-time
ultrasonography with Doppler оАЫow analysis. If signiоАЬcant
stenosis is present, surgical management may be considered.

Selected patients

оАЯe following additional tests can be considered on a targeted basis
in some patients, particularly if younger and with no known cardiovascular risk factors, or if there is an atypical clinical picture
(Fig. 13.38A and B).
оАБ Further carotid imaging (see Ch. 19).
оАБ Cranial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or computed
tomography (CT) may be indicated to rule out intracranial or
orbital pathology.
оАБ Echocardiography. Usually performed in young patients or
if there is a specific indication such as a history of rheumatic fever, known cardiac valvular disease, or intravenous
drug use.
оАБ Chest X-ray. Sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, leоАЭ ventricular hypertrophy in hypertension.
оАБ 24-hour ECG to exclude intermittent arrhythmia.
оАБ Additional blood tests

{ Fasting plasma homocysteine level to exclude
hyperhomocysteinaemia.

оАБ Visual оАЮeld testing conоАЬrms the defect, which rarely recovers.
оАБ FA shows delay in arterial оАЬlling and hypoоАЫuorescence of the
involved segment due to blockage of background оАЫuorescence
by retinal swelling (Fig. 13.39B and D).
оАБ Review in 3 months is warranted to review the appearance
of the fundus, the visual оАЬelds, provide advice on prognosis
and conоАЬrm that systemic management has been carried out
appropriately.

Central retinal artery occlusion

оАБ Symptoms. Sudden profound loss of vision, painless except in
GCA.
оАБ VA is severely reduced except if a cilioretinal artery supplying a critical macular area preserves central vision (see below).
Absence of light perception usually indicates either GCA or
ophthalmic artery occlusion. оАЯe prognosis is poor in all cases
unless recovery occurs in the оАЬrst few hours.
оАБ RAPD is profound, sometimes total (amaurotic pupil).
оАБ Fundus shows similar changes to BRAO but involving all retinal quadrants.

{ Syphilis serology.

{ Blood cultures.

Amaurosis fugax

Amaurosis fugax is characterized by transient painless monocular
loss of vision, oоАЭen described as a curtain coming down over the
eye. оАЯe Amaurosis Fugax Study Group divides the causes into оАЬve
categories (embolic, hemodynamic, ocular, neurologic and idiopathic), but in clinical practice it is typically used to refer to transient visual loss of embolic origin. It is common for patients to be
unaware of whether transient unilateral visual loss aоАЮects one eye
or the ipsilateral hemiоАЬeld of both (the latter indicating cerebral
rather than more anterior ischaemia). Embolic visual loss, which
may be complete, usually lasts a few minutes. Recovery is generally in the same pattern as the loss, although usually more gradual.
Frequency of attacks may vary from several times a day to once
every few months. оАЯe attacks may sometimes be accompanied
by an ipsilateral cerebral TIA, with contralateral neurological features. Investigation and systemic management of embolic-pattern
amaurosis fugax is the same as that of retinal arterial occlusion and
should be undertaken urgently because of the high risk of stroke.

Branch retinal artery occlusion

оАБ Symptoms. Sudden and profound painless altitudinal or sectoral visual оАЬeld loss. Branch retinal artery occlusion (BRAO)
can sometimes go unnoticed, particularly if central vision is
spared.
оАБ VA is variable. In patients where central vision is severely compromised, the prognosis is commonly poor unless the obstruction is relieved within a few hours (see below).
оАБ RAPD is oоАЭen present.
оАБ Fundus signs may be subtle.

{ Attenuation of arteries and veins with sludging and segmentation of the blood column (вАШcattle truckingвАЩ/вАЩbox-carвАЩ
appearance).

{ Cloudy white oedematous (ground glass) retina corresponding to the area of ischaemia (Fig. 13.39A and C).

{ One or more occluding emboli may be seen, especially at
bifurcation points (see Fig. 13.39A).

{ оАЯe aоАЮected artery is likely to remain attenuated. Occasionally, recanalization may leave absent ophthalmoscopic
signs.

{ оАЯe orange reоАЫex from the intact choroid stands out at the
thin foveola, in contrast to the surrounding pale retina, giving rise to a вАШcherry-red spotвАЩ appearance (Fig. 13.40A and B).

{ оАЯe peripapillary retina may appear especially swollen and
opaque.

{ An occasional small haemorrhage is not unusual.

{ Emboli are visible in 20%, when Nd:YAG embolysis may
be considered (see below).

{ In eyes with a cilioretinal artery part of the macula will
remain of normal colour (Fig. 13.40C).

{ Retinal signs can sometimes be subtle. Retinal oedema
may take several hours to develop.

{ Over a few days to weeks the retinal cloudiness and вАШcherryred spotвАЩ gradually disappear although the arteries remain
attenuated. Later signs include optic atrophy, vessel sheathing and patchy inner retinal atrophy and RPE changes.

{ Around 2% of eyes with CRAO develop retinal or disc
neovascularization.

{ Rubeosis iridis may occur in up to about 1 in 5 eyes, typically earlier than in CRVO (4вАУ5 weeks compared with 3
months, though sometimes later) and along with very poor
vision may indicate ophthalmic artery occlusion.
оАБ OCT may show a highly reоАЫective embolic plaque within the
superоАЬcial optic nerve head.
оАБ FA shows a variable delay in arterial оАЬlling and masking of
background choroidal оАЫuorescence by retinal oedema. A
patent cilioretinal artery will оАЬll during the early phase (Fig.
13.40D).
оАБ Electroretinography may be helpful to establish the diagnosis
if in doubt, particularly to distinguish from optic nerve disease, typically when signs are subtle. A diminished b-wave is
present.
оАБ Ophthalmic review. The patient should be seen by an
ophthalmologist after 3вАУ4 weeks and a minimum of

Cilioretinal artery occlusion

A cilioretinal artery is present in about one-third of eyes, providing the central macula with a second arterial supply derived
from the posterior ciliary circulation. Its main importance is
that when present it may facilitate preservation of central vision
following central retinal artery occlusion, provided the fovea is
supplied.
оАБ Isolated. оАЯis is rare (Fig. 13.41A and B). It may occur in
young patients with an associated systemic vasculitis.

оАБ Combined with CRVO. оАЯis (Fig. 13.41C) is not uncommon,
but occlusion is transient and the prognosis is better than in
isolated cilioretinal artery occlusion.
оАБ Combined with anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (Fig.
13.41D), typically aоАЮects patients with GCA and carries a very
poor prognosis.

Treatment of acute retinal artery
occlusion

Retinal artery occlusion is an emergency because it causes irreversible
visual loss unless the retinal circulation can be re-established before
the development of retinal infarction. оАЯeoretically, timely dislodgement of thrombus or emboli may ameliorate subsequent visual loss. In
primate studies, partial recovery is possible if the ischaemia is reversed
within 4 hours. оАЯe time when irreversible retinal damage occurs with
no visual recovery is not known in humans, but has been postulated to

be about 6 hours in studies determining the eоАЮect of tissue plasminogen activator on thrombolysis. Unfortunately, treatment is usually
not eоАЮective. In two-thirds the оАЬnal VA is worse than 6/120 and only
one-оАЬоАЭh are leоАЭ with a VA of 6/12 or better. оАЯe various therapeutic
options should be discussed before use, including the lack of evidence
for clear beneоАЬt and the associated risks. оАЯe following can be tried in
patients with an occlusion of less than 6 hoursвАЩ duration at presentation though evidence of beneоАЬt is limited:
оАБ Ocular massage using a three-mirror contact lens (allows direct
artery visualization). оАЯe aim is to mechanically collapse the arterial lumen and cause prompt changes in arterial оАЫow, improving
perfusion and potentially dislodging an embolus or thrombus.
One described method consists of positive pressure for 10вАУ15 seconds followed by release, continued for 3вАУ5 minutes. Self-massage
through closed eyelids can be continued by the patient.

оАБ Anterior chamber paracentesis using a 27-gauge needle to
withdraw 0.1вАУ0.2 ml of aqueous has been advocated by some
authorities. Povidone-iodine 5% and topical antibiotic are
instilled a few minutes prior to the procedure, with a short
course of antibiotic aоАЭerwards. It may be prudent for ocular
massage to be avoided following paracentesis.
оАБ Topical apraclonidine 1%, timolol 0.5% and intravenous acetazolamide 500 mg to achieve a more sustained lowering of
intraocular pressure.
оАБ вАШRebreathingвАЩ into a paper bag in order to elevate blood carbon dioxide and respiratory acidosis has been advocated, as
this may promote vasodilatation. Similarly, breathing a high
oxygen (95%) and carbon dioxide (5%) mixture, вАШcarbogenвАЩ,
has been advocated for a possible dual eоАЮect of retarding ischaemia and vasodilatation.

оАБ Transluminal Nd:YAG laser embolysis/embolectomy has
been advocated for BRAO or CRAO where an occluding
embolus is visible. Although experience with this technique
is limited, surprisingly good оАЬnal visual results have been
reported. A variable number of shots of 0.5вАУ1mJ or higher
(to a maximum of 2.4mJ) are applied directly to the embolus
using a fundus contact lens. Embolectomy has been said to
occur if the embolus is ejected into the vitreous via a hole in
the arteriole. оАЯe main complication is subretinal and vitreous
haemorrhage in approximately half, which may be curtailed
with pressure on the globe.
оАБ Thrombolysis. Extrapolating from successful treatment of
stroke and myocardial infarction, various strategies have
been used to deliver thrombolytic agents to the ophthalmic
artery, including local arterial (internal carotid and ophthalmic) and intravenous infusion. On the basis of observational data and in the absence of other effective treatment,
patients with acute CRAO who are seen within 4.5 hours of

onset of visual loss could be considered for treatment with
intravenous tissue plasminogen activator (tPA). The commonest regimen used is intravenous (IV) alteplase (0.9mg/
kg, with 10% given over 1 minute and the remainder over
59 minutes). Patients undergoing this treatment should be
fully informed of the benefits and risks of treatment. A large
multicentre trial of local intraocular thrombolysis with
recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (rtPA) showed no
benefit over conservative treatment that included isovolaemic haemodilution. This finding may reflect the rapidity
of retinal dysfunction that occurs secondary to ischaemia
and perhaps the resistance to dissolution of embolic, versus
thrombotic obstruction.

TIP To reduce the risk of stroke and ischaemic heart disease,
the systemic factors responsible for a central retinal artery
occlusion need to be determined and treated.

CHAPTER

Retinal Vascular Disease 541
#### 13

Systemic management following retinal
arterial occlusion

оАБ The risk of stroke is relatively high in the first few days
following retinal artery occlusion or amaurosis fugax.
Urgent referral to a specialist stroke clinic is imperative.
Of those with a CRAO who are sent for urgent systemic
evaluation, 30% will have severe carotid disease, coincidental stroke or extreme hypertension and 20% will have
simultaneous myocardial infarction or critical heart disease. Almost 95% will subsequently need a change in their
systemic medication and 25% will require urgent surgical
intervention.
оАБ General risk factors as discussed above should be addressed
and smoking should be discontinued. Urgent referral to an
appropriate physician is mandatory for signiоАЬcant cardiac
arrhythmia.
оАБ Antiplatelet therapy is commenced provided there are no
contraindications. An immediate loading dose of 600 mg may
be given. Alternative/additional agents include dipyridamole
and clopidogrel. If оАЬbrinolysis (see above) is being considered,
this should be discussed with a physician prior to starting antiplatelet treatment.
оАБ Oral anticoagulation (e.g. warfarin) may be prescribed for
some patients, particularly those with atrial оАЬbrillation.
оАБ Carotid endarterectomy may be indicated in patients with
symptomatic stenosis greater than 70%.

Asymptomatic retinal embolus

It is not uncommon to identify a retinal embolus on routine examination of an asymptomatic older patient. оАЯis indicates a substantially increased risk of stroke and ischaemic heart disease and
management should consist of evaluation and treatment of risk
factors discussed above. A higher threshold for carotid surgery is
appropriate.

OCULAR ISCHAEMIC SYNDROME

Introduction

Ocular ischaemic syndrome (OIS) results from chronic ocular
hypoperfusion secondary to severe (more than 90%) ipsilateral
atherosclerotic carotid stenosis. It typically aоАЮects older patients
and may be associated with diabetes, hypertension, cardio- and
cerebrovascular disease. оАЯe male:female ratio is about 2:1. Fiveyear mortality is around 40%, most frequently from cardiac disease. OIS, along with non-ischaemic CRVO, is sometimes termed
вАШvenous stasis retinopathyвАЩ but it may be prudent to avoid this
term.

Diagnosis

OIS is unilateral in 80%. оАЯe signs are variable and may be subtle
such that the condition is missed or misdiagnosed.

оАБ Symptoms. Gradual loss of vision over weeks or months,
although occasionally loss may be sudden or intermittent
(amaurosis fugax). Ocular and periocular pain may also be
present (40%). Patients may notice unusually persistent aоАЭerimages, or worsening of vision with sudden exposure to bright
light (вАШbright light amaurosis fugaxвАЩ), with slow adaptation.
оАЯe prognosis for vision is oоАЭen very poor, though patients
with better acuity at presentation are more likely to retain this.
About 25% will deteriorate to light perception by the end of
1 year.
оАБ Anterior segment

{ In diabetic patients the retinopathy may be more severe
ipsilateral to carotid stenosis.
оАБ FA. Delayed choroidal оАЬlling and prolonged arteriovenous
transit is the major feature. Non-perfusion, vessel wall staining
and retinal oedema may also be evident (Fig. 13.42BвАУD).
оАБ Carotid imaging may involve duplex ultrasonography, digital
subtraction angiography, MR or CT angiography.

Management

оАБ Anterior segment inоАЬammation is treated with topical steroid and a mydriatic as appropriate.
оАБ Neovascular glaucoma is managed medically or surgically
(see Ch. 11).
оАБ Proliferative retinopathy can be treated with PRP although
the outcome is considerably less certain than in proliferative
diabetic retinopathy. Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents may be
beneоАЬcial.
оАБ Macular oedema may respond to intravitreal steroid or antiVEGF agents, or to carotid surgery.
оАБ Carotid surgery. Endarterectomy or stenting may be performed to reduce the risk of stroke. It may be beneоАЬcial for
proliferative retinopathy and neovascular glaucoma and may
help to stabilize vision. Endarterectomy cannot be performed
where there is total obstruction, and in this situation extracranialвАУintracranial arterial bypass surgery is sometimes carried
out. It should be noted that an increase in ocular perfusion following surgery can sometimes be associated with a rise in IOP.

{ DiоАЮuse episcleral injection and corneal oedema.

{ Aqueous оАЫare with few cells (ischaemic pseudo-iritis).

{ Iris atrophy with a mid-dilated, poorly reacting pupil.

{ Rubeosis iridis is common, developing in up to 90% and
oоАЭen progresses to neovascular glaucoma. оАЯe IOP may
remain low due to poor ocular perfusion.

{ Cataract in advanced cases.
оАБ Fundus

{ Venous dilatation, arteriolar narrowing, deep round and
оАЫame haemorrhages and occasionally disc oedema (Fig.
13.42A) and cotton-wool spots.

{ Proliferative retinopathy with NVD and occasionally NVE.

{ Spontaneous arterial pulsation, most pronounced near
the optic disc, is present in most cases or may be easily
induced by exerting gentle pressure on the globe (digital
ophthalmodynamometry).

{ Macular oedema can occur.

542 Hypertensive Eye Disease

A B

C D

Fig. 13.42 Ocular ischaemic syndrome showing AV оАЯlling delay. оАЭAоАЮ Venous dilatation, arteriolar narrowing, a few scattered оАЮame haemorrhages and hard exudates, and disc oedema;
оАЭBоАЮ early FA (20оАЫs) and оАЭCоАЮ FA early phase (35оАЫs) showing delayed choroidal оАЯlling and prolonged arteriovenous transit; оАЭDоАЮ FA late phase (5оАЫmin) showing disc and perivascular hyperоАЮuorescence, with spotty hyperоАЮuorescence at the posterior pole due to leakage.

Surgery tends to be of greater beneоАЬt when performed before
the onset of severe ocular ischaemia.
оАБ Investigation and management of cardiovascular risk factors in conjunction with the appropriate medical specialists is
essential. OIS is occasionally the only manifestation of marked
systemic vascular disease. Full investigation should be carried
out, broadly similar to that for retinal arterial occlusion.

HYPERTENSIVE EYE DISEASE

Retinopathy

Systemic arterial hypertension is common. In addition to heart,
kidneys and brain, the eye is often affected by hypertension.
Ocular effects can be observed in the retina, choroid and optic
nerve. The primary response of the retinal arterioles to systemic hypertension is vasoconstriction, which is less marked
in older individuals because of involutional sclerosis conferring
increased rigidity. Arteriolosclerosis refers to hardening and

loss of elasticity of small vessel walls, manifested most obviously
by arteriovenous (AV) nipping (nicking) at crossing points. The
presence of this sign makes it probable that hypertension has
been present for many years, even if the BP is currently controlled. Mild AV changes may be seen in the absence of hypertension. In sustained hypertension the inner bloodвАУretinal
barrier is disrupted, increased vascular permeability leading to
flame-shaped retinal haemorrhages and oedema.
оАБ Grade 1. Mild generalized retinal arteriolar narrowing (Fig.
13.43A).
оАБ Grade 2. Focal arteriolar narrowing (Fig. 13.43B) and arteriovenous nipping (Fig. 13.43C). A вАШcopper wiringвАЩ opaciоАЬed
appearance of arteriolar walls may be seen (Fig. 13.43D).
оАБ Grade 3. Grade 2 plus retinal haemorrhages (dot, blot, оАЫame),
exudates (chronic retinal oedema may result in the deposition
of hard exudates around the fovea as a вАШmacular starвАЩ вАУ Fig.
13.43E) and cotton-wool spots.
оАБ Grade 4. Severe grade 3 plus optic disc swelling, which is a
marker of вАШmalignantвАЩ hypertension (Fig. 13.43F).

{ Reduced retinal arteriolar calibre (see Fig. 13.43A) is an
early pre-hypertensive sign and if identiоАЬed should prompt
BP monitoring.

{ Wider venular calibre is relatively speciоАЬc for impaired

{ Low arteriolar calibre and high venular calibre are both
thought to be markers of preclinical cardiovascular
disease.

{ Increased venular tortuosity (Fig. 13.44A) can be

described, notably conditions associated with high and
low vascular flow.

Choroidopathy

Hypertensive choroidopathy is rare but may occur as the result of an
acute hypertensive crisis (accelerated hypertension) in young adults.
оАБ Siegrist streaks are оАЫecks arranged linearly along choroidal
vessels and are indicative of оАЬbrinoid necrosis associated with
malignant hypertension (Fig. 13.45A).
оАБ Elschnig spots are focal choroidal infarcts seen as small black
spots surrounded by yellow haloes (Fig. 13.45B).
оАБ Exudative retinal detachment, sometimes bilateral, may
occur in acute severe hypertension such as that associated with
toxaemia of pregnancy.

PARACENTRAL ACUTE MIDDLE
MACULOPATHY (PAMM)

PAMM is an uncommon condition of the macula which occurs
mainly in individuals aged 60вАУ75 years, presumably as a complication of retinal microvasculopathy or systemic vascular disease. In
younger individuals no cause may be found. оАЯe patient notices
a sudden positive scotoma close to оАЬxation in one eye and has
no other visual or ocular symptoms. Fundus examination reveals
subtle whitish parafoveal lesions within the retina. When an OCT
scan is undertaken in the acute phase a placoid, hyperreоАЫective
band is seen at the level of the inner nuclear layer, with a normal
outer retina (Fig. 13.46). Partial resolution is the most common
outcome, although full resolution may occasionally occur. оАЯe
main diоАЮerential is acute macular neuroretinopathy, which can be
excluded with an OCT (see Ch. 12). оАЯere is no treatment. Systemic diseases like arterial hypertension and diabetes need to be
excluded.

SICKLE-CELL RETINOPATHY

Sickling haemoglobinopathies

Sickling haemoglobinopathies are caused by one or more abnormal
haemoglobins that induce red blood cells to adopt an anomalous
shape (Fig. 13.47) under conditions of physiological stress such as
hypoxia and acidosis, with resultant vascular occlusion. Mutant haemoglobin variants S and C can be found in combination with the
normal adult haemoglobin A or, less commonly, with other mutant
haemoglobin variants. Retinopathy can occur in sickle-cell disease
(homozygous for mutant haemoglobin S, i.e. SS), sickle-cell C disease (SC вАУ the most likely to develop severe retinopathy) and sicklecell thalassaemia disease. It is rare in patients with sickle-cell trait
(SA вАУ found in 10% of African-Americans), unless there is other coexisting systemic disease such as diabetes or an inоАЫammatory disorder. Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (CAI) should be avoided in these
disorders as they can precipitate sickling and vascular occlusion.

Anterior segment

оАБ Conjunctiva. Dark red corkscrew- or comma-shaped vessels
that are typically transient.
оАБ Iris. Patches of ischaemic atrophy, oоАЭen extending from the
pupillary edge to the collarette and occasionally rubeosis.
оАБ Hyphaema may be spontaneous or follow minor trauma.
Careful IOP control (avoiding CAI) is critical to reduce the
risk of RVO in the presence of a hyphaema.

NonоАЯproliferative retinopathy

оАБ Venous changes. Tortuosity (see Fig. 13.44) is very common
and is thought to be due to peripheral arteriovenous shunting.
оАБ Optic disc вАШsign of sicklingвАЩ. Dark red blots on the disc surface
due to small vessel occlusion.

оАБ вАШSalmon patchesвАЩ. OrangeвАУred mid-peripheral superоАЬcial
intraretinal haemorrhages (Fig. 13.48A) that may break
through to become preretinal or subretinal. оАЯe initiating
event is thought to be a vascular occlusion. Salmon patches
resolve to leave schisis cavities containing refractile deposits,
вАШblack sunburstsвАЩ (see below) when the RPE is suоАЪciently stimulated, or a combination lesion.
оАБ Black sunbursts. Patches of peripheral RPE hyperplasia
and chorioretinal atrophy (see Fig. 13.48A) that evolve from
some salmon patches. The extent and morphology of pigmentation is variable, but an outer pale band is generally
present.
оАБ Arteriolar changes. Occlusions can involve branch, central or
macular vessels (Fig. 13.48B) and if seen acutely may beneоАЬt
from measures such as 100% oxygen and exchange transfusion. вАШSilver wiringвАЩ of arterioles in the peripheral retina signiоАЬes previously occluded vessels. Corkscrewing of peripheral
vessels may also be seen.
оАБ Macular (retinal) depression sign. An oval depression in the
temporal macular retina due to retinal thinning following arteriolar occlusion, with irregularity of the light reоАЫex. Vascular
anomalies such as microaneurysms and epiretinal membranes
may occur.
оАБ Peripheral areas of whitening or darkening.
оАБ Angioid streaks (see Ch. 14) occur in up to 6%.

Proliferative retinopathy

Diagnosis

оАЯe development of proliferative retinopathy is usually insidious,
with no symptoms unless vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment occurs.

546 Thalassaemia Retinopathy

Fig. 13.47 Several sickle cells (arrows) in a peripheral smear
of a patient with homozygous (HbSS) sickle-cell anaemia.

оАБ Stage 1. Peripheral arteriolar occlusion.
оАБ Stage 2. Peripheral arteriovenous anastomosis (Fig. 13.49A)
proximal to non-perfused areas.
оАБ Stage 3. вАШSea fanвАЩ neovascularization (Fig. 13.49B) develops
at the edge of perfused retina, usually with a single supplying
arteriole and a single draining venule. Disc neovascularization
may rarely occur.
оАБ Stage 4. Vitreous haemorrhage from the NV.
оАБ Stage 5. Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment caused by a
retinal break associated with extensive оАЬbrovascular proliferation (Fig. 13.49C). Tractional detachment may also occur (Fig.
13.49D).
оАБ FA in stage 3 shows оАЬlling of sea fans and peripheral capillary
non-perfusion (Fig. 13.49E) followed by leakage from the NV
(Fig. 13.49F). Wide-оАЬeld imaging is especially suited to evaluation of this condition.

TIP Avoid the use of carbonic anhydrase inhibitors in patients
with sickel-cell anaemia as these drugs can cause sickling and
vascular occlusion.

Treatment

оАБ Observation if vitreous haemorrhage has not occurred, particularly in middle-aged and older patients. Many neovascular
complexes involute spontaneously as a result of auto-infarction
or оАЬbrotic strangulation, subsequently appearing as greyish
оАЬbrovascular lesions.
оАБ Laser or cryotherapy ablation of peripheral non-perfused retina is probably the optimal approach, though ablation of neovascularization may also be used. Ablation of feeder vessels is
now rarely performed due to a high incidence of subsequent
macular neovascularization.
оАБ Vitreoretinal surgery may be required for tractional retinal
detachment and/or persistent vitreous haemorrhage. Caution
is required as anterior segment ischaemia, potentially severe, is
very common following scleral explant application.

A

B

Fig. 13.48 Non-proliferative sickle cell retinopathy. оАЭAоАЮ RPE
hyperplasia (вАШblack sunburstвАЩ) and preretinal haemorrhages (вАШsalmon patchвАЩ) (arrow); оАЭBоАЮ FA showing macular ischaemia.

THALASSAEMIA RETINOPATHY

Thalassaemia is a common genetic disorder in which a mutation gives rise to abnormal haemoglobin with a consequent failure of normal red blood cell maturation. Ocular involvement
occurs in patients with thalassaemia major and thalassaemia
intermedia. Changes are caused by the disease itself as well as
treatment with blood transfusions and iron chelating agents
such as desferrioxamine. A major mechanism is tissue deposition of iron (siderosis) from the lysing of abnormal red cells.
The ocular features predominantly affect the posterior segment
and are non-proliferative, though vitreous haemorrhage has
been reported. Visual dysfunction is rarely severe. Manifestations include cataract, a smooth featureless iris, vascular tortuosity, angioid streaks, optic neuropathy and pigmentary retinal
mottling, including pattern dystrophy-like macular changes.
The fundus appearance may resemble that of pseudoxanthoma
elasticum.

548 Retinopathy of Prematurity

RETINOPATHY OF PREMATURITY

Introduction

Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) is a condition that aоАЮects the
developing retina of premature low birthweight infants and is one
of the commonest causes of childhood blindness worldwide. оАЯe
exact cause of ROP is unclear, but early exposure to high ambient
oxygen concentrations appears to be a key risk factor. With new
methods of monitoring oxygen levels in premature babies, oxygen
use as a major risk factor has diminished in importance. Systemic
illness is an additional risk factor (for example: anaemia, sepsis
and low vitamin E levels). In the early phase of ROP development
vessel growth is retarded by hyperoxia, but subsequently retinal
hypoxia promotes anomalous vascularization. оАЯe retina has no
blood vessels until the fourth month of gestation, when vascular complexes grow from optic disc hyaloid vessels towards the
periphery. оАЯe nasal retina is normally fully vascularized aоАЭer 8
months of gestation, the temporal periphery at or by 1 month aоАЭer
delivery. Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is believed
to play an important role in the vascularization process. In lowincome countries there has been a rise in the incidence of ROP in
recent years, which corresponds to the establishment of intensive
neonatal treatment regimens in premature babies who would not
previously have survived.

TIP Babies born before 32 weeksвАЩ gestational age or
weighing less than 1500 g should be screened for retinopathy
of prematurity.

Active disease

оАЯe clinical оАЬndings in ROP are described as below according to
the 2021 International ClassiоАЬcation of Retinopathy of Prematurity (ICROP, third edition).

Location

Concentric zones centred on the optic disc are described (Fig.
13.50). оАЯe location of the most posterior retinal vascularization or
ROP lesion denotes the zone. оАЯe term вАШnotchвАЩ is used to describe
an incursion by the ROP lesion of 1вАУ2 clock hours into a more
posterior zone.
оАБ Zone I is bounded by an imaginary circle, the radius of which
is twice the distance from the disc to the centre of the macula.
With a 28-dioptre binocular indirect lens, only zone I is seen if
any part of the optic nerve head is visible.
оАБ Zone II extends concentrically from the edge of zone I, with
a radius that extends from the centre of the disc to the nasal
ora serrata.
оАБ Zone III consists of a residual temporal crescent anterior to
zone II.

Staging of acute disease

оАЯis describes the abnormal vascular response at the junction of
immature avascular peripheral and vascularized posterior retina.

Staging for the eye as a whole is determined by the most severe
manifestation.
оАБ Stage 1 (demarcation line) is a thin, оАЫat, tortuous, grey-white
line running roughly parallel with the ora serrata. It is more
prominent in the temporal periphery. оАЯere is abnormal
branching or вАШarcadingвАЩ of vessels leading up to the line (Fig.
13.51A).
оАБ Stage 2 (ridge) arises in the region of the demarcation line, has
height and width and extends above the plane of the retina.
Blood vessels enter the ridge and small isolated neovascular
tuоАЭs may be seen posterior to it (Fig. 13.51B).
оАБ Stage 3 (extraretinal fibrovascular proliferation) extends
from the ridge into the vitreous. It is continuous with the
posterior aspect of the ridge, causing a ragged appearance
as the proliferation becomes more extensive (Fig. 13.51C).
The severity of stage 3 can be subdivided into mild, moderate and severe depending on the extent of extraretinal
fibrous tissue infiltrating the vitreous. The highest incidence of this stage is around the post-conceptual age of 35
weeks.
оАБ Stage 4 (partial retinal detachment) is divided into extrafoveal
(stage 4A вАУ Fig. 13.51D) and foveal (stage 4B). оАЯe detachment is generally concave and circumferentially orientated. In
progressive cases the оАЬbrous tissue continues to contract, and
the detachment increases in height and extends anteriorly and
posteriorly.
оАБ Stage 5 refers to total retinal detachment.
оАБ вАШPlusвАЩ disease signiоАЬes a tendency to progression and is characterized by dilatation and tortuosity of blood vessels (Fig.
13.51E) involving at least two quadrants of the posterior fundus. Other features include failure of the pupil to dilate and
vitreous haze. вАШPre-plusвАЩ disease is also described.
оАБ Aggressive ROP (вАШrushвАЩ disease) describes a severe, rapidly
progressing form of ROP located in zones I or II. It is characterized by its posterior location, prominence of plus disease
and ill-deоАЬned nature of the retinopathy. It is uncommon, but
if untreated usually progresses to stage 5, sometimes within a
few days.
оАБ Extent of disease is deоАЬned as 12 sectors using clock-hour
designations.

Type

Treatment guidelines at most centres have been revised based
on the Early Treatment of Retinopathy of Prematurity (ETROP)
clinical trial. оАЯe concept of вАШthreshold diseaseвАЩ formerly taken as
the criterion for treatment has been superseded, with outcomes
improved by earlier intervention. оАЯe outcome aоАЭer treatment varies depending on severity of disease; 30% of high-risk zone I ROP
will have an unfavourable visual outcome.
оАБ Type 1. Treatment is now recommended within 72 hours for
type 1 disease.

{ Any ROP stage in zone I when accompanied by plus
disease.

{ Stage 3 to any extent within zone I.

{ Stage 2 or 3 in zone II, together with plus disease.
оАБ Type 2 disease requires observation.

Later phases

оАБ Regression refers to disease involution and resolution. It may
be complete or incomplete. оАЯe оАЬrst sign is typically vascular
regression and tends to occur more rapidly aоАЭer anti-VEGF
treatment (1вАУ3 days) than aоАЭer laser photocoagulation (7вАУ14
days). Other clinical signs include resolution of intraretinal
haemorrhages, greater media clarity and better pupillary dilation. Regression of the ROP lesion is characterized by thinning
and whitening of neovascular tissue.
оАБ Reactivation refers to recurrence of acute phase features. It
is seen more frequently aоАЭer anti-VEGF treatment than aоАЭer
spontaneous regression and very rarely occurs aоАЭer complete
laser photocoagulation.

Screening

Formal criteria vary and risk factors and screening criteria developed in one country will not necessarily apply in another country.
In principle, babies born at or before 30вАУ32 weeksвАЩ gestational age,
or weighing 1500 g or less, should be screened for ROP. Severe illness in other premature babies may also prompt screening. оАЯis
involves indirect ophthalmoscopy with a 28 D lens or a 2.2 panfunduscopic Volk lens and scleral depression, or a wide-оАЬeld retinal camera with careful oversight. Screening should begin 4вАУ7
weeks postnatally. Subsequent review is at 1- to 3-week intervals,
depending on the severity of the disease, continuing until retinal
vascularization reaches zone III. оАЯe pupils in a premature infant
can be dilated with 0.5% cyclopentolate and 2.5% phenylephrine.
Topical anaesthetic is instilled and a neonatal eyelid speculum
used. Monitoring, especially for apnoea, is prudent during and
aоАЭer examination. About 10% of babies screened require treatment. Refractive error, strabismus and amblyopia are more common in children with ROP and long-term monitoring is required.

CHAPTER

Retinal Vascular Disease 549
#### 13

Techniques have evolved which allow small babies to be imaged
(Fig. 13.51F).

Treatment

оАБ Laser ablation of avascular peripheral retina (Fig. 13.52A and
B) has largely replaced cryotherapy because visual and anatomical outcomes are superior.
оАБ Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents. оАЯe BEAT-ROP (Bevacizumab Eliminates the Angiogenic оАЯreat of Retinopathy of
Prematurity) study results have changed the approach to the
treatment of ROP. As a general principle, intravitreal antiVEGF treatment should be used as оАЬrst line treatment for
zone I ROP, while laser therapy should be used for zone II
ROP. Bevacizumab appears to result in a lower rate of ROP
recurrence than ranibizumab. Allowing retinal development
to proceed normally without the destruction integral to laser
treatment is a potential advantage of anti-VEGF medication,
but there is mounting evidence that the disease can recur
following cessation of treatment. When compared to conventional laser treatment there is a greater chance of the eye
developing persistent peripheral vascularization with antiVEGF treatment, but a lower chance that the eye will develop
high myopia in time.
оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy for tractional retinal detachment not
involving the macula (stage 4A) can be performed successfully
with respect to anatomical (90% success) and visual outcome.
оАЯe visual outcome in stages 4B (e.g. 60%) and 5 (e.g. 20%)
is typically disappointing even with successful anatomical
reattachment.

Cicatricial disease

About 20% of infants with active ROP develop cicatricial complications, which range from innocuous to extremely severe. In
general, the more advanced or the more posterior the proliferative disease at the time of involution, the worse the cicatricial
sequelae. Findings range from moderate temporal vitreoretinal fibrosis and straightening of vascular arcades (Fig. 13.53A
and B) with вАШdraggingвАЩ of the macula and disc (Fig. 13.53C),
progressing to retrolental fibrovascular tissue that can lead to
falciform retinal fold formation (Fig. 13.53D) and to retinal
detachment (Fig. 13.53E), sometimes total (Fig. 13.53F) and
known as вАШretrolental fibroplasiaвАЩ (a term that has been used
synonymously with ROP in the past). Secondary angle-closure
glaucoma may develop due to progressive shallowing of the
anterior chamber caused by forward displacement of the irisвАУ
lens diaphragm with anterior synechiae formation. Lensectomy
and anterior vitrectomy may be tried, but the results are generally poor.

RETINAL ARTERY MACROANEURYSM

A retinal artery macroaneurysm is a localized dilatation of a retinal arteriole and has a predilection for older hypertensive (75%)
women. Dyslipidaemia is also associated. оАЯey are usually solitary
and 90% involve only one eye.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Insidious impairment of vision due to leakage
involving the macula. Sudden visual loss due to haemorrhage
is less common.
оАБ Fundus. A saccular arteriolar dilatation is typical, oоАЭen at a
bifurcation or an arteriovenous crossing on a temporal vascular
arcade. оАЯe aneurysm may enlarge to several times the diameter
of the vessel. оАЯere is associated retinal haemorrhage in 50%.
оАБ Course

{ Chronic leakage. Persistent retinal oedema with exudate formation (Fig. 13.54A) is common and may aоАЮect central vision.

{ Haemorrhage. Intra-, sub- or preretinal bleeding (Fig.
13.54B and C). оАЯe prognosis for central visual function
in those with submacular haemorrhage is generally poor.

{ Spontaneous involution following thrombosis and оАЬbrosis
is very common and may precede or follow the development of leakage or haemorrhage.

of arteriolar occlusion), or both. Subthreshold laser may be
as eоАЮective as standard photocoagulation. It may take several
months for oedema and exudate to fully absorb.
оАБ Intravitreal bevacizumab closed 95% of macroaneurysms in a
case series, with resolution of macular oedema.
оАБ Nd:YAG laser hyaloidotomy may be considered for a persistent premacular haemorrhage in order to disperse the blood
into the vitreous cavity (see Fig. 13.23), from where it may be
absorbed more quickly.
оАБ Intravitreal gas injection with face-down positioning may
shiоАЭ subretinal haemorrhage away from the macula. Adjunctive intravitreal recombinant tissue plasminogen activator
(rtPA) may be used.
оАБ Vitrectomy may be necessary for persistent vitreous
haemorrhage.

PRIMARY RETINAL TELANGIECTASIA

Retinal capillary telangiectasia is relatively common. Most cases
develop secondary to another retinal condition, typically involving
inоАЫammation or vascular compromise; for example, DR and RVO.
Primary retinal telangiectasia comprises a group of rare, idiopathic,
congenital or acquired retinal vascular anomalies characterized by
dilatation and tortuosity of retinal blood vessels, multiple aneurysms, vascular leakage and the deposition of hard exudates. Retinal telangiectasis involves the capillary bed, although the arterioles
and venules may also be involved (Fig. 13.56).

Idiopathic macular telangiectasia

оАЯis is described in Chapter 14

Coats disease

Introduction

Coats disease is an idiopathic retinal telangiectasia that generally
occurs in early childhood. It is associated with intraretinal and

{ Other complications: epiretinal membrane, macular
neovascularization.
оАБ FA. Uniform оАЬlling of the macroaneurysm is typical (Fig. 13.54D),
with late leakage. Incomplete оАЬlling is due to thrombosis.
оАБ OCT can demonstrate the lesion itself, but its main use is to
document and monitor macular oedema and, less commonly,
subhyaloid haemorrhage.
оАБ Vascular risk factors should be checked, especially BP and
serum lipids.

Treatment

оАБ Observation is indicated in eyes with good VA in which the
macula is not threatened and in those with mild retinal haemorrhage without signiоАЬcant oedema. In many cases macroaneurysms will spontaneously involute (Fig. 13.55A, C and D),
particularly following retinal or vitreous haemorrhage.
оАБ Laser treatment (Fig. 13.55B) may be considered if oedema
or exudates threaten or involve the fovea, particularly if there
is documented visual deterioration. Burns may be applied
to the lesion itself, the surrounding area (to reduce the risk

subretinal exudation and frequently exudative retinal detachment,
without signs of vitreoretinal traction. About 75% of patients are
male and 95% have involvement of only one eye. Presentation is
most frequently in the оАЬrst decade of life. Although it is not obviously inherited, a genetic predisposition may be involved as at least
some patients have a somatic mutation in the NDP gene, which is
also mutated in Norrie disease. It is now considered that Leber miliary aneurysms, previously regarded as a distinct condition, represent a generally milder form of the same disease, presenting later,
in a more localized pattern and carrying a better visual prognosis.
DiоАЮerential diagnosis from other causes of leukocoria in children,
particularly retinoblastoma, is important. Other retinal conditions
that may mimic Coats disease are familial exudative vitreoretinopathy, retinal haemangiomas in von HippelвАУLindau syndrome and
ROP. оАЯe prognosis is variable and is dependent on the severity of
involvement at presentation. Younger children frequently have a
more aggressive clinical course.

{ Intra- and subretinal exudates (Fig. 13.57C), oоАЭen aоАЮecting areas remote from the vascular abnormalities, particularly the macula. Progression to extensive exudative retinal
detachment (Fig. 13.57D) may occur.
оАБ Complications include rubeosis iridis, glaucoma, uveitis,
cataract and phthisis bulbi.
оАБ FA in mild cases shows early hyperоАЫuorescence of telangiectasis and aneurysmal dilatations (Fig. 13.57E) and late staining
and leakage (Fig. 13.57F).

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Unilateral visual loss, strabismus or leukocoria
(Fig. 13.57A).
оАБ Fundus

{ Telangiectasia and fusiform focal aneurysmal arteriolar
dilatations (Fig. 13.57B), often initially in the inferior
and temporal quadrants between the equator and ora
serrata.

Retinal Vascular Disease 557
#### 13

оАБ OCT may be useful for the assessment of the macula in cooperative older children.

Treatment

оАБ Observation in patients with mild, non-vision threatening
disease and in those with a comfortable eye with total retinal
detachment for whom there is no potential for restoration of
useful vision.
оАБ Laser ablation of points of leakage should be considered if
progressive exudation is documented (Fig. 13.58A and B).
Multiple repeated treatments over an extended term are commonly required.
оАБ Anti-VEGF therapy. Limited studies of anti-VEGF therapy
have been carried out, but initial results are promising, including as an adjunct to laser. Long-term safety in childhood
remains undetermined.
оАБ Intravitreal triamcinolone (2вАУ4 mg) has been used with good
eоАЮect in eyes with total exudative retinal detachment.
оАБ Cryotherapy, with a double freezeвАУthaw method, in eyes with
extensive exudation or subtotal retinal detachment although
this may result in marked reaction with increased leakage.
оАЯerefore, laser photocoagulation is still the preferred option
if possible.
оАБ Vitreoretinal surgery may be considered in eyes with signiоАЬcant tractional preretinal оАЬbrosis or total exudative detachment. оАЯere is a poor visual prognosis but successful retinal
reattachment may prevent the development of neovascular
glaucoma.
оАБ Enucleation may be required in painful eyes with neovascular
glaucoma.

EALES DISEASE

Introduction

Eales disease is an idiopathic occlusive peripheral periphlebitis.
It is rare in White people but is an important cause of visual
morbidity in young South Asian males. It is characterized by
three overlapping stages вАУ inflammatory, occlusive and retinal
neovascular вАУ and is diagnosed principally by clinical examination. Recurrent vitreous haemorrhage may occur in the late
stages. The visual prognosis is good in the majority of cases.
Tubercular protein hypersensitivity may be important in the
aetiology, but evidence is conflicting. Some patients may have
tubercular vasculitis.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Floaters or sudden visual reduction due to vitreous
haemorrhage.
оАБ Systemic neurological features have been reported.
оАБ Mild anterior uveitis is oоАЭen present.
оАБ Fundus. оАЯe condition is typically bilateral, though oоАЭen
asymmetrical.

{ Macular involvement with the same changes can occur but
is rare.
оАБ Complications include tractional retinal detachment, macular
epiretinal membrane, neovascular glaucoma and cataract.
оАБ FA will identify vasculitis and areas of non-perfusion
(Fig.13.59D). Wide-оАЬeld imaging is especially helpful.
оАБ Investigation should be performed to rule out other causes of
vasculitis (e.g. sarcoidosis, tuberculosis) and peripheral retinal
neovascularization (e.g. haemoglobinopathies).

Treatment

оАБ Steroids. Periocular, systemic, topical and intravitreal steroids
seem to be helpful in the inоАЫammatory stage.
оАБ Antitubercular treatment. оАЯis is strongly advocated by
some authorities but is controversial. It may be considered in
selected patients in combination with steroids, either to avoid
reactivation of prior tubercular infection (e.g. strongly positive skin test, QuantiFERON) or possibly when severe ocular
inоАЫammatory signs are present.
оАБ Scatter photocoagulation or cryotherapy of non-perfused
retina to reduce the neovascular stimulus.
оАБ Intravitreal VEGF inhibitors are commonly used in the neovascular stage.
оАБ Vitrectomy is helpful for persistent vitreous haemorrhage,
tractional detachment and macular epiretinal membrane.

RADIATION RETINOPATHY

Radiation retinopathy may develop following treatment of intraocular tumours by plaque therapy (brachytherapy) or external
beam irradiation of sinus, orbital or nasopharyngeal malignancies. It is characterized by delayed retinal microvascular changes
with endothelial cell loss, capillary occlusion and microaneurysm
formation. AоАЮected patients may also develop cataract and keratopathy. оАЯere is some evidence that it is more likely to occur in
genetically predisposed individuals. оАЯe interval between exposure and disease is variable and unpredictable, although commonly between 6 months and 3 years. оАЯere is a direct relationship
with radiation dose.
оАБ Signs

{ Capillary occlusion with the development of telangiectasis
and microaneurysms.

{ Peripheral periphlebitis (Fig. 13.59A): sheathing, superоАЬcial retinal haemorrhages and sometimes cotton-wool
spots. Pigmented chorioretinal scars may be seen.

{ Branch retinal vein occlusion.

{ Peripheral capillary non-perfusion with microaneurysms,
tortuosity, vascular shunts and neovascularization (Fig.
13.59B and C) and recurrent vitreous haemorrhage (onethird of eyes) may develop at the junction of perfused
and non-perfused retina. Disc new vessels can sometimes
develop. оАЯe vitreous haemorrhage tends to be limited and
to absorb over weeks, but can persist in some cases.

RETINOPATHY IN BLOOD DISORDERS

Leukaemia
Introduction

оАЯe leukaemias are malignancies of haematopoietic stem cells
involving abnormal proliferation of white blood cells. Acute leukaemia is characterized by the replacement of bone marrow with immature (blast) cells. Chronic leukaemia is associated, at least initially,
with well-diоАЮerentiated (mature) leukocytes and occurs almost
exclusively in adults. оАЯe four major variants of leukaemia are:
оАБ Acute lymphocytic (lymphoblastic) predominantly aоАЮects
children, in whom there is around a 90% 5-year survival rate.
оАБ Acute myeloid (myeloblastic) is most frequently seen in older
adults.
оАБ Chronic lymphocytic has a very chronic course and many
patients die from unrelated disease.
оАБ Chronic myelocytic has a progressive clinical course and
oоАЭen a less favourable prognosis.

Ocular features

{ оАЯe changes are best seen on FA (Fig. 13.60C and D).

{ Proliferative retinopathy (Fig. 13.60E).

{ Papillopathy (Fig. 13.60F). Radiation optic neuropathy
may occur but is less common as the nerve seems to be less
sensitive than retinal vessels.
оАБ Treatment is frequently unsatisfactory. Options include laser,
steroids and intravitreal anti-VEGF agents.
оАБ Prognosis depends on the severity of involvement. Poor prognostic features include papillopathy and proliferative retinopathy, which may result in vitreous haemorrhage and tractional
retinal detachment.

PURTSCHER RETINOPATHY

Purtscher retinopathy may follow severe trauma, especially chest
compressive, long-bone or head injury and involves occlusion and
ischaemia associated with microvascular damage. It is thought to
result from embolism and in some cases vascular occlusion by
other mechanisms such as complement-mediated white blood
cell aggregation. When it results from causes other than trauma
(e.g. fat or amniotic оАЫuid embolism, acute pancreatitis, preeclampsia, systemic vasculitides) it is sometimes referred to as
вАШPurtscher-likeвАЩ retinopathy. Presentation is with sudden bilateral
visual reduction, typically to 6/60 or less. Multiple unilateral or
bilateral superоАЬcial white retinal patches вАУ capillary bed infarcts
known as Purtscher оАЫecken (resembling large cotton-wool spots)
(Fig. 13.61A) are seen, oоАЭen associated with superоАЬcial peripapillary haemorrhages (Fig. 13.61B), typical cotton-wool spots and
disc swelling. Elevated complement 5a levels may be diagnostic
where there is no history of trauma. оАЯe acute fundus changes
usually resolve within a few weeks, but only a small proportion
will regain normal vision. Treatment of the underlying cause is
not always possible.

VALSALVA RETINOPATHY

оАЯe Valsalva manoeuvre consists of forcible exhalation against a
closed glottis, thereby creating a sudden increase in intrathoracic
and intra-abdominal pressure (e.g. weight-liоАЭing, blowing up balloons). оАЯe associated sudden rise in venous pressure may rupture
perifoveal capillaries leading to premacular haemorrhage of varying severity (Fig. 13.62A and B). Vitreous haemorrhage may also
occur. Treatment is with observation, or Nd:YAG laser membranotomy in some cases.

LIPAEMIA RETINALIS

Lipaemia retinalis is a rare condition characterized by creamywhite discoloration of retinal blood vessels (Fig. 13.63) in some
patients with hypertriglyceridaemia. In mild examples only
peripheral vessels are aоАЮected, but in extreme cases the fundus
takes on a salmon colour. оАЯe visualization of high levels of chylomicrons in blood vessels accounts for the fundal appearance.

Ocular involvement is more commonly seen in the acute than the
chronic forms and virtually any ocular structure may be involved.
Primary leukaemic inоАЬltration is fairly rare. Secondary changes
such as those associated with anaemia, thrombocytopenia, hyperviscosity and opportunistic infections are more common and
include intraocular bleeding, infection and vascular occlusion.
оАБ Fundus

{ Retinal haemorrhages and cotton-wool spots are common
(Fig. 13.64A-D).

{ Roth spots are retinal haemorrhages with white centres
(Fig. 13.64E and F), the whitish element is thought to
be composed of coagulated оАЬbrin in most cases. оАЯey
occur in acute leukaemia (Fig. 13.65) and in a range of
other conditions such as bacteraemia (classically subacute bacterial endocarditis), diabetes, hypertension and
anaemia.

{ Peripheral retinal neovascularization is an occasional feature of chronic myeloid leukaemia (Fig. 13.66A).

{ Retinal and choroidal inоАЬltrates may occur, most commonly posterior to the equator (Fig. 13.66B) and may
masquerade as posterior uveitis. In chronic leukaemia
they may give rise to a вАШleopard skinвАЩ appearance (Fig.
13.66C).

{ Scattered haemorrhages may occur in patients with thrombocytopenia (Fig. 13.66D).

{ Optic nerve inоАЬltration may cause swelling and visual loss.

Anaemia

оАЯe anaemias are a group of disorders characterized by a decrease
in the number of circulating red blood cells or in the amount of
haemoglobin in each cell, or both. Retinal changes in anaemia are
usually innocuous and rarely of diagnostic importance.
оАБ Retinopathy

{ Retinal venous tortuosity is related to the severity of anaemia but may occur in isolation.

Hyperviscosity

оАЯe hyperviscosity states are a diverse group of rare disorders
characterized by increased blood viscosity due to polycythaemia
or abnormal plasma proteins.
оАБ Polycythaemia is caused by the neoplastic proliferation
of erythrocytes with increased bone marrow activity and
hyperviscosity.

оАБ Waldenstr√ґm macroglobulinaemia is a malignant lymphoproliferative disorder with monoclonal IgM production, which
most frequently aоАЮects elderly men.
оАБ Ocular features include retinal haemorrhages and venous
changes (Fig. 13.68A and B) and occasionally RVO and conjunctival telangiectasia.

CYSTOID MACULAR OEDEMA 609

566 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

оАЮe macula (Fig. 14.1A) is a round area at the posterior pole, lying
inside the temporal vascular arcades. It measures between 5 and 6 mm
in diameter and subserves the central 15вАУ20¬∞ of the visual оАЭeld. Histologically, it shows more than one layer of ganglion cells, in contrast
to the single ganglion cell layer of the peripheral retina. оАЮe inner layers of the macula contain the yellow xanthophyll carotenoid pigments
lutein and zeaxanthin in far higher concentration than the peripheral
retina (hence the full name вАШmacula luteaвАЩ вАУ yellow plaque).
оАБ оАЯe fovea is a depression in the retinal surface at the centre of
the macula (Fig. 14.1BвАУD), with a diameter of 1.5 mm вАУ about
the same as the optic disc.
оАБ оАЯe foveola forms the central оАЬoor of the fovea and has a
diameter of 0.35 mm (see Fig. 14.1B). It is the thinnest part
of the retina and is devoid of ganglion cells, consisting only
of a high density of cone photoreceptors and their nuclei (Fig.
14.2), together with M√Љller cells.
оАБ The umbo is a depression in the very centre of the foveola
(see Fig. 14.1B) which corresponds to the foveolar light

reflex (see Fig. 14.1A), loss of which may be an early sign
of damage.
оАБ The foveal avascular zone (FAZ вАУ see Fig. 14.1B), a central
area containing no blood vessels but surrounded by a continuous network of capillaries, is located within the fovea
but extends beyond the foveola. The exact diameter varies
with age and in disease and its limits can be determined
with accuracy only by fluorescein angiography (average
0.6 mm).

Retinal pigment epithelium

оАБ Structure

{ оАЮe retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) is composed of a
single layer of cells that are hexagonal in cross-section. оАЮe
cells consist of an outer non-pigmented basal element containing the nucleus and an inner pigmented apical section
containing abundant melanosomes.

{ оАЮe cell base is in contact with Bruch membrane and at
the cell apices multiple thread-like villous processes extend
between the outer segments of the photoreceptors.

A B

C D

Fig. 14.1 Anatomical landmarks. оАЭAоАЮ Normal foveola (almost punctate central reоАЮex) and macula light reоАЮexes (band-like reоАЮex encircling the fovea); оАЭBоАЮ оАЮuorescein angiogram вАУ fovea
(yellow circle), approximate extent of the foveal avascular zone (red circle), foveola (lilac circle), umbo (central white spot); оАЭCоАЮ OCT showing the foveal depression; оАЭDоАЮ three-dimensional
OCT.

CHAPTER

Acquired Macular Disorders 567
#### 14

{ At the posterior pole, particularly at the fovea, RPE cells
are taller and thinner, more regular in shape and contain
more numerous and larger melanosomes than in the
periphery.
оАБ Function

CHAPTER

{ RPE cells and intervening tight junctional complexes
(zonula occludentes) constitute the outer bloodвАУretinal
barrier, preventing extracellular оАЬuid leaking into the subretinal space from the choriocapillaris and actively pumping ions and water out of the subretinal space.

{ Its integrity and that of Bruch membrane is important for
continued adhesion between the two, thought to be due to
a combination of osmotic and hydrostatic forces, possibly
with the aid of hemidesmosomal attachments.

{ Facilitation of photoreceptor turnover by the phagocytosis and lysosomal degradation of outer segments following
shedding.

{ Preservation of an optimal retinal milieu. Maintenance of
the outer bloodвАУretinal barrier is a key factor, as are the
inward transport of metabolites (mainly small molecules
such as amino acids and glucose) and the outward transport of metabolic waste products.

{ Storage, metabolism and transport of vitamin A in the
visual cycle.

оАБ Function. оАЮe RPE utilizes Bruch membrane as a route for the
transport of metabolic waste products out of the retinal environment. Changes in its structure are thought to be important
in the pathogenesis of many macular disorders вАУ for example,
intact Bruch membrane may be important in the suppression
of macular neovascularization (MNV).

CLINICAL EVALUATION OF MACULAR
DISEASE

Symptoms

оАБ Blurred vision and diоАЫculty with close work is an early
symptom. Onset can be rapid in some conditions, such as
MNV.
оАБ A positive scotoma, in which patients complain of something obstructing central vision, is a symptom of more
severe disease. This is in contrast to optic neuropathy,
which typically causes a missing area in the visual field
(negative scotoma).
оАБ Metamorphopsia (distortion of perceived images) is a common symptom that does not occur in optic neuropathy.
оАБ Micropsia (decrease in image size) is caused by spreading
apart of foveal cones and is uncommon.
оАБ Macropsia (increase in image size) is due to crowding together
of foveal cones and is uncommon.
оАБ Colour discrimination may be disturbed, but is generally less
evident than in even relatively mild optic neuropathy.
оАБ Difficulties related to dark adaptation, such as poor
vision in dim light and persistence of after-images, may
occur.

TIP Metamorphopsia is a common early symptom of macular
disease and can be evaluated with an Amsler grid.

{ оАЮe dense RPE pigment serves to absorb stray light.

Bruch membrane

оАБ Structure. Bruch membrane separates the RPE from the choriocapillaris and on electron microscopy consists of оАЭve distinct elements:

{ оАЮe basal lamina of the RPE.

{ An inner collagenous layer.

{ A thicker band of elastic оАЭbres.

{ An outer collagenous layer.

{ оАЮe basal lamina of the inner layer of the choriocapillaris.

Nerve fibre layer

Inner plexiform layer

Inner nuclear layer

Outer plexiform
(Henle) layer

membrane

INVESTIGATION OF MACULAR DISEASE

Fundus оАЬuorescein angiography
Introduction

Fluorescein angiography (FA) should be performed only if the
оАЭndings are likely to inоАЬuence management.
оАБ Fluorescence is the property of certain molecules to emit
light of a longer wavelength when stimulated by light of a
shorter wavelength. оАЮe excitation peak for оАЬuorescein is
about 490 nm (in the blue part of the spectrum) вАУ the wavelength of maximal absorption of light energy by оАЬuorescein.
Stimulated molecules will emit yellowвАУgreen light of about
530 nm (Fig. 14.3).
оАБ Fluorescein (sodium оАЬuorescein) is an orange water-soluble
dye that, when injected intravenously, remains largely intravascular (>70% bound to serum proteins). It is excreted in the
urine over 24вАУ36 hours.
оАБ FA involves photographic surveillance of the passage of оАЬuorescein through the retinal and choroidal circulations following intravenous injection.
оАБ Outer bloodвАУretinal barrier. оАЮe major choroidal vessels are
impermeable to both bound and free оАЬuorescein. However,
the walls of the choriocapillaris contain fenestrations through
which unbound molecules escape into the extravascular
space, crossing Bruch membrane but on reaching the RPE are
blocked by intercellular complexes termed tight junctions or
zonula occludentes (Fig. 14.4).
оАБ Inner bloodвАУretinal barrier is composed principally of the
tight junctions between retinal capillary endothelial cells,
across which neither bound nor free оАЬuorescein can pass; the
basement membrane and pericytes play only a minor role in
this regard (Fig. 14.5A). Disruption of the inner bloodвАУretinal
barrier permits leakage of both bound and free оАЬuorescein into
the extravascular space (Fig. 14.5B).
оАБ Filters

{ Cobalt blue excitation оАЭlter (Fig. 14.6). Incident white light
from the camera is оАЭltered so that blue light enters the eye,

{ YellowвАУgreen barrier оАЭlter blocks any blue light reоАЬected from
the eye, allowing only yellowвАУgreen emitted light to pass.
оАБ Image capture in modern digital cameras uses a chargecoupled device (CCD). Digital imaging permits immediate
picture availability, easy storage and access, image manipulation and enhancement. Modern devices also typically require
a lower concentration of injected оАЬuorescein to obtain highquality images, with a correspondingly substantially lower
incidence of adverse eоАЪects.
