оАБ Contraindications

{ Fluorescein allergy is an absolute contraindication and
a history of a severe reaction to any allergen is a strong
relative contraindication. Preventative anti-allergy pretreatment may be helpful in some cases.

{ Other relative contraindications include renal failure (a
lower оАЬuorescein dose is used), pregnancy, moderateвАУ
severe asthma and signiоАЭcant cardiac disease.

{ Allergy to iodine-containing media or seafood is not a
contraindication to FA or to indocyanine green angiography (ICGA).

Technique

Facilities must be in place to address possible adverse events. оАЮis
includes adequate staоАЫng, a resuscitation trolley that includes
drugs for the treatment of anaphylaxis, a couch (or reclining chair)
and a receiver in case of vomiting. SigniоАЭcant nausea and vomiting are less common using the lower оАЬuorescein concentrations
required by modern digital cameras.
оАБ Adequate pharmacological mydriasis is important to obtain
high-quality images. Media opacity such as cataract may
reduce picture quality.
оАБ оАЮe procedure is explained and formal consent taken. It is
important to mention common and serious adverse eоАЪects
(Table 14.1), particularly the invariable skin and urine staining.
оАБ оАЮe patient should be seated comfortably in front of the fundus camera and colour photographs, red-free (green incident
light, to enhance red detail) and autoоАЬuorescence images taken
as indicated.
оАБ An intravenous cannula is inserted; a standard cannula is oоАЩen
preferred rather than a less secure вАШbutterоАЬyвАЩ winged infusion
set. AоАЩer cannulation, the line should be оАЬushed with normal
saline to check patency and exclude extravasation.
оАБ Fluorescein, usually 5 ml of a 10% solution, is drawn up
into a syringe and injected over the course of 5вАУ10 seconds,

taking care not to rupture the cannulated vein (Fig. 14.7A
and B).
оАБ Oral administration at a dose of 30 mg/kg is an alternative if
venous access cannot be obtained or is refused. A 5 ml vial
of 10% (100 mg/ml) sodium оАЬuorescein contains 500 mg
and pictures should be taken over 20вАУ60 minutes following
ingestion.
оАБ Images are taken at 1вАУ2 second intervals initially to capture
the critical early transit phases, beginning 5вАУ10 seconds aоАЩer
injection, tapering frequency through subsequent phases.
оАБ With monocular pathology, control pictures of the opposite
eye should be taken, usually aоАЩer the initial transit phase has
been photographed in the index eye.
оАБ If appropriate, images may be captured as late as 10вАУ20
minutes.
оАБ Stereo images may be helpful to demonstrate elevation and
are usually taken by manually repositioning the camera sideways or by using a special device (a stereo separator) to adjust
the image. оАЮese are actually pseudo-stereo images as true
stereo requires simultaneous image capture from diоАЪerent
angles.

Angiographic phases

Fluorescein enters the eye through the ophthalmic artery, passing
into the choroidal circulation through the short posterior ciliary
arteries and into the retinal circulation through the central retinal
artery (Fig. 14.8). оАЮe choroidal circulation оАЭlls about 1 second
before the retinal. Precise details of the choroidal circulation are
not discernible, mainly because of rapid leakage of free оАЬuorescein from the choriocapillaris. Melanin in the RPE cells also blocks
choroidal оАЬuorescence. оАЮe angiogram consists of the following
overlapping phases:
оАБ оАЯe choroidal (pre-arterial) phase typically occurs 9вАУ15 seconds aоАЩer dye injection вАУ longer in patients with poor general
circulation вАУ and is characterized by patchy lobular оАЭlling of
the choroid due to leakage of free оАЬuorescein from the fenestrated choriocapillaris. A cilioretinal artery, if present, will
оАЭll at this time because it is derived from the posterior ciliary
circulation.
оАБ оАЯe arterial phase starts about a second aоАЩer the onset of choroidal оАЬuorescence and shows retinal arteriolar оАЭlling and the
continuation of choroidal оАЭlling (Fig. 14.9A).

оАБ оАЯe arteriovenous (capillary) phase shows complete оАЭlling
of the arteries and capillaries with early laminar оАЬow in the
veins in which the dye appears to line the venous wall leaving
an axial hypoоАЬuorescent strip (Fig. 14.9B). оАЮis phenomenon
reоАЬects initial drainage from posterior pole capillaries оАЭlling
the venous margins, as well as the small-vessel velocity proоАЭle,
with faster plasma оАЬow adjacent to vessel walls where cellular
concentration is lower.
оАБ оАЯe venous phase. Laminar venous оАЬow (Fig. 14.9C) progresses to complete оАЭlling (Fig. 14.9D), with late venous phase
featuring reducing arterial оАЬuorescence. Maximal perifoveal
capillary оАЭlling is reached at around 20вАУ25 seconds in patients
with normal cardiovascular function and the оАЭrst pass of оАЬuorescein circulation is generally completed by approximately 30
seconds.
оАБ оАЯe late (recirculation) phase demonstrates the eоАЪects of
continuous recirculation, dilution and elimination of the
dye. With each succeeding wave, the intensity of оАЬuorescence
becomes weaker although the disc shows staining. Fluorescein
is absent from the retinal vasculature aоАЩer about 10 minutes.
оАБ оАЯe dark appearance of the fovea is caused by three factors
(Fig. 14.10A and B):

Causes of hyperоАЯuorescence

оАБ AutoоАЮuorescent compounds, like lipofuscin in the retinal pigment epithelium, absorb blue light and emit yellowвАУgreen light
in a similar fashion to оАЬuorescein, but weaker. AutoоАЬuorescence can be detected on standard fundus photography with
the excitation and barrier оАЭlters both in place. Some modern

{ Absence of blood vessels in the FAZ.

{ Blockage of background choroidal оАЬuorescence due to the
high density of xanthophyll at the fovea. Xanthophyll has
an important function in the macula: it acts as an antioxidant, protecting the photoreceptors from the potential
toxic eоАЪects of light.

{ Blockage of background choroidal оАЬuorescence by the RPE
cells at the fovea, which are larger and contain more melanin and lipofuscin than elsewhere in the retina.

digital cameras have enhanced autoоАЬuorescence detection
capability, though imaging is most eоАЪective with scanning
laser ophthalmoscopy. AutoоАЬuorescent lesions classically
include optic nerve head drusen (Fig. 14.11A) and astrocytic
hamartoma, but with increased availability of high-sensitivity
imaging, patterns associated with a wide range of posterior
segment pathology have been characterized, e.g. areas surrounding geographic atrophy, which show an accumulation
of lipofuscin in the RPE cells (lipofuscin is chemically a complex mixture of lipid, oxidized proteins and trace metals which
results from the oxidation of membrane lipids and proteins by
free radicals) (Fig. 14.11B).
оАБ PseudoоАЮuorescence (false оАЬuorescence) refers to nonоАЬuorescent reоАЬected light visible prior to оАЬuorescein injection.
оАЮis passes through the оАЭlters due to the overlap of wavelengths passing through the excitation then the barrier оАЭlters.
It is more evident when оАЭlters are wearing out.
оАБ Increased оАЮuorescence may be caused by (a) enhanced visualization of normal оАЬuorescein density, or (b) an increase in
оАЬuorescein content of tissues.
оАБ A window defect is caused by atrophy or absence of the RPE
as in atrophic age-related macular degeneration (Fig. 14.12A),
a full-thickness macular hole, RPE tears and some drusen. оАЮis
results in unmasking of normal background choroidal оАЬuorescence, characterized by very early hyperоАЬuorescence that
increases in intensity and then fades without changing size or
shape (Fig. 14.12B and C).
оАБ Pooling in an anatomical space occurs due to breakdown of
the outer bloodвАУretinal barrier (RPE tight junctions):

{ In the subretinal space, e.g. central serous chorioretinopathy (Fig. 14.13A). оАЮis is characterized by early hyperоАЬuorescence, which, as the responsible leak tends to be only
small (Fig. 14.13B), slowly increases in intensity and area
(Fig. 14.13C), the maximum extent remaining relatively
well deоАЭned.

{ In the sub-RPE space, as in pigment epithelial detachment (Fig. 14.14A). оАЮis is characterized by early

hyperоАЬuorescence (Fig. 14.14B) that increases in intensity
but not in size (Fig. 14.14C).
оАБ Leakage of dye is characterized by fairly early hyperоАЬuorescence, increasing with time in both area and intensity. It occurs
as a result of breakdown of the inner bloodвАУretinal barrier due
to:

{ Vascular occlusion, which may involve the retinal arteries,
veins or capillaries (capillary drop-out вАУ Fig. 14.20A), or

{ Dysfunction or loss of existing vascular endothelial tight
junctions as in background diabetic retinopathy (DR),
retinal vein occlusion (RVO), cystoid macular oedema
(Fig. 14.15A) and disc swelling (Fig. 14.15B).

{ Primary absence of vascular endothelial tight junctions as
in MNV, proliferative diabetic retinopathy (Fig. 14.15C),
tumours and some vascular anomalies such as Coats
disease.
оАБ Staining is a late phenomenon consisting of the prolonged
retention of dye in entities such as drusen, оАЭbrous tissue,
exposed sclera and the normal optic disc and is seen in the
later phases of the angiogram, particularly aоАЩer the dye has leоАЩ
the choroidal and retinal circulations.

Causes of hypoоАЯuorescence

Reduction or absence of оАЬuorescence may be due to: (a) optical
obstruction (masking or blockage) of normal оАЬuorescein density
(Fig. 14.16) or (b) inadequate perfusion of tissue (оАЭlling defect).
оАБ Masking of retinal оАЮuorescence. Preretinal lesions such as
blood will block all оАЬuorescence (Fig. 14.17AвАУC).
оАБ Masking of background choroidal оАЮuorescence allows persistence of оАЬuorescence from superоАЭcial retinal vessels:

{ Deeper retinal lesions, e.g. intraretinal haemorrhages,
dense exudates.

{ Subretinal or sub-RPE lesions, e.g. blood (Fig. 14.18AвАУC).

{ Increased density of the RPE, e.g. congenital hypertrophy
(Fig. 14.19A and B).

{ Choroidal lesions, e.g. naevus (Fig. 14.19C).
оАБ Filling defects may result from:

оАБ Clinical оАЭndings, including the patientвАЩs age and gender,
should be noted before assessing the images.
оАБ Note whether images of right, leоАЩ or both eyes have been taken.
оАБ Comment on any colour and red-free images and on any preinjection demonstration of pseudo- or autoоАЬuorescence.
оАБ Looking at the post-injection images, indicate whether the overall timing of оАЭlling, especially arm-to-eye transit time, is normal.
оАБ BrieоАЬy scan through the sequence of images in time order for
each eye in turn, initially concentrating on the eye with the
greatest number of shots as this is likely to be the one about
which there is greater concern. On the оАЭrst review, look for
any characteristic major diagnostic, especially pathognomonic
features; for example, a lacy оАЭlling pattern or a вАШsmokestackвАЩ
(see later).
оАБ Go through the run for each eye in greater detail, noting the
evolution of any major features found on the оАЭrst scan and
then providing a description of any other оАЭndings using the
methodical consideration of the causes of hyper- and hypoоАЬuorescence set out above.

Indocyanine green angiography

Introduction

оАБ Advantages over FA. Whilst FA is an excellent method of
studying the retinal circulation, it is of limited use in delineating the choroidal vasculature, due principally to masking by
the RPE. In contrast, the near-infrared light utilized in indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) penetrates ocular pigments
such as melanin and xanthophyll, as well as exudate and thin
layers of subretinal blood, making this technique eminently
suitable. An additional factor is that about 98% of ICG molecules bind to serum protein (mainly albumin); considerably
higher than the binding of оАЬuorescein. As choriocapillaris

fenestrations are impermeable to larger protein molecules,
most ICG is retained within choroidal vessels. Infrared light is
also scattered less than visible light, making ICGA superior to
FA in eyes with media opacity. ICG stains the RPE in the late
stages of the angiogram.
оАБ Image capture. ICG оАЬuorescence is only 1/25th that of оАЬuorescein so modern digital ICGA uses high-sensitivity videoangiographic image capture by means of an appropriately
adapted camera. Both the excitation (805 nm) and emission
(835 nm) оАЭlters are set at infrared wavelengths (Fig. 14.21).
Alternatively, scanning laser ophthalmoscopy (SLO) systems
provide high contrast images, with less scattering of light
and fast image acquisition rates facilitating high-quality ICG
video.
оАБ оАЯe technique is similar to that of FA, but with an increased
emphasis on the acquisition of later images (up to about 45
minutes) than with FA. A dose of 25вАУ50 mg in 1вАУ2 ml water
for injection is used.
оАБ Phases of ICGA: (a) early вАУ up to 60 seconds post-injection,
(b) early mid-phase вАУ 1вАУ3 minutes (Fig. 14.22A), (c) late midphase вАУ 3вАУ15 minutes, (Fig. 14.22B) and (d) late phase вАУ 15вАУ45
minutes.

Adverse eоАЮects

ICGA is generally better tolerated than FA.
оАБ Nausea, vomiting and urticaria are uncommon, but anaphylaxis probably occurs with approximately equal incidence to
FA.
оАБ Serious reactions are exceptionally rare.
оАБ ICGA is relatively contraindicated in liver disease (excretion is
hepatic) and as with FA in patients with a history of a severe
reaction to any allergen, moderate or severe asthma and signiоАЭcant cardiac disease. Its safety in pregnancy has not been
established.

Indications

оАБ Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV): ICGA is far superior to FA for the imaging of PCV (see Fig. 14.23).
оАБ Exudative age-related macular degeneration (AMD). Conventional FA remains the primary method of assessment, but

оАБ Chronic central serous chorioretinopathy (CSR) in which it

Optical coherence tomography

Introduction

Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is a non-invasive,
non-contact imaging system providing high-resolution crosssectional images of the posterior segment. OCT is analogous
to B-scan ultrasonography but uses near-infrared light interferometry rather than sound waves, with images created by the
analysis of interference between reоАЬected reference waves and
those reоАЬected by tissue. Most instruments in current use employ
spectral/Fourier domain technology, in which the mechanical
movement required for image acquisition in older вАШtime domainвАЩ
machines has been eliminated and the information for each
point on the A-scan is collected simultaneously, speeding data
collection and improving resolution. Promising newer modalities include swept-source (SS) OCT that can acquire images at a
much higher rate and with extremely high retinal element resolution and better imaging depth. So-called вАШadaptive opticsвАЩ allows

correction of higher-order optical aberrations to improve resolution. Wide-оАЭeld, intraoperative, functional and Doppler (blood
оАЬow measurement) OCT applications may all have clinical utility
in the future.
оАЮe diagnosis and monitoring of macular pathology has been
revolutionized by the advent of OCT imaging, e.g. AMD, diabetic
maculopathy, macular hole, epiretinal membrane and vitreomacular traction, CSR and retinal venous occlusion.
оАЮis technology allows a distinction to be made between retinal detachment and retinoschisis.

TIP OCT imaging is key to the diagnosis, monitoring and
treatment of macular disease.

Normal appearance

Fine retinal structures such as the external limiting membrane and
ganglion cell layer can be deоАЭned (Fig. 14.24). Detailed quantitative information on retinal thickness can be displayed numerically
(Fig. 14.25). оАЮree-dimensional images can be constructed and
diоАЪerent retinal layers studied in relief. False-colour topographical
maps are rarely used these days.

OCT angiography

OCT angiography (OCTA) is a non-invasive diagnostic technique
that allows the blood оАЬow in the retina and choroid to be visualized without the need for an injection of contrast medium. оАЮe
disadvantage of this technology is that the classic abnormalities of
traditional angiography (leakage, staining, pooling) are not shown.
оАЮe images are based on the detection of red blood cell movement
within the microvasculature of the back of the eye, using a series
of OCT B-scans. оАЮese scans are performed vertically in the same
retinal position. DiоАЪerences between the scans generate detectable changing contrast as the red cells move through the vessels.
A two-dimensional horizontal map is then created of the microcirculation, within the various layers of the retina and choroid.
Importantly, it is the оАЬow that is visualized rather than the vessel
walls and оАЬow that is too slow or too fast may not be detected by
the technology.

Applications

оАБ Macular neovascular membrane:

{ Visualization of оАЬow in the outer retina.

{ Abnormal vasculature in areas featuring blood vessels (e.g.
choroid) (Fig. 14.26A and B).
оАБ Non-exudative neovascular membrane. In dry AMD to diagnose a non-exudative neovascular membrane.
оАБ Choroidal vessels. Visualization of abnormal choroidal vessels, particularly aоАЩer treatment.
оАБ Diabetic retinopathy:

{ Diagnosis of preretinal neovascularization and to diоАЪerentiate intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMA)
from new vessels.

{ Detection of microvascular changes without clinical
retinopathy.

{ To assess the deep retinal capillary plexus in macular
oedema.

{ To assess the microcirculation in patients with macular

оАБ Other: macular telangiectasia, polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (Fig. 14.26E and F), chronic central serous retinopathy,
some intraocular tumours.

TIP OCT angiography is a non-invasive imaging tool that
is useful for visualizing abnormal vessels in AMD, diabetic
retinopathy and other disorders.

Fundus autoоАЬuorescence

Imaging of fundus autoоАЬuorescence (FAF) using an enhanced
fundus camera or scanning laser ophthalmoscopy permits
visualization of accumulated lipofuscin in the retinal pigment
epithelium.
оАБ HyperautoоАЮuorescence. Many retinal diseases result in RPE
dysfunction and an accumulation of RPE lipofuscin, which
causes abnormal patterns of hyperautoоАЬuorescence on FAF
imaging. (a) It is particularly useful in the diagnosis and follow-up of patients with inherited retinal degeneration (see
Ch.15). (b) It can demonstrate more extensive macular disease
than is visible clinically (e.g. MEWDS, see Fig. 12.60C). (c) In
patients with geographic atrophy, FAF shows distinct areas
of hyperautoоАЬuorescence at the leading edges of lesions that
seems to precede retinal demise (see Fig. 14.11B).
оАБ HypoautoоАЮuorescence indicates areas of decreased lipofuscin, decreased RPE density or blockage of оАЬuorescence (e.g.
RPE atrophy, оАЭbrosis).
оАБ Limitations of FAF include a susceptibility to artifact from
media opacities.

TIP Fundus autoоАЮuorescence has become particularly helpful
in the diagnosis and management of retinal diseases and the
clinical use is likely to expand in the future.

WideоАЯоАЫeld imaging

Several wide-оАЭeld (also referred to as ultrawide-оАЭeld) highresolution imaging devices are now available (Fig. 14.27). оАЮese
are able to capture views of up to about 80% of the area of the retina
in a single image, oоАЩen without dilating the pupil. Some have the
facility of imaging FAF and FA and can provide useful additional
information.

DeоАЭnitions

оАБ Posterior pole: retina within the arcades and slightly beyond.
оАБ Mid-periphery: retina up to the posterior edge of the vortex
vein ampulla.
оАБ Far periphery: retina anterior to the vortex vein ampulla.
оАБ Wide-оАЭeld: single capture image centred on the fovea and capturing retina in all four quadrants posterior to and including
the vortex vein ampulla.
оАБ Ultrawide-оАЭeld: single capture 200¬∞ image of the retina
including the far periphery in all four quadrants.
оАБ Pan-retina: single capture 360¬∞ ora-to-ora view of the retina.

AGE-RELATED MACULAR
DEGENERATION

Introduction

Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is a degenerative disorder in which the structure and function of the macula deteriorates.
It is characterized by the presence of speciоАЭc clinical оАЭndings,
including drusen and RPE changes, in the absence of another disorder. Later stages of the disease are associated with impairment of
vision secondary to neovascularization or atrophy.

ClassiоАЭcation

оАБ A consensus committee has provided a clinical classiоАЭcation
of AMD (Table 14.2) and has standardized the nomenclature
for reporting neovascular AMD.
оАБ Main types. Conventionally, AMD has been divided as follows:

CHAPTER

Acquired Macular Disorders 583
#### 14

Degeneration (AMD)

CHAPTER

Category

of lesions within two disc
diameters of the fovea in either

No apparent ageing No drusen
changes No AMD pigmentary

changes No AMD pigmentary
abnormalities

<125оАЬќЉm)
No AMD pigmentary

Any AMD pigmentary

Any geographic atrophy

Pigmentary abnormalities: any deоАЮnite hyper- or hypopigmentary
abnormalities associated with medium or large drusen but not due to other
known disease.
Drupelets: a newly proposed term for small drusen (<63оАЭќЉm).
The size of drusen can be estimated by comparison with the approximately
125оАЭќЉm diameter of a retinal vein at the optic disc margin.

{ Dry (non-exudative, non-neovascular) AMD is the most
common form, comprising around 90% of diagnosed disease. Geographic atrophy (GA) is the advanced stage of
dry AMD. It has been suggested that the term вАШdry AMDвАЩ
should be used only to describe GA rather than the early
stages of AMD.

{ Wet (exudative, neovascular) AMD is much less common
than dry, but is associated with more rapid progression to
advanced sight loss. оАЮe main clinical entities are: MNV,
pigment epithelial detachment (PED), retinal angiomatous
proliferation (RAP) and polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV).

TIP The term вАШchoroidal neovascularizationвАЩ (CNV) is no
longer appropriate because the neovascularization may start in
the outer retina. Macular neovascularization (MNV) should be
used instead.

Epidemiology

оАБ AMD is the most common cause of irreversible visual loss in
industrialized countries. In the USA, it is responsible for severe
sight loss (better eye worse than 6/60) in about 50% of Whites,
15% of Hispanics and 5% of Black people, with irreversible
visual loss. оАЮe prevalence increases with age and symptoms
are rare in patients under 50 years of age.
оАБ In the UK, signiоАЭcant visual impairment (binocularly 6/18 or
worse) from AMD aоАЪects about 4% of the population aged
over 75 years and 14% of those over 90, with 1.6% of those
over 75 having binocular acuity of less than 6/60.

оАБ Patients with late AMD in one eye, or even moderate vision
loss due to non-advanced AMD in one eye, have about a 50%
chance of developing advanced AMD in the fellow eye within
5 years.

Risk factors

AMD is multifactorial in aetiology and is thought to involve a
complex interaction between polygenic, lifestyle and environmental factors.
оАБ Age is the major risk factor.
оАБ Race. Late AMD is more common in White individuals than
in other races.
оАБ Heredity. Family history is important. оАЮe risk of AMD is
up to three times greater if a оАЭrst-degree relative has the disease. Variants in more than 50 genes have been implicated in
AMD including two major susceptibility genes: the complement factor H gene CFH (1q31), which helps to protect cells
from complement-mediated damage (mutation increases
risk seven times when homozygous), and the ARMS2 gene
(10q26), which encodes a protein that is a component of the
choroidal extracellular matrix (mutation increases risk eight
times when homozygous). Genes related to lipid metabolism
are also thought to be important. оАЯere is no advantage in
undertaking genetic testing until studies conоАЭrm the value
of such testing for determining prognosis or the response
to treatment.
оАБ Smoking roughly doubles the risk of AMD.
оАБ Hypertension and other cardiovascular risk factors are likely
to be associated.
оАБ Dietary factors. High fat intake and obesity may promote
AMD, with high antioxidant intake having a protective eоАЪect
in some groups (see below).
оАБ Aspirin. оАЮere is a weak, but statistically signiоАЭcant association. However, the beneоАЭts of aspirin usage (which include
preserving the duration and quality of life by decreasing the
risk of stroke and heart attack) outweigh the theoretical risk of
exacerbating wet AMD.
оАБ Other factors such as cataract surgery, blue iris colour, high
sunlight exposure and female gender are suspected, but their
inоАЬuence remains less certain.

Drusen

Histopathology

Drusen (singular: druse) are extracellular deposits located at the
interface between the RPE and Bruch membrane (Fig. 14.28A).
оАЮey are composed of a broad range of constituents derived from
immune-mediated and metabolic processes in the RPE. оАЮeir precise role in the pathogenesis of AMD is unclear, but is positively
associated with the size of lesions and the presence or absence of
associated pigmentary abnormalities. Age-related drusen are rare
prior to the age of 40, but are common by the sixth decade. оАЮe distribution is highly variable and they may be conоАЭned to the fovea,
may encircle it or form a band around the macular periphery. оАЮey
may also be seen in the peripheral and mid-peripheral fundus.

584 Age-Related Macular Degeneration

A B

C D

E F

Fig. 14.28 Drusen. оАЭAоАЮ Histopathology showing homogeneous eosinophilic deposits lying

showing an increase in number and size of drusen; оАЭEоАЮ drusen with associated pigmentary
abnormalities; оАЭFоАЮ red-free image of оАЭEоАЮ. (Courtesy of S Chen вАУ оАЮgs C and D.)

Clinical features

CHAPTER

Acquired Macular Disorders 585
#### 14

CHAPTER

оАЮere is a strong association between the size of drusen (Fig.
14.28B F) and the risk of developing late AMD over a 5-year
period.
оАБ Small drusen (drupelets), sometimes termed вАШhardвАЩ drusen,
are typically well-deоАЭned whiteвАУyellow and by deоАЭnition
measure вЙ§63 ќЉm вАУ less than half the width of a retinal vein
at the optic disc margin вАУ in diameter. оАЮeir presence as the
only оАЭnding probably carries little increased risk of visual loss,
unless associated with pigmentary abnormalities.
оАБ Intermediate drusen are fairly well deоАЭned yellowвАУwhite focal
deposits at the level of the RPE measuring between 63 ќЉm and
125 ќЉm. Without accompanying pigmentary abnormalities,
they carry only a small risk of progression to late AMD over 5
years, but this increases to over 10% if pigmentary abnormalities are present in both eyes.
оАБ Large drusen are less well delineated yellowвАУwhite deep retinal lesions measuring over 125 ќЉm in diameter. оАЮe term вАШsoоАЩвАЩ
drusen is sometimes used synonymously. As they enlarge and
become more numerous (see Fig. 14.28C and D) they may
coalesce giving a localized elevation of the RPE, a вАШdrusenoid
RPE detachmentвАЩ вАУ see below. оАЮe presence of large drusen in
both eyes is associated with a 13% risk of progression to late
AMD over 5 years, but with accompanying bilateral pigmentary abnormalities this rises to about 50%.
оАБ Dystrophic calciоАЭcation may develop in all types of drusen.
оАБ Pigmentary abnormalities. Hyper- and hypopigmentation
(see Fig. 14.28E and F) not due to other retinal disease is associated with a signiоАЭcantly higher likelihood of progression to
late AMD with visual loss.

TIP There is a strong association between the risk of
developing advanced AMD, drusen size and macular
pigmentary change.

OCT

Medium-sized and large drusen are seen as hyper-reоАЬective irregular nodules beneath the RPE, located on or within Bruch membrane (see Fig. 14.29B).

Fluorescein angiography

FA оАЭndings depend on the state of the overlying RPE and on the
aоАЫnity of the drusen for оАЬuorescein. HyperоАЬuorescence can be
caused by a window defect due to atrophy of the overlying RPE,
or by late staining. HypoоАЬuorescent drusen masking background
оАЬuorescence are hydrophobic, with a high lipid content and tend
not to stain.

DiоАЮerential diagnosis

A number of conditions feature lesions similar to age-related drusen and at least some may have a similar pathophysiological basis.
оАБ Doyne honeycomb retinal dystrophy (malattia leventinese,
autosomal dominant radial drusen) is an uncommon condition in which fairly characteristic drusen (Fig. 14.29A) appear
during the second or third decades (see Ch. 15).

A

B

C

Fig. 14.29 оАЭAоАЮ Doyne honeycomb retinal dystrophy; оАЭBоАЮ cuticular drusen; оАЭCоАЮ FA showing hyperоАЮuorescent spots вАУ вАШstars in
the skyвАЩ appearance. (Courtesy of S Chen вАУ оАЮg. A; C Barry

- оАЮgs B and C.)

оАБ Cuticular drusen, also known as grouped early adult-onset
or basal laminar drusen (not to be confused with basal laminar deposit and basal linear deposit in AMD вАУ see dry AMD
below), tend to be seen in relatively young adults. оАЮe lesions

586 Age-Related Macular Degeneration

consist of small (25вАУ75 ќЉm) yellowish nodules (Fig. 14.29B)
that tend to cluster and increase in number with time and can
progress to serous PED. FA characteristically gives a вАШstars in
the skyвАЩ appearance (Fig. 14.29C). оАЮe condition has been
linked to a variant of the CFH gene.
оАБ Type 2 membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis is a
chronic renal disease that occurs in older children and adults.
A minority of patients develop bilateral diоАЪuse drusen-like
lesions. оАЮe CFH gene has again been implicated.

Antioxidant supplementation

Introduction

оАЮere is substantial evidence, notably from the Age-Related Eye
Disease Study (AREDS, now known as AREDS1) and the follow-up
AREDS2, that taking high-dose antioxidant vitamins and minerals on
a regular basis can decrease the risk of the development of advanced
AMD in individuals with certain dry AMD features. оАЮe recommendation was made in AREDS1 that individuals aged over 55 should
undergo examination for the following high-risk characteristics and if
one or more are present should consider antioxidant supplementation:
оАБ Extensive intermediate- (вЙ•63вАУ125 ќЉm) drusen.
оАБ One or more large (вЙ•125 ќЉm) drusen.
оАБ GA in one or both eyes.
оАБ Late AMD in one eye (greatest beneоАЭt in AREDS1).
In AREDS1 the reduction in risk of progression to advanced
AMD at 10 years was in the order of 25вАУ30% for those with the
more advanced of these signs at baseline who took supplements.
However, supplements did not discernibly reduce progression in
those with early or no AMD at baseline.

AREDS2

оАЮe regimen used in AREDS1 consisted of vitamin C, vitamin E, the
beta-carotene form of vitamin A and 80 mg daily of zinc (with copper to prevent zinc-induced anaemia). However, high zinc doses are
potentially associated with genitourinary tract problems and there
are data suggesting that 25 mg of zinc may be the maximal level that
is absorbed. Beta-carotene increases the incidence of lung cancer in
current and former smokers. AREDS2 looked at adjusting the betacarotene and zinc components and also whether additional or alternative supplements could enhance outcomes. AREDS2 found:
оАБ оАЮe carotenoids lutein and zeaxanthin are a safe alternative to
beta-carotene and are probably superior (possible 18% reduction in risk of advanced AMD above that conferred by the
AREDS1 regimen).
оАБ Lutein and zeaxanthin supplementation added to the original
AREDS1 regimen was only associated with a statistically signiоАЭcantly reduced (26%) risk of AMD in patients in whom the
dietary intake of these was not already high (self-described and
blood testing). оАЮere was evidence that competition for absorption between diоАЪerent carotenoids may have prevented the demonstration of superiority in other patients. оАЮis group also showed
a one-third reduction in the likelihood of cataract surgery.
оАБ Adding omega-3 fatty acids to the regimen did not seem to
enhance outcomes.

оАБ Lowering the zinc dose did not lead to a statistically signiоАЭcant
prognostic worsening and is likely to be associated with a lower incidence of side-eоАЪects such as gastrointestinal and urinary problems.
оАБ Recommended daily supplementation based on AREDS2:

{ Vitamin E (400 IU).

{ Vitamin C (500 mg).

{ Lutein (10 mg).

{ Zeaxanthin (2 mg).

{ Zinc (25вАУ80 mg; the lower dose may be equally eоАЪective).

{ Copper (2 mg; this may not be required with the lower zinc
dose).

Other considerations

оАБ A green leafy vegetable intake confers a lower risk of AMD
and for individuals with a strong family history of AMD and
those with early AMD who do not meet the AREDS criteria,
this may be a prudent lifestyle choice.
оАБ Cessation of smoking should be advised.
оАБ Protective measures against exposure to excessive sunlight
should be considered.
оАБ Some authorities consider that evidence still supports the regular consumption of oily оАЭsh.

TIP In a patient with dry AMD and high-risk characteristics,
the risk of developing severe AMD can be reduced with regular
anti-oxidant dietary supplementation.

NonоАЯexudative оАЭdry, nonоАЯneovascularоАЮ
AMD

Introduction

оАЮis is a common condition, with geographic atrophy threatening
central visual loss in up to 5 million people worldwide. Loss of
photoreceptors and RPE cells in the macula results in retinal atrophy, with the late stages referred to as geographic atrophy (GA).
Although the process of atrophy is slow, GA can eventually result
in signiоАЭcant loss of visual acuity. GA also remains an inevitable
long-term progressive outcome for many patients with wet AMD.
Lesion size is the main measure of disease progression and FAF
is the commonest imaging method that is used in clinical trials
to report lesion growth. Activation of complement factors C3 and
C5 plays an important role in the pathogenesis of dry AMD. Current research is aimed at manipulating the complement system by
inhibiting the eоАЪects of these factors in the macular region.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms consist of gradual impairment of vision over months
or years. Both eyes are usually aоАЪected, but oоАЩen asymmetrically. Vision may оАЬuctuate and is oоАЩen better in bright light.
оАБ Signs in approximately chronological order:

{ Numerous intermediateвАУlarge soоАЩ drusen, which may
become conоАЬuent.

{ Focal hyper- and/or hypopigmentation of the RPE (Fig.
14.30A and B).

588 Age-Related Macular Degeneration

{ Sharply circumscribed areas of RPE atrophy associated
with variable loss of the retina and choriocapillaris (Fig.
14.30C and D).

{ Enlargement of atrophic areas, within which larger choroidal vessels may become visible and pre-existing drusen disappear (GA вАУ Fig. 14.30E). Visual acuity may be
severely impaired if the fovea is involved. Rarely, MNV
may develop in an area of GA.

{ Visual cycle modulation by reducing the rate of vitamin A
processing using emixustat has not proved helpful in slowing the growth of atrophy.

{ Photocoagulation of drusen leads to a substantial reduction in their extent, but does not reduce the risk of progression to AMD.

expected performance. Intravitreal avacincaptad pegol (C5
inhibitor) or pegcetacoplan (C3 inhibitor) result in a reduction in geographic atrophy growth in the short term.

{ Drusenoid RPE detachment (see below).
оАБ OCT

{ Drusen вАУ see above.

{ Loss of RPE and morphological alterations of the overlying retina of increasing severity are seen in GA, including increased hyper-reоАЬectivity initially in the outer retinal
layers and eventual photoreceptor loss.

{ Outer retinal tubulations may be seen. оАЮese are thought
to consist of degenerating photoreceptors aggregated into
tubular structures that appear as roundish hyporeоАЬective
spaces (Fig. 14.30F), oоАЩen around the margin of GA.

{ SaоАЪron (20 mg/day): short-term results suggest an
improvement in retinal function.
оАБ Experimental surgery

{ Patients should be fully informed of the risks associated
with the surgery and the slender likelihood of long-term
central visual improvement.

{ Miniature intraocular telescope implantation may provide
beneоАЭt in selected cases.

{ RPE cells (derived from stem cells) implanted behind the
macula have resulted in improved vision in some patients.

{ Macular translocation surgery has a high complication
rate and has not been successful in improving vision.

{ Outer retinal corrugations. оАЮis recently described phenomenon is an undulating hyper-reоАЬective layer on OCT
thought to correspond to the histological оАЭnding of basal
laminar deposit, a layer that accumulates between the RPE
and the RPE basement membrane (the inner layer of Bruch
membrane) in AMD. Basal linear deposit is a distinct оАЭnding consisting of membranous debris laid down between
the RPE basement membrane and the inner collagenous
layer of Bruch membrane that may progress focally to form
drusen.
оАБ Microperimetry is an accurate method of testing central
vision (see Fig. 1.26).
оАБ FAF allows visualization of areas of atrophy and shows areas of
hyperautoоАЬuorescence in the junctional zone at higher risk of
progression to geographic atrophy (see Fig. 14.11B).
оАБ FA of atrophic areas shows a window defect due to unmasking
of background choroidal оАЬuorescence (see Fig. 14.12), if the
underlying choriocapillaris is still intact. Exposed sclera may
exhibit late staining.

{ Visual prostheses of various types are under investigation.

Management

оАБ Prophylaxis

{ Antioxidant supplementation if indicated.

{ Risk factors should be addressed, e.g. smoking, ocular sun
protection, cardiovascular, dietary.
оАБ An Amsler grid should be provided for home use, with advice
to self-test on a regular basis and to seek professional advice
urgently in the event of any change in vision.
оАБ Provision of low vision aids and visually impaired certiоАЭcation for individuals with signiоАЭcant visual loss, as this may
facilitate access to social and оАЭnancial support.
оАБ Potential new therapies

Retinal pigment epithelial detachment

Pathogenesis

Pigment epithelial detachment (PED) from the inner collagenous
layer of Bruch membrane is caused by disruption of the physiological forces maintaining adhesion. оАЮe basic mechanism is thought
to be the reduction of hydraulic conductivity of a thickened and
dysfunctional Bruch membrane, thus impeding movement of оАЬuid
from the RPE towards the choroid. Immune-mediated processes
may also be important.
Four types are recognised: serous, оАЭbrovascular, drusenoid
and haemorrhagic (discussed below).

TIP The diagnosis of pigment epithelial detachment relies on
careful examination and OCT imaging of the macula.

Serous PED

оАБ Symptoms. Patients can be asymptomatic, but usually present
with blurred central vision (sometimes induced hypermetropia) and metamorphopsia in one eye.
оАБ Signs

{ An orange dome-shaped elevation with sharply delineated
edges, oоАЩen with a pale margin of subretinal оАЬuid (Fig.
14.31A). Multiple lesions may occur.

{ An associated pigment band may indicate chronicity.

{ оАЮirteen diоАЪerent complement inhibitors have been studied in clinical trials of dry AMD. However, many have been
ineоАЪective despite the theoretical promise. Factors like the
timing of intervention, dosing schedule and selective permeability of these drugs may be responsible for the less than

{ Associated blood, lipid exudation, chorioretinal folds or
irregular subretinal оАЬuid may indicate underlying MNV.

{ If no drusen are seen, PCV (see below) should be suspected.
оАБ FA: a well-demarcated oval area of hyperоАЬuorescent pooling
(Fig. 14.31B) that increases in intensity but not in area with
time. An indentation (notch) may signify MNV.

CHAPTER

Acquired Macular Disorders 589
#### 14

CHAPTER

A B

C D

Fig. 14.31 Detachment of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE). оАЭAоАЮ Clinical appearance; оАЭBоАЮ
FA at 10 minutes showing hyperоАЮuorescence; оАЭCоАЮ ICGA at 10 minutes showing hypoоАЮuorescence; оАЭDоАЮ OCT showing separation of the RPE from Bruch membrane. Subretinal hypo/
hyper-reоАЮective material is present under the sensory retina.

оАБ ICGA: an oval hypoоАЬuorescent area with a surrounding hyperоАЬuorescent ring (Fig. 14.31C). Occult MNV (focal hot spot or
diоАЪuse plaque) is detected in over 90%.
оАБ OCT shows separation of the RPE from Bruch membrane by
an optically empty area (Fig. 14.31D). MNV may be indicated
by a notch between the main elevation and a second small
mound.
оАБ Natural course

{ Persistence with increasing atrophy and gradually worsening vision.

{ Patients aged over 60 have a worse prognosis (6/60 or less),
but speed of deterioration varies.

{ Resolution leaving GA with visual loss (spontaneous resolution with relative preservation of vision is more common
in younger patients).

{ RPE tear formation (see below) or haemorrhage from
MNV can occur, with sudden visual loss.

{ Up to a third of eyes develop clinical MNV within 2 years,
but the proportion is much higher angiographically.

оАБ Management

{ Observation may be appropriate in clinically stable
patients without readily detectable MNV, especially those
younger than 60.

{ Intravitreal injection of anti-vascular endothelial growth
factor (VEGF) agents may stabilize or improve vision and
should be considered, particularly when there is associated
MNV. However, there is a 5вАУ20% risk of RPE tear.

{ Combining photodynamic therapy (PDT) with intravitreal anti-VEGF or intravitreal triamcinolone injection
(IVTA) can also be eоАЪective, though the RPE tear risk
persists.

оАЭbrovascular PED represents a form of вАШoccultвАЩ MNV (Type 1, see

{ Signs. оАЮe PED is much more irregular in outline and

Haemorrhagic PED

Virtually every haemorrhagic PED has underlying MNV or polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV): the latter should always be
considered if no drusen are present.
оАБ Symptoms. Sudden impairment of central vision.
оАБ Signs

{ Elevated dark red dome-shaped lesion with a well-deоАЭned
outline (Fig. 14.33A and B).

Drusenoid PED

Drusenoid PED develops from conоАЬuent large soоАЩ drusen and is
oоАЩen bilateral.
оАБ Signs. Shallow elevated pale areas with irregular scalloped
edges (Fig. 14.32A).
оАБ FA. Early diоАЪuse hypoоАЬuorescence with patchy relatively faint
early hyperоАЬuorescence, progressing to moderate irregular late
staining (Fig. 14.32B).
оАБ ICGA. HypoоАЬuorescence predominates.

Retinal pigment epithelial tear

Tears may occur spontaneously, following laser (including PDT),
or aоАЩer intravitreal injection.
оАБ An RPE tear occurs at the junction of attached and detached
RPE. Two opposing forces play a role: traction from MNV
contraction and adhesive forces from the attached RPE. оАЮe
tear appears on the opposite side of the neovascular membrane
and the contracted RPE monolayer comes to rest on the side
of the MNV.
оАБ Risk factors

{ Older patients.

{ Long duration.

{ Large PED with a small ratio of MNV size to PED.

{ Wrinkles in the RPE on SD-OCT imaging.
оАБ Symptoms. Sudden fall in vision with foveal involvement.
оАБ Signs. A crescent-shaped pale area of RPE dehiscence is seen,
next to a darker area corresponding to the retracted and folded
оАЬap (Fig. 14.34A).
оАБ FA late phase shows hypoоАЬuorescence over the оАЬap due to
the thickened folded RPE, with adjacent hyperоАЬuorescence,
initially over the exposed choriocapillaris where the RPE is

absent and later due to scleral staining. оАЮe two areas are oоАЩen
separated by a sharply deоАЭned border (Fig. 14.34B).
оАБ OCT. Loss of the normal dome shape of the RPE layer in the
PED, with hyper-reоАЬectivity of the folded RPE (Fig. 14.34C
and D).
оАБ оАЯe prognosis for vision in patients with subfoveal tears is
poor. Good visual acuity (VA) is usually maintained if the
fovea is spared.

Macular neovascularization оАЭMNVоАЮ

Introduction

Macular neovascularization (MNV) consists of a blood vessel complex that extends through Bruch membrane from the choriocapillaris into the sub-RPE (type 1) or subretinal (type 2) space. оАЮe
neovascularization may also start in the outer retina (type 3), hence
the new term вАШmacular neovascularizationвАЩ (MNV) rather than вАШchoroidal neovascularizationвАЩ (CNV). оАЮe aim of the new nomenclature
is to improve accuracy of communication. MNV occurs in many
diоАЪerent disorders, usually when Bruch membrane and/or RPE
function has been compromised by a degenerative, inоАЬammatory,
traumatic or neoplastic process. AMD is the most common causative association, followed by myopic degeneration. оАЮe present
discussion relates to MNV arising de novo as the primary lesion in
neovascular AMD, but MNV may also develop secondary to retinal
angiomatous proliferation and polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy
(RAP and PCV вАУ see below), both of which are considered to be
variants of neovascular AMD. оАЮe prognosis of untreated MNV is
poor, with вАШcounting оАЭngersвАЩ vision a common outcome. An understanding of the pathogenesis has improved over recent years. оАЮe
promotion and inhibition of blood vessel growth by cytokines is
important, particularly vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF),
which binds to endothelial cell receptors, promoting proliferation
and vascular leakage. оАЮe inhibitory mediators pigment epitheliumderived factor (PEDF) and complement factor H (CFH) are also

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Acute or subacute painless blurring of vision, usually
with metamorphopsia. Haemorrhage may give a positive scotoma.
оАБ Signs

{ оАЮe MNV itself may be identiоАЭable as a greyвАУgreen or
pinkish-yellow lesion (Fig. 14.35A).

{ Associated mediumвАУlarge drusen are a typical оАЭnding in
the same or fellow eye.

{ Localized subretinal оАЬuid, sometimes with cystoid macular oedema (CMO).

{ Intra- and subretinal lipid deposition, sometimes extensive
(Fig. 14.35B).

{ Haemorrhage (Fig. 14.35C) is common, e.g. subretinal,
preretinal/retrohyaloid, vitreous.

{ оАЮere may be an associated serous, оАЭbrovascular drusenoid
or haemorrhagic PED.

{ Retinal and subretinal cicatrization (вАШdisciformвАЩ scar) in an
evolved or treated lesion (Fig. 14.35D).

Fluorescein angiography

FA was previously used to diagnose MNV and to plan and monitor
the response to laser photocoagulation or PDT.
оАБ Current indications include:

{ Diagnosis of MNV prior to committing to anti-VEGF
treatment. FA should usually be performed urgently on the
basis of clinical suspicion.

{ As an adjunct to diagnosis of an alternative form of neovascular AMD such as PCV and RAP.

{ Exceptionally, localization for extrafoveal photocoagulation, or guidance for PDT.
оАБ Terminology used to describe MNV on FA is derived from the
Macular Photocoagulation Study:

{ Occult (70%). New term: Type 1 MNV. оАЮe limits of the
neovascular membrane cannot be fully deоАЭned on FA (Fig.
14.36A D). Variants are оАЭbrovascular PED (see above) and
вАШlate leakage of an undetermined sourceвАЩ (LLUS). Type 1

MNV is commonly associated with AMD but can also
occur secondary to other conditions such as trauma and
pseudoxanthoma elasticum. It is present above Bruch
membrane, but below the RPE (Fig. 14.36E).

{ Classic (20%). New term: Type 2 MNV. Neovascular
membrane оАЭlls with dye in a well-deоАЭned вАШlacyвАЩ pattern during early transit subsequently leaking into the
subretinal space over 1вАУ2 minutes, with late staining of
оАЭbrous tissue (Fig. 14.37A D). Most MNV is subfoveal;
extrafoveal being deоАЭned as вЙ•200 ќЉm from the centre of
the foveal avascular zone on FA. It is present below the
neurosensory retina, but above the RPE (Fig. 14.37E).
Predominantly or minimally classic MNV is present
when the classic element is greater or less than 50% of the
total lesion respectively.

{ Retinal angiomatous proliferation (10%). New term:
Type 3 MNV. Neovascularisation originates from the
retinal circulation (typically the deep capillary plexus)
and grows towards the outer retina. Stippled hyperоАЬuorescence is seen over an area of elevated RPE that expands
to coalesce in the later stages of the angiogram (see Fig.
14.39C and D).

оАБ Type 3 MNV: OCT оАЭndings similar to type 1, with irregular
PEDs, subretinal оАЬuid and intraretinal cysts. In some patients
dilated vascular elements are seen at the outer border of the
lesion (see Fig. 14.39E).

Type 3 MNV оАЭretinal angiomatous
proliferationоАЮ

Introduction

оАЮe major component of the neovascular complex is initially
located within the retina. оАЮe process may originate in the deep
retinal capillary plexus or within the choroid; in the latter case with
the early formation of a retinal-choroidal anastomosis (RCA). оАЮe
disease is frequently bilateral and symmetrical and is probably
underdiagnosed. It may constitute 10вАУ20% of neovascular AMD
in White people.

Diagnosis

оАБ Presentation is similar to that of MNV, but PED and exudate
are more frequent. Haemorrhages are also more common and
tend to be superоАЭcial and multiple.
оАБ Stage 1: Intraretinal neovascularization (IRN). Dilated telangiectatic retinal vessels and small angiomatous lesions, typically accompanied by intra-, sub- and preretinal haemorrhage,
oedema and exudate (Fig. 14.39A).
оАБ Stage 2: Subretinal neovascularization (SRN) extends into the
subretinal space associated with increasing oedema and exudate. A serous PED may be present.
оАБ Stage 3: MNV. Perfusion is principally via the choroid, with
RCA formation. MNV is clearly evident clinically or angiographically; a disciform scar will oоАЩen form.
оАБ ICGA is diagnostic in most cases, showing a hot spot in mid
and/or late frames (Fig. 14.39B) and frequently a perfusing retinal arteriole and draining venule (вАШhairpin loopвАЩ when linked).
оАБ FA is usually similar to occult or minimally classic MNV
(Fig. 14.39C and D), but may show focal intraretinal
hyperоАЬuorescence.
оАБ OCT is commonly associated with marked intra- and subretinal оАЬuid (Fig. 14.39E). Neovascularization can be seen as a
hyper-reоАЬective area leading to a PED. Other features depend
on the stage.

Treatment

Type 3 MNV is extremely sensitive to anti-VEGF treatment, especially in the early stages. Favourable outcomes in combination with
PDT have been reported. Limited success has been reported for
other modalities, including PDT alone and photocoagulation of
feeder vessels, though the latter may be used in resistant cases.

Haemorrhagic AMD

оАБ оАЯe visual prognosis for most eyes with extensive subretinal
or sub-RPE haemorrhage is poor.
оАБ Clinical trials assessing surgical drainage of extensive subretinal or sub-RPE haemorrhage with MNV excision have

CHAPTER

Indocyanine green angiography

ICGA demonstrates MNV as a focal hyperоАЬuorescent вАШhot spotвАЩ
(Fig. 14.38) or вАШplaqueвАЩ. BeneоАЭts adjunctive to FA include:
оАБ Increased sensitivity in the detection of MNV, for example if
low-density haemorrhage, оАЬuid or pigment preclude adequate
FA visualization.
оАБ оАЮe distinction of MNV from other conditions that may have a
similar presentation, particularly PCV, RAP and CSR.
оАБ оАЮe delineation of occult MNV may still have utility for combined modality treatment and for patients who refuse intravitreal therapy.

Optical coherence tomography

Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is critical in the diagnosis
and quantitative monitoring of the response to MNV treatment.
Typically, MNV is shown as a thickening and fragmentation of
the RPE and choriocapillaris. Subretinal and sub-RPE оАЬuid, blood
and scarring are demonstrated. Outer retinal tubulations (see Fig.
14.30F) may be present, oоАЩen in a branching pseudodendritic conformation. On OCT the classiоАЭcation is based on the location of
the pathology.
оАБ Type 1 MNV: neovascularization is above Bruch membrane,
but below the retinal pigment epithelium and manifests as a
pigment epithelial detachment that is oоАЩen multilobulated in
appearance. OCT shows an elevation of the RPE by material
with heterogenous reоАЬectivity and vascular elements may be
seen (see Fig. 14.36E).
оАБ Type 2 MNV: neovascularization is below the neurosensory
retina, but above the retinal pigment epithelium. It may be
associated with subretinal hyperreоАЬective material and separation of the neurosensory retina from the RPE. Subretinal
оАЬuid, haemorrhage or intraretinal оАЬuid may be present (see
Fig. 14.37E).

not shown any signiоАЭcant improvement in prognosis and an

оАБ Anticoagulant treatment. If the patient takes a coumarin anticoagulant, liaison with the prescribing physician is worthwhile
to assess if this could be stopped as there is an association with
massive macular haemorrhage. Antiplatelet drugs do not usually require discontinuation, though aspirin may be associated
with a greater risk of MNV than other agents.
оАБ Alternative pathology (e.g. PCV, macroaneurysm) that may
be associated with extensive haemorrhage should always be
considered.

Treatment with anti-VEGF agents

оАБ Principles. Inhibitors of VEGF block its interaction with
receptors on the endothelial cell surface and so retard or

reverse vessel growth. оАЮey have become the predominant
means of treatment for MNV, dramatically improving the
visual prognosis. Genetic factors are associated with a variability in response so that visual outcomes can diоАЪer from patient
to patient. Intravitreal injection is the standard method of
administration. Notable (but rare) risks include retinal detachment, damage to the lens, RPE tears and endophthalmitis.
Sustained elevation of intraocular pressure (IOP) and sterile
uveitis may occasionally occur. Anti-VEGF treatment is not
associated with arterial or venous thromboembolic events. All
available anti-VEGF agents seem to have potential for beneоАЭt
in a range of vascular eye diseases. As a general principle, the
better the visual acuity at the onset of treatment the better the
visual acuity at follow-up. оАЮere is some evidence that younger
age at treatment onset may confer a worse outcome. Treatment
should be initiated within 2вАУ3 weeks of symptom onset.
оАБ Landmark studies. оАЮe following large, randomized, doublemasked, sham-controlled trials provide the evidential basis
for anti-VEGF-treatment in individuals with neovascular agerelated macular degeneration.

{ MARINA (Minimally Classic/Occult Trial of the AntiVEGF Antibody Ranibizumab in the treatment of Neovascular AMD) shows that ranibizumab treatment is more

outcomes.

{ ANCHOR (Anti-VEGF Antibody Ranibizumab for the

by 15 letters.

{ PIER concludes that the outcomes are better if ranibizumab is injected monthly than if it is injected monthly for
3 months and then 3-monthly for 1 year.

{ HORIZON and SAILOR studies conоАЭrm that intravitreal
ranibizumab has a good safety proоАЭle and is well tolerated.

{ PrONTO (Prospective OCT Imaging of Patients with Neovascular AMD Treated with Intraocular Ranibizumab)
shows that an OCT-guided retreatment strategy results in
similar visual acuity outcomes with fewer injections, than
a monthly schedule.

{ HARBOUR concludes that a 0.5 mg versus 2 mg monthly
versus вАШas-neededвАЩ injection of ranibizumab provides similar results in all treatment groups.

{ CATT (Comparison of Age-Related Macular Degeneration Treatment Trial) compares ranibizumab with bevacizumab intravitreal injections and shows no signiоАЭcant
diоАЪerences in outcome.

{ IVAN (Alternative Treatments to Inhibit VEGF in AgeRelated Choroidal Neovascularization) is similar to CATT,
but uses a dosing schedule of monthly injections for 3
months then three consecutive monthly injections whenever retreatment is required. It shows equivalent visual
outcomes.

{ VIEW 1/2 (VEGF Trap-Eye: Investigation of EоАЫcacy
and Safety in Wet AMD) shows that aоАЬibercept (injected

monthly) in the treatment of neovascular AMD.

{ LUCERNE and TENAYA: shows visual beneоАЭts with faricimab given at up to 16-week intervals and demonstrates
the potential to extend the time between treatments with
sustained eоАЫcacy.
оАБ Indications. All MNV subtypes respond to anti-VEGF therapy, but beneоАЭt is only likely in the presence of active disease
(Fig. 14.40) (this treatment is of no beneоАЭt in patients with
a mature оАЭbrotic disciform scar). Evidence for active MNV
includes оАЬuid or haemorrhage, leakage on FA, an enlarging
MNV membrane, or deteriorating vision judged likely to be
due to MNV activity. An eye with almost any level of vision
may beneоАЭt, although better VA at the outset is associated with

оАБ AоАЮibercept (Eylea) is a recombinant fusion protein that binds
to VEGF-A, VEGF-B and placental growth factor (PlGF). AоАЩer

{ Regular monthly injection is the regimen adopted in initial major trials. Overall, around 95% of patients maintain

slightly greater severity than less intensive administration.

anti-VEGF injection вАУ there are slight diоАЪerences for intravitreal steroid administration.
оАБ Patients can return to normal activity aоАЩer 24 hours, but should

TIP In patients with AMD secondary to MNV, the better the
visual acuity at the onset of anti-VEGF treatment the better the
оАЭnal visual acuity.

Treatment with photodynamic therapy (PDT)

VerteporоАЭn is a light-activated compound preferentially taken up
by dividing cells including neovascular tissue. It is infused intravenously and then activated by diode laser to cause thrombosis.
оАЮe main indication is subfoveal predominantly classic MNV with
visual acuity of 6/60 or better. Severe adverse eоАЪects are rare. PDT
should not be used in patients with porphyria. With the advent of
anti-VEGF treatment, PDT is now rarely used for MNV, but combination therapy (see below) and refusal of intravitreal treatment
remain indications. Reduced-intensity regimens have been used
with good eоАЪect in CSR.

Combination and other experimental therapies

Although anti-VEGF therapy has revolutionized the management
of MNV, further investigation is attempting to achieve outcomes
that are even better, particularly a reduction in the frequency of
intravitreal injections. To date, regimens that include the combination of PDT with anti-VEGF treatment have not been shown to be
superior to anti-VEGF treatment alone, but investigation is ongoing,
including parallel inhibition of anti-VEGF agents with inhibition of
other cytokines and with low-intensity macular radiotherapy. Other
avenues of potential therapeutic advantage are sustained-release
anti-VEGF systems and gene therapy utilizing adenoviral vectors to
facilitate the production of cytokines within the eye.

Laser

оАЮermal argon or diode laser ablation of MNV is now rarely used,
though may still be suitable for the treatment of small classic extrafoveal membranes well away from the macular centre and possibly
some cases of PCV and RAP.

PACHYCHOROID DISEASE SPECTRUM

оАЮis term refers to a group of macular disorders that share the
common features of a thick choroid, dilated and hyperpermeable choroidal vessels, attenuation of the choriocapillaris and
retinal pigment epithelial dysfunction. оАЮese changes may lead
to serous retinal detachment. Normal subfoveal choroidal thickness is approximately 300 ќЉm and the median choroidal thickness
is about 260 ќЉm (depending on the technique of measuring), but
varies considerably with age, gender and race. If a neovascular
membrane is present it tends to be a slow-growing type 1 MNV
producing оАЬat, irregular RPE detachments. оАЮese disorders can be
investigated using OCT and ICG angiography. оАЮe following variants are recognized:

{ оАЮree initial monthly injections followed by monthly
review with re-injection when deterioration occurs as
assessed by VA (e.g. loss of оАЭve letters or more) and OCT
(e.g. retinal thickness increase of 100 ќЉm or more).

{ вАШTreat and extendвАЩ entails administering three initial injections at monthly intervals and then gradually increasing
the period between injections until deterioration is evident. If possible, a tailored interval is determined for each
patient.

{ Minor intraocular inоАЬammation may occur aоАЩer the injection. оАЮe endophthalmitis rate is about 1%.
оАБ Bevacizumab (Avastin). In contrast to ranibizumab, bevacizumab is a complete antibody originally developed to target blood vessel growth in metastatic cancer deposits. Its use
for AMD and other indications is вАШoоАЪ labelвАЩ. It is very much
cheaper than ranibizumab and aоАЬibercept. Clinical trial results
suggest that it is approximately comparable to ranibizumab in
eоАЫcacy and safety, though some studies have suggested that
the risk of serious systemic adverse events is marginally higher
with bevacizumab than ranibizumab. Treatment strategies in
AMD are similar to those used for ranibizumab. оАЮe dose of
bevacizumab is usually 1.25 mg/0.05 ml.
оАБ Brolucizumab (Beavu). оАЮe drug is signiоАЭcantly smaller than
other anti-VEGF agents and has high binding aоАЫnity. It lasts
longer in the eye and thus permits less frequent dosing. оАЮe
HAWK and HARRIER studies show non-inferiority to aоАЬibercept with an improved dosing interval. Approximately half of
the patients on this agent can be maintained on a 3-monthly
injection schedule at 1 year.
оАБ Faricimab (Vabysmo). оАЮis is a new bispeciоАЭc antibody
that inhibits angiopoietin-2 and VEGF-A. оАЮe TENAYA and
LUCERNE trials show non-inferiority to aоАЬibercept at 2 years,
with dosing determined by visual acuity and central subоАЭeld
macular thickness measured with OCT. оАЮe drug is generally
well tolerated, but a small number develop intraocular inоАЬammation. Almost 80% of patients can be managed on a 12-week
dosing schedule and 60% on a 16-week schedule. It may have
a signiоАЭcant impact on care by reducing the frequency of
injections.
оАБ Pegaptanib (Macugen). Pegaptanib sodium was the оАЭrst antiVEGF agent approved by regulatory authorities for ocular
treatment. оАЮe results are similar to outcomes with PDT and
its use is now extremely limited.
оАБ Biosimilars. оАЮese are medicines that are highly similar to
other biological medicines that are already in use, with no
clinical diоАЪerences in terms of safety and eоАЪectiveness. Two
ranibizumab biosimilars have received regulatory approval for
use: Byooviz and Ongavia/Ranivisio. At least 11 biosimilars
to ranibizumab, aоАЬibercept and bevacizumab are in late-stage
development.
оАБ Technique of intravitreal injection. Various protocols are in
use. A typical approach is set out in Table 14.3 and applies to

Table 14.3 Technique of Intravitreal Injection

CHAPTER

Acquired Macular Disorders 601
#### 14

CHAPTER

вАҐоАБ The procedure and its risks should be explained to the patient and appropriate consent obtained.
вАҐоАБ The environment should be appropriate, e.g. a dedicated вАШclean roomвАЩ with adequate illumination.
вАҐоАБ The indication and the eye to be treated should be checked and the eye marked.
вАҐоАБ It should be conоАЭrmed that anterior and posterior segment examination (including IOP) has been carried out recently to
exclude contraindications.
вАҐоАБ ConоАЭrmation should be obtained that a syringe of the drug to be injected is available.
вАҐоАБ Bilateral injections are optimally administered at separate sessions to minimize risk, but if necessary diоАЫerent
instruments and drug batches should be used.
вАҐоАБ A surgical mask should be worn.
вАҐоАБ Topical anaesthetic and mydriatic agents are instilled.
вАҐоАБ Povidone-iodine 5% (chlorhexidine if allergic to iodine) is applied to the ocular surface and at least 3 minutes allowed
prior to injection.
вАҐоАБ Subconjunctival lidocaine 1% or 2% may be used to supplement the topical agent. Some practitioners prefer lidocaine
gel, though concerns have been expressed regarding the possibility of microorganism retention within this.
вАҐоАБ Hands are washed using a standard surgical procedure and sterile gloves donned.
вАҐоАБ The periocular skin, eyelids and lashes are cleaned with 5вАУ10% povidone-iodine.
вАҐоАБ As for other forms of intraocular surgery, a sterile periocular drape may be advisable.
вАҐоАБ The sterile pouch containing a pre-prepared syringe is opened, or a sterile syringe is used to draw up the appropriate
volume of drug from a vial of ready-prepared drug. A needle (typically 30-gauge, 0.5 inch) on the syringe is primed to
expel any air.
вАҐоАБ A sterile speculum is placed in the eye.
вАҐоАБ The patient is instructed to look away from the injection site вАУ this is most commonly inferotemporal because of
ease of access, though any quadrant can be used; the 3 and 9 oвАЩclock positions are avoided because of the risk of
neurovascular damage.
вАҐоАБ A gauge is used to identify an injection site 3.5вАУ4.0 mm posterior to the limbus (pars plana).
вАҐоАБ Forceps can be used to stabilize the eye and, if wished, to apply anterior traction to the conjunctiva so that the
conjunctival hole does not overlie the scleral track.
вАҐоАБ The needle is advanced perpendicularly through the sclera towards the centre of the eyeball and the required volume
of drug (usually 0.05 ml) is injected into the vitreous cavity. Some practitioners try to вАШstepвАЩ the needle track.
вАҐоАБ The needle is removed and discarded.
вАҐоАБ It has become usual practice not to use post-injection antibiotics as there is a suggestion that serial use may increase
the rate of infection by promoting bacterial resistance.
вАҐоАБ Elevated IOP can occlude the central retinal artery and it is important routinely to ensure this remains perfused after
the procedure by checking the patientвАЩs vision (subjectively is adequate), directly visualizing the artery, or optimally
by checking the IOP (particularly in glaucoma patients). If occlusion occurs, urgent paracentesis should be carried out;
simply lying down may restore blood оАЮow.
вАҐоАБ A clear plastic eye shield may be used until the local anaesthetic has worn oоАЫ and during sleep for the оАЭrst night or two,
but practice varies.

оАБ Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (see below).
оАБ Central serous chorioretinopathy (see below).
оАБ Pachychoroid neovasculopathy. оАЮis is possibly an early stage
of polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy and features macular
neovascularization above focal areas of pachychoroid. оАЮe
condition is treated with anti-VEGF injections combined with
full-dose PDT.
оАБ Pachychoroid pigment epitheliopathy (PPE). оАЮis condition
manifests with alteration of the retinal pigment epithelium,
usually without neurosensory detachment, although this may
occasionally occur. Patients present with normal visual acuity
and large choroidal vessels beneath elevations of RPE and subRPE drusen-like deposits.
оАБ Peripapillary pachychoroid syndrome. оАЮis rare condition
tends to aоАЪect elderly males. It is characterised by peripapillary
choroidal thickening associated with intraretinal оАЬuid and/or
subretinal оАЬuid on the nasal side of the fovea and occasionally
optic disc oedema. оАЮe condition is similar to chronic central
serous retinopathy. Macular neovascularization is not present.
Photodynamic therapy with verteporоАЭn may be considered,
but is not always needed.

Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy

Introduction

Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV, also called: pachychoroid
aneurysmal type 1 choroidal neovascularization) is characterized by
a branching vascular network of inner choroidal vessels with multiple terminal aneurysmal protuberances that appear to be the source
of bleeding and exudation. It is more common in patients of African and East Asian ethnic origin than in White patients and is more
common in women than men (5:1). оАЮe disease is oоАЩen bilateral
but tends to be asymmetrical. Risk factors include cigarette smoking, increased body mass index and increased levels of inоАЬammatory
biomarkers (e.g. C-reactive protein). Overall, it is relatively common
and the presence of prominent haemorrhage should lead to the consideration of PCV, particularly if there is an absence of drusen and
the patient is relatively young and Asian or African.

Diagnosis

оАБ Presentation is usually in late middle age with the sudden
onset of unilateral visual impairment.

602 Pachychoroid Disease Spectrum

оАБ Signs

{ Terminal swellings are frequently visible as reddish-orange
nodules beneath the RPE in the peripapillary or macular
area (Fig. 14.41A) and less commonly the periphery.

{ Multiple recurrent serosanguineous retinal and RPE
detachments.

{ Deterioration can be slow with intermittent bleeding and
leakage, resulting in macular damage and visual loss. Up to
50% may have a favourable outlook, with eventual spontaneous resolution of exudation and haemorrhage.
оАБ FA shows an intense localized area of hyperоАЬuorescence (Fig.
14.41B).
оАБ ICGA is the key investigation in PCV.

{ HyperоАЬuorescent nodules and a network of large choroidal
vessels with surrounding hypoоАЬuorescence appear in the
early phase. оАЮe polyp-like swellings rapidly begin to leak
(Fig. 14.41C F).

{ оАЮe previously darker surrounding region becomes hyperоАЬuorescent by the late phase.

{ A cluster of grape-like lesions may carry a higher risk of
severe visual loss.
оАБ OCTA shows the polyps (see Fig. 14.26F).
оАБ OCT

{ Multiple large pigment epithelial detachments, particularly the вАШdouble-humpвАЩ sign and the вАШinverted VвАЩ, associated with pachychoroid (Fig. 14.41G).

{ Round-oval polyps may be attached to the posterior surface of the PED.

{ A branching vascular network may be seen underlying a
оАЬat PED adjacent to the polyp.

Central serous chorioretinopathy

Overview

Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSR) is an idiopathic disorder
characterized by a localized serous detachment of the sensory retina at the macula secondary to leakage from the choriocapillaris
through one or more hyperpermeable RPE sites (see Fig. 14.13).
CSR has been classiоАЭed as one of the вАШpachychoroidвАЩ spectrum disorders; in eyes with unilateral CSR almost two-thirds of fellow eyes
have signs of pachychoroid pigment epitheliopathy. CSR typically
aоАЪects one eye of a young or middle-aged White man. оАЮe male to
female ratio is 3:1; females with CSR tend to be older. It presents in
two forms: acute (self-resolving within 3вАУ6 months) and chronic.
A SNP involving the complement factor H gene on chromosome
1 predisposes to the chronic form of CSR in White individuals.
оАЮe use of steroids in any form (including endogenous Cushing
syndrome) is signiоАЭcantly linked to this condition. Numerous
other risk factors and associations have been reported (including
Helicobacter pylori infection, renal dialysis, systemic hypertension,
psychological stress, pregnancy and sleep apnoea syndrome).

Clinical features

оАБ Symptoms. Unilateral blurring, metamorphopsia, micropsia
and mild dyschromatopsia.
оАБ Signs

{ VA is typically 6/9вАУ6/18, but may improve with a lowstrength convex lens (correction of acquired hypermetropia from retinal elevation).

{ Round or oval detachment of the sensory retina at the
macula (Fig. 14.42A and see Fig. 14.13).

{ оАЮe subretinal оАЬuid may be clear (particularly in early
lesions) or turbid. Precipitates may be present on the posterior retinal surface.

{ One or more depigmented RPE foci (oоАЩen small PEDs)
of variable size may be visible within the neurosensory
detachment. Small patches of RPE atrophy (Fig. 14.42B)
and hyperplasia elsewhere in the posterior pole may indicate the site of previous lesions and are typically seen easily
on FAF imaging (Fig. 14.42C).

{ Chronic lesions may be associated with substantial underlying atrophic change. Fluid can sometimes track downwards in a gravity-dependent fashion (gravitational tract),
best shown on FAF imaging (Fig. 14.42D) and can occasionally progress to bullous CSR (see below).

{ Choroidal thickening may be demonstrated.

Treatment

оАЮe favourable prognosis without treatment in a signiоАЭcant proportion of cases should be borne in mind and asymptomatic
polyps may be observed.
оАБ Combination treatment. оАЮe results of combination therapy
of ranibizumab plus verteporоАЭn PDT are superior to ranibizumab monotherapy in respect of best-corrected visual acuity
and complete polyp regression. Fewer injections are required
(EVEREST-II trial).
оАБ Anti-VEGF agents are less eоАЪective than when they are used
in typical MNV, but may suppress leakage and bleeding.
оАБ PDT alone may lead to regression of polyps and result in an
extended treatment eоАЪect. Early PDT provides a better outcome compared to delayed PDT. Consider PDT as a rescue
therapy in an eye with PCV that is refractory to a course of
anti-VEGF monotherapy.
оАБ Laser photocoagulation of feeder vessels or polyps may
be eоАЪective in selected cases, speciоАЭcally if the changes are
juxta-foveal.

TIP In a patient with AMD who has not responded to antiVEGF treatment after 3 months, undertake ICGA to check for
polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy.

{ Spontaneous resolution within 3вАУ6 months, with return to
near-normal or normal vision in around 80%. Recurrence
is seen in up to 50%.

{ Some patients (about 15%) follow a chronic course lasting
more than 12 months. Prolonged detachment is associated
with gradual photoreceptor and RPE degeneration and
permanently reduced vision. Multiple recurrent attacks
may also give a similar clinical picture.

{ оАЮe optic disc should be examined to exclude a congenital
pit as the cause of a neurosensory detachment (see Ch. 19).
оАБ Course

оАБ Bullous CSR is characterized by large single or multiple serous

TIP In acute central serous chorioretinopathy the visual acuity
can be improved with a plus lens and spontaneous resolution
usually occurs within 3вАУ6 months.

Investigation

оАБ Amsler grid conоАЭrms metamorphopsia corresponding to the
neurosensory detachment.
оАБ OCT shows an optically empty neurosensory elevation. Other
оАЭndings may include one or more smaller RPE detachments
(Fig. 14.42E), precipitates on the posterior surface of detached
retina and thickened choroid. Degenerative changes may be
seen in chronic or recurrent cases.
оАБ FA shows an early hyperоАЬuorescent spot that gradually
enlarges (an вАШink blotвАЩ вАУ Fig. 14.43A and B) or, less commonly,
forms a vertical column (вАШsmokestackвАЩ вАУ Fig. 14.43C and D)

followed by diоАЪusion throughout the detached area. An underlying PED may be demonstrated. Multiple focal leaks or diffuse areas of leakage can be evident, particularly in chronic or
recurrent disease.
оАБ FAF shows a focal decrease in fundus autoоАЬuorescence at the
leakage site and at sites of old lesions (see Fig. 14.42C and D).
A gravitational tract is sometimes seen.
оАБ ICGA. оАЮe early phase may show dilated or compromised
choroidal vessels at the posterior pole and the mid-stage areas
of hyperоАЬuorescence due to choroidal hyperpermeability. Subclinical foci are commonly visible.

Management

оАБ Observation is appropriate in many cases. All treatment
modalities can be associated with RPE tear formation, which
can also occur spontaneously.
оАБ Corticosteroid treatment should be discontinued, if possible,
particularly in chronic, recurrent or severe cases.
оАБ Oral spironolactone (40 mg twice daily) may result in faster
resorption of subretinal оАЬuid than no treatment in acute CSR.

606 Idiopathic Macular Telangiectasia

A B

Fig. 14.44 Subthreshold (micropulse) laser treatment of central serous chorioretinopathy.
оАЭAоАЮ Prior to treatment; оАЭBоАЮ after successful treatment.

оАБ Laser. Subthreshold (micropulse) diode laser to the RPE site of
leakage has shown good results (Fig. 14.44) in several studies
and is associated with signiоАЭcantly less retinal damage on OCT
than conventional photocoagulation.
оАБ PDT with verteporоАЭn at 30вАУ50% of the dose used for MNV
in conjunction with 50% light intensity typically leads to
complete resolution, including in severe chronic cases and is
associated with a considerably lower incidence of signiоАЭcant
choroidal ischaemia than higher-intensity regimens.
оАБ Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents are commonly used in
conjunction with PDT. However, bevacizumab alone is
not helpful unless there is evidence of coexisting macular
neovascularization.
оАБ Others. Case reports show beneоАЭt with a variety of agents,
including aspirin, beta-blockers and mifepristone, but controlled assessment is limited to date.

TIP All forms of corticosteroid treatment should be
discontinued if possible in patients with chronic or recurrent
central serous chorioretinopathy.

PERIPHERAL EXUDATIVE
HAEMORRHAGIC
CHORIORETINOPATHY

Peripheral exudative haemorrhagic chorioretinopathy (PEHCR) is
an uncommon disorder aоАЪecting predominantly older women. It
typically manifests with peripheral retinal haemorrhage, exudation
and PED associated with MNV. It is bilateral in a few patients. оАЮe
visual prognosis is oоАЩen good, but is poor if the macula is involved.
оАЮe aetiology is unknown, though it may be a form of neovascular
AMD. Some cases share characteristics with polypoidal choroidal
vasculopathy. Treatment for sight-threatening disease generally
consists of intravitreal anti-VEGF injection.

IDIOPATHIC MACULAR
NEOVASCULARIZATION

Idiopathic MNV is an uncommon condition that aоАЪects individuals under the age of 50 years and is usually unilateral. оАЮe diagnosis is one of exclusion of other possible associations of MNV in
younger patients, such as angioid streaks, high myopia and chorioretinal inоАЬammatory conditions (such as presumed ocular histoplasmosis). оАЮe condition carries a better visual prognosis than
that associated with AMD and in some cases spontaneous resolution may occur. Treatment is typically with an anti-VEGF agent.

IDIOPATHIC MACULAR
TELANGIECTASIA

Idiopathic macular telangiectasia (IMT) is a condition of unknown
pathogenesis. It may be more common than previously believed
and can be confused with DR, prior RVO and other causes of
macular vascular changes. A family history is present in a small
proportion of cases.

Type 1: aneurysmal telangiectasia

оАЮis may be closely related to Coats disease, or more speciоАЭcally
the milder form of Coats previously known as Leber miliary aneurysms. It generally involves only one eye and both the peripheral
retina and macula can be aоАЪected. Patients are typically middleaged males.
оАБ Symptoms. MildвАУmoderate blurring of vision in one eye.
оАБ Signs

{ Chronic leakage and lipid deposition (Fig. 14.45A).

{ Telangiectasia and microaneurysms. Early signs may be
subtle and more readily detected on red-free photography.

{ Larger aneurysms form as the condition progresses.

{ Macular oedema, including cystoid changes.

Type 2: perifoveal telangiectasia
оАЭMacTelоАЮ

MacTel is primarily a neurodegenerative condition that is usually
bilateral, although asymmetry and even apparent unilaterality is
possible. MacTel is associated with changes in the inner and outer
retina, ultimately leading to photoreceptor atrophy, development
of retinal clumps and abnormal neovascular complexes. Degeneration of M√Љller cells is thought to be an important pathogenic

mechanism. It is a genetically complex disease which has been
associated with reduced serum levels of serine. оАЮe prevalence may
have been underestimated in the past and could be as high as 0.1%
in people over the age of 40 years (about 2 million worldwide).
оАЮe age of onset is most commonly in the 6th and 7th decades of
life, but much earlier cases have been described. Clinically relevant
manifestations are conоАЭned to the вАШMacTel areaвАЩ, an oval region
with a radius of 6 [o] horizontally and 5 [o] vertically centred on the
foveola, with larger areas only involved when a secondary neovascular membrane develops. Atrophy of the photoreceptor layer is
responsible for the visual loss in advanced cases.
оАБ Symptoms. Blurring in one or both eyes, manifesting in the
early stages with diоАЫculty in reading. Visual distortion may
be a feature.
