{ Early changes are subtle, manifesting as a lack of foveolar
reоАЬex (Fig. 14.46A). OCT shows the pathological changes
at this stage (Fig. 14.46B).

Type 3: occlusive telangiectasia

оАЮis extremely rare condition presents in late middle age and carries
a poor visual prognosis. оАЮe manifestations relate to capillary occlusion rather than telangiectasia (progressive occlusion of parafoveal
capillaries with marked aneurysmal dilatation of terminal capillaries). It is distinct from type 1 and type 2 macular telangiectasia.

CYSTOID MACULAR OEDEMA

Introduction

Cystoid macular oedema (CMO) results from the accumulation of
оАЬuid in the outer plexiform and inner nuclear layers of the retina
with the formation of tiny cyst-like cavities (Fig. 14.47A). Fluid
may initially accumulate intracellularly in M√Љller cells, with subsequent rupture. Coalescence of smaller cavities may occur over
time with subsequent progression to a foveal lamellar hole with
irreversible impairment of central vision. CMO is a non-speciоАЭc
manifestation of any type of macular oedema. Causes include:
оАБ Ocular surgery and laser, e.g. phacoemulsiоАЭcation, panretinal
photocoagulation and miscellaneous other procedures.
оАБ Retinal vascular disease, e.g. diabetic retinopathy, retinal vein
occlusion.
оАБ InоАЮammation, e.g. intermediate uveitis, severe or chronic
uveitis of any kind.
оАБ Drug-induced, e.g. topical prostaglandin derivatives.
оАБ Retinal dystrophies, e.g. retinitis pigmentosa.
оАБ Conditions involving vitreomacular traction, e.g. epiretinal
membrane.
оАБ MNV.
оАБ Fundus tumours, e.g. retinal capillary haemangioma.
оАБ Systemic disease, e.g. chronic renal failure.

Diagnosis

{ Aneurysms are uncommon, but have been reported. Vascular leakage is mild; exudation with cystoid macular
oedema is not a feature of this disease.
оАБ OCT is a key aid to the diagnosis. оАЮe formation of hyporeоАЬective inner retinal cavities of variable size, morphologically distinct from those seen in CMO, are a characteristic оАЭnding (see
Fig. 14.46B). An inner lamellar cyst that enlarges with progressive
disease is commonly seen underlying the fovea. оАЮinning and disruption of the photoreceptor layers is also common, but is a late
sign. Pigment clumps are shown as intraretinal hyper-reоАЬective
plaques with posterior shadowing. оАЮere is usually no overall
macular thickening, because outer retinal thinning is counteracted by inner retinal thickening. Hyper-reоАЬective inner retinal
dots corresponding to telangiectatic vessels may be present.
оАБ FAF changes occur early in the disease course and may precede clinically detectable signs. Heightened foveal autoоАЬuorescence is a common early оАЭnding, which gradually increases in
extent (Fig. 14.46D).
оАБ FA in early disease shows bilateral perifoveal telangiectasia
with early leakage from the abnormal vessels progressing to
diоАЪuse leakage, though without CMO. оАЮe cystoid spaces
identiоАЭable on OCT do not show hyperоАЬuorescence on FA. FA
is also used to conоАЭrm MNV (Fig. 14.46E and F).
оАБ Macular pigment optical density (MPOD) imaging shows a
possibly pathognomonic pattern of oval reduction in density
corresponding to the late distribution of hyperоАЬuorescence on
FA. MPOD is preserved from 6¬∞ outwards.
оАБ Prognosis. Visual acuity generally does not deteriorate to less than
6/60, with an average acuity of 6/12. оАЮe development of subretinal neovascularization is most oоАЩen preceded by enlargement of
pigment clumps. Spread of the pigment increases the risk of developing poor vision and proliferative disease, particularly in females.
MNV in MacTel tends to carry a better prognosis than in AMD.
оАБ Treatment. Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents can decrease leakage on FA in the non-proliferative stage but are not helpful

оАБ Symptoms may include blurring, distortion and micropsia.
оАБ Signs

{ Loss of the foveal depression, thickening of the retina
and multiple cystoid areas in the sensory retina (Fig.
14.47B), best seen with red-free light using a fundus
contact lens.

{ Optic disc swelling is sometimes present.

{ A lamellar hole may be visible.

{ Features of associated disease.
оАБ Amsler chart demonstrates central blurring and distortion.
оАБ FA. A petaloid pattern is seen due to dye accumulation in
microcystic spaces in the outer plexiform layer (Fig. 14.47C).
оАБ OCT shows retinal thickening with cystic hyporeоАЬective
spaces and loss of the foveal depression (Fig. 14.47D). A lamellar hole may be demonstrated in advanced cases.

MICROCYSTIC MACULAR OEDEMA

Microcystic changes of the inner nuclear layer distinct from classic CMO can occur in eyes with optic neuritis and some other
forms of optic neuropathy. It is believed to be caused by retrograde degeneration of the inner retinal layers that manifests with
impaired оАЬuid resorption.

VITREOMACULAR INTERFACE
DISORDERS

Epiretinal membrane
Introduction

An epiretinal membrane (ERM) is a transparent, avascular, оАЭbrocellular structure that develops on or above the surface of the
retina. Proliferation of the cellular component and contraction of

the membrane leads to visual symptoms, primarily due to retinal
wrinkling, obstruction and localized elevation with or without
pseudocyst formation and CMO.
оАБ Idiopathic

{ No apparent cause, as a relatively common ageing process.
оАЮe prevalence of early ERM (cellophane) is approximately
6.5% and advanced ERM (preretinal macular оАЭbrosis),
approximately 2.5%.

{ Residual vitreous tissue remains on the retinal surface following cortical separation in around 50% of eyes, with subsequent
proliferation. оАЮe predominant cellular constituent is glial
cells, probably derived from the indigenous posterior hyaloid
membrane (PHM) cell population (laminocytes). ERM development can occur at any stage of posterior vitreous detachment (PVD). It is now believed that the process of PVD from
initiation to completion oоАЩen extends over the course of years.

{ About 10% are bilateral.

{ Tend to be milder than secondary ERMs.

CHAPTER

Acquired Macular Disorders 611
#### 14

оАБ Secondary

{ Occur following retinal detachment surgery (most frequent cause of secondary ERM), retinal break, panretinal
photocoagulation, retinal cryotherapy, retinal vascular disease, inоАЬammation and trauma.

{ Binocularity is dependent on whether both eyes are
aоАЪected by the causative factors.

{ Cell type more varied, with prominent pigment cells вАУ
thought to be derived from the RPE.

Diagnosis

CHAPTER

оАБ Symptoms. Blurring and metamorphopsia. Mild cases are
oоАЩen asymptomatic.
оАБ Signs

{ VA is highly variable, depending on severity.

{ An irregular translucent sheen (cellophane maculopathy)
is present in early ERM, oоАЩen best detected using green
(red-free) light (Fig. 14.48A).

{ As the membrane thickens and contracts it becomes more
obvious (macular pucker) and typically causes mild distortion of blood vessels (Fig. 14.48B).

{ Advanced ERM may give severe distortion of blood vessels, marked retinal wrinkling and striae and may obscure
underlying structures (Fig. 14.48C).

FullоАЯthickness macular hole

Introduction

Full-thickness macular hole (FTMH) is a relatively common cause
of central visual loss, with a prevalence of approximately 3:1000.
оАЮe onset is most common in females aged 60вАУ70. Careful examination of the fellow eye is important as macular holes are bilateral
on presentation in about 30%. оАЮe role of vitreomacular traction
(VMT) in the aetiology of macular hole has increasingly been
deоАЭned over recent years and is considered at greater length as a
separate topic later in this chapter. A new OCT-based classiоАЭcation has been published by the International Vitreomacular Traction Study (IVTS) Group and replaces the older Gass classiоАЭcation
of macular hole, which was based on careful clinical examination
and divided macular holes into four stages. Other causes of fullthickness macular hole include high myopia (which can lead to
macular retinal detachment) and blunt ocular trauma. Lesions that
may sometimes have a similar appearance include macular pseudohole and lamellar hole (see вАШVitreoretinal tractionвАЩ below). оАЮe
clinical appearance of a macular hole is typical, but the diоАЪerential diagnosis includes cystoid macular oedema, subfoveal drusen,
CSR and adult vitelliform macular dystrophy.

Symptoms

Presentation of a full-thickness dehiscence may be with impairment of central vision in one eye, or as a relatively asymptomatic
deterioration, оАЭrst noticed when the fellow eye is occluded or
at a routine sight test. Symptoms are absent or mild prior to the
development of a full-thickness lesion. Metamorphopsia may be
present.

Signs and OCT features

оАБ Vitreomacular adhesion вАУ VMA: no distortion of the foveal
contour. оАЮis term was proposed to denote the OCT оАЭnding
of oblique foveal vitreoretinal traction before the appearance
of clinical changes.
оАБ Vitreomacular traction вАУ VMT: distortion of foveal contour
in the absence of a hole. Clinically stage 1a: вАШImpendingвАЩ macular hole. Appears as оАЬattening of the foveal depression with an
underlying yellow spot. Pathologically, the inner retinal layers
detach from the underlying photoreceptor layer, oоАЩen with the
formation of a cyst-like schisis cavity. With loss of structural
support, the photoreceptor layer commonly undergoes centrifugal displacement. Clinically stage 1b: occult macular hole,
indicates foveal detachment and is characterised by a yellowish
ring (Fig. 14.49A).
оАБ Small or medium FTMH with VMT: small is less than
250 ќЉm or medium is 250вАУ400 ќЉm measured at its narrowest
point with OCT. Clinically stage 2: small full-thickness hole
measuring less than 400 ќЉm in diameter. оАЮe defect may be
central, slightly eccentric or crescent-shaped (Fig. 14.49B). A
dehiscence is present in the inner retina with persistent vitreofoveolar adhesion. оАЮe posterior hyaloid remains attached to
the fovea. Nearly all will progress to the next stage, with further visual decline.

{ Associated оАЭndings may include macular pseudohole
(Fig. 14.48D), CMO, retinal telangiectasia and small
haemorrhages.
оАБ Amsler grid testing typically shows distortion.
оАБ OCT shows a highly reоАЬective surface layer associated with
retinal thickening (Fig. 14.48E). Disruption of the innersegment/outer-segment junction may be associated with a
worse visual outcome following surgery. OCT is also useful to
exclude signiоАЭcant vitreomacular traction (see later).
оАБ FA has been superseded by OCT for routine assessment
of ERM, but if undertaken it highlights vascular tortuosity and demonstrates leakage. It is sometimes indicated to
investigate the cause of an ERM, such as a prior retinal vein
occlusion.

Treatment

оАБ Observation if the membrane is mild and non-progressive.
Spontaneous resolution of visual symptoms sometimes
occurs, typically due to complete separation of the ERM
from the retina. CMO or tractional detachment may require
fairly prompt surgery to minimize secondary degenerative
change.
оАБ Surgical removal of the membrane via vitrectomy usually
improves or eliminates distortion (the main beneоАЭt), with an
improvement in visual acuity of at least two lines in around
75% or more; in about a quarter VA is unchanged and around
2% get worse. оАЮe surgical complications are principally those
occurring aоАЩer vitrectomy (see Ch. 16). Removal of the internal limiting membrane (ILM) in concert with ERM peeling
may be beneоАЭcial, but remains controversial. Visual improvement commonly does not occur for several months postoperatively. Recurrence is rare.

оАБ Large FTMH with VMT: greater than 400 ќЉm in diameter.
Clinically stage 3: large full-thickness macular hole measuring more than 400 ќЉm associated with a red base in which
yellowвАУwhite dots may be seen (Fig. 14.49C). A surrounding
grey cuоАЪ of subretinal оАЬuid is usually present (Fig. 14.49D) and

an overlying retinal operculum (sometimes called a pseudooperculum) may be visible. Visual acuity is commonly reduced
to 6/60, but is occasionally better, particularly with eccentric
оАЭxation. An operculum (see Fig. 14.50C) consists primarily of
glial tissue and condensed vitreous cortex, though 40% contain

Fig. 14.49 Macular hole. оАЭAоАЮ Occult вАУ stage 1b (arrow); оАЭBоАЮ small full-thickness вАУ stage 2; оАЭCоАЮ fullthickness вАУ stage 3; оАЭDоАЮ three-dimensional OCT of stage 4 hole (with surrounding subretinal

photoreceptor elements. By deоАЭnition, there is persistent parafoveal attachment of the vitreous cortex.
оАБ Small, medium or large FTMH without VMT. Clinically
stage 4: full-thickness macular hole with complete posterior
vitreous detachment, suggested (but not conоАЭrmed) by the
presence of a Weiss ring. A signiоАЭcant proportion of idiopathic
macular holes have an associated ERM.
оАБ Spontaneously resolved macular hole. Macular holes may
heal spontaneously, oоАЩen resuming a near-normal or even
normal clinical and OCT appearance. A tiny outer retinal вАУ
oоАЩen inner-segment/outer-segment junction вАУ or other subfoveolar defect may persist aоАЩer spontaneous or surgical closure.
Solar retinopathy commonly gives a similar appearance, as
does scarring from several other conditions where the foveal
centre is a focus of damage.
оАБ Macular microhole refers to a small (<150 ќЉm) fullthickness foveal retinal defect and is synonymous with a
small full-thickness macular hole (stage 2 above). Symptoms
are often minimal and may consist of mild central blurring,
metamorphopsia (distortion) or disturbed reading vision.

Visual acuity is typically impaired less than with a larger
FTMH and the abnormality may not be noticed immediately. The full-thickness defect typically heals within a few
weeks and by the time of initial examination only a welldemarcated red spot rather than a visibly full-thickness
defect may be present. At this stage OCT may show only a
small defect in the inner-segment/outer-segment layer (see
Fig. 14.50D).

Investigations

оАБ Amsler grid testing will usually show non-speciоАЭc central distortion rather than a scotoma.
оАБ оАЯe WatzkeвАУAllen test is performed by projecting a narrow
slit beam over the centre of the hole vertically and horizontally, preferably using a fundus contact lens. A patient with a
macular hole will report that the beam is thinned or broken.
Patients with other pathology usually see a distorted beam of
uniform thickness.
оАБ OCT is extremely useful in diagnosis and staging (Fig. 14.50A
D) and can show the dynamic nature of the pathology.

оАБ FAF shows a markedly hyperоАЬuorescent foveolar spot in stages
3 and 4 and punctate оАЬuorescence in stage 2.
оАБ FA in a full-thickness hole shows an early well-deоАЭned window defect. In light of the eоАЪectiveness of OCT in this condition there is no need to undertake оАЬuorescein angiography.

TIP OCT is key to the conоАЭrmation of the diagnosis of macular
hole and for staging purposes.

Treatment

оАБ оАЯe clinical stage and duration are the most important variables that inоАЬuence the management and prognosis.
оАБ Observation. About 50% of stage 1 holes resolve following
spontaneous vitreo-foveolar separation, so these are managed
conservatively. About 10% of full-thickness holes also close
spontaneously, sometimes with marked visual improvement.

Spontaneous resolution is more common with smaller FTMH.
Treatment is not needed for spontaneously healed holes,
though vitrectomy with ERM peeling is sometimes indicated.
Patients whose other eye is healthy should be warned that
the risk of the fellow eye becoming involved within 5 years is
around 10%. оАЮe risk is less if there is already a PVD present.
оАБ Pharmacological vitreolysis with ocriplasmin may be suitable
for small early-stage holes. It is discussed under вАШVitreomacular tractionвАЩ below.
оАБ Intravitreal gas or air. Preliminary studies suggest that closure may follow an injection of a small bolus of gas into the
vitreous.
оАБ Surgery may be considered in stage 2 or greater holes and
for some lamellar holes. Symptom duration before surgery is
strongly and consistently associated with poorer anatomical and
visual outcome aоАЩer surgery. Better results are usually achieved
in patients with smaller holes present for under 6 months.

CHAPTER

Acquired Macular Disorders 615
#### 14

{ Operative treatment consists of vitrectomy, together with
(a) peeling of the ILM facilitated by vital dye staining, (b)
relief of vitreomacular traction by either induction of a
total PVD if not already present or removal of the perifoveal vitreous and (c) gas tamponade. оАЮe reattachment
rate is improved by postoperative face-down positioning
(8 hours daily for 5 days). An extensive period of postoperative positioning is not required with modern techniques.

{ оАЮe hole is closed in most cases and visual improvement
occurs over the course of months in 80вАУ90% of eyes, with
a оАЭnal visual acuity of 6/12 or better in approximately
65%. Worsening of visual acuity occurs in up to 10% of
eyes. Mild residual abnormality on OCT such as a defect
in the inner-segment/outer-segment junction or other
disturbance adjacent to the RPE is common and may also
provide a diagnostic indicator of a spontaneously healed
macular hole or microhole.

{ Complications are the same as those occurring aоАЩer vitrectomy (see Ch. 16).

Vitreomacular traction

Introduction

Physiological PVD (see Ch. 16) usually proceeds gradually over
an extended period to encompass complete separation from the
macula and optic nerve head: complete PVD. If adhesion of the gel
persists at the central macula, an anomalous PVD is present and
can precede a range of macular conditions.
оАБ Vitreomacular adhesion (VMA) refers to residual attachment of
the vitreous within a 3 mm radius of the central macula in the
presence of perifoveal vitreous separation. In most cases it constitutes a stage in a dynamic process of PVD so may not incur
pathological sequelae. оАЮere is no distortion of the foveal contour
or any secondary retinal changes. Focal VMA involves an area of
attachment of вЙ§1500 ќЉm diameter, broad VMA >1500 ќЉm.

оАБ Vitreomacular traction (VMT) is deоАЭned as the presence of
retinal changes on OCT with evident perifoveal (within 3 mm)
PVD. Distortion of the foveal surface contour and/or other
structural retinal changes may be present. Focal (Fig. 14.51A)
and broad types are deоАЭned as for VMA. Concurrent VMT is
associated with other macular disease, e.g. AMD, RVO, DR.
Isolated VMT is not associated with other macular disease.
оАБ Full-thickness macular hole (FTMH). A foveal lesion featuring interruption of all retinal layers from the internal limiting
membrane to the RPE (see above) (Fig. 14.51B). Other considerations include the status of the vitreous (with or without
VMT) and the presence of an identiоАЭable cause such as trauma.
оАБ Lamellar macular hole. оАЮis is a partial-thickness defect
of the inner retina at the fovea but maintenance of an intact
photoreceptor layer (see Fig. 14.51A). Its pathogenesis is
incompletely deоАЭned, but may develop from anomalous PVD,
sometimes following a foveal pseudocyst, or represent abortive
FTMH formation in some patients. Classically, lamellar hole
was described as a sequel to CMO.
оАБ Macular pseudohole. оАЮis lesion mimics the clinical appearance of a FTMH, but is caused by distortion of the perifoveal
retina into heaped edges by ERM, without any loss of retinal tissue and near-normal foveal thickness; there is a central defect in
the membrane. VMT may be present (see Fig. 14.48D).
оАБ Epiretinal membrane (ERM). ERM is independent of the
IVTS classiоАЭcation, but most eyes with broad vitreomacular
traction have an associated ERM. Vitreous remnants on the
retinal surface following PVD provide a mechanism for the
development of idiopathic ERM.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms in VMT include decreased vision, metamorphopsia, photopsia and micropsia, but are usually milder in lamellar
holes and pseudoholes and absent in VMA.
оАБ Signs in VMT may include retinal surface thickening, wrinkling and distortion, foveal pseudocyst, CMO, macular schisis

CHAPTER

A B

Fig. 14.51 Vitreomacular traction (VMT). оАЭAоАЮ OCT showing VMT; оАЭBоАЮ OCT of full-thickness macular hole without VMT, showing interruption of all retinal layers, macular oedema and a small
operculum.

616 Degenerative Myopia

or detachment and capillary leakage. оАЮe limit of the attached
gel may be visible as a whitish band or reоАЬex. Visible changes
may be subtle. Both lamellar holes and pseudoholes can appear
as a discrete reddish oval or round foveal spot.
оАБ OCT is the key investigation.

Treatment

Treatment of FTMH and ERM is considered under separate topics
above.
оАБ Observation. Spontaneous separation occurs in a proportion of patients with VMT and is more likely in milder cases.
Observation is appropriate in patients with VMA and in many
cases of VMT.
оАБ Pharmacological vitreolysis. Intravitreal injection of ocriplasmin, a recombinant form of human plasmin, releases
VMT in over 25% of eyes and may close macular holes where
VMT is present (40% versus 10% with placebo). Results are
generally inferior to vitrectomy. Smaller areas of VMT and
smaller macular holes have better outcomes. оАЮe presence of
ERM is a poorer prognostic indicator for ocriplasmin treatment. DiоАЪuse retinal dysfunction of uncertain mechanism,
including substantially decreased acuity, has been reported as
a rare side eоАЪect.
оАБ Pars plana vitrectomy with peeling of the adherent area and
any associated ERM usually gives good results in VMT. оАЮe
beneоАЭt of vitrectomy for lamellar and pseudoholes is less clearcut, but is probably greater if signiоАЭcant ERM is present.

DEGENERATIVE MYOPIA

Introduction

Myopia is the result of complex hereditary and environmental
factors. оАЮere is strong evidence for a causative association with
long-term intensive near visual activity, particularly reading
and using personal computers. A refractive error of more than
вИТ6 dioptres constitutes a common deоАЭnition of high myopia, in
which axial length is usually greater than 26 mm. It aоАЪects over
2% of adult Western European or American populations and may
be as high as 10% in East Asians. Pathological or degenerative
myopia is characterized by progressive anteroposterior elongation of the scleral envelope associated with a range of secondary ocular changes, principally thought to relate to mechanical
stretching of the involved tissues. It is a signiоАЭcant cause of legal
blindness, with maculopathy the most common cause of visual
loss.
Low-dose atropine (0.01%) instilled at bed-time can signiоАЭcantly slow the progression of myopia in children. Although there
is no consensus, a reasonable approach is to oоАЪer this treatment
to children between the ages of 5 and 15 years whose myopia is
increasing at more than 1 dioptre per year.
оАЮese children should also be encouraged to spend more time
outdoors in sunshine.
In children aged 7вАУ11 years with less than 5 dioptres of myopia, a high-add (+2.50D) centre-distance soоАЩ multifocal contact lens can be considered. оАЮe BLINK trial concludes that this

Table 14.4 Systemic Associations of High Myopia

Down syndrome
Stickler syndrome
Marfan syndrome
Prematurity
Noonan syndrome
EhlersвАУDanlos syndrome
PierreвАУRobin syndrome

slows the rate of myopia progression and that the response is
dose-dependent

Table 14.4 lists systemic associations of high myopia.

Diagnosis

оАБ A pale tessellated (tigroid) appearance is due to diоАЪuse attenuation of the RPE with visibility of large choroidal vessels (Fig.
14.52A).
оАБ Focal chorioretinal atrophy is characterized by patchy visibility of choroidal vessels and oоАЩen sclera (Fig. 14.52B).
оАБ Anomalous optic nerve head. оАЮis may appear unusually
small, large or anomalous with a вАШtiltedвАЩ conformation (Fig.
14.52C). Peripapillary chorioretinal atrophy is very common,
most commonly as a temporal crescent of thinned or absent
RPE.
оАБ Acquired optic disc pit formation is not uncommon and is
thought to be due to expansion of the peripapillary region as
the eye enlarges over time.
оАБ Lattice degeneration (see Ch. 16).
оАБ Lacquer cracks are ruptures in the RPEвАУBruch membraneвАУ
choriocapillaris complex characterized by оАЭne irregular yellow
lines criss-crossing at the posterior pole (Fig. 14.52D) in around
5% of highly myopic eyes and can be complicated by MNV.
оАБ Subretinal вАШcoinвАЩ haemorrhages (Fig. 14.52E) may develop
from lacquer cracks in the absence of MNV.
оАБ Fuchs spot (Fig. 14.52F) is a raised, circular, pigmented lesion
at the macula developing aоАЩer a subretinal haemorrhage has
absorbed.
оАБ Staphyloma is a peripapillary or macular ectasia of the posterior sclera (Fig. 14.53A and B) due to focal thinning and
expansion present in about a third of eyes with pathological
myopia. Associations include macular hole formation and
вАШdome-shaped maculaвАЩ (see below).
оАБ (Peripapillary) intrachoroidal cavitation, formerly described
as peripapillary detachment of pathological myopia (PDPM),
may occur adjacent to the nerve, commonly inferiorly (Fig.
14.53C). Clinically, it may be evident as a small yellowishorange peripapillary area typically inferior to the disc. It can
usually be identiоАЭed on OCT (Fig. 14.53D). Visual оАЭeld defects
are common and frequently mimic glaucoma.
оАБ Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RD) is much more
common in high myopia, the pathogenesis including increased
frequency of PVD, lattice degeneration, asymptomatic atrophic holes, myopic macular holes (see below) and occasionally
giant retinal tears.
оАБ MNV

{ 10% of highly myopic eyes develop MNV.

{ оАЮe prognosis is better in younger patients with myopiarelated MNV than in AMD.

{ Anti-VEGF therapy is generally the treatment of choice.
оАЮe injection frequency may be less than that needed for

оАБ Macular retinoschisis (foveoschisis) and macular retinal
detachment without macular hole formation may occur in

a result of vitreous traction. Some cases are associated with

оАБ Macular hole may occur spontaneously or aоАЩer relatively mild
trauma and is associated with the development of rhegmatogenous retinal detachment much more commonly than agerelated idiopathic macular hole. Myopic macular retinoschisis
and myopic macular hole may be part of the same pathological
process. Vitrectomy may be eоАЪective for both, but the best surgical technique remains undeоАЭned.
оАБ Peripapillary detachment is an innocuous yellowвАУorange
elevation of the RPE and sensory retina at the inferior
border of the myopic conus (anomalous optic nerve head
complex).
оАБ Cataract. Posterior subcapsular or early onset nuclear sclerotic.
оАБ Glaucoma. оАЮere is an increased prevalence of primary
open-angle glaucoma, pigmentary glaucoma and steroid
responsiveness.
оАБ Amblyopia is uncommon but may develop when there is a signiоАЭcant diоАЪerence in myopia between the two eyes.

оАБ Dislocation of the lens (natural or artiоАЭcial) is a rare but wellrecognized risk.

ANGIOID STREAKS

Introduction

Angioid streaks are crack-like dehiscences in brittle thickened and
calciоАЭed Bruch membrane, associated with atrophy of the overlying RPE. Approximately 50% of patients with angioid streaks have
a systemic association.
оАБ Pseudoxanthoma elasticum (PXE), a hereditary disorder of
connective tissue in which there is progressive calciоАЭcation,
fragmentation and degeneration of elastic оАЭbres in the skin,
eye and cardiovascular system, is by far the most common
association of angioid streaks (Gr√ґnbladвАУStrandberg syndrome). оАЮe disorder is caused by mutations in the ABCC6
gene. Patients develop a вАШplucked chickenвАЩ appearance of the
skin, most commonly on the neck, axillae and antecubital

CHAPTER

Acquired Macular Disorders 619
#### 14

fossae. Approximately 85% of patients develop ocular involvement of variable severity, usually aоАЩer the second decade of
life.
оАБ Paget disease is a chronic, progressive metabolic bone disease
characterized by excessive and disorganized resorption and
formation of bone. Angioid streaks occur in only about 2%.
It is thought that calcium binds to the elastin of Bruch membrane, imparting brittleness and fragility.
оАБ Haemoglobinopathies occasionally associated with angioid streaks are numerous, including sickle-cell trait and disease and thalassaemia. In these, a brittle Bruch membrane is
thought to be due to iron deposition.
оАБ Miscellaneous other associations have been reported. оАЮere
is probably no link between angioid streaks and EhlersвАУ
Danlos syndrome.

Diagnosis

CHAPTER

оАБ Signs

{ Grey or dark red linear lesions with irregular serrated
edges that intercommunicate in a ring-like fashion around
the optic disc and radiate outwards from the peripapillary
area (Fig. 14.54A). оАЮe streaks tend to increase in width
and extent slowly over time.

{ вАШPeau dвАЩorangeвАЩ (orange skin), also known as leopard skin,
mottled yellowish speckling (Fig. 14.54B) is common, particularly in cases associated with PXE.

{ Optic disc drusen are frequently (up to 25%) associated
(Fig. 14.54C and D).

{ Scleral depression is relatively contraindicated in these
eyes, due to the risk of further damage to Bruch membrane
leading to new angioid streaks or choroidal rupture.
оАБ Complications. оАЮough angioid streaks are typically asymptomatic at оАЭrst, visual impairment occurs eventually in more
than 70% of patients.

{ MNV is by far the most common cause of visual loss.

{ Choroidal rupture may occur following relatively trivial
trauma (Fig. 14.54E).

CHOROIDAL FOLDS

Introduction

Choroidal folds are parallel grooves or striae involving the inner
choroid, Bruch membrane, the RPE and sometimes the retina (chorioretinal folds). оАЮey are likely to develop in association with any
process that induces suоАЫcient compressive stress within the choroid, Bruch membrane and retina. Primary mechanisms include
choroidal congestion and scleral compression, and occasionally
tissue contraction. Choroidal folds should be distinguished from
retinal folds, which have a diоАЪerent pathogenesis (usually ERM).
Causes include:
оАБ Idiopathic (вАШcongenitalвАЩ) folds may be present in healthy, oоАЩen
hypermetropic, individuals in whom visual acuity is typically
unaоАЪected. оАЮe folds are usually bilateral. A syndrome of idiopathic acquired hypermetropia with choroidal folds has been
described вАУ in these patients elevated intracranial pressure
should always be excluded even without evident papilloedema
(see next) although a constricted scleral canal causing optic
disc congestion has been proposed as an alternative mechanism in some patients.
оАБ Papilloedema. Choroidal folds may occur in patients with
chronically elevated intracranial pressure, when they may
be associated with reduction of visual acuity that may be
permanent.
оАБ Orbital disease such as retrobulbar tumours and thyroid
ophthalmopathy may cause choroidal folds associated with
impaired vision.
оАБ Ocular disease such as pachychoroid disease, choroidal
tumours, inоАЬammation such as posterior scleritis, scleral
buckling for retinal detachment and hypotony.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. оАЮe eоАЪect on vision is variable and dependent on
the cause. Many patients are asymptomatic.
оАБ Signs

{ Parallel lines, grooves or striae typically located at the posterior pole. оАЮe folds are usually horizontally orientated (Fig.
14.55), but in pachychoroid disease tend to be diagonal.

{ оАЮe crest (elevated portion) of a fold is yellow and less pigmented as a result of stretching and thinning of the RPE
and the trough is darker due to compression of the RPE.

{ Foveal involvement by a streak.
оАБ Red-free photography demonstrates the streaks.
оАБ FA shows hyperfluorescent window defects due to RPE
atrophy overlying the streaks, associated with variable associated hypofluorescence corresponding to RPE hyperplasia.
FA is generally indicated only if MNV is suspected (Fig.
14.54F).
оАБ FAF. Streaks are autoоАЬuorescent. оАЮey are oоАЩen more extensive than clinically, which may conоАЭrm the diagnosis in subtle
cases. Peau dвАЩorange is shown.

Treatment

Following systemic investigation where appropriate, usually via
referral to an appropriate physician, observation is the approach
in most cases. Patients should be warned against participating
in contact sports and advised to use protective spectacles when
necessary. MNV should usually be treated with intravitreal
anti-VEGF agents, but commonly recurs or develops at a new
site.

{ Clinical examination should be directed towards the exclusion of optic disc swelling, as well as other ocular or orbital
pathology.
оАБ OCT allows diоАЪerentiation between choroidal, chorioretinal
and retinal folds.
оАБ FAF eоАЪectively demonstrates the folds and may show associated atrophy. If this is marked the appearance can be mistaken
for angioid streaks.
оАБ FA shows hyperоАЬuorescent crests as a result of increased background choroidal оАЬuorescence showing through the stretched
and thinned RPE and hypoоАЬuorescent troughs due to blockage of choroidal оАЬuorescence by the compressed and thickened
RPE.

оАБ Supplementary imaging. Ultrasound, computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance (MR) scanning of the orbits
or brain may be indicated. In elevated intracranial pressure

HYPOTONY MACULOPATHY

Introduction

Maculopathy may occur in eyes developing hypotony, deоАЭned
as IOP less than 6 mmHg. оАЮe most common cause is excessive
drainage following glaucoma оАЭltration surgery, particularly when
antimetabolites have been used at the time of surgery (see Ch.
11). Other causes include trauma (cyclodialysis cleоАЩ, penetrating
injury), chronic uveitis (by directly impairing ciliary body function and by tractional ciliary body detachment due to cyclitic
membrane) and retinal detachment. Systemic causes of hypotony
(usually bilateral) include dehydration, hyperglycaemia in uncontrolled diabetes, uraemia and treatment with hyperosmotic agents
or carbonic anhydrase inhibitors. оАЮe development of secondary
choroidal eоАЪusion may act to perpetuate the hypotony. With time
the hypotonous process can itself lead to further damage, including sclerosis and atrophy of ciliary processes. Prolonged severe
hypotony may lead to phthisis bulbi and loss of the eye. Treatment
to restore normal IOP is directed according to cause.

Diagnosis

оАБ VA is variably aоАЪected. Delayed normalization of IOP may
result in permanent visual impairment, though substantial
improvement has been reported following reversal of hypotony aоАЩer several years.
оАБ Fine retinal folds radiating outwards from the foveola, which
may also show CMO (Fig. 14.56).
оАБ Chorioretinal folds may radiate outwards in branching fashion from the optic disc. оАЮese are due to scleral collapse with
resultant chorioretinal redundancy.

{ оАЮe spot evolves to a sharply deоАЭned foveolar defect with
irregular borders (Fig. 14.57B), or a lamellar hole.
оАБ OCT shows foveal thinning with a focal hyporeоАЬective area,
the depth of which correlates with the extent of visual acuity
loss but which generally includes the photoreceptor inner and
outer segments.
оАБ Treatment is not available.
оАБ Prognosis is good in most cases with improvement of visual
acuity to normal or near-normal levels within 6 months. In a
minority, signiоАЭcantly reduced vision persists.

оАБ Miscellaneous. A variety of other features may be present,
related both to aetiology and secondary eоАЪects of hypotony,
including a shallow anterior chamber, choroidal eоАЪusion,
cataract, corneal decompensation, optic disc oedema, uveitis, wound leak or an unexpected оАЭltering bleb adjacent to a
wound (e.g. following cataract surgery), cyclodialysis cleоАЩ on
gonioscopy and retinal detachment.
оАБ Ultrasound biomicroscopy may show a cyclitic membrane
or cyclodialysis cleft if there is clinical reason to suspect
this.
оАБ OCT should be undertaken using horizontal and vertical rasters and will show chorioretinal folds.
оАБ B-scan ultrasonography will demonstrate choroidal eоАЪusions.
оАБ A-scan ultrasonography or interferometry may show reduced
axial length.

SOLAR RETINOPATHY

оАБ Pathogenesis. Retinal injury results from photochemical
eоАЪects of solar radiation aоАЩer directly or indirectly viewing the
sun (eclipse retinopathy).
оАБ Presentation is within a few hours of exposure with impairment of central vision and a small central scotoma.
оАБ Signs

{ VA is variable according to severity.

{ A small yellow or red foveolar spot (Fig. 14.57A) that fades
within a few weeks.

TORPEDO MACULOPATHY

This is a rare condition of unknown cause, which is characterized by a single hypopigmented torpedo-shaped lesion
temporal to the fovea in asymptomatic young people (Fig.
14.58A). The average age at diagnosis is 8 years, although it
can be seen in infants. The visual acuity is not affected, but a
small scotoma may be found on visual field testing. It is typically unilateral and non-progressive, with a good long-term
prognosis. Fundus autofluorescence shows a hyperfluorescent
border surrounding a hypofluorescent area (Fig. 14.58B). OCT
shows attenuation of the outer retinal structures and conservation of the inner retinal layers (Fig. 14.58C). No treatment is
required.

FOCAL CHOROIDAL EXCAVATION

Focal choroidal excavation (FCE) is a relatively common condition in
which one or more areas of macular choroidal excavation are detected
in one or both eyes of a patient, typically middle-aged and possibly
more commonly Eastern Asian, without a history of ocular disease
known to produce choroidal thinning. Vision is variably aоАЪected and
may be compromised by complications such as MNV, CSR and PCV.
Overlying pigment epithelial disturbance or small yellowish-white
deposits, sometimes vitelliform, are oоАЩen seen clinically. On OCT,
in вАШconformingвАЩ FCE the overlying RPE and inner-segment/outersegment junction follows the outwards indentation of the excavation
(Fig. 14.59). By contrast, in вАШnon-conformingвАЩ FCE the photoreceptor
layers are disrupted and appear to be separated from the RPE.

DOME-SHAPED MACULA

This is a rare condition seen in myopic adults. Approximately
half are highly myopic and in half the condition involves both

Fig. 14.58 Torpedo maculopathy. оАЭAоАЮ Clinical appearance; оАЭBоАЮ FAF of оАЭAоАЮ showing hypoautoоАЮuorescence in the centre and a halo of hyperautoоАЮuorescence at the edge of the lesion; оАЭCоАЮ

of ocular disease. The cause is not known, but the condition
may represent a type of localized staphyloma. The diagnosis
is made on OCT analysis where a serous macular detachment
is often found. The key to the diagnosis is to undertake vertical and horizontal linear OCT scans, as the ridge may only
be visible in one median (Fig. 14.60). In approximately 25%
the subretinal fluid disappears spontaneously in time. There
is no treatment; in particular anti-VEGF injections have no
effect. The vision usually does not deteriorate despite the OCT
appearance.

LOW VISUAL AIDS

Fig. 14.59 Vertical OCT scan of focal choroidal excavation.
(Courtesy of P Charbel Issa.)

Part of the effective management of patients with poor vision
as a consequence of bilateral macular disease is the use of
low visual aids. Skilled instruction on their use is key to
their successful implementation. All aids are based on simple

624 Charles Bonnet Syndrome (CBS)

A

B

Fig. 14.60 OCT of dome-shaped maculopathy. оАЭAоАЮ Horizontal scan; оАЭBоАЮ vertical scan showing
the dome and subretinal оАЮuid. (Courtesy of P Charbel Issa.)

magnification and good illumination. There are five basic types
of low visual aid:
оАБ Non-optical devices (adaptive aids), such as large print books,
talking clocks and оАЬuid level indicators.
оАБ Tints and оАЭlters. Grey lenses reduce light intensity and yellow
lenses improve contrast. An anti-reоАЬective coating on spectacles can be used to reduce glare.
оАБ Convex lens aids such as magniоАЭers are used for most patients.
Hand-held magniоАЭers are oоАЩen helpful, particularly if the illumination is good (Fig. 14.61A and B). оАЮe main advantage of
spectacle-mounted and dome magniоАЭers is that both hands are
free. A focussed image is always provided at a set distance, but
the amount of magniоАЭcation is limited and distortion at the
edges may occur.
оАБ Telescopes. оАЮese can be focussed from distance to near, but
have the disadvantage of a small and shallow visual оАЭeld.
оАБ Electronic devices and portable video magniоАЭers such as
reading machines, image scanners and closed-circuit television. оАЮese options are expensive but allow signiоАЭcant

magniоАЭcation (from 1.5√Ч to 45√Ч), with adjustable font sizes
(Fig. 14.61C and D).

CHARLES BONNET SYNDROME (CBS)

оАЮis syndrome can occur in some people with poor sight and consists of three features: visual hallucinations, ocular pathology and
intact cognition.
оАБ Demographic. CBS tends to happen in elderly people who
have lost most or all of their vision in both eyes and occurs in
up to 50% of all people with severe macular degeneration. It
may occur in young people and in those with other ocular disease (e.g. retinitis pigmentosa, glaucoma). оАЮe symptoms are
not caused by mental illness, dementia or medication.
оАБ Hallucinations. оАЮose aоАЪected are usually aware that the hallucinations are not real. оАЮe hallucinations are a consequence
of the visual association areas of the brain not receiving as
much information as usual (deaоАЪerentation), leading to the
brain responding by оАЭlling in the gaps with stored images.

оАБ Symptoms. оАЮere are two main types of hallucination associated with this syndrome: (a) simple repeated patterns taking

landscapes (Fig. 14.62B). Based on their frequency, the hallucinations can be classiоАЭed as episodic, periodic or continuous.

оАБ Management. оАЮere is no cure for the condition. оАЮe key to
the management is to explain and to reassure the patient that
the hallucinations will become less pronounced in time. Preliminary research suggests that inhibitory transcranial direct
stimulation using a weak electrical current may reduce the frequency of symptoms.

628 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

General

оАЯe inherited retinal degenerations (вАШretinal dystrophiesвАЩ) are a
group of disorders that commonly exert their major eоАЮect on the
retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)вАУphotoreceptor complex and
the choriocapillaris to cause a range of visual impairment. оАЯe
most common example is retinitis pigmentosa. Hereditary retinal diseases are now the leading cause of blindness certiоАЭcation
in the working-age population (age 16вАУ64 years) in the UK. Some
inherited retinal degenerations manifest in early childhood while
others do not present until later in life. Isolated dystrophies have
features conоАЭned to the eye, whilst syndromic dystrophies are part
of a wider disease process that also aоАЮects tissues elsewhere in the
body. In a patient with an inherited retinal degeneration, genetic
sequencing should be undertaken to determine the exact causative
gene, as mutations in a number of genes can cause the same clinical
phenotype (genetic heterogeneity) and diоАЮerent phenotypes can
be caused by the same gene (phenotypical heterogeneity). Mutations in more than 250 genes have been linked to inherited retinal
degenerations and a table of genes associated with these conditions
can be found online (https://sph.uth.edu/retnet/). IdentiоАЭcation of
the causative gene allows the identiоАЭcation of patients who might
beneоАЭt from novel therapies, although this may only be possible
within a clinical trial setting.

TIP Mutations in diоАЮerent genes (genetic heterogeneity) can
cause the same clinical phenotype in an individual with an
inherited retinal degeneration.

Anatomy

оАЯere are two types of retinal photoreceptor:
оАБ оАЯe rods are the most numerous (120 million) and are of the
densest concentration outside the macular region. оАЯey are
most sensitive in dim illumination and are responsible for
night, motion sense and peripheral vision. If rod dysfunction
occurs earlier or is more severe than cone dysfunction, it will
result initially in poor night vision (nyctalopia). Rod and cone
loss results in loss of visual оАЭeld.
оАБ оАЯe cones are far fewer in number (6 million) and are concentrated at the fovea. оАЯere are three types of cones: L (long;
the commonest) responding to red wavelengths of light; M
(medium) responding to yellow-green wavelengths; and S
(short, only about 2% of the total) responding to blue wavelengths. оАЯey are most sensitive in bright light and mediate day
vision, colour vision, central and оАЭne vision. Cone dysfunction
therefore results in poor central vision, impairment of colour
vision (dyschromatopsia) and occasionally problems with day
vision (hemeralopia).
оАБ Photoreceptor structure. Each photoreceptor consists of an
outer segment which is the light-sensitive portion of the cell
and an inner segment which is the manufacturing portion of
the cell, containing mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum
(Fig. 15.1). оАЯese segments are joined by the cilium. In each

RPE
Fig. 15.1 Photoreceptor structure. (RPE = Retinal pigment
epithelium.)

rod outer segment there are about 1000 separate discs, while in
the cones the discs are joined to a boundary membrane with an
aperture that allows the discs to be in contact with the extracellular space and therefore with each other. Photopigments
exist as transmembrane proteins within the discs. New discs
are formed in the region of the cilium and older discs are shed
from the tips of the outer segments and undergo phagocytosis
by the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE). For rods the discs are
shed in packets at the onset of morning light. оАЯe process is
slower for cones and occurs at night.

Inheritance

All inherited retinal degenerations are caused by genetic variants,
most of which are inherited and a few of which occur de novo. A
new mutation (allelic variant) can occur in an individual and can
subsequently be passed to future generations.
оАБ Autosomal dominant (AD) conditions oоАЬen exhibit variable
expressivity and tend to have a later onset and milder course
than recessive disorders.
оАБ Recessive conditions may be autosomal (AR) or X-linked.
оАЯey generally have an earlier onset and a more severe course
than AD conditions. In some cases, female carriers of X-linked
conditions show characteristic fundus оАЭndings.
оАБ X-linked dominant conditions are very rare and are usually
lethal in boys (e.g. Aicardi syndrome).
оАБ Mitochondrial DNA is inherited solely via the maternal line.
Inherited retinal degenerations associated with mitochondrial
DNA variants oоАЬen occur as part of a wider systemic disease.
Heteroplasmy describes the presence of more than one type of
mitochondrial DNA within a cell. Because cells contain thousands of mitochondria, a low level of heteroplasmy is common
in normal individuals. оАЯe presence and severity of an inherited retinal degeneration depends on the proportion of faulty
mitochondria. оАЯe manifestations of mitochondrial-inherited
ocular disease usually do not appear before adulthood, as
many cell divisions are required for a cell to accumulate
enough mutant mitochondria to cause symptoms.

CHAPTER

Inherited retinal degenerations 629
#### 15

оАБ Digenic conditions are due to the combined eоАЮect of mutations in two diоАЮerent genes.

TIP Mutations in the same gene can cause diоАЮerent
phenotypes (phenotypical heterogeneity) in an individual with
an inherited retinal degeneration.

ClassiоАЯcation

As well as division by inheritance pattern, dystrophies can be
considered as generalized, in which the clinical eоАЮects involve the
entire fundus (rod-cone or cone-rod, depending on which photoreceptor type is predominantly dysfunctional), or central (local,
macula) in which only the macula is aоАЮected. оАЯey can also be classiоАЭed according to the element that is the focus of the pathological
process (e.g. photoreceptors, RPE or choroid) and by whether they
are stationary (non-progressive) or progressive.

INVESTIGATION

AоАЬer taking a careful history (especially a detailed family history)
and undertaking a clinical examination, the main investigations that
are usually needed to make a diagnosis of inherited retinal degeneration are OCT and fundus autoоАЫuorescence (FAF). Supplementary investigations include electrophysiological and genetic testing.

Electroretinography
Introduction

оАЯe electroretinogram (ERG) measures retinal electrical activity.
When stimulated by light of adequate intensity, ionic оАЫow вАУ principally sodium and potassium вАУ is induced in or out of cells such
that a potential is generated. оАЯis retinal electrical activity can be
recorded with an electrode placed at or near the cornea and a reference electrode placed on the forehead. оАЯe potential between the
two electrodes is then ampliоАЭed and displayed (Fig. 15.2). оАЯe normal ERG is predominantly biphasic (Fig. 15.3A):
оАБ оАЯe a-wave is an initial fast corneal-negative deоАЫection generated by the photoreceptors.
оАБ оАЯe b-wave is a subsequent slower positive large-amplitude
deоАЫection. Although it is generated from M√Љller and bipolar
cells, it is directly dependent on functional photoreceptors and
its magnitude makes it a convenient measure of photoreceptor
integrity. Its amplitude is measured from the a-wave trough to
the b-wave peak. оАЯe b-wave is enhanced with dark adaptation
and increased light stimulus.
оАБ Reduced ratio. As a general principle, a reduced ratio between
a- and b-waves indicates inner retinal dysfunction.
оАБ оАЯe c-wave is a third (negative) deоАЫection generated by the
RPE and photoreceptors.
оАБ Latency is the interval to the commencement of the a-wave
aоАЬer the stimulus is applied.
оАБ Implicit time is the interval from the stimulus to the b-wave
peak.
Electroretinography may be used for the diagnosis of a range of
diоАЮerent retinal disorders on the basis of characteristic patterns of

Reference electrode

Amplifier

Active electrode

a-wave

b-wave

Fig. 15.2 Principles of electroretinography.

change, as well as monitoring of disease progress in dystrophies
and other conditions such as some forms of uveitis (e.g. birdshot
retinochoroiditis) and drug toxicity (e.g. hydroxychloroquine).

TIP High-quality retinal imaging techniques and speciоАЭc
genetic testing in a patient with an inherited retinal
degeneration is more commonly used than electroretinography.

Full-оАЯeld ERG

Guidelines for the preparation of a patient who requires an ERG
and the minimum set of responses that need to be obtained have
been published by the International Society for Clinical Electrophysiology of Vision (ISCEV-2022 update). A standard full-оАЭeld
ERG (Fig. 15.3B) consists of six recordings and is taken during
diоАЮuse stimulation of the entire retinal area. It is used to assess
generalized retinal disorders, but may not detect localized pathology. оАЯe оАЭrst three recordings are elicited aоАЬer a minimum of 20
minutes of dark adaptation (scotopic) and the last three aоАЬer 10
minutes of adaptation to moderately bright diоАЮuse illumination
(photopic). Several responses are usually averaged to improve the
signal-to-noise ratio. It may be diоАЪcult to dark-adapt children for
30 minutes and therefore dim light (mesopic) conditions can be
utilized to evoke predominantly rod-mediated responses to lowintensity white or blue light stimuli.

{ Rod responses are elicited with a very dim оАЫash of 0.01
cdвАҐs/m [2], resulting in a small b-wave and a barely visible
a-wave.

{ Combined rod and cone responses are elicited with a very
bright white оАЫash, resulting in a prominent a-wave and
b-wave.

{ Oscillatory potentials are elicited by using a bright оАЫash
and changing the recording parameters. оАЯe oscillatory

wavelets occur on the ascending limb of the b-wave and
are generated by cells in the inner retina.
оАБ Photopic ERG

{ Cone responses are elicited with a single bright оАЫash,
resulting in an a- and a b-wave with subsequent small
oscillations.

{ Cone оАЫicker is used to isolate cones by using a оАЫickering
light stimulus at a frequency of 30 Hz to which rods cannot
respond. It provides a measure of the amplitude and implicit
time of the cone b-wave. Cone responses can be elicited
in normal eyes up to 50 Hz, aоАЬer which point individual
responses are no longer recordable (вАШcritical оАЫicker fusionвАЩ).

Multifocal ERG

Multifocal ERG is a method of producing topographical maps of
retinal function (Fig. 15.4). оАЯe stimulus is scaled for variation in
photoreceptor density across the retina. At the fovea, where the
density of receptors is high, a lesser stimulus is employed than in
the periphery where receptor density is lower. As with conventional ERG, many types of measurements can be made. Both the
amplitude and timing of the troughs and peaks can be measured
and reported, and the information can be summarized in the form
of a three-dimensional plot which resembles the hill of vision.

Focal ERG

Focal (foveal) ERG can be used to assess macular disease, but is
rarely utilized.

Pattern ERG

A similar stimulus to that used in visual evoked potentials (see
Ch. 19), pattern reversal, is used to target ganglion cell function,

Fig. 15.4 Multifocal electroretinogram.

typically in order to detect subtle optic neuropathy and early glaucoma (see Fig. 11.25A and B).

ElectroоАЯoculography

оАЯe electro-oculogram (EOG) measures the standing potential
between the electrically positive cornea and the electrically negative back of the eye (Fig. 15.5). It reоАЫects the activity of the RPE and
the photoreceptors. оАЯe ISCEV (www.iscev.org) has determined
standards for clinical EOG. In general, diоАЮuse or widespread disease of the RPE is needed to signiоАЭcantly aоАЮect the response. оАЯe
EOG is always abnormal if there is widespread photoreceptor dysfunction. As there is much variation in EOG amplitude in normal
subjects, the result is calculated by dividing the maximal height
of the potential in the light (вАШlight peakвАЩ) by the minimal height of
the potential in the dark (вАШdark troughвАЩ). оАЯis is expressed as a ratio
(Arden ratio) with a normal value that is typically more than 1.7.

Dark adaptometry

Dark adaptation (DA) is the phenomenon by which the visual system adapts to decreased illumination and evaluation of this is particularly useful in the investigation of nyctalopia. оАЯe retina is exposed
to an intense light for a time suоАЪcient to bleach 25% or more of the
rhodopsin in the retina. Following this, normal rods are insensitive
to light and cones respond only to very bright stimuli. Subsequent
recovery of light sensitivity can be monitored by placing the subject
in the dark and periodically presenting spots of light of varying intensity in the visual оАЭeld and asking the subject if they are perceived. оАЯe
threshold at which the subject just perceives a light is recorded, the
оАЫashes repeated at regular intervals and the increased sensitivity of
the eye to light plotted: the dark adaptation curve (Fig. 15.6).
оАБ оАЯe cone branch of the curve represents the initial 5вАУ10
minutes of darkness during which cone sensitivity rapidly
improves. оАЯe rod photoreceptors are also recovering, but
more slowly during this time.
оАБ оАЯe вАШrod-coneвАЩ break normally occurs aоАЬer 7вАУ10 minutes
when cones achieve their maximum sensitivity and the rods
become perceptibly more sensitive than cones.
оАБ оАЯe rod branch of the curve is slower and represents the continuation of improvement of rod sensitivity. AоАЬer 15вАУ30 minutes, the fully dark-adapted rods allow the subject to perceive
a spot of light over 100 times dimmer than would be possible
with cones alone. If the оАЫashes are focused onto the foveola
(where rods are absent), only a rapid segment corresponding
to cone adaptation is recorded.

Genetic testing

оАЯis should be undertaken in association with a geneticist who has
access to a laboratory that can undertake the necessary tests.

Fig. 15.6 Dark adaptation curve.

оАБ Single gene testing is used in patients with a family history of
a known genetic mutation, in order to conоАЭrm the molecular
diagnosis.
оАБ Sequencing using targeted panels of all known inherited retinal
degeneration genes has been developed and yields high diagnostic rates. Once a genetic defect is identiоАЭed, Sanger single
gene sequencing can be undertaken to conоАЭrm the mutation.

TIP A monogenetic retinal degeneration can develop at
any age and although the genetic abnormality can often be
determined using modern techniques, a substantial number of
patients with an inherited retinal degeneration remain without a
genetic diagnosis.

GENERALIZED PHOTORECEPTOR
DYSTROPHIES

Retinitis pigmentosa

Introduction

оАБ DeоАЮnition. Retinitis pigmentosa (RP), or pigmented retinal
degeneration, denotes a clinically and genetically diverse group of
inherited diоАЮuse retinal degenerative diseases initially predominantly aоАЮecting the rod photoreceptors, with subsequent less
marked degeneration of cones and retinal pigment epithelium.
оАБ Prevalence. It is the most common hereditary retinal degeneration, with a prevalence of 1:3000 to 1:5000. оАЯe age of onset,
rate of progression, eventual visual loss and associated ocular
features are frequently related to the mode of inheritance.
However, the severity of disease at a given age can vary in different patients with the same gene defect.
оАБ Inheritance. RP may occur as a sporadic disorder or can be
inherited in an AD, AR or X-linked pattern. Sporadic cases
are also AD, AR or X-linked, but they have no other known
aоАЮected family members. оАЯe reasons for this include: (a)
incomplete penetrance, (b) AR inheritance in small families,
(c) non-paternity, (d) death before onset of the disease.

632 Generalized Photoreceptor Dystrophies

оАБ Genetic defect. Mutations in more than 100 gene loci are
known to cause non-syndromic RP. Genetic defects that cause
RP can be divided into the following: (a) those that aоАЮect the
phototransduction cascade, (b) the retinoid cycle, (c) photoreceptor structure, (d) other biological functions of the photoreceptor and RPE. Despite advances in genetics, about half of
aоАЮected individuals have no known molecular genetic abnormality. Most cases of AD-inherited RP are due to mutations of
the rhodopsin gene, which is important in the phototransduction cascade. X-linked is the least common inheritance pattern
and in 90% of cases is due to mutations in the RPGR gene,
which encodes a protein that is localized in the connecting
cilium of rod photoreceptors.
оАБ Syndromic RP. In 20вАУ30% of cases, RP, oоАЬen atypical (see
below), is associated with a systemic disorder. оАЯese conditions are usually of AR or mitochondrial inheritance.
оАБ Leber congenital amaurosis. Around 5% of RP belongs to
the very early-onset severe type grouped together as Leber
congenital amaurosis (see separate topic).

Diagnosis

оАЯe classic triad of оАЭndings comprises bone-spicule retinal pigmentation, arteriolar attenuation and вАШwaxyвАЩ disc pallor. оАЯere is
oоАЬen signiоАЭcant symmetry in the retinal appearance of the two
eyes. оАЯere may be a family history of RP and a pedigree should
be prepared.
оАБ Symptoms

{ Nyctalopia and dark adaptation diоАЪculties are frequently
presenting symptoms, but peripheral visual problems may
be noticed.

{ Reduced central vision tends to be a later feature, but can
be involved earlier if a cataract develops.

{ Photopsia is not uncommon.
оАБ Signs

{ Visual acuity may be normal. Contrast sensitivity may be
aоАЮected at an earlier stage than visual acuity.

{ Bilateral mid-peripheral intraretinal perivascular вАШbonespiculeвАЩ pigmentary changes and RPE atrophy associated
with arteriolar narrowing (Fig. 15.7A and B).

{ оАЯere is a gradual increase in density of the pigment with
anterior and posterior spread and a tessellated fundus
appearance develops due to unmasking of large choroidal
vessels (Fig. 15.7C).

{ Peripheral pigmentation may become severe, with marked
arteriolar narrowing and disc pallor (Fig. 15.7D).

{ оАЯe macula may show atrophy, epiretinal membrane
(ERM) formation and cystoid macular oedema (CMO).

оАБ RP sine pigmento is characterized by an absence or paucity of
pigment accumulation and is not a separate form of the disease.
Every patient with RP is initially вАШsine pigmentoвАЩ, as the bone
spicules only develop later, secondary to photoreceptor degeneration. Functional manifestations are similar to typical RP.
оАБ Complications include posterior subcapsular cataract (common in all forms of RP), open-angle glaucoma (3%) and posterior vitreous detachment.
оАБ Prognosis. AD has the best prognosis. X-linked is the most
severe form with reduction of central vision to 6/60 or less by
the оАЭоАЬh decade of life. AR is intermediate in severity
оАБ Investigation. Investigation for infectious conditions that
mimic RP (e.g. syphilis), is sometimes warranted.

{ FAF shows an abnormal perimacular ring of
hyper-autoоАЫuorescence (increased RPE lipofuscin
secondary to RPE dysfunction) and diоАЮuse, patchy hypoautoоАЫuorescence in the mid-periphery (Fig. 15.9A). Using
FAF as a stand-alone test, RP can be distinguished from a
normal fundus in 95% of cases.

{ OCT contributes to the analysis of macular involvement
in RP and has prognostic value. Macular oedema can be
detected in about 15% and macular atrophy in about 40%
of patients with RP.

{ Perimetry initially demonstrates small mid-peripheral
scotomas that gradually coalesce and may deteriorate to
leave a tiny island of residual central vision (Fig. 15.9B)
that may subsequently be extinguished. Microperimetry is
useful for central visual assessment.

{ Full-оАЭeld ERG is a sensitive diagnostic test, but is rarely
needed. In early disease it shows reduced scotopic rod
and combined responses (Fig. 15.10). Photopic responses
reduce with progression and eventually the ERG becomes
extinguished.

{ Dark adaptation is prolonged and measuring this using the
Dark Adaptation Test may be useful in equivocal early cases.

{ Genetic analysis may identify the particular mutation
responsible in an individual patient and facilitate genetic
counselling, including the risk of transmission to oоАЮspring.

TIP Identifying the precise mutation responsible for an
inherited retinal degeneration aids genetic counselling and can
facilitate enrolment in a clinical trial.

Management

оАБ Treatment. No speciоАЭc treatment is commercially available,
but gene therapy is showing promise in early trials. Luxturna
is an approved and commercially available gene therapy for
patients with RPE 65 mutations.
оАБ Annual follow-up is recommended in order to detect treatable
vision-threatening complications, provide support and maintain contact in case of therapeutic innovation.
оАБ Cataract surgery is oоАЬen beneоАЭcial.
оАБ Low-vision aid provision, rehabilitation and social service
access when appropriate.
оАБ Smoking should be avoided.

{ Myopia is common.

{ Optic disc drusen occur more frequently in patients with
RP.
оАБ Female carriers of the X-linked form may have normal fundi
or show a golden-metallic (вАШtapetalвАЩ) reоАЫex at the macula (Fig.
15.8A) and/or small peripheral patches of bone-spicule pigmentation. оАЯe most striking feature of the carrier state is
centrifugal hyperautoоАЫuorescent lines on FAF imaging (Fig.
15.8B).

оАБ Light exposure. Some forms of RP may be modiоАЭed by reducing light exposure and therefore blocking short-wavelength
light below approximately 550nm might be beneоАЭcial. Indoor
amber spectacles blocking to 511вАУ527 nm may improve contrast sensitivity and reduce glare.
оАБ CMO in RP may respond to oral acetazolamide and sometimes topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.
оАБ Dietary supplementation. оАЯe use of high-doses of vitamin
A has been explored, but there is no consensus on its potential beneоАЭcial eоАЮect, which might be gene-dependent. Caution is advisable in light of potential adverse eоАЮects. Several
other supplements and drugs (e.g. lutein, zeaxanthin, calciumchannel blockers) have shown potential beneоАЭts, but their eоАЪcacy and safety in RP have not been fully ascertained.
оАБ Retinotoxic medication should be avoided or used with caution. Examples include isotretinoin and other retinoids, phenothiazines, hydroxychloroquine, tamoxifen and vigabatrin.

TIP Therapeutic options that can be helpful in selected cases
of retinitis pigmentosa include phacoemulsiоАЭcation with IOL
implantation and medical treatment for macular oedema.

Atypical retinitis pigmentosa
Introduction

оАЯe term вАШatypical RPвАЩ has conventionally been used to group
together heterogeneous disorders clinically having features in
common with typical pigmentary retinal degeneration. оАЯe precise
conditions included within this category vary between authors.

Atypical RP associated with a systemic disorder
(syndromic RP)

оАБ Usher syndrome (AR, genetically heterogeneous) accounts
for about 5% of all cases of profound deafness in children and
about half of all cases of combined deafness and blindness.
оАЯere are three major types, ranging from type I (MYOA7
associated) (75%), which features profound congenital sensorineural deafness and severe RP with an extinguished ERG in
the оАЭrst decade, to type III (2%), with progressive hearing loss,
vestibular dysfunction and relatively late-onset RP. Systemic
features are widely variable and can include premature ageing,
skeletal anomalies, mental handicap and early demise. оАЯere
is oоАЬen a вАШsalt and pepperвАЩ pattern of retinal pigmentation
and optic atrophy. A study assessing the beneоАЭt of inserting
the MYOA7-associated gene is being undertaken in individuals with this genetic defect. оАЯe severe deafness that typically
occurs in type 1 Usher syndrome can be treated with a cochlear
implant (Fig. 15.11A).
оАБ BassenвАУKornzweig syndrome or abetalipoproteinaemia (AR)
is a condition in which fat and fat-soluble vitamin (A, D, E, K)
absorption is dysfunctional. оАЯere is a failure to thrive in infancy,
with the development of severe spinocerebellar ataxia. A blood
оАЭlm shows вАШthornyвАЩ red cells (acanthocytosis вАУ Fig. 15.11B).

оАЯe fundus exhibits scattered white dots followed by RP-like
changes developing towards the end of the оАЭrst decade. Other
features include ptosis, ophthalmoplegia, strabismus and nystagmus. Vitamin supplementation and a low-fat diet should be
implemented.
оАБ Refsum disease (AR) is rare and consists of genetically and
clinically distinct infantile and adult forms. Phytanic acid
accumulates throughout the body, with substantial and varied
skin (Fig. 15.11C), neurological and visceral features. Retinal changes may be similar to RP or take on a salt and pepper appearance and there may be other ocular features such as
cataract and optic atrophy A low phytanic acid diet can retard
progression.
оАБ BardetвАУBiedl syndrome (most cases AR; genetically heterogenous) is rare and occurs as a consequence of mutations
in the genes responsible for proteins involved in non-motor
cilia function. It can encompass a range of systemic abnormalities including polydactyly (Fig. 15.11D), dysfunction of
the kidneys and mental handicap. Visual symptoms include

nyctalopia, visual оАЭeld constriction and later, loss of visual
acuity. оАЯere is typically a bullвАЩs-eye maculopathy due to conerod dystrophy and less frequently RP. Almost 80% have severe
changes by the age of 20 years.

Sector retinitis pigmentosa

Sector (sectoral) RP (AD) is characterized by involvement of inferior quadrants only (Fig. 15.12). Progression is slow and many
cases are apparently stationary. Unilateral RP can also occur.

KearnsвАУSayre syndrome

Kearns-Sayre syndrome is rare and is most commonly due to single deletions in mitochondrial DNA. оАЯe retinal disease is not an
atypical form of RP. Patients typically present within the оАЭrst two
decades of life with chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia,
bilateral ptosis (see Fig. 19.101A) and subsequently develop a salt
and pepper retinal appearance, most striking at the macula (see
Fig. 19.101B). Cardiac abnormalities and other systemic problems
may occur.

636 Generalized Photoreceptor Dystrophies

Leber congenital amaurosis

Leber congenital amaurosis (AR, genetically heterogeneous) is a
severe inherited retinal degeneration aоАЮecting rods and cones. It usually has a recessive inheritance pattern and is genetically heterogenous.
Variants in three of the at least 28 diоАЮerent genes known to be responsible for this disease are inherited in an AD manner. Mutation in the
RPE65 gene is one of the major causes. Some cases are association
with mental handicap, deafness, epilepsy, central nervous system and
renal anomalies, skeletal malformations and endocrine dysfunction.
оАБ Presentation is with blindness at birth or early infancy,
associated with roving eye movements or nystagmus and
photoaversion.
оАБ Signs are variable but may include:

{ Absent or diminished pupillary light reоАЫexes.

{ оАЯe fundi may be normal in early life apart from mild arteriolar narrowing.

{ Initially mild peripheral pigmentary retinopathy (Fig.
Fig. 15.12 Sectoral retinitis pigmentosa. 15.13A), salt and pepper changes and, less frequently, yellow оАЫecks.

оАБ Treatment

{ Low visual aids, advice on schooling, rehabilitation and

{ A single subretinal injection of adeno-associated virus
(AAV)-mediated RPE65 gene therapy (Luxturna) has

of cases, responds to immunosuppressive treatment. Some
other genetic causes are currently subject to clinical trials.

to the preservation of an organized photoreceptor layer. Variants
of this gene (AR inheritance) are associated with a wide and complex range of phenotypes, the commonest of which is Leber congenital amaurosis in infants (10% of all cases), retinitis pigmentosa
in teens and cone-rod dystrophies at any age. Distinctive features
include the presence of nummular pigmented deposits and small
yellow or white dots (Fig. 15.15A). FAF shows circumferential
hyperautoоАЫuorescence in the macular region and alongside retinal arterioles. (Fig. 15.15B). OCT reveals preserved para-arteriolar
RPE and abnormally laminated, thickened retina (Fig. 15.15C).
оАЯere is no treatment, but CRB-1 gene therapy may oоАЮer help in
the future.

Cone and coneоАЯrod dystrophy
Introduction

Progressive cone and cone-rod dystrophies are a clinically and
genetically heterogenous group of inherited retinal diseases characterized by cone photoreceptor degeneration, which may be followed
by rod photoreceptor loss. оАЯe majority are AR, with some AD and
X-linked inheritance and mutations in at least 30 genes have been
implicated. As with other retinal dystrophies, the age of onset,
severity and rate of progression can vary substantially. AоАЮected
individuals typically present with progressive loss of central vision,
disturbance of colour vision and photophobia. оАЯe prognosis is
usually poor, with an eventual visual acuity of 6/60 or worse.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Gradual bilateral impairment of central and colour
vision, which may be accompanied by photophobia.
оАБ Signs. оАЯe features may evolve through the stages below.

Pigmented paravenous chorioretinal
atrophy

Pigmented paravenous chorioretinal atrophy (predominantly AD)
is usually asymptomatic and non-progressive. оАЯe ERG is normal. Paravenous bone-spicule pigmentation (Fig. 15.14) is seen,
together with sharply outlined zones of chorioretinal atrophy that
follow the course of the major retinal veins. Changes may also
encircle the optic disc. оАЯe optic disc and vascular calibre are usually normal.

CRB-1 associated retinal dystrophy

CRB-1 encodes a transmembrane protein with the main role of
maintaining the zonula adherens junctions between photoreceptors, M√Љller cells and the external limiting membrane and is key

{ оАЯe macula may be virtually normal or show non-speciоАЭc
central pigmentary changes or atrophy.

{ Golden sheen may be seen in X-linked disease (Fig.
15.16A).

{ A bullвАЩs-eye maculopathy is classically described, but is not
universal. Other causes of a bullвАЩs-eye appearance are given
in Table 15.1

{ Progressive RPE atrophy at the macula with eventual
atrophy (Fig. 15.16B).
оАБ Investigation

{ Colour vision: severe deuteronвАУtritan defect out of proportion to visual acuity in some patients.

{ OCT shows loss of the outer retinal layers in the macula
and foveal region (Fig. 15.17A). In advanced disease there
is complete atrophy of the macula.

{ FAF is oоАЬen the key diagnostic test, showing various annular patterns concentric with the fovea (Fig. 15.17B).

{ FA is never needed these days. However, if undertaken it
typically shows a round hyperоАЫuorescent window defect
with a hypoоАЫuorescent centre, resulting in a bullвАЩs-eye
appearance (Fig. 15.17C).

{ ERG: cone responses are subnormal, but rod responses are
preserved until late (Fig. 15.17D).

Treatment

Stargardt disease/fundus
оАЮavimaculatus

Introduction

Stargardt disease (juvenile macular dystrophy) and fundus flavimaculatus (FFM) are regarded as variants of the same disease
and together constitute the most common macular dystrophy
in adults and children. It is a common cause of central visual
loss in adults under the age of 50 years. The prevalence is 1 in
8000вАУ10 000 people. Three types are recognized: STGD1 (AR)
is the most common and is caused by mutations in the gene
ABCA4 (chromosome 1p), which affects the ability of the retina
to recycle the visual chromophore 11-cis-retinal. STGD3 (AD)
is caused by mutations in ELOVL4 and STGD4 (AD) is caused
by mutations in PROM1. Presentation is typically in childhood
or adolescence, but sometimes later. The prognosis is poor
and once the visual acuity drops below 6/12 it tends to worsen
rapidly.

642 Generalized Photoreceptor Dystrophies

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Gradual impairment of central vision occurs that
may be out of proportion to examination оАЭndings and manifests with diоАЪculty when reading. A quarter of childhoodonset STGDT1 do not have any retinal lesions at presentation
and malingering may be suspected, but OCT imaging soon
clariоАЭes this misconception.
оАБ Signs

{ оАЯe macula may initially be normal or show non-speciоАЭc
mottling and оАЭne оАЫecks (Fig. 15.18A), progressing to an
oval вАШsnail slimeвАЩ (Fig. 15.18B) or вАШbeaten-bronzeвАЩ appearance (Fig. 15.18C) and subsequently to atrophy (Fig.
15.18D) that may tend to a bullвАЩs-eye conоАЭguration. Rarely,
a small number of patients may develop macular neovascularization (MNV).

{ Numerous yellowвАУwhite round, oval or pisciform (оАЭshshaped) lesions at the level of the RPE. оАЯese may be conоАЭned to the posterior pole (Fig. 15.18E) or extend to the
mid-periphery (Fig. 15.18F).

{ New lesions develop as older ones become ill-deоАЭned and
atrophic.

оАБ Clinical trials have been initiated assessing the eоАЮect of: (a)
gene therapy using a lentiviral vector for ABCA4, (b) stem cell
therapy, (c) pharmatherapy to reduce lipofuscin accumulation,
(d) metformin taken by mouth.

Bietti crystalline corneoretinal
dystrophy

Bietti dystrophy (AR, CYP4V2 gene) is characterized by deposition of crystals in the retina and the superоАЭcial peripheral cornea.
It is a rare condition, but is more prevalent in Chinese and Japanese people than in other ethnic groups. оАЯe CYP4V2 enzyme is
involved in fatty acid oxidation, but the speciоАЭc function is not
understood and it is not known how this leads to the speciоАЭc signs
found in this condition. оАЯe rate of progression is variable. оАЯere
is no treatment.
оАБ Presentation. Young adults with slowly progressive visual loss
constitute the typical case.
оАБ Signs

{ SuperоАЭcial peripheral corneal crystals.

{ оАЯe structure and function of the parapapillary retina is
spared, but can be involved in late-stage severe disease.
оАБ Investigation

{ Visual оАЭelds show central loss (Fig. 15.19A) and microperimetry can accurately document progression.

{ FA: оАЯis has been superseded by OCT and FAF and is not
usually needed. оАЯe classic feature is a вАШdark choroidвАЩ due
to masking of background choroidal оАЫuorescence by lipofuscin accumulation in the RPE. оАЯe macula shows mixed
hyper- and hypoоАЫuorescence. Fresh оАЫecks show early
hypoоАЫuorescence due to blockage and late hyperоАЫuorescence due to staining and old оАЫecks show RPE window
defects (Fig. 15.19B).

{ Indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) shows hypoоАЫuorescent spots, oоАЬen more numerous than seen clinically.

{ FAF is key to the diagnosis in early cases and shows a
characteristic appearance with hyperautoоАЫuorescent оАЫecks
(Fig. 15.19C and E) and macular hypoautoоАЫuorescence.

{ OCT demonstrates outer retinal atrophy (Fig. 15.19D and F).

{ ERG: photopic is normal to subnormal, scotopic may be
normal.

{ EOG is commonly subnormal, especially in advanced
cases.

Familial benign оАЮeck retina

Familial benign оАЫeck retina (benign оАЫecked retina syndrome) is a
very rare AR disorder. Individuals with the condition are asymptomatic, so the condition is usually discovered by chance. Numerous diоАЮusely distributed yellowвАУwhite polymorphous lesions spare
the fovea and extend to the far periphery (Fig. 15.21). оАЯe оАЫecks
show autoоАЫuorescence and are probably composed of lipofuscin.
оАЯe ERG is normal and the prognosis is excellent.

Alport syndrome

Alport syndrome (predominantly X-linked) is caused by mutations
in several diоАЮerent genes, all of which encode particular forms of
type IV collagen, a major basement membrane component. It is
characterized by chronic renal failure and is oоАЬen associated with
high-tone sensorineural hearing loss by the age of 20. оАЯere are
scattered yellowish punctate оАЫecks in the perimacular area, which

{ Numerous оАЭne yellowвАУwhite crystals scattered throughout
the posterior fundus (Fig. 15.20A and B) are followed by
localized atrophy of the RPE and choriocapillaris at the
macula.

{ DiоАЮuse atrophy of the choriocapillaris subsequently develops, with a decrease in size and number of the crystals.

{ оАЯere is gradual conоАЫuence and expansion of the atrophic
areas into the periphery, leading to diоАЮuse chorioretinal
atrophy in end-stage disease.
оАБ Investigation

{ OCT demonstrates the crystalline deposits and macular
changes (Fig. 15.20C).

{ ERG is subnormal.

{ FA in moderate disease shows characteristic large hypoоАЫuorescent patches corresponding to choriocapillaris loss,
with intact overlying retinal vessels (Fig. 15.20D). оАЯe
patches become conоАЫuent over time.

TIP Stargardt disease is the most commonly inherited
macular dystrophy and is usually associated with mutations in
the ABCA4 gene.

Treatment

оАБ General measures should be considered as for RP. Protection
from excessive high-energy light exposure may be particularly
important.
оАБ Avoid vitamin A supplementation as it may accelerate lipofuscin accumulation.

are oоАЬen subtle (Fig. 15.22A), and larger peripheral оАЫecks, some
of which may become conоАЫuent, but rarely threaten vision (Fig.
