Congenital stationary night blindness
Introduction

Fig. 15.21 Familial benign оАЯeck retina.

Congenital stationary night blindness (CSNB) refers to a group
of disorders characterized by nyctalopia from birth, but nonprogressive retinal dysfunction. оАЯe fundus appearance may be
normal or abnormal. A number of genetic mutations have been
reported that result in abnormal phototransduction and postphototransduction transmission.

644 Generalized Photoreceptor Dystrophies

A B

C

Fig. 15.22 Alport syndrome. (A) Subtle perimacular оАЯecks; (B) peripheral оАЯecks; (C) infrared
image showing retinal оАЯecks and corresponding OCT showing temporal macular thinning
(arrow) and outer retinal disruption (arrowheads). (Courtesy of P Herrmann.)

With a normal fundus appearance

CSNB with a normal fundus appearance is sometimes classiоАЭed
into type 1 (complete) and type 2 (incomplete) forms that are generally due to mutations in diоАЮerent genes. оАЯe former is characterized by a complete absence of rod pathway function and essentially
normal cone function clinically and on ERG, the latter by impairment of both rod and cone function. Mutations in numerous genes
have been implicated, with X-linked, AD and AR inheritance patterns. оАЯe AD form is usually associated with normal visual acuity,
but many AR and X-linked patients have poor vision with nystagmus and oоАЬen signiоАЭcant myopia.

With an abnormal fundus appearance

оАБ Oguchi disease (AR). оАЯe fundus has an unusual goldenyellow colour in the light-adapted state (Fig. 15.23A), which
becomes normal aоАЬer prolonged dark adaptation (Mizuo or
MizuoвАУNakamura phenomenon вАУ Fig. 15.23B). Rod function
is absent aоАЬer 30 minutes of dark adaptation but recovers to a
near-normal level aоАЬer a long period of dark adaptation.

оАБ Fundus albipunctatus is an AR or AD condition that may
be the same entity as retinitis punctata albescens. оАЯe fundus
shows a multitude of subtle, tiny yellowвАУwhite spots at the posterior pole (Fig. 15.24A), sparing the fovea вАУ sometimes the
macula вАУ and extending to the periphery. In contrast to retinitis punctata albescens, the retinal blood vessels, optic disc,
peripheral оАЭelds and visual acuity are believed to remain normal, though the natural history is not yet absolutely deоАЭned.
FA shows mottled hyperоАЫuorescence, indicating depigmentation of the RPE. оАЯe ERG is variably abnormal; both cones and
rods may be aоАЮected.
оАБ Retinitis punctata albescens (AR or AD) is rare and is characterized by scattered whitish-yellow spots, most numerous at
the equator, usually sparing the macula and associated with
arteriolar attenuation (Fig. 15.24B). оАЯis form of оАЫecked retina may be a variant of fundus albipunctatus, as mutations in
RLBP1 (retinaldehyde-binding protein-1) have been identiоАЭed in both conditions. However, those identiоАЭed as having
retinitis punctata albescence tend to have a more serious and

progressive disease, while those with fundus albipunctatus
tend to have a stable disease with night blindness being the
major symptom. оАЯe relationship between these two conditions will become more transparent once the natural history
has been elucidated.

Congenital achromatopsia

оАЯis is a group of congenital disorders in which colours cannot
be perceived and visual acuity is reduced, particularly in brightly
illuminated environments (hemeralopia). Mutations aоАЮecting the
cone-speciоАЭc cyclic nucleotide gated (CNG) channel beta (B3)
and alpha (A3) subunits are responsible for about 50% and 25%
of complete achromatopsia, respectively. Animal models of the
disease have shown successful restoration of cone-mediated vision
following gene therapy for the CNGA3 and CNGB3 mutations.

Rod monochromatism (complete
achromatopsia)

In rod monochromatism (AR) visual acuity is poor, typically 6/60.
оАЯere is congenital nystagmus and photophobia. Colour vision is
totally absent, all colours appearing as shades of grey. оАЯe macula
usually appears normal, but may be hypoplastic. оАЯe photopic
(cone) ERG is abnormal and the scotopic may also be subnormal.

Blue cone monochromatism (incomplete
achromatopsia)

Blue cone monochromatism (X-linked) features only slightly subnormal acuity at 6/6вАУ6/9, but colour vision is completely absent.
Nystagmus and photophobia are not typical features. оАЯere is a
normal macula. оАЯe ERG is normal except for the absence of cone
responses to red and white light.

646 Macular Dystrophies

MACULAR DYSTROPHIES

Best vitelliform macular dystrophy

Introduction

Best vitelliform macular dystrophy (early- or juvenile-onset vitelliform macular dystrophy) is the second most common macular
dystrophy, aоАЬer Stargardt disease. It is due to allelic variation in
the bestrophin (BEST1) gene on chromosome 11q13. Bestrophin
is found on the plasma membrane of the RPE and functions as a
transmembrane ion channel. Inheritance is usually AD with variable penetrance and expressivity. Although there is signiоАЭcant
variability even in the same family, the prognosis is usually reasonably good until middle age, aоАЬer which visual acuity declines
in one or both eyes due to macular atrophy, scarring or MNV.

Diagnosis

оАБ Signs. оАЯere is gradual evolution through the following stages:

{ Pre-vitelliform is characterized by a subnormal EOG in
an asymptomatic infant or child with a normal fundus.

{ Vitelliform develops in infancy or early childhood and
does not usually impair vision. A round, sharply delineated (вАШsunny side up egg yolkвАЩ) macular lesion between 0.5
and 2 disc diameters in size develops within the RPE (Fig.
15.25A). оАЯe size of the lesions and stage of development
in the two eyes may be asymmetrical and sometimes only
one eye is involved initially. Occasionally the condition
may be extramacular and multiple.

{ Pseudohypopyon may occur when part of the lesion
regresses (Fig. 15.25B), oоАЬen at puberty.

{ Vitelliruptive. оАЯe lesion breaks up and visual acuity
drops (Fig. 15.25C).

{ Atrophic in which all pigment has disappeared leaving an
atrophic area of RPE.
оАБ Investigation

{ FAF: the yellowish material is intensely hyperautoоАЫuorescent. In the later atrophic stages hypoautoоАЫuorescent areas
supervene.

{ FA shows corresponding hypoоАЫuorescence due to masking
(Fig. 15.25D).

{ OCT shows material beneath, above and within the RPE
(Fig. 15.25E).

{ EOG is severely subnormal during all stages (Arden index
less than 1.5) and is also abnormal in carriers with clinically normal fundi.

may present with multifocal lesions. оАЯe relationship between
multifocal vitelliform lesions, juvenile vitelliform (Best) dystrophy and adult-onset vitelliform macular dystrophy is incompletely deоАЭned, though some cases of each are associated with
mutations in the same genes.

AdultоАЯonset vitelliform macular
dystrophy (foveomacular vitelliform
dystrophy)

When symmetrical yellow deposits that resemble Best disease
develop in the macula of older individuals (usually over 40
years), the term вАШadult-onset vitelliform macular (foveomacular) dystrophyвАЩ is used. In contrast to juvenile Best disease, the
foveal lesions are oоАЬen smaller. оАЯe lesions gradually increase
and then decrease in size over many years, leaving an area of
atrophic outer retina and RPE. оАЯis process is accompanied by
a loss of visual acuity. оАЯere is genetic heterogeneity including
mutations in the PRPH2, IMPG2 or the BEST1 gene. Vitelliform
macular lesions may also be observed in a wide range of other
conditions including acute idiopathic exudative polymorphous
vitelliform maculopathy and acute laser damage. оАЯis appearance may occur secondary to basal laminar drusen, reticular
pseudodrusen and chronic vitreomacular traction. Adult-onset
vitelliform macular dystrophy may also be grouped in the
phenotypical spectrum of pattern dystrophy (see next). оАЯere is
no treatment for the condition.
оАБ Symptoms. OоАЬen the condition is discovered by chance, but
may present in late middle or old age with decreased central
vision. оАЯe vision is oоАЬen reduced by one or more lines at
presentation and mild deterioration occurs subsequently. оАЯe
prognosis varies from case to case.
оАБ Signs. A round or oval slightly elevated yellowish subfoveal
deposit (Fig. 15.27A), generally smaller than the lesions of
Best disease, is seen in one or both eyes. оАЯere may be central
pigmentation and numerous associated drusen are present in
some cases. оАЯe material may persist, absorb, or break up and
disperse at a late stage, leaving atrophy of very variable severity.
Choroidal neovascularization sometimes supervenes.
оАБ Investigation

{ Fluorescein angiography is almost never needed, but can
occasionally be useful to show absence of MNV. In adultonset vitelliform macular dystrophy FA typically shows
central hypoоАЫuorescence surrounded by a small irregular
hyperоАЫuorescent ring. In the late phases the entire lesion
shows hyperоАЫuorescence (Fig. 15.27B).

{ FAF imaging shows intense hyperautoоАЫuorescence corresponding to the deposited material, which is typically much
more obvious than on clinical examination (Fig. 15.27C).
When atrophy supervenes, there is hypoautoоАЫuorescence.

{ OCT shows hyper-reоАЫective material between the RPE and
outer retina, similar to Best disease (Fig. 15.27D and see
Fig. 15.25E).

{ ERG and EOG are usually normal, indicating localized
retinal pathology.

Multifocal vitelliform lesions without
Best disease

Rarely, multifocal vitelliform lesions (Fig. 15.26), identical to
those in Best dystrophy but distributed around the macular vascular arcades and optic disc, may become manifest in adult life
and give rise to diagnostic problems. However, in these patients
the EOG is normal and the family history is negative. Occasionally genetically conоАЭrmed Best dystrophy with an abnormal EOG

648 Macular Dystrophies

Pattern dystrophy of the retinal
pigment epithelium

Pattern (patterned) dystrophy of the RPE encompasses several
clinical appearances which are autosomal dominantly inherited.
оАЯis group is associated with the accumulation of lipofuscin in the
RPE and manifests with yellow, whitish, grey or pigmented deposits at the macula in a variety of appearances. оАЯe most common
presenting symptom is reduced visual acuity or mild metamorphopsia in early adulthood or later, but many are asymptomatic.
оАЯe diоАЮerent clinical appearances are believed in many cases to
represent variable expression of a mutation in PRPH2, which
encodes peripherin-2, a protein involved in compaction of outer
segment discs of photoreceptors. Other genes and conditions can
give rise to a similar appearance and notably, a pattern dystrophylike appearance can occur in response to a range of pathogenic

MIDD. оАЯese dystrophies usually progress at a very slow rate and
the prognosis is generally good. оАЯere is no treatment. Based on
the pigment distribution the pattern dystrophies have been classiоАЭed into оАЮve prominent patterns. Importantly, one pattern may

оАБ ButterоАЭy-shaped pigment dystrophy: foveal yellow and

wing-like conformation (Fig. 15.28A). FA shows central and

оАБ Reticular dystrophy (Sj√ґgren): a network of pigmented lines

followed by reticular pigmentation that spreads to the periphery.
оАБ Multifocal pattern dystrophy simulating fundus оАЭavimaculatus: multiple, widely scattered, irregular yellow lesions which
may be similar to those seen in fundus оАЫavimaculatus.
оАБ Adult-onset foveomacular vitelliform dystrophy вАУ see above.
оАБ Fundus pulverulentus is extremely rare. Macular pigment
mottling develops.

North Carolina macular dystrophy

North Carolina macular dystrophy is a rare non-progressive condition. It was оАЭrst described in families living in the mountains of
North Carolina and subsequently in many unrelated families in
other parts of the world. Inheritance is AD with complete penetrance, but highly variable expressivity. A third are asymptomatic,
a third have mild central visual loss and a third have moderate central visual loss. A few develop MNV with severe central visual loss.
Most aоАЮected individuals have a mutation of the MCDR1 locus of
chromosome 6q, which results in dysregulation of the PRDM13
gene. Patients show a characteristic retinal phenotype and distribution of drusen that diоАЮers from the drusen found in patients
with AMD. Despite the variability of macular phenotype, multimodal retinal imaging reveals consistent оАЭndings.

оАБ Grade 1 is characterized by yellowвАУwhite, drusen-like peripheral (Fig. 15.29A) and macular deposits that develop during
the оАЭrst decade but may remain asymptomatic throughout life.
оАБ Grade 2 is characterized by deep, conоАЫuent macular deposits (Fig. 15.29B). оАЯe long-term visual prognosis is guarded
because some patients develop neovascular maculopathy and
subretinal scarring.
оАБ Grade 3 is characterized by atrophic macular lesions associated with variable impairment of visual acuity.
оАБ FAF shows characteristic peripheral hyperautoоАЫuorescent
streaks (Fig. 15.29C). оАЯe atrophic macula shows hyperautoоАЫuorescence in the early stages (Fig. 15.29D), followed by late
hypoautoоАЫuorescence.
оАБ OCT shows mild irregularity in the outer retina (Fig. 15.29E
and F).

Familial dominant drusen

Familial dominant drusen (Doyne honeycomb choroiditis, malattia leventinese) is thought to represent an early-onset variant
of age-related macular degeneration. Inheritance is AD with variable expressivity. A mutation in the gene EFEMP1 (chromosome
2p16) is responsible, which results in increased lipid accumulation
in drusen. SigniоАЭcant diоАЮerences in severity and progression can
occur in the two eyes of the same patient and between patients
in the same family. Typically, asymptomatic yellowвАУwhite, elongated, radially orientated drusen centred on the macula develop in
the second decade (Fig. 15.30A). оАЯe drusen may involve the disc
margin nasal to the disc. With age the lesions become increasingly
dense and acquire a honeycomb pattern (Fig. 15.30B). Initially the
visual symptoms are insidious and nyctalopia is not usually experienced. Central visual loss with acuity less than 6/60 may occur in
the seventh and eighth decades due to RPE degeneration, macular atrophy or occasionally MNV. Investigations include OCT and
FAF. оАЯe ERG is normal, but the EOG is subnormal in those with
advanced disease.

Sorsby fundus dystrophy

Sorsby fundus dystrophy (SFD) is a rare disorder that results in
bilateral visual loss, typically in late middle age. Inheritance is AD
with full penetrance but variable expressivity; allelic variation in
the gene TIMP3 (chromosome 22q) is responsible. оАЯe predominant pathological feature is conоАЫuent amorphous deposits found
between the basement membrane of the RPE and the inner layer
of Bruch membrane. Early presentation may be in the third decade
with nyctalopia, when conоАЫuent yellowвАУwhite drusen-like deposits may be seen in the macula, along the arcades and nasal to the
disc (Fig. 15.31A), or in the оАЭоАЬh decade with sudden visual loss
due to exudative maculopathy secondary to MNV and subretinal
scarring (Fig. 15.31B). When early-onset drusen are associated
with MNV in young adulthood this diagnosis should be considered. Peripheral chorioretinal atrophy may occur by the seventh
decade and result in loss of ambulatory vision. OCT shows reticular pseudodrusen. оАЯe ERG is initially normal but may be subnormal in later disease. Prolonged rod-mediated dark adaptation is a
sensitive test in early disease. Patients with MNV may respond to
anti-VEGF treatment.

Concentric annular macular dystrophy

оАЯe prognosis is good in the majority of cases of (benign) concentric annular macular dystrophy, an AD disorder, although a
minority develop progressive loss of acuity and nyctalopia. Presentation is in adult life with mild impairment of central vision. A
paracentral ring scotoma is present on visual оАЭeld testing.

Central areolar choroidal dystrophy

Central areolar choroidal dystrophy, also termed central choroidal
sclerosis, is a genetically heterogeneous but typically AD condition
presenting in the third or fourth decades with gradual impairment

of central vision. Non-speciоАЭc foveal granularity progresses to
well-circumscribed RPE atrophy and loss of the choriocapillaris
(Fig. 15.32A) and subsequently slowly expanding geographic atrophy with prominence of large choroidal vessels (Fig. 15.32B). оАЯere
is no treatment and the prognosis is for central vision is poor.

Dominant cystoid macular oedema

Bilateral CMO (AD) commonly presents in adolescence with
gradual impairment of central vision. Treatment is ineоАЮective and
macular atrophy inevitably ensues.

Sj√ґgrenвАУLarsson syndrome

Sj√ґgrenвАУLarsson syndrome (AR) is a neurocutaneous disorder
which occurs secondary to defective enzyme activity (fatty aldehyde dehydrogenase; ALDH3A2 encoded on chromosome 17p
with 11 distinct mutations). It is characterized by congenital ichthyosis, neurological problems and mild to moderate intellectual
disability. Presentation is with photophobia and poor vision, and
glistening yellowвАУwhite crystalline deposits develop at the macula
(Fig. 15.33), appearing during the оАЭrst 2 years of life. Pigmentary
retinopathy (50%), cataract and colobomatous microphthalmos
may also occur.

Maternally inherited diabetes and
deafness

Maternally inherited diabetes and deafness (MIDD) constitutes
around 1% of all cases of diabetes, with inheritance via mitochondrial DNA. Most patients develop progressive dystrophic
macular changes (Fig. 15.34A), but vision is not usually aоАЮected.
оАЯe abnormal fundal appearance can be diоАЪcult to see clinically, but FAF (Fig. 15.34B) and OCT (Fig. 15.34C) show typical
abnormalities.

GENERALIZED CHOROIDAL
DYSTROPHIES

Choroideremia

Fig. 15.33 Macular crystals in Sj√ґgrenвАУLarsson syndrome.
(Courtesy of D Taylor and C S Hoyt, from Pediatric Ophthalmology and Strabismus, Elsevier Saunders 2005.)

Choroideremia is a progressive diоАЮuse degeneration of the choroid,
RPE and photoreceptors. Inheritance is X-linked, so that males
are predominantly aоАЮected. However, it is important to identify
female carriers, as 50% of their sons will develop choroideremia
and 50% of their daughters will be carriers. оАЯe prognosis is very
poor. Although most patients retain some vision until the sixth
decade, severe visual loss occurs thereaоАЬer. оАЯe gene responsible
is CHM; a contiguous extended gene deletion can lead to deafness
and mental handicap.
оАБ Symptoms. Nyctalopia, oоАЬen beginning in adolescence, is followed by reduced peripheral vision and some years later, by loss
of central vision. Clinically problematic disease occurs almost
exclusively in males, but females may rarely also develop severe
disease due to skewed inactivation of the X-chromosome.

Gyrate atrophy

Gyrate atrophy (AR) is caused by a mutation in the OAT gene
(chromosome 10q) encoding the main ornithine degradation
enzyme, ornithine aminotransferase. DeоАЭciency of the enzyme
leads to elevated ornithine levels in the plasma, urine, cerebrospinal оАЫuid and aqueous humour. оАЯe visual prognosis is generally
poor, with legal blindness occurring around the age of 50.
оАБ Symptoms. Myopia and nyctalopia in adolescence, with a subsequent gradual worsening of vision.

HEREDITARY VITREORETINOPATHIES

Juvenile XоАЯlinked retinoschisis

Introduction

Juvenile retinoschisis is a rare congenital X-linked disease of the
retina caused by mutations in the RS1 gene (Xp22.13), which
encodes for retinoschisin. оАЯis protein is secreted by photoreceptors and is involved in intercellular adhesion. оАЯe condition
is characterized by bilateral maculopathy and can be associated
with peripheral retinoschisis. оАЯe basic defect involves splitting
of the retinal nerve оАЭbre layer from the rest of the sensory retina,
in contrast to acquired (age-related) retinoschisis in which splitting occurs at the outer plexiform layer. Vitreous haemorrhage
and retinal detachment are the main causes of signiоАЭcant visual
loss. оАЯe visual acuity deteriorates during the оАЭrst two decades as
a consequence of progressive maculopathy, but may then remain
reasonably stable until the оАЭоАЬh or sixth decades.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. оАЯe presentation is usually in boys between the ages
of 5 and 10 years, who are experiencing diоАЪculty with reading.
Less frequently, squint or nystagmus occurs in infancy associated with advanced peripheral retinoschisis and vitreous haemorrhage. Carrier females are asymptomatic and show no retinal
changes.
оАБ Signs

{ ERG is subnormal in early disease and later becomes
extinguished.
оАБ Treatment. оАЯere is no cure for this condition. Cataract surgery may result in a transient improvement of vision. Reduction in ornithine levels with an arginine-restricted diet can
slow progression, but the highly restrictive nature of the diet
makes adherence to the diet diоАЪcult. Vitamin B6 supplementation can reduce plasma ornithine in some patients.

Progressive bifocal chorioretinal
atrophy

In progressive bifocal chorioretinal atrophy (AD), the implicated
gene region overlaps with that responsible for North Carolina
macular dystrophy but the two conditions are believed to be due
to diоАЮerent mutations. Dual foci of chorioretinal atrophy develop
temporal and nasal to the disc, with inevitable macular involvement
(Fig. 15.37). Nystagmus, myopia and retinal detachment may occur.

{ оАЯe most common appearance is foveal schisis, appearing
as spoke-like striae radiating from the foveola, associated
with cystoid changes (Fig. 15.38A). Over time the striae
become less evident, leaving a blunted foveal reоАЫex.

{ Whitish drusen-like dots and pigment variation may be
seen.

{ Peripheral schisis predominantly involves the inferotemporal quadrant. It does not extend but secondary
changes may occur. оАЯe inner layer, which consists only
of the internal limiting membrane and the retinal nerve
оАЭbre layer, may develop oval defects (Fig. 15.38B) and
in extreme cases the defects coalesce, leaving only retinal blood vessels оАЫoating in the vitreous (вАШvitreous veilsвАЩ)
(Fig. 15.38C). Silvery peripheral dendritic оАЭgures (Fig.
15.38D), vascular sheathing and pigmentary changes are
common and retinal оАЫecks and nasal dragging of retinal
vessels may be seen.

{ Complications include vitreous and intra-schisis haemorrhage, neovascularization, subretinal exudation and rarely
rhegmatogenous or tractional retinal detachment.
оАБ Investigation

{ FAF shows variable macular abnormality, including spokelike patterns and central hypoautoоАЫuorescence with surrounding hyperautoоАЫuorescence.

{ OCT is useful for documenting maculopathy progression.
Cystic spaces in the inner nuclear and outer plexiform layers are commonly present (Fig. 15.38E), but the fovea may
simply appear disorganized.

spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia and early-onset osteoarthritis.
Deafness may be sensorineural or caused by recurrent otitis
media.
оАБ Kniest dysplasia is usually caused by a defect in the type II
collagen gene COL2A1, which is also involved in Stickler syndrome type 1. Inheritance can be AD, but most cases represent
a de novo mutation. High myopia, vitreous degeneration, retinal detachment and ectopia lentis can occur. Systemic features
may include short stature, a round face and arthropathy.

Diagnosis

оАЯe three characteristic ocular features are high myopia, vitreoretinal degeneration with an associated extremely high rate of retinal
detachment and cataract.
оАБ Signs

Treatment

оАБ Topical or oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (e.g. dorzolamide eye drops three times daily) may reduce foveal schisis
and improve visual acuity in some patients.
оАБ Vitrectomy may be required for vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment repair, but is technically challenging.
оАБ Gene therapy is under investigation, with the aim of restoring normal function of the protein abnormality underlying
retinoschisis.

Stickler syndrome
Introduction

Stickler syndrome (hereditary arthro-ophthalmopathy) is a
genetically heterogeneous disorder of collagen connective tissue.
оАЯe condition aоАЮects approximately 1 in 7500 to 9000 people. At
least eight clinically and genetically distinct sub-groups of Stickler syndrome are now recognized and it is possible to identify
the underlying genetic mutation in 95% of cases. Inheritance is
AD (STL1вАУSTL3) or AR (STL4-STL8) with complete penetrance
but variable expressivity. Stickler syndrome is the most common
inherited cause of retinal detachment in children. Importantly,
there is a high risk of childhood blindness secondary to bilateral
retinal detachment in the three dominantly inherited sub-groups.
Stickler syndrome should be considered in any infant with cleоАЬ
palate, deafness or joint hypermobility in association with myopia. In general, the prognosis has been poor, but is improving with
elevated standards of care.
оАБ Stickler syndrome type I (STL1 вАУ membranous vitreous
type) is the most common form and is the result of mutations
in the COL2A1 gene, which encodes type 2 procollagen. оАЯe
classic ocular and systemic features are present as originally
described by Stickler, though there is also a predominantly
ocular (non-syndromic) form of STL1.
оАБ STL2 (beaded vitreous type) is caused by mutations in the
COL11A1 gene. Patients have congenital non-progressive high
myopia, sensorineural deafness and other features of Stickler
syndrome type 1.
оАБ STL3 (non-ocular type) is due to mutations in the COL11A2
gene. AоАЮected individuals have the typical systemic features,
but no ocular manifestations.
оАБ STL4-STL8 (AR types) are extremely rare.
оАБ Systemic features include mid-facial hypoplasia, joint hypermobility (Fig. 15.39A), Pierre-Robin-type features (micrognathia, cleоАЬ palate вАУ Fig. 15.39B and glossoptosis вАУ backward
displacement of the tongue), biоАЭd uvula (Fig. 15.39C), mild

{ In STL1 patients exhibit an optically empty vitreous, a
retrolenticular membrane and circumferential equatorial
membranes that extend a short way into the vitreous cavity
(Fig. 15.39D and E).

{ In STL2 patients the vitreous has a оАЭbrillary and beaded
appearance.

{ Radial lattice-like degeneration associated with RPE
hyperplasia, vascular sheathing and sclerosis.

{ Retinal detachment develops in approximately 50% in the
оАЭrst decade of life, oоАЬen as a result of multiple or giant
tears that may involve both eyes (Fig. 15.39F). оАЯis should
not be confused with retinal dialysis.

{ Presenile cataract characterized by frequently nonprogressive peripheral cortical wedge-shaped, оАЫeck or
lamellar opacities is common.

{ Ectopia lentis can occur, but is uncommon.

{ Glaucoma (5вАУ10%) is associated with congenital angle
anomaly.

Treatment

оАБ Prophylactic 360¬∞ retinal laser or cryotherapy reduces the incidence of retinal detachment. Regular screening with prophylactic treatment of retinal breaks is essential. Long-term review
of all patients is mandatory.
оАБ Retinal detachment repair is challenging and vitrectomy is
generally indicated. Proliferative vitreoretinopathy is a particularly common complication and re-detachment may occur.
оАБ Cataract in Stickler is oоАЬen visually inconsequential, particularly when early. If surgery is required, a careful preoperative
retinal evaluation with treatment of breaks should be performed. Vitreous loss and postoperative retinal detachment
are relatively common.
оАБ Glaucoma treatment may be required. If early-onset and presumably related to angle anomaly, management is generally as
for congenital glaucoma (see Ch. 11).

TIP Prophylactic treatment is key to preserving vision in the
high-risk sub-groups of Stickler syndrome, as this reduces the
risk of visual loss secondary to retinal detachment.

Wagner syndrome

Wagner syndrome (VCAN-related vitreoretinopathy) is a rare
condition having some features in common with Stickler syndrome, but no association with systemic abnormalities. Erosive

vitreoretinopathy is now known to be the same disorder. Inheritance is AD and mutations in the gene VCAN can be responsible.
Severity is variable, with up to 50% developing retinal detachment,
oоАЬen before the age of 15.

{ Patients tend to have low to moderate myopia.

Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy

{ оАЯe key abnormal оАЭnding is an optically empty vitreous cavity (Fig. 15.40A) lacking structural elements and it is thought
this leads to reduced вАШscaоАЮoldingвАЩ support for the retina.

{ оАЯe peripheral retinal vasculature is deоАЭcient.

{ Greyish-white avascular strands and membranes extend
into the vitreous cavity and there may be a circumferential
ridge-like condensation of the gel at or anterior to the retinal periphery (Fig. 15.40B).

{ Peripheral retinal changes including progressive chorioretinal atrophy (Fig. 15.40C) occur and nyctalopia is commonly troublesome. оАЯe visual оАЭelds gradually constrict.

Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy is a rare, slowly progressive condition characterized by failure of vascularization of the
temporal retinal periphery, similar to that seen in retinopathy of
prematurity. оАЯe avascular peripheral retina leads to hypoxia and
stimulates neovascularization which can lead to vitreoretinal traction, subretinal exudation and haemorrhages, tractional retinal
detachment and foveal displacement. Inheritance is most commonly AD and rarely X-linked or AR, with high penetrance and
variable expressivity. To date, mutations in 10 genes have been
implicated which aоАЮect normal retinal angiogenesis. оАЯe clinical
appearance is oоАЬen asymmetrical and can vary signiоАЭcantly even
among aоАЮected members of the same family. Presentation is in
childhood. оАЯe prognosis is frequently poor, especially with early
aggressive onset.
оАБ Signs

{ Cataract is common in younger adults and glaucoma can
develop.
оАБ Investigation. FA shows non-perfusion due to choriocapillaris
loss (Fig. 15.40D). оАЯe ERG may initially be normal, but later
shows a reduction of scotopic b-wave amplitudes and diоАЮuse
cone-rod loss.
оАБ Treatment. Retinal breaks and detachment are treated as they
occur, but extensive prophylaxis is avoided.

{ High myopia may be present.

{ Stage 1: peripheral avascularity. There is abrupt termination of retinal vessels at the temporal equator.
Vitreous degeneration and peripheral vitreoretinal

{ Laser ablation of avascular retina is recommended, usually

Enhanced SоАЯcone syndrome and
GoldmannвАУFavre syndrome

оАЯis is a rare inherited progressive retinal degeneration which has
a highly variable phenotype. оАЯe human retina has three cone

{ Management depends on the clinical stage of the disease.

photoreceptor types: short-wave sensitivity (S), middle-wave sensitivity (M) and long-wave sensitivity (L). Most inherited retinal dystrophies exhibit progressive attenuation of rods and all classes of cones.
However, enhanced S-cone syndrome is characterized by hyperfunction of S-cones and severe impairment of M- and L-cones, with
non-recordable rod function. GoldmannвАУFavre syndrome, which is
caused by mutations in the same gene, represents a severe variant.
оАБ Inheritance is AR with variable expressivity. оАЯe gene implicated is NR2E3, which encodes for a ligand-dependent transcription factor.
оАБ Presentation is with nyctalopia in childhood and sometimes
hemeralopia (reduced vision in bright light). Colour vision is
decreased, but the tritan axis is preserved. Pigmentary changes
along the vascular arcades or mid-periphery may be associated in more advanced cases with round pigment clumps (Fig.
15.42A). Macular changes may include cystoid maculopathy
(without оАЫuorescein leakage) and schisis. Vitreous degeneration and peripheral retinoschisis can occur.
оАБ OCT shows disrupted foveal lamination with a thickened subfoveal ellipsoid zone (Fig. 15.42B).
оАБ ERG shows no rod response and the scotopic maximum
response and photopic ERG waveforms are similar.
оАБ Prognosis is subject to considerable clinical variability; while
some patients maintain good visual acuity and show little progression, some patients develop extensive retinal degeneration and visual loss. About 30% have a visual acuity of 6/36 or
worse by late middle age.
оАБ оАЯere is no treatment other than supportive measures.

662 Albinism

Autosomal dominant neovascular
inоАЮammatory vitreoretinopathy

A rare but interesting inherited (gene CAPN5) disorder, autosomal
dominant neovascular inоАЫammatory vitreoretinopathy features panuveitis, oоАЬen of onset in early adulthood. оАЯe initial symptom is typically
оАЫoaters due to vitritis, with the development of peripheral vascular
closure with peripheral and then disc neovascularization, fundus pigmentation, epiretinal and subretinal оАЭbrocellular membranes. Complications include vitreous haemorrhage, tractional retinal detachment,
CMO, cataract and neovascular glaucoma. оАЯe ERG shows selective
loss of b-wave amplitude. оАЯe prognosis can be poor. Peripheral retinal
photocoagulation and vitreous surgery may be required.

Autosomal dominant
vitreoretinochoroidopathy

Autosomal dominant vitreoretinochoroidopathy can be caused
by BEST1 mutations. Presentation is in adult life if symptomatic,
but frequently discovery is by chance. Vitreous cells and оАЭbrillary
degeneration develop, with a non-progressive or very slowly progressive encircling band of pigmentary disturbance between the
ora serrata and equator, with a discrete posterior border. Within
the band there can be arteriolar attenuation, neovascularization,
punctate white opacities and later chorioretinal atrophy. Complications are uncommon, but can include CMO, vitreous haemorrhage and cataract. Microcornea and nanophthalmos have been
described in some patients. оАЯe full-оАЭeld ERG is subnormal in
older patients only. оАЯe prognosis is good.

ALBINISM

Introduction

Albinism is a genetically determined, heterogeneous group of disorders of melanin synthesis in which either the eyes alone (ocular
albinism) or the eyes, skin and hair (oculocutaneous albinism)
may be aоАЮected. оАЯe latter may be either tyrosinase-positive or
tyrosinase-negative. оАЯe diоАЮerent mutations are thought to act
through a common pathway involving reduced melanin synthesis in
the eye during development. Molecular genetics allows 7 subtypes
of non-syndromic oculocutaneous albinism (OCA) to be determined (OCA 1вАУ7). Patients with oculocutaneous albinism have an
increased risk of cutaneous basal cell and squamous cell carcinoma.

TIP Patients with oculocutaneous albinism are at increased
risk of cutaneous basal cell and squamous cell carcinoma and
should take precautions to avoid exposure to sunlight.

TyrosinaseоАЯnegative oculocutaneous
albinism (OCA type 1)

Individuals who have tyrosinase-negative (complete) albinism
are incapable of synthesizing any melanin and have white hair
and very pale skin (Fig. 15.44A) throughout life with a lack of

melanin pigment in all ocular structures. оАЯe condition is caused
by mutations in TYR gene (chromosome 11q14), responsible for
the enzyme tyrosinase, usually with AR inheritance.
оАБ Signs

{ VA is usually <6/60 due to foveal hypoplasia.

{ Nystagmus is typically pendular and horizontal. It usually increases in bright illumination and tends to lessen in
severity with age.

{ оАЯe iris is diaphanous and translucent (Fig. 15.44B), giving
rise to a вАШpink-eyedвАЩ appearance (Fig. 15.44C).

{ оАЯe fundus lacks pigment and shows conspicuously large
choroidal vessels. оАЯere is also foveal hypoplasia with
absence of the foveal pit and poorly formed perimacular
vascular arcades (Fig. 15.44D).

{ оАЯe optic chiasm has fewer uncrossed nerve оАЭbres than
normal вАУ the majority of оАЭbres from each eye cross to the
contralateral hemisphere. оАЯis can be demonstrated with
visual evoked potentials.

{ Other features include high refractive errors of various
types, a positive angle kappa, squint and absent stereopsis.

TyrosinaseоАЯpositive oculocutaneous
albinism (OCA type 2)

Individuals who have tyrosinase-positive (incomplete) albinism
synthesize variable amounts of melanin. оАЯeir hair may be white,
yellow or red and darkens with age. Skin is pale at birth but usually
darkens by 2 years of age (Fig. 15.45A). Inheritance is usually AR.
оАЯe condition is caused by hundreds of diоАЮerent mutations in the
OCA2 gene (chromosome 15q12), which plays a role in tyrosine
transport in melanosomes. оАЯis is the commonest form of albinism in Africa and accounts for a third of cases worldwide.
оАБ Ocular signs

{ VA is usually impaired due to foveal hypoplasia.

{ Iris may be blue or dark brown with variable translucency.

{ Fundus shows variable hypopigmentation (Fig. 15.45B).

Oculocutaneous albinism OCA
(types 3вАУ7)

OCT type 3 is seen especially in Black Africans. оАЯe other subtypes are rare and have been reported in single families.

Systemic associations

оАБ ChediakвАУHigashi syndrome: a rare AR disorder arising from
a mutation of a lysosomal regulator protein. оАЯis leads to failure of phagolysosome formation and recurrent pyogenic infections (especially Staphylococcus).
оАБ HermanskyвАУPudlak syndrome: an extremely rare AR lysosomal storage disease with platelet dysfunction (easy bruising
and excessive bleeding). In some cases, pulmonary оАЭbrosis and
granulomatous colitis with bleeding may occur.
оАБ Waardenburg syndrome: an AD condition with a range of
systemic presentations including a white forelock, poliosis,
synophrys (вАШmonobrowвАЩ), deafness and sometimes limb and

neurological anomalies. Ocular features include lateral displacement of the medial canthi, hypochromic irides with

Ocular albinism (OA)

In this variant, involvement is predominantly ocular, with normal skin and hair, although occasionally hypopigmented skin
macules may be seen. Two types are recognized: OA1 (Nettleship Falls syndrome) and OA2 (ForsiusвАУEriksson syndrome).
Inheritance is always X-linked. Female carriers are asymptomatic, but may show partial iris translucency, macular stippling
and mid-peripheral scattered areas of depigmentation and granularity (Fig. 15.47). AоАЮected males have hypopigmented irides
and fundi.

CHERRY-RED SPOT AT THE MACULA

A cherry-red spot at the macula is a clinical sign seen in the context of thickening and loss of transparency of the retina at the
posterior pole. оАЯe fovea is the thinnest part of the retina, allowing persistent transmission of the underlying vascular choroidal
hue when the surrounding retina becomes relatively opaque.
Causes include:
оАБ Metabolic storage diseases. A group of rare inherited metabolic diseases, oоАЬen enzyme deоАЭciencies, leads to the pathological accumulation of lipid-based material in various tissues.
With the passage of time the spot becomes less evident due
to retinal nerve оАЭbre layer degeneration and consecutive optic
atrophy is seen. оАЯey are almost exclusively AR. Some individual storage disorders are:

{ GM1 gangliosidosis (generalized): neurological features
are severe, with death typically by the age of 2 years. Subtle
corneal clouding can occur.

{ Mucolipidosis type I (sialidosis): a late-onset form features
myoclonus and seizures, but is compatible with a normal
life span. A more severe form causes severe neurodegeneration and death in early childhood. Corneal clouding and
optic atrophy may be seen.

{ GM2 gangliosidosis: this encompasses two conditions,
TayвАУSachs disease (Fig. 15.48) and SandhoоАЮ disease. In

Fig. 15.47 Carrier of X-linked ocular albinism. (Courtesy of
M Pennesi.)

Fig. 15.48 Cherry-red spot at the macula in a baby with TayвАУ
Sachs disease.

both, there is progressive neurological deterioration, with
early blindness and death.

{ NiemannвАУPick disease: of the three main types (AвАУC),
only the оАЭrst two feature a cherry-red spot. Abnormal ocular motility occurs in type C (chronic neuropathic).

{ Farber disease: systemically, aphonia, dermatitis, lymphadenopathy, psychomotor retardation, renal and cardiopulmonary disease may develop. Ocular features additional
to cherry-red spot development include pinguecula-like
conjunctival lesions and nodular corneal opacity.
оАБ Central retinal and cilioretinal artery occlusion (see Ch. 13).
Retinal clouding results from oedema; the appearance is an
acute sign only.
оАБ Retinotoxicity due to speciоАЭc agents including quinine, dapsone, gentamicin, carbon monoxide and methanol.
оАБ Commotio retinae (see Ch. 22); oedema due to blunt trauma.

666 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

Anatomy of the peripheral retina

Pars plana

оАЮe ciliary body starts 1 mm from the limbus and extends posteriorly for about 6 mm. оАЮe anterior 2 mm consist of the pars
plicata, the remaining 4 mm, the оАЭattened pars plana. In order not
to endanger either the lens or retina, the optimal location for a
pars plana surgical incision or intravitreal injection is 4 mm and
3.5 mm posterior to the limbus in phakic and pseudophakic eyes,
respectively. An incision through the mid-pars plana will usually
be located anterior to the vitreous base (see below).

Ora serrata

оАЮe ora serrata (Fig. 16.1) is the junction between the retina and
ciliary body. In retinal detachment (RD), fusion of the sensory retina with the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and choroid limits
forward extension of subretinal оАЭuid (SRF) at the ora. However,
there is no equivalent adhesion between the choroid and sclera and
choroidal detachments may progress anteriorly to involve the ciliary body (ciliochoroidal detachment).
оАБ Dentate processes are tapering extensions of retina onto the
pars plana. оАЮey are more marked nasally than temporally and
display marked variation in contour.
оАБ Oral bays are scalloped edges of pars plana epithelium between
dentate processes.
оАБ Meridional folds (Fig. 16.2) are small radial folds of thickened
retinal tissue in line with dentate processes, most commonly

in the superonasal quadrant. A fold may occasionally exhibit a
small retinal hole at its apex. A meridional complex is a conоАЬguration in which a dentate process, usually with an associated
meridional fold, is aligned with a ciliary process.
оАБ Enclosed oral bays (see Fig. 16.2) are small islands of pars
plana surrounded by retina as a result of the meeting of two
adjacent dentate processes. оАЮey should not be mistaken for
retinal holes.

Vitreous base

оАЮe vitreous base (Fig. 16.3) is a 3вАУ4 mm wide zone straddling
the ora serrata, throughout which the cortical vitreous is strongly
attached. Following posterior vitreous detachment (PVD), the posterior hyaloid face remains attached at the vitreous base. Pre-existing
retinal holes within the attached vitreous base do not lead to RD.
Blunt trauma may cause an avulsion of the vitreous base, with tearing
of the non-pigmented epithelium of the pars plana along the baseвАЩs
anterior border and of the retina along the baseвАЩs posterior border.

Innocuous peripheral retinal
degenerations (Fig. 16.4A)

Peripheral retinal degenerations and other lesions carrying the
potential to lead to RD are described separately.
оАБ Microcystoid (peripheral cystoid) degeneration consists of
tiny vesicles with indistinct boundaries on a greyish-white
background, making the retina appear thickened and less
transparent. оАЮe degeneration starts adjacent to the ora serrata
and extends circumferentially and posteriorly with a smooth
undulating posterior border. Microcystoid degeneration is

Fig. 16.3 The vitreous base.

present in most adult eyes, increasing in extent with age. It is
not causally related to RD, though it may give rise to typical
degenerative retinoschisis.
оАБ Paving stone degeneration is characterized by discrete yellowвАУwhite patches of focal chorioretinal atrophy that may have
pigmented margins (Fig. 16.4B and C). It is typically found
between the equator and the ora and is more common in the
inferior fundus. It is present to some extent in at least 25% of
normal eyes.
оАБ Reticular (honeycomb) degeneration is an age-related change
consisting of a оАЬne network of perivascular pigmentation that
sometimes extends posterior to the equator (Fig. 16.4D).
оАБ Peripheral drusen. Clustered or scattered small pale discrete
lesions (Fig. 16.4E) that may have hyperpigmented borders.
оАЮey are similar to drusen at the posterior pole and usually
occur in the eyes of older individuals.
оАБ Pars plana cyst. Clear-walled cysts, usually small, are derived
from non-pigmented ciliary epithelium. оАЮey are present in

CHAPTER

Retinal Detachment 667
#### 16

5вАУ10% of eyes and are more common temporally. оАЮey do not
predispose to RD.

Sites of vitreous adhesion
Physiological

оАЮe peripheral cortical vitreous is loosely attached to the internal
limiting membrane (ILM) of the sensory retina. Sites of stronger
adhesion in the normal eye include:
оАБ Vitreous base (very strong).
оАБ Optic disc margins (fairly strong).
оАБ Perifoveal (fairly weak).
оАБ Peripheral blood vessels (usually weak).

Pathological

Abnormal adhesions may lead to retinal tear formation following
PVD, or to vitreomacular interface disease. Most are discussed in
detail later in this chapter.
оАБ Lattice degeneration.
оАБ Retinal pigment clumps.
оАБ Cystic retinal tuоАЫs.
оАБ Vitreous base anomalies, such as extensions and posterior
islands.
оАБ вАШWhite with pressureвАЩ and вАШwhite without pressureвАЩ.
оАБ Zonular traction tuоАЫs.
оАБ Vitreomacular traction (see Ch. 14).
оАБ Preretinal new vessels, e.g. proliferative diabetic retinopathy.

DeоАЯnitions

оАБ Retinal detachment (RD). RD refers to separation of the
neurosensory retina (NSR) from the RPE. оАЮis results in the
accumulation of subretinal оАЭuid (SRF) in the potential space
between the NSR and RPE.
оАБ Rhegmatogenous (Greek rhegma вАУ break) RD requires a fullthickness defect in the sensory retina, which permits оАЭuid
derived from synchytic (liqueоАЬed) vitreous to gain access to the
subretinal space. Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD),
as opposed to the presence merely of a cuоАЪ of SRF surrounding
a retinal break, is said to be present when оАЭuid extends further
than one optic disc diameter from the edge of the break.
оАБ Tractional RD. оАЮe NSR is pulled away from the RPE by contracting vitreoretinal membranes in the absence of a retinal
break.
оАБ Exudative (serous, secondary) RD. SRF is derived from the
vessels of the NSR and/or choroid.
оАБ Combined tractionalвАУrhegmatogenous RD results when a
retinal break is caused by traction from an adjacent area of
оАЬbrovascular proliferation.
оАБ Subclinical RD is generally used to refer to an asymptomatic
break surrounded by a relatively small amount of SRF, by deоАЬnition extending further than one disc diameter away from the
edge of the break but less than two disc diameters posterior to
the equator. It does not usually give rise to a subjective visual
оАЬeld defect. оАЮe term is sometimes also used to describe an
asymptomatic RD of any extent.

Ultrasonography
Introduction

Ultrasonography (US) utilizes high frequency sound waves that
produce echoes as they strike the interface between acoustically
distinct structures. B-scan (two-dimensional) US is a key tool in
the diagnosis of RD in eyes with opaque media, particularly severe
vitreous haemorrhage.

Technique

оАБ оАЮe examiner holds the US probe with the dominant hand
(Fig. 16.5A).
оАБ Methylcellulose or an ophthalmic gel is placed on the tip of the
probe to act as a coupling agent.
оАБ A cross-sectional view of the eye (Fig. 16.5B) is obtained and
the image can be viewed on a monitor.

CHAPTER

Retinal Detachment 669
#### 16

оАБ оАЮe B-scan probe incorporates a marker for orientation that
correlates with a point on the display screen, usually to the
leоАЫ.
оАБ Vertical scanning is performed with the marker on the probe
orientated superiorly (Fig. 16.6A).
оАБ Horizontal scanning is performed with the marker orientated
towards the nose (Fig. 16.6B).
оАБ оАЮe eye is then examined with the patient looking straight
ahead, up, down, leоАЫ and right. For each position a vertical
and horizontal scan can be performed.
оАБ оАЮe examiner then moves the probe in the opposite direction
to the movement of the eye. For example, when examining the
right eye, the patient looks to the leоАЫ and probe is moved to
the patientвАЩs right, the nasal fundus anterior to the equator is
scanned and vice versa. Dynamic scanning is performed by
asking the patient to move the eye, whilst the probe position
is maintained.

670 Peripheral Lesions Predisposing to Retinal Detachment

оАБ Gain adjusts the ampliоАЬcation of the echo signal, similar to
volume control of a radio. Higher gain increases the sensitivity
of the instrument in displaying weak echoes such as vitreous
opacities. Lower gain only allows display of strong echoes such
as the retina and sclera, though improves resolution because it
narrows the beam.

PERIPHERAL LESIONS PREDISPOSING
TO RETINAL DETACHMENT

Patients with any predisposing lesion, or indeed any high-risk features for RD, should be educated about the nature of symptoms of
PVD and RD and the need to seek review urgently if these occur.

Lattice degeneration

TIP Prophylactic treatment of lattice and snailtrack
degeneration is not required, as the lifetime risk of retinal
detachment secondary to these conditions is low.

Snailtrack degeneration

Snailtrack degeneration is characterized by sharply demarcated
bands of tightly packed вАШsnowоАЭakesвАЩ that give the peripheral retina
a white frost-like appearance (Fig. 16.9). It is viewed by some as a
precursor to lattice degeneration. Marked vitreous traction is seldom present so that U-tears rarely occur, although round holes are
relatively common. Prophylactic treatment is unnecessary.

Cystic retinal tuft

A cystic retinal tuоАЫ (CRT), also known as a granular patch or retinal rosette, is a congenital abnormality consisting of a small, round
or oval, discrete elevated whitish lesion, typically in the equatorial
or peripheral retina, more commonly temporally. It may be associated with pigmentation at its base. It is comprised principally of
glial tissue. Strong vitreoretinal adhesion is commonly present and
both small round holes (Fig. 16.10) and horseshoe tears can occur.
It is an under-recognized lesion, though this may change with the
adoption of wide-оАЬeld imaging. CRT are present in up to 5% of the
population (bilateral in 20%) and may be the causative lesion in
5вАУ10% of eyes with RD, though the risk of RD in a given eye with
CRT is probably well under 1%.

Degenerative retinoschisis

оАБ Complications do not occur in most eyes with lattice
degeneration.

{ Tears may develop consequent to a PVD, when lattice is
sometimes visible on the оАЭap of the tear.

{ Atrophic holes may rarely (2%) lead to RD. оАЮe risk is
higher in young myopes. In these patients the RD may not
be preceded by acute symptoms of PVD (see below) and
SRF usually spreads slowly so that diagnosis may be delayed.
оАБ Management. In an asymptomatic patient with lattice degeneration the areas of lattice do not need to be treated prophylactically, even if retinal holes are seen. However, the patient
should be advised of the symptoms of RD and written information should be provided. An associated asymptomatic
U-tear should be managed as discussed later in the chapter.

оАБ Prevalence. Lattice degeneration is present in about 8% of the
population. It probably develops early in life, with a peak incidence during the second and third decades. It is found more
commonly in moderate myopes. оАЮe lifetime risk of a RD in
an individual with lattice degeneration is approximately 1%.
However, lattice degeneration is present in about 40% of eyes
with RD. In these patients, detachment of the retina is caused
by premature PVD and tractional tears.
оАБ Pathology. оАЮere is discontinuity of the ILM with variable
atrophy of the underlying NSR. оАЮe vitreous overlying an area
of lattice is synchytic but the vitreous attachments around the
margins are exaggerated (Fig. 16.7).
оАБ Signs. Lattice is most commonly bilateral, temporal and superior.

{ Spindle-shaped areas of retinal thinning, commonly
located between the equator and the posterior border of
the vitreous base.

{ Sclerosed vessels forming an arborizing network of white
lines is characteristic (Fig. 16.8A).

{ Some lesions may be associated with вАШsnowоАЭakesвАЩ, remnants of degenerate M√Љller cells (Fig. 16.8B).

{ Associated hyperplasia of the RPE is common (Fig. 16.8C).

{ Small holes are common (Fig. 16.8D).

оАБ Prevalence. Degenerative retinoschisis (RS) is present in about
5% of the population over the age of 20 years and is particularly prevalent in individuals who are hypermetropic.
оАБ Pathology. RS is believed to develop from microcystoid
degeneration by a process of gradual coalescence of degenerative cavities (Fig. 16.11A), resulting in separation or splitting
of the NSR into inner and outer layers, with severing of neurones and complete loss of visual function in the aоАЪected area.
In typical retinoschisis the split occurs in the outer plexiform
layer and in the less common reticular retinoschisis at the level
of the nerve оАЬbre layer.
оАБ Symptoms

{ Photopsia and оАЭoaters are absent because there is no

{ It is rare for the patient to notice a visual оАЬeld defect, even

{ Occasionally symptoms result from vitreous haemorrhage
or a progressive RD.
оАБ Signs. RS is bilateral in up to 80%. Distinction between the

inner layer is thinner and tends to be more elevated in the
latter. DiоАЪerentiation is based principally on behaviour, with

{ Early retinoschisis usually involves the extreme inferotemporal periphery of both fundi, appearing as an exaggeration of microcystoid degeneration with a smooth immobile

be shown with optical coherence tomography (OCT) (Fig.
16.11C) and is easily distinguished from a rhegmatogenous RD (Fig. 16.11D).

{ оАЮe elevation is convex, smooth, thin and relatively immobile, unlike the opaque and corrugated appearance of a
rhegmatogenous RD (Fig. 16.12A).

{ оАЮe thin inner leaf of the schisis cavity may be mistaken,

cysts in the inner leaf are absent.

{ оАЮe lesion may progress circumferentially until it has

remains anterior to the equator. оАЮe reticular type is more
likely to spread posteriorly.

{ оАЮe presence of a pigmented demarcation line is likely to

{ оАЮe surface of the inner layer may show вАШsnowоАЭakesвАЩ, whitish remnants of M√Љller cell footplates, as well as sclerosis
of blood vessels and the schisis cavity may be bridged by
greyвАУwhite tissue strands.

{ Breaks may be present in one or both layers. Inner
layer breaks are small and round (Fig. 16.12B), whilst
the less common outer layer breaks are usually larger,
with rolled edges (Fig. 16.12C) and located behind the
equator.

{ Microaneurysms and small telangiectatic vessels are common, particularly in the reticular type.

{ If a visual оАЬeld defect is detectable, it is absolute, rather
than relative as in RD.
оАБ Complications are uncommon and are thought to be much
more likely in the reticular form.

{ RD is rare; even in an eye with breaks in both layers the
incidence is only around 1%. оАЮe detachment is almost
always asymptomatic, infrequently progressive and rarely

{ Posterior extension of RS to involve the fovea is very rare
but can occur. Progression is generally slow.

{ Vitreous haemorrhage is rare.
оАБ Management. оАЮough RD is rare, discussion of the symptoms is
prudent in all patients, especially those with double layer breaks.

{ A small peripheral RS discovered on incidental examination, especially if breaks are not present in both layers,
does not require routine review.

{ A large RS should be observed periodically, particularly if
breaks are present in both layers or it extends posterior to
the equator. Photography and visual оАЬeld testing are useful, with OCT imaging when posterior extension is present. OCT is also useful for distinguishing between RS and
RD (see Fig. 16.11C and D).

{ Retinopexy or surgical repair may be indicated for relentless progression towards the fovea, when complication by
RD should be excluded.

{ Recurrent vitreous haemorrhage may necessitate
vitrectomy.

{ Progressive symptomatic RD should be addressed
promptly. More than one procedure may be necessary.
Scleral buckling may be adequate for smaller RD with
small outer layer breaks, but vitrectomy is generally indicated for more complex RD.

Zonular traction tuft

оАЮis refers to a common (15%) phenomenon caused by an aberrant
zonular оАЬbre extending posteriorly to be attached to the retina near
the ora serrata, and exerts traction on the retina at its base. It is typically located nasally. оАЮe risk of retinal tear formation is around
2% and periodic long-term review is generally recommended.

White with pressure and white without
pressure

оАБ вАШWhite with pressureвАЩ (WWP) refers to retinal areas in
which a translucent whiteвАУgrey appearance can be induced
by scleral indentation (Fig. 16.13A). Each area has a fixed
configuration that does not change when indentation is
moved to an adjacent area. It may also be observed along
the posterior border of islands of lattice degeneration,
snailtrack degeneration and the outer layer of acquired retinoschisis. It is frequently seen in normal eyes and may be
associated with abnormally strong attachment of the vitreous gel, though may not indicate a higher risk of retinal
break formation.

including giant tears, occasionally develop along the posterior
border of WWOP (see Fig. 16.13B). For this reason, if WWOP
is found in the fellow eye of a patient with a spontaneous giant
retinal tear, prophylactic therapy should be considered. Regular review should be considered for treated and untreated eyes,
though evidence for the beneоАЬt of this is limited.

Myopic choroidal atrophy

DiоАЪuse choroidal/chorioretinal atrophy in myopia is characterized
by diоАЪuse or circumscribed (Fig. 16.14) choroidal depigmentation,
commonly associated with thinning of the overlying retina and
occurs typically in the posterior pole and equatorial area of highly

myopic eyes. Retinal holes developing in the atrophic retina may
occasionally lead to RD. Because of lack of contrast, small holes
may be very diоАЩcult to visualize.

POSTERIOR VITREOUS DETACHMENT

Introduction

PVD refers to separation of the cortical vitreous, along with the
delineating posterior hyaloid membrane (PHM), from the neurosensory retina posterior to the vitreous base. PVD occurs due to
vitreous gel liquefaction with age (synchysis) to form оАЭuid-оАЬlled
cavities (Fig. 16.15A) and subsequently condensation (syneresis),
with access to the preretinal space allowed by a dehiscence in the
cortical gel and/or PHM. оАЮe prevalence of PVD increases with
age; while less than 10% of individuals under 50 years have a PVD,
this rises to two-thirds of those over 70 years. It is usually a spontaneous event, but it can be induced by events such as cataract surgery, trauma, uveitis and panretinal photocoagulation. Perifoveal

Fig. 16.14 Myopic choroidal atrophy. (Courtesy of S Chen.)

hyaloid detachment is followed by foveal separation, then detachment from the posterior retina as far as the equator, attachment
initially being retained at the optic disc. Subsequently complete
detachment of the cortical vitreous as far anteriorly as the vitreous
base takes place (Fig. 16.15B). оАЮis process can occur in stages over
many months. With the exception of the vitreous base, physiological attachments to the retina and other structures are disengaged in
the course of a normal PVD. Overall, about 10% of all patients with
an acute symptomatic PVD will have a retinal tear. However, if a
vitreous haemorrhage is present, this rises to about 60%.

Clinical features

{ оАЮe detached PHM can oоАЫen be seen clinically on slit lamp
examination as a crumpled translucent membrane in the
mid-vitreous cavity behind which the cavity is optically
clear (Fig. 16.16A).

{ Haemorrhage may be indicated by the presence of red
blood cells in the anterior vitreous or as (usually small)
focal intragel collections, or preretinally, when it sometimes forms a crescent shape bordering the limit of PHM
detachment. Its presence should prompt a careful search
for a retinal break (40вАУ90%), particularly with larger
amounts вАУ in such cases breaks tend to be posterior.

{ Pigment granules in the anterior vitreous on slit lamp
examination (the Shafer sign or вАШtobacco dustвАЩ вАУ see Fig.
16.21) are larger, darker and less reоАЭective than red blood
cells. оАЮeir presence raises the possibility of a retinal break
(up to 95% sensitivity), with loss of continuity allowing
communication between the RPE and vitreous cavity.

{ Vitreous cells, if numerous, may signify the presence of a
break.

{ Retinal breaks (see below).
оАБ Investigation. B-scan ultrasound (Fig. 16.16B) can demonstrate the extent of PVD. OCT can show separation of the posterior vitreous face and retina (Fig. 16.16C).

оАБ Symptoms are usually, though not invariably, present.

{ Flashing lights (photopsia) in PVD is oоАЫen described as a
lightning-like arc induced by eye or head movement and is
more noticeable in dim illumination. It is almost always seen in
the temporal periphery. оАЮe mechanism is uncertain, but may
relate to traction on the optic disc and possibly at sites of vitreoretinal adhesion, including actual or potential retinal tears.

{ Floaters (myodesopsia) are mobile vitreous opacities most
evident against a bright pale background. оАЮey are oоАЫen
described as spots, cobwebs or оАЭies (muscae volitantes)
and are commonly present in individuals without a PVD,
especially myopes. A Weiss ring (Fig. 16.15C and D)
is the detached former attachment to the margin of the
optic disc and may be seen by the patient as a circle or other
large solitary lesion. Its presence does not necessarily indicate total PVD, nor does its absence conоАЬrm the absence
of PVD since it may be destroyed during the process of
separation. Floaters can also be due to vitreous blood.

{ Blurred vision. A diоАЪuse haze may be due to dispersed
haemorrhage within the vitreous gel, with a variable
accompanying reduction in visual acuity (VA). Bleeding
can arise from a torn retinal blood vessel or from the site
of a retinal break. Blurring can also be caused by a visually
signiоАЬcant PHM or оАЭoaters in the visual axis, which may
also cause impairment (usually slight) of acuity.

TIP A retinal break is usually present in a patient with a PVD
who has pigment granules (вАШtobacco dustвАЩ) in the vitreous on slit
lamp examination.

Management

Patients with substantial acute symptoms of a PVD should be
examined as soon as possible, usually within 24вАУ48 hours, with
greater urgency in the presence of risk factors including myopia,
a past or family history of RD, high-risk syndromes such as Stickler, pseudophakia and symptoms such as a visual оАЬeld defect,
reduced vision or very prominent оАЭoaters. Enquiry should be
made about the presence of any condition predisposing to nonPVD vitreous haemorrhage, usually diabetes mellitus. If there is
only a single small оАЭoater and no photopsia, evidence suggests
that the risk of retinal break in the symptomatic eye is insigniоАЬcantly higher than that of the asymptomatic fellow eye, so that
urgent assessment may not necessarily be required.
оАБ Examination. оАЮe anterior vitreous should be assessed for the
presence of blood and pigment. Careful retinal examination
including visualization of the ora serrata for 360¬∞ should be performed and should generally include binocular indirect ophthalmoscopy or contact lens scleral indentation. оАЮe asymptomatic
fellow eye should always be examined. If 10 or more vitreous cells
are present in a 1 mm slit lamp оАЬeld the incidence of a retinal
break in a fellow asymptomatic eye has been reported as over 30%.
оАБ Subsequent management. Recommendations for review vary
and the following is a general guide.

{ If there are no suspicious оАЬndings (e.g. vitreous blood) on
examination and no pre-existing risk factors as discussed

above then routine review may not be necessary. оАЮe presence of features associated with higher risk should lead to
review aоАЫer an interval of 1вАУ6 weeks depending on individual characteristics. Some authorities recommend further review in 6вАУ12 months.

{ Patients who present with multiple prominent оАЭoaters or
hazy vision should be reviewed as this has been found to be
associated with a higher risk of retinal break.

{ Discharged patients should be given clear instructions
emphasizing the need to re-attend urgently in the event
of signiоАЬcant new symptoms. Optimally, written information reiterating the advice should be provided. Assurance
can be given that in most cases the оАЭoaters will resolve and
become much less noticeable with time, though exceptionally vitrectomy is necessary.

{ If an area of the fundus cannot be viewed clearly due to

{ Presentation with diоАЪuse fundus-obscuring vitreous

to non-PVD vitreous haemorrhage) is associated with
a high risk of retinal break (60вАУ90%) and RD (40%). A
relative aоАЪerent pupillary defect should be excluded and
B-scan ultrasonography performed regularly until resolution, in order to exclude an underlying detachment or
identiоАЬable break. A very low threshold for vitrectomy
should be adopted, particularly in the presence of other
risk factors, notably previous RD in the fellow eye.

{ оАЮe management of retinal breaks is discussed below.

RETINAL BREAKS

Introduction

Retinal breaks develop in most cases as a result of traction at sites
of vitreoretinal adhesion and occur in up to about 1 in 5 eyes with
symptomatic PVD. In the presence of a break, retrohyaloid оАЭuid
has access to the subretinal space. Asymptomatic retinal breaks of
some sort are present in about 8% of the general population.

#### 16

gel; (B) ultrasound B-scan; (C) OCT of posterior pole.

Clinical features

оАБ Timing. Breaks are usually present at or soon aоАЫer the onset
of symptoms of PVD, although in a minority (up to 5%) tear
formation may be delayed by several weeks.
оАБ Location. Tears associated with PVD are usually located in
the upper retina and are more commonly temporal than nasal.
Macular breaks related to PVD are rare, but when they occur
are usually round and in a myopic eye. оАЮey are aetiologically
distinct from age-related macular holes.
оАБ Morphology. Retinal breaks may be оАЭat or associated with a
surrounding cuоАЪ of SRF. If оАЭuid extends more than one disc
diameter from the edge of a break, a RD is said to be present.

{ U-tears (horseshoe) consist of a оАЭap, its apex pulled anteriorly by the vitreous, the base remaining attached to the
retina (Fig. 16.17A).

{ Operculated tears in which the оАЭap is completely torn away
from the retina by detached vitreous gel to leave a round
or oval break (Fig. 16.17B). оАЮe separated retinal patch
is known as an operculum and can usually be seen suspended in the vitreous cavity in the region of the break,

which can be diоАЩcult to delineate вАУ this may be aided by
the presence of preretinal blood at the site.

{ Retinal holes are round or oval, usually smaller than tears
and carry a lower risk of RD, which when it does occur is
most commonly a slowly progressive shallow RD in a young
female myope. A PVD is not necessarily present, but if vitreous separation has occurred an operculum may be visible in
the nearby vitreous cavity. Round holes may occur in lattice
degeneration. Round holes leading to RD may be distinct in
most cases from the round atrophic retinal holes (Fig. 16.7C)
that are a variant of paving stone degeneration and probably
carry a lower risk, though clinically distinction is not easy.

{ A dialysis is a circumferential tear along the ora serrata and
is usually a consequence of blunt ocular trauma. Importantly, the vitreous gel remains attached to the posterior
margin. It typically appears as a large very peripheral break
with a regular rolled edge (Fig. 16.17D). оАЮe RD is oоАЫen
slowly progressive in the absence of a PVD.

{ A giant retinal tear (Fig. 16.17E and F) is a variant of
U-tear, by deоАЬnition involving 90¬∞ or more of the retinal
circumference. In contrast to dialysis, vitreous gel remains

attached to the anterior margin of the break. It is most frequently located in the immediate post-oral retina or, less
commonly, at the equator.

Management

оАЮe management of many categories of break remains imperfectly
deоАЬned and approaches diоАЪer between retinal subspecialists. оАЮere
has been a trend in recent years towards less aggressive prophylactic treatment of asymptomatic and operculated breaks, with
substitution by observation and patient education. Patients should
always be instructed about the symptoms of vitreous detachment
and RD, optimally supplemented by written information, and
should seek review urgently in the event of new visual symptoms.
оАЮe risk associated with prophylaxis is small, but includes new
break formation and epiretinal membrane formation. Severe complications are rare.
оАБ Risk factors for progression to detachment

{ Miscellaneous factors include a history of RD in the fellow eye, prior cataract surgery (particularly if vitreous loss
occurred), myopia, a family history of RD and systemic
conditions such as Marfan, Stickler and EhlersвАУDanlos
syndromes. Evidence suggests that prophylactic treatment
should be considered for asymptomatic breaks, including
round operculated and atrophic holes prior to cataract surgery, laser capsulotomy and intravitreal injection, particularly when other risk factors are present.

{ Symptomatic breaks associated with an acute PVD are at
higher risk than asymptomatic breaks detected on routine
examination.

{ Size. Larger breaks carry a higher risk of progression.

{ Persistent vitreoretinal traction. An operculated tear, in
which the focus of vitreous traction has detached from
the break, is safer than a break, typically a U-tear, in which
traction persists. Round holes are rarely associated with
ongoing vitreoretinal traction. Apparent demonstration of
a complete PVD clinically or on ultrasonography, with no
residual attachment in the region of the break, is a favourable feature, though cannot be relied upon.

{ Shape. U-tears are at higher risk than round holes.

{ Location. Superior breaks are at higher risk of progression
to RD, probably due to the protective eоАЪect of gravity upon
inferior breaks. With superotemporal tears, the macula is
threatened early in the event of RD. Equatorial breaks are
more likely to progress than oral breaks, as the latter are
usually located within the vitreous base.

{ Pigmentation around a retinal break indicates chronicity
and a degree of stability.

Retinal Detachment 679
#### 16

of 2вАУ4 weeks, then 1вАУ3 months, then 6вАУ12 months, then annually. оАЮe presence of an intact bridging vessel overlying the
break may indicate ongoing vitreoretinal traction вАУ which may
also cause vitreous haemorrhage вАУ and treatment should be
considered.
оАБ Asymptomatic U-tears. оАЮe risk of progression to RD is low
at 5%, which is a similar rate to that in treated symptomatic
U-tears and observation as for operculated tears is generally
safe in the absence of factors indicating higher risk.
оАБ Traumatic retinal breaks, including acute dialyses, should
always be treated.
оАБ An asymptomatic dialysis can sometimes be followed without treatment. However, most cases are treated surgically if an
associated RD is present.
оАБ Asymptomatic subclinical RD. Progression is not invariable in RD discovered incidentally, with about 10% becoming
symptomatic over 2вАУ3 years and a decision regarding intervention should be made on a case-by-case basis. For example,
many practitioners would prefer to treat rather than observe a
large superotemporal RD that extends posterior to the equator,
but may observe a small inferior longstanding RD. With any
option, fully informed patient consent and regular review is
vital. Surgery is generally indicated if progression occurs.
оАБ Asymptomatic оАЯat round holes do not require prophylactic
treatment, but some guidelines recommend review every 1вАУ2
years.

TIP A patient with a symptomatic retinal break associated
with an acute PVD is at risk of RD and needs rapid treatment.

Treatment techniques

Retinal breaks without RD can be treated with laser (via a slit
lamp or binocular indirect ophthalmoscopy [BIO]) or cryotherapy. In most cases laser is the optimal technique as it is more
precise, causing less collateral retinal damage, with a lower risk
of epiretinal membrane formation. Adequate treatment of the
base of a very peripheral lesion may only be possible with BIO
or cryotherapy due to the requirement for indentation to visualize the area. Cryotherapy may be preferred for multiple contiguous tears or extensive lesions and in eyes with hazy media or
small pupils.
оАБ Laser retinopexy. Using slit lamp delivery under topical
anaesthesia (occasionally regional or even general anaesthesia is required), typical settings are a duration of 0.1 second,
a spot size of 200вАУ300 ќЉm with a three-mirror contact lens
or 100вАУ200 ќЉm with a wide-оАЬeld lens and a starting power
of 200mW. оАЮe power should be adjusted as appropriate to
obtain moderate blanching. With head-mounted BIO delivery, the spot size is estimated and adjusted by adjusting the
condensing lens (usually 20 D) position. оАЮe lesion is surrounded with two to three rows of conоАЭuent burns (Fig.
16.18). With both forms of laser, care should be taken to
identify appropriate landmarks frequently to avoid inadvertent macular damage.

{ Aphakia, now rare, confers a higher risk.
оАБ Acutely symptomatic U-tears. Up to 90% of these lead to RD.
Treatment (see below) reduces the risk to 5% so urgent intervention is needed.
оАБ Operculated tears, particularly in a person who is asymptomatic, are believed to be at low risk of progression to RD and
can safely be observed in most cases. A recommended review
schedule (symptomatic or asymptomatic) is an initial interval

оАБ Cryoretinopexy. Subconjunctival or regional anaesthesia is
commonly required. For lesions behind the equator, a small

its rubber sleeve. оАЮe instrument should initially be purged (e.g.
