10 seconds at вИТ25¬∞C, repeating aоАЫer 1 minute). оАЮe treatment
temperature is set (typically вИТ85¬∞C). It is useful to check the
eоАЪectiveness of the instrument by activating it in sterile water
for 10 seconds, when a 5-mm ice ball should form. Under BIO
visualization, the lesion is indented and the foot pedal depressed
until visible whitening of the retina is seen. It is critical not to
remove the tip from the treated area until thawing occurs (2вАУ3
seconds). Care should be taken to maintain orientation of the
probe whilst the tip is not visible and not to mistake indentation
by the shaоАЫ of the probe for that of the tip. оАЮe lesion is surrounded by a single row of applications, in most cases achieved
by one or two applications to a tear. оАЮe eye is usually padded
aоАЫerwards and oral analgesia is commonly prescribed.
оАБ AоАЮer treatment the patient should avoid strenuous physical
exertion for about a week until an adequate adhesion has formed
(Fig. 16.19). Review should usually take place aоАЫer 1вАУ2 weeks.

RHEGMATOGENOUS RETINAL
DETACHMENT

Introduction

Pathogenesis

Rhegmatogenous RD aоАЪects about 1 in 10000 of the population
each year, with both eyes eventually aоАЪected in about 10%. In most
cases it is characterized by the presence of a retinal break in concert with vitreoretinal traction that allows accumulation of liqueоАЬed vitreous under the neurosensory retina, separating it from the
RPE. Even though a retinal break is present, a RD will almost never
occur if the vitreous is not at least partially liqueоАЬed and traction
is absent. Over 40% of RDs occur in myopic eyes and the higher
the refractive error the greater the risk. Vitreous degeneration and
PVD and predisposing lesions such as lattice and snailtrack degeneration are more common in myopia. Highly myopic eyes are also at
risk from RD due to small round holes in chorioretinal atrophy and
from macular holes. Vitreous loss during cataract surgery and laser
capsulotomy also carries a greater risk of RD in highly myopic eyes.

IdentiоАЯcation of retinal breaks

оАБ Distribution of breaks in eyes with RD is approximately as follows:
60% superotemporal quadrant, 15% superonasal, 15% inferotemporal and 10% inferonasal. оАЮe upper temporal region should
therefore be examined in detail if a break cannot be detected initially. It should also be remembered that about 50% of eyes with
RD have more than one break, oоАЫen within 90¬∞ of each other.
оАБ ConоАЭguration of SRF. SRF spread is governed by (a) gravity,
by (b) anatomical limits (ora serrata and optic nerve) and by
(c) the location of the primary retinal break. If the primary
break is located superiorly, the SRF оАЬrst spreads inferiorly on
the same side of the fundus as the break and then superiorly
on the opposite side, so that the likely location of the primary
retinal break can be predicted.
оАБ ModiоАЭed LincoоАЬвАЩs rules:

{ A shallow inferior RD in which the SRF is slightly higher
on the temporal side points to a primary break located
inferiorly on that side (Fig. 16.20A).

{ A primary break located at 6 oвАЩclock will cause an inferior
RD with equal оАЭuid levels (Fig. 16.20B).

{ In a bullous inferior RD, the primary break usually lies
above the horizontal meridian (Fig. 16.20C).

{ If the primary break is located in the upper nasal quadrant
the SRF will revolve around the optic disc and then rise on
the temporal side until it is level with the primary break
(Fig. 16.20D).

{ A subtotal RD with a superior wedge of attached retina
points to a primary break located in the periphery nearest
its highest border (Fig. 16.20E).

{ When the SRF crosses the vertical midline above, the primary break is near to 12 oвАЩclock, the lower edge of the RD
corresponding to the side of the break (Fig. 16.20F).

CHAPTER

Retinal Detachment 681
#### 16

Symptoms

оАЮe classic premonitory symptoms reported in about 60% of patients
with spontaneous rhegmatogenous RD are оАЭashing lights and оАЭoaters
associated with acute PVD. AоАЫer a variable period of time a curtainlike relative peripheral visual оАЬeld defect may ensue and can progress
to involve central vision. In some patients this may not be present
on waking in the morning, due to spontaneous absorption of SRF
while inactive overnight, only to reappear later in the day. A lower
оАЬeld defect is usually appreciated more quickly by the patient than an
upper defect. оАЮe quadrant of the visual оАЬeld in which the оАЬeld defect
оАЬrst appears is useful in predicting the location of the primary retinal
break, which will be in the opposite quadrant. оАЮe location of photopsia is of no value in predicting the site of the primary break. Loss
of central vision may be due to involvement of the fovea by SRF or,
infrequently, obstruction of the visual axis by a large bullous RD.

Signs
General

оАБ Relative aоАЬerent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) is
present in an eye with an extensive RD.
оАБ Intraocular pressure (IOP) is oоАЫen lower by about 5 mmHg
compared with the normal eye. If the intraocular pressure is
extremely low, an associated choroidal detachment may be
present. It may be raised, characteristically in SchwartzвАУMatsuo
syndrome, in which RRD is associated with an apparent mild
anterior uveitis, oоАЫen due to a dialysis secondary to previous
blunt trauma in a young man. оАЮe aqueous cells are believed in
most cases to be displaced photoreceptor outer segments that
compromise trabecular outоАЭow. Both the aqueous вАШcellsвАЩ and
the elevated IOP typically resolve following repair of the RD.

оАБ Iritis is common but usually mild and should be diоАЪerentiated from SchwartzвАУMatsuo syndrome (above). Occasionally
it may be severe enough to cause posterior synechiae and the
underlying RD may be overlooked.
оАБ Tobacco dustвАЩ consisting of pigment cells is commonly seen
in the anterior vitreous (Fig. 16.21). Substantial vitreous blood
or inоАЭammatory cells are also highly speciоАЬc.
оАБ Retinal breaks (see Fig. 16.17) appear as discontinuities in the

between the sensory retina and underlying choroid. However, in
eyes with hypopigmented choroid (e.g. high myopia), the colour
contrast is decreased and small breaks may be overlooked.

described below.

Fresh retinal detachment

оАБ The RD has a convex configuration and a slightly opaque
and corrugated appearance as a result of retinal oedema
(Fig. 16.22AвАУC). There is loss of the underlying choroidal

оАБ SRF extends up to the ora serrata, except in the rare cases

оАБ Macular pseudohole. Because of the thinness of the foveal

Longstanding retinal detachment

оАБ Retinal thinning secondary to atrophy is a characteristic оАЬnding and should not lead to a misdiagnosis of retinoschisis.

оАБ Intraretinal cysts (Fig. 16.23AвАУC) may develop if the RD has
been present for about 1 year. оАЮese tend to disappear aоАЫer
retinal reattachment.
оАБ Subretinal demarcation lines (вАШhigh waterвАЩ or вАШtideвАЩ marks)
caused by proliferation of RPE cells at the junction of оАЭat and
detached retina (Fig. 16.23D) are common, taking about 3
months to develop. Pigmentation tends to decrease over time.
Although representing sites of increased adhesion, they do not
invariably limit the spread of SRF.

Proliferative vitreoretinopathy

Proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) is caused by epiretinal and
subretinal membrane formation, contraction of which leads to
tangential retinal traction and оАЬxed retinal fold formation (Fig.
16.24). Usually, PVR occurs following surgery for rhegmatogenous RD or penetrating injury, though it may also occur in eyes

684 Rhegmatogenous Retinal Detachment

A B

C D

Fig. 16.24 Development of proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR). (A) Extensive vitreous syneresis; (B) total retinal detachment without PVR; shrunken vitreous is condensed and attached
to the equator of the retina; (C) early PVR with anteriorly retracted vitreous gel and equatorial

with rhegmatogenous RD that have not had previous retinal surgery. оАЮe main features are retinal folds and rigidity so that retinal mobility induced by eye movements or scleral indentation is
decreased. Progression from one stage to the next is not inevitable.
оАБ Grade A (minimal) PVR is characterized by diоАЪuse vitreous haze
and tobacco dust. оАЮere may also be pigmented clumps on the inferior surface of the retina. Although these оАЬndings occur in many
eyes with RD, they are particularly severe in eyes with early PVR.
оАБ Grade B (moderate) PVR is characterized by wrinkling of the
inner retinal surface (Fig. 16.25A), decreased mobility of vitreous
gel, rolled edges of retinal breaks, tortuosity of blood vessels and
retinal stiоАЪness (Fig. 16.25B). оАЮe epiretinal membranes responsible for these оАЬndings typically cannot be identiоАЬed clinically.
оАБ Grade C (marked) PVR is characterized by rigid full-thickness
retinal folds (oоАЫen star-shaped) with heavy vitreous condensation and strands (Fig. 16.25C and D). It can be either anterior
(A) or posterior (P), the approximate dividing line being the
equator of the globe. оАЮe severity of proliferation in each area

is expressed by the number of clock hours of retina involved
although proliferations need not be contiguous.
оАБ Advanced disease shows gross reduction of retinal mobility
with retinal shortening and a characteristic funnel-like triangular conformation (see Fig. 16.24D).
оАБ Management. PVR is usually managed by undertaking a pars
plana vitrectomy, with removal of the membranes. Peeling
the internal limiting membrane (ILM) at the time of surgery
in patients with advanced PVR improves the success rate of
surgery. It is sometimes necessary to perform a relaxing retinotomy to relieve traction. PerоАЭuorocarbon liquid is used to
stabilize the retina, which is then replaced with long-acting gas
or silicone oil (see later in this chapter).

DiоАЮerential diagnosis

оАЮe tractional and exudative forms of RD are described later in
the chapter.

Degenerative retinoschisis

See above.

Choroidal detachment

Causes of choroidal detachment (also known as ciliochoroidal or
choroidal eоАЪusion) include hypotony, particularly following glaucoma drainage surgery (see Ch. 11), sulfonamide drugs such as
acetazolamide and topiramate, uveitis, posterior scleritis, choroidal tumours and a cyclodialysis cleоАЫ following trauma or surgery.
Occasionally it occurs secondary to RD. Idiopathic cases are generally labelled as uveal eоАЪusion syndrome (see below).
оАБ Symptoms. Photopsia and оАЭoaters are absent because there is
no vitreoretinal traction. A visual оАЬeld defect may be noticed if
the choroidal detachment is extensive.
оАБ Signs

{ Low intraocular pressure is common as a result of the
cause and of concomitant detachment of the ciliary body.

{ оАЮe elevations are brown, convex, smooth and relatively
immobile (Fig. 16.26A). Four lobes are typically present.
Temporal and nasal bullae tend to be most prominent.

{ Large вАШkissingвАЩ choroidal detachments may obscure the
view of the fundus.

urgency of intervention include the presence of a superior or
large break, from which SRF is likely to spread more rapidly and
advanced syneresis as is found in high myopia. Patients with dense
fresh vitreous haemorrhage in whom visualization of the fundus is
impossible should also be operated on as soon as possible if B-scan
ultrasonography shows an underlying RD. оАЮe patient should not
eat or drink if urgent surgery is contemplated. Minimizing activity may be helpful and bed-rest with the head turned so that the
retinal break is in the most dependent position tends to reduce the
amount of SRF and therefore facilitates surgery.

TIP Urgent surgery is needed in a patient with an acute
progressive retinal detachment if the macula is threatened.
Even if the macula is oоАЮ the visual results are better if the
surgery is done within 72 hours.

Pneumatic retinopexy

Pneumatic retinopexy (Fig. 16.27) is an outpatient procedure in
which an intravitreal gas bubble together with cryotherapy or laser
are used to seal a retinal break and reattach the retina without scleral
buckling. оАЮe most frequently used gases are sulfur hexaоАЭuoride
(SF6) and the longer-acting perоАЭuoropropane (C3F8) (Fig. 16.28).
It has the advantage of being a relatively quick, minimally invasive,
вАШoоАЩce-basedвАЩ procedure. However, success rates are worse than those
achievable with conventional scleral buckling. оАЮe procedure is usually reserved for treatment of uncomplicated RD with a small retinal
break or a cluster of breaks extending over an area of less than two
clock hours in the upper two-thirds of the peripheral retina.

Principles of scleral buckling

Scleral buckling, sometimes referred to as conventional or external RD surgery as opposed to the internal approach of pars plana
vitrectomy (see below), is a surgical procedure in which material sutured onto the sclera (explant) creates an inward indentation (buckle вАУ Fig. 16.29). оАЮe purpose is to close retinal breaks
by opposing the RPE to the sensory retina and to reduce dynamic
vitreoretinal traction at sites of local vitreoretinal adhesion. оАЮis
technique should always be used for patients with a detachment of
the retina secondary to a post-traumatic dialysis.
оАБ Explants are made from soоАЫ or hard silicone. оАЮe entire break
should ideally be surrounded by about 2 mm of buckle. It is
also important for the buckle to involve the area of the vitreous
base anterior to the tear in order to prevent the possibility of
subsequent reopening of the tear and anterior leakage of SRF.
оАЮe dimensions of the retinal break can be assessed by comparing it with the diameter of the optic disc.
оАБ Buckle conоАЭguration can be radial, segmental, circumferential or encircling, depending on the size, conоАЬguration and
number of breaks.
оАБ Technique. оАЮe conjunctiva is incised (peritomy) to facilitate access, following which retinal breaks are localized (Fig.
16.30A) and cryotherapy applied. An explant of appropriate
dimensions and orientation is then sutured to the sclera and
the position of the buckle checked in relation to the break (Fig.
16.30BвАУD).

{ оАЮe elevations do not extend to the posterior pole because
they are limited by the vortex veins entering their scleral
canals (Fig. 16.26B). However, in contrast to RDs, they
extend anteriorly beyond the ora serrata.
оАБ Treatment is directed at the cause. Drainage via partialthickness sclerectomies is occasionally required.

Uveal eоАЮusion syndrome

оАЮe uveal eоАЪusion syndrome is a rare idiopathic, oоАЫen bilateral,
condition that most frequently aоАЪects middle-aged hypermetropic
men but can occur in association with nanophthalmos. оАЮe cause
is thought to be impairment of normal оАЭuid drainage from the
choroid via the sclera (which is sometimes of abnormal thickness
and composition) or vortex veins.
оАБ Signs

{ InоАЭammation is absent or mild.

{ Ciliochoroidal detachment followed by exudative RD.

{ Following resolution, the RPE frequently shows a characteristic residual вАШleopard spotвАЩ mottling caused by
degenerative changes in the RPE associated with a high
concentration of protein in the SRF.
оАБ DiоАЬerential diagnosis includes uveal eоАЪusion secondary to
other causes (see above), choroidal haemorrhage and ring
melanoma of the anterior choroid.
оАБ Treatment is usually with full-thickness sclerectomy, particularly in patients with nanophthalmos.

Surgery
Indications for urgent surgery

In general, an acutely symptomatic RD should be surgically
repaired as soon as possible, particularly if the macula is not
involved (see Fig. 16.22A). Other factors that may increase the

Drainage of subretinal оАЭuid

Drainage of SRF via the sclera (e.g. the D-ACE: Drainage-AirCryotherapy-Explant) surgical technique (Fig. 16.31A) is advocated by many practitioners, citing more rapid retinal reattachment
in the presence of deep or longstanding viscous SRF. Others prefer
to avoid external drainage because of the potential complications
associated with the technique, such as retinal perforation or incarceration in the drainage site (Fig. 16.31B) and choroidal haemorrhage (Fig. 16.31C and D) and would rather perform a pars plana
vitrectomy as a primary option.

Complications of scleral buckling

оАБ Diplopia due to the mechanical eоАЪect of the buckle is common. Early spontaneous resolution is typical, though intervention is sometimes necessary.
оАБ Cystoid macular oedema occurs in up to 25%, but usually
responds to treatment. Other macular complications include
epiretinal membrane (around 15%), persistent subfoveal

CHAPTER

Retinal Detachment 689

оАЭuid and foveal structural disruption, usually in macula-oоАЪ
detachments.
оАБ Anterior segment ischaemia due to vascular compromise. оАЮis is a particular risk with an encircling band
and with predisposing systemic conditions such as sickle
haemoglobinopathies.
оАБ Buckle extrusion, intrusion or infection (Fig. 16.32).
Removal is usually required, with intensive antibiotic therapy
if needed.
оАБ Elevated IOP. An early IOP spike usually resolves rapidly, but
occasionally can persist. Angle closure can occur.
оАБ Choroidal detachment usually resolves spontaneously, presumably as scleral oedema settles and allows improved vortex
vein function.
оАБ Surgical failure

{ Missed breaks. A thorough search should always be made
for the presence of multiple breaks, particularly if the conоАЬguration of the RD does not correspond to the position of
the primary break.

{ Buckle failure may occur due to inadequate size, incorrect positioning (Fig. 16.33AвАУD), or inadequate height.
оАЮe explant may have to be replaced or repositioned to
address the оАЬrst two, but drainage of SRF or intravitreal

{ Proliferative vitreoretinopathy is the most common cause
of late failure. оАЮe tractional forces associated with PVR
can occasionally open old breaks and create new ones. Presentation is typically several weeks postoperatively with
re-detachment.

{ Reopening of a retinal break in the absence of PVR can
occur as a result of inadequate cryotherapy or scleral buckling, or sometimes when buckle height decreases either
with time or following late surgical removal.

TIP The three commonest causes of failed retinal detachment
surgery are: proliferative vitreoretinopathy, failure to close all
breaks and the development of new breaks.

Pars plana vitrectomy

PPV is discussed later in this chapter.

TRACTIONAL RETINAL DETACHMENT

оАЮe main causes of tractional RD are proliferative retinopathy
secondary to diabetes, retinopathy of prematurity and penetrating
posterior segment trauma (see Ch. 22).

Pathogenesis of diabetic tractional retinal
detachment

Tractional RD is caused by progressive contraction of оАЬbrovascular
membranes over large areas of vitreoretinal adhesion. In contrast
to acute PVD in eyes with rhegmatogenous RD, PVD in diabetic
eyes is gradual and frequently incomplete. It is thought to be
caused by leakage of plasma constituents into the vitreous gel from
a оАЬbrovascular network adherent to the posterior vitreous surface.
Owing to the strong adhesions of the cortical vitreous to areas of
оАЬbrovascular proliferation, PVD is usually incomplete. In the rare
event of a subsequent complete PVD, the new blood vessels are
avulsed and RD does not develop. Vitreoretinal traction can be any
of the following:
оАБ Tangential, caused by the contraction of epiretinal оАЬbrovascular membranes with puckering of the retina and distortion of
blood vessels.
оАБ Anteroposterior, due to the contraction of оАЬbrovascular
membranes extending from the posterior retina, usually in
association with the major arcades, to the vitreous base anteriorly and/or bridging (trampoline).
оАБ оАЫe result of contraction of оАЬbrovascular membranes stretching from one part of the retina to another or between vascular
arcades, tending to pull the two involved points together.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Photopsia and оАЭoaters are usually absent because
vitreoretinal traction develops insidiously and is not associated
with acute PVD. A visual оАЬeld defect usually progresses slowly
and may be stable for months or even years.
оАБ Signs (Fig. 16.34A)

{ оАЮe RD has a concave conоАЬguration and breaks are absent.

{ Retinal mobility is severely reduced and shiоАЫing оАЭuid is
absent.

EXUDATIVE RETINAL DETACHMENT

Pathogenesis

Exudative RD is characterized by the accumulation of SRF in the
absence of retinal breaks or traction. It may occur in a variety
of vascular, inоАЭammatory and neoplastic diseases involving the
retina, RPE and choroid in which оАЭuid leaks outside the vessels
and accumulates under the retina. As long as the RPE is able to
compensate by pumping the leaking оАЭuid into the choroidal circulation, RD does not occur. However, when the mechanism is overwhelmed or functions less than normally, оАЭuid accumulates in the
subretinal space. Causes include:
оАБ Choroidal tumours such as melanoma, haemangioma and
metastases. An intraocular tumour should be considered the
cause of an exudative RD until proved otherwise.
оАБ InоАЯammation such as Harada disease and posterior
scleritis.
оАБ Bullous central serous chorioretinopathy is a rare cause.
оАБ Iatrogenic causes include RD surgery and panretinal
photocoagulation.
оАБ Choroidal neovascularization, which may leak and give rise to
extensive subretinal accumulation of оАЭuid at the posterior pole.
оАБ Hypertensive choroidopathy, as may occur in toxaemia of
pregnancy, is a very rare cause.
оАБ Idiopathic, such as uveal eоАЪusion syndrome (see above).

TIP An exudative retinal detachment (which is often caused by
a choroidal tumour) is characterized by вАШshiftingвАЩ subretinal оАЭuid.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Depending on the cause, both eyes may be
involved simultaneously.

{ оАЮere is no vitreoretinal traction, so photopsia is absent.

{ Floaters may be present if there is associated vitritis.

{ A visual оАЬeld defect may develop suddenly and progress
rapidly.
оАБ Signs

{ The RD has a convex configuration, as with a rhegmatogenous RD, but its surface is smooth and not
corrugated.

{ оАЮe detached retina is very mobile and exhibits the phenomenon of вАШshiоАЫing оАЭuidвАЩ in which SRF detaches the
area of retina under which it accumulates (Fig. 16.35A
and B). For example, in the upright position the SRF
collects under the inferior retina, but on assuming the
supine position for several minutes, the inferior retina
оАЭattens and SRF shiоАЫs posteriorly, detaching the superior
retina.

{ оАЮe cause of the RD, such as a choroidal tumour (Fig.
16.36A), may be apparent when the fundus is examined
or on B-scan ultrasonography, or the patient may have an
associated systemic disease responsible for the RD (e.g.
Harada disease, toxaemia of pregnancy).

{ вАШLeopard spotsвАЩ consisting of scattered areas of subretinal
pigment clumping may be seen aоАЫer the detachment has
оАЭattened (Fig. 16.36B).

Treatment

Treatment depends on the cause. Some cases resolve spontaneously, whilst others are treated with systemic corticosteroids

(Harada disease and posterior scleritis). In some eyes with bullous
central serous chorioretinopathy, the leak in the RPE can be sealed
by laser photocoagulation.

PARS PLANA VITRECTOMY

Introduction

Instrumentation

оАЮe diameter of the shaоАЫ of vitrectomy instrumentation has conventionally been 0.9 mm (20-gauge), but smaller 23-gauge, 25-gauge and
even 27-gauge sutureless systems (transconjunctival microincision
vitrectomy вАУ MIVS) are increasingly becoming the standard of care,
oоАЪering shorter operating time, less trauma and scarring, and faster
rehabilitation. Early concerns about an increased risk of postoperative
endophthalmitis do not seem to have been borne out and operative
success rates are comparable to larger-gauge instrumentation.

оАБ Intraocular illumination is supplied by a оАЬbreoptic probe
(light pipe вАУ see Fig. 16.37A). Initially, reduced illumination
was a problem with the narrow calibre MIVS systems, because
of the limitations of halogen bulbs. Brighter sources consisting of xenon and potentially less phototoxic mercury vapour
sources have been introduced that match or exceed the levels of 20-gauge systems. оАЮese minimize retinal phototoxicity
by оАЬltering out higher-energy ultraviolet and blue light. Some
systems oоАЪer adjustable lighting characteristics to increase the
contrast of particular structures. Wide-angle lighting and selfretaining вАШchandelierвАЩ sources are available вАУ the latter oоАЪer the
advantages of freeing both of the surgeonвАЩs hands to carry out
true bimanual surgery (which can be particularly useful in
challenging cases) and of reducing phototoxicity (by increasing the working distance of the light probe from the retina).
Extremely small-gauge dual chandelier probes are available to
eliminate shadowing from the working оАЬeld.

694 Pars Plana Vitrectomy

A

B

C

Fig. 16.37 (A) Illumination pipe; (B) cutter; (C) micro-forceps. Fig. 16.38 Viewing system for pars plana vitrectomy.

оАБ Vitreous cutter (Fig. 16.37B) has an inner guillotine blade
that oscillates at very high speed (from 1500 up to 5000вАУ7500
cuts per minute [cpm] in the latest вАШultra-high speedвАЩ cutters),
cutting the vitreous gel into tiny pieces and simultaneously
removing it by suction into a collecting cassette. Faster cutting speeds translate into a lower level of traction exerted on
the vitreoretinal interface during surgery. Safer more eоАЩcient
оАЭuidic control has been introduced in tandem.
оАБ An infusion cannula is required in order to maintain the
vitreous cavity pressure and volume. оАЮese are generally selfretaining in small-gauge systems.
оАБ Accessory instruments include scissors, forceps (Fig. 16.37C),
оАЭute needle and endodiathermy and endolaser delivery systems.
With the introduction of smaller-calibre cutters that are able to
manipulate оАЬbrovascular membranes more precisely and safely,
the requirement for additional instrumentation may decrease.
оАБ Wide-angle viewing systems consist of an indirect lens
beneath the operating microscope and an incorporated series
of prisms to reinvert the image (Fig. 16.38). оАЮe оАЬeld of view
extends almost to the ora serrata. Higher magniоАЬcation smaller
оАЬeld lenses are available for macular surgery.

Tamponading agents

оАЮese achieve intraoperative retinal оАЭattening in combination with
internal drainage of SRF and are commonly used postoperatively
for internal tamponade of retinal breaks.
оАБ Expanding gases. Although air can be used, an expanding gas
is usually preferred in order to achieve prolonged tamponade.
Postoperative positioning of the patient is used to maximize the
eоАЪective vector and maintain surface tension around the break.

{ Sulfur hexaоАЭuoride (SF6) doubles its volume if used at a
100% concentration and lasts 10вАУ14 days.

{ PerоАЭuoroethane (C2F6) triples its volume at 100% and lasts
30вАУ35 days.

20вАУ30% for SF6 and 12вАУ16% for C3F8). Low-pressure environments, typically air travel and nitrous oxide anaesthesia,
must be avoided until gas absorption is complete, as these will
increase the intraocular gas pressure.

TIP If gas has been used at the time of RD repair, air travel
should be avoided because the gas bubble can expand with a
change in atmospheric pressure and cause an acute rise in IOP.

оАБ Silicone oils have low speciоАЬc gravity вАУ they are lighter than
water and thus buoyant. оАЮey are commonly used for both
intraoperative retinal manipulation and prolonged postoperative intraocular tamponade and are particularly helpful in the
management of proliferative vitreoretinopathy. 1000 cs silicone is easier to inject and to remove whilst 5000 cs is more
viscous but may be less prone to emulsiоАЬcation.
оАБ Heavy liquids (perоАЭuorocarbons) have high speciоАЬc gravity and
thus settle inferiorly in the vitreous cavity (Fig. 16.39). PerоАЭuoron-octane is most commonly used as it allows good visibility and
low viscosity. It acts as an intraoperative mechanical device and
can be used to оАЭatten the posterior retina, open retinal folds
and improve visualization of additional membranes. Although
primarily developed for RD surgery, other indications include
haemostasis (by localizing bleeding), expression of liqueоАЬed
blood from behind the retina and anterior оАЭoating of dislocated
lens fragments or intraocular lens (IOL). Cases of retinal toxicity and inоАЭammation have been reported.

Indications

Although many simple rhegmatogenous RD can be treated successfully by scleral buckling techniques, PPV has greatly improved
the prognosis for more complex detachments. As techniques have
improved and surgeonsвАЩ familiarity and conоАЬdence has grown, the
threshold for vitrectomy surgery has fallen. Many vitreoretinal surgeons now feel that morbidity and success rates are better with vitrectomy than with scleral buckling for all pseudophakic and aphakic
RD and for those that would otherwise require external drainage of

{ PerоАЭuoropropane (C3F8) quadruples its volume at 100%
and lasts 55вАУ65 days.
оАБ Gas concentration. Because the eye is usually leоАЫ almost
entirely gas-оАЬlled at the end of the procedure, most are used
at only an isovolumetric (non-expansile) concentration (e.g.

SRF. A recent survey of vitreoretinal trends in the USA reveals that
more than 80% of primary RD surgery is undertaken using a PPV

Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment

оАБ When retinal breaks cannot be visualized as a result of haemorrhage, vitreous debris, posterior capsular opacity, IOL edge eоАЪects.
Vitrectomy is crucial to provide an adequate retinal view. Scleral
buckling carries a high risk of failure if any breaks are missed.
оАБ When retinal breaks are unlikely to be closed by scleral
buckling, such as giant tears, large posterior breaks and in the
presence of PVR (Fig. 16.40AвАУC).

Tractional retinal detachment

оАБ Indications in diabetic RD

{ Tractional RD threatening or involving the macula (Fig.
16.40D). Vitrectomy is always combined with anti-VEGF
agents and/or internal panretinal photocoagulation to
prevent postoperative neovascularization that may cause
vitreous haemorrhage or rubeosis iridis. Extramacular
tractional RD may be observed without surgery because,
in many cases, it remains stationary for a long time provided proliferative retinopathy has been controlled.

Technique
Goals of surgery

оАБ To separate the posterior hyaloid from the retinal surface.
оАБ To remove the epiretinal tissue in order to release the central
and/or peripheral retinal traction.
оАБ To close retinal breaks if present.

Basic vitrectomy

оАБ An infusion cannula is inserted (3.5 mm behind the limbus in
pseudophakic or aphakic eyes and 4 mm in phakic eyes) at the
level of the inferior border of the lateral rectus muscle. Limbal peritomy (conjunctival dissection) is required for conventional large-gauge systems, but is unnecessary in small-gauge
systems.
оАБ Further sclerotomies are made at the 10 and 2 oвАЩclock positions, through which the vitreous cutter and оАЬbreoptic probe
are introduced (Fig. 16.41). оАЮese sclerotomies are self-sealing
with modern small-gauge systems, though wound leak occasionally occurs (Fig. 16.42).
оАБ Excision. оАЮe central vitreous gel and posterior hyaloid face
are excised.
оАБ Two types of epithelial membrane are encountered:

{ Combined tractionalвАУrhegmatogenous RD should be
treated urgently, even if the macula is not involved, because
SRF is likely to spread quickly.
оАБ Indications in penetrating trauma

{ Non-vascular membranes found in PVR and macular
pucker.

{ Prevention of tractional RD. Unlike diabetic retinopathy
where epiretinal membrane proliferation occurs mostly on
the posterior retina, оАЬbrocellular proliferation aоАЫer penetrating trauma tends to develop on the pre-equatorial retina
and/or the ciliary body. Treatment is usually aimed at visual
rehabilitation and minimizing the tractional process.

{ Late tractional RD, which may be associated with an intraocular foreign body or retinal incarceration, occasionally
develops months aоАЫer otherwise successful surgery.

{ Fibrovascular membranes found in patients with proliferative diabetic retinopathy.
оАБ оАЫe surgical techniques employed involve delamination, segmentation and en-bloc dissection.
оАБ Intraoperative tissue staining can be helpful. In particular
trypan blue can be used to stain an epiretinal membrane and
triamcinolone to stain vitreous.
оАБ оАЫe above basic steps apply to all vitrectomy surgery. Subsequent steps depend on the speciоАЬc indication.
оАБ Sutures. Transconjunctival small-gauge systems do not require
postoperative suturing.

Proliferative vitreoretinopathy

оАЮe aims of surgery in PVR are to release both transvitreal traction by vitrectomy and tangential (surface) traction by membrane
dissection in order to restore retinal mobility and allow closure of
retinal breaks.
Localized оАЬxed retinal folds may be freed by the removal of the
central plaque of epiretinal membrane. оАЮis can usually be achieved
by engaging the tip of vertically cutting scissors or a pick-type
instrument in the edge of a valley between two adjacent folds (Fig.
16.43). оАЮe membrane is either surgically dissected or simply peeled
from the surface of the retina. Small-gauge cutters may be used in
some cases to engage membranes directly and forceps can facilitate
this. A relieving retinotomy should be considered if the mobility of
the retina is believed to be insuоАЩcient for sustained reattachment.

Tractional retinal detachment

оАЮe goal of vitrectomy in tractional RDs is to release anteroposterior
and/or circumferential vitreoretinal traction. Because the membranes
are vascularized and the retina oоАЫen friable, they cannot be simply
peeled from the surface of the retina as this would result in haemorrhage and tearing of the retina. оАЮe three methods of removing оАЬbrovascular membranes in diabetic tractional RDs are the following:
оАБ Delamination involves the horizontal cutting of individual
vascular pegs connecting a membrane to the surface of the
retina (Fig. 16.44A). оАЮis allows the complete removal of оАЬbrovascular tissue from the retinal surface (Fig. 16.44B).
оАБ Segmentation involves the vertical cutting of epiretinal membranes into small segments (Fig. 16.44C and D). It is used to
release circumferential vitreoretinal traction when delamination is diоАЩcult or impossible.
оАБ En-bloc dissection. оАЮe key step is to separate posterior hyaloid from the retina. An incision is made in the epiretinal membrane using an illuminated pick. оАЮe membrane is then peeled
away using an end-gripping membrane forceps (Fig. 16.45).

Complications

оАЮe rate of complications has decreased signiоАЬcantly in recent
years because of technological advances and specialized training
in the оАЬeld. Complications can nevertheless occur intraoperatively
and postoperatively.

Intraoperative complications

оАБ Posterior retinal breaks.
оАБ Peripheral retinal breaks.
оАБ Rarely, choroidal haemorrhage.

Postoperative complications

оАБ Retinal breaks and rhegmatogenous RD.
оАБ Retinal fold (Fig. 16.46). оАЮis is less likely to occur by placing
the patient face down immediately aоАЫer surgery.
оАБ InоАЯammation, particularly in patients with diabetes.
оАБ Raised intraocular pressure.

{ Overexpansion of intraocular gas, usually when the concentration or volume of expansile gas is inadvertently too
great. Medical measures alone may be suоАЩcient in some

CHAPTER

Retinal Detachment 697
#### 16

Fig. 16.43 Dissection of retinal folds in proliferative vitreoretinopathy.

cases, but if a substantial elevation of IOP occurs excess gas
can be removed at the slit lamp via the pars plana by using
a 30-gauge needle on a 1 ml syringe.

TIP Excessive concentrations of intraocular gas can result in
a signiоАЯcant rise in IOP. Excess gas can be removed at the slit
lamp by inserting a 30-gauge needle into the vitreous cavity via
the pars plana.

{ Silicone oil-associated glaucoma. Early glaucoma may
be caused by direct pupillary block by silicone oil (Fig.
16.47A). оАЮis occurs particularly in an aphakic eye with
an intact iris diaphragm. In aphakic eyes this can be prevented by performing an inferior (Ando) iridectomy at
the time of surgery to allow free passage of aqueous to the
anterior chamber. Intraocular gas can also cause pupillary block. Late glaucoma is caused by emulsiоАЬed silicone
in the anterior chamber (Fig. 16.47B) causing trabecular
obstruction and scarring. оАЮe risk may be reduced by early
oil removal, though glaucoma can still occur. A long tube
seton may be required (Fig. 16.47C).

{ Other mechanisms include ghost cell, inоАЭammatory and
steroid-induced glaucoma (see Ch. 11). Angle closure can
also result from ciliochoroidal eоАЪusion with anterior rotation of the lensвАУiris diaphragm, which may respond to
cycloplegia and steroids.
оАБ Cataract

{ Gas-induced. A large or long-lasting intravitreal gas bubble typically gives rise to feathering of the posterior subcapsular lens, but this is usually transient.

{ Silicone-induced. Almost all phakic eyes with silicone oil
eventually develop cataract (Fig. 16.47D).

{ Delayed Following vitrectomy, substantial nuclear sclerosis commonly develops, especially if the patient is over 50
years of age.
оАБ Band keratopathy is not uncommon with extended silicone
oil tamponade.

#### Vitreous Opacities
# 17

701

702 KanskiвАЩs Clinical Ophthalmology

Introduction

оАЯe vitreous is a transparent extracellular gel consisting of collagen, soluble proteins, hyaluronic acid and water. оАЯe total vitreous
volume is approximately 4.0 ml. оАЯe few cells normally present in
the gel are located predominantly in the cortex and include hyalocytes, astrocytes and glial cells. оАЯe vitreous provides structural
support to the globe while allowing a clear and optically uniform
path to the retina. Once liqueоАЮed or surgically removed it does not
re-form. Vitreous opacities can be caused by a variety of pathological processes primarily involving other ocular sites. Apart from
vitreous haemorrhage, the conditions discussed below are those in
which the vitreous gel is the primary site of pathology.

Muscae volitantes

Muscae volitantes (Latin for вАШhovering оАЭiesвАЩ), commonly referred
to as вАШоАЭoatersвАЩ, is an ubiquitous entoptic phenomenon of оАЭy-, cobweb- or thread-like condensations that are best seen against a pale
background. It is thought to predominantly represent tiny embryological remnants in the vitreous gel. A sudden exacerbation can
occur due to vitreous haemorrhage or, more commonly, a change
in the conformation of the gel, such as a posterior vitreous detachment (see Fig. 16.15).

Vitreous haemorrhage

Vitreous haemorrhage is a common condition with many
causes (Table 17.1). Symptoms vary according to severity. Mild
haemorrhage (Fig. 17.1A) causes sudden onset floaters and diffuse blurring of vision, but may not affect visual acuity, whilst a
dense bleed may result in severe visual loss (Fig. 17.1B). B-scan
ultrasonography in non-clotted vitreous haemorrhage generally shows a uniform appearance and once cellular aggregates
develop, small particulate echoes become visible (Fig. 17.1C).
Ultrasonography is critical in the evaluation of an eye with
dense vitreous haemorrhage to exclude an underlying retinal
tear or detachment (Fig. 17.1D). Treatment is dictated by severity and cause, but an increasingly low threshold is being adopted
for early vitrectomy (see Ch. 16) in cases of dense haemorrhage.

TIP Undertake ultrasound investigation in an eye with a
dense vitreous haemorrhage to exclude a retinal detachment or
choroidal melanoma.

Terson syndrome

Terson syndrome refers to the combination of intraocular and
subarachnoid haemorrhage secondary to aneurysmal rupture,
most commonly arising from the anterior communicating artery.
However, intraocular haemorrhage may also occur with subdural
haematoma and acute elevation of intracranial pressure from other
causes. оАЯe haemorrhage is frequently bilateral and is typically
intraretinal and/or preretinal (Fig. 17.2), although occasionally
subhyaloid blood may break into the vitreous. It is probable that
intraocular bleeding is due to retinal venous stasis secondary to

Table 17.1 Causes of Vitreous Haemorrhage

вАҐоАБ Acute posterior vitreous detachment associated either
with a retinal tear or avulsion of a peripheral vessel
вАҐоАБ Proliferative retinopathy
вАҐоАБ Diabetic
вАҐоАБ Retinal vein occlusion
вАҐоАБ Sickle cell disease
вАҐоАБ Eales disease
вАҐоАБ Vasculitis
вАҐоАБ Miscellaneous retinal disorders
вАҐоАБ Macroaneurysm
вАҐоАБ Telangiectasia
вАҐоАБ Capillary haemangioma
вАҐоАБ Trauma
вАҐоАБ Systemic
вАҐоАБ Bleeding disorders
вАҐоАБ Terson syndrome

increase in cavernous sinus pressure. Vitreous haemorrhage usually resolves spontaneously within a few months and the long-term
visual prognosis is good in most. Early vitrectomy may be considered in some cases.

Asteroid hyalosis

Asteroid hyalosis is a common degenerative process in which calcium pyrophosphate particles collect within the vitreous gel. It
is seen clinically as numerous tiny round yellowвАУwhite opacities
of varying size and density (Fig. 17.3A and B). оАЯese move with
the vitreous during eye movements but do not sediment inferiorly when the eye is immobile. Only one eye is aоАЬected in 75%
of patients. It rarely causes visual problems and the majority of
patients are asymptomatic. An association with diabetes has been
suggested, but is unproven. оАЯe prevalence of asteroid hyalosis
increases with age and aоАЬects 3% of those aged 75вАУ86 years. It is
more common in men than in women. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) (Fig. 17.3C) and ultrasonography (Fig. 17.3D) show
high reоАЭectivity foci.

TIP Because asteroid hyalosis rarely causes visual symptoms,
dual pathology should be considered if the visual acuity is
reduced.

Synchysis scintillans

Synchysis (synchisis) scintillans occurs as a consequence of chronic
vitreous haemorrhage, oоАЫen in a blind eye. оАЯe condition is usually discovered when frank haemorrhage is no longer present. оАЯe
crystals are composed of cholesterol and are derived from plasma
cells or degraded products of erythrocytes and lie either freely or
engulfed within foreign body giant cells.
Numerous оАЭat golden-brown refractile particles are seen (Fig.
17.4A), which tend to sediment inferiorly when the eye is immobile. Occasionally the anterior chamber may also be involved (Fig.
17.4B).

Amyloidosis

Amyloidosis is a localized or systemic condition in which there
is extracellular deposition of оАЮbrillary protein. Vitreous involvement typically occurs in familial amyloidosis, also characterized by polyneuropathy, prominent corneal nerves and pupillary
light-near dissociation. Vitreous opacities may be unilateral or
bilateral and are initially perivascular. Later they involve the
anterior gel and take on a characteristic sheet-like (вАШglass woolвАЩ)
appearance (Fig. 17.5A). оАЯe opacities may become attached to
the posterior lens by thick footplates (Fig. 17.5B). Dense opaciоАЮcation resulting in signiоАЮcant visual impairment may require
vitrectomy.

Vitreous cyst

Vitreous cysts can be congenital or acquired, acquired cysts being
caused by a range of pathology such as trauma and inоАЭammation.
Congenital cysts are pigmented or non-pigmented, the former
usually arising from the ciliary body pigment epithelium, the latter from remnants of the primary hyaloid vascular system. оАЯey
are generally оАЮxed вАУ non-pigmented cysts are typically attached
to the optic disc вАУ but can be found оАЭoating freely in the posterior
(occasionally anterior) segment (Fig. 17.6). Treatment is seldom
required, but laser cystotomy or vitrectomy can be performed for
troublesome symptoms.

Persistent fetal vasculature

In addition to non-pigmented vitreous cysts, remnants of the
hyaloid vessels can form a Bergmeister papilla, seen as a tuоАЫ
at the optic disc (Fig. 17.7), a Mittendorf dot on the posterior
lens surface and the more marked manifestations for which the
term persistent fetal vasculature is generally reserved, previously

Fig. 17.6 Vitreous cyst.

Fig. 17.7 Bergmeister papilla.

706 KanskiвАЩs Clinical Ophthalmology

Fig. 17.8 Congenital vitreous veil.

termed persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (see Ch. 20). A
congenital vitreous veil is a translucent, curtain-like structure
found in normal vitreous, which may be vascularized (Fig. 17.8).
It is extremely rare and is of unknown aetiology. Treatment is
not needed.

708 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

DeоАЯnitions

оАБ оАЯe visual axis passes from the fovea, through the nodal point
of the eye, to the point of оАЮxation. In normal binocular single
vision (BSV) the visual axes of the two eyes intersect at the
point of оАЮxation, the images being aligned by the fusion reоАЭex
and combined by binocular responsive cells in the visual cortex to give BSV.
оАБ Orthophoria implies perfect ocular alignment in the absence
of any stimulus for fusion; this is uncommon.
оАБ Heterophoria (вАШphoriaвАЩ) implies a tendency of the eyes to
deviate when fusion is blocked (latent squint).

{ Slight phoria is present in most normal individuals and is
overcome by the fusion reоАЭex. оАЬe phoria can be either a
small inward imbalance (esophoria) or an outward imbalance (exophoria).

{ When fusion is insuоАЫcient to control the imbalance, the
phoria is described as decompensating and is oоАЪen associated with symptoms of binocular discomfort (asthenopia)
or double vision (diplopia).
оАБ Heterotropia (вАШtropiaвАЩ) implies a manifest deviation in which
the visual axes do not intersect at the point of оАЮxation.

{ оАЬe images from the two eyes are misaligned so that either
double vision is present or, more commonly in children,
the image from the deviating eye is suppressed at cortical
level.

{ A childhood squint may occur because of failure of the
normal development of binocular fusion mechanisms or
as a result of oculomotor imbalance secondary to a diоАЩerence in refraction between the two eyes (anisometropia).

{ Failure of fusion, for example secondary to poor vision in
one eye, may cause heterotropia in adulthood, or a squint
may develop because of weakness or mechanical restriction of the extraocular muscles, or damage to their nerve
supply.

{ Horizontal deviation of the eyes (latent or manifest) is the
most common form of strabismus.

{ Upward displacement of one eye relative to the other is
termed a hypertropia and a latent upward imbalance a
hyperphoria.

{ A large angle kappa may give the appearance of a squint
when none is present (pseudo-squint) and is seen most
commonly as a pseudoexotropia following displacement of
the macula in retinopathy of prematurity, where the angle
may signiоАЮcantly exceed +5¬∞ (Fig. 18.1BвАУD).

Anatomy of the extraocular muscles

Principles

оАЬe lateral and medial orbital walls are at an angle of 45¬∞ with each
other. оАЬe orbital axis therefore forms an angle of 22.5¬∞ with both
lateral and medial walls, though for the sake of simplicity this angle
is usually regarded as being 23¬∞ (Fig. 18.2A). When the eye is looking straight ahead at a оАЮxed point on the horizon with the head
erect (primary position of gaze), the visual axis forms an angle of
23¬∞ with the orbital axis (Fig. 18.2B). оАЬe actions of the extraocular
muscles depend on the position of the globe at the time of muscle
contraction (Fig. 18.2C and D).
оАБ оАЯe primary action of a muscle is its major eоАЩect when the eye
is in the primary position.
оАБ Subsidiary actions are the additional eоАЩects, which depend on
the position of the eye.
оАБ оАЯe Listing plane is an imaginary coronal plane passing
through the centre of rotation of the globe. оАЬe globe rotates
on the axes of Fick, which intersect in the Listing plane (Fig.
18.3).

{ оАЬe globe rotates leоАЪ and right on the vertical Z axis.

{ оАЬe globe moves up and down on the horizontal X axis.

{ Torsional movements (wheel rotations) occur on the Y
(sagittal) axis which traverses the globe from front to back
(similar to the anatomical axis of the eye).

{ Downward displacement is termed a hypotropia and a
latent imbalance a hypophoria.
оАБ оАЯe anatomical axis is a line passing from the posterior pole
through the centre of the cornea. Because the fovea is usually
slightly temporal to the anatomical centre of the posterior pole
of the eye, the visual axis does not usually correspond to the
anatomical axis of the eye.
оАБ Angle kappa is the angle, usually about 5¬∞, subtended by the
visual and anatomical axes (Fig. 18.1).

{ оАЬe angle is positive (normal) when the fovea is temporal to the centre of the posterior pole resulting in a nasal
displacement of the corneal reоАЭex and negative when the
converse applies.

{ Intorsion occurs when the superior limbus rotates nasally
and extorsion on temporal rotation.
оАБ оАЯe anatomy of the muscles is illustrated in Fig. 18.4A and B.

Horizontal recti

When the eye is in the primary position, the horizontal recti are
purely horizontal movers on the vertical Z axis and have only primary actions.
оАБ Medial rectus originates at the annulus of Zinn at the orbital
apex and inserts 5.5 mm behind the nasal limbus. Its sole
action in the primary position is adduction.
оАБ Lateral rectus originates at the annulus of Zinn and inserts 6.9
mm behind the temporal limbus. Its sole action in the primary
position is abduction.

Vertical recti

оАЬe vertical recti run in line with the orbital axis and are inserted
in front of the equator. оАЬey therefore form an angle of 23¬∞ with the
visual axis (see Fig. 18.2C).
оАБ Superior rectus originates from the upper part of the annulus
of Zinn and inserts 7.7 mm behind the superior limbus.

{ оАЬe primary action is elevation (Fig. 18.5A); secondary
actions are adduction and intorsion.

{ When the globe is abducted 23¬∞, the visual and orbital axes
coincide. In this position it has no subsidiary actions and
can act only as an elevator (Fig. 18.5B). оАЬis is therefore the

{ If the globe were adducted 67¬∞, the angle between the

18.5C).
оАБ Inferior rectus originates at the lower part of the annulus of

{ оАЬe primary action is depression; secondary actions are
adduction and extorsion.

{ When the globe is abducted 23¬∞, the inferior rectus acts
purely as a depressor. As for superior rectus, this is the
optimal position of the globe for testing the function of the
inferior rectus muscle.

{ If the globe were adducted 67¬∞, the inferior rectus could act
to extort the eye.

Spiral of Tillaux

оАЬe spiral of Tillaux (Fig. 18.6) is an imaginary line joining the
insertions of the four recti and is an important anatomical landmark when performing surgery. оАЬe insertions are located progressively further away from the limbus in a spiral pattern. оАЬe medial
rectus insertion is closest (5.5 mm), followed by the inferior rectus
(6.5 mm), lateral rectus (6.9 mm) and superior rectus (7.7 mm).

Oblique muscles

оАЬe obliques are inserted behind the equator and form an angle of
51¬∞ with the visual axis (see Fig. 18.2D).
оАБ Superior oblique originates superomedial to the optic foramen. It passes forwards through the trochlea at the angle
between the superior and medial walls and is then reоАЭected

Muscle pulleys

оАБ оАЬe four rectus muscles pass through condensations of connective tissue and smooth muscle just posterior to the equator.
оАЬese condensations act as pulleys and minimize upward and
downward movements of the bellies of the medial and lateral
rectus muscles during upgaze and downgaze, and horizontal
movements of the superior and inferior rectus bellies in leоАЪ
and right gaze.
оАБ Pulleys are the eоАЩective origins of the rectus muscles and play
an important role in the coordination of eye movements by
reducing the eоАЩect of horizontal movements on vertical muscle actions and vice versa.
оАБ Displacement of the pulleys is a cause of abnormalities of eye
movements such as вАШVвАЩ and вАШAвАЩ patterns (see Figs 18.69 and 18.70).

Innervation

оАБ Lateral rectus. Sixth cranial nerve (abducens nerve вАУ abducting muscle).
оАБ Superior oblique. Fourth cranial nerve (trochlear nerve вАУ
muscle associated with the trochlea).
оАБ Other muscles together with the levator muscle of the upper
lid and the ciliary and sphincter pupillae muscles are supplied
by the third (oculomotor) nerve.

Ocular movements

Ductions

Ductions are monocular movements around the axes of Fick. оАЬey
consist of adduction, abduction, elevation, depression, intorsion

and extorsion. оАЬey are tested by occluding the fellow eye and asking the patient to follow a target in each direction of gaze.

Versions

Versions (Fig. 18.9, top) are binocular, simultaneous, conjugate
movements (conjugate вАУ in the same direction, so that the angle
between the eyes remains constant).
оАБ Dextroversion and laevoversion (gaze right and gaze leоАЪ), elevation (upgaze) and depression (downgaze). оАЬese four movements bring the globe into the secondary positions of gaze by
rotation around either the vertical (Z) or the horizontal (X)
axes of Fick.
оАБ Dextroelevation and dextrodepression (gaze up and right;
gaze down and right) and laevoelevation and laevodepression (gaze up and leоАЪ; gaze down and leоАЪ). оАЬese four oblique
movements bring the eyes into the tertiary positions of gaze by
rotation around oblique axes lying in the Listing plane, equivalent to simultaneous movement about both the horizontal and
vertical axes.
оАБ Torsional movements to maintain upright images occur on
tilting of the head; these are known as the righting reоАЭexes. On
head tilt to the right the superior limbi of the two eyes rotate
to the leоАЪ, causing intorsion of the right globe and extorsion of
the leоАЪ (laevocycloversion).

Vergences

Vergences (Fig. 18.9, bottom) are binocular, simultaneous, disjugate movements (disjugate вАУ in opposite directions, so that
the angle between the eyes changes, also termed disjunctive).
Convergence is simultaneous adduction (inward turning) and
divergence is outwards movement from a convergent position.
Convergence may be voluntary or reоАЭex. ReоАЭex convergence has
four components:
оАБ Tonic convergence, which implies inherent innervational tone
to the medial recti.

Dextroversion

Dextrocycloversion

Divergence

CHAPTER

Strabismus 713
#### 18

оАБ Proximal convergence is induced by psychological awareness
of a near object.
оАБ Fusional convergence is an optomotor reоАЭex that maintains
BSV by ensuring that similar images are projected onto corresponding retinal areas of each eye. It is initiated by bitemporal
retinal image disparity.
оАБ Accommodative convergence is induced by the act of accommodation as part of the synkinetic-near reоАЭex.

{ Each dioptre of accommodation is accompanied by a
constant increment in accommodative convergence, giving the вАШaccommodative convergence to accommodationвАЩ
(AC/A) ratio.

{ оАЬis is the amount of convergence in prism dioptres (ќФ)
per dioptre (D) change in accommodation.

{ оАЬe normal value is 3вАУ5 ќФ. оАЬis means that 1 D of accommodation is associated with 3вАУ5 ќФ of accommodative
convergence. Abnormalities of the AC/A ratio play an
important role in the aetiology of strabismus.

{ Changes in accommodation, convergence and pupil size,
which occur in concert with a change in the distance of
viewing, are known as the вАШnear triadвАЩ.

{ See fusional vergence and fusional amplitudes later in this
chapter.

Positions of gaze

оАБ оАЯe Sherrington law of reciprocal innervation (Fig. 18.11)
states that increased innervation to an extraocular muscle (e.g.
right medial rectus) is accompanied by a reciprocal decrease
in innervation to its antagonist (e.g. right lateral rectus). оАЬis
means that when the medial rectus contracts, the lateral rectus automatically relaxes and vice versa. оАЬe Sherrington law
applies to both versions and vergences.
оАБ оАЯe Hering law of equal innervation states that during any
conjugate eye movement, equal and simultaneous innervation
оАЭows to the yoke muscles (Fig. 18.12).

оАБ Six cardinal positions of gaze are identiоАЮed in which one muscle in each eye is principally responsible for moving the eye
into that position as follows:

{ In the case of a paretic squint, the amount of innervation
to both eyes is symmetrical and always determined by the
оАЮxating eye, so that the angle of deviation will vary according to which eye is used for оАЮxation.

{ For example, in the case of a leоАЪ lateral rectus palsy, if
the right normal eye is used for оАЮxation, there will be an
inward deviation of the leоАЪ eye due to the unopposed
action of the antagonist of the paretic leоАЪ lateral rectus
(leоАЪ medial rectus). оАЬe amount of misalignment of the
two eyes in this situation is called the primary deviation
(Fig. 18.13, leоАЪ).

{ If the paretic leоАЪ eye is now used for оАЮxation, additional
innervation will оАЭow to the leоАЪ lateral rectus, in order to
establish this. However, according to the Hering law, an
equal amount of innervation will also оАЭow to the right
medial rectus (yoke muscle). оАЬis will result in an overaction of the right medial rectus and an excessive amount
of adduction of the right eye.

{ Dextroversion (right lateral rectus and leоАЪ medial rectus).

{ Laevoversion (leоАЪ lateral rectus and right medial rectus).

{ Dextroelevation (right superior rectus and leоАЪ inferior
oblique).

{ Laevoelevation (leоАЪ superior rectus and right inferior
oblique).

{ Dextrodepression (right inferior rectus and leоАЪ superior
oblique).

{ оАЬe amount of misalignment between the two eyes in this
situation is called the secondary deviation (Fig. 18.13,
right). In a paretic squint, the secondary deviation exceeds
the primary deviation.
оАБ Muscle sequelae are the eоАЩects of the interactions described
by these laws. оАЬey are of prime importance in diagnosing
ocular motility disorders and in particular in distinguishing
a recently acquired from a longstanding palsy (see вАШClinical
evaluationвАЩ). оАЬe full pattern of changes takes a variable period
to develop:

{ Laevodepression (leоАЪ inferior rectus and right superior
oblique).
оАБ Nine diagnostic positions of gaze are those in which deviations are measured. оАЬey consist of the six cardinal positions,
the primary position, elevation and depression (Fig. 18.10).

Laws of ocular motility

оАБ AgonistвАУantagonist pairs are muscles of the same eye that
move the eye in opposite directions. оАЬe agonist is the primary muscle moving the eye in a given direction. оАЬe antagonist acts in the opposite direction to the agonist. For example,
the right lateral rectus is the antagonist to the right medial
rectus.
оАБ Synergists are muscles of the same eye that move the eye in
the same direction. For example, the right superior rectus and
right inferior oblique act synergistically in elevation.
оАБ Yoke muscles (contralateral synergists) are pairs of muscles,
one in each eye, that produce conjugate ocular movements.
For example, the yoke muscle of the leоАЪ superior oblique is the
right inferior rectus.

{ Primary underaction (e.g. leоАЪ superior oblique).

{ Secondary overaction of the contralateral synergist or yoke
muscle (right inferior rectus; Hering law).

{ Secondary overaction and later contracture of the unopposed ipsilateral antagonist (leоАЪ inferior oblique; Sherrington law).

{ Secondary inhibition of the contralateral antagonist (right
superior rectus; Hering and Sherrington laws).

Sensory considerations

Basic aspects

оАБ Normal BSV involves the simultaneous use of both eyes with
bifoveal оАЮxation, so that each eye contributes to a common
single perception of the object of regard. оАЬis represents the
highest form of binocular cooperation. Conditions necessary
for normal BSV are:

Fig. 18.12 Hering law of equal innervation of yoke muscles.

{ Normal routing of visual pathways with overlapping visual
оАЮelds.

{ Approximately equal image clarity and size for both eyes.

{ Binocularly driven neurones in the visual cortex.

{ Normal retinal (retinocortical) correspondence (NRC)
resulting in вАШcyclopeanвАЩ viewing.

{ Accurate neuromuscular development and coordination,
so that the visual axes are directed at, and maintain оАЮxation
on, the object of regard.

{ BSV is based on NRC, which requires оАЮrst an understanding of uniocular visual direction and projection.
оАБ Visual direction is the projection of a given retinal element in
a speciоАЮc direction in subjective space.

{ оАЬe principal visual direction is the direction in external
space interpreted as the line of sight. оАЬis is normally the
visual direction of the fovea and is associated with a sense
of direct viewing.

{ Secondary visual directions are the projecting directions of extrafoveal points with respect to the principal
direction of the fovea, associated with indirect (eccentric) viewing.
оАБ Projection is the subjective interpretation of the position of
an object in space on the basis of stimulated retinal elements.

{ If a red object stimulates the right fovea (F) and a black
object that lies in the nasal оАЮeld stimulates a temporal retinal element (T), the red object will be interpreted by the
brain as having originated from the straight-ahead position and the black object will be interpreted as having
originated in the nasal оАЮeld (Fig. 18.14A). Similarly, nasal
retinal elements project into the temporal оАЮeld, upper retinal elements into the lower оАЮeld and vice versa.

{ With both eyes open, the red оАЮxation object is now stimulating both foveae, which are corresponding retinal points.
оАЬe black object is now not only stimulating the temporal
retinal elements in the right eye but also the nasal elements
of the leоАЪ eye. оАЬe right eye therefore projects the object
into its nasal оАЮeld and the leоАЪ eye projects the object into
its temporal оАЮeld.

{ оАЬis retinotopic organization is reоАЭected back along the
visual pathways, each eye maintaining separate images
until the visual pathways converge onto binocularly
responsive neurones in the primary visual cortex.
оАБ оАЯe horopter is an imaginary plane in external space, relative
to both the observerвАЩs eyes for a given оАЮxation target, all points
on which stimulate corresponding retinal elements and are
therefore seen singly and in the same plane (Fig. 18.14B). оАЬis
plane passes through the intersection of the visual axes and
therefore includes the point of оАЮxation in BSV.
оАБ оАЯe Panum fusional space (or volume) is a zone in front of
and behind the horopter in which objects stimulate slightly
non-corresponding retinal points (retinal disparity).

{ Points on the nasal retina of one eye have corresponding
points on the temporal retina of the other eye and vice
versa. For example, an object producing images on the
right nasal retina and the leоАЪ temporal retina will be projected into the right side of visual space. оАЬis is the basis of
normal retinal correspondence.

{ Because both of these retinal elements are corresponding
points, they will both project the object into the same position in space (the leоАЪ side) and there will be no double vision.
оАБ Retinomotor values

{ оАЬe image of an object in the peripheral visual оАЮeld falls on
an extrafoveal element. To establish оАЮxation on this object
a saccadic version of accurate amplitude is required.

{ Each extrafoveal retinal element therefore has a retinomotor value proportional to its distance from the fovea,
which guides the amplitude of saccadic movements
required to вАШlook at itвАЩ.

{ Objects within the limits of the fusional space are seen singly and the disparity information is used to produce a perception of binocular depth (stereopsis). Objects in front of
and behind the Panum space appear double.

{ оАЬis is the basis of physiological diplopia. оАЬe Panum
space is shallow at оАЮxation (6 seconds of arc) and deeper
towards the periphery (30вАУ40 seconds of arc at 15¬∞ from
the fovea).

{ оАЬe retinal areas stimulated by images falling within the
Panum fusional space are termed Panum fusional areas.

{ оАЬerefore, objects on the horopter are seen singly and in
one plane. Objects in Panum fusional areas are seen singly
and stereoscopically. Objects outside Panum fusional areas
appear double.

{ Retinomotor value, zero at the fovea, increases progressively towards the retinal periphery.
оАБ Corresponding points are areas on each retina that share the
same subjective visual direction (for example, the foveae share
the primary visual direction).

{ Physiological diplopia is usually accompanied by physiological suppression.
оАБ BSV is characterized by the ability to fuse the images from the
two eyes and to perceive binocular depth:

716 Introduction

A

{ Motor fusion involves the maintenance of motor alignment
of the eyes to sustain bifoveal оАЮxation. It is driven by retinal
image disparity, which stimulates fusional vergences.
оАБ Fusional vergence involves disjugate eye movements to overcome retinal image disparity. Fusional convergence helps to
control an exophoria whereas fusional divergence helps to
control an esophoria. оАЬe fusional vergence mechanism may
be decreased by fatigue or illness, converting a phoria to a
tropia. оАЬe amplitude of fusional vergence mechanisms can
be improved by orthoptic exercises, particularly in the case of
near fusional convergence for the relief of convergence insufоАЮciency. Amplitudes can be measured with prisms or a synoptophore. Normal values are:

F T

{ Convergence: about 15вАУ20 ќФ for distance and 25 ќФ for near.

{ Divergence: about 6вАУ10 ќФ for distance and 12вАУ14 ќФ for
near.

{ Vertical: 2вАУ3 ќФ

{ Cyclovergence: about 8¬∞.
оАБ Stereopsis is the perception of depth. It arises when objects
behind and in front of the point of оАЮxation (but within Panum
fusional space) stimulate horizontally disparate retinal elements simultaneously. оАЬe fusion of these disparate images
results in a single visual impression perceived in depth. A solid
object is seen stereoscopically (in three dimensions) because
each eye sees a slightly diоАЩerent aspect of the object.
оАБ Sensory perceptions. At the onset of a squint two sensory
perceptions arise based on the normal projection of the retinal areas stimulated; confusion and pathological diplopia may
result. оАЬese require simultaneous visual perception, that is,
the ability to perceive images from both eyes simultaneously.
Young children readily suppress diplopia but it is persistent
and usually troublesome with strabismus in older children and
adults, when it arises aоАЪer the sensitive period for binocularity
(see below).

{ Sensory fusion involves the integration by the visual areas
of the cerebral cortex of two similar images, one from each
eye, into one image. It may be central, which integrates the
image falling on the foveae, or peripheral, which integrates
parts of the image falling outside the foveae. It is possible to
maintain fusion with a central visual deоАЮcit in one eye, but
peripheral fusion is essential to BSV and may be aоАЩected
in patients with advanced оАЮeld changes in glaucoma and
pituitary lesions.

Sensory adaptations to strabismus

оАЬe ocular sensory system in children has the ability to adapt to
anomalous states (confusion and diplopia) by two mechanisms:
suppression and abnormal retinal correspondence (ARC). оАЬese
occur because of the plasticity of the developing visual system in
children under the age of 6вАУ8 years. Occasional adults who develop
sudden-onset strabismus are able to ignore the second image aоАЪer
a time and therefore do not complain of diplopia.
оАБ Suppression involves active inhibition by the visual cortex of
the image from one eye when both eyes are open. Stimuli for
suppression include diplopia, confusion and a blurred image

{ Confusion is the simultaneous appreciation of two superimposed but dissimilar images caused by stimulation
of corresponding retinal points (usually the foveae) by
images of diоАЩerent objects (Fig. 18.15).

{ Pathological diplopia is the simultaneous appreciation of
two images of the same object in diоАЩerent positions and
results from images of the same object falling on noncorresponding retinal points. In esotropia the diplopia is
homonymous (uncrossed вАУ Fig. 18.16A), in exotropia the
diplopia is heteronymous (crossed вАУ Fig. 18.16B).

#### 18

A Left Right

Left Right

Fig. 18.15 Confusion.

from one eye resulting from astigmatism/anisometropia. Clinically, suppression may be:

{ Central or peripheral. In central suppression the image
from the fovea of the deviating eye is inhibited to avoid
confusion. Diplopia, on the other hand, is eradicated by
the process of peripheral suppression, in which the image
from the peripheral retina of the deviating eye is inhibited.

{ Monocular or alternating. Suppression is monocular when
the image from the dominant eye always predominates
over the image from the deviating (or more ametropic) eye,
so that the image from the latter is constantly suppressed.
оАЬis type of suppression leads to amblyopia. When suppression alternates (switches from one eye to the other),
amblyopia is less likely to develop.

{ Facultative or obligatory. Facultative suppression occurs
only when the eyes are misaligned. Obligatory suppression
is present at all times, irrespective of whether the eyes are
deviated or straight. Examples of facultative suppression
include intermittent exotropia and Duane syndrome.
оАБ Abnormal (anomalous) retinal correspondence (ARC) is
a condition in which non-corresponding retinal elements
acquire a common subjective visual direction, i.e. fusion
occurs in the presence of a small-angle manifest squint; the
fovea of the оАЮxating eye is paired with a non-foveal element
of the deviated eye. Binocular responses in ARC are never as
good as in normal bifoveal BSV. It represents a positive sensory
adaptation to strabismus (as opposed to negative adaptation by
suppression), which allows some anomalous binocular vision
in the presence of a heterotropia. It is most frequently encountered in small-angle esotropia (microtropia), but is less common in accommodative esotropia because of the variability of

the angle of deviation and in large-angle deviations because
the separation of the images is too great.
оАБ Microtropia is discussed further later in this chapter (see Fig.
18.28)
оАБ Consequences of strabismus

{ оАЬe fovea of the squinting eye is suppressed to avoid
confusion.

{ Diplopia will occur, since corresponding retinal elements
receive diоАЩerent images.

{ To avoid diplopia, the patient will develop either peripheral suppression of the squinting eye or ARC.

{ If constant unilateral suppression occurs this will subsequently lead to strabismic amblyopia.
