718 Amblyopia

Motor adaptation to strabismus

Motor adaptation involves the adoption of a compensatory head
posture (CHP) and occurs primarily in children with congenitally
abnormal eye movements who use the CHP to maintain BSV. In
these children loss of a CHP may indicate loss of binocular function and the need for surgical intervention. оАЬese patients may
present in adult life with symptoms of decompensation, oоАЪen
unaware of their CHP. Acquired paretic strabismus in adults may
be consciously controlled by a CHP provided the deviation is neither too large nor too variable with gaze (incomitance). оАЬe CHP
eliminates diplopia and helps to centralize the binocular visual
оАЮeld. оАЬe patient will turn the head into the direction of the оАЮeld of
action of the weak muscle, so that the eyes are then automatically
turned the opposite direction and as far as possible away from its
оАЮeld of action (i.e. the head will turn where the eye cannot).
оАБ A face turn will be adopted to control a purely horizontal
deviation. For example, if the leоАЪ lateral rectus is paralysed,
diplopia will occur in leоАЪ gaze. оАЬe face will be turned to the
leоАЪ, which deviates the eyes to the right, away from the оАЮeld
of action of the weak muscle and area of diplopia. A face turn
may also be adopted in a paresis of a vertically acting muscle
to avoid the side where the vertical deviation is greatest (e.g. in
a right superior oblique weakness the face is turned to the leоАЪ).
оАБ A head tilt is adopted to compensate for torsional and/or
vertical diplopia. In a right superior oblique weakness, the
right eye is relatively elevated and the head is tilted to the
leоАЪ (Fig. 18.17), towards the hypotropic eye. оАЬis reduces the
vertical separation of the diplopic images and permits fusion
to be regained. If there is a signiоАЮcant torsional component
preventing fusion, tilting the head in the same leоАЪ direction
will reduce this by invoking the righting reоАЭexes (placing the
extorted right eye in a position that requires extorsion).
оАБ Chin elevation or depression may be used to compensate for
weakness of an elevator or depressor muscle or to minimize
the horizontal deviation when an вАШAвАЩ or вАШVвАЩ pattern is present.

AMBLYOPIA

ClassiоАЮcation

Amblyopia is the unilateral, or rarely bilateral, decrease in bestcorrected visual acuity (VA) caused by form vision deprivation
and/or abnormal binocular interaction, for which there is no identiоАЮable pathology of the eye or visual pathway.
оАБ Strabismic amblyopia results from abnormal binocular interaction where there is continued monocular suppression of the
deviating eye.
оАБ Anisometropic amblyopia is caused by a diоАЩerence in refractive error between the eyes and may result from a diоАЩerence of
as little as 1 dioptre. оАЬe more ametropic eye receives a blurred
image, in a mild form of visual deprivation. It is frequently
associated with microstrabismus and may co-exist with strabismic amblyopia.
оАБ Stimulus deprivation amblyopia results from vision deprivation. It may be unilateral or bilateral and is typically caused by

Fig. 18.17 Compensatory head posture in a right fourth nerve
palsy.

opacities in the media (e.g. cataract) or ptosis that covers the
pupil.
оАБ Bilateral ametropic amblyopia results from high symmetrical
refractive errors, usually hypermetropia.
оАБ Meridional amblyopia results from image blur in one meridian. It can be unilateral or bilateral and is caused by uncorrected astigmatism (usually >1 D) persisting beyond the
period of emmetropization in early childhood.

Diagnosis

In the absence of an organic lesion, a diоАЩerence in best-corrected
VA of two Snellen lines or more (or >1 log unit) is indicative of
amblyopia. VA in amblyopia is usually better when reading single
letters than letters in a row. оАЬis вАШcrowdingвАЩ phenomenon occurs
to a certain extent in normal individuals but is more marked in
amblyopes and must be taken into account when testing preverbal
children.

Treatment

It is essential to examine the fundi to diagnose any visible organic
disease prior to commencing treatment for amblyopia. Organic
disease and amblyopia may co-exist and a trial of patching may
still be indicated in the presence of organic disease. If acuity does
not respond to treatment, investigations such as electrophysiology
or imaging should be reconsidered. оАЬe sensitive period during
which acuity of an amblyopic eye can be improved is usually up
to 7вАУ8 years in strabismic amblyopia and may be longer (into the
teens) for anisometropic amblyopia where good binocular function is present.
оАБ Occlusion of the normal eye, to encourage use of the amblyopic eye, is the most eоАЩective treatment. оАЬe regimen, full-time
or part-time, depends on the age of the patient and the density
of amblyopia.

{ оАЬe younger the patient, the more rapid the likely improvement but the greater the risk of inducing amblyopia in the
normal eye. It is therefore very important to monitor VA
regularly in both eyes during treatment.

{ оАЬe better the VA at the start of occlusion, the shorter
the duration required, although there is wide variation
between patients.

{ If there has been no improvement after 6 months of
effective occlusion, further treatment is unlikely to be
fruitful.

{ Poor compliance is the single greatest barrier to improvement and must be monitored. Amblyopia treatment beneоАЮts from time spent at the outset on communication of
the rationale and the diоАЫculties involved.
оАБ Penalization, in which vision in the normal eye is blurred
with atropine, is an alternative method. It may work best in
the treatment of mildвАУmoderate amblyopia (6/24 or better),
especially when due to anisometropic hypermetropia. Patch
occlusion is likely to produce a quicker response than atropine, which has conventionally been reserved for use when
compliance with patch occlusion is poor. Penalization oоАЩers
the particular advantage of being diоАЫcult to thwart even if the
child objects. It also creates less of a psychosocial problem than
patching, especially in the school-going child. Weekend instillation may be adequate.

TIP Organic disease needs to be excluded before
commencing treatment for amblyopia.

CLINICAL EVALUATION

History

CHAPTER

Strabismus 719
#### 18

оАБ Variability is signiоАЮcant because intermittent strabismus indicates some degree of binocularity. An equally alternating deviation suggests symmetrical VA in both eyes.
оАБ General health or developmental problems may be signiоАЮcant
(e.g. children with cerebral palsy have an increased incidence
of strabismus). In older patients, poor health and stress may
cause decompensation and in acquired paresis patients may
report associations or causal factors (trauma, neurological disease, diabetes etc.).
оАБ Birth history, including period of gestation, birth weight and
any problems in utero, with delivery or in the neonatal period.
оАБ Family history is important because strabismus is frequently
familial, although no deоАЮnitive inheritance pattern is recognized. It is also important to know what therapy was necessary
in other family members.
оАБ Previous ocular history including refractive prescription and
compliance with spectacles or occlusion, previous surgery or
prisms is important to future treatment options and prognosis.

TIP In children, the later the onset of esotropia, the greater
the likelihood of an accommodative component to the
deviation.

Visual acuity

Testing in preverbal children

оАБ Age of onset

{ оАЬe earlier the onset, the more likely the need for surgical
correction.

{ оАЬe later the onset, the greater the likelihood of an accommodative component (mostly arising between 18 and 36
months).

{ оАЬe longer the duration of squint in early childhood the
greater the risk of amblyopia, unless оАЮxation is freely alternating. Inspection of previous photographs may be useful
for the documentation of strabismus or CHP.
оАБ Symptoms may indicate decompensation of a pre-existent
heterophoria or more signiоАЮcantly a recently acquired (usually
paretic) condition. In the former, the patient usually complains
of discomfort, blurring and possibly diplopia of indeterminate
onset and duration in comparison to the acquired condition
with the sudden onset of diplopia.

{ оАЬe type of diplopia (horizontal, cyclovertical) should be
established, together with the direction of gaze in which it
predominates and whether any BSV is retained.

{ In adults it is very important to determine exactly what
problems the squint is causing as a basis for decisions
about treatment.

{ It is not unusual for patients to present with spurious
symptoms that mask embarrassment over a psychosocially
noticeable squint.

оАЬe evaluation can be separated into the qualitative assessment of
visual behaviour and the quantitative assessment of VA using preferential looking tests. Assessment of visual behaviour is achieved
as follows:
оАБ Fixation and following may be assessed using bright attentiongrabbing targets (a face is oоАЪen best). оАЬis method indicates
whether the infant is visually alert and is of particular value in
a child suspected of being blind.
оАБ Comparison between the behaviour of the two eyes may
reveal a unilateral preference. Occlusion of one eye, if
strongly objected to by the child, indicates poorer acuity in
the other eye. However, it is possible to have good visual
attention with each eye but unequal VA and all risk factors
for amblyopia must be considered in the interpretation of
results.
оАБ Fixation behaviour can be used to establish unilateral preference if a manifest squint is present.

{ Fixation is promoted in the squinting eye by occluding
the dominant eye while the child оАЮxates a target of interest
(preferably incorporating a light).

{ Fixation is then graded as central or non-central and steady
or unsteady (the corneal reоАЭection can be observed).

{ оАЬe other eye is then uncovered and the ability to maintain
оАЮxation is observed.

{ If оАЮxation immediately returns to the uncovered eye, then
VA is probably impaired.

{ If оАЮxation is maintained through a blink, then VA is probably good.

720 Clinical Evaluation

{ If the patient alternates оАЮxation, then the two eyes probably have equal vision.
оАБ оАЯe 10 ќФ test is similar and can be used regardless of whether
a manifest squint is present. It involves the promotion of diplopia using a 10 ќФ vertical prism. Alternation between the diplopic targets suggests equal VA.
оАБ Rotation test is a gross qualitative test of the ability of an infant
to оАЮxate with both eyes open. оАЬe test is performed as follows:

{ оАЬe examiner holds the child facing him or her and rotates
briskly through 360¬∞.

{ If vision is normal, the eyes will deviate in the direction
of rotation under the inоАЭuence of the vestibulo-ocular
response. оАЬe eyes оАЭick back to the primary position to
produce a rotational nystagmus.

{ When rotation stops, nystagmus is brieоАЭy observed in the
opposite direction for 1вАУ2 seconds and should then cease
due to suppression of post-rotary nystagmus by оАЮxation.

{ If vision is severely impaired, the post-rotation nystagmus
does not stop as quickly when rotation ceases because
the vestibulo-ocular response is not blocked by visual
feedback.
оАБ Preferential looking tests can be used from early infancy and
are based on the fact that infants prefer to look at a pattern
rather than a homogeneous stimulus. оАЬe infant is exposed
to a stimulus and the examiner observes the eyes for оАЮxation movements, without themselves knowing the stimulus
position.

{ Tests in common use include the Teller and Keeler acuity
cards, which consist of black stripes (gratings) of varying
widths and CardiоАЩ acuity cards (Fig. 18.18), which consist
of familiar pictures with variable outline width.

{ Low-frequency (coarse) gratings or pictures with a wider
outline are seen more easily than high-frequency gratings
or thin outline pictures and an assessment of resolution
(not recognition) VA is made accordingly.

A

B

Fig. 18.18 CardiоАЮ acuity cards, shown at a distance of 100оАЭcm.
(A) Car with thin outline (6/7.5); (B) оАЯsh with thick outline (6/15).

оАБ At age 2 years most children will have suоАЫcient language skills
to undertake a picture naming test such as the crowded Kay
pictures (Fig. 18.19A).
оАБ At age 3 years most children will be able to undertake the
matching of letter optotypes as in the Keeler logMAR (Fig.
18.19B), Sonksen or Otago crowded tests. If a crowded letter
test proves too diоАЫcult it is preferable to perform the crowded
Kay pictures than to use single optotype letters.
оАБ Older children may continue with the crowded letter tests,
naming or matching them. LogMAR tests are in common
usage and are preferable to Snellen for all children at risk of
amblyopia.

Tests for stereopsis

Stereopsis is measured in seconds of arc (1¬∞ = 60 minutes of arc;
1 minute = 60 seconds); the lower the value the better the stereoacuity. It is useful to remember that normal spatial resolution (VA) is
1 minute and normal stereoacuity is 60 seconds (also 1 minute, but
conventionally expressed in seconds). Various tests, using diоАЩering
principles, are employed to assess the stereoacuity. Random dot tests
(e.g. TNO, Frisby) provide the most deоАЮnitive evidence of high-grade
BSV. Where this is weak and/or based on ARC (see above), contourbased tests (e.g. Titmus) may provide more reliable information.

{ Since grating acuity oоАЪen exceeds Snellen acuity in amblyopia, Teller cards may overestimate VA. оАЬese methods
may not be reliable if a proper forced-choice staircase protocol is not followed during testing and neither method
has high sensitivity to the presence of amblyopia. оАЬe
results must be considered in combination with risk factors for amblyopia.
оАБ Pattern visual evoked potentials (VEP) give a representation
of spatial acuity but are more commonly used in the diagnosis
of optic neuropathy.

Testing in verbal children

оАЬe tests described below should be performed at 3вАУ4 metres from
the target, as it is easier to obtain compliance than at 6 metres,
with little or no clinical detriment. It is important to note that
amblyopia can only be accurately diagnosed using a crowded test
requiring target recognition and that logMAR tests (logarithm of
the minimal angle of resolution вАУ see Ch. 1) provide the best measure against which improvement with amblyopia therapy can be
assessed. оАЬese are readily available in formats suited to normal
children from 2 years onwards.

A

B

Fig. 18.19 (A) Kay pictures; (B) Keeler logMAR crowded test.

Titmus

оАЬe Titmus test consists of a three-dimensional polarized vectograph comprising two plates in the form of a booklet viewed
through polarized spectacles. оАЬe spectacles should be worn
before the plates are viewed. On the right is a large оАЭy and on the
leоАЪ is a series of nine squares (within each of which there are four
circles) and animals (Fig. 18.20). оАЬe test should be performed at
a distance of 40 cm.
оАБ оАЯe оАЮy is a test of gross stereopsis (3000 seconds) and is especially useful for young children. It should appear to stand out
from the page and the child is encouraged to pick up the tip of
one of its wings between оАЮnger and thumb.
оАБ оАЯe animal component consists of three rows of stylized animals (400вАУ100 seconds), one of which will appear forward of
the plane of reference.
оАБ оАЯe circles comprise a graded series measuring 800вАУ40 seconds; one of a set of four circles should appear to stand out
from the plate surface.
оАБ If the child has less than 800 seconds of stereopsis and prefers
оАЮxing with the right eye (leоАЪ suppression), the bottom circle of
оАЮrst square of four circles is displaced to the leоАЪ (and vice versa
if оАЮxing with the leоАЪ eye).

Strabismus 721
#### 18

A

B

Fig. 18.20 Titmus test.

TNO

оАЬe TNO random dot test consists of seven plates of randomly
distributed paired red and green dots viewed with redвАУgreen spectacles and measures from 480 down to 15 seconds of arc at 40 cm.
Within each plate the dots of one colour forming the target shape
(squares, crosses etc. вАУ Fig. 18.21) are displaced horizontally in
relation to paired dots of the other colour so that they have a different retinal disparity to those outside the target. Control shapes
are visible without the spectacles.

Frisby

оАЬe Frisby stereotest consists of three transparent plastic plates of
varying thickness. On the surface of each plate are printed four
squares of small randomly distributed shapes (Fig. 18.22). One
of the squares contains a вАШhiddenвАЩ circle, in which the random
shapes are printed on the reverse of the plate. оАЬe test does not
require special spectacles because disparity (600вАУ15 seconds) is
created by the thickness of the plate; the working distance must
be measured.

Lang

оАЬe Lang stereotest does not require special spectacles. оАЬe targets
(a star, moon, car and elephant) are seen alternately by each eye

722 Clinical Evaluation

A

B

Fig. 18.21 TNO test. Shapes are only visible to an individual
with stereopsis. (A) Test target, (B) redвАУgreen spectacles.

through the built-in cylindrical lens elements. оАЬe star is monocularly visible but provides a stereoscopic eоАЩect to observers with
binocular vision. оАЬe star helps to attract and maintain the interest of small children. Displacement of the dots creates disparity
(1200вАУ200 seconds) and the patient is asked to name or point to a
simple shape, such as a star, on the card (Fig. 18.23).

Tests for binocular fusion in infants
without manifest squint

Base-out prism

оАЬis is a simple method for detecting fusion in children. оАЬe test is
performed by placing a 20 ќФ base-out prism in front of one eye (the
right eye in Fig. 18.24). оАЬis displaces the retinal image temporally
with resultant diplopia.
оАБ оАЬere will be a shiоАЪ of the right eye to the leоАЪ to resume оАЮxation (right adduction) with a corresponding shiоАЪ of the leоАЪ eye
to the leоАЪ (leоАЪ abduction) in accordance with the Hering law
(Fig. 18.24B).
оАБ оАЬe leоАЪ eye will then make a corrective re-оАЮxational saccade to
the right (leоАЪ re-adduction) (Fig. 18.24C).
оАБ On removal of the prism both eyes move to the right (Fig.
18.24D).

Fig. 18.22 Frisby test.

Fig. 18.23 Lang test (arrow shows the star).

оАБ оАЬe leоАЪ eye then makes an outward fusional movement (Fig.
18.24E).
оАБ Most children with good BSV should be able to overcome a
20 ќФprism from the age of 6 months. If not, weaker prisms
(16 ќФ or 12 ќФ) may be tried, but the response is then more difоАЮcult to identify.

Binocular convergence

Simple convergence to an interesting target can be demonstrated
from 3 to 4 months. Both eyes should follow the approaching
target symmetrically вАШto the noseвАЩ. Over-convergence in the infant
may indicate an incipient esotropia. Divergence may be due
either to a tendency to a divergent deviation or simply lack of
interest.

Tests for sensory anomalies

Worth four-dot test

оАЬis is a dissociation test that can be used with both distance and
near оАЮxation and diоАЩerentiates between BSV, ARC and suppression.

{ оАЬe patient wears a green lens in front of the right eye,

{ оАЬe patient then views a box with four lights: one red, two

{ If BSV is present, all four lights are seen.

{ If all four lights are seen in the presence of a manifest

{ If two red lights are seen, right suppression is present.

оАБ Procedure. оАЬe two lenses are placed at 45¬∞ and 135¬∞ in front
of each eye and the patient оАЮxates on a focal light source (Fig.
18.26A). Each eye perceives an oblique line of light, perpendicular to that perceived by the fellow eye (Fig. 18.26B). Dissimilar images are thus presented to each eye under binocular
viewing conditions.
оАБ Results (Fig. 18.26C) cannot be interpreted unless it is known
whether strabismus is present.

{ If the two streaks intersect at their centres in the form of
an oblique cross (an вАШXвАЩ), the patient has BSV if the eyes are
straight, or harmonious ARC in the presence of manifest
strabismus.

{ If the two lines are seen but they do not form a cross, diplopia is present.

{ If only one streak is seen, there is no simultaneous perception and suppression is present.

{ In theory, if a small gap is seen in one of the streaks, a central suppression scotoma (as found in microtropia) is present. In practice this is oоАЪen diоАЫcult to demonstrate and the
patient describes a cross. оАЬe scotoma can be conоАЮrmed
with the 4 ќФ prism test (see below).

Bagolini striated glasses

оАЬis is a test for detecting BSV, ARC or suppression. Each lens has
оАЮne striations that convert a point source of light into a line, as
with the Maddox rod (see below).

TIP The 4оАЭќФ prism base-out test is useful to distinguish
bifoveal оАЯxation from foveal suppression (central suppression
scotoma), which occurs during binocular viewing in patients
with monoоАЯxation syndrome.

4оАЭќФ prism test

оАЬis test distinguishes bifoveal оАЮxation (normal BSV) from foveal
suppression (also known as a central suppression scotoma вАУ CSS)
in microtropia and employs the principle described in the 20 ќФ test
(the Hering law and convergence) to overcome diplopia.
оАБ With bifoveal оАЭxation

{ оАЬe prism is placed base-out (microtropia is commonly
esotropic not exotropic) in front of the right eye with
deviation of the image away from the fovea temporally,
followed by corrective movement of both eyes to the leоАЪ
(Fig. 18.27A).

{ оАЬe leоАЪ eye then converges to fuse the images (Fig. 18.27B).
оАБ In leоАЬ microtropia

{ оАЬe patient оАЮxates a distance target with both eyes open
and a 4 ќФ prism is placed base-out in front of the eye with
suspected CSS (the leоАЪ in Fig. 18.28).

{ оАЬe image is moved temporally in the leоАЪ eye but falls
within the CSS and no movement of either eye is observed
(Fig. 18.28A).

{ оАЬe prism is then moved to the right eye which adducts to
maintain оАЮxation. оАЬe leоАЪ eye similarly moves to the leоАЪ
consistent with the Hering law of equal innervation, but
the second image falls within the CSS of the leоАЪ eye and so
no subsequent re-оАЮxation movement is seen (Fig. 18.28B).

Synoptophore

оАЬe synoptophore compensates for the angle of squint and allows
stimuli to be presented to both eyes simultaneously (Fig. 18.29A).
It can thus be used to investigate the potential for binocular function in the presence of a manifest squint and is of particular value
in assessing young children (from age 3 years), who generally оАЮnd
the test process enjoyable. It can also detect suppression and ARC.
оАБ оАЬe instrument consists of two cylindrical tubes with a mirrored right-angled bend and a +6.50 D lens in each eyepiece
(Fig. 18.29B, top). оАЬis optically sets the testing distance as
equivalent to about 6 metres.
оАБ Pictures are inserted in a slide carrier situated at the outer end
of each tube. оАЬe two tubes are supported on columns that
enable the pictures to be moved in relation to each other and
any adjustments are indicated on a scale.
оАБ оАЬe synoptophore can measure horizontal, vertical and torsional misalignments simultaneously and is valuable in
determining surgical approach by assessing the diоАЩerent contributions in the cardinal positions of gaze.

Grades of binocular vision

Binocular vision can be graded on the synoptophore as below
(Fig. 18.29B, bottom).
оАБ First grade (simultaneous perception вАУ SP) is tested by introducing two dissimilar but not mutually antagonistic pictures,
such as a bird and a cage.

{ оАЬe subject is then asked to put the bird into the cage by
moving the arm of the synoptophore.

{ If the two pictures cannot be seen simultaneously, then
suppression is present.

Fig. 18.27 4оАЭќФ prism test in bifoveal оАЯxation. (A) Shift of both eyes away from the prism base;

{ Some retinal вАШrivalryвАЩ will occur although one picture is
smaller than the other, so that while the small one is seen
foveally, the larger one is seen parafoveally (and is thus
placed in front of the deviating eye).

{ Larger macular and paramacular slides are used if foveal
slides cannot be superimposed.
оАБ Second grade (fusion). If simultaneous perception slides can
be superimposed then the test proceeds to the second grade,
which is the ability of the two eyes to produce a composite picture (sensory fusion) from two similar pictures, each of which
is incomplete in one small diоАЩerent detail.

{ оАЬe classic example is two rabbits, one lacking a tail and
the other lacking a bunch of оАЭowers. If fusion is present,
one rabbit complete with tail and оАЭowers will be seen.

{ оАЬe range of fusion (motor fusion) is then tested by moving the arms of the synoptophore so that the eyes have to
converge and diverge in order to maintain fusion.

{ оАЬe presence of simple fusion without any range is of little
value in everyday life.
оАБ оАЯird grade (stereopsis) is the ability to obtain an impression
of depth by the superimposition of two pictures of the same
object which have been taken from slightly diоАЩerent angles.
оАЬe classic example is a bucket, which should be appreciated
in three dimensions.

Detection of abnormal retinal correspondence

ARC is detected on the synoptophore as follows:

A

B

Scotoma

Scotoma

Left Right

Left Right

Fig. 18.28 4оАЭќФ prism test in left microtropia with a central suppression scotoma. (A) No movement of either eye; (B) both eyes move to the left but there is absence of re-оАЯxation.

оАБ оАЬe subjective angle of deviation is that at which the SP slides
are superimposed. оАЬe examiner determines the objective
angle of the deviation by presenting each fovea alternately with
a target by extinguishing one or other light and moving the
slide in front of the deviating eye until no movement of the
eyes is seen.
оАБ If the subjective and objective angles coincide then retinal correspondence is normal.
оАБ If the objective and subjective angles are diоАЩerent, ARC is
present. оАЬe diоАЩerence in degrees between the subjective and
objective angles is the angle of anomaly. ARC is said to be harmonious when the objective angle equals the angle of anomaly
and inharmonious when it exceeds the angle of anomaly. It

is only in harmonious ARC that binocular responses can be
demonstrated. оАЬe inharmonious form may represent a lesser
adaptation or an artefact of testing.

Measurement of deviation

Hirschberg test

оАЬe Hirschberg test gives a rough objective estimate of the angle
of a manifest strabismus and is especially useful in young or uncooperative patients or when оАЮxation in the deviating eye is poor.
It is also useful in excluding pseudostrabismus. A pen torch is
shone into the eyes from armвАЩs length and the patient asked to

CHAPTER

Strabismus 727
#### 18

A B

Fig. 18.29 (A) Synoptophore; (B) optical principles and grading of binocular vision.

оАЮxate the light. оАЬe corneal reоАЭection of the light will be (more or
less) centred in the pupil of the оАЮxating eye, but will be decentred
in a squinting eye, in the direction opposite to that of the deviation (Fig. 18.30). оАЬe distance of the corneal light reоАЭection from
the centre of the pupil is noted; each millimetre of deviation is
approximately equal to 7¬∞ (1¬∞ вЙИ 2 prism dioptres). For example, if
the reоАЭex is situated at the temporal border of the pupil (assuming a pupillary diameter of 4 mm), the angle is about 15¬∞; if it is
at the limbus, the angle is about 45¬∞. As the pupil size varies, the
estimated angle varies.

Krimsky and prism reоАЬection tests

Corneal reоАЭex assessment can be combined with prisms to give a
more accurate approximation of the angle in a manifest deviation.
оАБ оАЯe Krimsky test involves placement of prisms in front of the
оАЮxating eye until the corneal light reоАЭections are symmetrical
(Fig. 18.31A and B). оАЬis test reduces the problem of parallax
and is more commonly used than the prism reоАЭection test.
оАБ оАЯe prism reоАЮection test involves the placement of prisms in
front of the deviating eye until the corneal light reоАЭections are
symmetrical.

CoverвАУuncover test

оАЬe coverвАУuncover test consists of two parts:
оАБ оАЯe cover test detects heterotropia. It is helpful to begin the
near test using a light to observe the corneal reоАЭections and to
assess оАЮxation in the deviating eye. It should then be repeated
for near using an accommodative target and for distance as
follows:

{ оАЬe patient оАЮxates on a straight-ahead target.

{ If a right deviation is suspected, the examiner covers the
оАЮxing leоАЪ eye and notes any movement of the right eye to
take up оАЮxation.

{ No movement indicates orthotropia (Fig. 18.32A) or leоАЪ
exotropia (Fig. 18.32B).

{ Adduction of the right eye to take up оАЮxation indicates right
exotropia and abduction, right esotropia (Fig. 18.32C).

Prism cover test

оАЬe prism cover test measures the angle of deviation and includes
the alternate cover test with prisms. It is performed with the patient
оАЮxing on a distant target in the primary position and in the eight
diagnostic positions of gaze. It is repeated in the primary position
with near оАЮxation and in patients with intermittent exotropia, with
оАЮxation in the far distance. It is performed as follows:
оАБ оАЬe alternate cover test is оАЮrst performed to establish the direction and approximate extent of deviation.
оАБ Prisms of increasing strength are placed in front of one eye
with the base opposite the direction of the deviation (i.e. the
apex of the prism is pointed in the direction of the deviation). For example, in a convergent strabismus the prism is
held base-out and in a right hypertropia, base-down before
the right eye.

TIP When undertaking cover testing, the patient must оАЯxate
on an accommodative target rather than on a light source.

Alternate cover test

оАЬe alternate cover test induces dissociation to reveal the total
deviation when fusion is disrupted. It should be performed only
aоАЪer the coverвАУuncover test.
оАБ оАЬe right eye is covered for several seconds.
оАБ оАЬe occluder is quickly shiоАЪed to the opposite eye for 2 seconds, then back and forth several times. AоАЪer the cover is
removed, the examiner notes the speed and smoothness of
recovery as the eyes return to their pre-dissociated state.
оАБ A patient with a well-compensated heterophoria will have
straight eyes before and aоАЪer the test has been performed
whereas a patient with poor control may decompensate to a
manifest deviation.

Fig. 18.34 Prism cover test.

оАБ оАЬe alternate cover test is performed continuously as stronger prisms are introduced, typically using a prism bar consisting of a column of prisms of progressive strength (Fig. 18.34).
оАЬe amplitude of the re-оАЮxation movement should gradually
decrease as the strength of prism approaches the extent of
deviation.
оАБ оАЬe end-point is approached when no movement is seen. To
ensure the maximum angle is found, the prism strength can be
increased further until a movement is observed in the opposite
direction (the point of reversal) and then reduced again to оАЮnd
the neutral value; the angle of deviation is then taken from the
strength of the prism.

Maddox wing

оАЬe Maddox wing dissociates the eyes for near оАЮxation (1/3 m)
and measures heterophoria. оАЬe instrument is constructed in such
a way that the right eye sees only a white vertical arrow and a red
horizontal arrow, whereas the leоАЪ eye sees only horizontal and vertical rows of numbers (Fig. 18.35).
оАБ Horizontal deviation is measured by asking the patient to
which number the white arrow points.
оАБ Vertical deviation is measured by asking the patient which
number intersects with the red arrow.
оАБ оАЬe amount of cyclophoria is determined by asking the patient
to move the red arrow so that it is parallel with the horizontal
row of numbers.

Maddox rod

оАЬe Maddox rod consists of a series of fused cylindrical red or
white glass rods that convert the appearance of a white spot of light
into a red or white streak. оАЬe optical properties of the rods cause
the streak of light to be at an angle of 90¬∞ with the long axis of the
rods. When the glass rods are held horizontally, the streak will be
vertical and vice versa.
оАБ оАЬe rod is placed in front of the right eye (Fig. 18.36A). оАЬis
dissociates the two eyes: the red streak seen by the right eye

Strabismus 729
#### 18

A

B

Fig. 18.35 PatientвАЩs view using the Maddox wing.

cannot be fused with the unaltered white spot of light seen by
the leоАЪ eye (Fig. 18.36B).
оАБ оАЬe amount of dissociation (Fig. 18.36C) is measured by the
superimposition of the two images using prisms. оАЬe base of
the prism is placed in the position opposite to the direction of
the deviation.
оАБ Both vertical and horizontal deviations can be measured in
this way but the test cannot diоАЩerentiate a phoria from a tropia.
оАБ оАЬe double Maddox rod test is used to determine cyclodeviations. Traditionally, a red Maddox is placed before the right
eye and a white Maddox before the leоАЪ. A Maddox rod is
placed in front of each eye in a trial frame or phoropter with
the lines arranged vertically so that the patient sees horizontal line images. оАЬe patient or examiner rotates the axes of the
rods until the lines are perceived to be parallel. To facilitate
the patientвАЩs recognition of the two lines, it is oоАЪen helpful to
dissociate the lines by placing a small prism base-up or basedown in front of one eye. оАЬe degree of deviation and the
direction (incyclo- or excyclo-) can be determined by the angle
of rotation that causes the line images to appear horizontal and
parallel.

possible results (showing what the patient sees).

Motility tests

Ocular movements

Examination of eye movements involves the assessment of smooth
pursuit movements followed by saccades.
оАБ Versions towards the eight eccentric positions of gaze are tested
by asking the patient to follow a target, usually a pen or pen
torch (the latter oоАЩers the advantage of corneal light reоАЭections
to aid assessment). Check versions and ductions (if required)
оАЮrst and then perform cover tests as discussed above. Versions
may also be elicited involuntarily in response to a noise or by the
dollвАЩs head manoeuvre in uncooperative patients.
оАБ Ductions are assessed if reduced ocular motility is noted in
either or both eyes. A pen torch should be used with careful
attention to the position of the corneal reоАЭexes. оАЬe fellow eye
is occluded and the patient asked to follow the torch into various
positions of gaze. Rotatory deоАЮciencies are qualitatively assessed.
A simple numeric system may be employed using 0 to denote full
movement and вИТ1 to вИТ4 to denote increasing degrees of underaction (Fig. 18.37). Using the lateral rectus muscle for example, вИТ4
would denote an inability to abduct the eye past the midline, вИТ3
an inability to abduct the eye more than 22.5¬∞ past the midline,
вИТ2 inability to abduct the eye more than 45¬∞ past the midline and
вИТ1 inability to abduct the eye more than 67.5¬∞ past the midline. In
the same way, overactions are assessed on a +1 to +4 basis. Both
are recorded pictorially. оАЬese measurements can be accurately
repeated with agreement between diоАЩerent observers.

-1

-2

-3

-4

Fig. 18.37 Grading of right lateral rectus underaction.

Fig. 18.38 RAF rule with convergence target.

Near point of convergence

оАЬe near point of convergence (NPC) is the nearest point on which
the eyes can maintain binocular оАЮxation. It can be measured with the
RAF rule, which rests on the patientвАЩs cheeks (Fig. 18.38). A target
is slowly moved along the rule towards the patientвАЩs eyes until one
eye loses оАЮxation and driоАЪs laterally (objective NPC). оАЬe subjective
NPC is the point at which the patient reports diplopia. Normally, the
NPC should be nearer than 10 cm without undue eоАЩort.

Near point of accommodation

оАЬe near point of accommodation (NPA) is the nearest point on
which the eyes can maintain clear focus. It can also be measured
with the RAF rule. оАЬe patient оАЮxates a line of print, which is
then slowly moved towards the patient until it becomes blurred.
оАЬe distance at which this is оАЮrst reported is read oоАЩ the rule and
denotes the NPA. оАЬe NPA recedes with age; when suоАЫciently far
away to render reading diоАЫcult without optical correction, presbyopia is present. At the age of 20 years the NPA is 8 cm and by the
age of 50 years it has receded to approximately 46 cm. оАЬe amplitude of accommodation can also be assessed using concave lenses
in 0.5-dioptre steps whilst оАЮxating the 6/6 Snellen line and reporting when the vision blurs.

Fusional amplitudes

Fusional amplitudes measure the eоАЫcacy of vergence movements.
оАЬey may be tested with prisms bars or the synoptophore. An
increasingly strong prism is placed in front of one eye, which will
then abduct or adduct (depending on whether the prism is base-in
or base-out), in order to maintain bifoveal оАЮxation. When a prism
greater than the fusional amplitude is reached, diplopia is reported
or one eye driоАЪs in the opposite direction, indicating the limit of
vergence ability. (For normal values, see fusional vergence above.)

Postoperative diplopia test

оАЬis simple test is mandatory prior to strabismus surgery in all
non-binocular patients over 7вАУ8 years of age to assess the risk of
diplopia aоАЪer surgery.

CHAPTER

Strabismus 731
#### 18

оАБ Corrective prisms are placed in front of one eye (usually the
deviating eye) and the patient asked to оАЮxate a straight-ahead
target with both eyes open. оАЬe prisms are slowly increased
until the angle has been signiоАЮcantly overcorrected and the
patient reports if diplopia occurs.
оАБ If suppression persists throughout there is little risk of diplopia
following surgery. However, in a consecutive exotropia of 35 ќФ,
diplopia may be reported from 30 ќФ and persist as the prism
correction mimics an esotropia.
оАБ Diplopia may be intermittent or constant but in either case
constitutes an indication to perform a diagnostic botulinum
toxin test (see below).
оАБ Diplopia is not restricted to patients with good VA in the deviating eye.
оАБ Intractable diplopia is diоАЫcult to treat.

Field of binocular single vision

оАЬe оАЮeld of BSV may be tested on Goldmann perimetry, tangent
screen or Hess chart and usually measures about 45¬∞ to 50¬∞ from
the оАЮxation point, except where it is blocked by the nose. It represents the parts of the visual оАЮeld of each eye that overlap and
where bifoveal fusion of the object of regard occurs (Fig. 18.39). In
patients with recent onset of an ocular motor paralysis or restrictive muscle defect, diplopia is usually the major symptom. оАЬe test
is extremely valuable in providing an exact repeatable follow-up
measurement and is also useful in the preoperative and postoperative management of these patients.

Hess chart and Lees screen

A Hess chart is plotted to aid in the diagnosis and monitoring of a
patient with incomitant strabismus, such as an extraocular muscle
palsy (e.g. third, fourth or sixth nerve paresis) or a mechanical or
myopathic limitation (e.g. thyroid ophthalmopathy, blow-out fracture or myasthenia gravis). оАЬe chart is commonly prepared using
either the Lees or Hess screen, which facilitate plotting of the dissociated ocular position as a measure of extraocular muscle action.
Information provided by the Hess chart should be regarded in the
context of other investigations such as the оАЮeld of binocular single
vision. оАЬe prism cover test is also very useful in the assessment of
incomitant squint.

Hess screen

оАЬe Hess screen contains a tangent pattern displayed on a darkgrey background. Red lights that can be individually illuminated
by a control panel indicate the cardinal positions of gaze within
a central оАЮeld (15¬∞ from primary position) and a peripheral оАЮeld
(30¬∞); each square represents 5¬∞ of ocular rotation. оАЬe eyes are
dissociated by the use of reversible goggles incorporating a red
and a green lens, the red lens in front of the оАЮxating eye and the
green lens the non-оАЮxating eye. Red points of lights are illuminated at selected positions on the screen. оАЬe patient holds a
green pointer and is asked to superimpose a green light over each
red light in turn. In orthophoria the two lights should be more or
less superimposed in all positions of gaze. оАЬe goggles are then

732 Clinical Evaluation

Fig. 18.39 Field of binocular single vision (clear area between the green colour) using a Goldmann perimeter. (Courtesy of ADN Murray.)

reversed and the procedure repeated. SoоАЪware is available that
facilitates the plotting of a Hess chart using a standard desktop
computer screen.

Lees screen

оАЬis apparatus (Fig. 18.40) consists of two opalescent glass screens
at right-angles to each other, bisected by a two-sided plane mirror
that dissociates the eyes; each of the eyes can see only one of the
two screens. Each screen has a tangent pattern (two-dimensional
projection of a spherical surface) that is revealed only when the
screen is illuminated. оАЬe patient is positioned facing the nonilluminated screen with his or her chin stabilized on a rest. Using
a pointer, the examiner indicates a target point on the illuminated
tangent pattern and the patient positions a pointer on the nonilluminated screen, at a position perceived to be superimposed on
the dot indicated by the examiner. оАЬe non-illuminated screen is
brieоАЭy illuminated by the examiner using a footswitch to facilitate
recording of the dot indicated by the patient. When the procedure
has been completed for one eye, the patient is rotated through
90¬∞ to face the previously illuminated screen and the procedure
repeated.

Interpretation

Fig. 18.41 shows a typical Hess chart appearance in right lateral
rectus paresis of recent onset.
оАБ оАЬe smaller chart indicates the eye with the paretic muscle
(right eye).
оАБ оАЬe larger chart indicates the eye with the overacting yoke
muscle (leоАЪ eye).

Fig. 18.40 Lees screen.

оАБ The smaller chart will show its greatest restriction in the
main direction of action of the paretic muscle (right lateral
rectus).
оАБ оАЬe larger chart will show its greatest expansion in the main
direction of action of the yoke muscle (leоАЪ medial rectus).
оАБ оАЬe degree of disparity between the plotted point and the template in any position of gaze gives an estimate of the angle of
deviation (each square = 5¬∞).

Changes with time

Progressive changes in the Hess chart with time are characteristic and useful both as a prognostic indicator and to guide
management.
оАБ In right superior rectus palsy, the Hess chart will show underaction of the aоАЩected muscle with an overaction of its yoke
muscle, the leоАЪ inferior oblique (Fig. 18.42A). Because of the
great incomitance of the two charts, the diagnosis is straightforward. If the paretic muscle recovers its function, both charts
will revert to normal.
оАБ Secondary contracture of the ipsilateral antagonist (right inferior
rectus) will manifest as an overaction which will lead to a secondary (inhibitional) palsy of the antagonist of the yoke muscle (leоАЪ
superior oblique), which will show up on the chart as an underaction (Fig. 18.42B). оАЬis could lead to the incorrect impression
that the leоАЪ superior oblique is the primarily paretic muscle.
оАБ With further passage of time, the two charts become progressively more concomitant, such that it may be impossible to
determine the initiating muscle weakness (Fig. 18.42C).

Examples

оАЬe clinical features of extraocular muscle palsies are discussed in
detail in Chapter 19

Refraction and fundoscopy

Dilated fundoscopy is mandatory in the context of strabismus,
principally to exclude any underlying ocular pathology such as
macular scarring, optic disc hypoplasia or retinoblastoma as the
cause of the deviation. More commonly, strabismus is secondary to
refractive error; hypermetropia (hyperopia), astigmatism, anisometropia and myopia may all be associated.

Cycloplegia

оАЬe most common refractive error to cause strabismus is hypermetropia. Accurate measurements of hypermetropia necessitate
eоАЩective paralysis of the ciliary muscle (cycloplegia), in order to
neutralize the masking eоАЩect of accommodation. In a young child
the risk of penalization amblyopia should be avoided by always
inducing cycloplegia in both eyes at one sitting, particularly if
atropine is used.
оАБ Cyclopentolate (0.5% under 6 months and 1% subsequently).
One drop, repeated aоАЪer 5 minutes, usually results in maximal cycloplegia within 30 minutes, with recovery of accommodation within 2вАУ3 hours and resolution of mydriasis within
24 hours. оАЬe adequacy of cycloplegia can be determined by
comparing retinoscopy readings with the patient оАЮxating for
distance and then for near (dynamic retinoscopy). Topical
anaesthesia with a well-tolerated agent such as proxymetacaine
prior to instillation of cyclopentolate is useful in preventing
ocular irritation and reоАЭex tearing, thus aоАЩording better retention of the cyclopentolate in the conjunctival sac and eоАЩective
cycloplegia.
оАБ Atropine (0.5% under the age of 12 months and 1% subsequently) has a somewhat stronger cycloplegic eоАЩect than cyclopentolate. In most cases this is clinically insigniоАЮcant, but may
be helpful in instances such as high hypermetropia or heavily
pigmented irides. As the onset of cycloplegia is slower, a parent
or carer may be supplied with topical atropine for instillation
at home twice daily over 1вАУ3 days prior to attendance (but not
on the day of examination) as either eye drops or ointment.
Drops are easier to instil, but there may be less risk of overdose
with ointment. оАЬe atropine should be discontinued if there
are signs of systemic toxicity, such as оАЭushing, fever or restlessness and immediate medical attention sought. оАЬe visual
eоАЩects may last for up to 2 weeks.

Change of refraction with age in childhood

Because refraction changes with age, it is important to check
this in patients with strabismus at least every year and more frequently in younger children and if acuity is reduced. At birth
most babies are hypermetropic. AоАЪer the age of 2 years there may
be an increase in hypermetropia and a decrease in astigmatism.
Hypermetropia may continue to increase until the age of about 6

years, levelling oоАЩ between the ages of 6 and 8 and subsequently
decreasing.

When to prescribe

Most children are mildly hypermetropic (1вАУ3 D). оАЬere is some
evidence that fully correcting hypermetropia in a normal child
may reduce physiological emmetropization.
оАБ Hypermetropia. In general, up to 4 D of hypermetropia
should not be corrected in a child without a squint unless they
are experiencing problems with near vision. With hypermetropia greater than this a two-thirds correction is usually given.
However, in the presence of esotropia, the full cycloplegic correction should be prescribed, even under the age of 2 years.
оАБ Astigmatism. A cylinder of 1.50 D or more should probably
be prescribed, especially in anisometropia aоАЪer the age of 18
months.
оАБ Myopia. The necessity for correction depends on the age
of the child. Under the age of 2 years, вИТ5.00 D or more of
myopia should be corrected; between the ages of 2 and 4
the amount is вИТ3.00 D. Older children should have correction of even low myopia to allow clear distance vision.
Under-correction and bifocals may retard progression and
are under investigation.
оАБ Anisometropia. AоАЪer the age of 3 the full diоАЩerence in refraction between the eyes should be prescribed if it is more than
1 D, with full hypermetropic correction in squint.

TIP In the presence of esotropia, the full cycloplegic
hypermetropic correction should be prescribed, even in
children under the age of 2 years.

PSEUDOSTRABISMUS

Pseudostrabismus is the clinical impression of ocular deviation
when no squint is present.
оАБ Epicanthic folds may simulate an esotropia (Fig. 18.47A).
оАБ Abnormal interpupillary distance, if short may simulate an
esotropia and if wide an exotropia (Fig. 18.47B).
оАБ Angle kappa is the angle between the visual and anatomical
(pupillary) axes (see Fig. 18.1).

{ Normally, the fovea is situated temporal to the anatomical
centre of the posterior pole. оАЬe eyes are therefore slightly
abducted to achieve bifoveal оАЮxation and a light shone
onto the cornea will therefore cause a reоАЭex just nasal to
the centre of the cornea in both eyes (Fig. 18.48A). оАЬis is
termed a positive angle kappa.

{ A large positive angle kappa (e.g. temporally displaced
macula) may give a pseudoexotropia (Fig. 18.48B and see
Fig. 18.1B).

{ A negative angle kappa occurs when the fovea is situated
nasal to the posterior pole (e.g. high myopia). In this situation, the corneal reоАЭex is situated temporally to the centre of the cornea and it may simulate an esotropia (Fig.
18.48C).

A

B

Fig. 18.47 Pseudostrabismus. (A) Prominent epicanthic folds
simulating esotropia; (B) wide interpupillary distance simulating exotropia.

HETEROPHORIA

Heterophoria may present clinically with associated visual symptoms when the fusional amplitudes are insuоАЫcient to maintain
alignment, particularly at times of stress or poor health.
оАБ Signs. Both esophoria and exophoria can be classiоАЮed by the
distance at which the angle is greater: respectively, convergence
excess or weakness, divergence weakness or excess and mixed.
оАБ Treatment

{ Orthoptic treatment is of most value in convergence weakness exophoria.

{ Any signiоАЮcant refractive error should be appropriately
corrected.

{ Symptom relief may otherwise be obtained using temporary stick-on Fresnel prisms and may be subsequently
incorporated into spectacles (maximum usually 10вАУ12 ќФ,
split between the two eyes).

{ Surgery may occasionally be required for larger deviations.

VERGENCE ABNORMALITIES

Convergence insuоАЫciency

Convergence insuоАЫciency (CI) typically aоАЩects individuals with
high near visual demand, such as students.
оАБ Signs. Remote NPC independent of any heterophoria and
poor fusional convergence amplitudes.
оАБ Treatment involves orthoptic exercises aimed at normalizing the near point and maximizing fusional amplitudes.
With good compliance, symptoms should be eliminated
within a few weeks but if persistent can be treated with
base-in prisms.
оАБ Accommodative insuоАЫciency (AI) is occasionally also present. It may be idiopathic (primary) or post-viral and typically
aоАЩects school-age children. оАЬe minimum reading correction
to give clear vision is prescribed but is oоАЪen diоАЫcult to discard.

Divergence insuоАЫciency and divergence
paralysis

оАБ Divergence insuоАЫciency is recognized these days as being
part of a common condition called вАШsagging eye syndrome вАУ
SES,вАЩ which occurs in elderly adults (see later in this chapter).
If symmetrical, age-related distance esotropia (ARDE) results
and if asymmetrical, unilateral hypotropia with excyclotropia
results (вАШcyclovertical strabismusвАЩ).

738 Esotropia

оАБ Divergence paralysis is a rare condition typically associated
with underlying neurological disease such as intracranial
space-occupying lesions, cerebrovascular accidents and head
trauma. Presentation may be at any age and it may be diоАЫcult
to distinguish from sixth nerve palsy. It is primarily a concomitant esodeviation with reduced or absent divergence fusional
amplitudes. However, it has none of the other clinical or radiological features of SES. It is diоАЫcult to treat; prisms are the best
option.

Near reоАЬex insuоАЫciency

оАБ Paresis of the near reоАЭex presents as dual convergence and
accommodation insuоАЫciency. Mydriasis may be seen on
attempted near оАЮxation. Treatment involves reading glasses,
base-in prisms and possibly botulinum toxin (orthoptic exercises have no eоАЩect). It is diоАЫcult to eradicate.
оАБ Complete paralysis in which no convergence or accommodation can be initiated may be of functional origin, due to midbrain disease or aоАЪer head trauma; recovery is possible.

Spasm of the near reоАЬex

Spasm of the near reоАЭex is a functional condition aоАЩecting patients
of all ages (mainly females). Diplopia, blurred vision and headaches are the presenting symptoms.
оАБ Signs

{ Esotropia, pseudomyopia and miosis.

{ Spasm may be triggered when testing ocular movements
(Fig. 18.49A).

{ Observation of miosis is the key to the diagnosis (Fig. 18.49B).

ESOTROPIA

Esotropia (manifest convergent squint) may be concomitant or
incomitant. In a concomitant esotropia the variability of the angle
of deviation is within 5 ќФ in diоАЩerent horizontal gaze positions. In
an incomitant deviation the angle diоАЩers in various positions of
gaze as a result of abnormal innervation or restriction. оАЬis section
deals only with concomitant esotropia. A classiоАЮcation is shown in
Table 18.1. However, all squints are diоАЩerent and not all оАЮt neatly
into a classiоАЮcation. For example, a microtropia may occur with a
number of the other categories. It is more important to understand
the part played by binocular function, refractive error and accommodation in the pathophysiology of each individual squint and to
tailor treatment accordingly.

Early-onset esotropia

Up to the age of 4 months, infrequent episodes of convergence are
normal but thereaоАЪer ocular misalignment is abnormal. Earlyonset (essential infantile) esotropia is an idiopathic esotropia
developing within the оАЮrst 6 months of life in an otherwise normal
infant with no signiоАЮcant refractive error and no limitation of ocular movements.

Signs

оАБ оАЬe angle is usually fairly large (>30 ќФ) and stable.
оАБ Fixation in most infants is alternating in the primary position
(Fig. 18.50).
оАБ оАЬere is cross-оАЮxating in side gaze, so that the child uses the
leоАЪ eye in right gaze (Fig. 18.51A) and the right eye on leоАЪ gaze
(Fig. 18.51B). Such cross-оАЮxation may give a false impression
of bilateral abduction deоАЮcits, as in bilateral sixth nerve palsy.
оАБ Abduction can usually be demonstrated, either by the dollвАЩs
head manoeuvre or by rotating the child.

{ Refraction with and without cycloplegia conоАЮrms the
pseudomyopia, which must not be corrected optically.
оАБ Treatment involves reassurance and advising the patient to
discontinue any activity that triggers the response. If persistent, atropine and a full reading correction are prescribed but
it is diоАЫcult later to abandon treatment without recurrence.
Patients usually manage to live a fairly normal life despite the
symptoms.

оАБ Should these fail, uniocular patching for a few hours will oоАЪen
unmask the ability of the other eye to abduct.
оАБ Nystagmus is usually horizontal.
оАБ Latent nystagmus (LN) is seen only when one eye is covered
and the fast phase beats towards the side of the оАЮxing eye. оАЬis
means that the direction of the fast phase reverses according to
which eye is covered.
оАБ Manifest latent nystagmus (MLN) is the same except that
nystagmus is present with both eyes open, but the amplitude
increases when one is covered.
оАБ оАЬe refractive error is usually normal for the age of the child
(about +1 to +2 D).
оАБ Asymmetry of optokinetic nystagmus is present.
оАБ Inferior oblique overaction may be present initially or develop
later.
оАБ Dissociated vertical deviation (DVD) develops in 80% by the
age of 3 years.

A

B

Fig. 18.50 Alternating оАЯxation in early-onset esotropia. (A)
Fixating with right eye; (B) оАЯxating with left eye. (Courtesy of
ADN Murray.)

CHAPTER

Strabismus 739
#### 18

оАБ DiоАЪerential diagnosis includes bilateral congenital sixth
nerve palsy, secondary (sensory) esotropia due to organic eye
disease, nystagmus blockage syndrome in which convergence
dampens a horizontal nystagmus and mechanical limitations
of eye movement such as Duane and M√ґbius syndromes and
strabismus оАЮxus.

Initial treatment

оАБ Timing of surgery. AоАЪer an initial period of variability, the
angle of esotropia becomes constant at about 4вАУ5 months
of age. Once any amblyopia and signiоАЮcant refractive error
have been appropriately corrected, the eyes should be surgically aligned within 4 months of the date of onset of constant
esotropia. Evidence is accumulating that surgical alignment
within that time frame may achieve a superior sensory outcome and improved binocular function.
оАБ оАЯe initial procedure can be either recession of both medial
rectus muscles or unilateral medial rectus muscle recession
with lateral rectus muscle resection. Angles larger than 50 ќФ
will require recession of both medical rectus muscles as well as
resection of one lateral rectus muscle.
оАБ Angles larger than 65 ќФ may require recession of both medial
rectus muscles as well as resection of both lateral rectus muscles.
оАБ Large recessions. Recessions of medial rectus muscles of
more than 6.5 mm have been abandoned as they lead to late
over-corrections.
оАБ SigniоАЭcant inferior oblique overaction should be dealt with
surgically.
оАБ An acceptable goal is alignment of the eyes to within 8 ќФ,
associated with peripheral fusion and central suppression (Fig.
18.52). оАЬis small-angle residual strabismus is oоАЪen stable,
even though bifoveal fusion is not achieved.

Subsequent treatment

оАБ Under-correction may require further recession of the medial
recti, resection of one or both lateral recti or surgery to the
other eye, depending on the initial procedure.

A B

Fig. 18.51 Cross-оАЯxation in early-onset esotropia. (A) Left оАЯxation on right gaze; (B) right оАЯxation on left gaze. (Courtesy of ADN Murray.)

оАБ Inferior oblique overaction may develop subsequently, most
commonly at age 2 years (Fig. 18.53). оАЬe parents should
therefore be warned that further surgery may be necessary
despite an initially good result. Initially unilateral, it frequently
becomes bilateral within 6 months. Inferior oblique weakening procedures include disinsertion, recession and myectomy.
оАБ DVD (Fig. 18.54) is characterized by up-driоАЪ with excyclorotation (extorsion) of the eye when under cover, or spontaneously

A

C

Fig. 18.54 Dissociated vertical deviation. (A) Straight eyes in

during periods of visual inattention. When the cover is
removed the aоАЩected eye will move down without a corresponding down-driоАЪ of the other eye. It is usually bilateral.
Surgical treatment may be indicated for psychosocial reasons.
Options include superior rectus recession with or without posterior оАЮxation sutures, resection or tuck of the inferior rectus
muscle and inferior oblique anterior transposition.
оАБ Amblyopia subsequently develops in about 50% of cases as unilateral оАЮxation preference commonly develops postoperatively.
оАБ An accommodative element should be suspected if the eyes
are initially straight or almost straight aоАЪer surgery and then
start to become convergent. Regular refraction is therefore
important.

TIP Inferior oblique overaction needs to be distinguished
from dissociated vertical deviation.

Accommodative esotropia

Near vision involves both accommodation and convergence.
Accommodation is the process by which the eye focuses on a near
target, by altering the curvature of the crystalline lens. Simultaneously, the eyes converge, in order to оАЮxate bifoveally on the target.
Both accommodation and convergence are quantitatively related
to the proximity of the target and have a fairly constant relationship to each other (AC/A ratio) as described previously. Abnormalities of the AC/A ratio are an important cause of certain types
of esotropia.

A

B

Fig. 18.55 Fully accommodative esotropia. (A) Right esotropia
without glasses; (B) straight eyes for near and distance with
glasses.

Refractive accommodative esotropia

In this type of accommodative esotropia, the AC/A ratio is normal
and esotropia is a physiological response to excessive hypermetropia, usually between +2.00 and +7.00 D. оАЬe considerable degree
of accommodation required to focus clearly on even a distant target is accompanied by a proportionate amount of convergence,
which is beyond the patientвАЩs fusional divergence amplitude. It
cannot therefore be controlled and a manifest convergent squint
results. оАЬe magnitude of the deviation varies little (usually <10 ќФ)
between distance and near. оАЬe deviation typically presents at the
age of 18 months to 3 years (range 6 months to 7 years).
оАБ Fully accommodative esotropia is characterized by hypermetropia with esotropia when the refractive error is uncorrected
(Fig. 18.55A). оАЬe deviation is eliminated and BSV is present
at all distances following optical correction of hypermetropia
(Fig. 18.55B). оАЬis may occasionally be seen in a baby (Fig.
18.56), which illustrates the point that irrespective of age all
children with esotropia should undergo cycloplegic refraction.
оАБ Partially accommodative esotropia is reduced but not
eliminated by full correction of hypermetropia (Fig. 18.57).
Amblyopia is frequent as well as bilateral congenital superior
oblique weakness. Most cases show suppression of the squinting eye although ARC may occur, but of lower grade than in
microtropia.

Non-refractive accommodative esotropia

In this type of accommodative esotropia the AC/A ratio is high so
that a unit increase of accommodation is accompanied by a disproportionately large increase in convergence. оАЬis occurs independently of refractive error, although hypermetropia frequently
coexists. Subtypes are set out below.
оАБ Convergence excess

{ Straight eyes with BSV for distance (Fig. 18.58A).

{ Esotropia for near, usually with suppression (Fig. 18.58B).

{ Straight eyes through bifocals (Fig. 18.58C).
оАБ Hypoaccommodative convergence excess

(accommodation is weak, necessitating increased eоАЩort,
which produces over-convergence).

{ Remote near point of accommodation.

{ Straight eyes with BSV for distance.

{ Esotropia for near, usually with suppression.

{ High AC/A ratio due to increased accommodative convergence (accommodation is normal, convergence is
increased).

Treatment

оАБ Correction of refractive error is the initial treatment.

{ In children under the age of 6 years, the full cycloplegic
hypermetropic refraction revealed on retinoscopy should
be prescribed, with a deduction only for the working

{ Normal near point of accommodation.

distance. In the child with fully accommodative refractive
esotropia this will control the deviation for both near and
distance.

functioning. It should be considered only if spectacles do not
fully correct the deviation and aоАЪer every attempt has been
made to treat amblyopia.

{ Bilateral medial rectus recessions are performed in
patients in whom the deviation for near is greater than that
for distance.

{ If there is no signiоАЮcant diоАЩerence between distance and
near measurements and equal vision in both eyes, some
perform unilateral medial rectus recession combined with
lateral rectus resection, whereas others prefer bilateral
medial rectus recessions.

{ In patients with residual amblyopia, surgery is usually performed on the amblyopic eye.

{ In partially accommodative esotropia, surgery to improve
appearance is best delayed until requested by the child.
оАЬis avoids early consecutive exotropia. It should aim to
correct only the residual squint present with glasses.

{ оАЬe usual оАЮrst procedure for convergence excess esotropia is recession of both medial rectus muscles. оАЬis relies
on fusion to prevent a distance exotropia; a few patients
become divergent aоАЪer surgery and need a further
procedure.

{ Medial rectus posterior оАЮxation sutures (Faden operation) or оАЮxation of the medial rectus to its surrounding
pulley (вАШpulley оАЮxationвАЩ) can also be used either as a оАЮrst
procedure, or in the case of under-correction, following
bimedial recessions.

Microtropia

Microtropia is a small angle (<10 ќФ) squint. Binocular cooperation in microtropia is more substantial than in most manifest deviations and it may be considered more a description
of binocular status than a specific diagnosis. This condition
is also known as monofixation syndrome. It may be primary
or secondary, the latter representing the sequela to strabismus
surgery or other treatment (e.g. optical, such as fully accommodative esotropia controlled with glasses to a microtropia rather
than true bifoveal BSV) for a larger deviation and occasionally
to other pathology.
оАБ Symptoms are rare unless there is an associated decompensating heterophoria.
оАБ оАЬe small manifest angle of deviation may not be readily
detectable on cover testing.
оАБ оАЬere is a prominent association with anisometropia, commonly hypermetropia or hypermetropic astigmatism, with
amblyopia (typically mild) of the more ametropic eye.
оАБ оАЬere is normal motor fusion as demonstrated by fusional
amplitudes.
оАБ Anomalous retinal correspondence (ARC) is present, with
associated abnormal BSV. оАЬe fovea of the оАЮxating eye acquires
an anomalous common visual direction with an extrafoveal
location in the deviating eye. Under binocular conditions
extrafoveal оАЮxation occurs in the deviating eye with a central
foveal suppression scotoma and peripheral fusion. However,
when the preferred eye is covered, оАЮxation in the deviating eye

{ AоАЪer the age of 8 years, refraction should be performed
without cycloplegia and the maximal amount of вАШplusвАЩ that
can be tolerated (manifest hypermetropia) prescribed.

{ For convergence excess esotropia bifocals may be prescribed to relieve accommodation (and thereby accommodative convergence), thus allowing the child to maintain
bifoveal оАЮxation and ocular alignment at near (see Fig.
18.58C). оАЬe minimum вАШaddвАЩ required to achieve this is
prescribed.

{ The most satisfactory form of bifocals is the executive
type in which the intersection crosses the lower border
of the pupil. The strength of the lower segment should
be gradually reduced and eliminated by the early teenage years.

{ Bifocals are also used in hypoaccommodative esotropia
where the AC/A ratio is not overly excessive and there is
a reasonable chance of discarding bifocal correction with
time.

{ At higher levels surgery is the better long-term option.
оАЬe ultimate prognosis for complete withdrawal of spectacles is related to the magnitude of the AC/A ratio and to
the degree of hypermetropia and associated astigmatism.
Spectacles may be needed only for close work.
оАБ Surgery is aimed at restoring or improving BSV, or at improving the appearance of the squint and so the childвАЩs social

Strabismus 743
#### 18

then returns to the fovea and the eye is aligned. Stereopsis is
usually present, but reduced.
оАБ оАЬe 4 ќФ prism test, discussed earlier in the chapter, is useful in
assessment.

Treatment

Treatment involves correction of refractive error and occlusion for
amblyopia as indicated. оАЬere is evidence that aggressive treatment
sometimes leads to normalization. Most patients remain stable and
symptom-free.

Other esotropias

Near esotropia (non-accommodative
convergence excess)

оАБ Presentation is usually in older children and young adults.
оАБ Signs

{ No signiоАЮcant refractive error.

{ Orthophoria or small esophoria with BSV for distance.

{ Esotropia for near but normal or low AC/A ratio.

{ Normal near point of accommodation.
оАБ Treatment involves bilateral medial rectus recessions.

Distance esotropia

оАБ Presentation is in healthy young adults, who are oоАЪen myopic.
оАБ Signs

{ Intermittent or constant esotropia for distance.

{ Minimal or no deviation for near.

{ Normal bilateral abduction.

{ Fusional divergence amplitudes may be reduced.

{ Absence of neurological disease.
оАБ Treatment is with prisms until spontaneous resolution or surgery in persistent cases.

A

B

Fig. 18.59 Sensory esotropia. (A) Secondary to a retinoblastoma in the right eye; (B) secondary to microphthalmos and
aphakia in Down syndrome. (Courtesy of ADN Murray вАУ оАЯg. B.)

Consecutive esotropia

Consecutive esotropia follows surgical over-correction of an exodeviation. If it occurs following surgery for an intermittent exotropia in a child it should not be allowed to persist for more than 6
weeks without further intervention.

Sagging eye syndrome

оАБ Introduction. оАЬis is one of the commonest causes of acquired
strabismus in elderly non-myopic individuals and occurs in association with blepharoptosis and deepening of the superior sulcus.
оАЬe condition arises as a consequence of age-related degeneration of the ligaments which interconnect the extraocular muscles.
оАЬis results in bilateral inferior sagging of the lateral rectus pulley,
causing esotropia and/or cyclovertical malalignment.
оАБ Symptoms. Initially the individual notices intermittent binocular
diplopia in the distance, which becomes permanent over a period
of several months. оАЬe distance deviation is rarely more than 10ќФ
оАБ Signs

Acute (late-onset) esotropia

оАБ Presentation is at around 5вАУ6 years of age.
оАБ Signs

{ Sudden onset of diplopia and esotropia.

{ Distance esotropia, with no near esotropia.

{ Normal ocular motility without signiоАЮcant refractive error.

{ Full horizontal ductions and clinically normal horizontal
saccadic eye velocities.

{ Underlying sixth nerve palsy must be excluded.
оАБ Management

{ Limited supraduction of both eyes.

{ Because the onset of comitant esotropia in an older child
may indicate an underlying neurological disorder, it is
important to check the pupil reоАЭexes and exclude optic
disc changes, nystagmus and a sixth nerve palsy. Neuroradiological examination may be needed.
оАБ Treatment is aimed at re-establishing BSV to prevent suppression using prisms, botulinum toxin or surgery.

Secondary (sensory) esotropia

Secondary esotropia is caused by a unilateral reduction in VA that
interferes with or abolishes fusion. Causes can include cataract,
optic atrophy or hypoplasia, macular scarring or retinoblastoma
(Fig. 18.59A and B). Fundus examination under mydriasis is therefore essential in all children with strabismus.

{ Blepharoptosis.

{ Deep superior sulcus.

{ If downward displacement of the lateral rectus pulley is
asymmetrical, unilateral hypotropia with excyclotropia
results (see: Cyclovertical strabismus).
оАБ Treatment. Most patients can be treated conservatively by
using a prism incorporated in distance spectacles, as they do
not have diplopia when undertaking near tasks. If surgery is
needed, resection or plication of the lateral rectus or recession
of the medial rectus can be considered.

High myopia esotropia (вАШheavy eye syndromeвАЩ)

оАБ Introduction. оАЬis is a cause of acquired esotropia in adults secondary to high myopia (average: вИТ18D; range вИТ12D to вИТ21D

744 Exotropia

and axial length 27вАУ35mm). оАЬe condition occurs as a consequence of shiоАЪing of the lateral rectus and superior rectus
muscles secondary to an elongated posterior portion of a
highly myopic eyeball. Imaging consistently demonstrates
inferior shiоАЪ of the lateral rectus and nasal shiоАЪ of the inferior
and superior recti causing the eye to shiоАЪ supero-temporally
from the pulley array, converting the lateral rectus from abductor to infraductor.
оАБ Symptoms. Patients present with diplopia associated with
limitation of abduction and elevation.
оАБ Treatment. Surgery involves loop myopexy, uniting adjacent
borders of the lateral rectus and superior rectus with nonabsorbable sutures. оАЬis condition responds poorly to lateral
rectus tightening or medial rectus recession alone.

Cyclic esotropia

Cyclic esotropia is a very rare condition characterized by alternating manifest esotropia with suppression and BSV, each typically lasting 24 hours. оАЬe condition may persist for months or
years and the patient may eventually develop a constant esotropia
requiring surgery.

EXOTROPIA

Constant (early-onset) exotropia

A

B

Fig. 18.60 Intermittent exotropia. (A) Eyes straight most of the
time; (B) left exotropia under conditions of visual inattention
or fatigue. (Courtesy of M Parulekar.)

ClassiоАЮcation

оАБ Distance excess exotropia, in which the angle of deviation is
greater for distance than near and increases further beyond 6
metres. Simulated and true forms are recognized.

оАБ Presentation is oоАЪen at birth.
оАБ Signs

{ Normal refraction.

{ Large and constant angle.

{ DVD may be present.
оАБ Neurological anomalies are frequently present, in contrast
with infantile esotropia.
оАБ Treatment is mainly surgical and consists of lateral rectus
recession and medial rectus resection.
оАБ DiоАЪerential diagnosis is secondary exotropia, which may
conceal serious ocular pathology.

{ Simulated (formerly pseudo-divergence excess) is associated with a high AC/A ratio or with вАШtenacious proximal
fusionвАЩ (TPF вАУ tonic fusional convergence that relaxes aоАЪer
occlusion). оАЬe distance angle initially seems to be larger
than the near angle, but the deviation for near and distance
is similar when the near angle is remeasured with the
patient looking through +3.00 D lenses (high AC/A controlling exodeviation) or aоАЪer 30вАУ60 minutes of uniocular occlusion to relax TPF, the latter with a normal AC/A
ratio).

Intermittent exotropia

Diagnosis

{ Presentation is oоАЪen at around 2 years with exophoria,
which breaks down to exotropia under conditions of visual
inattention, bright light (resulting in reоАЭex closure of the
aоАЩected eye), fatigue or ill health.

{ Signs. The eyes are straight with BSV at times (Fig.
18.60A) and manifest with suppression at other
times (Fig. 18.60B). Control of the squint varies with
the distance of fixation and other factors such as
concentration

TIP Children with intermittent exotropia tend to close an eye
when exposed to bright light.

{ True. оАЬe angle for near remains signiоАЮcantly less than that
for distance with the above tests.
оАБ Basic exotropia, in which control of the squint and the angle of
deviation are the same for distance and near оАЮxation.
оАБ Convergence insuоАЫciency exotropia, in which the deviation
is greater for near оАЮxation. It tends to occur in older children
and adults and may be associated with acquired myopia or
presbyopia.

Treatment

оАБ Spectacle correction in myopic patients may, in some cases,
control the deviation by stimulating accommodation and with
it, convergence. In some cases, over-minus prescription may
be useful.
оАБ Part-time occlusion of the non-deviating eye may improve
control in some patients and orthoptic exercises may be helpful for near exotropia.
оАБ Surgery. Patients with eоАЩective and stable control of their
intermittent exotropia are oоАЪen just observed. Surgery is
indicated if control is poor or is progressively deteriorating.
Unilateral lateral rectus recession and medial rectus resection

are generally preferred except in true distance exotropia when
bilateral lateral rectus recessions are more usual. Similar results
are achieved with either approach. оАЬe exodeviation is rarely
completely eliminated by surgery.

Sensory exotropia

Secondary (sensory) exotropia is the result of monocular or binocular visual impairment by acquired lesions, such as cataract,
corneal scarring (Fig. 18.61A and B) or other media opacity.
Treatment consists of correction of the visual deоАЮcit, if possible,
followed by surgery if appropriate. A minority of patients develop
intractable diplopia due to loss of fusion, even when good VA is
restored to both eyes and the eyes are realigned.

Consecutive exotropia

Consecutive exotropia develops spontaneously in an amblyopic
eye, or more frequently following surgical correction of an esodeviation. Postoperative divergence occurring hours to months aоАЪer
initial alignment is usually due to muscle slippage (see Fig. 18.75).
If it occurs years later, it is usually due to a stretched scar. Most
cases present in adult life with psychosocial concerns and can be
greatly helped by surgery. Careful evaluation of the risk of postoperative diplopia is required, although serious problems are uncommon. About 75% of patients are still well aligned 10 years aоАЪer
surgery, although re-divergence may occur.

Congenital cranial dysinnervation
disorders

Congenital cranial dysinnervation disorders (CCDD) or congenital innervation dysgenesis syndromes constitute a group of
disorders originally believed to be the result of congenital muscular оАЮbrosis, but now known to be the result of brainstem or
cranial nerve developmental disturbance, in most cases having
an identiоАЮable genetic basis. Systemic associations are recognized
for several.

Duane retraction syndrome

In Duane retraction syndrome (DRS) there is failure of innervation of the lateral rectus by a hypoplastic sixth nerve nucleus, with
anomalous innervation of the lateral rectus by оАЮbres from the third
nerve. оАЬe condition is oоАЪen bilateral. Up to half of patients have
associated systemic defects such as deafness, external ear abnormalities, speech disorder and skeletal abnormalities. Associated
mutations in several genes have been found. Approximately 10%
of cases are familial.

Clinical features

оАБ A face turn is typical and confers BSV with the face in the
turned position, thus avoiding amblyopia.
оАБ Complete or partial restriction of abduction.
оАБ Restricted adduction, usually partial.

оАБ Retraction of the globe on adduction as a result of cocontraction of the medial and lateral recti with resultant narrowing of the palpebral оАЮssure.
оАБ An up-shoot or down-shoot in adduction may be present.
In some cases this is produced by a tight lateral rectus muscle
slipping over or under the globe to produce an anomalous vertical movement.
оАБ DeоАЭciency of convergence in which the aоАЩected eye remains
оАЮxed in the primary position while the unaоАЩected eye is
converging.

TIP A face turn, which is commonly found in Duane retraction
syndrome, confers binocular single vision with the face in the
turned position.

ClassiоАЮcation (Huber)

оАБ Type I, the most common, is characterized by (Fig. 18.62):

{ Limited or absent abduction.

{ Normal or mildly limited adduction.

{ In the primary position, straight or slight esotropia.
оАБ Type II, the least common, is characterized by (Fig. 18.63):

{ Limited adduction.

{ Normal or mildly limited abduction.

{ In primary position, straight or slight exotropia.
оАБ Type III is characterized by:

{ Limited adduction and abduction.

{ In the primary position, straight or slight esotropia.

{ In some cases, phenotypic variants have been allied to differing genotypes.

Treatment

оАЬe majority of patients with Duane syndrome do not need any
surgical intervention.
оАБ Most young children maintain BSV by using a CHP to compensate for their lateral rectus weakness and surgery is needed
