only if there is evidence of loss of binocular function. оАЬis may
be indicated by failure to continue to use a CHP.
оАБ In adults or children over the age of about 8 years surgery can
reduce a head posture that is psychosocially unacceptable or
causing neck discomfort. Surgery may also be necessary for
cosmetically unacceptable up-shoots, down-shoots or severe
globe retraction.
оАБ Amblyopia, when present, is usually the result of anisometropia rather than strabismus.
оАБ Depending on the clinical features, unilateral or bilateral
medial rectus and/or lateral rectus recession are the procedures of choice. In order to improve abduction, transposition of the superior and inferior rectus muscles (or just the
superior rectus muscle) to the lateral rectus muscle may be
required.
оАБ оАЬe lateral rectus of the involved side should not be resected, as
this increases retraction.

M√ґbius syndrome

M√ґbius syndrome is a rare, usually sporadic, condition, the basic
components of which are congenital non-progressive bilateral
sixth and seventh cranial nerve palsies that are believed to relate to
a developmental abnormality of the brainstem.
оАБ Systemic features

{ Bilateral facial palsy, which is usually asymmetrical and
oоАЪen incomplete, giving rise to an expressionless facial
appearance and problems with eyelid closure (Fig. 18.64A).

Congenital оАЯbrosis of the extraocular
muscles

Congenital fibrosis of the extraocular muscles (CFEOM) is a
rare non-progressive, usually autosomal dominant, disorder
characterized by bilateral ptosis and restrictive external ophthalmoplegia. Numerous forms have been identified, of which
CFEOM1 is the most common and there is considerable genetic
heterogeneity. CFEOM1 is caused by mutations in the KIF21A
gene, which produces a protein used for intracellular transport
that plays a key role in some aspects of cranial nerve development and pathologically there is a hypoplastic superior division of the oculomotor nerve. Typically, vertical movements are
severely restricted with inability to elevate the eyes above the
horizontal plane.
In the primary position each eye is оАЮxed below the horizontal by about 10¬∞, with a corresponding compensatory chin elevation. оАЬe degree of residual horizontal movement varies markedly.
Ptosis is common (Fig. 18.65).

{ оАЬe оАЮоАЪh, eighth, tenth and twelоАЪh (Fig. 18.64B) cranial

{ Limb anomalies and mild mental handicap may be present.

{ Occasionally, third and fourth nerve palsy and ptosis.

Strabismus оАЯxus

Strabismus fixus is a rare condition in which both eyes are fixed
by fibrous tightening of the medial recti (convergent strabismus
fixus вАУ Fig. 18.66A), or the lateral recti (divergent strabismus
fixus вАУ Fig. 18.66B). Congenital and acquired forms have been
described.

Other CCDD syndromes with
ophthalmic features

Marcus Gunn jaw-winking syndrome (see Ch. 2) is now believed
to be a CCDD, as are horizontal gaze palsy and progressive scoliosis, many cases of congenital ptosis, congenital fourth cranial
nerve palsy and congenital facial nerve palsy.

Fig. 18.66 Strabismus оАЯxus. (A) Convergent; (B) divergent.

MONOCULAR ELEVATION DEFICIENCY

Monocular elevation deоАЮciency (MED), formerly double elevator palsy, is a rare sporadic condition that in at least some cases
may be categorized as a CCDD. It is thought to manifest primarily
with a tight or contracted inferior rectus muscle or a hypoplastic
or ineоАЩective superior rectus muscle. оАЬere is a profound inability
to elevate one eye across the horizontal plane, from abduction to
adduction (Fig. 18.67), with orthophoria in the primary position
in about one-third of cases. Chin elevation may be present. A baseup prism can be helpful.

BROWN SYNDROME

Brown syndrome is a condition involving mechanical restriction,
typically of the superior oblique tendon. It is usually congenital
but occasionally acquired. Recent evidence strongly suggests that
at least some congenital cases should be categorized as a CCDD.

748 Alphabet Patterns

elevation in abduction; (B) in upgaze; and (C) in adduction.

ClassiоАЮcation

оАБ Congenital

{ Idiopathic.

{ вАШCongenital click syndromeвАЩ, where there is impaired movement of the superior oblique tendon through the trochlea.
оАБ Acquired
оАБ Trauma to the trochlea or superior oblique tendon.
оАБ InоАЭammation of the tendon, which may be caused by rheumatoid arthritis, pansinusitis or scleritis.

Types

All have limited elevation in adduction (Fig. 18.68A).
оАБ Mild

{ No hypotropia in primary position.

{ No down-shoot in adduction.
оАБ Moderate

{ No hypotropia in the primary position (Fig. 18.68B).

{ Down-shoot in adduction (Fig. 18.68C).
оАБ Severe

{ Hypotropia in the primary position.

{ Down-shoot in adduction.

{ Chin-up head posture.

{ Face-turn away from the aоАЩected eye.

Treatment

оАБ Congenital cases do not usually require treatment as long
as binocular function is maintained with an acceptable head
posture. Spontaneous improvement is oоАЪen seen towards
the end of the оАЮrst decade. Indications for treatment include

adduction; (B) straight in the primary position; (C) down-shoot
in adduction. (Courtesy of ADN Murray.)

signiоАЮcant primary position hypotropia, deteriorating control and/or an unacceptable head posture. оАЬe recommended
procedure for congenital cases is lengthening of the superior
oblique tendon.
оАБ Acquired; treatable aetiology should be addressed speciоАЮcally.
Depending on the cause, acquired cases may beneоАЮt from
steroids, either orally or by injection near the trochlea.

TIP Congenital Brown syndrome does not usually require
treatment as long as binocular function is maintained with an
acceptable head posture.

ALPHABET PATTERNS

вАШVвАЩ or вАШAвАЩ patterns occur when the relative contributions of the superior rectus and inferior oblique to elevation, or of the inferior rectus and superior oblique to depression, are abnormal, resulting in
derangement of the balance of their horizontal vectors in up- and
downgaze. оАЬey can also be caused by anomalies in the position of
the rectus muscle pulleys. Assessment is by measuring horizontal
deviations in the primary position, upgaze and downgaze. оАЬey
can occur in both concomitant and incomitant deviations.

вАШVвАЩ pattern

A вАШVвАЩ pattern (Fig. 18.69) is said to be signiоАЮcant when the diоАЩerence between upgaze and downgaze is вЙ•15 ќФ

A

B

Fig. 18.69 вАШVвАЩ pattern. (A) Esotropia; (B) exotropia.

Causes

оАБ Inferior oblique overaction associated with fourth nerve
palsy.
оАБ Superior oblique underaction with subsequent inferior oblique
overaction, seen in infantile esotropia as well as other childhood esotropias. оАЬe eyes are oоАЪen straight in upgaze with a
marked esodeviation in downgaze.
оАБ Superior rectus underaction.
оАБ Brown syndrome.
оАБ Craniofacial anomalies featuring shallow orbits and downslanting palpebral оАЮssures.

Treatment

Treatment is by inferior oblique weakening or superior oblique
strengthening when oblique dysfunction is present. Without
oblique muscle dysfunction, treatment is as follows:
оАБ вАШVвАЩ pattern esotropia (Fig. 18.69A) can be treated by bilateral
medial rectus recessions and downward transposition of the
tendons.

Strabismus 749
#### 18

A

B

Fig. 18.70 вАШAвАЩ pattern. (A) Esotropia; (B) exotropia.

оАБ вАШVвАЩ pattern exotropia (Fig. 18.69B) can be treated by bilateral lateral rectus recessions and upward transposition of the
tendons.

вАШAвАЩ pattern

An вАШAвАЩ pattern (Fig. 18.70) is considered signiоАЮcant if the diоАЩerence
between upgaze and downgaze is вЙ•10 ќФ. A particular complaint
may be diоАЫculty with reading if the patient is binocular.

Causes

оАБ Primary superior oblique overaction is usually associated with
exodeviation in the primary position of gaze.
оАБ Inferior oblique underaction/palsy with subsequent superior
oblique overaction.
оАБ Inferior rectus underaction.

Treatment

Patients with oblique dysfunction are treated by superior oblique
posterior tenotomy, which will correct 20 ќФ of вАШAвАЩ pattern. Total

750 Surgery

tenotomy will correct up to 50 ќФ of вАШAвАЩ pattern. Treatment of cases
without oblique muscle dysfunction is as follows:
оАБ вАШAвАЩ pattern esotropia (Fig. 18.70A) is treated by bilateral medial
rectus recessions and upward transposition of the tendons.
оАБ вАШAвАЩ pattern exotropia (Fig. 18.70B) is treated by bilateral lateral
rectus recessions and downward transposition of the tendons.

SURGERY

оАЬe most common aims of surgery on the extraocular muscles are
to correct misalignment to improve appearance and, if possible,
to restore BSV. Surgery can also be used to reduce an abnormal
head posture and to expand or centralize a оАЮeld of BSV. However,
the оАЮrst step in the management of childhood strabismus involves
correction of any signiоАЮcant refractive error and/or treatment of
amblyopia. Once maximal visual potential is reached in both eyes,
any residual deviation can be treated surgically. оАЬe three main
types of procedure are:
оАБ Strengthening, to enhance the pull of a muscle.
оАБ Weakening to decrease the eоАЩective strength of action of a
muscle.
оАБ Vector adjustment procedures that have the primary aim of
altering the direction of muscle action.

TIP Before surgical realignment is undertaken any signiоАЯcant
refractive error and/or underlying amblyopia must be corrected.

Strengthening procedures

{ оАЬe tendon is disinserted from the sclera and the amount
of recession is measured and marked on the sclera with
calipers (Fig. 18.73C).

{ оАЬe detached end of the muscle is sutured to the sclera at
the measured distance behind its original insertion. Alternatively, a hang-back technique can be used (Fig. 18.73D).
оАБ Inferior oblique recession

{ оАЬe muscle belly is exposed through an inferotemporal
fornix incision.

{ A squint hook is passed behind the posterior border of the
muscle, which must be clearly visualized. Care is taken to
pick up the muscle without disrupting the Tenon capsule
and fat posterior to it.

{ An absorbable suture is passed through the anterior border
of the muscle at its insertion and tied.

{ оАЬe muscle is disinserted, and the cut end sutured to the
sclera 3 mm posterior and temporal to the temporal edge
of the inferior rectus insertion.

оАБ Resection shortens a muscle to enhance its eоАЩective pull. It
is suitable only for a rectus muscle and involves the following
steps:

{ оАЬe muscle is exposed and two absorbable sutures inserted
at a measured distance behind its insertion (Fig. 18.71AвАУE).

Disinsertion

Disinsertion (or myectomy) involves detaching a muscle from
its insertion without reattachment. It is most commonly used to
weaken an overacting inferior oblique muscle, when the technique
is the same as for a recession except that the muscle is not sutured.
Very occasionally, disinsertion is performed on a severely contracted rectus muscle.

Posterior оАЮxation suture

оАЬe principle of this (Faden) procedure is to suture the muscle belly
to the sclera posteriorly so as to decrease the pull of the muscle in
its оАЮeld of action without aоАЩecting the eye in the primary position.
оАЬe Faden procedure may be used on the medial rectus to reduce
convergence in a convergence excess esotropia and on the superior
rectus to treat DVD. When treating DVD, the superior rectus muscle
may also be recessed. оАЬe belly of the muscle is then anchored to the
sclera with a non-absorbable suture about 12 mm behind its insertion. Alternatively, instead of a posterior оАЮxation suture the muscle
belly can be sutured posteriorly to its pulley (вАШpulley оАЮxationвАЩ), which
has the same eоАЩect and avoids placement of the suture into the sclera.

Transposition

Transposition refers to the relocation of one or more extraocular
muscles to substitute for the action of an absent or severely deоАЮcient muscle. оАЬe most common indication is severe lateral rectus
weakness due to acquired sixth cranial nerve palsy (see Fig. 19.79).
Other applications include CCDD (e.g. Duane syndrome), alphabet patterns and monocular elevation deоАЮcit. A variety of techniques involving recti and oblique muscles have been described.

Adjustable sutures

Indications

оАЬe results of strabismus surgery can be improved by the use
of adjustable suture techniques on the rectus muscles. While

{ оАЬe muscle anterior to the sutures is excised (Fig. 18.71FвАУ
H) and the cut end reattached to the original insertion.
оАБ Plication (tucking) has a similar eоАЩect to resection, but has
the advantage that it is less traumatic, does not sacriоАЮce the
anterior ciliary vessels and can be easily reversed if needed. It
can be used to enhance the action of the rectus muscles and is
today frequently preferred to a resection. It can also be used
to tighten a lax superior oblique muscle in congenital fourth
nerve palsy (Fig. 18.72).
оАБ Advancement of the muscle nearer to the limbus can be
used to enhance the action of a previously recessed rectus
muscle.

Weakening procedures

Recession

Recession slackens a muscle by moving it away from its insertion.
оАБ Rectus muscle recession

{ оАЬe muscle is exposed and two absorbable sutures are tied
through the outer quarters of the tendon (Fig. 18.73A and B).

оАБ оАЬe muscle is exposed, sutures inserted and the tendon disinserted from the sclera as for a rectus muscle recession.
оАБ оАЬe two ends of the suture are passed, side by side, through the
stump of the insertion.

together to form a loop (Fig. 18.74B).
оАБ оАЬe conjunctiva is leоАЪ open.

Postoperative adjustment

оАЬis is performed under topical anaesthesia, usually a few hours
aоАЪer surgery when the patient is fully awake.
оАБ оАЬe accuracy of alignment is assessed.
оАБ If ocular alignment is satisfactory, the muscle suture is tied oоАЩ
and its long ends cut short.
оАБ If more recession is required, the noose knot is pulled anteriorly along the muscle suture, thereby providing additional
slack to the recessed muscle and enabling it to move posteriorly when the patient is asked to look towards the оАЮeld of
action of the recessed muscle (Fig. 18.74C).

оАБ Operative

{ Lost or вАШslippedвАЩ muscle (especially the medial rectus

оАБ Postoperative

{ Over-correction and under-correction are common and

develop, is usually maximal at 1вАУ2 weeks following injection and
has generally worn oоАЩ by 3 months. An extraocular muscle lengthens while it is paralyzed by botulinum and its antagonist contracts.
оАЬese changes may produce long-term improvements in the alignment of the eyes. Results are best when there is fusion to stabilize
the alignment. Side eоАЩects are uncommon, although about 16% of
adults and 25% of children may develop some degree of temporary
ptosis. оАЬe following are the main indications:
оАБ Postoperative small-angle residual strabismus (2вАУ8 weeks
following surgery). For example, aоАЪer a recession for esotropia
that has resulted in a small under-correction, an injection can
be given into the medial rectus muscle. AоАЪer the injection the
eye becomes divergent, with no adduction for 3 months. During this period that lateral rectus muscle shortens enough to
reduce the residual esotropia once the eоАЩect of the toxin has
worn oоАЩ.
оАБ To correct infantile esotropia by injecting both medial recti,
so that the eyes become divergent. оАЬe lateral rectus muscles
shorten and the angle of esotropia reduces and may even be
corrected. If not, the procedure can be repeated.
оАБ Active thyroid ophthalmopathy, inоАЭamed or prephthisical
eye when surgery is inappropriate.
оАБ To determine the risk of postoperative diplopia. For
example, in an adult with a consecutive left divergent squint
and left suppression, straightening the eyes may make suppression less effective resulting in diplopia. If postoperative
diplopia testing by correcting the angle with prisms is negative, then the risk of double vision after surgery is very low.
If testing is positive, then the left lateral rectus muscle can
be injected with toxin so that the eyes will either straighten
or converge and the risk of diplopia can be assessed over
several days while the eyes are straight. If diplopia does
occur, the patient is able to judge whether it is troublesome.
оАБ To assess the potential for BSV in a patient with a constant
manifest squint by straightening the eyes temporarily. оАЬe

BOTULINUM TOXIN
CHEMODENERVATION

Temporary paralysis of an extraocular muscle can be induced by
an injection of botulinum toxin (BT) under topical anaesthesia and
electromyographic (EMG) control. оАЬe eоАЩect takes several days to

A B

Fig. 18.76 Principles of botulinum toxin chemodenervation in
left sixth nerve palsy (see text).

deviation can then be corrected surgically if appropriate. A
small proportion of patients maintain BSV long-term aоАЪer the
eоАЩects of the toxin have worn oоАЩ.
оАБ In lateral rectus palsy botulinum toxin can be injected into
the ipsilateral medial rectus to give symptomatic relief during
recovery and to see whether there is any lateral rectus action
when there is medial rectus contracture (Fig. 18.76A). оАЬe
temporary paralysis of the muscle causes relaxation so that the
horizontal forces on the globe are more balanced, thus allowing assessment of lateral rectus function (Fig. 18.76B). Medial
rectus recession or muscle transposition surgery may subsequently be required to achieve ocular alignment (Fig. 18.77AвАУ
C). A similar approach can be used for fourth nerve palsy, with
injection into the ipsilateral inferior oblique or contralateral
inferior rectus.

758 Neuroimaging

NEUROIMAGING

Computed tomography
Physics

Computed tomography (CT) uses X-ray beams to obtain tissue
density values from which detailed cross-sectional images are
formed by a computer. Tissue density is represented by a grey scale,
white being maximum density (e.g. bone) and black being minimum density (e.g. air). Advanced CT scanners are able to acquire
thinner slices leading to improved spatial resolution, together with
faster examination times, without a proportionate increase in radiation dose. Images are acquired in an axial form and can be viewed
in any plane using computer reconstruction. оАЯis multiplanar
information can be an advantage over magnetic resonance (MR)
with regard to anatomical detail. CT is widely available, easy to
perform, relatively inexpensive and quick, but unlike MR exposes
the patient to ionizing radiation.

Contrast enhancement

Iodinated contrast material improves sensitivity and speciоАЮcity but
is contraindicated in patients allergic to iodine and in those with
renal failure. Contrast is not indicated in acute haemorrhage, bony
injury or localization of foreign bodies because it may mask visualization of these high-density structures.

Indications

оАБ Orbital trauma, for the detection of bony lesions such as fractures (blood, herniation of extraocular muscles into the maxillary sinus (Fig. 19.1A) and surgical emphysema).
оАБ Evaluation of the extraocular muscles in thyroid eye disease
(Fig. 19.1B). CT and MRI (see below) have complementary
advantages in the assessment of orbital disease.
оАБ Bony involvement of orbital tumours is better assessed using
CT than MR (Fig. 19.1C).
оАБ Orbital cellulitis for assessment of intraorbital extension and
subperiosteal abscess formation.

A B

C D

Fig. 19.1 CT scans. оАЭAоАЮ Coronal image of a blow-out fracture, showing a defect in the right
orbital оАЭoor and soft tissue prolapse (arrow) into the maxillary sinus; оАЭBоАЮ axial image showing
bilateral enlargement of extraocular muscles; оАЭCоАЮ coronal image showing loss of the left superior bony orbit (arrow) secondary to a tumour; оАЭDоАЮ axial image showing an acute parenchymal
haematoma in the right temporal lobe. (Courtesy of N Sibtain вАУ оАЯg. D.)

оАБ Detection of intraorbital calciоАЯcation as in meningioma and
retinoblastoma.
оАБ Detection of acute cerebral (Fig. 19.1D) or subarachnoid
haemorrhage, which is harder to visualize on MR within the
оАЮrst few hours of onset.
оАБ When MR is contraindicated (e.g. ferrous foreign body).

Magnetic resonance imaging

Physics

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) depends on the rearrangement of positively charged hydrogen nuclei (protons) when a tissue is exposed to a short electromagnetic pulse. When the pulse
subsides, the nuclei return to their normal position, re-radiating
some of the absorbed energy. Sensitive receivers pick up this
electromagnetic echo. Unlike CT, it does not subject the patient

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 759
#### 19

to ionizing radiation. оАЯe signals are analyzed and displayed as a
cross-sectional image that may be axial, coronal or sagittal.

Basic sequences

Weighting refers to two methods of measuring the relaxation times
of the excited protons aоАЭer the magnetic оАЮeld has been switched
oоАЬ. Various body tissues have diоАЬerent relaxation times so that a
given tissue may be T1- or T2-weighted (i.e. best visualized on that
particular type of image). In practice, both types of scans are usually performed. It is easy to tell the diоАЬerence between CT and MR
images because bone appears white on CT but is not clearly demonstrated on MR.
оАБ T1-weighted images are generally optimal for viewing normal
anatomy. Hypointense (dark) structures include vitreous (Fig.
19.2A) and cerebrospinal оАЫuid (CSF) (Fig. 19.2B). Hyperintense (bright) structures include fat, blood, contrast agents and
melanin.

A B

C D

Fig. 19.2 MR scans. оАЭAоАЮ T1-weighted axial image through the globe in which vitreous is
hypointense (dark) and orbital fat is hyperintense (bright); оАЭBоАЮ T1-weighted midline sagittal
image through the brain in which the CSF is hypointense in the lateral and fourth ventricles
(arrows); оАЭCоАЮ T2-weighted axial image through the globe in which vitreous and orbital fat are
hyperintense; оАЭDоАЮ T2-weighted axial image through the brain in which the CSF in the lateral
ventricles is hyperintense

760 Neuroimaging

оАБ T2-weighted images, in which water is shown as hyperintense,
are useful for viewing pathological changes because oedematous
tissue (e.g. inоАЫammation) will display a brighter signal than
normal surrounding tissue. Vitreous (Fig. 19.2C) and CSF (Fig.
19.2D) are hyperintense as they have high water content. Blood
vessels appear black on T2 imaging unless they are occluded.

Image enhancement

оАБ Gadolinium contrast acquires magnetic moment when
placed in an electromagnetic оАЮeld. Administered intravenously, it remains intravascular unless there is a breakdown of
the bloodвАУbrain barrier. It is only visualized on T1-weighted
images and enhancing lesions such as tumours and areas of
inоАЫammation will appear bright. Ideally MR is performed both
before (Fig. 19.3A) and aоАЭer (Fig. 19.3B) administration of
gadolinium for most clinical indications. Special head or surface coils can also be used to improve spatial deоАЮnition of the
image. Adverse eоАЬects with gadolinium are uncommon and
usually relatively innocuous.
оАБ Fat-suppression techniques are useful for imaging the orbit
because the bright signal of orbital fat on conventional T1weighted imaging frequently obscures other orbital contents.
Fat suppression eliminates this bright signal and better delineates normal structures (optic nerve and extraocular muscles)
as well as tumours, inоАЫammatory lesions and vascular malformations. оАЯe two types of fat-suppression sequence used for
orbital imaging are:

{ T1 fat saturation used with gadolinium allows areas suspicious using other techniques to be enhanced, e.g. suppressing orbital fat signal to visualize optic nerve sheath or
perineural lesions (Fig. 19.3C).

оАБ It cannot be used in patients with magnetic foreign objects
(e.g. cardiac pacemakers, intraocular foreign bodies and ferromagnetic aneurysm clips).
оАБ Substantial patient cooperation is required, including remaining motionless. It is poorly tolerated by claustrophobic patients
as it involves lying in an enclosed space for many minutes.

Neuro-ophthalmic indications

MRI is the technique of choice for lesions of the intracranial visual
pathways.
оАБ оАЮe optic nerve is best visualized on coronal STIR images
in conjunction with coronal and axial T1 fat saturation postgadolinium images. Axial T1 images are useful for displaying
normal anatomy. MRI can detect lesions of the intraorbital
part of the optic nerve (e.g. neuritis, glioma) as well as intracranial extension of optic nerve tumours.
оАБ Optic nerve sheath lesions (e.g. meningioma) are of similar
signal intensity to the nerve on T1- and T2-weighted images
but enhance avidly with gadolinium.
оАБ Sellar masses (e.g. pituitary tumours) are best visualized by
T1-weighted contrast-enhanced studies. Coronal images optimally demonstrate the contents of the sella turcica as well as
the suprasellar and parasellar regions and are usually supplemented by sagittal images.
оАБ Cavernous sinus pathology is best demonstrated on coronal
images; contrast may be required.
оАБ Intracranial lesions of the visual pathways (e.g. inоАЫammatory, demyelinating, neoplastic and vascular). MRI allows
further characterization of these lesions as well as better anatomical localization.

Angiography

Magnetic resonance angiography

Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) is a non-invasive method
of imaging the intra- and extracranial carotid and vertebrobasilar circulations (Fig. 19.4A) to demonstrate abnormalities such as
stenosis, dissection, occlusion, arteriovenous malformations and
aneurysms. оАЯe motion sensitivity of MR is utilized to visualize
blood оАЫow within vessels and does not require contrast. However,
because of the reliance on active оАЫow, thrombosed aneurysms may
be missed and turbulent оАЫow may lead to diоАЪculties in interpretation. оАЯe technique has limited facility in the detection of very
small aneurysms. MRA adds about 10 minutes to the standard
MRI acquisition time.

Magnetic resonance venography

Over recent years, increasing attention has been paid to intracranial venous system pathology, particularly dural sinus occlusion
and stenosis. Historically, the venous phase of conventional digital subtraction angiography (DSA) has been used for intracranial
venous assessment and still oоАЬers high sensitivity and speciоАЮcity.
However, both CT and MRI can be used to similar purpose and are
relatively non-invasive and low-risk compared to DSA. Technological advances have conferred greatly improved accuracy. Whilst

{ STIR (short T1 inversion recovery) is the optimal sequence
for detecting intrinsic lesions of the intraorbital optic
nerve (e.g. glioma- Fig. 19.3D; optic neuritis вАУ Fig. 19.3E).
STIR images have very low signal from fat but still have
high signal from water.
оАБ FLAIR (оАЫuid-attenuated inversion recovery) sequences suppress the bright CSF on T2-weighted images to allow better
visualization of adjacent pathological tissue such as periventricular plaques of demyelination (Fig. 19.3F).
оАБ DWI/ADC (diоАЬusion-weighted imaging and apparent diоАЬusion coeоАЪcient). DWI measures aberrance in expected Brownian motion of free water, and is useful in acute ischaemic
stroke to identify abnormalities at a very early stage вАУ within
minutes вАУ and in distinguishing ischaemic damage reversible
with treatment from irreversible damage such that intervention (e.g. with a thrombolytic agent) is unlikely to be of beneоАЮt.
оАБ FIESTA and CISS (fast imaging employing steady-state acquisition and constructive interference in steady-state) are newer
high-resolution sequences. In neuro-ophthalmology, a particular application may be the investigation of cranial nerve palsy.

Limitations

оАБ Bone appears black and is not directly imaged.
оАБ Recent haemorrhage is not detected, so MRI is inappropriate
in patients with suspected acute intracranial bleeding.

CTV (see below) is generally faster and oоАЬers high spatial resolution, it entails a signiоАЮcant radiation dose and always requires
contrast for image acquisition. Magnetic resonance venography
(MRV) can be used with contrast or using non-contrast enhanced
techniques, among other indications making it suitable for patients
to whom contrast cannot be given. Substantial advances in MRV
pulse sequences now allow excellent visualization of the venous
system (Fig. 19.4B) and increasing reliance on MRV seems to
be evident. However, local resource availability, experience and
expertise are critical in determining the choice of technique.

Computed tomographic angiography

Computed tomographic angiography (CTA) has been emerging
as the method of choice in the investigation of intracranial aneurysms (Fig. 19.4C). It enables acquisition of extremely thin slice
images of the brain following intravenous contrast. Images of the
vessels can be reconstructed in three dimensions and viewed from
any direction, aiding the approach to treatment. оАЯe investigation

is safe and quick and does not carry the 1% risk of stroke associated with conventional catheter angiography.

Computed tomographic venography

Computed tomographic venography (CTV) is a rapid highresolution technique in which patient motion artefact is of lesser
importance than with MRV. CTV is believed to be at least as sensitive as MRV in the diagnosis of cerebral venous thrombosis.
Both techniques may provide complementary оАЮndings in diоАЪcult
diagnostic situations. However, contrast is always required, and a
signiоАЮcant radiation dose is delivered. Visualization of skull base
structures is limited by bony artefact relative to MRV. оАЯe technique is similar to that of CTA.

Conventional catheter angiography

Conventional intra-arterial catheter angiography is usually performed under local anaesthetic. A catheter is passed via the femoral artery into the internal carotid and vertebral arteries in the neck

under оАЫuoroscopic guidance. Following contrast injection, images

may now be reserved for cases where CTA is equivocal or negative.

OPTIC NERVE

Anatomy

Circulation (Fig. 19.5A)

оАБ оАЮe ophthalmic artery arises from the internal carotid artery
and runs inferior to the optic nerve in the optic canal.
оАБ оАЮe central retinal artery is the continuation of the ophthalmic artery aоАЭer it passes into the optic nerve 8вАУ12mm behind
the globe. оАЯis supplies blood to the inner retina.

оАБ Long posterior ciliary arteries. оАЯe blood supply to the
optic nerve head arises from the circle of ZinnвАУHaller, which
receives blood from 4вАУ5 long posterior ciliary arteries, choroidal vessels and the pial arterial network.

General structure (Fig. 19.5B)

оАБ AоАЭerent оАЯbres. оАЯe optic nerve carries approximately 1.2 million aоАЬerent nerve оАЮbres, each of which originates in a retinal
ganglion cell. Most of these оАЮbres synapse in the lateral geniculate body, although some reach other centres, notably the pretectal nuclei in the midbrain. Nearly one-third of the оАЮbres
subserve the central 5¬∞ of the visual оАЮeld. Within the optic
nerve itself the nerve оАЮbres are divided into about 600 bundles
by оАЮbrous septae derived from the pia mater.
оАБ Surrounding layers (Fig. 19.5C)

{ оАЯe outer sheath comprises the arachnoid mater and the
tougher dura mater, which is continuous with the sclera;
optic nerve fenestration involves incision of this outer

{ оАЯe innermost layer is the delicate and vascular pia mater.

764 Optic Nerve

sheath. оАЯe subarachnoid space is continuous with the
cerebral subarachnoid space and contains CSF.

Anatomical subdivisions

оАЯe optic nerve is approximately 50 mm long from globe to chiasm. It can be subdivided into four segments (see Fig. 19.5A):
оАБ Intraocular segment (optic nerve head) is the shortest, being
1 mm deep and approximately 1.5 mm in vertical diameter.
оАЯe ophthalmoscopically visible portion is called the optic disc
(Fig. 19.5D).
оАБ Intraorbital segment is 25вАУ30 mm long and extends from the
globe to the optic foramen at the orbital apex. Its diameter is 3вАУ4
mm because of the addition of the myelin sheaths to the nerve
оАЮbres. At the orbital apex the nerve is surrounded by the tough
оАЮbrous annulus of Zinn, from which originate the four rectus
muscles.
оАБ Intracanalicular segment traverses the optic canal and measures about 6 mm. Unlike the intraorbital portion, it is оАЮxed
to the canal, since the dura mater fuses with the periosteum.
оАБ Intracranial segment joins the chiasm and varies in length
from 5 to 16 mm (average 10 mm). Long intracranial segments
are particularly vulnerable to damage by adjacent lesions such
as pituitary adenomas and aneurysms.

Visual evoked potential

оАБ Principle (Fig. 19.6). Visual (visually) evoked potential (VEP)
tests record electrical activity of the visual cortex created by
retinal stimulation. оАЯe most common indications in ophthalmology are the monitoring of visual function in babies
and the investigation of optic neuropathy, particularly when
associated with demyelination. It can also be used to monitor
macular pathway function and to investigate functional (nonphysiological) visual loss.
оАБ Technique. оАЯe stimulus is either a оАЫash of light (оАЫash VEP)
or a black-and-white checkerboard pattern on a screen that

periodically reverses polarity (pattern VEP). Several tests are
performed and the average potential is calculated.
оАБ Interpretation. Latency (delay) and amplitude are assessed.
In optic neuropathy both parameters are aоАЬected, with prolongation of latency and a decrease in amplitude. оАЯreshold
VEP (by using diоАЬerent-sized check stimuli) can detect early
or subclinical dysfunction as smaller check size responses may
become abnormal earlier than responses to larger stimuli.

Signs of optic nerve dysfunction

оАБ Reduced visual acuity for distance and near is common, but
is non-speciоАЮc. Acuity may be preserved in some conditions.
оАБ Relative aоАЭerent pupillary defect (see below).
оАБ Dyschromatopsia is impairment of colour vision, which in the
context of optic nerve disease mainly aоАЬects red and green. A
simple way of detecting a monocular colour vision defect is to
ask the patient to compare the colour of a red object using each
eye in turn or to undertake an Ishihara test.
оАБ Diminished light brightness sensitivity, oоАЭen persisting aоАЭer
visual acuity returns to normal, for instance following the
acute stage of optic neuritis.
оАБ Diminished contrast sensitivity (see Ch. 1).
оАБ Visual оАЯeld defects, which vary with the underlying pathology, include diоАЬuse depression of the central visual оАЮeld, central scotomas, centrocaecal scotomas, nerve оАЮbre bundle and
altitudinal (Table 19.1).

TIP Loss of red-green colour vision is a common
manifestation of optic nerve disease and can be rapidly tested
with Ishihara plates.

ClassiоАЬcation of optic neuropathy by
cause

оАБ InоАЬammatory. Optic neuritis, including demyelinating, parainfectious, infectious and non-infectious and neuroretinitis.
оАБ Glaucomatous. See Chapter 11

оАБ Ischaemic. Anterior non-arteritic, anterior arteritic, posterior
ischaemic and diabetic papillopathy.
оАБ Hereditary. Leber hereditary optic neuropathy, other hereditary optic neuropathies.
оАБ Nutritional and toxic. See also Chapter 21
оАБ Papilloedema. Secondary to raised intracranial pressure.
оАБ Traumatic. See Chapter 22
оАБ Compressive. Including secondary to an orbital lesion.

tumours and infective agents.

Optic atrophy

Introduction

Optic atrophy refers to the late-stage changes that take place in
the optic nerve resulting from axonal degeneration in the pathway
between the retina and the lateral geniculate body, manifesting

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 765
#### 19

with disturbance in visual function and in the appearance of the
optic nerve head.

Primary optic atrophy

Primary optic atrophy occurs without antecedent swelling of the
optic nerve head. It may be caused by lesions aоАЬecting the visual
pathways at any point from the retrolaminar portion of the optic
nerve to the lateral geniculate body. Lesions anterior to the optic
chiasm result in unilateral optic atrophy, whereas those involving
the chiasm and optic tract will cause bilateral changes.
оАБ Signs

{ Flat white disc with clearly delineated margins (Fig. 19.7A).

{ Reduction in the number of small blood vessels on the disc
surface.

{ Attenuation of peripapillary blood vessels and thinning of
the retinal nerve оАЮbre layer (RNFL).

{ оАЯe atrophy may be diоАЬuse or sectoral depending on the
cause and level of the lesion. Temporal pallor of the optic

A B

C D

766 Optic Nerve

nerve head may indicate atrophy of оАЮbres of the papillomacular bundle and is classically seen following demyelinating optic neuritis. Band atrophy is a similar phenomenon
caused by involvement of the оАЮbres entering the optic disc
nasally and temporally. It occurs in lesions of the optic chiasm or tract and gives nasal as well as temporal pallor.
оАБ Important causes

{ Optic neuritis.

{ Compression by tumours and aneurysms.

{ Hereditary optic neuropathies.

{ Toxic and nutritional optic neuropathy, which may give
temporal pallor, particularly in early/milder cases when
the papillomacular оАЮbres are preferentially aоАЬected (Fig.
19.7B).

{ Trauma.

Secondary optic atrophy

Secondary optic atrophy is preceded by longstanding swelling of
the optic nerve head.
оАБ Signs vary according to the cause and its course.

{ Slightly or moderately raised white or greyish disc with
poorly delineated margins due to gliosis (Fig. 19.7C).

{ Obscuration of the lamina cribrosa.

{ Reduction in the number of small blood vessels on the disc
surface.

posterior vitreous. Papillitis is the most common type of optic
neuritis in children, but can also aоАЬect adults.
оАБ Neuroretinitis is characterized by papillitis in association
with inоАЫammation of the RNFL and a macular star оАЮgure (see
below). It is the least common type and is only rarely a manifestation of demyelination.

According to aetiology

оАБ Demyelinating. оАЯis is by far the most common cause.
оАБ Parainfectious, following a viral infection or immunization.
оАБ Infectious. оАЯis may be sinus-related, or associated with conditions such as cat-scratch disease, syphilis, Lyme disease,
cryptococcal meningitis and herpes zoster.
оАБ Non-infectious causes include sarcoidosis and systemic autoimmune diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus, polyarteritis nodosa and other vasculitides.

Demyelinating optic neuritis

Overview

Demyelination is a pathological process in which normally myelinated nerve оАЮbres lose their insulating myelin layer. оАЯe myelin is
phagocytosed by microglia and macrophages, subsequent to which
astrocytes lay down оАЮbrous tissue in plaques. Demyelinating disease disrupts nervous conduction within the white matter tracts of
the brain, brainstem and spinal cord.
Demyelinating conditions that may involve the visual system
include the following:
оАБ Isolated optic neuritis with no clinical evidence of generalized demyelination, although in a high proportion of cases this
subsequently develops.
оАБ Multiple sclerosis (MS), by far the most common demyelinating disease (see below).
оАБ Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), previously called Devic disease, is a rare autoimmune disease where
antibodies form against aquaporin 4 protein in the cell membranes of astrocytes.

Fig. 19.8 Papillitis.

{ Peripapillary circumferential retinochoroidal folds, especially temporal to the disc (Paton lines вАУ see Fig. 19.7C),
sheathing of arterioles and venous tortuosity may be
present.
оАБ Causes include chronic papilloedema, anterior ischaemic
optic neuropathy and papillitis. Intraocular inflammatory
causes of marked disc swelling are sometimes considered
to cause secondary rather than consecutive atrophy (see
below).

Consecutive optic atrophy

Consecutive optic atrophy is caused by disease of the inner retina
or its blood supply. оАЯe cause is usually obvious on fundus examination, e.g. extensive retinal photocoagulation, retinitis pigmentosa or prior central retinal artery occlusion. оАЯe disc appears
waxy, with reasonably preserved architecture (Fig. 19.7D).

Glaucomatous optic atrophy

See Chapter 11

ClassiоАЬcation of optic neuritis

According to ophthalmoscopic appearance

оАБ Retrobulbar neuritis, in which the optic disc appears normal,
at least initially, because the optic nerve head is not involved. It
is the most common type in adults and is frequently associated
with multiple sclerosis (MS).
оАБ Papillitis is characterized by hyperaemia and oedema of the
optic disc, which may be associated with peripapillary оАЫameshaped haemorrhages (Fig. 19.8). Cells may be seen in the

{ It may occur at any age, but typically aоАЬects middle-aged
women.

{ оАЯe condition is characterized by bilateral optic neuritis
and within a few weeks, long-segment transverse myelitis
(demyelination of the spinal cord) (see Fig. 19.9D). Most
patients experience repeated attacks, separated by periods
of remission.

{ оАЯe NMO-IgG blood test, which is highly speciоАЮc, is positive in 70% of patients and is an important marker of the
condition.

{ Acute treatment involves the use of high-dose intravenous
steroids followed by oral steroids. If steroids are ineоАЬective,
plasmapheresis may be used. Immunosuppressive drugs
are used for long-term therapy. Eculizumab, inebilizumab
and satralizumab have recently been approved for use in
adults with NMOSD who are anti-aquaporin antibody
positive.
оАБ MOG antibody disorder (MOGAD) is a recently identiоАЮed
rare auto-immune disease that results in inоАЫammation of the

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 767
#### 19

optic nerve and spinal cord. Antibodies form against myelin
oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG). Bilateral simultaneous
optic neuropathy is a common presenting feature.
оАБ Schilder disease, a very rare relentlessly progressive generalized disease with an onset prior to the age of 10 years and death
within 1вАУ2 years. Bilateral optic neuritis without subsequent
improvement may occur.

Multiple sclerosis

MS is an idiopathic demyelinating disease involving central nervous system white matter. Infection with EpsteinвАУBarr virus (EBV)
signiоАЮcantly increases the chances of developing multiple sclerosis.
However, EBV in itself is not suоАЪcient to trigger MS and other
unknown factors play a role. It is more common in women than
men.
оАБ Presentation is typically in the thirdвАУfourth decades, generally with relapsing/remitting demyelination that may switch
later to an unremitting pattern and less commonly with progressive disease from the outset.

оАБ Systemic features may include:

{ Spinal cord, e.g. weakness, stiоАЬness, sphincter disturbance,
sensory loss.

{ Brainstem, e.g. diplopia, nystagmus, dysarthria, dysphagia.

Clinical features of demyelinating optic neuritis

{ Usual age range 20вАУ50 years (mean around 30).

оАБ Symptoms

{ Subacute monocular visual impairment.

{ Cerebral, e.g. hemiparesis, hemianopia, dysphasia.

{ Psychological, e.g. intellectual decline, depression, euphoria.

{ Transient features, e.g. the Lhermitte sign (electrical sensation on neck оАЫexion) and the UhthoоАЬ phenomenon (sudden worsening of vision or other symptoms on exercise or
increase in body temperature).
оАБ Ophthalmic features

{ Some patients experience tiny white or coloured оАЫashes or
sparkles (phosphenes).

{ Discomfort or pain in or around the eye is present in over
90% and typically exacerbated by ocular movement. It may
precede or accompany the visual loss and usually lasts a
few days.

{ Common: optic neuritis (usually retrobulbar), internuclear ophthalmoplegia, nystagmus.

{ Uncommon: skew deviation, ocular motor nerve palsies,
hemianopia.

{ Frontal headache and tenderness of the globe may also be
present.
оАБ Signs

{ Rare: intermediate uveitis and retinal periphlebitis.
оАБ Investigations

{ Lumbar puncture shows oligoclonal bands on protein electrophoresis of cerebrospinal оАЫuid in 90вАУ95%.

{ MRI shows changes in the optic nerve appearance on STIR
imaging. Almost always there are characteristic lesions in
the white matter of the brain (plaques) (Fig. 19.9AвАУC).

{ VEPs are abnormal (conduction delay and a reduction
in amplitude) in up to 100% of patients with clinically
deоАЮnite MS.

{ Temporal disc pallor may be seen in the fellow eye (see
Fig. 19.7B for similar appearance), indicative of previous
optic neuritis.
оАБ Visual оАЯeld defects (Fig. 19.10)

{ Visual acuity (VA) is usually 6/18вАУ6/60, but may rarely be
worse.

{ Other signs of optic nerve dysfunction (see above), particularly impaired colour vision and a relative aоАЬerent pupillary defect.

{ оАЯe optic disc is normal in two-thirds of cases (retrobulbar
neuritis); the remaining one-third show papillitis (see Fig.
19.8).

Association between optic neuritis and multiple
sclerosis

оАБ оАЯe overall 15-year risk of developing MS following an acute
episode of optic neuritis is about 50%. With no lesions on MRI
the risk is 25%, but the risk is over 70% in patients with one or
more lesions on MRI. оАЯe presence of MRI lesions is therefore
a very strong predictive factor.
оАБ MS is not considered a hereditary disease, but the probability
of developing the disease is higher in closely related relatives.
оАЯere is an association between MS and HLA DR15 and DQ6.
оАБ A substantially lower risk of developing MS when there are no
MRI lesions is conferred by the following factors, providing
critical support in deciding whether to commence immunomodulatory MS-prophylactic treatment following an optic
neuritis episode:

{ DiоАЬuse depression of sensitivity in the entire central 30¬∞ is
the most common.

{ Altitudinal/arcuate defects and focal central/centrocaecal
scotomas are also frequent.

{ Focal defects are frequently accompanied by an element of
superimposed generalized depression.
оАБ Course. Vision worsens over several days to 3 weeks and then
begins to improve. Most recover within 4 weeks. Initial recovery is fairly rapid and then slower over 6вАУ12 months.

{ Male gender.

{ Absence of a viral syndrome preceding the optic neuritis.

{ Negative test for EpsteinвАУBarr virus.

{ Optic disc swelling, disc/peripapillary haemorrhages or
macular exudates.

A B

C D

Fig. 19.10 Visual оАЯeld defects in optic neuritis. оАЭAоАЮ Central scotoma; оАЭBоАЮ centrocaecal scotoma; оАЭCоАЮ nerve оАЯbre bundle; оАЭDоАЮ
altitudinal.

{ Vision reduced to no light perception.

{ Absence of periocular pain.
оАБ Optic neuritis is the presenting feature of MS in up to 30%.
оАБ Optic neuritis occurs at some point in 50% of patients with
established MS.

TIP In optic neuritis secondary to multiple sclerosis the vision
usually improves without treatment over a period of several
months.

Prognosis

More than 90% of patients recover VA to 6/9 or better. Subtle
parameters of visual function, such as colour vision, may remain
abnormal. A mild relative aоАЬerent pupillary defect may persist.
Temporal optic disc pallor or more marked optic atrophy may
ensue. About 10% develop chronic optic neuritis with slowly progressive or stepwise visual loss.

Management following demyelinating optic
neuritis

Nearly 80вАУ85% of patients with MS experience a relapsing course.
If leоАЭ untreated most will develop neurological disability over time.
Disease modifying therapies early in the course of active relapsing
MS can prevent relapses and reduce neurological disability. Some
of these medications are associated with potentially serious side
eоАЬects and careful monitoring is required, preferably in a specialist
clinic.

Indications for steroid treatment in optic
neuritis

оАБ Starting treatment. Optic nerve axon preservation and оАЮnal
visual acuity may be improved by starting intravenous steroid
therapy within 48 hours of the onset of symptoms.
оАБ Speed of recovery. When the VA is worse than 6/12 within the
оАЮrst week of onset, treatment may speed up recovery by 2вАУ3
weeks and delay the onset of clinical MS over the short term.
оАЯis may be relevant in the patients with poor vision in the
fellow eye, but the limited beneоАЮt must be balanced against the
risks of high-dose steroids.
оАБ Steroid regimen. Intravenous methylprednisolone sodium
succinate 1 g daily (250mg six-hourly) for 3 days, followed by
oral prednisolone (1 mg/kg daily) for 11 days, subsequently
tapered over 3 days.
оАБ Oral prednisolone may increase the risk of recurrence of optic
neuritis if used without prior intravenous steroid.

Disease modifying therapies for MS

оАБ Interferon beta (IFN√Я) is used in the early stages, but can
cause transient оАЫu-like symptoms. Four interferon drugs are
currently approved to treat MS and vary in their administration routes and frequency of injection: subcutaneous IFN√Я-1a
and -1b, intramuscular IFN√Я-1a and long-acting subcutaneous
peginterferon beta-1a (given every 2 weeks). оАЯe long-term
safety proоАЮle and eоАЪcacy makes IFN√Я an important оАЮrst-line
therapeutic option.
оАБ Glatiramer acetate (subcutaneous injection) is used to reduce
the frequency of relapses of MS and possibly decreases disability progression. An injection site reaction occurs in about onethird. оАЯe method of action is not known, but it may possibly act
as a decoy, diverting the autoimmune response against myelin.
оАБ Monoclonal antibody treatment is showing promise in the
treatment of MS, but use of these agents has been associated
with systemic side eоАЬects, notably opportunistic infections
and abnormal liver function. оАЯe following medication can
be considered in diоАЪcult cases: natalizumab, ocrelizumab and
alemtuzumab.

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 769
#### 19

оАБ Natalizumab is used for highly active relapsingвАУremitting MS
in patients who have not responded favourably to treatment
with interferon beta or glatiramer acetate and reduces relapses
by about two-thirds, slowing disability by half.
оАБ Ocrelizumab is helpful for highly active disease and primary
progressive disease, but may rarely cause progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (which can be fatal).
оАБ Alemtuzumab may be the most cost-eоАЬective of these medications because of the favourable dosing strategy.

Approach to treatment of MS

оАБ A multidisciplinary approach is important in these patients.
оАБ First attack suggestive of MS: intravenous corticosteroids,
interferon beta or glatiramer acetate.
оАБ Relapsing MS: (a) inactive disease: regular clinical monitoring; (b) active and highly active disease: disease modifying
therapy (alemtuzumab, cladribine, оАЮngolimod, natalizumab,
ocrelizumab, mitoxantrone).
оАБ Progressive MS: ocrelizumab.

Parainfectious optic neuritis

Optic neuritis may be associated with viral infections such
as measles, mumps, chickenpox, rubella, whooping cough
and glandular fever and may also occur following immunization. Children are affected much more frequently than
adults. Presentation is usually 1вАУ3 weeks after a viral infection, with acute severe visual loss generally involving both
eyes. Bilateral papillitis is the rule. Occasionally there may be
a neuroretinitis or the discs may be normal. The prognosis for
spontaneous visual recovery is very good and treatment is not
required in the majority of patients. However, when visual loss
is severe and bilateral or involves an only seeing eye, intravenous steroids should be considered, with antiviral cover where
appropriate.

Infectious optic neuritis

оАБ Sinus-related optic neuritis is uncommon and is sometimes
characterized by recurrent attacks of unilateral visual loss
associated with severe headache and spheno-ethmoidal
sinusitis. Possible mechanisms include direct spread of
infection, occlusive vasculitis and mucocoele. Treatment
is with systemic antibiotics and, if appropriate, surgical
drainage.
оАБ Cat-scratch fever (benign lymphoreticulosis) is usually caused
by Bartonella henselae inoculated by a cat scratch or bite (see
below and also Ch. 12). Numerous ophthalmological features
have been described, notably neuroretinitis.
оАБ Syphilis may cause acute papillitis or neuroretinitis during the
primary or secondary stages (see Ch. 12).
оАБ Lyme disease (borreliosis) is a spirochaetal infection caused
by Borrelia burgdorferi transmitted by a tick bite (see Ch. 12).
It may cause neuroretinitis and occasionally acute retrobulbar neuritis, which may be associated with other neurological
manifestations and can mimic MS.

770 Optic Nerve

оАБ Cryptococcal meningitis in patients with acquired immunodeоАЮciency syndrome (AIDS) may be associated with acute
optic neuritis, which may be bilateral (see Ch. 12).
оАБ Varicella zoster virus may cause papillitis by spread from contiguous retinitis (i.e. acute retinal necrosis, progressive retinal
necrosis вАУ see Ch. 12) or associated with herpes zoster ophthalmicus. Primary optic neuritis is uncommon but may occur
in immunocompromised patients, some of whom may subsequently develop viral retinitis.

TIP Bilateral severe visual loss secondary to optic neuritis
may occur in children 1вАУ3 weeks after a viral infection, but the
prognosis for visual recovery is good.

NonоАЯinfectious optic neuritis

Sarcoidosis

Optic neuritis aоАЬects 1вАУ5% of patients with neurosarcoidosis. It
may occasionally be the presenting feature of sarcoidosis, but usually develops during the course of established systemic disease.
оАЯe optic nerve head may exhibit a lumpy appearance suggestive
of granulomatous inоАЮltration and there may be associated vitritis
(Fig. 19.11). оАЯe response to steroid therapy is oоАЭen rapid, though
vision may decline if treatment is tapered or stopped prematurely
and some patients require long-term low-dose therapy. Methotrexate may also be used as an adjunct to steroids or as monotherapy in
steroid-intolerant patients.

Autoimmune

Autoimmune optic nerve involvement may take the form of retrobulbar neuritis or anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (see below).
Some patients may also experience slowly progressive visual loss
suggestive of compression. Treatment is with systemic steroids and
other immunosuppressants.

Neuroretinitis

Introduction

Neuroretinitis refers to the combination of optic neuritis and
signs of retinal, usually macular, inflammation. Cat-scratch
fever is responsible for 60% of cases. About 25% of cases are
idiopathic (Leber idiopathic stellate neuroretinitis). Other
notable causes include syphilis, Lyme disease, mumps and
leptospirosis.

Diagnosis

Fig. 19.11 Sarcoid granuloma of the optic nerve head (arrow).

Fig. 19.12 Neuroretinitis showing disc swelling and a macular
star.

оАБ Symptoms. Painless unilateral visual impairment, usually
gradually worsening over about a week.
оАБ Signs

{ VA is impaired to a variable degree.

{ Signs of optic nerve dysfunction are usually mild or absent,
as visual loss is largely due to macular involvement.

{ Papillitis associated with peripapillary and macular
oedema.

{ A macular star (Fig. 19.12) typically appears as disc swelling settles. оАЯe macular star resolves with a return to normal or near-normal VA over 6вАУ12 months.

{ Venous engorgement and splinter haemorrhages may be
present in severe cases.

{ Fellow eye involvement occasionally develops.
оАБ Optical coherence tomography (OCT) demonstrates suband intraretinal оАЫuid to a variable extent.
оАБ Fluorescein angiography (FA) shows diоАЬuse leakage from
superоАЮcial disc vessels.
оАБ Blood tests may include serology for Bartonella and other
causes according to clinical suspicion (see also Ch. 12).

Treatment

оАЯis is speciоАЮc to the cause and oоАЭen consists of antibiotics. Recurrent idiopathic cases may require treatment with steroids and/or
other immunosuppressants.

NonоАЯarteritic anterior ischaemic optic
neuropathy

Introduction

Non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION) is
caused by occlusion of the short posterior ciliary arteries resulting
in partial or total infarction of the optic nerve head. Predispositions include structural crowding of the optic nerve head so that
the physiological cup is either very small or absent, hypertension
(very common), diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidaemia, collagen vascular disease, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, hyperhomocysteinaemia, sudden hypotensive events, cataract surgery and
sleep apnoea syndrome. оАЯis condition may occasionally follow air
travel or exposure to high altitude. Patients are usually over the age
of 50, but are typically younger than those who develop arteritic
ION (see below).

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms

{ Sudden painless monocular visual loss, which is frequently
discovered on awakening, suggesting that nocturnal hypotension may play a role.
оАБ Signs

{ VA is normal or only slightly reduced in about 30%. оАЯe
remainder have moderate to severe impairment.

{ Visual оАЮeld defects are typically inferior altitudinal but
central, paracentral, quadrantic and arcuate defects may
also be seen.

{ Dyschromatopsia is usually proportional to the level of
visual impairment, in contrast to optic neuritis in which
colour vision may be severely impaired when VA is reasonably good.

{ DiоАЬuse or sectoral hyperaemic disc swelling, oоАЭen associated
with a few peripapillary splinter haemorrhages (Fig. 19.13).

it does not appear to reduce the risk of involvement of the

Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic
neuropathy

Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AAION) is caused
by giant cell arteritis (GCA). About 50% of patients with GCA have
polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) at diagnosis, while around 20% of
PMR patients will develop GCA. PMR is characterized by pain and
stiоАЬness in proximal muscle groups, typically the shoulders and
biceps, that is worse on waking. Symptoms can be severe but generally respond dramatically to a lowвАУmedium dose (initially 15вАУ20
mg daily) of oral prednisolone. оАЯe causative relationship between
GCA and PMR remains uncertain.

Diagnosis of giant cell arteritis

GCA is a granulomatous necrotizing arteritis (Fig. 19.14A) with
a predilection for large and medium-size arteries, particularly the
major aortic branches and the superоАЮcial temporal (STA), ophthalmic, posterior ciliary and proximal vertebral arteries. оАЯe severity and extent of involvement are associated with the quantity of
elastic tissue in the media and adventitia and intracranial arteries
are usually spared as they possess little elastic tissue. Smoking, low
body mass index and early menopause may be independent risk
factors. Patients are usually elderly (average 70 years) and the condition is extremely rare under the age of 50. Women are aоАЬected
four times more commonly than men. оАЯe diagnosis of both
GCA and PMR is mainly made on the basis of clinical оАЮndings.
оАЯe American College of Rheumatology criteria may be helpful
in diagnostic decision-making (Table 19.2). оАЯe classiоАЮcation was
updated in 2022, to incorporate recent developments (like the halo
sign on temporal artery ultrasound).
оАБ Symptoms

{ Scalp tenderness, оАЮrst noticed when combing the hair, is
common.

{ Disc swelling gradually resolves and pallor ensues 3вАУ6
weeks aоАЭer onset.
оАБ Investigation should include assessment of the blood pressure and determination of the fasting lipid proоАЮle and blood
glucose. It is also very important to exclude occult giant cell
arteritis (see below). Atypical features may prompt special
investigations, such as neuroimaging.
оАБ Prognosis. Improvement in vision is common although recurrence occurs in about 6%. About 50% of eyes achieve 6/9 or
better, though 25% will only reach 6/60 or worse.
оАБ Fellow eye. Involvement of the fellow eye occurs in about 10%
of patients aоАЭer 2 years and 15% aоАЭer 5 years.

Treatment

оАБ оАЯere is no deоАЮnitive treatment.
оАБ Optic nerve fenestration has not been shown to be of beneоАЮt.
оАБ Some authorities advocate short-term systemic steroid
treatment.
оАБ Any underlying systemic predispositions should be treated.
оАБ Although aspirin is eоАЬective in reducing systemic vascular
events and is frequently prescribed in patients with NAION,

For purposes of classiоАЯcation, a patient shall be said to have giant cell
(temporal) arteritis if at least three of these оАЯve criteria are present.

{ Headache, which may be localized to the frontal, occipital
or temporal areas or be more generalized.

{ Jaw claudication (cramp-like pain on chewing), caused
by ischaemia of the masseter muscles, is virtually
pathognomonic.

{ Non-speciоАЮc symptoms such as weight loss, fever, night
sweats, malaise and depression are common.

{ Double vision may occur.

{ AAION (see below).
оАБ Other features

{ SuperоАЮcial temporal arteritis is characterized by thickened, tender, inоАЫamed and nodular arteries (Fig. 19.14B),
though the signs may be subtle.

{ Pulsation is initially present, but later ceases, a sign strongly
suggestive of GCA, since a non-pulsatile superоАЮcial temporal artery is highly unusual in a normal individual.

{ Ocular motor palsies, including a pupil-involving third
nerve palsy, can manifest.

examined because of the phenomenon of вАШskipвАЩ lesions in
which inоАЫamed segments of arterial wall are interspersed
with histologically normal areas. On meta-analysis the
estimated sensitivity of TAB has been reported to be 77%.
A negative TAB should not prevent ongoing treatment in
the presence of a convincing clinical picture of GCA as
approximately 20% have normal histology. A contralateral biopsy may be positive in 5% following a negative
initial biopsy.

{ Colour Doppler and duplex ultrasonography shows a
hypoechoic halo around the superоАЮcial temporal artery
lumen in around 75% due to oedema in the artery wall
(Fig. 19.14C). оАЯere is good evidence that ultrasound provides a valid non-invasive alternative to TAB. However, a
hypoechoic halo is not pathognomonic of GCA, as atherosclerosis and other inоАЫammatory arterial conditions
can cause a false positive halo. Doppler imaging is a useful aid to locating the artery for biopsy when it cannot be
palpated.

{ Extracranial large vessel imaging. Aortic imaging with
ultrasonography, MRA or positron emission tomography
(PET) scanning may be used to exclude aortic aneurysm
or dissection due to aortitis. Serial long-term imaging is
increasingly being advocated to exclude these potentially
life-threatening complications, the risk of which has now
been demonstrated to be substantially increased in and following GCA.

{ Shoulder joint (axillary) imaging. Recent work suggests
that particular inоАЫammatory features in the shoulder
joints and axilla on MRI and ultrasonography may be useful diagnostically in PMR.

{ Scalp gangrene may occur in very severe cases.

{ Rare complications include dissecting aneurysms, aortic
incompetence, myocardial infarction, renal failure and
brainstem stroke.
оАБ Investigations

{ Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is oоАЭen very high,
with a level of >60 mm/hr, although in approximately 20%
of patients it is normal, even lowвАУnormal.

{ C-reactive protein (CRP) is raised.

{ Full blood count: elevated platelets and normocytic normochromic anaemia are commonly present. Platelet level
is a stronger predictor for GCA than ESR or CRP.

{ Liver function tests are abnormal in one-third.

{ Autoantibodies are normal.

{ Temporal artery biopsy (TAB) should be performed if
GCA is suspected. Steroid treatment (see below) should
never be withheld pending biopsy, which should ideally
be performed within 3 days of commencing steroids.
Systemic steroid administration of duration greater than
7вАУ10 days may suppress histological evidence of active
arteritis although this is not invariable. In patients with
ocular involvement, it is advisable to take the biopsy
from the ipsilateral side. оАЯe ideal location is the temple
because it lessens the risk of major nerve damage. At least
2.5 cm of artery should be collected and serial sections

Treatment of giant cell arteritis without AAION

Treatment in the absence of visual symptoms is with oral prednisolone. An initial dose of 1 mg/kg daily is typical, the subsequent duration of treatment being governed by the response of
symptoms and the level of the ESR or CRP. Symptoms may recur
without a corresponding rise in ESR or CRP and vice versa. Most
patients need treatment for 1вАУ2 years, although some may require
indeоАЮnite maintenance therapy. Rapid tapering should generally
not occur, with added caution when the dose is reduced to below
about 10 mg a day. CRP may play an important role in monitoring
disease activity, as the level seems to fall more rapidly than the ESR
in response to treatment.

Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic
neuropathy

Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AAION) aоАЬects
30вАУ50% of untreated patients with GCA, of whom one-third
develop involvement of the fellow eye, usually within a week of the
оАЮrst. Posterior ischaemic optic neuropathy is much less common.
оАБ Symptoms

{ Sudden, profound unilateral visual loss not uncommonly
preceded by transient visual obscurations (amaurosis
fugax) and sometimes by double vision.

{ Severe visual loss is the rule. However, the upper or lower

{ A strikingly pale вАШchalky whiteвАЩ oedematous disc (Fig.
19.14E) is particularly suggestive of GCA.

{ Immunosuppressives such as methotrexate may be used

agents when extended treatment is required, though with
caution as their beneоАЮt is considerably less proven than

{ Tocilizumab (a humanized monoclonal antibody against

Other manifestations

оАБ Cilioretinal artery occlusion may occasionally be combined
with AAION (Fig. 19.14F).
оАБ Central retinal artery occlusion is oоАЭen combined with
occlusion of a posterior ciliary artery вАУ with resultant choroidal hypoperfusion вАУ as the two can arise from the ophthalmic
artery by a common trunk.
оАБ Ocular ischaemic syndrome due to involvement of the ophthalmic artery is rare.
оАБ вАШPseudo-Foster Kennedy syndromeвАЩ is a consequence of anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy, in which active disease in one
eye (Fig. 19.15A) is associated with contralateral atrophy in
the other eye secondary to a previous episode (Fig. 19.15B). It
should be distinguished from true Foster Kennedy syndrome,
which is uncommon and is classically caused by a frontal
space-occupying lesion, characterized by ipsilateral compressive optic atrophy and contralateral papilloedema secondary
to raised intracranial pressure.

Posterior ischaemic optic neuropathy

Posterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (PION) is much less
common than the anterior variety. It is caused by ischaemia of the
retrolaminar portion of the optic nerve supplied by the surrounding pial capillary plexus, which in turn is supplied by pial branches
of the ophthalmic artery. Only a small number of capillaries actually penetrate the nerve and extend to its central portion among
the pial septae. оАЯe diagnosis of PION should be made only aоАЭer
other causes of retrobulbar optic neuropathy have been excluded,
such as compression or inоАЫammation. Initially, the optic disc
appears normal, but pallor develops over weeks.
оАБ Operative (perioperative) PION develops following a variety
of surgical procedures, most notably involving the heart and
the spine. It occurs in about 0.02% of these procedures. оАЯe
major risk factors appear to be anaemia and intraoperative
hypovolaemic hypotension. Bilateral involvement is common
and the visual prognosis is poor. Prompt blood transfusion and
treatment of facial/orbital swelling may be of beneоАЮt.
оАБ Arteritic PION is associated with GCA and carries a poor
visual prognosis.
оАБ Non-arteritic PION is associated with the same systemic risk
factors as NAION, but is not associated with a crowded optic

{ Over 1вАУ2 months, the swelling gradually resolves and
severe optic atrophy ensues.
оАБ Prognosis is very poor. Visual loss is usually permanent,
although, very rarely, prompt administration of systemic
steroids may be associated with partial recovery.

TIP Immediate systemic steroid treatment should be
prescribed for a patient with ischaemic optic neuropathy
secondary to temporal arteritis, to reduce the risk of visual loss
in the fellow eye.

оАБ Treatment is aimed at preventing blindness of the fellow eye,
as visual loss in the index eye is unlikely to improve even
with immediate treatment. оАЯe second eye may still become
involved in 25% despite early steroid administration. оАЯe regimen is as follows:

{ Intravenous methylprednisolone, 500 mg to 1 g/day for 3
days followed by oral prednisolone 1вАУ2 mg/kg daily. AоАЭer
3 more days the oral dose is reduced to 50вАУ60 mg (not less
than 0.75 mg/kg) for 4 weeks or until symptom resolution and ESR/CRP normalization. A typical subsequent
regimen consists of reducing the daily dose by 10 mg/day
every 2 weeks until 20 mg/day is reached, with tapering
aоАЭerwards titrated against ESR/CRP and symptoms, e.g. a
2.5 mg reduction every 2вАУ4 weeks to 10 mg then a 1 mg
reduction every 1вАУ2 months.

{ Enteric-coated prednisolone may be appropriate, particularly in patients with a history of peptic ulceration.

{ Steroid treatment should be accompanied by bone and
gastrointestinal protection, e.g. a weekly bisphosphonate,
calcium/vitamin D supplementation and a proton pump
inhibitor.

{ Monitoring should be performed by a physician with
appropriate training and should look particularly for
steroid-related complications. A full blood count, ESR/
CRP, urea and electrolytes, random glucose and blood
pressure should be checked at each visit. Every 1вАУ2 years, a
chest X-ray or more sophisticated imaging should be performed to exclude an aortic aneurysm and bone mineral
density should be assessed.

{ Antiplatelet therapy, e.g. aspirin 600 mg stat then 100
mg/day should be commenced as this has been shown to
reduce the risk of visual loss and stroke.

Diabetic papillopathy

оАЯe cause of diabetic papillopathy is unknown, but it possibly represents a mild form of non-arteritic ischaemic optic neuropathy.
Younger patients (under 50 years) are more likely to be aоАЬected
and usually present with slight visual symptoms. оАЯe optic disc
shows either hyperaemic oedema or marked telangiectasia of the
inner surface microvasculature, which can be mistaken for neovascularization (see Fig. 13.24). Diabetic retinopathy is usually present, with macular oedema in 25% and retinal neovascularization in
about 10%. It should be diоАЬerentiated from NAION, which has a
poor prognosis. оАЯere is no treatment, but the disc oedema slowly
resolves over a period of 2вАУ10 months resulting in a оАЮnal visual
acuity of 6/12 or better in 80% of cases, subject to the eоАЬect of coexisting diabetic retinopathy.
оАБ оАЮe criteria for diagnosis include:

the ages of 15 and 35 years, although in atypical cases the condition may aоАЬect females and present at any age between 10 and 60
years. оАЯe diagnosis of LHON should therefore be considered in
any patient with bilateral optic neuropathy, irrespective of age.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Typically, acute or subacute severe painless unilateral (50%) loss of central vision occurs. In initially unilateral
cases, the fellow eye becomes similarly aоАЬected within weeks
or months.
оАБ Signs during the acute stage are oоАЭen subtle and easily overlooked and in some patients the disc may be entirely normal.

{ In typical cases there is disc hyperaemia with obscuration
of the disc margins (Fig. 19.16A).

{ Dilated capillaries on the disc surface, which may extend
onto adjacent retina (telangiectatic microangiopathy вАУ Fig.
19.16B).

{ Swelling of the peripapillary nerve оАЮbre layer
(pseudo-oedema).

{ Colour vision is likely to be subnormal.

{ оАЯere is oоАЭen a relative aоАЬerent pupillary defect.

{ оАЯe presence of diabetes (type 1 in 70%, type 2 in 30%).

{ Dilatation and tortuosity of posterior pole vasculature.

{ Optic disc oedema (unilateral in about 60%).

{ Mild optic nerve dysfunction.

{ Absence of ocular inоАЫammation or raised intracranial
pressure.

{ Subsequently, the vessels and pseudo-oedema regress and
severe optic atrophy supervenes (Fig. 19.16C), with nerve
оАЮbre layer dropout most pronounced in the papillomacular bundle.

{ Telangiectatic microangiopathy may be present in asymptomatic female relatives.

Leber hereditary optic neuropathy

Introduction

Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON) is a rare ganglion cell
degeneration that typically aоАЬects the papillomacular bundle. оАЯe
condition is caused by maternally inherited mitochondrial DNA
point mutations, most frequently (50вАУ90%) at nucleotide position
11778 (G to A) in the MT-ND4 gene; primarily aоАЬecting respiratory chain complex genes. It is the commonest inherited mitochondrial disorder. оАЯe condition typically aоАЬects males between

{ Surprisingly, the pupillary light reactions may remain
fairly brisk.
оАБ LHON plus refers to rare variants with additional manifestations such as neuromuscular dysfunction.
оАБ Prognosis is poor, although some visual recovery may
occur in a minority of cases even years later. Most patients
suffer permanent bilateral visual loss with a final VA of 6/60
or less. The prognosis is better if the age of onset is less
than 20 years and especially if the age of onset is less than

{ OCT. Patients show peripapillary retinal thinning. Unaffected carriers frequently show variable thickening of the
temporal RNFL, perhaps due to compensatory mitochondrial accumulation.

Treatment

оАБ Apart from symptomatic measures such as low vision aids,
treatment is generally ineоАЬective.
оАБ Mitochondrial вАШcocktailsвАЩ have been tried, which include various vitamins, co-factors (CoQ10), creatine and alpha-lipoic
acid, with anecdotal success. Subspecialist prescription of
one or more of these may be worthwhile, particularly in early
disease or prior to second eye involvement.
оАБ Counterintuitively, the cyanocobalamin (but not the hydroxocobalamin) form of vitamin B12 has been reported to worsen
outcomes.
оАБ Smoking and excessive alcohol consumption should be
discouraged.
оАБ Idebenone (900 mg/day) appears to be neuroprotective in
this condition and may have a role to play in some patients.
оАБ Intravitreal gene therapy is showing promising results; an
adeno-associated virus vector expressing normal ND4 complementary DNA is used in patients with the 11778 mutation.

Miscellaneous hereditary optic
neuropathies оАЭatrophiesоАЮ

оАЯis heterogeneous group of rare disorders are characterized primarily by bilateral optic atrophy. оАЯere is no eоАЬective treatment,
though the measures described above for LHON may be tried.

Dominant optic atrophy (Kjer type optic
atrophy, optic atrophy type 1)

оАБ Inheritance is autosomal dominant (AD). оАЯis is the most common hereditary optic neuropathy, with an incidence of around
1:50000. It is frequently due to a mutation in the OPA1 gene
on chromosome 3, which causes mitochondrial dysfunction,
but other genes can be responsible and X-linked and autosomal
recessive (AR) forms have been reported. оАЯere is usually high
penetrance but variable expressivity in the dominant forms.
оАБ Presentation is typically, though not always, in childhood with
insidious visual loss. оАЯere is usually a family history, but the
course may be variable even within the same family.
оАБ Optic atrophy may be subtle and temporal (Fig. 19.17A and B) or
diоАЬuse (Fig. 19.17C and D). оАЯere may be enlargement of the cup.
