оАБ Prognosis is variable (оАЮnal VA 6/12вАУ6/60) with considerable
diоАЬerences within and between families. Very slow progression over decades is typical.
оАБ Systemic abnormalities. Twenty per cent develop sensorineural hearing loss. Other features are less common.

Behr syndrome

оАБ Inheritance is AR; heterozygotes may have mild features.
оАБ Presentation is in early childhood with reduced vision.
оАБ Optic atrophy is diоАЬuse.
оАБ Prognosis is variable, with moderate to severe visual loss and
nystagmus.
оАБ Systemic abnormalities include spastic gait, ataxia and mental
handicap.

Wolfram syndrome

Wolfram syndrome is also referred to as DIDMOAD (diabetes
insipidus, diabetes mellitus, optic atrophy and deafness).
оАБ Inheritance. Three genetic forms are recognized, caused
by a variety of mutations in WFS1, which gives Wolfram
syndrome 1, CISD2 вАУ Wolfram syndrome 2 вАУ and probably

a form caused by a mitochondrial DNA mutation, with
inheritance being AR, AD or via the maternal mitochondrial line.
оАБ Presentation is usually between the ages of 5 and 21 years.
Diabetes mellitus is typically the оАЮrst manifestation, followed
by visual problems.
оАБ Optic atrophy is diоАЬuse and severe and may be associated with
disc cupping.
оАБ Prognosis is typically poor (оАЮnal VA is <6/60).
оАБ Systemic abnormalities (apart from DIDMOAD) are highly
variable, presumably in part due to genetic heterogeneity
and may include anosmia, ataxia, seizures, mental handicap,
short stature, endocrine abnormalities and elevated CSF protein. Life expectancy is usually substantially reduced.

Nutritional optic neuropathy

Introduction

Nutritional optic neuropathy (tobacco-alcohol amblyopia) is
an uncommon but underdiagnosed acquired optic neuropathy.

778 Optic Nerve

The mechanism is thought to be deficient mitochondrial function, similar to that of the heritable optic neuropathies, and
like these the papillomacular bundle is preferentially affected.
In Western developed countries the condition typically affects
individuals with high alcohol and tobacco consumption. Most
patients have neglected their diet and the features are most
likely to be due principally to deficiency in the B-complex vitamins, particularly cyanocobalamin (B12) and thiamine (B1), but
also riboflavin (B2), niacin (B3) and pyridoxine (B6). Copper,
folic acid and protein deficiency may also be important and
direct toxic effects of alcohol and tobacco may also be significant. A strict vegan diet can also be causative, as can dietary
deficiency in elderly patients. A similar clinical picture may be
seen due to toxicity from medication or environmental (e.g.
occupational) toxins. In under-resourced geographic regions,
nutritional aetiological factors due to inadequate dietary intake
predominate and nutritional optic neuropathy can be epidemic.
Pernicious anaemia sufferers may develop the condition due to
reduced vitamin B12 absorption.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms

{ оАЯe insidious onset of painless bilateral central blurring
associated with abnormal colour vision.

{ Enquiry should be made about potentially causative medication (see Ch. 21), environmental toxin exposure and a
vegan diet.

{ A family history of optic neuropathy should be excluded.

{ Peripheral neurological symptoms (sensory loss, gait disturbance) raise the suspicion of a peripheral neuropathy.
оАБ Signs

{ VA is very variably aоАЬected.

{ оАЯe discs at presentation are normal in most cases, but
some patients show subtle pallor, oоАЭen temporal, splintershaped haemorrhages on or around the disc, or minimal
oedema.

{ оАЯe pupil reactions are oоАЭen normal, but may respond
weakly in more severe cases.

оАБ Prognosis is good in early cases provided patients comply with
treatment although visual recovery may be slow. Colour perception returns more slowly than measured acuity. In advanced
cases there is likely to be a substantial permanent deоАЮcit, but
it is highly unusual for complete loss of useful vision to occur,
with preservation of peripheral оАЮeld usual even in quite severe
cases.
оАБ Blood tests. B12 (cobalamin) and folate (serum and red cell)
and possibly other vitamin levels such as B1 (thiamine) and B2,
a full blood count and оАЮlm (macrocytic anaemia) and serum
protein levels. Screening for speciоАЮc toxins or for LHON (see
above) mutations may be indicated. A high serum methylmalonate and/or homocysteine level may be an indicator of
potentially functional B12 deоАЮciency, even in the presence of a
lowвАУnormal B12.
оАБ VEP. оАЯe P100 amplitude is markedly reduced but with normal or near-normal latency.
оАБ MRI is commonly considered to rule out intracranial pathology, particularly when an atypical clinical picture is present
(e.g. marked asymmetry, disc pallor but good vision).

Treatment

Consideration should be given to co-management with a general
physician or neurologist. Compliance with treatment and review is
commonly poor in tobacco-alcohol amblyopia.
оАБ Dietary revision with formal nutritional advice, incorporating
increased fruit and leafy green vegetable intake.
оАБ Abstention from alcohol and tobacco is a priority, but is not
always achievable.
оАБ Vitamins. A daily multivitamin preparation, plus thiamine
(100 mg twice daily) and folate (1 mg daily). In someone who
is both folate and B12 deоАЮcient it is prudent to correct the
B12 deоАЮciency оАЮrst to avoid precipitating subacute combined
degeneration of the cord.
оАБ Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin (vitamin B12) injections.
оАЯe use of injected B12 has become less popular in recent
years, as evidence shows that even in malabsorption states
oral treatment may be eоАЬective. Hydroxocobalamin injections improve vision in tobacco-alcohol amblyopia, perhaps
by reversing cyanide toxicity and should be considered in
severe or unresponsive cases. Regimens range from 1 mg
weekly for 8 weeks to monthly for several months. Injections
every 3 months are typically continued for life.
оАБ Exposure to the identiоАЯed agent should be discontinued
immediately in cases due to medication or environmental
toxicity.

Papilloedema

Introduction

Papilloedema is swelling of the optic nerve head secondary to
raised intracranial pressure (ICP). вАШDisc swellingвАЩ and вАШdisc oedemaвАЩ
are non-speciоАЮc terms that include papilloedema but also a disc
swollen from other causes. All patients with papilloedema should
be suspected of harbouring an intracranial mass. Not all patients

{ Other ocular and systemic features of nutritional deоАЮciency should be sought (and investigations carried out
accordingly), e.g. KorsakoоАЬ syndrome, Wernicke disease
and pernicious anaemia. Xerophthalmia and beriberi
should be considered in developing nations.
оАБ Colour vision testing. A reduction in colour vision is disproportionate to the reduction in acuity. Red desaturation is likely
to be present. Ishihara chart testing is a simple, moderately
quantitative test that is widely available.
оАБ Visual оАЯeld defects are bilateral, relatively symmetrical, centrocaecal scotomas. оАЯe margins of the defects are diоАЪcult to
deоАЮne with a white target but are more substantive with a red
target.
оАБ OCT may show peripapillary RNFL thickening and will help
to exclude macular pathology.
оАБ Fundus autoоАЬuorescence (FAF) is useful to exclude macula
changes secondary to a cone or cone-rod dystrophy, that may
present with colour deоАЮciency and a central scotoma.

2

1. Aqueductal stenosis
2. Tumour
3. Blood, protein, venous obstruction

{ Cerebrospinal оАЫuid (CSF) is formed by the choroid plexus
in the ventricles of the brain.

{ It leaves the lateral ventricles to enter the third ventricle
through the foramina of Munro.

{ From the third ventricle, it оАЫows through the Sylvian aqueduct to the fourth ventricle.

{ From the fourth ventricle, the CSF passes through the

cerebral hemispheres.

B

{ Absorption is into the cerebral venous system through the
arachnoid villi.
оАБ Normal CSF pressure on lumbar puncture (not upright) is
10вАУ18cmH2O in adults.
оАБ Causes of raised ICP (Fig. 19.18B)

{ Idiopathic intracranial hypertension.

{ Obstruction of the ventricular system by congenital or
acquired lesions.

{ Space-occupying intracranial lesions, including
haemorrhage.

{ Impairment of CSF absorption due to meningitis, subarachnoid haemorrhage or trauma.

{ Hypersecretion of CSF by a choroid plexus tumour (very

Diagnosis of raised ICP

оАБ Headaches, which characteristically occur early in the
morning and may wake the patient from sleep, although
less commonly they can occur at any time of day. The pain
may be generalized or localized and may intensify with
head movement, bending or coughing. They tend to get
progressively worse over time. Very rarely, headache may
be absent.
оАБ Nausea, oоАЭen episodic and with associated projectile vomiting
may occur as an isolated feature or may precede the onset of
headaches.
оАБ Deterioration of consciousness as severity increases, initially
with drowsiness and somnolence. A dramatic deterioration in
the level of consciousness may be indicative of brainstem distortion and requires immediate attention.
оАБ Visual symptoms are commonly absent in mild or early raised
ICP.

{ Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (Fig. 19.19).

{ Cerebral oedema from blunt head trauma.

{ Severe systemic hypertension.

is extensive thrombosis of the posterior half of the superior

Fig. 19.20 Mechanism of sixth nerve palsy due to raised

{ Transient visual obscurations lasting up to 30 seconds in
one or both eyes are frequent in established papilloedema
and are sometimes precipitated by bending, coughing
or the Valsalva manoeuvre. Disc swelling due to other
causes is usually associated with more persistent visual
impairment.

{ Horizontal diplopia due to sixth nerve palsy caused by
stretching of one or both abducens nerves over the petrous
tip (Fig. 19.20) is a false localizing sign.

(average normal diameter approximately 5.5 mm ¬± 1 mm
on MRI).

{ In certain cases, vascular imaging such as venography may
be performed, to rule out cerebral venous sinus thrombosis.

{ Lumbar puncture (LP) must not be carried out until imaging has excluded a space-occupying lesion that might cause
downwards herniation of the intracranial contents towards
the LP-induced low-pressure area. Clotting abnormality,
including therapeutic anticoagulation, is also a contraindication unless reversed prior to the LP.

{ Vision is generally normal or minimally reduced. SigniоАЮcant reduction is a late feature in conjunction with secondary optic atrophy.
оАБ Neurological examination should be performed.
оАБ Investigations

Papilloedema is nearly always bilateral, but may be asymmetrical.
оАБ Early (Fig. 19.22A)

{ Mild disc hyperaemia with preservation of the optic cup.

{ B-scan ultrasonography can be used to aid in distinguishing between papilloedema and other causes of a swollen
or apparently swollen optic disc with 80вАУ90% sensitivity and speciоАЮcity by measuring the external diameter of
the optic nerve sheath (ONSD), which is substantially

for the measurement to be accurate and there is a degree of

19.21B) refers to an echolucent area in the anterior intraorbital nerve thought to represent increased separation of
the nerve and its sheath. Lateral gaze (вАШthirty-degree testвАЩ)
commonly leads to a 10% reduction in diameter on A-scan

and D), but not if normal or if increased ONSD is due to
inоАЮltration. Fluid-related ONSD can also be caused by
other pathology, such as inоАЫammation and trauma.

{ MRI to exclude a space-occupying lesion and/or enlarged
ventricles. MRI can also be used to measure ONSD

{ Indistinct peripapillary retinal nerve striations and disc
margins.

{ Enlarged blind spot.

{ SVP is absent in about 20% of normal individuals and may
be diоАЪcult to identify even when present. An identiоАЮable
venous pulsation in at least one eye means that the ICP is
normal at that point in time, bearing in mind that diurnal

оАБ Established (acute вАУ Fig. 19.22B)

{ Normal or reduced VA.

{ Severe disc hyperaemia, moderate elevation with indistinct margins and absence of the physiological cup.

{ Venous engorgement, peripapillary оАЫame haemorrhages
and frequently cotton-wool spots.

{ As the swelling increases, the optic nerve head appears
enlarged.

{ Circumferential retinal folds (Paton lines) may develop,
especially temporally (see Fig. 19.7C).

{ Macular fan: in younger patients small vesicles may form
in the superоАЮcial retina, converging on the fovea in a fan

with a macular star, composed of exudates.

Obese young adult women are the most commonly aоАЬected
group. Various medications including the contraceptive pill have
been implicated (strictly вАШsecondary intracranial hypertensionвАЩ
if a cause is identiоАЮed), as well as a range of conditions such as
systemic lupus erythematosus, Lyme disease and sleep apnoea
syndrome.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms and signs are similar to those of papilloedema,
with headache in over 90%. Pulsatile tinnitus may be experienced, and cranial nerve palsies and occasionally other
symptoms may occur. оАЯe long-term visual prognosis is usually good, but up to a quarter will have a degree of permanent
impairment.
оАБ Investigation is as for papilloedema: ONSD is increased
and MRI may show slit-like ventricles and flattening of
the pituitary gland (вАШempty sellaвАЩ sign). MRV is usually carried out to exclude cerebral venous sinus thrombosis or

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension

Introduction

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, previously known as benign
intracranial hypertension or pseudotumour cerebri, is characterized by elevated ICP that by deоАЮnition has no identiоАЮable cause.

782 Optic Nerve

A B

C D

Fig. 19.22 Papilloedema. оАЭAоАЮ Early; оАЭBоАЮ acute established; оАЭCоАЮ chronic; оАЭDоАЮ atrophic.

stenosis. Additional investigation for an occult cause should
be considered, especially in patients who do not fit the usual
profile.

Treatment

оАБ Weight loss, including via bariatric surgery, can be eоАЬective
and formal dietary intervention is strongly recommended.
оАБ Medical. Acetazolamide is the drug of оАЮrst choice, although
furosemide can also be considered. оАЯe use of steroids is controversial, but a short course is sometimes used in severe papilloedema. Intravenous mannitol is usually reserved for acute
exacerbations.
оАБ Lumbar puncture. оАЯis may be used for acute exacerbations,
but usually has a short-lived eоАЬect.
оАБ Surgical. In unresponsive cases optic nerve fenestration can be
helpful. Other options include lumboperitoneal shunting and
transverse dural sinus stenting, but these options have a high
complication rate.
оАБ оАЮe ophthalmologistвАЩs role is usually conоАЮned to diagnosis
and the monitoring of visual function with VA, colour vision
and оАЮelds, and optic nerve appearance/photography.

A tilted optic disc is a common anomaly, usually bilateral,
describing an apparently oblique entry of the optic nerve into
the globe. It is strongly associated with myopia and astigmatism.
Tilting is deоАЮned as being present when the ratio of the longest
diameter of the disc to the shortest (ovality index) is greater than
1.3 (Fig. 19.23A), but this will not include many subjectively
apparently tilted discs. A major implication of tilting is diоАЪculty
in excluding superimposed glaucomatous damage, as the temporal, particularly the inferotemporal, neuroretinal rim is frequently very thin.
оАБ Signs. Small, oval or D-shaped disc: the axis is most frequently
directed inferonasally, but may be horizontal or nearly vertical.

{ оАЯe disc margin is indistinct where retinal nerve оАЮbres are
elevated.

{ Peripapillary chorioretinal thinning may be marked and
situs inversus may be present. оАЯe temporal vessels deviate
nasally before turning temporally (see Fig. 19.23A).

Congenital optic disc anomalies

Tilted disc

respect the vertical midline.

Torsional disc

A torsional (torsioned) disc is said to be present when its long axis
is inclined at more than 15¬∞ from the vertical meridian, a line at 90¬∞
to a horizontal line connecting the foveola to the centre of the optic
disc (Fig. 19.23B). It is oоАЭen present in association with tilting and
is also associated with myopia.

Optic disc pit

оАБ Signs

{ VA is normal in the absence of complications.

{ оАЯe disc is oоАЭen larger than normal and contains a greyish
round or oval pit of variable size, usually temporal (Fig.
19.24AвАУC) but occasionally central or elsewhere. Pits are
bilateral in 10вАУ15%.

{ Visual оАЮeld defects are common and may mimic glaucoma.
A central defect may indicate a macular detachment.
оАБ Serous macular detachment develops in about half of eyes
with non-central disc pits (median age 30 years). оАЯe source
of the оАЫuid is unclear.

{ A schisis-like retinal separation that extends from the pit
is followed by serous detachment of the outer retinal layers
(Fig. 19.24D). It is important to examine the optic disc carefully in all patients with central serous chorioretinopathy.

acuity changes or if they develop metamorphopsia. Options
when macular detachment develops include:

{ Observation for spontaneous resolution, which occurs in
up to 25%, but may worsen the eventual outcome.

{ Laser may be considered if vision is deteriorating. Light
burns are applied along the temporal aspect of the disc.
оАЯe success rate is 25вАУ35%.

{ Vitrectomy with airвАУоАЫuid exchange and postoperative prone positioning may be considered if laser is
unsuccessful.

Optic disc drusen

Disc drusen are deposits, usually calciоАЮed, within the substance
of the optic nerve head that are present in up to 2% of the population, oоАЭen bilaterally (75%). оАЯey are usually sporadic but, in some
cases, have AD inheritance. оАЯey are thought to consist of focal
collections of axonal metabolic products.
оАБ Symptoms are usually absent, but some patients may experience episodic blurring, possibly due to transient ischaemia
related to a crowding eоАЬect.
оАБ Buried drusen. Particularly in childhood, drusen may be
obscured beneath the disc surface (Fig. 19.25A and B) and is a
common cause of pseudopapilloedema. A lumpy disc appearance, an absent cup and anomalous vascular patterns including
proximal branching, an increased number of major vessels and
tortuosity suggest drusen, as well as a lack of papilloedematous
features such as hyperaemia and vessel obscuration. Visibility
of drusen typically increases in the early teens.
оАБ Exposed drusen. Drusen at or close to the disc surface appear
as whitish pearl-like lesions of a range of sizes (Fig. 19.25C and
D). оАЯey tend to enlarge slowly over many years.
оАБ Retinal nerve оАЯbre layer thinning is common.
оАБ Visual оАЯeld defects of a range of conоАЮgurations are present
in up to 75% and are oоАЭen progressive, though a substantial
impact on sight is rare. No deоАЮnitive treatment is recognized,
though reducing IOP and other speculative measures may be
tried in selected cases.

{ FA does not usually show hyperоАЫuorescence of the detachment, which is demonstrated well on FAF and OCT.
оАБ Management. Long-term follow-up is unnecessary. However,
patients with this condition should be warned to return if their

Optic disc coloboma

оАЯe embryonic оАЮssure of the developing eye is located inferiorly
and slightly nasally and extends from the optic nerve to the margin of the pupil. A coloboma is a defect in one or more ocular
structures due to the оАЮssureвАЩs incomplete closure. оАЯe defect may
be unilateral or bilateral and usually occurs sporadically in otherwise normal individuals (Fig. 19.26), though numerous systemic
associations have been described, e.g. chromosomal abnormalities,
CHARGE syndrome, Goldenhar syndrome and central nervous
system abnormalities. An AD variety is caused by a mutation in
the gene PAX6
оАБ Signs

{ VA is oоАЭen decreased; amblyopia and refractive error may

{ A disc pit is sometimes associated (Fig. 19.27A). Pit-like
excavations are sometimes seen with a disc coloboma.

{ A disc coloboma may be associated with, or even continuous with, a large chorioretinal coloboma (Fig. 19.27B).

{ A focal, glistening white, bowl-shaped excavation,
decentred inferiorly so that the inferior neuroretinal
rim is thin or absent and normal disc tissue is confined

Morning glory anomaly

Morning glory anomaly is a rare, usually unilateral and sporadic
condition that has a spectrum of severity. Bilateral cases, which are
rarer still, may be hereditary. As in coloboma, a PAX6 gene mutation can be responsible. Systemic associations are uncommon, but
the most important is frontonasal dysplasia, characterized by midfacial anomalies, a basal encephalocele and other midline brain
malformations, including pituitary insuоАЪciency (Fig. 19.28A).

{ VA may be normal or impaired to a variable extent.

Optic nerve hypoplasia

A

Fig. 19.28 оАЭAоАЮ Hypertelorism and оАЭat nasal bridge вАУ note

оАЯe hypoplastic optic nerve, unilateral or bilateral, carries a diminished number of nerve оАЮbres. Its frequency seems to be increasing,
though it remains relatively rare. Young maternal age, primiparity
and maternal exposure to various agents have been linked. It may
occur as an isolated anomaly in an otherwise normal eye, but systemic associations are common (see below).
оАБ Symptoms

{ оАЯe double-ring sign is characteristic and consists of a
white ring of visible sclera surrounding a pigmented band
thought to consist of migrated pigment epithelium (Fig.
19.29A). A thin outer pigmented line may also be seen.
оАБ Ocular associations include astigmatism, amblyopia, оАЮeld
defects, foveal hypoplasia, aniridia and microphthalmos.
оАБ Systemic associations. Optic disc hypoplasia is associated
with a wide variety of developmental midline brain defects;
pituitary and hypothalamic deоАЮcits are common. Historically, the most frequent association has been considered to be
вАШsepto-optic dysplasiaвАЩ (de Morsier syndrome) вАУ bilateral optic
nerve hypoplasia, absent septum pellucidum, corpus callosum
agenesis (Fig. 19.29BвАУD) and hypopituitarism вАУ but it is now
believed that this complex does not exist as a speciоАЮc entity.
оАБ Investigation. All aоАЬected children should be screened for
systemic associations, especially hormonal deоАЮciencies and
developmental delay.

Myelinated nerve оАЮbres

Occasionally myelination of ganglion cell axons extends beyond
the cribriform plate and is visible on retinal examination in about

{ Severe bilateral cases present with blindness in early
infancy with roving eye movements or nystagmus.

{ Unilateral or less severe bilateral cases usually present with
squint.

{ Mild cases are easily overlooked.
оАБ Signs

{ VA ranges from normal to severely impaired.

{ A relative aоАЬerent pupillary defect may be present. Both
pupils may have sluggish light responses in bilateral cases.

{ Mild hypoplasia consists simply of a smaller than normal
disc. A foveola disc-centre distance of three or more times
the disc diameter strongly suggests hypoplasia.

{ Further along the hypoplastic spectrum, a small grey disc
is seen, with central cupping-like deоАЮciency of neural tissue and disc vessels emerging in a radial conоАЮguration. оАЯe
vessels are sometimes tortuous.

1% of the population: myelinated or medullated nerve оАЮbres

{ VA is likely to be reduced if the central macula is involved.

оАБ Imaging. FAF shows hypoautoоАЫuorescence and there is masking of background оАЫuorescence on FA. оАЯe RNFL is hyperreоАЫective on OCT.
оАБ Treatment consists of addressing refractive error, amblyopia
and squint if present.

Aicardi syndrome

Aicardi syndrome consists of a range of central nervous system and
other malformations in association with multiple bilateral depigmented chorioretinal lacunae clustered around a hypoplastic, colobomatous or pigmented optic disc (Fig. 19.31). Other ocular features
can include cataract and coloboma. Inheritance is X-linked dominant; the condition is lethal in utero for males. Death usually occurs
within the оАЮrst few years of life in aоАЬected girls.

Bergmeister papilla

оАЯis relatively common anomaly consists of remnants of the hyaloid vessels (Fig. 19.32); see also Chapter 17

{ Between 5% and 10% of cases are bilateral.

{ оАЯe appearance can be mimicked by neoplastic inоАЮltration
and other conditions.
оАБ Ocular associations include myopia, anisometropia, strabismus and amblyopia.

Acquired vascular abnormalities

оАБ Disc collaterals are veno-venular shunts that develop within
the existing vascular network to bypass an area of closure and
are most frequently associated with retinal vein occlusion. оАЯe
collaterals appear as distended оАЫat vessels that start and end on
the disc (Fig. 19.33A).
оАБ Optociliary shunts are usually unilateral anastomoses
between the retinal and choroidal circulations, consisting of
enlarged pre-existing peripapillary capillaries. оАЯe vessels
appear as large tortuous channels that disappear at the disc
margin (Fig. 19.33B). оАЯe most common association is an
optic nerve sheath meningioma. Other causes are discussed in
Chapter 4
оАБ Disc new vessels manifest as оАЮne, lace-like vessels that may
extend onto adjacent retina. оАЯe vessels may be оАЫat or elevated
above the disc and may be associated with gliosis (Fig. 19.33C).
Causes are discussed in Chapter 14

Miscellaneous anomalies

оАБ Peripapillary staphyloma is a non-hereditary, usually unilateral condition in which a relatively normal disc sits at the base
of a deep excavation whose walls, as well as the surrounding
choroid and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), show atrophic
changes (Fig. 19.34A). VA is markedly reduced and local retinal detachment may be present. Unlike other excavated optic
disc anomalies, it is rarely associated with other congenital
defects or systemic diseases.
оАБ Papillorenal (renal-coloboma) syndrome is an AD genetic
condition caused in most cases by a mutation in PAX2 and
characterized by renal and optic disc dysplasia. оАЯe discs do
not exhibit true hypoplasia, but a failure of angiogenesis. оАЯey
are normal in size with central excavation and replacement
of the central retinal vasculature by cilioretinal vessels (Fig.
19.34B).
оАБ Optic disc dysplasia is a descriptive term for a markedly
deformed disc that does not conform to any recognizable
category (Fig. 19.34C).

оАБ Megalopapilla is a typically bilateral condition in which both
the horizontal and vertical disc diameters are 2.1 mm or more,
or the disc area is greater than 2.5 mm [2] . Although the cup-todisc ratio is greater than normal, the cup should retain its normal conоАЮguration with no evidence of focal neuroretinal rim
loss. Although OCT may show peripapillary RNFL thinning,
macular ganglion cell complex imaging is typically normal.
оАБ Optic nerve aplasia is an extremely rare condition in which
the optic disc is absent or rudimentary and retinal vessels are
absent or few in number and abnormal. оАЯere may be retinal
pigmentary disturbance, especially at the site where the optic
disc might have been. Other ocular and systemic developmental defects may be present.

PUPILS

Anatomy
Light reоАЯex

оАЯe light reоАЫex is mediated by the retinal photoreceptors and subserved by four neurones (Fig. 19.35).
оАБ First (sensory) connects each retina with both pretectal nuclei
in the midbrain at the level of the superior colliculi. Impulses
originating from the nasal retina are conducted by оАЮbres that
decussate in the chiasm and pass up the opposite optic tract
to terminate in the contralateral pretectal nucleus. Impulses
originating in the temporal retina are conducted by uncrossed
оАЮbres (ipsilateral optic tract) that terminate in the ipsilateral
pretectal nucleus.
оАБ Second (internuncial) connects each pretectal nucleus to both
EdingerвАУWestphal nuclei. оАЯus, a uniocular light stimulus
evokes bilateral and symmetrical pupillary constriction. Damage to internuncial neurones is responsible for lightвАУnear dissociation in neurosyphilis and pinealomas.
оАБ оАЮird (preganglionic motor) connects the EdingerвАУWestphal
nucleus to the ciliary ganglion. оАЯe parasympathetic оАЮbres
pass through the oculomotor nerve, enter its inferior division

A

B

vessels.

and reach the ciliary ganglion via the nerve to the inferior
oblique muscle.
оАБ Fourth (postganglionic motor) leaves the ciliary ganglion and
passes in the short ciliary nerves to innervate the sphincter

pupil but subtler. оАЯus, the pupils respond weakly to stimulation of
the diseased eye and briskly to that of the normal eye. оАЯe diоАЬerence between the pupillary reactions of the two eyes is highlighted
by the вАШswinging оАЫashlight testвАЩ in which a light source is alternatively switched from one eye to the other and back, thus stimulating each eye in rapid succession. A leоАЭ relative pupillary defect is
characterized by the following:
оАБ When the normal eye is stimulated, both pupils constrict
(Fig. 19.36A).
оАБ When the light is swung to the diseased eye, the stimulus delivered to the constriction mechanism is reduced and both pupils
dilate instead of constricting (Fig. 19.36B).
оАБ When the normal eye is again stimulated, both pupils constrict
once more (Fig. 19.36C).
оАБ When the diseased eye is stimulated, both pupils dilate
(Fig. 19.36D).
It should be remembered that in aоАЬerent (sensory) lesions,
the pupils are equal in size. Anisocoria (asymmetrical pupil
diameter) implies disease of the eоАЬerent (motor) nerve or the iris
itself.

TIP In aоАЮerent (sensory) lesions of the visual pathway the
pupils are equal in size. Anisocoria (inequality of the pupillary
size) indicates an abnormality of the eоАЮerent (motor) pathway.

Horner syndrome оАЭoculosympathetic
palsyоАЮ

Anatomy

оАЯe sympathetic supply involves three neurones (Fig. 19.37):
оАБ First (central) starts in the posterior hypothalamus and
descends, uncrossed, down the brainstem to terminate in the
ciliospinal centre of Budge, in the intermediolateral horn of
the spinal cord, located between C8 and T2.
оАБ Second (preganglionic) passes from the ciliospinal centre to
the superior cervical ganglion in the neck. During its long
course, it is closely related to the apical pleura where it may
be damaged by bronchogenic carcinoma (Pancoast tumour) or
during surgery on the neck.
оАБ оАЮird (postganglionic) ascends along the internal carotid
artery to enter the cavernous sinus where it joins the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve. оАЯe sympathetic оАЮbres
reach the ciliary body and the dilator pupillae muscle via the
nasociliary nerve and the long ciliary nerves.

Causes

оАЯe causes of Horner syndrome are shown in Table 19.4. Most
commonly, isolated (without additional neurological features)
postganglionic Horner syndrome is microvascular in aetiology,
but this cannot be assumed.

Presentation

оАЯe majority of cases are unilateral. Causes of bilateral involvement
include cervical spine injuries and autonomic diabetic neuropathy.

Fig. 19.35 Anatomical pathway of the pupillary light reоАЭex.

Near reоАЯex

оАЯe near reоАЫex, a synkinesis rather than a true reоАЫex, is activated
when gaze is changed from a distant to a near target (see also Ch.
18). It comprises accommodation, convergence and miosis. Vision
is not a prerequisite and there is no clinical condition in which
the light reоАЫex is present but the near response absent. Although
the оАЮnal pathways for the near and light reоАЫexes are identical (i.e.
third nerve, ciliary ganglion, short ciliary nerves), the centre for
the near reоАЫex is ill-deоАЮned. оАЯere are probably two supranuclear
inоАЫuences: the frontal and occipital lobes. оАЯe midbrain centre for
the near reоАЫex is probably located more ventrally than the pretectal
nucleus and this may explain why compressive lesions such as pinealomas, preferentially involving the dorsal internuncial neurones
involved in the light reоАЫex, spare the near reоАЫex оАЮbres until later.

AоАЫerent pupillary defect

Absolute aоАЭerent pupillary defect

An absolute aоАЬerent pupillary defect (amaurotic pupil) is caused by
a complete optic nerve lesion and is characterized by the following:
оАБ оАЯe involved eye is completely blind (i.e. no light perception).
оАБ Both pupils are equal in size.
оАБ When the aоАЬected eye is stimulated by light neither pupil
reacts.
оАБ When the normal eye is stimulated both pupils react normally.
оАБ оАЯe near reоАЫex is normal in both eyes.

Relative aоАЭerent pupillary defect

A relative pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) is caused by an
incomplete optic nerve lesion or severe retinal disease, but never
by a dense cataract. оАЯe clinical features are those of an amaurotic

A

B

D

Painful Horner syndrome, especially of acute onset, should raise
the possibility of carotid dissection (Fig. 19.38).
оАБ Mild ptosis (usually 1вАУ2 mm) as a result of weakness of M√Љller
muscle and miosis due to the unopposed action of the sphincter pupillae with resultant anisocoria (Fig. 19.39A and B).
оАБ A key examination оАЮnding is that anisocoria is accentuated in
dim light, since in contrast to a normal fellow pupil the Horner
pupil will dilate only very slowly. оАЯe dark-induced anisocoria
diminishes with time spent in the dark environment.
оАБ Pupillary constriction to light and near stimuli is normal.

оАБ Hypochromic heterochromia (irides of diоАЬerent colour, the
Horner being lighter) may be seen if congenital or longstanding (Fig. 19.39C).
оАБ Slight elevation of the inferior eyelid (inferior ptosis) as a result

оАБ Reduced ipsilateral sweating, but because the sudomotor оАЮbres
supplying the skin of the face run along the external carotid
artery this occurs only if the lesion is below the superior
cervical ganglion. Patients may mistakenly interpret the normal side to be sweating excessively.
оАБ оАЯe cranial nerves and peripheral nervous system (e.g. T1 вАУ
grip strength), as well as the neck and regional lymph nodes
should be examined, if necessary by a neurologist.

Pharmacological tests

Apraclonidine or cocaine is used to conоАЮrm the diagnosis, the latter now less commonly (see below). Hydroxyamphetamine and
adrenaline may be used to diоАЬerentiate a preganglionic (abnormal оАЮrst- or second-order neurone) from a postganglionic lesion
(abnormal third-order neurone), though as the distinction may

{ Explanation: Cocaine blocks the re-uptake of noradrenaline secreted at the postganglionic nerve ending, which

solution.

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 795
#### 19

Treatment

Any identiоАЮed cause should be addressed as appropriate. оАЯe ptosis of Horner syndrome is mild but surgery can be considered at
the patientвАЩs discretion. Apraclonidine may be helpful as a temporizing measure.

Adie pupil

Introduction

An Adie pupil (tonic pupil, Adie syndrome) is caused by denervation of the postganglionic parasympathetic supply to the sphincter pupillae and the ciliary muscle and may follow a viral illness.
It is occasionally inherited in an AD pattern. Sites of dysfunction are presumed to be the ciliary ganglion and in HolmesвАУAdie
syndrome, the dorsal root ganglion involved in reоАЫex pathways.
It typically aоАЬects young women and presents in one eye in 80%,
though involvement of the second eye typically develops within
months or years.

Diagnosis

{ Large, regular (irregularity sometimes reported) pupil
(Fig. 19.41A).

оАБ Symptoms. Patients may notice anisocoria, or may have blurring for near due to impaired accommodation.
оАБ Signs

{ Explanation: In postganglionic Horner syndrome the dilator pupillae muscle develops denervation hypersensitivity
to adrenergic neurotransmitters due to its dysfunctional
local motor nerve.
оАБ Hydroxyamphetamine 1%. Two drops are instilled into each
eye. It is slightly more sensitive than phenylephrine testing.

{ оАЯe direct light reоАЫex is absent or sluggish (Fig. 19.41B).

{ Result: A normal or preganglionic Horner pupil will dilate,
but a postganglionic Horner will not.

{ On slit lamp examination, vermiform movements of the
pupillary border are typically seen.

{ Constriction is also absent or sluggish in response to light
stimulation of the fellow eye (consensual light reоАЫex вАУ Fig.
19.41C).

{ оАЯe pupil responds slowly to near, following which redilatation is also slow.

{ Accommodation may manifest similar tonicity, with
slowed and impaired focusing for near and prolonged refocusing in the distance.

{ Explanation: Hydroxyamphetamine potentiates the release
of noradrenaline from functioning postganglionic nerve
endings. In a lesion of the third-order neurone (postganglionic) there is no release of noradrenaline from the dysfunctional nerve.
оАБ Adrenaline 0.1% has an action similar to that of phenylephrine.

Investigation

Specialist neurological or neuro-ophthalmological assessment
should be sought for a conоАЮrmed Horner syndrome. An acute presentation should be regarded as an emergency. In contrast, if the
features have been present for more than a year and there are no
other localizing signs then the likely diagnostic yield from further
investigation is very low. оАЯe mainstay of investigation is imaging. CT or MR angiography examining the region from the aortic
arch to the circle of Willis will facilitate exclusion of neck (including carotid), apical lung, thyroid and skull base lesions. MR may
be utilized if greater soоАЭ tissue deоАЮnition is required, such as to
exclude a brainstem stroke. Plain X-rays and carotid ultrasound
imaging have limited utility.

{ In longstanding cases the pupil may become small (вАШlittle
old AdieвАЩ).
оАБ Associations include diminished lower limb deep tendon
reоАЫexes (HolmesвАУAdie syndrome вАУ Fig. 19.41D) and other
features of autonomic nerve dysfunction such as excessive
sweating (Ross syndrome), orthostatic hypotension and occasionally bowel obstruction or urinary retention. Typical Adietype pupils are oоАЭen present in generalized dysautonomia,
with a wide range of causes.
оАБ Pharmacological testing. Instillation of 0.1вАУ0.125% pilocarpine into both eyes leads to constriction of the abnormal
pupil due to denervation hypersensitivity, with the normal
pupil unaоАЬected. Some diabetic patients may also show this
response and very occasionally both pupils constrict in normal
individuals.

оАБ Syphilis serology (see Ch. 12) should usually be checked in

Treatment

Treatment is not usually required, but reading glasses may be
used to address near vision diоАЪculties and sunglasses or lowconcentration pilocarpine drops can be used for cosmetic reasons
and to reduce photophobia. оАЯoracic sympathectomy can be considered for excessive sweating.

Miscellaneous pupillary abnormalities

оАБ Physiological anisocoria (Fig. 19.42). Anisocoria of around
1 mm is present in about 20% of the normal population. оАЯe
asymmetry persists to the same proportion under diоАЬering
levels of illumination. Exceptionally, apraclonidine or cocaine
testing may be needed to exclude Horner syndrome.
оАБ Pharmacological mydriasis (Fig. 19.43). Dilatation of one
or both pupils due to instillation of a mydriatic agent can be
inadvertent (e.g. use of eye drops prescribed for someone else,
rubbing the eye aоАЭer touching a car sickness scopolamine skin

patch) or deliberate for the purpose of malingering. оАЯe pupil
does not constrict in bright light or on accommodation and
there is no response to any concentration of pilocarpine. оАЯere
are no other neurological features.
оАБ Argyll Robertson pupils (Fig. 19.44) are caused by neurosyphilis and have been attributed to a dorsal midbrain lesion
that interrupts the pupillary light reоАЫex pathway but spares the
more ventral pupillary near reоАЫex pathway вАУ lightвАУnear dissociation results. In dim light both pupils are small and may
be irregular. In bright light neither pupil constricts, but on
accommodation (near target) both constrict. оАЯe pupils do not
dilate well in the dark, but cocaine induces mydriasis unless
marked iris atrophy is present. AоАЭer instillation of pilocarpine
0.1% into both eyes, neither pupil constricts, distinguishing
Argyll Robertson pupils from bilateral longstanding tonic
pupils. Other causes of lightвАУnear dissociation are shown in
Table 19.5
оАБ Tectal (dorsal midbrain) pupils (Fig. 19.45). оАЯis phenomenon is a component of dorsal midbrain syndrome (see later).
оАЯe pupils are dilated in both dim and bright light. оАЯere is
lightвАУnear dissociation. Pilocarpine 0.1% has no eоАЬect.

A

B

C

Fig. 19.42 Right physiological anisocoria. оАЭAоАЮ In dim light the
right pupil is slightly larger than the left; оАЭBоАЮ in bright light both

оАБ Benign episodic unilateral mydriasis (BEUM). This
idiopathic condition (Fig. 19.46) occurs most commonly
in otherwise healthy young women and may represent
a migrainous phenomenon or a range of heterogeneous
aetiologies. Mydriasis tends to last for minutes or hours
before resolving completely. There may be mild associated
blurring and headache. The need for further investigation
should be assessed on an individual basis. There is no вАУ or
limited вАУ response to light or accommodation. Pilocarpine
0.1% induces no change, but pilocarpine 1% to both eyes
gives bilateral miosis.

CHIASM

Anatomy
Pituitary gland

оАЯe sella turcica (Turkish saddle) is a deep saddle-shaped depression in the superior surface of the body of the sphenoid bone in
which the pituitary gland lies (Fig. 19.47). оАЯe roof of the sella
is formed by a fold of dura mater, the diaphragma sellae, which
stretches from the anterior to the posterior clinoids. оАЯe optic
nerves and chiasm lie above the diaphragma sellae. Posteriorly, the
chiasm is continuous with the optic tracts and forms the anterior
wall of the third ventricle. A visual оАЮeld defect in a patient with
a pituitary tumour therefore generally indicates suprasellar extension. Tumours less than 10 mm in diameter (microadenomas) tend
to remain conоАЮned to the sella, whereas those larger than 10 mm

usually indicates suprasellar extension.

Parachiasmal vascular structures

оАБ Venous. The cavernous sinuses lie lateral to the sella so that
horizontally extending pituitary tumours affect the cavernous sinus and may damage the intracavernous parts of the
third, fourth and sixth cranial nerves. Conversely, aneurysms arising from the intracavernous part of the internal
carotid artery may erode into the sella and mimic pituitary
tumours.

Physiology

Pituitary hormones

оАЯe lobules of the anterior part of the pituitary gland are composed of six cell types. Five of these secrete hormones and the sixth
(follicular cell) has no secretory function. Pituitary adenomas of
mixed-cell type are common; any of the six cell types may proliferate to produce an adenoma. оАЯe current classiоАЮcation of pituitary
tumours is discussed further below.
оАБ оАЮe anterior pituitary lobe is under the control of various
inhibiting and promoting factors synthesized in the hypothalamus, passing to the pituitary through the hypothalamusвАУ
hypophyseal portal system:

{ Human growth hormone (HGH or GH) or somatotropin.

{ Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH).

{ Luteinizing hormone (LH).

{ Prolactin (PRL).

{ оАЯyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH).

{ Adrenocorticotrophic hormone (ACTH), which regulates
blood cortisol levels.

and oxytocin.

Pituitary adenomas

Tumour classiоАЮcation is based on the type of hormone secreted.

Ophthalmic features of large adenomas

Patients with a large pituitary lesion may оАЮrst present with vague
visual symptoms. A low threshold should be adopted for visual
оАЮeld assessment in patients with unexplained unilateral central
visual impairment and chronic headache.

TIP A visual оАЯeld test should be undertaken as the initial
step in a patient with unexplained unilateral central visual
impairment, in order to exclude compressive optic neuropathy.

(Fig. 19.53). Despite similar terminology, it is not linked
to a postоАЮxed chiasm. Patients may complain of diоАЪculty

be worse than when measured with each eye individually.

Investigation

оАБ MRI with gadolinium contrast (Fig. 19.54) utilizing multiple planes and thin sections demonstrates the relationship
between a mass lesion and the chiasm and is usually the preferred imaging modality. Adenomas are typically hypointense
on T1 and hyperintense on T2 images.
оАБ CT will demonstrate enlargement or erosion of the sella.
оАБ Endocrinological evaluation is complex, particularly as combined hormonal over- and under-secretion may be present and
is usually undertaken by an endocrinologist.

Prolactin-secreting (lactotrophic) adenoma

Lactotrophic adenoma or prolactinoma (formerly known as chromophobe adenoma, though lesions can be weakly acidophilic) is
the most common pituitary adenoma. Patients are typically young
to middle-aged adults. In women excessive prolactin secretion
leads to the infertilityвАУamenorrhoeaвАУgalactorrhoea syndrome and
in men may cause hypogonadism, impotence, sterility, decreased
libido and occasionally gynaecomastia and galactorrhoea. Up to
95% remain as microadenomas, though those that do not may
present initially with visual features.

Corticotrophic adenoma

A corticotrophic adenoma (a variant of basophil adenoma)
secretes ACTH and causes Cushing disease (Cushing syndrome
refers to the clinical picture of increased blood cortisol from any
cause), which may include central obesity and moon face, cutaneous striae, pigmentation and other features such as hypertension.

Somatotrophic adenoma

Somatotrophic (acidophil) tumours secrete excessive GH, causing
acromegaly in adults (once bone elongation has been completed)
and gigantism in children. Presentation is in middle age with

features including enlargement of the head, hands, feet and tongue,
coarseness of features with prominent supraorbital ridges and
nasolabial folds (Fig. 19.55), enlargement of the jaw with dental
malocclusion and hirsutism in females. оАЯere are many complications, including diabetes mellitus, hypertension, cardiomyopathy
and carpal tunnel syndrome.

Treatment of pituitary adenomas

оАБ Observation may be appropriate for incidentally discovered
and clinically silent tumours.
оАБ Medical therapy is usually the initial step and consists of the
reduction in tumour size and secretion using agents such as
dopamine agonists (e.g. cabergoline and the older bromocriptine) and somatostatin analogues such as octreotide, with supplementary hormonal correction as appropriate.
оАБ Surgery consists of tumour debulking rather than complete excision and is usually carried out endoscopically via a

trans-sphenoidal approach through a gum incision behind
the upper lip. Indications include the failure or intolerance of
medical management and sometimes decompression for acute
visual loss (see below). Visual оАЮeld improvement is fastest in
the earliest weeks and months following surgery.
оАБ Radiotherapy is rarely employed due to the risk of complications. Newer techniques include intensity-modulated radiation therapy and stereotactic radiosurgery.
оАБ Monitoring. Long-term ophthalmological review is required,
with serial assessment of visual function.

Pituitary apoplexy

Pituitary apoplexy (PA) is caused by acute haemorrhage into or
infarction of the pituitary gland and is usually associated with a
previously undiagnosed adenoma. Sheehan syndrome is infarction of the pituitary usually associated with childbirth and is

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 803
#### 19

A B

Fig. 19.55 Acromegaly. оАЭAоАЮ Facial features; оАЭBоАЮ hands.

generally regarded as a form of PA. PA typically manifests with the
sudden onset of a severe headache, nausea and vomiting, sometimes with meningism and occasionally reduced consciousness or
stroke. оАЯere is oоАЭen reduced VA and/or a bitemporal hemianopia
depending on the anatomical eоАЬects of the lesion. Double vision
due to compromise of the adjacent ocular motor nerves is common. Acute hormonal insuоАЪciency can lead to life-threatening
complications such as an Addisonian crisis. Investigations include
MR, urgent visual оАЮeld testing and hormonal assessment. Acute
medical management, including hormone administration and surgical decompression, may be necessary.

Craniopharyngioma

Craniopharyngioma is a slow-growing tumour arising from vestigial remnants of the Rathke pouch along the pituitary stalk.
AоАЬected children frequently present with dwarоАЮsm, delayed sexual
development and obesity due to interference with hypothalamic
function. Adults usually present with visual impairment.
Visual оАЮeld defects are complex and may be due to involvement of the optic nerves, chiasm or tracts.
оАЯe initial defect frequently involves both inferotemporal
оАЮelds because the tumour compresses the chiasm from above and
behind, damaging the upper nasal оАЮbres (see Fig. 19.52). MRI
shows a solid tumour that appears isointense on T1 images (Fig.
19.56). Cystic components appear hyperintense on T1 images.
Treatment is mainly surgical, but recurrences are common.

Meningioma

Intracranial meningiomas typically aоАЬect middle-aged women.
Visual оАЮeld defects and clinical signs depend on the location of the
tumour (Fig. 19.57).
оАБ Tuberculum sellae meningiomas oоАЭen produce a junctional
scotoma (see above and Fig. 19.57) due to their location.
оАБ Sphenoidal ridge tumours compress the optic nerve early
if the tumour is located medially and late if the lateral aspect
of the sphenoid bone and middle cranial fossa are involved

Fig. 19.56 Sagittal T1-weighted MR image of the pituitary and
supra-sellar region of a patient with a large craniopharyngioma. The optic chiasm is compressed to the extent that it
cannot be clearly distinguished on the surface of the tumour.

(Fig. 19.58A). A classic оАЮnding in the latter is fullness in the
temporal fossa due to hyperostosis (Fig. 19.58B).
оАБ Olfactory groove meningioma may cause loss of the sense of
smell, as well as optic nerve compression.
оАБ Treatment usually consists of surgery, but radiotherapy is
sometimes required. Observation alone may be adequate.

RETROCHIASMAL PATHWAYS

Optic tracts
Overview

Retrochiasmal pathology results in partial or total binocular visual
оАЮeld defects involving contralateral visual space. оАЯis hemianopia
(hemianopsia) involving the same side of the оАЮeld in both eyes is
homonymous, in contradistinction to the bitemporal hemianopia
seen in chiasmal compression, which produces heteronymous loss,
with opposite sides of the visual оАЮeld aоАЬected in each eye.

804 Retrochiasmal Pathways

Congruity

A homonymous hemianopia may be incomplete or complete. In
the context of incomplete hemianopia, congruity refers to how
closely the extent and pattern of оАЮeld loss in one eye matches that
of the other. Almost identical оАЮeld defects in either eye are therefore highly congruous, while mismatching right and leоАЭ visual
оАЮeld defects are incongruous. Hemianopia secondary to pathology in the anterior retrochiasmal visual pathways is characteristically incongruous, while that due to pathology further back
(e.g. the posterior optic radiations) manifests a higher degree of
congruity.

Clinical features

оАБ Homonymous hemianopia

{ оАЯe causes of optic tract disease are similar to those aоАЬecting the chiasm but the tract is particularly vulnerable when
the chiasm is preоАЮxed (see above).
оАБ Wernicke hemianopic pupil

{ оАЯe optic tracts arise at the posterior aspect of the chiasm,
diverge and extend posteriorly around the cerebral peduncles, to terminate in the lateral geniculate bodies.

{ Each optic tract contains crossed оАЮbres from the contralateral nasal hemiretina and uncrossed оАЮbres from the
ipsilateral temporal hemiretina.

{ Nerve оАЮbres originating from corresponding retinal elements are, however, not closely aligned.

{ Homonymous hemianopia caused by optic tract lesions is
therefore characteristically incongruous.

{ Lesions of the lateral geniculate body also produce asymmetrical hemianopic defects.

body but the pupillary оАЮbres leave the optic tract anterior
to the lateral geniculate body, projecting through the brachium of the superior colliculus to terminate in the pretectal nuclei.

{ An optic tract lesion may therefore give rise to an aоАЬerent

{ Characteristically, the pupillary light reоАЫex will be normal

when the involved hemiretina is stimulated (i.e. light is
shone from the hemianopic side).

оАБ Contralateral pyramidal signs may occur when an optic tract

Optic radiations

Anatomy

оАЯe optic radiations extend from the lateral geniculate body to
the striate cortex, which is located on the medial aspect of the
occipital lobe, above and below the calcarine оАЮssure (Fig. 19.59).
As the radiations pass posteriorly, оАЮbres from corresponding
retinal elements lie progressively closer together. For this reason,
incomplete hemianopia caused by posterior radiation lesions are
more congruous than those involving the anterior radiations.
Because these оАЮbres are third-order neurones that originate in
the lateral geniculate body, lesions of the optic radiations do not
produce optic atrophy. оАЯe optic radiations and visual cortex
have a dual blood supply from the middle and posterior cerebral
arteries.

Temporal radiations

оАБ Visual оАЯeld defect consists of a contralateral superior homonymous quadrantanopia (вАШpie in the skyвАЩ), because the inferior
оАЮbres of the optic radiations, which subserve the upper visual
оАЮelds, оАЮrst sweep antero-inferiorly (Meyer loop) into the temporal lobe around the anterior tip of the temporal horn of the
lateral ventricle (see вАШaвАЩ in Fig. 19.59).
оАБ Associated features are likely to include contralateral hemisensory disturbance and hemiparesis, because the temporal
radiations pass very close to the sensory and motor оАЮbres of
the internal capsule before passing posteriorly and re-joining
the superior оАЮbres. Other features of temporal lobe disease
include paroxysmal olfactory and gustatory hallucinations
(uncinate оАЮts), formed visual hallucinations and seizures,
with receptive dysphasia if the dominant hemisphere is
involved.

Anterior parietal radiations

оАБ Visual оАЯeld defect consists of a contralateral inferior homonymous quadrantanopia (вАШpie on the оАЫoorвАЩ) because the superior оАЮbres of the radiations, which subserve the inferior visual
оАЮelds, proceed directly posteriorly through the parietal lobe to
the occipital cortex. However, a lesion involving only the anterior parietal part of the radiations is rare. Hemianopia resulting from a parietal lobe lesion tends to be relatively congruous
(see вАШbвАЩ in Fig. 19.59).
оАБ Associated features of dominant parietal lobe disease include
acalculia, agraphia, leоАЭвАУright disorientation and оАЮnger agnosia. Non-dominant lobe lesions may cause dressing and constitutional apraxia and spatial neglect.

Main radiations

Deep in the parietal lobe, the optic radiations lie just external to
the trigone and the occipital horn of the lateral ventricle. Lesions in
this area usually cause a complete homonymous hemianopia (see
вАШcвАЩ in Fig. 19.59).
оАБ Optokinetic nystagmus (OKN), elicited with a rotating
striped optokinetic drum, may be useful in localizing the cause
of an isolated homonymous hemianopia.

{ Physiological OKN involves smooth pursuit of a target,
followed momentarily by a saccade in the opposite direction to оАЮxate on the next target.

{ If a homonymous hemianopia is due to a lesion in the parietal lobe, the smooth pursuit pathways towards the side of
the lesion are likely to be aоАЬected, making this component
of OKN defective. OKN will therefore be asymmetrical:
erratic when the drum is rotated towards the side of the
lesion, but regular when the drum is rotated away from the
side of the lesion.

{ If the lesion is in the occipital lobe, the smooth pursuit
pathways are intact and OKN will be symmetrical вАУ this is
the Cogan dictum, which also states that the parietal lobe
lesion is more likely to be a tumour and the occipital lesion
an infarction.

d. Anterior visual
cortex lesion

Calcarine fissure

806 Cerebral Visual Impairment (CVI)

Occipital cortex

Clinical features

оАБ Visual оАЯeld defects

{ In the striate cortex the peripheral visual оАЮelds are represented anteriorly. оАЯis part of the occipital lobe is supplied
by a branch of the posterior cerebral artery.

{ Central macular vision is represented posteriorly just
lateral to the tip of the calcarine cortex, an area supplied
mainly by a branch of the middle cerebral artery. Occlusion of the posterior cerebral artery will therefore tend
to produce a macular-sparing congruous homonymous
hemianopia (see вАШdвАЩ in Fig. 19.59).

{ Damage to the tip of the occipital cortex, as might occur
from a head injury, tends to give rise to congruous, homonymous, macular defects (see вАШeвАЩ in Fig. 19.59), although
macular sparing may sometimes occur with vascular
lesions of the occipital lobe.

Balint syndrome

Balint syndrome refers to the combination of simultanagnosia
(inability to discern the overall impression of an image whilst
distinguishing individual components), optic ataxia (defective
visually-directed reaching) and ocular apraxia (impairment of
voluntary saccades). It is typically due to parieto-occipital disease,
with a range of causes reported.

Posterior reversible encephalopathy
syndrome

Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) or reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy syndrome refers to a clinical
presentation thought to result from vascular endothelial dysfunction seen in patients with malignant hypertension or eclampsia,
treatment with some drugs (e.g. tacrolimus, ciclosporin) and rarely
other associations such as autoimmune disease. Cortical visual
deоАЮcits can occasionally be the presenting feature, others including headache and seizures.

CEREBRAL VISUAL IMPAIRMENT (CVI)

Introduction

вАШCerebral visual impairmentвАЩ is the term used to describe visual
disability which cannot be attributed to a disorder of the eyes and
anterior visual pathways. It is a consequence of abnormality in the
parts of the brain that process visual information. CVI is the commonest cause of visual disability in children living in industrialised
countries and aоАЬects up to 3% of mainstream school children. It
can be a diоАЪcult diagnosis to make in subtle cases where the visual
acuity is normal, particularly as brain imaging may reveal no signiоАЮcant abnormality. CVI can limit social interaction, mobility
and learning.

Causes

оАБ Children: especially premature birth and its cerebral complications (hypoxia and ischaemia), neonatal hypoglycemia, head
injury, infections (meningitis and encephalitis), hydrocephalus, developmental brain defects.
оАБ Adults: head injury, stroke, dementia.

Anatomical basis and outcomes of visual brain
injury

оАБ оАЮe posterior parietal lobes are connected to the occipital
lobes by the superior longitudinal fasciculus. оАЯis functional
pathway is called the вАШdorsal streamвАЩ, which creates a subconscious real-time dynamic three-dimensional reproduction
of our surroundings. оАЯis is mapped to body position and
allows accurate visually guided movement and visual search.
Balint syndrome (when severe) and dorsal stream dysfunction
(when less profound) results from bilateral posterior parietal
pathology.
оАБ Bilateral middle temporal lobe pathology slightly anterior to
the occipital lobes causes diоАЪculty in seeing moving targets
(dyskinetopsia).

{ оАЯe front of the calcarine cortex subserves the temporal
extremity of the visual оАЮeld of the contralateral eye, the
area of visual space that extends beyond the оАЮeld of binocular single vision and is perceived monocularly. A lesion in
this area may therefore give rise to a monocular temporal
оАЮeld defect in the contralateral eye, known as a temporal
crescent.
оАБ Associated features of visual cortex disease (cortical blindness) include formed visual hallucinations, denial of blindness
(Anton syndrome) and the Riddoch phenomenon (only moving visual targets perceived).

Causes

оАБ Stroke in the territory of the posterior cerebral artery is
responsible for over 90% of homonymous hemianopia with no
other neurological deоАЮcit.
оАБ Other causes include trauma, tumours and rarely migraine.
A range of uncommon inоАЫammatory (including autoimmune
and infective), degenerative and toxic disorders can also be
causative, but will usually manifest with more widespread
neurological features. Imaging and lumbar puncture are typically key to diagnosis. Mimicking by retinal disease such as
acute zonal occult outer retinopathy (AZOOR) may occur.

Benson syndrome

Benson syndrome (posterior cortical atrophy) is a rare condition
oоАЭen referred to as a visual variant of Alzheimer disease. Patients
may have normal VA, anterior and posterior segment examination, but commonly abnormal colour vision, homonymous оАЮeld
defects, visual agnosia, alexia and acalculia. оАЯe diagnosis is
commonly missed. Other degenerative conditions oоАЭen exhibiting early occipital cortical involvement include Alzheimer and
CreutzfeldtвАУJakob disease; presentation in the latter can be with
acute visual symptoms only. Electroretinography may be abnormal. In immunocompromised states, including drug-induced,
progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy can present with isolated occipital features.

оАБ Infarction of both occipital lobes that spares the middle
temporal lobes can conversely result in either an ability to see
moving objects but not static targets, or result in the Riddoch
phenomenon, which occurs when a person who is otherwise
blind can perceive moving objects. Retained ability to recognize emotion conveyed by facial expression despite cortical
blindness is called вАШeоАЬective blindsightвАЩ.

Symptoms

оАБ DiоАЫculty with visual complexity: diоАЪculty in seeing an
object when other stimuli are present (e.g. sounds) or where
there is a visually complex environment.
оАБ Visual оАЯeld preference. оАЯe child prefers to look at objects in
a particular visual оАЮeld.
оАБ Absence of visually guided reach: impairment of a childвАЩs
ability to look at an object while reaching for it.
оАБ Visual latency. оАЯe visual responses are slow and oоАЭen delayed.
оАБ Preference for familiar objects and rich, highly saturated
colours like red and yellow.
оАБ Staring. оАЯese children may have a need to look at rapidly
moving objects (e.g. оАЮxating on a rapidly moving fan) and
non-purposeful gaze (e.g. tendency to stare at a light source).
оАБ Atypical visual reоАЬexes. For example, delayed or absent protective blink response.

Management

оАБ Key issues. Early intervention, educational support and visual
rehabilitation are central to helping these individuals develop
and learn.
оАБ Identify the speciоАЯc visual diоАЫculty. Take a structured history from the patient/child and a family member.
оАБ Provide an explanation for the origin of the symptoms.
оАБ Devise strategies to compensate for the impairment, preferably by using a trained multi-disciplinary team.

OCULAR MOTOR NERVES

Third nerve
Nuclear complex

оАЯe nuclear complex of the third (oculomotor) nerve is situated
in the midbrain at the level of the superior colliculus, ventral to
the Sylvian aqueduct (Fig. 19.60). It is composed of the following
paired and unpaired sub nuclei:
оАБ Levator subnucleus is an unpaired caudal midline structure
that innervates both levator muscles. Lesions conоАЮned to this
area will therefore give rise to bilateral ptosis.
оАБ Superior rectus subnuclei are paired: each innervates the
respective contralateral superior rectus. A nuclear third nerve
palsy will therefore spare the ipsilateral and aоАЬect the contralateral, superior rectus.
оАБ Medial rectus, inferior rectus and inferior oblique subnuclei are paired and innervate their corresponding ipsilateral
muscles. Lesions conоАЮned to the nuclear complex are relatively
uncommon. оАЯe most frequent causes are vascular disease,
primary tumours and metastases. Involvement of the paired
medial rectus subnuclei cause a wall-eyed bilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia (WEBINO), characterized by exotropia
with defective convergence and adduction. Lesions involving
the entire nucleus are oоАЭen associated with involvement of the
adjacent and caudal fourth nerve nucleus.

Fasciculus

оАЯe fasciculus consists of eоАЬerent оАЮbres that pass from the third
nerve nucleus through the red nucleus and the medial aspect of
the cerebral peduncle (see Fig. 19.60). оАЯey then emerge from the
midbrain and pass into the interpeduncular space. оАЯe causes of
nuclear and fascicular lesions are similar, except that demyelination may aоАЬect the fasciculus.

808 Ocular Motor Nerves

оАБ Benedikt syndrome involves the fasciculus as it passes through
the red nucleus and is characterized by ipsilateral third nerve
palsy and contralateral extrapyramidal signs such as hemi
tremor.
оАБ Weber syndrome involves the fasciculus as it passes through
the cerebral peduncle and is characterized by ipsilateral third
nerve palsy and a contralateral hemiparesis.
оАБ Nothnagel syndrome involves the fasciculus and the superior
cerebellar peduncle and is characterized by ipsilateral third
nerve palsy and cerebellar ataxia.
оАБ Claude syndrome is a combination of Benedikt and Nothnagel
syndromes.

Basilar

оАЯe basilar part starts as a series of вАШrootletsвАЩ that leave the midbrain
on the medial aspect of the cerebral peduncle, before coalescing
to form the main trunk. оАЯe nerve then passes between the posterior cerebral and superior cerebellar arteries, running lateral to
and parallel with the posterior communicating artery (Fig. 19.61).
As the nerve traverses the base of the skull along its subarachnoid
course unaccompanied by any other cranial nerve, an isolated
third nerve palsy is commonly basilar. оАЯe following are important causes:
оАБ Aneurysm of the posterior communicating artery at its junction with the internal carotid artery (Fig. 19.62), typically
presents acutely as a pupil-involving painful third nerve palsy.
In a recent population-based study, compression from an
aneurysm only accounted for 6% of acute third nerve palsies.
Approximately two-thirds had pupillary involvement.
оАБ Head trauma, resulting in extradural or subdural haematoma,
may cause a tentorial pressure cone with downward herniation of the temporal lobe. оАЯis compresses the third nerve as it
passes over the tentorial edge (Fig. 19.63), initially causing irritative miosis followed by mydriasis and complete third nerve
palsy.

Intracavernous

оАЯe third nerve then enters the cavernous sinus by piercing the
dura just lateral to the posterior clinoid process. Within the sinus,
the third nerve runs in the lateral wall above the fourth nerve (Fig.
19.64). In the anterior part of the cavernous sinus, the nerve divides
into superior and inferior branches that enter the orbit through the
superior orbital оАЮssure within the annulus of Zinn. оАЯe following
are potential causes of intracavernous third nerve palsy:
оАБ Diabetes.
оАБ Pituitary apoplexy (see above).
оАБ Miscellaneous pathology such as aneurysm, meningioma,
carotid-cavernous оАЮstula and granulomatous inоАЫammation
(TolosaвАУHunt syndrome). Because of its close proximity to
other cranial nerves, intracavernous third nerve lesions are
likely to be associated with involvement of the fourth and sixth
nerves and the оАЮrst division of the оАЮоАЭh nerve.

Intraorbital

оАБ Superior division innervates the levator and superior rectus
muscles.

innervate the sphincter pupillae and the ciliary muscle.
Lesions of the inferior division are characterized by limited
adduction and depression, together with a dilated pupil. Both
superior and inferior division palsies are commonly traumatic or vascular.

Pupillomotor оАЮbres

Between the brainstem and the cavernous sinus, the pupillomotor
parasympathetic оАЮbres are located superоАЮcially in the superomedial
part of the third nerve (Fig. 19.65). оАЯey derive their blood supply
from the pial blood vessels, whereas the main interior trunk of the
nerve is supplied by the vasa nervorum. Involvement or sparing of
the pupil is important, as it frequently diоАЬerentiates a вАШsurgicalвАЩ from
a вАШmedicalвАЩ lesion:

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 809
#### 19

оАБ вАШSurgicalвАЩ lesions such as aneurysms, trauma and uncal herniation characteristically involve the pupil by compressing
the pial blood vessels and the superоАЮcially located pupillary
оАЮbres.
оАБ вАШMedicalвАЩ lesions such as occur in hypertension and diabetes
usually spare the pupil (Fig. 19.66). оАЯis is because the microangiopathy associated with medical lesions involves the vasa
nervorum, causing ischaemia of the main trunk of the nerve,
leaving the superоАЮcial pupillary оАЮbres intact.
оАБ оАЮese principles are not infallible as pupillary involvement
may be seen in some microangiopathic palsies, while pupillary sparing does not invariably exclude aneurysm or other
compressive lesion. Pupillary involvement may develop a
few days aоАЭer the onset of diplopia as an aneurysm expands.
Exceptionally, pupillary involvement may be the only sign of
third nerve palsy (basal meningitis, uncal herniation). Like
other features of third nerve palsy, pupillary involvement
may be complete or partial, so mild pupillary signs may be
clinically signiоАЮcant.

TIP Medical conditions (hypertension, diabetes) tend to
cause a pupil-sparing third nerve palsy, but this principle is not
infallible.

Signs

Partial involvement will give milder degrees of ophthalmoplegia.
оАЯe other cranial nerves and peripheral nervous system should
always be examined.
оАБ Profound ptosis due to weakness of the levator muscle (Fig.
19.67A).
оАБ Abduction and depression in the primary position (вАШdown
and outвАЩ вАУ Fig. 19.67B) due to unopposed action of the lateral rectus and superior oblique muscles. оАЯe intact superior
oblique muscle also causes intorsion of the eye at rest, which
increases on attempted downgaze.
оАБ Limited adduction due to medial rectus weakness (Fig.
19.66C and Fig. 19.67C).
оАБ Normal abduction as the lateral rectus is intact (Fig. 19.66D).
оАБ Limited elevation due to weakness of the superior rectus and
inferior oblique (Fig. 19.66E and Fig. 19.67D).
оАБ Limited depression due to weakness of the inferior rectus
(Fig. 19.66F).
оАБ Dilated pupil and defective accommodation due to parasympathetic palsy (Fig. 19.67).

Aberrant regeneration

Aberrant regeneration may follow acute traumatic and compressive, but not vascular, third nerve palsies. оАЯis is because the endoneural nerve sheaths, which may be breached in traumatic and
compressive lesions, remain intact with vascular lesions. Bizarre
defects in ocular motility, such as elevation of the upper eyelid on
attempted adduction or depression (the pseudo-Graefe or pseudovon Graefe phenomenon), are caused by misdirected regenerating axons that re-innervate the incorrect extraocular muscle (Fig.
19.68). оАЯe pupil may also be involved.

Causes of isolated third nerve palsy

Clinical evaluation and imaging facilitate identiоАЮcation of a cause
in most cases.
оАБ Microvascular disease associated with systemic risk factors such as hypertension and diabetes are the most common cause of third nerve palsy, accounting for about 40%
of cases. Marked periorbital pain is often associated, which
is therefore not helpful in distinguishing an aneurysmal
cause. In diabetes-related paresis, motility disturbance is
typically profound, but there is usually (80%) relative or
complete pupil sparing and when pupillary involvement
does occur some degree of reaction to light is almost always
preserved. As a general rule, where there is a complete third
nerve palsy and a normally responsive pupil the cause is significantly more likely to be microvascular than secondary
to an aneurysm.
оАБ Aneurysm of the posterior communicating artery at its junction with the internal carotid is a very important cause of isolated third nerve palsy with involvement of the pupil. Pain is
oоАЭen, though not invariably, present and the extent of pupillary involvement characteristically exceeds the severity of
motility dysfunction. An aneurysm of the internal carotid
within the cavernous sinus tends to also involve other cranial
nerves (see above).
оАБ Trauma, both direct and secondary to subdural haematoma
with uncal herniation. However, the development of third

nerve palsy following relatively trivial head trauma should
alert the clinician to the possibility of an underlying aneurysm
or tumour.
оАБ Miscellaneous uncommon causes include tumour, inоАЫammatory disease such as syphilis, Lyme disease and sarcoidosis, GCA and vasculitis associated with collagen vascular
disorders.
оАБ Episodic. Brief episodes of third nerve dysfunction with spontaneous recovery may be idiopathic or occur with migraine,
compression, ischaemia and alterations in intracranial pressure. Myasthenia gravis may mimic intermittent pupil-sparing
third nerve paresis.

Investigation

оАБ Vascular risk factor assessment similar to that for retinal arterial disease (see Ch. 13). Supplementary investigation may be
required if a rarer aetiology such as infection (e.g. syphilis,
Lyme disease) or vasculitis (including GCA) is suspected. In
some cases, a lumbar puncture may be needed.
оАБ CT angiography should be performed with great urgency if
the clinical features are suspicious of an expanding aneurysm,
especially if there is marked pupillary involvement with milder
motility dysfunction and only partial ptosis.
оАБ MRI brain and orbits with venography, including speciоАЮc
exclusion of a brainstem stroke or tumour, cavernous sinus or
orbital apex lesion.
оАБ Conventional cerebral angiography is occasionally indicated.

Treatment

A

B

Fig. 19.68 Left third nerve palsy with aberrant regeneration.
оАЭAоАЮ Lid elevation with reduced adduction on attempted right
gaze; оАЭBоАЮ lid elevation with depression of the eye (pseudo

оАБ Observation is usually appropriate in presumed microvascular
cases; the majority will resolve over weeks or months.
Temporary (e.g. Fresnel stick-on) prisms may be useful if the
angle of deviation is small, but uniocular occlusion may be
necessary to avoid diplopia if the ptosis component is partial
or recovering. Botulinum toxin injection into the uninvolved
lateral rectus muscle is sometimes used to prevent its contracture when recovery time is prolonged.
оАБ Surgical treatment of the ocular motility element and ptosis should be contemplated only aоАЭer spontaneous improvement has ceased, usually not earlier than 6вАУ12 months from
onset. Numerous innovative surgical techniques have been
described.

812 Ocular Motor Nerves

Fourth nerve

Anatomy

оАЯe fourth (trochlear) cranial nerve (Fig. 19.69) supplies only the
superior oblique muscle.
оАБ Key features

{ It is a very long and slender nerve, increasing its
vulnerability.

{ It is the only cranial nerve to emerge from the dorsal aspect
of the brain.

{ It is the only decussated (crossed) cranial nerve besides the
optic nerve, innervating the superior oblique muscle contralateral to its nucleus.

Internal carotid artery

Posterior communicating artery

Oculomotor nerve (III)
Abducens nerve (VI)
Posterior cerebral artery
Superior cerebellar artery

Basilar artery
Trochlear nerve (IV)

Fig. 19.69 Dorsal view of the course of the fourth (trochlear)
nerve

Signs

The acute onset of vertical diplopia in the absence of ptosis,
combined with a characteristic head posture, strongly suggests
fourth nerve disease. The main actions of the superior oblique
are intorsion (incyclotorsion) and depression in adduction.
Peripheral lesions cause ipsilateral and nuclear lesions contralateral superior oblique weakness. Left paresis is characterized
by:
оАБ LeоАЪ hypertropia (вАШleоАЭ over rightвАЩ) in the primary position
(Fig. 19.70A), increasing on right gaze (Fig. 19.70B).
оАБ Limitation of leоАЪ depression, most marked in adduction.
оАБ LeоАЪ extorsion, greatest in abduction.
оАБ Normal abduction of the leоАЭ eye.
оАБ Normal elevation of the leоАЭ eye.
оАБ A compensatory head posture (Fig. 19.70C) avoids diplopia:
vertical, torsional and worse on downgaze. To compensate
for weakness of intorsion there is contralateral head tilt to the
right. To alleviate the weakened depression of the eye the chin
is slightly depressed. As this is most marked in adduction, the
face may also be turned slightly to the right.
оАБ Bilateral involvement should always be excluded, particularly
following head trauma (Fig. 19.71A).

{ It has the fewest axons of any of the cranial nerves.
оАБ оАЮe nucleus is located at the level of the inferior colliculi ventral to the Sylvian aqueduct. It is caudal to and continuous with
the third nerve nuclear complex.
оАБ оАЮe fasciculus consists of axons that curve posteriorly around
the aqueduct and decussate completely in the anterior medullary velum.
оАБ оАЮe trunk leaves the brainstem on the dorsal surface, just caudal to the inferior colliculus. It then curves laterally around
the brainstem, runs forwards beneath the free edge of the
tentorium and, like the third nerve, passes between the posterior cerebral artery and the superior cerebellar artery. It then
pierces the dura and enters the cavernous sinus.
оАБ оАЮe intracavernous part runs in the lateral wall of the sinus,
inferior to the third nerve and above the оАЮrst division of the
оАЮоАЭh. In the anterior part of the cavernous sinus, it rises and
passes through the superior orbital оАЮssure above and lateral to
the annulus of Zinn.
оАБ оАЮe intraorbital part innervates the superior oblique muscle.

Causes of isolated fourth nerve palsy

оАБ Idiopathic lesions are common and many of these are thought
to be congenital although symptoms may not develop until
decompensation occurs in adult life due to reduced fusional
ability. In contrast to acquired lesions patients are not usually
aware of the torsional aspect, but may develop vertical double
vision that is oоАЭen appreciated as of sudden or subacute onset.
Examination of old photographs for the presence of a compensatory head posture may be helpful, as is the presence of an
increased vertical prism fusional range. Occasionally cataract
surgery can be associated with decompensation.
оАБ Trauma frequently causes bilateral fourth nerve palsy. оАЯe
long and slender nerves are particularly vulnerable as they
decussate in the anterior medullary velum, through impact
with the tentorial edge. Care must be taken not to mistake a
bilateral palsy for a unilateral lesion, particularly when corrective surgery is contemplated.
оАБ Microvascular lesions are relatively common, this aetiology often being presumed when appropriate systemic risk
factors are present in the absence of features of congenital
onset.
оАБ Aneurysms and tumours are extremely rare.

{ Right hypertropia in leоАЭ gaze and leоАЭ hypertropia in right
gaze, though orthophoria may be present.

{ Greater than 10¬∞ of cyclodeviation (measured using double
Maddox rods or by synoptophore вАУ see Ch. 18).

{ вАШVвАЩ pattern esotropia is oоАЭen present (Fig. 19.71BвАУD).
Absence of the secondary abduction function provided
by the superior oblique muscles in downgaze allows the
increased tone in the inferior rectus muscles to produce
an esodeviation.

{ Bilaterally positive Bielschowsky head tilt test (see below).

TIP Trauma is the commonest cause of bilateral fourth nerve
palsy.
