Parks three-step test

оАЯis clinical test allows isolation of a single weak muscle in patients
with vertical diplopia of acute onset. It is inaccurate in some circumstances, including patients who have undergone previous
extraocular muscle surgery.
оАБ Step one. In the primary position, the hypertropic eye is identiоАЮed, narrowing the aоАЬected muscle to one of the depressors
of the hypertropic eye (superior oblique or inferior rectus) or
one of the elevators of the hypotropic eye (superior rectus or
inferior oblique). In a fourth nerve palsy, the involved eye is
higher (see Fig. 19.70A).
оАБ Step two. оАЯe eyes are examined in right and leоАЭ gaze to
determine where the hypertropia is greater, thus assigning the
weakness to the two of the four previously identiоАЮed muscles
having the greatest vertical action in that position. In superior
oblique weakness (see Fig. 19.70B) the deviation is worse on
opposite gaze вАУ WOOG.
оАБ Step three

{ оАЯe Bielschowsky head tilt test (BHTT) is performed with
the patient оАЮxating on a target directly ahead, optimally at
3 metres (see Fig. 19.70D and E).

{ оАЯe head is tilted to each side in turn in order to assess the
muscles responsible for cyclotorsion, with observation to
determine the position in which the hypertropia is worse.
On tilt to one side, the superior oblique and superior rectus (note that both are superior) muscles of the eye of that
same side correctively intort and the inferior rectus and
inferior oblique (note both are inferior) of the contralateral

eye correctively extort. From the two muscles previously
isolated, one can be eliminated.

{ In fourth nerve palsy the deviation is better on opposite
tilt вАУ BOOT (see Fig. 19.70D). In practice, as the threestep test is almost always employed to conоАЮrm a fourth
nerve palsy, the BHTT alone is oоАЭen suоАЪcient for a working diagnosis.

Investigation

Vascular investigation is sometimes indicated. Neuroimaging is
not required routinely for an isolated non-progressive fourth nerve
palsy, but should be considered if improvement does not occur.

Treatment

Congenital decompensated and presumed microvascular palsies
commonly resolve spontaneously. Strabismus surgery is not infrequently required for traumatic and childhood cases, in the former
for troublesome diplopia and the latter for a substantial compensatory head posture. оАЯe approach depends on the pattern and
severity of weakness.
оАБ A small hypertropia under 15 prism dioptres (ќФ) can usually
be treated either by inferior oblique weakening or by superior
oblique tucking, though surgery to other muscles might be
required in some circumstances.
оАБ A moderateвАУlarge deviation may be treated by ipsilateral
inferior oblique weakening combined with, or followed by,
ipsilateral superior rectus weakening and/or contralateral
inferior rectus weakening if required; defective elevation is a
potential complication.

оАБ Excyclotorsion may need to be addressed, particularly in bilateral cases. оАЯe HaradaвАУIto procedure involves splitting and
anterolateral transposition of the lateral half of the superior

оАБ V-pattern esotropia secondary to bilateral fourth nerve palsy

Sixth nerve

Brainstem

оАЯe nucleus of the sixth (abducens or abducent) nerve lies at the
mid-level of the pons, ventral to the оАЫoor of the fourth ventricle
(Fig. 19.73). оАЯe оАЮbres (fasciculus) leave the brainstem ventrally at
the pontomedullary junction.
оАБ Nuclear lesion. A nuclear sixth nerve lesion also causes a
failure of horizontal gaze towards the side of the damage due
to involvement of the adjacent horizontal gaze centre (paramedian pontine reticular formation). Facial (seventh) nerve
оАЮbres wrap around the sixth nerve nucleus, so ipsilateral lower
motor neurone (LMN) facial nerve palsy is also common.
Isolated sixth nerve palsy is never nuclear in origin.
оАБ Foville (inferior medial pontine) syndrome is most frequently caused by vascular disease or tumours involving the
dorsal pons. It is characterized by ipsilateral involvement of
the оАЮоАЭh to eighth cranial nerves, central sympathetic оАЮbres
(Horner syndrome) and horizontal gaze palsy.
оАБ MillardвАУGubler (ventral pontine) syndrome involves the fasciculus as it passes through the pyramidal tract and is most frequently caused by vascular disease, tumours or demyelination.
As well as ipsilateral sixth nerve palsy, there is contralateral
hemiplegia and oоАЭen an ipsilateral LMN facial nerve palsy.
оАБ Raymond syndrome involves the pyramidal tract and sixth
nerve. In addition to an ipsilateral sixth nerve palsy there is
contralateral hemiplegia.

Fig. 19.73 The pons at the level of the sixth (abducens) nerve
nucleus.

Basilar

оАЯe basilar part of the nerve enters the prepontine basilar cistern,
passes upwards close to the base of the skull and is crossed by the
anterior inferior cerebellar artery (Fig. 19.74). It pierces the dura
below the posterior clinoid and angles forwards over the tip of
the petrous bone, passing through or around the inferior petrosal sinus, through the Dorello canal (underneath the petroclinoid
ligament), to enter the cavernous sinus.
оАБ A vestibular schwannoma (also called an acoustic neuroma,
although this is a misnomer as the tumour arises from the
Schwann cells of the nerve, rather than the actual neurones)
may damage the sixth nerve at the pontomedullary junction
(Fig. 19.75). оАЯe оАЮrst symptom is hearing loss and the оАЮrst sign
diminished corneal sensitivity. Hearing and corneal sensation
should be checked in all patients with sixth nerve palsy.
оАБ Nasopharyngeal tumours may invade the skull and its foramina and damage the nerve during its basilar course.
оАБ Raised intracranial pressure may cause a downward displacement of the brainstem. оАЯis may stretch one or both sixth
nerves over the petrous tip, when paresis is a false localizing
sign.
оАБ Basal skull fracture may cause unilateral or bilateral palsy.
оАБ Gradenigo syndrome, most frequently caused by mastoiditis
or acute petrositis, may result in damage of the sixth nerve at
the petrous tip. оАЯe latter is frequently accompanied by facial
weakness and pain and hearing diоАЪculties.

TIP Hearing and corneal sensation should be tested in
patients with a sixth nerve palsy, to exclude a vestibular
schwannoma (вАШacoustic neuromaвАЩ).

Intracavernous and intraorbital

оАБ оАЮe intracavernous section runs below the third and fourth
and the оАЮrst division of the оАЮоАЭh nerves. оАЯe sixth nerve is the
most medially situated and runs through the middle of the
sinus in close relation to the internal carotid artery. Occasionally, intracavernous sixth nerve palsy is accompanied by a
postganglionic Horner syndrome (Parkinson syndrome) due

оАЮssure within the annulus of Zinn to innervate the lateral rectus.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. Double vision is characteristically worse for a distant target and less or absent for near оАЮxation. A careful review
of systems should be conducted, including enquiry about other
neurological symptoms, GCA, trauma and symptoms of ear
disease.
оАБ Esotropia in the primary position due to relatively unopposed
action of the medial rectus (Figs 19.76A and 19.77A). оАЯe

worse for distance than near оАЮxation.

оАБ Bilateral acute sixth nerve palsy (Fig. 19.78) is considerably
less common than unilateral. оАЯe causes are similar, but it is
important to exclude elevated intracranial pressure.
оАБ A compensatory face turn is towards the side of the paralysed
muscle in unilateral palsy.
оАБ Neurological examination. оАЯe other cranial nerves and the
peripheral nervous system should be examined, if necessary, by
an appropriate specialist. In a child or any patient with relevant
symptoms, an otorhinolaryngological review may be sought.

Investigation

Idiopathic and presumed microvascular lesions are common (up
to 60%) in older patients, but a wide range of causes has been
reported and a low threshold should be adopted for neuroimaging and other investigations even with isolated paresis. Also, as
with third nerve palsy, improved imaging has led to increasingly
common identiоАЮcation of a cause in lesions that might previously
have been categorized as idiopathic. In contrast to fourth nerve
palsy, a decompensated congenital aetiology is thought to be rare.
оАЯe presence of bilateral paresis, additional relevant clinical оАЮndings or presentation in children and younger adults should prompt
thorough investigation.

TIP Children or young adults presenting with bilateral
sixth nerve palsy should undergo urgent neuroradiological
investigation to оАЯnd the cause.

Treatment

Seventh nerve

оАЯe seventh (facial) cranial nerve innervates the facial muscles,
stimulates secretomotor activity in the lacrimal gland, submandibular and sublingual salivary glands, is involved in the sensation

оАБ Observation with monocular occlusion or prismatic (e.g.
temporary Fresnel stick-on) correction of diplopia is appropriate in idiopathic and presumed microvascular lesions. Up
to 90% will recover spontaneously, usually over weeks to several months. Young children should be treated with alternate
patching to prevent amblyopia.
оАБ Botulinum toxin injection into the ipsilateral medial rectus
may be used to prevent contracture, assess residual function
and sometimes to facilitate prismatic correction with a large
deviation (see Fig. 18.76), but is rarely curative.
оАБ Surgery should be considered only when adequate time has
been allowed for maximal spontaneous improvement, typically at least 6вАУ12 months from onset.

{ Partial palsy (paresis) is treated by adjustable medial
rectus recession and lateral rectus resection in the
affected eye, aiming for a small exophoria in the primary position to maximize the field of binocular single
vision.

{ Complete palsy is treated by transposition of the superior
and inferior recti to positions above and below the aоАЬected
lateral rectus muscle (Fig. 19.79) coupled with weakening
of the ipsilateral medial rectus (sometimes with injection
of botulinum toxin вАУ вАШtoxin transpositionвАЩ) (see Fig. 18.76).
оАЯree rectus muscles should not be detached from the
globe at the same procedure because of the risk of anterior
segment ischaemia.
оАБ Permanent prism. Troublesome but mild residual deviation
may be treated with a prism incorporated into spectacles as an
alternative to surgery.

818 Ocular Motor Nerves

of taste from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue and controls the
amplitude of sound from the inner ear.

Key features

оАБ Facial muscles. оАЯe facial nerve provides motor innervation
of muscles that are responsible for facial expression.
оАБ Innervation. оАЯe facial muscles are innervated centrally by
the cerebral cortex and peripherally by the ipsilateral seventh
nerve. Central innervation is bilateral for the upper face and
unilateral for the lower face.
оАБ Palsy. Supranuclear lesions result in palsy of the lower face on
the opposite side and peripheral lesions result in palsy of the
upper and lower face on the same side.
оАБ Dry eye syndrome. Loss of parasympathetic оАЮbres causes
reduced tear production and loss of taste from the anterior
part of the tongue.
оАБ Hearing. Loss of stapedius function causes hyperacusis.

Anatomy (Fig. 19.80)

оАБ оАЯe facial nerve arises from the pons and consists of a large
motor root and a smaller sensory root.

Sensory and
motor roots

оАБ оАЯe two roots cross the posterior cranial fossa and pass out of
the cranial cavity through the internal acoustic meatus, next to
the vestibulocochlear (VIII) nerve.
оАБ AоАЭer entering the facial canal in the petrous temporal bone,
the two roots fuse and form the facial nerve, which soon
enlarges as the geniculate ganglion.
оАБ At the geniculate ganglion the facial nerve gives oоАЬ the greater
petrosal nerve, which carries preganglionic parasympathetic
оАЮbres to the lacrimal gland and mucous glands of the nasal cavity.
оАБ оАЯe facial nerve continues along the bony canal giving oоАЬ the
nerve to the stapedius muscle (sound regulation) and chorda
tympani (taste and parasympathetic оАЮbres destined for the
submandibular ganglion), before leaving the cranium through
the stylomastoid foramen.
оАБ оАЯe nerve then divides into the terminal branches at the posterior edge of the parotid gland, which then innervate the facial
muscles.
оАБ оАЯe corneal reоАЬex, which is tested by stimulating the cornea
with a thin strand of cotton-wool, results in the reоАЫex closure
of both eyelids. оАЯe aоАЬerent limb of this reоАЫex is transmitted by
the trigeminal nerve and the eоАЬerent limb by the facial nerve.

Physical signs

оАБ A supranuclear lesion results in paralysis of the contralateral
middle and lower parts of the face (Fig. 19.81A).
оАБ A nuclear lesion results in complete paralysis of the facial
muscles on the ipsilateral side.
оАБ Based on the anatomy, the precise location of the lesion is
possible:

{ Pons: ipsilateral facial paralysis, sixth nerve palsy, contralateral hemiplegia, paralysis of conjugate gaze to the side of
the lesion (PPRF).

{ Cerebro-pontine angle: ipsilateral facial palsy, decreased
secretion of tears and saliva, hyperacusis and loss of taste
to the anterior 2/3 of the tongue.

{ Eighth (vestibulocochlear) nerve: additional оАЮndings
include deafness and tinnitus.

{ Parotid gland: ipsilateral facial palsy manifesting with a feeling of numbness, associated with an expressionless face.

{ Ramsay Hunt syndrome results from reactivation of herpes zoster in the geniculate ganglion and manifests with:

A

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 819
#### 19

ipsilateral facial palsy, ear pain and vesicles on the face and
in the ear.

Causes

оАБ Bell palsy is an idiopathic form of facial nerve palsy and is
responsible for 60вАУ70% of cases (Fig. 19.81B). оАЯe symptoms
develop rapidly over several hours. оАЯe patient oоАЭen recovers within days to several weeks. оАЯe extent of nerve damage
determines the outcome. Residual symptoms may include
facial muscle weakness (Fig. 19.81C), a watery eye and synkinesis (вАШcrocodileвАЩ tears).
оАБ Trauma (10%) (see Fig. 19.81A). Check for periorbital or mastoid bruising and blood in the ear canal; can be iatrogenic.
оАБ Viral infection (5%): herpes simplex (most common), cytomegalovirus, EpsteinвАУBarr, mumps, rubella.
оАБ Lyme disease (oоАЭen bilateral).
оАБ Neoplastic (2%): parotid malignancy, vestibular schwannoma.
оАБ Diabetes.
оАБ Sarcoidosis (oоАЭen bilateral).

B

C

820 Supranuclear Disorders of Ocular Motility

оАБ вАШStroke mimicsвАЩ, e.g. hemiparesis and facial drooping caused
by hypoglycaemia in elderly individuals, migraine, or sepsis
are transient causes of focal neurological signs.

Management

оАБ Corneal protection, especially if the оАЮоАЭh nerve is also aоАЬected.
оАБ Corticosteroids. In Bell palsy systemic corticosteroids are
helpful if started within 48вАУ72 hours of onset: use prednisolone 60вАУ80mg daily for one week, then taper over one week.
оАБ Antiviral treatment (acyclovir or valaciclovir) added to systemic corticosteroids in idiopathic facial nerve palsy is beneоАЮcial. Although the rate of recovery is not aоАЬected, the number
of people leоАЭ with long-term eоАЬects (excessive tearing or an
abnormal facial movement) is reduced.
оАБ MRI scan of the brain should be undertaken if the palsy is
slow in onset or if there are any other neurological оАЮndings.
оАБ If bilateral, undertake a chest X-ray and serum angiotensinconverting enzyme (SACE) test to exclude sarcoidosis.
оАБ Surgery. Surgical decompression of the facial nerve in
Bell palsy is not beneficial. In patients with long-term
paralysis reconstructive surgery can offer some improvement but cannot fully restore natural movement or facial
expressions.

TIP If the onset of a facial nerve palsy is slow or if there
are other neurological signs, investigate with an MRI scan to
exclude intra-cranial pathology.

SUPRANUCLEAR DISORDERS OF
OCULAR MOTILITY

Conjugate eye movements

Conjugate eye movements (versions) are binocular movements
in which the eyes move synchronously and symmetrically in the
same direction. оАЯe main types are saccades, smooth pursuit and
non-optical reоАЫex movements. Saccadic and pursuit movements
are supranuclear in origin, controlled at both cerebral and brainstem levels. Non-optical reоАЫex function helps to stabilize the visual
оАЮeld with respect to head position. Disturbance of supranuclear
eye movement function causes gaze palsy.

Saccades

оАБ Function. Rapid voluntary or reоАЫex alignment of the fovea
with a target image, including transferral of оАЮxation from one
target to another.
оАБ Pathway. There are separate horizontal and vertical pathways. Many cortical areas and the superior colliculi are
involved in saccade generation, but classically initiation
occurs in the premotor cortex frontal eye fields, from where
impulses for horizontal movement pass to the contralateral paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) вАУ the
horizontal gaze centre (Fig. 19.82). Each frontal lobe initiates contralateral saccades. Vertical pathways are less well
mapped (see below).

Fig 19.82 Anatomical pathways for horizontal eye movements. LR = lateral rectus; MLF = medial longitudinal fasciculus; MR = medial rectus; PPRF = paramedian pontine reticular
formation.

оАБ Saccadic palsy. оАЯis usually happens aоАЭer damage or hyperactivity in the frontal eye оАЮeld and results in a saccadic palsy
to the contralateral side. If bilateral, there is a spasm of оАЮxation
and a need to use head trust to reоАЮxate. Hyperactivity (frontal
adversive seizures) causes a deviation of the eyes to the contralateral side.

Smooth pursuit

оАБ Function. To maintain оАЮxation on a target with slow smooth
movements once it has been located by the saccadic system.
оАЯe stimulus is image movement near the fovea.
оАБ Pathway. оАЯis is complex, involving several cortex regions as
well as the PPRF, the superior colliculi, cerebellum and other
structures. оАЯe pathways are ipsilateral, the cortex on one side
controlling pursuit to the same side.
оАБ Pursuit palsy. оАЯis usually happens following damage or
hyperactivity in the parieto-occipital lobe. оАЯe damaging
lesion causes a unilateral pursuit palsy to the ipsilateral direction. Hyperactivity causes a deviation of the eyes to the ipsilateral side usually associated with visual hallucinations.

Non-optical reоАЯexes

оАБ Function. оАЯe maintenance of eye position without conscious
input following a change of head or body position. Other,
visually directed (i.e. optical) mechanisms are also concerned
with visual оАЮeld stability. Both may be active in the dollвАЩs eye
(oculocephalic) reоАЫex, where the head of an unconscious
patient is rotated causing the eyes to move in the opposite
direction to preserve the image position on the retina
оАБ Pathway. Movements are mediated mainly via the vestibular
system. Signals originate in the inner ear labyrinths and in
proprioceptors that provide information concerning head and
neck movements. For horizontal movements, aоАЬerent оАЮbres
synapse in the vestibular nuclei and pass to the horizontal gaze
centre.

Abnormalities of horizontal gaze

Anatomy

AоАЭer initiation horizontal eye movements are generated in the
horizontal gaze centre (PPRF вАУ see Fig. 19.82) and mediated via
a common pathway. From here, impulses travel directly to the
ipsilateral sixth nerve nucleus and indirectly via internuclear neurones that cross the midline at the level of the pons and pass up
the contralateral medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), to motor
neurones in the medial rectus subnucleus of the contralateral third
nerve complex. Stimulation of the horizontal gaze centre on one
side therefore causes a conjugate movement of the eyes to the same
side.

Horizontal gaze palsy

A lesion of the horizontal gaze centre in the PPRF gives ipsilateral
horizontal gaze palsy, with an inability to look in the direction of
the lesion.

Internuclear ophthalmoplegia

A lesion in the MLF is responsible for the clinical syndrome of
internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO). Causes include demyelination, stroke and tumours. Strabismus surgery can be performed
for persistent diplopia.
оАБ Unilateral INO (Fig. 19.83). Double vision is not typically a
complaint.

{ Straight eyes in the primary position.

{ Convergence is intact if the lesion is anterior and discrete
but impaired if the lesion is posterior or extensive.

оАБ One-and-a-half syndrome. PPRF and MLF lesions combined
on the same side give rise to the вАШone-and-a-half syndromeвАЩ
characterized by a combination of ipsilateral gaze palsy and
INO. оАЯe only residual movement is abduction of the contralateral eye, which exhibits abduction nystagmus.

Vertical gaze palsy

Anatomy

Vertical eye movement initiation in the frontal lobes is simultaneously bilateral, with impulses mediated via the vertical gaze centre
in the midbrain (rostral interstitial nucleus of the MLF). From the

vertical gaze centre, impulses pass to the subnuclei of the eye muscles controlling vertical gaze in both eyes. Cells subserving upward
and downward eye movements are intermingled in the vertical
gaze centre, although selective paralysis of upgaze and downgaze
may occur in spite of this.

Parinaud (dorsal midbrain) syndrome

оАБ Signs

{ Straight eyes in the primary position.

{ Supranuclear upgaze and downgaze palsy on saccadic
movement (Fig. 19.85A and B).

{ Defective convergence.

{ Normal downgaze using dollвАЩs head manoeuvre (Fig. 19.85C).

{ Large pupils with lightвАУnear dissociation (see Fig. 19.45).

{ Lid retraction (Collier sign) (Fig. 19.85D).

{ ConvergenceвАУretraction nystagmus (Fig. 19.85E and F).
оАБ Causes

{ Children: aqueduct stenosis, meningitis and pinealoma/
pinealoblastoma (Fig. 19.86).

{ Young adults: demyelination, trauma and arteriovenous
malformations.

{ оАЯe elderly: midbrain vascular accidents, mass lesions
involving the periaqueductal grey matter and posterior
fossa aneurysms.

оАБ Supranuclear gaze palsy, initially primarily of downgaze and
subsequently upgaze.
оАБ Horizontal movements subsequently become impaired, with
eventual global gaze palsy.
оАБ Paralysis of convergence.
оАБ Pseudobulbar palsy.
оАБ Extrapyramidal rigidity, gait ataxia and dementia.

Parkinson disease

This is a chronic, progressive, neurodegenerative disorder
of the CNS secondary to pathology in the basal ganglia and
dopaminergic pathways. The cause is not known, but inherited
and environmental factors (especially previous head injury and
exposure to pesticides) play a role. There is no cure, but the
symptoms can be controlled with levodopa (L-DOPA), MAO-B
inhibitors or dopamine agonists. Clinical examination requires
sensitivity to both the physical disability and mental state of the
individual.

Clinical symptoms and signs

оАБ Early signs include: tremor (usually of asymmetrical onset),
rigidity, slowness of movement (bradykinesia) and altered
sense of smell.
оАБ Postural instability, sleep disturbance (REM sleep behaviour disorder) and neuropsychiatric problems may occur in
time.
оАБ Dementia is a late manifestation in up to 80% of aоАЬected individuals and is inоАЫuenced by age and duration of disease.
оАБ MRI shows absence of the swallow tail pattern in the substantia nigra (Fig. 19.87).

Ocular features

оАБ Visual abnormalities like impaired colour vision and reduced
contrast sensitivity are amongst the most frequent non-motor
symptoms in the early stages.
оАБ Convergence insuоАЫciency, leading to binocular horizontal
diplopia at near and diоАЪculty when reading.
оАБ Jerkiness and limitation of vertical eye movements.
оАБ Eye movement abnormalities. Saccadic and smooth pursuit
abnormality, including slow reaction time of horizontal gaze
and hypometria (вАШunder-reaching of taskвАЩ).
оАБ Decreased blink frequency, associated with the characteristic
вАШstareвАЩ and resulting in ocular surface dryness (see Fig. 2.67D).
оАБ Apraxia of eyelid closure and blepharospasm. Visual and
auditory hallucinations of a complex nature may occur in the
late stages in up to 40% of patients.

Skew deviation

оАБ Clinical signs. Skew deviation is an uncommon acquired ocular misalignment, in which the eyes are deviated vertically and
oоАЭen exhibit cyclotorsional disturbance. оАЯree categories have
been described, corresponding to diоАЬering motility patterns
and lesion sites. Both eyes can be deviated upwards, or one can
be hypertropic and one hypotropic.

Progressive supranuclear palsy

Progressive supranuclear palsy (SteeleвАУRichardsonвАУOlszewski
syndrome) is a severe degenerative disease presenting in old age.
Clinical features include:

оАЭEоАЮ use of the OKN drum; оАЭFоАЮ diagram showing the mechanism of convergenceвАУretraction
nystagmus вАУ convergence occurs because the medial rectus muscle is stronger than the

824 Nystagmus

Fig. 19.86 High-resolution midline sagittal T2-weighted MR
image in a patient with a pinealoblastoma (arrow) obstructing
the aqueduct of Sylvius causing a dilated third ventricle.

оАБ Cause. оАЯe most frequent cause is an abnormality of the prenuclear vestibular input secondary to a brainstem or cerebellar
stroke, which results in dysfunction of a primordial оАЮeld stabilization mechanism.
оАБ DiоАЭerential diagnosis. Fourth nerve palsy and myasthenia
gravis can occasionally present in a similar fashion.
оАБ оАЮe upright-supine test is helpful to conоАЮrm the diagnosis
of skew deviation: the patient оАЮxates on an object 1/3meter
away and the alignment is measured with the patient in an
upright position and then in a supine position. A positive test
is deоАЮned as a more than 50% decrease in vertical alignment
in the supine position, when compared to the upright position.
оАБ оАЮe prognosis depends on the cause.
оАБ Treatment. Prismatic correction in spectacles, botulinum
injections and, in persistent cases, vertical muscle surgery can
be considered.

NYSTAGMUS

Introduction
Physiological principles

Nystagmus is an involuntary oscillation of the eyes that can be a
physiological (e.g. following the rotation of an optokinetic drum)
or pathological phenomenon. In pathological nystagmus, each
cycle of movement is usually initiated by an involuntary, defoveating driоАЭ of the eye away from the target, followed by a reоАЮxating
saccadic movement.
оАБ Plane may be horizontal, vertical, or torsional.
оАБ Amplitude refers to the extent of excursion: оАЮne or coarse.
оАБ Frequency describes how rapidly the eyes oscillate: high, moderate or low.

ClassiоАЮcation

A schematic such as that shown in Fig. 19.88 can be used for
documentation.

Fig. 19.87 Axial MRI image of early Parkinson disease showing unilateral loss of the swallow tail sign in the substantia
nigra (circled). The arrows show the normal substantia nigra
on the opposite side.

оАБ Jerk nystagmus is saccadic with a slow defoveating вАШdriоАЭвАЩ movement and a fast corrective refoveating saccadic movement. оАЯe
direction of nystagmus is described in terms of the direction of
the fast component: right, leоАЭ, up, down or rotatory.
оАБ Pendular nystagmus is non-saccadic in that both foveating
and defoveating movements are slow (i.e. the velocity of nystagmus is equal in both directions).
оАБ Mixed nystagmus consists of pendular nystagmus in the primary position and jerk nystagmus on lateral gaze.

Physiological nystagmus

оАБ End-point nystagmus is a оАЮne jerk nystagmus of moderate frequency seen in extremes of gaze. оАЯe fast phase is in the direction of gaze (Fig. 19.89).
оАБ Optokinetic nystagmus (OKN) is a jerk nystagmus induced
by moving repetitive targets (e.g. OKN drum) across the visual
оАЮeld.

{ оАЯe slow phase is a pursuit movement in which the eyes
follow the target. оАЯe fast phase is a saccadic movement in
the opposite direction as the eyes оАЮxate on the next target.

{ If the OKN tape or drum is moved from right to leоАЭ, the
leоАЭ parieto-occipito-temporal region controls the slow
(pursuit) phase to the leоАЭ and the leоАЭ frontal lobe controls
the rapid (saccadic) phase to the right.

{ OKN nystagmus is useful for detecting functional (nonphysiological) blindness and for testing VA in the very
young. It can be helpful in the assessment of an isolated
homonymous hemianopia.

Vestibular nystagmus

оАБ Physiological vestibular nystagmus is a jerk nystagmus caused
by altered input from the vestibular nuclei to the horizontal
gaze centres. оАЯe slow phase is initiated by the vestibular nuclei

Fig. 19.89 Physiological nystagmus вАУ end-point. (Courtesy of JJ Kanski, Signs in Ophthalmology: Causes and DiоАЮerential Diagnosis, Mosby 2010.)

and the fast phase by the brainstem and frontomesencephalic
pathway. Vestibular nystagmus may be elicited by caloric stimulation as follows:

{ When cold water is poured into the right ear the patient
will develop leоАЭ jerk nystagmus (i.e. fast phase to the leоАЭ).

{ When warm water is poured into the right ear the patient
will develop right jerk nystagmus (i.e. fast phase to the
right). A useful mnemonic is вАШCOWSвАЩ (cold-opposite,
warm-same) indicating the direction of the nystagmus.

{ When cold water is poured into both ears simultaneously,
a jerk nystagmus with the fast phase upwards develops.
Warm water in both ears elicits nystagmus with the fast
phase downwards (cold вАШslows things downвАЩ).

{ An abnormal test indicates the presence of peripheral vestibular disease.

оАБ Pathological peripheral vestibular nystagmus (Fig. 19.90)
is caused by disease aоАЬecting the ear such as labyrinthitis,
M√©ni√®re disease and middle or inner ear infections. It tends to
be solely horizontal, vertical or torsional, increases in intensity
with gaze in the direction of the fast phase and is dampened by
оАЮxation. It is typically of оАЮne amplitude.

Infantile оАЭcongenitalоАЮ nystagmus

Introduction

Early-onset (usually the оАЮrst few months of life rather than truly
congenital) nystagmus can occur secondary to poor vision or
to a motor deоАЮcit with the nystagmus itself causing poor vision,
though the distinction is not always clear. It can be associated with

826 Nystagmus

a serious systemic condition, particularly of the central nervous
system. Infantile nystagmus is commonly pendular but may be
jerk, horizontal and uniplanar (direction of oscillation remains
constant regardless of direction of gaze). In contrast to adults with
acquired nystagmus, oscillopsia is not experienced even by adults
with congenital nystagmus. оАЯe nystagmus may be dampened by
convergence and is not present during sleep. оАЯere is usually a null
point вАУ a position of gaze in which nystagmus is minimal вАУ and a
compensatory head posture may develop to favour this. Investigation should seek to detect ocular and systemic associations.

Sensory deоАЮcit (aоАЭerent) nystagmus

Sensory deprivation nystagmus is the more common form of
infantile nystagmus and is caused by impairment of central
vision in early life (e.g. congenital cataract, macular hypoplasia,
albinism, Leber congenital amaurosis, optic nerve hypoplasia,

achromatopsia). In general, children with bilateral poor vision
under 2 years of age develop nystagmus, the severity of which is
associated with the degree of visual loss.

Congenital motor (eоАЭerent) nystagmus

A family history is common, with X-linked (dominant or recessive) inheritance the common mode. Presentation is about 2вАУ3
months aоАЭer birth and persists throughout life. VA is generally
better than with sensory deоАЮcit nystagmus, at 6/12вАУ6/36. In the
primary position there is low-amplitude pendular nystagmus that
may convert to jerk nystagmus on side gaze (Fig. 19.91).

Spasmus nutans

Presentation of this rare condition is between 3 and 18 months
of age with unilateral or bilateral small-amplitude high-frequency
horizontal nystagmus (Fig. 19.92) associated with head nodding.

Fig. 19.90 Peripheral vestibular nystagmus. (Courtesy of JJ Kanski, Signs in Ophthalmology:
Causes and DiоАЮerential Diagnosis, Mosby 2010.)

Fig. 19.91 Congenital motor (eоАЮerent) nystagmus, in this case pendular in the primary position
and on vertical gaze, but converting to a gaze-evoked jerk nystagmus on left and right gaze.
(Courtesy of JJ Kanski, Signs in Ophthalmology: Causes and DiоАЮerential Diagnosis, Mosby
2010.)

It is frequently asymmetrical, with increased amplitude in abduction. Vertical and torsional components may be present. An idiopathic form spontaneously resolves by age 3 years, but glioma of
the anterior visual pathway, empty sella syndrome and porencephalic cyst can also be causative.

Others

Other forms of nystagmus such as periodic alternating (see below)
can be infantile in presentation.

Acquired nystagmus

Latent nystagmus

Latent nystagmus is associated with infantile esotropia and dissociated vertical deviation (see Ch. 18). With both eyes open there
is no nystagmus, but horizontal nystagmus becomes apparent on
covering one eye; the fast phase is in the direction of the uncovered
оАЮxating eye. Occasionally an element of latency may be superimposed on a manifest nystagmus so that when one eye is covered
the amplitude of nystagmus increases (manifest-latent nystagmus).

Periodic alternating nystagmus

Periodic alternating nystagmus (PAN) is a conjugate horizontal
jerk nystagmus that periodically reverses direction. During the
active phase, the amplitude and frequency of nystagmus оАЮrst progressively increase then decrease. оАЯis is followed by an interlude
lasting 4вАУ20 s during which time the eyes are steady and may show
low-intensity, oоАЭen pendular movements. A similar sequence in
the opposite direction occurs thereaоАЭer, the whole cycle lasting
between 1 and 3 minutes. PAN can be congenital or due to cerebellar disease, ataxia telangiectasia and drugs such as phenytoin.

ConvergenceвАУretraction nystagmus

ConvergenceвАУretraction nystagmus is a jerk nystagmus due
to the co-contraction of extraocular muscles, often the medial

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 827
#### 19

recti. It can be induced by rotating an OKN drum downwards;
the upward refixation saccade brings the two eyes towards each
other in a convergence movement. There is classically associated retraction of the globe into the orbit. It is a component
of Parinaud dorsal midbrain syndrome (see Fig. 19.85). Causes
include lesions of the pretectal area such as pinealoma and vascular accidents.

Downbeat nystagmus

оАЯis is a vertical nystagmus with the fast phase beating downwards
(Fig. 19.93), more easily elicited in lateral gaze and downgaze. It
can be caused by lesions at the foramen magnum such as ArnoldвАУ
Chiari malformation and syringobulbia, drugs such as lithium
and phenytoin and a range of other conditions such as Wernicke
encephalopathy, demyelination and hydrocephalus.

Upbeat nystagmus

Upbeat nystagmus is a vertical nystagmus with the fast phase beating upwards in all positions (Fig. 19.94); causes include posterior
fossa lesions, drugs and Wernicke encephalopathy.

See-saw nystagmus

A pendular nystagmus, in which one eye elevates and intorts while
the other depresses and extorts. It can be due to parasellar tumours
(oоАЭen with bitemporal hemianopia), syringobulbia and brainstem
stroke.

Ataxic nystagmus

Ataxic nystagmus is a horizontal jerk nystagmus that occurs in the
abducting eye of a patient with an INO (see above).

Bruns nystagmus

оАЯis consists of a coarse cerebellar horizontal jerk nystagmus in
one eye and оАЮne high-frequency vestibular nystagmus in the other
and can be caused by cerebellopontine angle tumours such as vestibular schwannoma.

Fig. 19.92 Spasmus nutans вАУ the nystagmus in this patient is pendular, uniplanar and of equal
amplitude in all directions of gaze. (Courtesy of JJ Kanski, Signs in Ophthalmology: Causes
and DiоАЮerential Diagnosis, Mosby 2010.)

828 Nystagmus

Fig. 19.93 Downbeat nystagmus (see text). (Courtesy of JJ Kanski, Signs in Ophthalmology:
Causes and DiоАЮerential Diagnosis, Mosby 2010.)

Fig. 19.94 Upbeat nystagmus. (Courtesy of JJ Kanski, Signs in Ophthalmology: Causes and
DiоАЮerential Diagnosis, Mosby 2010.)

Treatment of nystagmus

оАБ Amblyopia and refractive error should be managed as appropriate. Contact lenses and other refractive measures such as
the combination of high minus contact lenses and high plus
spectacle lenses may be helpful.
оАБ Medication such as baclofen and gabapentin may be helpful.
оАБ Botulinum toxin injection into the extraocular muscles has
had some success but can be unpredictable and long-term
treatment is required.
оАБ Surgery for nystagmus with a null point is aimed at moving
muscles in order to mimic muscle tension while the eyes and
face are straight and may be performed to address a compensatory head posture. Recession of all horizontal recti has been
successful in reducing the amplitude of nystagmus in some
patients without a signiоАЮcant null point.

Nystagmoid movements

Nystagmoid movements resemble nystagmus, but the initial pathological defoveating movement is a saccadic intrusion.

Ocular оАЯutter and opsoclonus

оАЯese entities consist of saccadic oscillations with no intersaccadic
interval; in ocular оАЫutter oscillations are purely horizontal and in
opsoclonus they are multiplanar. Causes include viral encephalitis,
myoclonic encephalopathy in infants (вАШdancing eyes and dancing
feetвАЩ), as a transient idiopathic occurrence in healthy neonates, or
may be drug-induced.

Ocular bobbing

Ocular bobbing manifests with rapid downward conjugate eye
movements with a subsequent slow driоАЭ up to the primary

position. Causes include pontine lesions (usually haemorrhage), cerebellar lesions compressing the pons and metabolic
encephalopathy.

Superior oblique myokymia

Superior oblique myokymia is a rare condition characterized by
intermittent episodes of oscillopsia and diplopia in one eye (usually the right eye). оАЯe cause is not known, but the condition may
follow ocular or head injury and rarely brainstem tumours. Some
cases occur secondary to compression of the fourth nerve root
by the adjacent superior cerebellar artery, resulting in ephaptic
impulse transmission. оАЯere is no deоАЮnitive treatment, but oral
or topical beta-blockers can be helpful. In persistent cases, superior oblique muscle weakening procedures may be tried. It should
be distinguished from orbicularis oculi muscle myokymia (tic)
which is common, innocuous and usually settles spontaneously.

OCULAR MYOPATHIES

Myasthenia gravis
Introduction

Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an autoimmune disease in which antibodies mediate damage and destruction of acetylcholine receptors
in striated muscle. оАЯe resultant impairment of neuromuscular
conduction causes weakness and fatigability of skeletal musculature, but not of cardiac and involuntary muscles. оАЯe disease
aоАЬects females twice as commonly as males. MG may be ocular,
bulbar (aоАЬecting the cranial nerves arising from the lower brainstem) or generalized. Congenital and juvenile forms are rare. A
similar clinical picture is found in the LambertвАУEaton myasthenic
syndrome mediated by antibodies against pre-synaptic voltagegated calcium channels. оАЯis is a paraneoplastic phenomenon
associated with a lung tumour in 60%. Patients positive for antiMuSK (muscle-speciоАЮc kinase) antibody may have a distinct form
of MG. A range of drugs can exacerbate MG and should be avoided
if possible; those with ophthalmic relevance include many antibiotics and beta-blockers.

Systemic myasthenia

оАБ Symptoms are typically of onset in the third decade and
may include painless fatigue, often brought on by exercise,
commonly in conjunction with ptosis and diplopia. Fatigability affects the musculature of the limbs, facial expression,
ocular movements, chewing and speech. Bulbar symptoms
include dysphagia and dysarthria; difficulty with breathing
is rare.
оАБ Signs. оАЯe most important feature is peripheral weakness,
particularly of the arms and proximal leg muscles, with wasting in longstanding cases. оАЯere is characteristically a lack of
facial expression (Fig. 19.95). Ocular features are discussed
below.
оАБ Myasthenic crisis can be fatal due to respiratory distress if not
treated promptly.

Fig. 19.96 Asymmetrical right-sided ptosis with slight left lid

Ocular myasthenia

{ If one eyelid is elevated manually as the patient looks up,
the fellow eyelid may show оАЮne oscillatory movements.
оАБ Diplopia is frequently vertical, although any or all of the extraocular muscles may be aоАЬected. A pseudo-internuclear ophthalmoplegia may be seen. Patients with stable deviations may
beneоАЮt from muscle surgery, botulinum toxin injection or a
combination of both.

Ocular involvement occurs in 90% of cases and is the presenting feature in 60%. Two-thirds of patients have both ptosis and
diplopia.
оАБ Ptosis is insidious, bilateral and frequently asymmetrical (Fig.
19.96).

{ Typically, worse at the end of the day.

{ Worse on prolonged (60 second) upgaze due to fatigue.

{ Cogan twitch sign is a brief upshoot of the eyelid as the
eyes saccade from depression to the primary position.

830 Ocular Myopathies

оАБ Nystagmoid movements may be present on extremes of gaze.
Bizarre defects of ocular motility may also occur so that MG
should be considered in the diоАЬerential diagnosis of any ocular
motility disorder that does not оАЮt with a recognized pattern.

Investigation

оАБ Ice pack test (Fig. 19.97). оАЯis tests for an improvement aоАЭer
an ice pack is placed on the ptotic eyelid (or other aоАЬected
muscle) for 2 minutes, as cold inhibits the breakdown of acetylcholine by acetylcholinesterase. It is around 75% sensitive
but highly speciоАЮc.
оАБ Antibody testing supports a diagnosis of MG and predicts
the likelihood of thymoma. Testing is confounded by recent
(within 48 hours) general anaesthesia with muscle relaxants.

{ Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies. Present in
around 90% of systemic cases but only 50вАУ70% of patients
with ocular myasthenia. Rarely present in LambertвАУEaton
syndrome.

{ MuSK protein antibodies are positive in 50% of those negative for AChR antibodies; positive patients are less likely
to have ocular features and thymoma.

{ Striational antibodies. Antibodies against several contractile elements of skeletal muscle (e.g. titin) may be present.
оАЯey are found in 80вАУ90% of those with thymoma вАУ and
one-third of those without вАУ and can be a marker of more
severe MG.

{ Voltage-gated calcium channel antibodies are characteristic of LambertвАУEaton syndrome.
оАБ Edrophonium (Tensilon) test (Fig. 19.98). Edrophonium
is a short-acting anticholinesterase that confers a transient
improvement of weakness in MG. оАЯe estimated sensitivity is 85% in ocular and 95% in systemic MG. Bradycardia
is uncommon and in rare instances deaths have occurred.
Resuscitation facilities and appropriate expertise must be
readily available on site in case of emergency and its use may
be limited to cases in which less invasive tests have given
equivocal results.

{ Atropine 0.3 mg is given intravenously to minimize muscarinic side eоАЬects.

{ An intravenous test dose of 0.2 ml (2 mg) edrophonium
hydrochloride is given. If deоАЮnite symptomatic improvement (or adverse reaction) is noted, the test is terminated.

{ оАЯe remaining 0.8 ml (8 mg) is given aоАЭer 60 seconds if
necessary.

Neuro-ophthalmology 831
#### 19

оАБ оАЮyroid function testing should be performed as autoimmune thyroid disease can be associated. оАЯere is also an association with rheumatoid arthritis, pernicious anaemia and
systemic lupus erythematosus.

Treatment

An anticholinesterase agent such as pyridostigmine may be used
alone in mild disease, but is usually combined with steroids and
other immunosuppressive treatment (e.g. azathioprine). Plasmapheresis and intravenous immunoglobulins are short-term measures to address acute illness; emergency respiratory support is
rarely required. оАЯymectomy is performed if a thymoma is present
and leads to improvement in 75%.

Myotonic dystrophy

Introduction

Myotonic dystrophy is characterized by delayed muscular relaxation
aоАЭer cessation of voluntary eоАЬort (myotonia). оАЯere are two forms: the
classic form, dystrophia myotonica 1 (DM1), is caused by a mutation
in the dystrophia myotonica protein kinase gene DMPK. DM2 (proximal muscle myopathy, PROMM) involves the gene CNBP; DM2 has
fewer systemic features (although cataract is frequent) and a better
long-term prognosis, but is less common. Inheritance in both forms is
AD; sporadic cases are rare. DM1 is discussed below.

Diagnosis

{ Common. Most develop early-onset cataract, initially seen
as an iridescent dust (sometimes resembling the commonly confused Christmas tree morphology) and subsequently progressing to cortical and subcapsular spokes,
oоАЭen in a stellate conformation. Ptosis and hypermetropia
are also extremely common.

{ Uncommon. Motility dysfunction (strabismus, nystagmus,
abnormal saccades and smooth pursuit), lightвАУnear dissociation, iris vascular tuоАЭs, mild pigmentary retinopathy,
optic atrophy and hypotony.

Successive generations tend to exhibit progressively earlier onset
and greater severity of disease (вАШanticipationвАЩ) due to a progressive
increase in the size of the responsible genetic defect (a repeated
trinucleotide sequence) at fertilization.
оАБ Symptoms typically оАЮrst occur in the thirdвАУsixth decades with
weakness of the hands and diоАЪculty in walking.
оАБ Systemic features

{ Peripheral. DiоАЪculty in releasing grip, muscle wasting and
weakness.

{ Central. Mournful facial expression caused by bilateral
facial wasting with hollow cheeks (myotonic facial appearance вАУ Fig. 19.99) and slurred speech from involvement of
the tongue and pharyngeal muscles.

{ Other. May include frontal baldness in males, somnolence,
hypogonadism, endocrine abnormalities, cardiomyopathy,
pulmonary disease, intellectual deterioration and bone
changes.
оАБ Ophthalmic features

832 Ocular Myopathies

Fig. 19.99 Myotonic dystrophy. Typical facial appearance,
frontal baldness and left cataract.

оАБ Investigation. Genetic testing will conоАЮrm the defect and can
be performed prenatally.

Treatment

Genetic counselling, cardiac monitoring and symptomatic treatment such as cataract surgery, eyelid crutches and frontalis suspension. Patients should be warned of a substantially increased risk of
anaesthetic complications.

Chronic progressive external
ophthalmoplegia

Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) refers to a
group of disorders characterized by ptosis and slowly progressive
bilateral ocular immobility. оАЯe ocular features may occur in isolation
or in association with KearnsвАУSayre syndrome or oculopharyngeal
dystrophy.

Oculopharyngeal dystrophy

Oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy (OPMD) is caused by a
mutation in PABPN1. Like myotonic dystrophy, OPMD is a trinucleotide repeat disorder; inheritance is AD or AR. Clinical
onset is typically in early middle age, systemic features including

Isolated CPEO

оАБ Ptosis, usually the оАЮrst sign, is bilateral and may be asymmetrical. Surgical correction may improve a compensatory head
posture but does not restore normal lid movement and risks
corneal exposure.
оАБ Pupils are usually not involved.
оАБ External ophthalmoplegia (Fig. 19.100) begins in young
adulthood and is typically symmetrical. It is characterized
by a progressive course without remission or exacerbation.
Initially upgaze is involved and subsequently lateral gaze is
aоАЬected so that the eyes may become almost totally оАЮxed.
Because of this symmetrical loss of eye movement, diplopia
is rare although reading may be a problem due to inadequate
convergence.
оАБ Investigation. Electromyography shows myotonic and myopathic potentials; serum creatine kinase is elevated.
оАБ Treatment involves exercise and prevention of contractures. A minority of patients with diplopia may beneоАЮt from
surgery.

KearnsвАУSayre syndrome

KearnsвАУSayre is a rare myopathy associated with mitochondrial
DNA deletions. оАЯese patients have a cerebral folate deоАЮciency
despite a normal serum folate level, suggesting that there is a
failure of the mechanism responsible for movement of folate
across the choroid plexus. Presentation is in the оАЮrst and second
decades with an insidious progressive external ophthalmoplegia.
It is usually sporadic, though inheritance can occur. No proven
treatment is currently available.
оАБ Clinical features

{ оАЯe classic triad is chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia with ptosis (Fig. 19.101A), cardiac conduction
abnormalities and pigmentary retinopathy, the latter typically in a вАШsalt and pepperвАЩ appearance that is most striking
at the macula (Fig. 19.101B). Mild visual impairment and
nyctalopia may occur.

{ Fatigue, proximal muscle weakness, deafness, diabetes,
cerebellar ataxia, short stature, renal disease, endocrine
abnormalities and dementia may be present.
оАБ Investigations

{ Genetic testing.

{ Lumbar puncture: elevation of CSF protein.

{ Electrocardiography demonstrates cardiac conduction
defects and should be performed periodically; pacemaker
implantation may be required.

{ Histology of extraocular muscles shows вАШragged red
оАЮbresвАЩ due to intramuscular accumulation of abnormal
mitochondria.

{ Endocrine screening is important.

MILLER FISHER SYNDROME

Miller Fisher syndrome is a rare form of the acute polyneuropathy GuillainвАУBarr√© syndrome and because of its ocular features
may present to an eye-care professional. It typically aоАЬects the
extraocular muscles as the оАЮrst manifestation, with a classic clinical triad of ophthalmoplegia, gait and trunk ataxia, and areоАЫexia.
Anti-GQ1b antibodies are present in most cases.

NEUROFIBROMATOSIS

NeuroоАЬbromatosis type 1

NeuroоАЮbromatosis is a disorder that primarily aоАЬects cell growth
in neural tissues. оАЯe two main forms are neuroоАЮbromatosis type

1 (NF1) and type 2 (NF2). Both may show segmental involvement
in which the features are conоАЮned to one or more body segments.
NF1 (von Recklinghausen disease) is the most common phacomatosis, aоАЬecting 1:4000 individuals. Inheritance is AD with irregular
penetrance and variable expressivity, though about 50% have new
mutations. оАЯe NF1 gene (neuroоАЮbromin) is on chromosome 17
and is normally involved in cell growth regulation mechanisms.
Lack of neuroоАЮbromin promotes excessive cell growth, leading to
deregulation and tumour formation. оАЯe presence of optic nerve
glioma and Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas вАУ see Ch. 20) are
important ophthalmic diagnostic signs; genetic testing has around
95% speciоАЮcity вАУ a few positive individuals will not develop NF1.
оАЯe National Institute of Health have determined speciоАЮc diagnostic criteria.

Systemic features

оАБ NeuroоАЯbromas may develop anywhere along the course of
peripheral or autonomic nerves or on internal organs but
do not occur on purely motor nerves. оАЯey appear as either

834 NeuroоАЯbromatosis

A B

Fig. 19.101 KearnsвАУSayre syndrome. оАЭAоАЮ Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia with
ptosis; оАЭBоАЮ вАШsalt and pepperвАЩ pigmentary retinopathy.

solitary nodules (Fig. 19.102A) or more diоАЬuse plexiform
lesions, sometimes with associated soоАЭ tissue overgrowth
(elephantiasis nervosa) (Fig. 19.102B) and may also involve
internal organs.
оАБ Skin. Caf√©-au-lait macules are light-brown patches most commonly found on the trunk (Fig. 19.102C). оАЯey appear during
the оАЮrst year of life and increase in size and number throughout childhood.
оАБ Axillary or inguinal freckles usually become obvious around
the age of 10 years and are pathognomonic.
оАБ Skeletal abnormalities may include short stature and facial
hemiatrophy.
оАБ Intracranial tumours, primarily meningiomas and gliomas.
оАБ Associations include malignancy (especially malignant
peripheral nerve sheath tumours), gastrointestinal stromal tumours, hypertension and learning diоАЪculties. Recent
research suggests that autism spectrum disorder aоАЬects nearly
half of all NF1 patients.

Ophthalmic features

Regular eye examinations are critical from the time of diagnosis to
detect lesions such as optic nerve glioma.
оАБ Eyelid plexiform neuroоАЯbroma gives a characteristic
S-shaped deformity of the upper lid (Fig. 19.103A) and classically is texturally reminiscent of a вАШbag of wormsвАЩ.
оАБ Orbital

{ Optic nerve glioma (15вАУ40%), a pilocytic astrocytoma,
typically occurs in young children. It gives a fusiform
enlargement of the nerve (see Fig. 19.103E) and may
present with slowly increasing painless proptosis (see Fig.
4.39B), visual impairment (oоАЭen marked), optic atrophy
and strabismus. It can be bilateral and may extend posteriorly to involve the chiasm, optic tract and hypothalamus, sometimes with obstructive hydrocephalus. Slow
growth is typical.

{ Other orbital neural tumours, e.g. neurilemmoma
(schwannoma), plexiform neuroоАЮbroma and meningioma.

{ Spheno-orbital encephalocoele is caused by absence of the
greater wing of the sphenoid bone (see Fig. 19.103F), characteristically causing a pulsating proptosis.
оАБ Iris lesions

NeuroоАЬbromatosis type 2

NeuroоАЮbromatosis type 2 (NF2) is less common than NF1. Mutations in the NF2 gene on chromosome 22 are causative. оАЯis gene
encodes a protein (Merlin) that remodels cells and regulates cellular growth. Inheritance is AD, but 50% are sporadic. Various diagnostic criteria have been described, but oоАЭen include vestibular

{ Bilateral Lisch nodules (at least 95%) are hamartomas that
develop during the secondвАУthird decades, seen as tiny
nodular pigmented lesions protruding above the iris surface (Fig. 19.103B).

{ Congenital ectropion uveae (Fig. 19.103C) is uncommon
and it may be associated with glaucoma.

{ Mammillations (see Fig. 9.18D) are rare.
оАБ Prominent corneal nerves.
оАБ Glaucoma is not a common association. When present, it is
usually unilateral and congenital and about 50% have ipsilateral neuroоАЮbroma of the upper eyelid and facial hemiatrophy.
оАБ Fundus

{ Choroidal naevi may occur. NF1 patients with naevi are at
increased risk of developing choroidal melanoma.

{ Retinal вАШcorkscrewвАЩ vessels are found in about a third of
patients and do not leak.

{ Choroidal hyper-reоАЫective nodules are multiple small, оАЫat,
pigmented lesions, which are commonly seen on OCT
analysis (Fig. 19.103D). оАЯese are found in over 80% of
patients and are highly sensitive and speciоАЮc for NF1.

{ Other lesions that may be more common than in unaоАЬected
individuals include congenital hypertrophy of the RPE,
myelinated nerve оАЮbres, combined hamartoma of the retina
and RPE (possibly increased only in NF2) and retinal astrocytic hamartoma identical to those seen tuberous sclerosis.

Ophthalmic features

оАБ Cataract aоАЬects about two-thirds of patients. оАЯe opacities
develop prior to the age of 30 years and may be posterior subcapsular or capsular, cortical or mixed.
оАБ Fundus. Epiretinal membrane is frequent and combined hamartoma of the retina and retinal pigment epithelium is relatively common.
оАБ Ocular motor defects (10%).
оАБ Less common. Optic nerve sheath meningioma, optic nerve
glioma, unilateral Lisch nodules, abnormal electroretinogram.

HEADACHE

Introduction

Patients with headache are commonly seen in ophthalmic practice. Any person who presents with a new, severe, unexpected
headache, papilloedema, a new neurological defect or features of
giant cell arteritis needs urgent investigation. Primary headache
has been classiоАЮed using the International Headache Society ClassiоАЮcation Criteria (ICHD-3).

ClassiоАЬcation

оАБ Migraine without aura
оАБ Migraine with aura
оАБ Tension-type headache
оАБ Cluster headache
оАБ Medication overuse headache

Migraine

Introduction

Migraine is characterized by recurrent headaches widely variable
in intensity, duration and frequency. оАЯe prevalence of migraine is
higher in women (18%) than in men (6%). Migrainous headache is
commonly unilateral, associated with nausea and vomiting and may
be preceded by, or associated with, neurological and mood disturbances. However, all these characteristics are not necessarily present
during each attack or in every patient. Migrainous aura can occur
without headache and is a common presenting symptom to ophthalmologists. A family history of migraine is frequently obtained.

Migraine without aura

Migraine without aura (common migraine) is characterized by
headache with autonomic nervous system dysfunction such as
pallor and nausea, but without the stereotypical neurological or
ophthalmic features of classical migraine (see below), such that
it oоАЭen goes undiagnosed. Premonitory features are oоАЭen vague
and may include changes in mood and poor concentration. оАЯe

836 Headache

A B

C D

Neuro-ophthalmology 837
#### 19

severe, with considerable variation between attacks even in the
same individual.
оАБ Other pathology can rarely mimic a classical migrainous
presentation.
оАБ Consider this diagnosis when there are three of the following,
on at least two occasions:

{ One or more fully reversible aura symptoms indicating
focal cerebral cortical and/or brainstem dysfunction: (a)
visual symptoms like zigzag lines and/or scotoma, (b) sensory symptoms like pins and needles, (c) speech and/or
language symptoms like aphasia, (d) motor weakness, (e)
brainstem symptoms such as vertigo.

{ At least one aura symptom develops gradually over at least
5 minutes, or two or more symptoms occur in succession.

{ At least one aura symptom is unilateral.

Fig. 19.104 Axial gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted MR image through the posterior fossa showing bilateral vestibular
schwannomas.

{ No single aura symptom lasts longer than 60 minutes.

headache is unilateral pounding or throbbing, starting anywhere
but usually spreading to involve one-half or the whole head.
Retro-orbital pain may be mistaken for ocular or sinus disease.
During the attack, which lasts from 4 hours to 3 days, the patient
is frequently photophobic and phonophobic and may seek a
quiet, dark environment.
Consider the diagnosis if there are at least оАЮve attacks fulоАЮlling
the following criteria:
оАБ Headache lasting 4вАУ72 hours.
оАБ оАЯe headache has at least two of the following:

{ Unilateral location

{ Pulsating quality

{ Moderate or severe pain intensity

{ Aggravation by physical activity
оАБ During the headache at least one of the following: nausea/
vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia.

Migraine with aura (classical migraine)

оАБ Migraine with aura is more readily characterized.
оАБ An attack is heralded by a binocular visual aura that aоАЬects the
central visual оАЮeld on one side and typically lasts 5вАУ30 minutes.
Initially a binocular negative scotoma is commonly present,
but may go unrecognized or be perceived as a vague visual disturbance. Associated positive phenomena develop aоАЭer a few
minutes and may consist of scintillating scotomas (zig-zags or
fortiоАЮcation spectra), вАШheat hazeвАЩ distortions, or less commonly
other features such as tunnel vision, progressing slowly across
the оАЮeld over several minutes. Full visual recovery within 30
minutes is typical.
оАБ Other forms of aura are less common: unilateral altered or
abnormal sensation (paraesthesia), weakness or disturbance of
speech (dysphasia).
оАБ Headache follows the aura and is usually hemi-cranial (on the
side opposite the hemianopia) and accompanied by nausea
and photophobia. It may, however, be absent, trivial or very

{ Headache follows the aura within 60 minutes, but may
begin before or during the aura.
оАБ It is also important that clinical assessment does not suggest an
underlying disorder or that investigation has ruled this out; for
instance, very rarely a migrainous visual оАЮeld defect or other
aural feature may be permanent, when migraine should be a
diagnosis of exclusion.
оАБ Occipital epilepsy is very rare; the patient typically sees
coloured circles during an attack.

Other forms of migraine

оАБ Chronic migraine is deоАЮned as a headache that occurs on 15
or more days each month for more than 3 months in a patient
who has at least оАЮve attacks of migraine without aura, or occurs
on 8 or more days each month for more than 3 months, where
the headache is relieved by triptan or ergot medication. оАЯere
should be no secondary cause for the headache or evidence of
medication overuse. Approximately 3% of patients with episodic migraine will progress to chronic migraine in the following year. However, up to 70% may revert from chronic to
episodic migraine with eоАЬective treatment.
оАБ Retinal migraine manifests with visual disturbance that may
be similar to classical migraine but aоАЬects only one eye. It is
a controversial entity, some authorities believing that most
cases should be regarded as presumed recurrent ocular vasospasm rather than as true migraine. Young women are most
commonly aоАЬected, but it is a rare condition (it is common
for binocular hemiоАЮeld symptoms to be misinterpreted by
a patient as monocular). оАЯere is oоАЭen a personal history of
migraine. It may be prudent to investigate as for retinal embolization (see Ch. 13) and peripheral vasospasm (e.g. Raynaud
phenomenon), with appropriate onward referral if necessary.
Permanent monocular visual loss is common with recurrent
episodes, probably due to vasospastic infarction and avoidance
of potential precipitants and prophylactic treatment should be
considered.
оАБ Ophthalmoplegic migraine is rare and typically starts before
the age of 10 years. It is characterized by a recurrent headache
followed aоАЭer a variable period by transient cranial (oоАЭen

838 Neuralgias

third) nerve palsy. It is now believed to be due to demyelination in most cases.
оАБ Familial hemiplegic migraine is characterized by a failure of
full recovery of focal neurological features aоАЭer an attack of
migraine subsides.
оАБ Basilar migraine occurs in young females and is characterized
by a typical migrainous aura associated with a variety of symptoms related to vertebrobasilar arterial insuоАЪciency.

Treatment

оАБ Lasts between 30 minutes and 7 days.
оАБ At least two of the following:

{ Bilateral location.

{ Pressing or tightening quality (e.g., a tight band around the
forehead), of mild or moderate intensity.

{ Not aggravated by routine physical activity.

оАБ General measures include the elimination of conditions and
agents that may precipitate an attack of migraine, such as coffee, chocolate, alcohol, cheese, oral contraceptives, stress, lack
of sleep and long intervals without food. Improved hydration
is associated with better migraine control.
оАБ Treatment of an acute attack may be with simple analgesics
and, if appropriate, an anti-emetic such as metoclopramide.
Other drugs, usually reserved for patients who are refractory
to analgesics, include sumatriptan and ergotamine tartrate.
Occasionally intravenous agents are used
оАБ Prophylaxis should be considered for patients who experience
at least six headache days per month or at least three headache
days with severe impairment.

{ Most migraine prophylaxis drugs require patients to be
treated for a minimum of eight weeks before response to
treatment can be assessed. Treatment should be discontinued
and a new option considered if the patient has an adverse side
eоАЬect or if the patient does not have an adequate response.

{ Common reasons for non-response include (a) insuоАЪcient
drug dosing, (b) inadequate duration of treatment and
(c) untreated comorbidities that are known to exacerbate
migraine.

{ оАЯe main migraine preventative drugs are antidepressants
(amitriptyline), angiotensin-receptor blockers (candesartan), angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (lisinopril), antiepileptic drugs (valproate and topiramate) and
beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol).

{ Start with a low dose and increase depending on results
and continue for 2вАУ3 months before changing.

{ Systemically administered monoclonal antibodies to calcitonin gene-related peptide or its receptors (erenumab,
fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab) are a novel
class of preventative treatment aimed at reducing the frequency of episodic migraine, chronic migraine or cluster
headaches. оАЯese drugs should not be used in pregnancy.

{ оАЯe use of botulinum toxin (BT-A) to reduce muscle
spasms has been shown to be eоАЬective prophylactic treatment in some patients with chronic migraine.

TensionоАЯtype headache

оАЯis is an extremely common form of headache. оАЯe pain radiates
from the back of the head, the neck and periorbital region and can
cause pain behind the eyes. It typically aоАЬects both sides of the
body. Consider the diagnosis if there are at least 10 episodes fulоАЮlling the following criteria:

{ оАЯere must be no nausea or vomiting and no more than
one episode of photophobia or phonophobia.
оАБ Pain medication, such as aspirin and ibuprofen, is oоАЭen eоАЬective treatment and tricyclic antidepressants are useful for
prevention.
оАБ Cognitive-behavioral therapy and physiotherapy can be helpful in some patients.

оАЯe following conditions should be considered in the diоАЬerential
diagnosis of ocular or periocular pain, particularly in the absence
of detectable physical signs.
оАБ Cluster headache typically aоАЬects young to middle-aged men.
It is of particular interest to ophthalmologists because it is
associated with ocular features and may initially be misdiagnosed as a local ocular problem. оАЯe condition is characterized
by a stereotyped headache accompanied by various autonomic
phenomena occurring almost every day for a period of some
weeks.

{ оАЯe headache is unilateral, oculotemporal, excruciating,
sharp and deep.

{ It begins relatively abruptly, lasts between 10 minutes and
2 hours and then clears quickly.

{ оАЯe patient cannot keep still and is agitated, in contrast to
a patient with migraine who would rather lie quietly in a
dark room.

{ It may occur several times in a 24-hour period, oоАЭen at
consistent times, not infrequently in the early hours of the
morning.

{ Once the вАШclusterвАЩ is over, there may be a long headachefree interval of several years.

Medication overuse headache

оАБ Headache occurring on at least 15 days per month associated
with a pre-existing headache disorder.
оАБ Consider this diagnosis in a patient when:

{ Medication has been used regularly for more than 3 months.

{ One or more drugs are being used for acute and/or symptomatic treatment of headache such as ergotamine, triptans, simple analgesics, or opioids.
оАБ Patients should be counselled and warned of the risks of medication overuse.
оАБ Triptans and non-steroidal anti-inоАЫammatory drugs can be
stopped abruptly; patients should aim to stop the oоАЬending
drug for at least 1 month.
оАБ Abrupt withdrawal of medication may precipitate a withdrawal headache, which lasts on average 3.5 days, but can last
up to 10 days.

NEURALGIAS

{ Associated autonomic phenomena may include lacrimation, conjunctival injection and rhinorrhoea.

{ оАЯere may be a transient or permanent postganglionic
Horner syndrome.

{ Acute treatment: use subcutaneous and/or nasal sumatriptan and commence inhaled oxygen (оАЫow rate: over
10l/min).

{ Preventive treatment usually does not eliminate attacks,
but reduces the frequency and intensity of symptoms.
Verapamil 120mg taken three times daily reduces the
headache burden by 50% aоАЭer 1вАУ2 weeks of treatment.
оАЯere is a role for topiramate, baclofen and galcanezumab in episodic cluster headache. Clinical trials have
started with LSD (lysergic acid diethylamide) and psilocybin, based on anecdotal reports of long-term reduction
of symptoms using sub-hallucinogenic doses for short
periods (e.g. three doses).
оАБ SUNCT (short-lasting, unilateral, neuralgiform headache
attacks with conjunctival injection and tearing) syndrome.
оАЯis consists of attacks of unilateral orbital, supraorbital or
temporal stabbing or pulsating pain lasting from 5 to 240 seconds, occurring at a frequency of 3вАУ200 per day, accompanied
by marked ipsilateral conjunctival injection and lacrimation.
By deоАЮnition at least 20 episodes should have occurred for
diagnosis. Triggers such as touching the face are common.
An underlying disorder is occasionally present (secondary
SUNCT).
оАБ Paroxysmal hemicrania (Sjaastad syndrome). оАЯis consists of
severe unilateral headache that is typically ocular, frontal and/
or temporal and occurs in brief recurrent episodes (usually
оАЮve or more per day) associated with one or more ipsilateral
autonomic phenomena (tearing, injection, nasal congestion or
watering, lid swelling or ptosis). It is more common in women
and is much less common than cluster headache. оАЯe pain
responds well to indomethacin, in contrast to SUNCT, which
also features less severe pain, a male preponderance and more
marked autonomic features.
оАБ Trigeminal neuralgia is characterized by brief attacks of
severe pain that start in the distribution of one of the divisions of the trigeminal (оАЮоАЭh) nerve. оАЯe pain is paroxysmal
and sharp, usually occurring in multiple bursts in rapid succession lasting a few seconds. Attacks can be triggered either
by cutaneous stimulation such as shaving or by motor activity such as chewing. Facial sensation is normal. Treatment
involves antiepileptic drugs such as carbamazepine, phenytoin
and sodium valproate and occasionally surgical decompression of the trigeminal nerve. A major diоАЬerence from cluster
headache, SUNCT syndrome and paroxysmal hemicrania is
that autonomic features are sparse or absent.
оАБ Primary (idiopathic) stabbing headache (comprising ophthalmodynia periodica and ice pick syndrome) is characterized by short, sharp, jabbing ocular pains, typically located
around the orbit or temple, oоАЭen described as similar to the
sensation of being stabbed by a nail or needle and lasting from
less than a second up to a few seconds. оАЯe pains may occur
as isolated episodes or in brief repeated оАЫurries. Frequency

CHAPTER

Neuro-ophthalmology 839
#### 19

varies from a very occasional episode up to dozens per day. By
deоАЮnition there are no other accompanying symptoms and no
causative disorder is identiоАЮable. оАЯere is usually no trigger. It
is more common in women, and children can be aоАЬected.
оАБ Raeder paratrigeminal syndrome typically aоАЬects middleaged men. It is characterized by severe unilateral headache
with periocular pain in the distribution of the оАЮrst division
of the trigeminal nerve associated with an ipsilateral Horner
syndrome. оАЯe pain may last from hours to weeks before it
resolves spontaneously. Carotid dissection (blood entering the
potential space between the inner and outer layers of the artery

  - see Fig. 19.38) must be excluded with urgency.
оАБ Herpes zoster ophthalmicus frequently presents with pain
2вАУ3 days before the onset of the characteristic vesicular rash.
Zoster sine herpete denotes shingles without the development
of a rash.
оАБ Occipital neuralgia is characterized by attacks of pain that
begin in the occipital region but may spread to the eye, temple
and face.

TIP Immediate investigation is needed when the headache is
new, severe and unexpected, particularly in an older person.

FACIAL SPASM

Benign essential blepharospasm
Introduction

Essential blepharospasm is an uncommon but distressing idiopathic disorder that oоАЭen presents in the sixth decade and aоАЬects
women more commonly than men. It is characterized by progressive bilateral involuntary spasm of the orbicularis oculi and upper
facial muscles (Fig. 19.105). In severe cases blepharospasm may
temporarily render the patient functionally blind. Common precipitants include stress and bright light (photophobia is a common
accompanying symptom), with alleviation by relaxation and talking. It does not occur during sleep. In combination with oromandibular dystonia it comprises Meige (Fig. 19.106A) or Brueghel
syndromes. оАЯere is occasionally an underlying identiоАЮable cause,
particularly basal ganglia disease, but in a clinically typical case
investigation is not required.

Treatment

Prior to commencing treatment, it is important to exclude reоАЫex
blepharospasm, most commonly due to ocular surface disease and
extrapyramidal conditions such as Parkinson disease. A cranial
and peripheral nervous system examination should be performed.
оАБ Tinted spectacles frequently provide amelioration.
оАБ Treating ocular surface disease may be helpful.
оАБ Medical treatment with a great variety of drugs has been
reported to ameliorate speciоАЮc types of blepharospasm, but
their eоАЪcacy is disappointing.
оАБ Botulinum toxin injection (e.g. 2.5вАУ5 units injected subcutaneously at 3вАУ4 periocular sites) affords relief in most
(95%) patients by temporary paralysis of the injected

840 Disorders of Circadian Rhythm

muscles (Fig. 19.106B); typically, repeat injections are
required every 3 months. Common but temporary adverse
effects include ptosis, lagophthalmos, dry eye and occasionally diplopia.
оАБ Surgery. Myectomy is now rarely performed but can be considered in patients intolerant or unresponsive to botulinum toxin.

Hemifacial spasm

Hemifacial spasm is a unilateral condition that presents in the
оАЮоАЭhвАУsixth decades of life. It is characterized initially by brief
spasm of the orbicularis oculi that later spreads along the distribution of the facial nerve (Fig. 19.107). оАЯe condition is commonly
idiopathic, but may stem from irritation of the seventh cranial
nerve at any point in its course. Facial hyperkinesia may also occur
several months or years aоАЭer Bell palsy. Neuroimaging should be
performed to exclude a compressive aetiology. Treatment is similar
to that of essential blepharospasm.

Fig. 19.105 Essential blepharospasm.

DISORDERS OF CIRCADIAN RHYTHM

Introduction

A circadian rhythm is a biological process that persists in constant conditions (like darkness), is reset by exposure to light, and
oscillates over a period of about 24 hours. Disorders of circadian
rhythm can result in daytime sleepiness, poor sleep quality at night
and reduced alertness when awake. Jet-lag is a consequence of disrupted circadian rhythm.

Anatomy

оАЯe receptors responsible for circadian rhythm are a subset of
ganglion cells in the retina that contain melanopsin. Melanopsin
photoreceptors respond to blue light (peak absorption around
480 nm). оАЯe resulting stimulus passes via the retinohypothalamic tract in the optic nerve to the suprachiasmic nucleus (SCN)
located in the hypothalamus, then to the pineal gland, leading to

Fig. 19.107 Hemifacial spasm.

A B

Fig. 19.106 Meige syndrome. оАЭAоАЮ Blepharospasm and oromandibular dystonia; оАЭBоАЮ after
botulinum treatment. (Courtesy of ADN Murray.)

the production of melatonin. оАЯis hormone peaks at night and
drops during the day.
Ocular disease causing abnormal circadian
rhythm

оАБ Bilateral ocular blindness.
оАБ Dense cataract.
оАБ Advanced glaucoma.
оАБ Retinitis pigmentosa.

Treatment

оАЯe normal circadian rhythm can be reproduced with oral melatonin. PhacoemulsiоАЮcation with intraocular lens implantation can
improve sleep quality at night and reduce daytime sleepiness in
the elderly.

Neuro-ophthalmology 841
#### 19

NEURO-OPHTHALMOLOGY OF SPACE
FLIGHT

Long-duration space оАЫight can lead to changes in the eye and optic
nerve. оАЯis is known as space оАЫight-associated neuro-ocular syndrome (SANS). Approximately two-thirds of astronauts with оАЫights
lasting more than 6 months experience a decrease in distance and
near VA, which can remain unresolved for years. Optic disc oedema,
globe оАЫattening, choroidal folds and retinal cotton-wools spots have
been reported. оАЯe precise cause of this syndrome is unknown, but
presumably these changes occur as a consequence of orbital and cranial оАЫuid shiоАЭ brought about by prolonged exposure to microgravity.
Mechanical changes in the optic nerve induced by a chronic upward
shiоАЭ in the brain position may also play a role.
