Cavernous haemangioma

Introduction

Cavernous haemangioma of the retina and optic nerve head is a
rare unilateral congenital hamartoma. It is usually sporadic but
occasionally can be inherited as autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance in combination with lesions of the skin and CNS.
Mutations in several diоАЫerent genes have been implicated. Histopathology shows multiple thin-walled dilated channels with surface gliosis.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms may occur secondary to vitreous haemorrhage.
More frequently the lesions are detected by chance.
оАБ Signs

оАБ Clinical features

{ Retinal haemangioblastoma is one of the оАЭrst manifestations of the disease with an age of onset of about 25 years,
although about 5% can present under the age of 10.

{ CNS haemangioblastoma involving the cerebellum (Fig.
20.49), spinal cord, medulla or pons aоАЫects about 25% of
patients with retinal tumours.

{ Clusters of saccular aneurysms resembling a вАШbunch of grapesвАЩ
(Fig. 20.50AвАУC), with associated greyish оАЭbrous tissue.

{ Because of sluggish оАЬow of blood, the red cells may sediment and separate from plasma, giving rise to вАШmenisciвАЩ or
оАЬuid levels within the lesion.

{ A lesion occasionally involves the optic nerve head.

{ Phaeochromocytoma.

{ Renal carcinoma and pancreatic islet cell carcinoma.

{ Cysts of testes, kidneys, ovaries, lungs, liver and pancreas.

{ Polycythaemia, which may be the` result of factors released
by a cerebellar or renal tumour.

{ Endolymphatic sac tumours develop in the inner ear in
10%, with consequent hearing and balance diоАЪculties.
оАБ Screening is vital as it is impossible to predict which patients
with retinal haemangiomas will harbour systemic lesions. Relatives should also be screened because of the dominant inheritance pattern of the disease. оАЯe following screening protocol
should be regularly performed in patients with established
VHL and relatives at risk.

{ Annual physical examination, retinal examination from
age 2 to 5 years (6-monthly from ages 10 to 30 years),
renal ultrasonography from age 15 years, 24-hour urine
collection for estimation of vanillylmandelic acid and
catecholamine levels from age 2 to 5 years to detect
phaeochromocytoma.

{ Audiometry should be performed if there are any hearing
or balance problems.

{ Two-yearly abdominal and brain MRI scans from the age
of 15. If CNS lesions are symptom-free, treatment may not
be required.

{ It is thought safe to discontinue screening at around age 60
years if no abnormality has been identiоАЭed.

{ Genetic testing is indicated in all patients with suspected
VHL and in оАЭrst- and second-degree relatives. With

{ Haemorrhage and epiretinal membrane formation can
occur.
оАБ OCT demonstrates the aneurysmal vessels (Fig. 20.50D).
оАБ FA highlights the sedimentation of erythrocytes and shows
delayed оАЭlling in the venous phase and lack of leakage.

Treatment

Rarely, vitrectomy may be necessary for non-absorbing vitreous
haemorrhage, but photocoagulation should be avoided as it may
precipitate haemorrhage and enlargement of the tumour.

Congenital retinal arteriovenous
communication (racemose
haemangioma)

A congenital arteriovenous communication, the more severe
forms of which are oоАЮen termed racemose haemangioma, is a rare
sporadic congenital malformation, with unilateral involvement in
single or multiple sites of the same eye, most commonly temporally. Occasionally reported complications include haemorrhage,
exudation and vascular occlusion, though the vision is commonly
unaоАЫected and the condition discovered at a routine examination.
A visual оАЭeld defect may be present. Some patients may harbour
ipsilateral brain, facial bone and skin lesions (Wyburn-Mason syndrome), particularly those with more severe retinal changes.
оАБ Group 1 consists of an anastomosis with the interposition of
an abnormal capillary or arteriolar plexus. A congenital retinal

888 Primary Intraocular Lymphoma

A B

C D

Fig. 20.50 Cavernous haemangioma. (A) Small peripheral lesion; (B) lesion adjacent to optic
nerve; (C) larger lesion showing оАЯbrosis; (D) OCT of lesion in (C) demonstrating aneurysms.

macrovessel is oоАЮen present (Fig. 20.51A). оАЯis is an aberrant blood vessel, oоАЮen larger than normal and usually a vein,
located in the posterior pole; it may cross the fovea and horizontal raphe. Areas of capillary non-perfusion and foveal cysts
may be seen. оАЯe anomaly is non-progressive and is usually
associated with good vision.
оАБ Group 2. Direct arteriovenous communication, without an
intervening capillary bed. оАЯe intervening channels may be
intermediate or large in size (Fig. 20.51B).
оАБ Group 3. Many large-calibre tortuous anastomosing blood vessels that are oоАЮen more numerous than normal, with venules
and arterioles having a similar appearance. оАЯe changes extend
from and are most evident in the region of the optic disc. With
time the dilatation and tortuosity become more marked and
sclerosis may be seen (Fig. 20.51C) but no leakage is seen on
FA (Fig. 20.51D).

Vasoproliferative tumour

Retinal vasoproliferative tumour is a rare gliovascular lesion
that can be primary (80%) or secondary to conditions such as

intermediate uveitis, ocular trauma and retinitis pigmentosa. Secondary lesions may be multiple and occasionally bilateral depending on the underlying aetiology. Histology shows glial cells and a
network of оАЭne capillaries with some larger dilated vessels. Presentation is usually in the third to оАЭоАЮh decades, with blurring of vision
due to macular exudation. A reddish-yellow globular gliovascular
mass (Fig. 20.52A) is seen, most frequently in the inferotemporal
periphery; retinal vessels may be seen entering the lesion posteriorly. Although the tumour is benign it can cause profound visual
loss. Complications include subretinal exudation (Fig. 20.52B),
exudative retinal detachment, macular oedema and оАЭbrosis and
haemorrhage. Treatment with cryotherapy or brachytherapy
induces regression of the tumour and exudation, but the visual
prognosis is poor if there is maculopathy.

890 Primary Intraocular Lymphoma

lymphadenopathy, constitutional symptoms and, occasionally,
CNS involvement. оАЯe main classiоАЭcation and ocular manifestations are as follows:
оАБ Hodgkin disease may cause anterior uveitis, vitritis and multifocal fundus lesions resembling chorioretinitis.
оАБ Non-Hodgkin lymphoma can manifest with conjunctival
involvement, orbital involvement, Mikulicz syndrome and
uveal inоАЭltration.
оАБ CNS B-cell lymphoma may be associated with intermediate
uveitis and sub-RPE inоАЭltrates.
оАБ Primary vitreoretinal lymphoma (PVRL) represents a subset
of primary central nervous system lymphoma (PCNSL), a variant of extranodal non-Hodgkin lymphoma. оАЯe PCNSL cells
are large, pleomorphic B lymphocytes with large multilobular
nuclei, prominent nucleoli and scanty cytoplasm. оАЯe tumour
arises from within the brain, spinal cord and leptomeninges,
is aggressive and has a poor prognosis. About 20% of patients
with PCNSL have ocular manifestations, which can precede
or follow neurological involvement. Most patients with PVRL

{ Occasionally coalescence of sub-RPE deposits may completely encircle the fundus.

{ Other features include retinal vasculitis, vascular occlusion, exudative retinal detachment and optic atrophy.

subsequently develop CNS symptoms, with a mean delay of 29
months. Most of those aоАЫected are older adults.
оАБ Primary uveal lymphoma is a rare entity, usually of B-cell origin, that tends to follow an extended relatively benign course
and will not be considered further in this section.

Ocular features

оАБ Symptoms. Unilateral оАЬoaters, blurred vision, red eye or
photophobia. оАЯe symptoms frequently become bilateral aоАЮer
a variable interval.
оАБ Signs of PVRL

{ Mild anterior uveitis with cells, оАЬare and keratic
precipitates.

{ Vitritis may impede visualization of the fundus.

{ Large, oоАЮen multifocal, subretinal inоАЭltrates (Fig. 20.53).

{ Absence of cystoid macular oedema (CMO) is an important diagnostic indicator, since this is almost always present in true inоАЬammatory vitritis.

Neurological features

оАБ Symptoms. An intracranial mass may cause headache, nausea,
personality change, focal deоАЭcit or seizures. Leptomeningeal
disease may cause neuropathy and spinal cord involvement
may cause bilateral motor and sensory deоАЭcits.
оАБ Clinical neurological examination may demonstrate abnormalities such as cranial nerve palsies, hemiparesis and ataxia.

Investigation

оАБ OCT will conоАЭrm an absence of CMO in most cases, aiding
diоАЫerentiation from vitritis and may demonstrate subretinal
inоАЭltrative lesions.
оАБ FA shows blockage with a granular characteristic, due to the
presence of sub-RPE accumulation of lymphomatous cells
(вАШleopard skin spotsвАЩ).
оАБ Ultrasound may show vitreous debris, elevated subretinal
lesions, retinal detachment and thickening of the optic nerve.
оАБ Cytology of vitreous samples or subretinal nodules.
оАБ Immunohistochemistry based on cell-surface markers allows
identiоАЭcation of the lymphocytic proliferation, which is of a
B-cell type in most patients.
оАБ MRI of head and spine with gadolinium contrast may detect
one or more intracranial tumours, diоАЫuse meningeal or periventricular lesions and/or localized intradural spinal masses.
оАБ Lumbar puncture may demonstrate malignant cells in the
CSF in a minority of patients with abnormal MR imaging, but
a positive result avoids the need for brain or eye biopsy.

Treatment

Treatment of both the eye and the brain is oоАЮen indicated. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy are the mainstays, but an optimal
algorithm has not been established. Treatment limited to the eye
may not improve survival, but local treatment may be preferable
if there is monocular disease and no evidence of involvement
elsewhere.
оАБ Radiotherapy has long been the оАЭrst-line treatment for PVRL,
but recurrence is common and complications such as radiation
retinopathy and cataract can occur. It now tends to be reserved
for some cases of bilateral disease but may be associated with
lower recurrence rates than chemotherapy alone. It is better
tolerated by younger patients.
оАБ Intravitreal methotrexate is useful for recurrent disease, but
close monitoring is needed to detect ocular complications and
any recurrence.
оАБ Systemic chemotherapy with a variety of agents such as methotrexate can prolong survival in patients with CNS disease.
оАЯis can be given in combination with whole brain irradiation but neurotoxicity is a problem. A variety of methods have
been developed to overcome the bloodвАУbrain barrier. Systemic
treatment is usually eоАЫective for ocular disease and this is
preferred to ocular radiotherapy in some centres because in
addition to avoiding radiation-induced complications it may

CHAPTER

Ocular Tumours 891
#### 20

improve survival. Monotherapy for primary intraocular lymphoma (PIOL) with ifosfamide or trofosfamide has also been
successful.
оАБ Biologic agents involving speciоАЭc anti-B cell monoclonal
antibodies (e.g. rituximab), may represent a useful alternative,
but are generally given intravitreally because of poor penetration through the bloodвАУbrain barrier.

TUMOURS OF THE RETINAL PIGMENT
EPITHELIUM

Congenital hypertrophy of the RPE
Introduction

Congenital hypertrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium
(CHRPE) is the term used to encompass three entities with distinct features and implications: solitary CHRPE, grouped CHRPE
and atypical CHRPE.

Solitary (unifocal) CHRPE

оАБ A оАЬat or minimally elevated dark-grey or black (Fig. 20.54A),
round, oval or larger scalloped-edged lesion with well-deоАЭned
margins, usually located near the equator or in the peripheral
fundus.
оАБ A depigmented halo located just inside the margin (Fig.
20.54B) and depigmented lacunae (Fig. 20.54C) are common,
particularly as a lesion matures. Some lesions may become virtually totally depigmented.
оАБ Median diameter is 4вАУ5 mm but lesions can be much smaller
or very large, especially in the far periphery (Fig. 20.54D)
and may cause diagnostic diоАЪculty unless the possibility of
CHRPE is borne in mind.
оАБ A posterior pole location is uncommon (2%).
оАБ Histopathology shows densely packed RPE cells replete with
large melanosomes вАУ a combination of cellular hyperplasia and
hypertrophy.
оАБ Although formerly regarded as following a consistently stable
course, a slight enlargement of the involved area over time is
very common and the development of a presumed adenomatous or adenocarcinomatous nodule arising within the lesion
(see below) occurs in around 2%. Long-term review is therefore now advised.
оАБ Solitary CHRPE does not have an association with increased
gastrointestinal malignancy risk.

Grouped (multifocal) CHRPE

оАБ Multiple lesions, much smaller than those of solitary CHRPE
and without haloes or lacunae, orientated in a pattern simulating animal footprints (вАШbear tracksвАЩ вАУ Fig. 20.55A), oоАЮen conоАЭned to one sector or quadrant of the fundus with the smaller
spots located more centrally.
оАБ оАЯere are typically several groups.
оАБ Rarely the lesions may be depigmented (вАШpolar bear tracksвАЩ вАУ
Fig. 20.55B).
оАБ Only one eye is involved in almost all cases.

оАБ оАЯe characteristic clinical appearance and monocular distribution serve to distinguish typical grouped CHRPE from the
atypical multifocal variant associated with an increased risk of

Atypical congenital hypertrophy of the RPE

оАБ Multiple oval, spindle, comma- or оАЭshtail-shaped lesions of
very variable size associated with irregularly hypopigmented
margins and perilesional areas (Fig. 20.56).
оАБ Both eyes are involved.
оАБ оАЯe lesions have a haphazard, not sectoral, distribution and
may be pigmented, depigmented or heterogeneous.
оАБ To emphasize the distinction with the CHRPE variants that
are not associated with gastrointestinal malignancy, the alternative term вАШretinal pigment epithelial hamartomas associated
with familial adenomatous polyposisвАЩ (RPEH-FAP) has been
proposed.
оАБ Systemic associations

{ Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is an autosomal
dominant condition characterized by adenomatous polyps
throughout the rectum and colon, which usually start to
develop in adolescence. It results from a germline mutation
in the gene APC; mutations falling within a certain codon
range in this gene are associated with the presence of related
CHRPE. Patients undergo regular colonoscopy and polyp
excision. If untreated, virtually all patients with FAP develop
carcinoma of the colorectal region by the age of 50 years. As
a result of the dominant inheritance pattern, genetic testing
and intensive survey of at-risk family members is imperative. Between 70% and 80% of patients with FAP have atypical CHRPE lesions, which are present at birth.

{ Gardner syndrome is characterized by FAP, osteomas of the
skull, mandible and long bones, and cutaneous soоАЮ tissue
tumours such as epidermoid cysts, lipomas and оАЭbromas.

{ Turcot syndrome is an autosomal dominant or autosomal
recessive condition characterized by FAP and tumours of
the CNS, particularly medulloblastoma and glioma.

Combined hamartoma of the retina and
RPE

Introduction

Combined hamartoma of the retina and RPE is a rare lesion that
is probably congenital. It may be more common in males. It typically occurs sporadically in normal individuals but sometimes in
association with a systemic disorder, particularly neuroоАЭbromatosis type 2, which should be considered particularly in children
or if bilateral lesions are present. Histopathology shows thickening of the RPE and sensory retina with prominent glial and
vascular tissue.

Diagnosis

оАБ Symptoms. оАЯe most common presentation is with decreased
vision or strabismus in early childhood, though symptom
onset in late childhood or early adulthood is also frequent.
оАБ VA is 6/60 or worse in 40вАУ50%, usually secondary to macular
involvement.
оАБ Fundus

{ Slightly elevated deep grey or brown вАУ sometimes orange,
yellow or green вАУ pigmented lesion that blends into the
adjacent RPE at its margins (Fig. 20.57A).

{ SuperоАЭcial whitish epiretinal membrane (ERM) formation
with retinal wrinkling. Occasionally vitreoretinal interface
changes are marked, sometimes with focal tractional retinal detachment.

{ Tortuosity and prominence of overlying retinal vessels,
with evidence of feeder and draining vessels.

{ оАЯe lesion is usually located at the posterior pole, oоАЮen
in a juxtapapillary or macular site; peripheral lesions
are uncommon. Dragging of the disc or macula may be
present.

{ Uncommon оАЭndings include secondary macular oedema,
exudation (Fig. 20.57B), choroidal and preretinal neovascularization, retinoschisis and retinal detachment.

Fig. 20.57 Combined hamartoma of the retina and retinal pigment epithelium. (A) Lesion surrounding the optic disc; (B) larger peripapillary lesion with оАЯbrosis and peripheral hard exudates; (C) peripapillary lesion; (D) FA of lesion in (C); (E) OCT showing full-thickness retinal

оАБ FA shows early hyperоАЬuorescence of the vascular abnormalities and blockage by pigment; the late phase may show leakage
(Fig. 20.57C and D).
оАБ OCT demonstrates localized full-thickness retinal and RPE
disorganization (Fig. 20.57E). ERM formation has variously

Treatment

оАБ Monitoring for complications.
оАБ Amblyopia therapy is important in young children.
оАБ SpeciоАЬc treatment for macular neovascularization (MNV)
and leakage where indicated.
оАБ Vitrectomy for ERM has provided beneоАЭt in some cases.

Congenital simple hamartoma of the
RPE

Congenital simple hamartoma of the RPE is a rare entity, usually incidentally diagnosed in asymptomatic children and young
adults. It is typically a small (1.5 mm or less) jet-black nodular
lesion, with well-deоАЭned margins, that appears to involve the full
thickness of the retina and RPE and to protrude into the vitreous
cavity (Fig. 20.58A). A feeding artery and draining vein are generally seen. оАЯe lesion is typically located immediately adjacent to
the foveola. VA is usually normal, but is occasionally impaired as a
result of central foveal involvement or traction.

Adenoma and adenocarcinoma of the
RPE

Adenoma of the RPE is an oval pigmented lesion that most
commonly arises in the peripheral fundus, sometimes from
an area of solitary CHRPE. Associated vitreous inоАЬammatory cells and retinal exudation are frequently present. Associated prominent feeding and draining vessels may develop. оАЯe

CHAPTER

Ocular Tumours 895
#### 20

behaviour of an adenocarcinoma is thought to be similar to that
of an adenoma. Most lesions are simply observed in the absence
of vision-threatening complications, as metastasis has not been
reported. Fine-needle biopsy is sometimes required for diagnostic
clariоАЭcation.

Hyperplasia and migration of the RPE
simulating uveal melanoma

Rare cases have been reported of massively proliferating RPE cells
simulating a uveal melanoma, including by extraocular extension.
True malignant tumours of the RPE are extraordinarily rare.

PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES

Paraneoplastic retinopathies are rare diseases that might be missed
or misdiagnosed by the unwary observer. Many of the patients
present with visual symptoms before the primary malignancy is
diagnosed. It is therefore important for clinicians to be familiar
with these syndromes in order to detect the underlying malignancy as early as possible.

Bilateral diоАЮuse uveal melanocytic
proliferation

Bilateral diоАЫuse uveal melanocytic proliferation (BDUMP) is a very
rare paraneoplastic syndrome occurring usually in patients with
systemic, oоАЮen occult, malignancy. It is characterized by the proliferation of benign melanocytes in the outer choroid oоАЮen manifesting with multiple naevus-like choroidal lesions (Fig. 20.58B), but
a variety of other anterior and posterior ocular segment features
have been reported, including red-grey subretinal patches, rapidonset cataract, episcleral and conjunctival lesions. Vision can be
severely diminished. оАЯe mechanism is uncertain, but a circulating
factor may be responsible in many cases. Detection of an occult

A B

Fig. 20.58 Other pigmented lesions. (A) Congenital simple hamartoma of the retinal pigment
epithelium; (B) naevus-like lesions in diоАЮuse uveal melanocytic proliferation. (Courtesy of
A Leys.)

896 Paraneoplastic Syndromes

primary malignancy might enable early treatment to enhance
survival. Treatment of BDUMP itself is generally unrewarding вАУ
improvement with plasmapheresis has been reported вАУ although
successful treatment of the underlying primary tumour may be
followed by regression of the BDUMP, but without improvement
in vision.

Cancer-associated retinopathy

Cancer-associated retinopathy (CAR) is most frequently associated with small cell bronchial carcinoma. Visual symptoms precede
the diagnosis of malignancy in half the cases, typically by several
months but sometimes several years. Subacute visual loss occurs
over weeks to months, associated with photopsia. Colour impairment, glare, photosensitivity and central scotoma are attributed to
cone dysfunction. Night blindness, impaired dark adaptation, ring
scotoma and peripheral оАЭeld loss occur due to rod dysfunction.
оАЯe fundus oоАЮen appears normal on presentation, but attenuated
arterioles, optic disc pallor and mild RPE changes develop as the

disease progresses. оАЯe electroretinogram (ERG) is abnormal at an
early stage under both photopic and scotopic conditions. As with
BDUMP, CAR should prompt a thorough search for an underlying
malignancy, though this is not always found and the condition is
then regarded as an autoimmune retinopathy.

Melanoma-associated retinopathy

оАЯe presentation of melanoma-associated retinopathy (MAR)
diоАЫers from CAR in that the visual symptoms usually arise aоАЮer,
rather than prior to, the diagnosis of cutaneous melanoma. оАЯere
may be concurrent vitiligo. Autoantibodies from MAR sera react
against bipolar cells in human retina. Clinical and electrophysiological data also implicate bipolar cells as the target in MAR.
Generally, symptoms and signs are less marked than in CAR. оАЯe
ERG shows marked reduction of dark-adapted and light-adapted
b-wave and preservation of a-wave (normal photoreceptor function), characteristic of bipolar cell dysfunction. оАЯe prognosis for
vision is usually good.

EYELIDS

оАБ Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are
used for the treatment of many solid tumours. оАЯere is a dosedependent risk of side eоАЮects. Trichomegaly is a common оАЭnding, while blepharitis and dry eye syndrome are less common.
Cicatricial ectropion is rare.
оАБ Imatinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor and is used for the
treatment of chronic myeloid leukaemia and gastrointestinal
stromal tumours. Up to two-thirds of patients develop periorbital oedema, usually 5вАУ8 weeks aоАЬer starting treatment.
оАЯis is a mild problem and does not require discontinuation of
medication. Ptosis has also been reported.
оАБ Dasatinib is used for acute lymphoblastic leukemia and rarely
can cause eyelash poliosis, which is reversible on stopping the
medication.
оАБ Ipilimumab is used for the treatment of metastatic malignant melanoma. Symptoms and signs similar to thyroid eye
disease have been reported. оАЯis usually responds to systemic
steroids.
оАБ Bortezomib is a proteasome inhibitor which is used to treat
multiple myeloma and predisposes to recurrent chalazia.
оАБ Isotretinoids are used in the treatment of acne and can cause
blepharoconjunctivitis, xerosis, corneal opacities, night blindness and pseudotumour. оАЯese side eоАЮects are reversible on
stopping the medication.

CORNEA

Vortex keratopathy (cornea verticillata)
Clinical features

In approximately chronological order:
оАБ Fine golden-brown opacities form an irregular horizontal line
in the lower corneal epithelium of both eyes, similar to that of
the common age-related HudsonвАУSt√§hli iron line.
оАБ Several irregular branching horizontal lines form a pattern
resembling the whiskers of a cat.
оАБ With an increasing number of branches a whorled pattern
develops, centred on a point below the pupil and swirling outwards, usually sparing the limbus.
оАБ Associated pigmented clumps and iron deposition have been
described.

Causes

{ Amiodarone can also cause anterior subcapsular lens
deposits and optic neuropathy (see below).
оАБ Antimalarials

{ Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine are quinolones
used in the treatment of certain autoimmune connective
tissue diseases and in the prophylaxis and treatment of
malaria.

{ In contrast to chloroquine retinopathy (see below), keratopathy bears no relationship to dosage or duration of
treatment. оАЯe changes are usually reversible on cessation of therapy and sometimes clear despite continued
administration.
оАБ Others. Numerous other drugs have been reported as occasional causes of vortex keratopathy.
оАБ Fabry disease.

оАБ Amiodarone

{ Amiodarone is a cardiac antiarrhythmic agent.

{ Virtually all patients develop keratopathy (Fig. 21.1A),
oоАЬen soon aоАЬer commencement. In general, the higher the
dose and the longer the duration of use, the more substantial the corneal changes.

{ Vision is minimally impaired in around 5%, with loss of
a single line of Snellen acuity, mild blurring and haloes.
оАЯis is rarely suоАЫcient to recommend discontinuation of the drug. оАЯe keratopathy reverses (slowly) on
discontinuation.

Chlorpromazine

Chlorpromazine is used as a sedative and to treat psychotic mental illness. Some patients on long-term therapy may develop subtle
diоАЮuse yellowish-brown granular deposits in the corneal endothelium, Descemet membrane and deep stroma (Fig. 21.1B) within
the palpebral оАЭssure area. Anterior lens capsule deposits and retinopathy may also occur (see below).

Argyrosis

Argyrosis is a discoloration of ocular tissues secondary to silver
deposits and may be iatrogenic or from occupational exposure.
Keratopathy is characterized by greyish-brown granular deposits
in Descemet membrane (Fig. 21.1C). оАЯe conjunctiva may also be
aоАЮected.

Chrysiasis

Chrysotherapy is the therapeutic administration of gold, usually in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. Chrysiasis is the
deposition of gold in living tissue and typically occurs only
after prolonged administration. Virtually all patients who have
received a total dose of gold compound exceeding 1500 mg
develop corneal deposits, characterized by dust-like or glittering purple granules scattered throughout the epithelium and
stroma, concentrated in the deep layers and the periphery (Fig.
21.1D). The findings are innocuous and are not an indication
for cessation of therapy. In some cases, the deposits clear after
stopping treatment whilst in others they may persist. Other
toxic effects of gold are innocuous lens deposits and, occasionally, marginal keratitis.

Amantadine

Amantadine is an oral agent used in the treatment of Parkinson
disease and related conditions. Some patients develop diоАЮuse white
punctate opacities that may be associated with epithelial oedema,
1вАУ2 weeks aоАЬer commencement of the drug, which resolve with
discontinuation.

CILIARY EFFUSION

Topiramate

Topiramate is an anticonvulsant and is also used in the treatment
of migraine. It can cause acute angle closure with associated myopia secondary to ciliochoroidal eоАЮusion. A similar phenomenon
has been reported with other drugs, especially other sulfonamides
(including acetazolamide) and bupropion.
оАБ Presentation is usually within a month of starting treatment,
with blurred vision and sometimes haloes, ocular pain and
redness.
оАБ Signs include shallowing of the anterior chamber and raised
intraocular pressure.
оАБ Treatment consists of reducing the intraocular pressure and
stopping the drug.

оАБ Prognosis is usually good provided the complication is
recognized.

TIP Topiramate is used in the treatment of migraine and may
result in acute angle closure secondary to swelling of the ciliary
body.

LENS

Steroids

Systemic and topical steroids can lead to cataract formation. оАЯe
resultant opacities are initially posterior subcapsular (Fig. 21.2A),
with subsequent anterior subcapsular involvement. Individual vulnerability seems to vary; children may be more susceptible than
adults. оАЯe relationship between dose and duration and cataract
formation is unclear, though higher doses and length of treatment
are associated with greater risk. Early opacities may regress if steroids are discontinued, though sometimes progression may occur
despite withdrawal.

900 Uveitis

TIP Excessive use of topical steroids can result in a posterior
subcapsular cataract and/or secondary glaucoma.

Other drugs

оАБ Chlorpromazine may cause the deposition of innocuous, оАЭne,
stellate, yellowish-brown granules on the anterior lens capsule
within the pupillary area (Fig. 21.2B) in 50% of patients who
have received a cumulative dose of 1000 g. оАЯe deposits persist
despite discontinuation.
оАБ Gold causes innocuous anterior capsular deposits in about
50% of patients on treatment for longer than 3 years.
оАБ Allopurinol, used in the treatment of gout, increases the risk
of cataract formation in elderly patients with a high cumulative
dose or extended treatment duration.

UVEITIS

Rifabutin

Rifabutin is used mainly in the management and prophylaxis
of mycobacterial infections. It may cause acute anterior uveitis (AAU), typically associated with a hypopyon. оАЯe associated
vitritis may be mistaken for endophthalmitis. Concomitant use
of drugs such as clarithromycin and оАЪuconazole that inhibit the
metabolism of rifabutin will increase the risk of uveitis. Treatment
involves dose reduction or withdrawal of the drug.

Cidofovir

Cidofovir is used in the management of cytomegalovirus (CMV)
retinitis in acquired immunodeоАЭciency syndrome (AIDS). AAU
with few cells but marked оАЭbrinous exudate may develop following
repeated intravenous infusions. Vitritis is common and hypopyon
may occur with long-term administration. Treatment with topical

steroids and mydriatics is usually successful, obviating the need to
discontinue therapy.

Bisphosphonates

Bisphosphonates are a class of drugs that retard absorption of
bone, commonly in osteoporosis but also in several other conditions. оАЯey activate a sub-group of T cells and it is thought that
uveitis (usually anterior) and scleritis sometimes seen following
administration is related to this. InоАЪammation typically occurs
within 2 days of commencement of a bisphosphonate, or earlier
aоАЬer intravenous administration.

Sulfonamides

Uveitis has been reported associated with sulfonamide treatment,
but is rare. Sulfonamides can also precipitate ciliary eоАЮusion with
myopia and angle closure (see above) and are a well-documented
cause of StevensвАУJohnson syndrome.

Fluoroquinolones

Systemic administration of оАЪuoroquinolone antibiotics, particularly moxiоАЪoxacin, has been associated with acute extensive
anterior segment pigment dispersion (see also bilateral acute iris
transillumination (BAIT) and bilateral acute depigmentation of
the iris (BADI) in Ch. 11). It is not clear whether inоАЪammation is
the primary mechanism, which may be phototoxicity due to sensitization by the drug in predisposed individuals.

Tumour necrosis factor inhibitors

Although these drugs (e.g. etanercept, inоАЪiximab, adalimumab)
have been adopted for the treatment of ocular inоАЪammation, paradoxically uveitis has also been documented as an adverse eоАЮect.
оАЯe induction of sarcoidosis has also been reported.

Diagnosis

Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine
Introduction

Chloroquine was used to prevent and treat malaria in the past,
but is less oоАЬen prescribed in recent times because of resistance
to the medication. Hydroxychloroquine is used to treat long-term
inоАЪammatory disorders of the joints and skin. оАЯese medications
are concentrated in melanin-containing structures of the eye, such
as the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and choroid. Hydroxychloroquine is generally safe and cost eоАЮective but can cause permanent loss of vision because of retinal toxicity, particularly if
the recommended dose is exceeded. Retinal adverse eоАЮects may
be more common than previously believed and progression can
occur despite discontinuation of the drug. SigniоАЭcant damage can
occur without signs on fundus examination and optical coherence
tomography (OCT) scanning of the macula has therefore emerged
as a key part of screening. Renal impairment and the use of tamoxifen increases the risk of toxicity. Obesity may lead to miscalculation of the safe dose, as hydroxychloroquine is not stored in fat.
Vortex keratopathy may occur (see above).
оАБ Chloroquine can result in retinal toxicity. оАЯe risk of toxicity increases signiоАЭcantly when the cumulative dose exceeds
300 g. Individual susceptibility seems highly variable.
оАБ Hydroxychloroquine is much safer than chloroquine, particularly if the dose is kept to less than 5 mg/kg real weight/
day. оАЯe risk of developing retinal toxicity has been reported
to be 7.5% for an individual taking the medication for more
than 5 years. Between 20% and 50% of people taking hydroxychloroquine for more than 20 years will have some signs of
retinopathy. оАЯe risk increases with a cumulative dose over
1000 g, equating to a standard twice-daily dose of 200 mg for
around 7 years.

A B

оАБ Premaculopathy consists of early functional and structural
changes prior to visible fundus signs. оАЯe goal of screening is
to detect toxicity at this stage prior to irreversible damage.

{ High-resolution OCT imaging is relatively sensitive and
shows parafoveal outer retinal lining, loss of the cone outersegment tip-line and disruption of the inner-segment/
outer-segment line as early features. оАЯe inferotemporal macula is the earliest location of hydroxychloroquine
toxicity.

{ When available, multifocal electroretinography (see Ch.
15) can demonstrate early changes.

{ Subtle central visual оАЭeld defects (e.g. Humphrey 10-2,
Amsler grid) may be detected, but standard perimetry is
less sensitive than the imaging modalities above.

{ Mild colour vision defects may be present, but the commonly used Ishihara chart is of relatively low sensitivity for
this purpose.
оАБ Early maculopathy is characterized by a modest reduction of
visual acuity вАУ VA (6/9вАУ6/12) and subtle macular disturbance.
Fundus autoоАЪuorescence (FAF) and macular pigment density
assessment may be useful (Fig. 21.3A). With progressive loss of
the perifoveal outer retina, the preserved central foveal tissue
results in the вАШоАЪying saucerвАЩ sign on OCT scanning (Fig. 21.3B).
оАБ Progression of retinopathy through moderateвАУsevere reduction in VA (6/36вАУ6/60) is associated with corresponding
deterioration of the clinical appearance to give a вАШbullвАЩs eyeвАЩ
macular lesion, characterized by a foveolar island of pigment
surrounded by a depigmented zone of RPE atrophy, which
is itself encircled by a hyperpigmented ring (Fig. 21.4A). A
more substantial macular lesion follows, with widespread RPE
atrophy surrounding the fovea (Fig. 21.4B). Retinal arterioles
may become attenuated and pigment clumps can form in the
peripheral retina (Fig. 21.4C). Fluorescein angiography (FA)

junction in the fovea (arrow вАУ вАШоАЮying saucerвАЩ sign). (Courtesy of I Yusuf.)

902 Retina

typically shows hyperоАЪuorescence in a вАШbullвАЩs eyeвАЩ pattern. OCT
has superseded the use of this investigation.

Screening

оАБ Patient education: the importance of regular screening should
be emphasized.
оАБ Baseline assessment before or soon aоАЬer commencement of
treatment is advisable, including documentation of functional
visual parameters together with an OCT scan of the macula.
оАЯis serves as a basis for future comparison and also helps
to exclude pre-existing maculopathy as a potential relative
contraindication.
оАБ Annual review. In the absence of special risk factors, routine
annual review with ancillary testing (central visual оАЭelds, FAF
and SD-OCT) should begin 5 years aоАЬer starting treatment.
Review one year aоАЬer starting treatment: (a) in children, (b)
those on chloroquine treatment or (c) if there are supplementary risk factors such as age-related maculopathy, substantial
renal impairment, or tamoxifen use.
оАБ Discontinuation of the drug should be discussed with the
patientвАЩs rheumatologist if toxicity is suspected.

Phenothiazines

оАБ оАЯioridazine is used to treat schizophrenia and related psychoses. оАЯe normal daily dose is 150вАУ600 mg. Doses that
exceed 800 mg/day for just a few weeks may be suоАЫcient to
cause reduced VA and impairment of dark adaptation. Progressive retinal toxicity can occur.

{ вАШSalt and pepperвАЩ pigmentary disturbance involving the midperiphery and posterior pole is an early feature (Fig. 21.5A).

the RPE and choriocapillaris (Fig. 21.5B).

оАБ Digitalis (digoxin) is used as second-line treatment for atrial
оАЭbrillation and symptomatic heart failure. It has a narrow

grey lesions in the outer retina and RPE. Visual impairment is

A rare side eоАЮect is optic neuritis, reversible on cessation of
therapy.
оАБ Canthaxanthin is a carotenoid used, oоАЬen in quite high doses,
to simulate sun tanning. Over prolonged periods it may cause
the deposition of innocuous glistening yellow inner retinal
deposits in a doughnut conformation at the posterior poles
(Fig. 21.6B). оАЯe deposition is slowly reversible. Because of
this side eоАЮect the FDA has not approved the use of canthaxanthin вАШtanning pillsвАЩ in the USA.
оАБ MethoxyоАЮurane is an inhalant general anaesthetic. It is
metabolized to oxalic acid, which combines with calcium to
form an insoluble salt and is deposited in tissues including the
RPE. Prolonged administration may lead to renal failure and
secondary hyperoxalosis. Ocular involvement is characterized
by calcium oxalate crystals scattered throughout the retina
associated with mild visual impairment and subsequently by
RPE hyperplasia at the posterior pole (Fig. 21.6C).
оАБ Nitrofurantoin is an antibiotic used principally in the treatment of urinary tract infections. Long-term use may result in
slight visual impairment associated with superоАЭcial and deep
glistening intraretinal deposits distributed in a circinate pattern throughout the posterior pole.

Table 21.1

Other drugs causing retinopathy

оАБ Interferon alfa is used in a range of conditions, including
hepatitis C and numerous malignant tumours. Retinopathy occurs in some patients, particularly those on high-dose
therapy, and is characterized by cotton-wool spots and retinal
haemorrhages (Fig. 21.7A). FA shows areas of focal capillary
non-perfusion. Changes usually resolve spontaneously with
cessation of therapy and in the majority of patients the visual
prognosis is good. Less common ocular side eоАЮects include
cystoid macular oedema, extraocular muscle paresis, optic disc
oedema and retinal vein occlusion.
оАБ Desferrioxamine (also known as deferoxamine) is a chelating
agent used to manage chronic iron overload, generally to prevent haemosiderosis in patients with conditions requiring regular transfusion. It is most commonly administered via slow
subcutaneous infusion. Patients present with rapid visual loss.
Initially the fundi may be normal or show only mild macular
greying, but within several weeks mottled pigmentary changes
develop (Fig. 21.8A), associated with abnormal electrodiagnostic оАЭndings. FA shows widespread punctate hyperоАЪuorescence (Fig. 21.8B).
оАБ Nicotinic acid is a cholesterol-lowering agent. A minority of
patients develop retinopathy when doses greater than 1.5 g
daily are used, in the form of a cystoid maculopathy suggestive

оАБ Pentosan polysulfate is the only medication that can be
used in the treatment of interstitial cystitis. A characteristic progressive symmetrical maculopathy may occur with
long-term use (usually more than 7 years) and high cumulative exposure. Prolonged dark adaptation, nyctalopia and

secondary to changes in the macular region. There is no
treatment.
оАБ Alkyl nitrites (вАШpoppersвАЩ) are recreational drugs used before
sexual activity and can rarely cause a toxic maculopathy. оАЯe
patients are usually young and present with bilateral loss of
visual acuity and photopsia. Clinically a small yellow spot can
be seen in the fovea, which is easily seen using autoоАЪuorescence
imaging (Fig. 21.9A). OCT shows a selective defect in the foveal
inner-segment ellipsoid band (Fig. 21.9B). оАЯere is no treatment, but spontaneous recovery of visual function can occur.

OPTIC NERVE

Ethambutol

Ethambutol is used in the treatment of tuberculosis, generally in a
multidrug regimen. Its overall safety proоАЭle is favourable, but acute
or chronic optic neuritis can occur. Toxicity is dose- and durationdependent, with an incidence as high as 18% at a daily dose over 35

mg/kg daily. It is rare (<1%) with a standard daily dose of 15 mg/kg
daily. Toxicity typically occurs between 3 and 6 months of starting
treatment, though it has been reported aоАЬer only a few days. Renal
dysfunction may confer a higher risk of toxicity as ethambutol is
excreted via the kidneys.
оАБ Symptoms may be absent, but typically include painless bilateral blurring, usually central though sometimes paracentral or
peripheral. Impairment of colour vision may be noticed.

оАБ Signs include minimal to severe reduction in VA, normal or
slightly swollen optic discs with splinter-shaped haemorrhages
and normal or sluggish pupils. RedвАУgreen dyschromatopsia is the
most common objective abnormality of colour vision, but subtle
(undetectable on Ishihara testing) blueвАУyellow defects may be an
early оАЭnding. Loss of contrast sensitivity is an early sign.
оАБ Visual оАЭeld defects can be central or peripheral.
оАБ Prognosis is good following cessation of treatment, although
recovery can take time. A minority sustain permanent visual
impairment, with optic atrophy.
оАБ Screening. Baseline VA and Ishihara testing are prudent prior
to starting ethambutol and the patient should be advised of
the necessity of reporting any visual disturbance. Repeat testing should be performed every 6 months in patients on the
standard dose. Ethambutol should be stopped immediately if
toxicity develops, with consideration also given to discontinuation of isoniazid if it is being used synchronously (see below).

TIP To reduce the risk of toxic optic neuropathy, the dose of
ethambutol should not exceed 15 mg/kg daily.

Isoniazid

Isoniazid may very rarely cause toxic optic neuropathy. оАЯe risk is
higher when given in combination with ethambutol.

Amiodarone

Optic neuropathy, probably secondary to demyelination, aоАЮects
1вАУ2% of patients on long-term amiodarone treatment. It is almost
certainly not dose-related. Distinction from non-arteritic anterior
ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION), which also aоАЮects patients
with systemic vascular disease, may be diоАЫcult and it has been
suggested that NAION is more common in patients on amiodarone. DiоАЮerentiation is clinically important as it is key to a decision

906 Visual Cortex

about whether to discontinue the drug. оАЯe presence of a crowded
optic disc, speed of onset, bilateral involvement, duration of disc
swelling and features of systemic amiodarone toxicity may be helpful in this regard.
оАБ Presentation is with sudden or insidious unilateral or bilateral
visual impairment, aоАЬer a mean period of 6вАУ9 months taking
the drug. About one-third of patients are asymptomatic.
оАБ Signs in a majority are unilateral or bilateral optic disc swelling
that may persist for a few months aоАЬer medication is stopped.
Corneal features are discussed above.
оАБ Investigation

{ Cranial imaging may be indicated when bilateral disc
swelling is present.

{ Visual оАЭeld defects are of varied conоАЭguration and severity
and may be reversible or permanent.

{ Prognosis is variable; cessation of the drug usually
improves vision, but 20% may deteriorate further. Final
VA of worse than 6/60 occurs in around 20% of eyes.

{ Screening has been suggested by some authors, but
no consensus exists as to its beneоАЭt. Patients should be
warned of the risk and cautioned to report visual symptoms immediately.

Vigabatrin

Bilateral concentric, predominantly nasal visual оАЭeld constriction occurs in many patients taking the anti-epileptic drug
vigabatrin due to photoreceptor and ganglion cell damage. It is
uncommon at a cumulative total dose of <1 kg, but very common
at >3 kg. A maximum daily dose of 3 g is recommended in adults.
Vigabatrin should be avoided in patients with pre-existing оАЭeld
defects and should not be prescribed in conjunction with other
potentially retinotoxic drugs. оАЯe risk is higher in males than
females.
оАБ Presentation is usually months or years aоАЬer starting treatment with bilateral concentric or binasal visual оАЭeld defects.
Blurring and symptoms related to the defects may occur, but

there may be no symptoms, with normal VA unless scotomas
are at or close to оАЭxation.
оАБ Signs may be absent but optic atrophy may be present. Other
subtle signs can include peripheral atrophy, arteriolar narrowing, abnormal macular reоАЪexes and surface wrinkling.
OCT can detect peripapillary retinal nerve оАЭbre layer atrophy,
including prior to the development of оАЭeld defects.
оАБ Prognosis. Changes persist if treatment is stopped, but sometimes do not progress if continued.
оАБ Screening. A baseline visual assessment, including visual
оАЭelds and OCT, is recommended prior to starting treatment
and thereaоАЬer every 3 months. In young children and others
unable to perform оАЭeld or OCT testing, the optimal monitoring modality has yet to be determined.

Methotrexate

Methotrexate is a very rare cause of acute or gradual visual loss
due to optic neuropathy. Reduced VA, dyschromatopsia and visual
оАЭeld loss occur and could be linked to folic acid metabolism, levels
of which should be checked. Vitamin B12 and folate supplementation may be helpful.

SildenaоАЯl

Non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy is a rare side
eоАЮect. Cyanopsia is more common, but clears spontaneously.
AоАЮected individuals report intensely blue-coloured vision, diоАЫculty distinguishing redвАУgreen colours and photophobia.

VISUAL CORTEX

Bevacizumab

Bevacizumab is used for colon and rectal tumours and may rarely
result in corticular blindness, which is not fully reversible on stopping the medication.

EYELID TRAUMA

Periocular haematoma

A вАШblack eyeвАЩ, consisting of a haematoma (focal collection of blood)
and/or periocular ecchymosis (diоАЯuse bruising) and oedema (Fig.
22.1A), is the most common blunt injury to the eyelid or forehead
and is generally innocuous. It is, however, critical to exclude the
following more serious conditions:
оАБ Trauma to the globe or orbit. It is easier to examine the
globe before the lids become oedematous. Once swelling is
established, gentle sustained pressure to open the lids will
often displace tissue fluid sufficiently to allow visualization
of the anterior segment. It is critical not to apply any force
on the globe itself until its integrity has been confirmed.
Urgent imaging such as computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or bedside ultrasonography (taking meticulous care to avoid applying pressure on
the globe) should be considered if there is suspicion of an
underlying injury to the eyeball and adequate clinical visualization is not possible.
оАБ Orbital roof fracture, especially if the black eye is associated
with a subconjunctival haemorrhage without a visible posterior limit (which would indicate the anterior extension of a
posterior bleeding point) (Fig. 22.1B).
оАБ Basal skull fracture, which may give rise to characteristic
bilateral ring haematomas (вАШpanda eyesвАЩ вАУ Fig. 22.1C).

TIP Bilateral periocular haematoma (вАШpandaвАЩ eyes) can be a
sign of a skull-base fracture.

Laceration

The presence of a lid laceration, however insignificant, mandates careful exploration of the wound and examination of the
globe and adnexal structures. Any lid defect should be repaired
by direct closure whenever possible, even under tension, since
this affords the best functional and cosmetic result (Fig. 22.2A
and B).
оАБ SuperоАЯcial lacerations parallel to the lid margin without gaping can be sutured with 6-0 black silk or nylon. оАЮe sutures are
removed aоАЭer 5вАУ6 days.
оАБ Infection is always a risk, even from a small laceration (Fig. 22.2C).
оАБ Lid margin lacerations invariably gape without careful closure
and to prevent notching must be sutured with optimal alignment (see Fig. 2.57C):

{ A 5-0 silk vertical mattress suture is inserted in line with
the meibomian gland oriоАЬces, about 2 mm from the wound
edges and 2 mm deep and leоАЭ untied.

{ Tarsal plate edges are brought together with partialthickness lamellar 5-0 absorbable (e.g. polyglactin) sutures,
which are tied anteriorly.

{ оАЮe lid margin silk suture is then tied so that the cut edges
slightly pucker the wound. оАЮe ends are leоАЭ fairly long, e.g.
2 cm.

оАБ Lacerations with mild tissue loss just suоАЫcient to prevent direct primary closure can usually be managed by

оАБ Lacerations with extensive tissue loss may require major

оАБ Canalicular lacerations should be repaired within 24 hours
(Fig. 22.3A). оАЮe laceration is bridged by silicone tubing
(Crawford tube), which is threaded down the lacrimal system
and tied in the nose, following which the laceration is sutured.
Alternatively, repair of a single canaliculus can be performed
using a monocanalicular stent (e.g. Mini Monoka вАУ Fig. 22.3B)
and, if necessary, suturing its footplate to the lid using 8-0
material. оАЮe tubing is leоАЭ in situ for 3вАУ6 months.
оАБ Tetanus status. Ensure that the patientвАЩs tetanus immunization
status is satisfactory aоАЭer any injury. Without prior immunization, 250 units of human tetanus immunoglobulin are given
intramuscularly (IM). If previously immunized but a booster
has not been administered within the last 10 years, subcutaneous tetanus toxoid should be given.
оАБ Foreign body. If there is any suspicion of a foreign body in
the soоАЭ tissues of the lid or orbit (Fig. 22.4A and C), a CT scan
should be undertaken (Fig. 22.4B and D).

TIP There is always a risk of orbital cellulitis after a
penetrating lid laceration, particularly if there is a retained
foreign body.

ORBITAL TRAUMA

Orbital оАЯoor fracture
Introduction

A blow-out fracture of the orbital оАЪoor is typically caused by a sudden increase in the orbital pressure from an impacting object that
is greater in diameter than the orbital aperture (about 5 cm), such
as a оАЬst or tennis ball, so that the eyeball itself is displaced and
transmits rather than absorbs the impact (Fig. 22.5A). Since the
bones of the lateral wall and the roof are usually able to withstand
such trauma, the fracture most frequently involves the оАЪoor of the
orbit along the thin bone covering the infraorbital canal. Occasionally, the medial orbital wall may also be fractured. Fractures
of the orbital rim and adjacent facial bones require appropriately
tailored management. Clinical features vary with the severity of
trauma and the interval between injury and examination. Care
should be taken to ensure that a full evaluation for head and systemic injury has been performed and any necessary interspecialty
referrals initiated.

Diagnosis

оАБ Visual function, especially acuity, should be recorded and
monitored.
оАБ Periocular signs include variable ecchymosis, oedema (Fig.
22.5B) and occasionally subcutaneous emphysema (a crackling
sensation on palpation due to air in the subcutaneous tissues).
оАБ Infraorbital nerve anaesthesia involving the lower lid, cheek,
side of nose, upper lip, upper teeth and gums is common as the
fracture frequently involves the infraorbital canal.
оАБ Diplopia may be caused by one of the following mechanisms:

{ Haemorrhage and oedema in the orbit may cause tightening of the septa connecting the inferior rectus and inferior
oblique muscles to the periorbita, thus restricting movement of the globe. Ocular motility usually improves as the
haemorrhage and oedema resolve.

{ Diplopia typically occurs in both upgaze (Fig. 22.6A
and C) and downgaze secondary to mechanical entrapment within the fracture of the inferior rectus or inferior
oblique muscle or adjacent connective tissue and fat (Fig.
22.6D).

{ Forced duction and the diоАЯerential intraocular pressure
test (increasing IOP as a restricted muscle presses on the
globe) are positive. Diplopia may subsequently improve
if it is mainly due to entrapment of oedematous connective tissue and fat, but usually persists if there is signiоАЬcant
involvement of the muscles themselves.

{ Direct injury to an extraocular muscle, associated with
a negative forced duction test. оАЮe muscle оАЬbres usually
regenerate and normal function oоАЭen returns within about
2 months.
оАБ Enophthalmos (Fig. 22.6B) may be present if the fracture
is severe, although it tends to manifest only after a few days
as initial oedema resolves. Late enophthalmos is uncommon, even in large fractures. Six months after a large
orbital floor fracture only 20% will have enophthalmos of
more than 2 mm.
оАБ Ocular damage (e.g. hyphaema, angle recession, retinal dialysis) should be excluded by careful examination of the globe,
although this is relatively uncommon in association with a
blow-out fracture.
оАБ Hess chart testing (Fig. 22.7) to map eye movements is useful
in assessment and monitoring.
оАБ CT with coronal sections (Fig. 22.8 and see Fig. 22.6D) aids in
evaluation of the extent of a fracture and determination of the
nature of maxillary antral soоАЭ tissue densities, which may represent prolapsed orbital fat, extraocular muscles, haematoma
or unrelated antral polyps.

Treatment

оАБ Initial treatment generally consists of observation, with the
prescription of oral antibiotics. Ice packs and nasal decongestants may be helpful. оАЮe patient should be instructed not
to blow his or her nose, because of the possibility of forcing
infected sinus contents into the orbit. Systemic steroids are
occasionally required for severe orbital oedema, particularly if
this is compromising the optic nerve.

оАБ Subsequent treatment is aimed at the prevention of permanent vertical diplopia and/or cosmetically unacceptable
enophthalmos.

{ Small cracks without herniation do not require treatment
as the risk of permanent complications is small.

{ Fractures involving up to one-half of the orbital оАЪoor, with
little or no herniation, no signiоАЬcant enophthalmos and
improving diplopia, also do not require treatment.

{ Fractures involving more than one-half of the orbital оАЪoor
may develop enophthalmos if leоАЭ untreated.

{ Fractures with entrapment of orbital contents, enophthalmos of greater than 2 mm and/or persistent and signiоАЬcant
diplopia in the primary position should be repaired within
2 weeks. However, it has recently been reported that in
patients with enophthalmos of less than 2 mm there is no
diоАЯerence in outcome between those undergoing surgery
at 2 weeks and those undergoing surgery aоАЭer 6 months.
In patients with diplopia and tissue incarceration but without muscle entrapment, improvement oоАЭen occurs without surgery. If the diplopia persists, delayed surgery is still
likely to achieve resolution of diplopia except in extreme
upgaze.
оАБ вАШWhite-eyedвАЩ fracture is a subgroup for which urgent repair
is required to avoid permanent neuromuscular damage. оАЮe
scenario is generally seen in patients less than 18 years of age,
typically with little visible external soоАЭ tissue injury and usually aоАЯects the orbital оАЪoor (see Fig. 22.6C). It involves the
acute incarceration of herniated tissue in a вАШtrap-doorвАЩ eоАЯect
and occurs because of the greater elasticity of bone in younger
people (see Fig. 22.6D). Patients may experience acute nausea, vomiting, headache and persistent activation of the oculocardiac reоАЪex can occur. CT features may be subtle.
оАБ Surgical repair is performed via a transconjunctival or subciliary incision or via the maxillary sinus, with elevation of
the periosteum from the orbital оАЪoor, freeing of trapped
orbital contents and repair of the bony defect with a synthetic
implant.

Roof fracture

Introduction

Roof fractures are rarely encountered by ophthalmologists. Isolated fractures, caused by falling on a sharp object or sometimes a
relatively minor blow to the brow or forehead, are most common
in children and oоАЭen do not require treatment. Fractures due to
major trauma, with associated displacement of the orbital rim or
signiоАЬcant disturbance of other craniofacial bones, typically aоАЯect
adults.

Diagnosis

A haematoma of the upper eyelid is typical, together with periocular ecchymosis. оАЮese oоАЭen develop over the course of a few
hours and may progressively spread to the side opposite the fracture. Other features of orbital wall fracture as discussed above may
be present. Large fractures may be associated with pulsation of the
globe due to transmission of cerebrospinal оАЪuid (CSF) pressure,
best detected with applanation tonometry.

Treatment

Small fractures may not require treatment, but it is important to
exclude a CSF leak, which carries a risk of meningitis. Sizeable
bony defects with downward displacement of fragments usually
warrant reconstructive surgery. General management is similar to
that of an orbital оАЪoor fracture (see above).

Blow-out medial wall fracture

Medial wall orbital fractures are usually associated with оАЪoor fractures. It is uncommon to оАЬnd an isolated fracture of the medial
wall. Signs include periorbital ecchymosis and frequently subcutaneous emphysema, which typically develops on blowing the nose.
Defective ocular motility involving abduction and adduction is
present if the medial rectus muscle is entrapped. CT will demonstrate the fracture. Treatment involves release of incarcerated tissue and repair of the bony defect (Fig. 22.9).

Lateral wall fracture

Acute lateral wall fractures (see Fig. 22.11D) are rarely encountered by ophthalmologists. Because the lateral wall of the orbit is
more solid than the other walls, a fracture is usually associated
with extensive facial damage.

Orbital haemorrhage

Introduction

Orbital (retrobulbar) haemorrhage is important chieоАЪy due to the
associated risk of acute orbital compartment syndrome with compressive optic neuropathy and can lead to irreversible blindness of
the aоАЯected eye in severe cases. It can occur without or in association with an orbital bony injury. Iatrogenic orbital haemorrhage
is not uncommon, typically resulting from a peri- or retrobulbar

B

local anaesthetic block performed to facilitate intraocular surgery.
Rare causes include bleeding from vascular anomalies and occasionally spontaneous haemorrhage due to poor clotting.

Diagnosis

Proptosis, eyelid oedema and ecchymosis, haemorrhagic chemosis, ocular motility dysfunction, decreased visual acuity, elevated
intraocular pressure, optic disc swelling and a relative aоАЯerent
pupillary defect are among the possible signs.

Treatment

Treatment should be started immediately if progressive visual
deterioration occurs. Canthotomy alone is rarely adequate.
оАБ Canthotomy. AоАЭer clamping the incision site for 60 seconds,
scissors are used to make a 1вАУ2 cm horizontal full-thickness
incision under local anaesthesia (e.g. 1вАУ2 ml lidocaine 1вАУ2%
with adrenaline) at the angle of the lateral canthus (Fig.
22.10A).
оАБ Cantholysis. Following canthotomy, the lower lid is retracted
downwards and the inferior crus of the lateral canthal tendon
is cut (Fig. 22.10B) using blunt-tipped scissors, directed inferiorly and inserted adjacent and parallel to the lateral orbital
rim between conjunctiva and skin and angled away from the
eyeball. Blood is gently encouraged to drain. If necessary, the
superior limb of the tendon can also be cut, but this carries a
substantial risk of damage to adnexal structures.

TRAUMA TO THE GLOBE

Introduction
Terminology

оАБ Closed injury is commonly due to blunt trauma. оАЮe corneoscleral wall of the globe is intact.
оАБ Open injury involves a full-thickness wound of the corneoscleral envelope.

914 Trauma to the Globe

A B

Fig. 22.10 Surgical treatment of acute retrobulbar haemorrhage. (A) Lateral canthotomy
(arrow showing anterior orbital rim); (B) disinsertion of inferior crus of the lateral canthal tendon.

оАБ Contusion is a closed injury resulting from blunt trauma.
Damage may occur at or distant to the site of impact.
оАБ Rupture is a full-thickness wound caused by blunt trauma. оАЮe
globe gives way at its weakest point, which may not be at the
site of impact.
оАБ Laceration is a full-thickness defect in the eye wall produced
by a tearing injury, usually as the result of a direct impact.
оАБ Lamellar laceration is a partial-thickness laceration.
оАБ Incised injury is caused by a sharp object such as glass or a
knife.
оАБ Penetrating injury refers to a single full-thickness wound,
usually caused by a sharp object, without an exit wound. A
penetrating injury may be associated with intraocular retention of a foreign body.
оАБ Perforation consists of two full-thickness wounds, one entry
and one exit, usually caused by a missile.

Investigation

оАБ Plain radiographs may be taken when the presence of a foreign body is suspected (Fig. 22.11A). A metallic foreign body
which is within the eye will move up and down if the radiographs are taken in upgaze and downgaze.
оАБ Ultrasonography may be useful in the detection of intraocular foreign bodies, globe rupture, suprachoroidal haemorrhage
and retinal detachment. It should be performed as gently as
possible if there is a possibility of an open globe injury, taking
great care not to apply pressure on the globe.
оАБ CT is superior to plain radiography in the detection and localization of intraocular foreign bodies (Figs 21.11BвАУD). It is also
of value in determining the integrity of intracranial, facial and
intraocular structures (see Fig. 22.11C).
оАБ MRI is more accurate than CT in the detection and assessment
of injuries of the globe itself, such as an occult posterior rupture, though not for bony injury. However, MRI should not be
performed if a ferrous metallic foreign body is suspected.
оАБ Electrodiagnostic tests may be useful in assessing the integrity of the optic nerve and retina, particularly if some time has

passed since the original injury and there is the suspicion of a
retained intraocular foreign body (IOFB).

TIP MRI scanning should not be undertaken if a ferrous
metallic foreign body is suspected in a patient with a
penetrating injury.

Blunt trauma

оАЮe most common causes of blunt trauma are sporting injuries
and assault. Severe blunt trauma to the globe results in anteroposterior compression with simultaneous expansion in the equatorial plane (Fig. 22.12) oоАЭen associated with a transient but severe
increase in IOP. Although the impact is primarily absorbed by the
lensвАУiris diaphragm and the vitreous base, damage can also occur
to the posterior pole. оАЮe extent of ocular damage depends on the
severity of trauma. оАЮe prognosis varies, but is usually determined
by the extent of retinal injury.

Cornea

оАБ Corneal abrasion involves a breach of the epithelium (Fig.
22.13A) and stains with оАЪuorescein (Fig. 22.13B). It is a painful condition, exacerbated by ocular movement. If located
over the pupillary area, vision may be signiоАЬcantly impaired.
Details of treatment are discussed under вАШRecurrent corneal
epithelial erosionвАЩ in Chapter 7
оАБ Acute corneal oedema may develop following blunt trauma,
secondary to focal or diоАЯuse dysfunction of the endothelium
and is sometimes seen underlying a large abrasion. It is commonly associated with folds in Descemet membrane and stromal thickening, but usually clears spontaneously.
оАБ Tears in Descemet membrane are usually vertical (Fig.
22.13C) and most commonly arise as the result of birth trauma.

Hyphaema

Hyphaema (haemorrhage in the anterior chamber) is a common
complication of blunt ocular injury. оАЮe source of bleeding is

typically the iris root or ciliary body face. Characteristically, the
blood settles inferiorly with a resultant вАШоАЪuid levelвАЩ (Fig. 22.14A),
except when the hyphaema is total (Fig. 22.14B). Uncontrolled
high IOP can result in ischaemic optic neuropathy and staining of
the cornea (Fig. 22.14C) (see Ch. 11).

Anterior uvea

Fig. 22.12 Pathogenesis of ocular damage by blunt trauma.

оАБ Pupil. оАЮe iris may momentarily be compressed against the
anterior surface of the lens by severe anteroposterior force,
with resultant imprinting of pigment from the pupillary margin. Transient miosis accompanies the compression, evidenced
by the pattern of pigment corresponding to the size of the
constricted pupil (Vossius ring вАУ Fig. 22.15A). Damage to the
iris sphincter may result in traumatic mydriasis, which can be
temporary or permanent. оАЮe pupil reacts sluggishly or not at
all to both light and accommodation. Radial tears in the pupillary margin are common (Fig. 22.15B).
оАБ Iridodialysis is a dehiscence of the iris from the ciliary body
at its root. оАЮe pupil is typically D-shaped and the dialysis is

seen as a dark biconvex area near the limbus (Fig. 22.15C).
Retroillumination shows the extent of the injury (Fig.
22.15D). An iridodialysis may be asymptomatic if it is
covered by the upper lid. However, monocular diplopia
and glare sometimes ensue if the dehiscence is exposed
in the palpebral aperture. Traumatic aniridia (360¬∞ iridodialysis) is rare. In a pseudophakic eye, the detached iris
may be ejected through the cataract surgical incision (Fig.
22.16A). The symptoms of glare can be reduced and the
cosmetic appearance can be improved by inserting a prosthetic iris (Fig. 22.16B).
оАБ Ciliary body (see below).

Intraocular pressure

It is important for IOP to be monitored carefully, particularly in
the early period following trauma (see Ch. 11). Elevation of IOP
can occur for a variety of reasons, including hyphaema and inоАЪammation. In the presence of hypotony, it is important to exclude an
occult open injury. Tears extending into the face of the ciliary body
(angle recession) are associated with a 6вАУ9% risk of late glaucoma
(Fig. 22.17).

Lens
оАБ Cataract formation is a common sequel to blunt trauma. Postulated mechanisms include direct damage to the lens оАЬbres themselves and minute ruptures in the lens capsule with an inоАЪux of
aqueous humour, hydration of lens оАЬbres and consequent opaciоАЬcation. A ring-shaped anterior subcapsular opacity may underlie a
Vossius ring. Commonly opaciоАЬcation occurs in the posterior subcapsular cortex along the posterior sutures, resulting in a оАЪowershaped (вАШrosetteвАЩ) opacity (Fig. 22.18A) that may subsequently
disappear, remain stationary or progress to maturity (Fig. 22.18B).
Cataract surgery may be necessary for visually signiоАЬcant opacity.
оАБ Subluxation of the lens may occur, secondary to tearing of
the suspensory ligament. A subluxated lens tends to deviate
towards the meridian of intact zonules. оАЮe anterior chamber
may deepen over the area of zonular dehiscence if the lens
rotates posteriorly. оАЮe edge of a subluxated lens may be visible under mydriasis (see Fig. 22.18B) and trembling of the iris
(iridodonesis) or lens (phakodonesis) may be seen on ocular
movement. Subluxation of magnitude suоАЫcient to render the
pupil partly aphakic may result in uniocular diplopia. Lenticular astigmatism due to tilting may occur.

918 Trauma to the Globe

A B

Fig. 22.16 Traumatic aniridia in a pseudophakic eye. (A) Retroillumination showing lens
implant and ciliary processes; (B) prosthetic iris. (Courtesy of C Barry.)

is asymmetry of anterior chamber depth. оАЮe anterior chamber
of an aоАЯected eye is classically deep, with posterior rotation of
the irisвАУlens diaphragm and IOP in the aоАЯected eye is low. оАЮe
rupture is oоАЭen found slightly behind the insertion of the rectus
muscles where the sclera is thinnest. Gentle B-scan ultrasonography can demonstrate a posterior rupture, but CT or MRI may be
necessary. оАЮe principles of repair of a rupture in the sclera are
described later.

Vitreous haemorrhage

Vitreous haemorrhage may occur, commonly in association with
posterior vitreous detachment. Pigment cells (вАШtobacco dustвАЩ) can
be seen оАЪoating in the anterior vitreous and though not necessarily associated with a retinal break, should always prompt careful
retinal assessment.

Fig. 22.17 Gonioscopic appearance of angle recession
(arrowheads indicate the extent of the recession).

оАБ Dislocation due to 360¬∞ rupture of the zonular оАЬbres is rare.
оАЮe lens may dislocate into the vitreous (Fig. 22.18C), or
less commonly, into the anterior chamber (Fig. 22.18D). An
underlying predisposing condition such as pseudoexfoliation
should considered.

Globe rupture

Rupture of the globe may result from severe blunt trauma. оАЮe
prognosis is poor if the initial visual level is light perception
or worse. оАЮe rupture is usually anterior, in the vicinity of the
Schlemm canal, with prolapse of structures such as the lens,
iris, ciliary body and vitreous (Fig. 22.19A and B). An anterior
rupture may be masked by extensive subconjunctival haemorrhage. Rupture at the site of a surgical wound (e.g. cataract, keratoplasty, vitrectomy) commonly follows substantial blunt force.
An occult posterior rupture can be associated with little visible
damage to the anterior segment, but should be suspected if there

Commotio retinae

Commotio retinae is caused by concussion of the sensory retina
resulting in cloudy swelling that gives the involved area a grey
appearance (Fig. 22.20A and B). It most frequently aоАЯects the temporal fundus. If the macula is involved, a вАШcherry-red spotвАЩ may be
seen at the fovea. Severe involvement may be associated with intraretinal haemorrhage that can involve the macula. оАЮe prognosis in
mild cases is good, with spontaneous resolution in around 6 weeks.
Severe commotio may result in progressive pigmentary degeneration (Fig. 22.20C) and macular hole formation.

Chorioretinitis sclopetaria

оАЮis term describes a closed globe injury that follows a highvelocity projectile passing into the orbit close to the outside of the
globe. Deformation from the sudden force causes the chorioretinal
layers to split and retract leaving the intact sclera exposed (Fig.
21.20D). Complications include vitreous haemorrhage and, occasionally, retinal detachment. оАЮe prognosis depends on the cause
of the injury (low muzzle energy has a worse prognosis than a high
muzzle injury), the location of damage and whether the macula is

involved. Most patients experience improvement of vision aоАЭer the
injury, but only 15% achieve 6/6 vision.

Choroidal rupture

Choroidal rupture involves the choroid, Bruch membrane and
retinal pigment epithelium (RPE). It may be direct or indirect. Direct ruptures are located anteriorly at the site of impact
and run parallel with the ora serrata. Indirect ruptures occur
opposite the site of impact. оАЮe visual prognosis is poor if the
fovea is involved (Fig. 22.21A). A fresh rupture may be partially
obscured by subretinal haemorrhage (Fig. 22.21B), which may
break through the internal limiting membrane with resultant
subhyaloid or vitreous haemorrhage. Weeks to months later,
on absorption of the blood, a white crescentic vertical streak
of exposed underlying sclera concentric with the optic disc
becomes visible (Fig. 22.21C and D). An uncommon late complication is the development of a choroidal neovascular membrane in the region of the rupture.

Retinal breaks and detachment

Trauma is responsible for about 10% of all cases of retinal detachment (RD) and is the most common cause in children, particularly
boys. A variety of breaks may develop in traumatized eyes either at
the time of impact or subsequently.
оАБ A retinal dialysis (Fig. 22.22A) is a break occurring at the ora
serrata, caused by traction from the relatively inelastic vitreous gel along the posterior aspect of the vitreous base. оАЮe tear
may be associated with avulsion of the vitreous base, giving
rise to an overhanging вАШbucket-handleвАЩ appearance comprising a strip of ciliary epithelium, ora serrata and the immediate
post-oral retina into which basal vitreous gel remains inserted.
A traumatic dialysis occurs most frequently in the superonasal and inferotemporal quadrants. Although they occur at the
time of injury, they do not inevitably result in RD. In cases
where detachment occurs, subretinal оАЪuid commonly does not
develop until several months later and progression is typically
slow.

оАБ Equatorial breaks are less frequent and are due to direct retinal disruption at the point of scleral impact.
оАБ RD secondary to a giant tear may occasionally be seen (Fig.

оАБ A macular hole may occur either at the time of injury or following resolution of commotio retinae (Fig. 22.22C).

severe injury and needs to be distinguished from a retinal

Traumatic optic neuropathy

Traumatic optic neuropathy follows ocular, orbital or head
trauma and presents with sudden visual loss that cannot be
explained by other ocular pathology. It occurs in up to 5% of
facial fractures.
оАБ ClassiоАЯcation

{ Direct, due to blunt or sharp optic nerve damage from
agents such as displaced bony fragments, a projectile, or
local haematoma.

{ Indirect, in which force is transmitted secondarily to the
nerve without apparent direct disruption due to impacts
upon the eye, orbit or other cranial structures.
оАБ Mechanisms include contusion, deformation, compression or
transection of the nerve, intraneural haemorrhage, shearing
(acceleration of the nerve at the optic canal where it is tethered to the dural sheath, thought to rupture the microvascular
supply), secondary vasospasm, oedema and transmission of a
shock wave through the orbit.
оАБ Presentation. оАЮough major head injury is not unusual, associated trauma may be deceptively minor. Indirect neuropathy
is considerably more common than direct. Vision is oоАЭen very
poor from the outset, with only perception of light in around
50%. Typically, the only objective оАЬnding is an aоАЯerent pupillary defect. оАЮe optic nerve head and fundus are initially normal, with pallor developing over subsequent days and weeks
(Fig. 22.23). It is important to exclude reversible causes of traumatic visual loss such as compressive orbital haemorrhage (see
above).

оАБ Investigation. Assessment should be individualized. Some
clinicians request CT, MR or both for all cases, others limit
imaging to patients with observed visual decline. CT is more
eоАЯective in the demonstration of bony abnormalities such as
optic canal fracture, but MRI is superior for soоАЭ tissue changes
(e.g. haematoma). With either modality, very thin sections are
recommended.
оАБ Treatment. Spontaneous visual improvement occurs in up to
about half of patients with an indirect injury. However, if there
is initially no light perception the prognosis is poor. Several
treatment options have been advocated but no clear beneоАЬt has
been shown and all carry signiоАЬcant risks.

{ Steroids (intravenous methylprednisolone) should be
considered for otherwise healthy patients with severe
visual loss or in those with delayed visual loss. If used,
these should be started within the оАЬrst 8 hours, but the
optimal regimen has not been determined and their

(CRASH study) patients receiving steroids were at higher

{ Optic nerve decompression (e.g. endonasal, transethmoidal) may be considered if there is progressive visual
deterioration despite steroids. Compression of the nerve by
bony fragment or haematoma may also be an indication.
However, optic canal fracture is a poor prognostic indicator and there is no evidence that surgery improves the
outlook, whilst carrying a signiоАЬcant risk of complications.

Optic nerve avulsion

Optic nerve avulsion is rare and typically occurs when an object
intrudes between the globe and the orbital wall, displacing the
eye. Postulated mechanisms include sudden extreme rotation or
anterior displacement of the globe. Avulsion may be isolated or
occur in association with other ocular or orbital injuries. Fundus
examination shows a striking cavity where the optic nerve head
has retracted from its dural sheath (Fig. 22.24A and B). оАЮere is no
treatment and the visual prognosis depends on whether avulsion
is partial or complete.

Abusive head trauma

Abusive head trauma (вАШshaken babyвАЩ syndrome) is a form of physical abuse occurring typically in children under the age of 2 years.
Mortality is more than 25% and it is responsible for up to 50% of
deaths from child abuse. It is caused principally by violent shaking,
oоАЭen in association with impact injury to the head. оАЮese children
should be examined in conjunction with a specialist paediatrician
whenever characteristic ophthalmic features are identiоАЬed. оАЮe
pattern of injury results from rotational acceleration and deceleration of the head, in contrast to the linear forces generated by a fall.
Direct trauma is not the main mechanism of brain damage. Brainstem traction injury causes apnoea and the consequent hypoxia
leads to raised intracranial pressure and ischaemia.

TIP A small child with characteristic ophthalmic features
of abusive head trauma (especially unexplained retinal
haemorrhages) should be examined in conjunction with a
specialist paediatrician.

оАБ Presentation is frequently with irritability, lethargy and vomiting, which may be initially misdiagnosed as gastroenteritis or
other infection because the history of injury is withheld.
оАБ Systemic features may include signs of impact head injury,
ranging from skull fractures to soоАЭ tissue bruises. Subdural
and subarachnoid haemorrhage is common and many survivors suоАЯer substantial neurological handicap. Multiple rib and
long bone fractures may be present. In some cases, examination оАЬndings are limited to the ocular features.
оАБ Ocular features

{ Periocular bruising and subconjunctival haemorrhages
(Fig. 22.25A).

{ Retinal haemorrhages, bilateral or unilateral (20%), are the
most common feature. оАЮe haemorrhages typically involve
multiple layers and may also be pre- or subretinal (Fig.
22.25B). оАЮey are most obvious in the posterior pole, but
oоАЭen extend to the periphery.

{ Poor visual responses and aоАЯerent pupillary defects.

{ Visual loss occurs in about 20% of cases, largely as a result
of cerebral damage.

Penetrating trauma

Introduction

Penetrating injuries are three times more common in males than
females and typically occur in a younger age group (50% aged
15вАУ34). оАЮe most frequent causes are assault, domestic/occupational accidents and sport. Serious eye trauma can be prevented by
the appropriate use of protective eyewear. оАЮe extent of the injury is
determined by the size of the object, its speed at the time of impact

924 Trauma to the Globe

A B

Fig. 22.25 Abusive head trauma (shaken baby syndrome). (A) Subconjunctival haemorrhage;
(B) fundus haemorrhages involving diоАЭerent levels. (Courtesy of G Kontos вАУ оАЯg. A; R Bates вАУ
